Original_Search_Title: NDDC Moves to Stop Youth Restiveness in N/Delta
Headline: Nigeria: NDDC Moves to Stop Youth Restiveness in N/Delta
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) has embarked on several youth empowerment schemes that would drastically reduce youth restiveness in the Niger Delta region.
Ambassador Sam Edem, Chairman of the NDDC told media executives at an end-of-year dinner in Lagos that the commission had just started the full implementation of the Niger Delta Regional Master Plan geared towards the massive generation of jobs and provision of infrastructure in the oil-rich region.
He said that the commission has in recent times shifted emphasis to agriculture, aquaculture and skill acquisition programmes to provide jobs for the youth of the Niger Delta.
Edem said that NDDC has specifically given attention to the massive cultivation of cassava in all the nine states, adding that 495 hectares of its cassava will be ready for harvest between January and February next year.
Also, according to the NDDC boss, the commission has planted rice in 300 hectares in Akwa Ibom state and will soon cultivate another 700 hectares in Delta ,Rivers,Cross Rivers and Ondo state.
In order to further boost rice production in the region,the NDDC has completed arrangements for the rehabilitation of the Peremabiri rice project in Bayelsa and the establishment of a 50 tonne per hour rice processing factory in Akwa Ibom state.
Ambassador Edem said the commission has trained about 9,500 youths in various skills acquisition and has completed plans to double that figure next year in order to enhance their ability to acquire new jobs or to become self employed.
Ambassador Edem said that the NDDC recently intervened in the failed portions of the East-west road between Benin and Ore in Ondo State to ease the problem of transportation during the yuletide and New Year festivities. It would be recalled that the NDDC has been maintaining the portion of the road between Port Harcourt and Warri for the past four years.

Original_Search_Title: Where Eight Or Ten Are Gathered...
Headline: Uganda: Where Eight Or Ten Are Gathered...
Body_Text:
Kampala — ... Nsenene is in their midst. If you find more than 20 people gathered in a brightly lit bushy area at midnight, the reason is most likely, nsenene. Whether in Masaka or Nabisunsa, wherever the grasshoppers land, they attract a large crowd, including children and adults
If you find more than 20 people gathered in a brightly lit bushy area at midnight, the reason is most likely, nsenene. The nsenene, an edible type of grasshopper, reach full maturity and fly out of the ground in the rainy season, which occurs twice a year from April to May and October through November in Uganda.
Whether in Masaka or Nabisunsa, wherever the grasshoppers land, they attract a large crowd, including children and adults who gather at various grasshopper-collecting sites day or night, groping in damp thickets in an attempt to catch the feisty insects.
While it is estimated that about 1,000 families are directly involved in large-scale grasshopper collection, there are countless other people whose lives change with the arrival of the nsenene.
In temperate zones, grasshoppers spend most of their life underground as eggs, and as young and adult, grasshoppers live only up to three months. The first nymph to hatch tunnels up through the ground, and the rest follow. To those with a sharp nose for business, the nsenene season isn't just another chance to feast on grasshoppers; it is an opportunity to rake in the big bucks. A few people have made a fortune from trapping this winged delicacy. Some of these have reportedly bought land and Kamunye taxi vans, among other possessions.
John Ssekamate, one of those involved in the business validates the claims. 'Quite a number of people look forward to the season as an opportunity. Catch as many (grasshoppers) as you can, and you will realise more profits. And depending what one's status is, people have become millionaires," he says.
Ssekamatte owns about 42 metallic drums in Nateete, a grasshopper-picking site on the outskirts of Kampala. The grasshopper collectors insert iron sheets into empty drums, which they then lean against a pole and a wired lighting system. Attracted to the light, the nsenene hit the iron sheets and slide into the drum where they are trapped.
Ssekamatte explains that each drum has a capacity of 100 kilos of grasshoppers and with a 'good catch' it would amount to two sackfuls of nsenene.
Each sack, which is normally half full to allow the grasshoppers breathing space, is sold at Shs25, 000 during a good season and at Ushs70, 000 in times of scarcity. Ssekamatte says that this year's sales have not been as good as previous years owing to the persistent rains.
He claims that they caught many more grasshoppers in the early 90s. Kalule Nkonge, who owns 13 drums in the Nateete area adds that problems like the cholera threat have cost them a lot of business ever since the Ministry of Health cautioned the public against eating the grasshoppers and other snacks sold in markets and along the roadside. Hiring a generator when there is load shedding is another problem for the Nsenene businessmen.
The grasshopper business is short-lived and one has to make the best out of it. The picking season will go on for approximately a month and when the season is over, the pickers must find other jobs. Kalule works in a furniture shop, a stone's throwaway from the nsenene catching site while Ssekamate is a casual labourer.
When the nsenene have been caught, they are packed by the sack load and brought to city markets. At Nakasero market, you can buy anything from chicken, mops, vanilla beans, pineapples, electrical wires and during the rainy seasons of April and May or October and November, grasshoppers.
Mawe Robert, 23, is just one of the six or so men and a lone woman who alternate between lounging and selling fried nsenene in woven baskets and competing for customers while chewing on the same grasshoppers. They flank the passageway to a smaller walled-in complex where Nalongo Aisha, 37, one of four women who cook the nsenene with diced green peppers and purple onions in big vats of oil. Beads of sweat cover her face as she takes kilos of grasshoppers from wiggling and live to fried.
With sharp white teeth, no doubt aided by the nsenene's high nutritional value, a shaved head, and ears that jut out from his head, Mawe is a self-made business man who turns a quick profit during the grasshopper season to support his family of seven and save a little.
He has ten assistants helping him trap the sacks of grasshoppers (he doesn't pay them - they just get to keep some) and four women preparing the insects for frying. Mawe turns the green bugs into translucent golden brown fried treats.
Mawe starts his day at six in the morning, unloading the previous night's catch at
Nakasero Market. He sells all day, goes home for supper and to wash up, then from 9.00 p.m until three in the morning, works alongside his team catching nsenene.
However, his day isn't over at three: from that point on, he removes the grasshoppers from the large drums in which they are collected and puts them in sacks. Mawe finishes his work at five in the morning just in time to start the next day.
Mawe started working with grasshoppers the hard way: plucking their legs and wings, a tedious task that's hard on both the hands and the pocket. The four girls who now work for Mawe each make only Shs4,000 per day (about $2).
While one of the girls had been working at it for two years, the others are a few days old on the job. The girls, between 13 and 20 years old, each complained of wounds on their fingers from the grasshoppers' sharp thorax. Their hands were covered in brown sap and cassava flour and also showed telltale signs of their work: cuts and calluses.
After working for a while at Nakasero Market, a generous friend who owns several taxi buses in town lent Mawe Shs2 m to get started. The technical set up is simple: some guys in Masaka figured out the innovative and relatively inexpensive way to trap hordes of grasshoppers back in 1995 using high wattage florescent bulbs attached to long tin sheets tilted at an angle and tin drums.
Outside the trapping complex, the entire community from Kamwokya suburb gathers nightly to collect the stray grasshoppers that escape the tins and fall to the ground. Children and some adults chase after the nsenene blinded by the florescent light, plucking them off each other's clothes, scrambling to get a position closer to the wall to catch more castoffs, all eager to collect more themselves; some for money and some for snacks. For the most part, its all fun and games - everyone gets to stay up past their bedtime and go to school groggy but with a belly full of a high-protein treat.
But it isn't always fun and games. There are many documented cases of people chasing grasshoppers into the streets and getting hit by cars; others electrocuted while setting up the grasshopper traps. Despite his hours of toil, at the end of the day, Mawe only manages to save about Shs 2,500 per day.
He left school after Senior Two, and he expects to pick up where he left off in the next few years if he keeps saving at his current rate. For now though, money is always the problem. As the eldest child, he's expected to bring home enough money to support his family. The only time he can do this is during nsenene season.
The rest of the year, he sells plastic utensils at a booth in the market, bringing home Shs3, 000, only when he can. "When I don't earn anything, I cannot be happy, I can't go home and see my family," he says.
Mawe doesn't want his brothers to follow him into this business because he hopes they'll find better, more stable jobs. But in the meantime, he'll keep at it. He likes his work, gets a thrill from the sell. And he doesn't mind eating the unlimited grasshoppers either.
"Also me, I like to chew them. I chew on one each time," he says, popping another fried creature into his mouth.

Original_Search_Title: New Law Enhances US Efforts to Assist, Provide Funds to Congo
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: New Law Enhances US Efforts to Assist, Provide Funds to Congo
Publisher: United States Department of State (Washington, DC)
Body_Text:
Washington, DC — At least $52 million annually for fiscal years 2006 and 2007 will be provided to the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) under legislation signed December 22 by President Bush. These funds are intended to enhance the United States' and international organizations' efforts to fight political, social and economic challenges facing the African nation.
The act provides for bilateral assistance programs and establishes U.S. political, social, civil and economic policy objectives for the DRC, a country that earlier in 2006 held its first fully democratic election since 1960. (See related article.)
Recent conflicts in the nation have resulted in 4 million dead, 800,000 refugees scattered throughout nine neighboring countries and the collapse of state services.
The act states that the United States should work with international partners to encourage other nations to increase their contributions to the DRC. International donors, led primarily by the United Nations and the European Union, were active in the DRC in the months leading up to the election by providing aid, funds and peacekeeping support to the nation. For the DRC to continue to make progress, the international community must maintain its support, experts on the region said in a panel discussion at the Brookings Institution in Washington December 18. (See related article.)
The government of the DRC must be committed to achieving the policy objectives outlined by the United States and other international donors, the act states. The act allows the U.S. secretary of state to withhold assistance if the government of the DRC does not make sufficient progress toward accomplishing these objectives.
The act also recommends that the U.S. president appoint a special envoy for Africa's Great Lakes region to help improve stability and security in that region of Africa.
U.S. AID HELPS IMPROVE DRC'S ECONOMY, democratic institutions
The act also will provide funds that could complement current U.S. projects to improve health care, agriculture, democratic institutions and education in the DRC.
The United States Agency for International Development (USAID) currently provides key health services in the country, including malaria treatments, routine vaccinations and HIV/AIDS prevention and care. It also supports a program to help farmers grow more and better crops, and distributes improved seeds. As part of this program, USAID has assisted 50,000 farmers in growing cassava, a staple crop in the DRC.
In addition, USAID trains Congolese teachers and helps to encourage and assist girls' education by providing scholarships. The agency also has trained local communities in how to repair roads destroyed in conflicts. (See related article.)
Leading up to the election, the United States helped establish democratic institutions, new political laws and electoral guidelines and structures. It continues to help the nation maintain its democratic institutions.
(USINFO is produced by the Bureau of International Information Programs, U.S. Department of State. Web site: http://usinfo.state.gov)

Original_Search_Title: Rebuilding the Breadbasket After the Conflict
Headline: Congo-Brazzaville: Rebuilding the Breadbasket After the Conflict
Body_Text:
Rome — IFAD’s latest programme in the Congo is set to start in December 2006 around the town of Dolisie in the south-west. During the civil wars in the 1990s the region was the scene of widespread destruction.
Agricultural production capacities were ruined. Three quarters of the livestock had either been killed or looted. There had been massive movements of civilians fleeing to the capital, Brazzaville, and to Pointe Noire, the main port on the Atlantic coast.
The Nyari, Lekoumou and Bouenza departments were the breadbasket of the populated south before the conflict started, says Luyaku Nsimpasi, IFAD’s country programme manager for the Congo.
Most donors in the country have concentrated on the emergency and relief aspects. IFAD’s focus is on the revival of production capacity. The priorities are to give the rural population access to drinkable water and to improved seeds and cuttings for main food crops such as cassava. The Rural Development Project in the Nyari, Bouenza and Lékoumou Departments will also focus on market access, with the building and maintenance of feeder roads.
The main challenge to working in a post-conflict situation has been addressing the lack of formal institutions in the programme area.
That has been a major obstacle, says Nsimpasi. A programme must have the support of local institutions. When they have all but disappeared or are in disrepair, in a post-conflict situation, we have to rely on farmers organizations, local communities, NGOs, as the link between IFAD and the rural population.
Nsimpasi says that he really hopes the programme can start as planned in December 2006, after the loan agreement was signed in May 2006. The objective is that this southern region becomes again the thriving breadbasket it once was.

Original_Search_Title: Setting Up Farmers' Field Schools for Cassava Producers
Headline: Cameroon: Setting Up Farmers' Field Schools for Cassava Producers
Body_Text:
Rome, Italy — With public demand for cassava rising rapidly in Cameroon, new field schools to improve farmer productivity have been set up across the country over the past two years by the Roots and Tubers Market-Driven Development Programme (PNDRT).
Over 300 farmers were trained in 2005 to use a new technology package that helps to increase production and protect crops.
The programme has been so successful that we have doubled the number of schoolsÂ fields to 20 this year, says Andre Mbairanodji, the officer in charge of production and processing for the PNDRT.
The field schools are organized in the same villages where the farmers who are being trained live. Usually two parcels of land are cultivated next to each other: one according to the farmers’ traditional knowledge and the other using new technologies introduced by the schools.
The trainees are shown different ploughing techniques and are made aware of soil conservation issues that are crucial in the case of cassava as it is known to impoverish soils, says Mbairanodji. They are also trained on how to fight against pests, using biological means such as useful insects against bad insects attacking the crops. They are also told about the use of chemicals in the production of cassava in case the above methods to fight against predators are not efficient. All this is done using better varieties of cassava that are healthier and more productive.
The fields are visited every month so the farmers can see for themselves how differently both sets of crops are growing. They can see the way the leaves have grown, or whether diseases are developing. At the end of the training, the production from both parcels of land is weighed and their productivity compared.
About 80 per cent of the trainees are women as the production of roots and tubers is traditionally their domain. Men are also involved in the training’s harder work, such as uprooting tree stumps or building hillocks. Our target is to double the national cassava production in the near future, says Mbairanodji. But we are also planning to focus on other crops such as yam, potatoes and sweet potatoes.
The trainees have also been introduced to marketing techniques to make sure that they will be able to sell their soon-to-be-increased production. The main aim is to facilitate the exchange of information along the market chain.
Sometimes farmers don’t know what to do with their harvest when just a few kilometres away there are buyers looking for crops and vice versa, says Mbairanodji. Surely these people can start talking to each other.
Related links:
Rural poverty in Cameroon
Improving marketing strategies in Central and West Africa

Original_Search_Title: Aggrieved Farmers Threaten to Take Over Secondary School
Headline: Ghana: Aggrieved Farmers Threaten to Take Over Secondary School
Body_Text:
Twifu Praso — ABOUT 65 Aggrieved farmers of Twifu Praso in the Central Region whose lands were affected by the acquisition of new site by government to elevate Twifu Praso Secondary School into a model one, are threatening to take over the school in a "military style" for lack of compensation.
Speaking to The Chronicle last Friday at Twifu-Praso, Mr Mark Abraham, the Secretary of the affected farmers, Nana Assiedu III, Gyasehen of Twifu Traditional Area, Thomas Kwesi Effah, landlord and a farmer as well as Kwabena Agyei-Boafo, landlord and victim, said they were ready to prevent students from occupying the new school when school reopens on January 16, 2007 because government had not paid an amount of ¢1.769 billion due them.
The students are expected to move from their old site to the new site, located at Twifo Daman, close to the Pra River next year when school reopens.
Mark Abraham told the paper that in 2003, a bulldozer grazed their land and destroyed cocoa farms, oil palm plantations, citrus farms, cassava and other crops without consulting them.
According to him, three farmers, who could no longer bear the hardships, had died as a result of the forceful destruction of their crops, which served as their main source of livelihood.
He alleged that Mr. Abraham Dwuma Odoom, Deputy Local Government Minister, who was then the District Chief Executive of Twifu-Hemang-Lower-Denkyira District (THLD) was behind their woes because he (Odoom) had informed government that he had acquired the land from its owners free for the establishment of the school.
Irate Abraham said, Mr. Oduom never consulted them and there was no document to prove that they consented to the building of that school, which is 85% completed. He said although they were not against the establishment of the school, they have been cheated since the former DCE failed to consult them on the land's acquisition and consequent failure to compensate the farmers of their destroyed crops.
They are now yelling for compensation of their destroyed crops with interest before they negotiate with government on the forcefully acquisition of the land.
Mr. Abraham said on June 14, 2004, they registered their displeasure by demonstrating in presence of Hon. Kwadwo Baah Wiredu, Finance and Economic Planning Minister while inspecting progress of work on the school with TV3 crew from Accra. After demonstrating, they petitioned President Kufuor but wondered whether their petition got to the President.
Nana Assiedu, he Gyasehen said the Ministry of Education and Ghana Education Service were refusing to pay the compensation because Mr. Odoom told them that he acquired the land on free basis.
When contacted, Mr. Agyeibi-Kessie, DCE of THLD, confirming the story, said the farmers had not been compensated for the past three years and that he was doing everything possible to enable them receive their compensation.
All efforts to get Mr. Oduom's side of the story proved futile as he refused to answer several phones calls throughout the weekend to Monday.

Original_Search_Title: Leaders in South Sign New Performance Contracts
Headline: Rwanda: Leaders in South Sign New Performance Contracts
Body_Text:
Kigali — As the year draws to the end, local leaders in the southern province have intensified the signing of new performance contracts aimed at guiding them on service-delivery next year.
On Sunday, December 17, after reviewing the achievements registered during the year 2006, the leaders discussed modalities of overcoming the challenges in 2007 and signed it as their guiding performance contract.
They also agreed to make a five-year development plan aimed at wiping out poverty and famine.
"The biggest challenge in the southern province is that we were unable to store some food as we had planned, leading to premature destruction of most crops. We did not harvest enough and therefore did not keep some produce in our stores as had been planned," the Governor of the Southern Province, Eraste Kabera said.
The leaders pledged to encourage residents to intensify the growing of food crops as cassava, maize, and fruits while concentrating on cash crops as coffee and tea next year. Other activities in the contracts include focusing on crafts, tourism and formation of about 100 youth associations.

Original_Search_Title: Bugesera Food Problem to Be Solved
Headline: Rwanda: Bugesera Food Problem to Be Solved
Body_Text:
Kigali — Farmers in Bugesera District, Eastern Province have pledged to end the food shortage in the area.
Speaking to The New Times last week, Odette Mukankuranga, the Coordinator of Cooperative Inyingi y'ubuhinzi, disclosed various initiatives like rice-growing, aimed at reversing the problem. With 948 active members of the cooperative, the Kibaza wetland was reclaimed in 2003, with the help of World Food Programme (WFP). It has since managed to produce 240 tonnes of rice per year from only thirty hectares, Mukankuranga said.
She said the production was expected to double after another twenty the wetland which between Ruhuha and Ngeruka Sectors are ploughed.
In a related development, a group of ninety youth under the Jeunesse Ouvieres Catholique (JOC) association has engaged in cassava production.

Original_Search_Title: MP Spends ¢398 Million On Projects
Headline: Ghana: MP Spends Â¢398 Million On Projects
Body_Text:
The Member of Parliament (MP) for the Kpone-Katamanso Constituency, Hon. Nii Laryea Afotey Agbo, a.k.a. Lion has undertaken a number of development projects throughout the constituency with his share of the HIPC and GETFund funds.
According to him, he has spent about ¢398 million on the various projects he has undertaken in his constituency between January and December, this year.
Speaking in an interview with the paper last Saturday, the Kpone Katamanso legislator said he has purchased three (3) corn-mills and three (3) cassava grinding machines at a cost of ¢33 million out of his share of the HIPC Funds for some women at three villages in the constituency namely; Gonteng, Old Saasabi and Mensah Bar, for income-generating activities.
He continued that about a month ago, he gave out ¢60 million out of his share of the HIPC Funds to be used to purchase electricity poles, to help provide electricity to the people of Zeenu, also in his constituency.
Lion, further explained that he gave out also ¢30 million, again from his share of the HIPC Funds for work to be carried out on the Ashaiman Jerusalem Community and Zeenu Dam site project.
"I have also bought light poles worth ¢20 million to be used to help extend electricity to residents of Kubekro Numbers 1 & 2, all villages in my constituency, from my share of the GETFund.
"Out of my GETFund resources, I have also given out ¢100 million for the painting and provision of electric ceiling fans for the Primary and Junior Secondary Schools (JSS) buildings at the Michel Camp, also in my constituency", the Hon. MP stated.
According to him, he has purchased a number of light poles to be erected in areas such as Kpone, Zeenu, Kpone-Kokompe and Gbezile all in his constituency and bulbs at a cost of ¢155 million also from the GETFund adding "In fact the workers have already started erecting the light poles in some of the areas".

Original_Search_Title: Biofuel - Nigeria Needs 7bn KG of Cassava Annually
Headline: Nigeria: Biofuel - Nigeria Needs 7bn KG of Cassava Annually
Body_Text:
The ambition by Nigeria to achieve 10 percent ethanol for fuel can only be attained when production of cassava grows to about 7 billion kilograms annually, a research has shown.
The International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA), Ibadan which revealed the outcome of a research recently said the 10 percent attainment of Ethanol in fuel would require massive production of cassava and sugarcane.
Under the biofuel initiative of the federal government the ethanol programme is expected to improve automotive exhaust emissions in the country, reduce domestic use of petrol, free up more crude for export and position Nigeria for development of green field.
IITA research revealed that ethanol is generally produced by the fermentation of sugar, cellulose, or converted starch. Preliminary estimates are that the Nigerian market could absorb 3 million liters of ethanol daily.
An analysis showed that the current national average Cassava yield of 15 tons per hectare will be marginal while the current sugarcane yield of 60 tons per hectare are sufficient. To this end, improving Cassava yield becomes an imperative, said the Group Management Director of the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporations (NNPC) Engr. Funsho Kupolokun recently.
He said Nigeria would be richer by $150m (about N21bn) annually when the bio-fuel initiative comes into being.
Kupolokun said as part of the implementation of the project, special research initiatives would be sponsored by NNPC to boost cassava and palm-oil output within the country and that the venture would lead to the setting up of several ethanol production plants at an average cost of $60m each. Nigeria and Brazil had signed a pact to enable the later to add ethanol to gasoline (which should begin in 2006).
Business Trust gathered that total production capacity of cassava in the country is within the range of 150,000 metric tone.
Since 2003 the demand of cassava in the country has grown after the introduction of the presidential Initiative on cassava by President Obasanjo government.
IITA said about 7 varieties of cassava are recommended for their high yield and processing quality: TMS 30572, NR 8082, NR8083, TMS 4(2) 1425, TMS 81/00110, TMS 92/0326 adding that an additional 10 varieties are in the process of being released.

Original_Search_Title: Tens of Thousands of Villagers On the Run
Headline: Central African Republic: Tens of Thousands of Villagers On the Run
Body_Text:
Kaga Bandoro — Around Dikba Elisi's oldest child's neck is a plastic whistle, so that if the family is forced to flee into maize fields and the children get lost, the adults have a better chance of finding them.
Elisi, her three young children and sister, are living under two pieces of tin they salvaged, sleeping on a single bed, hidden in swaying fields three kilometres behind their old village. Another attack, she said, was unlikely - there was nothing left to burn or steal since the family's straw-roofed shack was razed to the ground in mid-November and all the family's furniture, food and money were stolen.
"All you see here is what we saved - the rest was burned or stolen," Elisi said.
The family is among 220,000 people estimated by the United Nations in its 2007 funding appeal to have been displaced internally in the Central African Republic (CAR) and into Chad and Cameroon as a result of conflict. About 45,000 refugees are in southern Chad, living in four sites at Amboko, Yaroungou, Gondje and Dosseye run by the UN refugee agency, UNHCR.
On a trip through the north-central region of CAR last week, in village after village IRIN saw the charred ruins of houses and harvests. On the road north of the government-controlled town of Kaga Bandoro, 300 km north of Bangui, the capital, aid agencies counted 1,700 houses burned on one stretch less than 100 km long, with thousands more believed to have been targeted elsewhere.
There is no front-line in this war - just villages in areas held by non-government forces; those held by army loyalists; and the zones in between. The porous boundary between them has shifted steadily south and east since armed raids first started around the northern CAR towns of Paoua, Markounda and Boguila in June 2005.
By April this year the rebel movement had a name: l'Armée populaire pour la restauration de la république et la démocratie (APRD), headed by Lt Bedaya N'Djadder, a former gendarme who defected from the government's service.
In October, another front opened up in the remote northeast of the country, with a new movement, the Union des forces démocratiques pour le rassemblement (UFDR), which has said it is protesting against the 'exclusionist' policy of the CAR government, claiming it has an ethnic bias.
According to diplomats and analysts, the CAR is now split into two distinct geographical zones - the sparsely populated northeast, and the heavily populated north-central area, the focus of current fighting. A third dynamic is in the remote far west of CAR on the border with Cameroon. There, banditry and kidnappings have prompted 30,000 people to flee into Cameroon.
Since the beginning of November, the regular army has been burning villages around Kaga Bandoro, say local villagers. "About 2,000 houses were set on fire on the road between Kaga Bandoro and Ouandago," Albert Vambuel, the archbishop of Kaga Bandoro, told IRIN.
The government denies the accusations. In an interview on 14 December, Colonel Jean Christophe Bureau, the prefect of the town of Kaga Bandoro, said the rebels were living with villagers who refuse to collaborate with the national army to restore order in the region. "The army is using rockets and mortars to retaliate and these weapons sometimes burnt houses," he said, rejecting allegations that the army deliberately set fire to houses.
Vambuel added that agricultural produce was also destroyed in the fires. "The rebels are in areas well known by the army but the soldiers do not want to go after them," he said, adding that rebels were using villagers as a shield.
According to Refugees International, in a statement on 14 December, conditions are dire for displaced people who desperately need shelter, food, healthcare, clothing, blankets, soap, and potable water. The absence or limited availability of clean water and medical care has also raised the risk of diseases such as malaria and typhoid.
In Kaga Bandoro, about 600 families had moved from their villages into the town. "We are caring for 3,000 displaced people to whom we are distributing food such as soya, sugar and cooking oil," said Jean-Blaise Nguepoukpoudou, the head of the Catholic humanitarian NGO, CARITAS.
CARITAS said it needed more supplies. It had received 18 tonnes of food from the United Nations World Food Programme. Médecins sans Frontières (MSF) is also distributing food, despite the difficulties in gaining access to some areas after rebels destroyed bridges. "Security is the main need," Teresa San Cristobal of MSF. "There is an absolute lack of protection."
Refugees International urged government to stop the army, including the presidential guard, from committing human-rights abuses, and to hold perpetrators accountable. "People are subsisting on mostly cassava and wild roots since some of the markets have been destroyed by bandits and agriculture has been interrupted by the insecurity," it noted while calling on international agencies to respond to the crisis.
For a country with a life expectancy of 41 years, which is ranked 169 out of 175 countries on the Human Development Index, the situation in the north-central region only makes matters worse.
At the launch of the appeal for US$49.5 million for humanitarian projects in CAR in 2007, on 30 November, the UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs stated: "Regional dynamics and the success or failure of democratic consolidation and socio-economic recovery make the CAR one of the world's most fragile yet unknown crises, and potentially a hotspot threatening international peace and security in Central Africa."
For Elisi and her children living out in the open fields, exposed and scared, there is no hope of returning home yet. "We are cold, the children are hungry, and we are all getting sick. We cannot go back to the village, but we cannot stay here. I do not know what we will do," she told IRIN.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Why Nigeria Must Be Africa's Financial Hub-Soludo
Headline: Africa: Why Nigeria Must Be Africa's Financial Hub-Soludo
Body_Text:
Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) Governor, Professor Charles Soludo, has said that the goal of the Financial System Strategy 2020, which is to make Nigeria Africa's financial hub, is important to sustaining the nation's economy.
Speaking, last week, on "Financial System Strategy 2020: The Next Development Frontier", at the second edition of NTA annual public lecture series, Soludo observed that, "as Africa's potential largest economy, it needs to be the dominant financial services market for sustainable economy."
He explained that, "Usually, a financial hub has much larger financial services than the requirement of the local economy. Examples of such hub are those of New York, London, Hong Kong, Singapore, Tokyo, Dubai, Zurich and Seoul. The closest hubs to Nigeria are Dubai, Zurich and London."
He noted that South Africa is a mini-hub for Southern Africa while Nigeria is a mini-hub for Western and Central Africa. But, no major hub exists in Africa. "The Gulf of Guinea oil exploration and its collateral fallouts, along with the positive outcome of reforms across Africa, places Nigeria at the vortex of an emerging international financial activity. If Nigeria does not act to take advantage of this emerging opportunity, another nation will move to fill the emerging need," he averred.
Soludo observed that, "Several countries have used the concept of the hub to orchestrate economic growth namely Dubai, Singapore, Hong Kong. Some hubs grew organically out of natural need and historical developments namely Londan, Zurich and New York. Some grew out of circumstance and accident of history namely Hong Kong, while others grew out of deliberate decisions to create them and use them to attract and retain developments namely Dubai, Singapore and Panama."
Nigeria, he noted, has the advantage of location, large economy, language, population size and potential skill, adding that the country, however, does not have the luxury of waiting to grow organically, hence, it is, therefore, proposed that it adopts, deliberately, the engineering approach which is through deliberate policies and actions.
Soludo observed further that the country has the needed economic potentials to support its emergence as Africa's financial hub. He said, for example, "Nigeria comprise 79 per cent of the gross domestic product (GDP) of ECOWAS sub-region. One in every six blacks in the world is a Nigerian; The country is the world's sixth in gas reserves; Nigeria's foreign direct investment (FDI) is one of the highest in Africa. It has one of the highest returns on investment in real dollar terms.
Furthermore, he noted, "Nigeria has sustained macroeconomic stability, including stable exchange and appreciating local currency, backed by the highest level of reserves, in Africa, of about $42 billion. Nigeria has a rapidly growing non-oil sector, which is recording phenomenal growth in some sectors, with telecoms growing at 30 per cent per annum. In cassava, it is now world's highest producer, solid minerals, services and even manufacturing are growing and Nigeria's banking sector is the fastest growing in Africa while the growth of the nation's capital market is about the fastest in the world." He said, in addition to these, "Nigeria's base GDP (2005 year end) was $113 billion and the average growth rate for 2003 to 2004 has been 7.4 per cent while Non-oil grew at about 8.8 per cent.
Also, more than half of Nigeria's population is made up of teenagers or youths, which implies a vibrant population for the future which is necessary to unleash and take advantage of economic growth".

Original_Search_Title: Posho is One of the Reasons Africans Have Remained Dull
Headline: Uganda: Posho is One of the Reasons Africans Have Remained Dull
Body_Text:
Kampala — EVER since Vice-President Gilbert Bukenya criticised maize as a staple food in schools, several people have raised dust about his statement. Head teachers claim that change of menu will mean a hike in school fees. Other people have doubted Prof. Bukenya's nutritional education.
I am neither a nutritionist nor a head teacher, but a parent whose children feed on posho and beans daily in school.
And, as someone who has been through school, it is difficult for me to doubt a medical professor's word, especially about nutritional needs.
Parents have deliberately abandoned their children to a worthless way of life in terms of diet. It is easier to argue about how we have been feeding on maize for ages and how maize is very useful, than to question why, in all these years, Uganda and Africa in general, still depends on 80% manual labour for agriculture, with no brains to add value to what we produce.
Most Africans have weak brains because of what they eat. Since we believe that what we eat has brought us this far, we may not see anything wrong with it. We only believe it is the people who criticise it who have a problem.
I know many people do not eat posho in their homes in the same proportions their children do at school. Why, then, do we deny the fact that our children are fed poorly in schools, where we pay so dearly.
The World Health Organisation (WHO) says hunger and malnutrition remain some of the most devastating problems facing the majority of the world's poor.
Nearly 30% of humanity is suffering from one or more forms of malnutrition, disability, mental and physical retardation. This in turn retards national socio-economic development.
About 60% of the 10.9 million children under five years in the developing world every year die of malnutrition. Iodine deficiency, the greatest single preventable cause of brain damage and mental retardation, affects more than 700 million people, most of them in developing countries.
Over 2,000 million people suffer from iron deficiency. Vitamin A deficiency remains the single greatest preventable cause of childhood blindness.
Intrauterine growth retardation, resulting in low birth weight and mental retardation affects 30 million babies annually.
Need we, then, question Prof. Bukenya's concerns about the nutritional needs of our children? However, if people want to know why he is using rice as the best tool, there is evidence.
Dr Salon Florentino, a public health and nutrition specialist, says rice has the highest food value compared to other cereals.
The Consultative Group on International Agricultural Research micronutrient project is working to improve human nutrition through staple foods including wheat, rice, maize, cassava and beans, by boosting their iron, zinc, Vitamin A and iodine content. So far, they have described rice as a food with a lot of minerals and vitamins.
According to the Food and Agricultural Organisation (FAO), maize has three uses: It is a human food, it is feed for livestock, and it is raw material for industry. In developed countries more than 60% of the maize produced is used in poultry and animal feeds.
In recent years, even in developing countries where maize is grown as a staple food, most of it has been used as an ingredient in animal feeds.
James Mulwana, a dairy farmer, feeds his cows on maize to increase milk yields.
In 2004, FAO celebrated the International year of rice under the theme, "Rice is life". According to FAO, rice is the predominant staple food in 34 countries worldwide and provides 20% of the world's dietary energy supply, while wheat supplies 19% and maize 5%.
The general feeling here is that rice is for the rich only. The poor can, if they are lucky, taste it only on festive days. The difference between the cost of rice and posho could be as little as sh200. Rice is quicker and easier to cook, making it cheaper in terms of time and fuel.
If school farms included rice in their agricultural activities, they would complement the school diet and lower food costs.
Instead of criticising Prof. Bukenya's advice, why don't we find alternatives to improve our children's nutrition. We do not have to feed them poorly just because that is the way our parents fed us. They need to think and plan better, because they hold the future of this nation.
The writer is the press secretary to the vice president

Original_Search_Title: Food Security . . . Taking Africa Out of the Hunger Pit
Headline: Africa: Food Security . . . Taking Africa Out of the Hunger Pit
Body_Text:
Lagos — The role of agriculture in addressing the continent's economic development is a foregone conclusion. Business as usual will not improve food productivity on the continent. Africa needs to change its ways in order to be able to feed its people and ensure its main source of economic development - agriculture - grows and develops. Prosperity by the farmer will influence economic growth and the management and handling of other factors such as infrastructure. Innovative agriculture, acquired through novel relationships with technology developers, and developed through mutually satisfactory relationships with partners in research, development and commercialisation, should lead to prosperity for the smallholder farmer, to food security and economic growth for Africa.
Sixty-eight-year-old Mr. Chijioke Ibekwem (not real name) has known no other occupation since his childhood apart from farming. As soon as the first signs of rain are noticed every year, he gets his family ready for farming. While Egonma, his wife of 40 years and their three young children will concentrate on maize, cassava, melon ad other such crops that ar regarded as fit only women, Chjioke concentrates on yam, which the Igbo regard as the king of crops. Year after year the Ibekwem family will till the same land, cultivate the same crops, use the same methods and get almost the same results.
The sad story of the Ibekwems can be replicated all over Africa. Old farmers, whose legs and hands are tired, hold sway in various parts of the continent. Poor quality crops, both food and cash, remain the basis of agriculture in various parts of Africa with the result that harvest does not match the labour imput.
It was in order to reverse the trend that the Nigerian goververnment recently hosted a Food Security Summit in Abuja. The summit was declared open by President Olusegun Obasanjo. The objective was to address the critical food security problem in Africa with a view to finding a lasting solution.
One of the organisations invited to make presentation at the Abuja summit is the African Agricultural Technology Foundation (AATF) based in Nairobi, Kenya. Dr Mpoko Bokanga, executive director of the foundation represented the foundation at the summit. A paper made available to THISDAY stating the foundation's vision, notes:
"The world has declared war on hunger - but in Africa, images abound of pot-bellied, emaciated, wide-eyed and lifeless children - some still sucking their dead mother's breasts - stretching out empty bowls for something anything to arrest the pangs of hunger they suffer; of vultures, waiting patiently for the only sign of surviving human life - the little boy on stick thin legs - to take his last breath before it pounces.
"The world has declared war on conflicts and violence - but in Africa, images abound of starry-eyed boy soldiers, innocence snatched from them, dragging weighty AK 47 rifles through the battle fields; of the helpless father, his manhood taken away, trekking through the long winding dusty road to nowhere his little boy on his shoulders and his worldly possessions - a thin strip of over used sponge that serves as his mattress -- strapped to his back, just to put distance between him and the horrors he leaves behind.
"The world has declared war on disease - but in Africa, images abound of the old lady, with sunken cheeks and wide eyes - they say she is only 21 - lying in her sagging single bed in the carton and polythene hovel she calls home hoping for a good Samaritan to pass by.
"These are the images of Africa that speak of Africa and tell the African story. These are the images that splatter overseas media, from which Africa gains its reputation. But Africa can be beautiful. Where are the beautiful and melodious sounds of African children playing hoop, tending to their parent's animals and playing tricks on each other? Where are the sounds of singing that fill the air as Africans celebrate bumper harvests or the marriage of their children or the coming of age of their youngsters? Where are the voices of women happily sharing stories as they harvest in the fields among chirping birds and children happily playing around them? Where are the images of the proud African peoples standing majestically and beautifully for all to see?
A revival of Africa's agriculture is necessary for faster realisation of the continent's development. But it requires commitment to genuine social change that will benefit the continent. Business as usual won't suffice. A different approach to dealing with issues that have dogged the continent for years is required. To make a real difference in its current state, Africa should aim for a higher state of being in addition to attaining food sufficiency. It should aim to take up its rightful place in world trade and economy and to sit at the same table with the rest of the world as a contributor to issues relevant to the survival of the human race and it can only do this if it achieves its own economic development."
According to Bokanga, the rationale for the establishment of AATF was to establish links between private and public sector institutions that own technological innovations in developed nations with African stakeholders in agricultural development such as the National Agricultural Research Systems, community-based associations, non-governmental organisations, and national private sector agribusinesses. The goal of AATF, he said, is to facilitate access to advanced scientific and technological resources and their adaptation for use in specific projects intended to increase the productivity of small holder farmers in sub-Saharan Africa.
The foundation was established as an Africa-based and African-led entity, based in Africa but registered as a charity under the laws of England and Wales with the specific objective of relieving poverty in Africa by facilitating public-private partnerships for the transfer and use of innovative agricultural technologies by small holder farmers and, in particular, resource-poor farmers. The aim is contribute to increased productivity, higher farm output, increased food security and greater income generation potential to pull farmers out of poverty.
The foundation's strategy for achieving its objectives is to act as the principal and 'responsible party' in facilitating public-private partnerships on a project-by-project basis in response to African farmers' needs. It endeavours to assemble all the necessary components for each project, balancing concerns for expense, simplicity, effectiveness, risk minimisation and regulatory compliance. More specifically, AATF:
- Consults with African stakeholders to identify priority crops and key constraints for resource-poor farmers.
- Consults with potential technology providers, in both the private and public sectors, to identify technologies that can address those constraints.
- Negotiates with potential partners to develop a project business plan that specifies the role of each partner institution, and determines how and where the technology will be used.
- Enters into license agreements to access and hold proprietary technologies royalty free and ensure freedom to operate for all the components of the technologies.
- Sub-licenses partner institutions for further research as needed to adapt the technologies to small holder farming conditions.
- Issues sub-licenses to test the adapted technologies for regulatory compliance.
- Issues commercial sub-licenses for production and distribution.
- Carries out risk assessment and develop risk mitigation strategies
- Monitors compliance with the requirements of sub-licences and with risk mitigation measures to minimise the probability of technology failure.
- Facilitates the work of appropriate partner institutions to ensure that links in the value chain are connected, effective and result in products of technology getting to farmers at competitive prices and farmers' surplus harvests getting to market to generate income for farmers.
- Creates partnerships within African countries and with external stakeholders to foster over time the development of necessary indigenous capacities.
The core function of AATF is to acquire access to one institution's intellectual property and to carry it through other institutions until it reaches small holder farmers. In the implementation of its projects, AATF abides by all relevant international laws and treaties, as well as national laws concerning intellectual property rights and genetic resources in the countries in which it operates. Proprietary technologies are legally acquired and are used by AATF and its partners/collaborators to help make the technological advances necessary to achieve AATF's charitable objectives.
According to Bokanga, with regard to the protection of cells, genes, molecular constructs, plants, varieties and traits, and to the extent permitted by applicable laws, AATF and its partners/collaborators would consider the effects that patenting has on access to, as well as the distribution and use of the protected product before they proceed with an application for intellectual rights protection. Where appropriate, recipients of germ plasm and products generated by the project will be required to publicly recognise the source of the original material or product. AATF and its partners/collaborators will ensure that agreements granted to recipients to apply for intellectual property protection do not in any way waive the rights of AATF and the project collaborators to challenge excessive protection, by recourse to administrative and/or court proceedings.
The executive director said the foundation bases its activities on three strategic thrusts. The first is negotiating access to proprietary technologies that enhance the productivity of agriculture in Africa. In this regard, the foundation engages in technology scoping, interaction with technology developers, and negotiation. It keeps abreast of the latest information about agricultural production constraints and priorities in Africa and is familiar with major national, regional and Africa-wide policies on agricultural development.
The second is managing partnerships for project formulation, product development and deployment to introduce innovative agricultural technologies to African farming systems. Here, AATF identifies and develops partnerships with the most appropriate organisations for product development. These may be public or private organisations at the national or international level. AATF may also pursue access to funds for limited and targeted subsidies or output-based contracts. Partnerships may also include investments for information provision (such as farm-level demonstrations).
The last but not the least strategic thrust, Bokanga said, is managing knowledge and information to support technology identification and development, and the policy environment. AATF develops access to information on production constraints in African agriculture, the technical characteristics of candidate technologies, and relevant regulatory, biosafety and intellectual rights property requirements. AATF also develops its own knowledge base about issues related to the enabling environment for agricultural technology.
Bokanga told THISDAY that AATF takes action for the farmer and by extension for the hungry and deprived in Africa. Its approach to reviving Africa's agriculture demonstrates courage in exploring new ways of doing business. Aware of the risks involved in charting new waters, he said the foundation has chosen to trust in the goodwill and willingness of others, especially the private sector, to lend a helping hand by bringing useful products to small holder farmers for a win-win outcome. The Foundation's approach to agricultural development - while different from what others have been doing - does not rock the boat, but provides, so to speak, additional steering power to take Africa out of the hunger pit.
He said the reason why agriculture is not performing is that it has not changed. "We cannot satisfy the needs of three times the population using the same method," adding that Africa could no longer feed its population. He said 30 per cent of food aid goes to Africa and that the continent has small population compared to Asia or Latin America.
On the kind of crops the foundation devotes attention to, Bokanga said AATF concentrates on staple foods. He mentioned maize as a major staple crop in Africa that is endangered. Maize, he said, is grown in northern Nigeria, east Africa and southern Africa. "Maize is a victim of a weed called striga. This plant throws its seed in the soil and stay in the soil for two years. It will take nutrients out of maize. It is found in northern Nigeria. There has been a lot of attempts to control the weed without success until a breakthrough from a German company called BASF. The technology is such that the seed of the variety is taken to the herbicide and the striga seed would die on contact with the maize. Within five years you can wipe out all the striga seeds."
Bokanga said BASF gave the right of research to an institute in Kenya but without power to commercialise. "AATF had to negotiate with BASF that now agreed to commercialise. The research institute does not have the resources to commercialise and we got a company to do it and we paid for producing the seeds. We also got non-governmental organisations (NGOs) to test the seeds," he further said. The Striga problem for maize cultivation is most severe in Nigeria than anywhere else. It is estimated that over 800,000 hectares of maize land in Nigeria are infested with Striga. Kenya has only 200,000 hectares infested. The results in Kenya show that by disseminating the IR maize varieties in Striga infested fields, farmers can harvest an additional one ton of maize per hectare. Therefore, if the IR maize varieties are disseminated in the 800,000 hectares infested with Striga in Nigeria, the additional maize harvest would be at least 800,000 tons of maize valued at US$ 1.2 billion! This is the expected impact that the introduction of IR maize varieties would make in Nigeria.
He said that the striga-free programme would come to Nigeria in 2007. He noted that if a farmer keeps seeds for planting there would be no increase in productivity, adding that key to technology transfer is giving the farmer access to output market.

Original_Search_Title: Beware of China
Headline: Nigeria: Beware of China
Body_Text:
Lagos — China is a dangerous nation. One of the last bastions of communism, the country's despots must be given credit for managing to somehow convince the rest of the world that they are an okay communist nation. While the two other remaining communist outposts, North Korea and Cuba, groan heavily under the weight of sanctions and have attained international pariah status, China has thrived economically and politically. It has done this through shrewd planning and realpolitik. China is now widely seen as the successor to the U.S.S.R in the world that is increasingly becoming bipolar. It is also an emerging economic power. And despite the country's penchant for oppressing its neighbours and its citizens-it invaded Tibet, supports North Korea's regime, interferes in Cambodia and never misses the opportunity to intimidate Taiwan by staging mock invasions and issuing inflammatory statements-the International Olympic Community overlooked all these crimes and awarded them the right to host the 2008 Olympics. This is China-a country that clearly understands international politics is not about playing fair.
The usually circumspect United States of America is so enamoured with China that it rewards its abysmal human rights record by celebrating the entry of U.S companies into China while they turn a blind eye to China's human rights abuses.
It is with great displeasure that I have observed China's increasing influence on the Nigerian economy. It is now very obvious that China is one of the major architects of Nigeria's economic problems and I will not be surprised if there were Chinese think tanks devoted to ensuring that the most populous black nation on earth remains a bottom feeding underdeveloped nation. Let us take for instance the Nigerian consumer goods market. Nearly everything Nigerians consume is now imported from China and the government makes no effort to stem the tide. Even when the Nigerian government announced that it had banned foreign textiles, it still allowed Chinese businessmen to erect a hideous Chinatown building in Oworonsoki where banned textiles were freely peddled by Chinese nationals.
The Chinese and other Asians who have discovered Nigeria are so unscrupulous that they actually come to the Nigerian market and undercut Nigerian traders with the imitation goods. This has happened repeatedly with hair extensions and Nigerian manufacturers are tired of complaining about the matter. But because of the Nigerian government's inferiority complex and desire for all forms of Foreign Direct Investment, China floods Nigeria's markets with goods that they would not dare to export to Europe or America.
Chinese companies have also become synonymous with death and disaster in Nigeria. The Chinese and other Asians have formed the habit of running slave factories in Nigeria. Here, they subject Nigerians to the most inhuman working conditions. These shoddy companies make billions in profit, pay little in taxes and wages and move their profits out of the country.
Nigerian economists do not realize that they have to buckle up while the oil lasts. They have to realize that only fervent nationalism and realpolitik will take Nigeria out of the doldrums. Oil should be used as a bargaining chip, lest this country becomes a vassal state of China. While most common Nigerians complain that the price of Cassava is very high, Nigeria exports cassava to China instead of finding new methods of improving our agricultural output for our own consumption. We have to start becoming nationalistic.
Asian jails are full of Nigerians imprisoned without trial and yet when Chinese citizens are apprehended for crimes in Nigeria, they are set free. When the government has the good sense to shut down an Asian company, they return and set up the same company under a different name. I am not xenophobic. I am just annoyed that the Nigerian government always manages to make the worst decisions. Instead of seeking a partnership with a responsible pacifist and progressive nation like Japan who have a lot to offer in terms of technological transfer and other benefits, we have chosen to become a vassal state of China. Recall that it was the same Chinese that won the huge contract to revamp our railway system during the days of Abacha.
They took the money, resuscitated some obsolete trains and laughed all the way to the bank. The simple truth is that the Chinese have always had a policy of exploitation towards black Africa. Most Chinese people are deeply racist and consider Blacks a subhuman species. Some states have even done worse. Recently, the Yobe State Government announced its partnership with North Korea, the most paranoid nation and one of the most dystopian nations in the world. Could someone please explain what benefits Yobe State is supposed to derive from a country that is in the same situation as Niger and Ethiopia? We Nigerians have to get rid of our inferiority complex if we are to become a developed country. It seem that most of our companies and government agencies feel that once one is White or Asian, he is automatically good at what he does.
Perhaps that explains why expatriate technicians come here and lord over well-qualified and educated Nigerian engineers and subpart copywriters are made creative directors by advertising agencies. It is pretty obvious that Nigeria is one of the few countries left in the world today with no concrete plan for development and while it is obvious that they have want to exploit us, the government just does not get the point. I think the Nigerian government should become more circumspect in its dealings with the Chinese government and realize that if we did not have so many natural resources, the Chinese would not be here. As the price of oil reaches new highs, we should do our best to take advantage of it while it still lasts.
- Olisa wrote from Lagos

Original_Search_Title: Agric Minister Worried About Food Insufficiency
Headline: Ghana: Agric Minister Worried About Food Insufficiency
Body_Text:
Kumasi — THE MINISTER of Food and Agriculture, Mr. Ernest Debrah has expressed grave concern about Ghana's inability to feed itself although 60% of the population are farmers which situation he says is responsible for 2.4 million of the population being malnourished.
He lamented that as an agricultural country with most of scholarships specializing in the field, it was worrisome that Ghana was still not sufficient in food and was rather using huge sums of monies to import food at he expense of developmental projects.
According to him, Ghanaians should not be complacent about the current drop of malnutrition figures from 5.6 million in 1996 to 2.4 million in 2003 cautioning hat there are more challenges ahead, which call for hard work.
He has, therefore, as a matter of urgency, called for a concerted effort on the part of Ghanaians to work and halt this lamentable menace confronting the country instead of making empty promises.
"Ghanaians are noted for making good speeches but poor at implementing concepts," he said adding that this does not augur well for the development.
The Minister was launching the 8-year Root and Tuber Improvement and Marketing Programme (RTMP) sponsored by the International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD) in Kumasi over the weekend.
The programme is designed to enhance the food security and income status of the poor in the country.
Minister Debrah noted that Roots and tubers which account for 44% of the Agricultural Gross Domestic product and 17% of the country's total GDP, has been identified as having the potential to reduce poverty in Ghana adding that it could support an accelerated economic development since roots and tubers are the most resilient crops and cheap energy providers the majority of the population.
He noted that apart from improving farming production and enhancing their incomes, RTMP would provide training in enterpreneurship, management and best practices for 34,000 farmers, processors and traders.
According to the Minister, additional 5,000 processors units would be developed through credit support to processors.
He announced that the second phase of the RTMP has been designed to contribute to the modernization of agriculture by providing root and tuber crop farmers with sustainable access to improved planting materials as well as production and processing technologies that would help the farmers achieve enhanced incomes.
Debrah observed that the government known for what it stands for democracy has always sought to strengthen the principles and practices of good governance and assured hat the RTMP would contribute to the deepening of democracy by providing support to local organizations to engage in policy dialogue and participatory planning and well as implementation and evaluation processes.
Describing the programme which started from 1999 to 2003 as successful, the Minister disclosed that a number of farmers supplied with planting materials improved high yielding cassava varieties raising the average cassava farm yields of peasant farmers from 7 tons per hectare to 12.04 tons per hectare at a time four improved varieties of yam were released.
Mr. Mohammed Mansouri, IFAD Ghana Programme Manager, cited IFAD's unique mandate as "developing and financing projects and programmes to enable rural poor to overcome poverty through low-interest loans and grants.
According to him the programme would adopt a business development approach and Build up the market based provision of goods and services by private operators.
He said " implementation would be based on flexible and participatory panning procedures including organization of consultation meeting from the preparation of annual work plans and Budgets to the assessment of results and impact," hoping that the programme would contribute to the achievement of a sustainable food security and nutrition and extend to the Millennium Development Goals in the rural areas through out the country.
Mr. Akwasi Adjei Adjekum, national Programme Co-ordinator assured that RTMP would have an impact on the wider rural economy and contribute to tan expansion of the rural micro and small enterprises in Ghana.
He noted that the programme would lead to an increased non-farm employment for both unskilled and skilled labour in rural areas.

Original_Search_Title: My Blueprint to a Greater Nigeria - Gov. Adamu
Headline: Nigeria: My Blueprint to a Greater Nigeria - Gov. Adamu
Body_Text:
Lagos — My Call to National Service- Being Text of a Speech by His Excellency, Dr. Abdullahi Adamu, Executive Governor of Nasarawa State and Presidential Aspirant Under the Umbrella of the Peoples Democratic Party at the Public Presentation of The Bridge to a Greater Nigeria Presidential Campaign Manifesto, Shehu Musa Yar'Adua Center, Abuja, Wednesday, December 06, 2006
About Seven weeks ago, I officially collected a form to indicate my interest in running for president in next year's general elections on the platform of my party, the Peoples Democratic Party, PDP. I now reaffirm that quest as a humble, privileged patriot.
The president is the highest political office holder in our country. I am aware of the weight of the office. I am prepared to bear that weight. The office is the symbol of our nationhood. I am aware of its sanctity. I am prepared to protect its sanctity. It is
not an office for the power-hungry. I am aware of power-hungry men and women who seek it. I am not power-hungry. Those who seek this office must be unquestionably committed to the weal and the welfare of our nation and its people. I am aware of the
enormity of that commitment. I promise here and now that I will bear any burden, no matter how heavy it may be; I will discharge any responsibilities, no matter how tasking it may be, and I will pay any price, no matter how stiff it may be, to make our country a strong, united, egalitarian and prosperous nation.
I stand before this august assemblage of compatriots in response to the call of our dear country. It is not a call for self-aggrandizement. It is a call to national service. The call to national service is the highest call known to mankind and all those who are called to serve their nation are truly privileged men and women. I obey our nation's call to service with a great sense of humility and a deep and abiding gratitude to the almighty Allah. I know that I am taking this step with the fond wishes of all Nigerians. To them, I express my sincere gratitude.
In my current position as governor of Nasarawa State, I have carried out national assignments that gave me a full insight into the problems, the hopes and the aspiration of Nigerians in every part of the country. I was the pioneer chairman of both the Nigeria
Governors' Forum and of the Northern Governors' meeting. Both positions deeply impressed on me the urgent need to build bridges across the gulf of ethnic, sectional and religious differences. I emerged from carrying out the assignments of those august bodies fully persuaded that unless we begin to build bridges to link our past with the present and the present with the future our efforts at true nation-building would be but isolated reactions to the problems of economic and social development that confront us as a young and ambitious nation.
This is the election season. Tens of my compatriots are offering themselves for elective offices at state and federal levels. We welcome their desire to serve the nation and its people. The 2007 general elections are certainly more than a periodic change of batons in
the executive and the legislative arms of government. They will test both our commitment to democracy and the resilience of our democracy. We must resolve to
pass this litmus test. Our political ideologies are essentially different approaches to the same goal; the goal of serving our nation at whatever level it may please God to give us the opportunity. They must not be sources of discord and enmity. Our collective
commitment to free and fair elections, devoid of violence, must be unquestionable and unambiguous.
Our politics must be a civilised social game of ladies and gentlemen. The political parties must not turn themselves into combatant forces and nomination processes into a battle. Our ambition to seek elective offices must not be greater than the interest of the
nation and the people we wish to serve. The destiny of our nation is in our hands. All of us, the rich and the poor, leaders and the led, the powerful and the powerless, have our individual parts to play to make our nation truly great. Let us not fail to play our part, no matter how insignificant we may think it is.
The task of transforming Nigeria is beyond any one individual. It is our collective task as Nigerians. If we work as a team under a committed and visionary leader who knows where he wants the country to be and how he can take it there, then our country will cease to be potentially great. It will become great. In his two terms in office as president, Chief Olusegun Obasanjo has consistently demonstrated incontestable dedication to the patriotic task of making our nation great. His reform programme in the economic and social sectors is aimed squarely at both rebuilding and repositioning our country for the great challenges of the new century. He has laid the solid foundation for the transformation of our nation. We must resolve to build on that foundation. It is the bridge to our future greatness. The broken bridge in our national policies and programmes is the bane of our national development.
Each time a new administration abandons the policies and programmes of its predecessor, it breaks the bridge between the past and the present and slows down the pace of our national development. Our nation's slow pace of development is the price we pay for it.
Obasanjo's reform must be the watershed in our national development. It must now be forward ever, backward or standing still never.
It is the luck of man, if luck it is, to be saddled with problems. Old problems linger, requiring a new thinking and a new approach; new problems crop up, asking for urgent attention. Lingering social, economic and other problems are facts of life in all
human societies. We must be prepared for these when the batons change in Aso Rock next year. They will task our capacity for a new and creative solution.
Some forty-five years ago, the late President John F. Kennedy, urged his fellow Americans not to ask for what their country could for them but what they could do for their country. All of us must ask ourselves the same question at this time in the political history of our nation. What can we do for our country? I have pondered hard and long over the question. I have had the privilege to serve my country as a minister. I participated in the writing of a new constitution for the country twice. I am privileged to be the governor of my state. In all these, I have put my nation first and served and still serve it faithfully. Each opportunity for serving my country renews my faith in
it. The flames of patriotism burn more brightly. In offering myself for national service as president, I am putting myself once more in the service of my country. My manifesto, The Bridge to a Greater Nigeria, which I present to the country through this distinguished audience today, detail the road map to the future of our country and its people. This is not
a document of grandiose promises. It contains no incantations for a magical transformation of the country. It is a pragmatic document, the fruit of a careful study of where we are, where we want to be and how to get there. Its purpose is to make Nigeria's
greatness a manifest reality. The philosophy of my manifesto is anchored on the objectives and goals of the Nigerian State enunciated in the Second National Development Plan, 1970-74. It was the ambition of that development plan, to make
Nigeria:
a) a united, strong and self-reliant nation; b) a great and dynamic economy;
c) a just and egalitarian society; d) a land of bright and full opportunities for all
citizens; and e) a free and democratic society. These represent my dream for my country. They also reflect my ardent belief in the capacity of my fellow country men and women to make this dream come true. I seek to be the leader of the Team Nigeria that will
make this dream come true for all of us. My manifesto ranks the economy as the first item on the agenda. Our country is richly blessed with human, mineral and agricultural resources. The management of these wonderful blessings is the most contentious issue in the country. A nation's destiny is tied up with the management of its economy. This was why the Obasanjo administration made economic development its priority. It is for the same reason that we make it our priority too. We will continue with the reform in
this very vital sector. We take seriously the warning by economic experts that unless we get the management of the economy right, nothing will go right for us. We
must get the management of the economy right. Once we do that, all the rest shall be added unto us. Some aspects of the economy need intervention and some need reformation. We must not confuse the two. We shall intervene, where necessary, to set a new tone for our economic progress; and we shall reform, where necessary, to correct course or re-direct our steps. Economic reforms or interventions make sense only in
the context of their favourable impact on the lives of the people. Whatever is done to put the economy right must have one objective, and that is making life less brutish and more meaningful to the generality of our people. Extreme wealth for the lucky few and extreme
poverty for the many must never be the goal of our economic transformation. As our brothers, Ndigbo like to say, let the eagle perch and let the hawk also perch.
One notable fact about the privatization programme of the Obasanjo administration it that it has progressively put the private sector in the driver's seat of our economic development and thus reduced the intrusive visibility of the public sector in the
management of the economy. Conventional economic wisdom posits that the primary role of government in the economy is best limited to policy formulation and monitoring. We can see that thanks to the privatization programme, the creative energy of our
people that was bottled up for so long because government was in the driver's seat has now been released. Our young men and women are rising up to the challenges of our socio-economic transformation. However, given our level of economic development, the
right of the government to intervene in economic activities where necessary is neither unwise nor retrogressive. We must respect conventional wisdom but we must not abdicate our right to do the right thing for the right reasons.
In brief but specific terms, we will streamline and delineate economic policy and process to ensure adequate and creative response to the challenges of the 21st century and globalization. Our economy is basically a cash economy. This has a number of obvious
problems for our people and our economic growth. A cash economy is a sworn enemy of family financial planning and saving. This is why the rest of the world has moved from cash to a cashless economy. We must move along with the rest of the world. We plan, in due consultation with the banks, to move the country out of the cash economy. The cashless economy is the most logical and frontal attack on the menace of armed robberies in the country. The petroleum and gas sector lays the golden eggs for our economy. We will reform this sector to ensure greater indigenous investments in it and create a level playing field for both indigenous and foreign entrepreneurs. In partnership with the private sector, we will work to reduce the cost of doing business in the country to attract greater foreign investments into the economy. We will deepen the current reform in
the financial sector. The banks will be strengthened and made a driving force of our economic development.
The support of the banking sector is critical to private sector development. The current general policy of the banks tends to favour commerce. This short term policy is at the expense of long-term investments. In the circumstances, the manufacturing sector suffers.
Less than one per cent of our industries are operating at anything near their installed capacities because the banks are generally not too keen to support long-term investments and plans by manufacturers. I have no doubt that many of our young men and women
