would be middle-level entrepreneurs today, contributing their own valuable quota to our economic development if the banks would support their creative endeavours. If we must develop and develop as rapidly as we would wish, then we must take urgent steps to
support the manufacturing sector of the economy. We shall evolve a policy by which the banks must set aside two and half to three per cent of their annual profit to funding long-term investment proposals by Nigerian companies. Corruption has been the blood sucker in the life of our economic growth. The current fight against it will be intensified to free our country once and for all from the shackles of this monster. We will strength
all the agencies set up by this administration to fight corruption. This fight is much a moral war as it is a legal battle. We must combine the two to ensure that there will be no hiding place for the corrupt in our country.
I believe that the public services of the federal and state governments must be guided by a code of ethics. I have just published a book, Code of Ethics for Public Officers and Civil Servants in Nigeria, in which I have prescribed a uniform code of ethics for all elective and appointive office holders. My main objective is to give the country an objective basis on which the conduct of our public officers and civil servants can best be judged. I believe that as part of the war against corruption, we must promote ethical conduct and reward honesty in all our citizens. This is the path to our moral rectitude. For very obvious reasons, we will give education pride of place in our national development programme. Our educational system must move away from the current pre-occupation with the issuance of degree or diploma certificates. Our tertiary institutions must not be
certificate mills but educational institutions whose products are properly equipped to use their hands and their brains as productive members of the society. The quality of education is critical to social and economic development. We will take immediate steps to re-evaluate current policies and programmes in education and tailor them to suit the demands and the exigencies of the new century. The products of our educational institutions must once more be respected world-wide for the depth of their learning and the application of their knowledge in solving the many problems that beset mankind. Government meant well when it decided to encourage private universities. But
it would seem that the can-do Nigerian spirit is already threatening this policy. The growing number of private universities is not an indication of our general commitment to higher education. It more likely stems from the feeling that a private university is
the new money-spinner in the land. We will arrest this current trend. The National Universities Commission will be strengthened by an act of parliament to police
these institutions and ensure the survival of the best and the committed.
Energy is critical to economic development. We will build on the current efforts of the Obasanjo administration to substantially increase power generation and distribution. We will tackle this lingering and embarrassing national problem and free our country from the main consumer of generators. Our ultimate objective in the first one year of our
administration is to make the current epileptic power supply a matter of history by ensuring that we achieve a combined thermal and hydro power generation of 10,000 megawatts. Our dilapidated distribution lines will be replaced. Darkness must be banished from the land.
The volatility of the Niger-Delta region is clearly inimical to our national cohesion and development. This region deserves special attention and we will give it the attention it deserves. We will create a department of Niger/Delta in the presidency to be headed by a minister. This department will be fully funded to tackle, in conjunction with the
oil-producing communities, the NDDC and the oil companies, the ecological and other developmental problems peculiar to the region. We will also develop a Niger/Delta master plan to turn the region that produces our oil wealth into a region that fully
enjoys our oil wealth and thus end the violent activities of aggrieved youths in the area.
No nation can progress if lives and property are not secure. The security and the welfare of the people are the primary responsibilities of government. Our country has become a victim of anti-social behaviours resulting in frequent cases of armed robberies, murder, drug trafficking and other petty crimes, all of which have combined to give a poor impression of the real situation in our country. We will adopt a pragmatic rather than a routine approach to this admittedly burgeoning problem with a comprehensive
national security and safety plan. We will establish a National Crime Prevention and Control Commission to effectively co-ordinate and monitor law enforcement,
security and intelligence agencies. We will make our security men and women proud of their uniforms. We will enhance their social status with increased pay and allowances. We will also ensure that all cadres of our security forces are properly trained and equipped with vehicles and communication and other facilities to aid their work. We will introduce neighbourhood police patrols throughout the country. In line with this, we will reform the judiciary and make it truly the bulwark against petty tyrannies of the rich and
the powerful in our midst. Justice would not be delayed and therefore no citizen of this great country would be denied justice. We will rekindle faith in the rule of law as a bulwark against anarchy. Despite our fabled oil wealth, more than seventy per
cent of our people still derive their livelihood from peasant agriculture. Oil money is easy money. It cannot last for ever because oil is a depletable natural resource. Our salvation still lies in agriculture. A nation that cannot feed itself cannot be a truly independent nation. We will introduce an accelerated national food crop production programme as
a prelude to a true green revolution in our country. We will do the same for our cash crops such as cocoa, palm produce, groundnuts, cotton, beniseed, rice and cassava. The Food and Agricultural Organisation, FAO, of the United Nations has commended Nigeria for its food production so far. But we still import food. We must turn the table and become a food basket for the rest of the world. In partnership with the private
sector, we will encourage investments in the rural areas to improve the quality of life there and arrest the rural-urban drift. We must restore pride to
farming.
Nigeria must be a country of healthy citizens. In the first quarter of our administration, we shall launch a national blue-print for achieving health for all over a period of fifteen to twenty years in line with the millennium development goal. This blue-print, tagged
The Bridge to a Healthy Nigeria, incorporates a detailed programming of ridding the country of HIV/AIDS, the malaria scourge, guinea worms and a host of preventable diseases affecting our children and young people.
Shelter is critical to the basic comfort of all human beings. This country has had a series of housing policies, all of which became victims of policy summersaults. Consequently, are people are under-housed or, if you like, under-sheltered. The symbol of the PDP is an umbrella, a universal symbol for shelter. My party takes the provision of shelter for all Nigerians seriously. Here again, the conventional wisdom is to shift this responsibility
for housing to the private sector. But clearly, the private sector cannot go it alone. It must partner with the public without doing injury to the conventional wisdom. Housing for all will be our goal. The big challenge here is to make the poor man his own landlord. We will meet this challenge by bringing ownership of personal houses within the reach of the
average salary or wage earner throughout the country. To achieve this objective, the mortgage institutions and the banks must be made responsive to the housing needs of the rich as well as of the poor. As I said at another occasion, "our private mansions can give us no joy if all around them is the pitiful sight of the homeless."
Our country is blessed with natural tourist attractions such as unusual rock formations, caves, warm springs, game reserves, extensive and beautiful beaches and breath-taking waterfalls. These are natural sights waiting to be tapped as local and foreign exchange earners. The presidential committee on tourism chaired by the president himself has done a lot of work in this area. Nine states are members of this committee. But that is not to suggest that the tourist attractions in the country are limited to these nine states. Every state in this country has something unique to it as a tourist attraction. The World Bank and the World Trade Organisation have jointly produced a national tourism master plan for Nigeria based on an inventory of tourist attractions throughout the country. We will set up a national tourism commission to implement the national tourism master plan. The commission, with the active assistance of federal and state governments as well as
indigenous and foreign entrepreneurs, will embark on a systematic commercial development of our tourist sites. Our country will be made the preferred option
for world tourists.
Our ambivalence towards the traditional institution is common knowledge. The traditional institution is the backbone of our culture and tradition. Our royal
fathers are the custodians of our culture and tradition down the length and breadth of this country. Despite the mixed fortunes that the institution has suffered since our independence forty-six years ago, it still waxes strong. There is no greater tribute to
its resilience and relevance than that today retired generals, deputy and assistant inspector-generals and commissioners of police, former permanent secretaries,
university professors and other professionals such as doctors and lawyers sit on the thrones of their forefathers. It is particularly instructive that President Obasanjo himself is the Balogun of Owu kingdom. He did not find it beneath his exalted office to act as the Olowu of Owu at one time. Yet the traditional institution is denied a constitutional or even administrative role in the social and political administration of the country. Clearly, this is a case of a bad omission by those who drafted our current constitution. It is my considered view that an institution that parades such a highly experienced group of men in various disciplines and professions cannot be consigned to the backwards of our nation on the unacceptable excuse that they should be insulated from politics. We will remedy this error of the head with appropriate constitutional amendments to give our traditional rulers a role in government, particularly at the grassroots level. This role may be
constitutional or administrative or both. We, in Nasarawa State have since amended the local government law to create an administrative role for our traditional rulers. The next logical step is to make it national. Our traditional rulers have always helped to maintain law and order at local and community levels. That role must be given back to them. We will do just that. We cannot afford to mothball our traditional institution.
I have given you but a peep into my manifesto. It is the veritable blue print to a greater Nigeria. I lay no claim on the monopoly of wisdom. As I said earlier, governance is not a one man show. It is everyone's show. I offer myself as a bridge builder in our national development. I believe in building bridges of understanding between and among all the ethnic nationalities in the country. In the absence of such bridges we but pay lip service to our national unity.
I believe in building bridges of mutual appreciation and respect for our cultural differences. Our national strength lies in the rainbow collection of our ethnic nationalities. Our weakness lies in mutual suspicion. I believe in building bridges of understanding between all religious faiths in the country. We must turn religion into a unifying force throughout the country. I believe in the free flow of views across these
bridges in the best tradition of the democratic ethos. Our democracy is strengthened by our mutual willingness to abide by its tenets.
Nigeria has risen from the ashes of its past. We must not let it sink back into a confused state. The hour of decision is around the corner. Let us have the courage to make the right choices from among the good men and women who are offering themselves for elective positions at federal and state levels. The choices we make will determine what we think of our country and what we want to make of our country.
Thank you and May God continue to bless Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: Plan for 2007 Presented to Parliament
Headline: Mozambique: Plan for 2007 Presented to Parliament
Body_Text:
Maputo — The Mozambican government on Wednesday presented the country's parliament, the Assembly of the Republic, with an economic and social plan for next year, characterised by continued high growth, and relatively low inflation.
The Planning and Development Minister, Aiuba Cuereneia, said that the target for 2007 is a growth rate of seven per cent.
Agriculture remains the major factor in the country's Gross Domestic Product, and is expected to grow by 12 per cent, particularly since normal to above normal rainfall is expected during the 2006-07 growing season. The expected growth, however, is not due to any rise in productivity, but to an increase in the area under cultivation.
Thus the total area sown with grains is expected to rise by 17.7 per cent - from 2.3 million hectares to 2,7 million. The 2007 grain harvest is projected at 2.4 million tonnes, a 14.6 per cent increase on the 2006 harvest. Most of this is maize: maize production is expected to rise by 15.3 per cent to 1.77 million tonnes.
Cassava production is targeted to increase by 8.4 per cent to 8.18 million tonnes, while the production of beans should rise by 8.9 per cent to 239,000 tonnes.
As for export crops, a strong recovery is expected in cashew nuts. The cashew harvest fell by 13.6 per cent this year, to 62,821 tonnes. But in 2007 it is expected to rise to 75,000 tonnes (an increase of 19 per cent). Of this total, 38,550 tonnes will be processed in Mozambican factories, while the rest will be exported raw (to India).
Cotton production is forecast to rise by 10 per cent, to reach 110,000 tonnes. Sugar production will also rise by ten per cent: the country's four sugar mills are expected to produce over 308,000 tonnes.
Cuereneia said the mining industry will grow by 11 per cent.
Half of this is growth in limestone quarrying. It also includes the first production from the Moma heavy mineral sands, in the northern province of Nampula. The Moma mine, run by the Irish company Kenmare, is expected to produce 389,000 tonnes of titanium ores in 2007.
The transport and communications sector is expected to grow by 14.2 per cent. Most of this growth is in road transport, and in the continued expansion of mobile telephone services.
Cuereneia said that the construction industry should grow by eight per cent. This growth is driven by a vast programme of road and bridge construction and maintenance.
The target for growth in manufacturing industry is 3.9 per cent. Since the MOZAL aluminium smelter has already reached close to its maximum capacity, industrial growth now comes from other sectors, notably clothing (27.9 per cent), food and drink (11.4 per cent), and tobacco processing (15 per cent).
Turning to the social sectors, Cuereneia said that 761 new schools will open in 2007 - 43 secondary schools and 718 primary schools.
The number of pupils should rise by 12.5 per cent, reaching 4.9 million. Of these, 3.95 million will be in first level primary education (grades one to five). 639,000 will be in second level primary schools (grades six and seven), about 308,000 in the first cycle of secondary education (grades eight to ten), and only 43,210 in the second, pre-university cycle (grades 11 and 12).
For this expansion, a further 9,000 new teachers will be recruited. Cuereneia hoped that 37 per cent of these will be women.
A major drive against illiteracy is continuing, and Cuereneia said that 36,000 adult literacy teachers will be recruited in 2007.
As for the health service, 2007 will see a start made on building seven new rural hospitals, a general hospital in the southern city of Matola, three new health centres, and three health training institutions The Ministry of Health also envisages a vast expansion in the provision of anti-retroviral therapy for people infected with HIV, the virus that causes AIDS. As of the end of November, 38,000 patients were receiving the life-prolonging anti- retroviral drugs. The target is to push this up to 96,420 people by the end of 2007.
As for inflation, the government hopes to bring the annual rate down to six per cent in 2007. The target for 2006 was 7.5 per cent, and inflation in the first 11 months of the year was 7.1 per cent.
Export earnings, Cuereneia said, should rise by nine per cent, to reach 2.292 billion US dollars. The government also planned to keep the country's net international reserves at a level sufficient to cover four months of imports.

Original_Search_Title: Food Crisis Looms in the North, East-Central
Headline: Burundi: Food Crisis Looms in the North, East-Central
Body_Text:
Bujumbura — A food crisis is looming in the north and east-central Burundi, a consultant at the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) has said.
However, Methode Niyongendako said the severity of the food crisis in Ngozi, Kirundo, Kayanza, Muyinga, Karuzi and Cankuzo provinces would not be as bad as last year.
Adverse climatic conditions are to blame, with poor rains in September, the planting season for maize and beans, followed by heavy rains in November, which caused flooding in some areas, damaging crops in the wetlands.
Another reason is the high population density in provinces such as Kayanza and Ngozi. "Every year brings a 3.5 percent increase of the population, exerting a pressure on the land," Niyongendako said.
In addition, yam yields have been declining in the past 10 years, while cassava, another staple, is affected by the mosaic virus. Niyongendako said the population relied on the two crops mainly during the lean season from September to January, so the food crisis is not expected to be too severe during this period.
The FAO warned of the looming food crisis in the November issue of its bulletin, Système d' Alerte précoce et Surveillance de la Sécurité Alimentaire au Burundi. It said a joint team of the FAO, the UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA) and the UN World Food Programme (WFP), which visited the Muyinga and Kirundo provinces to assess the situation, found that "nearly 80 percent of the adult population and 65 percent of children in the two provinces were eating one meal per day".
The bulletin also reported that residents of these provinces had run out of food stocks and were mainly depending on local markets for food being brought in from neighbouring Rwanda and Uganda.
"Even if the situation is difficult, it will not reach the alarming proportions of the year 2005 at the same period," FAO said in the bulletin.
However, at a meeting on Friday with Ngozi Governor Felix Niragira, administrators of Kiremba and Marangara reported that 71 people had already died of hunger in their two communes - 18 in Kiremba and 53 in Marangara; while another 50 had reportedly fled to Rwanda. These reports have not, however, been confirmed independently.
According to an assistant coordinator of agricultural emergency operations at the Burundi office of the FAO, Alexandre Huynh: "People are not eating enough, they may also suffer from malnutrition but there are no people dying of hunger now."
However, he said, the food shortage could worsen if rains fail until the end of December. "Each day without rains compromises the chances of the next harvest of beans," Huynh said. Beans are one of Burundi's staples.
WFP has started food distribution in the affected provinces. An assistant information officer at the WFP, Isidore Nteturuye, says 70 percent of the food aid for December and January would be distributed - or 20,000. For December alone, Nteturuye said 230,500 households would be targeted, with each family receiving 31 kilograms of cereals and legumes.
FAO is also scheduled to undertake an emergency response by distributing seeds. FAO programme officer Gerald Madodo said 4,000 households would receive sorghum seeds by the end of December. He said 240,000 households received beans, maize and vegetable seeds in September.
As a long-term strategy to make the population more self-reliant, the FAO is boosting the distribution of mosaic-virus-resistant cassava seeds.
In 2005, the Burundian government had declared famine in Cankuzo, Ngozi, Kirundo, Muyinga and Rutana provinces. In February this year, the government received pledges of US$170 million from donors to help it deal with emergency needs.
Apart from the international emergency aid, the government also set up the National Solidarity Fund for Food Security, through which civil servants, private companies and individuals could contribute money to feed hundreds of thousands of people who were facing food shortages.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Preliminary Results Show Ravalomanana Victory
Headline: Madagascar: Preliminary Results Give Ravalomanana Victory in Madagascar Polls
Body_Text:
Marc Ravalomanana is poised for a second term as Madagascar's president as preliminary results point to a comfortable lead for the incumbent leader in 3 December presidential polls.
Preliminary results from the Ministry of Interior and Administrative Reform (MIRA) gave Ravalomanana 55 percent against around 12 percent for his nearest rival, Jean Lahiniriko. Lahiniriko is a former speaker of the National Assembly.
Turnout across the country was 61.5 percent of about seven million people eligible to vote, according to the Interior Ministry.
A total of 14 candidates contested for the presidency of the Indian Ocean island. These included Elia Ravelomanantsoa, the only female candidate; Pierrot Rajaonarivelo of the Association of the Rebirth of Madagascar party; former church minister, Richard Andriamanjato; and Roland Ratsiraka, mayor of the port city of Toamasina and nephew to former president, Didier Ratsiraka.
Randriamahenina Theodore, director of Democratic Promotion under MIRA, said all the results will be handed over to the Constitutional High Court which will be required to announce the final results within 10 days.
The responsibilities for election administration in Madagascar are divided among MIRA, the National Electoral Council (NEC) and the High Constitutional Court (HCC).
MIRA is responsible for organising elections while the NEC is charged with the supervision and oversight of the electoral process. The HCC is responsible for the final verification and announcement of the results.
The inauguration of the president-elect is expected in early January.
According to Madagascar's constitution, Ravalomanana, 57, needed to win more than 50 percent of the votes to avoid a run-off.
The president is elected by direct popular vote for a five-year term and the country's electoral system provides for the conduct of a run-off in the event that there is no clear winner from the first round.
This was the eighth presidential elections since Madagascar's independence from France 1965 and it would be the first for an incumbent president to win re-election if Ravalomanana is confirmed winner.
The incumbent president has since coming to power in 2002, however, managed to stabilise the economy and won the confidence of major multilateral institutions such as the World Bank and International Monetary Fund.
With an economy largely dependent on agriculture - which along with fishing and forestry contributes at least 25 percent of Gross Domestic Product (GDP) - the island also produces coffee, cassava, bananas, maize, sugarcane, potatoes and rice.
Mining and tourism sectors have shown signs of recovery and the government has projected increases in mineral exports from about US$100 million to US$150 million per year over the next 10 years.
The biggest boost to the Malagasy economy has been the discovery of oil which has recently triggered a scramble by international oil conglomerates. Initial projections are that the country could produce 60,000 barrels per day in three to four years. With potential revenue of billions of dollars, this oil boom would make the industry the biggest contributor to GDP.

Original_Search_Title: Graduate Finds Gold in Farming
Headline: Uganda: Graduate Finds Gold in Farming
Body_Text:
Kampala — A LOOK of contentment crosses Kaggwa Kasolo's face when you ask him what he has achieved from agriculture.
"I have achieved a lot since I started farming in 2001," he asserts.
Kasolo, 37, who got a diploma in Tropical Agriculture from the Nile Agriculture Institute, Jinja, in 1995 and a degree in the same field from Makerere University in 2001, says nothing is as profitable as agriculture.
Today, he boasts of 700 heads of cattle, a modern residential house at Bukolooto trading centre in Kayunga district and a Pajero vehicle. He is also able to pay school fees for his 16 siblings using proceeds from maize, cassava and milk.
"My late father, Dezideriyo Kasolo, left two square miles of land in Busaana sub-county. After school, I decided to use that land for farming instead of going to the streets of Kampala to look for jobs. A friend of mine gave me sh100,000 which I used to start farming," Kasolo explains.
He used sh40,000 to buy maize seeds, sh10,000 to buy cassava planting material and sh50,000 on labour. During the April 2001 planting season, Kasolo planted 90 acres of maize and 300 acres of cassava. He says during the harvesting season in August, the same year, he collected 2,250 bags of maize.
"A kilogramme of dry maize was selling sh200," he recalls.
"During that period, cassava mosaic and other crop diseases were on the increase, but my crops were not affected," he says.
In March 2002, Kasolo reaped 2,500 bags of cassava from the 300 acres. A bag was selling sh8,000, giving him sh20m.
His produce was bought by boarding schools and traders from Kayunga, Mukono, Luweero and Nakasongola districts.
"With the proceeds, I bought a second-hand pick-up truck at sh5m to ease my transport and used the balance of sh28m to buy 72 dairy cattle," he adds.
Kasolo, who employs over 15 people, explains that his livestock farms, in Bbaale and Busaana sub-counties have 700 heads of cattle.
He observed that in 2003 and 2004, he did not produce much, but being a professional farmer, he introduced crop rotation to restore soil fertility.
Today, Kasolo collects 600 litres of milk a day from his farm. He sells each litre at sh400.
This harvesting season, Kasolo plans to use the proceeds from maize, cassava and milk to start poultry and piggery farms, targeting the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting slated for November next year in Kampala.
He also plans to start selling bulls and milk in large quantities to schools and traders.
Kasolo advises graduates to join farming instead of wasting time looking for jobs on the streets.
Born in 1969 at Ndeeba village in Kayunga district, Kasolo has 37 siblings, 20 girls and 17 boys.
At the age of six, he joined Ndeeba Church of Uganda Primary School where he completed his primary. For O' and A' Levels, Kasolo went to Ndeeba SSS, also in Kayunga.

Original_Search_Title: Farming Briefs
Headline: Uganda: Farming Briefs
Body_Text:
Kampala — No market
KAMPALA - The Government should take a lead role in supporting the private sector in the development of markets for agricultural produce, the National Agricultural Advisory Services (NAADS) board chairman Dr. David Kazungu has said. "NAADS supports enterprise development, which has led to increase in production but without a growing market demand or organised market to absorb the produce," said Kazungu. He was speaking at the sixth NAADS-Government and donor review meeting at Hotel Africana recently. He said many farmers are stuck with their produce.
Model villages
MUKONO - The LC5 chief, Francis Lukooya, has hailed the concept of model villages, saying it will promote food security. "Farmers will be able to learn new farming skills which will help them increase production," he said, while addressing farmers in Koome Islands recently. Lukooya called for more patrols by the Maritime Police on Lake Victoria to arrest illegal fishermen.
Poisonous food
KUMI - Eating fermented cassava and potatoes increases aflatoxin level, a mould which is poisonous. Dr Akileo Kaaya from the Department of Food Science at Makerere University warned residents to stop eating fermented cassava and potatoes, saying they can cause cancer. This was in a speech read by C J Mutyaba of Kawanda Agricultural Research Institute during a workshop at Lukiiko Hall, recently. He advised the participants to promote aflatoxin awareness by training small-scale farmers.
Maize prices
KASESE - Over 4,000 maize farmers from Kabarole, Kyenjojo, Kasese and Kamwenge districts have petitioned the Government to set the minimum price of maize at sh1,500 a kilogramme and also re-introduce a price stabilisation fund. The petition was handed over to the resident district commissioner, Charles Chemasuet, by the farmer's representative, Abel Ngomayondi, during the launch of the regional maize advocacy campaign in Fort Portal recently. In the petition, farmers also demanded that the Government sets up a maize organisation similar to the Uganda Coffee Development Authority.
Rotate crops
MUKONO - Farmers should practice rotational farming to ensure sustainable soil fertility, the district agricultural officer, Doreen Kataama, has said. Touring Nakifuma sub-county, recently, Kataama said, "Just as a human being cannot survive on the same food, so is the soil. Growing the same crop over and over reduces soil fertility." She advised farmers to use dry grass and leaves for mulching to avoid soil erosion in the wake of the current heavy rains.
Cane market
MUKONO - The Sugar Corporation of Uganda Ltd (SCOUL) has urged sugarcane outgrowers to increase production to exploit its ready market.
In an interview recently, S.C. Khanna, the chief executive, blamed the low supply of sugar in the country on low sugarcane supply. "This is a chance for outgrowers to earn big from our ready market," he said. Khanna said SCOUL would continue availing farmers with sugarcane seeds, training and loan facilities.

Original_Search_Title: MTN Paints Scribes Yellow
Headline: Uganda: MTN Paints Scribes Yellow
Body_Text:
It has become a norm for MTN Uganda to host the media at a "yellow party" every year. Scribes who turned up for this year's party last week were convinced this was the highlight of their nightlife on their social calendar.
Time keeping was good and by 9 p.m. Ange Mystique was filled with all categories of journalists; editors, newsreaders, reporters, salesmen and needless to say, a few flukers.
Snacks ranged from yellow cassava to chicken and drinks flowed.
Many drank until they started staggering, though some were overheard complaining that it was not a good idea for MTN to stage the party on a Wednesday with two more working days ahead.
The night wasn't lacking on live performances from some of Uganda and East Africa's finest artistes. When Obsessions took to the stage, there was no room for who-does-what in their respective media houses but rather who-is-who on the dance floor. They sang some of their latest ballads Jukira and Jangu.
Nubian Li and 2006 Pam artiste of the year, Bobi Wine also moved the crowds with songs like Bada, Adam ne Kawa and Ghetto but the night went to Kenyan star Nameless who was imported by MTN specifically for the media party.
When he sampled Ninanoki (featuring Amani), the song that introduced him to the Ugandan audience in 2002, the audience went crazy and it was a non-stop thrill as he sang other songs like Juju and his latest hit Sinzia, which was the epitome of the show.
When performances ended like close to midnight, it was back to business as usual and the pressmen had to decide whether to stay and shake to the favourite music or retire for the next day's work.

Original_Search_Title: Two Billion-Cedi Milk Processing Factory for Juapong
Headline: Ghana: Two Billion-Cedi Milk Processing Factory for Juapong
Body_Text:
A TWO billion cedi Milk Processing Factory is being established at Juapong in the North Tongu District of the Volta Region to convert cattle products into various forms of food for both local and international consumption.
The establishment of the factory, which was almost completed, had the relevant structure, put up as well as equipped with the necessary machines after a feasibility study was conducted on the viability of the factory.
The North Tongu District Chief Executive, Hon. Moses Asem who made this known to The Chronicle at Mafi Adidome, the District capital said what was left for the factory to start production was the connection of electricity to the factory.
Mr. Asem said the establishment of the factory became necessary due to the fact that the District was rated as the highest in cattle production in the country but unfortunately was also one of the Districts that harbour high numbers of unemployed youth in the country.
The DCE said having identified the potentials and the problems in the District, the NPP Government has initiated the move to establish the milk factory to help make judicious use of the thousands of cattle being produced in the area.
Mr. Asem noted that the factory would also help in creating jobs for the youth, which he said forms part of government's strategy to reduce poverty and create wealth for the people.
He observed that with the advent of the establishment of the factory, it would go a long way to improve on the living conditions of cattle farmers as the factory would enable them to earn a living wage, adding that cattle farmers would refrain from selling their animals at random and at a cheaper rates when ever they were in need of money.
Mr. Asem said the factory would produce Milk, Fan milk and Chips, which would also be a source of business to thousands of the youth apart from the many factory hands because there would be retailers of the various products thereby making life more comfortable to the people.
The North Tongu DCE noted that it was becoming increasingly clear to some of the people that the NPP Government cares for them and urged them to embrace and support government programmes geared towards improving the standard of living of the people.
Contributing, some of the inhabitants in the District said they were breathing the air of hope that new programmes would be introduced in the District as they have seen a new factory being established at Juapong and the National Youth Employment Programme being implemented in the District.
They commended Mr. Asem for his efforts to implement government programmes that they noted would definitely improve on their life,observing that cassava processing centres have been established at Podoe, Mafi-Asiekpe Mafi, Anfoe and Dorfor-Afawode which was making life more comfortable to the people especially women.
"We thank the DCE and the Regional Minister, Hon. Kofi Dzamesi for their hard work. We are too small to tell them about how we feel about them but once we have seen you, tell them that we secretly admire them and that the NPP we know is these two personalities in the Region".
They noted that for the years that the DCE has been in office, he always tries to involve the Member of Parliament for North Tongu, Hon. Charles Hodogbe and anytime the two met at functions they saw them sharing ideas. "We head that in some areas DCEs and MPs do not work as a team as well as hat one another because of political differences but we think the situation here is rather the opposite we see them and we appreciate their efforts".
Some of the residents however said they were hopeful that the Juapong Textile Limited (JTL) would soon be reactivated because in recent times, they saw a number of vehicles entering the factory premises and at times they could hear sounds of the equyipments in the yard.
When this reporter visited the factory last Thursday, there were signs of maintenance work on the factory equipment being carried out by Engineers as well as efforts to restore telephone lines.
However some officials in the factory yard were tight-lipped to comment on any new development at the factory and rather directed that information on any activity at the factory could be obtained at the Ministry of industry in Accra.

Original_Search_Title: Shell Donates N35m to Six Communities
Headline: Nigeria: Shell Donates N35m to Six Communities
Body_Text:
Warri — SHELL Petroleum Development Company (SPDC) has donated over N35million to six rural and riverine communities in Warri South, South- West, Isoko North and Ekeremo local government councils of Delta and Bayelsa States.
The amount covers four 16 seater Hiace buses donated to Kokodiagbene and Angalaweigbene communities; Micro-credit loan to Kantu, Omadino and Kokodiagbene communities; a rice processing mill to Ayamassa community and a Cassava mill valued at N2,437,940 to the Ovrode community respectively.
Making the presentation on behalf of the SPDC, Mr.Yemi Awolola the Interface Cordinator, land Areas, SPDC-West and the Asset Manager, Northern Swamp, Mr. M Ikhureigbe said the donation is aimed at empowering host communities, stressing that these empowerment tools would serve as a good example of their sustainable approach to community development with emphasis on the full participation of the benefiting communities in project identification, planning, execution, operation, maintenance and monitoring.

Original_Search_Title: Shell Donates N35m to Six Rural Communities
Headline: Nigeria: Shell Donates N35m to Six Rural Communities
Body_Text:
Warri — IN demonstration of care and good neighbourliness, the Shell Petroleum Development Company (SPDC) has donated over N35million to six rural and riverine communities in Warri South, South- West, Isoko North and Ekeremo local government councils of Delta and Bayelsa States.
The amount covers four 16- seater Hiace buses donated to Kokodiagbene and Angalaweigbene communities; Micro-credit loan to Kantu, Omadino and Kokodiagbene communities; a rice processing mill to Ayamassa community and a cassava mill valued at N2,437,940 to the Ovrode community respectively.
Making the presentation on behalf of the SPDC, Mr.Yemi Awolola the Interface Cordinator, land Areas, SPDC-West and the Asset Manager, Northern Swamp, Mr. M Ikhureigbe said the donation is aimed at empowering host communities, stressing that these empowerment tools would serve as a good example of their sustainable approach to community development with emphasis on the full participation of the benefiting communities in project identification, planning, execution, operation, maintenance and monitoring.
Congratulating the benefiting communities and women co-operative groups, the SPDC challenged them to make a success out of the various schemes haven been trained in cooperative development, entrepreneurial development, small business marketing ,banking and financial management, monitoring and project supervision adding that it is their hope that the micro- credit schemes and the buses would be put into maximum use for their benefit.
They called on the communities to always provide a good working environment for the company's operation to enable them achieve the highest standards and integrity in sustainable community development approaches.

Original_Search_Title: Have the Reforms Reformed Us?
Headline: Nigeria: Have the Reforms Reformed Us?
Body_Text:
Lagos — "Have all these reform measures translated to the alleviation or eradication of poverty among poor Nigerians? The definitive answer is NO! Poor Nigerians have had it hard on them because we have a government that care for the rich and do not consider the plight of the common man. For an administration that has increased the price of petroleum products five times in six years without due consideration of involving the masses in a dialogue is most shameful and sad. With all the reforms going on, privatisation of government companies, where government officials use 'fronts' in buying over government companies, and one man wants to own all syndrome which we are told is quietly causing the feud between the two top men in Abuja, is annoying. Much for a President who does know that price of 'garri', the staple food of the common man in Nigeria, is most disturbing. We are trapped firmly in the period of big man politics, a politics founded totally on individuality.Yes I do agree that 'Nigerians have not significantly benefited from these reforms not because the reforms are fundamentally flawed but simply because of the socio-political and institutional weaknesses of the Nigerian state.
The gains and dividends of reforms are concentrated in the hands of the ruling class, their cronies and some sections of the Nigerian elite. Take, for instance, the setting up of the corporation called TRANSCORP. To me, it is laudable but I do not see that company making it in time to come, for we know that Nigerians are good at starting and setting up companies and schemes. Like the Onwuka High Tech Company that today is moribund, sustainability will be the bane of that corporation - mark it! Though I am told that the major founders of the corporation belong to the clique called AGIP (Any Government In Power).
Did I hear you say "the telecommunications sector which used to be comatose has been completely revolutionized? The refineries are back in operation and fuel queues have virtually disappeared following the deregulation of the petroleum and gas sectors of the economy"? Well that may be true but do you know that Nigerians pay the highest tariff for airtime in the world? Yes it is true. So what kind of reform is that, that chokes the people and milks them? Can't the government intervene? Surely they can, but they will not, because the big guys in Apo Villa are given free airtime by these selfish profit-driven major IPOs who claim they are doing us well. Most sarcastically they come to splash us with their "bonanza" and yearly elaborate anniversaries, while they rake in billions. What about the refineries? Did you say they are working? For your information, Kaduna Refinery is closed, Port Harcourt Refinery's capacity utilisation is below 50 per cent, etc.
Whatever petroleum product you find in the country today is imported, forget about all the talk of our refineries working; it is all misinformation. Poverty is still prevalent in the country despite the government's effort of creating over 1,000,000 jobs as claimed since inception. Where are the jobs.? Have you seen one? It is all misinformation and propaganda. The present government's disappointment is more than enough for us to bear. Take the "Heartbeat" State (Edo) for example. It is classed amongst the most undeveloped states at present in the country. There has been no project undertaken by the government here that has ever been completed. The fruit juice factory was commissioned three years ago by the President but believe me till date, not a single fruit juice has been produced.
Today, the factory is over-grown with weeds with no single equipment installed. Take the cement factory, road construction, cassava processing factories, to mention but a few. Some projects have commenced but till date none has been commissioned even after seven years in power. The roads in the state are so bad that the slightly motorable roads are the ones done by the Federal Government or its parastatals. During the raining season, don't come out when it rains, for you will be overwhelmed by flood. Houses are destroyed each year, people die from flood disaster in the city, and each quarter the state is given ecological funds, which goes into the hands of a select few. The state is laden with corrupt politicians and civil servants who sing the praise of the helms men, nobody opposes the men-on-top. All are held bound by the gangsterism established in the state. Come to my state and see 'official corruption' personified.
Even the outgoing Nigeria Labour Congress President summarised my state's plight, as "... one of the states that have suffered poor governance for so long and even till now".
•Mr. Uwagbae, a social critic, writes from Edo State.

Original_Search_Title: Invest in Upland Rice, VP Tells MPs
Headline: Uganda: Invest in Upland Rice, VP Tells MPs
Body_Text:
Parliament — THE Vice President Gilbert Bukenya has called upon MPs to invest in what he described as the "lucrative" Upland rice business to help their constituencies fight poverty.
Bukenya was on December 7 speaking at an Upland rice sensitisation meeting at Parliament, where he said Upland rice has been identified as major tool in poverty eradication.
"We are encouraging farmers to grow rice, but MPs need to show their people the importance of this venture. We cannot be able to fight poverty without agriculture and Upland rice is the way to go if we are to help our people," Bukenya said.
Some of the MPs agreed to visit Bukenya's Upland rice farm located at Kakiri. The MPs heard that Bushenyi, Pallisa, Lira, Apac, Bugiri, and Kamwenge among other districts have the best rice qualities the farmers want. It is said to have a good aroma and cakes well, which has made it popular countrywide especially among commercial farmers.
Among other things, Prof Bukenya is advocating for improved agricultural practices including the production of high value crops like, simsim, sunflower oil, cassava and groundnuts for commercial purposes.
"Our leaders must consolidate their resources and help our people out of poverty. Let us put politics aside and be optimistic. Your people learn a lot from you. We must invest in this programme to help them directly using what they can afford," Prof Bukenya said.
He said there are different rice varieties, which are high yielding, early maturing, resistant to most of the rice pests and diseases and above all environmental friendly.
In 2004 the VP launched a poverty eradication campaign urging the people to grow upland rice varieties and ever since the venture has continued to expand, helping farmers fight poverty.

Original_Search_Title: Shell Spends N12.7 Billion On Devt
Headline: Nigeria: Shell Spends N12.7 Billion On Devt
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — Shell Petroleum Development Company (SPDC), has said within 2005, it went into different developmental intervention efforts in the Niger Delta worth $97.5 million (about N12.675 billion), aimed at improving the lives of the people.
This was in addition to other empowerment programmmes in partnership with organisations which have similar developmental interventions.
According to a statement from the oil company, it went into a $73 million road construction in partnership with the Niger Delta Development Commis-sion on the construction of the Ogbia-Nembe road in Bayelsa, which they said would open up a lot of communities.
The agreement is to the effect that SPDC would fund 70 percent of the total cost, while NDDC would contribute 30 percent and would connect several communities hitherto not linked by road in both Rivers and Bayelsa states.
It also said it has started implementation of the $20 million Cassava Enterprise Development Programme (CEDP) since 2004, in partnership with the USAID, on a sharing formula of 75 per cent cost sponsorship by SPDC, while USAID contributes 25 per cent.
The programme has the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA), as the implementing partner and has recorded appreciable progress with the establishment of three medium scale processing mills in Abia, Akwa Ibom and Rivers.The mills, said to have commenced processing of odourless cassava flour, are located in Abak (Akwa Ibom), Umuekechi (Abia) and Onne (Rivers), while another six centres for the mechanised production of garri have been completed in six states.
The garri scheme trains farmers in cultivation, processing and marketing of cassava products which has created employment for over 1000 people. Other areas of intervention to empower host communities the statement said, include the SPDC/Globacom partnership where 225 beneficiaries had communication kiosks given to them in remote areas of the Niger Delta which success has led to the planning for additional 400 of such centers and mini cyber cafes where they would be needed.
On the health front, they said that their $4.5 million intervention fund focuses on checking communicable diseases as they have set up health centers after the completion of the baseline survey.
They in addition provided 7,000 mosquito treated bed nets and 108,000 doses of World Health approved anti-malaria drugs. They hope to commence another $18 million intervention on biodiversity, strengthening good governance and tackling HIV/AIDS control and prevention.
The report was given at a forum organized for Non Governmental Organisation to exchange ideas of how best interventions should be patterned in the Niger Delta for optimal effectiveness.

Original_Search_Title: Help for Child-Headed Homes
Headline: Zambia: Help for Child-Headed Homes
Body_Text:
Zambia is grappling with the growing problem of thousands of child-headed homes, created by one of the highest HIV infection rates in the world.
Kapiri Mposhi, a commercial hub in central Zambia, about 200km north of the capital, Lusaka, has one of the highest levels of HIV prevalence in the country and a significant number of child-headed homes. UNAIDS estimates the national HIV rate at 17 percent.
The town is situated halfway between the industrial Copperbelt province and Lusaka on the Great North road, the only highway linking Zambia to Tanzania in the northeast, and is a mandatory stop for truckers. It is also a significant stop on the railway line connecting Zambia and Tanzania.
"Because it is a transport hub, frequented by truckers, the town has become a commercial sex centre with a high HIV/AIDS prevalence rate," said James Zulu, a spokesman for the Zambia Red Cross Society, which runs programmes supporting child-headed households.
The UNAIDS '2006 Report on the Global AIDS Epidemic' estimated that Zambia had about 710,000 AIDS orphans, of which over six percent were homeless and less than one percent were accommodated in orphanages. The rest either fend for themselves in their parents' homes or are looked after by elderly grandparents.
According to Charles Mwinuna, district director of health in Kapiri Mposhi, there are over 850 officially registered orphans and vulnerable children (OVC) in a population of less than 300,000 but the unofficial number could be higher. "We have not been able to establish as yet how many of these vulnerable children are currently in child-headed homes, but we know that they should be in big numbers because of the locality and socioeconomic nature of this town, which has put people at greater risk of contracting HIV/AIDS."
Many orphaned children end up as sex workers along the highway, but there are exceptions. Memory Kawaya, 16, has looked after her two younger siblings for the past seven years. The family of three lives in a grass-thatched hut on the outskirts of town. She attends schools and sells mushrooms to help support the family, and she and her siblings have twice tested negative for HIV. Kawaya is determined to maintain her negative status by abstaining from sex.
"Many people have been approaching me for sex and [they] promise to give me money, but I refuse to give in because of what I have heard and seen about HIV/AIDS. I want to become a teacher after completing school, so that I can look after my brother and sister - that's why I don't even think about any sexual relationship," she said.
Kawaya was nine when she lost her mother, and her two siblings were aged seven and five. "We do not have any relative to look after us ... we have been living like this and we often eat only once per day - we can't afford three meals. I am only happy that we are all in school, although I am usually frustrated by my headmaster, who is always harsh with me for not looking as smart as other pupils," she said. They grow cassava, beans and maize in an adjoining plot to help feed themselves.
The Red Cross Society has started initiatives working with vulnerable children. "We have identified 734 orphans and vulnerable children in the town, whom we support with groceries, clothing and school fees," said Zulu. The Red Cross caregivers also visit child-headed households in Kapiri Mposhi to ensure they are coping.
Zulu said the society was also encouraging older children to run small businesses, mostly vending, to support their families. "We do not want to encourage child labour, but we provide them with small amounts of money to buy groceries or other foodstuffs to sell."
[Produced in partnership with the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies: www.ifrc.org]

Original_Search_Title: Residents to Transform Farmlands
Headline: Rwanda: Residents to Transform Farmlands
Body_Text:
Muhanga — Plans are underway to transform farming activities through building terraces on farmlands for the residents of Cyeza, the Mayor of Muhanga district Augustine Hategeka has said.
In an exclusive interview with The New Time, Hategeka said the district in collaboration with Food for the Hungry International (FHI) will sponsor and monitor the three month project in which at least one hundred (100ha) hectares of terraces have to be prepared by the residents.
"The district has a plan of eliminating poverty and hunger by transforming agriculture. It is against this background that we negotiated with FHI to help the residents to transform the hills into modern farmlands," he said
"The residents will prepare the terraces and each farmer will be paid FRW600 per day. This will benefit them financially and also transform the farmlands," Hategeka remarked
The Executive Secretary of Cyeza, Francois Uhagaze said that the support was fundamental to residents and they have chosen to plant beans and cassava in the short and long term sessions respectively.
"The residents are prepared and most of them have taken study tours on how to use and maintain terraces. The farmers involved will also benefit in various ways and these include residents in Musengo, Busozi villages," Uhagaze indicated.

Original_Search_Title: Obj Recounts Efforts At Diversifying Economy
Headline: Nigeria: Obj Recounts Efforts At Diversifying Economy
Body_Text:
PRESIDENT Olusegun Obasanjo said in the past seven years, his administration has initiated several policies and programmes toward diversifying the economy from oil to non- oil exports.
The President has also applauded Odua Chambers of Commerce, Industry Mines and Agriculture (OOCIMA) for staging the ongoing Agro-Nigeria 2006 trade fair, at Lekan Salami Stadium, Adamasingba, Ibadan.
In an address while declaring the trade fair opened, Obsanjo cite for examples,the introduction of 10 per cent cassava inclusion in bread policy, which he said has opened a very wide employment and marketing windows for farmers and investors.
Other drives by his administration also included the folowing among others:
*The special agricultural loan of N50 billion earmarked for farmers in 2006 cropping season at an unprecedented interest rate of 8 percent.
*Subsidies ranging from 25 to 75 per cent are being extended to some critical agricultural inputs and serervices (fertilizers, seeds, seedling, fingerlings, agro-chemicals, tractors and implements, etc.).
*Outright ban on certain food items (canned foods, fruit juice, poultry products, vegetable oil, etc) in which the country has comparative advantage and potentials for self-reliance.
*Protectionist tariff and surcharges are also in place on rice, wheat, etc. These measures are aimed at protecting Nigeria from being a dumping ground for cheap apd highly subsidized 'farm produce from Europe which portends a serious disincentive to our local farmers.
*Reduced or zero tariffs on Agricultural inputs and Equipments as a means of boosting our mechanization level and reducing farm drudgery. The risk on agricultural investments is being allayed through the activities of the Nigerian Agricultural Insurance Company (NAIC). The annual premium is about 7 per cent and the Federal and State Governments pay 50 per cent of the premium while the farmers pay the balance.
*National Agricultural Development Fund (NADF) Bill has been forwarded to the National Assembly .
The bill when it becomes a law will make it mandatory for all stakeholders to contribute to the funding of agriculture. This would provide' adequate funding for research, training, extension etc.
When finally established, NADF would guarantee enough funds to procure fertilizers
His words: "The vision of this administration to grow the Nigerian Economy by 10 per cent starting from 2006, with agriculture playing a central role stems from the fact that agriculture remains the vital hub for solving our fundamental socio - economic problems, providing employment for multitude of the people, contributing over 40 percent of the nation's GDP and over 80 per cent to the non-oil export earnings." "I want to most sincerely appeal to the consciences of the stakeholders in this sector to regard and appreciate the effort of this present administration in bringing agriculture back to the world trade map and regaining its position as the major contributor to the nations GDP and Foreign exchange earning.
"I look forward to OOCCIMA and key players in the industry to collectively bring back the old Cocoa
Plantation, Cotton, Groundnut, and Sugarcane with value addition which will make impact in changing the fortune of our great nation," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Duke - I Don't Subject Myself to Captive Mentality
Headline: Nigeria: Duke - I Don't Subject Myself to Captive Mentality
Body_Text:
Lagos — One thing that has been said about Donald Duke is that, 'he is an oyinbo man, both names are English and always wearing suit.' I think I'm only being myself by my dress code which is what makes me comfortable. And I think it is practicable considering the work that I do. My name, my dress code are part of my heritage. I am not going to change that for anything. I want to be myself all the time.
Ok, why do you want to be President of Nigeria?
My aspiration is for a better country. I feel this is a country with resources, blessings, with endowment but one which needs a leadership that is futuristic. Just like any parent attends to the needs of his children for the future; that is why he sends them to school. He knows that the benefit of that education will not come right now. I think it is about time we built a nation for the future. What do we aspire to be? We talk of greatness. We all have lofty ideas about our country. But are we daring enough to step forward to achieve them? We saw the same outlook in Cross River State. We saw a society that could do more than it was doing. We live in a society that could offer very much more than it is doing. There is a lot of things we can do than we have done. So, why am I aspiring to be President of Nigeria? It is simple. I am fortunate. I have been tested and by the experience that I have garnered over the years, I think it will be a disservice to keep that away from the benefit of Nigerians.
One criticism or praise that you get is that, 'Duke is a fine boy, a gentleman, he can't hurt a fly.' Therefore, can you entrust him with the rough edges of Nigeria, with the militants of the Niger Delta, the almajiri of the North? Can you bite? Can you command the Army? Are you not too gentle for this game?
You know, each time I hear that, I feel very good with myself, because if in all the turbulence, I can still project the picture of someone who is calm and simple and cannot even hurt a fly, I think have succeeded. Let me tell you something. Cross River, with 25 ethnic groups, remains peaceful not because the people from time to time do not resort to violence or do not have issues that incite them to be violent. But we have kept a good eye on the situation there. We have shown leadership. We 've been able to hold down the society and keep it orderly. I don't think the leadership that we need in Nigeria is the type that portrays the ambience of beastiality. A leader in the modern world ought to remain calm in the face of adversity. I think that question really portrays our success in all the clamours, in all the agitation that goes on, particularly in the state where the revenue is low and one can still remain calm; that means we are doing very well.
Looking at Nigeria, what will be your first priority if elected president?
Finding the right hands to manage this country. You see, we all agree that the bane of Nigeria is leadership. So the first challenge of any leader is to ensure that you have the right quality of people at different levels, borne out of experience, exposure and the ability to perform. Then other issues will come in. Let me liken it to a football club or a football team. The first thing you do is to get the team together. Then you go for the championship. For me, the first challenge will be having a great team in place. The issue that challenges all of us, we know. We know where the shoe pinches us. We know for instance, that for us to develop a people, we need to have a sound mind. We need to do something about education.
Education for the past 20 years has been tottering. So we need to invest heavily in education. We need to ensure that the minds of our children can compete with their counterparts in the world. We need great infrastructure to move us from one part of our country to the other, to move information, to move goods and services.
Nigeria is a very important country in Africa and the entire world. There is a lot of competing interests - China, US. How are going to manage these competing interests?
First of all, you 've got to look inward. In this world, you are not attractive to anyone unless you have something to offer. So, you 've got to develop those things that you have. When we talk of competing interests, with United States and China, everyone is looking towards oil because that is what we have. For us to be able to negotiate with the rest of the world, we must possess the ability to deal beyond oil. Otherwise, you 'll compromise. You 've got to develop other sectors. Even though oil is our resource, it is not oil that employs the bulk of our people. We 've got to deal with them on that basis. Of course, you give them some advantage. In dealing with the nations, what can they get from you? But let us say that oil is not an end in itself. We 've got to look at other sectors.
Let me give you an example. Take Thailand. Thailand doesn't have an embassy in Nigeria. But we have an embassy in Thailand. Statistics shows that up to 15 or 25 per cent of Thailand rice is sold in Nigeria. Yet they have not found any reason to establish an embassy in Nigeria. I am told that the reason we have an embassy in Thailand is because we have a lot of boys trading there. So we felt we needed an embassy there. I think we need to be more strategic than that. Here we are on the other hand, buying a lot of wheats from the United States. So, you need to trade off. And again, you look at your trading sector, the commerce. You trade with the whole world yet you don't make impact on anyone. You 've got to be more strategic. It has to be a trade where both countries engage significantly with each other to make them respect each other. We can focus our trade on seven countries, bearing in mind the size of our population, bearing in mind our resources. We talk of oil and all that. We need to be forward thinking. We need to look at those areas that are important to us. It is only when you do that, that the world will treat you with respect.
There is a perception in the public that you withdraw yourself from the public, that you keep to yourself and suddenly you come out that you want to be president of the country. For example, people in Kaduna and Bauchi are bound to say, who is Governor Donald Duke? How are you dealing with this situation when you are just coming out of Calabar?
Obviously, you haven't been to Calabar. Like you said, that is perception. I think it is wrong. I 'll say that I take time to socialise. And you know again, I try to be moderate. If I am a socialite, you 'll describe me as an owambe governor. If I want to work on my responsibility, I can't be everywhere. For now, my responsibility is to the people of Cross River. I socialise moderately. But you know this is opportunity cost in everything that we do. We all have 24 hours in a day, but I prefer to use my 24 hours for my people. Again, those who say 'you don't attend a lot of functions...' for every function a governor attends, a lot of resources are going into that. When you look at the cost involved in that, fiscal responsibilities take its toll where and when you go. But that doesn't mean that I don't move around. I have a good rapport throughout the country. But you will not find me in every social function.
What some of your colleague are saying is that you are never in the meeting whenever they are taking decision. And if you are there, that you always find an excuse to disappear.
I don't know where you got that impression from. I don't think that is true because you were never there too.
But really, do you think you can win the confidence of the governors?
I enjoy tremendous goodwill from my colleagues; indeed, far more than I ever imagined. I think it is borne out of respect. You don't need to have a number of paid visits or courtesy calls every other day, to get that measure of respect. Rather, they appreciate what we are doing, the contribution we make and I think that has been giving me a measure of respect. I thank God for that. But on truancy and absenteeism that you are alluding, I don't know where you got that one from.
There are two major challenges in your quest. One is the unwritten agreement that power will shift from South to North. How do you think the North will respond to your candidacy when they believe it is their turn? And how will you assuage their feelings in that regard, should you emerge the candidate?
First, on this turn by turn Presidency, even (President Olusegun) Obasanjo himself will deny that he is unaware of this unwritten accord. The challenges we face in the nation goes beyond where the President comes from. It has to be the merit of the person and I know that in a nation such as ours, it is difficult to share the consent that we shouldn't rotate the Presidency. But I think what we should do is to look for the merit and then we can talk of the balancing. I don't share the idea of the rotation of the Presidency.
What I think as a nation, is that we should try and let me take you back to 1999. In 1999, we were desolate. We were very very, very low. We needed someone who commanded international respect. We had international disrespect, a national non-gratta at the time. We needed someone to take us out of that. We also needed someone who had a track record of achievements. I think that person happened to be Obasanjo. In terms of international respect and acceptability, he was by far the most acceptable person. I think those were the criteria more than anything else.
No. It was the need to compensate the South-west over the cancellation of June 12 presidential election.
I think that came third - the issue of South-west because they were not just looking for anybody from the South-west. He was not the only one who contested from the South-west. They were only looking for someone who could get for Nigeria international respect and then of course, he happened to come from the South-west.
If you want to go beyond that, why are we talking about North-South rotation? We 've had changes of leadership which had not been based on North-South dichotomy in the past whether it was military or civilian. We had leadership that had moved from one part to another within the Northern divide. So, I want to hope that I won't be bound by that. I am putting myself forward as someone who has been tested and within the collective will of the people with the passion to deliver on that.
There is demand for resource control in the Niger Delta. There is criminality in the Niger Delta, but far even more potent crisis is the growing poverty in the North, the growing unemployment. Check all the statistics, especially from multilateral institutions. How will you deal with these problems?
The biggest challenge that faces all of us is that of the unemployed people. Poverty is the end result. The major challenge is the unemployment of our young people. And when you are unemployed, you experience hopelessness and frustration. What you have in the Niger Delta is not different from what you have in other parts of the country. The militancy in the Niger Delta is to distrupt oil production and kidnap. The situation is not different from what happens in other parts of the country.
How are you going to deal with them? Immediately, you 've got to gather the resources for work. We 've got to invest in ourselves. I'm not talking here about crude oil. I'm talking of the brain. In a short while, you get the infrastructure working. Right! And also, you 've got to get a value added economy. An economy that is working. An economy that is labour induced. Let me give you an instance, if you look at this room now, what do we have now? You have airconditioners, household appliances. What are we doing in Nigeria? We are creating jobs for people in other parts of the world. I think we 've lost that advantage where we say globalisation of the world makes other economies attractive to us. But we must add value to everything that comes in.
In other words, you ought not import air conditioners and other appliances. Everything that comes into the country, we must try as much as possible to add value while you are trying to develop your economic base. The advantages of doing this is one, to employ people; two, to transfer technology. Nobody has been talking about transfer of technology for a long time. We just must add value to production.
All these promises you have made, are you sure you 'll be able to carry them out, when there will be some people who will claim to have helped you to get there?
I have been in this thing for a while. There is no problem whatsoever with people supporting you. It is welcome. But at the end of the day, don't compromise on the job. People have to do the jobs. I don't have any problem giving out contract, but don't compromise the integrity of the contract. In other words, you can't say because you supported me, therefore I should give you contracts. In 1999, we received support from people. We had to patronize them because we had to patronize people. You have to patronize the people who supported you and people who did not, but not the integrity of the job. In other words, the integrity of the job must not be deflated and desecrated. Once you have an idea, people will fall in line. They will say, 'I respect you for that.' Everyone knows that that is Duke.
This whole PDP process is a process of arrangee. Given the arrangement you are going to reach to emerge, do you have the gut to go beyond that and deliver what you have to deliver?
You see, I don't subject myself to captive mentality regardless of what you thought you had given. I am not your captive. I have to be a leader. What this nation needs is a leadership and you have got to give it. Like in a family, the head of a family is a father. The beauty is that we have a track record. You see, in 1999, we had promises. Some of us have been able to deliver on the promises. Some of us have not. Some of us have tried and failed. But now, they want to be President. But if you look back, where we needed to be firm, we have been firm, where we needed to be fatherly, we have been fatherly. I will not advocate rigidity. That is not leadership. But I will remain firm in my commitment to the Nigerian people. At the end of the day, people can know how dexterious you are in the political jobbing. But I believe once you aspire to be the leader of a nation like Nigeria, you don't live for the moment. You live for history.
Are you concerned that some delegates have been captured by some contender. We are talking about 4,000 people here. For instance, we hear that a governor who is also in the race has already paid for all the hotels in Abuja.
I heard that too. But you see, I want to believe that the delegates are men and women of integrity and they have concern for the rest of the populace. I have been hearing a lot of things, but do think that the delegates are different modes of human beings that do not share the passion for the rest of Nigeria? But secondly, I'm also concerned about politicking. I had woirked with delegates on two national conventions, it doesn't work like that. Before we leave Calabar, we have sorted out our accommodation. If you book all the hotels in Abuja, even if you have access to my delegates, it doesn't change anything. We know it. We 've heard of it. You hide delegates and on the last day, you bring them out. Most governors ensure that they have accommodation. For us in Cross River, we have already had our accommodation.
There is an example given here by the French ambassador that surmises the problem of education in Nigeria. There is an oil company trying to employ 20 or so people. 300 applied. They found later that only seven qualified. That means education has no skill anymore. How do you want to address this?
What is the crisis of education? First of all, in the last 15 years, our educational sector has been a laboratory. We have moved from five years of secondary schools to 'A' level, to three years of university and 6-3-3-4 system, and now, it is 9-3-3-4. The sysem has been disrupted, even bastardised. When we were in secondary school, there was no classroom where we had more than 30 children in a class. The average class today is a hundred.
You all can recall those text books, Nelkon, Lambert etc. You had these books. Ask yourself, those children, do they have these books? What about teachers. When I was in secondary school, all my teachers were graduates. They were international, they were British, they were Nigerian. Today, teachers in secondary schools are at best NCE holders. You cannot give what you don't have. That is the problem. We are not investing in the sector.
When we took over in Cross River State, we had an education summit to determine how many students we had and how many classrooms would be required. How many teachers were required so that we could have the teachers and address the issue of books. We did not have the resources to take a copyright of Stone and Cozens and so on. So we set up a book writing committee. We wrote 79 books. We published these books and ensured that we could get for each student throughout the year at N600.00. So it was affordable. Stone and Cozens in the market is N5,000 for a new one. That is one. Two, we looked at the teachers and because we didn't have enough resources to employ all the teachers we wanted - we needed 7,000 teachers in Cross River. We needed 75 new schools to ensure that we didn't have more than enough students in a class.
In other words, we would not have more than 540 children in a school, which we considered a manageable situation. We needed books. We needed desks. It took us almost four years to get a headway. Besides, we set up close to 40 new schools. And we were going to match them with teachers. We already had a working force and if we downsize or retire them, you are going to pay them pensions, you are going to pay them gratuities. So you 've got to make them work. So we transferred them to teaching cadre. We were able to have 4,000 new teachers from the 7,000. So, we have been able to raise the number of teachers that we require. We you have been able to ensure that those books are affordable - N600. I can assure you that every secondary school child in Cross River has the complete set of books. We bought new desks. We had to design new schools that will capture what we try to do.
We need to invest in education. We need the policy. We need good teachers. We need books. We need to re-establish a reading culture. We need to have a right environment. Our children don't have it. You have to get back to the basics and ensure that everybody has books.
If you are asked to be a running mate, will you accept it?
Right now, my focus is to be President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, not to be Vice President or all that. You see, there are political acrobats. They say 'if this one no go happen, this one go fall.' I don't belong to that category. And believe me, this is where my passion is. I have never thought of that. It is not in my programme.
Up till now, corruption is still on the rise. Are you prepared for instance, to take on President Olusegun Obasanjo if he's found wanting after May 29,2007?
What is good for the goose is good for the gander. Okay? What it means to be a leader is to do what is right and you have no problem. The law is there. When you want to play a game, then that is where the problem is. It boils down to the earlier question; Are you strong enough to lead this country? For me, I 'll do what is right because I live for these things.
What is your plan to improve poor power supply?
Let me talk about power generation. Like I said, the entire Nigerian problem can be reduced to one word, lack of productivity. And power is a critical aspect of productivity. In fact, if you don't have power, you don't have life. For you to have productivity, material productivity, you need power. I think the problem with the power sector is the structure. The oil company takes the oil, they don't require the gas for now. But that gas they flair is a resource. So, it is criminal to allow them take what they want and destroy what they don't want. We must compel them to convert that gas into energy.
Since they are flaring it anyway, they have no right to charge for it except infrastructure to gather it and distribute it. So, let the oil company for now resolve towards achieving this. You can have your ideas and all that. Let the oil companies be compelled to generate all the powers we require. I'm not talking of 10,000 megawatts here. To me, for us to get to where we ought to be, we need 60,000 mega watts in minimum.
Now, transmission should be done by an exclusive agency, whether you call it NEPA or PHCN, that is focussing on transmission line and every agent and all resource put into power sector will go into transmission alone. Once power gets to a stage, leave it with the state government. You cannot have one agency that is generating, transmitting and distributing. So, you must build on a solid structure. You privatize generation because oil companies are doing it and it is in the national interest, it is not negotiable.
