It is also possible that the people that have been attending the meetings might not accept the result of that consensus.
I think Ibori has tried in assisting the situation as it relates to governorship election in the state by putting that process together, I have taken full advantage of that process by knowing the aspirants that I did not know before and starting a new relationship with a number of them and I think I will emerge the candidate that most of the aspirants will be most comfortable with. It is very possible that Governor Ibori will not have the opportunity to use that veto because before we get to that point, we, among ourselves, would have determined from the kind of negotiations that are already going on, who the consensus candidate should be.
One, he must be somebody that has the capacity to carry everybody in the state, all the 25 local governments together, he must be able to recognize political leaders, especially the gubernatorial aspirants in making sure that their dreams for the state that will bring the best to the state are incorporated into the programme of the candidate that will represent all their interest because nobody comes into a governorship race without a plan. Once we are able to determine that, the issue of who becomes the next governor will become simpler for us and I think the aspirants are mature enough to know that the PDP cannot have more than one flag bearer and once the discussions continue the way they are going, I think that consensus will be arrived at or you might have a situation where it will become clear who the two or three frontrunners are and we will align with any one of the frontrunners that will represent your interest and I believe that most of the aspirants will support the emergence of Mr. Albert Okumagba as the candidate of best choice, as a candidate that will deliver the greatest value to the people of Delta.
I observe that most of the gubernatorial aspirants in the state are seeing no evil, hearing no evil and speaking no evil against Governor Ibori. Is it that he has performed so well or all of you are just afraid so as not upturn the apple cart?
The issue .of being afraid of Ibori does not arise, he is our governor. He has been our governor for over seven years now. I think that within the ambit of the challenges of the state, he has done remarkably well based on the kind of team he put together to work with him. But I come with a different kind of experience; I also have networks that put me in the position to provide the same kind of leadership. I will build upon his foundation, some of these foundations are very good but if our per capital income today is $400 per Deltan, am telling Deltans that I will make it 10 times that over a four-year period, that is to say that our per capital income is likely to move from $400 to a minimum of $4,000 within the span of the four years I will be governor. For those who understand the implication of figures like this, what I am saying is that I will create wealth, I will create an environment that will make it possible for a Deltan to become a wealth creator for himself and his community, his local government, senatorial district and the state.
An Okumagba government will put Deltans to work, an Okumagba government will make Delta a model state that will be emulated by other states in terms per capital income, in terms of the hospitality industry we are going to put in place, in terms of the conversion of our tourist sites into major tourist attractions in Nigeria, in terms of converting the stadia that Ibori has built into major centres for producing sportsmen. We are going to be the biggest fish farming state in Nigeria . We are going to be one of the biggest producers of palm kernel; we are going to be one of the biggest producers of cassava. We will fast track the agro-allied industry to the extent that we will not only process what we produce in Delta but we also process what go to the neighboring states.

Original_Search_Title: Over 50 Senators Will Not Retain Their Posts
Headline: Nigeria: Over 50 Senators Won't Return
Body_Text:
Lagos — Over 50 per cent of the present 109 senators in the Upper Chamber of the National Assembly will not retain their posts when the new senate reconvenes in June next year.
The development became clear yesterday when the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) which produced more than 70 per cent of the incumbent senators held the primary elections to determine its candidates for next year's general elections.
Many of the top ranking senators on their own volition decided not to seek re-election or failed to secure their party's tickets at the primaries.
Senate President, Ken Nnamani led the pack of the senators who will not come back next year as he decided not to seek re-election. His seat may however be taken by the incumbent governor of his state, Enugu, Dr. Chimaroke Nnamani, who yesterday got the party ticket conceded to him by the only aspirant in the zone, Dr. Ray Nnaji.
Other senators who voluntarily decided not to seek re-election are Senate Chief Whip, Senator Udoma Udo Udoma, former Senate President, Adolphus Wabara, Prof. Iya Abubakar, Senator Chris Nshi, Emma Agboti and Senator Tunde Ogbeha.
Senators Tokunbo Afiku-yomi, Musiliu Obanikoro both from Lagos State, Senator Kola Ogunwale (Osun), Senator Ibikunle Amosun (Ogun), Senator Ifeanyi Araraume (Imo), Prof. Oserihiemen Osunbor, Senator Isaiah Balat (Kaduna) and Senator Idris Kuta are seeking the governorship tickets.
At yesterday's primaries, senators Iyabo Anisulowo (Ogun), Fidelis Okoro (Enugu), Patrick Osakwe (Delta), Senator Isa Maina (Federal Capital Territory) and Robert Koleosho (Oyo) and Felix Ibru (Delta) among others were defeated by new aspirants who will now fly the PDP flags in the general elections next year.
Among the incumbent senators who yesterday secured the PDP ticket to seek re-election are Senators Iyiola Omisore (Osun), James Manager (Delta), Teslim Folarin (Oyo), Ike Ekweremadu (Enugu), Gbemi Saraki-Fowora (Kwara), Simeon Ajibola (Kwara), Ahmed Baba (Kwara), Senator Bello Hayatou Gwarzo (Kano), and Lee Maeba (Rivers).
The following are reports from our correspondents in the states who covered proceedings at the primary elections.
Ekiti
In Ekiti, violence disrupted the primaries as thugs suspected to be hired assassins were on rampage, killing one, attacking and wounding many prominent indigenes of the state includilng two gubernatorial aspirants, a member of the House of Representatives, among others, who narrowly escaped death.
But, a Mobile policeman was shot dead in the process, while another one was shot from the back.
Besides the two apirants, Chief Dapo Alibaloye of the Action Congress (AC)and prof Segun Ojo, other lucky people include chairman of the state Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), Mr Ropo Adesanya, a member of the House of Representatives, Mr Samuel Adeyemi and chairman of the National Universities Commission, Chief (Mrs) Abiola Ayomoche, who is also a senatorial aspirant from Ekiti North.
They were attacked at different ocassions in their various communities, across the state in the operation which began on Friday evening and lasted till Saturday morning.
In Ado-Ekiti,one of their prime targets escaped the hoodlums' gun shot by a whisker as he ran for safety in a nearby bush when the assassins invaded an hotel, where a reception was planned for members of the PDP electoral committee posted to the state.
But the state PDP chairman, Mr. Adesanya was unlucky as the assassins descended on him and injured him with the butt of their gun in the head.
According to Adesanya who is currently responding to treatment at the state government house clinic, he would have been shot dead but for the distraction caused by an alarm raised over the presence of policemen in the hotel.
Adesanya said, however, that during the attack, he lost the mobile policeman attached to him to the hoodlums who shot and killed him.
Meanwhile, the state Administrator, Brig. Gen. Tunji Olurin who has since visited Adesanya at the clinic sympathised with him on his ordeal.
An AC gubernatorial aspirant, Chief Dapo Alibaloye who spoke with newsmen at his Ire-Ekiti country home yesterday, said he escaped the assassins' bullet through the grace of God.
Besides, Alibaloye who was hit on the head by the gun butt of the assassins, one other person within his household, Adedayo Dada and a Mopol providing security for him were also attacked.
Infact, at the time of visiting Aribaloye's residence clot of the blood of the police was still found on the floor at the entrance of the house.
But at the residence of Prof. Adesegun Ojo, a PDP governorship aspirant, the 7-man gang met resistance from the private security who confronted them frontally.
During the attack on Ojo, the hoodlums nearly lost two members of their gang who sustained serious injury in the head and arms from gunshots by the vigilante groups providing security in the compound.
Osun
In Osun State, ahead of yesterday's primaries, the state chapter of the PDP was able to reach a consensus agreement which led to the adoption of three senatorial candidates to fly the party's tickets in the general elections.
In Osun East, Senator Iyiola Omisore, was adopted as a consensus candidate, just as Alhaji Isiaka Adeleke, former civilian governor of Osun State was adopted as consensus candidate to replace Senator Olasunkanmi Akinlabi, who stepped down for the former governor.
In Osun Central, retired Assistant Inspector General of Police (AIG), Simon Oduoye, was adopted as consensus candidate. He replaces Senator Kolawole Ogunwale who is running for governorship in 2007.
Kwara
In Kwara State, the senatorial primaries was marred by violence and was boycotted by two aspirants of the party for alleged partisanship on the part of the party's leadership in the state.
The aspirants, Chief Bode Oyedepo from Kwara South and Hon. Yinusa Yahaya from the Kwara North stayed away from the venue of the primaries. They alleged that the primaries was staged managed by the power that be in the party and vowed to protest the issue to the national secretariat of the PDP, Abuja.
While Yahaya contested against incumbent senator Mohammed Ahmed for the Kwara North seat, Oyedepo had sought to slug it out with Simeon Ajibola, also an incumbent senator for Kwara South.
It was gathered that the PDP leadership had favoured Ahmed and Ajibola and only resorted to the primaries when efforts to make the challengers step down failed.
Protests by Oyedepo and his group who complained about cheating that led to the violence. The fracas was later brought under control and even a camera belonging to a journalist who was monitoring the primary was reportedly smashed by the secretary of one of the local governments in the zone.
Reacting to the development, Chief Press Secretary to governor Bukola Saraki, Alhaji Mashu'd Adebimpe denied the alleged involvement of goverment in the confusion that trailed the primaries."
In Kwara Central senatorial district, the daughter of the Strongman of Ilorin Politics, Senator Gbemisola Saraki was the only the candidate adopted by consensus for the 2007 elections in the state.
Governor Saraki who monitored the early part of the primary at Bode Saadu for the Northern senatorial district described the election as peaceful, saying he was informed that Yahaya stepped down for Ajibola.
Yahaya on his part also alleged that some of those who came as delegates were accredited at the Kwara State Government House the previous night. But Ahmed disagreed and explained that the procedure for accrediting delegates followed the laid down rules.
Oyo
Protests yesterday trailed the conduct of the senatorial primaries in Oyo State as one of the senators seeking re-election, Robert Koleosho representing Oyo North lost his return mandate, while another Teslim Folarin representing Oyo Central was re-elected by the delegates.
In another dimension, the son of Chief Richard Akinjide (SAN), Second Republic Justice Minister, Yomi lost his bid to pick a senatorial ticket while the son of the strongman of Ibadan politics, Kamoru Adedibu got a sweeping victory in Oyo South.
Koleosho was defeated by Gbenga Babalola who, in 2003 had conceded the mandate after he won the primaries on the prodding of the strongman.
While the outcome of the Northern senatorial primaries was still sketchy as at press time, some delegates including three other aspirants at the CRIN staff school, venue of the Southern senatorial primaries walked out to protest the conduct of the primaries which they said was antithetical to the guidelines of the party.
The protesting aspirants included, Chief Bayo Ilaka, Yomi Akinjide and Kayode Afolabi. They claimed that the primaries were conducted to favour the incumbent who is backed by Adedibu.
Ilaka alleged that most of his delegates were denied entry into the venue of the primaries while some of his delegates who entered the venue alleged that all the delegates from the four Oyo councils; Atiba, Afijio, Oyo East and Oyo West councils were not allowed to vote. They all staged protest walk out around 1.30pm
Owing to this, THISDAY gathered that Adedibu and his henchmen allegedly decided to steal the show by ensuring that it takes charge of proceedings at the venue, a development, which led to the massive protest that greeted the distruption by delegates and other concerned indigenes of the state as a result of the distruption of proceedings.
Anambra
However, in Anambra State, it was a long battle as a total of twenty two aspirants went into the field to contest for the three senatorial tickets in the State in yesterday's PDP primaries.
A breakdown of figure showed that the Anambra Central Senatorial District had the highest number of aspirants with nine, followed by Anambra south with seven aspirants, while Anambra North had six aspirants.
Among the top contenders were two incumbent senators, Mrs. Joy Emodi and Dr. Ugochukwu Uba. Emodi contested in the North, while Uba sought PDP's ticket in the South senatorial zone.
Others are the former Anambra State Governor, Dr. Chinwoke Mbadinuju, who alongside Chief Obinna Uzor, Okey Enemuo slugged it out with Senator Uba.
In the North Senatorial Zone, Emodi was challenged by Mrs. Jessie Balonwu, Prince John Emeka , among others. In the central zone, where the incumbent Senator, Dr. Ben Obi did not show interest in contesting on the platform of the PDP, the fight was between Sir Ikechukwu Abana, Chief Annie Okonkwo, Dr. Jerry Ugokwe, Hon. Chudi Offodile, Chief Nnamdi Okoye, Dr. Ausbeth Ajagu, among others.
Before the commencement of the primaries yesterday, Anambra State PDP chairman, Comrade Tony Nwoye ensured that materials were handed over to the Returning Officers in the presence of security operatives and journalists, and warned that any report of rigging at any centre would lead to a nullification of such results.
Enugu
In Enugu State, Governor Chimaroke Ogbonnaya Nnamani of Enugu State yesterday emerged as the senatorial flagbearer for the People's Democratic Party (PDP) in Enugu East Senatorial District for the 2007 general election.
With this development, indications are that Gov Nnamani who claimed he has been under pressure by some groups in the state to run for the presidential race may have abandoned his ambition.
Government officials had claimed that Nnamani has never indicated interest in going to the Senate when the rumour gained ground in the state about three days ago.
But party sources told newsmen last night in Enugu that Nnamani was among two others that emerged the winners of the PDP senatorial congress in the state.
The name of the winner of the Enugu East senatorial zone was Dr. C. O. Nnamani which is believed to be no other person than the governor, although close aides claimed they were not aware of the governor's interest in going to the senate.
The Ebeano political group had asked the former Commissioner for Sports in the State, State, Mr. Ray Nnaji, to resign his position and contest for the senatorial seat, which he did about three weeks ago.
He was said to have picked his nomination form, until three days ago, when Gov Nnamani changed his mind and asked him to step down.
Nnamani was said to have returned his fully completed forms for the senate last Thursday apparently under cover.
The latest development implies that Gov Nnamani will now replace the Senate President, Dr. Ken Nnamani who is representing the same Enugu East Senatorial District if he wins at the polls.
Other winners of the PDP senatorial primaries in the state include Chief Ayogu Eze who contested against Senator Fidelis Okoro who together with Senator Nnamani have been at war with Gov Nnamani.
Ayogu Eze, a journalist turned politician, who will represent Enugu North Senatorial District if he emerges winner in the forthcoming election, has been Commissioner for Information and Special Adviser under Nnamani,
In Enugu West, Senator Ike Ekweremadu who won the primaries contested against Mrs. Joy Ezeilo, a senior lecturer with University of Nigeria Enugu Campus, and also a human right activist.
Delta
The primaries in Delta State ended with upsets as two incumbent senators, Felix Ibru and Patrick Osakwe, lost their bids to secure PDP's tickets for re-election in the Central and North Senatorial zones, respectively.
In the North, Dr. Mrs. Mariam Ali, wife of PDP National Chairman, Senator Ahmadu Ali, defeated Senator Osakwe, just as Prof. Efenikaya, defeated incumbent Senator Ibru at the Central Senatorial Zone.
In the South, Senator James Manager won his ticket to seek re-election.
But there was some trouble in which Ned Nwoko, presidential aide on Cassava Policy Initiative and former House of Representatives member was arrested by the police for allegedly being in possession of rifles with bullet catridges.
FCT
In the Federal Capital Territory (FCT), no clear winner emerged in yesterday's primaries. Incumbent Senator Isa Maina, was beaten to fifth position by both House of Representatives member Sidi Ali and wife of Ambassador Babagana Kingibe, Hajia Ireti Kingibe.
There will be a run-off between Mrs. Kingibe and Ali this Wednesday.

Original_Search_Title: HIV/AIDS Ravages Leave Families in Anguish
Headline: Tanzania: HIV/AIDS Ravages Leave Families in Anguish
Body_Text:
Arusha — World AIDS Day observed on December 1st was an opportunity to remember the more than 25 million people worldwide who have died from AIDS and support the over 38 million people who are currently living with HIV. It was also a time to recommit ourselves to compassionate action and the promise of partnerships that are creating new hope. At family level the devastations are unbearable.
Vumilia (not real names) is an attractive 35- year- old woman. She is a widow and readily admits that her husband passed away due to complications brought about by HIV/AIDS. Vumilia has one daughter, 14 year old Kristy who has just graduated from standard 7 and is awaiting her exam results. Vumilia also lives with her 22-year old step daughter, Laura. In addition, she takes care of two children left behind by her in-laws who passed away due to cases related to HIV/AIDS. They live in the house that was left behind when her husband died in 1998. She has lived there for 16 years.
There is a small shamba that surrounds the house. Vumilia and her family plant maize and cassava in this half-acre piece of land. " This food is not enough to feed me and my family so I am forced to rent another small piece of land in order to grow food," Vumilia explained.
Vumilia's story is not a new one, nor is it surprising in Tanzania nowadays. One could say that the majority of our families have been touched by HIV/AIDS in one way or another. The most scary aspect of HIV/AIDS is that its ascent is fast and sure, seemingly with no impediments on its way. It is taking a frightful grip in today's society.
Vumilia was one of the lucky few who got to know World Vision Tanzania after her husband died. At that time, her church had a program called "Huduma kwa Watoto" (Assistance for Children). The church was sensitizing villagers to go for HIV testing. In 2000, after receiving advice from HW she decided to go to Bukoba town to be tested because her stomach was constantly bothering her. She saw a female counsellor who tested her and gave her the dreaded results.
She was extremely ill and bed-ridden when World Vision staff received news about her and made a trip to her home. They found her emaciated and miserable, lying on her bed and unable to move. She was taken to the World Vision funded VCT centre where she got medication.
"Every month I make the trip to Bukoba, 94 kilometres away, to get my CD-4 count taken and receive ARV's that are free through government programs," she revealed.
Due to the strength and effects of the drugs, she receives assistance of quarterly rations of food, as she does not have a constant supply of balanced diet.
Vumilia tries her best to visit the clinic regularly but sometimes she is unable to make it. This past October she was unable to get her drugs because she could not afford the 8,000 tshs bus fare for a round trip to Bukoba town.
The doctor has ordered her to reserve her strength and to stop doing any manual labour. She cannot work in anyone's farm any more to earn any money for her upkeep and that of her family. Her biggest dream is that her daughter is able to get further education. She believes that her daughter will get passing grades for standard seven but fears that she will be unable to go to secondary school.
Vumilia would like to be able to get capital to start a small business on her property to sell small food items such as tomatoes and onions and household necessities to make a little money for her daughter's education.
World Vision Tanzania has the budget capacity to sponsor 25 orphans through high school, but due to high need in the area, is squeezing 40 orphans into this program. Money in the Rushwa ADP is a huge challenge so a lot of children like Vumilia's daughter, Kristy, who have the grades to make it to secondary school, are left behind. They end up on the islands working for low wages and sleeping with fishermen to make ends meet. In this way, these young girls acquire HIV at a very young age and spread it fast among the fishing community and the village boyfriends they left back home. This becomes an endless circle that leads to more deaths in this small, rural community. World Vision Tanzania is making the effort to give vocational training to young people so that they get out of the practice of leaving the village for employment, as this practice has proved to be deadly for the villagers.
The VCT centre has also taken a central role in counselling, testing and assisting all the villagers. To help those who are positive to live their lives positively, and those who are negative to keep this negative status.

Original_Search_Title: A Pig for a Name
Headline: Uganda: A Pig for a Name
Body_Text:
Kampala — MONDAY
The Marathon is finally over and though I was not the winner, there were many others behind me. I used my experience from my boxing days to complete the race. The thing is to look around and find motivation for success. I looked around and there were old people, fat people, short people and women! I decided that none of them would win me!
TUESDAY
My next challenge is to see how to cash in on the ongoing name-for-pig project. Who cares if some mzungu believes I can be called Hornsleth or whomever, as long as he parts with a domestic animal. Such a name for me, is better than names like Zirabamuzaale, Nakuzambwa, Munakukama or Bakalikwila!
WEDNESDAY
There is a problem with rearing pigs and goats: Laura has refused to be called Mrs. Hornysleth; the doctor has given me a long list of things I am not to eat or die. These include all dairy products. I am to eat only cassava, potatoes and katogo!
THURSDAY
The rains have heard our cries for the re-filling of Lake Victoria. Next week a few friends and I will visit the lake to make an environmental impact assessment of this month's rains on the lake. But the people in Bwaise and Kalerwe are cursing the day they agreed to settle in those swamps!
FRIDAY
It is Friday when I had promised Laura an outing but the good Lord might yet be with me and more rains come down. As much as she loves an outing, the girl hates cold and muddy nights in Ssebbagala's pot holed City. If it rains the outing is off and some money is saved and I remain loaded!
SATURDAY
Now that they have kidnapped an artiste just for making a film and showing it, I have to be very careful since I am an upcoming film writer and producer! My head is full of film projects, I am afraid if they see what is inside my medulla oblongata they may kidnap me! I need to go to Police for protection?
SUNDAY
Since God is everywhere and today Umeme has had mercy on us, I will stay and attend church via television. I will either tune in to God via California or our own miracle Centre here in Rubaga.

Original_Search_Title: Two More Killed in Floods As State Says It Will Build Roads
Headline: Kenya: Two More Killed in Floods As State Says It Will Build Roads
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Three more people died in floods yesterday as the Government promised to rebuild roads in Northern Kenya in the next 10 days.
Weather experts also predicted that the heavy rains would go on into January.
The whereabouts of 14 people was unknown by yesterday as Mnazini, Banghi and Galili villages in Tana River districts were cut off by the floods.
The Red Cross Society of Kenya regional disaster preparedness coordinator Mr Mahdi Mohamed described the situation as very critical, following an outbreak of cholera and other diseases in the area.
Food supplies
"More than 20,000 people have been marooned by the floods in this area and need urgent supplies of food, shelter, medicine, nets and other essential facilities," he said.
Galili location Chief Waticho Lango said 14 people had been marooned at Banghi village.
The minister for Roads and Public Works, Mr Simeon Nyachae, said all materials needed to rebuild culverts in the area were already on site.
Engineers from his ministry were waiting for the water to subside to lay the culverts, which were washed away.
The minister who presided over a graduation ceremony at the Kenya Institute of Highway Building and Technology, said restoring the roads in the area would take longer than in Coast province, because the water had not yet subsided.
Work is also expected to start soon on bridges linking Lodwar and Kapenguria towns and another one in Mwingi district.
Said Nyachae: "I wish to assure Kenyans that my ministry has moved very swiftly to have the situation rectified and it will normalise very soon".
Access to rural areas was being improved through the Roads 2000 programme targeting 34 districts at a cost of Sh6.2 billion, the minister added.
In Budalangi, District Officer Nicholas Gibiti said Mr Ondwasi Khadoka, 40, drowned as he uprooted submerged cassava on his farm.
And more than 1,000 people have been displaced by the floods.
It is the first time in the past two years for the people in the area to be displaced following the erection of dykes on River Nzoia.
In Migori, four head of cattle were killed in floods. The animals were grazing on the plain of Kabuto in Nyatike division when they were swept away by the raging waters.
Families in Nyora and Kabuto started moving to higher ground.
In Nyatike, several bridges have been swept away, disconnecting it from the rest of Migori District. On thursday the officer in charge of operations at the Disaster response centre in Nairobi Major Stephen Sane had indicated that government focus will shift to the Western part of Kenya following the heavy rains being experienced in the area.
Consequently the department has directed the provincial administration to ensure those living in lowlands prone to floods move out to higher grounds, he added.
In Kericho, the Kurkut Bridge in Konoin was washed away.
Residents are unable to move their farm produce to markets.
In Isiolo, 3,000 families have been left without a means of livelihood after three irrigation schemes - Badan Bora village, with 147 farms, Fafi with 66 farms and Malkasericho with 20 farms - were submerged.
Search starts
In Makueni, police and local residents were yesterday searching for the body of a 16-year-old student at Makueni Boys High School, who drowned in a dam on Thursday afternoon.
Makueni police boss Nyambu Mwakio said Mutie Muthui drowned while swimming in Mosengoni dam.
The body of a 30-year-old man, who was last week swept away by a swollen river in Mbooni Division was found yesterday morning.
In Keiyo, the disaster management committee has appealed to families living in landslide prone areas to move to other areas.
River Miriu in Nyando district burst its banks yesterday, displacing more than 8,000 families.

Original_Search_Title: Aguariavwodo, NDDC boss  reveals- I Wasted Four Years at Home 'Cos My Dad Couldn't Pay My Fees
Headline: Nigeria: Aguariavwodo, NDDC boss  reveals- I Wasted Four Years at Home 'Cos My Dad Couldn't Pay My Fees
Body_Text:
MANAGING Director of the Niger-Delta Development Commission (NDDC), Mr. Emmanuel Aguariavwodo spoke to Saturday Vanguard in Port-Harcourt recently on how he stayed at home for four years after finishing his primary education in 1968 because he had no money to go to secondary school until one day when Olorogun Micheal Ibru heard about his plight and offered him scholarship.
Even after he obtained his first university degree, he still had to sell printing papers in Lagos to survive until he got a job as a journalist. God took control of his life thereafter, as he was compensated with the NDDC job after losing his re-election bid to the House of Representatives. However, he was suspended by President Olusegun Obasanjo some years later but still got his job back. Why was he really suspended and how did he get a clean bill of health? Mr. Aguariavwodo who is in the governorship race in Delta State on the PDP platform answered this and more in this encounter.
My childhood memories
My earliest memory as a child while growing up was when I held a flag in my hand and marching in the field in 1953, I was in primary one then and I think the defunct Mid-West region had just been created out of the Western region. It was interesting holding the national flag. I also remember going to farm with my mother to uproot cassava every morning before going to school in the village.
There was this day I went to farm one morning with my mother, it was very cold and when I got back, I said I would wash just my hands, my legs and face, my father was so mad that he gave me some very good lashes and carried me on his bicycle to the school and dumped me in the headmaster's office. I did not find it funny because I was brought before the whole class, it was then my father left and after a while, the headmaster started advising me that he knew that I was a brilliant boy but I should not do that again.
I also remember that when the oil companies came, they dumped chemicals into the stream that we used to swim when we are coming back to the farm and I did not know that I was swimming in toxic-infested water. These are some of the things I could remember about my early childhood apart from school, which was very interesting. I was the only child of my father for 15 years and apart from that incident in which my father took me to the school, there was really nothing that he flogged me for. I was also a chorister in the church, African Church and I still sing up till now and I handle it proficiently by even assisting others who want to go into singing, particularly singing to the Lord.
Which means that you love praise music?
Yes, completely.
You dance also?
Yes, very well.
Like David danced to the Lord?
I am telling you (laughter).
At home for four years because there was no money to pay my fees at secondary school
Life was not really easy, never mind the position we find ourselves today. I finished my primary school in 1968 but I could not proceed to secondary school because my father could not pay my fees and we went to Lagos later to join my father. It was not until 1972 before I was able to enter secondary school. So, for four years I was in Lagos doing nothing.
It was Olorogun Michael Ibru who came to my rescue; he gave me full life scholarship. I actually told him that I wanted to become a mechanic but he looked at me and laughed. He handed me over to somebody and said I should go to school until I am tired of schooling. So, that's how I went to school. And then I finished secondary school, went to university, did my NYSC in Kaduna, worked there for two years, came to Lagos where I had no work and to survive, I was selling printing papers.
I was actually hanging out with a friend until I got a job as a journalist in the African Guardian. And I can tell you that hanging out on street corners to sell papers is not an easy thing because you are in-between the Indians and those that owned shops in Idumota, the Igbos. I don't know who is smarter, the Indians or the Igbos (laughs). If you are caught in-between them, you cannot survive.
There was this Indian who wanted to employ me for N100 when I told him I had a BSC degree but I felt it was scandalous and opted to still be selling papers and hanging out with friend until I got a job. These were challenging times and of course, life went on and I left Guardian, I went to work with the man who gave me scholarship to go to school. I served him for seven to eight years before going out to work for myself.
How did you land the NDDC job?
I was a member of the House of Representatives and wanted to go back after my first tenure but somehow at the PDP primaries, I was not favoured. And I said well, thank God, if I had been given the opportunity to serve my people for four years and the system did not want me back, glory be to God. But four or five months after I left the House, the job came.
How is the NDDC coping with militants in the Niger-Delta region?
NDDC is coping very well in the Niger- Delta; it has come of age in carrying out the roles for which it was set up. What is needed now is for it to continue to carry out its programmes, depending on how much money it has in its own purse.
What exactly is the funding problem of the NDDC, we hear that some of the contributors are failing the commission?
Nobody is actually failing the commission in terms of funding at this point in time. What people are looking at is whether because of the expectations of the people whether that money is adequate and not that the contributors to the funds of the NDDC are failing. No, nobody is failing.
So, many groups in the region have cried on behalf of the commission in terms of inadequacy of funds, are you saying that you are getting your dues from the contributors?
Yes, we are getting what we are supposed to get, whether it is adequate or not is a different matter. People believe that because of the performance of the NDDC that if more funds are given to the NDDC, it will do even more. That is the issue, it is not that we are not getting what we are supposed to get from the Federal Government and the oil companies, we are getting from them and they are paying as and when due.
Are the militants troubling the NDDC as they trouble the oil companies?
No, we are helping to develop the area, the environment as it were, so we don't have any problem with them. It was in the early years of the NDDC that because of the expectations and the need for empowerment of the youths, which then was seen as either giving of cash, that was misconstrued and that had to stop.
Now, nobody talks about such things in the NDDC, we implement programmes which affect lives of the people, the different youth groups, women and cooperative societies. These are things they will use to create employment for themselves and even create wealth. So, that era of coming to us for money has passed.
How youths tried to swindle NDDC in the past
They would come, for instance, and tell you that they were coming to visit the NDDC to discuss some issues, then they would hire buses and when the buses arrive, you would have to pay for the buses, and then we told them that if you had money to pay for the buses, you must have some money, so why are you asking us for money to pay? They would say that they chartered the buses on trust and we need to pay for them. But all that was in the past.
We are now involved in programmes that touch their lives as I said earlier. For instance, the transport sector, which we looked into and provided buses, boats to empower them. We are also engaging them in agriculture.
Don't you think that there is some sense in the resort to hostage taking by militants because that has made the Federal Government to pay the region a little more attention in the past few months?
No, it is not necessary to take hostages. It is not recommended and it should be stopped. That puts us in bad light and creates the impression that we are a violent people. Niger-Delta people are not violent people, I have always said that. If the issues that led to such violence were tackled promptly in those days, in the early years when the Warri crisis started, these issues would not have resulted but we are not a violent people. So, hostage taking and all that should be stopped. I was involved in hostage negotiation in those early years when I was in the House of Representatives, that was about 1999 when the youths hijacked one helicopter with two pilots from Rivers state and working with an oil company.
A group of representatives and I went and negotiated with the hijackers up to the point when we negotiated them out of the ransom they wanted paid and what they were then asking for was the money they had used to cook for the white men, which we said well, if the SPDC wants to oblige with that, fine, if not, nobody was going to pay you any ransom. So, I have never believed that is the way to ask for development to come to our people. Hostage taking is a criminal act, it has nothing to do with the demand of rights of our people, yes, we need more but that should be discussed.
But the argument of the militants is that the NDDC and other development agents have failed the region and that is why they resorted to such means to draw attention to the plight of the people...
I have said earlier that the NDDC has not failed at all. The NDDC has done very well and I will say it all over at any time that if we tell you, for instance, that we have built a road somewhere, it is there. If we tell you that we have awarded a contract, we have awarded; if we tell you it is completed, it is completed; if we say it is being put to use, it is being put to use. We don't make promises and fail. If the project is not yet on stream because of the way we do our business where we tie every project to a particular time, which provides the guarantee based on which funds were released. We hold that bank if a contractor misbehaves; we had to negotiate with banks in the past for money to be returned to us for contracts that were not properly executed. So, we are a very well-run organization.
In what ways are state governments in the region helping or not helping the NDDC in the development of the Niger Delta?
We are partners with the state governments. Whatever we do, projects that we carry out, we turn it to them after we complete them because we don't have the structure to run schools and hospitals for instance. We're co-operating in that regard. In fact, we have gone beyond that with our master plan completed, we now have the partners for sustainable development, which include the federal, state, local governments and oil companies, where we all look at all what we are doing to avoid duplication of efforts, save time and resources.
We know that some money belonging to the NDDC for contracts are still trapped in some failed banks, what are you doing to retrieve these funds?
No, not much money, but as you know, almost all government parasatals have money in all these banks. It is not just NDDC but that matter is being handled by the Central Bank of Nigeria and relevant directorates because most of the failed banks as you know are being acquired by other banks.
We observe that a lot of people in the NDDC are going for political offices in Delta, Rivers, Bayelsa, Akwa Ibom. You want to be the next governor of Delta...
Well, let me tell you about myself. I contested for the office of the governor of Delta State in 1997 and the structures, which I formed in 1997/1998, are still there and then I contested election into the House of Representatives under the former APP in 1999 and I won.
Why did you not consider remaining in NDDC to help develop your region?
I have been under tremendous pressure from my people to come and contest the governorship race because of the need to place Delta on a proper footing, continue and build upon what the present administration has done, to bring the state to a new frontier and a new horizon. Like I said, the NDDC is like a training ground. We have followed the President and seen how he does things and we have learnt a lot. Some of us in the NDDC believe that the reforms of President Olusegun Obasanjo are good for this country and should be implemented in the different states where we come from. So, those are the things that are propelling me.
Specifically, if I am given the mandate to rule Delta State, I will on May 29, 2007 declare free education at primary and secondary levels, inclusive of books. Not that you will say free education and somebody will go to school without books, without benches and all that. Secondly, 30 per cent of my cabinet will be made up of women in line with the doctrine of the United Nations that we should encourage women at all levels and my total belief is that if you empower one woman, you empower a nation.
I will also tackle agriculture and make sure every ward in the state has one tractor each to boost agricultural production, while storage facilities would be provided. The aim here is to provide enough food and also generate employment. We can only store when there is enough production and so, we must boost production. These are just parts of the programmes we are going to put in place to touch the lives of the people when we come into power.
What really happened sometime ago when you were suspended as the managing director of the NDDC?
Well, there were some administrative issues which were not completely captured by some of the people I worked with and reports were made to Mr. President, which led to that situation. But when investigations were carried out, they saw that no wrong had been done and I was brought back to office.
Are you ready for possible probe by the ICPC and EFCC?
Yes, of course.

Original_Search_Title: Way Out of N-Delta Crises, By 65 Nobel Laureates
Headline: Nigeria: Way Out of N-Delta Crises, By 65 Nobel Laureates
Body_Text:
Lagos — In an apparent internationalisation of the struggles of the people of Niger Delta region, 65 Nobel laureates comprising the Commission of Nobel Laureates on Peace, Equity and Development in the Niger Delta Region of Nigeria have proposed measures to prevent the spiral of deadly violence in the oil-rich yet impoverished Niger Delta region.
The group of laureates has also alereted Nigerians on the dangers of postponing the general elections of 2007.
The report, which was released yesterday, is with a view to ensuring that Nigeria's Niger Delta crisis does not escalate and spill across Nigeria's borders.
According to Elie Wiesel, who received the Nobel Prize for Peace in 1986, "the Commission was established to address injustice, mitigate suffering, and prevent conflict from escalating and spilling across Nigeria's borders. The Commission is an objective and independent body beholden to no government or enterprise."
On the 2007 elections, the laureates warned: "The Commission maintains that under no circumstances should the 2007 elections in Nigeria be postponed or the constitution amended to prevent the peaceful transition of power. Nigeria's history of rigged elections is just part of its problems with governance. To crack down on corruption, the Commission calls on the government to prosecute high-profile violators and reduce the number of officials covered by constitutional immunity. They also urge civil society to expand its watchdog role."
The laureates specifically focused on the development/environmental crisis in the country.
The areas of focus include:
"- Proposes that oil companies in the Niger Delta states publish audits of their revenues.
"- Calls upon oil companies to establish a "Community Investment Fund" earmarking a percentage of gross revenues directly to local community organizations working in the fields of health, education, micro-credit, and infrastructure development.
"- Asks oil companies to clean up oil spills, eliminate gas flares, and provide special compensation to communities devastated by environmental degradation.
"- Proposes that oil companies train and hire residents from affected populations in the Delta region."
Nigeria's Professor Wole Soyinka, who won the Nobel Prize for Literature in 1986, has been known to be an advocate of a peaceful resolution to the Niger Delta crisis, as against the frontal approach of the Federal Government of Nigeria.
On the 2007 elections, other Nigerians have also expresed fears that the election may not hold as a result of signals emanating from the Independent National Electoral Commission, INEC, which is engaged in a registration exercise - the exercise, in the estimation of some Nigerians, has not ben successful.
But INEC Chairman, Profesor Maurice Iwu, insists that the elections would hold and that all INEC needs is the patience of Nigerians.
Niger Delta: What Nigeria must do now
The Commission of Nobel Laureates on Peace, Equity and Development in the Niger Delta Region of Nigeria is an endeavour of the Elie Wiesel Foundation for Humanity (EWF). The Commission is an objective and independent body beholden to no government or enterprise. Recommendations are intended to mitigate human suffering and prevent violence that could engulf Nigeria and spill over its borders
Background
The Niger Delta region includes 6 states of which Bayelsa, Rivers, and Delta states produce the most oil and suffer from significant violence. The region is a maze of creeks, streams, and swamps formed by the Niger River as it divides into 6 main tidal channels before spilling out into the Atlantic Ocean. Before oil was discovered by Royal Dutch Shell near the village of Oloibiri in 1956,1 the Niger Delta region had the most extensive lowland tropical and fresh water forests, aquatic ecosystems, and biodiversity in West Africa.2 Residents cultivated rice, sugarcane, plantain, palm oil, yams, cassava, and timber.3 At least seven million Ijaw, Ogoni, Itsekiri, Andoni, Ibibio and a host of other peoples speaking distinct languages live in Bayelsa, Rivers, and Delta states alone. Most of the oil and gas reserves are found on Ijaw territory.
Windfall
Since 1970, Nigeria has earned at least $300 billion from energy development. It took in $45 billion in 2005 alone. With about 40 billion barrels of proven oil reserves, Nigeria currently produces about 2.4 million barrels of oil/day. It is estimated that Nigeria will produce 4 million barrels/day by 2010.4 It is also home to the world's seventh largest supply of natural gas.5 Nigeria's low-sulfur oil - so-called "sweet" crude - is much sought after by refineries in the United States, which purchases about 40% of current production.6 Nigeria provides 11% of U.S. oil.7 It is the fifth largest source of oil for the United States.
The U.S. Department of Energy estimates that over 4,000 oil spills discharging more than two million barrels of crude have occurred in the Niger Delta since 1960.8 Oil slicks cover the region. Blow-outs and leaks affect creeks, streams, and related traditional sources of livelihood, poisoning the water supply, destroying mangrove forests, eroding soil plots, and killing aquatic life.9 Hundreds of well-sites have flares, which come from the burning of associated gas. The flares heat up everything nearby and turn day into night, releasing 25 million tons of CO2 and 12 million tons of methane annually.10 Resulting sulfuric acid mists damage plants and forests.11 Flares pollute rain water, cause acid rain,12 and contribute to climate change.13
Many residents suffer from oil poisoning. Crude oil enters the body through skin absorption, ingestion of food and water, and inhalation of oil and dust particles.14 Oil poisoning also causes respiratory ailments.15 In addition, residents suffer from a plethora of water borne diseases such as malaria, dysentery, tuberculosis, typhoid, and cholera.16 Life expectancy is low and child morbidity rates are high.17
Poverty
According to the United Kingdom's Department for International Development, "Nigeria has some of the worst social indicators in the world."18 For example, the World Bank estimates that 92.4% of Nigerian women are living on less than $2/day in 2006.19 Though the Niger Delta's energy assets provide nearly three-quarters of government revenues and roughly 90% of foreign exchange earnings, they have not resulted in socio-economic development. Unemployment hovers at about 90%. Many schools and hospitals lack staff, equipment, and necessary supplies.20 Most riverine areas remain unconnected to electricity grids. Bayelsa State has only one multi-lane, paved road in the entire state, which leads only to the capital. In April 2006, President Olusegun Obasanjo promised a $1.8 billion road-building project for the Niger Delta region. No action was taken. Many Niger Delta residents see his pledge as yet another opportunity for elites to enrich themselves with "bogus contracts."21
Violence
Disenfranchised by rigged elections in 1999 and 2003, many youths have joined local militias that sabotage oil facilities and kidnap oil workers. Though violence has been sporadic to date, radicalization is on the rise with some groups threatening to disassociate from Nigeria unless the government satisfies demands for political rights, economic development, and social justice. Many young people are migrating from their villages to Port Harcourt and other urban areas. The Niger Delta region has the highest incidence of HIV/AIDS in Nigeria; prostitution and teenage pregnancies are on the rise.22 There is also a spiral of hooliganism, robbery and spontaneous violence.23
The Movement for the Emancipation of the Niger Delta, among others, vows to continue attacking oil installations and Nigerian military units until oil assets are brought under local control. Impoverished and hopeless youths are fed up with the present state of affairs, which degrades the environment and loots local wealth. As a result of recent violence and the kidnapping of international oil workers, Nigeria's overall production of petroleum has been reduced by 25%. At the official level, President Obasanjo has named two peace envoys: human rights activist Fr. Matthew Kukah for the Ogoni dispute with Shell, and Ken Wiwa, son of slain Ogoni leader Ken Saro-Wiwa, for the region as a whole. Delta NGOs have undertaken a variety of dialogue and conflict prevention initiatives. However, civil society representatives warn that violence will escalate if the Nigerian authorities do not address its root causes. The detention of community leaders, like Dokubo Asari, might also spark further conflict.
Governance
Nigeria's military governments centralized control of the oil industry under the presidency. The 1969 Petroleum Act transferred all energy earnings to the federal government and the 1978 Land Use Act assigned all land ownership to state governments. 24 The 1999 Constitution further vests predominant powers in the executive branch. The constitution does, however, stipulate that 13% of oil revenues be returned to the oil-producing states in addition to their share of revenues distributed to all 36 states. President Obasanjo also serves as oil minister, thereby limiting public scrutiny of the country's corrupt oil sector.25
Corruption
Corruption undermines economic growth, jeopardizes financial stability, and undermines confidence in government institutions. It also leads to criminality that has corrosive effects on governance and the rule of law. Transparency International ranks Nigeria 152 out of 159 countries in its corruption index.26 Federal and state governments have stolen or wasted $380 billion since the country became independent in 1960.27 The culture of corruption is pervasive affecting state government as well as local officials; 31 of the 36 state governors are currently under investigation for crimes and corruption.28
The fact that Nigeria's coffers are awash in oil revenue further fuels corruption by national and local political elites. All state government allocations are given directly to the governor by the federal government, who is then supposed to distribute funds to local governments. Allocations to state governments are sizable. Rivers State, for instance, received $74 million and Bayelsa State $57.6 million in July 2006.29 In practice, governors withhold large portions of such allocations for dubious projects, which are frequently awarded to cronies. Kingpin politicians are the primary beneficiaries of oil bunkering operations.
For example, the governor of Bayelsa State was recently impeached for corruption. It was discovered that he used public funds to acquire several multimillion dollar properties in the United Kingdom, set-up seven British bank accounts, and buy a yacht.
Elections
Local and international monitors reported ballot stuffing and tally altering during elections in 1999 and 2003. According to the European Union, the 2003 elections fell far short of international standards for a free and fair ballot. Opposition parties were shut out of nearly all layers of government leaving voters with no real political choices and no alternatives for social action. Election victors in the Niger Delta, nearly all members of the ruling Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), are firmly aligned with party barons that put them in office.
Serious concern exists about elections scheduled for April 2007. There is widespread speculation that President Obasanjo's supporters may provoke a national crisis to justify a delay or annulment. The Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) is grossly unprepared and, in a strategy of calculated incompetence reminiscent of 2003, local monitors warn that INEC could declare that it is unable to organize the election, and thus give President Obasanjo the opportunity to declare a state of emergency and extend his term in office.
Oil companies
Shell, Mobil, Chevron, Agip, Elf, and Texaco are major partners with the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation, which retains 55-60%30 of earnings from joint ventures and heavily taxes the balance.31 These companies have been criticized in recent years for their environmental practices in the Niger Delta. They have also drawn fire for assisting the Nigerian military and endorsing its heavy-handed tactics.32
Kidnappings of Nigerian and foreign oil workers have increased in recent years. In 2004, Shell acknowledged that its business practices had inadvertently contributed to conflict, poverty, and corruption.33 Shell and several other oil companies have sought to address the situation by taking steps to improve their human rights practices and channel more development funds directly to communities. The Nigerian Extractive Industries Initiative (NEITI) holds companies and the government to a series of rights standards and best practices. It also requires regular audits. The first such audit was held in 2005. In early 2006, the federal government revealed a significant discrepancy between what the oil companies paid to government and what the government recorded. Community leaders routinely complain that promised funds often do not arrive, or that the companies do not follow through on their commitments.
A local NGO leader warns: "The people are tired of government corruption, killing and stealing. Frustration is rising and things are getting worse. There is real concern about an outbreak of violence across Nigeria. Violence will explode, especially in the Niger Delta, if there is any rigging or attempts to stop the election."34
The Way Forward
Fundamental problems must be addressed to reduce the likelihood of conflict escalation:
* The people of the Niger Delta have no duly elected representation at any level of government and thus have no formal political voice.
* The Niger Delta produces the majority of Nigeria's wealth, but still enjoys only a small portion of its returns.
*The rise of the militias in the Niger Delta is a direct result of massive unemployment and lack of socio-economic development.
* Wealth earmarked for the Niger Delta is substantial, but it is largely stolen by politicians and their supporters who benefit from continued crisis.
* The Niger Delta's ethnic diversity has been manipulated by political leaders to keep communities from collaborating on region-wide initiatives.
Nigeria's new government can demonstrate its credibility by taking the following steps:
Elections
The 2007 elections must not become the spark that ignites the Niger Delta's simmering fuse. Under no circumstances should the elections be postponed or the constitution amended to prevent the peaceful transition of power. The international community should impress upon President Obasanjo the opprobrium Nigeria would face if he manipulates events to try to hang onto power.
Nigerians must be confident that the electoral process is free and fair. To this end, the international community should support Nigerian monitoring groups and deploy large numbers of international monitors across the country, including to the Niger Delta states. Monitors should not parachute-in, but, rather, stay in-country until all the votes are counted and the results are verified. International donors can also help by supporting voter education and providing technical assistance to INEC. Nigerian authorities should facilitate the registration of international election monitors. Empowering an ombudsman to receive complaints from civil society representatives and INEC personnel about irregularities would enhance transparency and accountability. Special attention should also be paid to protect the free functioning of political opposition parties.
Corruption
Nigeria's federal and state governments should more adequately resource official anti-corruption bodies such as the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC). Donors should also finance NGOs working in the field of anti-corruption. To show that that it is serious about cracking down, the government should prosecute high-profile violators, including presidential allies. Reducing the number of officials covered by constitutional immunity would demonstrate that no one is above the law. Setting-up regional branches of the national anti-corruption commissions and a new anti-corruption ombudsman - supported by passage of the Freedom of Information Act - would help enhance the watchdog function of local communities.
Development
More equitable development is critical to mollifying aggrieved Niger Delta residents. The 2005 NEITI audit of oil revenues should be published ensuring that oil companies in NEITI are fulfilling their obligations. Remaining companies that have not signed onto NEITI should do so. To make sure that government projects actually meet local needs, civil society groups should be more extensively involved in development planning by government agencies and international donors. Oil companies can enhance socio-economic development by training and hiring local residents. They could also establish a "Community Investment Fund" channeling a specific percentage of gross revenues directly to local community organizations working in the fields of health, education, micro-credit, and infrastructure development.35 The Niger Delta Development Corporation (NDDC) should be eliminated in order to reduce bureaucracy. Eliminating the NDDC would also enable more funds to be channeled directly to community associations, in a context of proper auditing and community oversight.
The Community Investment Fund could also support clean-up, conservation and restoration activities. In addition, oil companies should clean up oil spills and eliminate gas flares. They should also provide special compensation to communities devastated by environmental degradation. The expanded use of renewable energy technologies would further reduce dependence on fossil fuels thereby limiting damage to local ecosystems.
Security
It is unrealistic to expect local militias to disarm, demobilize and reintegrate until after upcoming elections. Even then, Nigerian authorities will have to take verifiable steps to address problems before local militias give up the gun. Meanwhile, oil companies can start building confidence by phasing out their security payments to official security agencies and militia groups, emphasizing instead "community based security" for site protection. Future disarmament efforts should be based on a sustainable package of amnesty, weapons buyouts, job programs, and sensible law enforcement initiatives.
Methodology
This report is based on field interviews and secondary research. International and Nigerian experts have also contributed.
Signatories

Original_Search_Title: Woman Candidate Out to Grab Madagascar Presidency
Headline: Madagascar: Woman Candidate Out to Grab Madagascar Presidency
Body_Text:
Antananarivo — With the election of Ms Ellen John-Sirleaf of Liberia as the first woman president in African history, women on the continent are pushing hard in politics. Filled with the concepts of gender equality, African women want to deviate from mere political objects who merely clap and cheer during male-dominated elections.
Madagascar is no exception, where a woman now lines up for the country's hotly contested male-dominated presidential race set for this Sunday.
Ms Elia Ravelomanantsoa is the only woman in a 14-candidate race. She is a determined woman aged 46 who is a well-known figure in the field of human rights. She heads many organisations, including women contractors of Madagascar. She also heads a communication group, SYNERGY-FCB, which is an event-driven and recognised agency.
Ms Ravelomanantsoa is not new in political circles, as she enjoys a great wealth of political and business experience.
Instead of a party, Ms Ravelomanantsoa says she prefers to vie under the banner of a movement that aims to shape better living conditions for the people of the large Indian Ocean island. She believes that a change of government will solve the problems of Madagascar and has called on all forces, associations and groups to support her agenda.
Of course, her call has been answered.
A network of the reformed Christian Churches of more than four thousands churches has decided to throw its weight behind Elia.
Another heavyweight group called G5, which comprises natives of the five provinces of Madagascar's six provinces also decided to support Elia to realise her presidential ambitions.
Ms Ravelomanantsoa's candidature has become a source of attraction, with over 80 articles being published on her within a month.
Her supporters believe their candidate is a few months away from recording her name in history book for being the second woman president of Africa.
While not as contentious as five years ago, this year's pre-election period has seen controversy, including an apparent botched coup by a renegade general and the rejection of the candidacy of President Ravalomanana's potential top challenger.
President Ravalomanana is still the odds-on favourite but as campaigning hits fever pitch, a new possible rival emerged in Mr Herizo Razafimahaleo, a veteran politician and former deputy prime minister.
The two-time former unsuccessful presidential hopeful drew a large crowd to a rally in the capital, attracting some 15,000 people, nearly the same as President Ravalomanana did at a gathering in the same venue last weekend.
In a play on President Ravalomanana's "stay the course" campaign slogan, Mr Razafimahaleo told supporters the president could "no longer stay the course," and called for a peaceful political change in leadership.
"I thank the ex-president for the little he has done, but he can no longer accompany us on our course," he told the crowd, pledging not to exact "political vengeance" if elected.
To loud cheers, he vowed to end chronic power shortages facing the island, raise civil servants' salaries and remove surcharges on certain goods and medicines.
The size of the rally surprised some here as Mr Razafimahaleo won only four per cent of the votes in the 2001, a sharp decline from the 15 per cent he took in 1996.
But he and the president are the only candidates using helicopters to campaign across the island of around 17 million people, renowned for its unique and diverse number of lemur species.
Amid the campaign, a group of 11 candidates have signed a declaration in support of renegade General Randrianafidiosa who earlier this month urged the army to force President Ravalomanana to resign and holed up with some soldiers at a military base, sparking a firefight in which at least one was killed.
In October, several clashes broke out between police and supporters of Pierre Rajaonarivelo, an exiled opposition leader whose plans to return to contest the poll were blocked by authorities.
Mr Rajaonarivelo, an ally of former Madagascan president Didier Ratsiraka who was ousted by Mr Ravalomanana after the last polls, was repeatedly denied permission to fly to Madagascar from exile in France.
The country's last presidential elections ended in violence and chaos when Mr Ratsiraka refused to concede defeat to Mr Ravalomanana.
The impasse split the island in two - with two capitals, two governments, and a divided army - until Mr Ravalomanana was officially proclaimed president in May 2002.
Improvement of living standards and a smooth transition of political institutions have become the main campaign issues in the current campaign.
Some 6.3 million Malagasy nationals are expected to take part in the poll.
The president is elected by direct popular vote for a five-year term and the country's electoral system provides for the conduct of a run-off in the event that there is no clear winner from the first round.
Campaigning officially kicked off on November 12 and is due to end on the eve of the poll on December 2.
Price increases
The economy is a key campaign issue, with most candidates promising the electorate a better and more prosperous future once they accede to power.
The opposition is using a recent spate of fuel and rice price increases to blame Mr Ravalomanana for hardships faced by the poor. The incumbent president has since coming to power in 2002, however, managed to stabilise the economy and won the confidence of major multilateral institutions such as the World Bank and International Monetary Fund.
With an economy largely dependent on agriculture - which along with fishing and forestry contributes at least 25 per cent of Gross Domestic Product (GDP) - the island also produces coffee, cassava, bananas, maize, sugarcane, potatoes and rice.
Mining and tourism sectors have shown signs of recovery and the government has projected increases in mineral exports from about $100 million to US$150 million per year over the next 10 years.
The biggest boost to the Malagasy economy has been the discovery of oil which has recently triggered a scramble by international oil conglomerates. Initial projections are that the country could produce 60,000 barrels per day in three to four years. With potential revenue of billions of dollars, this oil boom would make the industry the biggest contributor to GDP.
Ms Ravelomanantsoa is the only candidate who has chosen to campaign on a "national" ticket.
During her rallies, Ms Ravelomanantsoa has called for calm among the electorate to ensure the process was conducive for transparent, free and fair polls.
She has written to other Southern African Development Community (SADC) leaders, the African Union and France urging them to send election observer missions to ensure transparency in the build-up to the polls. (Agencies)

Original_Search_Title: What We Have Against This Budget - NDC
Headline: Ghana: What We Have Against This Budget - NDC
Body_Text:
The following is the full text of "National Democratic Congress (NDC) Response to the 2007 Budget Statement and Economic Policy of Government".
"Ladies and gentlemen of the Media, the NDC registers its appreciation and gratitude to you for taking time off your busy schedules to honour our invitation to our press conference on the Government's 2007 Budget Statement and Economic Policy presented to Parliament by the Minister of Finance and Economic Planning on 16th November 2006. Given time constraints, our response would highlight a number of significant areas as our contribution to the debate.
Other details would be provided by our relevant Spokespersons in Parliament in the course of the week.
'The 2007 Budget philosophy is Growth within Economic Stability. The Budget is anchored on the premise that Ghana is on the Path of Sustained Economic Renaissance. The 2007 Budget is based on objectives of GPRS I and II, the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) and the African Peer Review Mechanism (APRM) Report Recommendations on the economy. The emphasis is on Private Sector Development; Human Resource Development and Good Governance and Civic Responsibility. With the Government believing in the equitable distribution of the wealth created, the Budget Statement entreats Ghanaians to create more and share more.
'The Philosophy of "Property Owing Democracy Government" of the NPP may believe in creating more wealth but it certainly does not believe in sharing the wealth created equitably. Evidence abounds in this country on how social goods and services are distributed based on partisan political considerations.
"Much as we accept that crude oil prices rose during the year as the table below shows, prices of commodities which Ghana trades in also rose significantly. In addition, debt forgiveness under HIPC and MDRI released enormous resources and there can be no excuse for the poor performance of any sector of the economy.
"Commodity Price (2006) January May-July September Crude Oil $/bbl 63.86 74.24 63.49 Gold $/oz 550.77 673.55 600.49 Cocoa Pound/mt 900.00 933.00 840.00 Diamond $/carat 31.23 38.33 32.12 Bauxite $/mt 24.36 25.18 "GDP growth in 2006 is projected at 6.2% but the growths by sector figures do not confirm this. A comparison of the 2006 performance with 2005 makes this clear.
Table A1: contribution to growth for 2005 and 2006 out-turn Sector 2005 2006 out turn (Proj'n) Agric 1.5% 2.1% Industry 1.5% 1% Services 5.5% 5.9% Percentage Growth by sectors in 2006 Target Proj. Out turn A: Agriculture 6.2 5.7 i. Crops & Livestock 6.2 6.0 ii. Cocoa 12.2 8.7 iii. Forestry & Logging 5.6 2.6 iv. Fishing 3.6 3.6 B: Industry 6.4 7.3 i. Mining & Quarrying 6.3 3.0 ii. Manufacturing 5.0 4.2 iii. Electricity & Water 4.0 23.0 iv. Construction 7.5 8.2 C: Services 5.5 6.5 i. Transport & Communication etc 6.0 7.2 ii. Wholesale, retail & trade 6.2 7.5 iii. Finance & Insurance 5.7 7.6 iv. Govt. Services 5.0 5.7 v. Comm, Social & Personal Service 4.2 4.2 vi. Prod. Of private non-profit services 3.8 4.5
"From Table A1, growth rate for the three sectors indicated in the Budget Statement puts the GDP growth rate for 2005 at 5.5% and that for 2006 at 5.9%.
For 2006 the Budget's projected growth rate of 6.2% is attributed to water and electricity sub-sector contribution to GDP, which increased phenomenally from 0.1% in 2004 to 0.3% in 2005 and to 0.7% in 2006.
The 2006 Budget estimated electricity and water production growth rates of 4.0%. But the projected out-turn shows a growth rate of 23% and it is this "significant performance from water and electricity" which partly accounts for the increase in GDP growth.
With power cuts, load shedding black outs, coupled with limited or no water supply to most communities in the country we find it difficult to believe that electricity and water production could be the "major contributing sub-sector" to the GDP growth. So far we have not seen the 'significant Government investment" in the electricity and water sub-sector which is expected to increase the overall growth in the sector. Has government investment in electricity and water led to overall growth in the sub-sector or not? Have the populace seen any improvement in the quantity and quality of power and water supply to be convinced that it is one of the major contributory factors to GDP growth?
The Budget Statement as proof of the "robustness of real sector growth and the resilience of the economy" claims that:
i) SSNIT collections from January-August 2006 increased by 30.5% compared to January-August in 2005 is this phenomenon due to increased employment, increased number of contributors, increased salaries to workers, or improved collection of arrears of SSNIT contributions?
ii) VELD registered 42,181 new motor vehicles compared to 36,051 in 2005. How does registration of vehicles show robustness and resilience of the economy?
iii) Cement production increased from 1,276,576 metric tons to 1,349,644 metric tons between 2005 and 2006. How did this translate into accommodation for the low and middle income groups?
Did this increase in cement production solve the acute housing problem in the country?
iv)It is laughable that the Budget Statement uses job vacancy adverts in the "Daily Graphic" as a measurement for increased job offering. How can a Government gloat when only 6,788 jobs are advertised for a whole year?
"Receipts "Revenue expected in 2006 is projected at 43,468.2 billion cedis but out-turn is projected at 41,357.1 or 4.9% below Budget estimates. This is in spite of the windfall receipts of 1,826.4 billion from the MDRI. Domestic tax revenues, with the exception of personal income tax; petroleum tax and export duty all other tax receipts fell below Budget estimates. Why did this happen and what steps is government taking to ensure that targets are met in the future? Grants as receipts for 2006 was 91% of programmed grants in spite of the fact that all HIPC and MDRI programmed grants were fully received.
Project and Programme grants fell short by 11%; Loan receipts fell short of projected receipts by about 14% and Dependence on foreign aid to support budget increased in 2006 as evidenced by 2005 tax revenues accounting for 58.39% of total receipts while tax revenue for 2006 accounted for only 56.39% of total receipts.
"Payment: "Projected external debt service is 3,392.3 billion cedis less than estimated. This is due to debt write off under the MDRI. Payments into the DACF and GETFUND were less than budgeted for, since tax revenue shortfalls were encountered. Personal emoluments projected actuals exceeded Budget estimate of 9,999 billion cedis by 982.5 billion cedis due to as Government would put it, 'extra-ordinary pressures on the public sector labour market'.
"Net borrowing from domestic sources to finance Budget will amount to 2,216 billion cedis by year end. The Minister of Finance attributes this to "unanticipated transfers to VRA" and "under recovery at TOR" etc. Can the Minister inform us of total transfers to VRA bearing in mind that in the Supplementary Budget, an amount over 350 billion cedis was also transferred to VRA. How does Government intend solving the low tariffs paid to VRA by VALCO? What factors account for the under-recovery at TOR since the NPA is supposed to be fully recovering all ex-refinery costs.
"With the overall Budget balance recording a deficit of 5591.8 billion cedis or 4.9% of GDP as opposed to Budget estimate of 4.5% of GDP, with domestic primary balance showing a deficit of 2.1% of GDP against a projection of 1.4% of GDP and with the domestic debt-to-GDP ratio of 10.1% against the target of 8.7% why are we being told that the economy is on track?
"Public Debt "As at end 2005, the stock of public debt stood at US $8,382.5 million or 77.96 trillion cedis. As a result of the MDRI and HIPC debt relief, public debt as at end 2006 is equivalent to US $4,670.59 million or 43.44 trillion cedis. This debt stock after the MDRI and HIPC relief is still higher than the 41 trillion cedis debt stock that the NPP Government on assuming power in 2001 claimed and still claims the NDC Government bequeathed to them and for which they have been castigating the NDC Government for creating an 'economic mess'. The NPP Government should provide details of all loans they have contracted since they assumed office and the use to which those loans have been put.
"Exports and Imports The country's merchandise exports are expected to fetch US $385.7 million made up of:-
US $(m) Cocoa 1,269.8 715,660 tonnes Gold 1,302.1 2,086 mil oz Timber 196.7 Other 1,089.1 O/W VALCO 132.2 Total 3,857.7 "Total imports including oil for 2006 are expected to be US $6,852.4 million. Our merchandise exports, therefore, cover only 56.3% of our total import bill. Unrequited transfers are expected to be US $8.0m as announced by the President recently.
"The much touted reserves of 3.3 month of imports (US$1.78 billion) was achieved mainly as a result of HIPC and MDRI debt relief initiatives and not as a result of exceptional export performance.
"Outlook for 2007 "For 2007 the following targets have been set: - GDP growth rate of 6.5% minimum - Year end inflation of 7-9% - Average inflation of 8.8% - International reserves of at least three months' of import cover - Domestic primary deficit of 0.6% of GDP and - Overall Budget deficit of 3.2% of GDP
"From 2007, Ghana will wean itself off IMF financial assistance but we will still utilise the Policy Support Instrument (PSI) under which we shall continue to: I) Seek policy advice II) Seek and obtain technical assistance in the design, implementation and monitoring of economic programmes and III) Use the IMF's goodwill to obtain benefits from our relationship with the international community.
"In simple English, the Fund will continue to be our external examiner only that this time it would not grant us balance of payment support. The Government would adhere to the debt sustainability criteria established under the MDRI rules. It would, therefore, not resort to borrowing at usurious rates. Concessional loan and grant financing would be the financial choice. We endorse this policy since come 2009 the NDC Government would not like to inherit an economy steeped in debt.
"2007 resource Mobilisation and Allocation
"Resource mobilization is as follows: Cedis billion "Total receipts 54,315.9 Domestic revenue (tax & non-tax) 37,532.2 Grants, loans HIPC & Mdri inflows 16,783.7 "Allocation of resources is as follows: Cedis billion "Total allocation 54,315.9 Statutory payments 14,818.1 Discretionary payments 39,497.8 - o/w i) Personal emoluments 13,167.0
ii) Investments 11,565.2 -o/w a) Domestically financed 2,598.9 b) Foreign financed 8,966.3 "In 2007 about 31% of our budget is to be donor funded. Investement which is to propel growth, accounts for only 21.3% of budgetary expenditure. Out of the Total investment of 11 565.2 billion cedis only 2 598.9 cedis or 4.2% is financed from our own domestic resources.
"Wages and salaries "From the budget statement provision has been made for only 20% increase in salaries.
