All the talk of enhanced salaries effective January 2007 by ministers and NPP propagandists is only nothing but propaganda. Average salary increases in 2007 cannot exceed 20%. The Public Sector wage reforms have taken centre-stage since the budget was read. According to the Finance Minister, the Public Service has 650,000 employees. Out of this number 350,000 are employed by 110 subvented organisations and 300,000 in the civil service. These 650,000 employees' remuneration accounts for 9.5% of GDP. The minister states that the public sector wage bill of 9.5% of GDP is twice as high as what obtains in Nigeria and the Gambia. In spite of this seeming high expenditure on public sector, the government itself and all Ghanaians agree that basically the public service emolument as a per cent of GDP is high because our GDP is low. We need to improve on productivity to improve on our GDP. "The forward to the budget raises the important issue of what is considered problems at the labour front, resulting partly from distorted public sector salary structure which is also poorly administered. To this government intends to use the budget to implement a new comprehensive public sector pay reforms that emphasises equally pay for work of equal worth. This issue is not new. It was the central theme of Ghana's income policy prepared as far back as 1997. This policy is contained in the "final report of Technical Assistance to Facilitate the Development implementation of the medium to long term public sector salaries and wages policy", as at December 1997, price waterhouse. The main report is 210 pages. Since 2001 we have not been told what is deficient a report, what steps have been taken to correct or review it for implementation. What is going to be new in the budget 'fair wage commission"? would it be based on the price waterhouse report or a "new comprehensive public sector pay reform" as the budget suggests? I entreat you ladies and gentlemen of the media to invest some time reading the said report and see whether we are simply re-inventing the wheel or inventing new issues in the public sector wage policy debate?
"Sectoral performance "Agriculture "There is no new thinking in the agricultural sector. The vast Accra and Afram plains remain unutilised.
The downstream waters of the Volta, Pra, Tano, Bia rivers just flow into the see. No effort is made to utilize these water resources to produce food to feed the people. The agric sector statements in the budget are business as usual, full of unsubstantiated statistics on roots and tubers, ruminants, cereals and legumes.
"Cocoa "Cocoa production for 2005/2006 is projected in para, 167 to be 715 of the budget to be 660 metric tonnes whilst in para 310, the projection is 740,458 metric tonnes. For 2006/7 cocoa production is projected to be 600,000 metric tones. Unfortunately no explanation is given or this low production projection inspite of the on-going hi-tech programme designed to increase production every year. Could this be attributed to the disincentive producer price paid to the farmer?
"Producer price paid to the cocoa farmer has been as follows: 2003/2004 9,000,000/mt 2004/2005 9,000,000/mt 2005/2006 9,000,000/mt 2006/2007 9,150,000/mt
"The Budget Statement in para 312 states that "farmers producer price have increased threefold since 2001" the Minister was being economical wit the truth and mischievous by using 2001 as the base year. He did not state that for three years the farmers' producer price was kept at 9 million cedis per tonne and that after keeping the cocoa farmers income fixed for 3 years all that the hard working cocoa farmer gets a\is an increase of only 150,000 cedis mt in 2006/7. the cheating of the cocoa farmer is taking place at a time when the minister himself admits that FOB cocoa prices in 2006 averaged 900 pounds mt or US$ 1 620mt. "Having borrowed the Cocoa Farmers Housing Scheme idea from the NDC's 2004 manifesto, it is unfortunate that the NPP Government sets this fund up with a mere five billion cedis as see fund as stated in the 2006 budget and repeated in the 2007 Budget. It is worth noting that to date only 1 billion cedis has been released to only 33 farmers. Meanwhile the Energy commission can spend 15 billion cedis renting its offices in Accra for only a 5-year period.
"Energy The NPP has allowed a 10 year Volta Lake drought cycle to wreak havoc on the country. Until the water level hit the 236.5ft level the repair of the generating equipment at Aboadze Thermal plant was at a "slow track" speed. T was after the lake sank to the very low level of 236.5 ft that this project was as the minister pts it, "fast tracked". For 5 _ years, the government never increased the power supply capacity by even Ikw. For over 2 years the Osagyefo Barge has been sitting on the seas not producing a kwhr of energy.
"The Government says it is relocating the Osagyefo Barge from Effasu to Tema at a cost of $50 m. the government says "VRA is procuring this year" a 126 MV power plant. We hope that provisions precedent to the success of this project have been made in terms of location and other infrastructure decisions if both the Osagyefo Barge and the VRA's 126 MW plant are to supply power by August 2007 as stated in the Budget.
"The Budget statement indicates that a 300mw thermal plant is to be installed and commissioned in 2009. Further more, that discussion are on-going wit the Chinese Government for implementation of the 400 MW Bui Dam project. We note that both of these projects will provide medium term solutions to our energy problem. However, to propel the country into a middle income economy by 2015, the additional electric power needs of the country run into 2000-3000mw. This calls for a long term integrated national electrification plan.
* "The stability, reliability, and un-interruptibility of power is an imperative for competitive business environment if we hope to retain existing investors and attract new ones into the country.
* "Improvement to the current power capacity and delivery systems can reduce costs considerably to industrial, commercial and residential consumers. The frequent outages of power, brownings, light outs, power fluctuations are not attractive to business and investors.
"Renewable Energy The Government has not paid much attention to renewable energy. The pilot Appolonia Biogas Project has been made to go to waste. Lip service has been paid to the solar energy project.
The LPG project has been priced out of the market. The savannah forest is being denuded to provide fuel wood and charcoal for cooking. In sort the government has no renewable energy policy. The government's energy budget is almost wholly donor funded. Out of 956,690 million cedis budget only 51,824 million cedis or 5.4% is funded by GOG.
"Trade, Industry, PSD And PSI "With only two years of its eight year term mandate, remaining, the Government says it's "National Industry Policy will be competed". When/ We are not told. Instead of fashioning a well thought out industry policy, Government intends to implement what it calls District Industrialization Programme (DIP) under which each of the 138 districts would be allocated unspecified seed capital to establish a "flagship industrial project". It is instructive that the entire budget document is silent on the much touted PSI on cassava starch to which so much resources have been spent. That the Ayensu Starch Company has collapsed and with it the PSI on cassava project, is now a certainty. The NPP government must account fully to the people on this cassava PSI and use whatever lessons learnt from the initiate for the District Industrialization Programme. The Trade and Industry component of the budget offer nothing new but business as usual.
"Tourism "This sector is supposed to be the major foreign exchange earner within the next couple of years. Unfortunately, what is supposed to be done in 2007 is nothing to write home about. Not much thinking has gone into the development of tourism in Ghana.The Budget Statement is also vey silent on Diasporan affairs for which a whole Ministry has been set up.
"Fisheries "The Minister states in the budget that we are to await the "Fisheries Policy and Management plans to be finalized, gazzetted and implemented in collaboration with District Assemblies" before we can experience "sustainable increases in fish production". In the meant time for our fish protein we have to rely on imported fish at the expense of our local fishing industry.
"Housing and Water "The priority of this government is to house the Upper Class. To this end all the in-filling plots at Cantonments, Ridge, Roman Ridge, and Airport Residential Area gave been transformed and very plush expensive houses to be aquired and inhabited by the Upper Class have been built on them.
"The low cost houses for the poor to cost less than 10,000 dollar promised in the 2001 budget are yet to see the light of day. For 2006, government states that "It continues the construction of 1,138 flats at Borteyman, Nungua and that construction of 1,400 flats at Kpone all in Greater Accra Region and 192 flats at Asokore Mampong, Ashanti Region were started" Rather surprisingly, for 2007 "the Ministry will contract 20,000 housing units annually over the next five years". The Government wants us to believe that whereas it has taken over two years to construct about 3,730 flats, it can construct 20,000 in only one year.
"The annual ritual of giving a list of boreholes, hand dug well etc. without indicating locations has become boring. We also hope that urban water projects which have featured in the last three budget statements will finally be completed. We need to be told, the cost of the Peduase Lodge rehabilitation, the contractors and material and equipment suppliers.
"Policy Initiatives for 2007 Budget "The setting up of the Private Funding Initiative (PFI) to design, finance, guild and operate schools, offices and residential building, etc. is a laudable idea but nothing new. The private sector virtually builds and operates all the pre-school and some basic schools. Private initiatives are fast growing at the tertiary education level. The residential housing sector is largely private sector funded whilst office buildings are also largely private sector run.
"We believe that PFI participation in road construction, health facilities is an ideal way to improve the roads, health, energy and railway sub-sector. But we do not have to set up another public body (Ghana Investment Corparation) to oversee this activity or invest in overseas multinationals to hedge against falling commodity prices etc. This is another job for the boys venture.
"We believe that state-owned enterprises properly resources both financially and in terms of human capacity, can be vehicles for growth. The Chinese companies which are winning road, building and stadia contracts in Ghana are all state-owned. The proposal to prepare public sector entities to long-term funding on domestic capital markets is laudable and we hope it can be actualized.
"We endorse the issue of the golden jubilee bonds but we think the amount targeted is too small. We must through this bond issue undertake specific infrastructural work as a reminder to what the present generation did to remember our golden jubilee.
"It is a pity that in spite of all past initiatives such as paying all public servants through the banks, the Akuafo Cheque System etc, still "only 10 percent of the bankable population has bank accounts". The public's trust in the banking system is still far from satisfactory. Out economy is too much a cash-based economy. To buy a set of furniture one must have the full amount. There are no credit facilities to cover such activities. The latent demand can only be realised if one has the cash.; the credit system must be used to stimulate demand and drive supply also.
"The Government has been trumpeting all over the place how it has reduced interest rates because the Bank of Ghana Prime Rate is less than 15 percent and the base rate of the banks hovers around 20 percent.
Unfortunately the actual lending rate of the banks which is between 25 percent and 30 percent is what is of interest to the borrower and in the ECOWAS sub-region, Ghana's interest rates are too high and make our products uncompetitive.
"Since Prof. Soto came to Ghana and made reference to the "Deal Capital" in Ghana, no concrete steps have been taken to actualize the "Soto" concept. In fact, of late the banks in demanding immovable properties as collateral also demand other additional security.
In fact, some banks demand collateral which is more than 100 percent the value of the project.
"The capitalization of the banks in Ghana is a major drawback to our march towards middle income status. The example in paragraph 1262 of the Budget Statement brings to the fore the problems our banks pose. The capitalization of the 7th biggest bank in Nigeria is bigger than that of all the banks in Ghana. Our banks cannot be used to achieve any growth targets beyong what we are experiencing now. In 2007 we must start to improve our banks capitalization to provide the fuel to drive the growth vehicle.
"Revenue Enhancement Measure "The proposed flat registration of land fees is discriminatory. Land at Bunkprugu is worth much less than East Legon land and charging a flat 350,000 cedis registration fee for up to an acre of land anywhere in Ghana is unfair. We believe that the 3% timber export levy has killed the industry and the earlier it is reviewed the better.
"Reducing the excise duty on cigarettes can reduce smuggling but the movement from ad valorem to specific excise may not necessarily make locally produced alcoholic and non-alcoholic excise duty lower.
"The NPP collapsed some businesses as a result of the ill-advised imposition of a National Reconstructive Levy. If it now has realised that this levy must be removed no praises are required. Is it not a pity that with all the noise the NPP made about broadening the tax net after six years in office, we are being told that only 20% of potential tax payers do pay income tax and that only 350,000 employees in the private sector pay income tax. The government's "innovative" tax collection measure linking income tax collection to registration of vehicles will not cure the disease. We recall that past governments made tax clearance certificates a requirement for various transactions. We must determine why some of these measures were abolished before we reintroduce the same measures in the current limited proposal on cars only.
"The tax relief proposed in Section 1301 is laudable. Since 50,000 cedis per annum increases in reliefs are insignificant.
"It is amazing how the NPP Government always wants to put more money in the pockets of the rich in society. Which people enjoy furnished accommodation, vehicles with fuell and drivers, etc. in our society? The reduction of taxes of benefits in-kind from 15% to 10% for accommodation and 12.5% capped at 3.5 million cedis per month for vehicles can only benefit the Upper Class.
"After amending the vehicle importation laws to allow the old rickety right-hand drive double-decker buses to be imported into the country, the NPP Government deliberately opened the floodgates for our country to be flooded with all kinds of over-aged vehicles. We note with concern that all the double-decker buses have broken down. We hope the Government has now recognised the folly in its policy. We will wait for the Bill to be laid in Parliament on this issue before commenting further.
"The reduction in its final withholding tax is a step in the right direction. Similarly the VAT Clearance Certificate introduction is also laudable.
"An Endowment Fund for science and technology research being set up is a good idea but the seed capital of 5 billion cedis is grossly inadequate. The seed fund for this project must be increased.
"The incentives granted to local pharmaceutical industries are welcome.
"What the local rice and poultry industries need is not only for schools, hospitals and security agencies to be forced to buy local rice and poultry but to ensure that that the duties and taxes on imported poultry and rice are reviewed to make locally-produced rice and poultry competitive to all customers.
"The problems facing the NHIS are now legendary. By end 2006, only 34% of the population would have been registered and only 18% would have been issued with ID cards. The problems anticipated by the NDC in Parliament and which the NPP ignored and single-handedly passed the NHIS Law are being experienced now. The Scheme is simply not working; the "obnoxious cash and carry" system is still operative. In the 2007 Budget, "Internally Generated Funds" of the Ministry of Health which is a euphemism for "cash and carry" accounts for about 9% of the sector's funding.
This NHIS has become an albatross. We accept the programme to annually renew vehicle registrations.
"Ladies and Gentlemen of the Media, in sum we say that the two issues that the budget seeks to address, namely the public sector wage crises and the energy crisis have not been addressed frontally by the budget. All it seeks to do is to postpone them, hoping the good people of Ghana would in no time forget or as usual allow sleeping dogs to lie. However, it is our considered opinion that the two issues are too important to die because they have been created by the Government where for over five and a half years they allowed the problems to fester and get compounded."
Thank you (end full text).

Original_Search_Title: Aids Orphans: Making it on Their Own Together - and Thriving
Headline: Africa: Aids Orphans: Making it on Their Own Together - and Thriving
Body_Text:
Nairobi — ­ As the international community readies tobserve the nineteenth World AIDS Day with the theme, “The Promise of Partnerships,” one partnership growing across East Africa is a beacon of hope in the face of the pandemic.
It is a partnership of children orphaned by AIDS and child-headed households ­ who are making it on their own by working together.
In sub-Saharan Africa there are more than 11 million children now orphaned by AIDS. By 2010, UNICEF estimates the number will nearly double as more parents die. *
But today, across Rwanda, Tanzania, Uganda and Kenya, nearly 18,500 of those orphaned children are learning how to cope on their own, build small businesses, and care for their younger siblings.
They’re working together through the time-honed, grownup model of working collaboratives­ with a little guidance from caring adults in their villages and the support of a program called Giving Hope.
Sponsored by the global humanitarian agency Church World Service, the program plans to help 26,000 children in more than 5,300 child-headed households over the next three years.
Rev. John L. McCullough is Executive Director and CEO of New York-headquartered Church World Service. McCullough says, “This is not the typical ‘OVC care and support’ plan (Orphans and Vulnerable Children in the humanitarian vernacular).”
There are other programs for African AIDS orphans that focus primarily on keeping children in school, says McCullough.  But, he says, the innovative Giving Hope program focuses first on keeping young families together by helping child heads of households become self-reliant—through agricultural, vocational and life-skills training, and by forming youth working groups, where kids help kids build houses, grow food, start small businesses, pool money and resources, extend micro-credit to one another, and help keep their younger siblings in school.
“CWS and its African partners are seeing that the Giving Hope program has two main benefits,” says McCullough. “It’s restoring families and creating new family and social structures for child-headed households through the youth working groups approach, and it’s building for the future of Africa’s next generation.”
In Nairobi, Church World Service East Africa Director Dan Tyler says that the program is helping adolescent orphans identify their existing skills and talents “and building new ones that will form the foundation of self-reliance. We’re building the strengths of tomorrow’s Africa,” he says, “by working with the younger?and most vulnerable?generation.”
Begun as a partnership of the YWCA Rwanda and Church World Service, the Giving Hope program is entering its fourth year in Rwanda. The program launched this year in Kenya and Uganda and is in its second year of activity in Tanzania.
All too often, older orphaned youths living in rural African areas­ when suddenly faced with the responsibilities of caring for their younger siblings ­ will run off to the nearest city to eke out an existence. The Giving Hope program is changing that outcome for many.
Some youths are so buoyed by their successes in the Giving Hope program that they’re adopting other orphans into their families.
Teams of Giving Hope program social workers are working with district governments who alert them to children in need. As the social workers begin to work with individual child-headed families, they provide a small amount of seed money, help the children form working groups, provide basic business training, help the groups select community income-generating projects, and serve as advisors and parental figures.
In Kagasa, outside Kigali, Rwanda, one rural working group of orphaned youths is now growing tomatoes as a joint project, learning how to establish and grow a business in the process. The income they’re earning helps each young household head pay for family members’ healthcare, school uniforms and materials for their younger siblings.
The Kagasa group includes 46 children and nine member households. Seven of those households are headed by children and two by community volunteers, or nkundabana (pronounced “hun-dah-bah-nah”), usually elder caregivers.
African kids developing business plans:
As other Giving Hope children’s groups do, the Kagasa group established a mutual fund. But before they opened their account, they were required to do what any adult-run business does: They had to present a business plan.
The group’s first tomato crop generated 50,000 Rwandan francs, just covering the original seed loan the Giving Hope program extended to them. But in four months, the group increased its income fourfold, shared the income, and each member household received 20,000 francs (approximately US $40) to begin their own activities Now each house has its own bank account.
Outside of Rwanda’s capital Kigali, 20-year-old Evariste and his 17-year-old sister Christine run a small storefront business from their home. The dirt floor is swept clean, and tomatoes, potatoes, sacs of grain and batteries are assembled neatly on the shelves.
Evariste’s growing business acumen shows up in a handwritten sign that says, “All loans tomorrow.”
His Giving Hope youth working group helped construct the house and shelves. Paper cutouts hang from the ceiling as decorations from the store’s inauguration.
“When Giving Hope staff had us meet other children,” Evariste says, “we got new friends. We created our group and selected our advisors, and then were called to a training on creating a small business. I was asked to develop a proposal and to think of an idea.
“Because I was told I must do this on my own, I learned I had initiative. And now I am respected in my community. They are my customers. They come to visit me, as do friends from my group.”
Having friends and community is a new experience for Evariste and his group peers.  Being orphaned by AIDS still carries a strong stigma in many African countries. In Rwanda, people tend not to say outright that someone has died of AIDS.
In Rwanda’s Nyamabuye District, 20-year-old Mediatrice cares for three younger sisters, a 13-year-old brother Tuyisenge, and a foster child, Cecile. Cecile is HIV-positive.
After both of Mediatrice’s parents died of HIV/AIDS in 2001, Mediatrice became head of the family. All of the children were malnourished.
Mediatrice attended Giving Hope trainings and learned about children’s rights, life skills, household management, nutrition and food security, micro-business planning and management, health and hygiene, HIV/AIDS, and how to make animal feeds.
Her family is now self-sufficient; two of her sisters are back in primary school, and three of her sisters have taken vocational training classes in sewing, hairdressing and postcard making. They sell cassava cakes and beignets, cassava flour, palm oil and kerosene at the market and from their home.
They’re also raising pigs, goats, ducks and vegetables for their own use and to sell at market. The children are now healthy, and they’ve started saving for the future.
Meditrice says, “Before Giving Hope, some of us were street children, or child laborers who worked for food. We were isolated, sad, brokenhearted, physically and sexually abused.
“Now,” she said, “we have organized into groups and we help each family have a dream and to become integrated into the community. We are teaching our neighbors to accept child rights and to prevent HIV.”
All of the Giving Hope kids are trained to have a dream and work for their dream.
In a new Giving Hope program in Machakos in Kenya’s Central Province, George
is a youth group member. Outside George’s home a good-sized kitchen garden is thriving in its second planting. George has a dream to see his village have well water and electricity and hopes to go to university and become an engineer who will bring electricity to the village.
Another youth working group member Francesca dreams of having a fishpond.
AIDS has orphaned over 613,000 children in Rwanda and 1,731,000 in Uganda. Even in a country like Uganda where HIV prevalence is declining, the numbers of orphans will remain high.
In Uganda, Church World Service’s Giving Hope program is working with the Church of Uganda’s Planning Development and Rehabilitation division in three of the church’s diocese, West Buganda, Bunyoro-Kitara and Kigezi Diocese.
In the Uganda program’s first year, Giving Hope teams will work directly with 367 orphaned households?an estimated 1,059 children­ and plans to enlist about 15,000 people in surrounding communities, including family members of the targeted orphans, in supporting the children’s progress.
In Tanzania, Church World Service is working with the country’s Evangelical Lutheran Church of Tanzania/North Western Diocese, in the Kagera region where AIDS was first diagnosed. There are more than 34, 260 registered orphans in the church’s existing Huduma ya Watoto  (service for children) or HUYAWA program.
In the Tanzanian program, now in its second year, Church World Service and HUYAWA are working with 240 youth working groups representing 1,620 orphaned households which include 5,724 children.
In Gitarama, Rwanda, after 18 teenagers graduated from their youth working group’s vocational training class in tailoring, nine of them set up a shop in town and nine rented space in the market. They share a common bank account and make daily deposits. After paying the rents, they share the profits?which go toward siblings’ school needs, crops and foods. The shop also rents wedding gowns, and the young women recently acquired contracts with two schools to make uniforms.
The Giving Hope program’s principles were developed through Church World Service’s participation in United Nations, USAID and faith-based working groups. Giving Hope programs are supported through a three-year commitment from the St. Marys United Methodist Church Foundation, St. Marys, Georgia, and are part of CWS’s multi-year Africa Initiative.
Church World Service is a relief, development, refugee assistance and human rights agency working in 80 countries.
Sources: United Nations
http://www.un.org/events/tenstories/story.asp?storyID=400

Original_Search_Title: Nakapiripirit Gets Food Security Scheme
Headline: Uganda: Nakapiripirit Gets Food Security Scheme
Body_Text:
Kampala — NAKAPIRIPIRIT district has launched a food security scheme through which every sub-county will identify a crop to be cultivated as a food reserve.
The project, funded by the National Agricultural Advisory Services (NAADS), will identify crops that performed well in the different sub-counties in the district.
NAADS district coordinator Dr. Mark Ilukol recently said sh5m had been allocated to each of the 10 sub-counties to start the scheme.
Ilukol said the successful introduction of rice, millet, potatoes and cassava in the district proved that Karamoja could grow any crop. He said NAADS had also introduced technologies once considered to be too foreign and impossible to implement in Karamoja.
He said the expanded cultivation of groundnuts and the introduction of the "epuripur" sorghum had enhanced household incomes.
"What we have remained with is to get a sustainable market for our crops," Ilukol said.
He, however, said that the district had a shortage of extension service providers.
"We only have two veterinary officers and a few animal husbandry officers to serve the whole district," Ilukol said.
District agricultural officer Mario Tengei said Nakapiripirit was one of the food deficit districts in the country. "More than 90% of the people still live on one meal a day," Tengei said.

Original_Search_Title: Dr. Kumbuor Batters 2007 Budget - Accuses Gov't On Energy And Wage Issues
Headline: Ghana: Dr. Kumbuor Batters 2007 Budget - Accuses Gov't On Energy And Wage Issues
Body_Text:
THE MAIN opposition party, the National Democratic Congress (NDC) has punched holes in the 2007 budget of the government, presented to Parliament by the Minister of Finance and Economic Planning, Mr. Kwadwo Baah-Wiredu on Thursday November 16.
Delving into all areas of the budget, the NDC accused the government of woefully failing to address two key issues, namely; public sector wage and energy crises, pointing out that, "All that it seeks to do is to postpone them, hoping the good people of Ghana would in no time forget or as usual allow sleeping dogs lie."
"However, it is our considered opinion that the two issues are too important to die because they have been created by the government, where for over five-and-half years they allowed the problems to fester and get compounded."
The Minority Spokesperson on Finance and Economic Planning, Dr. Ben Kumbuor, speaking at his maiden press conference as the shadow finance minister, accused the NPP government of allowing a ten-year Volta Lake drought cycle to wreak havoc on the country, adding that, "until the water level hit the 236.5ft level the repair of the Thermal generating equipment at Aboadze was at a 'slow track' pace. It was after the lake sank to the very low level of 236.5ft that this project was, as the Minister put it, 'fast tracked," he said.
According to him, for the past five and half years, the NPP government never bothered to complete the combined cycle plant at Aboadze and also that the government has never increased the power supply capacity by even a kilowatt unit. Additionally, he said for over 2 years, the 'Osagyefo' barge has been sitting on the seas, not producing a kw/hr of energy.
He stated that the stability, reliability and uninterruptibility of power was imperative for a business environment and that if we hoped to retain the existing investors and attract new ones into the country, there was the need to improve upon the current power capacity and delivery systems, which can reduce costs considerably to the industrial, commercial and residential consumers.
"The frequent outages of power, browning, light outs, power fluctuations are not attractive to business investors," he argued.
Speaking on renewable energy, Dr. Kumbuor said the government has been paying lip service to the solar energy project.
He said the LPG project has been priced out of the market and that the savanna forest is also being denuded to provide fuel wood and charcoal for cooking.
On the cocoa sector, Dr. Kumbuor, who was flanked by the National Chairman of the NDC, Dr. Kwabena Adjei, Hon. Lee Ocran, MP for Jomoro, Dr. Kwame Ampofu, former spokesperson on Energy, Mr. Albert Kwasi Zigah, MP for Ketu South, Hon. Ken Dzirasah, MP for South Tongu, Mr. Ato Ahwoi, a leading member of the party, and a host of others, including, Mr. Elvis Afriyie Ankrah, Deputy General Secretary in charge of operations, said the Minister was being economical with the truth and mischievous by using 2001 as the base year.
"He did not state that for the three years the farmers' producer price was kept at ¢9million per tonne and that after keeping the cocoa farmer's income fixed for 3 years all that the hardworking cocoa farmer gets is an increase of only ¢160,000/month in 2006/7.
The cheating of the cocoa farmer is taking place at the time when the Minister himself admits that FOB cocoa prices in 2006 averaged $900/Mt or US$ 1620/Mt."
Demanding an explanation from the government over the collapse of certain companies, more especially, the President's Special Initiatives (PSI) on cassava, Dr. Ben K, as he is popularly called by his colleague MPs said, "The NPP government must account fully to the people on this cassava PSI and use whatever lessons learnt from the initiative for the District Industrialization Programme. The trade and Industry component of the budget offers nothing but business as usual."
Continuing, he had to say, "With only two years of its eight year term mandate, remaining, the government says its National Industrial Policy will be completed. When? We are not told. Instead of fashioning a well thought-out industrial policy, government intends to implement what it calls a District Industrialization Programme under which each of the 138 districts would be allocated unspecified seed capital to establish a flagship industrial project."
He contended that it was instructive to note that the entire budget document was silent on the much-touted PSI on cassava starch, to which so much resource have been spent.
Dr. Kumbuor, expressed concern about the housing and water project, saying the government's housing scheme was meant for the upper class.
The NDC, however, commended the government on the reduction in the withholding tax rate and the introduction of VAT Clearance Certificate, but added that the government deserved no praise for the removal of the National Reconstruction Levy, on the basis that, the NPP has collapsed some businesses as a result of 'ill-advised imposition of the levy.
On health, the NDC legislator enumerated the problems facing the NHIS, which he described as legendary.
According to him, by the end of 2006, only 34% of the population would have been registered and only 18% would have been issued with ID cards, adding that the problems anticipated by the NDC in parliament, which ere ignored by the NPP and single-handedly passed the NHIS Law are being experienced now.
"The scheme is simply not working the 'obnoxious' cash and carry" system is still operative," he asserted. He continued that "in the 2007 the internally-generated funds of the Ministry of Health, which is an euphemism for 'cash and carry' accounts for about 9%of the sector's funding."
According to Dr. Kumbuor, the NHIS has become an albatross. He said the NDC accepts the programme to annually renew vehicle registration.

Original_Search_Title: Stakeholders' Forum or Good Governance?
Headline: Nigeria: Stakeholders' Forum or Good Governance?
Body_Text:
Yours sincerely was a participant at the last controversial Presidential Stakeholders' Forum on Education at Abuja.
I therefore bear testimony that feverish mass assemblage of people officially talked at (not necessarily talked with!) on policy issues always after discovery of some huge rot under the advertised banner of a stakeholders' forum cannot and should not be substitute for policy formulation and policy implementation by those elected and appointed to govern. Simply put, stakeholders' forum is no substitute for good governance. Stakeholders' forums are assuming notoriety as official platforms for governmental policy grandstanding increasingly aimed at concealing failed governance when the actual expectations of the electorate of those elected and appointed to govern is service delivery, service delivery and service delivery ad infinitum. The concept of stakeholders has been promoted by varying donor agencies and multilateral institutions such as International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World Bank. This is after much considerable global criticism of the top-down approach of these "development partners" to development issues in developing countries. It was not uncommon for instance for poverty alleviation policies conceived in Washington, D.C. to be rammed down the throats of some poor consumers in Malawi. Stakeholders' forum is a smart but nonetheless top-down device to either legitimise or rationalise policy preferences through pretence at some involvement of the people about their plight. Even at that, it is doubtful if the multilateral institutions which invented this cleaver legitimisation device ever imagined that routine collection and summoning of potpourri of onlookers, mass-dignified as stakeholders would become substitute for genuine governance as it is increasingly the case in Nigeria in recent times. We have had stakeholders' forum on banking sector, forum on aviation sector, forum on census, forum on Niger Delta, forum on textile, forum on cassava, etc, yet the more we look the less we see qualitative value addition for time and resources tied down for hours of alleged stockholding by stakeholders. While on the one hand, stakeholders' forum gives the impression of presidential concern for the sectors under searchlight, your guess is as good as mine as to the efficacy of these unstructured periodic televised sermons on the mountain. For one, these stakeholders' forums often take place after (and not) before abysmal degeneration in the respective sector. The forums are more reactive rather than proactive betraying elementary principle of good governance which is all about envisaging problems and challenges within a broad vision and mission of elected government officials. Judging from the quality of mass media camera coverage of each forum, observers note that the grandstanding has tuned into another public relations exercise under the jack-boot of the President and his Ministers to say we care about the rot even if the problems persist. This observation has been reinforced by the painful fact that every forum is followed by deeper rot in the respective sector instead of advertised promised changes. For instance, the stakeholders' forum on aviation called in the wake of serial air disasters has been followed by greater carnage. Countless gatherings on Niger Delta have been greeted with more deadly raids and hostage takings by the militants. Textile industry and manufacturing sector in general slides from bad to worst under the regime of countless stakeholders' forums. The strikes in Unity schools worsen after a stakeholders' forum and it took good governance via traditional institutions for conflict resolutions to get the striking teachers back to work and not some grandstanding called stakeholders' forum. Of what relevance are mass gatherings that increasingly tie down working executives who should actually be working to solve problems after which the problems get further compounded? Many observers note that the problem with the last census exercise was that governors and other relevant stakeholders who should be working for proper head count at local levels were receiving presidential lectures at "Stakeholders' Forum" in Abuja.
The bane of stakeholders' forums is their false claim to mass participation. Even the arrangement of sitting departs from the time-tested town halls in which local chiefs rob shoulders with their peoples in the great task of communal governance of problem identification and problem solving. The sitting arrangement of a typical stakeholders' forum is imperial: the President and his select ministers predominate in a totalitarian manners dispensing some wisdom to the army of assumed ignoramuses. I have not seen any forum in which all the President and his courtiers did was to listen and listen. On the contrary, in all forums the President did most if not all the talking; introduction and summary and even acted master of ceremony. In not a few forums the President actually banged tables repeatedly. What manner of participation can anybody have in this kind of garrison forum tagged stakeholders' forum? The last presidential forum on education was one huge exercise at presidential top-down talk down. President and Minister Obiageli Ezekwesili consumed five hours out of the estimated six-hour long forum talking to a converted audience about the imperatives of education while the duo determined who should and should not speak and for how long. There was a false impression that all the participants at the forum that included the late Eminence Sultan Maccido were all employees of the Presidency. Nothing could be more patronising for a government who admitted abysmal failure in education and whose officials should do more listening than talking the obvious with poorly-developed power points and twisted statistics. There was nothing ministerial about the Education Minister who was indulged to give repetitive talks and grandstand on what is clearly obvious to all: collapse of education!
The point cannot be overstated that stakeholders' forums should not be substitutes for good governance. There is a false impression that the platforms provide fresh ground for new initiatives and ideas. This further betrays good governance. Nigeria is not short of ideas and policy options arrived at through consensus and mass participation. The ideas and policy options contained in vision 2010 for instance and even the last controversial Political Reform Conference were and are still far more robust than feverish top-down "resolutions" of endless stakeholders' forums.

Original_Search_Title: Agip Spends N2.6bn in Agric Intervention Scheme
Headline: Nigeria: Agip Spends N2.6bn in Agric Intervention Scheme
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — The Nigerian Agip Oil Company Limited and its parent company ENI Group have invested N2.6bn ($20m) in the Agricultural intervention built into its community relations scheme in Nigeria.
The intervention which is executed under the "Green River project," encourages farmers to form cooperative societies in four Niger Delta States of Delta, Imo Rivers and Bayelsa who they train and supply free farming implements.
The scheme which weekend, celebrated its 11th farmers day where agro-mechanisation multifunctional kits were distributed to them as well as prizes for best performing farmers, was held at Obrikom, Rivers State.
The scheme which started in 1987 now supplies improved seedlings, mosaic cassava resistant varieties, rice, fingerlings, improved yam seedlings, snail rearing, improved banana and plantain suckers.
The highlights of the programme now shows that Agip has through the Green River Scheme established a plant propagation center dedicated to the multiplication of improved materials and fingerling production and distribution covering an area of 59,000 square meters while 23 extension and farm officers attend to the farmers on full time basis.
They in addition set aside N30m in a Trust Fund account for micro credit scheme for five years at the disposal of the 280 farmers cooperatives.
Qauntifying the successes and impact of the scheme on the overall agricultural output, the scheme has so far supplied 95,000 bundles of improved variety of cassava stems, 174,884 seed yam and 22,163 plantain suckers were distributed free of charge to the farmers.
Also, 526 goats, 3,100 agric duck/fowl and 145 pigs were given free to the farmers who are constantly visited by the extension officers for help in carrying out the replacement of improved systems of farming.
Agip puts the value of 229,318 fingerlings distributed under the scheme since 2000 at N18m while income generated through the use of mechanized kits, motorized boats and agro processing units at N22.8 m.
The total quantifyable benefits generated by the scheme was put at N151m while the cumulative quantifyable benefits accruing to the Green River Project and its affiliated beneficiaries like the Cooperative societies was put at $5.5 m.
Speaking at the occasion, the Managing Director of Agip, Mr. Antonio Panza expressed delight that the scheme was achieving the aim for which it was established as the people were now moving towards improved standards of living through the opportunities they exploit in the scheme.
He said the scheme was conservatively affecting at least 250,000 homes who through improved mechanization through light machineries was adding further fillip to the simplification of their farming methods.
He said the pace at which the scheme was yielding results was going to help Nigeria in attaining the millennium development goals, empowerment in consonance with NEEDS and SEEDS.

Original_Search_Title: Cancer Cases On the Rise - GAEC
Headline: Ghana: Cancer Cases On the Rise - GAEC
Body_Text:
Professor Edward H.K. Akaho, Director General of the Ghana Atomic Energy Commission (GAEC) has said within the next 20 years there could be 200 million cancer cases worldwide with 75 per cent coming from developing countries.
He said realising the upsurge of cancer in the country the Commission had requested the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA), for assistance to upgrade and expand the Centre for Radiotherapy and Nuclear Medicine at the Korle-bu and the Komfo Anokye Teaching Hospitals and to establish a new one at Tamale to provide care for the patients.
Speaking at the 40th anniversary celebration of the GAEC in Accra, he said these two centres had witnessed an increase in the number of new patients of which experts claimed that 70 per cent of the reported cases had reached advanced state.
He said nuclear medicine in synergy with radiotherapy services had proved important since it provided tumour marker services for various cancers of the prostrate, liver and the colon.
He said the IAEA had assisted Ghana to prepare a proposal to put in place a Programme of Action for Cancer Therapy (PACT) and that the programme being an initiative of Professor Mohammed Elbaradei, IAEA's Director General would enable early detection through screening including curative and palliative treatments.
Professor Akaho said GAEC had realised the need for human resource development to manage and utilise its nuclear facilities of the radiotherapy and nuclear medicine centres, adding that there was presently a wide gap between the older generation of experienced nuclear and younger inexperienced staff.
He said the GAEC was established by an Act of Parliament Act 204, now superseded by Act 588 of 2000 with vested responsibility for all matters related to the peaceful uses of atomic energy.
He said though scientific research started far back in 1952 at the Physics Department of the then University of Gold Coast, (now University of Ghana) work in radioisotope application gained ground in 1959 with Ghana signing the Non Proliferation Treaty in 1970 after she had joined the IAEA in 1970.
He said the main function of the Commission included peaceful application of nuclear techniques and biotechnology for the sustainable development of Ghana ensuring that nuclear safety and security were not compromised.
Professor Akaho said the Research Reactor commissioned in 1995 and other nuclear analytical facilities had been used for analysis in geological, medical, biological and industrial materials for various sectors of the economy.
He said the agriculture sector had benefited a lot from the commission in the area of gamma mutation breeding and use of tissue culture technique for cocoa, plantain, banana, pineapples, yams, sugar cane coconut and cassava.

Original_Search_Title: Edo Deputy Speaker Bares It All
Headline: Nigeria: Edo Deputy Speaker Bares It All
Body_Text:
Benin City — Mr. Omo Ezomo is the Deputy Speaker of the Edo state House of Assembly. A very humble and astute politician, his peers defer to him because of his composure and experience, being the only lawmaker in Edo to have made it three times to the House, this time representing Owan West local government council of the state.
Just last Saturday, the National Association of Nigerian Students (NANS) gave him an award as the most performing lawmaker in the state. Saturday Vanguard cornered this politician from the Edo North senatorial district of Edo state for this chat and the soft-spoken legislator revealed how he fell in love with his classmate while he was in secondary school. Somehow, he bowed out of it when he discovered that the girl was only interested in the relationship because of the academic assistance he was rendering to her. He spoke on other issues including why he wants to go the House of Representatives in 2007.
The award
I feel elated and happy because what I thought was not being recognised had been appreciated by a body like NANS. I am motivated by that honour especially when I had a similar one about a month ago from Students Word Assembly. They voted me as the student legislator of the year. All these are motivating me to do more. We have a say that you praise the doer so that he can do more. I try harder to ensure that anytime I find myself in a position, I would go to any extent to be able to represent the position of the people.
The secret of my success in politics
Well, I believe that I have made my mark as a legislator being in the House for the third time. You will understand that the Nigerian political terrain is not easy. But added to that is my peculiar way of handling issues. Otherwise, it would have been difficult for me to have represented my people three times in the House of Assembly. I say so because I am well focused, I have the interest of my people at heart.
I have been able to assist them by way of infrastructural development and others. And all these I do without making noise. Mine is to prove a point that politics is not all about noise making but achievements. That is why I feel glad that even without making noise, what ever I have been doing as a legislator, I have been able to attract the attention of a body like NANS and others. So, I want to thank then for considering me worthy of the honour.
How I came into politics
Well, it all started in the year 1991 at the rudimentary stages of the NRC and the SDP. But before then, I was a very keen observer of political events and political history. Apparently, I was inspired by the political history I read and that propelled me to participate in the registration of the NRC then.
Eventually, I was elected as the ward secretary and thereafter I became the secretary of a body of the ward chairmen in my locality. That exposed me to the political giants in Edo politics. Prominent among them was Alhaji Inn Umoru. So, I find myself dining and wining with political leaders.
But as God would have it, elections for State Assemblies started and there was a call for me to contest because the leaders in my locality were impressed with my work as secretary. So, I went for it and before long, I was in the House of Assembly. That was after I proved bookmakers wrong to win in an environment which was predominantly SDP.
In fact, that was my first major breakthrough in politics and it was not an easy one. But unfortunately, our tenure did not last when Abacha struck and started his political programme. After the death of Abacha, the Abubakar transition started.
The major party was the PDP and I found myself in the party. But the truth is that all I needed was to belong to the party, I did not even have the plans to contest elections again for the House of Assembly. Spurred on by leaders and youths in my locality, I decided again to have a shot at the State Assembly election and by the grace of God I won again. That was my second time in the House of Assembly.
And in 2003, because I still enjoyed the confidence of my people, they still re-elected me. And by this time, I became the Deputy Speaker of the House. Let me say that my rise to that position was just step by step.
My Background
I was born into a humble family in Aviesi, Owan West Local Government Area. It is a predominantly rural area and I went through the rigours of village life throughout my primary and secondary education. I will say that my upbringing was not the type of those who were born with a silver spoon. But my parents were known big farmers in the village. So, food was not a problem for us. They tried hard to ensure that education was given to us. And I remained very grateful to them.
After my secondary school, I came to Benin City and that was my very first time of experiencing life in the city. I can say that it's when I came for my A/Level in the institute of continuing education that I came to terms with the realities of city life. And I saw the difference between the village life and the city life. And I want to say that in a way, being born in the village has not in any way retarded my progress except that by the time I came to town, I was more encouraged, more educated and more useful to the society where I belong.
Village life
At that time, it was difficult for you not to have gone to the stream two times before dawn. It's after that that you could start preparing for school. Sometimes, I got back from school at about 2 or 3pm and would still find myself going straight to the farm to get some firewood or cassava. On weekends, you had to follow your parents to the farm as well.
You know, these were not exercises for the weak ones but somehow they were necessary to build me to have more confidence as a man because I believe that to fix tomorrow, you have to be strong today. I was brought up in a way where I had to respect labour and eke out a living for my self.
Today, I am now in a position where my children are privileged not to encounter such difficulties. It is also necessary for them to know that whatever they are enjoying now, I did not get them on a platter of gold. I worked for them and they should also work hard to earn their own living.
My father's death almost ruined me
As I said earlier, my parents were farmers but they were able to send me to secondary school. But unfortunately, I lost my father when I was abut getting to class four and it all seemed like my world had ended. Luckily, my mum rose to the occasion. There is somebody today I refer to as a father whose house is opposite mine in the village. He was an army officer. After the death of my father, he came back home and heard how brilliant I was in school and he felt very sad that I was going to end my education because of the death of my dad.
Fortunately, he started paying my school fees throughout my class four and five. That was how what could have caused a major hiccup to my secondary education was averted through that man sent to me by God. And today I remain very indebted to him. The best I could do for him is to call him father and that is how he refers to me as his son. He has long retired from the army as a captain. When I look at my life I see him as a model. Maybe I would not have been a lawmaker today if not for him. But I give God the glory.
Social life in the village
When we were growing up, it was very difficult for you to wear trousers then. So, anybody that wore trousers was regarded as one of the richest persons in the community. What we were used to was shorts. But generally I was more of a reserved person who has more regards to his look than to go to party.
Occasionally, I socialised and the experience was worth it. As regards girlfriends, you know those days, girls would follow you based on what you can boast of. For me, for certain reasons they came after me. But it was more of platonic relationship.
Though when I was in class four, I had a relationship that appeared more serious but now when I reflect back it appears to be more of fantasy than love. Actually, why I said it was all fantasy was that I realised that she did not love me sincerely but because of what I was doing for her in the class she was pretending she loved me. And when I discovered that I bowed out of the relationship.
How I met my wife
In my first term in the House of Assembly, I was a first class bachelor, that was in 1991. So, because I have a future to protect, I was not really going for anything on skirt but I know I needed a serious relationship. The chance came when my wife now came to do her Industrial Attachment at the State House of Assembly. I saw her and she attracted me. What I love in a woman is, first of all, the natural beauty. Artificial beauty scares me off in a woman. I think that spurred me to chat her up and I was struck by her intelligence and that was how it started. I proposed to her, she agreed and today we are married with kids.
But we had not even spent our honeymoon when Abacha struck. The marriage was just two days when Abacha took over. So, we found ourselves out of office. That's why I said that we were meant for each other; if not she would have left me then because things became very difficult.
I don't believe in godfatherism in politics
I see myself as a man destined for victory. I believe that it is only God that is my godfather since I have been contesting elections. It is difficult for me to point at a particular personality I can regard as my godfather. Such a person does not exist and that is why I am very strict as a politician. It is difficult for anybody to come and dictate to me on what to do because I am not obliged to you. I am only obliged to the electorate.
So, I do not have had a political godfather and I do not intend to have one. Even now that I want to go and represent my people in the House of Representatives, I am not relying on any person but God because I believe that all powers belong to God. I want to serve my people at that level based on my experience so far.
There are things that we need and it will only take people like me at the federal level to attract such developments to our people. That is my mission and with God on my side, my people will smile.

Original_Search_Title: Why LRA Could Not Eat Meat
Headline: Uganda: Why LRA Could Not Eat Meat
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Lord's Resistance Army, under the leadership of Joseph Kony, blends of Christianity and African traditional beliefs to give its members reason to fight, as seen in Kony's chief priest, Abonga Papa's notes reproduced here.
In the course of the war, Kony would get messages from the spirit known as Lakwena, which he would issue as instructions to his fighters. Abonga Papa, in his capacity as the chief priest, recorded the messages.
Message from Lakwena to the Messenger on May 31, 1994 at 6:00pm:
I don't want laziness as we move because that is a sign of evil. Be active in the mission given to you. Make sure you become a real soldier and know your role.
Breaking the rule by speaking Acholi will lead to imprisonment for both the Stockrees Brigade and those from Control Alter Brigade.
Prayer during the journey: Ladies had to say prayers at 8:00pm and male officers at 12:00 midnight.
In all cases, officers should lead the holy rosary prayers.
In the camps, noise is not allowed in the evening, except in the operation room when discussing battle plans.
On Friday, men should carry palm leaves and ladies get their Bibles. There should be no bullets in the chamber of your gun during tomorrow's prayer but place it back carefully before we get on the move.
We are going to have visitors who are not strangers. They will help us fight and chase Museveni. These people give us the necessary support to win the war.
Being disciplined is having wisdom during your work or someone who knows his work.
Women should not cover their hair.
Men should not use razor blades to shave their hair, but can use scissors.
The spirit would also alert the LRA about imminent attacks, as in the incident recorded on May 29, 1993 when it warned that the invasion and defeat of Sudan would lead to the fall of LRA.
You, the LRA soldiers, should know that before the Lord, you are not different from other people, but you are more important to the holy spirits because they came to work for you. There is a plan to attack a country that borders us before the year ends. If Uganda is not destabilised, then that country will be defeated, marking the end of LRA. But if the country wins the war or Uganda gets destabilised, then we shall get out of the bush. We LRA are like millet seeds carried away and hidden by ants, but not seed that a farmer has kept.
Keep the small army that you have. Good and bad are of the Lord, therefore the world can not be without sin because the Lord wants both good and bad to be there, then who are you who does not want the bad (sin)?
We the LRA are going to be the beginning of peace that will reign forever.
A warning message is recorded on December 15, 1993 reassuring the LRA fighters.
The plan to attack you will not take place because they know that you have known. There is a plan to capture or kill the LRA senior commanders. However, the reason why you have not been attacked is because you have followed your confession/promises. And so now the following must be adhered to:
Pray earnestly for 40 days. Do not send soldiers to attack the enemy unless they attack you, but continue with small operations. Pray, but with no songs so that enemy plans fail.
The out come will be:
A long drawn-out war will begin in Uganda and will destroy the unity of northern Uganda. The liars, lazy and deceitful will perish.
More guidelines:
Do not eat what you have not cooked and don't greet a person who is not cleansed.
LRA who are a month old and have not gone to headquarters should be taken to Lakwena (Messenger).
When we start the 40 days of prayer, oil should be mixed for people to lick.
Quarrels and anger are not acceptable during prayer period.
During prayer don't eat only home food to enable your plans end well.
Do not admit a person who wants to join the LRA anyhow because he may have a plan against you.
Do not talk to your enemies without authority from the commander.
Rules from Lakwena, June 10, 1994:
If you are found speaking Luo (Acholi/Lango) you will be punished with 500 strokes of cane. Everyone is to speak Swahili or English.
When you are in a procession and want to get out of the line, ask for permission then move in your unit or department.
You should not leave the column for more than one minute. You are not to gather in a group unless you are eating. If found you will be given 500 strokes of the cane.
You should not be in the huts/tent by 4:00am.
No carrying of heavy luggage, except dry rations like simsim paste, cassava.
No noise in your location. If found, the commander will be given 500 canes.
If you have a confused girl in your unit, release her so that she gets killed by the enemy.
No entering a house without a commander's order. The punishment is death. No movement should be heard at 7:00pm. If found you will be shot on the spot.
Act only when the order is given. Do not abduct until you are instructed to.
Shea butter oil or simsim oil smeared on the chest show that we are now saved from the enemy and have won the war.
Rules from Lakwena. June 17, 1994
All soldiers should not have sexual intercourse beginning today.
All soldiers must not eat meat, but fish. This must be enforced if we want to overthrow Museveni. And everyone should have a palm leaf and rosary.
Before prayers start everyone shall draw a good picture of a heart on their palm.
No killing of wild and domestic animals. Only eat honey.
The President of Sudan said Koran and rosary defeat the enemy.
Don't touch a pig, nor stay in a home with pigs or step on pig's droppings. Pigs are like leaves used in the toilet.
In 1994, the first peace talks between the Government and the LRA were held in northern Uganda. Betty Bigombe, the then minister for northern Uganda's pacification, led the government delegation. Below is what Kony told Bigombe at an August 9, 1994 meeting.
First of all, I would like to thank the Almighty God for the sacrifice you made to have these talks. I don't disagree with the talks as long as they are genuine. I want peace to return to our country.
I welcome the peace talks with the whole of my heart but I am worried about your side because there is lack of truth. Most of the time, NRA and some elders are interfering and covering up.
Betty, if you find that Government army misunderstands you, keep off, because you still have your big family to look after. Leave us, the LRA and NRA, to understand one another.
Mr. President may misunderstand you for pushing for these talks and meeting us and get annoyed with you so I don't want you to spoil your relationship with him. At least there is a history that you have tried to bring peace in the land but stupid people spoilt it.
Both NRA and LRA have seen the good and the bad side of the war.
If NRA soldiers would like to attack us, please mother, let them come. We are ready to meet them.
Mother, when we talked with you last time, fools disorganised our talks. And when I decide to talk peace sometimes people think I am defeated in the battle. Others are saying that we are going to launch an operation This is foolish talk.
Betty, I know you are bright and you know I am also wise. I don't want people to play with my mind. Giving past references where efforts to have talks have failed are all tactics.
Poor preparation made you be beaten twice because you lacked truth in your words. I am a servant of God and will keep speaking the truth. How much money did the Government lose in carrying out operations compared to when we talked peace? God's servants have disturbed me the way they did Moses in the biblical days.

Original_Search_Title: Luweero Anglican Bishop Shuffles Clergy
Headline: Uganda: Luweero Anglican Bishop Shuffles Clergy
Body_Text:
Luweero — Anglican Bishop, Rt. Rev. Evans Mukasa Kisekka on Sunday shuffled the diocese leadership.
This was during the annual diocesan council meeting at Luweero Diocesan Guest House.
Can. Elidad Nsubuga, who has been serving as the diocesan secretary for five years, was promoted to the position of provost.
Nsubuga becomes the first provost in the diocese.
He will be in charge of Luweero Cathedral and two other nearby parishes.
Rev. Steven Kasozi was moved Wobulenzi Church as Canon.
Kisekka asked the new leaders to start income generating projects to suppliment the little income they earn as church ministers. He said it would soon become compulsory for every priest to own at least three shambas of cassava, bananas or potatoes on the church land.
"I shall move out to monitor the progress of these projects," Kisekka said.

Original_Search_Title: Economy Dominates Polls
Headline: Madagascar: Economy Dominates Polls
Body_Text:
Improvement of living standards and a smooth transition of political institutions have become the main campaign issues as Madagascar edges closer to D-Day on 3 December.
With 6.3 million Malagasy nationals expected to elect the country's leader for the next five years, political parties have been jostling to position their candidates for president of the Indian Ocean island.
A total of 14 candidates, including incumbent President Marc Ravalomanana, will contest the eighth presidential polls since Madagascar's independence in 1965.
These include Elia Ravelomanantsoa, the only female candidate who has chosen to campaign on a "national" ticket.
Other challengers include Pierrot Rajaonarivelo of the Association of the Rebirth of Madagascar (AREMA) party; former speaker of the National Assembly, Jean Lahiniriko; former church minister, Richard Andriamanjato; and Roland Ratsiraka, mayor of the port city of Toamasina and nephew to former president, Didier Ratsiraka. AREMA is the former party of Didier Ratsiraka.
The president is elected by direct popular vote for a five-year term and the country's electoral system provides for the conduct of a run-off in the event that there is no clear winner from the first round.
Campaigning officially kicked off on 12 November and is due to end on the eve of the poll on 2 December.
Initial campaigning was disrupted by a military coup on 17 November, but the takeover bid was later foiled after other army officers refused to join renegade army officer, General Andrianafidisoa.
The economy is a key campaign issue, with most candidates promising the electorate a better and more prosperous future once they accede to power.
The opposition is using a recent spate of fuel and rice price increases to blame Ravalomanana for hardships faced by the poor.
Inflation has risen from 8.7 percent at the end of 2000 to 30 percent in September.
The incumbent president has since coming to power in 2002, however, managed to stabilise the economy and won the confidence of major multilateral institutions such as the World Bank and International Monetary Fund.
With an economy largely dependent on agriculture - which along with fishing and forestry contributes at least 25 percent of Gross Domestic Product (GDP) - the island also produces coffee, cassava, bananas, maize, sugarcane, potatoes and rice.
Mining and tourism sectors have shown signs of recovery and the government has projected increases in mineral exports from about US$100 million to US$150 million per year over the next 10 years.
The biggest boost to the Malagasy economy has been the discovery of oil which has recently triggered a scramble by international oil conglomerates. Initial projections are that the country could produce 60,000 barrels per day in three to four years. With potential revenue of billions of dollars, this oil boom would make the industry the biggest contributor to GDP.
Ravalomanana came to power following a controversial 2002 presidential poll when his rival, Didier Ratsiraka also claimed victory.
Other candidates - including Ravalomanana - are preaching national reconciliation during the campaign, motivated by a need to avoid the repeat of the 2002 debacle when then president, Didier Ratsiraka, refused to concede defeat to Ravalomananana. The deadlock resulted in the creation of two armies, capital cities and governments.
Ravalomanana urged thousands of supporters who turned out for his first campaign rally on 12 November to keep calm and resist the temptation to react to provocation by rivals.
"Do not react to provocations, it is not worth it," Ravalomanana told his supporters.
During her rallies, Ravelomanantsoa has called for calm among the electorate to ensure the process was conducive for transparent, free and fair polls.
She has written to other Southern African Development Community (SADC) leaders, the African Union and France urging them to send election observer missions to ensure transparency in the build-up to the polls. sardc.net

Original_Search_Title: Minister Urges Promotion of Nigerian Products
Headline: Nigeria: Minister Urges Promotion of Nigerian Products
Body_Text:
Minister of Commerce, Dr Aliyu Modibbo, has asked Nigerian diplomats to prioritise the marketing and promotion of Nigerian goods, services and resources at their duty posts.
Modibbo was speaking in Berlin, Germany, while presenting promotional materials for his flagship initiative, "Commerce 44", to the staff of the Embassy in Germany at the start of his advocacy visit to that country.
The visit will also take him to Britain and China.
A statement from the ministry in Abuja yesterday quoted the minister as saying that the country's manufactured products and services, agricultural produce as well as solid minerals could now compete in the global market-place.
Moddibo said that the items which include Commerce 44 posters and roll-up banners, would be circulated to the country's diplomatic missions in all the target markets covered by the initiative.
The Commerce 44 initiative aims at promoting 11 agricultural produce, 11 solid minerals and 11 manufactured products and services in 11 target markets.
They include the US, Japan, China, India and Turkey, the European Union, ECOWAS sub-region, Southern and Central Africa countries, the Middle East and South East Asia.
The minister identified some of the agricultural produce covered by the initiative to include cocoa, cotton, cassava, ginger, sheanut and gum Arabic.
Others are sesame seed, poultry, cashew nuts, floriculture as well as fruits and vegetables.
Products such as beverages, vegetable oil, footwear, leather, shrimps and pharmaceuticals will benefit just as rubber and rubber products, textile and garments, iron and steel, film and music and other services.
Also on the list are 11 solid minerals including Tantalite, Coal, Gold, Silver, Ilmenite-Rutile, Cassiterite, Bitumen, Lead-Zinc, Iron ore, Wolframite and Manganese.
Modibbo explained that Nigeria was already witnessing considerable activity in both the listed products and the target markets and asked for the support of the missions in expanding the nation's non-oil export. (NAN)

Original_Search_Title: Food Crisis Hits Mpigi
Headline: Uganda: Food Crisis Hits Mpigi
Body_Text:
Mpigi — RESIDENTS of Mpigi district are suffering a serious food crisis. According to the District Production Coordinator, Herman Ssekiwunga the shortage is due to inadequate rains in the previous season and delayed rain for the current season.
"In addition to inadequate rain, vermin and laziness in the community also contributed to the ongoing food shortage," Ssekiwunga told Daily Monitor on Monday.
The most affected sub counties are Maddu, Kabulasoke, Ngando, Budde and part of Mudduuma. Ssekiwunga said the district is planning to purchase some cassava cuttings and other seeds under the Plan for Modernisation of Agriculture (PMA) and Local Government Development Plan to avert the situation.
He said the district has already purchased and distributed 13 tonnes of upland rice to all sub-counties in the district to be planted on 433 acres of land.
"Each farmer who had well-prepared land got 15 kilogrammes. We expect to distribute more as rains continue to fall," Ssekiwunga said.
He said the district has also invited the National Banana Bacterial Wilt Control Initiative officials to train its agriculture sector staff in banana wilt control.
He said three staff members had already attended a national training session in Mityana and are expected to extend the skills to other farmers. Ssekiwunga said the sector also received five Japanese volunteers who have been attached to Buwama, Kamengo, Kyegonza, Kibibi and Mpigi to work with the extension staff.

Original_Search_Title: Sironko Farmers Embrace Exotic Goats for Milk
Headline: Uganda: Sironko Farmers Embrace Exotic Goats for Milk
Body_Text:
Kampala — PEASANT farmers in Sironko and Mbale districts have adapted to keeping British Toggenburg (BT) goats for milk production.
The goats believed to have high nutritious milk are donated by Farm-Africa, a non-government organisation, aimed at reducing poverty amongst farmers in Bugisu region.
"This milk has been tried before and is the reason we are promoting the adaptation among the households," explained Christine Alokit Olaunah, the Farm-Africa project coordinator, Mbale district.
"The milk is easily digestible and people with allergies do not find problems with it," she explained to the delegation of farmers from Kenya, Tanzania and Ugandan during their visit to farmers in Samazi parish, Bulegeni Sub-county.
The BT goats eat less and occupy less grazing space. They have excellent udder development, and produce good quality and quantity milk with a good length of lactation.
All the visited farmers in the region confessed getting at least two to three litres of milk per day per goat.
"The milk is consumed by the members of the farmer's organisation and when sold out a litre costs between sh1,000 to sh1500 compared to sh250 to sh300 for cow's milk," said Edinani Wamasali of Bugidyonyi Sub- county, Sironko.
He said their organisation has 35 registered members out of which 31 members each received two female local goats on credit.
With Farm-Africa's help, community members identify the most vulnerable families especially those affected by HIV/AIDS or headed by women. The identified people are given two goats on credit. After sometime, the families are allowed to crossbreed the goats with a Toggenburg buck to increase on the milk yields. The Toggernburg buck which is used by farmers for cross breeding is given at Sub-county level.
Buck services are accessed by both members of the group and non-members at an agreed fee, which ranges between sh500 for members of the organisation and sh1000 for non-members.
"The money is used to purchase fodder for the buck and basic health services like de-worming," explained Wamasali, a buck keeper.
Olaunah said when the BT crosses with the local goats, the farmer gets an offspring of 50%Toggernburg. After one-and-half years, they carry out buck rotation to avoid in breeding which gives the farmer 75% Toggenburg.
"This is a better product because it gives farmers more milk compared to the pure BT goats," explained Olaunah.
The goats are fed on multi-purpose fodder trees like calliandra, leucaena and Gliricidia or sesban sesbania, which are useful and cheap source of protein.
Currently 1,591 local goats and 51 Toggenburg goats have been given out to farmers benefiting 39 farmers groups and 1,464 households in the three districts since the launch of the programme three years ago.
"FARM-Africa has delivered an innovative crossbreeding and animal healthcare programme to increase the milk, productivity of local dairy goats, enabling the most vulnerable families to feed themselves better and earn money by selling surplus milk," Dr Christie Peacock, the executive director of FARM-Africa said.
"We also manage the Maendeleo Agricultural Technology Fund, which gives small grants to groundbreaking agricultural projects involving smallholder farmers," she said. The project helps farmers in northern Uganda to fight the deadly mosaic virus, which has had a devastating effect on one of the most important staple crops-cassava.

Original_Search_Title: Acholi Still Remains Uganda's Food Basket
Headline: Uganda: Acholi Still Remains Uganda's Food Basket
Body_Text:
Twenty years of civil strife in northern Uganda may have destroyed the communities of one of Uganda's most resilient populations, but it certainly never tampered with their spirit and love for hard work.
In situations where you expect thousands of internally displaced people in camps holding out bowls for financial assistance and food portions from sympathisers, the Acholi people either out of desperation or strong will to pick up their pieces and move on, have dived straight into what they do best - farming to sustain themselves.
With peace slowly crawling back to the region and the people still keeping their fingers crossed for the peace process in Juba, South Sudan, hundreds of IDPs mostly widows and orphans are sneaking into their outer fields to sow a seed or two of their favourite crops to exploit the current rainy season.
They have placed their faith in God before anything else. Speaking during the ordination of Gulu Bishop Sabino Odoki, the LC5 chairman for Gulu district Norbert Mao told residents, "As you go home, there is a basic message, I want to give you. God loves you. Gulu has great potential. We are not war victims we are war survivors."
And the Vice President Gilbert Bukenya has vowed to stick with the people of northern Uganda as they return home after years holed up in camps because of rebel havoc.
