"Now that a new era has begun in northern Uganda, we shall be here to help people rebuild their lives, spiritually, physically and socially," he said.
"Together with the government, we shall work to improve the social and health services that were destroyed during the war. I urge all religious leaders to instil moral discipline among the youth who are the most affected."
Bukenya asked residents to unite, love one another and pray for total peace to return.
"We need people to return to their normal lives and embark on development," he said.
The Vice President was on a one-week sensitisation and mobilisation tour of the northern districts of Gulu, Adjumani, Amuru and Pader.
Bukenya who is advocating for modern agriculture practices to boost production in the region was met by an equally enthusiastic audience of returnees who are demanding for improved farm tools and seeds to quickly implement his call for food production. "But we are using hand hoes to clear bushes. We would appreciate modern farm implements like tractors to clear the fields quickly," the executive director of Caritas Gulu, Father Felix Opio said.
Caritas is a Catholic NGO that resettles residents particularly women and children.
Among other things Bukenya is advocating for improved agricultural practices including the production of high value crops like upland rice, simsim, sunflower oil, cassava and groundnuts for commercial purposes.
He also wants returnees to each acquire a cow to benefit from the sale of milk.
"I will bring high yield quick growing rice to fully support farming in this region. People are going back to their homes, but we must know how they will resettle there," Bukenya said.
The VP gave out 250 tonnes of rice to farmers through Caritas.
At Lolim camp in Amuru district, one farmer Ben Okeny Latim defied the odds and planted over 80 acres of upland rice. He has already harvested 400 bags worth over Shs16 million. He says he has another 400 bags waiting to be harvested.
"I wanted to grow more rice but I don't have the equipment," Latim said.
"My brother in the United States of America promised to buy me a combined harvester to ease the work. Currently I use manual labour and lose most of my rice in the process. If people here listen to the vice president's message, they will be able to get money from rice especially if they work as a group."
According to Latim, production is limited by lack of modern equipment to cultivate the fields like tractors and ox-ploughs.
According to Bukenya politicians must work together to identify commercial farmers who can pool their resources together and access markets for their produce. He said land in the north was good for agriculture production because it is flat and fertile. He said he would ask Caritas Africa for a tractor to help farmers.
"Our leaders must consolidate their resources and help our people out of poverty. Let us put politics aside and be optimism," he said.
The leader of the opposition in parliament and also member of Parliament for Agago County Prof. Ogenga Latigo said leaders must draw collective practical solutions on issues of development for the good of the people. Over 1.5 million people have been displaced by the 20-year-old insurgency since 1996.
Before 1996, most northern Ugandans lived directly off the land as small-scale farmers.

Original_Search_Title: 2007 - I'll Focus On People -Odili
Headline: Nigeria: 2007 - I'll Focus On People -Odili
Body_Text:
Lagos — RIVERS State Governor and leading presidential aspirant for 2007, Dr. Peter Odili has promised to focus on the people, if elected president.
Dr. Odili stated this a an interactive session with media executives in Lagos yesterday, noting that his government will ensure the resuscitation and change of attitude and respect for the dignity of labour.
Elaborating, Dr. Odili said "Re-orientation is a necessity, a change of attitude, a must. We need to change our attitude. We should restore the dignity of labour and get our people back to work
"When the environment is right people can express themselves. Take the agricultural sector, the greatest employer of labour, the Federal Government already has identified target crops that have economic value.
Today we are virtually becoming the highest exporter of cassava. There are so many things for which cassava can be used including intravenous fluid. So once you get the infrastructure right the sky is the limit for Nigeria", he said.
The Governor who also dwelt on his achievements in office in the last seven and a half years explained that the leader the country deserves now is one capable of sustaining the gains recorded under President Olusegun Obasanjo's administration.
According to him, all we need to do is create the enabling environment that will encourage the people to perform in all sectors of the economy.
While expressing optimism of emerging as presidential flag bearer for the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), Dr Odili said if eventually elected, he will bring Agriculture back to the days of the groundnut Pyramids in the north as well as ensure food sufficiency.
He described the contest for the party's ticket as a very serious business with 19 candidates as at last count vying for the same position.
The Governor however added that he has embarked on nationwide consultations having been to 32 states including Abuja to seek for support mostly from delegates to the party's December 16th convention which he said will be the climax of the preparations.
Flaunting his record of achievements in Rivers State, Gov. Odili said the first ever 380 megawatts Independent Power Plant (IPP) built by his administration will soon be commissioned to boost the industrialization of the state.
He added that the plant also has infrastructure in place for additional 800 megawatts for the next government to improve on the IPP in future.
He recalled that his administration inherited only 10 mw of electricity supply from the national grid which was not sufficient to boost the economy of the state.
He explained that in spite of additional benefits, power is basic to the industrialization of any state adding that the additional power generation will assist immensely to catch up with the failure to invest in the power sector in the last 20 years.
The Governor also listed the award of special scholarship to 400 students from the state to study abroad and the free education policy of his administration as steps taken to relieve the citizens of the burden of providing qualitative education for their children.
He explained that local governments hitherto inaccessible by roads in the state now have good roads while the last batch of the local governments will become accessible by roads latest March 2007.
Gov. Odili also recalled his administration's steps to combat insecurity in the state particularly in Port Harcourt, with the donation of 130 vehicles to the state police command adding that he was encouraged to embark on this with the influx of investors now residents to the state.
The Governor was however full of praises for President Obasanjo for the boldness in fighting corruption in public places.
m into this year's Champion Insurance Day/Luncheon

Original_Search_Title: IDPs Build Roads But Stay Stuck in Camps
Headline: Uganda: IDPs Build Roads But Stay Stuck in Camps
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE bushy and overgrown roads of Oyam district, once prone to ambushes by the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) rebels and once a prime source of insecurity, are now one of the glimmers of hope for Oyam residents.
There is an overpowering feeling of fear among displaced communities in northern Uganda who have learnt of the pending truce agreement between the LRA and the Government, but who have not been able to 'return' to their previous lives. The internally displaced persons (IDPs), who have chosen to leave the camps and return to their homes, have found that on top of insecurity, they lack enough food.
However, DANIDA, the Danish government's aid agency, has started Voucher-for-Work, an agricultural programme in Oyam aimed at paving the path for the successful return of the displaced people.
The programme, already operating in the West Nile, is also aimed at restoring farming and infrastructure.
"We have now opened the roads," says Beatrice Okello, the chairman of Ngai sub-county in Oyam. The IDPs of Ngai have built 12 access roads and five woodlots. Under Voucher-for-Work, communities gauge what they collectively need to improve on. For their services, the farmers are paid with vouchers redeemable for agricultural inputs at the Uganda National Agro Input Dealers Association stores.
High quality inputs such as improved seeds, tools, fertilisers and pesticides are provided through the dealers.
For the nearly 5,000 participants in the programme, good feeder roads are the priority. The participants have so far constructed 120 kilometres of road worth sh150m.
"All the IDPs know is handouts," Warwick Thompson, Denmark's senior adviser to the agricultural sector, says of the 1.2 million displaced people, adding, "Though the good thing is they have never stopped working."
RALNUC, an NGO, hopes that vouchers will offset the effects to the local market, such as fluctuating prices, that relief distribution by groups like the World Food Programme (WFP) have caused.
In Oyam, the farmers have never been fed by the WFP, but they have been forced to flee to camps to escape the LRA and the Uganda People's Defence Forces. They only leave the camps to grow food -- and with the dilapidated state of the roads to their villages, even that task has been difficult.
"We were running away from the rebels," says Felix Ocan, who has spent six years at Opit Camp in Gulu District.
Ocan has been working since July, when the voucher exchange programme began.
When asked why he has not returned home, the growing crowd around Ocan bursts into laughter: the idea of home still provokes terror within the camp. "Going home became impossible," Christopher Odongo, the coordinator of Voucher-for-Work in Oyam, says.
With a hoe in hand, Bisatina Ayet says she is waiting for the road to reach her village. Until then, she will continue cultivating beans in a nearby garden.
A total of 51,411 people in Opit leave the camp everyday to grow beans, simsim, cassava, sweet potatoes and millet, Peter Ogwang, the leader of the camp, says.
Though they have not experienced an attack since July 2003, with a hospital and market nearby, the allure of the camp remains great.
The farmers in Otwal Camp in Otwal sub-county, Oyam District, have taken advantage of the vouchers to pay for tools to clear the roads, rather than buying inputs. While they wait for the next season to harvest seeds, the people still demand for soap, sugar, salt and oil from RALNUC in the dry period.
Existing in temporary homes and experiencing acute hunger, the IDPs find it difficult to place long-term planning ahead of fast returns.
"When people are in camps, they are like prisoners; we give them take-home rations to work in their gardens such that they stop relying on the WFP," says Solomon Asea, the field monitor at the Lira WFP office.
Since July 3, the organisation has provided food rations for eight camps in Lira as the bulk of a resettlement "take-home" package for around 185,000 IDPs.
But even the office acknowledges that the food is not enough. It relies on other agencies to come into the area to fulfil other basic needs such as roads and health services, the WFP says.
"We are praying for peace to come," says Molly Alum, the secretary of the Lira District Farmers' Association.
For the displaced residents of the north, peace will not come until it is in the form of permanent safety and food security.

Original_Search_Title: Of Oshiomhole, Edo State And 2007
Headline: Nigeria: Of Oshiomhole, Edo State And 2007
Body_Text:
Kaduna — Since its creation over 30 years ago, Edo State had never had it so bad in terms of governance. In the good old days, Edo State always set the pace for others to follow. But in this new dispensation, the seven years of People Democratic Party's administration in the state, led by Governor Lucky Igbenedion is a colossal waste to the state, as billions of monthly allocation accruing to the state have not improved the life of the citizenry.
Past leaders like Professor Ambrose Ali, late Major General George Inih, John Odigie Oyegun and others who have vision worked hard and developed the state. Indeed they developed it to an extent that all the present government needs is to improve on it and bring joy and hope to the people of the state by making education, water and other social amenities accessible and affordable. But today Edo State has been turned into a lame duck that cannot work even at thirties; the vision of our past leaders had been punctured by the PDP-led government to pave way for hunger, unemployment and utter hopeless in the state.
In the seven good years of Lucky Igbenedion's administration, nothing has changed. The roads within the state have been the same since 1999. Flood has taken over the entrance to many houses due to lack of drainages; the streets are full of potholes which make it almost impossible to drive in Benin City, especially during the rainy season. In short, I cannot point at any road project commissioned by the governor since 1999.
In the education sector, the vibrancy of our educational system is deliberately being killed to our chagrin. There are shortage of teachers and learning facilities virtually in all public schools in the state. Teachers are not employed to meet the ever increasing number of students. No new infrastructure has been erected while the existing ones built over 30 years ago are left to rust without maintenance.
This ugly development has forced many parents to withdraw their children from public schools. They now prefer private schools with all their exorbitant fees. Those who cannot afford private schools for their wards have since resigned to fate.
Also, in the last seven years of our democratic experience in Edo State, the dream of actualizing our aspiration and hope for better life could be likened to a stillborn child. We voted for our own people to represent and protect us but we are paid back with hunger, poverty and poor infrastructural development occasioned by misrule. Things that will improve our lives are not paid the attention they deserve.
Today, gully erosion is threatening every part of the state with no respite in sight. The gully erosion at Benin, Auchi and Uromi had swallowed up over a thousand houses thereby rendering many families homeless. Yet we have a government supposedly elected by the people to address their problems.
When other states were busy commissioning one project or the other, ours' was busy dredging the Ipoba River with billions of naira. Of what benefit is the dredging of Ipoba River to the people of Edo State when education and social infrastructures are seriously begging for attention?
The fertilizer processing factory at Auchi, Cassava factory at Ebpoma and Juice factory at Ekhor 'fraudulently' commissioned by President Obasanjo in his first tenure to portray the PDP as performing government in the state never functioned for one day. What a clandestine way of deceiving the sensibilities of the people of the state.
Bad governance is no longer hidden in the state despite the huge monthly allocation and 13 percent oil derivation to oil producing state. We have nothing to show for it except the epileptic power supply; the eyesore masquerading as roads are death trap to motorist; no portable water; no infrastructural development; poverty and unemployment. In short governance in the state is in abysmal situation and has no legacy to be remembered for.
The Edo people groan and bite their fingers while watching the state collapsed like a pack of cards. Crime and criminals are having a field day. Armed robbers now operate with impunity, unleashing terror on innocent citizens in broad daylight with security agents scampering for safety too. No one is secured any longer.
The Irony of it all is that Governor Lucky Igbenedion performed creditably when he was he was the Chairman of Oredo Local Government. But on becoming the state governor, he surrounded himself with people best described as garage boys (Agbero) who hijack his government and constituted themselves to unleash terror on anyone who dare questioned the political emptiness and misrule of his administration.
Worse still, while the people groaned under the yoke of misrule, the political godfathers who imposed the governor on the people either turn blind eyes to his misrule or engage in cozy relationship to have their shares of the monthly allocation.
Now that 2007 elections draw closer, these same political godfathers who have mortgaged the future of the state are once again standing on the podium appealing to the people to accept their sons and godsons as the next this or next that in the state. However, Edo people are no fools; they still recollect what transpired yesterday and will not dare make the same mistake twice.
The time has come for the people of Edo to reflect on the state's leadership in the past seven and half years to guide them in choosing a leader endowed with all it takes to lead them to the Promised Land. We need a leader that can provide a meaningful development in the state; a leader who will not loot the state treasury to build houses in Asokoro, Abuja: a leader who will not serve few godfathers but the entire people of the state.
We have comrade Adams Oshiomhole who is offering himself to serve his people. Oshiomhole's antecedent as the President of the Nigeria Labour Congress speaks volumes. He has what it takes to liberate the people from the shackle of hunger, unemployment, decay and hopeless occasioned by bad governance. Let all people of goodwill in the state unite and give Adams Oshiomhole a chance irrespective of the political party under which he chooses to serve, because the People Democratic had failed woefully.
Bello lives behind Jabi Central Mosque, FCT, Abuja.
Edo people are no fools; they still recollect what transpired yesterday and will not dare make the same mistake twice. The time has come for the people of Edo to reflect on the state's leadership in the past seven and half years to guide them in choosing a leader endowed with all it takes to lead them to the Promised Land

Original_Search_Title: Food Reserve Agency Buys 382,000 Tonnes of Maize
Headline: Zambia: Food Reserve Agency Buys 382,000 Tonnes of Maize
Body_Text:
THE Food Reserve Agency (FRA) has bought 382,000 tonnes of maize as at yesterday, Agriculture and Cooperative Minister Ben Kapita told Parliament.
Mr Kapita said in a ministerial statement that the Government would ensure that all the maize grain from farmers was bought.
He said the Government had in this year's Budget projected to spend K 50 billion to buy 80,000 tonnes of maize but because of the bumper harvest, the country had recorded more yields than it had anticipated.
"The original plan was to purchase 80,000 tonnes of maize, 1,200 tonnes of rice, 2,400 tonnes of cassava, 1,200 tonnes of soya beans and 1,200 tonnes of ground nuts" Mr Kapita said.
In view of the bumper harvest, the Government instructed the FRA to ensure that it procured all the crops. The FRA would also continue exporting maize so that the money realised from exports could be used to pay farmers.
The minister also said the Government managed to send 14,967 tonnes of D Compound fertiliser and 30,000 tonnes of Urea to all the districts.
The discrepancy in the tonnage was because Nitrogen Chemicals of Zambia (NCZ), was facing numerous operational problems.
Mr Kapita said this in response to Bangweulu MP Joseph Kasongo (PF) who wondered why there was such a huge difference in the quantity of fertiliser distributed.
Works and Supply Minister Kapembwa Simbao told the House that the construction of the Chembe Bridge, which was commissioned by President Mwanawasa on September 17, would be completed on April 28 2008.
The project, which is financed, by the Government and the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) was expected to gobble at least K 46 billion.
The bridge, constructed by a Chinese firm, China Henan had a life span of 100 years. Mr Simbao said the Government was also in the process of rehabilitating the Pedicle road.
Home Affairs Minister, Crispin Musosha, told the House the cases of Karavinas terrorising people in Western Province had reduced because of extensive police patrols.
He also said the amnesty that the Government had introduced on illegal firearms were yielding results as many were coming forward to surrender the arms.
Mr Musosha said this in response to a question by Nalolo MP Mubita Mwangala (MMD).
Local Government and Housing Minister, Silvia Masebo, told the House that the Government has spent close to K 1.5 billion to implement the decentralisation policy.
The money was used in the sensitisation and civic awareness, sector development and fiscal transfers.
Her counterpart at Education Geoffrey Lungwangwa said the ministry had plans of establishing high schools in densely populated areas particularly in rural places.

Original_Search_Title: Voters' Registration - We Reject This Evil Plot to Rig 2007 Polls
Headline: Nigeria: Voters' Registration - We Reject This Evil Plot to Rig 2007 Polls
Body_Text:
FROM Lagos to Suleja, and on to Abakaliki and other nooks and crannies of Nigeria, there have been widespread complaints and protests by political parties, politicians and other Nigerians concerning the on-going voters registration exercise. The more worrisome has been the steady breakdown of the Direct Data Capture machines imported for the purposes of conducting more credible polls next year.
Today, the parties and politicians point accusing fingers at the Federal Government and the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) that the shoddy shows in virtually all the states, are pointers to the fact that the exercise had been originally designed to benefit some people and punish others.
They weep that the inadequacy of the machines was another ploy to disenfranchise millions of willing voters and a sure way to create room for the perpetration of electoral fraud when the time comes.
Senator Mojisoluwa Akinfenwa, factional national chairman of Alliance for Democracy (AD) told Saturday Vanguard that he has not been able to register because the necessary things needed for the registration are not available in his area.
"But I want to continue to trust the INEC chairman, Professor Maurice Iwu who has pledged his commitment to ensure that the exercise is successful.
"I am expressing optimism that those saddled with the preparation for the general elections next year would not disappoint. I hope they are patriotic enough to the point of ensuring that Nigerians are not disenfranchised because of the way things are going.
"I hope they would not do things in such a manner that this country could go up in flames because of people's disappointment. Anything that happens now could affect the elections. So, INEC should not fail us".
Alhaji Lai Mohammed, the spokesperson of Action Congress, AC, described the whole exercise as a failure. "I mean, it is obvious that it has failed," he says. "The so-called Direct Data Capture Machine has not been able to capture anything. It is a shame and should be discarded. We should go back to the old system which has served us successfully over the years and I also want to add that we should adopt the modified open ballot system during the elections".
Chief Chekwas Okorie of the All Progressive Grand Alliance (APGA) cannot simply conceal his anger.
"Honestly, I had my doubts from the onset and I expressed these doubts but people felt I was talking out of sentiments because I had a running battle with Professor Iwu but the things I said were not personal.
"I did say that if we want to procure the machines, they should not come from a place or a country that is having a running battle with Dora Akunyili's NAFDAC because of the fake drugs being produced in that country. I said if we should use Direct Data Capture Machines, we should order from reputable countries like the United States, a country that would not allow a substandard product.
"But for whatever reasons, we bought these machines that broke down even at the point of demonstration. As we've all seen, the machines are breaking down everywhere. They are not even available. It's a national disgrace.
"I am sure President Obasanjo wants to hold a credible election and leave behind a great legacy but I don't think he should achieve that result with the people he has at the INEC secretariat. So, this is the time to act and do something decisively."
Delta
Although the INEC Resident Electoral Commissioner in Delta state, Alhaji Ismaila Abdukareem has continued to assure Deltans of the readiness of the commission to get all eligible votes registered, many residents of the state are still skeptical that this may not come to pass by the close of the exercise next month.
Indeed, there have been protests and disenchantment because of the slow nature coupled with the fact that there are insufficient Direct Data Capture Machines to cater for the 25 local government areas of the state.
The All Nigeria People's Party (ANPP) in the state, led by Barrister Veronica Bamuza-Mutu had, in a letter of protest dated November 14, 2006, said that it is "to our dismay and frustration that only 25 machines, one per local government area instead of the 906 machines we were assured that were available. To worsen the situation, many of the machines have broken down. What is going on makes the voters revalidation exercise a child's play.
"It is sad to note that the INEC workers in the field are requesting voters to travel from the rural areas to the local government headquarters to register. How could these poor people pay transport fare worth thousands of naira to register their names?".
Also worried that they might eventually be disenfranchised, the people of Okpanam in Oshimili local council, recently held its Izu-Ani (highest and largest elders meeting) where they resolved to take their plight to the governor over the development.
The Odogwu of Okpanam, Chief Peter Dunkwu, who spoke on behalf of the community noted that "we have decided to cry out because we know the importance of this exercise and that is why we had sensitised our people. Some of the farmers have abandoned their farms and waiting to be registered. But as I speak to you now, no INEC staff or their machine for capturing images had been sent here (Okpanam) since the exercise commenced about a week ago."
Similarly, there have been series of complaints from other parts of the state. Also, in Ndokwa West, not more than 50 persons were registered in the first week of the exercise because "the machine could not capture our thumb-prints."
It has been complaint galore. But the INEC boss, Alhaji Abdulkareem, in response to the myriad of complaints insisted that the situation would be addressed. He said, Wednesday, that the commission had just taken delivery of twenty more Direct Data Capture machines, noting that the state now has 67 DDC machines for the exercise and that each of the 25 local government would receive at least two or more machines.
PDP's presidential aspirant and ex-Nigeria's Ambassador to Germany, the Rev. (Mrs.) Ogbon-Day was full of disgust when asked by Saturday Vanguard to assess the progress of the exercise so far. Hear her: "There is nothing to asses there, it is a failure. How voters registration will be going and a presidential aspirant like me cannot even register is baffling. One of the requirements is that an aspirant has to be registered and attach evidence of his or her registration. As I am speaking to you today, (November 14) and you can see a woman sitting by me, she is running for House of Assembly, we have not set our eyes on any INEC voters' machine.
Today, we hear that one moneybag aspirant has hijacked the only one machine, carried it to a hideout, did whatever pleased him and returned it; the next thing they said was that it's broken down. They have carried it to Asaba and all that, excuse me, what kind of rubbish is that? What kind of machine are we talking about; even the machines they use to grate cassava in the villages do not break down in this manner.
"This is most ridiculous; I cannot believe that this is my country. I think the solution is that anybody who is of age should go and vote and let us scrap this crap of voters' registration. When people are limited in the way I am seeing it now, it is a way of controlling who goes into the government, it is not right. It's an evil way to manipulate the polls. Where did they bring these machines from?
"If INEC needs more hands, let them get more hands and why are they asking why we want to register in areas where we come from. Why not? Because I come from somewhere and I want to be registered there and also be able to mobilize my people to register."
Ogbon-Day said that the fact that eligible voters could not be registered and the registration machines were not available were obvious indications that the "we are in troubled waters as far as the 2007 elections are concerned."
Chairman of the Egbema Political Forum (EPF) in the state, High Chief Jimmy Taylor, lamented the non-availability of registration materials in the Egbema clan, saying that his people have not set eyes on the one registration machine that was made available to Warri North local government area. He said the group had since petitioned the chairman of INEC and the resident commissioner in the state but as at the time of speaking to this paper, no response has been received.
Also, the Ijaws of Warri, led by Comrade Festus Koremene of the National Ijaw Human Rights Organisation complained that besides the delayed take-off of the exercise and frequent breakdown of the machines or equipment, the entire process in Warri South local government area has been hijacked by politicians with vested interest in the 2007 elections.
The torrents of complaints do not mean that some people have not been registered. At Sapele, last week, the chairman of the Ijaw Stakeholders' Forum, Mr. Iroro'netaye Clarke, confirmed to Saturday Vanguard that he had just been registered but equally spoke of the pains his people were going through due to the absence of the direct data capturing machines.
Also, Itsekiri leader, Chief Rita Lori-Ogbebor, stormed the INEC office in Warri, Tuesday, over the non-inclusion of Esisi ward where she had always voted in the past elections from the INEC register. She suspected that the development was a design to disenfranchise her and many other Itsekiris in the 2007 elections.
Ibadan
When the registration of voters kicked off on October 24, 2006, expectations were high that all necessary facilities had been provided to ensure the successful completion of the exercise. Such high hopes had since vanished into thin air.
While some complained of unavailability of materials, some complained that some thugs hijacked some of the materials meant for the exercise. The first person to raise alarm was the former governor of Oyo State, Alhaji Lam Adesina, who claimed that only 20 machines were available out of over 1,119 machines approved for the state, alleging that most of the machines had been taken to the house of a PDP chieftain in the state.
According to Adesina, "our people went round and there was nothing on ground to show that the exercise has commenced in the state. The report from Saki was that the only machine there was bad. So, the people had to go back home. Besides, I learnt that out of the over 1,119 machines expected to be used in Oyo, only 20 were distributed and I wonder how many people these will serve." According to him,"I heard that some of the machines have been taken to individuals' houses for use but I want to believe that this is not true, I am using this opportunity to warn the INEC against dancing to the tune of any person or political party because in doing so the commission will be playing with fire and we all know that when fire burns, it burns disastrously. Let the INEC not tamper with the political destiny of the people of Oyo state.
However, spokesperson for the commission, Mr. Ayodele Folami, who admitted that the machines were not sufficient for the exercise, said that the execise began at the right time in most of the local government councils in the state.He confirmed that the only one machine sent to Saki was faulty. "They called me this morning to alert me and we have sent another one to the place. By now, the exercise must have begun in earnest in Saki." While corroborating the insufficiency of materials, the Oyo state INEC Resident Electoral Commissioner, Alhaji Isyaku Maigoro, admitted that the commission was facing shortage of materials for the on-going voters registration exercise which resulted in the slow take-off of the exercise in the state. .. According to him, out of the 1,199 Direct Data Capture Machines (DDC) appropriated for Oyo State, the commission has so far received 36. "At the moment, the total number of Direct Data Capture Machines in Oyo State is 36 but four of this number are technically faulty."
He added that with the number of the machines at that time, the commission could only cover 31 of the 33 local governments in the state and the functional machines had been delivered among the three senatorial districts of the state 'using the local government density as the distribution yardstick'. But some reprieve came the way of the commission when on Wednesday, the resident INEC boss in the state said that he had received additional 52, bringing it to 88 of the 1,199 of the direct data capture machines so far supplied to the state. He would not be drawn into saying if the exercise, in his own estimation, has been successful.
Said Maigoro: "The exercise is moving steadily with the limited number of machines at our disposal. Our prayer is that we have the machines supplied in large numbers. Our major problem is the insufficiency of machines. Out of 88 that are on ground now, 18 of them are faulty and have been sent for repair. The faulty ones have been taken to the technical department in Lagos ."
He, however, faulted the claims of some people that they had not seen the machine in their areas. "That is not true. It is because they have not got to their units. What they can say is that it has not got to their turn. We move from one unit to another. At least, we register 500 people in a unit."
Senator Mojisoluwa Akinfenwa, factional national chairman of the Alliance for Democracy (AD) says the situation is worrisome "because if they deny anybody that chance to register, they have denied him his civic duties as a Nigerian to vote. And this kind of thing can lead to crisis because it could delay our smooth transition to another democratic experience. I know they will certainly extend the time because the time slated for it can't be okay. I know that INEC knows the seriousness of the matter and I trust that it cannot toy with it.
In Enugu
From Enugu State, the exercise has witnessed a lot of hitches especially inadequate supply of Direct Data Capture machines. At the inception of the registration, three local government areas were selected from each of the three senatorial zones in the state but following the deployment of more DDC machines, it was extended to more councils. Yet, this has not helped in any way in improving the performance of the registration officials as most of the machines perform below capacity owing to battery problem.
Only 39 DDC machines were allocated to the state by INEC as at last week while additional 15 were supplied this week. All though each council now has at least three registration machines, most of them are not functioning. Hardly any of the machines registers an average of ten persons without complaints from the operators.
Reports from most local government areas indicated that several eligible voters who are eager to get the cards that will enable them participate in the next electoral process, have not succeeded in doing so and the frustration they have experienced in their fruitless attempts seems to be having its toll as some now threaten to forget about participating in the forthcoming elections.
However, top politicians and government officials from the state including the Senate President, Chief Ken Nnamani, Governor Chimaroke Nnamani, senators and members of the House of Representatives had no difficulties obtaining their cards as the State INEC Commissioner, Alhaji Abdul Bulama, made special arrangements for their registration.
It is generally feared that unless the deadline for exercise is extended, majority of the people of the state will not be registered. But INEC's spokesman in the state, Mr. Simon Amadi dismissed the fear, saying that with the increasing number of DDC machines being dished out, he was optimistic that all eligible voters in the state would be registered.
Meanwhile, technicians have been hired to maintain and effect repairs on any faulty DDC machines while a generating set has been sent to each of the local government areas to recharge the batteries.
Rivers
Protests and series of complaints have trailed the on-going voters registration and revalidation exercise in Rivers State, causing politicians and electorate to dub the exercise a failure. Some residents, particularly at the grassroots, are not even aware that such an exercise is taking place in the country. When this paper confronted the INEC chairman in Rivers state, Chief Rowland Uwa, he referred this correspondent to speak with those who heard his announcement on radio and television, last week, where he called for understanding of the public. Uwa who declined further statement on the issue said "I am at a meeting right now".
But the state Organising Secretary of the All Nigeria People's Party, ANPP, Mr. John N. Geoffrey said "the entire exercise is a fraud. There is an evil plan in the offing. Let him (INEC chairman in Rivers) go to various councils and see the situation there. We can't even find the machines".
On his part, presidential aspirant of Hope Democratic Party (HDP), Chief Andrew Owuru said "the whole thing started wrongly and government should do something about it. We are worried. The exercise should have started earlier than now. Why should government even import machines to register Nigerians?"
Also, the chairman of Alliance for Democracy in Rivers, Mr. Mike Nwielaghi described the exercise as an "unfortunate effort. In fact, it is an exercise in futility. Where are the machines? We are being disenfranchised because if we are not registered, we can't vote. The time is fast running out and no one has registered. We should better stop wasting time and money and think of something else to do."
Kano
Here, the residents have experienced all sorts of problems while trying to register today in order to be eligible to vote in the forthcoming elections. The hitches are not different from what have been the recurring complaints- absence of adequate direct data capture machines and their frequent breakdown even where available.
Romanus Ikonne, oneof the people waiting to be registered painted the true picture of the frustration. Said he: "We're all tired of what the INEC officials are doing. I have been coming here for the past three days. Each time, you're given a particular number but you're never registered.
"If you bring the number the next day, those controlling the lines would tell you that you can't make use of yesterday's number because they have fresh number for the day. It's been that way. Many of us are tired of wasting our time on the queues.
I won't kill myself if they refuse to register me."
Indeed, that has been the disposition of many of the people in Kano who have not been registered. But the case of Linus Ukadike, a 70-year-old man is even more pathetic. Laments Ukadike: 'I am an old man, I have never witnessed this kind of exercise before. They say they want it to be electronic voting and that it will reduce rigging during the election. Well, that's okay but I only hope Nigerians will all get registered because it is too slow.
"Old people like me should be given priority in the registration centres because we don't have the strength to stand for so long. I also call on the government to provide more machines and more INEC workers because the exercise is just too slow, if there many machines it will be faster.
However, the state INEC spokesman, Garba Lawan said "we're aware of some of the shortcomings at the beginning of the exercise. We're aware that the chargers have not been working well. So, we got some generators in all the registration centres. And what happens is that while working during the day if the batteries run down, they will stop registration for at least thirty minutes and recharge and continue. But after each day's work, we take all the machines to a central centre in INEC local government office where we have a standby generator."
On the complaints about shortage of voters registration machines, he said they have received additional machines and now they are progressing. According to him, they are currently covering 12 local government councils out of the 44 local government councils in the state, and declared that they are optimistic that within the stipulated time frame, INEC in Kano will get all eligible voters registered.
Anambra
More than two weeks after the commencement of the voters' registration exercise by the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC), the people of Anambra state have found it extremely difficult to locate the very few registration centres scattered across the state, even as PDP stalwart, Chief Chris Uba, who registered at his Uga Ward I centre, in Aguata local government area, has urged Nigerians to exercise patience with INEC, saying that the commission would make up.
And even as INEC maintains that it is ready to execute a smooth exercise in the state, the exercise is being marred by apparent apathy arising from the people's inability to locate the registration centre.
Since the exercise commenced, it is only when notable members of the society like the governor and party leaders are registered that the people get to know that the exercise is still on-going.
Interested members of the public have been going round looking for registration centres without success.

Original_Search_Title: Coping With Life in the City - K'la's Poor
Headline: Uganda: Coping With Life in the City - K'la's Poor
Body_Text:
When I was in secondary school, one of my favourite subjects was geography. There were bits of the subject I disliked, and others that held me spellbound. One topic, rural-urban migration, has stuck in my memory all these years. Here we learnt about how and why people left the countryside and headed for urban settings.
We were told that, lured by 'the lights' they all came in pursuit of a better life, away from the drudgery of their parents' and relatives' peasant existence. The general expectation was that by moving to the city and earning generous salaries and wages, they would ascend the socio-economic ladder and cut their ties to the land.
While some hoped one day to go back and retire as respected members of society on generous pensions, others expected permanently to settle down in the city and not to return. In many ways this, minus the bit about generous pensions, still largely captures the dreams, expectations, and aspirations of rural-urban migrants today.
There are, of course, exceptions. These are the so-called circular migrants who live in the so-called dual system, shuttling back and forth between the city and the village where they hope eventually to escape from the uncertainties and worries of urban living. The circular migrant, as all keen observers would know, cuts across all social classes in any city or town.
Not all migrants, though, realise their dreams of cutting ties to the land while resident in the city. Casual observation of life in many parts of Kampala reveals that the dreams and expectations many migrants might have had of surviving on wages and salaries evaporate on arrival in the city.
Consequently, to buffer themselves against risk of starvation, they devised multiple strategies to keep afloat. Experts describe the actions individuals take to counter and respond to adversity by a variety of terms. Most commonly used are 'coping strategies' or 'survival mechanisms'.
In Kampala, despite some people enjoying the fruits of the 'minimum recovery' ushered in by a government already preparing the country for 'take off', some people lead lives very much like those they hoped they had left behind in villages.
One of the more striking scenes in parts of the city's central business district and suburbia is of men and women taking advantage of empty spaces for subsistence farming. While a few idle rich may do this as a hobby or form of physical exercise on their own plots, for others using public land, it is a means of supplementing poor diets.
A run through the suburbs reveals hundreds of allotments bustling with maize, yams, beans, cassava, sweet potatoes, pumpkins, vegetables and, in some cases, matooke. As in rural areas, there are no extension services and production technology is rudimentary, the simple hoe being the principal instrument.
Despite the city growing rapidly and becoming more affluent in the last 20 years, urban subsistence farming constitutes a vital safety net for many. Its importance can be seen in one neighbourhood through which I usually pass on my way home. Every evening, along a long stretch of road, one finds women and children roasting and selling maize harvested from a large open space next to an estate housing employees of one arm of the state.
This activity points to other means through which Kampala's residents living on the economic edge seek to keep afloat: petty trading. Home-based micro businesses -- many run by members of various professions or their wives -- are part of the city's large informal sector. A leisurely walk through several neighbourhoods reveals kiosks selling snacks, liquor, soft drinks, sweets, vegetables and fruit and, in some cases used clothes.
As we celebrate the achievement of minimum recovery, a new threat looms on the horizon for the involuntary farmers: the city's rapid physical expansion. The expansions is eating up spaces that have until now been available for agriculture. As sleek multi-million dollar hotels, office blocks, shopping malls and mansions go up, where should the urban peasant look for supplementary sustenance?

Original_Search_Title: Udenwa And the Presidential Challenge
Headline: Nigeria: Udenwa And the Presidential Challenge
Body_Text:
Lagos — It has become so apparent that the next president of the Federal Republic of Nigeria after President Olusegun Obasanjo will have a lot of problems to contend with whether he emerges from the South East, South/South or any of the Northern geo-political zones. This is taking into cognizance the emerging controversies that are presently playing itself out in the political firmament of the , nation. The fact that apart from the South West that seems to have accepted that in . the present circumstance they cannot produce' the next president, the remaining five geo-political zones are at each other's throat. And that is an indication that a lot of horse trading would finally come into play on which zone takes a shot at the presidency. The political parties are by their actions not settled on where the next president should emerge from. For now, majority of the political parties have their chairmen coming from the South East but this is not to say the aspirants from the zone nursing presidential dream have gone to the rest.
However, while Nigerians are presently worried about the on-going voters registration exercise of the Independent National Electoral Commission, INEC that appears to be wobbling, Nigerians are also watching the scenario and the hordes of presidential aspirants that have so far thrown their hats into the ring. Most of them possess rare qualities that ought to put them head first among others. This is taking into cognizance their healthy and wealthy backgrounds as well as their standing across the length and breadth of the nation. The fact that they have criss-crossed the nation wooing supporters and putting their score cards on the table are enough reasons to show that the electorate would have a hectic time making a serious choice.
While the electorate would first be judged by what they have done in their home states especially the serving governors, it does appear that time would soon come for the separation of the boys from the men. This is why the teeming supporters of the incumbent governor of Imo State, Chief Achike Udenwa have continued to nurse the feeling that at the end of the day he stands to have a healthier background. Firstly, the fact that it is incontrovertible as to his ability to carry all and sundry to the next level of development, Udenwa has continued to pursue this ambition with the dignity and candour worthy of a president of a powerful country called Nigeria. Secondly, the peace existing in Imo State has become a reference point to other states especially against the background of the political tensions and killings that has characterized the country's market' towards another phase of political stability.
Agreed that governing a state does not necessarily give a wider understanding to what it would mean to govern a nation as diverse as Nigeria, it must be pointed out that he who is unable to hold his people together cannot invariably hold the nation together. If there is nothing that has been achieved by the present administration of President Obasanjo, the fact that in spite of the turmoil and rancour that has plagued these seven years, Nigeria still remains an indivisible entity. Therefore, the incoming president would also need to have the ability and capability to maneuver through the political conflagrations to further cement the cord that bind the people together. Udenwa in the past seven years has fought from all sides to ensure that no matter the political differences the communal understanding that had existed before him is not torn to shreds.
In the past seven years, Udenwa has established himself as a man of peace capable of maintaining his stand and giving direction even when it is clear that opposition are not only out to destroy the state but themselves invariably. This is significant in the sense that right from the days of his electioneering campaign he had left no one in doubt as to his belief in the corporate existence of Nigeria. This is evident is his belief that a true Federal Republic of Nigeria is not only practicable but necessary for the nation to move forward and assume her leadership role in the comity of African nations.
Today Imo State has become the cynosure of all eyes in terms of development as well as investment in people. There are so many things that were not in existence prior to 1999 that has become a landmark. For instance the ultra modern secretariat situated at New Owerri had been in the pipeline since the first civilian governor of Imo State left office. Successive administrations abandoned the project. Today, that edifice stands as a monument in Udenwa's honour and would remain a reference point for generations to come.
Again the establishment of the Imo State University Teaching Hospital that has been reputed to be comparable only to the National Hospital in Abuja is another edifice. The machineries and equipment that have been brought for the equipping of the hospital still remain a marvel in the eyes of the people. Today, thousands of cases that would ordinarily have been referred abroad or to any of the existing teaching and specialist hospitals are now taken care of there. The Endocrine equipment that enables medical doctors detect ailments and perform operations without cutting a sing flesh is still a wonder to those who have had their illnesses taken care with the machine.
Not too long age the governor formally launched the Emergency Ambulance Services, the first of its kind anywhere in the country. The major aim of the programme is to ensure that ambulance services are made available to accident victims as soon as it occurs in such a way that the victims begin to receive first aid treatment right from the scene of the incident before getting to the nearest hospital. Already, six ambulance centres have been opened at strategic spots on the highways of Owerri. Orlu and Okigwe and these centres are equipped with telephone lines that could be reached at any time of the day or night for emergency ambulance services. This programme that has become the hallmark of American project towards reducing the loss of lives on the highways has commenced operations and is gradually reducing deaths on the highways.
There is also the Redemption Songhai Farm at Nekede in Owerri West local government that has practically reduced the prices of edible commodities in the state. This farm plays dual roles. Firstly, it has been used as a training ground for thousands of Imolites with an eye for agriculture. To this end the 27 local government areas have sent not less than 1000 trainees in various aspects of farming that in turn after the course return to their local government areas to also train others interested in the programme. Secondly, the farm has also ensured that some of the commodities that ordinarily would come from other parts of the country are grown there and sent into the markets at subsidized rates. This is why food items like chicken, eggs. vegetables, carrot, pineapples, cucumber, lettuce. snail, plantain, cassava have become regular feature on the table of the citizenry.
The issue of roads and electricity is better eye marked than ear marked.. There is no single district in the state that has not witnessed tremendous uplift in the area of roads and not just mere construction but asphalt that has been estimated to last several years from now. The capital city of Owerri could be said to have witnessed a large share of the roads as more networks have been opened to support the already existing ones. To this end, someone going to Port Harcourt from the Imo Airport need not venture into the capital city but can swerve off the Polytechnic Road and join the Port Harcourt High way at Obinze through the ,Federal University of Technology. Those already in the city and moving towards the Federal Secretariat can also turn off at the West End through Imo Concord and drive straight to Port Harcourt Road. Perhaps if the contractor handling the Nekede/ Emii/ Emekuku/Uratta road had done his job according to specifications and within the time frame, vehicles would have been able to divert from Nekede junction and join the Owerri/Okigwe road without venturing into the capital city. All these were done with a view to reducing the heavy traffic jam that has become a regular feature in the city today. What one may quickly add is that these hold ups have come into been because business is booming against the background of the convivial environment made possible by the government of the day.
This is why the quest to become the next president of the nation is not an ambition aimed at ego boosting but a call to service of his fatherland by one -who has completely regained the years of the locust in his state. Nobody would have believed that such a tremendous transformation was possible in a state like Imo taking into cognizance the rots and upheavals that had become her lot since the days of the military dictatorship. Udenwa, to those who know him well is not a man given to frivolities. He would not say what he cannot do and would not do what he has assured the people he would do. This is exactly what has set him apart from the pack that are seeking the mandate of the people. And this is the greatest asset with which he would be approaching the teeming population of the country asking for an opportunity to replicate the developmental institutions that he had already put in place in Imo state.
But above all, no one would deny. him the fact that in spite of several provocations and mudslinging he has remained focused on the project of not only sustaining the present democratic experiment but also making life better for the people of Imo.
It may be said that there exist political differences among the stakeholders in the state especially since the commencement of power struggle for 2007. However much as these struggles cannot be said not to exist the fact remains that the governor has been able to contain all to the extent that the breakdown of law and order has not occurred nor has any politically motivated killing taken place. If at the end of the day such becomes the case, it would be glaringly clear that Udenwa has no hand in it. Without doubt some of the skirmishes are the brainchild of some of individuals with the intent of over turning the peace in Imo in order to paint the state and the governor black. Aware of this, he has maintained his position that power is not the exclusive preserve of any individual or group but that of God alone.

Original_Search_Title: Japanese Govt Donates ICT Gadgets Worth $41,000 to LG
Headline: Nigeria: Japanese Govt Donates ICT Gadgets Worth $41,000 to LG
Body_Text:
Warri — THE Japanese Government, yesterday, at Ogbe-Ijoh, headquarters of Warri South- West Local Government Council of Delta State, donated Information and Communication Technology (ICT) gadgets worth over 41,000 United States Dollars to the council to assist the Women Economic Empowerment Programme of the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) in the state.
The programme meant to principally support women entrepreneurship and technical skills in selected communities provides relevant and appropriate equipment such as farm implements, cassava and palm oil processing, palm-kernel cracking machines, biogas etc to women groups is being organised in Akwa-Ibom, Delta and Rivers States in Nigeria.
The Japanese Human Security Fund is funding the pilot project with the United Nations Development Fund for Women (UNIFEM) as the implementing agency working in collaboration with the Afro Centre for Development, Peace and Justice (AFRODEP), a non-governmental organisation to facilitate implementation of the pilot project in the council area.
According to the programme specialist of Unifem, Mrs. Tolu Lewis-Tamoka who presented the ICT gadgets including complete computer system, screen projectors, 5KVA power generating sets, books on entrepreneurship amongst others to the council chairman, Mr. David Tonwe, represented by his vice, Mr. Daniel Akada, their assistance is "informed by the subordinate status of women and girls in the areas of economics, education, health and politics", stressing that giving them access to the use of ICT equipment through training would take them and their families out of poverty level.
About 40 women drawn from the 20 wards in the council area have been undergoing training in variou222s fields since February this year.
The council boss expressed his appreciation to the Japanese government for the equipment, saying that they would be deplored in Women Development Centre of the council to enhance skill acquisition and capacity building.

Original_Search_Title: Meat Price Up
Headline: Uganda: Meat Price Up
Body_Text:
Kampala — BEEF, chicken and goat meat prices have gone up due to low supply, a survey has shown.
A kilogramme of beef costs sh3,000 from sh2,700, while goat meat goes for sh4,000 from sh3,500. Chicken costs between sh6,000 and sh15,000, depending on the size.
Musa Tamale, a butcher at Nakasero Market, said, "There is low supply of cattle to Kampala markets and we are yet to establish the reasons. This has forced prices to go up."
The increase in the price of chicken was attributed to the high costs of grinding maize feeds and low supply due to the fear of bird flu.
Food prices remained stable as supplies of matooke and vegetables were maintained.
A kilogramme of maize flour remained at sh900, millet flour sh1,500 and cassava flour sh600.
Beans, rice and maize remained at sh1,000, sh1,500 and sh400 a kilogramme respectively.
Traders said they expect plenty of supplies mid December when the rains decrease and there is enough sunshine.

Original_Search_Title: 'Integrate Concept of Farmer Study Groups'
Headline: Zimbabwe: 'Integrate Concept of Farmer Study Groups'
Body_Text:
Harare — GOVERNMENT has been urged to integrate and institutionalise the concept of farmer study groups and field schools as an alternative agriculture extension method to empower newly-resettled and smallholder farmers.
Apart from encouraging the active involvement of farmers, the methods tap into existing knowledge and the experience in farming among farmers, participants at a recent conference on farmer study group initiatives said.
The conference was attended by representatives of farmer organisations, extension service providers and donors spearheading initiatives to empower smallholder farmers from Tanzania, Kenya, Uganda, Mozambique, Zambia, Sweden, the Netherlands, Madagascar and Zimbabwe, among others.
Participants said these methods of farmer-driven study offered effective and efficient extension services and sharing of community-based knowledge, especially in the face of attendant shortages of extension workers.
Zimbabwe has been losing experienced extension workers to other countries in the region.
Farmer groups of five to 15 in a locality normally use study books among themselves with guidelines on sustainable land use and ways of growing crops like cassava.
They only consult qualified extension staff on a subject they fail to understand or agree on.
The initiative is being implemented at a low scale in several countries in eastern and southern Africa and borrows from experiences in south-east Asia where the concept has been successful.
"There is need for more emphasis on the expansion of the programme to cover most areas in our countries because it gives attention to the needs of farmers at a particular locality.
It also imparts and provides valuable information to farmers at a pace they choose," said James Okoth of Uganda.
He said current extension services do not support the farmer up to the time they sell their produce.
Other participants said the methods cut on resources as they are applied in groups who meet regularly.
Mr Davison Masendeke, an official in the Department of Agricultural Research and Extension Services, said study groups and farmer field schools were more practical as farmers would learn by doing.
"This methodology is more critical if the gains of the land reform are to be realised because more people are now in need of extension services. This also means that farmers are not lectured on how to farm," said Mr Masendeke.
The conference, held in Kadoma, sought to find ways of enhancing farm production and, thus, improve livelihoods among smallholder farmers.
Participants came up with draft action plans on the institutionalisation of farmer study groups and farmer field schools in their countries.

Original_Search_Title: Surviving With a Fragile Peace
Headline: Uganda: Surviving With a Fragile Peace
Body_Text:
On the rough and bumpy ride from Gulu town to Anaka trading centre about 35kms west of Gulu town, the road is full of signs of life.
Trucks laden with matooke from Paidha in Nebbi district head in the opposite direction to town. Young men energetically peddle bicycles loaded with merchandise, beads of sweat dripping from their faces. People can be seen by the roadside, working in their green fields of groundnuts, beans and cassava. There is even a lone UPDF soldier in his garb walking along the road, not with a gun but a hoe!
Tilling the land: IDPs have used the relative peace in the region to engage in more farming to increase on their food supplies reducing their dependence on the World Food Programme
For the past year, the north has seen the kind of peace that it hadn't had for long.
The United Nations head of Humanitarian Affairs, Jean Egeland was recently quoted as saying the security situation in the region is the best in the last 10 years.
"For the moment, we are able to sneak to our gardens and farm on our land. We don't know for how long it will be but we are praying that it stays that way," said Cosmas Okello as he clutched onto a crucifix dangling from his neck, hands fixed on his bicycle that is his mode of transport to and from the garden.
Hopeful
"If it continues like this, it won't be necessary for us to wait for the few mugs of the white man's food," he added in reference to the World Food Programme (WFP) that has been responsible for feeding nearly two million people holed up in the camps during the worst moments of fighting between rebels and government forces.
In Anaka IDP itself, on a bright Saturday afternoon, dozens of kids are playing soccer on a barren ground under a huge leafy tree.
A few metres a way from them, Ben Obwot and his wife Betty Akot are seated under a rough shade that he once used as a kiosk when the security situation was so bad.
"The only way I could get some money then was to open up this kiosk. But now it serves as a shade that I use when peeling my cassava from the garden. Things have somehow improved. You can even look at the kids playing freely. The only thing is we don't know how the talks in Juba will end."
On this particular day, he was preparing his cobs of maize, freshly harvested from the garden for drying.
"This is my own maize that I grew because of this smell of peace that we are currently sniffing. I will use some of it to feed my family, some I will sell to buy school uniforms for my children."
As the government and the LRA talk about ways of peacefully resolving the conflict, locals in the region who for 20 years now have been living in dingy and stuffy IDPs with barely enough to eat are taking advantage of the relative peace to increase on their food production.
Margaret Odora the L5 Councillor says "It's almost a year now that we have been having some peace. People have been able to farm on their land and get their own food. They now don't have to solely depend on World Food Programme food donations."
Even as they engage in food production, the IDPs have began facing challenges like any other ordinary peasant farmers in the country. For example, what was once the Anaka sub-county district agriculture extension office is now somebody's residence.
From the forest: Cosmas Okello, a resident of Anaka IDP camp on his way back to the camp from the garden.
WORKING HARD: Ben Obwot and Betty Akot working on maize from their garden.
"We want government to start using that house. So they can get us tractors to rent at cheaper prices and teach us better farming methods," said LC1 Chairman, Otto Ojumbe.
Anaka IDP camp and its environs neighbour Kabalega National Park. At the moment, residents and their leaders say one of the biggest problems they are facing is from a herd of elephants from the park that are roaming the county, uprooting trees and destroying their gardens.
Some of the trees reportedly broken by the marauding elephants can be seen along the road from Gulu town to Anaka trading centre.
According to Gertrude Odora, the elephants recently attacked a man riding to town and smashed his bicycle. Luckily, he escaped unhurt.
"The issue of the roaming elephants is of great concern among the farmers in Nwoya County. I have already raised the matter with the Uganda Wild Life Authority. I held a meeting with the management, they were very positive and have promised to act on the animals but they should do it quickly before people start hunting them down because the gardens they are destroying are the people's source of livelihood," said MP Simon Oyet.
He says because of improved farming among the IDP's, he has started a project in which the IDPs will get 3,000 bicycles at a fairly cheap price of Shs50,000 each to ease the transportation of their farm products from their gardens to the camps or for sell in town. Presently, a new bicycle costs between Shs80,000 and Shs130,000 depending on the type.
"150 bicycles have already been given out to various farmers in the county. In the next three years, the plan is to give out 3,000 bicycles that I hope will help a lot in boosting local production because it's the most important means of transport in the villages," added the MP.
For the moment, the IDPs may be focused on increasing their food supplies and tilling their land but their ears remain glued to the radio, always eager for the latest from Juba, the Southern Sudan capital where their hopes now hang.

Original_Search_Title: The Stranded Village
Headline: Nigeria: The Stranded Village
Body_Text:
This is a story about a village that got left behind while the rest of the world moved on. In order not to give offence to anyone, alive or dead, I have presented it as a travel tale.
Forty-eight years ago, I became a member of a group in Lagos that was named "The Contemporary Society". It functioned as a sort of standing symposium, drawing its topics, as you may have guessed, from matters related to contemporary affairs.
When the Society, as a change from its usual fare of debates and discussions, decided to go to Badagry for a field study, I volunteered to make a reconnaissance trip to the town. My job was to assess the conditions there, and recommend places of interest that were worth visiting. I was also to check out the route and report on travelling conditions. Badagry, in those days, seemed to be aeons away from Lagos, and getting there was not a piece of cake (and still isn't, for people who have to get past those places called Iyana-Iba, Volkswagen, and Okokomaiko, along the Lagos-Badagry expressway).
The expressway did not exist then, of course, and a relatively isolated Badagry was of interest only to its inhabitants, historians and a clutch of tourists. Badagry had for years been lulled into slumber by its reputation as a former slave port as well as its fame as the place where the seeds of Christianity were first sown in Nigeria.
As it turned out, the real value of my reconnaissance visit lay in what I had to say about the difficulty of getting there. In those pre-expressway days, the only way to get to Badagry from Lagos was by a road that passed through Agege, Ota and Ado, and terminated at Badagry. The distance was about 96 kilometres, and it took about three hours by car.
I set out from Lagos on a Sunday morning. The first part of the journey was pleasant, made even more so by Radio Brazzaville which, at that time, was probably the most listened to African broadcasting station within transmitting distance of Nigeria. You would be right to think that all that Radio Brazzaville existed for was to broadcast the vibrant music of a group known as "O.K. Jazz". The group could not have been better named, for the sound it sent over the airwaves was definitely okay.
When I was about half way to Badagry on that Sunday, what was supposed to be an all season Trunk B road deteriorated to stretches of fine dust that caught the wheels of my car and denied them traction.
In theory, what you do when the wheels of your car get stuck in sand is to "rock" the car, putting it alternately in forward and reverse gear and making small rocking motions until, presumably, the sand under the wheels gets sufficiently compacted to give the treads of the tyres some purchase. That is the theory; it sometimes doesn't work that way.
That Sunday afternoon I learnt about the folly of "rocking" a car to the music of O.K. Jazz. All that happened was that the wheels sank even deeper into the sand with every pressure I applied to the throttle and, soon, I was in it up to my hubcap.
Lesson Two: If your car is stuck in sand, and rocking it proves to be futile, get out and go and get some help in pushing or digging the car out. This only works, of course, if help is nearby. Fortunately, I had just driven past a cluster of houses before I got caught in the sand, so I now made my way back there, hoping to be lucky.
The village was in cassava country, and like the other villages in the area it was inhabited by cassava farmers. A Trunk B road ran through the village, on the strength of which I expected it to have that ultimate symbol of rural change and progress, a one-pump petrol station. It didn't have one, possibly because the cassava business was then not thriving.
The first man to whom I spoke was a palm wine tapper, something I deduced entirely from the fact that he was dressed like one, and was standing at the door of a shed where palm wine was being sold from a huge earthen pot that was half-buried in the ground to keep its content cool. After I told him about my problem, he was good enough to call together six brawny lads who pushed my car on to firmer ground, enabling me to continue my journey.
When I finally got back to Lagos and presented my report at the next meeting of the Contemporary Society, among the things I listed as the tourist attractions on offer were a few unremarkable old chains that were said to date back to the days of the slave trade. I also mentioned another land mark, the very first two-storey house to be built anywhere in Nigeria. I don't know what the house now looks like, but when I saw it in 1958 it was, to put it mildly, certainly looking its age. I also went out of my way in my report to mention a creaky, wind-powered water pump that, though not officially regarded as a tourist attraction, deserved to be listed as one. It was still in use, and for more than five minutes I stood there, watching and listening as it creaked and groaned and sighed while spewing water into waiting buckets. Every community should have one.
Two weeks after my reconnaissance visit, the other members of the Contemporary Society made the trip to Badagry for the field study, but I didn't go with them; I had had enough of that "all season" Trunk B road to last me for a while.
But "a while" doesn't last forever. Years later, when I began to raise a family, one of the ways I hit upon for entertaining my children was to fill a picnic hamper with food and pack all of us into the car for a day trip to Badagry. On one occasion I made a brief stop at the village outside which, years before, my car's wheels had been caught in the sand. The cassava business must have been thriving since my earlier visit. More houses had been built, there was now a small open-air market, and a church was under construction.
The cassava business seemed to have moved downstream to gari making, and two or three women were sitting under a tree, peeling cassava, while another was grating the cassava. To show my gratitude for the help I had received from the villagers on my previous visit I was tempted to buy a bottle of palm wine, but a cholera epidemic was raging in Lagos around that time, and not wishing to take any risks I bought a bunch of bananas instead, and we moved on.
You may ask what all this has to do with missing out on progress and development. Well, more than four decades after that reconnaissance visit, I passed that way again. The only sign that I could see of any change was a row of sturdy wooden poles standing knee-deep in tall grass, holding up sagging power lines that proclaimed the emergence of the village from the Dark Age, so to speak. NEPA had arrived, and the villagers, like others in all the villagers in that area, were unquestioningly optimistic that things could only get better. As proof, several rusted gabled roofs had television aerials attached to them, each one a crude device made from discarded fluorescent light tubes fixed in place like a cross, giving the houses an appearance of sanctity. I wondered if there was much point in owning a television set, even if it is only black and white, when your area is invariably the first to be plunged into darkness whenever NEPA was "load shedding".
The main industries remained palm wine tapping and gari making, with the gari exported for sale at the rotating weekly markets in the district.
Standing outside one of the houses (I never found out whose it was) was the rusting carcass of a car that had long been stripped of everything that was of any value. About a dozen lizards, steeped in lethargy and looking like miniature dragons, clung to the unplastered walls of the house outside which the remnant of the car stood. Not far from a communal well a goat dozed in the sun, and I had to look a second time to convince myself that what I saw basking atop the goat's head was a lizard. The lizard and the goat appeared to be good friends.
It is not difficult to guess what had happened. When the expressway was built, making the journey from Lagos to Badagry easier and shorter, the village lost its relevance. The jungle reclaimed much of the old "all season" trunk road, and what remained of its tarred surface became useful only for drying cassava.
A fifteen kilometre "bush" road now connects the village with the expressway. This bush road winds its way through tall grass, and frequently dissolves into swamp. The village, it says all too clearly, is going nowhere. It has become stranded because the expressway bypassed it, missing it by just eight kilometers.

Original_Search_Title: Military Acts to Help Flood Victims
Headline: Kenya: Military Acts to Help Flood Victims
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It was a sea of hopeful smiles yesterday as military personnel stepped in to help residents of the Coast afflicted by floods.
Sadly, only 3,550 Msambweni residents received relief food supplies from the Kenya Cereals and Produce Board and the Kenya Red Cross Society.
However, this was literally a mere drop in the ocean as the relief supplies could not reach more than 200,000 residents still marooned in other parts of the province.
Only 1,400 residents managed to swim to higher grounds at Jego Primary School, Vanga, while another 600 took shelter at Chiuni Primary School.
Another 550 were at Kidomaya Primary School and 1,000 at Majoreni - all eagerly awaiting help.
The relief supplies contained 250 bags of maize weighing 50kg each, four bags of legumes each weighing 50kg and three boxes of vegetable oil weighing 22kg.
The Red Cross also supplied 100 boxes, each containing five plates, five cups, five spoons, one knife and two sufurias with lids.
According to the Clinical Officer Chrispine Odhiambo and Nursing Officer Eddah Dzame, they also carried anti-malarials and other drugs.
Despite obvious signs of fatigue, the old, the middle-aged, young mothers with babies strapped on their backs and school children gathered their last strength in a moment of hope as they ran, stumbled and crawled towards the military helicopter commandeered by Major Chrispine Odhiambo.
As the 11-seater helicopter touched the ground at Jego Primary School and Ngozi Girls Secondary School in Vanga Location, 20km from the Tanzania border at 8.30am, residents hustled and bustled in excitement.
In the dormitories that have been temporarily converted into a home for many, mothers and children slept on the cold floor with no mats, bedding or mosquito nets.
One mother quietly suckled her four-day old baby on the cold floor.
Mbeyu Mashaka, 35, a mother of eight told The Standard that she was fast asleep six days ago when she woke up at midnight to find the floor flooded.
