"I heard some of my children coughing and screaming as they swallowed the water and started sinking. It was a nightmare. I screamed and carried all my eight children as I called out to neighbours to help. Everyone was trying to save their own lives.
"I had to make a quick choice between my children and the chicken. I did not wait. I ran off and watched from a distance as my four beloved chicken and their 10 chicks were washed away. I consoled myself, 'Mbeyu, you can get four other chicken but not eight other children.'"
A farmer, Mr Bakari Mko, 48, narrated how he carried his 80-year-old mother, Ms Naujingo Mweni, on his shoulders and swam with her against the floods.
"The rains pounded with a rage I had never seen before. I asked myself, 'My God, what have we done wrong?' I have lost all my crops - cassava, maize everything. But thank God my mother is safe."
The Red Cross officer in charge of the Regional Disaster Preparedness and Response centre in Mombasa, Mr Mahdi Mohammed, acknowledged that the cargo is too little but explained that the aircraft could only land on safe areas at the moment.
The marooned areas are swampy and many are likely to sink if they attempted crossing, he said.
"Our head office has commissioned four M6 special swamp trucks that were used in the Tsunami hit areas. This should allow us to access the worst hit areas," he said.
Meanwhile, road engineers from Nairobi yesterday began the assessment of the extensively damaged roads and bridges in Coast Province.
A Ministry official, Mr Michael Kamau, who is leading a team of 15 road engineers, said the latest flush floods had tripled the cost of infrastructure repair.
Addressing a joint press conference with the Coast Provincial Commissioner Mr Ernest Munyi in Mombasa, Kamau said emergency repairs would be made especially to restore links between Kenya and Tanzania.
The PC said helicopters from the Kenya Army and Air Force were airlifting relief food and medical supplies to Vanga and other parts of Kwale district where thousands of people were marooned or displaced by floods.
"The Government is responding to the effects of the flush floods and we will ensure there is no hunger-related death," Munyi said.

Original_Search_Title: Anglogold Ashanti Donates Oil Palm And Corn Mill
Headline: Ghana: Anglogold Ashanti Donates Oil Palm And Corn Mill
Body_Text:
The Executive Officer of Anglogold Ashanti, Africa Open Pit Mines, Mr. Fritz Neethling, has said that the company will continue to form partnership with its host communities, share their traditions, environment and values, adding that, 'we want the communities to be better off for Anglogold Ashanti having been there'.
He was speaking at the commissioning of a ¢138 million processing plant for oil palm fruit, kennel, cassava and corn to boost its Alternative Livelihood Programmes in the communities within its operational area at Adieyie near Tarkwa last week.
The Executive Officer said that he believes the provision of the facilities is in the right direction and hoped that this would go a long way to enhance the living conditions of members of the communities.
The cutting of the tape for the commissioning of the plant which included a boiler and a digester was done by Mrs. Marilize Kruger, Anglogold Ashanti, Corporate official. She said that it was a delightful moment to donate such a facility that would impact positively on the life of the people in the host communities.
The Managing Director of Iduapriem Mine, Mr. David Renner, said it was gratifying to note that the 'Hand-in-Hand' seed that was sowed a year ago is now bearing fruits for the benefit of the communities.
He noted that the mill will add value to the oil palm fruits, kennel, cassava and corn that are produced in greater quantities, make life better and that should reflect in the economic empowerment of the beneficiary communities. 'The staff from our Safety Department would support with training and orientation of the operators to ensure that life is protected at the plant site', he added.
He charged beneficiaries to take ownership of the mill and ensure proper maintenance to enable the facility to lasts for the benefit of future generation.
The Tarkwa Programme Manager of Opportunities Industrialisation Centres International (OICI), the NGO contracted by the Mine for the Alternative Livelihood Programme, Mr. Henry Yankson, said he and his staff will do whatever it takes within their capacity to ensure the success and sustainability of the 'Hand-in-Hand' Programme. This, he said, will improve the quality of life and income generating opportunities for the less advantaged youth and adults, in the communities in the Mine's concession.
The Odikro of Adieyie community, Nana Sampson Cudjoe, praised Anglogold Ashanti for the 'Hand-in-Hand Programme and especially the facility. He said it will relieve members of the community from travelling about 15 kilometres to Tarkwa to grind corn, cassava and process palm fruits and kennel.

Original_Search_Title: Rural Women Dazzle Audience with Product at Exhibition
Headline: Nigeria: Rural Women Dazzle Audience with Product at Exhibition
Body_Text:
Rural women from Moro local government area of Kwara state yesterday amazed guests with their product at an exhibition organised by the Raw Materials Research and Development Council in Abuja.
The exhibition is meant to showcase finished products from Nigerian raw materials.
The women, who are the Araromi women cooperative society, brought a moisturizer cream called 'Shea butter'.
The cream which has a rich content of Stearic Oleic acids and natural Vitamin E is meant to smoothen skin.
The cream which sells for N200 is produced by the rural women of Moro LGA, who used an RMRDC, upgraded indigenous technology that ensures Ginnamic acid (responsible for the healing power of Shea butter.
The project supervisor, Mrs. Odegide explained to City News reporter how they helped one of the participants with crushing and yielding machines in the processing of shea butter which is mainly done by women to upgrade their functions. She said the material for making shea butter is a tree found in the bush. "The tree which is used for shea butter is a free gift of nature and is easy to come by, it yields for about 25 to 50 years" she said.
She also told City News that the finished products are used by many countries in Europe because it is a natural product. "Shea butter can be used for the production of soap, food, cosmetics and other manufactured products" she said.
City News visited the Niji-Lukas stand where one of their engineers, Engineer Akin Agbokoye explained how their flash drier machine can be used for the processing of cassava products into starch and flour.
Also present at the exhibition was manufactures of leather products such as sandals and bags, soap and ceramic materials.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava, Sorghum to Drive Agrobusiness
Headline: Kenya: Cassava, Sorghum to Drive Agrobusiness
Body_Text:
Some of East Africa's widely grown crops are among the commodities that will run agro-business in the 21st century.
Stakeholders in the agricultural sector were told on November 1 that sorghum, cassava, soy beans, palm oil and Jathropha curcas, are the five crops that will run agri-business this century.
The remarks were made by Mr. Peter Kegode, an agrobusiness specialist in Kenya, during a one-day seminar organised b y the Nairobi Stock Exchange (NSE) to familiarise stakeholders in agri and agro-processing sectors on the financing opportunities available through the stock market.
The five crops, Kegode said, have a wider product map with a potential for the processing of non-food, animal and human food products.
Jathropha for instance, is currently used in India to run all the country's trains. The initial imports of Jathropha by India were solely sourced from Kenya. Its production in Kenya has however dwindled over time.
India's train network has the largest number of passengers per year in the world.
Jathropha, also known as physic nut, is a crop that can be used in the production of bio-fuel that can be used in running a wide range of engines. After residual decantation and filtration, pure Jathropha oil is used as a suitable fossil diesel substitute.
The press-cake that is an extraction byproduct is used as a high-grade organic fertiliser.
Jathropha is also increasingly being recognized as a reforestation crop in tropical countries. The crop thrives on degraded soils and because of its drought resistance it can play a crucial role in combating desertification.
Jathropha is not eaten by animals and therefore can be used as a fence to protect food crops. Its seeds contain about 35% oil. Experts say that depending on seed yields, a hectare has a potential to produce up to 2200kg of oil. Its raw oil can be obtained by simple cold pressing of the seeds.
Ironically some of these crops, especially sorghum and cassava, are easily available in the region but remain under-utilised. In countries such as Uganda where cassava is widely grown, it remains largely used as a food crop with limited or no industrial use derived from it.
Speakers and participants in the seminar underscored the urgent need for a paradigm shift in the agricultural policy so as to link increased crop production to optimum utilisation of the potential benefits. Participants said there was so much emphasis on increased production without a focus on the business end in crop production.
Research institutions such as the Kenya Agricultural Research Institute (KARI) were urged to have a balanced policy on research on increased yields.

Original_Search_Title: Ana Marks 25th Anniversary in Grand Style
Headline: Nigeria: Ana Marks 25th Anniversary in Grand Style
Body_Text:
Yenagoa — The celebration of the 25th anniversary of the Association of Nigerian Authors (ANA) at the Gloryland Cultural Centre in Yenagoa the Bayelsa State capital can be described as a reasonable success. Especially looking at the minute human and financial resources available for the Local Organising Committee of the anniversary celebration. However that did not deter the determination with which the committee and other members of ANA Bayelsa, worked to realize their objective. This objective was the successful organization of a befitting anniversary for ANA.
The opening and closing ceremonies were never short of bigwigs in the literary landscape of Nigeria which included the grand old child of the river Nun, Pa Gabriel Okara and Elechi Amadi, our concubine who agreed to share his muse-wife with us.
Without doubt it was indeed a daunting task to have given the hosting rights of the silver jubilee to a young state and indeed a young ANA like Bayelsa. An ANA that is only 11-writer strong. The task was enormous but accomplishable. So it is with heart that I say that they have done marvelously well.
The ultra-modern theatre hall of the Gloryland Cultural Centre was the joker of the organizers. It was near perfect. There wouldn't have been a better place in the whole of Bayelsa that would have comfortably seated and hosted an event of this magnitude and importance. The proportionately large stage was well lighted with shades of flourishing colours and the surface textured to help grasp the feet of actors and dancers alike. The proximity of the stage to the audience was only a fine line of red carpet and a sitting level elevation which could disappear immediately performance was in progress. Although the echo of the dome was a little damp but it was able to carry the voices on stage with an acceptable wavelength. No wonder why the convention play Drums of the Delta held the attention of the audience with music and drums movement and other choreographic modulation in confluence of the poignancy of stage direction and transition, costume and the projection and integration of chorus.
The command performance of Drums of the Delta by Ben Binebei stewed the senses of the informed audience in the social realism of the deltaic regions of Nigeria: The disagreements between the Old and the New; the suspicion existing between community leaders and the generality of their communities. On the whole, the aroma of distrust, mistrust and internal wrangling of destructive tendencies disfigured all groups alike and pervaded the air and it came to an ultimate climax when the leader of the youths is killed and a tremulous reconciliation was reached.
One of my note is that, the gallery of the Gloryland Cultural Centre can seat a capacity crowd without groaning and also wherever any spectator seats to watch the stage is never inconveniencing. This assertion was tested when the play Drums of the Delta lost heat, strength and intensity both physical and spiritual at the middle of the performance. It became too long and dragging and action was slack. The plot became overstretched and windy. Nonetheless, the actors picked up what was left and gave an impressive dénouement. And all these while, the audience was alert on their comfortable folding seats.
The theme of the silver jubilee "literature and history ANA and the development of Nigerian Literature" was aptly spelt out by the keynote address while the sub-themes which were discussed by the seminar papers presented expressed the ardent desires for the way forward for the creative and the ideological direction of Nigerian writing in general.
Gloryland also hosted the ANA annual business meeting where ANA progress report was given and discussed. The trend of discussion showed that the current national executives led by Dr. Wale Okediran has a lot of followership and trust. The representative of the state chapters also affirmed their confidence in the executives since they suggest that it has been transparent enough in all its dealings with the state chapters. It was there that the Imo State Chapter was given the hosting rights for the 26th edition of the convention come next year.
Excursions were planned for Twon Brass Agip Installation, Ox Bow lake and the Niger Delta University. But they did not take place because the trips coincided with the PDP State Convention which took place through out the federation. That Bayelsan afternoon was going to be a little restive and so it was. That also necessitated the last minute cancellation of the venue for the award nite which was originally scheduled for the state house Creek Haven, Yenagoa. The presence of this gubernatorial declaration also made accommodation difficult for ANA members. Approximately, the governor of Bayelsa State, his Excellency Dr. Goodluck Jonathan and the Secretary to the State government Amb. Bolade Igali, all apologized on behalf of the state and the LOC.
In other words, accommodation was adequately applied for until the politicians came to town and made the hoteliers change their minds. They must have been offered something much more than ANA Bayelsa could ever offer and that left the LOC gaping. This development may not be unconnected with the fact that the LOC could not get the N7 million approved by the government for the event as at Saturday, 4th November 2006. But for the show to go on, the committee had to source for loans from financial institutions which amounted to a little over N4 million. Here I say that maybe the event would have fared better that it did if all the resources were available. This cap-in-hand connection ANA has with the government was one of the most regretted issues discussed in the business meeting. But it is true that the organization runs on charity.
The prizes for the awards and all other financial needs are met by the members of the association, government parastatals, private companies and well meaning individuals. For example the three additional prizes for this year's awards namely Neco Teen Author Prize (prose), Mazariyya Teen Author Prize (poetry) and Senator Ken Nnamani Prize for Igbo literature are from individuals and parastatals. These and most of the awards of the 14 categories of ANA literary awards are without endowment and this means that when the donor leaves office, the awards die with their financial absence.
Ultimately,the award nite was also hosted by the main gallery of the Gloryland Cultural Centre. The venue helped the stars to glitter the more. The arena was immensely beautified by the excellent combination of light and decoration was made with colourful and shimmering satin and chiffon. Other shapes were made with ribbon dressing and balloons of different shapes and sizes made there opulent presence and the occasional bust from them gave a startling beauty to the ears. By the time the make up of Gloryland was done. It could easily pass for the banquet hall of Creek Haven.
Together with good food and choice drinks, the Bayelsan rib-cracker Mike Ogbolosinger spiced the night with his social realistic jokes which left guests laughing with contentment.
The publishing companies were ably represented but my wish was that they should be more frequent at ANA conventions. Repesented were Kraft Books press, Cassava Republic press and Treasure books. Of these three, Kraft books enjoyed a lot of limelight as an average number of awards were carted away by writers from their stable.
Out of the 14 categories only one did not have any awardee. It is the chevron prose prize for environmental issues. I hope writers will take up creative writing towards that direction.
Also ANA fellowship award was extended to all the past presidents of ANA and the late Major-General Mamman Vasta, although not a past president, but had done commendably well for ANA in time past. Again, all the partners of ANA and supporters of the literary Arts in Nigeria were honoured with ANA Jubilee awards.
There also was the presentation of a book on 70 contemporary Nigerian poets edited by Nduka Otiono, the literary editor of New Age Newspapers and Odoh Diego Okenyodo, the literary editor of the Weekly Trust Newspapers.
The award winning books include Vincent Egbuson's Womandela; Ahmed Yerima's Hardground; Unoma Nguemo Azuah's Sky High Flames; Uche Peter Umez's Sam and the Wallet; Olabisi Are's Home of the Braves; Odili Ujubuonu's Pregnancy of the gods; Ifeanyi Ajaegbo's Dead men walking and other stories; Tubal Cain's Mystery in Our Stream; Ademola Dasylva's Songs of Adamolugbe.
The event looked from Thursday 2nd November to Sunday 5th November 2006.

Original_Search_Title: In Memory of Uganda's Forgotten Heroes
Headline: Uganda: In Memory of Uganda's Forgotten Heroes
Body_Text:
Kampala — Mzee Aurelio Vuje, whose photographic mind earned him the nickname 'camera', is also for the same reason called kamaera, the Madi word for 'it remembers me.' The octogenarian is truly entitled to such names since nothing can be fitting to describe his memory.
For everything Vuje did, there was a substantive reason and when he rummaged through his metallic box that morning, the village knew he was up to something.
He fished out a khaki pair of shorts and a shirt; that priceless memoir of his soldierly escapades, washed it carefully and ironed it in starchy water left from boiled cassava tubers. He shined his army boots and cleared his bugle as he narrated how they - as veterans - introduced the idea of independence in Uganda and "Suku-leke or women's knickers in the area. "Suku-leke actually means sooka oleke or first wait, a cry by the Baganda women who wore knickers as they restrained the impatient male veterans from the north who were not used to knickers," Vuje said.
Next morning, Vuje's trumpet pierced through the village as he marched to the town's central flag post in front of what was then the office of the colonial district commissioner.
He sang songs of victory in praise of Britain and the allied forces and damnation to Japan and Germany. To the inquisitive crowd of children and a few adults, Vuje talked of the great world war and how Africans helped in winning it. He amused the crowd when he said that by the end of the Second World War, a wasted effort of his resulted into a delicately-moulded baby girl of his, born to a Burmese woman.
Full of knowledge, generosity and humility, Vuje, one of the few surviving ex-service men of the Kings African Rifle (KAR) or Keeya, was celebrating the memory of his colleagues in the ceremony of the 'Three Elevens', when at the 11th hour of the night of the 11th day of the 11th month of the year 1918, the armistice to end the First World War was signed. Since then, that day has been marked as the armistice or Veterans' Day in honour of those who not only died in the two world wars, but also diligently served in the armies of their respective nations to bring honour and dignity to mankind.
In the Commonwealth, it is a day of commending the patriotism of the veterans in serving their motherland.
Politicians all over the world know very well that veterans form a very strong social and political lobby, capable of tilting electoral fortunes. The veterans are regarded with dignity and some countries assign a ministry to their welfare.
Vuje is one of the few surviving Ugandan veterans of the World War who rendered service to the British cause without any terminal benefits.
In Uganda as in other parts of the Commonwealth, come November though not necessarily at the eleventh hour of the eleventh day, the British High Commissioner, military attaches from Commonwealth countries and officials representing the army and the Police will turn up at the 11, 11, 11 celebrations at the memorial tomb at the Constitutional Square, where a handful of the surviving ex-service men will routinely ask for their terminal benefits before retiring for bottles of soda and chicken, as the memory of the veterans fades away.
While Britain is eternally debted to the veterans for fighting alongside them, Ugandans owe much to them for bringing the idea of struggling for independence.
In his book, The Kings African Rifle, Lt. Col. Moyse Bartlett studied the military history of East and Central Africa where KAR served in the pacification and prosecution of the two major wars in which the Uganda Rifle was outstanding.
Formally constituted on January 1, 1902, the Uganda Rifles had been trained and raised long before that by chartered companies like the Imperial British East African Company of Capt. Frederick Lugard and Emin Pasha's troops in the north.
Having great soldiery qualities of courage, cheerfulness, endurance and outstanding loyalty, the Ugandan Rifle soon formed part of the six regiments of the KAR such as the central African Regiment, the East African Rifle, the Indian Regiment and the British Somaliland Regiment," Bartlett said.
Based in Bombo and Jinja, the Uganda Rifle played a major role in establishing British administration in East, Central and the Horn of Africa.
During the First World War of 1914-1918, the Uganda Rifle took part in conquering the vast German territory in central and southern Africa, while in the Second World War of 1939- 1945, they fought outside the continent on the side of Britain and the allied forces in Japan - Burma (now Ceylon) and India.
Rising from scantily-equipped levies with outdated arms employed on local expeditions on ill-armed tribes, the Uganda Rifle worked and fought hard from its nucleus of 270 porters, 75 Sudanese soldiers, each having 11 rounds of ammunition and an old maxim gun. Led by Khedive Ismile under the command of Selem Bey, the Uganda Rifle formed Emin Pasha's troops who joined hands with Lugard to get training under Captain W.H Williams. The number, which grew to 650 men, learnt to respond to the bugle calls, advance with order, aim with care and take cover.
The Uganda Rifle fought hard in defence of Kenya between 1939-1940. Following Adolf Hitler's demand for the lost territories, the British War Office on August 22, 1939, sent a war alert, which was dispatched from Nairobi on September 3, 1939. Thereafter, 1,500 Ugandans joined the KAR depot battalion in Jinja to leave for Iringa.
By the time Hitler invaded France and Belgium on May 10, 1939, Ugandan forces of the 6th KAR batallion at Bombo were ready to join their Kenyan counterparts at Lokitaung to defend Somaliland. With their help, Mogadishu fell on February 5, 1941. Members of the Uganda Rifle of 1-6 KAR batallion under Lt. Col. Blackden assaulted Aba in Ethiopia and on April 6, 1941, Gen. Witheral captured Addis Ababa, with the help of East Africans.
Ugandans helped in securing the Madagascan port of Diego Suarez following the loss of Singapore in 1942.
Apart from providing internal security, the Ugandan Rifle defended the construction of the Uganda Railway, by undertaking punitive expeditions in the Kenyan highlands against the marauding Nandis. They secured the Ugandan border and the northern frontiers.
According to Lt. Col. Bartlett, members of the Uganda Rifle could not be paid terminal benefits because the War Office in London had turned down a request by Governor Collville for Britain to regard the Uganda Rifle as Her Majesty's Forces.
"Troops of this kind are regarded as the Police or constabulary over which the War Office has no responsibility," Bartlett quoted in his book.
In his book, My African Journey, former British Prime Minister and statesman, Winston Churchill, who christened Uganda 'The Pearl of Africa' was full of praise for the forces and commanders of the Uganda Rifle among whom, he talked of meeting the first commander who rode a bicycle from Mombasa to Kampala.
As the memory of these gallant sons of Uganda fades, it is saddening to note that all their efforts were in vain. Perhaps when the Queen comes to Uganda in November next year to attend the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting, she will make 11, 11, 11 a worthy memory for the few surviving veterans who live in abject poverty, relishing a memorable past in partnership with British veterans who are enjoying full benefits.

Original_Search_Title: Olorogun Oskar Ibru - Wealth is Not Enough
Headline: Nigeria: Olorogun Oskar Ibru - Wealth is Not Enough
Body_Text:
THE best way to make money is through inheritance- I want to leave a good inheritance for Delta." That was Olorogun Oskar Christopher Eyovbierere James Ibru reminiscing on his good fortune. Though it has not always been the silver spoon for him, through dint of hard-work and a great vision, his father Olorogun Michael Ibru built the family name and empire into the colossus it is today. Many know of the family's big businesses, but it would appear that their biggest asset is the land holding. Put simply, if you know the numerous land holdings in Lagos and environs, you probably have not seen anything yet until you get to areas around the ancestral homes of old Mid-West.
On pedigree, there is a lot that can be said about the Ibru family. And that goes back to patriarch of the family, Olorogun Peter Epete Ibru, who taught his children through personal example, the fear of God and the value of perseverance and tenacity of purpose. He was an ordinary dispenser in the colonial times, who through great vision sent his children to some of the best schools of the time. He saw then that knowledge will be power. Another lesson the Ibru clan are well positioned to teach Nigerians is the importance of family unity. Through three generations, the family remains united in ways that are uncommon.
These are Olorogun Oskar Ibru's antecedents and inheritance. As the first son of the third generation, he has positioned to fit into very big shoes. And it would have been a surprise if Olorogun Michael Ibru waited until his passing before this transition is made. In fact, there is evidence that that transition started a long before now. Oskar is not only in charge of the large estate, but he is also working to consolidate the businesses and even raise te bar.
He is today in hunt for the governor's office of Delta. That must have been a move that took many by surprise. That would be because like this reporter, you didn't know the real man. I had a rare pip into who the man is; and God be my witness: he is not only deep, he is also warm, he is at home with his people, and very passionate about his people of Delta. If you like what Donald Duke has done with Cross River so far, chances are that you will like what Oskar will do with Delta.
In a tribute to the man, a long standing friend of his, Barrister Sam Kargbo said: " I have known OCJ and have been his friend since 1995. If I were to write a testimonial for him I would not hesitate to tell the world that he is one of the most dependable and trustworthy characters that I have ever associated with. He is generous, caring and protective of the weak." Kargbo says the Olorogun is very much like another mutual friend of theirs Adamu Aliero, current governor of Kebbi State who because he wanted to give back to the state its pride of place in education, abandoned his expansive business for politics. One hopes he is making a success of that mission.
But Oskar has expressed admiration for Bukola Saraki's agricultural programme in Kwara, Ibori's Songhai Programme in Delta, and of course, Donald Duke's visionary leadership of Cross River.
If you look properly you will see consistency running through these preferences. All of those chief executives are doing things with agriculture and levelling on areas of comparative advantage. Oskar is concerned about creating wealth. He is concerned about employing the jobless. He is concerned about actualising the huge potentials of Delta. He has put his vision into this simple song: You eat, you work; you work, you eat. So work, work; and eat and eat." Simple as that may sound, it about captures the present frustration of many A Niger Delta citizen, and their hopes for the future. It can be unbearably suffocating to have to wash your hands with spittle in the midst of an ocean. Delta can ignore all of its oil wealth and gas potential and still build an economically advanced state from its numerous other resources. And Oskar should know.
The Ibru empire over which he seats atop today is built on agriculture and agro-allied business. They did not pioneer the concept of "integrated farming", but it is trade they know very well. The Ibru name is synonymous with fishing in Nigeria. But it has been expanding since then. It has added oil palm estates, poultry, cassava, piggery, crocodile farming, and now plans to add maize too. The oil palm estate somewhere around Ofosu on the Benin -Lagos expressway is in the thousands of hectares. I have seen a few pig farms, but I have not seen the kind owned by the Ibrus, and which Oskar is reviving in Owho, the paternal grandmother's village. This reporter saw the cassava farm too, spreading almost as far as the eyes can see. It was a tour de force, absolutely amazing!

Original_Search_Title: Lagos Promotes Cassava Processing Option
Headline: Nigeria: Lagos Promotes Cassava Processing Option
Body_Text:
Lagos State Commissioner for Agriculture & Cooperatives, Asipa Kaoli Olusanya has stated that the State Government is promoting diversification of processing options of cassava which includes unfermented cassava flour for bread and confectioneries, dry fufu, tapioca and other industrial uses such as starch and dextrose.
He disclosed this at the commissioning of the modern community - based cassava processing centre and distribution of 1000 new improved coconut seedlings at Iragon, Badagry as part of the Agricultural Week celebrations.
He commended Governor Bola Tinubu for approving the counterpart funding which the State has been paying in support of the FADAMA infrastructure building programme in the State, adding that the processing centre, which employs the village adoption technique in its implementation, is built for the community to own and maintain free of charge and it will enable the farmers to switch from one product to another depending on the market situation. He also noted that Badagry division as part of the 180km stretch of the State coconut belt, would continue to serve as the base for the rehabilitation and development of over 13,000 hectares of old coconut groves that are declining in productivity.
He urged the farmers to process well packaged and sealed cassava products which can be exported to Nigerians in diaspora and that the gift of the centre is a test case, which if well maintained, better and bigger facilities will be provided in future since the agricultural extension officers are still on ground to offer further assistance. He noted that some of the non-traditional uses of cassava which are in focus today include: unfermented cassava flour for bread and other confectionaries, cassava starch/mash for industrial uses, cassava pellets and chips for pharmaceutical industries, and cassava chips and pellets for export.
He advised the community to set up a committee to ensure sustainability of the facilities and to allow neighboring communities benefit from the facility aside from all residents of the community and the State in general. He declared that the State government decided to plant new coconut seedlings in order to complement the old coconut groves in the area and presented letters of allocation to 20 coconut farming communities in the area to collect the new seedlings under the aegis of the "food for life Programme" of the State.
Lagos commissions Johnson Agric complex
Lagos State Government has commissioned the Johnson Agiri Complex, Oko-Oba, Agege consisting of the Animal Hospital, Veterinary Diagnostic Laboratory, Ambulatory Livestock Support Services, Livestock Inputs Quality Control Laboratory, Cooperative Training Institute and Soil Laboratory.
Governor Bola Tinubu who was ably represented by the Honourable Commissioner in the Ministry of Agriculture & Cooperatives, Asipa Kaoli Olusanya performed the commissioning as part of the first Agricultural Week celebration in the State.
Governor Tinubu equally directed all livestock clinics, veterinary centres and livestock farms in the State to register with the Ministry of Agriculture & Cooperatives within the next six months, adding that any veterinary centre, clinic or farm that does not comply would be closed down and prosecuted with effect from March 2007.
He also used the opportunity to re-emphasise the ban placed on the movement of birds into Lagos State to the general public and that government will begin the removal of chickens, rams and other livestock from roadsides in the metropolis to designated markets.
He disclosed that the prototype meat vans to be used in conveying meat from the abattoir which evolved through collaboration with all stakeholders are currently on display and would be launched soon.
Speaking earlier, the Honourable Commissioner in the Ministry ably represented by his Permanent Secretary, Mr. Wale Raji, stated that to forestall any future outbreak of livestock diseases, ambulatory livestock support services programme has been introduced such that the State has been divided into six zones for effective coverage and each will be manned by a team of veterinary officers and necessary support staff.
He said that each team is to carry out surveillance and provide livestock extension to all farms within their zones and report any outbreak of animal disease for quick intervention, adding that, despite the establishment of the hospital, the State will collaborate with National Veterinary Institute (NVRI), being the body internationally recognized for diagnosis of animal diseases.

Original_Search_Title: Rains Disrupt Food Supplies
Headline: Uganda: Rains Disrupt Food Supplies
Body_Text:
Kampala — FOODSTUFF supplies have been disrupted due to heavy rains that have made roads to rural areas impassable, a weekly survey has revealed.
Traders said commodity prices remained stable but said distribution would be affected if the rains continue.
"Roads have become slippery. We expected more beans and maize but we are getting little supplies," Julius Kasangaki, a trader at Nakasero Market, said.
"Prices are still stable, but we are not sure what will happen the next day because if the situation continues, we might have shortages and a price increment," Kasangaki said.
Paul Muyigi of St. Balikuddembe Market said the rains would contribute to more food production that will lead to price reduction.
A kilogramme of maize and millet flour remained at sh900 and sh1,500 respectively.
Cassava flour was sold at sh600 from sh400 a kilogramme while beans and rice remained at sh1,000 and sh1,500 respectively. A kilogramme of beans was sold at sh1,300.

Original_Search_Title: Coval Cautions Rice Dealers
Headline: Liberia: Coval Cautions Rice Dealers
Body_Text:
The Consumer Volunteers Association of Liberia, (COVAL), has urged rice dealers in the country to remain patriotic by keeping rice sale in accordance with the Commerce Ministry's regulatory price list.
"We've noted that dishonesty to one another was one of the major reasons for our war, and as such, if we wish to end the war finally, Liberians should remain honest in every little thing they do", emphasized COVAL.
The association welcomed the continuous availability of rice in all grades, which represent different prices and would give every Liberian the opportunity to eat what they can afford.
It also commended the Commerce Ministry for the new rice policy, which it noted, has empowered more Liberian businesses including the Fahmah Business Center to import rice into the country.
Additionally, the Consumer Volunteers Association has urged the Ministries of Commerce and Agriculture to empower individual and community farmers to grow more rice as a means of cutting down the huge money being spent to import rice.
The association said motivation by both ministries would help erase the negative concept of killing national leaders for rice. "We also wish to call on Liberians to get away from making rice a priority and eat other food like cassava, yam and plantain.

Original_Search_Title: EU To Reproduce Agagu's Projects in N/Delta
Headline: Nigeria: EU To Reproduce Agagu's Projects in N/Delta
Body_Text:
Akure — Impressed by the infrastructural and developmental programmes of Governor Olusegun Agagu in Ondo State, the European Union has expressed willingness to replicate the projects and programmes in six states of Niger Delta.
The EU representative, Dr Mariam Abu, who lauded the ingenuity of Agagu in the areas of infrastructures provision, said the next Micro Project Programmes for the nine Niger Delta states (MPP9) would be tailored towards Agagu's initiatives in developing the rural areas.
Abu, who spoke during a visit of EU officials and National Planning Commis-sion, Abuja to Agagu, said the solar-powered water schemes and rural electrification projects of the Agagu administration would be copied for the MPP9 project, because they were apt for rural areas.
Other projects of the government, which attracted the EU delegation, were the various intervention programmes in education, health and agriculture.
Abu, whose team was on a three-day assessment visit of various developmental projects of the EU MPP9 in the state, also hinted that the body was set to finance more cassava and oil processing units in the state.
She, however, appealed to the state government to make available its programme plans to the union, saying such would assist the agency in policy planning and execution.
Responding, Agagu said that the need to impact positively on the people's lives had driven his administration to evolve various strategies for the development of all sectors of the state.
He urged all donor agencies with the intention of executing projects in the state to collaborate with the state government in order to avoid duplication of projects aimed at alleviating the suffering of the people.
According to him, "the needs of various communities differ and there is need for better coordination between the government and donor agencies so that we can jointly move the society forward.

Original_Search_Title: Okumagba Assures On Even Development
Headline: Nigeria: Okumagba Assures On Even Development
Body_Text:
Warri — PDP governorship aspirant in Delta State, Mr. Albert Okumagba, yesterday, said that no community and tribe, whether in the upland or coastland will be left behind undeveloped if voted into office as governor in 2007.
Mr. Okumagba stated this in Warri, while offering himself to the people for greater service to the state and the country.
According to him, "there will be enough for everyone. We shall sow together, reap together and share together".
The aspirant said that if given the mandate, his administration would prove once and for all time that Delta State could and would survive without oil.
"Oil will not be discounted but the time has come to loosen its choking grip on the state's economy, revenue and our people.
"Our plan is to aggressively seek and develop huge and vibrant alternative economies that will run on their own, compete favourably with oil and open new windows of wealth creation for all Deltans and non Deltans who may choose to make Delta State their new home", he said.
Mr. Okumagba asserted that the state was blessed with over 13 major raw materials and agricultural produce, including clay, bauxite, kaolin, gravel, fish, cassava, rubber, oil palm and timber, which are available in commercial quantities, pointing out that "everyone of these resources can sustain its own industry."
He also said that the state's airport and sea ports were goldmines that offer huge potentials waiting to be tapped, adding that a comprehensive plan in all these areas and more had been developed and would be made available to the citizens of the state in due course.

Original_Search_Title: Governor Implores Leaders On Hunger
Headline: Rwanda: Governor Implores Leaders On Hunger
Body_Text:
Kigali — The Provincial Governor, Eraste Kabera, has urged local leaders in the province to grow more food crops to fight hunger and poverty. Kabera made the remarks last week during a consultative meeting aimed at laying strategies of implementing and increasing food production especially cassava, to eliminate hunger and fight poverty in the province.
The meeting held at Muhanga district offices was attended by all district mayors, security officials, executive secretaries, provincial agronomists and experts in cassava production.
Kabera said that it was a time for people to use the natural resources in their country to eliminate hunger by increasing food production and fighting poverty in the province.
"We have to take a stance and see to it that hunger is history in the province. This province has a potential of feeding Rwanda and other countries if we consider food crops like cassava which has a potential of feeding the region and sustain the economic needs of people today," Kabera stated.
He noted that fighting poverty without eliminating hunger is elusive and according to research findings, most of the human problems rotate around hunger.
"If we consider our goal of eliminating hunger by increasing food production, then the existing markets for cassava in Rwanda and abroad will be in our province," Kabera said.
Participants however, noted that there was need to improvise mosaic free cassava tubers and sensitise the farmers to increase cassava growing through sector agronomists and use farmers association to attain the goal.
"This programme is achievable but first, farmers have to implement it by increasing the number of hectors of cassava. We shall certainly provide the market and transportation of the product" Alphonse Nyaruhirira, a consultant in rural development said. He added that at least 30 million cassava stems are to be planted beginning December.

Original_Search_Title: Vitamin A Fortified Potato to Combat Blindness
Headline: Uganda: Vitamin A Fortified Potato to Combat Blindness
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDANS can now combat malnutrition and blindness.
A team of reseachers at Namulonge and Kabanyoro Research Institute have come up with a Vitamin A fortified sweet potato variety. The sweet potato, which is orange, has carotene, the most important source of Vitamin A. Vitamin A is essential for good eyesight and lack of it can cause blindness or, in milder cases, inability to see or drive at night.
Prof. Patrick Rubaihayo, a plant breeder, who led the team of researchers said the variety was not genetically modified, but a product of pure breeding done in line with the United Nations' Millenium Development Goals (MDGs).
"It is purely a product of normal breeding. We are now testing our product with farmers. It looks ordinary but, it is improved nutritionally," he said.
Rubaihayo said, as scientists, this was their contribution to the realisation of the millennium development goals.
"Our long term objective is to end hunger, though in the short term, we are focussing on raising levels of nutrition among our people," he said.
The product is got by enhancing the quality of the local sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas) by fortifying it with Vitamin A.
The millennium goals were adopted five years ago by the world's governments as a blueprint for building a better world in the 21st century. They include hunger,poverty reduction, education, maternal health, gender equality, combating child mortality, AIDS and other diseases.
Rubaihayo said the sweet potato was of great nutritional value to communities that grow and consume it. It yields the highest level of edible energy among other energy givers such as rice, maize and cassava.
The crop is cultivated in over 100 developing countries.
With more than 133 million tonnes in annual production, sweet potatoes rank fifth most important food crop after rice, wheat, maize and cassava, according to the Food & Agricultural Organisation. In Africa, sweet potato production is concentrated in countries around the Lake Victoria Crescent where it is the second most important root crop after cassava.
Uganda is one of the biggest producers of sweet potatoes in East and Central Africa, and worldwide, it is the second biggest producer of the crop after China.
The importance of sweet potatoes lies in its ability to grow and produce edible food storage roots in marginal environments where other food crops fail, which makes it a valuable crop for poor farmers.
The crop can also be stored in the soil as a famine reserve and has a high productivity per unit area, which makes it an ideal food security crop. The sweet potato is also increasingly becoming a cash crop in many parts of Uganda.
In the last decade, the importance of the sweet potato increased greatly in East and Central Africa due to pests and disease which have affected the production of alternative staples such as cassava, bananas and maize.
Rubaihayo says increasing sweet potato production will help improve household incomes and food security in Africa.
According to the FAO, despite high potential for food security, research shows that sweet potato yields in the region is below its genetic potential. This is partly due to virus-induced diseases such as sweet potato weevils.
The high incidence of the viral diseases have been attributed to use of infected planting materials and secondary spread of the viruses by vectors, whose occurrence has been reported abundant along the Lake Victoria Crescent. The vegetative propagation, usually by taking cuttings from a previous crop, also increases the risk of build-up of viruses. To reduce the virus build-up, use of virus-tested planting materials is recommended.
Rubaihayo said millions of women and children in sub-Saharan Africa suffer from Vitamin A deficiency. Apart from the high energy and carotene contents, sweet potatoes are a good source of iron and zinc.
About 21% of children under five years worldwide have Vitamin A deficiency. Iron deficiency is also most prevalent among women of childbearing age in developing countries.

Original_Search_Title: Wild Animals Threaten Luweero Farmers
Headline: Uganda: Wild Animals Threaten Luweero Farmers
Body_Text:
Kampala — The National Agriculture Advisory Services (NAADS) programmes which are meant to promote food security in Luweero and Nakaseke districts could be frustrated by wild animals.
"Every other day, warthogs (wild pigs), velvet monkeys and bushbucks plunder farmers' gardens, eating cassava, sweet potatoes, watermelons, cabbages and tomatoes," says John Ngondwe, the vermin control officer. in Luweero and Nakaseke districts
Ngondwe says farmers in the two districts had embraced the NAADS programmes but they have lost hope.
"The NAADS programmes are likely to fail. The vermins are increasing day and night," he adds.
Baboons terrorise farmers in Kikyusa, Butuntumula, Makulubita and Kamira sub-counties while in Wakyato, Ngoma and Kanyogoga, leopards feast on goats and calves.
Ngondwe says buffalos terrorise cattle keepers in Ngoma sub-county, especially Kijimba village. "When buffalos attack the herds, they scatter cattle and herdsmen flee in fear," he says.
"It's a pity, Uganda Wildlife Authority (UWA) simply advises local people to abandon their land for the wild animals, instead of relocating the game to gazetted areas," he says.
"We have never been assisted by UWA. They responded once when the President ordered them to relocate the elephants," he says.
Before the 1981-1986 NRA war in Luweero, the semi-arid sub-counties of Ngoma and Wakyato were the only ones inhabited by wild animals. But by the end of the war, wild animals had spread to other sub-counties in Luweero and Nakaseke districts.
"As people fled their homes, wild animals roamed freely in the gardens that had turned into bushes and forests," he says.
However, when the National Resistance Movement (NRM) took over power, a vermin control office was opened up in Luweero district. However, Ngondwe says the vermin control section lacks logistics like ammunition.
"Since the section is under the district, funding is almost non-existent," he says
Nalongo, a farmer in her 50s and neighbour to Robert Lumu, who recently survived an attack by a crocodile, said warthogs recently destroyed her two-acre cassava garden.
"The local authorities have not helped us," she said.The warthog is an ugly animal. It has a large head with big warts, which unlovely as they are, protect the eyes when the animal is passing through thorny shrubs.
Warthogs can grow to over two feet and may weigh up to 100kg. The upturned canines of their upper jaw may be eight to 11 inches long, and make formidable weapons which it uses when attacked.
They breed twice in three years and a litter may have six or more piglets. They are diurnal animals and are often seen digging for roots, insects and worms.

Original_Search_Title: Bunyoro is Still Stuck in the Past
Headline: Uganda: Bunyoro is Still Stuck in the Past
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Bunyoro region has one of the largest sugar factories in the country at Kinyara. This region has got some of the most fertile soils in Uganda. It grows tobacco on a fairly large scale and has many game reserves and a big game park.
The region has two lakes: Albert and Kyoga. However, the people of Bunyoro remain some of the poorest in Uganda.
According to Poverty Alleviation Project (PAP) figures, almost 55% of people in Bunyoro are poor, compared to a national average of 38% in 2005.
Bunyoro is composed of three districts that might soon become five. These include Masindi from which a new one called Buliisa has been carved and Hoima from which Kibaale was created about five years ago. There might also be Kagadi, to be carved out of Kibaale. According to the 2002 population census records, the three districts have a population of about 700,000 people. It is a combination of hills and valleys, most of which have got very fertile soils, fit for any kind of agriculture.
However, the countryside is dotted with grass-thatched huts, one of the signs of an impoverished community.
Historical power
At one time, Bunyoro controlled present-day Ankole, Toro and parts of Bulemezi, Gomba, Kooki and Kyaggwe of Buganda. From the tough Omukama Kamurasi through Omukama Kabalega to the hardened Abarusura, who are regarded as the first ever organised army in present-day Uganda, the Banyoro have a very rich history as fighters and defenders of their rights. The fiercest battles against colonialism were fought on Bunyoro soil.
This is the land where one of the earliest organised groups, the Bachwezi, lived. Upon the extinction of the Bachwezi, the Babiito dynasty set in. It is the 18th dynasty of the Babiito that is still in power today. The questions asked is: Where did all this vigour go? The disintegration of Bunyoro started about 300 years ago and even the efforts of Kamurasi and Kabalega could not stop it until 1965 when a referendum to return the two lost counties of Buyaga and Bugangaizi was carried out. Even then, huge chunks of land in Bunyoro are still controlled by absentee landlords, a creation of the colonial era. "Kabalega's refusal to accept forceful European colonisation resulted in the 1893 British invasion and total destruction of Bunyoro-Kitara. The result was forceful British colonisation which resulted in the 1893-99 brutal war which left millions of Banyoro dead, not to mention the destruction of her economy," Henry Ford Miirima, the Press Secretary of the Omukama, says.
He explains that subsequent colonial policies sentenced Bunyoro to her current poverty levels and backwardness.
When the colonialists captured Bunyoro after the defeat of Kabalega, they humiliated the Banyoro further by introducing Baganda chiefs, allegedly to teach the Banyoro basic things, like bathing and using pit latrines. Such Baganda chiefs included the ruthless and cunning Temusi Miti, who at one time even ordered that any kind of debate in Bunyoro was to be held in Luganda. This was certainly psychological torture for the Banyoro.
In Buganda, a Munyoro became the subject of negative references, all of which were intended to psychologically disorient the Banyoro. "Oli musiru ng'omunyoro (you are as stupid as a Munyoro), Oli mujama ng'omunyoro (You are as dirty as a Munyoro) were some of the derogatory statements coined. History analysts believe that it is the treatment of the Banyoro by the British and their Baganda collaborators that adversely affected development in this region.
After Uganda attained independence in 1962, Banyoro tried to regain some lost glory when they won a referendum over the lost counties, against Buganda. They supported UPC, the then ruling party and later the National Resistance Army's guerilla war. Since the 1996 polls, they have overwhelmingly voted for the National Resistance Movement with an average of 85% for the 1996, 2001 and 2006 elections.
Natural wealth
There is no other region in Uganda that is as naturally gifted as Bunyoro. The soils rank as some of the most fertile in Uganda. Other than West Nile, Bunyoro is the largest producer of tobacco in Uganda.
Bunyoro has one of the largest sugar factories in the country at Kinyara supported by hundreds of outgrowers. Kinyara produces at least 70,000 tonnes of sugar per year.
The Banyoro also grow maize, millet, cotton, coffee, groundnuts, cassava and potatoes. They keep cattle, goats, sheep and practise poultry. They also practice bee-keeping. There are several game parks and reserves in the region. Bunyoro has a fair share of Lake Albert and now there is oil. There are also prospects of gold in Kibaale, especially around the Kisita hills on the border between Mubende and Kibaale districts. According to Miirima, poverty in Bunyoro was caused by the colonialists. "Oil was first discovered in Bunyoro around 1906, however, the colonialists refused to drill it because they thought it would make Bunyoro very powerful," Miirima says.
He adds that although the region has the Murchison Falls National Park and several other game reserves, it does not benefit directly from these national resources because the proceeds go the national coffers.
There is also a stereotype that the Banyoro are rather lazy and that it is this laziness that the bafuruki (foreigners) exploited to halt progress in the region. It is said that if a typical Mukiga comes to Bunyoro, within just three weeks, he would have tilled about three acres of land and in the following three weeks he would have planted maize and other crops. At the end of six months, he would be selling his maize. Most of the casual work at major installations like Kinyara Sugar Works and now at the oil exploration sites is done by Alur, largely because the indigenous Banyoro are deemed lazy.
Bunyoro is further endowed with forests like Budongo and Kiryandongo, which have the highly prized mvule and mahogany trees. Nevertheless, most of the licensed pitsawing is done by Bakiga, who sell the timber in Kampala.
A survey by Sunday Vision among the sugar-cane outgrowers also indicates that most are either Alur, Bagisu or Bakiga and not Banyoro.
Land tenure system
According to the Banyoro, the land tenure system was and still is one of the measures the colonialists effected to make them poor. For instance, most of Kibaale is owned by absentee landlords who are mainly Baganda. Hence even the Banyoro who occupy this land as tenants cannot do anything developmental on this land. They can only practise subsistence farming on the land.
Most of the forests, though planted by Omukama Kabalega, have been declared national assets. This means that the Banyoro do not directly benefit from them.
Other than foreigners who were imposed on Bunyoro through a historical accident, more Ugandans have migrated to Bunyoro. This is largely because of the Banyoro's accommodative nature and perhaps promixity to the war-ravaged north and eastern Congo.
The region has got thousands of refugees in Masindi and Hoima. In Hoima, refugees occupy areas like Kyangwali in the south, while in Masindi they occupy Kiryandongo.
On top of these refugees, other tribes have migrated to Bunyoro. The most talked about are of course the Bakiga, who have settled in parts of Hoima and Kibaale.
These bafuruki, as the Banyoro call them, have even gone ahead and captured leadership positions in Bunyoro. In the 8th Parliament, out of four seats in Kibaale, three were won by Bakiga. The Banyoro have only one. Big swatches of land in Kigumba and Wanseko have been bought off from poor indigenous Banyoro by rich refugees. Although the Bafuruki are considered residents of Bunyoro, their patriotism and allegiances might not be fully in Bunyoro.
The infrastructure
Many Banyoro cite the poor road network in Bunyoro as another obstacle to development. It has taken more than 20 years for the Government to tarmac Busunju-Kiboga-Hoima road, which is the gateway to Hoima.
It has also taken another 20 years to tarmac the Kafu-Masindi road, which is the gateway to Masindi and to the Murchison Falls National Park. At the moment, there is no serious talk about tarmacking the Masindi-Hoima road used by hundreds of sugar cane outgrowers and cereal farmers. There is also a general lack of political mobilisation among the Banyoro. This might be as a result of several decades of resignation and self-pity.
Given the fact that the Banyoro have more potential and more natural resources than their neighbours, through effective mobilisation, they can certainly realise their full potential.
Instead of mobilising the population for development, most of the political elite are occupied with personal feuds and wrangles. The local council leaders of the three districts spend most of the time fighting against MPs and ministers from the region.
"Let us stop fighting each other and stop being the laughing stock of Uganda. Let's mobilise our people and fight poverty," Bunyoro-Kitara Diocese Bishop Nathan Kyamanywa says.

Original_Search_Title: Flour Prices Up
Headline: Uganda: Flour Prices Up
Body_Text:
Kampala — Prices of flour have gone up due to the high cost of operating machines by the grain millers, a weekly survey has revealed.
A kilogramme of maize flour was sold at sh900 from sh800, while millet flour went for sh1,500 from sh1,200.
Cassava flour was sold at sh600 from sh400 a kilogramme, while beans and rice remained at sh1,000 and sh1,500 respectively. Prices of other foods also rose.
The Government recently announced a 41% power increment on commercial industries' which includes grain millers.
Traders in Kampala's major markets said prices are expected to go up further in the next few months and many millers will close.
"Electricity prices have gone up. This has forced grain milling owners to hike prices in order to maintain the tariff costs," Julius Kasangaki, a trader, said.
"Most of them have closed. The remaining ones charge high prices to remain in business. This has forced flour prices to go up, " Kasangaki said.
He said continued demand for food from southern Sudan had also contributed to higher prices.

Original_Search_Title: UNIDO Constructs Hydro-Power Project in Enugu
Headline: Nigeria: UNIDO Constructs Hydro-Power Project in Enugu
Body_Text:
Awgu — According to the United Nations statistics, over 2 billion people lack access to modern energy services, whereas the vast majority lives in rural areas of the poorest regions of the world. Among other things, the lack of energy causes social and economic poverty, under-development, unemployment and low living standards. It also contributes largely to under-utilisation of rural human resources, leads to stagnant rural economy and lack of development of value-adding industry.
But according to the United Nations Industrial Development Organisation (UNIDO), which is siting N722 million multi-purpose hydro-power project, located along Enugu-Port-Harcourt express way, "access to energy is a pre-requisite for sustainable development and for fighting poverty. Availability of affordable and sustainable energy is critical to the achievement of the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs)."
By supporting access to energy, UNIDO seeks to contribute to halving extreme poverty and hunger, achieving universal primary education, promoting gender equality and empowering women, reducing child mortality and ensuring a friendlier environment. This informed the decision to support the small hydropower project in Ezioha Mgbowo community in Enugu State.
The initial idea was that the Federal Government of Nigeria through the Federal Ministry of Water Resources develop a small Earth Dam for rural water supply to Mgbowo community. Major components of the scheme include an earth dam, side spillway, pen-stock, powerhouse, water treatment works, water reticulation, two service reservoirs (50,000 gallons capacity) each and a Community Development Centre, including an Information Communication and Technology (ICT) Centre.
Above community is expected to construct its Development Centre (to be equipped by UNIDO), which will accommodate various business activities to be established and owned by members of the community. Such activities include cottage industries like maize milling, rice milling, garri processing and palm oil processing; business centre and internet Café; a recreation Hall with television room and a 10 ton cold room storage. The Development Centre, currently at roofing level, is due for commissioning with the hydropower station sometime in November, going by the pace of work going on there.
According to Nwachukwu, "this is a pilot project, the first of its type in Nigeria. We are at the roofing level for the Development Centre, the hydro-power station is about 95 percent completed, the 30-kilowatt turbine has been installed and we plan to test run it in a few days. The total project is around 30 percent completed and we expect that by March 2007, it would have been completed."
Explaining further, the Project manager said, "water is supposed to be pumped into the reservoir and thereafter it will be treated and supplied to the community. The project was originally conceived as a water project but the hydro-power component is an value added component because it would not consume the water and thereafter the water could be treated and supplied to the communities."
"This is geared towards maximising what we have for the benefit of the community. And where there is a potential for hydropower, UNIDO wants to harness it for productive use so that the communities can reap huge benefits from it. This does not in any way affect the original concept. The hydro-power station would be commissioned next month and the people would be able to benefit from utilising power generated to process their agro products from the farm."
"The turbine is 30 kilowatt capacity but all the energy and power generated would be utilised by the agro processing industry and the ICT centre in the day time. In the night, instead of wasting such power, some of the communities would have to be given light by using low energy lamps so that each of them would have a point of light."
Stressing UNIDO's role, he said, "UNIDO has provided the hydro-power components, mechanical components, the agro-processing equipment and they have also promised to equip the ICT centre. The policy is that you provide the physical infrastructure while they equip for you."
The people of Ezioha community are Ibo, and the farmers among them live within the community and are actively engaged in farming producing crops such as maize, rice, cassava and yam. Palm oil extraction is the pre-occupation of the women and this is done in a local way.
The population of the people is about 2000 with an average family size of 8. The number of households is estimated at 250. Their settlement pattern is in clusters, which make it possible to effectively share amenities.
Speaking on the project, an old woman, Monica, (carrying a basket with some farm produce on her head) said in broken English: "We like wetin una de do for us here. We know say na better thing wey go help the people. So make una no stop. We like am well well."
Others who declined to give their names, believed that the hydropower project would reduce their hardship and empower them economically, socially and politically; and that lacking infrastructure would be provided for them.
What started as a micro hydro-power dam project, would translate into economic, social and political wealth and relevance for the people of Awgu local government. Maize harvested from farms is sold off due to lack of processing and storage facility. Rice harvested is processed locally and milled at mills located in the neighbouring community about 2km away. Cassava is processed into garri or fofoo on a low scale.
However, a community based organisation (CBO) Ezioha Mgbowo Welfare Association, has been pencilled down to manage the proposed Small Hydro-power scheme. The project was designed by consultants to the Federal Ministry of Water Resources and contracts for its construction awarded to an indigenous contracting firm in March 2005.
Construction work at the dam site commenced in April 2005 and is expected to be completed by December 2006. The Anambra Imo River Basin Development Authority is the supervisory agency for the project.

Original_Search_Title: Nakaseke Passes By-Law On Food Security
Headline: Uganda: Nakaseke Passes By-Law On Food Security
Body_Text:
Nakaseke — Nakaseke district Chairman Ignatius Koomu has said every homestead in the district will be compelled to have a permanent plantation of food crops in an effort to fight famine and poverty in the district.
Koomu said the policy that was passed by the district executive starts early next year. "We have passed a resolution that each homestead in the district must have a plantation of food crops before engaging in any other business," Koomu said.
He was speaking at the district headquarters offices in Kiwoko town on October 28. He said they were focusing on planting cassava and sweet potatoes.
The district was faced with a long drought that culminated into famine and increased poverty. The law is also aimed at compelling residents especially the youth who have abandoned the agriculture sector for tree cutting to rejoin food production.
Many farmers have been engaged in growing upland rice as a cash crop and burning of charcoal, ignoring the growing of food crops.
"After harvesting the rice farmers it sell all leaving them with nothing for consumption," Koomu said. "Despite the huge amounts of money yielded from the rice, much of it is spent lavishly by fathers, leaving the mothers and children without money for food or education."
This, he said has affected the food security, education and the health of people in the district. "Before a farmer plants any cash crop, they must first show us a plantation of cassava and potatoes grown to sustain the family all year. We shall arrest all those who defy the order," Koomu said.

Original_Search_Title: Newmont Ghana Gold Assists MOFA With Motorbikes
Headline: Ghana: Newmont Ghana Gold Assists MOFA With Motorbikes
Body_Text:
Newmont Ghana Gold Limited, as part of its social responsibilities to sustain development in livelihood and improve agriculture, has presented ten Yamaha motorbikes to the Asutifi District Office of the Ministry of Food Agriculture (MOFA).
The motorbikes, which cost an amount of $3,500 each, were presented to 10 extension officers who are in connection with Newmont's Agricultural Improvement and Land Access Program (AILAP) at Kenyasi.
Presenting the bikes, the AILAP Coordinator, Mr. Duut Nelson, entreated the beneficiaries to use the bikes for the work expected, by increasing the frequency of visiting the farmers in the communities to offer technical advises.
He said the availability of the bikes was an opportunity to improve upon the technical ability of the farmers to increase agricultural diversity, productivity, total production and market access over levels of existing prior to the Ahafo project construction.
The Deputy Coordinator of AILAP, Albert Amankwah, disclosed that the program is to improve returns to agriculture in the Asutifi district and the distribution of the bikes was a major boost.
He said the program was confined to farmers who received crop or building compensation in the mine-take area.
According to Mr. Amankwah, AILAP is a community investment program to assist farmers affected by the project and is not a compensated package, disclosing that 1700 farmers have been registered to get on board with the program next year.
The program proposes the growing of particular food and cash crops for beneficiary farmers to choose from, which includes maize, cassava, plantain, vegetables, chilly pepper, citrus, oil palm, cocoa, rice, cowpea and soya bean.
The Asutifi District Director of MOFA, Kwaku Minkah Fordjour, who received the keys on behalf of the extension officers, comprising mostly males and only two females, commended Newmont for the effective collaboration to ensure efficient food production in the area.
He disclosed that during the years 2004 and 2005, total production of crops fell in the district as a result of farmers' attitude of abandoning their cultivated fields with the notion of receiving huge packages of compensation for such lands from Newmont.
Mr. Fordjour said the introduction of the AILAP program has helped to reverse the trend and there have now been improvements in production, adding that the program has created an avenue for 300 farmers to part-take in the cultivation of cash crop in the district.
He commended Newmont for assisting the District MOFA in the fight against the recent armyworm outbreak.
The beneficiaries assured the judicious use of the motorbikes and would see to the improvement of food production in the district to help boost the economy of the country.

Original_Search_Title: Kaunda is a Man to Admire, But Africa Doesn't Need Such Leaders
Headline: Africa: Kaunda is a Man to Admire, But Africa Doesn't Need Such Leaders
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A photograph on the cover of the October 19 issue of the Daily Monitor, Uganda's main independent newspaper, shows President Yoweri Museveni shaking hands with Zambia's former president Kenneth Kaunda (clutching his signature white handkerchief).
Looking on is Rwanda's President Paul Kagame. The picture was taken at the Sandton Convention Centre, Johannesburg, during the Annual Business Leaders' Forum. What is striking about the photo is that, with their heads and chins clean-shaven, Museveni and Kaunda look to be about the same age. However, Museveni is 62 years old, and Kaunda is all of 82!
In the 1960s, Kaunda was part of a left-leaning group of leaders called the Mulungushi Club, along with Tanzania's Mwalimu Julius Nyerere and Uganda's Milton Obote. Both have since died. Kaunda, however, is still reasonably healthy, good-humoured and keen-minded.
What's his secret? A journalist who has followed Kaunda's routine closely, thinks he has it figured out. One, his diet. Kaunda is a vegetarian. But he goes one better; he only eats uncooked food. Mainly fruits, vegetables and juice.
In the morning, he has apple juice with raw groundnuts. For lunch he will usually eat a banana, raw sweet potatoes or raw cassava. In the evenings, he will add a vegetable and fruit platter - mangoes, pineapple, avocado.
On a continent where we have had presidents hunting game in the national parks for Christmas and birthday treats, Kaunda showed a lot of character by staying away from meat altogether.
Second, he has good physical habits. He's been described as a "great" golfer.
Third, he is a not an excitable man, and he's deeply religious. And we all know that he is a musician (playing an accordion, no less).
In addition, unlike an African strongman like Gabon's Omar Bongo, Kaunda avoided the temptation to amass a football team of wives, and an army of children.
During the debate, Kaunda said Africa was a continent at the crossroads because of its failed economic policies, and conflicts arising from bad leadership. Museveni and Kagame offered two different views of the way forward.
Kagame argued for strength of institutions and against the cult of individuals, and made the case for presidential term limits. Museveni, who had the constitution amended last year to allow him to run for a fifth term in office, argued for the importance of visionary leadership.
It was a legitimate philosophical divide that is nearly 200 years old.
In Africa today, it presents the two contending political traditions. When Kaunda was president, it was whether Africa should follow the "capitalist" or "socialist" path. Related to that, was whether one-party rule or multiparty democracy was the "most suited to Africa's unique conditions." In the mix, was whether military rule could be justified.
ALL THREE QUESTIONS HAVE BEEN largely resolved. The capitalist path, its imperfections notwithstanding, has trumped socialism. Multiparty democracy is the norm in most of Africa. Military rule is totally out of fashion.
We also have early indications as to which fork, at Kaunda's crossroads, most of Africa will take. The highest placed five sub-Saharan African countries in the latest World Economic Forum Competitiveness Report (2006/2007), are South Africa at 48th; Mauritius at 58th; Botswana at 83rd; Namibia at 86th; and Kenya at 96th.
In common, these countries have term limits, rule of law rather than individuals and, except for Kenya, established governing parties. Looking to the future, then, it seems Museveni will lose the bet. After all, with all his individual virtues, Kaunda was still a disaster as president.
Charles Onyango-Obbo is Nation Media Group's managing editor for convergence and new products.

Original_Search_Title: Is Mining Everything?
Headline: Ghana: Is Mining Everything?
Body_Text:
I DO not know if you read the image-building write-up by the Media Relations Unit of Ghana Chamber of Commerce as published in the Wednesday, October 25, 2006, issue of the Daily Graphic.
The statements and statistics were truly impressive, as the write-up presented a picture of a caring Chamber not only concerned about corporate profits but also about the communities in which the mines operate.
A few excerpts: "The Chamber carries out its mission without compromising the basic principles of honest, transparency, good governance, good corporate citizenship, commitment and unity."
The write-up states that the Chamber organised a two-day conference on environment and community relations at Nsuta, from October 6 to October 7, 2006. The comment by the write-up states, "The choice of the theme shows how sensitive the Chamber is to the impact of their operations." (Emphasis mine).
On the benefits of mining, the statement notes, "Mining contributes directly to the local economy by employing workers and generating income at the mine. It supplies houses, educational centres, hospitals/medical clinics and other social infrastructures."
It goes on, "On the national front, mining currently accounts for over five percent of Gross Domestic Product (GDP), but if industry multiplier effects are added, the contribution of the GDP increases to about eight percent."
The statement cites South Africa, Australia and Sweden as examples of countries where mining had brought benefits to the people.
On the social responsibility of the Chamber of Mines, the statement notes, "Social responsibility contributions to host communities between 2003 and 2005 alone amounted to ¢174 billion.
These voluntary payments and expenditures are made in addition to the statutory payments such as royalties and other taxes.
Royalty payments from 2003 to 2005 were ¢646billion, while dividend payments over the same period amounted to over ¢111billion."
On the environment, the statement notes, "The Chamber and its members have collectively affirmed the increasingly critical role of the industry in the socio-economic development of the country and the corresponding need to operate responsibly in order to mitigate the impact of mining on the communities and the public in general." (Emphasis mine).
The statement continues, "Our members abide by all the laws of the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and operate responsibly in order to minimise the impact of our activities on host communities."
The statement positively affirms, "Mining will continue to play a central role in the economic development of this country."
On the face of it, who can argue against this last statement by the Chamber of Mines? Who can ignore the economic benefits the country derives from the taxes, royalties and employment generated by mining in the country?
The Ghana Chamber of Mines has its Chief Executive Officer Ms Joyce Aryee, a well-educated woman with experiences in governance as one-time Secretary for Education in the regime of the Provisional National Defence Council (PNDC).
Ms Aryee is also in God's work with her Salt and Light Ministries. You can find her 'preaching' on Fridays in The Chronicle It is great that the statement by the Chamber of Mines Media Relations Unit hides some ugly, unpleasant and ungodly realities that ought to be properly addressed so that a cure can be found instead of palliatives. The wounds inflicted on the mining communities are many as death-dealing.
Whether by sheer coincidence or design, the publication of the statement by the Chamber of Mines came five days after the chiefs and people of Etwebo, Akoti and Chirano had, at a press conference in Accra, accused the Chirano Gold Mines as "being insensitive to the people in their catchment area." (See the Daily Graphic Friday, October 20, 2006. Page 48) Chirano Gold Mines is an Australian mining company.
The Graphic reports the chief and his elders as saying that "the operations of the company had a negative impact on the lives of the members of communities instead of the growth and development that the company promised them."
