The report goes further to state that the activities of the company had resulted in a decisive destruction of the foundation upon which the community derived its basic and fundamental livelihoods and freedom.
According to the chief, Nana Alue Yaw Sako II, the farm lands, cocoa plantation, water resources and the environment as a whole had been destroyed by the activities of Chirano Mines.
The chief added that "the company valued each cocoa tree at ¢25,000, palm tree at ¢125,000, cassava and pineapple at ¢3,000 and plantain at ¢8,000.
What should be noted is that, once a mining company has paid the so-called compensation for farm products, the farmland no longer belongs to the people and systematic degradation sets in as mining activities begin.
It is said that, where a farmland had not been cultivated with crops, the mining company did not pay even one pesewa as compensation.
At least this is what one Mr. E. Ofori Yeboah from Ahafo in the Brong Ahafo Region told me in his letter to The Pioneer entitled Newmont Ghana Paying Compensation for Only Crops? (See The Pioneer of Wednesday October 11, 2006 ).
Mr. Ofori Yeboah claimed that uncultivated lands of several acres belonging to him and his family had simply been taken over by Newmont without any compensation.
Who gave the land belonging to the family of Mr. Ofori Yeboah to Newmont? Was it the chief and his people? Was it the Government? Why was no compensation paid, as he claims?
Older established mining companies like Ashgold may show greater social responsibility in their areas of operation.
But it is generally the case that communities where mining activities take place suffer more than they gain.
The sometimes irreparable degradation of the land, arsenic, cyanide and mercury poisoning, tuberculosis and skin diseases, extreme poverty as the people no longer have recourse to the use of their farm lands, blasting that destroys their houses and the criminal pollution of their only sources of life-giving water become their lot.
The guilty ones can be found among some of these new, fly-by-night companies out to make a quick profit and they leave for their countries of origin where more stringent environment laws will not make them get away with the destruction they cause.
Today, even forest reserves have not escaped the rapacity and destruction of mining.
When we have taken away all the gold and destroyed the land especially through surface mining, what shall we leave as a legacy for posterity?

Original_Search_Title: Biofuel Begins to Attract As Crude Oil Prices Fluctuate
Headline: Ghana: Biofuel Begins to Attract As Crude Oil Prices Fluctuate
Body_Text:
Accra — When Ghana's Vice President Alhaji Aliu Mahama visited Thailand at the beginning of September this year, he was fascinated by that country's level of technological advancement in biofuel generation.
According to media reports including that of the Ghana News Agency, the Deputy Director of the Royal "Chitralada" Projects, Ms Rosarin Smitabhim told the Vice President that Thailand would be switching to the use of bio-diesel and gasoline next year.
She said currently the official vehicles of the King were being powered solely by biofuel. The Royal projects include a solar energy system that could serve a large area of Bangkok, the use of rice chaff for charcoal production, recycling of used household oil for soap and the manufacturing of candles from bee wax.
Alhaji Mahama on his part said Ghana would tap the expertise of Thailand in large scale production of bio-fuel for national consumption
He said although Ghana had set in progress the development of bio-diesel, the stride Thailand has made by way of research would illuminate the path for Ghanaian researchers.
Although Ghana has made strides in the production of bio-fuel since the last few years, lack of financial investment has delayed the dreams of Ghanaian experts in biofuel such as Onua Amoah, who intends to cultivate Jatropha on commercial basis.
Last Thursday the Energy Commission held a stakeholders forum to discuss, ways of improving the production of biofuel in the country in the wake of the dwindling energy resources in the country as well as the escalating world oil prices.
Ato Simpson another Biofuel expert who works with Onua Amoah told Public Agenda in an interview at the meeting that only six thousand hectares of Jatropha has been planted by their company. He said there is the need to plant about 270,000 hectare to market the fuel in commercial quantities.
Experts say though fossil fuels or petroleum products will slowly be replaced by other forms of energy, the oil industry will continue to play a central role in its substitution.
Natural gas, hydrogen, bio fuels, biomass liquid fuels and liquid gas have all been identified as alternatives to fossil fuels.
In a speech read for him at the Stakeholders meeting, Ghana's Minister for Energy and Mines Mr. Kofi Adda said the falling prices of crude oil in the world market should not stop Ghanaians from looking for alternative sources of fuel in the country, such as biofuel for vehicles, cooking and lighting.
Mr. Adda said extreme dependence on petroleum products involves both risks from the energy security and environmental points of view.
Though crude oil prices have fallen from about 76 dollars a barrel some weeks back to around 62 dollars in recent times, there is no guarantee that the price will drop further, making it more pressing for Ghana to exploit alternative sources of revenue.
"Modern biofuel technology will make us cultivate fuel to grow our economy, protect our available energy source and environment, as well as reduce dependency on imported fuel," he said.
The main resource required for the production of biofuel is land and that is available in Ghana compared to other African countries. "We have abundant arable land and a stable climate good for growing energy crops and food."
According to him, if the right technological know- how are used, Ghanaian farmers will be proud to say, "We are energy producers."
Ghana he said is blessed with plant resources such as oil palm, maize, sugar cane cassava and Jatropha which can be processed into ethanol and biodiesel.
The Minister further said recent developments of the biofuel technology through institutional linkages has come up with a National Biofuel Policy Recommendations document and licensing requirements for biofuel producers. These documents have been developed as a guideline to encourage entrepreneurs and business developers to transfer some of their laboratory trials to large industrial production and for business, which have already started production to increase their production capacities to usher in the biofuel era in Ghana.
Mr. Adda suggested institutional training, research and human resource development, science and technology to ensure the smooth running of the biofuel projects in Ghana.
"It is only drastic implementation decisions and commitment that can bring biofuel at parity with fossil fuel in terms of availability, cost effectiveness, technology, quality of service and marketing," he said.
Supporting Adda, the Managing Director of Biodisel 1 Company Mr. Christian Kofi Marfo said he had developed biofuel from soybeans which cost ¢22,000 per gallon.
He proved his point by putting several litres of the soybean fuel in a four wheel drive vehicle and moving it. But the point remains that biofuel from soybeans are more expensive than fuel from Jatropha which will cost about 15,000 cedis per gallon compared to petrol which costs about ¢36,000.
Dr. Gorge Afranie of the Koforidua Polytechnic on his part said there is an urgent need to develop biofuel since petroleum products have come under threat from escalation of prices and disruption of flow.
However Third World Advocacy organizations are worried about the biofuel projects in Ghana and other third world countries.Their beef is that, these projects will benefit advanced countries to the detriment of the developing countries.
According to one of such organizations, the Institute of Science in Society, based in London various European countries have established goals to increase their use of biofuels as a substitute to gasoline and diesel.
For instance the European Union has established that by the year 2010, six percent of fuels will be biofuels and hopes that by 2020 the percentage will increase to eight percent.
Critics say it is unlikely that Europe will dedicate its soils to the growth of these types of crops and as such third world countries will provide the land and cheap labour and the environmental effects caused by large plantations from which the biofuels are grown and refining.
According to the Institute, companies dedicated to the business of biodiesel have placed their sights on Latin American, African, Asian and Pacific countries, since they consider that these countries can obtain raw material at competitive prices.
According to declarations made by the CEO of the DI Oils, a renewable Energy company in the UK, they are working with plantations of crops known as Jatropha for the production of biodiesel from Ghana to the Philippines, passing through India, Madagascar and South Africa. Up till now they have established 26,000 hectare and have the intention of expanding to nine million Hectares in the future.
UK-based DI Oils predicted in 2004 that the world market for biodiesel would grow by 14.5 percent annually to 2.79 million tonnes by 2010. Currently, Brazil is said to be the largest supplier of biofuel to the UK.
Countries such as Thailand, India, Guatemala, Mali, Cambodia Tanzania, Argentina, Malawi and Madagascar are all doing studies or are benefiting from biofuel projects.

Original_Search_Title: OVOP to Open Shops
Headline: Malawi: OVOP to Open Shops
Body_Text:
Lilongwe — The One Village One Product (OVOP) secretariat will soon establish shops in Blantyre and Lilongwe where all OVOP products will be stocked and sold to members of the public, The Chronicle has learnt.
The new OVOP National Coordinator, Kamia Kaluma Sulumba told The Chronicle in an interview that the shops will be opened so that farmers have easy access to a market for their products.
"Most of the farmers face problems in marketing their products as they fail to link up with potential buyers. Eventually they end up selling their products at very low prices. By opening these shops, they will be linked easily with potential buyers," said Sulumba.
She said some products under the OVOP concept are already on the market and are being sold in leading supermarkets, especially in the capital Lilongwe.
Sulumba explained that OVOP would like producers to get a little more from their primary products by processing them into secondary products so they can compete on the market.
"We encourage and support the farmers to add value to their products so they can fetch higher prices on the market," she said adding the secretariat is currently assisting various OVOP groups in packaging their products.
She said her office has also helped to market the Malawian OVOP products to international buyers at the African Fair held last September in Tokyo, Japan where Malawi was represented by Minister of Local Government and Rural Development, George Chaponda.
"A lot of people at the fair expressed some interest in buying our products and very soon we will be exporting them," said Sulumba.
Augustine Phiri, a farmer from Nankhaka Cassava group under OVOP in Lilongwe welcomed the idea of opening shops saying their products would now be readily available on the market.
He said his group produces banana wine, cassava flour, cakes, biscuits and other products from its Mbwandimbwandi gardens.
"We have nice products but unfortunately, not a lot of people know them. By opening the shops, we will eventually be known on the market," said Phiri.
He said the Malawi Bureau of Standards has already been to the farm and has approved and certified the products.
He said some of the products from his farm are being stocked in Seven Eleven Supermarkets in Lilongwe.
"Sometimes we market the products to people in various offices but then we can not reach out to everyone. With the opening of the shops, our products will reach a lot more people," said Phiri.
The One Village One Product project is an initiative copied from Japan under which local communities identify one or a few locally unique products, concentrate resources on its production and sell it to the domestic market or beyond.
The Malawi Government has embraced the OVOP concept as one of its main programmes to reduce poverty and engineer economic growth to transform the lives of Malawi's largely rural population.

Original_Search_Title: Scrapping School Tax Not Enough
Headline: Uganda: Scrapping School Tax Not Enough
Body_Text:
Kampala — Last week during a function at St. Lawrence School, President Yoweri Museveni said the Government was working on scrapping income tax on educational institutions.
Currently, schools are paying a 30% tax on their profits like bars or discotheques, which provide no far reaching strategic benefit to the country.
This is an inspired intervention and one that is easily 20 years over due. The schools can re-invest the saved money into improving infrastructure, paying better salaries or pass on the savings to the parents.
Under-developed economies that transformed into industrial power houses have had massive investments in education as a key factor in effecting transformation.
Education is the great social leveller by which millions can improve their lot and that of their families.
On average, the better educated a person is, the better their income prospects and therefore the healthier they are and the more productive they will be.
The Government's Universal Primary Education (UPE) is therefore a step in the right direction.
However, despite its best intentions, the Government has failed to keep pace with demand.
Over the last 10 years, there has been a proliferation of private schools. These have mopped up the surplus students government institutions are unable to carry but also through increased competition have improved their facilities while maintaining affordable fees.
The cancellation of income tax recognises the role the private sector plays in bridging the gap the Government has failed to fill. It also recognises the crucial role education plays in transforming economies from an agricultural, peasant-led to industrial, middle class-driven economies.
But the Government can and should do more.
A study carried out in the 1990s commissioned by the Uganda Investment Authority (UIA) showed that Uganda has a comparative advantage over its regional neighbours in this sector.
A comparative advantage suggests a country has the natural endowments to be competitive in a market but greater input - capital and strategic thinking, need to be injected to realise this competitive advantage.
Scraping direct tax is one of the many interventions the Government can make to promote the education sector's competitiveness.
To start with, the Government needs to come up with a strategy with an end to fulfiling our competitive advantage in education.
Naturally, such a strategy will include a commitment to greater public spending in the sector, a well laid out incentives regime for investors and plans to continuously improve the curriculum to reflect the changing world environment in which our products and services have to compete.
In addition, the Government needs to promote the notion that institutions need to cooperate in ensuring best practices in management, teaching and research by sharing in order to increase the sector's competitiveness.
Through sharing, schools will also be able to negotiate more effectively for sectoral government interventions.
The formation of clusters is important in enhancing competitiveness. It is the main reason we have software development in Silicon Valley, car manufacturing in Detroit, the wine lands of Italy, the fashion centres of France and ship building in South Korea.
The collective momentum of clusters can propel the constituent companies along. And so it can for Uganda's education sector. A collective effort to improve the standards sector-wide will make Uganda a byword for education excellence and easier to market to potential investors.
These interventions can be replicated in the Information and Communications Technology (ICT), health, finance, tourism and agriculture sectors.
At the military training school in Singo, Luweero more than 10 years ago, a group of more than 1,000 of the brightest high school graduates were asked what Uganda's cash crops were.
The traditional answers came fast and thick, "coffee tea cotton ". The silence was deafening when the instructor added "maize cassava matooke millet..." to the list.
The point was made, any crop is a cash crop as long as there is a market for it.
Education services are fast taking over from traditional exports as the country's top forex earner.
We should be more proactive and not rely solely on divine providence to take advantage of this opportunity.

Original_Search_Title: Bunyoro a Potential National Food Basket
Headline: Uganda: Bunyoro a Potential National Food Basket
Body_Text:
Kampala — Morris Baguma whistles as he harvests comb after comb of maize from his shamba. His 20-acre shamba is located in Kinyabutunzi village, Bugahya county in Hoima district. His wife and farm helper follow him, clearing away whatever comb Baguma might have failed to pick. This is one of the most fertile areas of Bunyoro. "I am going to dry the maize, then sell it later to the buyers from the city," he says.
Bunyoro is one of the areas in Uganda that are gifted with fertile soils. Where as Ankole can only grow bananas and keep cattle, Bunyoro can grow cereals including maize, sorghum, groundnuts, millet and rice, and keep all kinds of animals. Potatoes, Cassava and other food crops can all sustainably be grown in Bunyoro. It can also grow tobacco and sugar canes. There is also fish from Lake Albert. Cereals are mainly grown in the fertile areas of Bugahya in Hoima, Buyanja, Buliisa and areas that formerly formed part of Budongo forest in Masindi.
"I grow more than 15 acres of maize every year," says Achilles Ahebwa, of Byeriima in Buyanja County, Masindi District. However, many other farmers in the area cannot grow maize. Ahebwa is lucky that about one year ago, he received a set of ox-ploughs from Bunyoro-Kitara Diocese that enabled him increase acreage. There are about 50 farmers in the area with big acreage of uncultivated land.
"If every farmer in our area gets at least a pair of ox-ploughs, we shall have an increase in maize produced per a year," Ahabwe says. Even with the lack of mechanisation or better farm tools, maize farmers in Buyanja produce an average of 50 bags (5,000kg) each. But poor infrastructure and lack of maize processing factories means much of the maize is sold in the villages at as low as sh100 per kilogramme. Much of the maize is bought by traders from Kampala's suburbs such as Kisenyi.
"I wish we could get our own flour-making factories. Factories that will pay us more than we are being paid at the moment," Joram Bagadya of Buliisa says. If maize farmers in Bunyoro can get just a small percentage of the kind of mechanisation in say Kapchorwa, farmers here would be better off.
"Goats and other animals can also be kept in Bunyoro," says Reverend Jonathan Kajura of Hoima Diocese, who is in charge of a small ruminants project to help multiply the number of domestic animals kept in Bunyoro. At the moment, the project has about 70 goats. "We are going to expand it and turn it into a breeding centre for goats," he says.
Throughout the hills and plains of Bunyoro, goats and sheep graze gracefully by the roadside. However, no single person owns more than 100 or more. Mobilisation for large scale animal keeping has been low.
"Goats and pigs can be kept here in large numbers, but we don't have them. We only have a few," Victor Samuel, a fisherman on the shores of Lake Albert, says. Deep in his Rift Valley homestead, he is keeping 20 goats, sheep and pigs.
Hoima district chairman, George William Bagonza, says plans are underway to multiply the number of small ruminants in the district. Bunyoro's local chicken are rated among the best in laying eggs and nursing their offsprings to maturity. David Byaruhanga, a NAADS extension worker and farmer from Bugambe Sub-County in Hoima District, says Bunyoro chicken can be turned into a real gold mine.
"We have started new technologies of rearing Bunyoro indigenous chicken that will help bring out the untapped potential," Byaruhanga explains. His new technology involves keeping chicken in enclosures as they lay eggs. The chicken are synchronised to lay more eggs than they normally do, thus hatch more chicks than usual. Byaruhanga is hopeful that with this improved local chicken technology, Banyoro will benefit more from their famed chicken.
Fish from Lake Albert is not processed; it is sold to the local market. However, according to Bagonza, several fish processing companies have expressed interest in setting up a fish processing factory on the shores of Lake Albert. Lake Albert is the second largest lake in Uganda, and the second largest producer of fish after Lake Victoria.
However, most of this fish is consumed locally and cheaply without any added value, just because there are no fish processing factories there.
"Some Koreans have expressed interest in setting up a fish processing factory here," says Bagonza. Once this is done, fishermen will earn a little more. Compared to Lake Victoria that has over 17 fish processing factories, Lake Albert has none!
"Our target now is to mobilise investors to come here and start factories that will help process these foods and add value," Baguma says. His counterpart in Masindi, John Birija, has similar dreams. The three obstacles that are delaying Bunyoro from becoming a national food basket are lack of mechanisation, lack of value adding factories and poor infrastructure.

Original_Search_Title: 'Nigeria Has Waxed Stronger Under Obasanjo'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Nigeria Has Waxed Stronger Under Obasanjo'
Body_Text:
Yola — As the governorship race in Adamawa State enters the crucial and last lap, one of the front runners for Dougerie lodge, the seat of power in the state, Dr. Jonathan Silas Zwingina, who is also the Senate Deputy Leader, spoke with our correspondent, Matthew Onah in Yola, few minutes after picking his nomination form
Sir, you are the first candidate to pick the PDP governorship nomination form in Adamawa State, why the rush?
I have been preparing myself for the challenge of leadership at the gubernatorial stage for sometime and have laid all the necessary structures that will see to a successful campaign for the office of the Governor of Adamawa State. My campaign structure is one of the most effective and I am happy to be the first to pick the nomination form.
I shall also be the first in the elections God willing. You will notice that I am also the only candidate, picking the form personally, all other candidates sent in their campaign directors. That shows how serious I am.
What do the Adamawa electorate expect from you as their Governor come 2007, if elected
You know, all my life have revolved around Adamawa State, I have been a mobilisation director, a member of the executive council of the state, I was a lecturer in the college of preliminary studies, now the state polytechnic, so I am most conversant with the problems and prospect of Adamawa State. More importantly considering my vast experience, capabilities and network. I stand a better chance of harnessing our endowments for the transformation of our state.
I have reduced my programme to a thirteen point agenda, which covers every facet of our life. First you know, there can only be development in an atmosphere of peace and security, my administration's cardinal agenda is to restore freedom, equity and fairness. Ensure equity in the treatment of all citizens irrespective of religion, tribe or class. I shall cooperate with the security agencies through an enhancement programme that would boost their capacities to check crime.
Secondly, Adamawa State is endowed with one of the most verse farmland in this country, I shall institute programmes that would revamp our agriculture. Procure fertilizers and agricultural machineries and make it available to our peasant farmers at subsidized rate. I shall set a yearly target for the production of cassava for export as well as wheat, sorghum, rice, maize and yam.
On education, our educational infrastructures shall be modernized, emphasis shall be laid on science and female education. I shall concentrate on information communication technology ICT to enable us keep pace with other parts of the world.
What is your relationship with other stakeholders in the Peoples' Democratic Party (PDP) in the State?
I have a cordial relationship with all. We are all favourably disposed to each other. You know, this is democracy. People chose who leads, most of the crisis in the political parties is because, leaders are imposed. We in Adamawa PDP would allow free and fair democratic process in the choice of who emerges as our candidate.
Nigeria is 46 years now, how do you foresee the future of democracy?
The future of Nigeria is great. We have a very bright future. We are more united now than ever before, and this is through the singular leadership of President Olusegun Obasanjo. He has through actions and programmes stabilize the country. There is a feeling of oneness and togetherness for the first time, since independence, nobody is talking about succession again. The North, and East are all talking of integration and cohesion. Only fringe elements are disgruntled. There is more business and social integration than ever. Federal institutions like federal character commission have ensure that there is no undue domination of any tribe in federal institutions like the armed forces and civil service. The constitution has guaranteed secularity of religion.
In spite of minority elements who have been trying to create discomfort in the system in engaging in violence, mob attack, oil bunkering, armed robbery and religious zealotry in north and south, Nigeria is emerging stronger and stronger through the leadership of President Obasanjo. His reform programmes have effectively position Nigeria to maximize her potential and develop.
What, in your opinion, 2007 election going to be like
The process leading to the 2007 elections and the elections itself would strengthen democracy in the country. On our part, the National Assembly have passed the electoral act of 2005, which, if, followed to the letter and spirit will curb electoral malpractices and ensure a free and fair elections. It sets a limit on electoral expenditure, therefore checking cash and carry politics, it has also criminalize certain aspects of our politics. All the violence and built up arms are just apprehension of the unknown. There is an unjust fear or insecurity of the electoral process. However we would ensure that there is transparency in the electoral process. Once there is transparency, confidence building machineries would restore credibility, and consequently peace. There should be no interference from any quarter. Let the people decide.
The federal government should also beef up the capacity of the Nigerian Police Force and other security agencies to meet up with the challenges of effectively policing a multi-dimensiona,country like Nigeria

Original_Search_Title: SCOUL Can Look Elsewhere for Sugarcane Plantation!
Headline: Uganda: SCOUL Can Look Elsewhere for Sugarcane Plantation!
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE debate started by President Yoweri Museveni to degazett part of Mabira forest to SCOUL to boost sugar production, investment, jobs, and tax revenue should not be rested until consensus based on valid facts is reached.
While launching the Government Media Centre two months ago, Museveni said he had asked NEMA to give him unique benefits that Mabira adds to the environment so he could drop his proposal. Now, there have been very many commentaries from business, and ecological sides but the piece by Mr. Gaster Kiyingi, PR manager of the National Forestry Authority "Why emulate a cannibal who eats his children?" published on October 3 was excellent and informative, which I hope the president has read, and should be accepted.
Kiyingi gave and illustrated not only the ecological benefit but included the monetary costs as well. I believe that the NRM sympathisers, supporters, activists and officials who enthusiastically support Museveni should lead opposition to this proposal, and offer alternatives options instead of degrading Mabira. We should do this first to show there are divergent views to Museveni's within the NRM, and that he listens to well- informed, intended, and articulated facts.
The opposition, and external critics must be denied any credibility and political capital in defending this issue because NRM supporters are just sitting on their hands with mouths shut! Although not an environmental expert like Ken Lukyamuzi or Afuunaddula, and as we wait for NEMA's red-tape response, I know that Mabira, and the Mpanga, Kyasonzi, Muduma, and Nawandigi clusters in Mpigi district, which are already degraded are the only natural tropical rain forests in the central region (Buganda).
The others are the little dots along Lake Victoria shores, while the whole of Bulemezi and Singo are just shrubs. This alone is enough caution to Museveni to tread carefully on Mabira because there is no evidence that government or private individuals are planting forest covers in Buganda.
Degazetting Mabira could set Budongo, Maramagambo, Kibale, or Bwindi impenetrable forests as the next target to investors who want land on the cheap!
Reliable sources say that the trade ministry is routing for SCOUL to take Mabira because dealers in timber, firewood, charcoal, and peasant crop cultivators have already degraded the forest, and therefore no problem giving to a major investor! This view ought to be dismissed because it was government failure that led to encroachment, and it is still its duty evict them so Mabira regenerates.
Forest encroachers are illegal occupants and are not protected by any law just like those who had settled on the Kampala-Entebbe road reserve who were evicted and their property demolished.
The initial burden should be on the Ministry of Finance, and Economic Planning, National Planning Authority, Uganda Investment Authority (UIA), and SCOUL to bring out data that degazetting forests for sugarcane growing is indeed a viable and sustainable option, and that there are no other alternatives.
Some experts on sugar technology from Brazil, Cuba, Malawi, Kenya, and Sudan have recently stated that Uganda uses obsolete methods in addition to poor variety cane leading to low productivity per hectare. And yet for months SCOUL has chosen to keep quiet instead of challenging this view if it were false!
Fred Babweteera, a plant scientist writing from the University of Oxford actually referred us to a FAO website showing Uganda to have the lowest yield per hectare at 12.8 t/ha, Kenya at 86 t/ha, Sudan 85 t/ha, Malawi 105 t/ha. If land is SCOUL's major problem, let them plant and nurture to maturity an equivalent area of artificial forest cover of the part given to them from Mabira in either Nakasongola, Iganga, Bugiri, Tororo, Kumi, Bukedea or Soroti, which are bare.
Even if these figures were not accurate, SCOUL can contract sugarcane outgrowers within Mukono, and the neighbouring districts of Jinja, Kayunga, Wakiso, Luwero, Mpigi and Nakasongola. In fact this would be in line with government's overall economic strategy of empowering households with reliable income.
The president has said that he would not want SCOUL to acquire land far away from its factory at Lugazi, as this increases production costs.
This may be true only if SCOUL and government does not plan road and railway network. Importers of sugar from other continents make profits in Uganda, and so SCOUL can as well provided the other factors of production such as reliable energy, water, communication, skilled manpower, and transport network costs are sustainable, and this is what government ought to do, not cutting down forests.
Government has leased out the railway network hopefully to an able company that will refurbish old lines and build new ones so that SCOUL, Kinyara, and Kakira find it affordable, convenient and reliable to transport sugarcane from as far as Amuru, Atiak, and Patiko, and Aswa where the line to Southern Sudan is supposed to pass.
Before 1986, Ugandans believed that Ankole, which is 400km away could only bring to Kampala cattle for slaughter because it was then the main market. But today with Buganda crying for Federo instead of production, potatoes, matooke, milk, tomatoes, cabbages, and even onions come from as far as Kisoro, Kabale, Kanungu, Kasese, and Ruhinda in Bushenyi making profits, and yet no one advocates that these farmers and traders be protected from long distances!
Similarly, producers of cassava, sweet potatoes, groundnuts and millet from Kitgum, Gulu, Lira, Amuria, Katakwi, and Bukwa districts make money by bringing raw products often more than 400km to Kampala.

Original_Search_Title: 'Grow Cassava in Drought Prone Areas'
Headline: Zimbabwe: 'Grow Cassava in Drought Prone Areas'
Body_Text:
Harare — FARMERS should grow cassava in drought prone areas where the crop complements maize and small grains.
Cassava farming has low production costs as it does not require a lot of fertilizer, grows well under high temperatures and poor soils but fetches more money on the market while it can also be used for domestic and industrial consumption.
Vice President Joice Mujuru launched the cassava project in November 2005 in Magunje, Mashonaland West, to increase food production after intermittent droughts.
In an interview, the director of community development in the Ministry of Women Affairs, Gender and Community Development, Mr Christopher Chingosho said seedbeds had already been established and some farmers had begun planting.
The farming season for cassava is from October to December.
"The community has accepted the crop but our problem is that we do not have enough seed to meet the demand," said Mr Chingosho.
He said cassava had more benefits compared to other crops.
Cassava can be made into sadza, chips and flour for bread, and the leaves can be eaten as vegetables or for stockfeeds and can produce glucose, which can be used in place of sugar.
It can also be used for binding papers, for paste in the textile industry, to cure foot and mouth disease in livestock and in the industrial production of Ethanol as a source of fuel.
"Our goal is not to end at planting cassava and consume it locally but we also want to venture into processing and export the crop to other countries especially China where it is needed," said Mr Chingosho.
He said cassava was not prone to diseases because it could stay underground for more than 36 months and that the market was readily available.
"We are making efforts to source seeds from our neighbouring countries like Mozambique, Malawi, Zambia and the Democratic Republic of Congo who are at an advanced stage than us but our major setback is lack of money," he said.
Mr Chingosho said major setbacks were lack of transport to ferry the seeds to the farmers, lack of manpower, office equipment especially computers and residential accommodation for staff.
"We are appealing to donor agencies for help in buying seed," said Mr Chingosho.

Original_Search_Title: Loud Pat On the Back for Ministry of Health
Headline: Uganda: Loud Pat On the Back for Ministry of Health
Body_Text:
The Ministry of Health pulled off a major public relations coup this week when it ordered the Police to impound vehicles belonging to the ministry that were being used outside official business hours. By the end of the weekend the police had "harvested" eighty vehicles, some found loaded with charcoal, cassava tubers, beans still on their stems, live chicken and matooke among others.
To manage this highly commendable action, the Permanent Secretary Mr Muhammed Kezaala picked from the shelf a dusty government standing order issued by the Ministry of Public Service about three years ago to fight abuse of government vehicles.
The standing order restricted use of government vehicles to working hours (8:00am-5:00pm) strictly on working days. But in flagrant and criminal disregard of that instruction, many public vehicles bearing red number plates are continuously found parked in places like bars, pork joints, lodges and hostels at the most odd hours of the night.
That the Ministry of Health has led in implementation of the order is a first and it's a wake up call for all the other departments to quickly follow suit. Its also throws down the gauntlet to the Police who have apparently been reluctant to enforce this useful government order. The police did not have to wait for Kezaala's directive to effect observance of the law.
The cost of maintaining the fleet of more than 11,000 publicly-owned vehicles (of which ministry of health alone accounts for 3,000) is too high at Shs23 billion every year for a cash-strapped country like Uganda. Mark you, a government report released earlier gave the rare indication that transporting government employees was bleeding the country.
Discipline in usage of facilities given to workers especially within the civil service and elsewhere is indisputably the first step towards effective service delivery, which is in very short supply in this country.
The action by the police to impound the vehicles must not be a one off but a continuous exercise until a culture of self-discipline is re-instituted amongst government workers - and inculcated in the current Public Sector Reform Programme. The logical next step should be to punish the offending officers. They must explain whether the live chicken they were carrying was emergency medicine for the thousands of dying patients who have no access to hospitals or drugs because facilities are being misused.
The nature of sanctions taken must be made public and thereafter the general public should be encouraged to become a kind of vigilante where they start reporting all breaches.
The government should also move and do a cost-benefit analysis of its fleet.

Original_Search_Title: Mosaic Destroys Cassava in Lango
Headline: Uganda: Mosaic Destroys Cassava in Lango
Body_Text:
Kampala — CASSAVA mosaic has attacked cassava plants in Lango, causing concern among farmers over the fear of looming hunger.
Farmers in Lira, Apac, Oyam, Amolatar and Dokolo districts fear that the disease that has stunted the growth of cassava plants might result in famine.
"The disease attacks the leaves of plants as early as two weeks after emerging from the soil," Ojok-Atwali said.
Dickens Okulla said the plants attacked by disease do not bear tubers. "We are not reaping from what we sowed. Our sweat is not yielding anything," Margie Ajungu said.
Albino Oponi said the disease has caused famine, as cassava is a staple food crop in the region. He said rodents, pigs and monkeys were also a threat as they eat the tubers.
Ben Emer, an agricultural officer, said cassava mosaic can be detected when one sees mottled yellow leaves and it causes stunted growth. He said the disease is caused by a virus and spreads by planting infected plants.
Emer said destroying infected plants would control mosaic. He advised farmers to plant mosaic resistant varieties, which they can obtain from extension workers and also look out for uninfected cuttings.
He advised farmers to seek advice from their sub-county extension workers for proper farming methods and practices.
Cassava is a biennial crop that matures from 6 months to 1.5 years depending on the variety.
It has two varieties; bitter cassava with dark green leaves, red veins that contain cyanide. This makes it bitter and poisonous. The second type is sweet cassava, the preferred variety because of its sweet taste.
Emer said cassava requires well-drained fertile soils and planting be done at the beginning of the main rains, with spacing of 1.5m by 1m.

Original_Search_Title: 13 Candidates to Challenge Incumbent President Ravalomanana in Polls
Headline: Madagascar: 13 Candidates to Challenge Ravalomanana in Madagascar Polls
Body_Text:
Madagascar will complete southern Africa's 2006 calendar for presidential elections with its 3 December poll that has attracted 13 challengers to face incumbent President Marc Ravalomanana.
Nominations for candidacy were confirmed by the country's constitutional high court on 19 October. Three other applicants were rejected.
Since coming to power in 2002, Ravalomanana embarked on widespread economic and social reforms that stirred the southern African country out of decades of recession. He has won the support of Bretton Woods institutions - the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund (IMF) - who are supporting his economic liberalisation policies.
In 2004, Ravalomanana led a bid for his country to join the Southern African Development Community (SADC). The Indian Ocean island was formally accepted into the SADC family at the 25th Summit in Botswana in 2005.
Although current poverty levels are high, Madagascar's economic potential is enormous. The country is the largest producer of vanilla in the world in value terms, but its farmers have suffered from perpetually low commodity prices.
With an economy largely dependent on agriculture - which along with fishing and forestry contributes at least 25 percent of Gross Domestic Product (GDP) - the island also produces coffee, cassava, bananas, maize, sugarcane, potatoes and rice.
Mining and tourism sectors have shown signs of recovery and the government has projected increases in mineral exports from about US$100 million to US$150 million per year over the next 10 years.
The biggest boost to the Malagasy economy has been the discovery of oil which has recently triggered a scramble by international oil conglomerates. Initial projections are that the country could produce 60,000 barrels per day in three to four years. With potential revenue of billions of dollars, this oil boom would make the industry the biggest contributor to GDP.
With intra-regional trade in SADC expected to increase from the current 25 percent of all international trade to as high as 60 percent by 2008, Ravalomanana's policies have placed his country at a vantage point to benefit from the expanded regional economic activity.
He will be seeking re-election to form a new government that will consolidate the gains achieved so far.
However, tension has been rising in the country ahead of the elections. Opposition parties have used the rise in inflation - that stands at about 30 percent - as a campaign weapon against President Ravalomanana's bid for a second term of office.
The election comes against a background of a controversial aftermath of the 2002 poll in which two rival candidates claimed presidency, threatening security in the Indian Ocean island.
Ravalomanana's main opponent in the forthcoming poll would have been exiled opposition leader Pierrot Rajaonarivelo who was endorsed as presidential candidate for the Association of the Rebirth of Madagascar (AREMA) party, formerly under Didier Ratsiraka.
Ratsiraka lost the presidency following the 2002 poll but did not immediately concede defeat leading to a seven-month political crisis, which is widely blamed for a 12 percent drop in GDP that year.
He later fled to France in the same year, paving the way for restoration of order on the island.
Rajaonarivelo, who faces prison at home, was unable to return to register for the polls. His lawyers filed papers in his absence but the court ruled that the law requires the presence of the applicant.
Rajaonarivelo, who was convicted of misuse of funds in absentia, has been threatened with arrest as soon as he returns to Madagascar. He was sentenced to 15 years with hard labour and also banned from holding public office.
Rajaonarivelo served as vice-prime minister under Ratsiraka who has remained an influential figure in the AREMA party.
Formerly an independent kingdom, Madagascar became a French colony in 1886, but regained its independence in 1960.  The island state is located east of Mozambique.
Madagascar, whose official languages include French and Malagasy, has a population of about 17 million people.
This year, presidential elections in the SADC region were held in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) and Zambia, the latter holding its polls on 28 September.
The DRC had its initial presidential election on 31 July while the run-off is set for 29 October.
The run-off follows failure by the two front-runners - incumbent Joseph Kabila and Jean Pierre Bemba - to amass the required 50 percent-plus-one of the vote, in line with the constitution.  sardc.net

Original_Search_Title: Mukono Classrooms Collapse
Headline: Uganda: Mukono Classrooms Collapse
Body_Text:
Kampala — TWO classroom blocks at different schools in Mukono district collapsed last week due to heavy rains, John Semakula reports.
The schools are Living Hope Nursery and Primary School in Nagojje sub-county and Kiwafu Primary School in Kimmenyedde sub-county.
According to the bursar of Living Hope, the building collapsed on Monday during a downpour.
Deborah Nanfuka said 18 pupils were injuried and rushed to Kawolo Hospital.
The LC1 chairman, Emmanuel Suna, said the heavy rain destroyed cassava, potato, beans and maize gardens.
At Kiwafu, a parent at the school, Hajji Hassan Mugerwa, said the building, which housed four classes, collapsed on Thursday evening.
No pupil was injured as they had all returned home.

Original_Search_Title: Dar Group Buys Mozambican Textile Factory
Headline: Tanzania: Dar Group Buys Mozambican Textile Factory
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A memorandum of understanding between IGEPE and MeTL was signed in April and later approved by the Mozambican government, writes FAUSTINE RWAMBALI One of the biggest business outfits in East Africa, Mohammed Enterprises Tanzania Ltd (MeTL) has acquired Texmoque, a giant textile factory in the northern Mozambican of Nampula.
The MeTL managing director, Mohammed Dewji told The EastAfrican last week that MeTL will invest $20 million in rehabilitation and modernisation of the textile factory in Mozambique, adding that production will resume by mid 2007 with a workforce of 800 people.
Documents formalising the transfer of ownership were signed in Maputo on October 6, by Silvester Sechene, the director of the Mozambican government Institute for the Management of State Holdings.
Texmoque, a state-owned textile mills, closed its operations in 1994. Multiplier, a Portuguese company acquired 70 per cent of its shares in 1996 and took over its management. However, it failed to revive the unit and start production for various reasons, including lack of investment and a non-conducive atmosphere for the textile industry in Mozambique at the time.
IGEPE, the state agency, was given the task of looking for a suitable investor to revive Texmoque and sent a delegation to Tanzania in 2005.
MeTL's experience in reviving privately owned textile mills at Afritex Ltd, Tanga and 21st Century Textiles Ltd, in Morogoro seemed to impress the Mozambican government delegation.
The two factories were closed for many years but were revived by MeTL. They are now operating at near full capacity.
A memorandum of understanding between IGEPE and MeTL was signed in April and later approved by the Mozambican government. The sale agreement was signed on June 29 and the handover took place on October 9.
Mr Dewji said that MeTL would start the rehabilitation and modernisation work immediately with the production of African prints and later diversify its products to cover a larger local and international market. It also intends to go into ginning and cotton growing in the future.
Founded in the 1980s, MeTL, with a workforce of 5,000 is a group of companies operating a range of industries mainly for export.
It manufactures and markets a wide range of food products, cleansing materials, textiles and entertainment electronics and household appliances. In 1996, the company embarked on a major programme of sisal, cashewnut, cassava, cotton and tea. It now produces wheat flour, maize flour, milled rice, sweetened sugar, juices and fabrics.
Currently, the company is developing a refinery with a fractionation plant and a soap manufacturing plant in Dar es Salaam.
The group now owns and operates 10 different manufacturing companies.
In its agricultural division, MeTL has acquired more than 35,000 hactres of agricultural farmland on 16 different estates in Tanzania, mainly for cashew, tea and sisal. One of its success stories in the textile industry is Afritex Ltd (AL). This is an integrated textile mill with spinning, processing and printing facilities.
The factory has the capacity to produce carded cotton yarns and 8 million metres of dyed and printed material. It was acquired in 2000.

Original_Search_Title: Extending National Development Programmes
Headline: Nigeria: Extending National Development Programmes
Body_Text:
Lagos — THE ongoing national economic reforms are deficient because they do not go far enough to cover all the strategic areas of development and also because they have generated considerable job losses due to retrenchment of workers. Nigerians expect far reaching reforms which would affect other sectors such as the social, religious, judicial and political activities. Even the critical population factor has been left out.
Nigeria has always had a population problem defined as a situation in whch the population is growing faster than real resources. The quantitative aspect of the population problem is that with an assumed population growth rate of about three per cent, the Gross Domestic Product should be of the order of five to eight per cent. The rapid population growth implies that the average Nigeria will remain impoverished and also that some sectors of the economy will be adversely affected. Such sectors include agriculture, employment, education, health and housing. Two years ago, the federal government announced a new population policy.
This purported new policy has neither been publicised nor implemented. For now, the population explosion continues. The proclaimed poverty eradication schemes cannot be effective unless the prevailing baby boom is stemmed. It is not clear why the presidency has been nonchalant about executing the new population policy.
One is not impressed by the haphazard attention given to agriculture in the course of the ongoing economic reforms. Apart from the casual effort to promote the distribution of fertilizer and the enhanced cultivation of cassava, there has no strategic mobilisation for agricultural production. The N50 billion said to be allocated to farm credit has not been accessed to needy farmers who have been crying out in recent weeks to no avail. What is the point of announcing publicly loans made available to farmers when they cannot get them? As we celebrated the World Food Day on October 16, 2006, most citizens are even ignorant of what the current national food policy is. There should be another "Operation Feed the nation" programme comprising mass mobilisaation for food production and accelerated food production scheme. All the citizens in the country should be mobilised and assisted to engage actively in food production.
The starting point in the creation of adequate awareness among the citizenry about the need to engage in the production of one food item or the other. We should resuscitate the "School-to-land" scheme mobilising thousands of unemployed school leavers and assisting them to take to farming. The various public and private sector organisations and institutions including the universities, research institutes and also the military should be motivated to embrace large-scale farming. All available facilities including all fertile lands in various parts of the country should be mobilised and leased to individuals and organisations who demonstrate serious interest in the production of any of the basic crops.
In addition to the cultivation of such crops as rice, maize, sorghum, millet, yam, cassava and soya beans, each state government will be required to promote the production of some basic crops in which it enjoys comparative advantage. State governents should in turn charge local governments to mount special programmes to produce identified crops. Appropriate incentives should be given to both the small farmers and the large scale farmers. Effort should be intensified to develop further the National Centre for Agricultural Mechanisations which has the responsibility for standardisation of farm machinery and equipment and the production of locally designed prototypes. Universities, polytechnics and research institute should be encouraged to accelerate the development of local fabrications capabilities for the production of private sector in the commercialisation of prototypes should also be actively encouraged.
Necessary incentives should be provided to private sector to establish food processing plants in various parts of the country. Small scale farmers should undertake on-farm storage of substantial quantities of agricultural produce to supplement government strategic grains reserve as a means of ensuring national food security. Furthermore, private commodity traders should assume responsibility for the buffer stock scheme as part of the effort to achieve inter-seasonal stabilisation of stocks and prices.
The ongoing economic reforms have not properly addressed the housing crisis in the country. There is pressing need for reviewing the Land Use Act to provide more affordable land for housing and to remove the starched-red-tapism and conservative bureaucracy as well as corruption in the issuance of certificates of occupancy. The concept of sites and services, housing cooperatives and the use of portakabins to augument the dearth of housing stock must be incorporated into the housing system.
Nigerians are astonished that judicial reforms have not been given a paramount place in the ongoing reform processes. This country has one of the worst dilatory procedures for judicial administration in the world. In his book "The judicial process and the third republic," Dr. Akinola Aguda described it as "The slow motion judicial process." He commented: "A judicial process which permits those awaiting trial to remain in prison custody for a period of ten years or those already condemned to be hanged to remain in the death row for fifteen years can have no other effect on the whole government machinery than one of distabilising it." He added: "The incredibly slow wheel of the judicial process in our country has been a matter of comment by many. The partinent point that needs to be made and which must be made very strongly is that while such slow judicial process may be annoying to the elites and the rich and sometimes may be to their advantage, it is oppressive to the socially and economically disadvantaged. One obvious consequence of this is that the socially and economically disadvantaged citizens may decide not to pursue their claims in the courts. If one such citizens is foolhardy enough to attempt the tedious and hazardous journey toward the realisation of an expectation of justice, he may finally discover that he and his family have become bankrupt without achieving their goal." The reduction of backlogs of court cases may be achieved by remarkable increase in the number of magistrates and judges, introduction of suspended sentences, use of verbatim reporters and devising a statistical system to provide the courts with reliable data concerning all aspects of judicial administration.
Beyond the economic reforms, Nigeria needs critical reforms of the anachronistic traditional system. Most of our native laws and customs are obsolete and a hindrance to modern development. Thegovernment of the federation should revisit traditional norms of giving titles, marriages, obsequies and others. The relegation of women as second class citizens must be jettisoned and gender equality firmly established. Treatment of widows should be sanitized and equity in land allocation properly instituted.
With the current breed of politicians, Nigeria will collapse because of their recklessness, corruption and total lack of scruples. Money politics should be exterminated. We should discard the present presidential system and return to the more participative and democratic parliamentary mode of governance, There should be no room for the position of first ladies since it is not covered by the extant constitution. The incessant scheme of political patronage should be rationalised as an anti-corruption strategy.
Nigeria needs an ethical revolution to cleanse our augean stables. There must be a moral regeneration and leadership by example. Every level of society should embark on intensive moral education to sanitise the decaying polity. Churches and mosques should be investigated and monitored to ensure they are really serving as puritanic agents of radical moral transformation.

Original_Search_Title: Staff Shortage Hampers Implementation of Women's Projects
Headline: Zimbabwe: Staff Shortage Hampers Implementation of Women's Projects
Body_Text:
Harare — AN ACUTE shortage of staff, coupled with inadequate funding and motor vehicles, among other required equipment, has hampered the effective implementation of various projects that had been lined up by the Ministry of Women's Affairs, Gender and Community Development.
The ministry has no support staff in various provinces and districts, yet it intends to engage in cassava, sugar, jatropha and poultry projects, among others.
This was revealed by the ministry's finance and administration director, Mr Philip Chizema, while giving oral evidence before the Parliamentary Portfolio Committee on Youth Development, Gender, and Employment Creation.
Mr Chizema said the ministry currently had a staff complement of around 160 against a requirement of more than 1 400.
The majority of the existing staff is stationed at head office whereas most of the ministry's projects are community-based.
The Public Service Commission froze recruitment for all vacant posts from the beginning of the year to August this year, further compounding the problem.
Out of $320 million the ministry bid under this year's budget, it got a paltry $85 million from the fiscus and had spent $55 million by mid year.
"In districts and wards, we do not have support staff like drivers and office orderlies. Many senior positions in the ministry are held by people in an acting capacity and this affects us.
"The issue of vehicles is a major concern. We have not received a positive response from the Ministry of Finance, yet our officers should move around supervising the projects," he said.
For the whole ministry, he said, only the minister, her deputy and the permanent secretary had a computer, printer and fax machine while the whole ministry had no photocopying machine, and needed more than 1 000 computers.
He said the ministry had made its budget estimates hoping that the inflation rate would have dropped to around 200 percent as envisaged by the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe.
The chairperson of the committee, Gutu South legislator Cde Shuvai Mahofa (Zanu-PF), urged the ministry to prioritise staff matters when they prepare for their next budget.
"The issue of staff is very important as it enables the ministry to run. You are lucky in that you get some of your money from donors because you deal with women and youths," she said.
Secretary for the ministry Dr Perpetua Gumbo said they were working towards importing additional cassava cuttings from neighbouring countries such as Zambia, Mozambique, the Democratic Republic of Congo and Tanzania. She said they would have wanted one hectare in every province for cassava by end of June this year but could not achieve this owing to resource constraints.
Growing of cassava would go a long way in ensuring food security for the country since it does not require much rain, she said.
The ministry had lined up various other projects like jatropha, poultry and sugar but they were being hampered by lack of funding.
She said the ministry was committed to elevation of women in decision-making positions and economically empower them.
The ministry has been involved in various campaigns educating the public about the provisions of the Domestic Violence Bill currently before Parliament, said Dr Gumbo.

Original_Search_Title: Redefining Populist Policies
Headline: Nigeria: Redefining Populist Policies
Body_Text:
Lagos — In a recent write up, Mr. Kayode Komolafe noted that: "one of the huge ironies of our time is that some significant steps being taken in the realm of governance are being eclipsed by politics of bitterness". He cited the goings-on at the centre as all pervading and giving the semblance of governance practically being at a stand still. Nothing can be farther from the truth. In actual fact important activities such as the public hearing of the Fiscal Responsibility Bill were going on. In short the politics of bitterness is threatening to engulf us as a nation and overshadow some of the benefits that have been clearly gained from some acts of good governance.
Indeed Komolafe's observation could also be said to be true in some states. High voltage pronouncements by some politicians seeking office in the next years' elections make it sound like the past three - and -a -half years have been for nothing and no gains have been made. In recent times the Ogun State Government has come under attack for what critics describe as non-performance. These pronouncements are based upon what the announcers regard as the non implementation of "populist programmes".
There has been a constant onslaught of fault finding and nit-picking over projects which ordinarily have a mass appeal and by any stretch of the imagination meet developmental standards. But in this season of politicking, meaningful programmes and policies are being couched in less than charitable terms as part of political plans to erode any goodwill that may have been amassed. It may also be an attempt to distort the truth and cast fear and doubts in the minds of the people This of course is all in the name of politics!
Last week, the former Governor of Ogun State, Chief Segun Osoba in an interview with a Sunday newspaper pointed out that his administration had concentrated on populist programmes. He went on to criticise the present government under Governor Gbenga Daniel for focusing on projects such as the farm settlements and the cargo airport.
One has taken the trouble to research on the definition of the word populist. It means: "emphasizing or promoting ordinary people, their lives, or their interests". It appears that the populist programmes being referred to during the former governor's administration centres solely on projects such as water, electricity and roads. These are the kinds of projects popularly known as traditional projects because they have become the norm and are what some politicians promise as the gateway to prosperity and upliftment. Indeed it's a shame that in the 21st Century all that our people are required to expect from their leaders are these so-called traditional projects- Projects that simply promise and boast of the immediate; the here and now. The world has however long moved beyond seeking an immediate alleviation to poverty. The preference these days is for a lasting and enduring solution.
Perhaps for this reason a redefinition of populist programmes is needed to assist those aspiring to political office and other political animals to look beyond the immediate solutions to problems and begin to re-think their strategies and focus on programmes that would provide long term solutions to the social ills.
The Farm Settlements in Ogun State were built during the Awo era over forty years ago. The late sage Chief Obafemi Awolowo clearly saw the wisdom in promoting agriculture as the mainstay of the economy and built the settlements as part of his policy to engage the people in farming. It was part of the golden era of cocoa, oil palm, and the maxim at the time was "life more abundant'. Indeed it was a feature of the broader revolution going on in Western region. These policies had the hallmark of a populist stance because it provided employment and a boost to agriculture within the communities. Sadly the post - Awo years witnessed a departure from agriculture as a main source of revenue and the structures were left to deteriorate.
But how come the policies of farm settlements popularly hailed by Awoists are no longer deemed as populist in character? The truth is that the populist nature of these projects remains undeniable. But due to the season of politicking have instead become a subject of bitter debate as aspirants continue to lambaste the Ogun State Government over its efforts to not only rehabilitate the settlements but to build additional farm estates around the State.
Today, the farm settlements are not only fully operational and functional but provide employment, stimulate wealth and job creation. The settlements have been rehabilitated and have been provided with basic amenities and the occupants are working literally round the clock. For instance the Farm settlement in Ago-Iwoye and other places have been rejuvenated - an initiative of by the current administration after years of neglect by previous (populist?) administrations. The oil palm production has been made easier by the provision of an oil mills, activities going on include piggery, poultry, sheep rearing, and cultivation of crops such as cassava, tomatoes and maize etc. The Farm Estates built in various parts of the State are also thriving. Each one consists of eight residential blocks and possesses the basic amenities such as water and electricity. The Farm Estate in Owowo, Ewekoro Local Government for instance is an example of how the Ogun State Government has successfully implemented its Back-to the-Land Programme to gainfully employ youths in Ogun State and increase agricultural output.
How much more populist can a government get than with these commendable steps taken to refocus on agriculture through the rehabilitation of farm settlements and construction of farm estates? There may be a need for some of our politial leaders to begin to look at the broader picture and elevate their thoughts from the small time narrow view to the bigger and brighter picture. This is not to say that water and electricity projects are not key to our existence. Development practitioners refer to them as life-changing projects and any government worth its salt would do well not to ignore them. They however cannot be the sum total of a society and this is what some of our politicians have failed to understand. The lives of our people can be further enhanced by long-term development projects. Known as life-sustaining projects within development circles, long term projects if handled rightly possess the potential to usher in sustainable development.
Another relevant example of a populist programme that has unfortunately generated angst in some quarters is the regeneration of the Olumo Rock into a tourist centre. That singular act of governance has boosted tourism, provided employment, created wealth and jobs etc. The economic and commercial gains going-on within the immediate environs where the centre is located are undeniable. The Adire market has received a boom in activities and the entire project has tapped into the entrepreneurial spirit of the people. But suppose Olumo Rock did not undergo the process of regeneration? There is no doubt that not only would a great economic and commercial opportunity have been lost but also the chances been created for the people to rediscover themselves would have been lost forever too. The importance of Olumo Rock to the Egbas should not be lost even on Chief Osoba who carries the title of Akinrogun of Egbaland. He can still dignify his position within the communities and be politically gracious to admit that such projects clearly in the populist mould possess the potential to bring in meaningful and lasting changes to our society.
Being populist is not all about continuously focusing on the obvious and seemingly mundane. It's about continuously searching and looking out for opportunities and taking maximum advantage to the benefit of the people. It is certainly a way of promoting the interests and enhancing the lives of the people on a long term basis. Reflecting and acting with the bigger picture in mind says a lot about us as a nation. It is indicative of the fact that we no longer are content to remain at the basic and primordial levels but prepared to take on the risks involved in achieving long term, meaningful and enduring development. This is really what populists programmes may entail as a way of promoting the interests of the people.

Original_Search_Title: Bayelsa, USAID, Shell to Raise $25 Million for N/Delta
Headline: Nigeria: Bayelsa, USAID, Shell to Raise $25 Million for N/Delta
Body_Text:
Abuja — Bayelsa State Government, United States Agency for International Development (USAID/Nigeria) and Shell Petroleum Development Company, would today launch a $25m partnership to improve living standard and social services in the Niger Delta region.
The partnership which is aimed at the people of the state and the Niger Delta would provide $25m for cassava production and processing, aquaculture value chain development, integrated HIV/AIDS service provision, and other development projects to be agreed upon by the partners. Of the total amount, about $10 million has been earmarked specifically for Bayelsa State residents with a view to improving their livelihoods and services available to them.
A statement by the United States Embassy in Abuja yesterday described the partnership with Bayelsa State as a welcome development stating that "Bayelsa State is a committed partner in development and dedicated provider of public services to its people."
According to the statement, "With this partnership, USAID, Shell, and the Government of Bayelsa State will be contributing to the development objectives of improving livelihoods and improved health services. Through the agreed-upon activities, the partners will foster economic empowerment and improved health standards in Bayelsa State and the Niger Delta."
USAID has been working in Nigeria since the 1960s and provides $80-$100 million annually in development assistance to Nigeria in the areas of democracy and governance, agricultural development and economic growth, basic education, reproductive health and child survival, and HIV/AIDS.

Original_Search_Title: We'll Meet Food Needs, Says Kirwa
Headline: Kenya: We'll Meet Food Needs, Says Kirwa
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenya will meet the Millennium Development Goal on food sufficiency, the Government has announced.
Agriculture minister, Mr Kipruto arap Kirwa said a raft of programmes were on course to meet the date of 2015.
As part of the efforts, the Government has set aside Sh200 million to purchase certified seeds to distribute to farmers free of charge, Kirwa said on Thursday.
The country is on the "right path on food security," Kirwa said, adding in last year's drought, half of the affected districts benefited from national food reserves.
"The country is on track as far as the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) on food security are concerned," said a confident Kirwa. A further Sh200 million will be released to reintroduce the "orphan crops," like millet and sorghum.
"Orphan crops because they are not commercial we are promoting them like cassava which has doubled in acreage in Western province," Kirwa said.
Other measures to arrive at the goal include controlling birds destroying grains worth million in the field.
The Government plans to spend Sh60 million in controlling birds to salvage grains worth Sh1 billion.

Original_Search_Title: I Swear, I Didn't Betray My Boss, Says Akala
Headline: Nigeria: I Swear, I Didn't Betray My Boss, Says Akala
Body_Text:
Ibadan — After 9 months on the saddle as the Oyo state helmsman, controversy still trails the emergence of Otunba Christopher Adebayo Alao-Akala, erstwhile deputy to the impeached Governor of Oyo State Senator, Rashidi Ladoja. In an encounter with Ademola Adeyemo in Ibadan Akala explains the circumstances that surrounded the impeachment saga and how he emerged as 'governor'
Otunba Adebayo Alao Akala was at pains when he met with political journalists as he tried to explain the circumstances that led to his emergence as the 'Governor' of Oyo state following the controversial impeachment of his former boss Senator Rashidi Ladoja. Akala, a retired Senior Police Officer and former local government council chairman who said he was bombarded with barrages of questions over the moral implications of his ascendance to the office of the Governor said it was not the first time a governor would be removed and his deputy will step in. " I wondered why all these noise about me. There was nothing peculiar about my emergence as 'Governor' that should warrant all these attacks, Balarabe Musa was impeached in the second republic, " Akala explained that as deputy governor he was extremely loyal to his boss to the extent that he narrowly escaped death three times in the hands of those who planned Ladoja's removal.
Swearing that he did not betray his former boss Akala explained that he was fully behind Ladoja when the political crisis started between Alhaji Lamidi Adedibu and Ladoja group . According to him the crisis got to a stage that the former governor could not attend party functions again for fear of attack "As a deputy governor I was loyal to my former boss because as Policeman I was trained to be loyal, I knew there was a governor who was the chief executive and I am supposed to take instructions from him, I don't do anyting unless my boss ordered me . Even when the problem started my boss was no longer coming out I was the one attending most of the functions"
assing that he was attacked in Osogbo at a party function. " Even in Abuja during our party's convention at the Eagle's Square, I was attacked by Adedibu loyalists. Ladoja did not attend I led Oyo state's delegation, I faced the attack , I was beaten up, I came back home without my glasses. "
Akala also declared that he became the 'governor' because those plotted the impeachment did not have a choice, " It was the constitutional provison that made me the governor, If the planners had a choice they would have preffered that one of theem should be governor but you see they did not have a choice in the matter"
Asked if there was no element of betrayal in the impeachment saga Akala who got up from his seat said " I swear in the name of God that I did not betray my former boss. What is the meaning of betrayal, I swear I did not hold meeting with anybody for the purpose of removing my boss or making me a governor , If they have a choice one of them would have been here now ?"
According to him, he had gone into hiding a month before his swearing in as a governor for fear of been killed by the political opponents and had to come out for the swearing-in after a lot of pressure and assurance of safety "I fled Ibadan on 2nd of December 2005 after the Governors Office was bombarded, my office was attacked, live bullets were shot at my office many things got damaged. My experience as a Police officer helped me to escape otherwise I would have been killed. On the day of impeachment they were looking for me I was afraid to come out because of my experience of Dec 2, they had to use a very good friend of mine who is a member of House of Representative to get me from hiding, on my way to Ibadan that day I had to pass through Osun state first before coming to Ibadan out of fear."
Akala also said that despite what happened the relationship between him and his his former boss was still cordial. "He knows that I was loyal to him and that I was not involved in his removal and in any case there is no permanent enemy or friend in politics. Politics is about interest, don't be surprised if we find ourselves in the same political camp again in the future."
Akala also spoke on the believe that Ladoja was removed because of his refusal to make available government money for the use of Alhaji Lamidi Adedibu,his former godfather. According to him, having a leader or a godfather is a normal phenomenon in politics. "In politics we cannot do without king makers or godfathers, and he who install a governor can also remove him, all what is require is tactfullness. As governor, you have to be tactful and be focussed. As for me I have a good relationship with Alhaji Adedibu, he is my political mentor and leader and I can assure you that he has not been making any unnecessary demand from me
I dont know what you mean by settlement but I can manage anybody because of my trainning of giving unto Ceasar what is Ceasar's "
Akala also denied that he is a puppet governor under the control of Adedibu saying that the strongman of Ibandan politics dies not interfere in how he runs his government. "Baba (Adedibu) is almost 80 years at that age there is nothing that you do that can change him Adedibu is politics politics is Adedibu. You cannot separate them he has a lot of experience so while I discharge my duty as governor, Adedibu is helping me to play politics while I concetrate on governance. In fact I am the one using Baba he is not using me"
He continued: "Adedibu is not as bad as you think, people are misunderstanding him. He is a philantropist who loves to help the needy. He enjoys helping people he is not a greedy person In Oyo state today you cannot become anything politically without passing through him, Even in Kwara we also have Saraki as the political kingmaker, nobody becomes governor in kwara without Saraki so in Oyo nobody make it without going to Molete"
Away from politics the 'governor' reeled out some of the achievements of his nine months old government saying that "We have not been sitting down idly. Within the 9 months I have spent so far I have been able stabilise the polity to maintain peace in the politically fragile state which is very important, I have a live programme on radio and televison every month where people ask questions about the government freely and got immediate response, beside we have been able to give quality education to the children we maintain 30 pupil per class, bought books for the children , pay their WAECand NECO examination fees , we have invested heavily in education, opened more primary health care clinics, equipped hospitals with drugs, build more hospitals. We are also addressing the issue of abundant foods through our efforts cassava that had been sold for N2,500 is now N500 in the market, our Ethanol industry is almost ready , we have given loans to the farmers to help them . We are also fixing roads and constructing new ones.
On whether he will seek for a re-election in 2007, Akala confirmed saying that "It was local government Chairmen that bought nomination form for me . 'I have the support of my party , they said I should come back in 2007. So I have decided to run. I will be returned to Office in 2007, I have a rich political profile, deputy governor was not my first appointment.
