I was a local goverment chairman before becoming the deputy I beat popular parties in Ogbomoso,AD and PDP then because I contested and won election under APP' he submitted.

Original_Search_Title: Chirano Mines Renege On Promises
Headline: Ghana: Chirano Mines Renege On Promises
Body_Text:
The Chiefs, Elders and members of Etwiebo, Akoti and the entire Chirano communities in the Bibiani-Anhiawso-Bekwai District of the Western Region have raised concerns about the operations of Chirano Gold Mines, an Australian gold mining company, in Ghana.
Speaking on behalf of the communities, the Abakumahene of Chirano, Nana Elue Yaw, told the press yesterday in Accra that the mining company has reneged on almost all its promises to the community since it started operations in the Etwiebo and Akoti area about a decade ago.
He said the quality of life of his people had been deteriorated due to lack of access to income generating opportunities, basic sources of livelihood and some fundamental freedoms such as the freedom of movement, respect and safe environment.
He therefore called on the government to force the company to fulfill all the promises it made to the communities.
Nana Elue Yaw therefore called on the government and the company to raise the compensation entitlement figures and ensure their timely payment, conduct series of business and investment skills training for all recipients of compensation to enable and facilitate their effective use of their monies and provide alternative and adequate farmlands for those who have been displaced by the company.
He further stressed that the company ceased forthwith any further pollution, with particular reference to water pollution, and further provide sustainable alternative clean water sources for the communities as well as stop any further repression of members of the communities.
"We want them to effect and promote as a matter of urgency the company's social responsibility in areas such as electricity, health, education, sanitation and public safety," he added.
Earlier, he had said the activities of the company had resulted in a decisive destruction of the foundation upon which they used to access and enjoy basic and fundamental sources of livelihoods and freedoms.
He said farmlands, cocoa plantations, water resources, and the environment had been destroyed by the activities of Chirano Gold Mines.
"We no longer have the golden opportunity of shifting cultivation, as access to land for farming has been constrained by the activities of the company.
Our water resources, which used to provide us with a variety of economic benefits, have either been destroyed or polluted, thus denying us the opportunity of using them," he added.
He told the press that movement of people between settlements in the wild has been restricted by the blasting and other activities of the company.
He also said the limited access to farmlands, as well as the destruction of cocoa farms and water resources have created employment uncertainties in the area.
The company's failure and inability to employ even a section of the large army of the youth who have been displaced was also criticized.
He was particularly not happy with compensations paid because they could not sustain them after their crops and property were destroyed.
He said a matured cocoa tree was valued and paid at ¢25,000; palm tree (agriculture) ¢125,000; cassava ¢3,000; plantain ¢8,000; and pineapple ¢3,000.
Worse still, he said the payment for the monies had been unduly delayed and that "to date, there are still several farmers who have not as yet received their compensation entitlement."
He was disappointed that several discussions with the District Chief Executive had yielded nothing.
Meanwhile, he said on June 16, 2006 when the citizens of the communities went on a peaceful demonstration to protest the corporate and state injustices, the police and soldiers responded swiftly by arresting nine people including one chief and some of his elders.
"This repressive attitude and slow response on the part of government regulators to act on our concerns and complaints give us cause to believe that the company and indeed mining companies in this country are treated with kid-gloves when it comes to protecting public and community interest," he stated.

Original_Search_Title: South West - Tiko Mayor Acclaimed for Donating Farm Tools
Headline: Cameroon: South West - Tiko Mayor Acclaimed for Donating Farm Tools
Body_Text:
Mutengene, a road junction Town of Tiko Sub/Division was the theatre of agric excellence October 16. The South West Governor, Eyeya Zanga made one of his rare outings to preside at this year's joint event to mark the 11th Rural women s Day and the 26th World Food Day.
Women of the five zones of Tiko, Mutengene, Modika, Mondoni and Misselele came out en masse to show their abilities in food production. Cassava, Cocoyams, Yams, Plantains, Colocacia, maize and just any other food crop were displayed for grading and prizing. The Lebang women carried the day in Colocacia as best farmers of the specialty.
The Mayor of the Tiko Council, Rosine Toto Ndongue donated prizes made of farm tools like wheel/Trucks, to encourage the farmers for better yields. Other prizes to the winners included wheel/barrows, cutlasses, buckets, watering cans, hoes and seeds.
Chanting, dancing and merry making gripped the day in Mutengene after the various personalities had done with their thought provoking speeches. The Provincial Delegate of Agriculture for the South West, Francis Leku Azenaku, underscored that 70 percent of the world was hungry. Azenaku emphasized that relentless efforts be deployed to improve on food production. He explained Government s timeless endeavours to ensure food self-sufficiency.
The women Empowerment Delegate for the South West, Mrs. Niger Thomas, voiced that 80 percent of food was produced by women. Unfortunately for these women the conditions under which they worked remained deplorable, she stressed.. A Women representative from Modika indexed the Gendarmerie and the Police for constant harassment of drivers carrying produce to the market, which causes food losses through delays. She mentioned other hurdles like lack of markets, excess taxes that kill initiatives and the absence of women in decision-making circles. Other obstacles faced by women especially in Cameroon include lact of credit, insecure land tenure, poor road infrastructure, low prices and poorly developed business relations, limited education, poor health among many. Easier said than done, the women once more left Mutengene only jubilating over their farm tools offered by Mayor Rosine Toto whom they considered as the one paying attention to their plight.
The Tiko Mayor urged the women to strive for education and recreation especially with the newly opened Tiko Council Social Centre. The Centre offers training in Computer sewing, hair-dressing, cooking, and a library with a highly trained personnel.

Original_Search_Title: Mama Tendo in Katanga Slum
Headline: Uganda: Mama Tendo in Katanga Slum
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Mama Tendo Foundation has started outreach seminars for people living in slums in Kampala. Until recently, the foundation targeted the educated public but is now spreading out to cover even the less privileged.
The seminars aim at educating the families on care during pregnancy, breastfeeding, nutrition and on how to raise children in the tough environments.
The initiative is a non-profit association funded by United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF). The seminars kicked off on Sunday at Kimwanyi Zone in Katanga.
Elizabeth Masaba, a nurse and counsellor at Kawempe Health Centre, trained the women on the basics of a balanced diet during pregnancy, after birth, breastfeeding and nutrition of the children under five years of age.
Masaba also explained the essential nutritional values in various foodstuffs and fruits such as pineapples, mangoes, watermelon, groundnuts, bananas, beans, vegetables, cassava, pumpkins and fish.
Catherine Ruhweza aka Mama Tendo demonstrated to the women on how to make Ekitoobero (mixture of protein and carbohydrate foods) for the young children.
About 100 families attend the seminar. "In the past, we have had the Mama Tendo seminars which target working parents with young families. But this is the second type of seminar (outreach).
Similar ones will be held in Owino-Kisenyi, Kifumbira and Entebbe Kitooro," explained Ruhweza, "There is a high prevalence of HIV, poor nutrition, high levels of prostitution and neglect of children. The seminars will seek to address these problems," she said.
The New Vision asked some of the local women and men in the Kimwanyi zone what they had learnt from the seminar. Below is what they had to say.
We have always been told that every pregnant mother needs to be fed well. The one thing that is new to me is not to stick to one kind of fruit or food. According to Mama Tendo seminar, every type of food; a balanced diet, so to speak, is equally essential. I have also learnt that when a child's excreta is green, then I should go for check up. I think this was a good seminar if I have to be a healthy mother, look for school fees and sustain my children.
Viccy Najemba, 45
Mama Tendo has taught me to feed well - especially on greens, fruits, beans and so on. But these items are expensive and although we would love to do what they tell us, you find you are constrained financially for someone like me who is a teen mother, and I don't have a job to count on.
Resty Nabukenya, 17
I am only waiting to see the results. I am going to try out what Mama Tendo has said in term of nutrition. There is something else she said that touched me - the way we do not really care about our children as they grow up, despite this being a slum. If my three children resorted to prostitution, I think I would die. This seminar is a constant reminder that parents should pay greater attention to their children's behaviour.
Fazila Kasoga, 25
Breastfeeding for two years! Yes, that is what I have learnt, although with scepticism. It's hard to believe. When you are breastfeeding, you should feed well. So how can a mother, who spends the whole day without enough meals, be able to breastfeed for two years?
Anna Kobusingye, 25
I have a daughter. Since I am not yet pregnant with my second child, I am going to practice family planning. But the issue is not so clear. There are some things you fear to ask in public. Maybe if Mama Tendo would come back to us one by one. That is when each one of us would be able to ask more questions and maybe understand how family planning works. We need more information on our reproductive health.
Aisha Nakato, 20
I am happy with this; very, very happy! Although I am an old man and don't have toddlers at home any more, I have grand children, who I think will benefit from this. But the problem is with the men, at times. For example, look around! There are very few men who came to attend the seminar, yet it could benefit them. Is it because it's the women who actually look after the children?
Hassan Wasswa
LCI chairman
This is an eye opener to the young mothers in this area. Some women don't know what to do even when they are mothers. This shouldn't stop here. Government should go on and bring more people like Mama Tendo to help us sensitise the grassroots.
Emmanuel Magezi
vice-chairman in charge of security affairs
I have four kids. The first born is nine years old, while the last born is three. It's been quite a hustle bringing them up. You may come and you demonstrate, show us what to do. But all that may not really count at the end of the day, if you are too poor. Here, how to survive is the problem, then the rest will follow. That is why you saw some women laughing in a cynical manner at what was being taught. Because they know it is easier said than done.
Hadija Tebasungwa, 28
For my two children, I remember I only went for medical check up when I was about to give birth. I have learnt that this is wrong. You need constant medical checkups when you are pregnant.
Juliet Nalwada, 24
I have learnt everything (pause). I have three children. During my pregnancies, I didn't think it was so important to test for HIV/AIDS. But now, I will think about it, next time I am pregnant.
Rehema Nakiwala, 22

Original_Search_Title: Investing In Agriculture For Good Security
Headline: Liberia: Investing In Agriculture For Good Security
Body_Text:
This year's theme marking the 26th observance of World Food Day: "Investing in Agriculture for Food Security," as chosen by the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), is extremely significant to the post-war environment in which the Liberian nation that had once striven along the lines of subsistence farming, watching limited production of crops but yet with farmers ever-prepared to accord relevant hospitality to others as a result of internal abundance, must equally conform to by virtue of the flaring rice-bird mentality that has again begun creeping in the post-election period.
Not viewed in the least sense of trying to win a prizes that do not suit the article, but primarily evolving from the continuous commitment of this writer who also possesses specialized knowledge in the field, it is only proper to critically review this year's theme within the post-war context of trying to help the reconstruction process as regards food security.
Notwithstanding an oversight created in the budgetary allocations for the current year by the Unity Party-led government, placing agricultural production below education that is equally relevant to the process, it is however worth recalling experts who have contributed to food security in Liberia over the decades.
Prominent among them have been Messrs Amram BenZvi, Gady Nadler, Shlomo Samy Levy and Francis R. Mann of the defunct Liberia Agricultural Land Development and Mechanization Corporation (AGRIMECO, Inc.), Drs. K.N.
Sen and Peter Aagaard of the Lofa County Agricultural Development Project (LCADP), S.S. Virmani, the late Patrick K. Worzi, Domity Sarwo, the late Director Andrew Paye and current Director Dr. Qwelibo Subah of the Central Agricultural Research Institute (CARI).
No longer wishing to revisit articles written over the many years on agriculture production in which emphasis were placed on the need to attract poor rural farmers into the sector through the availability of seeds, tools and implements, whether at the mechanized or modern farming levels, experimental formats laid during the period of the past Liberian civil crisis ought to provide indicators that indeed, Liberia possesses the requisite arable land that if put to full use, would have citizens feeling proud of fruits obtained by sweat of their own brows.
Scientifically and without much bragging, Liberia's staple, RICE, otherwise known as Oryzae Sativa in the language of those who know the various processes leading to its consumption, would in the simplest form emphatically state that unless one refrains from wearing clean suits as if in an executive office, put on torn-trousers rain boots and prepared to have the feel of hard-hands through the constant use of cutlasses, hoes, shovels, diggers, sickles and other equipment needed to produce and not talk, talk and talk, one would have done absolutely nothing boasting about agricultural production in the country.
Sensing the very weak points and naivety of many Liberians at achieving such, the administrations of the late 18th and 19th presidents of Liberia, the late Dr. William V.S. Tubman and Rev. Dr. William Richard Tolbert, Jr. are recorded for having helped in transforming traditional practices that often yielded into self-sufficiency of the family to massive production through mechanized and modern farming methods.
Unarguably and more challengingly, production of crops, not only rice, in various parts of Liberia gained increasing wave therefrom, watching the involvement of the citizenry throughout the republic involved into the process through well-defined policies of government.
It was therefore not strange that agricultural institutions such as the Liberia Agricultural Company (LAC), AGRIMECO, an Israeli-supported company that helped increased production in many areas in Liberia, such as Foya Airfield, Solumba, Shello, home of veteran Paramount Chief and farmer, the late Tamba Taylor, Kpandehgisia, Tangia, etc. in upper Lofa, as well as in Zleh Town in Grand Gedeh County, Kpain and Fendell Farms in Montserrado, Garwula Tombe Project in Grand Cape Mount County, Decoris in Maryland County and had just begun negotiations to venture into the forest of Yangaryai in Grand Cape Mount County when a catastrophic event occurred out of grossly-misconstrued concepts about trends of events of the Tolbert-led administration in promoting food security in Liberia.
Other agricultural development projects that were not only involved production of the staple, but cocoa, coffee, oil palm, cola nut, pineapples, oranges, cassava, lemons (tangerines) and a variety of citrus products, soon began actualizing the concept in alleviating the unnecessary problems that bore true identification of destructive politics.
Exceptional of the intentions of politicians, however, projects such as the LCADP, Bong County Agricultural Development Project (BCADP), Small Rice Seedholders Project in Gbarnga, Bong County, Gbedin Rice Seed Project and that of Susukuu in the southeastern province, then considered a stepping-stone of the Movement for Justice in Africa (MOJA), became evidences to the promotional drive of the administration that in various ways proved to be quite accountable and transparent, given its records that still remain quite historic in the growth and development of the Liberian nation.
Prior to and even following the demise of President Tolbert, who had been nick-named "Speedy, Higher Heights, Big Daddy, etc.," just as the late President Samuel Kanyon Doe was named in the 1980s as "Tarnue, SKD, etc.," the former produced professional farmers who did not only prove their worth in offices but in the fields, notably amongst whom were former Agriculture Ministers James T. Philips, Jr., Ms.
Florence Chenoweth, Alfred T. Suah, the late Alfred Fromayan, Joseph Nyuma Boakai, now Vice President of the Republic of Liberia, Deputy Ministers the late Das Sirleaf, Moses D. Elliott, currently in the United States of America, Assistant Director Dr. Moses Pewu, an agronomist, the late Mr. Jeremiah Moinba Tulay who became affectionately known as "No Problem, Only Management" and became Project Manager of LCADP, Daniel Toe Goe, former Peace Corps Director in Liberia and Project Manager of the BCADP prior to the Liberian civil crisis, amongst others.
Recent years have unmistakably watched former Agriculture Minister Dr. Roland Massaquoi and current Minister Dr. Chris Toe who helped thoroughly operationalize the Liberia Produce Marketing Corporation during the mid-1980s pursuing identical and undisturbed professional paths in attaining food security in Liberia.
Whatever these past contributions and experiences may mean to the ordinary mind in the post-war setting, especially with President Ellen Johnson Sirleaf persistency at encouraging Liberians to return to the soil in the production and consumption of crops, countries and institutions in full support of the national reconstruction drive only need be encouraged to infuse more capital into the industry, specifically the United States that has since supported the Nation in the sector through its PL-480 food program, intended to buttress efforts of farmers although evidentially misunderstood over the years as gifts for waste, to begin anew in this direction, since "round pegs and square holes" will never again become the case out of sobriety of the population over competency of individuals appointed to public posts.
Investing in agriculture for food security, as rightly chosen by the FAO as theme for this year's observance, must therefore not be viewed as mere usage of words but must be nationally construed by all war-weary and peace-seeking Liberians to mean the re-focusing of minds to the available rich soil that serves as the best alternative in tackling the nation's post-war problems.
Lest this writer be misunderstood as is pervasive amongst a population said to have nearly 85 per cent illiteracy rate, EDUCATION, as prioritized in the first year of the Unity Party-led administration, is considered proper in view of the long years of capitalization by individuals upon the high illiteracy of our people as primary factor to the many anomalies that have occurred. Mind you, even those who have not gone through the formal process of schooling, pointedly involving our unlettered fathers and mothers, most of whom reside in the hinterland, one would still be surprised to truly learn that as informal as they have been, they equally possess knowledge that those calling themselves formally educated cannot easily cope.
Granted the rice, rice issue greeting the embryonic administration of President Sirleaf, with many forgetting that it constitutionally requires six years of tenure but may likely exceed the period based upon steady progress, emphasis placed on food security this year by the FAO must indeed ring bell that the days of "mouth-talks" out of --- can I say "political" advocacy and agitation intended to promote selfish causes? ---disappear in the midst of the suffering population.
After all, the City of Monrovia is not a place to prove our might on account of flowery gardens and beautiful houses obtained through whatever means, but how responsive we become to the yearnings and plights of the majority who reside in the interior from where most agricultural activities take place in feeding the Liberian nation.

Original_Search_Title: Kayunga Farmer Turns to Pawpaw, Targets CHOGM
Headline: Uganda: Kayunga Farmer Turns to Pawpaw, Targets CHOGM
Body_Text:
Kampala — KANGULUMIRA sub-county in Kayunga district is well-known as pineapple land because pineapple seems to be a must-grow crop.
"Almost every household has grown the succulent fruit," says Ronald Kizito, 36.
In 2004, Kizito bought 50,000 suckers which he planted on a 10-acre piece of land. Four months later, he had his maiden harvest of 500 fruits and at sh1,000 per fruit, he earned sh0.5m.
But the trend has since changed. Today Kizito tells a different story of frustration and disillusionment. The ambitious farmer has embarked on another project of large scale pawpaw growing.
Kizito says if he has to survive the stiff competition, he must diversify. "I have gone into pawpaw growing because very few people have planted it."
He says the crop is on high demand yet with low supply. "CHOGM, for example, is around the corner. There will be a high demand for pawpaws and I will reap big," he boasts.
He expects the first harvest from his 40 trees in February, 2007, but also plans to plant more. One pawpaw costs between sh200 and sh1,000 at Nakasero Market.
He has a pick-up truck that transports his fruits to Kampala and other nearby markets.
Kizito says the earnings from pineapples have dwindled becauses there are very many pineapple growers, "and, of course, the higher the supply, the lower the price," he explains.
Kizito says the earnings from pineapples are no longer regular. In some seasons, he earns only sh0.2m. And with five labourers, the earnings are sliced, because he has to pay the workers.
To beef up his earnings, Kizito now intercrops pineapples with bananas, cassava and paw paws.
"I intend to add vanilla to my list but its price is demoralising. The crop also has no ready market, yet it requires a lot of attention," he says.
At its peak in 2003, a kilogramme of green vanilla went for up to sh100,000. Today it costs between sh500 and sh1,500.
Consequently, most farmers in Kayunga and neighbouring areas have cut down their vanilla plants and resorted to growing other crops.
On average, Kizito earns between sh0.5m to sh0.8m. The banana bacterial wilt disease has, however, attacked his plantation. About 50 trees are affected.
The disease causes premature ripening or yellowing of the banana fruits, drying up its leaves.
To fight it, Kizito has embarked on slashing the affected trees. "I slash 10 trees every week and I am afraid the whole plantation is affected," he says.
He has no kind words for the district authorities whom he says have not done enough to rescue the farmers. "There is no sensitisation. We have been left totally on our own," he says.
An ambitious man, not only does Kizito want to earn big from his business, he also wants to become a role model.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture Faces Obstacles
Headline: Namibia: Agriculture Faces Obstacles
Body_Text:
Windhoek — Although Namibia has a favourable policy environment for agricultural investment, many obstacles prevent small agricultural producers from producing a surplus apart from feeding themselves.
Lack of credit and markets, together with insecure land tenure, remain some of the hurdles that the small and emerging producers of agricultural products face, which President Hifikepunye Pohamba says is beyond their control.
In a speech that was read on his behalf countrywide during the commemoration of World Food Day yesterday, the President said Namibia enjoyed peace and stability and has modern road infrastructure and water management, but the country's small-scale producers face problems with tariffs, non-tariff barriers, low prices for their products, the HIV/AIDS pandemic and poorly developed business relations with agribusiness at the commercial end of the agricultural supply chain.
In addition to these, Namibian small-scale farmers also face other natural challenges such as drought, floods, pests and diseases.
He said the country should work hard to improve the capacity of small-scale rural farmers to produce a surplus and feed themselves.
While there was a need to encourage farmers to produce surplus crops, the President said authorities should also be mindful of the fact that producing bumper harvests that end up rotting in storage facilities would discourage them from producing more.
Through many government activities and programmes such as aquaculture, community-based natural resource management, green schemes and national horticulture initiatives, Pohamba said, Namibia has managed to put itself on the list of countries that are committed to eradicating hunger and poverty.
While green schemes would increase local production of maize, mahangu, cassava, fruit and vegetables, the National Horticulture Initiative, spearheaded by the Namibia Agronomic Board, would reduce the country's dependence on South African products because for the country to prosper, it should be able to feed itself, otherwise it would be held hostage by others.
Pohamba said aquaculture, which the Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources is promoting, has the potential to reduce poverty.
"This will serve as an additional source of income for rural households.
Indeed, the inland fisheries sector can play an important role in improving the quality of lives of our people," he added.
This was the 26th World Food Day and 14th International Day for the Eradication of Poverty, which was commemorated under the themes: "Working Together Out of Poverty" and "Investing in Agriculture for Food Security".
Both themes, according to Pohamba, highlight the importance of restoring, energizing and building up agriculture production through the input of resources through public and private investments.
Hunger and poverty have become urban and rural phenomena in developing countries as they are closely linked to poor agricultural production and adverse weather conditions.
In the Khomas Region, the main event was held at Esselmanhaar in Dordabis in the Windhoek Rural Constituency, where Governor Sophia Shaningwa delivered the President's speech.
As part of the commemoration, the Ministry of Agriculture, Water and Forestry distributed food parcels, T-shirts and caps to kids in some of the informal settlements in Windhoek.
A parade comprising of a truck with local musicians, who entertained people, and other vehicles attracted hundreds of people, mainly children, as it passed by.
Although the ministry planned to visit four informal settlements, namely Ombili, Havana, Okahandja Park and Goreangab, the officials could only cover Okahandja Park and Ombili.
At Okahandja Park, the officials distributed food parcels each containing a fruit, hot dog and drink to more than 250 children at the Hand in Hand for Children Okahandja Park Soup Kitchen.
The centre caters for 300 orphans and vulnerable children who are fed on porridge and soup twice a day from Monday to Saturday every week.

Original_Search_Title: World Food Day Celebrated in South
Headline: Namibia: World Food Day Celebrated in South
Body_Text:
Mariental — More than 600 people from the community of Mariental, including teachers, learners, elders, regional councillors and Governor Katrina Hanse, gathered on Saturday at the Sportsground in commemoration of the 26th World Food Day (WFD) and the 14th International Day for the Eradication of Poverty (IDEP).
Under the theme: "Invest in Agriculture, For Food Security" the masses experienced an information and cultural extravaganza, with performances from schools, DD. Guibeb Primary and Mariental High. Empelheim illustrated diverse channelling of concern with a Tswana - and Gumboot dance accompanied by a powerful poem.
President Hifikepunye Pohamba, in a keynote address read by governor Hanse, said the commemoration is taking place at a time when the world is in a crisis with public attention focused on conflicts and wars. The President stated that the 800 million people, who are stalked by food insecurity find it difficult to act positively towards the achievement of humanity's quest for 'a world free from hunger and poverty' should not be forgotten.
"Progress in eradicating poverty and reducing hunger has been disappointingly slow and, as such, we must work together to boost food production. This must always be at the centre of national and international poverty-reduction programmes."
Pohamba also addressed the debilitating factor of poverty weakening nations illustrating it with undernourished mothers giving birth to underweight babies, whose health and growth may be compromised for the rest of their lives.
"Children who go to bed hungry cannot fight off diseases and infections, nor can they concentrate properly at school. As a result, they lose out on opportunities for learning so that they can escape the hunger-poverty trap."
The complex connection between hunger and poverty is the direct influence in the formation of this year's theme, he said.
"In many ways, hunger and poverty have become urban and rural phenomena in developing countries, closely linked to poor agricultural production and adverse weather conditions," he said, adding that 70 percent of the world's population still live in rural areas, where agricultural production provides either food directly or indirectly.
"Namibia is no exception. That is why during the World Food summit in 1996 the Heads of State and governments committed themselves to promoting public and private investments in agriculture as a contribution to the goal of reducing by half the number of hungry by 2015. I believe that the target set in 1996 can still be met. However, it rests with individuals themselves, their families, communities and governments working with the private sector and international co-operating partners," the President asserted.
The speech included the Green Scheme where the Ministry of Agriculture and Forestry aims to encourage local production of staple foods such as maize, mahangu, cassava as well as vegetables and fruit. It is estimated that the Green scheme in Namibia will cost more than N$2 billion over a period of ten years.
The Namibian Agronomic Board is also spearheading the national Horticultural project, which is aimed at promoting the production and marketing of local goods, by supporting the establishment of collection centres for agricultural produce such as vegetables and fruit.
He also mentioned that grape production from the Karas Region has penetrated the European markets and will soon be exported to the United States of America.
The President further noted that the Ministry of Agriculture, Water and Forestry promotes local commitment to wildlife and natural resources, including aquaculture in tandem with the Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources, are going ahead to boost capacity for smallholder farmers.
"I believe that, through various activities and initiatives, we have managed to put Namibia on the list of those countries committed to the eradication of hunger and poverty. Thus, what we need to do is to participate consistently in future campaigns and efforts in order to maintain the momentum," Pohamba said.

Original_Search_Title: CET Tariff Threatens 230,000 Jobs -- Ethanol Manufacturers
Headline: Nigeria: CET Tariff Threatens 230,000 Jobs -- Ethanol Manufacturers
Body_Text:
BARLEY few days after Ropes manufacturers petitioned the Presidency to reverse the recently hiked import duty on their raw materials by the Federal Ministry of Finance, which jerked it up from 5 per cent to 60 per cent, the Ethanol Manufacturers and Blenders group of the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN) has equally called to the Federal Government to review the new ECOWAS Common External Tariff (CET) on their excise duty and imported packaging materials saying that the CET scheme threatens the N8 billion ethanol plant under construction, and the over 230,000 jobs in the sector.
Addressing newsmen in Lagos on the impact of the new CET, Chairman of the sector, Mr. Joseph Odumodu, said: "We wish to draw the attention of Government and relevant Agencies to the recent amendment to the import duty for bottled wines and spirits (finished products), which slashed tariff from 80 per cent to 20 per cent (Whisky wine), 90 per cent to 20 per cent for Sparkling wine, Varamouth 90 per cent to 20 per cent and others reduced from 70 per cent to 20 per cent, while local manufacturers are required to pay 40 per cent excise duty in addition to 50 per cent on their imported packaging materials ."
Odumodu, who is also the Managing Director /CEO,May & Baker PLC, said that the Blending Group of the sector currently pays more than N150 million as taxes and excise duties. He pointed out that the exhorbitant fee on import and excise duty encourages importation of finished products, and warned that local manufacturers compete fairly in the market. This, he said, would lead to factory closures and the laying off of the workforce in the sector which is not good for the economy.
Also speaking, the acting Chairman of the sectoral group, Mr. Dunnie Oyebuile, said that implementation of the new CET tariff will adversely affect the Cassava to Ethanol backward integration embarked upon by the Ethanol producers as directed by Federal Government for which, he said, they have made over N8 billion investment.
He said the programme can only be sustained if local blenders remain in the business of local production. and appealed to the government to save the sector from extinction as this would lead to massive unemployment.
Said he: "To make local manufacturers remain in business and be competitive, we hereby make the following appeal: That the Government should in addition to the current 20 per cent duty, impose additional levy of 50 per cent on the above finished products in view of the fact that the earlier import on them ranged between 70 per cent and 90 per cent. Remove the Excise Duty currently imposed on local manufacturers.

Original_Search_Title: Farm Project Helps Aids Orphans Get Food, Income
Headline: Kenya: Farm Project Helps Aids Orphans Get Food, Income
Body_Text:
Bondo — A UN project is helping to sustain farming know-how and other life skills among Kenyan children orphaned by AIDS.
The Junior Farmer Field and Life Schools project (JFFLS), which has been running for two years, teaches basic farming techniques and social health, including family planning, alcohol abuse and gender equality, to 120 orphans at four primary schools in the village of Odhuro in the district of Bondo in western Kenya's Nyanza province.
"Agricultural skills are traditionally taught to children by their parents, but the problem is many parents are dying before they get the opportunity to pass on the skills," said Edwin Adbenya, a JFFLS evaluator and monitor. "We are facing an inter-generational gap that leaves orphaned children without the necessary skills and knowledge for their future livelihoods."
HIV prevalence in Nyanza is 15 percent, more than double the national average. One-third of the country's orphans are estimated to have lost their parents to AIDS.
Peris Adiembalo, 17, lost both her parents to the pandemic before she was a teenager and moved in with her older sister and her husband, neither of whom were employed. JFFLS taught her to plant a variety of fruits and vegetables. "Now that we are growing vegetables at home I feel strong and I don't have as many diseases as I had before," she said.
According to the UN's Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO), a consistent and well-balanced diet improves energy levels and performance in school, and reduces the need for children to abandon their education in search of a job.
The JFFLS school's farm grows sweet potatoes, cowpeas, chickpeas, onions, cassava and medicinal plants, all of which are sold. "Cash crops are important because we want the children to see farming as a business," said Julius Were from Bondo's district agriculture office and an advisor to the scheme.
"In the beginning I saw farming as a punishment," said Adiembalo. "I saw it as a waste of energy - now I see it as a source of food, a source of income, and I know that even if I don't have any work I can at least construct a farm."
More than half Odhuro's 700 school children are classified as orphans and vulnerable children, adding stress and a financial burden to their extended families.
Grandparents are also involved in the project. Mary Ngonga, 55, says she is too old to work in the fields and care for children, but HIV/AIDS has left eight orphans in her care.
Over lunch, the grandmothers sing about the devastating impact of HIV/AIDS and the burden of responsibility the virus is placing on their shoulders at an age when they had expected others to care for them, but their involvement in the JFFLS makes the task a little more manageable.
Anne Anam, who works with JFFLS, says the programme has offered orphans fresh hope. "I am very positive they are going to do very well," she said. "You heard them talking about being doctors and pilots - they now have that future."
With extreme poverty forcing so many children in the region to drop out of the education system after primary school, there are calls for the scheme to be expanded to include all students, not only orphans.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Dr. Moses Makayoto - Award Winning Innovator
Headline: Kenya: Dr. Moses Makayoto - Award Winning Innovator
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Dr Moses Makayoto may not easily ring a bell as a top civil servant but his papers and innovations have given him global recognition across the oceans.
He was the brain behind the popular Mama Safi detergent, which you could not miss in many households in the 1980s.
Then Dr Moses Makayoto led the research that came up with the cheap detergent that was made from local materials. For him it was an uphill task convincing East Africa Industries (now Unilever East Africa), to use local resources since the company had always relied on imported materials.
His argument was that locally obtained materials could be as useful if the right technology was applied. Armed with determination, he convinced the company executives and Mama Safi became a reality.
The scientist says 90 per cent of the ingredients used to manufacture the detergent came from local materials.
Mama Safi was popular because it was cheap and therefore affordable to many households. However, East Africa Industries stopped producing it because it was competing against one of its own products, Omo.
It is not easy to describe Makayoto in one word. He is a pioneer scientist, award winning innovator and nationalist extraordinaire all rolled into one.
He cuts the image of a suave executive, but he admits he is more at ease in the laboratory or workshop as opposed to sitting behind a sleek mahogany desk in a plush office.
One cannot miss the scientist's zeal, thoroughness and commitment while working on something innovative - be it a simple device to assist Small and Medium Scale Entrepreneurs (MSEs) improve their business or a more complicated industrial solution.
But Makayoto is a globetrotter, having travelled nearly halfway round the world either to acquire knowledge or dispense it.
The Kirdi Chief Scientist with his children (Standing from left) Reynolds, 18, and Sissel, 17. Seated with Makayoto is Fiona Anyango, 15.
And because of his innovations and scientific prowess, Makayoto has scooped several prestigious awards and made indelible record.
His award winning innovations and research, locally and internationally, speak volumes about the man's career as an applied scientist.
Last month, Makayoto was nominated as recipient for the Gold Award by the International Biographical Institute of the US, which is a Gold Award in Science for his deeds in Kenya. The American Biographical Institute's 40-year goal has been to provide factual and current information on men and women the world over who make positive contributions to society. The Gold Medal is an honour reflective of struggles endured to ascend to the top.
In the nomination citation to Makayoto, the institute says: "This is a major honour for which you have been nominated. The institute and its worldwide network of researchers and editors join in respect and admiration of your deeds, achievements, and talent."
While admiring the fact that there are many male and female scientists in the continent, Makoyoto says African scientists should borrow a leaf from his book and leave their academic cocoons to propel the continent towards industrialisation.
"The time is now. African scientists must against all odds lead the way to the continent's industrialisation through their work and initiative," Makayoto says.
He believes Kenya can be industrialised by 2020 given the right environment and infrastructure.
Makayoto has been recognised for his exceptional effort to use his knowledge in applied science to find practical solutions to everyday problems.
His remarkable contribution in chemical engineering has won him awards, including the prestigious International Scientist of the Year Award by the International Biographer (IBC), Cambridge, United Kingdom for the edition 2000-2004. The award comprises Life Fellowship Diploma bronze medal. The winner also becomes a fellow of the American-based International Biographical Association (IBA) and his name is listed in a directory for outstanding scientists of the 21st Century.
The IBC annually reviews thousands of biographies from around the world and selects a few outstanding scientists for the award.
In 2001, Makayoto was the only black African listed in the Who's Who in Science and Engineering by the American IBA.
Currently, he is the Principal Research Officer and Programme co-ordinator at the Kenya Industrial Research and Development Institute (Kirdi).
Other than the international awards for his outstanding work in science, innovations and engineering, Makayoto is also recognised locally and has been honoured with the Presidential award Order of the Grand Warrior of Kenya (OGW).
The award, presented by President Kibaki in 2004, was in recognition of his service in national development. Makayoto had prior to this award also won the Defence College Award for top lecturer in Industrial Research and Development and served as director/manager of Productivity Centre.
Because of his academic achievements in studies and research, he now joins a prestigious list of top scientists who have been honoured over the last century.
Makayoto developed a computer-aided software while doing his doctorate degree in chemical engineering at the Technical University of Norway in 1985.
The information technology-based method calculates the ingredients involved in a chemical engineering process better than calculation methods, which are cumbersome and wasteful.
"The design reduces the amount of time and resources invested in the processes by more than half," says the engineer.
The design encompasses financial requirements, equipment specification, environmental safety and human resource needs necessary in any chemical engineering," he adds. The use of the software was adopted around the world and earned Makayoto corporate membership in the Chemical Engineering Institute of the United Kingdom, where he is a chartered engineer.
However, the scientist's memorable achievement is innovating a pesticide used to control filth fly, especially in refugee camps.
He developed the pesticide while working at the International Centre for Insect Physiology and Ecology (Icipe). He was the first to head the biotechnology research unit at the centre.
"The filth fly was causing a lot of problems in refugee camps and my team at Icipe decided to do something about it," he says.
The scientist says the pesticide, made from microbes, has no side effects.
Makayoto has, for about 10 years, been working at Kirdi where he develops technologies to help micro and small enterprises (MSEs) and small-scale farmers.
He is spearheading the development of honey processing technology, energy efficiency and micro-gene technology for cotton production in the food and technology division.
Previously, he had been involved in programmes to develop efficient ways of using alternative energy sources, including biomass and hydropower.
Makayoto says the equipment for honey processing is expensive and most small-scale farmers cannot afford it.
"However with the use of simple technology we have been able to design cheaper but effective models," he says.
He says the institute recently held a bee conference, which deliberated on the gaps in the industry. "We bring the farmers together and hear their problems and then figure out how we can come up with simple inexpensive solutions," says Makayoto.
He adds: "We realised, for instance, that many bee farmers across the country were experiencing problems with acquiring the honey extractor and the honey warmer. We developed cheaper and reliable alternatives."
He says the institute is working with four MSEs, which are currently applying its technological approaches.
Much as the scientist concurs that Kenya's products that emanate from the MSEs are good, they lack the quality technological touch that would propel them to the international markets. "Our products from the MSEs are good but unless you improve them, the products would not compete internationally," advises Makayoto.
At the leather development centres, under the auspices of Leather and Food technology division at Kirdi, Makayoto says, they have set engineering design service centres based on branding and prototyping.
"It is vital that once you have come up with your own unique prototype, you should brand it to avoid theft," he says.
The division also deals in adding value to fruits, cassava and potatoes before selling them. Makayoto's department has also come up with maize shellers, nutcrackers and juice extractors to help farmers minimise waste during harvesting.
"There is a lot of wastage during and after harvest. It is important that we adopt post-harvest technologies that would minimise loss," says Makayoto.
"The absorptive capacity among prospective users of these technologies is still low. We project to use the incubation approach - whereby we bring somebody and give them space to adopt these technologies," he says.
However, at the core of the development and adoption of these technologies, he says, is the issue of patenting.
He says it is important to develop a policy framework that would protect the intellectual property rights of local innovators.
"A lot of innovations and research work done by our people are being lost through the infringement of our intellectual property rights. It is important to develop policies that would help put in place measures to stop plagiarism as well sensitise people on the importance of patenting their innovations or work," he says.
Kirdi is developing Intellectual Property Rights Policy to be applied in all its research and technical work.
"We are currently coming up with an intellectual property policy framework, with such a policy in place we will not have worry about losing our innovations," he says.
However, Makayoto has a problem with the way research is being handled in Africa. He says research receives little funding - about 0.3 per cent of the Gross Domestic Product.
"This has made many talented scientists to flee the continent," he says.
In Kenya, he says, the situation is not different, adding that the Government's contribution towards research is a drop in the ocean.
"Much as the Government tries to allocate more resources towards research, whatever is given is usually little," he says.
He adds: "I would have wished that research development is allocated 1 or 1.5 per cent of the GDP."
The other problem affecting research development, he says, is infrastructure.
"So long as our research institutions are poorly developed we cannot hope to advance in research. Most of the institutions are shells with little in terms of the tools and materials for research," explains Makayoto.
He notes that even in instances where the institutions are fully furnished, some of them still lack materials used in research.
He says research has traditionally relied on donor funding, which he says has compromised innovativeness among local scientists.
"What usually happens is that whoever is funding research would like to control the direction the research takes, often this is to fulfil their own interests," he says.
He adds that the so-called foreign scientists seconded to be involved in the research often steal the information and sell it abroad without the knowledge or benefit of local scientists.
He also detests the reliance on foreign researches to run local industries.
"Usually these industries would prefer to use research done in their mother countries than the ones produced by local scientists and there is no law barring them from doing that," he says.
Makoyoto says Kenyans with resources should endeavour to start indigenous industries that would apply local research.
"I'm not just being patriotic but I'm also being realistic because research, like any other commodity, competes in the market and if we think people will buy our research just like that we are mistaken," he says.
He says that there is also poor linkage between research institutions and industry. This has led to many scientists being frustrated.
"Our universities for instance produce many researchers and research products but due to poor linkage between the universities and industries, the uptake has been very poor," he says.
Other Government research institutions, he says, also churn out many innovations that are not taken up by industries. Makayoto was born in Kendu Bay in Rachuonyo District in 1953. He graduated with Bachelor of Science from the University of Nairobi in 1977. In 1979, he obtained a Masters in Science, Chemical Engineering option, from Dar-es-Salaam University and in 1985 he did his doctorate in Chemical Engineering at the Technical University, Norway.
He is a member of several professional bodies, including international Biographical Centre (UK), American Institute of Chemical Engineers, Institute of Chemical Engineers (UK) and Chartered Engineer (UK).
Makayoto has attended several professional courses in chemical engineering, biotechnology, chemistry, management and policy formulation.
He has more than 20 years experience in research work. Apart from his current position at Kirdi, Makayoto has taught applied chemistry at Kenyatta University and was a senior chemical engineer at the Ministry of Energy.
He has a wide experience and training on intellectual property rights and has published widely and supervised several postgraduate and technical students.
When he is not busy in the laboratories, Makayoto is supervising chemical engineering doctorate students locally and internationally.
On projects and proposal development and implementation, he has handled World Bank, European Economic Commission, Finnida, UNHCR and USaid.
Makayoto, who is married with three children, is fond of music, dancing and public speaking.
He cites Prof Thomas Odhiambo, the Icipe founder, as his mentor and role model.
And as he goes about the business of scientific and industrial innovations, Makayoto would like to leave a mark by imparting all that he has learnt to many Kenyans.

Original_Search_Title: Elephants Invade Farms In Lofa - Destroy Several Crops
Headline: Liberia: Elephants Invade Farms In Lofa - Destroy Several Crops
Body_Text:
Reports emanating from Lofa County speak of elephants invading villages and farms, as well as destroying crops before returning into the forest.
Although the situation is being investigated, several aid workers and residents of the county who claim to have returned from some of the affected villages and towns, told The Analyst in Monrovia that the repeated attacks on crops by the animals have caused massive displacement of villagers in several villages around the provincial city of Voinjama.
Among the towns and villages are Tenebu, Kollieman, Dayzaybah, Ziama's Farm, Maimai, etc.
Several citizens from the county who visited the National Legislature recently to brief their representatives on the situation, told reporters that due to the frequency of elephant attacks on villages and farms, normal farming activity,which is a major source of income for ordinary Lofans, has come to a standstill because farmers are afraid to go on their farms for fear of being attacked by the elephants.
They said the situation is dangerous for the nation, particularly Lofa County, which has for many decades been referred to as one of the breadbaskets of Liberia, due to its high level of food and crops production.
Fayah Tamba, believed to be in his late 50s from the village of Tenebu, lamented that farmers in the county were no longer in the position to carry out farming activities as wild elephants from the Lofa forest continue to chase them, destroying their rice and cassava farms.
Oldman Tamba, flanked by his fellow villagers,mainly elderly women, indicated that local authorities in the county have hired the services of experienced hunters to kill the animals, although their efforts to stop the elephants destruction have been unsuccessful, since their destructive activities have been extended to other towns and villages in the district.
Besides Tenebu, Oldman Tamba disclosed that possibility exists that the elephants may extend their activities to places like Barkedu, John's Town, Tusu and the Konia forest in Zorzor District, while further extending to those of the Wologisi Mountain area and the Kolahun forest.
He said farming activity in these areas have been interrupted due to the appearance of the animals.
Oldman Tamba was however candid in stating that no major casualty has occurred especially involving human since destruction by the elephants began, but warned that the invasion of villages and destruction of farms would undermine the county's reconstruction and recovery drives, since farming remains an integral part of the lives of the people of Lofa County.
"Any attempt to therefore ignore the problem would re-awaken hardship in the county," he pointed out.
Recently, an assessment team from the Forestry Development Authority (FDA) visited the county, following news reports about the elephants' invasion.
FDA authorities who toured some of the affected farms, confirmed the destruction of crops by the animals and noted that they seem to be moving in a pair of four (family).
Meanwhile, the people of Lofa County are calling on the Liberian Government and international partners, specifically the Worldwide Fund, to assist in curbing the destruction of crops by driving away the elephants from their towns, villages and farms, to enable them rebuild their lives.
A keen observer of the situation however expressed the need to have the FDA equally take into account the need to preserve the endangered species that had since left the soil of Liberia since the period of the 1960s, when trained elephant hunters such as Vonenyeazu Yamah killed them by the dozens.

Original_Search_Title: Ukrainians Are Not Rude to Nigerians -Ambassador
Headline: Nigeria: Ukrainians Are Not Rude to Nigerians -Ambassador
Body_Text:
Recently, the Ukrainian Ambassador to Nigeria, Oleg Skoropad, and members of the Ukrainian community in Abuja celebrated their 15th Independence Anniversary, bringing Ukraine's relations with Nigeria into lime light. He represents a country that has a growing economic relationship with Nigeria with a turn over of $225 million in 2005. Speaking with our reporter, Elk-anah Chawai, he bares his mind on the nature of Nigeria-Ukraine political and economic relations. He also reacts to allegations that Ukrainians are rude to Nigerians at their embassy. The excerpts:
Tell us about yourself
I got to Nigeria in November 2004. I came here after serving in the foreign office for 15 years. Nigeria is my first position as ambassador. I had served mostly in international, regional economic organizations and the United Nations. I am an economist and that is why I was offered this job in Nigeria. I have a big economic interest in Nigeria.
I have to say that Nigeria-Ukraine relations have been smooth since our independence. We have very good relations in the political level and we are working closely with Nigeria in the United Nations framework, in peacekeeping operations and in many other things, but economical cooperation is our priority.
The turnover of trade is growing every year but it is still far from what we can achieve. Unfortunately, there is a negative balance of payment with Nigeria; I mean our balance of trade is 95% and 5% of trade are imports from Nigeria but we are coming with investors here.
We have also invested here on students. Every year, we send hundreds of students to Ukraine and we are ready to continue these practices since those people are our friends and those people who graduated from Ukrainian universities are working in different sectors of the economy in Nigeria.
How politically close is Ukraine to Nigeria?
We are very close. We have very good cooperation in the United Nations and in peace-keeping operations. Last year, our presidents met in the UN in New York during the just concluded 61st General Assembly. President Viktor Yushchenko invited President Obasanjo to Ukraine and we are working on that. We hope this will take place before the end of this year.
We have a number of legal agreements that we have to sign in order to lay down the legislative basis for our cooperation. The agreements are based on trade, economic cooperation, protection of investment activities, agreement and cooperation in the field of science and technology among other business issues.
We are planning to sign these agreements in Kiev when President Obasanjo visits Ukraine. That is very important for the two countries so that our investors can come here; that there can be guarantee and security for our businesses and that their investments can be protected.
We are working and supporting some special areas in Nigeria such as agriculture. Ukraine is one of the biggest suppliers of fertilizers to Nigeria and we are ready to step up our activities in this sector.
We are also a big supplier of farm equipments and tractors for Nigerian farms.
Another area of cooperation is solid minerals; we are working on this subject. Already, some business men have visited Nigeria.
We want a clear picture of where to work and we are ready to share our technical know-how and technologies since Ukraine is rich in solid mineral. We have that experience and we are ready to work here in the area of zinc, solid metallic ores, iron ore and coal.
And of course, oil and gas is also our interest. We are ready to come to Nigeria since we possess technologies on how to develop and rehabilitate abandoned wells; how to use geological tools and geophysics and we have all the necessary equipments.
In transportation services, we have heavy trucks, automobile and different types of specialized equipment to bring. We have planes and helicopters; by the way, one of the biggest Ukrainian companies in Nigeria-Ukrainian Helicopter, Nigeria-is here in Abuja. They are ready to offer services and they are working to get their license.
We have very good cooperation in the military affairs.
Our military troops were together in some areas in Africa like Sierra Leone and Liberia. We provide equipment and armament. We have established good contact with the Presidential Committee on Police Equipment Fund and we are working on that. We are ready to donate equipments and offer scholarship to the policemen. We are still planning it but the total cost could be up to seven million dollars all together which will include training, equipments and facilities. But first we like to see your delegates and we have invited your delegates to Ukraine to see what we can offer. We are in the process of starting negotiations.
Nigeria-Ukraine relations go beyond politics and economy. We talk of migration and movements and when we talk of that, we talk of visas. There have been some complains that embassy officials are rude to Nigerians when they come for visas. There are also speculations that the visa regime in the embassy is very harsh. What is your response to these allegations?
I don't think so. We have the same regime like any other embassy. We have rules and regulations in our country and we have to follow them. But I don't think we are creating additional obstacles or difficulties for your people. That is not correct. It is not the embassy that is making these rules but it is our government. We have the same rules and regulations applicable to all other countries and peoples.
Sometimes, unfortunately for us and Nigerians, some so-called students and applicants don't even know where to go to and why they are going or what they are going to do in the Ukraine.
That is considered by us as possible illegal immigrants; they don't have money, they don't have anything and therefore those people can't go.
But at the same time, there are real students who will like to go and they can't go to the Ukraine and we are very upset about this.
There are still some illegal people, some companies and some others who are trying to do business, capitalising on this situation and that is not good.
It is not only a problem with Nigeria since we have obligations with the European Union countries. We are close to the EU and we are close to Russia and other countries and we have to respect all of that.
But for your bonafide business men, we are open. If you go to Ukraine and you have relations, you could be given one year visa or a multiple visa and there is no problem with that, but you have to be careful.
But people say your visa process is strict?
I won't say it is strict. It is normal as it should be and not more than that. If you interview a person and he doesn't know the aim of his visit to the Ukraine, how can we give him visa; what is he going to do in Ukraine?
Some of them come and they say they want to go to Europe from Ukraine. We can't do that. There is no special restrictions because we dealing with Nigerians; it is the same everywhere.
Are you saying that the allegations are unfounded?
I have never heard of such expressions; that our approaches are rude...no...it is normal like any other embassy.
Like I have said, we have processed over the last few months about 500 student visa applications and most of them got the visa. But if a person doesn't know why he is going to Ukraine, we don't give visa.
You have mentioned many things about what Nigeria can get from Ukraine, but what can Nigeria get from Ukraine?
We might be interested in exchanging our technology for crude oil since we have a shortage of this and since we are an importing country. Ukraine gets only about 20 percent from within. We would like to diversify our sources of energy. We might consider importing minerals, timber and agricultural products.
I know that Nigeria has big prospect with imports on cassava and we may consider using this for our chemical and food industries. We have to bring our specialists here and we need more information.
Nigerians have to offer us what we can buy. This is our request to Nigerians to offer us more and more products to choose from.
Ukraine has been participating very well in peacekeeping missions in Africa. What is its cooperation with Nigeria in this area?
We have our technical battalions that are in Sierra Leone and Liberia. We are working on Darfur. But this question is quite sensitive because it has to be considered by the United Nations. If there is a mandate, Ukraine is ready to work.
Peacekeeping is heavy on a country's expenditure; what is the catch in peacekeeping operations?
We need peace in the world. Often times, we do it in areas where we have economic interest-we supply products, machineries and equipments there and that is why we want these areas to be peaceful. We want to see peace and stability in the region.
Ukraine is well known for the Orange revolution which may have marred relations with Russia; are these countries still allies or is your country fully capitalist?
We are a market economy country. Whether socialist or capitalist, our country is a market economy country like Sweden or Norway. I don't know whether you call those countries socialist or capitalists. They were more socialist than us in the past.
We are now officially recognized by the European Union as a market economy. Our relations with other neighbouring countries are based on that principle. Russia considers us as a market economy.
We don't have specialized economic relationships that are linked with political situations. Ukraine and Russia have projects and factories built in Nigeria like the Ajaokuta Steel and many others built by the Soviet Union at the time we were together.
We are working with Russia on the completion of Ajaokuta steel plant. For us, it is not a problem. If it is profitable for the countries, it's no problem.

Original_Search_Title: Budget of Consolidation
Headline: Nigeria: Budget of Consolidation
Body_Text:
Lagos — Being text of the 2007 budget speech presented by President Olusegun Obasanjo at the joint session of the National Assembly in Abuja on Wednesday
It is with great honour that I present to this Joint Session of the National Assembly, the 2007 Budget Proposal of the Federal Government of Nigeria.
As with the budgets for the last three years, the 2007 budget has been prepared against the background of a Medium-Term Expenditure Framework, which takes account of forecast revenue and expenditure for the next three years.
The preparation of the 2007 budget has followed a well-defined participatory process aimed at ensuring that the spending proposals presented therein are properly linked with our long-term poverty reduction strategies as articulated in the National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy (NEEDS) and the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs).
Building on the work done in 2005, the process for preparing the 2007 budget commenced with the development and documentation of Medium-Term Sector Strategies for 18 key Ministries, which account for about three quarters of the spending by Ministries, Departments and Agencies (MDAs).
These Medium-Term Sector Strategies detail fully costed initiatives and expenditure plans within the limits of available resources and set out well-defined performance measures including outputs and outcomes. These strategies have been prepared with the active participation of Civil Society, the Organised Private Sector and members of the relevant Committees of this Distinguished and Honourable Assembly.
I am personally gratified by your enthusiasm, commitment and invaluable contribution to the development of these strategies, which form the basis of, and accompany, the 2007 budget, which I will lay before you.
As part of our continuous efforts to strengthen public expenditure management and ensure transparency, accountability and value for money, the 2007 budget presents unprecedented level of details for our spending proposals. Specifically, we present before you, Payroll proposals with detailed Nominal Rolls, including names, file numbers and other information on the employees on the payroll of the Federal Government, Overheads proposal with detailed breakdown of cost components and unit costs, Capital spending proposals with project details, full information on project funding history, percentage completion and estimates of full completion costs.
Providing this level of detail has helped us to make significant spending efficiency gains and it is our hope that this level of detail will further foster transparency, accountability and value for money. We will use the payroll data, which we have now gathered to build an Integrated Personnel and Payroll Information System to better control payroll spending.
What is the 2007 budget all about? It is about "Accelerating Physical and Human Infrastructure for Wealth Creation and Poverty Reduction". It builds upon and consolidates the work that has been done with the budgets for the past three years and continues the reform of our national economy. As with the 2006 budget, it makes ample provision for improving physical infrastructure, particularly, water, power and roads. We have also accorded priority to social safety nets and measures aimed at reducing the incidence of poverty and improving our social indicators.
Specifically, the 2007 budget provides:-
- N11 billion for the dredging of the River Niger from Lokoja to Warri;
- N36 billion for the construction of the much awaited East-West Road;
- N21 billion for the construction of the Kano-Maiduguri Road dualisation;
- N17 billion for the completion of the Gurara Water Project;
- N80 billion for Pensions under the Pay-As-You-Go System;
- N50 billion to continue with and accelerate the on-going reforms of the public service.
Consistent with our commitments under the Paris Club Debt deal, the 2007 budget channels all the debt relief gains amounting to N110 billion to carefully selected poverty reduction initiatives and programmes in Health, Education, Water, Power, Housing, FCT, Youth and Agriculture, as additional, incremental spending for the attainment of the MDGs.
The 2007 budget is about the ordinary citizen, it is about accelerating the provision of basic infrastructure to improve the quality of life of our people and to enable businesses to thrive, create jobs and thereby reduce the incidence of poverty.
REVIEW OF BUDGET 2005
The total projected revenue for 2005 was N1.6 trillion (this included N165 billion from the "Excess Crude" account and N100 billion from independent revenue).
In the course of the 2005 fiscal year, revenue projections had to be revised downwards because of the situation in the Niger Delta. Total actual revenues in 2005 was therefore only about N1.4 trillion.
Against the actual revenues of N1.4 trillion in 2005, the 2005 Appropriation Act authorized aggregate spending of N1.8 trillion, representing a growth of 38%. This was made up of statutory transfers of N90 billion, debt service of N356 billion and MDA spending of N1.4 trillion representing a growth of 52%. The MDA spending comprised Recurrent (Non-Debt) Expenditure of N737 billion and Capital Expenditure of N617 billion respectively.
As a result of the significant reduction in available revenues, we could only implement N1.5 trillion out of N1.8 trillion authorised by the 2005 Appropriation Act. While N90 billion was planned for Transfers, actual Transfers was N79 billion because transfers to the Niger Delta Development Commission and the Universal Basic Education Commission are dependent on actual revenues. For Debt Service, N356 billion was planned but actual Debt Service was N394 billion.
The sum of N737 billion was planned for Recurrent (Non-Debt) while actual spending was N730 billion. For capital spending, N617 billion was appropriated and N519 billion was released and cash-backed but only N494 billion was utilised. Total capital budget implementation was thus 80% after taking into account the proportion of the spending that spilled over into 2006.
The fiscal deficit for 2005 was N100 billion or 0.8% of GDP, well below the budget forecast of N354 billion or 2.8% of GDP. The fiscal deficit for 2005 was financed from privatisation proceeds and signature bonuses received.
REVIEW OF BUDGET 2006
The 2006 Budget was predicated on oil production of 2.5 million barrels per day and a benchmark oil price of US$35 per barrel. Based on these assumptions, we projected that total revenue available to fund the federal budget would be N1.5 trillion.
Early in the second quarter of 2006, we lost production of 600,000 barrels per day from the Joint Venture operations due to disruptions in the Niger Delta. These losses are likely to continue until year-end. Due to these losses, we estimate that revenue accruing to the Federation Account will be N570 billion less than planned. The impact of this on the federal budget is about N216 billion.
In order to ensure smooth implementation of the budgets of the Federal, State and Local Governments, we sought your understanding to draw up to N570 billion from the excess crude account to make up for this shortfall. Half of this sum was distributed in July 2006. The other half will be distributed in October after reviewing the situation for the first three quarters of the year.
As at 31 August 2006, out of the N92 billion appropriated for Transfers, a total of N52 billion had been released.
For Debt Service, implementation stood at N159 billion or 55% of the appropriated sum of N290 billion. The Recurrent (Non-Debt) Expenditure comprising Payroll and Overheads was implemented in full. For Capital Expenditure, a total of N426 billion, or 75% of the appropriated amount of N569 billion has been released.
With respect to healthcare, we have completed the rehabilitation and re-equipping of four of our Teaching Hospitals and they are now centres of excellence for tertiary healthcare, training and research. Another four are to be completed very soon. A total of 200 Primary Health Care Centres have been completed and fully equipped. An additional 93 Primary Health Care Centres are close to completion. Contracts for the construction and equipping of an additional 150 such Centres and the refurbishing of 100 existing Centres will soon be awarded.
National HIV/AIDS prevalence rate has dropped in 2005 to 4.4% from 5.0% in 2003. This development is a direct result of the increased awareness advocacy that we have embarked upon and the establishment of 190 Voluntary Counselling and Testing (VCT) Centres nationwide. We are supporting infected persons with Anti-retroviral drugs through over 100 Treatment Centres which we have established nationwide.
To combat malaria, over 10 million Insecticide Treated Nets have been distributed. We are presently procuring 3 million additional Insecticide Treated Nets for distribution to pregnant women and children under the age of 5. We have trained about 4,000 health workers and over 3,000 community leaders on the new malaria treatment policy. Two and a half million doses of new treatment drugs have been distributed to 18 States and the FCT. Another 2.9 million doses are currently being distributed to the remaining 18 States. These drugs are being distributed free of charge for use by children under the age of 5.
We have procured chemicals and spray pumps in readiness for the commencement of house-to-house and outdoor spraying to destroy mosquitoes.
We believe that these will help reduce the incidence of malaria and malaria related deaths.
Works
As at 31 August 2006, we had released N52 billion or 71 % of the N73 billion appropriated for capital for Works for the construction, rehabilitation and repairs of roads nationwide. Road construction, rehabilitation and repair works are at various stages of completion. Roads such as, Ibadan-Ilorin, Abuja-Keffi, Kaduna Eastern Bypass, Kano-Kaduna Dual Carriageway, Bodo-Bonny, Eleme Junction Fly Over, Warri-Benin, Benin Bypass and Onitsha-Owerri, are receiving priority attention.
Power
On Power, we have made significant progress in the rehabilitation and expansion of generation capacity, transmission and distribution. Work is progressing on the seven new medium-sized Power Plants in the Niger Delta Region, at Ihovbor, Sapele, Gbarain, Egbema, Calabar and Omoku. 4 additional Power Stations in Geregu, Papalanto, Omotosho and Alaoji are now nearing completion. The procurement process for the development of the 2,600 MW Mambilla Hydro Power Station is almost completed and all contracts for the procurement process are expected to be finalised soon.
Following the enactment of the Electricity Power Reform Act, we have made far-reaching institutional changes in the power sector. These changes include the unbundling of NEPA, deregulation of the sector to admit participation by other tiers of government and the private sector, the establishment of the Nigerian Electricity Regulatory Commission and the Rural Electrification Agency.
We are on course to complete about 557 electricity constituency projects, which were awarded in 2006. We have commissioned 2 Pilot Solar Projects in Katsina and Bauchi States and have recently awarded the contract for another 2 such projects in Cross River and Ogun States.
Aviation
We are taking decisive steps to reposition the aviation sector. We are deploying modern and efficient navigational aids through the Total Radar Coverage Project and the Safe Tower Project. We are rehabilitating and resurfacing runways, erecting perimeter fencing and upgrading meteorological services to ensure the safety of air travel.
We have procured search and rescue training equipment and are strengthening institutional capacity in the aviation sector. Our immediate goal is to ensure that the aviation industry passes the International Civil Aviation Authority (ICAO) audit slated for November 2006.
Agriculture
Our strategic programmes in Agriculture are helping to ensure sustainable production of adequate nutritional food for Nigerians and for the export market.
Output of staples such as maize, millet, sorghum, cassava, rice, vegetable oil and yam have increased tremendously as a direct result of the Presidential Initiatives in these areas and our fertilizer procurement and distribution programme through which we continue to subsidize the price of fertilizer. Specifically, annual production of cassava tubers has increased by about 14 million metric tonnes, annual production of rice has increased by more than 1 million metric tonnes and annual production of maize has increased by well over 1 million metric tonnes.
To curb post harvest losses and protect the gains of increased production, we have increased our silo storage capacity to 1 million metric tonnes with the recent completion and commissioning of four additional silo complexes.
