Lwamunda CFR, which has 4,696 hectares of forestland, is located in Nalya-Nasese village, Muduuma sub-county, Mpigi district.
"On August 24, the veterans invaded the forest, cut down two acres of forestland and up-rooted residents' crops. They left the land bare," said Esther Nekesa, the Lwamunda CFR supervisor.
"The National Forestry Authority (NFA) officials intervened fast," she said.
The forest, on the Kampala-Mityana highway, had originally been encroached on by local residents, who used the undergrowth to grow cabbages, rice, cassava, beans and tomatoes.
"We advised the veterans to apply to NFA so that they can be considered for the re-afforestation programme.
"Their application was forwarded to the headquarters but after about a fortnight, the veterans grew impatient. They said we were delaying them yet they wanted their issue to be handled urgently," explained Maj. Samwiri Rwaboogo, the NFA operations coordinator.
Rwaboogo said re-afforestation programme applications have to go through various committees before they are approved.
On September 13, the residents came back using about 10 power chain saws instead of axes and resumed felling mostly first-class trees: Nkoba, Mpewere, Nkalati, Musizi, Mituba (Ficus mucuso), Kirundu and Namukaago, many of them over 30 years old. As they felled trees, lorries were on standby at the site to load, a witness said.
The forest is also home to various monkey species, baboons, ante-lopes, birds and wild pigs. Monkeys now have to travel a long distance in search for Mituba seeds, which they feed on.

Original_Search_Title: Horsfall Tasks N/Delta Governors On Devt
Headline: Nigeria: Horsfall Tasks N/Delta Governors On Devt
Body_Text:
Lagos — Adviser to President Olusegun Obasanjo on the Marshal Plan for the Niger Delta, Chief A.K. Horsfall, has called on state governments in the region to give more responsibilities and resources to the actual communities affected by oil exploitation for their own development as such communities know their own priorities.
Horsfall, currently on a week-long projects monitoring visit to Ondo State made the call while inspecting various completed and on-going projects being implemented by service providers in Ondo State, including Ondo State government, Ondo State Oil Producing Areas Development Commission (OSOPADEC), Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC), Owena River Basin Authority and the Federal Ministry of Communications.
Among the projects he inspected were employment-generating projects such as the Honey Processing Factory; the Sunshine Fisheries Limited, Alagbaka, the Sunshine Cassava and Food Processing Plant; Awo Yaya and the newly constructed Federal, State Co-operation Primary School at Igbado under the
UBE scheme and several other projects by the same service providers.
Of all the projects he inspected, Horsfall said he had the greatest impression about the quality and utility of OSOPADEC projects, particularly the 12 billion naira 35 kilometer Igbokoda-Ayetoro road project, which traverses the marshland oil producing territory of the state, aiming to reach Ayetoro and ultimately to the Atlantic offshore.
He expressed satisfaction that the project is being constructed by OSOPADEC in a territory previously considered impassable and cost ineffective, adding that the project demonstrates the propriety of giving responsibilities and resources to the actual communities affected by the ravages of oil exploitation.
The Presidential adviser also said that it is projects of this nature that will ultimately open up the Niger Delta for total development.
He therefore enjoined other state governments of the Niger Delta to emulate the good example of the Ondo State government by creating a commission for the utilization of the 13% derivation fund, with at least 60% going to the communities.

Original_Search_Title: Mobile Phone Purchases Rise
Headline: Ghana: Mobile Phone Purchases Rise
Body_Text:
THE ACTING Government Statistician, Prof. N. Nsowah Nuamah, has emphasized that Ghanaians are buying more mobile phones in the country than ever; hence contributing to stable prices of food items in the basket for measuring the Consumer Price Index (CPI).
This, he said, has helped in the current price stability of foodstuffs in the markets, inasmuch as excess expenditure on food alone in an economy was never a good sign.
Speaking to the media during a Consumer Price Index survey at the Makola Market in Accra yesterday, he noted that getting the attention and cooperation of sellers in the various markets to give concrete data was sometimes poor, but with continuous persuasions some have now understood the meaning of the exercise and were helping in some way.
He continued that in order to get the full attention of the market women, in the proper positioning of the economy by government, calendars would be designed with their pictures, whiles a dinner party and discussions would as well be held with them in November for their maximum support concerning the provision of data.
"The aim of this exercise was to enable our journalists learn firsthand how the CPI market reading is conducted and to sensitize the public on the CPI bi-monthly price collection".
Foodstuffs such as a pineapple weighing 133grams costs ¢5,000; four oranges weighing 0.700kg went for ¢1,000; and four pawpaws weighing 1,600grams cost ¢5,000.
A 0.9litre of coconut oil also went for ¢7,000, while palm oil of 0.79litre was ¢7,000.
Garden eggs (egg plants) weighing 450grams went for ¢2,000; 4 average-sized tomatoes have the same price and weight as the garden eggs, and seven pieces of ginger weighing 200grams cost ¢2,000.
12 fingers of banana cost ¢6,000, weighing 1.100kilo; the weight of cassava (5) was 3kilos and priced at ¢5,000.
Others included snails (5), weighing 500grams, costing ¢20,000; one American tin of maize for ¢6,000 weighed 2700grams, amongst others.
The Assistant Statistician-Field Section, Greater Accra, Mrs. Josephine Djatugbe Dekoleadenu, took journalists through the Consumer Price Index market reading at the Makola market to familiarize themselves with the market reading.
In an interview with this paper, Mr. Ebo Duncan, Head of National Accounts, stated that controlling money supply would help stabilize the economy and as well enhance the effectiveness of prices on foodstuffs.

Original_Search_Title: Again, Honour Beckons On Abdullahi
Headline: Nigeria: Again, Honour Beckons On Abdullahi
Body_Text:
Lagos — Today, in the ancient town of Owu, in Ogun State, Nasarawa State governor, Alhaji Abdulahi Adamu, will add another feather to his cap. He will be conferred with the chieftaincy title of Teruru of Owu kingdom by the Olowu of Owu, Oba Dosumu.
This conferment will mark the second time that the governor will bag chieftaincy awards in the South- west of the country. Only in May last year, Adamu was conferred with the title of Aare Obateru of Ile-Ife by the Ooni of Ife, Oba Okunade Sijuade.
Born on July, 23, 1946, Adamu, trained both as an engineer and lawyer, began his political career in 1977, when he was elected into the Constituent Assembly, a body responsible for the drafting of the countryÃ­s Second Republic Constitution of 1979.
Thereafter, he became a pioneer member of the National Movement which later metamorphosed into the National Party of Nigeria (NPN).
He became the first Secretary of the Party in December 1978 and later the party's chairman in Plateau State between 1982 and 1983 before the military truncated that republic. He had hitherto served as a member of theNational Executive Committee (NEC) of the party.
Building on his experience at the Constituent Assembly, Adamu was again appointed into the National Constitutional Conference during the military administration of General Sani Abacha, the conference mandated with the task of drafting a new constitution for the Fourth Republic.
In March 1995, he became Minister of State for Works and Housing of the Federal Republic of Nigeria where he served till November 17, 1997.
One of the founding member of the People's Democratic Party (PDP), on whose platform he contested the governorship of Nasarawa, Adamu emerged winner. He was subsequently sworn in as the governor and re-elected in 2003 for a second term of office.
As governor, he had served as chairman of Governor's Forum as well as the chairman, Northern States Governors' Forum. He equally served as chairman, committees on the National Identity Card and Registration of Voters in 2002. When the Federal Government set up Citizens and Rights Committee with a view to finding solution to the recurring problem of indigene/settler conflicts in the country, he became the chairman.
Adamu is a custodian of countless honorary awards. Among the awards he has bagged are doctorate degrees (Honoris Causa) in Law, Rivers State University of Science and Technology, Port Harcourt and Nnamdi Azikiwe University, Awka respectively. He has also bagged a honorary doctorate degree (Honoris Causa) in Engineering at the Federal University of Technology, Akure, Ondo State and Doctor of Science (D.Sc.), the University of Port Harcourt.
Adamu, a technocrat and farmer, has been visible in the management of ethnic crisis which has engulfed his state since inception. For instance, at the heat of the outbreak of violence among the Tivs and other groups in Awe council area and other parts of Nasarawa South senatorial zone, Adamu reportedly took a position, insisting that every tribe or group of people in the state had the right to live happily without being undermined by any other tribe or group.
This position was in contrast with the pressure being mounted on him from every other tribe at the time, to consider some tribes as superior to others.
Adamu, who is the Sarkin Yarkin Keffi (the people's warrior), was a staunch advocate of the botched third term agenda of President Olusegun Obasanjo.
This, many have viewed as his major shortcoming. Besides, he is also noted as a rigid man, who for instance, sent some delegates out of the venue of public hearing on the review of the Constitution in February this year.
But for these two major weaknesses, Adamu's government is said to have recorded some feats in governance. For instance, it recently entered into a contract with a North Korean firm for the construction of a 25,000-seater stadium in the state.
Besides, Nasarawa State has been portrayed as a highly enabling business environment for industries in various sectors of the economy. The government is also said to have established various industries which are already in operation. These include the beef processing factory, sesme seed processing factory, a bagging plant and a cassava processing plant as well as precious stone-cutting and polishing centre.
The state government is said to have established most of these companies to kick-start industrial development in the state and now these companies are slated for privatization.
The government is also said to have built five functioning factories at a time some state governments are selling their companies.
They are Nasara sacks and Packaging plant, Akwanga, Soap factory, Akwanga, fertilizer plant in Lafia, the state capital, beef factory, Masaka, lapidary plant Nassarawa.
Consequent upon these achievements, the Owu traditional council has considered Adamu worthy of being installed as the Teruru of Owu. For the Sarkin Yakin, again, honour beckons even in a foreign land

Original_Search_Title: Gani Says Obasanjo's Shares Breached Code of Conduct
Headline: Nigeria: Transcorp Shares - Gani Drags
Body_Text:
Lagos — Frontline lawyer, Chief Gani Fawehinmi (SAN), yesterday dragged President Olusegun Obasanjo before the Code of Conduct Bureau in Abuja, asking it to charge the President to the Code of Conduct Tribunal for alleged contravention of Paragraph 13, Part 1 of the Fifth Schedule of the 1999 Constitution.
Specifically, the learned senior advocate said the President's acquisition of shares in a mega corporation, Trans-National Corporation (Transcorp) Plc, through Obasanjo Holdings Limited OHL), breached the code of conduct for public officers.
The paragraph the President allegedly breached states: "A public officer who does any act prohibited by this Code through a nominee, trustee, or other agent shall be deemed ipso facto to have a committed a breach of this Code."
Gani in a petition dated October 3, addressed to the chairman of the bureau, stated nine grounds of protest, claiming in the main that Obasanjo grossly abused his office as President and contravened the provisions of the Constitution when he (Obasanjo) allegedly used his company, Obasanjo Holdings Limited, to acquire substantial shares in Transcorp Plc.
Consequently, he prayed the bureau to seize or forfeit the President's interests in Transcorp to the Federal Government.
Gani said he was filing his petition pursuant to Item 12, Part 1, Fifth Schedule and Item 3(E), Part 1, Third Schedule of the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, 1999, which prescribes: "any allegation that a public officer has committed a breach of or has not complied with the provisions of this Code shall be made to the Code of Conduct Bureau."
He said he also relied on Item 3 of the schedule, which states: "the Bureau shall have power to receive complaints about non-compliance with or breach of the provisions of the Code of Conduct or any law in relation thereto, investigate the complaint and, where appropriate, refer such matters to the Code of Conduct Tribunal."
Describing his claims and reliefs as urgent, Gani said Obasanjo's alleged interests in Transcorp amounted to conflict of interest with his official duty contrary to Item 1 of the Code of Conduct for Public Officers contained in Part 1, Fifth Schedule of the 1999 Constitution, saying the acquisition or owning of substantial shares/interest was done through his company, Obasanjo Holdings Limited.
He said not only was the President present at the official launch of the corporation on July 21, 2005 but that Obasanjo announced some concessions to the corporation as part of government support and encouragement.
Gani listed the concessions: Licence to build a 400,000 barrels per day refinery; Licence to build an independent power plant; access to the Federal Government cassava project for construction of cassava processing exports facility; designated land mass for construction of free port facilities; continued support to help open up markets on the African continent and to make Transcorp a partner in Nigeria's current policy on private/public partnership, creating additional opportunities to develop large scale projects in oil and gas, power and Information and Communications Technology (ICT).
He said government favours to the corporation had manifested in its acquisition of some public concerns, including four oil blocks, (OPL 218, 219, 209 and 220) allocated to it on July 21, 2005 by Obasanjo during its launching; Nicon-Hilton, Abuja and Nigerian Tele-communications Limited (NITEL).
According to him having made these concessions to the corporation, it was wrong for him to acquire interest in it, saying the testimonies of Transcorp Chairman, Dr. Ndi Okereke-Onyiuke, and two trustees of OHL, Elder Daniel Atsu and Mr. Lucky Egede, confirmed the President's interest in the corporation.
Consequently, he said that the President's alleged conduct breached the code particularly his Oath of Allegiance and Oath of Office wherein he swore not to allow any personal interest to influence his official conduct or official decisions.
The learned senior advocate, therefore, prayed the bureau to prefer a charge against the President at the Code of Conduct Tribunal for contravening paragraph 13, Part 1 of the Fifth Schedule of the 1999 Constitution; and that the interests acquired by him in Transcorp Plc through Obasanjo Holdings Limited should be seized or forfeited to the Federal Government as provided under Item 18 (2)(c) of the Code of Conduct for Public Officers contained in the Fifth Schedule, Part 1 of the 1999 Constitution.

This position was in contrast with the pressure being mounted on him from every other tribe at the time, to consider some tribes as superior to others.
Adamu, who is the Sarkin Yarkin Keffi (the people's warrior), was a staunch advocate of the botched third term agenda of President Olusegun Obasanjo.
This, many have viewed as his major shortcoming. Besides, he is also noted as a rigid man, who for instance, sent some delegates out of the venue of public hearing on the review of the Constitution in February this year.
But for these two major weaknesses, Adamu's government is said to have recorded some feats in governance. For instance, it recently entered into a contract with a North Korean firm for the construction of a 25,000-seater stadium in the state.
Besides, Nasarawa State has been portrayed as a highly enabling business environment for industries in various sectors of the economy. The government is also said to have established various industries which are already in operation. These include the beef processing factory, sesme seed processing factory, a bagging plant and a cassava processing plant as well as precious stone-cutting and polishing centre.
The state government is said to have established most of these companies to kick-start industrial development in the state and now these companies are slated for privatization.
The government is also said to have built five functioning factories at a time some state governments are selling their companies.
They are Nasara sacks and Packaging plant, Akwanga, Soap factory, Akwanga, fertilizer plant in Lafia, the state capital, beef factory, Masaka, lapidary plant Nassarawa.
Consequent upon these achievements, the Owu traditional council has considered Adamu worthy of being installed as the Teruru of Owu. For the Sarkin Yakin, again, honour beckons even in a foreign land

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Falls to 6 Percent
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Falls to 6 Percent
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE annual headline inflation rate for September dropped to 6.2% from 7.5% in August.
"Prices of foods like sweet potatoes and cassava went up by 1.7%, while matooke prices went up in Kampala, Jinja, Mbale, Masaka and Mbarara except in Gulu. Fish, milk and eggs' prices increased in a number of centres due to seasonal and distribution factors. Prices of fruits, some vegetables and cereals went up due to low supply," Steven Bahemuka, the acting principal statistician at the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (UBOS), said.
He was releasing the Consumer Price Index at the finance ministry in Kampala.
The monthly headline inflation went up by 0.8% due to an increase in the prices of foodstuffs.
Prices of other commodities like charcoal, local brew, household items, soap and toiletries also rose.
Transport fares and medical charges also increased.
Vincent Musoke, the price statician at UBOS, said the transport fares rose because of speed governors.
"Police restricted tax operators to carrying three passengers per row. The charges had to increase to compensate for that gap," he said.
Musoke said the medical charges shot up as a result of high import taxes for goods from Germany, India and the UK.
Bahemuka said October rates would be similar to September's or slightly higher.

Original_Search_Title: Nostalgia
Headline: Nigeria: Nostalgia
Body_Text:
Independence anniversary celebration inadvertently brings the subject of history to the centre stage, which at least for once challenges the daily dictatorship and pre-eminence of the twin-subject of economics and politics in our lives.
This year's 46th independence anniversary was no exception. The celebration was officially declared "low-key" but not without another generous official public holiday (on a Monday 2nd October!) to enable us certainly deepen our idle and unproductive capacity; further entrench poverty and illiteracy (schools also close today!).
What else could explain the official declaration of a public holiday for a "low-key" celebration if not our preference for do nothing (no thanks to easy oil money!) even as we declare commitment to wealth generation from the roof top? The reform can boast of some bright sides - the notable anti-corruption but one dark side of the Obasanjo administration's reform agenda is the business as usual attitude with respect to frivolous public holidays which legitimise idleness and undermine productive planning altogether especially in the private sector said to be the engine of growth! This year alone, we have declared scores of days as public holidays (New Year day, Easter days, census days, democracy day, etc) and there will be more given a number of expected festivals! When actually are we working?
The point cannot be overstated that economic recovery eludes Nigeria without productivity and time is the main critical success factor in productivity improvement. Since independence day observance has been reduced to prayers this year, observers noted that last Friday and Sunday offered enough time for reflections and supplication on the state of the nation such that by today, Nigeria should get back to the world of work already dominated by competing nations like China, Malaysia, etc.
In any case, work is also an act of worship! Veterans of independence struggle are nostalgic to recollect that it was sleeplessness and round-the-clock hard work of the founding fathers and mothers of the nation that conferred independence just as today's idleness and undeserved leisure may inadvertently be pushing us back to dependency and neo-colonialism.
In any case, when we "are at work", many children are still malnourished, as many as 70 per cent of the population live below a dollar per day, life expectancy still below the average of middle income country that we are, inflation is still double digits, primary school enrolment is still far from the idle, power supply is still miserable. The critical question is: is it when we idle away via generous underserved rest that we are willing to improve on all these development fundamentals? The impressive growth rates of post-colonial Nigeria were built against the backdrop of rigid productive national calendar within the context of national development planning, which made productive and educational planning possible.
In the anniversary speech President Olusegun Obasanjo took an exception to the picture of despair by reminding the nation of the progress since 1999. Very few will certainly disagree that there has been some remarkable improvement in key parameters of development. However, even at that, many note that the indicators in post-colonial Nigeria were even far more impressive. It was certainly good news that Nigeria's cassava and cocoa production has more than doubled.
Great news that we are exporting cassava to China. Some economic revival indeed! But economic historians recollect that in the 50s and 60s, Nigeria was the largest exporter of groundnuts, palm produce and second exporter of cocoa. In fact, these three crops constituted 70 per cent of Nigeria's exports while cotton, rubber and timber were also significant. The post-independence leaders were nonetheless still not content with this impressive performance in agriculture.
They refused to remain celebrating being "Europe's small farmers". On the contrary, they worked tirelessly on local addition via manufacturing activities which in turn led to the establishment and spread of labour intensive industries like food and beverages and textile relying on local agricultural produce. It is a sad commentary that the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN) reported recently that as a result of mass closures of these import-substituting industries, Nigeria is back to the pre-independence era in terms of de-industrialisation occasioned by sundry infrastructural crisis that made industry uncompetitive.
Thus, 46 years after, we cannot possibly stop celebrating being Europe's small farmers to becoming new China's small farmers. We just must also add value to cassava like other products at home through employment-generating value-adding activities. Also remarkable is the fact that from the 60s to the mid-80s, Nigeria ran a regime of single digit inflation and interest rate that averaged four and six per cent respectively which in turn nurtured good investment climate and good quality of life. Thus, if inflation has reduced from the high point of 14.5 percent in 2000 to 10.7 percent in 2006, we are still far from where we used to be and it's not over yet until we improve on colonial legacy.
Lastly, when it comes to leadership, many Nigerians are far more nostalgic and even romanticise the past while bemoaning the present. Standard literature on Nigeria in the 60s and 70s had no chapter on corruption but on industrialisation, agriculture, productivity, labour and compensation and transport.
The recent revelations by the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) that some state governors are under-developing their states as it were through systemic graft and corruption that run into billions of naira shows the widening gap between the leaders and the mass of people in post-colonial Nigeria. Every student of history knows how Europe underdeveloped Africa through forced labour, exploitation and extraction of surplus value, but it was unthinkable that Nigeria's "elected" leaders that were expected to articulate the aspirations of the masses will so much impoverish them through alleged open robbery.
We always know the few rich exist while there are the poor. But it was unthinkable that the greatest wealth by the very few governors will coexist with the greatest poverty of the governed majority.

Original_Search_Title: Bauchi to Establish Electronics Coy
Headline: Nigeria: Bauchi to Establish Electronics Coy
Body_Text:
Bauchi State has concluded arrangements to establish an electrical and electronics company to stimulate industrialisation.
Gov. Ahmadu Mu'azu made this known Friday in Bauchi while declaring open the 17th Meeting of the Nati-onal Council on Industry.
He said efforts were underway to set up a mini sugar company and cassava proce-ssing plant at Waya Dam.
The establishment of those companies were meant to enhance industrial growth, create wealth and reduce poverty, the governor said.
Mu'azu said the Bauchi state government had deci-ded to adopt a policy of liber-alisation and privatisation of all industries in the state.
The governor said a com-mittee had been constituted to appraise all ailing indu-stries in the state with a view to privatising them.
The government had provided 189.05 hectares of land for the federal gover-nment's dry port project in Bauchi, he said.
Mu'azu stressed that the project was designed to serve the entire North-East sub-region, complete with custom facilities for import and export.
"The project is expected to be financed through priv-ate sector initiative under the Build-Own-Operate and Tra-nsfer (BOOT) arrangement," Mu'azu said.
The National Council on Industry is the highest policy making body on industrial matters in the country.
It is the forum where all commissioners in-charge for industrial matters in the 36 states of federation and the minister of industry meet to discuss issues affecting the industrial sector. (NAN)

Original_Search_Title: Council to Commercialise Plants in Kaduna, Kano, Katsina
Headline: Nigeria: Council to Commercialise Plants in Kaduna, Kano, Katsina
Body_Text:
The Kaolin Beneficiation plants in Katsina and Kano States, Phosphate Beneficiation and Intergraded Groundnut oil mill, Sokoto state, Granulated Limestone, Calabar and fruit juice processing plant in Kaduna State are to be commercialised by the Raw Materials Research and Development Council {RMRDC}.
According to the Dire-ctor General of the Council, Professor Azikiwe Peter Onwualu arrangements to divest from these plants have since commenced.
He said the essence is to allow private sector particip-ation and make them more viable and efficient.
Speaking while receiving the Members of the House of Representatives committee on Science and Technology in his office last week, On-wualu said the Council built and presented to Kebbi State, a Fish smoking Kiln to assist the rural women.
He told the Committee that "Arrangements have been concluded to set-up a salt processing plant in each of the salt producing commu-nities in the Nasarawa, Ebo-nyi and Kebbi as part of the council's strategy for women empowerment and emer-gence of rural industries".
He mentioned some of the target programmes of the councils as production of a research and development blueprint on cassava, prom-otion of high quality cassava flour and glucose syrup production, funding of the research and development on Ethanol production from cassava among others.
Other progress made by the Council Onwualu said, Include replacement of asb-estos with saw dust in brake pad production, production of dry mango chips and extraction and evaluation of calabash seed oil and the pos-sible use of lipid - free extract as animal feed.
Chairman of the Committee, Honourable Festus Adegoke urged the Council to spread its activities to different parts of the country.
"RMRDC is doing exactly what we are expecting them to do- touching the lives of ordinary Nigerians... but we expect more from you", said Adegoke.

Original_Search_Title: Kasubi Market
Headline: Uganda: Kasubi Market
Body_Text:
Kampala — The moon is shining brightly as energetic men offload trucks of produce. It is Friday night and tomorrow is the big market day, so naturally it's a night out - only in Kasubi, instead of beer sipping, the overnight activity is the offloading.
The market looks beautiful, lit as it is by countless paraffin candles (tadooba), each announcing the location of a trader.
The smoke from the candles merges with the dust raised by shoppers and the market looks misty.
It also looks like a massive kitchen with charcoal stoves everywhere; some chicken warming here and maize and potatoes roasting there, not to mention sizzling barbecues and frying fish and chapati.
A man walks by nibbling at a maize cob as a woman stuffs smoked fish into her shopping bag. Nearby is a young man with his arm around a girl's waist.
Christopher Kiwanuka, the Kasubi LC1 chairman says, "When Kabaka Muteesa I chose Nabulagala to be his capital in 1882, he changed its common name to a royal one - Kasubi. He had grown up in a village called Kasubi in Kyaggwe in the present day Mukono District."
"When he became king, he had to relocate to the Banda palace. However, with time, His Majesty got bored with the old palace that he had occupied for over two decades," says Steven Mpanga, a custodian at the Kasubi Tombs.
The Kabaka decided to establish his palace on Nabulagala Hill. It was a huge, sophisticated grass-thatched hut on 64 acres of prime land.
"He built over 600 houses there, 84 of them for his 84 official wives," Mpanga says. All the houses have since collapsed leaving just the palace which UNESCO in 1991 added to the list of the world's cultural heritage centres.
When Muteesa died in 1884, he was buried in his palace, which now known as Kasubi Tombs, houses the graves of four Buganda kings.
It was only prudent of the king to designate a place, just outside the palace, where thousands of his royals could do business.
Mpanga says, "He had told them, 'Don't sit about doing nothing. Work. The world is changing.'"
The Kabaka was referring to the influx of whites and Asians that was commercially driven. However, seeing no point in working, his people leased out the land he had given them to commoners.
Soon those commoners developed the land into a market. The market got the name Munaku Yeegulira, which means even the poorest can buy.
"That was around 1883. It operated according to the barter trade system of the time, where beads, bangles and bark cloth were the common commodities," says Mpanga.
He adds, "The market saw the coming of cowries in the late 1880s. Coins were introduced in 1902. The locals used the coins for around 18 years until notes were introduced in 1920. The notes were commonly called 'cheque'. However, they were difficult for the people to 'bank' because they would rot in their moneybags or pots, unlike the good old coins."
Mpanga says the market boomed for close to a century until the construction of the Kampala-Hoima highway began and threatened to cut the market into two. The market was then shifted from the eastern side of the royal hill where the present market is, to the western side which instantly took on the name Munaku.
"However, by this time, permanent commercial buildings had already been built in the area. Some traders therefore stayed, as the rest relocated to Munaku," he adds, "These (the ones that stayed) sustained the township of Kasubi with only a tiny market remaining."
This tiny market slowly grew to become the market that now lies between two roads - Kawaala Road and the Hoima highway.
Walking through Kasubi Market on any day, what will catch your attention is how the commodities on sale encroach on the roads.
Looking at the rows of traders displaying their heaps of matooke and mounds of watermelon and pineapples and pawpaws, and you can't help but believe that Uganda is a pearl.
Just centimetres off the highway, a man sings the praises of his cabbages, an elderly woman dances beside the maize roasting on her stove to 'Beera Nange' a popular gospel song playing in one of the shops while an adolescent girl fries offals, a basket of steamed potatoes by her side.
Buses, taxis, and trucks, carefully make their way through the market as buyers stand in a kind of a corridor between the cars and heaps of commodities on the roadside. Bicycles and bodabodas add to the commotion.
The market doesn't close until around midnight, when traders cover their commodities with pieces of canvas and head for their homes, leaving everything under the protection of the royal spirits.
By 4:00am the next morning, a new day is knocking at the door. Any other day, business would not start this early, but today is Saturday - the busiest day of the week.
Trucks of produce are arriving from all over the country - Gulu, Mbale, Luweero, Mbarara, Hoima - loaded with literally everything.
Atop one of the trucks, a woman stands on sacks of cassava overseeing the young men offloading them.
Her name is Yudita Namukasa and she owns the truck and its contents. She's been travelling all night from Nakasongola where the cassava is grown in plenty. She has a pen and a book in her hands, busy keeping track of her goods. She says, "I was a petty trader at first. Now I go to up-country farmers to buy directly from them. I deal mostly in potatoes and cassava, and sometimes maize."
By 5:00am, people have begun arriving with shopping bags in their hands. On foot, by taxi, on bicycles, bodabodas, and pushing wheelbarrows, the shoppers come. Some are taking the produce to smaller markets in the neighbourhood, the wheelbarrow pushers are going to hawk the food, others are taking the food to restaurants while others are shopping for their homes.
An hour later, to the east of Kasubi, God begins to paint the Ivory Tower red and orange One begins to see the many tall students' hostels in Kikoni outside Makerere University.
The new day is full of hope for Kasubi, the royal market.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo - Time to Reject Pseudo Leaders
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo - Time to Reject Pseudo Leaders
Body_Text:
Broadcast by His Excellency, President Olusegun Obasanjo, GCFR on the occasion of the 46th Independence Anniversary ofThe Federal Republic of Nigeria Abuja, October 1, 2006
MY dear compatriots,
Happy Independence Anniversary. Today is the 46th Anniversary of our independence and four years from now, we will be celebrating half-a-century of nationhood. Yes, we have had some mis-steps and challenges; mistakes have been made, difficulties have been created, but in all these God has made us to continue to survive as one strong indivisible nation. God has made us to surmount difficulties that have compromised the health and integrity of many nations. God has given us victory over challenges and God continues to direct, guide and sustain our steps and our progress. For all these and more, I give glory to God Almighty.
In our statement on Thursday, I asked that both Nigerian Moslems and Christians should make this weekend one of praise, thanksgiving and reflection. We must also use the opportunity to pray for the souls of those that departed this world through accidents and other causes in the past year while thanking God for the strength He gave us to bear the losses and move on as a people and a nation.
It is important for us to refresh our minds of where we started from in this Administration, where we are and, of course, where we want to be. I will take some key indicators to illustrate why we need to continue to thank God unceasingly. In 1999, we were a nation in deep crises, shunned by the world and steeped in anger, violence, and instability at home and unsure of ourselves. Our dignity, pride and morale had been thoroughly debased, contaminated, corrupted and compromised. Today, we are loved by all, elected to important positions in international organizations, receiving tourists and investors at an unprecedented rate, and respected wherever we go. Our GDP growth at 1990 constant prices which was a mere 1.19% in 1999 rose to 6.23 in 2005 with the non-oil sector increasing from 4.37% in 1999 to 8.21% in 2005. The growth rate in agriculture which was 5.28% in 1999 stood at 7% in 2006, solid minerals rose from 3.79% to 9.50%, telecommunication from 5.39% to 28.96%; manufacturing from 3.44% to 9.41%; while wholesale and retail trade increased from 2.50% in 1999 to 12.32%.
Inflation which was as high as 14.5% in 2000 has been reduced to 10.7%, our foreign debt which was US$28.3 billion in 1999 has been reduced to US$5.3 billion through an US$18 billion debt relief from the Paris Club and repayments by government. Our external reserve was a mere US$3.7 billion in 1999. Today we are almost US$45 billion. Our agricultural policies are yielding very positive results as we are now self-sufficient in poultry and poultry products and vegetable oil. We no longer rely on imported juice drinks and our programme for producing fruits and vegetables locally is making very good progress. We are almost self-sufficient in fish and fish products as well as in rice. Our cassava production as well as cocoa and other crops have more than doubled and our strategic grains reserve for the first time reached 150,000 metric tonnes.
We are now exporting cassava to China and other destinations just as we are opening new trade routes to other parts of the world. Our core welfare indicators are all on the positive trend. Life expectancy has increased slightly from 54 to 57 years, our HIV/AIDS prevalence rate has declined from 5.4 in 1999 to 4.4 in 2005, enrollment into primary, secondary and university educational institutions has more than doubled. Adult literacy rate has increased by 5.37%, and employment rates have gone up by 7.40%.
We have invested heavily in core sectors of the economy such as water, health, and transportation. Our banking reforms have produced fewer but stronger, more reliable and efficient banks. We overhauled the pensions system, introducing an effective and efficient contributory pensions scheme. We also introduced a national health insurance scheme for the first time in our nation's history.
Our economic policies were effective enough to earn us favourable ratings from credible international rating organisations, Fitch and Standard and Poor for the first time. These improvements in our socio-economic profiles have positively impacted on our per capita income and per capita private consumption. Without doubt, and on a comparative basis, we have done well. Our goal is to give hope, confidence and comfort to all Nigerians. It is our desire to open up new vistas of opportunities and possibilities for all. It is our design to generate, strengthen and sustain the "can-do" spirit in all Nigerians. We must thank God Almighty for His love, kindness, blessings and care. I must personally thank all Nigerians for their understanding, love, sacrifices, and support for our reform programme. We never said it was going to be easy. But as we tolerated and watched our country decay and deteriorate until it got to the precipice of collapse, so must we be courageous enough to withstand the pains of reform in order to reap its gains in full. We have done very well so far.
Impressive as these indicators of progress may be, we are still far from where we should have been if we had gotten it right since 1960 and where we now want to be. In the past we did not do many things right. But the few things that we did right, we did not sustain doing them right. This time with the Reform Agenda and the progress so far, we know that things are happening and we are doing right and we intend to stay on course. Staying on course requires that we maintain focus and commitment in our Presidential Initiatives and drive for self-sufficiency in food production and food security, increased exports to earn foreign exchange, and emphasis on small and medium scale enterprises such as processing and manufacturing. Staying focused and on course requires that we continue to work hard to make Nigeria the major tourist destination in Africa through better infrastructure, service delivery, security and safety, and better and more attractive packaging of our products. We must produce more and add value to products for both domestic and export markets.
Remaining on course means that we must complete our on-going power projects that will give us 10,000 mega watts of power supply by the end of 2007 and to continue to invest in more power generating initiatives like Mambila, solar energy, wind energy and eventually nuclear energy. We must complete our inter-modal transportation system, safer, more efficient and affordable air transport, and waterways transportation to reduce pressure on roads, ensure better roads network, and continue to make progress on the new multi-billion dollar modern railway lines. We cannot leave out ICT especially the computerization of our schools, hospitals, businesses, and ministries to cut waste, secure information, promote efficiency and service delivery. We must participate in outsourcing. Our micro- and macro-economic policies must continue to emphasise transparency, accountability, expansion, growth and product diversity. In particular, the war against corruption must be deepened and sustained if we are going to ensure the appropriate deployment of our scarce resources to the right sectors and eliminate fiscal decay and indiscipline. There will be no relenting on the fight against corruption. We have put our hands on the plough and there is no looking back, going back or sliding back.
We must continue to ensure and assure security of life and property all over the country. This is why we also have to improve on the quality of men and women in politics. Our political reforms, within the ruling Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) and in other sectors are designed to deepen political discourse, make politics issue-based, eliminate violence and indiscipline, expand opportunities for participation for women, encourage the emergence of new leaders, build bridges of understanding and cooperation, promote the values of tolerance, accommodation, and dialogue, and build a credible political structure for socio-economic advancement.
The sustenance of these measures in our Reform Agenda must continue. We cannot afford to falter, be diverted, distracted, confused, discouraged, intimidated or reversed. We must remain focused and resolute. This is where our future, the well-being of our children, the security of our nation, and the progress and stability of our communities lie. There is no alternative to being consistent, supporting each other, exchanging and embarking on best practices, insisting on service delivery, fighting waste and corruption, and maximizing opportunities for promoting growth, development and democracy. We must continue to enthrone equity, justice and fairness in all our affairs and interaction in this land. Those who believe that progress has not been made are either enemies of progress who are also blind and deaf. They are people who may never see any good in their own country and who spend time celebrating every minor error or impute motive into every disaster. Some are so mindless and callous that they gloat and seek political advantage in the misfortune of other Nigerians.
Clearly, these are people that want to see us back to the days of rent seeking, corruption, waste and violence which they obviously regard as progress because they benefited from the rot of the time. We have collectively rejected this bitter past and we are determined to ensure that no one or group of persons will take us back to those days of sweat, pain, tears and blood. We must continue to believe in God, in our country and indeed, in ourselves. Where do we want to be in the future as a people and a country? We want to have the basic human needs available to all, affordable, and delivered efficiently and effectively. We want electricity- generation, transmission and distribution- that will make us an industrializing country by the year 2020.
We want an inter-modal transportation that will modernize and expand our railway, road network and inland and coastal waterways. We want an educational system that will give us very close to 100% literacy with every Nigerian who can benefit from tertiary education having access to it, to acquire skills that will serve a modern knowledge economy. In the health sector, we want a life expectancy that will make every Nigerian live up to and beyond the Biblical three-score and ten with all the programmes, institutions, and management facilities required to sustain a truly healthy population. Industrially, we want an economy that will be among the 20 largest in the world; an economy that will be based on modern information and technology, management system, financial administration and transaction, and a pervading culture of excellence, efficiency, and service delivery- an economy that will export skill and knowledge to earn revenue in addition to other products.
To get to these goals and more, we must remain steadfast, courageous, disciplined, focused, and unwilling to tolerate corruption, bad leadership, and abuse of power in any form. That is the way to build and strengthen the foundations of a nation and build the confidence of a people. We must be ready to stand firm, sure-footedly and be counted. In the area of leadership, our country has seen more than its fair share of pseudo-leaders, ethnic and religious entrepreneurs, and those who define themselves as leaders rather than allow the people to so define them. We have seen those that struggle to attain leadership positions only to use it for personal aggrandisement, the suffocation of civil society, political opportunism, and corruption.
On this our independence day, we must collectively resolve to resist and reject such people, policies and programmes that have not served the interest of Nigeria. They do not mean well for Nigeria and our future cannot be anchored on such diabolical and dubious credentials and interests. In spite some drawbacks we witnessed in the past, politics in Nigeria has made some significant progress. This is evidenced in the emergence of a new generation of politicians, the conduct of elections, the culture of constitutionalism, the development of political party platforms and the effort by some parties to strengthen themselves. Nigeria has made good progress. But we are still far from where we should be or where we ought to be. We have no substitute for democracy and must continue to embrace, enlarge and deepen it.
If we are to make steady and sustainable progress, we must collectively resolve to fight political corruption and violence, election manipulation, the imposition of candidates, the culture of empty politicking, and the marginalization of women in the power and political process. We must fight those that continue to see our today and tomorrow with the lenses of our dark past; people with little to contribute to easing our pains and building a holistic and sustainable foundation for posterity. Development and greatness do not just happen for a nation. They must be made to happen and they must be sustained.
What we need for our future is not just a commitment to a culture of constitutionalism, fair competition, respect for the rule of law and genuine democratic politics and practice, but also honest, loyal, patriotic, sensitive, knowledgeable, consistent, reliable, compassionate, tested, and God-fearing leadership at all levels. Men and women who will live by the words of our national pledge. Men and women with vision and sense of mission. We all must not be afraid of reform and what is new and positive in our new brand of politics, leadership and socio-economic trend.
As we approach the 2007 elections, we must all pray hard and work together to ensure a peaceful transition. We must be vigilant and the starting point must be our families, communities, and respective political parties. We must have the courage to speak out and to insist on non-violence, fairness, due process, and justice. We must put Nigeria first and always above other interests. We must see politics not as an end in itself, but as a means to getting the very best for the society to provide service for all. We must reject those that wish to divide us along class, ethnic, gender, religious, and communal lines when it suits them.
If after 46 years of independence we cannot begin to identify and strengthen the bonds and bridges that bind us as people, it is not too late to begin now. God will complete what He has begun in the Nigeria Project. The reform agenda is being owned by the people, who, on their own, will defend the institutions and processes. The National Assembly is continuing to consider some Bills that will help give legal backing to many of the reform institutions, structures and programmes. And, the international community has appreciated and accepted our reform and will not accommodate or tolerate anyone that attempts to undermine investor confidence and our growing economy. In every way, the future of our dear country is in the hands of all Nigerians. I will like at this juncture to commend and thank those young men and women and the not so young men and women who have so far dedicated and committed themselves and their lives to the implementation of our reform agenda and what is best for Nigeria. Surely, they will be richly rewarded by God and by history. We are marching on and we are marching forward in the name of God Almighty.
Fellow Nigerians, as we celebrate, let us reflect deeply on our past experiences and think very seriously about our collective future. We have come out of the dark past and as we continue to work together and plan together, our dear country, in God's name, will continue to make progress. We are a strong, God-fearing, proud and productive people. We must continue to hold our heads up and high, keep God in our hearts, let the common good guide our actions, and believe that with God all things are possible. I wish you all a most pleasurable independence anniversary. May God continue to bless our dear country, Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Production - Ihonvbere Warns Against Glut in Global Market
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava Production - Ihonvbere Warns Against Glut in Global Market
Body_Text:
Benin — AS the demand for Nigeria cassava in the international market increases, the Special Adviser on Project Monitoring and Evaluation to President Obasanjo, Prof. Julius Ihonvbere has cautioned against a situation where the rush for cassava export could cause a glut in the global market, especially China which is Nigeria's target export market.
Professor Ihonvbere who was represented by Dr. Tunde Lakoju at a two-day workshop on cassava production organised by a Benin-base Co-operative Society, Out Eyotor, specifically called on Government Agencies to properly guide cassava farmers on how to export the root crop to avoid a glut, even as he called for processing cassava to its different alternatives locally.
Professor Ihonvbere who alluded to the fate that befell cocoa farmers in Nigeria, advised that the nation should not in the euphoria for the demand for cassava in the global market abandon other cash crops.
"We must not in the euphoria of a global demand for cassava abandon other cash crops. We must deliberately invest in cassava production. Government agencies must advice cassava farmers on how to go about its production, on how to invest in other to avert the export of cassava to China which is the major market".
In his speech, the president of Out Eyotor Co-operative Society, Elder Peter Agbonkonkon Ogbeide frowned at the difficult requirements placed of banks on the way of cassava formers seeking for loans.
He therefore, called on Government and Commercial banks to modify their lending policies to farmers and to protect local fabricators of agric processing machines from aggressive foreign competition.
He disclosed that Out Eyotor has entered into contract with technical partners for the manufacture of ethanol, a project he said would save Nigeria about 200, million dollars annually.
Elder Ogbeide also disclosed that Out-Eyotor was floating a micro finance bank with capital base of one billion naira to assist farmers.
Director General of Small Enterprises Development Agencies of Nigeria (SMEDAN) who commissioned SMEDAN information centre in Edo as part of activities of the workshop called on businessmen to patronize the centre and enhance their business.

Original_Search_Title: FG Approves Imo, Abia for NDDC Intervention
Headline: Nigeria: FG Approves Imo, Abia for NDDC Intervention
Body_Text:
Owerri — THE Federal Government has approved the accommodation of Imo and Abia States in the presidential intervention in the coastal areas under the aegis of the Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC).
Announcing this in Owerri, after presenting 50 pieces of 300KVA transformers and refuse trucks to Imo State government, the NDDC Chairman, Ambassador Sam Edem, said the organisation would soon communicate the approval to the governors concerned."
This development has settled the dust raised by the controversial coastal states issue. Such intervention areas among others, include youth empowerment and poverty alleviation, as well as riceand cassava cultivation", Edem said. Speaking earlier when he paid a courtesy call on Governor Achike Udenwa, the NDDC boss listed theCommission's areas of concentration in the state to include cassava cultivation in Egbema and Umuagwo, provision of 12 boreholes and procurement of 4 flying boats for the waterways. Others include provision of three refuse trucks and 18 bins for environmental sanitation, the proposed establishment of a model school at Oguta, procurement and distribution of science equipment for 40 schools in the state, as well as possible establishment of a rice processing plant in the state.

Original_Search_Title: 2007 - I Can Lead Delta State - Okowa
Headline: Nigeria: 2007 - I Can Lead Delta State - Okowa
Body_Text:
Asaba — Dr. Ifeanyi Okowa is the Delta State Commissioner for Health. He has served the Ibori administration in several capacities since 1999. He spoke on the Ibori administration since 1999, his stewardship and his readiness to lead the state when called upon. He spoke to Kingsley Nwezeh in Asaba.
The issue in Delta politics as we approach the 2007 general elections is the issue of power shift. How would you react to the cry of marginalization by some sections of the state?
On the issues of marginalization, I would rather prefer not to use that word because the State was created in 1991 and the governor stayed only for two years after which there was a military period and Governor James Ibori came in since 1999 and I want to believe that he has tried to carry everybody along as much as possible. You know that even in governing the state it may not be possible to equalize everything at the same time but by and large looking at the democratic processes, the first two years and now shifted to governor Ibori who has a very large heart and has actually treated the state like one family in the past seven years. By 2007, he would have spent 8 years and if he has ruled the state like one family, it is only just fair that if we have a plate on the table, the plate should revolve round. I want to believe that the North and the South (Delta North and South Senatorial Districts) truly do deserve to take position of governor of the state if not for anything, for equity sake and we also know that there are many persons who are eminently qualified to be governor and even within the Ibori family (serving members government) there are people who are qualified to be governor and these people have a lot of experiences and we have been under the tutelage of Governor James Ibori and we believe that a lot of people will do well especially who are either within the cabinet, elected officials within the state House of Assembly, members of the National Assembly from the state who have related very closely with him so I think it is really a matter of equity for people to actually ask for the governorship. It would be a good thing if we realize that if the governorship has to move to the Northern or Southern Senatorial District, it would be a mark of a tested and trusted relationship between ethnic groups in the state.
One expects that with the quantum of funds accruing to the state from the federation account and the perceived inequitable distribution of development projects in the state, would you say that the cry of marginalization is justified?
I want to believe truly that developmental projects are noticeable in all the three senatorial districts of the state. I don't want to believe that any senatorial district has been left behind. I don't want to truly believe that. I have not done a process of assessment to get the figures to truly quote that now. I do know that when "Facts behind the figures" was put before the public last year, a few local governments appear to have had less than others but on the long run with the figure that was put forward to us, it did not appear that any place was totally ignored or actually marginalized and you have to work with figures. We know there were differentials in the figures but not reasonable enough to entirely give credit to the issue of marginalization but people keep on talking that some local governments have more than others but the facts behind the figures actually put such problems to the public to make them realize the positions we were in and to address such little problems that may have inadvertently led credence to such beliefs otherwise, I do not think there was any policy of this government that was actually geared towards marginalizing any senatorial district. The figures that came out in facts behind the figures would not even support marginalization.
If you are giving the opportunity to oversee the affairs of the state, how would you go about it?
Actually, I have been in government for quite some time. I started as secretary to a local government council in 1991 for 8 months. When Ika Local Government was split into two, I contested election and won overwhelmingly as chairman of Ika North East Local Government and I was chairman for three years and within that period, I gained a lot of experience, I have played my politics and since 1999, I have been a commissioner in the state. By and large, I feel that I am one of the best experienced Deltans in terms of governance. I have been at the State Government, I have been at the grassroots level. I have effective experiences and by the Grace of God good performance at these two levels and that actually equips anybody for a higher office.
By the Grace of God and my political family in Delta State, with their cooperation in if I am asked to stand in after James Ibori to overlook the state and the family, I would say that considering the level of experience I have gotten, I should be able to do exceedingly well because with the background I have gotten in this government, I know where we have done well, I also have identified where we may not have done very well and then it would be necessary to improve on the strength that we have. I should be able to do that and strengthen those areas.
You have served as a commissioner for Agriculture and Mineral Resources and a second term as Commissioner for Health in the past 7 years, can you tell Nigerians the impact of your stewardship on Deltans?
Yes truly, I have been in the cabinet for the past seven years apart from a few months that we were out of office. Outside that , I resumed in July 1999 at the Ministry of Agriculture and Natural Resources. I actually came here at a time when it was as if agriculture was not part of government programme at all because I met a situation where in the previous year their budget was like nothing, close to nil. And the staff moral was very low so the first thing I did was to identify myself with the staff and make them realize that I have come for a change and that they should still have confidence in what they were doing and that means that I was going to touch a lot of places but it was not easy for it to take off. Time was sluggish because they were not used to doing things quickly so we have to identify those who were ready to work and we had to work very late in the night and we were able to come up with a few programmes. In spite of my very short stay in the ministry, we tried to revive many things they used to do. They touched on issues that had a lot of impact. Within that one year plus, I was able to start what they called a replanting scheme for oil palms because that was a major advantage of the former Bendel State in Nigeria.
Delta State was basically a major oil palm producer. I thought that we needed to replant because most of the farmers that had their oil palm mill and large plantation was such that they were several years old and not productive so we needed to start that plant and to double that on a yearly basis from 2,000 to eventually 4,000. That was my plan for the ministry. Luckily I was able to do that in the first one year and it was quite successful and we had to bridge it up because we had to take care of the economically low and therefore, we had to give approvals to people who needed about two hectares, planting of two hectares government gave them the assistance by giving them some seed money to be able take care and nuture it at the beginning. But we also had some community programmes. In some communities, we had to look forward to 50 hectare plantation involving about 25 families to be selected by the community. And in a study that was done thereafter when I was already in another ministry, it was found out that in 1,000 hectares, at least 2000 plus seedlings were well planted and actually surviving at that time. But unfortunately, I did not stay in that ministry for that long so I was not able to carry out the next phase which was supposed to be a 2,000 hectare. I fear that if we had a consistent approach to it, we would have been able within the 8-year tenure to do the 20,000 hectare project and a lot of Deltans by today would have been much better for it especially the farmers but I thank God that I started it, the commissioners that came after did not want to follow up the project but I know that those that participated in it are quite happy that till today, a lot of them still see me and refer to their gain the oil palm is already yielding.
The major issue in Agriculture in Nigeria today is the issue of cassava processing into pillets chips and starch. I truly noticed that from my first year because the first work free day, I went round and I found out that there was quite some problem in every part of the state and I needed to know their problems and in talking with them it became obvious that because the production is much this year and the price is low, it is easier for everybody to just processes into the normal garri and the next day, people run out of it because production is low and I said we need to encourage them to plant more, we need to give them better planting equipment, and we needed to go beyond mere processing of garri into chips . We noticed it a far back as 2000 and we came up with this programme where we were able to start up on a small scale and we needed to buy equipment that would make things easier for the farmer. Small scale machines that should be able to process cassava into garri, pillets and chips and others that will be used in feeding livestock where we had farmers organized into a cooperative in nine locations in the state where we provided small scale machines for them. By the time I left the ministry, we had fully installed about seven and it was something they were supposed to improve upon. Some were located in Ugheli, Abraka and some parts of Isoko area which are cassava producing areas but I wont lie to you the principle of non-continuity of programme actually has been a major problem in the ministry of agriculture. If you talk to the present commissioner of agriculture, you will realize that it was a real vision because down the line, five years after, the Federal Government has come up with it so these things we saw it at the tail end of 1999. My predecessor did not find the need to continue with the project but thank God that the Federal Government has come up with the project and in fact I noticed something ahead of time.
Is this policy inconsistency a lack of cohesion in government?
Well, the Governor has a very liberal heart that he believes that the commissioner should be able to initiate his policies and able to continue with the programmes of his predecessor that can carry his ministry along. He does not have this overbearing influence on the ministries but unfortunately some people are different and I do not want to comment on this. It is not because of the government policy but because of the persons. You can technically kill something because you have an interest. People have lots of drives. One may be to succeed and the other may be some other drive.
What happened in Water Resources Ministry?
Moving over to water resources ministry, that was the first time the ministry was created. We had the urban water board, the rural water board, we were actually in charge of overseeing these two places and ensuring that we had proper water flow in the various locations. I came into a situation where we had some existing schemes. In the urban water board, we had 60-64 schemes that were already on but they were such that less than 40 percent of it was functional and by the time I left two years after, we have been able to sustain the functionality of the existing water schemes to about 80 percent at that point in time because you would realize that the water schemes biggest problem was a question of power outage because the thing that drives production of water is actually power based and because of the server have not been able to get a compact power supply and because we have these power fluctuation voltage. You either have a long period where you cannot pump water and beyond those periods that you cannot pump water, you actually find out that in between, you get a few more damages because of the fluctuations power source.
Realising that those were actually the major thing, and if you needed to move forward, you needed to realize that there was a problem of power supply so how do we do this thing. There were two major things that we thought of. In the urban areas, we can go ahead and support with generator but in the rural areas where people cannot pay their water bill because they don't even have money to pay, what do we do. If you supply generators who will foot the bill and if the state government wants to provide funds for that how long can they do it? We decided on an option, less generator-based to supply of supplement the little source of power in the urban areas so we needed to do something new in the rural areas and we realized in one of our meetings that there was a pilot team in Abuja, just one pilot team in the whole country then, the solar power water supply scheme where you can use rays of the sun and then convert it to power and we were the first team to actually go into that programme on a mass basis. An we were able to establish 52 schemes that were based on solar power supply and they were quite functional and by the time I left, there were just about one or two of them that had some fault and they were about being rectified so we found out that it was the best policy for the rural area and I thank God that that policy has remained consistent even in the ministry of water resources now some of our schemes excepting in the Delta North area where you have to go very deep to get water in that case, you may not be able to use the solar power source and we were commended in 2003 when the minister of water resources and rural development was here in the council meeting and got exposed to what we were doing. By the time I was leaving the ministry of water resources, we had increased the number of water schemes to 180 as at the time I left.
Now, how have you handled the all important health sector?
Since 2003, I have been in the ministry of Health. I came into health as the pioneer health commissioner within Ibori's administration and I have done well. I have done well because I am aware that we have the primary heath care programme, the secondary health care and we have done well in developing or starting to develop the secondary health care sector, the hospitals which are actually under the ministry of health and the state government. One major project was the reacquisition and reequipping of 12 hospitals by NCOH firm. We did quite well though it is not quite fulfilled in all they were supposed to do as they disappointed us when it came to some of the specialized equipment but by and large it was a good project and well done. When I came what I had to do was to improve upon what was done and also do what is supposed to be done.
We started with the other hospitals that needed renovation. As I am speaking to you today, I doubt if there is any hospital that has not had renovation to a reasonable level and further construction to improve in the facilities. I will implore you to go round to Warri Central Hospital, Kwale, the one at Ugheli and the one in Sapele, you will be quite amazed at the level of development, so we have developed futher infrastructure in all these and in the smaller hospitals, we have done further renovation and now we have 44 hospitals and all of them are in a state that we can say we are proud of all of them.
The other thing is that we had to do massive re-equipment of all these hospitals and provide specialist equipment for consultants in some of the hospitals because that was also lacking many of the consultants had nothing to work with so we had to ensure that we provide specialist equipment in addition to other medical equipment that are usually required so we have been at that programme and most of the hospitals are happy. While the infrastructure is good, the equipment is there for them to work with so the next thing is for them to assert themselves because there were a lot of indiscipline when they had nothing to work with and in order to stop that level of indiscipline we needed to improve the staff morale.
If I may come in here the State House of Assembly debated a motion seeking to ask all medical doctors employed by the state government who engage in private practice to resign. What is your take?
There is a general policy in the country today, in fact I discussed it with some of them (House members) who came to ask for my views that in that a doctor during working hour must stick strictly to the ethics of work but after 4 o'clock when you close from work and you are not on call, you can afford to use your services elsewhere and you are allowed to do so. As long as you are not on call, if you are on call, you must be on call as it happens in many parts of the world, you can go out and work elsewhere, you are providing services because we do not have enough so you cannot just seat idle but we cannot allow doctors to do that during working hours but outside working hours, they are allowed to provide services so it is not only in Delta State, it happens in every other state.
So on welfare, we were able to pay them 22 percent salary increase for health workers generally. In fact the pay for a health worker here is far higher that the pay of any health worker in any part of the country, it is equally comparable with that of the Federal Government.
We needed to look into the primary healthcare that is the one you have in every community which is about 80 percent of the people. These ones are domiciled in the local government councils. We had 315 health care centers when we came in. A lot of them over 80 percent of them were in a state of dilapidation from that of infrastructure to that of equipment and that made it a major problem even roads were not better so people had to patronize patent medicine stores and native doctors so we needed to revive this trend and in trying to revive it, we took two policies, one was to go to the state house Assembly with a bill to ask the creation of a state primary health care development agency with a counterpart local government healthcare development authorities and we succeeded in doing that. We set out with a target of doing 130 with the hope that the local councils will complete the rest. We have at the moment 130 health institutions and by 2007 we should have 150 hospitals. Having done the renovation of infrastructure, we now realized that we have to do massive reequipping of the hospitals as most of the equipment were obsolete. I had to carry out an advocacy programme to members of the state House of Assembly and they had what you call constituency projects where you determine the project most needed by your constituency and because of my advocacy and relationship with them, many of them have actually come to realize the need to provide these equipment to their various communities. A lot of them are making the provision of these equipment to their communities as part of their constituency projects. Last year alone, we were able to provide a lot of equipment to the primary health care centers. I was hoping that before the end of the year, we should be able to do 50 to 60 primary healthcare centers.
Our target by the time this administration winds up in 2007 is to be able to reequip nearly all the primary health centers we have in this state.
We also provided drug stock to all the local government areas in the state under the drug revolving scheme which they have never had in the past, we had to do it from the state which means that we are going to have a functional primary health centers in all the local government councils in the state which will help in reducing marternal and infant mortality rate which are the two key things we have set out to achieve under the Millenium Development Goals (MDGs) by 2015.
We also have tertiary hospitals. We want a state of the art tertiary hospitals for the teaching hospital in Oghara which is going to serve as the teaching hospital for Delta State University , Abraka. It is a massive hospital which is being constructed at the cost of N6 billion. We are hoping that by October, we should be able to complete it and complete the equipment, I am sure that we should be able to complete the project by the end of the year. It is made up of the main hospital structure and other structures and ancillaries and the second phase has the administrative block and students hostel, residential accommodation with all other structures and the auditorium, it is quite a massive project. And we hope that it is going to be a star project for this state.
If you are giving the opportunity to oversee the affairs of the state, how would you go about it?
Actually, I have been in government for quite some time. I started as secretary to a local government council in 1991 for 8 months. When Ika Local Government was split into two, I contested election and won overwhelmingly as chairman of Ika North East Local Government and I was chairman for three years and within that period, I gained a lot of experience, I have played my politics and since 1999, I have been a commissioner in the state. By and large, I feel that I am one of the best experienced Deltans in terms of governance. I have been at the State Government, I have been at the grassroots level. I have effective experiences and by the Grace of God good performance at these two levels and that actually equips anybody for a higher office. By the Grace of God and my political family in Delta State, with their cooperation in if I am asked to stand in after James Ibori to overlook the state and the family, I would say that considering the level of experience I have gotten, I should be able to do exceedingly well because with the background I have gotten in this government, I know where we have done well, I also have identified where we may not have done very well and then it would be necessary to improve on the strength that we have. I should be able to do that and strengthen those areas.

Original_Search_Title: 'Agriculture'll Be My Priority'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Agriculture'll Be My Priority'
Body_Text:
Lagos — Chief Godson Echegile, one of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP)'s gubernatorial aspirants in Delta State, yesterday said the state will become a front-runner in agricultural transformation next year.
Echegile said this in an interactive session with agriculture experts in Asaba, adding that he will partner with local and foreign investors to trigger the oil palm sector into an engine of the economic growth through oil palm enhancement programme.
He highlighted core areas to include revamping of tree crop development, which will involve tree regeneration, rehabilitation, forestation and afforestation.
Oil palm, rubber, forest species and other perennial participation will be done to guarantee market and income incentives for farmers.
He said there will be linkages between farmers and industries utilising agricultural produce such as maize and cassava, to ensure market diversification and stability.
There will also be a community land grant facility to encourage communities to oblige land for commercial farmers applying modern technology for commercial farming system of mutual benefits between them and the host local communities.
While commending the laudable efforts of Governor James Ibori, Echegile said his administration would encourage government private sector partnership and provide incentives through enabling environment based approach.

Original_Search_Title: Ashanti Region, Potential Business Hub of Ghana
Headline: Ghana: Ashanti Region, Potential Business Hub of Ghana
Body_Text:
Ashanti Region, located within the middle portion of the country and one of Ghana's fastest growing regions, has in diverse ways contributed to the development of Ghana.
The Ashanti region is not only known for its cultural, agricultural and tourism prospects, but also its capital, Kumasi, is the second biggest city in Ghana and also considered as the Garden City of West Africa.
