Kumasi, being one the fastest growing cities in West Africa, holds great potentials for boosting Ghana's economy in terms of investments and foreign exchange if a lot more attention and support is given to the region than it already has.
The people of the Ashanti region are predominantly farmers. They cultivate cash crops like cocoa, cashew, cola and timber that are mostly exported to earn foreign revenue for the country.
Food crops such as cassava, plantain, tomatoes and other citrus fruits, all of which could aid in expanding the agricultural sector, with the right storage facilities.
The region is indeed famous for its spectacular history and the great men and women who in diverse ways played very important roles in making the Ashanti region what it is today.
Education The Ashanti region has contributed immensely to the educational sector of the country. This achievement is expressed in the Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology (KNUST), which happens to be one of the nation's prestigious institutions recognized internationally, and has produced many great men like Mr. Kofi Annan, UN Secretary General, and Alhaji Alui Mahama, Vice President of Ghana.
The Ashanti region also houses most of the nation's finest second cycle institutions such as Opoku Ware secondary School, Prempeh College, Yaa Asantewaa Secondary School, St. Monica's Girls Secondary School, among others.
Another very vital fact that cannot be over looked is the Otumfuo Education Fund.
This initiative by the Asantehene Otumfuo Osei Tutu, overlord of the Ashanti tribe, has provided and still provides scholarships for many brilliant but needy students within the Ashanti region including non-Ashantis.
A laudable initiative, which if followed by other traditional leaders, organizations and individuals will go a long way to promote the human resource sector of this nation.
Sports Sports is one other area that is deeply appreciated in the region. The renovation of the Baba Yara Sports Stadium can only be said to be appropriate and timely for the hosting of the 2008 African Cup of Nations.
It is a known fact that the people of Kumasi and Ashanti in general are very passionate as regards the teams they support; hence most of Ghana's international soccer matches are scheduled for the Baba Yara Sports Stadium in Kumasi.
The Ashanti region can also boast of very talented sportsmen who on many occasions represent Ghana through the Black Stars, Black Satellites and Starlets.
Most of these young players can be found in great soccer clubs like the Kumasi Asante Kotoko, the King Faisal, and the Obuasi Goldfields, all of which are among the top teams in the premiership and feature in international matches.
Agriculture There is no disputing the fact that the Ashanti region is a very suitable place for investments and has so far produced very fruitful yields to its investors in the agriculture sector.
The people of the Ashanti are predominantly farmers who produce a substantial portion of the total agricultural produce that are exported to foreign countries such as cocoa and cashew as well as the perishable ones that get to the local markets.
There are still many areas in the agriculture sector that both the government and private individuals and organizations could invest in.
Tourism
Touching on Tourism, the Ashanti region has quite a significant amount of tourist sites that has already contributed in boosting the tourist sector of the country and could do more should the necessary attention and support be given to it. This could be in the form of the Ashanti Regional Tourist Board lobbying government to upgrade all the roads that leads to the various tourist sites in the region. Lake Bosomtwe is presently the world's largest natural lake and attracts many tourists whenever they visit the country, yet the road leading to Abonno, the village where the lake can be found is very poor and can even be described as dangerous and demands immediate attention. The Ashanti Regional Tourist Board could further market the tourist sites in the region by sensitizing the public on the need to visit these sites. They should also create a website on Tourism in the region for prospective visitors, particularly those in the Diaspora. The region could also be included in the celebration of some of the national festivals like Panafest and the Emancipation as is being done to the coastal regions.
Industry
The industrial sector in the Ashanti region has over the years chalked several successes and aided largely to Ghana's economy. The region houses many of the thriving companies in the country including the Obuase Gold Mines, Guinness Ghana Limited and Latex Foam Company among others.
Gold
is currently said to be the country's biggest foreign exchange earner. Furthermore, government could rehabilitate some of the manufacturing companies that are presently out of business. A couple of such companies are the Shoe factory and the jute factory.
Transport
With Kumasi being one of the cities in Ghana slated to host the CAN 2008, it would therefore be appropriate if government could turn the Kumasi Airport into an international airport to ease the movement of people to and from Kumasi during the tournament.
It has also been observed that most of the smaller roads that link Kumasi to its nearby villages are untarred and in very bad shape.
This tends to lead to most farm produce going to waste since majority of these foodstuffs are produced in the villages and do not get to the city markets in time.
The Asafo interchange is one project that if completed could go a long way to ease the heavy traffic around the area, thereby enhancing the transportation system in the Kumasi metropolis.
However, it seems as though work on the project has become sluggish, thus attracting the attention of the Vice President as well as the Transportation Minister.
Culture
If there is one thing that makes the Ashantis stand out among the other regions, it is indeed their rich and unique culture that attracts many tourists that visit the country.
The stories about Otumfuo Osei Tutu I and the legendary Okomfo Anokye and the Golden Stool are ones that fascinate many.
If properly organized, these could make the culture of the Ashanti people unparalleled in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Uganda Loses 11 Percent of Produced Cassava
Headline: Uganda Loses 11 Percent of Produced Cassava
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDA losses 11% (590,000 metric tonnes) of cassava production per annum to waste out of an estimated 5,373,000 metric tonnes. According to the World Food Programme balance sheet, 2002, 41% is utilised for animal and poultry feeds, while 48% goes for human food. Patrick Kalunda, the post-harvest officer, said this during a workshop at Jokas Hotel in Bweyogerere.
Research shows increased production, but farmers are still constrained with low incomes from cassava that could be attributed to pre- and post-harvest losses, fluctuating prices, poor marketing, lack of knowledge, low processing and demand.
Dr. Ambrose Agona, principal research officer (Post-Harvest specialist) working with the National Agricultural Research Organisation (NARO) at Kawanda Agricultural Research Institute, says the CFC Cassava project is meant to address these problems.
"The intervention entry point is processing cassava into dried chips for the food industry," he explained. "The programme looks at cassava production, chip processing, marketing and utilisation, shifting from traditional practices of manual peeling, slicing, drying, milling and local market sales of cassava and its products."
The quality of traditionally processed dried cassava is heavily compromised. Agona says drying of chips takes five to seven days, thus yielding poor quality chips. According to baseline surveys, it was established that cassava production was low because farmers were relied mainly on indigenous varieties that give low yields.
Working through farmers' groups, the project provided 100 bags of high yielding varieties of TME14 and 2961 cuttings that were recommended for processing and food respectively. After a year, the bags yielded over 1,200 bags. The project has selected two sites, Kibuku and Kibale sub-counties in Palisa district as cassava pilot projects.
Cassava, a major staple food in Uganda, is also still grown on subsistence level. The crop provides a source of income to the large number of households in rural and urban communities.

Original_Search_Title: Crop Marketing
Headline: Zambia: Crop Marketing
Body_Text:
There have been concerns about how the crop marketing exercise has been handled by the Food Reserve Agency (FRA) and clearly many farmers are not pleased with the whole exercise.
Firstly, some farmers are worried whether their crop will be bought at all because they are unable to access some of the depots including the satellite depots that the FRA has opened.
Secondly, there is the issue of delayed payments to farmers who delivered their crops and from reports so far, frustration and anger is slowly setting in.
Many are worried that they may not have the money in time to meet family needs as well as prepare for this coming season.
We appreciate the logistical and financial difficulties that the FRA is facing especially that there seems to be another bumper harvest this year.
There is demand from maize farmers right through to cassava and rice farmers to have their crop bought and this is obviously not an easy undertaking for the agency.
But one thing is clear and that is FRA must as much as possible buy all the crop and transport it to safe storage.
The rainy season is about to begin and this means FRA must somehow double its efforts to meet the target of buying as much crop as possible.
The agency must now begin to find ways of finding extra storage space and this must include hiring of sheds to ensure that crops do not go to waste.
But we must also call upon other players in the agriculture industry that they must not entirely leave the buying process to the FRA alone.
Here we are talking about the private sector, which includes millers. The millers must also be seen to take active and bigger role if the marketing of crops is to be successful.
After all the millers are the one who sell end products like mealie meal and stockfeed.
They must also be prepared to venture into remote areas and help promote the farmers by buying their produce.
While it true that FRA is the major player, we expect that the millers will also help develop the agricultural industry by ensuring that they buy produce from farmers at a price that will not put the farmers out of business.

Original_Search_Title: UN Agency Challenges Nigeria On Industrial Development
Headline: Nigeria: UN Agency Challenges Nigeria On Industrial Development
Body_Text:
An agency of the United Nations at the weekend in Abuja challenged captains of Nigerian economy to steer the country's economy towards independent of international support for industrial development.
Speaking at the occasion of signing a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with African Business Round Table (ABR) on one hand and the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) on the other, the Director General of UNIDO in the West African subregion, Dr.Kandel Yumkella said: "No UN agency can help Nigerians to develop the industrial base of their economy. The people need to wake up to the reality of the challenges facing them. The government needs to create enabling environment for private participation in the industrial development of the country for the sake of creating jobs for the mass of jobless youth.
Dr Yumkella told the ministers of commerce and that of industry, Dr Aliyu Moddibo Umar and Ambassador Fidelis Tapgun respectively who were in attendance at the occasion that it is their responsibilities to encourage the government to foster good grounds for adding value to the export of Nigeria through the industrial development of the country." It is only when value to add to the cassava, palm oil, e.t.c exported out of Nigeria that jobs can be created for these teaming jobless youth," he explained.
According to him, China with its 1.36billion population has been able to industrialize its economy through the process of adding value to its resource and not just exporting them for others to create jobs for their youth out of it.
He reckoned that Malaysia is now noted for the biofuel it produces by simply adding value to palm fruit it once obtained from Nigeria.
Earlier, the Executive President of the ABR, Alhaji Bamanga Tutur, noted that no nations can be able to sustain itself without industrializing. He explained that public -private partnership as well as increased private participation is required for a sustained industrial development of Nigeria. Hence, he said, the need for ABR to sign an MOU with UNIDO.
Also the representative of the executive secretary of ECOWAS, Dr. Nan Joof, noted that its secretariat has benefited from a considerable numbers of programmes and activities of UNIDO. Thus, he explained, the MOU has become necessary in other to further cement its relationship with UNIDO.
Dr. Joor highlighted UNIDO is expected to assist towards achieving a number of activities in the ongoing reform programme of ECOWAS.

Original_Search_Title: Alima Mahama - Government is Doing a Lot Toward Women Empowerment
Headline: Ghana: Alima Mahama - Government is Doing a Lot Toward Women Empowerment
Body_Text:
Various women groupings countrywide have benefited from the disbursement of a 124 billion cedis facility accruing from the establishment of the Women Development Fund, Hajia Alima Mahama, Minister for Women and Children's Affairs (MOWAC) has said.
She said the Fund has helped to generate employment, improved food security and reduced poverty among the womenfolk, especially those living in the rural areas.
Hajia Mahama, who was speaking at a day's durbar for women groups at Hohoe, said additional 17 billion cedis was released by government, this year, to Rural Banks, National Investment Bank (NIB), Ghana Commercial Bank (GCB) and disbursed as micro credit support for businesses, small-scale farming and petty trading activities.
She expressed the hope that these interventions would lead to the overall achievement of gender equality and empowerment, survival, protection, development and growth of women and children.
Hajia Mahama stated that her Ministry had organized training workshops for 7,983 women beneficiaries of the micro credit scheme to improve their financial management skills and help them acquire business expansion techniques to ensure high loan recovery.
The Minister said 10 tractors were procured to support some women farming groups and that 43 agro-processing equipment including shea-butter, gari, oil palm and groundnut equipment had also been procured to add value to their produce.
She announced that two sets of cassava processing equipment, a tractor had been provided to support women groups at Mafi-Zongo, Nkwanta and Damanko in the Volta region.
Hajia Mahama said government policies were in progress in support of free maternal deliveries, promotion of improved maternal child health, control of trachoma diseases and blindness, promotion of family planning, control the spread of HIV/AIDS, good sanitary practices and the overall expansion of health facilities.
She said the establishment of the Girls Education Unit was to ensure that girls got the same opportunities in accessing education as boys, noting that, this has proved successful especially in relation to Science, Mathematics and Technical Education for girls, which is now at the district level.
Mr John Peter Amewu, Hohoe District Chief Executive, noted that outmoded cultural norms, beliefs and practices, which made women subservient to their men counterparts, militated against rapid development of the countryside.
He said the Assembly had given out credit facilities to assist women in small-scale agro-processing and businesses.

Original_Search_Title: Tortuous Trek Up to Crash Site
Headline: Nigeria: Tortuous Trek Up to Crash Site
Body_Text:
Kaduna — When Weekly Trust got to the village of Ticha Mbaper and asked the youths who took part in the rescue with the youths of Mbakunu to take us to the site of the crash. We got on motorcycles from the village and drove to the foot of the mountain. Aondasee Gbaa, 20, who was also among the rescue team pointed up ahead at the peak of the mountain and asked if we thought we could manage the climb. He said it was a three hour climb if we made it fast without taking much rest on the way.
The climb up the mountain was in three phases. On the first phase of the climb we encountered several farmlands dotted all around the vicinity with thick foliages of trees further adding darkness all around us. While on our way we met farmers sitting on yam ridges holding some parts of the crashed plane. Aondaasee pointed at the shafts and metals they are holding and said: "See, people are already going up to scavenge for parts of the plane. They will go to town and display it to curious sightseers who have been trooping to this area. They hope to make money out of the show."
As our reporters climbed higher, there were pieces of the planes scattered at intervals along our path. It was like a signpost to us, pointing out directions we should take. The mountain path was broken and slippery at several points and we began to imagine to ourselves the courage of Detimbir Chia and others who braved it to the top of the mountain, despite all the difficulty of the terrain. Aondasse said: "As you can see it is not an easy climb at all. But we were born here. We are used to the terrain."
Just as we embarked upon the second phase of our climb we came across a military beret possibly blown by the wind from the crash site. The youths avoided the beret saying, "No, we no fit touch this one. Abeg."
At this point we were all sweating profusely and drawing air fast into our nostrils and panting with out mouths wide open. We begged the youths to please hold on so we can get some rest. Here, the valley below faces you. It is a wide expanse of village huts that have now become a speck and the vast trees scattered in the distance. All around us is mountains. Kusa came closer and sat near us. He pointed at the distance and said: "You can see Cameroon from this point. Just look up ahead of you after that distant point. That is Cameroon. You know we share a common boundary with them."
We asked for our water bottles and began to take deep draughts and gulp very fast, still struggling for breath and panting. When we began to take too much Kusa exclaimed with warning, "Please, please, enough. If you take too much water I am afraid you cannot continue with this climb. We've not covered half of the journey yet."
On the way we saw the road path created by the villagers still looking fresh as some of the cut grasses and tree shrubs were lying by the side of the path. Thaddeus Akeabee, 21, said: "On the day of the rescue, there was no road that we could pass to evacuate the survivors of the crash. So we have to start creating road paths fast as we rushed down with the wounded soldiers in the crash. It was not easy at all. We were cutting weeds fast and felling down small trees to create a quick passage. It was slippery at several points. Soldiers who later joined us from the primary school during the rescue operations were slipping and falling down. But we kept on all the same, holding the survivors and bringing them down. It was a tough rescue operation."
We now came to a water fall, the Awum water fall said the youths. The water trailing down the mountain made splashing noises as it fell. It was cool and comfortable there and we all sat down to enjoy the cool and regain our strength. This was when the youths began to take small gulps of water. They began to refill our already exhausted water bottles for us. We took a gulp. The water tasted refreshing. But we remembered the warning not to take too much. Joseph Gbaa came over to us and said: "On the day of the rescue operation we didn't take any water at all until we got to this point, despite all the energy and strength we expended in carrying the soldiers and clearing a road path on the mountain. Our throats were perched and thirst was killing us."
The two of us were sweating and panting for breath as we listened to the youths and jotted down their accounts of the events of that day. Our body temperature began to rise and a dizziness came over us. Without waiting, one of us dropped his note pad and camera and rushed under the water fall. "Thank God, wai, the heat is beginning to leave my body now," he said as the water splashed over him. The youths began to laugh at us. Tsehemba said: "It is not easy. A first timer will not find the climb easy at all. After this, you city people, I know you might be thinking of visiting the clinic."
The youths now explained that we still have one more phase to go before we get to the crash site. We kept on climbing the steep slopes. But by now we were hardly walking at all. We were virtually crawling, holding on to trees and stepping on the holes on the path to haul ourselves up. Kusa said: "From here you begin to see Cameroon a little more clearly." We turned behind us and beheld one of the most beautiful and breath-taking scenery we have ever encountered. The mountains in the distance were looking blue, the sky was a clear white and the trees and rice farms were dark green. We paused for a while, taking in the beauty all around us. It was a great consolation to our flagging spirit. Akpeabee jokingly asked: "Do you men want to turn back from here? You are almost dying." No way, we said. We won't turn back, having come this far.
We plodded on, still walking or crawling until we encountered taller and stronger columns of trees on our right. Akpeabee said: "Not to worry now. We are almost close to the crash site." A fresh surge of energy began to course through our systems. From this point, the tall elephant grasses had been levelled completely and there were many scattered bits and pieces from the aircraft. A heavy sorrow settled over our hearts and all of us were talking gently now.
We came across five columns of trees that have their upper-parts chopped off sharply. The top was like the shaft of a weapon. Joseph Gbaa, explained: "The plane was flying low. The pilot thought he was on a clear field. But the plane hit the trees, smashing and cutting off their tops from the middle up. The left side of the plane's wing came off immediately. The plane would have fallen on the Cross River State section of the mountain or probably the Cameroon side of the mountain, but that big tree there (he said pointing at a tree at the edge of the mountain) was the one that arrested its fall. The plane smashed the other four trees. But this tree was the one that arrested its fall. The plane hit the tree and crashed on its right side. Parts of the tail end have also been broken already."
We now came to the crash site proper. The smashed engines of the military aircraft showed clearly from the bowels of the plane. There were scattered bits of shoes, clothes, shirts, cotton wool stained with blood and the exhausted plastic packs of drips everywhere. We all paused for a while. We began to imagine the courage of Detimbir as he came upon the site of the crash and met the wounded soldiers screaming for help. It must have been scary and blood curdling for the 14-year-old boy.
We sat down to regain our lost breath. The youths told us the site is approximately 37 km from Obalinku Local Government Area of Cross River State. We peered through the dense trees and could see the thatched roofs of the villages scattered on the valleys of the mountain. The crash site was a theatre of destruction and devastation. Vast columns of trees have made a canopy over the plane. We could understand how difficult it could be for the army rescue helicopter to sight it.
Joseph Gbaa said: "Most of us passed the night here on the first day of the crash with the survivors and dead bodies. They were initially six that were alive. But one of them later gave up the ghost. It was such a terrible day for us."
Journey into the interior
The two villages of Mbakunu and Ticha-Mbaper are located in Adikpo, a village community in Kwande Local Government Area of Benue State. Adikpo is located on a flat land surrounded by mountains. It is about two and a half hours drive from Makurdi, the capital of Benue State. Life here is mostly agrarian with farmers drying rice beets on the road side in the sun. As we entered the community it was a vast stretch of untarred road that faced us. We began to fear if our vehicle could make the journey. We kept asking for directions from the villagers, asking them if our vehicle could access their villages. They assured us their villages are accessible by road.
The community is friendly and it is easy to notice that they are very hardworking people. On our way we came across several farmlands of yam, rice and cassava. The farmers were busy working on the farmlands. Village maidens passed on our way balancing loads of fire woods that would be used for the evening's cooking.
The road is full of pot-holes, a virtual death trap. We had to drive our vehicle at a mere 20 km/hr to avoid the gallops, pot-holes, narrow roads, uncompleted bridges and narrow bends. It was such a slow and tortuous journey.
We got to Ajio, a village community. There were thatched mud houses around. The villagers were sitting outside their houses, staring at us. We asked for directions and they pointed to our left. We once again took the road until we arrived at a road block mounted by the youths of the village near a bridge. They were community self-help groups, filling the pot-holes with sands. We gave them a tip and thanked them. Our guide told us these are communities where inhabitants still use ordinary razor blade to shave their hairs. There is nothing like clippers here," he said.
The abundance of swamps makes rice a thriving crop here. We came across a petrol black market with only four litres of fuel on display. Apparently, these people don't consume much fuel. Not a single vehicle was plying the road as we came and the motorcycles we saw only passed along occasionally.
We arrived Koti-Mbaduku, another village community on our way. At a junction we asked for the direction to Mbakunu village, where the plane crash took place. They said we should take the road to our left. A small cardboard sign leaning against a slope says: make call here N40. It occurred to us it was because the villages have access to communication that Detimbir was able to make the call that saved the lives of the five wounded soldiers.
Just ahead of us, we saw large poles of electricity without a single wire passing through them. Our guide hissed and said: "Look at politicians. They just bring empty poles without electricity. What will the people do with ordinary pole? Climb on it or what?"
We stopped at an abandoned bridge project that must have been embarked upon decades ago. Thankfully, the stream passing under the bridge is shallow and the bridge had space enough to accommodate our vehicle. Since we left Makurdi, on our way, we kept seeing several posters of photogenic and smiling politicians vying for the governorship seat of Benue State. We were wondering what these politicians would do to raise the quality of life in these poor communities. Benue State alone has twenty three gubernatorial aspirants and that is counting several more that have not yet emerged. Our guide said: "The competition is so tough that those who want to contest are keeping it secret until the last minute for fear they would be assassinated if they declare their intentions now."
We came across several schools on our way, even though most of them look dilapidated. But it gladdened our hearts when we saw the children of poor farmers putting on school uniforms, coming back from school. The youths could manage to communicate intelligently.
After about an hour and a half of slow crawling, our vehicle arrived at the village of Ticha-Mbaper. The inhabitants surrounded our vehicles immediately, knowing we came because of the plane crash. The youths who participated in the rescue operation offered to take us up the mountain where the plane crash took place.
The return journey
After taking photographs, we began the return journey back. The return was easier, but you simply cannot walk down, even if you are tired. You have to keep running or walking fast because of the steepness of the mountain. This time around we were slipping and falling. Kusa said: "It is under this condition that we evacuated the wounded soldiers, backing them and running down the slippery slope or holding them by their legs and shoulders. We fell several times. But we kept on doggedly. We were anxious to get them to the village primary school where there were already soldiers waiting with vehicles."
Once more we took consolation and drew strength from nature: on the return trip the yellow orb of the setting sun was suspended in the air, lending a surreal beauty to the landscape ahead of us. Several times, the thought of God came to our minds. This was one of the many wonders of nature, something we don't get the opportunity to see in the cities where we reside. What with the tall buildings, noisy traffic and smoke from vehicles exhaust and dust from construction sites.
Darkness fell over us and we were holding shrubs and small trees to work our way down. All the while, we kept thinking of the courage of the youths, carrying wounded soldiers down these slippery and steep slopes on a day of heavy rains and bad weather, with darkness covering everywhere. The top brass of the military command, including the chief of Army Staff, Lt. Gen. Owoye Azazi all came to thank them for helping to save the lives of the five wounded soldiers.
The villagers told us that no governor has ever stepped into their community in history until George Akume, the present governor. His first visit was to attend the burial of one of his commissioners and the second was as a result of the plane crash. Even as we entered the village with our vehicles, the villagers were staring at us curiously because they hardly see vehicles at all. A woman farmer who saw our vehicle coming from the distance as she walked on the narrow road, scampered quickly to the bushy road side, exclaiming "ye, ye, ye, ye."
May the soul of the thirteen military men who lost their lives in the crash rest in peace (Ameen).

Original_Search_Title: Transcorp Shares - Why Obasanjo Must Go
Headline: Nigeria: Transcorp Shares - Why Obasanjo Must Go
Body_Text:
Lagos — The following is the full text of the press statement issued by Chief Gani Fawehinmi LL.D, SAN on the Constitutional implications of the part- ownership of Transcorp by President Olusegun Obasanjo.
In the last one week, facts have emerged that the President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, General Olusegun Obasanjo substantially owns, through Obasanjo Holdings Limited, Transnational Corporation of Nigeria Plc (TRANSCORP).
This is a very serious revelation with damning constitutional consequences.
Summary of the facts of President Obasanjo's part-ownership of TRANSCORP
1.TRANSCORP was incorporated on Wednesday, 17th November 2004 and officially launched on Thursday, 21st July, 2005 at the Presidential Banquet Hall, State House, Abuja with President Olusegun Obasanjo as the Special Guest of Honour.
2.Its corporate objectives are designed to meet six (6) revenue centres, namely Oil and Gas, Agricultural and Agro Allied Business, Information Technology, Power, International Trade and Free Trade Zone/Industrial park.
3.During its formal launch on Thursday, 21st July, 2005 President Olusegun Obasanjo announced some concessions to the corporation as part of government support and encouragement. These include licence to build a 400,000 barrels per day refinery, licence to build an independent power plant, access to the Federal Government cassava report for the construction of cassava processing exports facility, designated land mass for the construction of free port facilities, continued support to help open up markets on the African continent and to make TRANSCORP a partner in Nigeria's current policy on private, public partnership, creating additional opportunities to develop large scale projects in oil and gas, power and Information and Communications Technology (ICT).
4.So far, TRANSCORP has acquired:
(a)Four Oil blocks, OPL 218, 219, 209 and 220 allocated to it on 21st July, 2005 by General Olusegun Obasanjo when it was launched.
(b)Nicon-Hilton, Abuja in October, 2005 for $105 million dollars
(c)NITEL on 3rd July, 2006 for $750 million dollars.
5.Dr. (Mrs.) Ndi Okereke-Onyiuke on 13th September, 2006 said President, Olusegun Obasanjo is a subscriber to TRANSCORP through Obasanjo Holdings Limited.
6.The President acquired the shares following his public expression on the day TRANSCORP was launched and aired live on television.
7.On Thursday September 14, 2006, Elder Daniel Atsu and Barrister Lucky Egede (Trustees of Obasanjo Holdings Limited) said:
i.That the TRANSCORP shares were bought by Obasanjo Holdings Limited and that its business activities should be separated from the person of President Olusegun Obasanjo.
ii.Shortly before the inception of the Olusegun Obasanjo administration in 1999, a non-recourse Trust deed was executed by the President in favour of Elder Daniel Atsu and Barrister Lucky Egede.
iii.That by the said Trust, Elder Atsu and Lucky Egede were to run the business and the social service interests of the President at their absolute discretion.
iv.That the opportunity to invest in TRANSCORP came to the Trustees in the normal course of business like so many other business opportunities.
v.That one of the subsidiary companies named in the Non-recourse Trust is Ibad Limited. The company is engaged in the manufacturing, distribution and marketing of agricultural equipment and materials and they have supplied and installed agricultural equipment and materials for people in and out of government in the ordinary course of business.
vi.That their clientele includes the farm of the wife of the Vice President in Adamawa State.
vii.That all cheques received by Ibad were received in the ordinary course of business.
viii. That they accepted responsibility for all investments of Obasanjo Holdings Limited and its subsidiary companies as the Trust gave them absolute powers.
Constitutional Implications and Consequences of the part-ownership of TRANSCORP by President Olusegun Obasanjo
The use by the President of Obasanjo Holdings Limited to own substantial part of TRANSCORP is wrong, illegal and unconstitutional for the following reasons:-
1.The Code of Conduct for Public Officers contained in the Fifth Schedule, Part I, Paragraph 13 of the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, 1999 provides as follows:-
"13.A public officer who does any act prohibited by this Code through a nominee, trustee, or other agent shall be deemed ipso facto to have committed a breach of this Code."
The admission by the Trustees (Elder Daniel Atsu and Barrister Lucky Egede) of Obasanjo Holdings Limited, compounds the constitutional illegality of the President's involvement in TRANSCORP.
2.At the time TRANSCORP was formed and four (4) oil blocks allocated to it with attendant licences, the President, General Olusegun Obasanjo was the Minister of Petroleum Resources a position he held from May 29, 1999.
Consequently, for the President to allocate oil fields or blocks as the Minister for Petroleum Resources to TRANSCORP in which he has substantial shares, is clearly an abuse of office under Section 15(5) of the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, 1999 which provides that:-
"The State shall abolish all corrupt practices and abuse of power."
3.A major aspect of the President's reforms programme is Privatization of major industries. Nigerian Telcommunications Limited (NITEL), a public concern which belongs to the major sector of the Nigerian economy was privatized. It was sold by the government of President Olusegun Obasanjo to TRANSCORP substantially owned by the President. It is both a corrupt act, a violation of the Code of Conduct and constitutes a shameless abuse of power by the President.
4.For the Federal Government to divest its shares in NICON Hilton Hotel, probably the biggest hotel in Nigeria, and for the hotel to be bought by the President's partly-owned company, TRANSCORP also amounts to a gross abuse of power, a corrupt act and a violation of the Code of Conduct.
5.Undoubtedly today in Nigeria, TRANSCORP constitutes the wealthiest and the most powerful commercial private institution which power and wealth have been brought into being by the involvement of the President as there was no TRANSCORP before President Obasanjo came to power on 29th May, 1999. What the President has done by setting up TRANSCORP and making it the pillar of the private sector violates Section 16(2)(c) which states as follows:
"2.The State shall direct its policy towards ensuring - (c) that the economic system is not operated in such a manner as to permit the concentration of wealth or the means of production and exchange in the hands of few individuals or of a group."
It is more constitutionally sacrilegious where the President belongs to that group, TRANSCORP.
6.It is clear from the formation of TRANSCORP and its use to take over the constitutional responsibility of the Federal Government, that the President has violated Section 16(4)(a) of the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, 1999 which says that:-
"4.For the purposes of subsection (1) of this section - (a)the reference to the "major sectors of the economy" shall be construed as a reference to such economic activities as may, from time to time, be declared by a resolution of each House of the National Assembly to be managed and operated exclusively by the Government of the Federation; and until a resolution to the contrary is made by the National Assembly, economic activities being operated exclusively by the Government of the Federation on the date immediately preceding the day when this section comes into force, whether directly or through the agencies of a statutory or other corporation or company, shall be deemed to be major sectors of the economy."
And this came into force on the 29th of May 1999.
7.By his Oath of Allegiance contained in the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution, the President on May 29, 1999 and May 29, 2003 swore to preserve, protect and defend the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, 1999. The said Constitution says in Section 1(1) as follows:
"1(1). This Constitution is supreme and its provisions shall have binding force on all authorities and persons throughout the Federal Republic of Nigeria."
Consequently, what the President has done in relation to the setting up of TRANSCORP with Obasanjo Holdings Limited owning substantial shares through President Olusegun Obasanjo appointed trustees Elder Daniel Atsu and Barrister Lucky Egede amount to gross violation of the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, 1999.
I therefore plead with the National Assembly:
(a)To pass a resolution declaring TRANSCORP as an unconstitutional commercial organization.
(b)To set forth the process of impeaching the President under Section 143 of the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, 1999 with regards to his involvement in TRANSCORP.
I also wish to call on the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) to invoke its powers under Sections 7, 29 and 34 of the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission Act, 2004 and to confiscate all assets and properties acquired by TRANSCORP.
The Nation must not allow the President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria to benefit from his corrupt acts, abuse of power, disregard of the constitutional provisions including gross violations of the Code of Conduct in total violation of the Oath of Office of the President contained in the Seventh Schedule which prescribes inter alia as follows:-"? that I will not allow my personal interest to influence my official conduct or my official decisions; that I will to the best of my ability preserve, protect and defend the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria; that I will abide by the Code of Conduct contained in the Fifth Schedule to the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria?"
CONCLUSION
With the mind bugging revelations in the last two weeks on how public funds have been diverted into private hands and with the President violating the Constitution by his part-ownership of TRANSCORP through his Obasanjo Holdings Limited, the Nation has been engulfed in a cloud of despondency, shock and fear for tomorrow.
The President has become a lame duck. The leadership of General Olusegun Obasanjo has violated the Twenty-one (21) Irrefutable Laws of Leadership by John C. Maxwell. It is therefore difficult for the Nigerian people to continue to follow their President who has failed to follow the Twenty-one (21) Irrefutable Laws of Leadership. Two of the Twenty-one (21) Irrefutable Laws of Leadership concern:
(a)Law of Solid Ground based on Trust (i.e. Trust is the Foundation of Leadership); and
(b)Law of Legacy (i.e. A Leader's lasting value is measured by Succession).
Leadership succession under General Olusegun Obasanjo is today in shambles. I doubt whether Nigerians can trust General Olusegun Obasanjo any longer. His integrity has passed the expiry date.

Original_Search_Title: WFP to Open Mill to Process Zambian-Grown Cassava
Headline: Zambia: WFP to Open Mill to Process Zambian-Grown Cassava
Body_Text:
Lusaka — The United Nations World Food Programme (WFP) has announced it is setting up a cassava milling plant in northern Zambia, to facilitate the processing of locally purchased cassava to feed the 69,000 refugees currently being assisted by WFP in the country.
By early 2007, WFP will purchase 250 metric tons of cassava from local farmers every month, to process as fortified flour for the refugees.
WFP's long-term goal is also to encourage the commercial processing of cassava for consumption by Zambians.. With that in mind, WFP has selected a management team for the mill from the private sector. WFP expects to invest a total of USD $250,000 to set up the facilities by the end of this year.
"The New Partnership for Africa's Development (NEPAD) has been encouraging farmers to grow cassava instead of maize, because it gives a higher yield per hectare and is resistant to drought. So far, it is not produced commercially in Zambia," said David Stevenson, WFP Country Director for Zambia.  "By establishing a cassava mill and building a market for the crop, WFP aims to stimulate this new agricultural sector as well as develop a reliable food source for refugees."
WFP has an agreement with the government to purchase as much food as possible locally. "For this reason, we particularly welcome cash contributions towards the refugee assistance programme," Stevenson said. "WFP's food pipeline for the refugees is well resourced through to January 2007, and by then we hope to have funding to establish the steady purchase of local cassava."
Cassava is indigenous to Africa and has been referred to as "drought insurance" by local agriculture and food security experts. The tuber roots are rich in carbohydrates, while the leaves provide protein.  In addition, the flour will be fortified to provide a variety of micronutrients. Alongside the milling plant, WFP is setting up a laboratory to test the flour for its nutritional value.
Donors to WFP's refugee assistance programme in Zambia for this year include:  Sweden, US$893,000; United Kingdom, US$885,000; Canada, US$877,000; United States of America, US$770,000; Netherlands, US$734,000; Germany, US$638,000; Belgium, US$181,000; private, US$500,000.

Original_Search_Title: War Against Fake Drugs . . . So Far So What?
Headline: Nigeria: War Against Fake Drugs . . . So Far So What?
Body_Text:
Lagos — The five-year leadership of the National Agency for Food and Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC) received another round of accolades recently at the public presentation of a report on the level of counterfeit and substandard medicines in Nigeria. It was sponsored by the World Health Organisation (WHO) and the British Department for International Development (DFID).
And as usual, it was another round of celebration for an agency that has done so well on the local scene that the international arena is always eager to share in its success. The source of its celebration came from the report itself, which says that the level of counterfeit drugs in the country has dropped from a staggering average of 41 per cent in 2001 to 16.7 per cent this year. But the country representative of DFID in Nigeria, Dr. Cassidy, aptly expressed the caveat on this celebration when he said that NAFDAC must not relent in its war against counterfeit and sub-standard products.
Apparently revealing his grasp of the report even before its presentation to the public, Cassidy, however, commended the leadership and the entire staff of NAFDAC for the work it has been doing over the years, stating that the report "makes it very clear that a lot of improvement have been made in this fight." He also said that the study was necessary so that all stakeholders in drug control and administration would be able to have a clearer direction in making sure that good medicines are available to the Nigerian people. Messages also came from the representatives of the WHO, Pharmaceutical Society of Nigeria, drug manufacturers, Association of Community Patent Vendors, and other stakeholders in the war against drug counterfeiting.
The report started with a brief introduction tracing the history of counterfeit and sub-standard drugs in Nigeria, developing countries and the world. The global situation of bad quality medicine, according to the report, dates back to the 17th century when Cinchonic bark suffered adulteration. Vietnam in the present century has suffered heavily from sub-standard anti-malarial and Artesunate; likewise Myanmar. Developed countries were not left out in the counterfeiting scourge. Or what could one say of a certain Allan Valentine who was arrested in the United Kingdom for faking Diazepan and Viagra in his Wembley warehouse?
The methodology of the study, according to the researchers, used to determine the quality of drugs in circulation in Nigeria was by collecting selected medicines from four major study centres in the country - Kano, Lagos, Onitsha, and Jos, each contributing 25 per cent of the total. Medicines with high consumer rate, high unit cost and a listing on the Essential Drug List of the Federal Government made the selection. The study was also to determine the percentage of drugs registered with NAFDAC. The researchers explained that the choice of the four centres was based on the fact that they are the major drug distribution centres in the country.
"In sourcing the samples, made up of tablets, syrups, capsules and injections, we made sure they were each selected from one batch and one brand. Three hundred and thirty-eight samples were gathered and divided into two portions; one portion was sent for laboratory analysis at a WHO-commissioned laboratory in Thailand while the second portion went to NAFDAC Directorate of Registration and Regulation for determination of their registration status," the researchers said. Consideration was also given to chemical component of the drugs rather than their generic or non-generic nature. All samples analysed contained active pharmaceutical ingredients as indicated on their respective labels. Some of the samples collected are Chloroquine, Artesunate, Arethemeter, Paracetamol, Nifedipine Retard, Ampicillin, Cloxacillin and Piroxican, among others. The study determined what makes a drug sub-standard or fake by using the following globally approved criteria:
Fake drugs fall under the first two criteria while sub-standard drugs fall in the third. And a standard drug must meet the condition specified in the fourth bullet.
In its findings, the research team discovered that out of 60 samples from Jos, only 36 passed while 24 failed the laboratory test. From the samples gathered in Onitsha, 79 out of 137 passed. Of the samples gathered from Lagos, 50 passed living the rest as failures. 56 samples made it from those gathered in Kano while 40 failed. In total, 221 samples from the 385 gathered made the pass list, while the remaining 164 failed the laboratory test, which was repeated by a second independent analyst.
Giving further insights into the result, the research discovered that one in every 15 Chloroquine injections failed the test, six in every 26 Chloroquine syrups also failed, another six in 27 Choloroquine tablets didn't pass, while seven in every seven Nifedine Retard failed. In total summary, sub-standard drugs constitute 35.6 per cent of total drugs in circulation in the country. Another 16.1 per cent went the way of drugs not registered with NAFDAC, while 47.5 per cent is the share of genuine drugs with correct quantity and packaging; 0.3 per cent went to drugs with wrong NAFDAC registration number, and drugs with a different manufacturer's tag take up 2.9 per cent. The highlight is that the level of counterfeit medicines in Nigeria is now down to 16.7 per cent and non-compliance to licensing authorisation has fallen from 68 per cent in 2001 to 19 per cent in 2006.
In this era of Millennium Development Goals, which find a bloodline in the health reform programme as outlined in the National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy, President Olusegun Obasanjo could hardly hide his delight. Represented by the Minister of Intergovernmental Affairs and Social Development, Dr. Grace Oguche, Obasanjo said during the presentation: " Today, we are witnessing another bold step of NAFDAC in scientifically quantifying the present level of counterfeit and sub-standard medicines in Nigeria. I understand that no such study, carefully designed, using the best available resources, has ever been carried out in the country. This study I was informed, was made possible with the technical input of WHO, which also provided an international consultant, with the sponsorship of DFID and NAFDAC personnel playing key roles."
The report is the first of its kind, according to the NAFDAC consultants that carried out the study. WHO and DFID also acknowledged this. It is, however, important to trace the history of similar studies in the country in order to affirm the unprecedented tag placed on this particular one. What makes it unprecedented?
Four previous studies had been carried out on the subject matter in Nigeria. Poole, WHO and the Federal Ministry of Health estimated in 1989 that the level of fake drugs in the country was 25 per cent while admitting that 75 per cent was inconclusive. Ochekpe et al gave 41 per cent in 1993. Shakoor, Taylor & Dyrehen reported 48 per cent in 2001. And former deputy Director-General of the World Health Organisation, the late Prof. Adeoye Lambo, gave us 54 per cent. The 2005 WHO report on this issue focused on 20 countries, 60 per cent being developing countries, no emphasis on Nigeria. In the present study, the WHO was also involved. But the fact that NAFDAC, the primary organ here, is the initiator of the study makes it different from every other one before it. One should also note that Dr. Ochekpe, who gave the 41 per cent figure in 1993, is one of the three NAFDAC consultants that conducted this present research; so he should know how unprecedented this report is, having been part of previous researches. The study dug deep to source its samples from the grassroots. Samples were sourced from public and private hospitals, pharmacies, dispensaries and patent vendors in Kano, Jos, Lagos and Onitsha market, where the wholesale distributors are concentrated. It was thorough, with in-depth analysis of the methodology used and the results derived. The recommendations that came out of it will go a long way in directing the arsenals of NAFDAC in the war against fake drugs. Among these recommendations are: Strengthening of control mechanism at points of entry; strict monitoring of distribution channels, and consolidating on the already established quality assessment control at countries of origin.
And from stakeholders present at the occasion came some other recommendations. The president of the Nigerian Medical Association, Dr Dan Gana, recommended the establishment of zonal medical stores for proper monitoring of distribution channels, as practised in Sweden and Ghana. The National Association of Community Patent Vendors also want a collaboration with NAFDAC so that it can efficiently carry out its on-going in-house cleaning exercise - an initiative of the association. It is expected that these recommendations will be adopted by the drug and food-regulating agency. This expectation was confirmed by the Director-General of NAFDAC, Prof Dora Akunyili, when she said: "The result of the study will form one of the benchmarks on which activities of the agency in the last five years will be evaluated. It will also be the platform for future studies that will be conducted on fake and counterfeit medicines in Nigeria." And not one whose dreams for the future will be overwhelmed by the achievements of the moment, Akunyili added: "Our dream is to handover to our children, a country free from fake/counterfeit drugs and other sub-standard regulated products and create a strong regulatory environment that will enable our food and drug industries become major players in Africa and indeed the world."
A dream rooted in the president's resolve, as expressed in his speech: "Nigeria is on the right part to where it should be, even though the present position is still not acceptable. All hands must continue to be on deck so as to ensure an acceptable global level. Even one per cent below universal standards is unacceptable to any responsible government because life is too precious to be lost through avoidable circumstances like taking of fake drugs."
In all, the report was considered unprecedented by all present, despite questions raised on its methodology, especially the markets from which the samples were sourced, the non-inclusion of multivitamins and the little significance placed on antiretroviral drugs. Nigeria must indeed be free from chalks in capsules, cassava-enhanced tablets, drugs stored under sunlight, injections with fraudulently extended expiry dates and deaths of drug users who have no reason to die because they are innocent of drug abuse.

Original_Search_Title: Questions Over Obasanjo's Shares in Transcorp
Headline: Nigeria: Questions Over Obasanjo's Shares in Transcorp
Body_Text:
Last week Wednesday, Chairman of Transnational Corporation, Dr. Ndi Okereke-Onyuike confirmed speculations that President Olusegun Obasanjo holds equity shares in the company. Her revelation came barely 48 hours after the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) raided the Head Office of the company reportedly in a bid to 'investigate' the shareholding structure and operations of the company.
Okereke-Onyuike told the House Committee on Capital Markets that the president had subscribed to the shares of the company when it was established. Even though she did not specify the amount of shares held by the president, her revelation confirmed media reports that the president owns between 200million to 600million shares in Transcorp. Although throughout the nearly two years of its existence, there have been strong speculations that the president had significant shares in the company, no concrete evidence had been brought forward to substantiate the claim until about a month ago when it was reported in the papers that the president had between 200million to 600million shares being held in 'blind trust' in the corporation.
The shares are reportedly being held for the president in blind trust by Obasanjo Holdings, the parent company for all his firms which include Obasanjo Farms, OHL Quarry, OHL Properties and Tempo Foods and Packaging Limited. Since Transcorp was established, it has enjoyed tremendous presidential waivers in the different sectors of its operations. This is apart from the preferential treatments it got in its bid to buy some public companies like NITEL and Nicon Hilton (now Transcorp Hilton), in the on-going privatization programme of the federal government.
The recent revelation by Okereke-Onyuike no doubt brings to question the anti-corruption stance of the Obasanjo-led government. The many privileges and presidential waivers that the company has enjoyed within the short period of its existence are no doubt in question because these waivers were given by the president to a company in which he is a major shareholder. Transcorp upon inception got approval to build a $250 million (about N33.25 billion) refinery in Lekki Free Port Zone in Lagos.
The facility is expected to be one of the biggest refineries in the country when completed. President Oluse-gun Obasanjo, while launching the company at the State House Banquet Hall, in Abuja, also announced other concessions to the company, which include a license to build an Independent Power Plant (IPP) as well as an exclusive access to government's cassava report for the construction of a cassava processing facility. The company bought 75percent shares in NITEL under circumstances which many have described as dubious since only 51percent shares of the company were advertised for sale to a core investor. More so, this was done even after Orascom, the Egyptian telecom company that failed to meet the reserved price, was said to have offered to increase its offer to meet the reserved price.
The revised offer of Orascom was reportedly turned down by the federal government because it wanted to restart a new sale process that would enable Transcorp to acquire the company under what the Bureau of Public Enterprises (BPE) called "negotiated sale." But Transcorp is not the only company in which the president has been reported to have acquired stakes, in the last few years.
During the consolidation programme that saw almost all the banks in the country running to the capital market to raise funds in order to meet the N25billion capital base, Obasanjo was said to have acquired significant shares in a number of banks in the country, in most cases using relatives and aides as fronts, like in Transcorp when his shares were held in 'blind trust.' The president is said to have acquired significant shares in United Bank for Africa (UBA) through one of his aides during the consolidation programme. A reliable source close to the bank told Daily Trust that during one of the meetings of the management of the bank, one of the management staff asked the Group Managing Director (GMD), Tony Elumelu whether it was true that the president had shares in the company.
Elumelu was said to have avoided giving a categorical answer to the question thereby raising suspicion amongst management that the president may be a major shareholder in the company. The seemingly unusual closeness of the GMD to the presidency can only but lend credence to these suspicions. Besides UBA, the president is also said to have up to 20 percent stake in Unity Bank following the merger of First Interstate Bank with other banks, like Bank of the North (BON) and Intercity Bank amongst others. Daily Trust was reliably told that prior to the merger, the president was the majority shareholder in First Interstate Bank. In a bid to ensure that it scaled through, the bank along with BON and others were given some waivers by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN).
This saw the cancellation of the debts owed by them to the apex bank. Some of the affected banks then began merger talks that led to their consolidation as Unity Bank. Although Okereke-Onyuike said the president has instructed his company to divest its shares from Transcorp, it remains questionable that the president has shares in the corporation which the presidency has repeatedly denied.
According to her "Transcorp is not Obasanjo's company. The day it appeared in the newspapers, I personally went to President Obasanjo to ask on which name he had bought the shares so that I could tell Nigerians. He said he did not buy, but that he had asked Obasanjo farms to buy but he had asked them to divest. He said he instructed his son who is managing the farm to divest but he (Obasanjo's son) travelled.
"After 30th September, I will be able to say. If I see the names of thieves and vagabonds that looted the treasury, I will cancel them. They will not be shareholders of Transcorp. It is after this that names of shareholders would be filed with the registrars," Onyiuke said. While the president, like any other citizen of the country, has the right to own private properties and investments, the way and manner he invests and where he gets the money to do so must be put under scrutiny to ensure he does not abuse his office as president of the Federal Republic of Nigeria to illegally acquire wealth for himself.
In the last two weeks, there has been heightened tension in the presidency, following EFCC's indictment of Vice President Atiku Abubakar of corruption and Obasanjo's subsequent reference of the case to the National Assembly for consideration. One of the allegations against the VP is that he engaged in fraudulent withdrawals from the Petroleum Technology Development Fund (PTDF). Last week, Atiku Abubakar provided evidence indicting the president and his family of benefiting from the controversial account.
This came barely 24 hours after the president provided financial documents exposing Atiku's complicity in the PTDF deal. Atiku alleged that the Obasanjo benefited from the PTDF deposit in Trans International Bank (TIB) as well as from campaign funds in the account of MOFAS Shipping Line. "The documents available to us, some of which are attached to this statement, shows that the president, his family, businesses, native community and the PDP of which he has proclaimed himself the life leader, have benefited tremendously from money deposited in the accounts he is now dissociating himself from," a statement signed by Atiku's media officer, Garba Shehu, said.
The VP provided bank documents to substantiate the claims, saying that president's Personal Assistant, Mr. Bodunde Adeyanju made over 100 sorties to TIB, Abuja (located at Tofa House in the Central Business District) between 1999 and 2004. "The truth of the matter is that there is a "big linkage" between Chief Obasanjo and Otunba Fasawe contrary to the claims that the president has made. There are cheques worth over N100 million issued to IBAD Nigeria Limited, a construction company solely owned by Obasanjo from Fasawe's MOFAS TIB accounts. There are also payments Fasawe directly made to the Obasanjo Africa Leadership Forum and to Obasanjo Campaign Organization', Atiku said.
The recent revelation by the Chairman of Transcorp and the evidence provided by the VP indicting the president of corrupt practices undoubtedly raises more questions than answers on the federal government's seeming war against corruption. This no doubt raises the question on whether the much talked about anti-graft agency - EFCC - can really summon the courage to investigate activities of the president from 1999 to date.
Currently, questions are being asked as to why it took this long for the EFCC to launch an investigation into the activities and operations of Transcorp. The ability of the commission to conduct a sincere probe into the shareholding structure and operations of the firm is also being questioned.
As Nigerians wait to hear the outcome of EFCC's probe of the company, and other possible dealings of the Commander-in-Chief, the commission would do the nation a lot of good if it hastens its investigations and tell the public what it has found out about Obasanjo's Transcorp shares and the sources through which this and other investments were financed.

Original_Search_Title: Emulating Gani Audu
Headline: Nigeria: Emulating Gani Audu
Body_Text:
THE setting was grand for the Award of Excellence to the best 30 local government chairmen in Nigeria by the management of Daily Independent newspaper, which includes who's who in Nigerian politics at the Shehu Musa Yar'Adu Centre, Abuja.
With the calm weather and friendly atmosphere walked in a friend of mine Professor Godwin Sogolo, formerly of the Institute for Peace and Conflict Resolution Abuja, whom I have lost contact with for some time. As we were exchanging pleasantries came in Mr. Ted Ewere, Editor-in-Chief of Daily Independent newspaper. Seeing these mighty men of the pen was an indication that something serious was in the making. In no time there was an unusual gathering of local government chairmen from different political parties complete with posters indicative of success. Hearing about the event ignited my curiosity as I was informed that the occasion was a meirt award night for the best 30 local government elected chairmen in Nigeria.
With the introduction of Chief Tony Anenih, PDP Board of Trustees Chairman as the Chairman of the occasion and the Minister of the Federal Capital Territory, Matlam Nasir el-Rufai, as the Chief Host, further heightened my desire to witness the event. Soon it was the turn of Gani Audu to receive his award. When it was announced that Chief Anenih would present the award as a mark of honour since both came from Edo State, it was greeted with an emotionally- charged thunderous ovation enhanced by the beating of traditional goge. This marked a milestone in the life of Gani Audu, the Executive Chairman of Etsako West Local Government Area, a place noted for real estate of brilliant political folks.
Like the Biblical Samaritans who did not only believe in Jesus Christ because of what they were told but because they had seen Him and heard him, I decided to collect the list of the projects executed by him. Since most of the towns in Etsako West Local Government are along the road leading to my town Ogbona in Etsako Central Local Government Area that made him among the best 30 local government chairmen. Then came the opportunity for me last weekend on my way from Abuja to my town to see my mother. I discovered that Gani Audu is a social politician of uncommon character and uncommon passion to see his constituency developed. And today he has an impressive array of awards to show for his devotion to service at a time when failure is more common among local government chairmen than success.
The catalogue of projects executed by him include the Ozemoya Cassava Processing Factory in Auchi; a few metres away a road is under construction to give access to the General Hospital that has often been threatened by erosion. To put an end to this he quickly awarded the contract to Systems Construction Company. Also to his credit is the renovation of Etsako West Primary School, Abotse and the construction of the Secretariat-Police access road of about two kilometers.
The renovation of and construction of primary schools and primary health care centres had attracted visits by officials of UBE and National Primary Health Care Centre to Etsako West Local Government.
The tarring of a trunk road inside Agbede town has particularly reaffaimed this writer's belief in Gani Audu. This being in line with author, James Allen's philosophy that: "A man is literally what he thinks, his character being the complete gum of all his thoughts".
Here is a man who believes in Nelson Bosweu School of thought that: "The difference between greatness and mediocrity is often how an individual views a mistake".
This writer is therefore of the candid belief and indeed all well-meaning Nigerians that other elected local government chairmen should emulate Gani Audu instead of engaging in financial "lootocracy". It is this culture of looting that has created a gulf between them and the electorates, thereby leaving them in the Bermadu Triangle of betrayal.

Original_Search_Title: Only Justice And Equity'll Make Democracy Work
Headline: Nigeria: Only Justice And Equity'll Make Democracy Work
Body_Text:
The Chinua Achebe Foundation Interview Series
Disclaimer: The views expressed in the interview are not necessarily those of the Chinua Achebe Foundation. The Chinua Achebe Foundation, an intellectual and cultural organisation, believes in the right of every Nigerian to express their opinion.
MBU, Matthew Tawo--Lawyer,Politician, Diplomat and a permanent fixture in Nigerian political affairs for more than fifty years-- is a political giant. Chief Mbu was born on November 20, 1929, in Okundi, Cross River State. He received his early education at Okundi Primary School from 1937 to 1940. He also attended the Kakwagon Seminary School between 1941and 1943 before proceeding to Middle Temple and University College, London, from 1955 to 1959 where he received the LLB and the LLM. Chief Mbu was subsequently called to the Bar, Middle Temple.
Chief M.T. Mbu's political career began with his membership of Parliament from 1952 to 1955. He has also served his country in various capacities including Federal Minister of Labour, 1954; High Commissioner to UK, 1955 to 1959 and Representative of Nigeria, Washington DC. 1966. He holds the distinction of being the youngest Nigerian ever to serve in the federal cabinet.
Between 1960 and 1966, Chief Mbu returned to serve again in Parliament. During the latter stint in Parliament, he also doubled as Federal Minister of Defense for Naval Affairs. He was appointed Chairman, Eastern Nigeria Public Service Commission in 1967, and became Member Constituent Assembly, 1977-78. He was first national vice-chairman, Nigeria People's Party, 1979-81 before "decamping" to the National Party of Nigeria from 1981-83. On several occasions, Chief Mbu has represented his Nation as an ambassador to foreign countries including a recent appointment as Ambassador to Germany. He has also served as the Pro-Chancellor, Obafemi Awolowo University, Ile-Ife, Nigeria.
Ambassador Mbu is married to Katherine Anigbo and blessed with six children. His hobbies include tennis and swimming. Chief Mbu is at the moment leader of the South-South Peoples Assembly, (SSPA); a non-political organization that was created to help promote the interests of the peoples of South-South geo-political zone.
Excerpts of the Interview:
Sir, you have been a permanent fixture in the history of Nigeria. Do you feel a sense of frustration that the country has not achieved the progress it ought to have made by now?
Well, I think any of us who played a role in the First Republic must feel a great sense of disappointment. At Independence, we all dreamt of rapid emancipation as a people, and not just politically. Having achieved independence from Britain, we pushed to becoming economically independent ourselves; for self-sufficiency in our lives. Some of these expectations, sadly, have not yet been realized due to the instability the nation has suffered since 1966. Even so, no one conceived of the idea that we would ever become victims of a military coup. Such horrific events only occurred in Latin America and elsewhere.
But after the coup in Togo in 1963, we began to wonder about Nigeria. There were problems in the Western Region that had led to "Operation Wetie" in 1964, and eventually the coup in 1966. We were shocked that we could be the victims of this sort of political change. I remember my friend, Peter Pan (Peter Enahoro) asking: "After the Nigerian coup, what will happen in Ghana?" This is because, Ghana and Nigeria seemed intent on copying each other. Not long after this, Ghana, of course, had its first coup. Nkrumah was visiting China at the time; he never returned home. The combined expectations of his country and ours at independence never matured.
Chinua Achebe has this, by now, popular theory about leadership being the trouble with Nigeria. Perhaps, we should ask you to address this question, sir, as you are in the leadership cadre.
That can be said without mincing words. Leadership has been our problem. Whether Nigeria has been blessed with the right leadership has been one of the rather inexplicable problems that this nation has faced from its inception. From the days of those who fought for independence--the days of Dr Nnamdi Azikiwe, Awolowo, Sardauna--can it be said that Nigeria has had the right leadership? Can it be said of the revolutionary leadership of Murtala Muhammed that he lasted long enough to prove himself? Whether or not he would have been successful, we cannot now know. Even with Sir Abubakar Tafawa Balewa, it is not certain whether, if he had been given the chance, he would have provided us with the right leadership. There has always been an element of instability in the assessment of the merits or demerits of any particular leadership in Nigeria. So for me, the struggle of getting and finding an effective leadership for Nigeria continues.
Would you say that corruption has always been part of leadership in Nigeria?
No, I would not say so. Because in my day, corruption like it is today never existed. One could single out those who were found to be corrupt, since they were so few! Most of us were not corrupt, and we remained incorruptible, never taking a penny for ourselves. There was transparency, honesty and probity in public office. So, I would not say that corruption has been with us; rather it has become institutionalised by successive military governments. The military brought corruption, promoting it to such a degree that it has become part of Nigerian governance.
How much damage to the development of a vibrant and patriotic leadership cadre for the country has military rule contributed, in your opinion?
A great deal, when you consider that since independence, the military has held power longer than the civilians. Sir Abubakar Balewa was eliminated through a coup; Shagari was overthrown after four years. The present one under President Obasanjo has endured the longest-- for five or six years. For the 45 years of Nigeria's independence, the military dominated the scene, so there really has been no training ground for succession in the political sense; successive military leadership in the country has created a vacuum as well as a military hierarchy entrenching mediocrity and corruption.
However, the excuse of the military, in taking over government, was that civilians were corrupt...
That is simply not true. A highly respected and dignified man like Sir Tafawa Balewa died a pauper. I can tell you this; he had no money, he had no home. Chief Michael Okpara, when he came back from exile, we had to contribute money to build a house for him; he was not corrupt. The men in the military sought reasons to stage coups for their own selfish reasons; theirs were never patriotic acts to be justified in any way!
So, I insist that military leadership, in this country, is the architect of corruption, because even though one may point to one or two Nigerians who were corrupt, they were the ten per centers -- in the minority. The military introduced what had previously never been known in the history of Nigeria -- situations where contracts that had never been executed were being paid for. Enormous sums of money for contracts would be awarded, despite the fact that the job had not been done. While I was a member of the cabinet, no civilian minister had the temerity, or even ventured close to such corrupt behaviour. And that is why what Obasanjo is trying to do is a bold step, a courageous act in the fight to tackle corruption, and I would like every patriotic Nigerian to support him.
Yet, there are those who think his approach is merely targeted at political opponents?
No, that is not what I think. You know Nigerians have their own version of things; for me, I support what he is doing. I believe that he is a courageous man to have tackled corruption head on, knowing the factors of our political institutions. So, whatever people might think, I feel differently and support him.
There is a great deal of buzz about the South-South producing the president in 2007. Does it have a solid case?
I believe that it is the desire of every region in Nigeria to produce the president of the nation. I do not think there is anything wrong if the next president comes from the South-South. If it can muster enough support for a candidate, and the country votes for such a person, why not?
But here's the dilemma...we have a situation where you are the chairman of a group called the Southern Forum while the North has formed its own version. Is it not an indictment, a contradiction that you, once a champion of Nigerian unity, are now polarising the country into North and South?
Not really. In 1914, Sir Frederick Lugard amalgamated the Protectorate of the North and the South. But as you might know from history, the real motivation behind the amalgamation was to enable the British to source, from the South, the money to sustain their administration of the North. This would have been impossible without recourse to money from the colonial office, so the Southern Protectorate of Nigeria was founded, comprising the Western and Eastern Regions, which had resources enough to sustain the Protectorate of the North.
The British, thereby, contrived to bring about the marriage between the beautiful damsel from the South, and the handsome husband from the North.
Now I say this, because you implied that a division of the nation, vis a vis North and South, is being caused as a result of talk of a Southern Forum. We have always argued that there has never been a North and South. Even with the amalgamation, the Northern Protectorate was comprised of the northern provinces of Nigeria while the Southern Protectorate comprised both the Western Region, as well as the Eastern Region of Nigeria. So you see that there never was any clear cut dichotomy of North and South. What we are trying to do now, under the umbrella of the Southern Forum, is to try for a correction of that anomaly.
I am a disciple of The Right Honourable Dr Nnamdi Azikiwe who pursued relentlessly, till death, the achievement of one Nigeria, one people, one destiny. What we today, in the Southern Forum, are saying is that the imperfections of the past should not be allowed to continue; we should attempt to have an equitable, indivisible, united, one nation, known as Nigeria. These are some of the things we have advocated, or have been canvassing for in the past. Let us have a true federation, a federation of equal status, a federation expressed not just physically, but fiscally.
The issue, at the bottom of all of this, is really one of power...
Power must be seen to be the property of all the federating units of Nigeria. It should not be a privilege enjoyed only by a portion of the federation. And we argue, among other things, that the following things must be put right. As I have mentioned, a truly physical and fiscal federation must exist. By fiscal, I mean that we must, of necessity, go back, and revisit the 1963 Constitution that clearly spelt out the principle of derivation based on sources of income produced by the states. Under that Constitution, the state of origin retains fifty percent of the resource, and fifty percent is then paid into what is now referred to as the Federation Account. Secondly, one of the six geo-political zones presently suffers an inequality of states, and that is the Southeast zone.
