It is argued that long-standing distortions in land, labour and product markets mean that in many countries the distribution of land is highly unequal with vast tracts of potentially productive land lying idle while small producers are forced to scrape a living on fragile and often environmentally fragile lands.
FAO, Multilingual Thesaurus of Land Tenure (2003) defines agrarian reform as "a collection of activities and changes designed to alter the agrarian structure of a country and the ways of using land. It invariably has political, economic and socio-cultural dimensions. The objectives of land reform are generally to improve qualitatively and quantitatively the levels of agricultural production and to improve the standards of living of agricultural producers. Such reforms will often involve elements of redistribution of land and changes to the land tenure system".
Empirical studies on the economic implications of land reforms have largely tended to focus on equity and productivity considerations. Focus has been specifically on the inverse relationship between small farm size and productivity (ILC, 2006). It has been argued for example that the operation of the inverse relationship means that a more equitable distribution of land could result in an increase of anything between 10 and 30 percent in food production. The following paragraphs are a review of land reforms in selected Asian countries based on this inverse relationship.
The land reform programme of Japan imposed a ceiling on land holdings of one hectare. The landowners were compensated in cash and development bonds. In the course of the reform, the actual tillers were given full ownership rights for the holdings they had previously cultivated and received a subsidised mortgage. Labour productivity increased annually by 5 percent and land productivity by 4 percent between 1954 and 1968. Key factors for the success of the reform were an existing well-developed extension service, land records and an efficient bureaucracy.
In Taiwan the land reform placed a ceiling of one hectare. The former landowners were compensated in industrial bonds, which they invested in the urban-industrial zone. Between 1953 and 1960 the annual production and consumption of inputs was 23 percent and 11 percent respectively.
Vietnam experienced similar productivity gains from breaking up large collective farms into tiny family units. Laws enacted in 1981 and 1987 aimed at improving agricultural productivity through increased incentives of individual farmers and recognised land use rights of individual households. These reforms have resulted in an impressive growth of agricultural output, transforming Vietnam from a food-deficit country into a food-surplus country. Rice production increased from 12 million tonnes in 1981 to 22 million tonnes in 1992. In addition, there has been a significant increase in the area under industrial/commercial crops including rubber, coffee, tea, coconut, fruits and vegetables, while the area under inferior crops such as cassava and sweet potatoes has declined.
A critical factor for success of the land reform in South Korea has been the equally thorough development and support to local village government to assume land administration function. Thus the country has been able to maintain continuous agricultural and rural development. In the course of the reform 65 percent of the agricultural land was redistributed. A ceiling on all individual holdings was set at three hectares of good cropland and land in excess of this ceiling was distributed in units of one hectare to former tenants. This low ceiling enabled nearly 76 percent of the total agricultural households to own land for the first time. Under the impact of the reforms agriculture achieved an annual growth rate of almost 4 percent.
In the early 1950s, agriculture's contribution to Gross Domestic Product stood at around 47 percent while primary exports stood at a lowly 0.04 percent. By 2005, the contribution of agriculture to South Korea's economic development had fallen to a paltry 3.3 percent while value-added exports were a mammoth 76.6 percent.
The following lessons can be drawn from the experiences of the Asian countries at land reform. Carried out properly, land reform can impact positively on productivity. Numerous studies have shown that small family farms are more productive than large farms per unit of land because of their differential advantage in labour cost and the superiority of soil quality. There is greater labour intensity and more attention to the land and crops with the use of household labour.
Owner operated family farms are generally more efficient in the use of land and other inputs than large farms operated with supervised wage labour. Secure property rights promote long-term investments in enhancing productivity and in implementing conservation. Another important lesson is that a well-thought-out land reform programme could lead to sustainable management of natural resources.
Lastly, carried out properly, land reform can have a positive impact on poverty. Studies in the Philippines have shown that land reform has induced a decline in the incidence of poverty among land agrarian reform households from 47.6 percent in 1990 to 45.2 percent in 2000. In contrast, the incidence of poverty among non-land reform beneficiaries increased from 51.1 percent in 1990 to 56.4 percent in 2000.
The challenge for the Fast Track Land Reform Programme in Zimbabwe is to ensure that it scores highly in the three dimensions of poverty alleviation, enhanced agricultural productivity and sustainable management of natural resources.

Original_Search_Title: Nigerians Ready to Pay More for Regular Electricity -Survey
Headline: Nigerians Ready to Pay More for Regular Electricity -Survey
Body_Text:
"I don't mind paying more when they privatize the power holding company, if only I could have constant electricity".
That is how Chinwendu Ugwu, a roadside mobile phone operator sighted opposite the Federal Secretariat along the busy Shehu Shagari Way in Maitama, Abuja responded when confronted with the question of her readiness to pay more for improved electricity supply if the Power Holding Company of Nigeria {PHCN}is eventually privatized.
Though 26-year old Chinwendu who wore a fairly old Nigerian wax with green pull-over said she makes just enough to prevent her from starving, the thought of having constant electricity would elate her even at a slightly higher cost. Government says it has spent more than N200 billion on efforts to improve electricity supply since 1999. In the 2006 budget, it says it would spend not less than N78 billion on the power and steel sector alone, with N16.96 billion going on the execution of rural electrification projects now at substation l evel across the country.
Persistent erratic supply in the country has however informed government's decision to devise further strategies for checking the situation. After renaming the old National Electric Power Authority {NEPA} as Power Holding Company of Nigeria, government decided to remove itself from complete ownership of the company by selling it to willing and capable individuals or organizations with the hope of improving service delivery. As a result, the company was broken down {unbundled} into 18 units for the generation, transmission and distribution of electricity to the people.
An opinion poll by Business Trust suggests that many Nigerians are ready to pay more for constant electricity. Timothy is a hawker of fairly used imported suits. He spoke to Business Trust under the sun while standing close to a woman who sold banana and oranges with her child breastfeeding on her laps. He says he is not very sure the supply situation would change even when the PHCN is sold, but agrees that regular power supply is worth paying for.
"Now", he said, "we pay N300 in our room in Gwagwa, I combine with a friend in one room, but every time they come and threaten to cut our wire because of light bill, the annoying thing is that we don't get electricity more than three days in a whole month, so if they can increase the bill so that light would become constant we are ready to pay more, but I am sure in the Nigerian situation, nothing will change".
Timothy however reminded Business Trust that he may not even stay long in Gwagwa after that day because the Federal Capital Territory demolition team was already poised to pull down his apartment any moment after that.
Just as he spoke, there was a sudden stampede, the woman who sold banana and oranges beside him quickly grabbed her bag and tray, her child almost falling off, Timothy too, ran off, and so were the other hawkers, many who made the place look like a mini mart. But they were marketing their w ares in an undesignated location, and the city authorities are always after them, when suddenly a task force team arrives, everyone grabs his things and escapes, or he would be arrested, have his wares confiscated and even prosecuted. Funmi James is a Lagos- based student.
She spotted a green pair of jeans trousers with a purple top and ate groundnuts as she spoke; "I'm from Lagos. We live in a face-to-face apartment. Every family in our compound has a small power generating set, which is switched on at all times and placed in the corridor. The gas emitted from the generators is not good for our health. If power is constant, we will pay the price."
Yusuf Kankia put on a brown caftan with a cap to match. It was his lunch time and he was conversing with two other friends, Balarabe Ali and another man. Balarabe says he works in the presidency, and does not mind additional cost for improved power supply. "If really light is going to be constant, believe me sincerely, we are read y to pay more, because people need constant power supply, but in as much as people will pay higher, and light is still erratic, then obviously Nigerians will be disappointed and will not be happy to subscribe to the privatization of PHCN.
"Let me take the GSM as example, even as their services are not so good, at least people are satisfied that they can communicate, they are still paying, so if light will be constant, obviously, I don't mind paying higher", said Yusuf Kankia, a civil servant. Dr. Peter Osuagwu had just parked his car at a nearby park when Business Trust met him.
He looked very much a contented man, clean shaved and dressed in a three-piece suit; "For some of us who lived abroad for so many years, regular power supply is what we need, no matter the charges. In Nigeria irregular power supply is the norm. If the Power Holding Company of Nigeria (PHCN) increases its tariff and provides constant electricity, we will be prepared to pay more."
But it is not all Nigerians who believe that the sector should be privatized as electricity supply is seen as part of the social services to be provided by government. Owobu Michael was chief driver at the Office of Secretary to the Government of the Federation. He now does private business with his family in Suleija, Niger State. Owobu says he would not be happy to pay more as he believes nothing good will come out of the sale of PHCN. "I don't want to pay more, and I don't want them to sell the PHCN in the first place, they continue selling everything, every government property, but they are the ones that are still buying these things, it is not good. I am not interested in selling it, they can afford to pay anything if the bill is increased, but most of us are even finding it difficult to pay the present bill, let them leave it as it is, we are the people, we say PHCN should work even without selling it, everyone knows what is going on", he said.
Mr Owobu's opinion was supported by Thomas Tur noe, a cassava farmer who came to Abuja from Shendam in Plateau State to market his farm produce. He could not hide his anger for the failure of the Nigerian Cassava Growers Association to fulfill its promise to market the harvested cassava which he said was wasting in spite of having registered his investment with the association for that purpose.
"We know it is inevitable, the way this government is, even if we say we don't want them to sell PHCN, I'm sure they'll go ahead to sell it, but we know that power supply is not likely to improve even if they sell it and increase the bills, it is not likely to improve unless they change the entire system from the head to the least man in the office. As far as I am concerned I have no say because they are just imposing the sale on Nigerians. Even if we continue to cry from now till tomorrow or next year, they will go ahead with their plan, but they are not working for the masses but themselves", he said.
"I'm not going to pay more even if they improve the service," declares 56-year-old Alhaja Subair, as she believes that what she pays for power at the moment is too high for the services she receives. Considering the cost of running generating sets, respondents would prefer to pay higher for better services. "Everyday, I spend N200 to fuel my generator in Mararaba. I'm willing to pay more if power should improve," says Oguike John, a senior civil servant with a federal ministry.

Original_Search_Title: Boosting Continent's Farm Yields
Headline: Africa: Boosting Continent's Farm Yields
Body_Text:
Emily Miyanda is luckier than most farmers in hunger-plagued Zambia. With 6 hectares of irrigated land only a dozen kilometres from the capital, Ms. Miyanda produces maize and vegetables for the big Lusaka market. She hires her neighbours to help with planting and harvesting. But even with those advantages, she is squeezed between the high costs of what she needs to buy and the low prices her produce fetches at market. "We'd like some government support in buying seeds and fertilizer," Ms. Miyanda told the BBC earlier this year. "That would make life easier."
For tens of millions of people in rural Africa, life is getting harder. Reliant on erratic rains, working exhausted soil and hobbled by decades of underinvestment and neglect by national governments and international donors, many have sunk deeper into poverty as African agriculture - the mainstay of the region's economy - continues to face neglect. A growing number of African governments and UN and non-governmental agencies argue that unless urgent efforts are made to raise crop yields, build transportation and marketing systems and adopt modern, sustainable farming methods, the continent will fail to reach its development goals and the rural majority will reap only meagre harvests.
Development blueprint
Follow us on WhatsApp | LinkedIn for the latest headlines
It is difficult to overestimate the importance of agriculture to Africa's economic prospects. More than 65 per cent of sub-Saharan Africa's estimated 750 million people are engaged in agriculture, and the sector generates more than a quarter of gross domestic product in most countries. Farm produce accounts for about 20 per cent of Africa's international trade and is a major source of raw materials for industry.
In recognition of its importance, proponents of the continental development plan, the New Partnership for Africa's Development, released NEPAD's Comprehensive Africa Agriculture DevelÂ­opÂ­ment Programme (CAADP) in 2003 (see Africa Recovery, January 2004). Its goals are ambitious - to reach rural growth rates of 6 per cent annually by 2015, integrate and invigorate regional and national agricultural markets, significantly increase agricultural exports, transform Africa into a "strategic player" in global agricultural science and technology, practice sound environmental and land management techniques, and reduce rural poverty. The plan identifies four key areas, or pillars, of an investment plan for revitalizing the rural economy, including:
expanding the acreage of irrigated African farmland and improving land management and farming techniques to preserve and improve soil quality
investing in rural infrastructure, including roads and railways, storage and processing facilities, markets, communications systems and reliable supply networks for farmers
making food production a high priority, both to combat hunger and for export, and improving emergency responses to natural disasters and conflicts
strengthening African agricultural research and development, including in advanced technologies and farming methods, and disseminating advances quickly and efficiently to farmers, suppliers and buyers.
The CAADP plan emphasizes coordinated regional action in each of the continent's diverse climate zones and relies on existing regional economic organizations and national governments for implementation. The total cost of the programme from 2002 through 2015 is estimated at $251 bn - about $18 bn a year. Although the blueprint envisages donor financing for a portion of the total, analysts note that Africa's agricultural imports currently average about $19 bn annually - suggesting that a significant portion of the costs can eventually be covered by African countries as they cut their spending on imports.
'Mined of life'
However, the challenges to success are as large as the potential consequences of failure. The UN's World Food Programme estimates that more than 30 million Africans are currently in need of international food aid. Researchers for the African Union reported in January that annual population growth has outstripped food production on the continent since 1993, resulting in a 20 per cent rise in the number of hungry people - from 176 million to 210 million. Commercial exports have fared no better, with Africa's share of world trade declining for nine of its 10 largest export crops.
One important obstacle to increased productivity has been the steady deterioration of Africa's soils, noted Mr. Amit Roy, the head of the International Fertilizer Development Centre (IFDC), a US-based institute that promotes agricultural advancement in developing countries. "Today three-quarters of Africa's farmlands - some 170 mn hectares [420 mn acres] is degraded," he told reporters in New York in late March. "As a result, grain yield has stagnated at 1 tonne per hectare, compared to the world average of about 3 tonnes." Crops consume upwards of 45 kilogrammes of nutrients and minerals from each hectare of land every season, he noted, a process known as nutrient mining. "When farmers plant the same fields season after season and cannot afford to replace the soil nutrients taken up by their crops, the soil is literally mined of life."
An estimated 8 mn tonnes of nutrients are depleted annually. Replenishing the nitrogen, potassium, phosphorus and other minerals absorbed by plants is therefore vital to keep crop yields from declining. Part of the answer lies in better farming methods, including expanding the range of crops grown, improving soil conservation practices and utilizing improved seeds and technology. But the key to launching a "revolution" in African agriculture, Mr. Roy told Africa Renewal, is much greater use of fertilizer.
In Asia and Latin America, farmers have dramatically improved harvests by improving the productivity of existing land, whereas in Africa, farmers have sought greater output by cultivating more land. "Traditionally, African farmers use the slash-and-burn method," he said. "They burn off a section of land and farm it for a season or two, then clear another plot and leave the old field fallow." But population increases and growing land shortages have forced farmers to cultivate the same fields repeatedly, stripping the land of nutrients and resulting in smaller harvests and less income. Such pressures have also led farmers to clear land poorly suited for cultivation, which contributes to soil erosion and yields only marginal increases in harvests.
An estimated 50,000 hectares of Africa's forests and 60,000 hectares of savannah are lost to such methods annually - resulting in severe environmental degradation and contributing to a 20-year decline in agricultural production per capita.
According to the CAADP, African farmers currently use far less fertilizer than their counterparts in other regions of the world. "A strong relationship exists between the level of fertilizer use and cereal yield," notes the programme.
In a study prepared for the 9-13 June Africa Fertilizer Summit, called by the African Union to focus attention on the African soils crisis, IFDC researchers Julio Henao and Carlos Baanante noted that fertilizer use in Africa is less than a tenth the world average of 100 kilogrammes. Nearly two-thirds of Africa's fertilizer is consumed in just five countries, Ethiopia, Kenya, South Africa, Zimbabwe and Nigeria, primarily in the commercial and export sectors. "Negligible fertilizer use by smallholder farmers," they asserted, "is a major factor in the region's declining farm yield per person, which has exacerbated hunger and undernutrition."
High costs, short supply
Heavy reliance on imported fertilizers, combined with high transportation costs and the absence of suppliers in the countryside, has meant that African farmers pay between two and six times the average world price for fertilizer - when they can find it at all. The IFDC study estimated that it costs more to move a kilogramme of fertilizer from an African port to a farm 100 kilometres inland than it costs to move it from a factory in the US to the port. With millions of African family farmers surviving on less than a dollar a day, imported fertilizer is simply unaffordable.
Yet evidence suggests that even modest increases in the use of fertilizer - whether nitrogen, phosphorous or potassium - can have dramatic results. In Ethiopia, one study found that just one bottle cap's worth of chemical fertilizer on each plant increased millet yields exponentially. The technique, known as "micro-dosing," is regarded as particularly appropriate for Africa's small-scale farmers, because it reduces costs and avoids damage to fragile soils from excessive chemical use.
Citing the potential environmental risks to African soils and water sources from too much chemical fertilizer being applied to farms - as sometimes happened during Asia's "green revolution" - proponents of sustainable agriculture in Africa argue that farmers should use more animal manure, compost and other organic fertilizers. If farmers better integrate stock-raising with crop cultivation, cattle and other livestock could provide them with not only more manure but also with animal traction for ploughing fields and hauling crops after they are harvested.
While organic fertilizers are important, agrees Mr. Roy, he points to a serious limitation. "The quality of animal manure is dependent on the quality of the food the animals are fed." With much soil severely depleted, he says, "the fodder contains little of the nutrients needed by crops." Organic fertilizers alone "are simply not the answer to the crisis of Africa's soil fertility. We need to increase the use of both organic and chemical fertilizers."
The CAADP makes the same point. Under the new, integrated approach to African agriculture advocated by NEPAD, "mineral fertilizers and organic matter are treated as complements rather than substitutes."
One way to make chemical fertilizers more available and affordable is to increase local production. This can reduce costs, ease the pressure on foreign currency reserves and shorten the supply chain to farmers. Although Africa consumes only about 1 per cent of global fertilizer production and currently produces even less, prospects for the commercial manufacture of fertilizers are good. Nitrogen is among the most common elements on earth, but converting it into use for plants is energy-intensive. West Africa's vast and largely untapped natural gas resources, notes Mr. Roy, therefore make the region ideally suited for the manufacture of nitrogen fertilizer. Africa also has ample deposits of phosphorus and already exports the mineral to Chinese and Indian farmers. If these minerals can be utilized in local production, Africa would need to import only potassium fertilizer.
But investment in fertilizer production will only come in response to increased demand from farmers, Mr. Roy asserts. Persuading Africa's family farmers that purchasing fertilizer is worth the money and effort will require significant improvements in rural transport networks and infrastructure, an expanded network of rural farm suppliers and markets, and greater financial returns, including protection from price fluctuations and subsidized Northern competitors.
Fertilizer no 'silver bullet'
"Fertilizer is not a silver bullet for Africa's agricultural problems," Mr. Roy admits. "The fertilizer doesn't help if it arrives too late, or the crops aren't watered or you can't sell the harvest. Farmers know this. That is why CAADP is so important. It addresses the needs of farmers comprehensively."
In the face of erratic and unreliable rainfall in large swaths of East and Southern Africa, expanding the acreage of irrigated land is also urgent. In February, UN Food and Agriculture Organization Director-General Jacques Diouf told African agriculture ministers gathered in Bamako that just 4 per cent of sub-Saharan Africa's arable land is irrigated, compared with 38 per cent in Asia. Although much of Africa has abundant water supplies, he noted, "the region uses less than 3 per cent of its water resources, the lowest percentage of the developing world." NEPAD researchers have estimated the initial investment of irrigating 20 mn more hectares of African farmland at $37 bn, with an additional $31 bn in operating costs through 2015.
In the absence of such resources, some innovative local initiatives show promise. In Dowa, Malawi, Glyvyns Chinkhuntha has built a gravity-powered irrigation system for his 20-hectare farm using only hoes and shovels. Drawing on water from a nearby river, he has created a system of irrigation canals only as wide as a hoe blade. By blocking or unblocking individual channels with dirt, he is able to direct water where it is needed. This proves, Mr. Chinkhuntha told the US newspaper the Christian Science Monitor, that homegrown solutions to Africa's agricultural problems do exist. "Africa would have plenty to eat," he said, if farmers could gain more education and training. "All the knowledge and information is tucked up in offices," he lamented, instead of at work in the fields.
Rebuilding rural supply networks and marketing systems so farmers have the means and incentives to produce more is another major challenge. Government-run agriculture marketing boards used to perform some of these functions, providing stable prices, credit extension services, improved seeds and technology to local farmers. But as South African researchers Raj Patel and Alexa Delwiche have noted, most boards were liquidated in the 1980s and 1990s as a result of privatization policies promoted by the International Monetary Fund and World Bank. "Now, in addition to enduring direct exposure to international market fluctuations, farmers are often unsure when private buyers will appear, and are thus forced to sell cheap to the first trader," they wrote in a 2002 study for the US non-governmental organization Food First. The difficulties of transportation and shipping also mean that outÂ­lying areas often are not covered by private buyers, a problem faced by suppliers of fertilizers , seeds and other farm inputs as well.
Mr. Roy says that expanding private sector involvement in rural marketing and supply activities is a long-term solution. But he acknowledges that high poverty rates in the countryside and the need for "public goods" like roads and markets give African governments a key role in creating incentives for private investment.
In 1998, a pilot programme launched by the government and donor agencies in Malawi distributed small "starter packs" of fertilizer and improved seeds to virtually every rural household. Although the project was successful, its high cost and lack of sustainability limited its adoption on a larger scale. In Kenya, Mr. Roy says, the government chose to enable the private sector through greater investment in infrastructure, while other governments have used a combination of subsidies and private sector incentives to try to meet farmers' needs.
Finally, Mr. Roy observes, "Africa needs to do a better job in applying science and technology to agricultural problems, and getting those advances to the farmer more quickly." Here too there are encouraging signs. In 1997, scientists at the West Africa Rice Development Association developed hardy new varieties that combine the best of African and Asian types to sharply improve yields (see Africa Renewal, January 2004). NEPAD and the Nigerian-based International Institute for Tropical Agriculture are working to promote and improve another West and Central African staple, cassava, through the pan-African cassava initiative .
Expanded and more effective extension services can also bring big dividends by improving land- and water-management techniques, introducing new techniques (such as rotating crops and mixing different crops on the same farm) and bringing scientific expertise and new technologies to farmers quickly.
"The African farmer is primarily a woman farmer," Mr. Roy concludes. "And she is a good farmer who can feed her family and her continent if she is given the tools and the opportunities to do so."

Original_Search_Title: Is Cassava Africa's New Staple Food?
Headline: Africa: Is Cassava Africa's New Staple Food?
Body_Text:
It had not rained for months in Southern Africa four years ago and the fields of maize outside of Lusaka, Zambia, stood wilted and gray, a total loss. But despite one of the most severe droughts on record, Melania Chipungu's cassava fields remained vibrant green, swaying in the breeze.
"I used to grow maize, but it was difficult and I would lose my crop whenever the rain was poor. But I don't worry about the drought since I turned to cassava," Mrs. Chipungu told Africa Renewal. "Cassava is easier to grow. All I do is keep the weeds out - no fertilizers, no long hours of hard work. I have more time to meet with other women and do things that I enjoy."
Cassava's tuber roots, which look similar to a sweet potato, are rich in carbohydrates. The leaves provide about the same amount of protein as an egg. The crop can be turned into high-quality starch, flour and animal feed. It is also used to make paper and gum. Impressed by cassava's many advantages, Mrs. Chipungu and about twenty other female farmers formed the Mitengo Women's Association to help each other grow the crop. Experts from the Zambian Ministry of Agriculture trained them in how to best grow the plant, said Mrs. Chipungu, who is chair of the association.
No longer ignored
"Many African farmers have ignored cassava in favour of maize. But cassava is now increasingly finding its way into the diets of many Africans," says Richard Mkandawire, who heads the agriculture programme of the New Partnership for Africa's Development (NEPAD). He notes that in the face of chronic food shortages, governments are increasingly turning to food crops like cassava that are cheaper, hardier and easier to grow.
NEPAD, an African-owned vision and strategic framework adopted by African leaders in 2001 to revive development throughout the continent, is promoting the shift to cassava. Recognizing that small farmers are the key to feeding poor families, NEPAD is encouraging farmers to grow cassava as part of an overall strategy to ensure a reliable supply of food.
"Cassava is the only food staple that farmers can harvest early in the rainy season, when hunger is most acute. Maize stocks usually run out at the end of the year before the new crop is ready for harvest," the Zambia-based Dr. Haggblade says.
Cassava is the crop of choice, he says, because "in a normal year, a farmer gets about only 2 tonnes of maize per hectare. Cassava will yield three times as much food."
Improving quality
In a bid to increase the number of cassava growers, African leaders are working with scientists to provide farmers with high-quality seeds and information on the best methods for planting and growing cassava. NEPAD officials are now working with the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) to improve cassava so it can yield more, mature faster and resist diseases better. The institute, based in Ibadan, Nigeria, says it has developed a variety that can be harvested within 12 to 24 months and provide yields of more than 40 tonnes per hectare.
Mrs. Chipungu has noticed the difference. "My life improved after I began growing cassava. The new improved varieties, Tanganyika and Mwelu, mature within a year. They also give us big healthy tubers that fight disease better than the old plants," she said.
A sound crop is just the beginning. One of NEPAD's major goals is to help farmers grow enough that they can sell cassava as well as feed their families. Nigeria, the world's leading cassava producer according to the UN Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), is encouraging its farmers to grow cassava. The Nigerian government estimates that cassava could earn up to $5 bn, about one-third of the income it receives from the sale of crude oil.
'I have money in my pocket'
From their 20-30 acre plots, the women of the Mitengo association hope to export cassava to neighbouring countries. To do this, they will need assistance to buy processing and packaging machines to produce quality goods that can compete on an international market. Many of the women are responsible for supporting themselves.
"We are saying to women, it doesn't matter if you are alone or married, you have to use your hands to survive. My husband is dead and I have eight children, so I can't give up," Mrs. Chipungu said. "Since I turned to cassava, I have money in my pocket. I can buy seeds to grow cabbages and tomatoes to feed my family. With support, many more women can become independent."
In this campaign for cassava growing, experts from NEPAD and FAO are helping to spread IITA's improved plants to more than a dozen countries. Although cassava is not a silver bullet and it will not be able to remedy all of Africa's food shortages, given the means, cassava farmers will be able to produce food for hungry people across Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Bugesera Residents Decry Poverty
Headline: Rwanda: Bugesera Residents Decry Poverty
Body_Text:
Eastern Province — Residents in Bugesera District are decrying the severe poverty in the area, attributing it to last year's prolonged drought that hit the area. They say that their cash crops were destroyed, leading to serious food shortages, making them unable to meet essential basic needs.
In an exclusive interview with some of the residents in the area on September 1, one Jacqueline Nikunze of Nyamata Sector said this year her harvests have been very low due to low rains.
She said besides drought the soils are too poor to permit planting of various kinds of food crops.
"We have been depending on sorghum and cassava for business, but now you can not find any due to unfavorable agricultural conditions. Cassava has been destroyed by pests and diseases which have contributed to severe poverty because we have nothing to sell to meet some basic needs," she added.
Vestine Azia, 65, a widow and an elder in Kamabuye, former Ngenda Sector shared the same sentiments with Nikunze adding however, that the prices for the basic needs have skyrocketed.
"Nowadays I find if hard to raise money for school fees and books. My mother is too poor to support my education. I only earn a living by working in the neighbor's banana plantations, and the pay is not enough for my basic needs," sixteen year-old Jean Batista Uwababyeyi told this reporter.
When contacted, Antoine Rusingizandekwe, the Executive Secretary for the district observed that most people in the area live below the poverty line and said the district authorities are trying to remedy the situation.
"We have laid economic strategies to enable the community generate income. We are now working closely with peasants. They have responded to the government's call to grow more food crops to help them fight famine and poverty," he said, adding that all Sectors in Bugesera have been offered enough land to facilitate the residents plant more maize and various cash crops.

Original_Search_Title: NNPC to Import Ethanol Fuel
Headline: Nigeria: NNPC to Import Ethanol Fuel
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) yesterday said it would soon commence the importation and distribution of limited quantities of ethanol fuel into the country.
The corporation said it has awarded contract for the re-structure of two major product reception and distribution facilities at Mosimi and Atlas Cove in Lagos to facilitate the delivery and pumping of ethanol fuel to other parts of the country.
The Group Managing Director of the corporation, Engr. Funsho Kupolokun who disclosed this at the signing of consultancy agreement with the Federal University of Agriculture, Makurdi on development of ethanol, said the decision to import the product is to help firm up the market and provide the necessary experience in distribution and handling.
Kupolokun said a comprehensive feasibility study is to be launched in the fourth quarter of this year, while Federal Government is expected to announce a package of incentives aimed at promoting the bio-fuel project.
Speaking on the essence of the collaborative agreement with the University, the GMD said there is need to develop sufficient local production capacity for sugar cane and cassava to feed the ethanol plant on a sustainable basis.
"The success of the bio-fuels programme is anchored on local availability of high quality feed stock in sufficient quantity from both out-growers and integrated plantations to feed the ethanol plants on sustainable basis", he said.
He said the challenge is for the industry to seek ways of producing high yield seedling and disease-resistant varieties, seedling propagation and replication and development, applying modern farm practices to maintain reasonable production level.
According to him, the consultancy agreement will therefore enable us to establish the framework and set out the principles under which the University would provide technical assistance to NNPC on automotive bio-mass ethanol programme.
The out-going Vice Chancellor of the Federal University of Makurdi, Prof. James Ayatse described the effort at actualizing use of bio-fuel as a novel innovation geared towards further improving the nation's economy.
He said the expectations are that NNPC would utilize its enormous economic influence in strengthening agricultural production in the country. He said the Federal University of Agriculture possesses the manpower and expertise to give the needed support for the ethanol project."
"In this regard we are looking forward that through this collaboration the weakness in our system will be strengthened by NNPC while the technical and capacity needs of the corporation will be provided by the institution", he said.
He said the corporation's assistance is needed in the area of research infrastructure and equipment to enable the school generates the kind of quality research needed to kick-start local production of ethanol.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Growing Successful in North
Headline: Uganda: Banana Growing Successful in North
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE traditional and colonial belief that bananas can not grow well in northern Uganda, has been proved wrong.
"The Langi and Acholi have missed out on banana and coffee. These crops also do well in our soils," says Grace Akullu, a prosperous banana farmer in Amac sub-county, Lira district.
Akullu, a former primary school teacher with a diploma in agriculture, told The New Vision at an interview recently, that she sells a bunch of bananas from sh3,000 to sh5,000 in Lira town and Amac market.
"From the proceeds, we have built a four-bedroom permanent house and I can pay fees for my two children in secondary school," she said.
Akullu said menvu (sweet bananas) and bogoya were selling like hot cakes in markets and towns.
In Uganda, bananas are a major crop in the central, eastern and western regions. The north is a preserve for cotton, millet and cassava growing. But Akullu's experiment is an eye-opener to farmers in the north.
Akullu said the time for harvesting depends on the variety, but generally the fruit first appears 10-18 months after planting the sucker. "If the plantation is well managed, we realise about 10 tonnes of fruit per hectare every year," she said.
After harvesting, Akullu says she covers the stump with soil to prevent banana weevils. Apac district agricultural officer, Yuvan Ogwang said bananas grow well where there is much rainfall, but not prolonged rainfall. He said bananas grow in a wide-range of soils with good drainage and fertility," he said.
Akullu says old leaves are pruned and used as mulching, leaving five to six leaves on a plant. "We always mulch the plantation for good yields. That is the reason why we reap much during harvest," she says.
"We sometimes interplant with beans and groundnuts to conserve soil and prevent soil erosion." She says at harvest, a normal bunch has nine to 12 half spirals or hands (groups of finger-like bananas) clearly separated from each other. "Each hand has 12-16 individuals fingers weighing about 25-39gm," she says.

Original_Search_Title: Why Rural Areas Are Not Developed - Ezea
Headline: Nigeria: Why Rural Areas Are Not Developed - Ezea
Body_Text:
Enugu — A Peoples Democratic Party gubernatorial aspirant in Enugu State, Chief Okey Ezea, has blamed the under-development of the rural areas on the failure of the local government councils to channel their resources towards the provision of infrastructures.
He also blamed the poor state of affairs in the third-tier of government to lack of monitoring, insisting that the councils needed to be monitored and made to discharge their responsibilities to the grassroots. "There has not been an integrated approach to the development of the rural areas. But this is important if we are going to develop the grassroots. The councils should be monitored and made to develop their areas. For instance, every council must rehabilitate or construct certain kilometers of roads over a period of time," Ezea said.
Speaking with reporters in Enugu, the former Deputy Superintendent of Customs expressed concern over what he described as the gloomy future for youths in Enugu State, saying the involvement of majority of them in criminal activities portends danger for the progress of the state.
He called for a re-orientation of the youths, pointing out that the present situation where many of them were being prepared by politicians to manipulate the coming elections is indicative of the social crisis the youths are facing.
Ezea expressed fears that Enugu might not be able to compete with other states in the country in the future if the youths were not integrated into the society through gainful employment and development. He pledged to pay attention to critical growth sectors of the state like agriculture, tourism, and manpower training and development, if elected as governor. The guber aspirant also promised to establish demonstration farms in the state, and encourage youths and university graduates to go into farming. On agriculture, Ezea said he would exploit the agricutural resources of the state by investing in agriculture, especially in cassava cultivation. which could be exported toarn foreign exchange for the state.

Original_Search_Title: New Budaka MP Calls for Unity
Headline: Uganda: New Budaka MP Calls for Unity
Body_Text:
Budaka — THE Budaka Woman Member of Parliament, Ms Pherry Kabanda Sabano, has unveiled plans to rejuvenate the district by promoting unity among the people.
The newly elected MP told Daily Monitor in an interview last Friday that the district is currently divided on political grounds, which is a hinderence to development.
"My task is to bring unity among the people of Budaka and counsel them to work together to realise development. It is hard to do this soon after elections, mostly for those who have supported candidates who may have lost," Kabanda said.
Kabanda also promised to fight for the girl child education, saying many girls have dropped out of school, some to work as housemaids to earn a living. She said parents still need to be sensitised on the importance of education.Kabanda said to avert the problem of food insecurity, which the district is usually prone to, she has started a cassava multiplication project to prevent cassava shortage, which is the major food crop in the district.
She urged women to form groups under which they can be trained on how to access credit and engage in productive ventures to help raise income by liasing with micro-finance institutions.
Kabanda promised to get in touch with the private sector enterprise development and the Uganda Small Scale Industries Association to train youth in the district on enterprise development to improve their capacity to produce and market goods effectively.
On the issue of water shortage, Kabanda pledged to discuss the matter with the Minister of State for Water, Ms Jennipher Namuyangu, to drill more boreholes and protect some wells.
She said she would take the responsibility to ensure that each sub county gets a health centre with drugs.

Original_Search_Title: Firewood Scarcity Affects Health of Tororo Children
Headline: Uganda: Firewood Scarcity Affects Health of Tororo Children
Body_Text:
Kampala — FIFTY year-old Alice Etyang of Alupe village in Tororo, a mother of eight, considers herself lucky when she collects a small bundle of firewood in less than half a day.
She walks long distances in search for firewood, but many times returns with bundles of twigs and grass.
"It is easier to get food than firewood in this village," she says.
The last time she got firewood was more than a month ago. Sometimes she uses tobacco plants left in the garden after harvesting as an alternative "but this has a bad smell and spoils the aroma of the food."
Etyang says she uses maize stalks and dry cassava stems to cook, but this does not produce enough heat.
She is not alone. In fact, the situation has become worse in the area. Etyang says her neighbour was once dragged to an LC court for trespassing on a neighbours' garden to pick firewood.
She says in the past, people were free to collect firewood from people's gardens, but now the situation worsened, it has become an issue for the LCs to intervene and settle disputes. This is undermining the spirit of sharing resources in the village.
"There is also a case when someone cut a tree belonging to his neighbour and had to be pay," Etyang's son says.
Cooking food that take long to get ready, like, beans, has become history in Alupe and the surrounding villages.
As a result, children are being denied nutritious diets like beans that have for long been a cheap source of proteins.
"There are many cases where children suffer from stomachache because they are fed on the previous day's cold food," Etyang says.
The scarcity of firewood comes after years of felling of trees that are burnt into charcoal, which is the main source of money for some of the villagers.
They have turned to growing of tobacco for sale to the nearby border villages of Kenya. This too has has contributed to the firewood scarcity as large amounts of firewood are needed to dry the tobacco leaves.
Currently, there is a growing interest to plant trees. The residents are being motivated by Mahanga Environment Management Organisation (MAHEMO), a local NGO, that started working in the area recently. They gave out about 2,000 seedlings to the villagers to plant. Each homestead was given 15 seedlings.
The restoration of the vegetation cover would also shelter the soil from being eroded and deposited into the nearby Malaba river. It flows from parts of Mt. Elgon in Manafa district, but its water becomes dark brown, an indicator that it is heavily silted as it winds through Tororo and Busia on its way to Lake Victoria, where it deposits the soil.
"This undermines the aquatic life including fish," says Emmanuel Oketcho, an environmentalist working with MAHEMO.
Oketcho says the banks of the river used to be covered by reeds and papyrus, but disappeared due to repeated cultivation.
"The soil has become barren in many parts of the district and locals consider the river banks fertile," he says.
Oketcho says once the ground is left bare, massive erosion would set in destroying the lakes and undermining fishing, which is the main business for many fishermen.
Under MAHEMO's activities, planting fruit trees is being promoted to persuade communities to give in part of the land near the river and protect it from being eroded. But planting trees is only part of the solution, according to Jane Nabuunya, the national project coordinator of the Confidence Building and Stakeholder involvement, under the Nile Basin Initiative.
She says the health extension services have also crumbled and there is no longer awareness, saying disease and poverty are manifestations that the countryside is lacking services.
MAHEMO's activities are funded by the Nile Transboundary Environment Action Plan, which is one of the undertakings of the NBI. However, the seeds are not enough. Etyang says the village is big and, therefore, needs more trees to cover the entire landscape.
Etyang says she has planted all the trees she was given. "I want you tell the government that we need more awareness on tree-planting and income generation."

Original_Search_Title: Benue 2007 - Idomas Have Date With History - Hon. Idoko
Headline: Nigeria: Benue 2007 - Idomas Have Date With History - Hon. Idoko
Body_Text:
Otukpa — Hon. David Idoko is a Federal lawmaker. He represents Ado/Okpokwu/ Ogbadibo Federal Constituency in the House of Representatives. In this interview with SIMON IMOBO-TSWAM in Otukpa, the headquarters of Ogbadibo Local Government Area, Hon. Idoko opened up on his re-election bid, his support for the 2007 Idoma governorship project, his stewardship in Abuja and the poor performance of council chairmen in the state. EXCERPTS:
Sir, the general assumption is that incumbents are interested in second term. So are you too going for a second term?
Yes, I want to say I am very interested. To the extent that it is a constitutional right, I am.
Do you seek this re-election strictly on the basis that it is your constitutional right as an incumbent?
No, it is not just because as an incumbent, I see re-election as merely a constitutional right. On the contrary, I consider re-election a bonus; a privilege. In the last elections, many wanted to return to the National Assembly, but could not. So outside what you call my constitutional right to aspire for a second term, I seek it mainly because I have performed fairly well. And this is not sounding immodest; neither is this my opinion. This is the consensus opinion of journalists who cover the National Assembly. Many papers, have adjudged my performance on the floor of the house to be far above average. So to the extent that I am adjudged to have acquitted myself excellently as a federal lawmaker, I want to say: yes, I am very interested in seeking a second term.
As I have said, when I first went there in 2003, I didn't know which committees would enable me to make more impact or would enable me to attract more dividends to my constituency. And the things I attracted into the federal budget I did not know how best to follow them up.
Another thing, in the first year, the budget implementation was around 60 per cent , 65 per cent in the second year and higher in the third year. In this fourth year, the hope is that the percentage of implementation would be even higher. If it is, then the projects I have attracted to my constituency, comprising Ado / Okpokwu /Ogbadibo Federal constituency, would be more felt.
Could you mention some of these projects you keep referring to?
There are over 30 water projects in my constituency. But of particular note is the Otukpa water supply project in Ogbadibo, costing over N21m. There is the Erodo rural electrification project. The holes have been dug, in some cases, the poles have been put in place. Before the year ends possibly, the people may have light. There is also a health clinic at Ado. There are other projects at Ikpogo, Opialu, Ola-Idu Ojigo, Ogodumu, Eke-Okposu, Omlokpo-Eke, all valued at between N13m to 41m that I have been able to attract into the budget for my constituency. So in representation, I have done well, or, rather, I have been adjudged to have done well for my people. And it is not only the press; the people are in agreement too.
For instance, I have presented petitions of five of my constituents on the floor of the House; three of the petitions have been treated and two are receiving due attention. Of the three, one has been returned to his job, another, a police inspector, in fact, got favourable judgement and is awaiting the execution of the judgment and yet another, with a 20-year old gratuity problem, his brother is an SSS man, has been solved. Then, God has used me to help people here and there. I have given notes to organizations with a view to helping my unemployed constituents, particularly the ones with university certificates, to get jobs. Some organizations obliged or promised; some did not for various reasons. My office has also been very helpful to constituents who lost their homes in Abuja through the on-going demolitions. I have provided some with financial assistance to relocate to safer places with their families and/or belongings. And in other cases, I have personally taken part in helping them to relocate.
In spite of your busy schedule?
O yes, they put me there. Their votes put me there in that office, so their problems too should concern my office. Yes, you can say I am busy, but I am never too busy for my people.
And I have also assisted them to access loans that is, agric loans via cooperative societies. Some youths from my constituency, but based in Abuja, came to me and I helped them access a N2.5m facility, the cassava sellers secured a N1m loan also through my assistance. I tried to venture into rendering financial assistance to indigent students; but decided to stop this year because of arising complications. This was outside my purview, I was using my earnings, but people thought I had collected money for scholarships. People would come to me: students or parents, and accuse me of bias, of being selective. So I have suspended it. When I leave office, I will continue with it under an educational foundation, possibly named after one of my mentors in life.
You politicians sometimes say: the more, the merrier. From the different posters one sees around, it seems some people want to displace or replace you in Abuja. Are you worried?
Worried? What for? I am rather challenged to better market myself to the electorate. If you are on a seat, and others don't want it or wish for it, it is not a good enough seat. Aspiring for any office is a constitutional right. The choice is for the people to make. If my constituency feels that another person's presence at the National Assembly will serve their interests better, that is their prerogative. Again, if they feel that my score-card is averagely impressive, that I have lived up to the challenge of effective, responsible and responsive representation, then I do not only intend to present my humble self before them, I equally intend to beg to be given a second chance. And with all sense of responsibility, I want to say: if you are a new person, you will queue up; you will have to acquaint yourself first with the rudiments of law-making, net-working, collective bargaining etc before settling down to the proper legislative business/over-sight functions. It is thes e that put me in solid advantage. This advantage is my cutting edge, and as I go back to them, I will be putting it before the electorate.
One hears that zoning is going to be introduced. What ....
(Interrupts ) When I contested, it was not zoned. So we can't change the rules midway - let us all have a level playing field. In 2003, democracy was given a chance; I believe in 2007 too, democracy will still be allowed to have its course. I contested against Hon. Sam Obande, Hon. Donny Roberts. Even though the competition was democratically competitive, the people chose me. And let me say, in the entire nine local governments that constitute Zone C, my victory was the only victory that was accepted in good faith and not challenged in any tribunal.
The current political issue in the state is: power-shift. Your voice is no longer being heard on the issue. Is it because you are rather too pre-occupied with your election project or is it that you have come to see it as impracticable?
If you knew me to be vocal on the power shift issue, specifically the Idoma governorship project, I want to assure you that I am still vocal. I have not changed or shifted grounds one inch. I have supported it on the floor of the House of Representatives, in the national and the local press, through interactions at different functions within and outside the state. But as I have always said, we can negotiate it: we can reach out to our Tiv brothers, tell them of our longing, our quest, develop an understanding with them; let them know our quest is simply to foster unity, to create in us a sense of belonging and that of a common identity. Is it impracticable? I say, no. Is it practicable? I say very much so. After all, someone has defined politics as the art of the possible. I believe come 2007, Idomas have a date with history. So I say let us all sit down and have a proper, fruitful and consensus-reaching dialogue on this matter. And I think, the earlier we do this, the better for us as a party and people. With the INEC guidelines out, I believe time is of the essence.
From going round the country, would you say Benue is at par with the country in terms of development?
I want to say it depends on the parameters one is using. If it is education, Benue has done very well. The infrastructural development at the Benue State University, for instance, is wonderful. I am talking of the College of Health Sciences and the Medical School projects particularly. The University of Mkar, Gboko, has taken off and the application for Apa University is being processed. So in educational development, no one can reasonably fault the Akume administration.
But having said that, let me also say there is a disjoint in infrastructural development. If you compare what councils get, for instance, and what is on the ground, you will know what I am talking about. I believe most chairmen have not pulled their weights well. Some keep mouthing projects that have been on-going since 1999. I ask: at what cost and time? We genuinely desire development in our councils, but most chairmen are just doing cash flow; they are operating without budgets. They just wait for allocation, do cash flow and wait for another month's allocation.
Any advice?
I would suggest that community leaders and elders, may be traditional rulers, look at the budget proposals and budget performance of council chairmen - how projects are conceived, distributed and executed. Once a chairman has made a budget, he should adhere to it, that's due process. At the moment, it does not obtain in the various councils. And sometimes, companies win multi-million Naira contracts without resumes, showing performance history, technical competence or even operational addresses. May be as democracy matures, these things will change, and people will begin to see legislators -whether they are councilors or representatives or senators - as part of government and not as irritating or noisy appendages.

Original_Search_Title: Bread. . . Any Alternative to Wheat?
Headline: Nigeria: Bread. . . Any Alternative to Wheat?
Body_Text:
Lagos — When President Olusegun Obasanjo visited the Federal Institute of Industrial Research (FIIRO), Oshodi in April 2004, he was very impressed when he was briefed about the FIIRO's research success on cassava-wheat composite bread making.
He saw the product on display, thinking that the nation will save not less than N5 billion in foreign earning if the nation can adopt a 10 per cent cassava and 90 per cent wheat composite bread, not only that, with the use of cassava flour for bread and confectioneries baking, huge post-harvest loses normally experienced by cassava farmers will be reduced and also lead to increase in cassava output due to ready market, thereby enhancing farmer's income. With this, more jobs will also be created and the nation will be better in terms of food security.
An Impressed President subsequently summoned the meeting of stakeholders for Abuja where he directed FIIRO, NAFDAC, SON, and the Federal Ministry of Justice to work on the project while Justice Ministry will work out the legal backing. At the meeting FIIRO was mandated to carry out the demonstration of the composite bread making technology in all the states of the federation in order to convince the doubting flour millers association, bakers, cassava growers and other stakeholders on the workability of the cassava-wheat composite bread making technology.
FIIRO's Director- General, Dr Oluwole Olatunji explained to THISDAY that in compliance with the directives of the President, the demonstration was carried out at the institute's baking laboratory, Oshodi in the presence of all the stakeholders. "The stakeholders were surprised about the success of the project. At this point, they were convinced beyond reasonable doubt that it is possible to produce cassava-wheat composite bread on commercial quantity using FIIRO's recipe and common bakery equipment."
According to Olatunji, to further convince the Nigeria populace FIIRO had to embark on a nation wide demonstration exercise which was funded by FIIRO, while state governments were asked to contribute N500,000 each for the purchase of raw materials needed for demonstration, promotional documents and the mobilisation of resource persons. RMRDC also contributed a sum of N9 million to finance project vehicle and other infrastructural facilities for high quality cassava flour production and other logistics
"The objective of the consumer acceptability study was to determine the acceptability of the new product among the general populace." THISDAY also learnt that successful demonstrations were carried out in Akwa-Ibom, Rivers and Oyo States. "Other states that signified interest, but yet to meet the financial pre-requisites are Lagos, Osun, Ogun, Kwara, Ekiti, Adamawa, Ondo, Kebbi, Anambra, Nasarawa, Abia and FCT. Once this is done the demonstration will be carried out," Olatunji said
He also disclosed that the success of the cassava-wheat flour composite bread production depends on adequate supply of High Quality Cassava Flour [HQCF] "FIIRO had developed a process technology for production of HQCF and a pilot plant for its production had been in place many years ago. The Institute is also working with indigenous fabricators to mass produce cassava flour equipment to make them readily available and affordable for investors. The Institute has to date also trained about 120 individuals on how to produce the cassava-wheat composite flour," he said.
Listing the advantages of the project, Olatunji said that apart from the fact that Nigeria will save about N5 billion in foreign exchange, the production will open business for local equipment fabricators thereby resulting in the development of local engineering fabrication industry to supply needed such as dryer, hammer, mill grater, hydraulic press, mash stirrer and cake granulator apart from the capacity building in the sector.
Olatunji further explained that the "production of cassava and other cereals flours for substitution in the bakery industry would translate to greater level of production of cassava tubers whose annual production output is currently estimated at 41 million tonnes. With the ready market to absorb cassava, it is fast becoming gold among other agricultural commodities.
"The economic gains, the socio-economic benefits and the overall gain to the economy from substitution of wheat flour with cassava flour will be quite enormous and unquantifiable. Aside from the direct gains and benefits there would be a great multiplier effect on the economy as a whole," he added.
Olatunji however expressed happiness that the cassava-wheat composite bread project technology had received acceptance and tremendous support from the stakeholders mainly flour millers, cassava producers and the bakers.
"To further confirm the acceptability, the flour millers association of Nigeria recently signed a memorandum of understanding with the Bank of Industry to float N500 million funds to boost the production of cassava," he disclosed.
However, the Flour Millers Association of Nigeria (FMAN) said that 300, 000 metric tonnes of high quality cassava flour will be required per annum in order to meet the local demands.
According to the association, this demand could only be met with efficient dryer that could process cassava into unfermented, high grade flour from grated and pressed cassava mash within 24 hours after harvest, thus calling on FIIRO, IITA and other relevant government agencies to support the Cassava Equipment Fabricators Association of Nigeria (CEFAN) to develop an efficient and cost effective made-in-Nigeria flash dryer for the production of high quality cassava flour for the baking industry.
The call was made at the meeting of the presidential taskforce on the implementation of the national policy on 10 per cent of cassava flour into bread with stakeholders at IITA, and chaired by the Special Assistant to the President on Food Security, Oluwatoyin Adetunji.
In view of the call and the huge cost of imported dryers, a locally developed flash dryer made with stainless steel fixed with a durable burner fuelled by diesel, kerosene, or used engine oil was considered a better option.
Addressing the participants at the meeting, IITA Food Technologist/Post Harvest specialist, Lateef Sanni, remarked that more than 300 units of flash dryers with capacity for 3-5 tonnes per day are required in Nigeria to meet current demands of high grade. According to him, there is the need for uniformity of flash dryer in terms of equality and quantity. He called for openness among the manufacturers so that they can borrow from each others expertise in the production of a standard, efficient and acceptable flash dryer which could be mass produced for the success of the industry.
Sanni also assured CEFAN members that IITA was willing to support this initiative by bringing into the country any required components of the machine that are not readily available in Nigeria. To boost production of cassava and high grade flour, the milling sector recently established a N500 million fund to support Nigerian cassava growers, processors and equipment fabricators.
Announcing this at the meeting, Yemi Ogunyemi, of the Flour Mills of Nigeria Plc said the fund was being administered by the Bank of Industry (BOI), and added that CEFAN and cassava processors should approach the bank to know how to benefit from the fund.
Also the association of flour millers stated that they were not opposed to the concept of cassava blended bread, but wondered if their cassava flour requirement could be met. On their part, the Association of Master Bakers and Caterers of Nigeria praised the inward looking approach of the government and pledged their readiness to evolve a new baking technique to ensure a good formula for the composite wheat plus cassava flour that might be used.
The FIIRO DG however said that Nigerians stand to gain a lot if the implementation of the cassava project is pursued to the logical conclusion. He also praised Obasanjo for his support for the project, saying that the legacies of the Obasanjo's administration will go along way to lift Nigeria to greater social and economic height.
The composition of the cassava-wheat flour composite bread and confectioneries are cassava flour, flavour wheat improver sugar, salt, butter,/fat, water, yeast and preservatives. The unit operations in the production of 10 per cent cassava bread are:
Mixing: The composite flour (10:90 cassava/wheat flour) and other ingredients as weighed are mixed in the mixing machine to obtain homogenous dough.
Milling: The dough obtained from the mixing is milled in a dough-milling machine.
Scaling: dough is weighed according to the required size. The weighed dough is put in the bread pans and are covered and stacked in a warm, place to allow proofing for sometime.
Baking: The proofed dough is transferred into an oven to bake at the required temperature before sent for cooling and later sliced with a machine before packaging and sealed.

Original_Search_Title: A Dark And Stormy Night
Headline: Malawi: A Dark And Stormy Night
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Nankapakapa drifted over a greying sea, its muttering diesel pushing us into the immense expanse of Lake Malawi. Our landing was a black hump crouching in the water, almost lost in the gathering dark. A faint point of light glimmered high up on the shore, the only sign that we were in fact heading for sanctuary from the dark waters of the lake which, in the hour-long crossing from Cape Maclear, had begun to froth and heave.
A mweru had begun blowing that afternoon, even as we were travelling slowly down from Lilongwe by bus. The wind was unexpected -- this wind from the south normally starts blowing only in the spring -- and the boatman made little attempt to hide his nervousness as we embarked on the crossing. "I think I will stay here tonight," he said, as the launch thumped into a protected bay on the southern edge of the island. By now the wind was roaring, hissing through the trees, and surf was pounding the shore. We agreed. It would have been madness to go back.
Later, staring into the dark towards Cape Maclear, I felt tiny and isolated. The wind thrummed on the tent canvas. The tent shook like a wet dog. The mainland was just 12km away but in weather like this it may as well have been another planet. And therein lies the contradiction of Lake Malawi and the few resorts that crouch on its shoreline. Remote and sometimes difficult to get to, there comes a pleasing realisation that travelling there demands commitment of a sort that makes the destination sweeter by many degrees.
There are few roads here, and even fewer airfields. Most travelling is done by boat, normally in boats like the solid, wood-hewn Nankapakapa, or lake steamer, and sometimes, for those who do not mind taking on the considerable fears of the unknown, by kayak.
It is strange to stand on the shore of this great lake, 560km long, 75km wide at its widest point and 706m deep at its deepest and think that all this fresh water is of little use in a country where subsistence agriculture is the rule and where peasant farmers live or die by the rains. Most of the lakeshore is unblemished by development -- certainly there are hardly any irrigation schemes. China, which has made no effort to hide its ambitions in Africa, is fiercely interested in Malawi. There will come a time when China's legions come to the lake for R&R. For now, though, it is a vast and empty shore.
The first stop on my journey was Mumbo Island, a chunk of forest-encrusted granite on which Kayak Africa has built a magnificent camp. Five safari tents perch on wooden, balconied platforms, under thatch roofs, and look out over the lake. The forest and jumbled boulders hide the tents from each other. Gravelled paths lead from the tents to middle of the outcrop where a bar and a couple of hammocks and some scattered tables hide under the trees.
Any enquiries about what there is to do will elicit a blunt, "not much" from people who've been on the island for a while. This is not strictly true -- a dive instructor is on hand to lead dives into the clear waters, and there are a couple of kayaks to explore the rock's bays and coves -- the point of Mumbo is to do nothing, but swing in a hammock and stare at the lake. We did make a couple of brief sorties in one of the double kayaks. But to be truthful, I was happier in the hammock, listening to the fish eagles crying over spilt fish. Occasionally, we swam in the bay, diving down to swallow mouthfuls of sweet water.
Breakfast, lunch and supper -- simple food -- rolled out of the kitchen at the appointed hour. The gas fridge seemed to be permanently full of Carlsberg -- "greens" in the Malawian vernacular -- no matter many how many I drank. The days began to merge into an unbroken thread of happiness, punctuated by temporary upheavals when I lost at bau, the perennial board game of central and east Africa. Every second day or so, Nankapakapa would thump over from Cape Maclear, bringing more Carlsberg, Malawian gin, milk, vegetables and a load of wide-eyed passengers, smitten at first sight of the island. We would trudge down to the beach, tanned, barefoot and shirtless, green in hand, to greet the new arrivals like wary castaways. A few days of this and return to the other life seemed pointless, insulting even.
On my second trip, I spent one night on Mumbo, then paddled across the lake in the company of other kayaks, firstly to the far Malawian shore on the lake's eastern side, and then along the coast to Mozambique. There are few things quite as daunting as leaving the safety of an island and pushing off into big seas in a little boat. The fear at leaving Mumbo behind was tempered by the fact that once on the other side, we would travel lightly along the Mozambican shore, camping wherever we found safe landing at the end of the day. Now I like camping but the thought of a proper bed with sheets was compelling and gave vigour to my paddling arms.
The first real bed was to be at Nkwichi Lodge, nine days after pushing from Mumbo. Our arrival on the day was magnificent. Rounding a rocky point, we saw for the first time in many days a stretch of fine sand, fronted by bush, a couple of baobabs and wild mango trees and at the far end, a jumble of granite boulders. "Nkwichi," said the guide.
"Where?"
"In the boulders." He pointed.
"Nah, you're kidding."
"In the boulders." He paddled on, leading us to the far end of the bay where we ground our kayaks up on the sand. A woman in a kikoi walked down to us. "Hi," she said. "We wondered when you'd get here."
We were led onto a deck hidden in the rocks and resuscitated with the inevitable greens, although on this side of the lake Laurentinamone, one of Mozambique's finer treasures, is available in abundance,
Nkwichi defines the grail of "low impact", which so many places try and so few get right. The suites, rondawels of wood, thatch, and reed, spread out behind the main house under wild mango and miombo trees.
Nkwichi, like the other lodges on the lake, is blessed with an abundance of nothing to do. The waters of the bay are warm and safe. There's decent snorkelling in the rocky coves, and an Indian canoe to go exploring in. A sailing dhow is on hand to take guests roaming up and down the coast.
Travelling on this part of the lake is like voyaging into an anthropological experiment. There are few roads here to serve the scattered villages and fishing camps, and people live here in almost lost time, relying on the lake for food and water. Agriculture, where it exists, is at a basic level, usually a couple of mealie fields, cassava crops and the like. If anyone wants to go anywhere they must either hitch a ride on a fishing dugout or hike to the nearest road, which means miles of weary trudgery on an embroidery of paths.
Our guide took us from the lodge to see a massive baobab. There are plenty of them on this coast -- unlike the hardwood forests, which have been eviscerated, the soft-wood baobabs have little commercial value and this giant has been left alone in splendid silence for 2 500 years.
The same could not be said for the surrounding wilderness area which, over the decades of Mozambique's war, has been plundered by armies, ivory hunters and starving povo.
This is starting to change as wildlife is reintroduced into the Manda Wilderness Area, a community-based game reserve project for which the lodge is the drawcard. The conservation area selected by local communities is approximately 100000ha, with 45km of lakeshore.
We could not stay to explore, however. It was early in the day and there was significant pressure to paddle on to Likoma Island, a little chunk of Malawi lying a few kilometres off the Mozambique coast.
Our destination was Kaya Mawa, the last of the great lake lodges, a gathering of stone huts cleverly built in and over the surrounding rocks. It is owned and run by a couple of Brit expats and has less of the appealing lostness of the other lodges.
The upside is that it is fairly easy to get to and Likoma Island is a fascinating place to roam around for a couple of days.
The coast is lined with baobabs and there are dozens of deep-blue bays, inhabited, it seems, only by curious schoolchildren playing in the sand. I never figured out why they weren't in school on a weekday, but the kids were friendly, like everybody else here.
Likoma is not quite comatose but it does feel as if the world sailed past one night and nobody here noticed. Like so much else in this strange land, it has its own surprises. The biggest of these is its cathedral, built almost entirely from imported bricks, iron, beams and stained-glass windows.
It's dedicated to Saint Peter, whose statue fittingly faces the lake that rules everything in these parts.

Original_Search_Title: 'ANPP Can't Go It Alone' - Gov. Aleiro
Headline: Nigeria: 'ANPP Can't Go It Alone' - Gov. Aleiro
Body_Text:
Kaduna — Governor of Kebbi State ALHAJI MUHAMMAD ADAMU ALIERO was very busy, holding a round of meetings, when our reporters arrived at his Asokoro Governor's Lodge for this interview. As chairman of the ANPP's Convention Planning Committee, he was hosting several meetings all at once, in different sections of the house, and only just managed to keep this interview appointment. He spoke on many issues, including the ANPP, his administration in Kebbi State and other issues.
The ANPP national convention has been shifted by a week. As chairman of the Convention Planning Committee, are you confident that the convention will hold this time around?
It is obvious that we are going to have our convention. Why we postponed it was simply because there are so many things that need to be done. In a number of states that we conducted congresses, we have complaints, petitions and appeals and we have not sat down in the convention planning committee to look into them. And we have not resolved some of the crises as a result of disputes during the state congresses. And we feel there is need for us to sit down, look at all these before we go into the convention. If we had gone into convention like that, we will end up having two executives committees of the party representing a particular state, and that would have sent wrong signals. So we really want to make sure that all contesting parties are attended to, disputes are resolved and where necessary, we conduct fresh congresses, if we are not able to resolve it amicably. So on the 1st and 2nd (of September) the ANPP convention will definitely hold, insha Allah.
