ANPP's former national chairman [Chief Don Etiebet] created a lot of rancor in the party and eventually fled to the PDP. How confident are you that the person that will take over now will not do the same thing?
Well, to start with, it's unfortunate and regrettable that (Don) Etiebet left ANPP, and it's more unfortunate and even lamentable for him to have gone into PDP. This virtually confirmed most of the allegations against him - that he was being used as a stooge by the PDP to destablise the ANPP. A man of integrity and respect should not allow himself to be used in this manner. Now, we are going into a proper convention. We will do everything possible to avoid the pitfalls of the past. Right now, all the leaders of ANPP are engaged in searching for a credible, responsible, incorruptible personality within the ANPP. Somebody that cannot be compromised; somebody that stands and says "no" and his "no" is no anywhere. We are looking for that candidate; we are searching in all the 36 states including Abuja. And God willing we will have that kind of person and we will make him our national chairman. And we are sure if we do that, he will not disappoint us like Etiebet disappointed us.
After your national chairman emerges, people will be looking forward to see the ANPP's presidential candidate. When will you hold your party's presidential primaries?
Right now we are looking towards November 2006 for us to have the national convention where the ANPP presidential flagbearer will be nominated. The state congresses - where the governors, state and national assembly members, councilors and council chairman will also be nominated - will also hold around the same time. We want to make sure that other parties do not take advantage of our holding our convention earlier. The likely fall out we may have, we don't want other parties to take advantage of it. That is why we are shifting the date of our convention till November and possibly even December - we have not even decided on the date, but I can assure you that we will do it in such a way that we will try to reconcile all the candidates that are vying for the presidency, as well as those that are vying for the governorship at the state level. We will try to have a consensus so that we don't have to go into primaries where there will be problem of factions.
In 2003, ANPP lost two of the states that it controlled, and recently, two of your governors left for DPP and PDP. Is it likely that others will soon leave, maybe perhaps including Your Excellency?
(General laughter). Well, I have said it at several fora and let me repeat it now. I will be the last person to leave ANPP. Everything I am looking for in ANPP, I've gotten it, and I have no reason whatsoever to leave ANPP. I will be a betrayal, it will be a great disservice to my people if I leave ANPP. Recently, if you are familiar with politics of Kebbi State, you will see that so many people have decamped from PDP and other parties to ANPP. And some of them told me very clearly that look, Mr. Governor, if you know you are leaving your party, don't allow us to leave ours. We are joining you simply because we believe in your leadership. So for this reason, I assure you personally that I will not leave the ANPP. I cannot rule out the possibility of going into fusion with other parties, merger, coalitions - these are possible. But for me to leave ANPP, it will never happen. For those who have left ANPP, I can assure you we are doing everything possible to make sure we win tho se states. ANPP is not going to lose those states where the governors have decamped to other parties. We have put the machinery in motion to ensure that we win such states.
For sometime now, we have heard of the alliance talks between ANPP and other parties. Have these talks collapsed?
Gov Aliero: Not completely. Some of us disagreed with the national caretaker committee on the statement they made that they support merger, coalition and alliance but ANPP is not going to lose its name, colour and symbol. I think that's too arrogant. I am not criticizing the leadership of my party, but I am saying the obvious. I will appeal to the leadership of the party to reconsider its stance on this statement. There is no way ANPP can win the presidency of this country as the party is constituted today. We need to reach out, we need to get progressive forces across the country to come up and team with us in order to win national elections. If we remain where we are, quite honestly we will be consumed and we will become completely annihilated. So it is in the interest of the ANPP and all the teeming supporters nationwide to support merger, acquisition or coalition. There is no way we can do it alone.
Do you feel threatened that DPP's umbilical cord was cut in neighbouring Sokoto State and it may easily spill over into Kebbi State?
DPP is non-existent in Kebbi State, and even the major opposition parties in Kebbi State today, on daily basis, we are receiving decampees into ANPP. As far as we are concerned there's no party that's a threat to ANPP in Kebbi State. We are sure of wining elections in 2007. DPP only exists in Kebbi State on the pages of newspapers. So it's not a threat in any way. It's not even worth commenting on. I don't even want to be quoted commenting on DPP. If AD, Conscience Party, Action Congress can cause problem to ANPP in Kebbi State, then DPP can cause it.
EFCC has been going round the states and was recently in neighbouring Zamfara State. Are you jittery that EFCC may soon arrive in Kebbi?
They were in Kebbi before going to Zamfara, Sokoto and others. In fact I invited EFCC to Kebbi State. We had a serious case of fraud that went on for about four and a half years before it was uncovered. We did our own investigation and we felt we needed a superior investigative body to conduct thorough and unbiased investigations. I also subjected myself to investigation, even though I have immunity, and I challenged all those that were accused to come forward with any evidence of what I did that was wrong and give it to the EFC. All those that served as commissioners of finance during that period were arrested and detained by EFCC. A substantial amount of money was recovered by the EFCC and given to the government of Kebbi state. Even the accountant general, permanent secretary, director of central account - these were the key figures that keep the funds of the government - they were all arrested. If there is any fraud committed by government, certainly it has to go through these offices. So I am not jittery that EFCC is in Zamfara or Sokoto or any other state. They are doing their statutory responsibility. And I support the war on corruption, only that it should be done with decency in such a way that there is no sacred cow. Whoever commits an offence should be investigated and punished. I don't believe that EFCC is being used as a political tool as is being said in some quarters.
You have been very busy of late establishing the Kebbi State University of Science and Technology. How far has the project gone and can you possibly conclude it before your tenure runs out?
A university cannot be constructed overnight. It has to be done over a long period of time. There are phases, and we are following these phases. Phase I would be the construction of the faculty buildings, accommodation for students and staff, senate building, lecture halls and I think the library. Essentially we are constructing buildings for two faculties and a library complex, hostel accommodation for students and accommodation for lecturers. I assure you that before the end of the year, we will be able to complete phase I, then phase II will start by next year. Whoever takes over from me will do phase III, until such a time when the university is finally completed. I agree we started it late, and it's a project that is worth doing. It's a project that people of the state count very much on and it's a project that will make the state educationally advanced. That's why we are pursuing it.
Why did you build an airport in Birnin Kebbi, a beautiful one, when there are no scheduled air services?
Well, you know that many airports constructed by the federal government did not have scheduled air services to begin with. Some still do not have such. There are certain things that need to be done - getting licence from the Aviation ministry, making arrangements with the airlines before they commence commercial flights. Mr. President has directed the Minister of Aviation to give a license to the airport to operate. And on several occasions I booked an appointment with the Minister but unfortunately, whenever I went there, they always told me that he was out. We have schedule officers that are pursuing this license and the moment it is obtained, we will start operating scheduled flights. We have contacted a number of airlines and they are interested in operating flights from Kebbi to Abuja, Lagos and Kano. I assure that you very soon, this will start.
There is this talk by some people in Birnin Kebbi that you built the airport because you had a quarrel with Governor Bafarawa of Sokoto, and he said you should stop flying through Sokoto airport. Is this true?
(long silence, then laughter). It sounds funny and ridiculous. I didn't have any quarrel with Governor Bafarawa. Quite alright, he is no longer in my party. But we often meet and talk. I was in Sokoto about four days ago. We entered the same plane, we sat down in the same plane, we discussed and dispersed. It is not true that I had a quarrel with Bafarawa. We were friends even before we came into ANPP, and we will continue to be friends. Politics will never separate us. To be honest with you, there is no way we can quarrel over such a small matter.
Does it worry you that former Emir of Gwandu Mustafa Jokolo has regained his freedom and may cause some political upset for you?
Repeat that question, please.
(Repeats the question).
No, I am not worried at all. I'm not perturbed. I am happy that he has regained his freedom, if at all he has regained his freedom. And as far as I am concerned, nobody has curtailed his freedom. He is a free citizen of Nigeria, free to move in any part of the country and he is not in any way a political threat to me or to the teeming supporters of my party in Kebbi State. As far as I am concerned, no individual can constitute a political threat to my political career. My fate is with God almighty, and only He can decide my fate, not an individual.
Do you communicate with him at all? Did you condole with him that armed robbers entered his house?
I'm just hearing it from you.
It's in the papers [pointing to a stack of newspapers on the table].
I haven't read the papers today. (General laughter).
What is your plan after you vacate the governor's office next year?
My next plan of action is to continue to play an active role in the politics of Kebbi State and the country at large. Once in politics, you will always remain in politics. I will do my very best to always play a positive role in the politics of this country, and to contribute in the economic development of Kebbi State.
Do you intend to contest for another post?
Oh yes, I may likely go for an elective position. I've not made up my mind, but most likely I will go for an elective position.
You have been governor for seven years now. Are your footprints heavy in the state?
I have made heavy imprints in all sectors. Let's start education. At the time we came in, we met the education sector in very serious crises - crises in the sense that there were no classrooms, no dormitories, teachers had deserted their posts. The major thing we had to do was to revive that sector. In 1998 - 99, the number of students that passed SSCE in Kebbi was not up to six per cent. Because of what we did in the sector, this number started rising from five, to ten, twenty, thirty, today we have up to about 80 per cent who passed SSCE. Last year, they said Kebbi state had the highest rate of examination malpractice. It surprised me. I did an investigation and discovered that it was only about 70 students, not even indigenes of Kebbi state, they came and registered here and were caught in examination malpractice. This year, we emerged as the fourth highest in exams ethics, from the last position. This shows we have worked hard to eliminate exam malpractice in Kebbi. We bu ilt a number of schools, apart from the ones we rehabilitated, upgraded and improved. Over 100 schools have been constructed - day schools and boarding schools. I've spoken about the university. The College of Agriculture, College of Education and the Polytechnic, Birnin-Kebbi, all these institutions, at the time we came had their courses not accredited by the various accreditation bodies. We had to provide the necessary funds for their accreditation. We had to also improve on the structures we met. New ones were also constructed. We spent over N1billion to resuscitate the tertiary education sector in Kebbi state. A number of primary schools - well over 6, 000 classrooms - were constructed from 1999 to 2005. And this is still going on.
In the health sector, we rehabilitated all the eight general hospitals we inherited. We upgraded five health centres to general hospitals, we equipped all these hospitals with modern equipment. We established one of the best Schools of Nursing and Midwifery in the state. We also established a kidney dialysis centre at the Sir Yahaya Memorial Hospital. We established a VVF centre where many women are treated. We have one of the best eye centers established by my administration. They have operated on well over 5, 000 blind patients in collaboration with an NGO. A number of health centres have been constructed in the state. I can say that in virtually all the wards we have, we have heath centres constructed either singly by the state government or in collaboration with the local government. Some were constructed by the federal government.
Under rural development, we have provided electricity, pipe-borne water for rural dwellers and access roads. When we came in 1999, only 18 towns in the state were connected to the national grid. But today we have 518 that are connected to the national grid, which means we have connected about 500 towns and villages from 1999 to date. We drilled well over 2,500 boreholes with overhead tanks. We also improved the water supply in Birnin Kebbi, Zuru, Argungu, Yauri, Jega and Aliero. We have also embarked on urban water supply in Gwandu, Dirin Daji, Kangiwa, Kamba and Riba. These are towns that will have about one million litre capacity tanks and reticulation system of between 10 to 18 kilometers.
Under housing, about 1, 350 houses were constructed from 1999-2006. And more are under construction. In Birnin-Kebbi, we have constructed well over 50km of township roads and drainages.
In Argungu 10km were constructed, in Jega we had about 3km and an additional 5km would be constructed. In Aliero about 8km, Koko about 5km, Yauri and Zuru have 8km constructed respectively.
In Kebbi, we also constructed federal roads and asked for a refund. It doesn't matter whether we get the refund or not. The important thing is that these roads are in Kebbi State and nobody can take them elsewhere. I can remember the Kebbi-Jega road, a distance of only about 38km. Back in 1999, people often spent more than 2 hours on this road. Mr. President himself was in Kebbi in 2000AD, and I decided to take him to that road to see how bad it was. And because of the bad condition of that road, he had a flat tyre, and the security people were very annoyed. After seeing it, the President gave me the go-ahead to do the rehabilitation. I did it, and now the road is one of the best in the country. It is now six years since its rehabilitation, but there is not a single pothole. And from the way it was constructed, it will last for another 10 years. It is the same story with so many other roads. We embarked on rehabilitation of these roads. We have done Gulumbe-Aliero road, about 40km; we have done Saran Dosa-Karaye-Suru road, about 32km; we have done Natsini-Augie road, about 25km; we have done Wasagu-Bena road, completed up to Niger State, about 50km. Presently we have the following roads under construction: Zuru-Mahuta-Koko road, about 70km, Bunza-Dakin Gari-Kende road 75km, Kaoje-Lolo, about 125km, Koko-Besse, almost completed, Yauri-Zamare, completed but not commissioned, about 25km.
Now let me go to agriculture. Over 85% of our people rely on agriculture. Every year we procure large consignments of fertilizer and distribute to farmers at subsidized prices. In addition to that, from 1999 to 2006 we bought over 500 tractors and gave them to cooperative associations in Kebbi State. We buy these tractors at about N6 million and we give them to farmers at N3.5million. Bulls and ploughs were also bought and distributed to farmers. We give them time to pay. We also distributed over 150,000 irrigation pumps for dry season farming. Today we have over 50,000metric tons of various grains in warehouses. We do sell to the public at subsidized prices, especially when the prices group. We bring them out to stabilize the prices. When we give input to farmers, we don't insist on getting money from them. We ask them to bring their produce to pay in kind. Very briefly, these are some of the areas in which we left indelible footprints.
Where are those Chinese farmers that you brought to the Yauri area?
The commercial farmers from China are operating in Wara, and they are engaged in livestock ands rice production. They are providing employment to over 2, 000 people in that area. It's a pilot project, they are trying to expand it to other local governments, particularly where we have large rivers. We are also going to engage in ethanol production through the use of cassava. We have signed a memorandum of understanding with Chinese investors to establish an ethanol plant that will cost $90 million. It's a joint project between the state government, NPC and the Chinese investors. Commercial farming in Kebbi state has come to stay.
You are a commercial farmer yourself, Your Excellency. I drank some milk from your farm at Kado here.
We are operating on a very small scale, but if this N50billion farming loan scheme [of the Federal Government] becomes to reality, I will take like N250 million [loan] and improve on that farm.
More than two years ago, your neighbour the Governor of Zamfara publicly nominated his successor. Who is your own anointed successor?
Probably it is a bit premature now to appoint a successor. In any case, successor is not by appointment, if democracy is what we are practicing. It should be left to the people to decide. Just like it was the decision of Kebbi people to elect me in 1999, I want the same people to say we want Mr. A, B, C or D to be my successor. It will be wrong for me to nominate or to appoint a successor. That will go against the wish of the people. I will rather create a level playing field for all to come and indicate their interest. Once my party decides, I will do my very best to campaign to make sure that that man so nominated succeeds in the governorship election in 2007.
You sound as if you are disinterested in the matter of a successor. Is it really possible for an incumbent to allow for a real level playing field in the succession battle?
Yeah, it is possible. I don't believe in imposition. I don't say I am not interested, certainly I am interested. But there are certain formalities that need to be followed even if I want to indicate my interest. I want to see where the wind is blowing before I can follow it. I don't want to go and bring one person. If I do that, I may end up getting some boycott, and that will lead to protest votes. And I don't want that. Rather I am a democrat, I want to carry everybody along. I want to follow the principles of democracy.
What do you consider your most trying moment in office?
There is no such moment for me.
Even when there were crises, like the one at Jega?
Oh, if you are talking about that, yes. If you face disturbances like that of Jega, you will feel disturbed. A situation where human lives are involved, a situation where if you don't handle it properly, it will escalate - for that reason I can consider this a trying moment. Luckily we saved the situation. Because I didn't wait for the Commissioner of Police to come and give me a report. Immediately the council chairman informed me, I mobilized my convoy and we went to the crisis spot. We found houses being set on fire, people being beaten, properties being looted and we tried to control the situation. And our going there was very timely because we really saved a lot of lives.
We dispersed the few people that were involved in looting, we made some arrests, the people that needed help we took them to the hospitals, those that needed to be accommodated, we provided temporary accommodation to them. And above all we appealed to the communities and the non-indigenous people, we preached peace, and within one or two days, the situation was brought under control. We did not stop there. Even churches that were burnt, we had to reconstruct them. And probably its one of the few crises in the North where a governor took the initiative of constructing churches that were burnt or demolished as a result of sectarian crises. That sent a signal to the people that government doesn't condone religious disturbances in the state, that we regard all believers as equal, regardless of where you come from. From that moment we said bye-bye to religious crises in Kebbi state.
Do you have any time at all for relaxation, or all you do is governance and politics?
Yes, there is time for everything. I do some exercise. Because there is no way you will stay in office for 10 hours, 12 hours and then be healthy without some exercise. I do go for jogging, I do play squash. Atimes I drive for long distances. I drive myself to my farm alone, and it gives me peace, it gives me satisfaction. Infact I even forgo any game to walk in my farm for about one and a half hours and I become satisfied. This is how I relax.

Original_Search_Title: Benue And Akwaya's Notion of Development
Headline: Nigeria: Benue And Akwaya's Notion of Development
Body_Text:
Makurdi — If we go by the writings of prominent authors such as Lucyian Pye (1969) and several others we would agree that development has several angles to it such as the human aspect, infrastructure and institutional. Whenever I am writing on George Akume and the administration which he heads, I often segment his developmental strides in the following perspective: human development, infrastructure, agriculture, education, and industrialization.
By my nature I take strong reservation to joining issues with writers who out of excitement draw conclusions on issues without cross-checking their facts particularly, reacting to prejudiced write - ups that seek to promote mediocrity and ignorance about the issues at stake. my apathy to rejoinders is also borne out of the fact that it draws unnecessary attention to a myopic writer with a mind set to distort facts and mis-inform the public just because of his of her privileged position in a newspaper house, which he or she utilize to achieve personal goals.
I am constrained to respond to Mr. Onah Iduh's piece entitled" Benue and Akwaya's notion of Development". Which appeared in the Weekly Trust of 24th - 30th July 2006. This is because even the previous write - up: "Benue State and the failure of leadership" which appeared at the back page of Weekly Trust of 9 July, 2006 which informed Akwaya's rejoinder to say the least was an exhibition of high-level ignorance, deliberate distortion of facts and a display of a vicious mind set at ethnicism. It is indeed a pity that most Nigerians in the 21st century rather than building on issues would prefer to delve into pre-colonial ethnic divide as a subject of analysis when they should be concerned with the nature of the class system that has so much permeated the fabric of the polity to warrant the state in which our society may have found itself.
To begin with Benue State was created over 30 years ago and has passed through more than 15 governors. Most of the previous governors were however, military personnel as such they are never reckoned with when it comes to talking about assessing them. Nevertheless, the point has to be made that George Akume did not give birth to Benue as others have also occupied the seat he is now occupying and we can only appreciate his failure or successes when we look at the history of the state and the antecedent of its past leaders vis-à-vis what George Akume has been able to put on ground.
When one reflects on the concept of development which is the subject of Iduh's hypothetical analysis some of which he claims have element of empiricism. One finds it difficult to situate the opic-centre of his paradigm and focus. For one Iduh has copiously quoted from renowned figures like Daniel Patrick Moynihan, a former Senator of the USA Senate and a renowned professor of political science Okello Oculi but he, either by default or design left the main issue at stake hanging.
Iduh's two skewed write-ups did not attempt an analysis of what constitutes development. Herein lies the paradox of writing with a mindset that seeks to destroy rather than inform and document for posterity. One may ask Mr. Iduh what he means by development. Is development all about skycrapers and infrastructure? On the other hand, is development a dynamic and encompassing paradim?
If we go by the writings of prominent authors such as Lucyian Pye (1969) and several others we would agree that development has several angles to it such as the human aspect, infrastructure and institutional.
Whenever I am writing on George Akume and the administration which he heads, I often segment his developmental strides in the following perspective: human development, infrastructure, agriculture, education, and industrialization.
If we take a critical look at the policy of the George Akume administration you will agree that given his background as a sociologist with his hindsight for the history of the people and their poverty stricken nature, Akume has never lost sight of the fact that he needs to strike a balance between the needs of the people rather than engaging in deceitful venture that may not have direct bearing on the lives of the people. In this perspective, he has ensured regular payment of salaries including teachers, civil servants and those in institutions of higher learning. This policy has allowed for continuity in learning and research. It has prevented strike action by civil servants and teachers thus creating a conducive atmosphere for development of man and his environment. Again, he has also increased the scholarship of Benue indigenes schooling in higher institutions from N2000 to N5000 and has made scholarship automatic. Liberalization of contract award to the people has also en hanced the living condition of the people. Today in Benue State the squalor that had been the bane of the people is fast diminishing as people are constructively engaged in agriculture, small scale businesses and being qualitatively educated. If one may ask, could it have been possible for a state like Benue to have had its university that is state owned fully accredited if not with the support of a governor who has shown commitment to its progress and development? Similarly, why did the Nigerian University Commission rate the V.C of Benue State University the best and its council the best of all state universities. Are these not positive development? Also, in 2006 the Joint Admission and Matriculation Examination, result recently released, out of the over 817,000 candidates who sat for the examination, a Benue candidate emerged the best and even made history as the first in the North. Today, Benue State is blessed with four senior advocates in the legal profession. Before t he advent of the George Akume administration, there was no single senior advocate, which points to the fact that we are developing.
In the area of road development, I would like to put on record that between 1992 to 1999 there was no single road in Benue State that was graded not to talk of tarring yet, Iduh would not comment on this and ignorantly joined issues with a leadership that within seven years of its tenure tarred about 18 roads in Makurdi four in Gboko, four in Oturkpo ans is currently working 417 kilometers of road's that cut across the state.
At the level of industrialization, it may interest Iduh to note that since the creation of Benue State more than 30 years ago, the first visible development at the industrial site came about with the advent of the George Akume administration, which has built the first cassava processing factory in Nigeria, fruit juice, Benkims plastic, fertilizer blending plant, while private individuals have also put in place six new factories for bottle making, rice processing, water, bene-seed processing and others. Akume has provided electricity and water at this layout. Similarly, work is in progress at the Igumale cement factor while feasibility studies in Mbatiav cement factory is expected soon. Other industries in the making are: Ethanol plant, tomato pury and mango juice, sugar cane production and K/Ala fruit juice.
In the area of health, the Benue State University Teaching Hospital adjudged one of the best in West Africa will soon be completed. The college of Health Sciences has been completed. It may interest Onah Iduh to note that it took the federal government over 40 years to put in place the ABU Teaching Hospital while the University of Jos's Teaching Hospital is still operating at the old General Hospital. When you compare the number of years and amount that it took the federal government to build and provide these facilities mentioned with what Akume has done within a short period, you can only appreciate him for his efforts.
It may interest Iduh to note that there are three tiers of Government - Federal, State and Local Government. Each of these tiers has its role to play in the development of Benue State. In a democracy, each of the tiers is independent. Akume cannot coerce or force the Federal Government into carrying out its obligations to the state. The same applies to the Local Government Areas. The much he an do is to utilize the available resources at his disposal for the development of the state.
In conclusion, and for lack of space I am limiting my response here but would want to challenge Mr. Onah Iduh to prove me wrong. As an indigene of Benue State, I think I am a major stakeholder in the state. Benue people should learn to encourage their leaders by being objective in their assessment of events in the state. Having worked and stayed in Benue state most of my life, I can claim knowledge of the state and its development. One thing is certain, Benue's development is a collective approach but not the preserve of the government in power alone. If I may ask, with all the high number of rich Benue indigenes how many have come to invest in the state? The paradox of Benue State and its development is that the indigene prefer to invest outside the state but would come to seek the people's votes at election time. Iduh may not understand the economic of modern development, which is private sector driven. Government only provides the enabling environment for business to thriv e. Iduh should appeal to Benue indigenes in high places and those with wealth to invest back home. His inability to suggest ways and means by which Benue people would abandon their self defeatist tendencies and rise up to the several challenges, which the Akume leadership is throwing up for Benue people is in itself retrogressive, reactionary and antithetical to a progressive mind and societal development. Iduh is enslaved to cultural parochialism and a victim of his mindset. If Iduh is surprised at Akume's rising popularity, he should seek closer contact with him to unravel the mysteries behind the man's popularity.

Original_Search_Title: CBN Seminar: Participants Discuss How Foreign Reserves Should Be Utilised
Headline: Nigeria: CBN Seminar: Participants Discuss How Foreign Reserves Should Be Utilised
Body_Text:
Kaduna — The Central Bank of Nigeria organised a three day seminar on the dynamics of exchange rate management. The various resource persons argued for the effective utilisation of the current foreign reserve accumulation to launch Nigeria development agenda.
Participants at a 3-day seminar on the dynamics of managing exchange rate in Nigeria have profered workable solution on how the nation's foreign reserve should be proparly utilised to achieve maximum development for the country. Resource persons were drawn from CBN, the University community and international organisation.
The conference which held between 28-30 August 2006 had finance correspondents, stakeholders in the finance sector and resource person as participants Alhaji Mohammed Nda, CBN director of Foreign Operation presented a lead paper on the challenge of effective foreign reserve management. He defines foreign reserve as "consisting of official public sector foreign assets that are readily available to and controlled by the monetary authorities for direct financing of payment imbalances and regulation of the magnitude of such imbalances through intervention in the exchange markets to effect the currency exchange rates and for other purpose. He explained that "The accumulation of reserves is a global phenomenon and not peculiar to Nigeria. Global official reserves increased from about $2 trillion at the end of 2002 to over $4 trillion in January, 2006, with emerging and developing countries accumulating the bulk of the world's foreign reserve.
"The rise in oil prices has now shifted foreign reserve accumulation from Asia (excepting China and India) to the oil producing countries. Many of these countries are accumulating reserves on account of the lessons they learnt during the various financial market crises of the 1990's. They have continued to build reserves to shield them from volatile capital movements."
He noted that Nigeria, like other oil producing countries, has accumulated reserves since 2004 following the upsurge in oil prices occasioned by the general tension in the Middle East, regional conflicts, and increasing global demands for oil, especially from China. Nigeria's current external reserves had reached the unprecedented level of $38.07 billion by July 31, 2006. The big challenge before us is how to manage the phenomenal increase in our foreign reserve that we don't feel the impact when the prices come crashing, as they surely will. How do we manage the investment of this reserve in our microeconomic sectors to diversify the economy?
Dr. Ayo Odusola, UNDP Senior economist argued that the accumulation in the foreign reserve has enable the CBN to harmonise the official exchange rate and the parallel market. He noted that the major lesson we can learn from Malaysia and Mexico in 1998 and 2002 is that we have to be careful the way we handle our capital account. He explained that you don't just open your capital account to all and sundry. It must be a capital account that is what we called guided by the policy directive in terms of agenda of government. He said no country ever leaves it free like that and think it will impact positively on the economy. he further clarified that leaving the capital account open has ripple effect that dislocate the whole economy from getting its own bearing for several years because that exactly what happened in Mexico for several years before they could relocate themselves."
Professor Mike Obadan, of university of Benin in his reaction said that the deregulation of the economy that started with SAP targeted the diversification of the oil sector. That the deregulation since 1985 has failed to achieved the set target of generating 1.0billion dollar from non-oil sector. The challenge of the current accumulation is to see how we can diversify the economy to generate revenue and foreign exchange from the non-oil sector that still remain untapped. That governement has started with cassava which is in serious demand in India and China. That there is the need to focus on other sector. Malaysia has oil but they de-emphasise it and concentrate on real production and manufacturing.
Alhaji Nda observed that Nigeria's dependence on oil for over 90 percent of its foreign exchange earnings, which makes its capital account vulnerable to the fluctuations in crude oil prices, poses a lot of challenges for the management of the country's economy. Nigeria's high level import bills have contributed to the fluctuation of our foreign reserves over the years and consequently rendered its management more difficult.
Certain important measures could facilitate the effective management of our huge accumulation of foreign reserve, the most important of which is the enactment of the Fiscal Responsibility Act to provide for prudent management of the reserve and ensure long-term macroeconomic stability and greater accountability and transparency in fiscal operations within a medium-term fiscal policy framework.
Tied to the issue of fiscal responsibility is the recent call for the establishment of a national investment fund. It is an open secret that the recent build-up in the nation's external reserve has mainly been due to unsustainable upsurges in oil prices, which are however already fuelling the search for alternative energy sources, especially in the USA. The national investment fund is intended to invest excess earnings from oil into projects that could shield the nation from the volatility of oil prices. Countries such as Botswana, Venezuela, Norway, Sao Tome and Principe, to name a few examples, have successfully established such funds. What these countries have done is to secure the proceeds from oil and other minerals and finance their budgets through other sources.
The national investment fund serves the twin objectives of stabilising volatile fiscal revenues and creating a capital pool to provide alternative sources of foreign exchange.
As part of its effort to manage our foreign reserve effectively, CBN has outsourced its component parts to external fund managers in line with the best practices worldwide. A recent survey by UBS Investment Bank showed that 70% of central banks around the world now use the services of external managers. A portion of the reserve would be invested in bonds, which yield higher returns than fixed deposits.
CBN has appointed J. P. Morgan Chase to take custody of all securities purchased by its external fund managers. The apex bank has also come up with the idea of allocating the external reserve to strategic assets, the aim of which is to take its risk tolerance into account in optimising long-term reforms of the reserve portfolio. Strategic asset allocation (SAA) would split the reserve into liquidity, investment, and stable tranches to reflect the capital preservation, liquidity, and return objectives of the bank's reserve management.
Foreign reserve is held by a country for a number of reasons. It supports and maintains confidence in the monetary policy and exchange rate management, including intervention in the foreign exchange market in support of the national currency, naira for example. This is evident from the relative stability of our foreign exchange market since 2004 following the historic merger of the wholesale Dutch auction system and bureau de change rates.
Foreign reserve also services the foreign currency liabilities and debt obligations of country. For instance Nigeria was able to pay the Paris Club $12.4 billion from its foreign reserve to secure a debt relief of $18 billion.
Foreign reserve finances government expenditures abroad, e.g. the import of goods and services for government programmes. It provides a defence against emergencies or disasters and could equally be invested in income generating instruments such as treasury bills, bonds, time deposits, etc.
Nigeria's foreign reserve has grown in leaps and bound in the past three years. The trend started in 2003 after the American invasion of Iraq, which resulted in the escalation of prices as Iraqi oil failed to reach the international markets. Some of the participants argued that the windfall of the 1990's cannot be said to have been judiciously utilised. The surge in oil prices pushes countries like the United States to search for alternatives to oil as their major source of energy. If the US succeeds, Nigeria could be in trouble because the US is our major oil trading partner.
During the oil boom of the mid-seventies which resulted in the build up of external reserves, these reserves were diversified into an array of financial instruments including foreign government bonds and treasury bills, foreign government guaranteed securities, special drawing rights (SDRs), fixed term deposits, call accounts and current accounts, providing significant investment income as well as liquidity. However during the glut in the global oil markets which led to the collapse of prices and consequently depleted the reserves, the reserves were largely held in current account and treasury bills. This underscores the need to diversify the sources of a country's foreign exchange inflow.
Other participants also argue that beyond this, however, the fiscal and budgetary violations of the executive arm of government must be treated with more seriousness.
In the last seven years we have witnessed increasing fiscal rascality in the way and manner that members of the executive arm of government handle the budget. This can no longer be condoned if we are to benefit in the long-run from the accumulation of foreign reserve.
In conclusion, the present level of our foreign reserve is a golden opportunity to launch our development agenda. Much as we appreciate CBN's strategic management however the needs of ordinary Nigerians dictate the translation of our excess funds into meaningful life. That boils down to the issue of diversification. We have noted the efforts with cassava, but a lot more must be done to ensure that a country as blessed as Nigeria does not depend on one single but volatile product.

Original_Search_Title: Some Labels That Define Our Cultures
Headline: Uganda: Some Labels That Define Our Cultures
Body_Text:
In the first part of a two series article, it is revealed that there are 24 million Ugandans, divided into 65 indigenous communities, according to the Constitution. Each of these groups, though have striking similarities, live a unique lifestyle. Diana Apio explores what defines these vibrant communities
The Karimojong
If the Europeans were to throw a big party to celebrate their triumph over our cultures, they would easily invite most of the tribes in Uganda - but not the Karimojong. Not even the cliché of 'we shall not wait for Karamoja to develop' seems to have any impact on their style and pace of life.
As evidenced by their determination to continue moving half nude even in this day and era where the fashion craze seems to be hitting from all corners, the Karimojong have continued to hang on to their tradition.
Countless strands of multi-coloured beads around the waists, necks and bangles and tattoo marks on the face is what still makes their women stand out. Sometimes, the front teeth are plucked off as a sign of beauty.
The men on the other hand wear attires that only cover the private parts.
Each one has a small traditional Karimojong stool, which he carries with him everywhere he goes. They are friendly, very proud and very organised socially with small groupings based on family ties (both blood and marriage bonds), clans, territorial groupings, generations and age brackets.
The elders assume leadership roles by virtue of their assumed wisdom while the young generation - warriors implement the elders' decisions. It is also through their various rituals and ceremonies that the Karimojong are united. The elders are also respected as priests and likened to the divine Akuj whom they look at as the all powerful.
Wheras money is the source of pride and wealth in many other cultures, the Karimojong ideally worship cattle. To have as many cattle as possible is their ultimate goal. Such is the determination that they do not even mind raiding other areas to get the cattle. To them, all cattle belongs to them and they must get it at all cost. Their main activity therefore is cattle herding, which also explains their strong attachment to milk and beef.
A full Karimojong meal entails milk, blood, butter and meat. Special dishes are made by women for their husbands and lovers such as Emiuna, angodiny, aking etc. There is the common tale that a man has to wrestle his wife to-be to prove his manhood before being allowed to take her away. This is, however, is not very common today, but also, you do not just marry from any clan.
Elders sit and analyse the integrity and reputation of the man who intends to marry their daughter before they approve the marriage. The wind of modernisation may be blowing in their direction, but from the way things are, the Karimojong are not just about to let go of their traditions. In the region, they can be compared to the distinctively prominent masaai who are known to carry their culture with them everywhere they go.
Acholi
When it comes to culture, the acholi are so passionate and determined that they will not allow even the 20-year political insecurity to wipe away their roots. Watching the jolly girls wriggle and twist their waists as they perform the dingi dingi or larakaraka dance, one cannot help but notice the pride with which they represent their homeland.
At a tender age of about 6, a typical village acholi girl can challenge any adult on the dance floor. With well-calculated footsteps and rhythm, she will go about the various folk songs and traditional dances like she were born with them.
An acholi will not mind speaking their language anywhere and everywhere. However, respect and unity is emphasised amongst them. Although a child is born in one family, it grows up in a communal village setting, taking part in the various activities.
According to Mr James Latigo, all members of Acholi households acknowledge the authority of its heads - the adult male known as won paco (owner of the home) who presides over a collection of households built close to one another round a circle of bare earthcalled dye-kal. The huts are made of mud brick walls with rough wood frames to hold the thatch roof while the floors are carpeted in mud or cow dung to make it smooth.
Activities are done communally in work groups known as awak. While the men hunt and cultivate the fields, the women team up to do domestic chores. During the evening meal, people sit in groups according to sex, age group, usually around or near a fire place in the compound called wang-oo.'
Latigo says "The men take their place at the kongo - wooden seats built in tiers all round the fireside. The women sit on the ground not far away and the older children sit apart also."
It is in such settings that ideas are exchanged and isssues settled or discussed. Folksongs and stories make up the better part of the evenings with the elders telling stories of their days. Even the dancing is done communally, the most common being bwola (the chief's dance), lalobaloba, otiti, myel awal (funeral dance), apiti, labongo, myel wanga and atira (performed on the eve of a battle). Most of these dances are performed with a drums, spears and shields. It is no wonder that the acholi portray a tight bond amongst themselves wherever they go.
Alur
If you are planning to host the Alur chief at any of your functions, then you might as well keep your chairs or exclude him from the list of guest seat allocations. The Alur do not just allow their king, fondly known as Rwoth to sit on any chair. He has a special stool called Kom pa Rwoth (the kings chair) that he carries to every function - even if it means moving with it across districts. This stool is strictly used by only the king. "May be some rituals are done on it," is all Ms Rose Tuape, the vice chair person of Packwach Town Council could say about the stool.
As a requirement, the king also has to jump over a live sheep before crossing the boarders beyond his land. This is a form of blessing for his journey. The sheep may then be slaughtered and prepared for the people remaining behind. It should also not be a shock to you when he arrives clad in leopard skin with a whisker (locally called Olusu made from the tail of a cow) in hand. Leopards are believed to be brave and smart amongst the Alur.
To give the king's house the sacredness it deserves, one has to remove their shoes when entering. The floor is carpeted in skin. However, while many of us might generally refer to them as the Alur, they are divided into three groups: the Jonam, Padyere and Okuro.
According to Ms Tuape, the Alur's can differentiate each other basing on the different accents each group assumes. "The Jonam speak like the Acholi, the Padyere speak typical Alur while the Okuro have a mixture of some Maddi words," she says. Also, wach group has their own king.
A typical Alur meal will include Angira (Greens mixed with otigu), Kwen Ongura (Cassava that is sometimes mixed with sorghum) and Angara (salty fish with very tiny bones).
Because the Alur live around river Nile, fishing is one of the most common activities among the men while women do most of the garden work. While most of their women are known for wearing kitenge materials, which they get by virtue of their proximity to DR Congo, gomesis are also a common traditional outfit. Like elsewhere, there is evidence that the strong wind of modernisation has swept through Alur land. Originally, traditional attire was called okowsho, made out of animal skin.
Tuape says to soften it, it was smeared with locally made oil and made into a likeness of a skirt for women and a small piece for covering the breasts, while the men simply wrapped it around their waist.
If you intend to marry an Alur girl, then all you have to do is invite the girl's family to see the cows you are hoping to stake, get their list of required items, deliver them and the girl is yours. Jaluo.com describes the Alur as naturally peace-loving who prefer conformity to dissent.
continues on Sunday

Original_Search_Title: FRA Helping to Market Maize and Other Crops
Headline: Zambia: FRA Helping to Market Maize and Other Crops
Body_Text:
ZAMBIA has this year yielded surplus maize production that has never been achieved since 2001. The Food Reserve Agency (FRA) is facilitating, marketing and maintaining the strategic reserves.
FRA acting food reserve and marketing manager, Richard Lisimba says the agency has managed to purchase about 172,000 tonnes of maize in less than two months, adding that they are determined to buy the remainder before the onset of the rains.
Meanwhile, Lisimba reveals that Government has allowed the agency to export 100,000 tonnes of maize. The proceeds from the sale will be ploughed back into the purchase programme.
"I have no doubt we may buy up to 300,000 tonnes as the 200,000 tonnes is meant for the strategic reserve while the 100,000 tonnes is for export," he said.
FRA would buy as much crop as possible provided there is more funding from Government.
"Having come out of droughts and food shortages, we cannot afford to go back there because it is our responsibility to ensure that nobody goes hungry again," says Lisimba.
Apart from maize that the agency is still purchasing, it is this season planning to buy 2,400 tonnes of cassava, 1,200 tonnes of rice, 1,200 tonnes of soybeans and 1,200 tonnes of groundnuts.
Although, unscrupulous traders have been buying crops from uninformed small- scale farmers at low prices and later selling it to FRA at a higher one, Lisimba explains that this won't happen this year. He adds that FRA is operating a total of 60 main depots and each of them has a truck that is used to transport the crop from the mobile depots to the main depots.
This has lessened the burden of transportation on the farmer's part.
"There is no reason to sell the crops that farmers sweated for to unscrupulous business men when FRA is available to buy the maize at a good price nearest to them," stresses Lisimba.
He further discloses that FRA is offering the best prices on the market and has the money to pay the farmers as it believes that farming is still a business and its responsibility is to ensure that farmers continue producing.
"Before we moved into the market, there were stories that farmers were being offered K15, 000 per 50kg bag of maize. FRA has stepped in and we are paying K38, 000 per 50kg bag."
On the contrary, Agriculture Support Programme (ASP) Agri Business Advisor, Ivan Stubbs says the 200,000 metric tonnes that FRA has targeted is just 14 per cent of this year's crop. This leaves 86 per cent of the crop in the hands of the private sector.
Stubbs says the market is saturated with the maize and soybeans because exports were banned at the beginning of the season, He discloses that the market price range for a 50kg bag of maize is between K15,000 and K28,000.
"The soybean market is still flooded with farmers receiving K1, 500 per meda in several outlying areas," he says.
He attributes this to the carry over stocks from last year of about 20,000 tonnes of soybeans.
This year's crop is estimated at 40, 000 tonnes which is equal to the country's annual utilisation. This means that the 20,000 tonnes is still lying idle and until it is exported it will just go to waste.
Stubbs also explains that the strong Kwacha on the other hand has not benefited farmers as majority grow export crops such as tobacco, cotton and paprika.
"When the Kwacha is strong, it means the Kwacha price will normally drop as we have seen the cotton price dropping from K1, 200 to K850 per kg," he says.
He further says the earlier ban of exports by Government, coupled with the late funding of FRA caused the local market to flood. This forced farmers to sell a 50kg bag of maize between K15, 000 to K20, 000.
According to Stubbs, this price is far below the production cost. He urges farmers to learn to diversify into small and medium Agri businesses such as local chickens, goats or pigs.
He adds that it is a common trend in the farming sector to have low prices and low yields when there is a good harvest and droughts respectively. This disadvantages farmers as they fail to sell and end up making mediocre profits.
For this reason, farmers should perceive that consistence is also the key to sustainability. The marketing of crops such as cotton, tobacco and maize has been a flop this season but agriculturists predict that the prices of these crops next year will be supper to this end it would be wise for growers to consistent-PALISAH

Original_Search_Title: Agric Produce to Grace Italian Expo
Headline: Zimbabwe: Agric Produce to Grace Italian Expo
Body_Text:
Harare — ZIMBABWE'S agricultural produce may for the first time grace exhibition shelves at the Italian International Food Exhibition in Parma in October 2007 if the current dialogue between Government, Africa Produce Project and other key stakeholders in farming is pursued to fruition.
The Minister of Agriculture, Dr Joseph Made, revealed this in Harare yesterday when he hosted the visiting Italian representatives of Africa Produce Project.
Dr Made said Government welcomed the prospect of having Zimbabwean agricultural produce showcased at international level in an event that is held every two years to give industry, producers of food crops and the processors an opportunity to interact.
"Obviously, this is an interesting development considering that it would be some kind of incentive to our producers who will also learn how to process their own produce...
"This feat has always been achievable if our farmers got proper extension with the sector also getting adequate resources for research and training, timely input distribution and the right infrastructural development to enable farmers to woe international business partners for the good of production," Dr Made said.
He was, however, quick to mention that issues of poor mechanisation and inadequate instruction on proper utilisation of fertilizers (both organic and inorganic) have always been the biggest challenges bedevilling most of Africa's agro-based economies.
Cde Made added that Zimbabwe's national average yield per hectare for both the small and large-scale producer was one metric tonne per hectare, while the world average was between six and eight tonnes per hectare, which meant that it had the capacity to compete at any level.
Dr Made also hinted that potential and willing investors intending to come to Zimbabwe and enter into partnerships with farmers would receive the necessary support and protection they needed from all players involved in agriculture -- the monetary authorities, industry and Government.
He conceded that he was delighted to have inquiries on farming partnerships, especially on maize and cassava, coming from as far afield as Italy, which was a sure sign that the position of Zimbabwe on the world food production graph could be easily restored.
"Such a development links the corporate world with the producers and they will get adequate incentives to produce with a known targeted market while the act of exhibiting in Italy itself is enough to tell processors and consumers about our farming capabilities," Dr Made said.
He also told the Italians that Zimbabwe had the most favourable climatic conditions for the production of several crops without the involvement of artificial means.
Cde Made said Zimbabwe was also the first country to produce the single line maize hybrid ahead of other maize producing countries of the world.
On the other hand, the Italian ambassador to Cote d'Ivoire, Mr Paolo Sannella, who was part of the delegation, said such exhibitions would dispel the belief that African countries, Zimbabwe included, were infamous for their abject poverty and suffered from structural and organisational problems, a myth held the world over.
"Africa is failing to completely exploit its resources because of lack of technology in both the cannery and processing industry so it can not export value-added products or even conserve the products to use them progressively and locally," Mr Sannella said.
Mr Sannella also indicated that international co-operation between farmers and processors of their produce helped open opportunities for export and facilitated the creation of homogene categories of products.
The food exhibition has been held every two years since 1941 and has always created a discussion forum for customers and suppliers in a sector which is vital for every economy in the world. More than 1 000 manufacturers alongside more than 30 000 professional visitors from all over the world, have graced the exhibition centre over the years, according to the statistics released in 2005.
The specialist event also brings together experts from the vegetable, fish and meat preservation industry and gives producers the opportunity to romance with the latest idea being implemented in the dairy industry while parading the new technologies for inter-sector and food related industries.

Original_Search_Title: Changes in Food And Nsima Culture Affect Our Lives
Headline: Zambia: Changes in Food And Nsima Culture Affect Our Lives
Body_Text:
Lusaka — SHARING and eating food with others is a sign of social communion, a "being together." The sharing from the bounty of nature's nourishment expresses unity of persons and purpose.
When people share meals, relationships deepen. We may notice that those who are mentally and socially close often would have shared some meal together.
This eating is more than just feeding the physical body. Eating together expresses some spiritual bond amongst the persons involved.
Cultures and habits develop around food and eating. Food affects cultures and behaviour and cultures affect feeding. The contents and preparation of various foods affect life and living in various ways.
The food and culture around the food can nourish us or sometimes limit us.
A common food in this region of Africa is nsima. Some call it nshima, inzhima, buhobe, bwali, ugali, papu, and sadza, amongst many variations.
There are those who feel a strong bond with nsima, especially that made from maize meal commonly known as "mealie-meal." They refuse to consider the starch component of other food types.
When outside Africa, they may feel deprived without maize nsima. This has more to do with attitude and habit than any fact that maize nsima is necessarily the best food in the world.
It also has to do with emotions packaged in nsima. Nsima can be made from various flours, from cassava to millet and sorghum. Some, for such reasons as taste, availability, and economy, use a combination of the flours.
Maize meal only became widespread some few decades ago. Maize was brought into the nsima belt by people from Portugal. Nsima is more than maize flour heated and cooked into lumps. Like many types of food, culture and emotions surround nsima and the way it is eaten.
It is a training ground for character. It is also an observation ground. As towards many foods, nsima eating has culture and discipline developed around attitudes and procedures.
It is a process, or used to be so in many places, from which young persons learn habits and attitudes that help them face life. Around the meal are issues like who washes hands first and who starts to eat first and "opens the floor." A meal is about relating to other persons.
Each meal session will have some captain or meal commissioner that directly or indirectly assists the meal session. This may be linked to age or other seniority of the persons involved.
There is order to the way a person moulds the nsima lump. The accompanying "relish" must relate to the nsima in certain ways. And there is order to the way one eats. To some people, eating habits show discipline rating and whether one is well brought up.
Changes in food and nsima culture are affecting our quality of life. Changes in our social and economic lives affect our nsima and food culture.
In urban areas, many persons no longer eat nsima from one common plate but have lumps given on individual plates. Even then, the pot the nsima comes from is the same. Although it varies in places, the principle of discipline and order still apply.
Crucial to enjoyment of nsima is the cooking. Besides technical skill, cooking is an extension of the emotions and character of the person cooking.
The quality of nsima is in direct relation to the skills and emotions, at a particular time, in the person cooking. The person cooking nsima must at cooking be in a good frame of mind. An angry and unsettled person may not cook good nsima. For that reason, many persons will refuse to eat nsima cooked by an angry or stressed person.
The emotions of the person are moulded in the food they produce.
When some people feel discomfort after eating some nsima meal, they may seek reasons in the temperament of the person that cooked.
The mixing of ingredients must be done in right amounts and at the right time. No nsima is the same. Each is packaged with emotions of the time of cooking and eating. It is not easy to reproduce nsima in a factory. Another delicate food is chikanda, very good for those of us that are vegetarians.
Chikanda requires the right type, coming from the right soil, and the right approach. Otherwise, many persons try but find difficulties in being consistent.
They bring out good chikanda at one time and then a poorly baked one the next. Consistently good chikanda- making requires masters to prepare it.
For some decades, whenever I can, I get my chikanda from a Mrs Ngwira who is found at Kangwa's shopping centre in Libala. The lessons of chikanda apply to nsima. Nsima making is art and craft. It demands deep care. Recently, with various brothers and sisters in various places of Zambia, we have been moaning the decline of good nsima making.
Whether one's palate and taste likes it light or heavy, and hot or cool, like us, good nsima should come off in nice grains. Good nsima tastes so great that it can be eaten without accompanying "relish." Good nsima does not stick to the hand. Neither does it stick to the pot it is made in. Those who cook well were admired and sought for in marriage and families.
It was not a physical thing but something to do with the person's emotional expression and balance. As it seeks to nourish persons, meal preparation is sacred. Cooking is an expression of love and nourishment. For that reason, some people will personally cook nsima and not task the house worker.
Some people avoid nsima that is mass cooked. Under good cooking, good nsima can still be made for a large number of persons.
In places where people preparing or handling food are poorly paid and oppressed, it shows in the quality of nsima and food. There is no joy in the mechanical cooking and there will be no joy in the meal.
Poor nsima is a sign of the changing emotional situation of our society.
The more stressed we are, the poor the nsima. Healthy emotions, aided by good skills, will produce good meals. On other issues, it is not easy to understand why Zambia's new Demographic and Health Survey's national collection of data, scheduled for August 2006 to August 2007, was allowed to take place at peak ofElections 2006 campaign time.
The survey may already have been planned for this period and the elections dates were announced when survey planning and arrangements for logistics were already in place. But in communities, such combination of projects may not help the survey and election process.
Those involved in the survey and elections will have to do adjustments in their work. Ideally, having known about the election dates, one would have expected the survey to be rescheduled to after elections.
Again, on another issue, the signing of the Kazungula Bridge project by presidents Festus Mogae of Botswana, Levy Mwanawasa of Zambia, and Zimbabwe's Robert Mugabe must, although it could have happened earlier, be commended as major action for further removing mental and physical distances and limitations that affect persons in the region, persons who, beyond politics and state boundaries, are foremost brothers and sisters that should be living and working together.
Most often, most solutions to most challenges can be found within and around us.

Original_Search_Title: Delsa Calls for Repositioning of Delta Ports
Headline: Nigeria: Delsa Calls for Repositioning of Delta Ports
Body_Text:
Warri — UNDERUTILISATION of Delta ports in the face of huge potential and opportunities has been identified as the major problem facing shippers in Delta State, even as there is the need to reposition and redress the teething problems facing the ports.
The president of the Delta Shippers Association (DELSA) Mr. Edema Abrakata made these remarks weekend, in his welcome address at a seminar entitled: Promoting Non-Oil exports in Delta State "organised by the association in Warri.
According to him, there is need to reposition and redress the teething problems facing the ports and seeking ways to put the Delta ports into full operation under any operator or management, and called on all shippers in the catchments area to always use the ports considering the economic implication of shipping through other parts, stressing that the DELSA is working towards a better tariff regime for the ports.
In a lecture entitled " Exports financing and documentary credits," the Group Managing Director and Chief Executive officer of Wema Bank Plc, His Excellency, Adebisi Omoyeni, stated that "the beauty of non-oil exports is that you reap from your efforts because your earning comes directly to you and not the federal purse , " noting that there are windows of opportunities in the export of cocoa, cashew, gari, gallstones, ginger, cassava, bones, sesame seeds, pepper, honey, charcoal and spices.

Original_Search_Title: Venezuelan President Hugo Chavez Ends Visit
Headline: Angola: Venezuelan President Hugo Chavez Ends Visit
Body_Text:
Luanda — Venezuelan president Hugo Chaves Frias ended this evening his five-hour official visit to Angola, meant to reinforce the two countries' cooperation relations.
The South American nation's leader, who left the Angolan capital, Luanda at 8.20pm, headed his country delegation that held talks with the local side, meant at analysing future areas for cooperation, with highlight to oil sector.
After the official talks, both delegations released a final communiqué, in which Venezuela, the world's fifth biggest oil producer, expressed its will to support Angola in oil field, due to its greater potential and experience.
The two countries' officials, at the presence of host President Jose Eduardo dos Santos and Venezuelan leader Hugo Chavez, signed an agreement in oil sphere that will boost the exploration of both nations' potentialities.
After expressing the satisfaction for the climate of understanding that led the talks between the two oil producing nations, José Eduardo dos Santos and Hugo Chaves gave green light to the respective ministers of Foreign Affairs to continue with the bilateral consultations and negotiations toward a cooperation accord to be signed soon by both officials.
Angola and Venezuela started diplomatic relations on 25 September 1986, but a legal cooperation framework has never been defined.
World's fifth biggest oil producer, Venezuela expressed interest to open a permanent diplomatic mission in Angola.
It is a Presidential Federalist Republic with 22 states, one federal district (Caracas) and 72 federal dependencies. The Climate is tropical and until the year 2000, the rate of illiteracy was 07 percent.
The local currency is Bolivar, whereas the main production is sugar cane, banana, maize, rice, orange, cassava and sorghum.
Venuezela is amongst the five main oil exporters. It produces 3.7 million barrels per day and is expected to reach 5 million by 2012.
On 03 July 2000, presidential and legislative elections were held in the country, won by Hugo Chaves with 55 percent of the votes.
A referendum was held in 2004, with 58,25 percent of the voters supporting the continuation of the president.
