Original_Search_Title: Group Mobilises for Egwu's Presidential Bid
Headline: Nigeria: Group Mobilises for Egwu's Presidential Bid
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — Home support for Governor Sam Egwu's Presidential bid grew stronger as two groups "Divine Mandate 2007, Dr Sam Egwu for President" and "Sam Egwu Divine Mandate 2007" emerged to drum support and convince the governor to join the race.
In separate press conferences, the groups advised the Peoples Democratic Party, (PDP) to jettison its speculated intent of zoning the Presidency to any particular geo-political zone insisting that aspirants to the exalted position should be judged on their individual merits.
They maintained that even if the PDP decides to zone the Presidency, it was the turn of the Eastern region comprising southeast and south-south to produce the next president, since according to them, Nigeria was originally founded on a tripodal socio-political structure, namely north, west and eastern region, adding that other regions except the east had taken their turns in producing presidents of the country.
National President of Divine Mandate 2007, Dr Sam Egwu for President, and former state chairman of PDP, Prince Joe Igwe Nomeh, said the Association which was formed in April 2006, was made up of like minded people across the country, who have been following the good works of Dr Egwu for several years and have been working tirelessly since then to actualize the Presidential ambition of Governor Egwu.
Nomeh recalled Egwu's peaceful and mature handling of the political crises between 2001 and 2002 between the then Abuja group and the home group, which made the state come out of the imbroglio without much casualty.
He further described Egwu as a detribalized Nigerian and a man of peace who does not discriminate between indigenes and non-indigenes in the distribution of government appointments.
On the other hand, the coordinator of Sam Egwu Divine Mandate 2007, Mr Paul Okorie, an Engineer, said the group's support for Egwu's aspiration was a vote for the continuation of President Obansanjo multi-faceted reform programmes, nothing that Governor Egwu had understudied Obasanjo's administration as shown by the number of reforms he had introduced in the state in the areas of education, fiscal responsibility, healthcare, and Agriculture, in addition to having served variously as member of Presidential committees in the areas of rice production and cassava processing among others. "In stating the foregoing, we are not unaware that some other aspirants have been talking glibly about continuing with President Obasanjo's reform programmes if elected President, but we say that Egwu does not belong to the heedless bandwagon who may not have any knowledge of reforms beyond the mere sound of the word itself....He is adequately equipped intellectually, spiritually and ps ychologically to do the job," he stated.
Okorie, who is the state Commissioner for Works, said part of the objectives for the formation of the Association, was to harmonize and systemize the various calls by a number of well meaning Nigerians, groups and corporate bodies for Dr Egwu to present himself for the office o the President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria in the 2007 general election.
He added: "For us, the ambition to have Governor Egwu clinch the Presidency of the nation in 2007 elections is not a pipe dream; we are willing and determined to walk hazardous paths, discuss with all shades of Nigerians and negotiate with all interest groups to ensure that Egwu emerges democratically as President of Nigeria in 2007".
On the multiplication of pressure groups bearing the same name and championing the same cause, a member of the group and former commissioner for Finance, Chief Anthony Agbo, said it was a good omen that many groups were sharing common names, which according to him, demonstrate that their message was divine, adding also that some presidential aspirants have up to 200 different pressure groups working for them.

Original_Search_Title: Senate Rejects VAT Increase
Headline: Nigeria: Senate Rejects VAT Increase
Body_Text:
The senate yesterday rejected the 100% increase in the rate of value added tax (VAT), payable by companies and other businesses in the country, saying it will have an adverse effect on the purchasing power of citizens.
The senate, after a rigourous consideration and debate, also passed, alongside the VAT Bill, the Bill for the establishment of a Federal Inland Revenue Service (FIRS). The government has also abolished some levies on freight shipping, the Federal Executive Council announced in Abuja yesterday. The senators said the usual 5% should be retained as VAT and not the 10% as earlier proposed. Proponents said the bill, apart from seeking to increase the rate of the value added tax, "is aimed at using the tax to redistribute wealth as well as increase revenue yield in the country."
But chairman of the senate Committee on Finance, Senator Effiong D. Bob, said findings of his committee revealed that if the tax rate is increased, the citizens will be at the receiving end a s it will have a telling effect on their demand for goods. He said: "The proposed 100% increase in the rate of the value added tax will adversely affect the manufacturing sector as it will affect the capacity to demand for their products (by the citizens) thus resulting in high industrial inventory."
The committee had noted that in the last one year, the basic income of Nigerians has been eroded given the increase in the prices of petroleum products, the implementation of the National Health Insurance Scheme (NHIS) and the new contributory pension scheme. "Therefore, the committee rejects the proposed increase in the value added tax regime. We recommend that the existing provision of 5% value added tax be retained to the benefit of Nigerians," Senator Bob, who heads the 11-member finance committee, said.
He charged the federal government to see reason in entrenching appropriate administrative mechanisms to plug loopholes of fraud and evasion of taxes rather than increasing t he regime. He also reiterated the "urgent need" to assist and increase the capacity utilisation of the manufacturing sector in order to provide employment for the nation's youth. Earlier, the senators also passed the FIRS Bill. Following a motion sponsored by the Senate Majority Leader, Senator Dalhatu Sarki Tafida, the Bill was read for the third time, taken and passed. According to the proponents of the Bill, the service, if established, will "assess, collect and enforce payment of taxes as may be due to the government or any of its agencies as well as review the tax regimes and promote the application of tax revenues to stimulate economic activities and development."
At the Federal Executive Council (FEC), meeting, the government also approved the abolition of some types of shipping charges. National Information Minister, Mr. Frank Nweke Jnr., along with his counterpart from the Ministry of Commerce, Dr. Umar Aliyu Modibbo, explained that the Council's decision is part of government's efforts to review the Export Expansion Grant (EEG), in order to make it more efficient and to put the ports system under a "one stop shop". Explaining further, the Minister of Commerce said it took his ministry over fourteen weeks to process the claims of the Exporter Expansion Grant and some charges by the consulting firm at the port on all commodities for export in the country. The memo on this decision, he said, will be forwarded to the Council of States to indicate the need to clear all charges.
On the promotion of Nigeria's agricultural products and to encourage its export especially now that some value has been added to the country's products, efforts are being made to see how Nigeria can increase the number of its products which it was known for, hence the need for the Commerce Ministry to present an initiative called " Commerce 44," which identifies some products in the solid minerals sub-sectors and also in the agricultural sector.
Another issue approv ed at yesterday's FEC meeting was the request from the Ministry of Agriculture to complete the strategic grains reserve silo project in Kaduna. The contract was awarded to Northern Properties Nigeria Limited, at the total cost of N245 million in the government's efforts to provide adequate facilities for the agricultural storage in the country. It would be recalled that it is from about 33 silos built all over the country in such strategic reserves that the country has made a large food donation to the neighbouring Niger Republic and some African countries and also stocks are released to help stabilise prices.
Dr. Modibbo Aliyu further explained that in the efforts to diversify focus from non-oil sector of the economy and in trying to market Nigeria and its products his ministry has identified what he called "eleven agricultural products that are doing well. The products which he said use to be the mainstay of the economy in the past. The eleven agricultural products he state d are, Cocoa, Cotton, Cassava , Gum Arabic, Ginger, Sesame seed, Shearnuts , Cashew, Poultry, Floriculture, Fruits, Vegetables and Nigerian food items which he said all tallies well under the manufactured products being exported to other neighbouring countries these will henceforth be formalised to boost trade between Nigeria and other countries. Under the new system, the minister added, eleven areas were chosen under beverages like soft drinks, fruit juices "even our water is now doing well in the markets." These, he said, deserves government's attention to help them boost their morale.

Original_Search_Title: Hugo Chavez in Luanda
Headline: Angola: Venezuelan President Arrives
Body_Text:
Luanda — The president of the Republic of Venezuela, Hugo Chaves Frias, arrived Thursday in Luanda at 03:07 pm (local time) with a view to strengthening bilateral cooperation with Angola.
Hugo Chavez, who has come for a few hours official visit, was welcomed at Luanda's "4 de Fevereiro" International Airport, by Foreign minister, João Bernardo de Miranda.
Soon after the visiting head of state was driven to the Presidential Palace, for due military honours.
This will be followed by an official lunch host by president Jose Eduardo dos Santos, in the presence of officials from both countries governments.
Meanwhile, official talks between ministerial delegations from both countries are taking place this afternoon with discussions of issues concerning cooperation, with highlight to oil matters.
A private meeting will be held between the two heads of State.
This evening the visiting president will receive separate courtesy greetings from local parliament speaker, Roberto de Almeida, and prime minister, Fernando da Piedade Dias dos Santos.
Angola and Venezuela started diplomatic relations on 25 September 1986, but a legal cooperation framework has never been defined.
World's fourth world oil producer, Venezuela expressed interest to open a permanent diplomatic mission in Angola.
It is a Presidential Federalist Republic with 22 states, one federal district (Caracas) and 72 federal dependencies. The Climate is tropical and until the year 2000, the rate of illiteracy was 07 percent.
The local currency is Bolivar, whereas the main production is sugar cane, banana, maize, rice, orange, cassava and sorghum.
Venuezela is amongst the five main oil exporters. It produces 3.7 million barrels per day and is expected to reach 05 million by the year 2012.

Original_Search_Title: Duties On Cocoa, Coal to Go
Headline: Nigeria: Duties On Cocoa, Coal to Go
Body_Text:
Abuja — FEDERAL Government yesterday abolished levies on 33 commodities to promote their export to 11 destinations in the world under a new policy code-named "Commerce 44".
The approval was granted by the Federal Executive Council (FEC) presided over by President Olusegun Obasanjo based on a memorandum presented to it by the Commerce Minister, Alhaji Aliyu Modibbo.
But the tax exemption on the 33 products would be effective when the National Council of State endorses it.
Minister of Information and National Orientation, Mr Frank Nweke (Jnr), and his Commerce counterpart, Alhaji Aliyu Modibbo who addressed State House Correspondents at the end of the FEC meeting, listed the products that would be exempted from tax in three categories; namely: Agriculture, Manufacturing and Solid Minerals sub-sectors.
Modibbo said that to further push the initiative to success, the federal government plans to abolish several export duties and taxes, reduce the delays in processing of exports at the ports and the refund of export expansion grant to exporters which currently takes up to 14 weeks.
Those which export will be promoted include 11 products from the agricultural sector such as cocoa, cotton, cassava, gum Arabic, ginger, sesames, shearnut, cashew, fruits, vegetables and Nigerian food items.
Those listed in the manufacturing sub-sector are beverages, soft drinks, bottled water, footwear, leather, textiles and garment, shrimps, rubber and pharmaceutical products, iron and steel, film and music.
Also to enjoy a tax-free regime are 11 products selected from the solid minerals sub-sector of the Nigerian economy; tin, zinc, coenobites, iron ore, kaolin, coal, barite, precious stones, clay products, among others.
While the targeted markets for these products are the of European Union (EU) countries, Japan, China, Iran, North America, Thailand, India, ECOWAS, the rest of Africa, the Caribbean, Turkey, and Latin America.
According to the two ministers, the taxes abolished by the meeting include the 1 per cent charged by a consulting firm at the Ports, the N25, 000 NAFDAC levy per container on commodities, the charge by the Ports concessionaires, the sale tax of N1,000, and other charges collected by states.
The commerce minister said that based on effective implementation of the Commerce 44, FEC agreed that the policy be in three stages according to the three tiers of government.
"At the Federal level we have the Commerce 44 National Council which will meet once in year to access exactly how much we have exported to see if we have met the 10 per cent growth in the non-oil sector. And then, at the State level we will have the Commerce 44

Original_Search_Title: Smuggling'll Worsen Joblessness
Headline: Nigeria: Smuggling'll Worsen Joblessness
Body_Text:
Abuja — MINISTER of Commerce, Dr Aliyu Moddibo Umar on Monday said that, if the activities of smugglers are not curtailed, the country will soon witness more growth in the number of the unemployed.
Moddibo, who spoke to journalists in his office in Abuja disclosed that the country would soon have a National Mudu (measurement) policy to help forestall the practice of Nigerians buying different quantities of same products at the same price.
The minister explained that he will ensure that the country will not be known for only cassava as the most important exportable products as the Commerce Ministry has concluded research into the 11 other products which will be developed for the international market.
He said that move by the ministry to reduce its departments to only five was in line with the ongoing reforms in the ministry adding it would also make the ministry more relevant and efficient.
He explained that Nigeria is blessed with numerous agricultural products like cocoa, tea, pepper among others and even if they cannot be exported, Nigerians can consume them, if properly processed.
On smuggling, the minister who noted the textile sector was most affected, pointed out that if the trend where clothing materials mostly inferior ones are snuggled into the country and sold at cheaper prices, the remaining textile firms in the country many eventually close-down leading to more joblessness.
He further said that the move to introduce the national Mudu (measurement policy was to ensure that there is a uniformed quantity of products sold at a particular price.
On why prices of agricultural products differ from zones, the minister said that the government will continue in the drive towards the provision of storage facilities all over the country.
"Why is Coca-Cola and other soft drinks sold at the same price all over the country? Why some products having uniform price while others don't, this is what we will work on to achieve this," he stated.

Original_Search_Title: Flour Mills Records N86.6bn Turnover
Headline: Nigeria: Flour Mills Records N86.6bn Turnover
Body_Text:
Lagos — Flour Mills Group of companies posted an annual turnover of about N86.5 billion for the year 2005, as against N66.8 billion that was recorded in the previous year.
Its profit before taxation for the year under review stood at N6.3billion, and profit after tax and minority interest also rose from N1.5 Billion to N4.6 billion.
Interestingly, the company recorded a very impressive performance. It attached a growth of about 27 percent in turnover which went up from N50.96 billion to N64.86 billion. Profit before Taxation of N4.3 billion was not only a record but it also surpassed the projected figure of N2.3 billion in the Rights Circular issued in October 2004. The result represents a 46 per-cent increase over Profit before Taxation of N1.7 billion posted in 2005. While, after tax profit, went up from N1.3 billion to N3 billion, an increase of 131%.
This came as the company's Managing Director (MD), Chief Emmanuel Ukpabi yesterday revealed that the company has recently acquired a N2.3 billion gas plant to boost the company production capacity.
Upkabi who briefed media men in Lagos yesterday on the modernization of the company noted that the enhanced bottom line for the company was largely attributable to improved efficiencies resulting from huge investments in state-of-the-art mills and ancillary equipment, power generating capacity, of human capital resources and a significant reduction in financial cost.
"In view of the impressive result, the Directors are pleased to recommend to shareholders at the Annual General Meeting, payment of a cash dividend of N990,080,000 that is 85 kobo per ordinary share of 50 kobo each, which is payable net of withholding tax. The dividend proposed is slightly higher than the forecast of 80 kobo in the Rights Circular dated October 27, 2004. Total dividend payable represents 21 percent increase over N815,360,000 distributed to shareholders last year" he added.
In line with the Company's capitalization policy, Ukpabi stated the Directors have considered it desirable to plough back part of its earnings in order to strengthen the capital base, adding that, the balance sheet lay good foundation for future growth.
This, he said, will be achieved through capitalization of part of the revenue reserves so that Flour Mills Group of companies was well placed to pursue its ambition geared towards its modernization programme that will meet its current and future capital commitments.
On the newly acquired gas plant, the Managing Director stated that it will help the company cut down on production cost.
"The landing cost for the seven plants is n1.5 billion, excluding installation cost and the cost of building the house where they are seated" he added.
Ukpabi also revealed that Flour Mills Group has started to implement the Federal Government directive on the inclusion of cassava in the production of flours.
Though, this did not go without some teething problems, but added that the company was thinking toward that direction. According to him, Flour Mills have also acquired about 10,000 achers of land for the cultivation of cassava

Original_Search_Title: Commerce 44 Initiative, Others Approved At Weekly FEC Meeting
Headline: Nigeria: Commerce 44 Initiative, Others Approved At Weekly FEC Meeting
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Federal Executive Council, at its August 30 weekly meeting in Abuja approved the 'Commerce 44' initiative aimed at refocusing and expanding the non-oil sector of the nation's economy.
Broken down into three stages comprising committees at the federal, state and council levels, the Commerce 44 initiative involves the tax-free export of 11 categories each of solid minerals, agricultural products and groups of locally-manufactured items to 11 international markets, broken down as follows:
- Agricultural products - cocoa, cotton, cassava, gum Arabic, sesame seeds, cashew, sheanuts, ginger, vegetable, fruits and other Nigerian food items.
- Solid minerals - kaolin, coal, bauxite, barite, tin, zinc, tantalite, columbite, iron ore, clay products (pottery), gems and precious stones.
- Manufactured products - textiles, films and music, vegetable oil, rubber products, foot-wears, shrimps, pharmaceutical products, leather, beverages and soft-drinks as well as bottled water.
The international markets include Japan, North America, India, China, ECOWAS, European Union, Turkey, Iran, Thailand, the Caribbean and Latin America.
To ensure the success of the initiative, Government will abolish the following duties and taxes:
- N25, 000 levy by the National Agency for Food and Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC) per container
- One per cent export duty
- N1, 000 sales tax
- Cocoa development duty, and
- Several other charges levied at state levels.
Government will also reduce delays in the processing of exports at the ports and accelerate the refund of Export Expansion Grant (EEG) to businessmen, which currently take up to 14 weeks.
Other approvals at the meeting include
- Resort International Limited, as the preferred bidder for the redevelopment of the Federal Secretariat, Ikoyi, Lagos, into 360 residential units/flats. The company bid the highest price of N7 billion. On completion, a two-bedroom flat would be sold for N35 million, three-bedroom N45 million, four-bedroom N60 million and a four-bedroom penthouse will go for N75 million. These houses will be sold to Nigerians in Diaspora.
- N245 million for the completion of the strategic grains reserve silos project in Kaduna. This project is part of the strategy of the Federal Government aimed at ensuring food security, making donations to neighbouring African countries and releasing stocks to stabilise food prices in case of inflation.
Commerce Minister, Dr. Aliyu Modibbo, and Information and National Orientation Minister, Mr. Frank Nweke (Jnr), briefed the press after the meeting.

Original_Search_Title: FG Abolishes Export Taxes for 33 Products
Headline: Nigeria: FG Abolishes Export Taxes for 33 Products
Body_Text:
Abuja — IN its bid to encourage local manufacturers to export their products to 11 international markets across the world, the Federal Executive Council (FEC) yesterday approved the scrapping of export levies for 33 products under a new Commerce Initiative known as "Commerce 44."
By implication, the affected commodities: 11 solid minerals, 11 agricultural produce and 11 groups of locally manufactured items, are to be exempted from taxes.
Ministers of Information and National Orientation, Mr. Frank Nweke and Commerce Minister, Dr. Aliyu Modibbo, briefed State House corespondents on the deliberations of the meeting that also approved the sale of Federal Government houses in Ikoyi, Lagos State.
Minister of Commerce, Dr Modibbo, who spoke on the Commerce 44 Initiative said the products and markets were identified after extensive research, adding that the taxes abolished by the meeting included the one per cent charged by a consulting firm at the ports, the N25,000 NAFDAC levy per container on commodities, the 11 per cent containers charge by the ports concessionaires, the sale tax of N1,000 as well as other charges collected by states.
The affected commodities from the agricultural sector were cocoa, cotton, cassava, gum Arabic, ginger, sesames, shenut, cashew, fruits, vegetables and Nigerian food items.
Those listed in the manufacturing sub-sector are beverages, soft drinks, bottled water, footwear, leather, textiles and garment, shrimps, rubber and pharmaceutical products, iron and steel, film and music, and services.
From the solid mineral sector are tin, zinc, coenobites, iron ore, kaolin, coal, barite, precious stones, and clay products, among others.
The minister said the targeted markets for these products or the 11 destinations were European Union countries, Japan, China, Iran, North America, Thailand, India, ECOWAS, the rest of Africa, the Caribbean, Turkey, South and East Asia and Latin America.

Original_Search_Title: VP in Bilateral Business Talks With Thais
Headline: Ghana: VP in Bilateral Business Talks With Thais
Body_Text:
Vice President Aliu Mahama on Tuesday discussed with the Thai Government Ghana's desire to boost technical cooperation in the spirit of South-South cooperation.
He used his one-and-half-hour meeting with the Deputy Prime Minister, Dr Surakiart Sathirathai in Bangkok to explain why the two developing countries should share expertise in cassava and fish production with emphasis on tuna in Ghana for the economic advantage of the two countries.
Mr. Yaw Asamoa Buabeng, Special Assistant to the Vice President, disclosed aspects of the discussions to journalists after a closed-door meeting at Thailand's State House.
He said Ghana was also tapping Thailand's expertise in the large-scale production of anti-malaria and anti-retroviral drugs to boost the fight against malaria and HIV/AIDS, respectively.
Thailand is currently producing anti-malaria drugs in Gabon, Tanzania, The Gambia, Senegal and Mali.
Also on the drawing board is to get Thailand to capitalise on its immense expertise in the textile industry to invest in Ghana's Free Zone Project to benefit from the Africa Growth and Opportunity Act (AGOA), which ensures a ready market for textiles from Africa in the US.
Mr. Buabeng said the Vice President also sought the assistance of Thailand to boost works on the Accra-Kumasi trunk road and other infrastructure development projects.
Another issue of interest to Ghana is to see the Ghana-Thailand Trade Commission come into fruition in October 2006. Mr. Buabeng said Ghana is also seeking technical assistance to initiate a social housing scheme for inner city dwellers. The scheme involves the provision of soft loans to beneficiaries to be able to rehabilitate existing structures and rebuild or construct new homes.
Vice President Mahama was the guest of honour at the Grand Palace of Thailand's Monarchy, which also accommodates the Emerald Buddha Temple, revered by 85 per cent of Thai Buddhists.
The Palace, which has an area of 218,400 square metres, was built in 1782 and is one of the tourist attractions in the country.
The public monument comprises Government offices, the Royal Chapel of the Emerald Buddha, a reception chamber where royal paraphernalia are kept, a library and galleries.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria to Earn $150m From Bio-Fuel
Headline: Nigeria to Earn $150m From Bio-Fuel
Body_Text:
Nigeria will earn the sum of $150 million annually when the bio fuel project being embarked on by the federal government comes on stream, Funso Kupolokun, the group managing director of the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation [NNPC] has said.
Kupolokun who disclosed this recently at a lecture In Lagos said the project is part of the nation's alternative energy development strategy which is aimed at utilizing potentials in cassava and sugar cane to produce ethanol. He explained that under the initiative, two types of automotive fuel would be developed namely, ethanol fuel and palm oil diesel which will provide good alternative to petrol. To this end, Kupolokun said special research initiatives would be sponsored by the NNPC to boost cassava and palm oil output in the country as part of the implementation process of the project. The venture, according to him, would bring about the establishment of several ethanol production plants at an average cost of S160 million each.
Kupolokun further said his company has secured a grant of 70,000 Euros from the Renewable Energy Efficiency Partnership [REEP] from Germany to provide support to the detailed feasibility studies at target locations. While saying that the implementation of a renewable energy project is one major priority of his company in the year, Kupolokun explained that work for the modification of its import reception facilities in Port Harcourt and Mosimi had commenced in preparation for the distribution of biofuel products.
Cooperative agreement he added, were being considered with institutions such as the University of Agriculture Makurdi, the International Institute for Tropical Agriculture, Ibadan and the Nigerian Cereal Research Institute [NCRD]to execute the research aspect of the bio fuel project.

Original_Search_Title: The Electoral System Must Be Reformed - Nwuruku
Headline: Nigeria: The Electoral System Must Be Reformed - Nwuruku
Body_Text:
Lagos — Recently, former Minister of State for Industry, Chief Lawrence Nwuruku expressed his intention to contest the 2007 governorship race in Ebonyi State. Nwuruku who contested the governorship of the state in 2003 under the banner of All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP) is now seeking to contest on the platform of the Peoples Democratic Party ( PDP). In this interview with our Correspondent, KENNETH OFOMA, Nwuruku bares his mind on political developments in the state and why he thinks he deserves to rule. Excerpts:
What should be the major development focus of governor Egwu's successor in office?
It is hinged on integrated rural comprehensive development. Ebonyi State is a complete rural community; therefore integrated rural development is very important for the state. By integrated rural comprehensive development, I mean electricity, roads and hospitals in every ward. In every locality, the government should identify the peculiar crop or domestic product they have comparative advantage in. That would constitute the basis for a small cottage industry in that area. Again, the state government should buy bulks of that product from the farmers for storage and marketing thereby enhancing the economy of the people. If you do not enhance the economic power of the people, they would remain poor. But if some industrial layout is provided in the Ngbo area, where a lot of cassava is produced, there should be a cassava-processing industry to produce garri and cassava flakes and flower. In Ishiagu it could be pottery and quarry, ceramics industry could thrive there. Hospital, roa d and electricity must be a matter of priority. Every community must be energized through rural electrification. If you have electricity and good roads, there would be a multiplier effect in terms of both employment opportunity and industrialization. People could start small-scale enterprises like hairdressing, vulcanizing and garri processing industries. So as a matter of priority, there must be integrated rural development.
During the recent stakeholders meeting, Governor Sam Egwu said there would be an accountability forum where former political office holders would give account of their stewardship; as a former political office holder, what have you done before now?
I really thank God who has been kind to me. It is God that directed my steps. Right from my stint as chairman of my local government, God was in control. I was elected chairman of Ikwo Local Government Area in December 4, 1987 and it was my performance there that catapulted me to where I am today. With just N700,000.00 out of the N1 million the Head of State, President Ibrahim Babangida shared among local government councils I developed Ikwo Local Government Area. The local government won the award of the best rural developed local government in terms of rice mill industry I brought there as well as parceling the area into plots for residential and industrial purposes. In terms of rural development, there is no ward in Ikwo local government area that you cannot access by road between 1987 and 1989 when I served. But prior to that time it was impossible to connect the communities by road. So despite the fact that the area is marshy I connected every nook and cranny with roads.
There was rice mill industry for the women and I also renovated the whole of the local government area putting it into good shape. In the area of water supply, we brought more boreholes for the people. During the incidence of the rice disease, we brought treated rice en-mass for Ikwo people. That is why they come to me to ask that what I did for them should be done for other people at the state level. I cannot list all I have done but let me do a brief summary. When I was a commissioner, I made sure that my community, Enyibichiri got a hospital. When I was a commissioner for Rural Development, I ensured that electricity came to Ikwo. As I said earlier we increased the number of boreholes in every nook and cranny of what was called Abakaliki Division then. We also distributed guinea worm drugs. It was also at this time that I initiated my scholarship scheme and I started with my clan. I made sure that every of the four wards in my clan had at least two graduates trained by m e. Then as I moved up to the position of commissioner of the Independent National Electoral Commission, I said I must do something for scholarship education first. That is why I praise Dr. Sam Egwu for what he has done for the educational development of this state. I believe in giving good education to the people first. Right now none of the twenty wards in my place in Ikwo has not got at least three graduates trained by me. Even in overseas scholarships, I have one in Cuba studying medicine, one from Ebonyi South and two from Ebonyi North. They are in Cuba studying medicine on my sponsorship.
Then you come to primary healthcare, I influenced the building of six primary health facilities in Ebonyi. Then the building of 26 UBE schools in the entire state. They are there for all to see and I have the documents. In partnership with the governor, because I always tell the governor that we are going to do this or that; I influenced the executive council signing the memo where I said that Ebonyi State should be categorized as one of the educationally disadvantaged areas. What does that mean? It influences the joint allocation where Ebonyi is the only state from the South East that is branded an educationally disadvantaged area. Like the ongoing Rural Telephony Project, it was during my time that we championed it, with that, in every local government area there should be telephone. Even the NTA here, in partnership with Jerry Gana, we influenced that it should be in Abakaliki alongside Radio Nigeria. The administrative secretary of INEC in Anambra State then Ije Ije I emp loyed him. I made sure that he was there. And I made sure that Ebonyi was graded as an ecologically challenged place so as to affect the allocation from the INEC. We made sure that these things are reflected. There are so many other things we did like poverty alleviation. For instance the first executive secretary of any parastatal to come from Ebonyi was my first job, because I made sure he was appointed. That is National Automotive Council (NAC). That is the first appointment I facilitated. But that was in concert with the governor. All through there were more than 50 persons appointed into various positions. In my time for the first time, Ebonyians got appointed into Boards of parastatals. I have the list to show that I influenced those appointments, Boards Chairmen, Members and Directors of Parastatals and others. Even this one standing there (pointing at a man) was my product. I made sure he was appointed, he is now in Nigeria Standards Organization. There are so many o f such opportunities that God used me to provide for our people. I am not taking the glory. There is no how people would come to me and ask for a favour and I failed to assist.
There is this belief that in 2003, you were in PDP but you left to contest for the governorship on the ticket of ANPP. Now you are back to PDP and many people are saying that based on the acrimony generated in 2003, it would be difficult for the incumbent who is expected to play a major role in deciding who succeeds him, to anoint a candidate who once left the party. They say he would rather want to give it to those who have been with him all through. How do you react to that?
Let me tell you in every situation we should give thanks to God. Whatever happened, God knows why it happened. I do not want to go deep into that. It should not have happened but God made it happen. But there was relative peace in Ebonyi, and I made it possible during my electioneering campaigns. Nobody could come forward to say authoritatively that I engineered any violence. Rather, I abhorred it!
I still maintain that there should be no thuggery. I keep saying that instead of violence, killing and maiming because I want to be Governor, God should not allow me. There should be peace and fair play to everybody. So there was nothing like that. Again, I tell you that after the election, I was given money from so many directions urging me to go to court. Look, God knows why it happened. For goodness sake, I was the chairman, Ebonyi State Creation Movement and I know how we suffered. I know the contributions we made for the state. Then in PDP, I am a founding father. But because of one reason or the other we had to change direction to show our grievances. Of course, there is no regret about this. God knows why, at least after the elections I did not go to court for the sake of peace and unity in the state. How can I go to court with Dr. Sam Egwu, my former senior prefect? How can I drag him to court? What should we be fighting for? So, there was no acrimony. I maintained, t hat bygones should be bygones. People should know that we made it possible for PDP to win the first election in 1999, therefore, you must count one before you count two. People keep saying that is the problem on my way to becoming the Governor this time, that I ran in 2003 but they forgot what happened in 1998 and 1999. You must count one before you count two. We are the founding fathers of PDP. Therefore, nobody should hold 2003 against me, because I never showed any acrimony or approved violence. I never sponsored thuggery and I did not say any thing bad against the state. On top of that, I was told to go to court and I refused. I love my state and PDP is my baby. When I was in ANPP, it was like fish out of water for me. That was not my home. I would not have been proud if l had won election under the ANPP, most of the people with me were in PDP.
There are two issues here. One is what will happen to projects started but uncompleted by Governor Egwu. Then the issue of violence, how do you react to the recent killing of Dr. Ayodeji Daramola governorship aspirant in Ekiti State?
Let me assure you that there is nothing like an abandoned project. There must be continuity, I believe in continuity. When I was the chairman of Ikwo Local Government, I planned for many things, so if we had continued till 1989, Ikwo would have been a changed place. We planned to have a stadium; we parceled the area by subdividing Ikwo into plots for residential, commercial and industrial purposes. People were coming from Enugu to pick plots, Ikwo would have been a town by now, but because of lack of continuity, people come in and bring their own projects. For goodness sake, there must be continuity, We must continue and finish up the project, there should be nothing like abandoned projects. Coming to killings, it is terrible who will you rule, who will you serve when you kill your people? How can you start killing instead of focusing on areas of development? That means you have no programme. That means that thugs and ungodly people are coming into the race. I am calling on t he authorities of this country to sanitize the electoral system. Let me tell you, I believe in what EFCC said, let us go into the past of everybody. If Obasanjo reforms every sector and fails to reform the electoral system, I do not think he has succeeded. He must restore confidence in the people once more. You can go to the people and win elections because we know who is who. There is nobody in Ebonyi that we do not know. You are coming out to contest election, we will want to know what you did as a student, both in secondary school and the university. There is nobody who should not influence development in his community in one way or the other, but the practice now is that when once you go and steal money somewhere, you come in and demand to be this or that. I do not have money but for goodness sake, I have the people. I love them, I love Ebonyians. I wish to say this now, that if given the opportunity to serve Ebonyi people as the next governor of the state, the average E bonyians indeed all Ebonyians will go on the street unconsciously kneel down and thank God for bringing such a good man to us. That is my target. I have no divisive tendencies in me. I have a large heart to unite everybody both from south, central and north. I do not belong to one tribal cocoon alone, I belong to all Ebonyians, I will unit everybody and we shall move on.

Original_Search_Title: Ebonyi to Build $80 Million Ethanol Plant
Headline: Nigeria: Ebonyi to Build N10.4 Billion Ethanol Plant
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — Ebonyi State Government has joined the rank of states in Nigeria seeking alternative energy sources, following the signing of a Memorandum of Understanding, (MOU), with a Chinese-supported Nigeria firm to establish a $80m(N10.4bn) Ethanol producing factory that would utilize Cassava and Sugar cane as major raw materials.
Speaking during the MOU signing ceremony which took place at the Government House, Abakaliki, Governor Sam Egwu, noted that apart from creating an unusual wealth base, the initiative would classify the state among the oil-producing states and expressed optimism that the venture would be actualized in record time.
Egwu who was represented by his deputy, Prof. Chigozie Ogbu, explained that when completed, the factory would produce 20,000 gallons of Ethanol every year, by using close to 150, 000 metric tones of Cassava annually, adding that about 700 cassava farmers would be put to work to meet up with that quantity of raw materials.
'Today, what we want to do is to enter into a memorandum of understanding with Viscount Energy (Nigeria) Limited and their foreign partners to establish a factory here in the state that would utilize both cassava and sugar cane to produce Ethanol,' he stated, adding that the factory would engage no less than 100 permanent staff and establish a large cassava and sugar plantation in the state.
Speaking further on the initiative, Egwu said Ethanol could be mixed with Petrol to drive cars, saying it would not only reduce the price of fuel but that it is cleaner than the premium motor spirit alone. Speaking further, he discovered that the factory would help the state tackle the problem of unemployment which he said flows from the number of school leavers and the free education programme of his administration.
Justifying the establishment of the factory in the state, Egwu recalled that since energy was the sole drive of the nation's economy for many years and would continue to be for a long time. Therefore he reasoned that the effort to diverse the economy and bring new inputs into what we do to generate funds in the state and Nigeria as a whole is a continuous one.
The state is known for large scale production of cassava, and we have been trying to establish a lot of agro-based industries, so this is in addition to the energy needs of the country,' he added.
Responding, the representative of Viscount Energy Limited, Mr. Peter Osayande, noted that special independent power generating system would be specifically dedicated to the state for an un-interrupted power supply, adding that within the plant configuration, there would be facilities for the generation of electricity, part of which would be consumed by the plant itself, while the bulk of the energy would be hooked up to the national grid.
Speaking on the likely benefits of the venture, Osayande disclosed that the project would have a multiplier effect on the economy of the state since according to him it has become ,a forerunner of the project in south east Nigeria saying that there was a global search for energy alternative to hydrocarbon.

Original_Search_Title: The Ten Percent Cassava-Flour Policy
Headline: Nigeria: The Ten Percent Cassava-Flour Policy
Body_Text:
In line with the Federal Government's policy on the production of composite flour which requires the utilization of at least 10 per cent of cassava as input, the Flour Millers Association of Nigeria (FMAN), recently established a N500 million Cassava Empowerment Fund as revolving loan to assist cassava processors in meeting their demand for cassava flour, which is currently estimated at 300,000 metric tones. Sunday Vanguard sought the opinion of stakeholders over the development. Excerpts:
ALHAJI Aliko Dangote, presi-dent, Flour Millers Asso-ciation of Nigeria (FMAN) and chairman, Dangote Group.
"We are doing this because we want to support the government's efforts in terms of making the 10 per cent of cassava flour into the flour we are currently processing. We know that it is a government policy, but without our assistance- in terms of funding the processing, we will not be able to get the 300,000 metric tones of cassava flour. As you know, 300,000 metric tones of cassava flour is a tall order and unless we come up with something of this nature, there is no way this can be achieved.
So, it is on this note that we agreed to put money together as an association and believe that the best way to disburse this money to processors is through an institution like the Bank of Industry, because they have the knowledge and know-how to assess the credit risks of all these cassava processors. We hope that the people we are assisting will not go to the other market (garri processing market).
We hope that they will help us in making cassava flour available, because we are desperate to have it in large quantities. We want to ensure that we fulfill what the government asked us to do by using 10 per cent of cassava flour in processing our composite flour and if the cassava flour is available, we are ever willing to use more than the required 10 per cent to be able to achieve the goal of the Federal Government's cassava initiative policy.
Ms Evelyn N. Oputu, managing director/CEO, Bank of Industry
"When the government came up with the cassava initiative to ensure that 10 per cent of the flour is from domestic cassava, little did we imagine that the Flour Millers Association of Nigeria will take on the role of ensuring that it is demonstrated and carried out to a logical conclusion. They are putting their money where their hearts are. Usually, we find that people talk a lot and nothing happens. But they have demonstrated beyond reasonable doubt about their commitment to our national growth.
We have managed, in the past, more than Nl0 billion for the National Automotive Council and from this, FMAN discovered that BOI was well-positioned to be able to ensure that this fund reaches the people they are targeting and that the industrialization process of transforming cassava flour may indeed be carried out through the BOI process. We are assuring Nigerians that the confidence reposed in BOI would be upheld. We'll ensure that the fund continues to grow and the people, the farmers, will go to the next step of their businesses. We will ensure that the fund reaches them appropriately."
Comrade Markus Magaji, national president, Cassava Produce Promoters & Exporters Association of Nigeria (CASPPEAN)
"The N500 million loan initiative is a good initiative. In fact, we have been at the forefront of this move because it would add impetus to the Federal Government's cassava empowerment initiative. Already, CASPPEAN is working in collaboration with our members, who are interested in assessing the loan, in preparation of feasibility studies which would be submitted to the Bank of Industry before the end of this month. Presently, we cannot meet the 300,000 metric tones of cassava flour as being demanded by flour millers; the international demand for cassava produce in addition to the local demand for garri with the current level of cassava produced in the country. But we believe the N500million loan would be a great boost towards realising this objective".
Alhaji Lateef Oguntoyinbo, national vice president, Association of Master Bakers & Caterers Of Nigeria
"We hope that the initiative will work. The Flour Millers have provided N500 million as revolving loan to cassava processors to produce enough cassava flour for the millers. There is a directive from the Federal Government that 10 per cent composite cassava flour should be included. So, ours is to buy the flour and use it for production for the masses. We have been buying flour from the millers and have been using it. We have no problem with the initiative"

Original_Search_Title: Nugu Season is Back
Headline: Uganda: Nugu Season is Back
Body_Text:
Kampala — YOU could say Ragga Dee started it all with the song Nugu off his Empeta album. But in his latest album, 2005 PAM Artiste of the Year Mesach Semakula proves that the spirit of nugu songs is far from dead. His song Ndeka Nkole Bwenjagala off his 2006 album First Aid is aimed at journalists who Mesach feels have unfairly victimised him since he became a big star standing out from Eagles Production.
It would seem the season of nugu songs is upon us again because Chagga, too, has one out called Nali Kubo that accuses Jose Chameleone of having used him as a stepping stone to fame. Red Banton is also reputed to be polishing off a song that attacks Chameleone for having 'used' him to get to where he is. Red Banton then goes on to allude that Chameleone has taken to buying horses because his fellow musicians who are human beings will no longer talk to him.
Earlier in the year, on their debut music album, the Obsessions also turned in a nugu song of sorts called Omuntu Tyagalika which was sung by the group's leader Ronnie Mulindwa. The song looked back to the old Obsessions and the members who deserted the group for various reasons and who thought the group would collapse without them.
However, when it comes to our three biggest male singers, the number of songs they have written denigrating others comes to a sub-genre in itself. But not only that: the reasons why they have continued to make veiled attacks at each other in their songs have evolved, depending on where they were at in their careers.
Although Bebe Cool has always claimed in interviews that he was the bigger star than Jose Chameleone back when they were both starting out their careers in Nairobi, Kenya, the fact is that Chameleone was the first to make it in Kampala with his hit single, Mamma Mia.
Bebe Cool can still bring himself to admit, "I decided to come home because I saw how Jose had made it by pushing one song for six months. Why couldn't I do the same?"
And as Bebe Cool confessed in another interview, nothing sells better than controversy, especially if you have nothing to lose and everything to gain. In this case, Bebe Cool teamed up with another upcoming singer going by the stage name of Bobi Wine and they came up with a song called Funtula expressly written to humiliate Chameleone.
They said that in the beginning, before he earned the nickname of "Cha-million" because he demanded a million shillings before he stepped on stage to perform, he was supported by a woman: the famous Dorotia in whose honour he even wrote a song. This very same woman who was the mother of his first child, had supported him by letting him live in her houses in Nairobi and in Kampala when Chameleone could not afford to pay his own rent, much less buy food.
Instead of disgracefully cornering the two singers who were besmirching his name as they would in Ange Noir in 2005, leading to a public outcry that artistes' egos had gone out of control, Chameleone turned the attacks on him into a money-spinner with a reply song called Nekolera Mali. The song become so popular that many taxis in Kampala painted it on their emergency windows as an apt motto for their trade.
After the fights that got Chameleone such bad press that he says, "Uganda was becoming too hot. I had to go for a holiday in the US where I had some performances to do," the tables turned. With their fame reaching career highs, suddenly Bobi Wine and Bebe Cool found themselves writing more nugu songs. Only this time, it was in reply, defending themselves.
Bobi Wine especially seems to have made it a signature, with nearly all his albums, right from his debut Kagoma to 2006's Omwana Wa Bandi all containing at least one nugu song that tries to reply his detractors. In the 2006 album, Kabaseke goes back to his roots to explain why success took so long to come to him unlike his great rival Chameleone. Bobi's defensiveness stems from the accusations that he became famous because of riding on the back of other artistes, especially Juliana Kanyomozi, through his duets.
In a very humorous song designed to answer all these accusations, Bobi says that while some artistes like Chameleone planted cassava in Kawempe, he was unfortunate in the type of plant he chose to cultivate in his own base in Kamwokya. He chose to grow matooke, never realising that matooke takes a very long time to sprout fruit, thus the long time it took before he became a household name with Taata wa bana in 2004. You could say these three singers have come full circle in their careers.

Original_Search_Title: I'd Like to Be Governor, Says Delta SSG
Headline: Nigeria: I'd Like to Be Governor, Says Delta SSG
Body_Text:
EVEN though he is yet to formally declare his aspiration, political watchers already know and not a few eyes are on him at the moment in Delta state as the beautiful bride in the attention-grabbing battle for Government House, Asaba in 2007. One way or another, possibly because of his predilection to bridge building, he cuts a dash with the key political leaders and the diverse power blocs in the decidedly political composite state.
Saturday Vanguard met the Secretary to the Delta State Government, Dr. Emmanuel Uduaghan, a consummate politician and one of the most dependable buddies of the Delta State Governor whose ambition or dream, about seven or eight years ago, was just to be a personal physician to the governor because many years ago, he not only admired but looked with awe at a senior colleague in the medical profession who was the personal physician to the former governor of the defunct Bendel State, the late Prof Ambrose Alli. Well, Ibori who is both his cousin and po litical mentor stunned him with the job of Commissioner for Health. As if the first surprise was not enough, the governor in his words: further challenged him with the strategically significant portfolio of SSG.
And behold, having tasted the political apple, the eyes of the University of Benin trained medical doctor has opened. He has become wiser. In this very hassle-free encounter in the oil city of Warri, Uduaghan, beginning with his life as a village boy at Mosogar town where he domiciled with his martinet grandmother, tells the story he has never told before about his growing up; his childhood days with the governor; the turning point in his life; and more. Excerpts:
I was a typical village boy
I grew up at Mosogar in Ethiope West local government area of Delta state with my grandmother who hailed from Oghara and Mosogar. She took me when I was just three years old from my parents and from then on, we were together. As for me, my childhood was quite interesting and I was a typical village boy at that time. I started my primary school there, I did not finish there for I moved to Ogharefe when I was in Pry 5 to stay with my auntie, late Mrs Comfort Ibori but most of my boyhood was in Mosogar. My father, whose father was Itsekiri, was a policeman and he used to move from place to place because he had two wives while my mother was from Esan in Edo State. My grandmother was fond of taking my father's children to live with her. I think I was the second person she took. My grandmother was a disciplinarian and that entailed a lot of things. First, she was very strict on punctuality. She made sure we were the first to get to school.
That discipline is in us. There were things we did then as children. She was a farmer and made sure she exposed us to farming. Sometimes, I use to wake up quite early and moved with other villagers to go and tap rubber. In the process of tapping rubber, you put a cup under it for the rubber latex to gather and you come back later to collect them into a bucket. If we were going to school that day, we don't go to collect the rubber; the owner of the rubber plantation would do that. Also, we cleared the farm plant cassava, uproot them when it was time and carry the tubers to the village for processing into garri. Since where I was living was close to the riverside, we were also involved in fishing after school. I lived a complete village life really- farming, fishing and all that. In the evenings, my grandmother gathered all of us that were living with her for a typical moonlight stories. She told us a lot of things about tortoise and all that.
Primary education was free in my days
Well, there was no time I faced difficulty in payment of school fees during my primary and secondary education. My primary education was virtually free, all your parents needed to do was maybe buy one or two textbooks and exercise books because it was free primary education then. When I finished at Oghara, I had to go and meet my uncle in Lagos whom I stayed with for one year. I took the common entrance for Government College, Ughelli, Academy in Sapele and others. Many of these schools admitted me, including the Saint Patricks College, Asaba but then, I passed the entrance examination for the federal government college and was first of all admitted to the Federal Government College, Sokoto but before I could travel to resume there, it was changed to Federal Government College, Warri. But even in the secondary school, I did not have problem with my school fees, first I have an uncle, the one next to my father who took over the payment of my school fees because my father had m any children. He paid my school fees even up to the university level.
The monkeyshines I played as a child
The kind of trouble I was always getting into with my grandmother was sometimes not being in the house when I was supposed to or at times, playing at a time she considered too wrong or maybe I disappeared to go and swim because I loved swimming. My grandmother had this very long cane with which she used to flog anybody including teachers. One day I went to the school, I was the mail boy and as the mail boy, I used to go the Post Office at Jesse to pick up letters. I trek there say once in a month to collect letters and because I was picking mails, I became interested in collection of stamps so when I brought the mails, as you are taking your letter, I will collect the stamp and because of that, I got some addresses. I got to know that one can write for catalogues so I was just writing various companies and they were sending me several letters but all I was interested was the stamps.
There was an occasion I did not go to the post office and the headmaster sent somebody there and the person went and brought the letters to him. He saw a lot of letters for me and the next thing, he brought me out in front of the assembly and said I should stand on top of the table and was very angry that I was writing letters to companies for businesses. I did not quite understand his anger because all I was interested in was the stamps and he did not give me the opportunity to explain. He flogged me that morning in the school and I when I got home, I told my grandmother because when they flog you in the school, you must report at home and if the teacher was right in flogging you, she would now flog you her own. So we used to have double punishment for any offence committed in the school.
I never expected I would become SSG
God has been very gracious to me. I never even expected I will become the Secretary to the Delta State Government (SSG), I did not even expect I will become the Commissioner for Health. I just wanted to be a good doctor, a family man with a wife and not many children. That is why I have only two children: a girl and a boy. I never wanted to have a large family. Actually, after my first degree, I wanted to read gynecology, I had done the primary and was in the Delta Steel Company (DSC), Ovwian-Aladja then but I changed my mind somewhere and did anesthesia, which was a shorter course. I had to leave the DSC at a time because I was not happy with what I was seeing there because the facilities there became appalling and the company could no longer afford the things we use to work with. I actually started a clinic, which was gradually coming up before politics came in.
I am not a millionaire. I'm just a government official
You are asking me when I made my first N1 million, did I tell you I have N1 million (laughs), I don't have the million you are talking. So I cannot talk of remembering when I made my first million, I am not a millionaire in the first place (laughs again).
So what are you if you are not a millionaire?
I am just a government official. My salary is not up to one million. Anyway, as for my first car, it was a Volkswagen Beetle car. I had just finished from the medical school in 1980 and gone for housemanship. I had this uncle, late Baba Aladura, Dr. G.I. Otubu, he was the head of the Eternal Sacred Order of the Cherubim and Seraphim whom I visited at that time and he said, doctor, I mean? I was virtually the second doctor in my family. When I finished my discussion with him, he asked me, doctor, how did you come and I told him I came by public transport. And he shouted, no, how can I be using public transport. He said no, no, no, I should go to Sapele to see one of my aunties. By the time I got to Sapele the next week, there was a cheque waiting for me and it was a cheque that could fetch me a brand new beetle. So, my first car was a brand new Beetle, BD 5848 BB. That was in 1980. It was a car that was with me for quite a long time.
Emergence of a political bridge-builder
It is the handiwork of God. He used the present governor of Delta State, Chief James Onanefe Ibori. When I left DSC and was doing my private practice, I was not really into politics although I was politically conscious; I would say I was an armchair critic. I knew a lot about politics but I was not attending political meetings Chief Ibori was in Abuja, Lagos, you know what I mean. He was the one that really wanted to be in politics and when we discussed, he told me he was going into politics. This was during the late Abacha era and I said okay. So down the way, I now joined the All Nigeria Congress. When it came to the time for registration, he called me from Abuja and said that place you are in now is not going to be registered. He said that five parties were going to be registered and that I should go into the defunct GDM. Of course, in everything I was doing in politics, it was mostly according to his direction. But for those of us who were in GDM, he gathered us together and said that was not the end and we should plan to see how we could be part of the party that was going to emerge. That was how we became the founding members of the PDP in the state and he was our leader in PDP. And while there, as he (Ibori) was moving, I was also moving with him and so when he became the governor, knowing who I was, because we grew up together, he challenged me with the Ministry of Health, which I handled to the best of my ability and after that, he challenged me again with the office of the SSG.
You said you and the governor grew up together?
Yes, yes, we grew up together
You were playing together or both of you schooled together?
No, we are actually cousins to each other. We practically grew up in the same house with my grandmother and later with the mother at Oghara, but we grew up in the same place.
As your grandmother flogged you at times for some misbehavior, what was the governor being flogged for?
Ha, ha, ha, ha, when you see him, ask him.
Turning point in my life
It was when I had to leave the DSC because it was a decision that I took without consulting anybody. I just got married then and my wife had our first child, I did not even consult her. The work became very boring to me and the final nail was the day I went to our main office and the kind of things I saw was very annoying. I just told myself that I could not continue with this kind of job anymore. I simply told her the decision I had taken when I came home and when she asked me where are we moving to, I told her that God would provide. It was a turning point in my life in the sense that I now had to face life squarely. The other one was when I was appointed Commissioner for Health because really, when Chief James Ibori won as governor, I never thought I would be Commissioner, I never lobbied for it, and I was more interested in continuing my profession in government. All I wanted was to be the personal physician to the governor. It was something I have always dreamt of becomi ng.
When the late Prof Ambrose Alli was the governor of the defunct Bendel state, he had a personal physician whom I knew and I was always admiring him from a distance. So it was actually the personal physician to the governor that I wanted to be. But the governor called me when he was putting together his team, he was calling people one by one and told me that there are two ministries he is very interested in- Ministry of Health and Ministry of Education and that he wanted to put people that he knew and trusted to handle the two ministries. He told me that he knew my ability because we grew up together and he knew what I could do. So he told me he was going to entrust me with the Ministry of Health and so, I would say I was one of the persons who knew the ministry I was going to even before I was appointed. That again was another turning point in my life.
God is my talisman
I neither wear talismans nor consult oracles. God is the Person that has put me in the position I have attained today so I fear Him. And He is the only one I fear, I dont fear any creature made by God because he is not greater than me. I however only respect people. God created all of us equal. So my fear is only for God because He is the One that has exalted me and the only One that can reduce me. God has monopoly over life and death, He can make you succeed or fail, He can do or undo, He has control over me and He is the only One that commands my worship and adoration. I try as much as possible to be what He wants me to be but of course, as human beings, we have our human weaknesses. So, I serve him, I worship, I pray to him, I consult him, I ask him questions, I seek His direction and counsel when I want to do things. He also gave me a very prayerful wife, she is very prayerful, and she intercedes a lot on my behalf. My mother was also doing that when she was alive. She is dead now for about three years and I also have a mother-in-law who is prayerful.
A look ahead
Yes, all these years, I have hung my stethoscope and is still hanging until after 2007 but one thing I know I miss a lot is my medical practice. From my plans at present, it is not likely I am prepared to go back immediately. If I have to go to medical practice, it would be more as an administrator, not mainly clinical practice.

Original_Search_Title: Disbursement of N50b Agric Loan Begins in Sokoto
Headline: Nigeria: Disbursement of N50b Agric Loan Begins in Sokoto
Body_Text:
The disbursement of the N50 billion agricultural loans introduced by the federal government this year, commenced on Tuesday in Sokoto State.
During the exercise, more than N98 million was distributed to successful farmers in the state.
The loan scheme is being managed by the Nigeria Agricultural, Cooperatives and Rural Development Bank. (NACRBD)
Speaking at the ceremony to mark the disbursement of the loans, Gov. Attahiru Bafarawa said that the state government had paid N200 million as its counterpart contribution to the loans scheme.
Bafarawa, who was represented by the Commissioner for Agriculture, Alhaji Sadiq Sanyinna, said government had also set up a multi-sectorial committee to oversee the implementation of the scheme.
He said the implementation committee was headed by the Deputy Governor, Alhaji Garba Mohammed.
The governor warned the beneficiaries against misusing the money and urged them to repay the loan promptly.
The chairman of the All Farmers Association (AFAN) in the state, Sen. Bello Gada, said that the first batch of the beneficiaries were mainly cassava farmers.
According to him, no fewer than 10,000 farmers, belonging to about 98 farmers' cooperative groups and societies registered for the scheme in the state.
Gada said that some of the commercial banks were "foot-dragging" in the release of their counterpart contributions to the scheme and urged the federal government to prevail on them to comply.
Each of the benefiting groups and societies was given between N500,000 and N2 million, depending on the number of farmers in the groups.

Original_Search_Title: 300 Farmers' Groups Share N300m in FCT
Headline: Nigeria: 300 Farmers' Groups Share N300m in FCT
Body_Text:
Abuja — NATIONAL Fadama Implementation Agency (NFIA) on Tuesday said it has successfully coordinated 320 Fadama User Groups in the Federal Capital Territory (FCT), Abuja.
National Co-ordinator of FCT Fadama Implementation Agency, Dr. Mike Awazie at a news conference in Abuja stated that over N300 million has so far been disbursed to such groups in the area.
According to him, "owing to the need to improve on the living conditions and farming strategy of the rural farmers in FCT, we went into the rural communities and with the assistance of World Bank, which granted us US $7 million, we have been able to touch on the lives of these farmers positively."
Citing over 170 projects located in various rural communities in Abuja, Chief Awazie stated that "the various Fadama User Groups now have value added to them through their produce from which they source their daily sustenance."
He explained that apart from sensitizing the farmers on modern methods of Fadama farming techniques, his agency provides 90 per cent of the needed funds, while the benefiting groups provided only 10 per cent of the funds.
"With these funds, we have been able to provide 92 Fadama Coordination Groups with needed funds and facilities as well as 800 Motorized Pumps in parts of FCT.
"In addition, we had to assist farmers in Abaji, Yabu, Waku, Ashaha, Gwagwa, Kujw, Rubochi, Karshi, Municipal and Bwari Fadama Centers with these facilities ranging from access roads to their farms and motorized hand pumps for the irrigation of their farms.
"Similarly, we have provided hinters with the technical know-how to cultivate grass cutters, to access fish ponds, poultry, cassava processing machines and other facilities, which helped in no small measure in uplifting their income. Beggars, who initially were roaming the streets of Abuja, now have acquired skills, which made them productive and kept them off the road", he emphasised.

Original_Search_Title: LRA Ambush Ambulance
Headline: Uganda: LRA Ambush Ambulance
Body_Text:
Kampala — REBELS of the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) yesterday afternoon ambushed and set ablaze an ambulance belonging to Kalongo missionary hospital.
It had drugs and other medical supplies destined for Kalongo hospital in Pader district, the army said.
Northern army spokesman Lt. Chris Magezi told journalists in his Gulu office the rebels opened fire on an ambulance UG 177M, at about 2:30pm.
"About six medics who were on board jumped out of the ambulance and fled in the bush unhurt and were rescued by UPDF soldiers who were nearby. The rebels burnt the ambulance and looted some drugs," Magezi said.
He said the ambush took place at Pacer village on the Pader-Kalongo road.
Magezi said the rebels were led by LRA's Lt. Col. Okuti, who is still roaming Pader district with his troops.
"Our troops are pursuing these attackers and we hope they will be punished in due course," he added.
He said on Saturday the rebels ambushed and killed a cyclist identified as Dennis Ocan near Kitgum high school and stripped him naked.
Magezi said the UPDF 23rd battalion in Amiel sub- county, Pader district, recovered six sacks of cassava that the rebels had dropped.
"If the remnants of the LRA who are in northern Uganda are still carrying out these barbaric acts on the civilians, what moral authority does the LRA leader of their peace team, Martin Ojur, have to talk about unilateral ceasefire? They are busy making ridiculous and unrealistic demands in Juba yet the remnants continue to commit atrocities. An ambulance is a protected vehicle in conflict areas used to attend to the sick," he said.

Original_Search_Title: MCA for E/R Targets Irrigation Agriculture
Headline: Ghana: MCA for E/R Targets Irrigation Agriculture
Body_Text:
Koforidua — THE EASTERN Regional Minister, Mr. Yaw Barimah has disclosed that funds that would be allocated to the Region under the Millennium challenge Account (MCA) programme, would be used to support farmers to ensure all year production through irrigation.
According to him, the agriculture programme under the MCA, would be implemented in the Afram Plains, Kwahu South, Fanteakwa, Yilo Krobo and Manya Krobo where beneficiary farmer's would be supported to achieve the objectives behind the MCA Mr. Barimah disclosed this when he met Eastern Regional Journalists at the Regional "meet the press" at Koforidua recently.
He said more than 6000 farmers have been selected to cultivate about 3000 hectares of land of crops, including maize, yams, cassava, vegetables, groundnut pineapples, mangoes and banana.
About the bad nature of roads in these farming Districts which serves as contributing factor to large sum of food crops getting bad, The Regional Minister assured that roads would be constructed to open up the Afram plains 24 hours for transportation of humans and food.
The roads, he noted, would be constructed to connect Konongo in the Ashanti Region and certain towns in the Volta Region as well.
He said, the 2005 farming season saw a significant growth in crop production in the Region with maize at 263.5 metric ton, Rice 26.4, cassava 3.040, plantain 700, cocoyam 414.8 and yam 646.0 tons, all-exceeding 94% in production.
However he was not happy that livestock such as cattle, sheep, goat and poultry have decreased in production in the rate of 70% due to diseases and pest infestations. Accordingly, under the farmers Based Organization Programme, the Ministry of Food and Agriculture (MOFA) has facilitated the formation of 100 farmer's groups and about ¢ 750.0 Million has already been expended on the programme.
An amount of ¢ 237,880,000 Million allocated for these groups project was used to support 38 seed growers for the 2005 - 2006 farming season he revealed.
For relief agencies supporting farmers the regional minister mentioned the Adventists Development and Relief Agency (ADRA) for supporting farmers in twelve districts in the Agro - forestry business.
Additionally, it has also provided communities with bore holes, storage facilities and credit and so far 9,865 farmers comprising 7,273 males and 2592 females have benefited from this package, chronicle learnt.
Meanwhile, the Regional Minister has admitted that it was unfortunate the sports facilities in the Region lacked the basic sporting amenities.
However, he was quick to disclose that, plans were far advanced to develop the three stadia in the Region by providing electricity, water, urinals dressing rooms and seats for spectators at Koforidua, Nkawkaw and Oda Sports stadia.
He also disclosed that his outfit has been holding meetings with football associations to develop strategies that would result in formation of football clubs that would be based in the Region and compete eventually compete in the premiership football division.

Original_Search_Title: Taraba: Lame duck at 15
Headline: Nigeria: Taraba: Lame duck at 15
Body_Text:
Jalingo — Taraba State will be exactly fifteen years old on August 27. Ordinarily, it calls for celebration but alas, 15 years after its creation; it still remains a lame duck that cannot walk on its feet! There is virtually no single functional infrastructure in the entire state despite the generosity of the monthly federal allocation to the state. On a few counts, we have no roads, no potable water, no functional hospital and clinics and worst of all, the vibrancy of our people in agricultural activities is slowly and painfully being killed by our leaders in government. Have we taken the path of actualising our aspirations, ambitions and hopes for a better life as government was brought closer to the people 15 years ago? Is it worth celebrating?
Out of our 15 years of statehood, nine were dominated by the one and only civilian governor ever produced in the state, Reverend Jolly Nyame while the other six years witnessed four different military administrations. An attempt was made by the regime of Navy Captain Ambrose Afolahan and Col. A. A. Awoniyi to find a direction to the path of development, but the state was short-changed by time as these two military officers could not stay long to put the state on an unshakable path of development. However, despite their short stay, they constructed official and residential quarters, which are up to date housing the state workforce.
Our nine years of democratic experience can be likened to a stillborn child. We voted for our own people to represent and protect our interests, but we were paid back with poverty, hunger and ill-health. Our democratic years were a complete waste. Administration was left in the hands of juveniles who mortgaged our future and that of our grandchildren to bank loans and overdrafts.
It is only in Taraba State that 15 years after its creation, young men who served and those still serving in the Nyame administration are far richer than the state government. They can boast of big and well-furnished mansions which are by far better than the government house. Their fleets of flashy cars are no match for government vehicles and most often compete with the governor's official cars. The visit by the EFFC to the state revealed the big boys with fat accounts while the state coffers are always in red and overdrawn with bank facilities. It is rumoured that they choose party time which is a common feature in the government house to present memoranda to the governor when he is at his peak to which he always appends his signature without reading. Those in that clique become millionaires overnight at the expense of our brothers and sisters who live in penury. Like a huge joke, however, Rev. Jolly Nyame who disappointed God and the people is now backing Danladi Baido, hi s Commissioner for Finance to succeed him. Baido, apart from being green in administration, has a shrouded educational background. Just a little over 30 years old and unmarried, Baido has great flare for night partying like his boss.
But are we cursed? Has the magnanimity of giving us our own state turned to a sin that we should be in the bondage of spoilers? The names of Ambassador Idris Waziri, Alhaji Ahmed Yusuf, Kabiru Hamayaji and Senator Abdul-Azeez Ibrahim featuring on the gubernatorial list are not far from the Nyames and Baidos.
