Most of these characters were beneficiaries and identified with Nyame's style of administration. Some of them failed to attract federal presence to the state despite their closeness to the federal government. In fact, not even the cassava planting programme. As we recollect (celebrate?) our 15 years of experience, there are great men we should pay tribute to for their personal and individual contributions. One of such is Alhaji Gidado Abubakar. Alhaji Abubakar was a seasoned administrator who brought his experience to the state shortly after its creation. He was the engine room of the Afolahan administration that saw massive construction of structures in just four months.
He also deserves commendation for his reconciliatory role during the Tiv/Jukun conflicts, the Kuteb/Chamba carnage and the Tiv/Fulani imbroglio. I recall with nostalgia the day he wept openly before the late Governor Abubakar Barde urging him as an elder statesman to summon elite meeting to halt the senseless killing across the state. He did not see any difference between himself, a Muslim Fulani with the Christian Jukuns, Kutebs, Chambas and Tiv ethnic groups. He remained a bridge-builder who saw everyone as part of him. It will be too hasty for elders like us to forget his days as the director of sports in the defunct Gongola State. He introduced youths to sports, some of whom gained professional employment in Nigeria, Europe and the U.S. To date, they attribute their success to the vision of Gidado Abubakar for shaping their lives.
Although we have lost so much in the last years, but we cannot continue to be hopeless; we still have ample opportunity to progress and the time has come for our T. Y. Danjumas, Professor Sa'ad Abubakars, Obadiah Andos, Alhaji Ibrahim Tumbas, Yakuba Tukurs and other elite, as well as the traditional institutions, youth and women associations, the academia and business tycoons to shop for credible and conscientious leaders like Gidado Abubakar to recover our nine wasted years.
As we turn 15, the 2007 election is giving us another opportunity to redefine our future as a people. It is time we forgot about religion, ethnicity and even political party and agree on who we can entrust the future of the state to. We must stand up against these agents of corruption, wastage and ineptitude trying to drag us to yet another perpetual bondage and state of hopelessness.

Original_Search_Title: In Osun, New Grounds in Poverty Alleviation
Headline: Nigeria: In Osun, New Grounds in Poverty Alleviation
Body_Text:
Lagos — The partnership between the Osun State Government and the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) has caught the attention of the private sector, with small scale entreprenuers as the beneficiaries
For industrialists and agro-allied experts in Osun State, it is yet another opportunity to break new grounds, with new opportunities provided by the UNDP -Osun State Sustainable Human Development Fund (OSSHDF). Apparently spurred by the need to see Osun State developed, Governor Olagunsoye Oyinlola sometime in December 2003, inaugurated the board of trustees of the UNDP-OSSHDF. The board, which is an assemblage of prominent indigenes of the state, was charged with mobilising and sourcing for fund from government, individuals and the private sector "towards meeting the objectives of the then sixth country programme of the UNDP." This include to transform the state industrially, combat poverty and improve quality of life among the people.
The board is chaired by Mr. Segun Aina, former managing director and chief executive officer of defunct Fountain Trust Bank and former President, West African Bankers Association (WABA) (Nigerian Chapter). He is also the second vice-president of the Chartered Institute of Bankers in Nigeria (CIBN).
Shortly after inauguration, the board went into action. In February, 2004, a fund-raising was held in Oshogbo. Over N250 million was realised with the state government contributing N150 million. Not long after, another fund-raising was held at the Miccom Golf Hotels, Ada, Osun State. This phase was targeted at involving the organised private sector, individuals, corporate bodies and charity organisations.
A whopping N100 million was raised in cash, cheques and pledges. Another N200 million was expected through institutional/corporate pledges through the Small and Medium Enterprises Equity Investment Scheme (SMEEIS)'s programme of banks, a report by the board stated. The focal point of the second fund raising were the organised private sector, individuals, corporate bodies, companies, charity organisations and non-governmental organisations.
To demonstrate his commitment to the cause, Oyinlola announced the donation of his salary for a year. The fund-raising train later moved out of the state. The first point of call was Lagos where some funds were raised for the project on December 16, 2004. Barely a year later, another fund-raising was held in Abuja. That was precisely on August 10, 2005. Again, the governor pledged his annual salary for the project.
So far, over N350 million has been realised. Of this amount, about N250 million is said to have been committed to various ongoing projects and programmes.
Already, the UNDP-OSSHDF is quietly providing the needed boost for entrepreneurs and industrialists in the state. One of such is the UNDP - OSSHDF micro credit/middle level micro-credit in November 2004.
Also, on the occasion of the 2004 International Day for the Eradication of Poverty in Osogbo, "N30 million in cheques were distributed to three leading micro finance institutions (MFIS) in the state.
Equally, over 400,000 people in the 30 local government areas of the state were said to have benefited from the fund disbursed through the institutions. The second phase of the scheme has since started. According to the board's chairman this phase "is targeted at individuals and companies whose businesses require funds of not more than N500,000."
As a result of the successes recorded, the board has decided to put in more money into the scheme. Another sum of N30 million to be spread to beneficiaries in every local government area is to be injected by the board for the middle level micro credit scheme.
In addition to this, the board has also disbursed N60 million to 600 cassava farmers across the state. The farmers belong to the Cassava Growers Association structured on local government basis. This arrangement made the disbursement and monitoring of the scheme effective.
Similarly, plans are said to be in top gear to include rice and fish farming projects in the UNDP- OSSHDF agricultural funding programme. The aim is to use the combination of all the programmes to "propel economic empowerment, additional employment and industrial growth within the state."
Also to give its programmes the scientific foundation and as a follow-up to the pledge by bank officials present at the launch of the development fund at Ada, that the financial institutions would support Osun State-based industries and businesses to access fund meant for SMEIS, the board is said to have commissioned the Centre for Industrial Research and Development (CIRD), Obafemi Awolowo University, Ile -Ife to organise seminars for small-scale industrialists in the state. Various topics relating to how SMEs can be developed in an accelerated manner in Osun State will be discussed.
Such issues include easy access to funding, investment opportunities and managerial skills necessary for SME's success.
A novel strategy to encourage the well-to-do and organisations in all communities to assist small-scale entrepreneurs, is the introduction of collaborative community focused micro-credit scheme where individuals and organisations within a particular community are expected to put down an amount not below N500,000 to be distributed within a given community as short-term micro-credit and later recycled to other beneficiaries within the same community.
To kick-start the project, whose fund is managed by the board's accredited Micro Finance Institutions (MFI's), some members of the board have belled the cat. For instance, the Segun Aina Foundation has announced the contribution of N1 million for beneficiaries from Otan Ayegbaju where Aina hails from.
Also, Chief Olu Falomo, another prominent member of the board has also made an announcement of N500,000 to be shared among those interested in his Ifosan community in Ilesa. The secretary of the board, Mrs. Iyabode Opeyokun, on her part, has expressed desire to make available the sum of N250,000 for use in Ijebu - Jesa.
The board, in its bid to raise N1billion for the scheme has appealed to corporate donors which have been identifying with poverty alleviation programmes such as Nigerian Breweries, MTN Foundation, V-Mobile and others for support and collaboration.

Original_Search_Title: 5,000 Families Benefit From Agricultural Project in Luanda
Headline: Angola: 5,000 Families Benefit From Agricultural Project in Luanda
Body_Text:
Luanda — At least 5,000 peasant families of Luanda province have benefited from agricultural tools, in the ambit of the rural extension programme meant to reduce famine and poverty, in the 2005/2007 period, Angop learned.
The five-year programme will be run by the Institute of Agrarian Development and support from the Provincial Department of the Ministry of Agriculture and is to be implemented in the districts of Cacuaco and Viana.
The head of the Department of Agriculture and Rural Development, Gaspar Antonio de Freitas, said the first phase of the programme involved Akz 36,167,338 worth of seeds, fertilizers, generators, pumps and mills. One dollars is beign rated about Akz 80,00.
Gaspar de Freitas added that tilled area of the two districts represent 9,316 hectares, although there is also cultivation of cassava in Samba and Kilamba-Kiaxi districts.
Meanwhile, the source considered as poor last year's production.

Original_Search_Title: Cafe Viva's Rice is Worth a Try
Headline: Uganda: Cafe Viva's Rice is Worth a Try
Body_Text:
I first made the acquaintance of Dr. Vali Jamal almost a couple of years ago in the company of a fellow who was slated to be his partner. As providence turned out they parted ways with the chap turning up to be a cad and a duplicitous sort of person.
It is an ill wind that blows nobody any good and the departure of the fellow turned out to be a blessing in disguise for since he Jamal was now forced to sink or swim. The former was never an option and today Café Viva is alive and well and holding it own admirably.
What is surprising is that a Trinity economist decided to become a restaurateur and in Uganda no less! But then again Jamal is not an airborne "parachute" Asian Johnny Come lately.
His grandfather was already ensconced in Arua at the turn of the last century circa 1904.
Starting a restaurant in Uganda of all places is at best a daunting task by any accounts and it has turned to be an arduous quest that has by no means been easy sailing.
Fortunately Jamal having travelled in over 55 countries while working with the ILO has given him a great deal of exposure to different culinary fare and this is amply reflected in the menu at his place. Needless to say he has encountered many a sticky wicket, but with indomitable stamina and courage the worst is behind and Café Viva is slowly making its mark upon Kampala diners. It has greately improved.
The menu is eclectic in nature and for those of us who were there at the start, by all accounts the standard of the food has greatly improved since the inception. Jamal is regularly in the kitchen and while I was there he whipped up a great penne with cilantro pesto that I found to be simply scrumptious.
I am told that Bebe Cool is a regular and on the 26th they will be doing an "Oldies" night with an Indian-African fashion show.
With three floors including a garden and a balcony that is buzzing with revellers in the evening, the potential for growth and improvement is tremendous.
Rating

These views are purely the reviewer's personal reaction to food, ambience and service with price being factored into consideration. Bear in mind that the listings and prices are subject to change without notice.
Place: Café Viva
Rating: XX
Location: Kampala Road, next to Crane Chambers opposite City Sqaure.
prices: Breakfast special 7am - 10 am omelet, toast and tea 2,500/-Starters and snacks available throughout the day from 500/- for cassava chips to 2,000/- for a pizza slice, salads, soups and sandwiches between 4,000/- and 4,500/-, Traditional food from 2,500/- for vegetarian fare to 5,000/- for chicken stew with all the accompaniments. Continental dishes are between 3,500/- for Mexican chapatti with chips to 5,500/- for fish fillet (Nile perch) with chips and salad. Café Viva also offers Indian food for around the 4,000/- mark with curry of the day costing 7,000/-.Stir fries such as gingered chicken are 8,500/- and desserts go for 6,000/-.
Recommended dishes:Penne with cilantro, pilao, tandoori chicken.
Service: The staff appears eager to please.
Ambience: Room for great improvement.
Parking: Problematic during the day otherwise on the street with guards who look after ones vehicle.
Prices: Pretty much on the money
Open: Seven days until late.
Sound Level: With three floors from which to choose you are bound to find a quiet area.
Reservations: Not necessary

Original_Search_Title: Female Agriculture Extension Officers Needed in Volta
Headline: Ghana: Female Agriculture Extension Officers Needed in Volta
Body_Text:
The Volta Regional Director of Women in Agriculture Development of the Ministry of Food and Agriculture (WIAD), Mrs. Lucy Selormey, has observed that the formation of women groups in the Volta Region to embark on agriculture activities has made significant improvement in their living standards.
She announced that there were 80 different women groups working in areas such as farming, food processing, fish-mongering among others, being supported by the Social Investment Fund, which provides the groups with infrastructure and financial support through rural banks.
Mrs. Selormey made this known at a one-day sensitization programme on effective strategies in enhancing small-scale businesses and marketing techniques for the Peki-Dzake Miwoenenyo Women Gari Processing Group last Thursday, organized by WIAD in collaboration with Dove Development Organization; an NGO in Peki-Dzake in the South Dayi District of the Volta Region.
Continuing, she expressed concern about lack of adequate Women Extension Officers in the region to work with the various women groups, adding that it was more ideal for women instead of men extension officers to assist women in their agriculture activities because the women groups would be more serious and more comfortable dealing with their own kind, and urged parents to encourage their female children to avail themselves to be trained as agriculture officers.
Mrs. Selormey noted that the formation of the group system by women to embark on productive ventures has started to make an impact in the lives of women in the region as poverty levels of women would reduce drastically and also reduce the urban drift in search of jobs.
She however advised women groups in the region to observe sound hygienic practices in their activities and reminded them that the health needs of thousands of people largely depended on the type of food and the environment and conditions under which food items were prepared and sold to the public.
She asked them to act as agents of change through their activities in their communities.
A government appointee at the South Dayi District Assembly, Madam Happy Bansah, commended the Miwoenenyo Women's Gari Processing Group for their efforts to improve their standards of living and cautioned them against divisive tendencies like gossiping and other unhealthy practices, which were likely to destroy the group.
She however remarked that the NEPAD school-feeding programme, from which Peki-Dzake schools have been selected to benefit, was welcome news to the Miwoenenyo Women's Gari Processing Group.
She said the poverty reduction component of the NEPAD feeding programme made it possible for the community to be the source of supply of food to the programme, adding that as a recognized group, they would be supplying gari to the feeding programme.
The chairperson of the Peki-Dzake Women's Group, Mrs. Victoria Akrong, admitted that even though they were striving hard to improve on their standards of living, they were not ready to meet market demands due to the fact that the association lacked the necessary equipment to support them in their activities.
She disclosed that they had one cassava-processing machine and said this contributed to low production level of the Group.
According to Mrs. Akrong, the Group could have processed between 14 and 23 bags of gari a month instead of the current 10 to 11 bags that was currently their limit.
She therefore appealed to government, NGOs and individuals to help them purchase the relevant equipments to boost their production.
The chairman of the Dove Development Organization, Mr. Sefiamor Bekui, said his organization would continue to give the needed assistance to self-help women groups in order to help eradicate poverty among women in their region.
Mr. Bekui announced plans to acquire land for the Peki-Dzake Women's Gari Processing Group to enable them start a farm to cultivate their own cassava, and said the Dove Development Organization had so far supported 28 women groups in the region.

Original_Search_Title: Monarch Gives Panacea to N/ Delta Problems
Headline: Nigeria: Monarch Gives Panacea to N/ Delta Problems
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — Abrogation of the Land Use Act, which makes all land in the country crown lands has been described as the only solution to agitations in the Niger Delta, since it would open the way for oil-bearing communities to benefit directly from resources in their land.
Head of Egi Kingdom, His Royal Highness Eze Elem Chuku, said this during the celebration of the new yam festival and cleansing of the land ceremony, to herald peace and the beginning of the traditional calendar.
Chuku blamed the Federal Government for problems in the Niger Delta, saying it was wrong to tag the youths restive, while asking for their rights.
He said if not for the intervention of Elf petroleum which is giving them certain things, the community would not have had anything, after bearing the brunt of exploitation and exploration, pointing out that participation of the firm in their festival was commendable.
He queried the rationale behind the law which makes owners of land only claimants of the ground not beyond six feet of it, pointing out that if government could make such laws, one day indigenes would wake up to discover that they can no longer plant cassava and yams, because a law has been passed.
The youths are not restive. What is left to a man when all his liberty is lost? I want to do this and they say no way and when you cry out they say you are restive. How am I restive? They know what they are doing; the mineral is in their soil. Other hard and substantial mineral like cement, gold and potash and other things there are no land decree on the. It was when they saw oil in the Niger Delta that they promulgated land decree which has stopped us from owning six feet of this land. How can you see this and say man should not talk? It is wickedness.
ìAny leader that will change the idea of six feet on the ground will go to heaven. The law is evil. We are not the first to strike oil, all over the world whoever owns the land owns what is in it. Why must our own different? If the boys are worried, they donÃ­t go to school and they donÃ­t have work. The youths are not restive, give them what they want and they will no longer protestî, he said.
The royal father said he does not like fights which claim their youths of the area because they may have potentials of being very important and well placed individuals which will help the community in future.
He assured that those who have their hands in such unjust laws would reap their benefits of wickedness, pointing out that what was happening to most of them which they blame on evil was due to their past deeds.
Asked of his views about the spate of assassination, he replied that those so killed deserve it because of what they may have done in their past life, adding, ìBoth the assassin and the assassinated are all playing their roles which God said are goodî.

Original_Search_Title: Kampala's Smoking Cars
Headline: Uganda: Kampala's Smoking Cars
Body_Text:
Kampala — Kampala's traffic produces more toxic smoke than the average coffee shop in Amsterdam. It is more chaotic than 15 old taxi parks combined in an area half the size of the current place.
It is more dangerous than a blind Boda-Boda driver on a 3000cc motorcycle. It follows less rules than an anarchist rebel stuck on a deserted island and it moves slower than a crippled turtle. All these things make the overall traffic system in Kampala function less efficient than a child who is trying to staple jelly to the living room ceiling.
The result of this chaotic, lawless, dangerous mess is the cost of many lives, the obstruction of efficient commercial transportation, the heavy pollution of the Ugandan sky and the reason for all Ugandans to be at least one hour late for their appointments in Kampala.
Is there a sustainable solution besides turning the roads of Kampala into one massive golf course? I believe there is. Bicycles. Bicycles are safe, bicycles are clean, bicycles are cheap, bicycles don't take up a lot of space, bicycles don't use petrol and riding a bicycle is a good exercise and will help Ugandans to burn off their carbohydrate overdose indulged during their heavy matooke- posho - kalo - lumondi - rice - cassava - lunch.
How do we get Kampala on the bicycle? I propose all government officials to set an example for the rest. The introduction of bicycles for political leaders will create a huge saving in the annual budget normally spent on government vehicles. This spare money can be invested in re-construction of roads with special lanes for bicycles.
What about the people who now find their monthly income in the transportation sector? The taxi drivers could start bicycle repair shops. The Boda Boda drivers could open up bicycle parking venues and the drivers of the government vehicles could switch from driving cars to massaging government officials' legs after they have been riding their bikes all day from meeting to meeting.
One last problem remains, how do we get government officials to move their big butts out of their big cars? The answer is simple. They will receive special bicycles. Bicycles that will give them the status they deserve, bicycles that will give them the comfort they are used to and bicycles that will distinguish them from the rest of the people, just as they have always put themselves above the rest of the people.
Their bicycles will be painted in the colours of the national flag. Their bicycles will have an extra soft seat and their bicycles will even have silver bells. And the president? The president should get an extra special one. Maybe one with a golden bell, or a even a little parasol, to protect him from the sun, but most importantly, the little flag that normally flows in the wind on the front of his Mercedes, will proudly represent his concern for the wellbeing of Uganda from the back of his bicycle.
The author is a Master student, Faculty of Social Science, Free University of Amsterdam

Original_Search_Title: Kitara Secondary School Closed
Headline: Uganda: Kitara Secondary School Closed
Body_Text:
KITARA Senior Secondary School, one of the oldest school in Bunyoro Kitara Kingdom, was on Tuesday closed and students sent home following an abrupt decision by the school's Board of Governor's meeting held at the school.
The abrupt closure of the school follows Monday's strike where students destroyed two houses belonging to Mr Patrick Kugonza, a neighbour of the school.
The school and Kugonza are fighting for 10 acres of land, with each party claiming ownership.
The students on Monday held a demonstration, which ended up being violent, accusing Kugonza of grabbing the school land, saying it was unacceptable to them.
The students led by the school prefect, Mr Denis Sseguya, first marched to the streets of Hoima town in protest and eventually attacked Kugonza's houses. They cut down gardens of cassava, sweet potato, beans and eucalyptus trees.
When Daily Monitor visited the school the headmaster, Mr Christopher Byenkya, confirmed the closure following a meeting chaired by the school chairman Board of Governors, the Rev. Can. David Kaahwa.
Byenkya said 850 students were sent home after the school authorities discovered that the students' continued stay at the school would cause more destruction to both the school and the community around.
Byenkya said the students would report back to school for the third term which commences on September 18 according to the school academic calendar. The official closing date of this term is today. When contacted by Daily Monitor, Kugonza said he had reported the matter to the police.
"I have a list of students who participated in the demolition of my property and it has been handed over to Hoima Police for better management," Kugonza said.
The Hoima District Police Commander, Mr Charles Imalingat, confirmed the destruction of properties and closure of the school.
"We have heavily deployed at the school because we suspect students will come back and destroy property. Investigations are under way to bring the culprits to book," Imalingat said.

Original_Search_Title: 'I'm Overwhelmed to Receive Mr. President'
Headline: Nigeria: 'I'm Overwhelmed to Receive Mr. President'
Body_Text:
Lagos — It was not a tea party affair when President Olusegun Obasanjo visited Akwa Ibom State last week. As soon as he arrived the state through Margaret Ekpo International Airport, Calabar, work commenced in earnest as he commissioned and inspected a lot of projects embarked upon by the Akwa Ibom State Government. Even the President gave the indication that he was ready to work till late hours if need be.
On his first day of the visit, characteristic of Obasanjo at a civic reception organised for him at Ibom Hall, Uyo, he did not mince words when he said that Attah would be commended if there were projects in the state, but will not hesitate to give a poor mark should there be nothing on ground. Well, one aspect of his prediction came through, as what he saw on his first outing impressed him beyond doubt that the state has worn a new look.
He said, "I want to say at this initial stage that what I have seen this afternoon when we arrived, I think 'i nobi joke'. What I have seen is better than what I saw before. When I see more, I will say more but suffice it to say that Uyo as a city is taking a new shape".
The President's visit, which was the fourth since the inception of his administration, was designed to give him the ample opportunity to see things for himself in a state which detractors said nothing good was happening there.
However, as Attah clearly stated in his speech that the vision of the government is to transform the state into a prosperous highly educated, technologically driven, ethnically harmonious and with strategic policies and programmes.
It wasn't the governor's intention to bore Obasanjo at the civic reception with facts concerning their stewardship for the benefit of their people since May, 1999. Rather, Attah said the President's visit, would enable him pass judgment.
The terrain of Akwa Ibom was not new to Obasanjo. Some of the places visited and the roads he used during the Nigerian civil war somehow were still evergreen in his memory. At least, he was able to point this out when the minister of works who in among his entourage could not give a vivid explanation of roads that are under the Federal Government care and control during an interactive section at Judiciary Multi-purpose hall.
There is no gainsaying the fact that the President commissioned and inspected a lot of projects in Akwa Ibom State.
At the Ibom Hal Attah used the opportunity to remind Obasanjo that the state, is the home state of some his friends both living and dead such as late Brigadier-General Uduak Jacob Esuene and Obong Philip Effiong, the brave soldiers and elder statesmen.
Also, the state of course, is home state of Secretary to the Federation (SGF), Obong Ufot Ekaette, who has served Obasanjo since May 1999. In a way to show respect to an 'obedient servant', Obasanjo, who had promised to pay him a personal visit in a return 'match' to the state, kept to his word this time around.
On the second day of his visit and after he had commissioned Idongesit Nkanga Secretariat phase 11, inspected the Ibagwa bridge, he then went to Ikot Abasi. He commissioned the federal water project, inspected the Ibom independent power plant and drove straight to Ekaete's house, a tiny community at Ekot Edor in Onna Local Government, where he had lunch.
Ufot, in a brief chat with THISDAY, said, "I am overwhelmed as to have Mr. President visit my house. It's something that I will cherish all my life and also the people of this town will continue to appreciate this fantastic thing. After working with the President this long and he promised last time he visited, that whenever he comes back to the state that he will call at my home. Of course, as a man of his words, he is doing so at this time. I am extremely proud and graceful to God for what is happening today", SGF said.
As a prominent personality, Ekaette commended the state government, which he said has done quite a lot. He said a lot of roads have been commissioned, with a new innovation of having a six lane road. I think we should congratulate the governor and the state government for the work, he said.
He noted that governance is a dynamic process and instead of enumerating those things that may be lacking in the state, he rather said development depends mostly on what one meets on the ground and the amount of resources at his or her disposal. But for whatever assessment, he is convinced that the state has improved in so many ways.
A lot of projects earned the state government a lot of accolade. These are the Ibom independent power plant, the construction of Ikot Atabrikang-Akuta-Opolom-Okorutip-Iwochang road and two bridges by the Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) and the commissioned electrification project which will transverse about 25 communities. The project cost was put at over N363 million.
Mr. Timi Alaibe, executive director of finance, NDDC, explained that the project schedule of the roads and the two bridges which are been constructed at the cost of N6 billion by Viche Nigeria Limited, would take about two years for it to be completed. On the scope of work, is the construction of 6.87 km, with two lane single carriageway with asphalted surface, construction of bridge of 1.31m and 2.600m length and the dredging/sand filling of swamp.
The socio-economic importance of the road, Alaibe said, will link two major oil producing local government areas in Akwa Ibom State. He revealed that over 400 youths have worked on the project, most of them as artisans, semi skilled and skilled workers, while some have been trained in the area of carpentry, masonry, iron bending, welding, driving and as operators of various equipment.
The road/bridge project according to him, will boast agriculture and fishing, while the produce will be transferred to the urban areas to improve the economic mainstay of the people. It is expected that the project will impact directly on over one million people living and doing business in and around Ibeno communities, Alaibe said.
The electrification project on its own will also improve the economic and social life as well as enhance security, which are basic forms of securing stability in the region. Small and medium scale industries was also said to be on the increase. According to the project engineer, the technology is one of the newest in the world in electric installations.
The use of fibre glass as insulators, which, the engineer said has not been used especially in the country is used to withstand environmental problems that is been encountered in the Niger Delta area.
0basanjo, in commissioning the electrification project, wondered why such viable economic communities should be cut-off infrastructurally. He therefore called on the youths not to allow their restiveness to destroy the projects since it was built for their own interest.
The arrival of the President at the Ibom Airport at Okobo later in the evening was momentous. "It is important to understand the driving force behind the project. If Nigeria is the heart of Africa, Akwa Ibom is the womb of the cradle of Nigeria. Even as a passenger airport, the state needs it. But this is more than a passenger airport." Attah told the President.
To create economic viability and realise the dire need of such a facility, Attah said the state came up with the idea of a maintenance, repair and overhaul facility (MRO). When completed, the airport he said will be West Africa's first and only airport with world class self-contained aircraft maintenance facility providing aircraft maintenance and logistics support services to Nigerian and other African air carriers and operators.
The governor revealed that it took the state 18 months of searching and negotiation to find a standard airport. Names worthy of mention that contributed to the success of the airport are Ambassador Howard Jetter, former US ambassador to Nigeria and Mr. Eme Ekanem, the 'son of the soil', but residing in Washington.
The project is fully funded by the state government from the sale of some of its shares from Vmobile and other private sector funds. The airport project, the governor said, has come to be accepted as a possessive transformative transportation for this part of the Niger Delta.
DynCorp International, the contractor handling the project, assured the government that the President will use the airport before May 2007. The recommended site is located on a land measuring 2,600 hectres and bordering three local government areas of Uruan, Okobo and Nsiat Atai.
Obasanjo however noted that he has not in doubt that technically, the airport is a thing of beauty and not only that the contractor should also make it a thing of economic viability. "I am very happy to see it and I have been briefed on it at least two times in the past. I am happy that the course of the economic reforms that we are carrying out has helped us to have this type of project. I want Nigeria to be the hub of air transportation in Africa. But why we are doing that, we must also remember that there are millions of Nigerians, who even if they can afford air travel, may not need the airport, but prefer to travel by roads. The roads should be taken care of", Obasanjo requested.
Nothing was left out during the visit. A gala night and church service were held in honour of the President. In each of those occasions, there were meaningful insights as to why government exists. At the gala night, the governor reeled out some of his achievements and also made tangible requests from the Federal Government. About seven cultural troops had an indoor display of what they are noted for.
Other areas which the state government has made a lot of progress include, the agricultural sector, with the cultivation of about 5,000 hectres of raw cassava. That aspect has gulped about $350,000 which came as counterpart funding for cassava production for both local consumption and export. It is a programme that is supported by the United State Agency for International Development (USAID).
Other areas include poverty alleviation, significant investments in education, health and human capital sub-sector. Attah said about N5.8 billion has been spent in the renovation of 55 hospitals, polytechnics and health centers in the 31 local government areas of the state. Also, N2.57 billion has been spent to build three new general hospitals in Abak, Ini and Okobo local governments.
Following the request that more special science centres be built in the state by the President, Attah was glad to inform Obasanjo that seven model science secondary schools has been built to compliment the existing ones. The schools will be put in use at the beginning of the next school year in October. Schools libraries have been equipped with furniture and equipment supplied to cover 210 schools.
Attah's request to the President was not out of place. Due to projects cited in both Calabar and Akwa Ibom, especially the Tinapa is Calabar, Attah urged the President to consider the dualisation of the Calabar-Odukpani-Itu-Ikot Ekpene road and similarly, the Aba-Ikot Ekpene-Uyo-Oro road, all federal roads, the governor told Obasanjo at the Ibom civil hall.
Obasanjo's third day of the visit was crowned with a church service, after which, he proceeded to commission and inspect other projects. At Mbiabet Ikpe, he inspected a rice farm, commissioned Nto-Ndang-Ikot Ukana road and finally took his exit Chopper back to Margaret Ekpo International Airport.
The church service, held at the Immanuel Methodist Cathedral Church, Uyo, was very apt. While the outgoing Prelate of the Methodist Church, Nigeria, His Eminence, Sunday Mbang spoke on the topic, "I know you". He said that God sees everything the leaders and people of this country are doing. He urged Nigerians to eschew corruption, insecurity, and abuse of power, injustice, favoritism, violence and suppression of people's right.
Apparently preparing the minds of Nigerians for his exit in May 2007 and if most of the economic reform programmes recorded by his administration will surpass the test of time, Obasanjo assured that God will not abandon Nigeria until He (God) has completed what He promised the nation even if he is still the President or not.
"I know God will not abandon this country. He will finish His work. This is not a job that will finish in two, four and even in ten years. God will continue to take us gradually to the promise land and we will get there", he said.

Original_Search_Title: Report Highlights Rise in Angola's Cassava Production
Headline: Southern Africa: Report Highlights Rise in Angola's Cassava Production
Body_Text:
Maseru — Angola is among the fifth biggest producers of cassava of the Southern Africa Development Community (SADC) in the 2006-2007 period, Angop learned Wednesday in Maseru (Lesotho).
The information is contained in a document adopted Wednesday in Maseru by the organisation's Council of Ministers.
The report that focuses on the general situation of food security in the southern region of the continent, underlines that the production of cassava for 2006/2007 is estimated at 22,03 tons in five countries, namely Angola, Malawi, Mozambique, Tanzania and Zambia.
Based on some projections, the report estimates that Angola is likely to need humanitarian assistance, due to its shortage of cereal for the 2006/2207 period that are estimated at 1,514 tons, against the 696 tons the country is expected to produce.
The data mention that although the country does well in the production of cassava, it has deficits in cereals like maize, rice and wheat.
According to the report, the 22 million tons of cassava expected for 2006/2007 in SADC represent 19 percent above the figure of the previous year that was of 18.47 million tons.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava - Nigeria Not Yet a Player - Adetunji
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava - Nigeria Not Yet a Player - Adetunji
Body_Text:
Lagos — Special Assistant to the President on Food Security, Mrs Toyin Adetunji, said today in Abuja that the country was not yet a player in the cassava international market.
She said even as the world largest producer of cassava compared to Thailand, Brazil and Europe, "Nigeria is yet to attain the market power".
Adetunji said that Nigeria had a land area of 92.4 million hectares out of which more than 70 million hectares were suitable for agricultural activities.
He said that 39.2 million hectares of the land was good for permanent pasture, 2.8 million heacters for permanent crops and 27.9 hectares for arable crops.
She said that the annual production output was 45 million metric tonnes of cassava which stood at 19 per cent of the world's production.
"Even at these figures Nigeria still remains the world largest producer of cassava," she said.
She said that the country had potential to increase its production capacity with the available resources to meet both subsistence and commercial demand.
The government, she said, was determined to ensure that cassava assumed the fulcrum for driving the economy in terms of food security and job creation.
She said that government had identified some of the constraints against tapping the potential that abound in cassava production and processing.
Poor linkage between farmers, processors, fabricators, inadequate land preparation, poor mechanization technology, poor road network and high cost of production were the constraints, she said.
Adetunji said that the federal government had intensified efforts to commercialise cassava through funding of research projects on how to improve yields, create awareness and market opportunities.
She said that there was also direct intervention on integrated cassava programme being co-funded by government and other organisations and carried out by International Institute for Tropical Agriculture (IITA).
She said that IITA, National Root Crops Research Institute (NRCRI) and Root and Tuber Expansion Programme (RTEP) had produced disease resistant and high yielding cassava verities and processing techniques.
IFAD also assisted RTEP to promote adaptive research, multiplication of improved verities, marketing, channels and expansions.
While NRCRI has the national mandate to develop and promote improved varieties of cassava through production of breeder seed.
She said that five new verities had been developed and were at present being multiplied nationwide.
The PA said that in addition government had established Export Expansion Grant to encourage exporters, the 10 per cent policy and the provision of good road network by government at all levels.
Adetunji said that the policy of 10 per cent cassava flour inclusion in bread and other confectionery would generate additional demand of 380,000 metric tonnes of cassava flour annually.
She added that the policy alone would generate more than one million jobs at both the up stream and down stream sectors, while SON and NAFDAC were set to enforce standards for cassava producers.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Donates Seeds to Displaced
Headline: Uganda: WFP Donates Seeds to Displaced
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE UN World Food programme (WFP) has donated sh386m worth of agricultural seeds for the 1.4 million people displaced by the LRA.
WFP dispatched 270 tonnes of sorghum, maize, greengram, simsim and cow peas for IDPs in Gulu, Kitgum and Pader districts, WFP acting country director Alix Loriston has said.
Loriston said some beneficiaries had returned home, others were in transit resettlements and some were still in camps.
"There has been a steady improvement in overall security in Acholi and Lango since January, which has allowed the IDPs to access more farming land in satellite camps," Loriston said.
"These seeds should help the IDPs to reap something substantial out of the November harvest, to reduce dependence on WFP relief food," he added.
In a statement last week, WFP said in May, the Food Agriculture Organisation (FAO) and other partners provided food, seeds, tractors and other faming tools for over 35,000 IDPs.
It said the US$1m project was to encourage IDPs to voluntarily return home and enable them restart their livelihood.
It said by September, more than 100,000 IDPs are expected to return home and WFP will assist them with food, seeds and tools.
It said WFP had also provided tractors in Acholi, Lango and Teso and helped communities to set up seed multiplication centres.
Some 200 acres of land have been planted with cassava, sweet potatoes and manual cuttings for multiplication and distribution to IDPs in August, it said.
Loriston said WFP was moving from relief to recovery programmes in northern Uganda.

Original_Search_Title: Niger, Chinese Coy in Ethanol Production Pact
Headline: Nigeria: Niger, Chinese Coy in Ethanol Production Pact
Body_Text:
TheNiger state govern ment has signed a memorandum of understanding (MOU) with a Chinese company for the establishment of Ethanol plant. Speaking at the signing ceremony today in Minna, the secretary to the state government, Mr Adams Erena, who represented the government said the project would gulp N11.6 billion (about $90m). He said the Chinese company would serve as a consultant to the project.
Erena said the company was expected to source 85 per cent of the project cost through a soft loan from the Chinese government on three per cent interest rate. He explained that the balance of 15 per cent would be sourced by the state government from local banks. The state government, he added, had already released N30 million to local cassava farmers to facilitate mass production of the commodity in the state, said the project when completed, would have the capacity of converting 150,000 metric tonnes of cassava into fuel energy.
He said the fuel energy would be sold at cheaper ra te for both local consumption and exports. He said that ethanol would be cheaper than petroleum products, and assured the Chinese partners of government's readiness to ensure that its part of the agreement was fulfilled.
Responding, the Managing Director of the company, Mr. Wang Qingxin, assured the state government that the project would be completed within two years from the date of the agreement. He said the signing of the agreement marked the beginning of more economic relationship between the Chinese government and Niger state, noting that the project would benefit the peoples of China and Nigeria. (NAN)

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Growers Cry Out to Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava Growers Cry Out to Obasanjo
Body_Text:
Warri — MEMBERS of the Cassava Growers Association in Delta State have cried out to President Olusegun Obasanjo that the Federal Government's directive to flour millers to blend their wheat with cassava flour to boost massive production of cassava and grow the local economy was being sabotaged by flour millers.
President of the association, Chief R. Emecho in a letter, entitled, "A cry-out from Cassava Growers Association in Delta State" to Mr. President, said that the members embarked on massive production of cassava as part of the presidential initiative on cassava because they believed that the initiative "is capable of growing the local economy and contributing to the transformation of the Nigerian economy".
"At the moment, we have over 500 hectares of matured cassava ready for the market. Our challenge is that we do not have ready buyers for cassava. We have found out that the flour millers in the state are not blending their wheat with cassava, thereby, blocking the major outlet for our cassava.
"Mr. President Sir, if this situation continues, we will soon be out of business because we continue to pay for labour to brush the farm in addition to the fact that wild animals are also eating the tubers. Besides, other people who would have started their own farms may be discouraged based on our bitter experiences. This can spell doom for the good initiative of having "Nigeria bread" like most other countries of the world", he said.
"Our plea", the cassava growers told President Obasanjo, "is that you set up a high powered monitoring team to ensure full compliance with the 10 per cent composite flour production by the flour millers. The monitoring team should visit all flour mills, particularly the ones in Delta and Edo states to ascertain their level of compliance".

Original_Search_Title: Swamps of Insurgency
Headline: Nigeria: Swamps of Insurgency
Body_Text:
Washington, DC — "Over the past quarter century, unrest in the Niger Delta has slowly graduated into a guerrilla-style conflict that leaves hundreds dead each year. The battle lines are drawn over the region's crude oil and gas that make Nigeria the number one oil producer in Africa and the world's tenth largest crude oil producer." - International Crisis Group
This AfricaFocus Bulletin contains the press release and executive summary of a new report on the Niger Delta, by the International Crisis Group (http://www.crisisgroup.org), as well as a summary of recent events in the Delta from the UN's Integrated Regional Information Network (IRIN). The Crisis Group report is the second of a new series on Nigeria; the first "Nigeria: Want in the Midst of Plenty,: appeared in July.
The UNDP's Niger Delta Human Development Report, released this year but too long to excerpt here, provides extensive statistics and background information on the Delta (report  available at http://hdr.undp.org/reports/view_reports.cfm?type=3). The report notes that "The Niger Delta is a region suffering from administrative neglect, crumbling social infrastructure and services, high unemployment, social deprivation, abject poverty, filth and squalor, and endemic conflict."
For previous AfricaFocus Bulletins on Nigeria, see http://www.africafocus.org/country/nigeria.php
++++++++++++++++++++++end +++++++++++++++++++++++
The Swamps of Insurgency: Nigeria's Delta Unrest
Africa Report N 115
International Crisis Group
http://www.crisisgroup.org
Dakar/Brussels, 3 August 2006
Contacts: Andrew Stroehlein (Brussels) +32 (0) 2 541 1635 Kimberly Abbott (Washington) +1 202 785 1601
Dakar/Brussels, 3 August 2006: The Nigerian government and international oil corporations must change direction if they are to reduce the risk of violent meltdown in the Niger Delta.
The Swamps of Insurgency: Nigeria's Delta Unrest, the new report from the International Crisis Group, examines the potent cocktail of poverty, crime and corruption fuelling a militant threat to the stability of the region and to the country's reliability as a major oil producer. Several steps are required to reverse the situation.
The government needs to forge far-reaching reforms to administration and its approach to revenue sharing. Oil companies should involve credible, community-based organisations in their development efforts. And Western governments must pay immediate attention to improving their own development aid.
"Attempts to secure energy production have too often been heavy handed, alienating large segments of the population and boosting support for militants", says Mike McGovern, Crisis Group's outgoing West Africa Project Director. "There have been some laudable attempts to initiate development, but many have been poorly executed or hijacked by outsiders and local elites".
Over the past quarter century, unrest in the Niger Delta has slowly graduated into a guerrilla-style conflict that leaves hundreds dead each year. The battle lines are drawn over the region's crude oil and gas that make Nigeria the number one oil producer in Africa and the world's tenth largest crude oil producer. Since January, fighters from a new group, the Movement for the Emancipation of the Niger Delta (MEND), demand the government withdraw troops, release imprisoned local leaders and grant oil revenue concessions to Delta groups.
The Nigerian Federal Government should initiate a credible, sustained dialogue on control of resources with Niger Delta civil society groups, including militants, activist leaders, women and youth drawn from nominees submitted by councils of regional ethnic groups. The state governments ought to engage more fully with professional non-governmental organisations that demonstrate a capability and willingness to assist communities to take responsibility for their own development.
Energy companies should improve measures to ensure transparency of contracts and other community payments, including for surveillance, development projects and compensation for land use and pollution.
The international community should press the Nigerian government to institute resource-control reforms and negotiate in good faith with Niger Delta groups, and encourage oil companies headquartered in their countries to be transparent about revenue and payments.
"Immediate action on these issues is crucial if open conflict is to be averted", says Suliman Baldo, Director of Crisis Group's Africa Program.
Executive Summary and Recommendations
A potent cocktail of poverty, crime and corruption is fuelling a militant threat to Nigeria's reliability as a major oil producer.
Since January 2006, fighters from a new group, the Movement for the Emancipation of the Niger Delta (MEND), have fought with government forces, sabotaged oil installations, taken foreign oil workers hostage and carried out two lethal car bombings. MEND demands the government withdraw troops, release imprisoned ethnic leaders and grant oil revenue concessions to Delta groups. The Nigerian government needs to forge far-reaching reforms to administration and its approach to revenue sharing, the oil companies to involve credible, community-based organisations in their development efforts and Western governments to pay immediate attention to improving their own development aid.
The root causes of the Delta insurgency are well known. Violence, underdevelopment, environmental damage and failure to establish credible state and local government institutions have contributed to mounting public frustration at the slow pace of change under the country's nascent democracy, which is dogged by endemic corruption and misadministration inherited from its military predecessors.
Nigeria had estimated oil export revenues of $45 billion in 2005 but the slow pace of systemic reforms and the lack of jobs, electricity, water, schools and clinics in large parts of the Delta have boosted support to insurgents such as MEND. Militants appeal to the kind of public disaffection that prompted ethnic Ogoni leader Ken Saro-Wiwa to protest the military-led government and Royal Dutch/Shell before his execution in November 1995.
A decade later, the potential consequences of this conflict have escalated in both human and economic terms across a swathe of territory 30 times the size of Ogoniland. Nigerian and international military experts have recognised that the crisis requires a negotiated political resolution. Any attempt at a military solution would be disastrous for residents and risky for the oil industry. Most facilities are in the maze of creeks and rivers that are particularly vulnerable to raids by well-armed militants with intimate knowledge of the terrain. But inaction risks escalating and entrenching the conflict at a time when tensions are already rising in advance of the 2007 national elections.
MEND increasingly serves as an umbrella organisation for a loose affiliation of rebel groups in the Delta. It has not revealed the identity of its leaders or the source of its funds but its actions demonstrate that it is better armed and organised than previous militant groups. Observers warn that a worst-case scenario could lead to a one to two-year shutdown of the oil industry in the Delta, where most of Nigeria's 2.3 million daily barrels of crude oil originate.
Illegal oil "bunkering" - theft - has accelerated the conflict and provided militant and criminal groups with funds to purchase arms.
Another source of funding are the discreet payments oil companies make to militant leaders in return for "surveillance" and protection of pipelines and other infrastructure. This practice, frequently cloaked as community development, has fueled conflict through competition for contracts and by providing income to groups with violent agendas. Oil companies also pay allowances, perks - and sometimes salaries - to "supernumerary police", as well as regular duty police and soldiers deployed to protect oil installations. Security forces consider these plum postings and are alleged to use excessive force to protect company facilities and their jobs.
President Olusegun Obasanjo's government has begun important reforms but these must be deepened if peace is to succeed. Yet, his government has downplayed the seriousness of the insurgency. Senior officials have dismissed the militants as "mere" criminals and defended security crackdowns that have embittered locals, making it easier for armed groups such as MEND to gain new recruits. In an effort to deflect growing public impatience, government officials have demanded oil companies spend ever larger amounts on community projects. Oil industry officials counter that, after taxes and royalties, the federal government collects the vast majority of earnings on a sliding scale - 90 per cent of industry profits when oil prices are above $60. The companies rightly place the primary responsibility for political solutions to the crisis - including increased development - on the government but they also chafe at the suggestion that their own development strategies have failed.
Transparent and participatory development schemes can foster hope and accountability in Delta communities. Development efforts led by the European Commission and Pro-Natura International provide models for an approach that could reverse the cycle of poverty and violence - but only if their scale is significantly broadened to include a wide range of groups in oil producing areas. Government must also tackle corruption by making development initiatives more transparent. Otherwise, even dramatic increases in spending will be wasted.
Recommendations
To Nigeria's Federal Government:
1. Initiate a credible, sustained dialogue on control of resources with Niger Delta civil society groups, including militants, activist leaders, religious leaders, women and youth drawn from nominees submitted by councils of ethnic groups in the Niger Delta states.
2. Institute while this dialogue is proceeding a derivation formula of between 25 and 50 per cent of mineral resources, including oil and gas, to all Nigerian states, and phase this in over five years in order to avoid budgetary shock to non-oil producing states and to encourage exploration and production of other mineral resources throughout Nigeria.
3. Amend or repeal the 1978 Land Use Act to expand the opportunity for communities to seek compensation for land through legal means and to allow a more transparent adjudication process of potential land seizures.
4. Seek in parallel with the dialogue on control of resources an agreement with militants that includes a phased withdrawal of troops from Delta towns, concurrent with a weapons-return amnesty program that pays militants and gang members market rates for guns and enrols them in skills and job training and that pays attention as well to the needs of girls and women who may not carry guns but have roles within those bodies (such as forced wives or cooks).
5. Bring the increasing number of quasi-independent local government institutions formally into federal structures as part of an effort to rationalise local governments in Niger Delta states, particularly in areas where these are unworkably large or combine substantively distinct ethnicities or communities.
6. Ensure that security force personnel are paid on time and in full in order to help prevent dependency on oil company payments and illicit and corrupt practices; increase enforcement of penalties for corruption and consider raising salaries; clarify the chain of command; and change the uniform of the "supernumerary police" that provide security services for the energy companies.
7. Refashion the government/transnational oil company joint ventures that control production to offer residents a substantial ownership stake along the lines of what corporate majors including Royal/Dutch Shell, ExxonMobil and Conoco have done in Canada's Arctic.
To the State Governments:
8. Engage more fully with professional, non-governmental organisations that demonstrate a capability and willingness to assist communities to take responsibility for their own development.
9. Accelerate steps to implement poverty reduction strategies outlined in State Economic Empowerment and Development Strategies (SEEDS) that have been developed in conjunction with Nigeria's national umbrella anti-poverty strategy, NEEDS.
10. Make budget details publicly available and respond to queries about specific spending patterns and projects.
To the Energy Companies:
11. Improve measures to ensure transparency of contracts and other community payments, including for surveillance, development projects and compensation for land use and pollution, and in particular:
(a) honour company commitments and ensure that payments are made in full, by bank transfer - not in cash - to the intended recipients;
(b) conclude agreements wherever possible that provide for individuals and local communities to be compensated for land use and pollution; and
(c) seek independent mediation or arbitration when agreements are in dispute.
12. Prioritise long-term ability to operate in Nigeria over short-term production goals and seek community assent before proceeding with production-related projects.
13. Develop partnerships with non-governmental, community-based bodies with a demonstrated ability to provide skills training and capacity building for development projects, including women's and religious groups that have played significant roles in mediating among various ethnic groups and actors in the past decade.
To the U.S., the EU and EU Member States with major oil interests in Nigeria (the UK, France and Italy):
14. Press the Nigerian government to institute resource-control reforms and negotiate in good faith with Niger Delta groups, and encourage oil companies headquartered in their countries to be transparent about revenue and payments.
15. Condition assistance to the government upon greater transparency in federal and state budgets, particularly with regard to energy revenues.
16. Lobby China and India to sign the Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative.
To the United Nations and the wider International Community:
17. Offer the good offices of a neutral country without oil interests in Nigeria to mediate between the federal government and Delta groups, an idea already accepted in principle by MEND.
18. Consider delaying or postponing cooperation with state governments that have a poor record for delivering public services or controlling graft, and do not work with government or party officials who provide weapons or funding to armed groups for political purposes.
No respite for people of Niger Delta
July 26, 2006
Integrated Regional Information Networks
http://www.irinnews.org
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations]
Yenagoa, 26 Jul 2006 (IRIN) - When oil began seeping from pipelines owned by Italian oil company Agip recently, Nigerian newspapers reported that the spill was caused by sabotage.
Agip denied it had been forced to cut production because of an alleged attack, but acknowledged that its network had been damaged and that repairs were underway.
It is unclear exactly what happened to the Agip pipelines but that is nothing unusual in Nigeria's troubled delta region, where sabotage, accidents, oil siphoning and deteriorating infrastructure all mean the same thing to millions of local villagers: more pollution.
"Oil spills have become a great environmental tragedy in Nigeria, polluting streams, farmlands, the air and destroying lives," said Nnimmo Bassey, head of Environmental Rights Action (ERA), which is affiliated with the international environmental group Friends of the Earth.
Prior to the oil boom of the 1970s, Nigeria's main exports were agricultural products. Although the majority of the population identifies farming as their livelihood, investment in the agricultural sector over the years has been sidelined in favour of oil.
Since December last year, more than 10 major oil or gas pipelines have been blasted allegedly by militants belonging to the Movement for the Emancipation of the Niger Delta (MEND). Foreign oil workers are routinely taken hostage, although they are released within days or weeks unharmed.
Nigeria's oil output has been slashed about one-quarter because of recent leaks, sabotage and unrest in the delta.
Royal Dutch Shell, Nigeria's biggest oil producer, said on Tuesday that a leak to an oil pipeline in Rivers State had cut its output there by 180,000 barrels per day. The source of the leak was not immediately clear.
Militants claim to be fighting for an increased share of oil wealth for the delta's inhabitants, many of whom live without electricity, running water or access to education. Although the country now has a democratic government after decades of military rule, the people of the Niger Delta complain that little has changed in terms of their standard of living.
Environmental Damage
Each pipeline blast has caused a major oil spill, and in one case in December at least eight people were killed when a fire borne by an oil slick swept through their homes.
In the report "Nigeria: Want in the Midst of Plenty," the Brussels-based Crisis Group said recently that despite more than US $400 billion in oil revenue over the past three decades, nine out of ten Nigerians live on less than US $2 a day.
Crisis Group said growing tensions in the delta were a direct result of decades of environmental harm and political neglect.
While oil companies blame most of the spills on sabotage and vandalism, activist groups insist that more of the damage and spills come from poorly maintained pipelines.
"The spillage has been there long before the militants," said Peter Ajube, spokesman for Ijaw Youths Council (IYC), an influential activist group campaigning for the rights of the delta's dominant ethnic group, the Ijaw.
"We don't like what the militants are doing because we're non-violent, but we know that most of the spills are caused by aged pipelines," said Ajube. "And whenever you have a spill it is the communities in the area that suffer, losing their fishing areas, losing their farms and source of drinking water."
Inhabitants of Igbomotoro, in Bayelsa state, suffered short- and long-term effects from an oil slick that came from a ruptured pipeline on Nun River in July.
"I lost my fishing nets used to trap fish in the river along with a night's catch," said Inikro Alaowei. "I don't expect any harvests either later this year from my cassava farm, which was also affected."
A communal forest serving Igbomotoro was also swamped by the oil, destroying a source of food and traditional plant medicines.
Alienated from Land and Resources
Community members expect no solace since oil companies as a tradition do not pay compensation for ecological damage caused by sabotage. The rationale is to discourage wilful vandalism in expectation of compensation, a practice the companies blame for a larger share of oil spills than the activists and the communities accept.
In other cases, residents damage pipelines in an effort to siphon oil to sell. The practice, known as 'bunkering', is highly dangerous. Scores and sometimes hundreds of people die each year if the gushing fuel catches fire as they scramble to scoop it up.
The heightened threat to the environmental health of the Niger Delta resulting from oil operations are highlighted in a recent human development study published by the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) in Nigeria.
"Oil spills and gas flares in particular have destroyed natural resources central to local livelihoods," said the report.
Gas flaring produces greenhouse gases and exposes communities to heat, noise and air pollution.
The UNDP report said people in the delta have been alienated from their land and resources, leaving them frustrated with both the oil companies and governments that have failed to regulate them.
Royal Dutch Shell, the biggest international oil operator in Nigeria, accounting for roughly half the country's exports of 2.5 million barrels daily, also has more onshore operations in the delta than other major oil companies. Figures released by the company show it has more than 1,000 oil wells in the region linked by more than 6,000 km of pipeline network.
Shell acknowledges the extent of its presence in the delta poses a major environmental challenge, which it says it is working hard to manage.
"Our environmental programme is geared towards reducing the negative impact of our operations on the environment," Shell states on its website. In this regard the company has since 1997 made environmental sustainability a key principle to be considered in all business undertakings. This has resulted in increased environmental monitoring and more rapid response to remedy situations created by spillages.
Lack of Enforcement
Inyang Duke, an environmental expert from the University of North Carolina visiting Nigeria, said strict enforcement of regulations is key to improving environmental practices in the delta.
Nigeria recently acquired patrol boats to help monitor the delta.
However, observers say it is difficult to affectively monitor much of the region because of dense mangroves.
"Nigeria has the right (environmental) regulations and policies but lacks the technical capacity to implement and enforce them," Duke said.
One major reason for this failure is the government's awkward position as regulator and primary beneficiary with the majority stake in joint venture operations run by oil multinationals that produce nearly all the country's oil, said Duke.
"You have to separate the regulated from the regulator; there must be no conflict of interest," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Over $600 Million Invested in Agro-Industrial Zone
Headline: Angola: Over $600 Million Invested in Agro-Industrial Zone
Body_Text:
Malanje — At least USD 605.314 million will be used by the Angolan State for the implementation of Malanje Agro-Industrial Zone, with an objective to create work for more than 62,000 people.
This was announced on Wednesday by the Brazilian company Odebrecht consulting officials, during the presentation of Malanje rural organisation structural plan management proposal.
According to the consultant Carlos Matias, once the zone starts working, Angola's economic production might have a growth of 1.6 million tones of harvest.
He stated that Malanje agro-industrial zone will be managed by a company named Rural Development Society (SDR).
At the zone they will install factories of juice, two of sugar and alcohol, cassava, limestone as well as soya industry, including a warehouse to dry and store cereals and a slaughter-house.
According to studies made by the specialists involved in the project, the annual profit will be of USD 563 million. On the first three years of construction of the zone, 373 roads will be built, including 196 kilometres of electric lines.
The proposal for its approval will happen in September, when it will be presented at the Cabinet Council. The Agro-Industrial zone will be concluded in eight years.
With 411,000 hectares, the zone will be irrigated through Capanda dam, in an extension of 18,000 hectares.

Original_Search_Title: Bugesera Residents Embark On Food Production
Headline: Rwanda: Bugesera Residents Embark On Food Production
Body_Text:
Eastern Province — After a period of prolonged drought and food shortages in Bugesera District, people have said that the environment is now favorable to embark on food production for both consumption and income generation. The farmers in Maranyondo Village said on Aug 7 that they will utilise the current rains to grow enough food for storage even in dry season.
"What we suffered is enough for us to learn and use the current rains to grow enough food for storage even during disaster periods. The situation is now favorable and we are able to grow food for both our consumption and sell to meet some of the basic needs like medical care, clothing and shelter," Silas Mugabo, one of the vegetable growers in the area, said.
He added that residents can now grow various agricultural food crops like beans, maize, cassava, sweet potatoes, vegetables and bananas to supply to the markets and generate income.
"Residents are having access to food and water, unlike last year where farms and valleys were dry," added Mary Niyonsaba.
"My family now gets adequate food harvested from my farm. I also harvest enough potatoes and cabbages to supply to trading centers and community markets," Maria Umwari, another farmer in Bugesera, said.
Timothy Kayira, an area water technician told The New Times that the government and humanitarian agencies have installed water tanks and boreholes in the area in an attempt to provide adequate water to the population for irrigation of their crops and domestic usage.
He pointed out that residents have been sensitized on proper use of water and growing of enough food for storage.
Bugesera District was hit by drought during 2005.

Original_Search_Title: New Crops Tie Inflation At 5 Percent
Headline: Uganda: New Crops Tie Inflation At 5 Percent
Body_Text:
Kampala — A ROBUST harvesting season of various food items in various parts of the country has kept underlying inflation for June at 5.0 percent, a positive start for the new financial year.
Despite warnings by the Uganda Bureau of Statistics that it is too early to predict a lower trend in the next 11 months, the rate underscores government focus on keeping inflation under five percent for the whole year.
The ongoing harvesting season led to lower prices for a number of staple foods, especially matoke, sweet potatoes, Irish potatoes and cassava in all centers except Masaka.
"Prices of beans, groundnuts, onions, passion fruits, sweet bananas and oranges went down. Prices of cereals also dropped in the centers of Kampala, Jinja, Mbale Mbarara and Gulu due to increased supply," the Director Macroeconomic Uganda Bureau of Statistic, Mr Matthew Ssewanyana, said while releasing the Price Consumer Index for the month of July, at Statistics House in Kampala.
"However, partly due to quarantine on the movement of animals and animal products from some districts in western and southwestern Uganda, prices of meat, chicken and milk went up in some centers," Ssewanyana said.
In the financial year 2004/05 Uganda's underlying inflation opened at 3.8 percent, while in the financial year 2005/06 it opened at 6.4 percent. On the other hand Uganda also saw its annual headline inflation rate for the year ending July 2006 dropping to 5.8 percent from 7.2 percent registered for the year ended June 2006.
Ssewanyana said government's efforts of having a manageable (low) inflation rate is always undermined by food crop prices, which is dependent on weather variations and at times external factors like high oil prices in the international market.

Original_Search_Title: Magric to Set Up Starch Plant
Headline: Uganda: Magric to Set Up Starch Plant
Body_Text:
Kampala — Magric is to set up a starch-processing plant in Kampala that will save the beverage industry millions of dollars used to import white sugar, the company's managing director has said.
John Magnay said the plant will be commissioned by March 2007.
He said the plant's products will serve as a substitute for the white imported sugar worth $12m (sh22b) annually for the beer and soft drinks industry.
Magnay, who is also the chairman of Uganda Grain Traders Association, said the plant that will be called Starcas, (STARach from CASsava) would convert starch to maltose and fructose sugars, which are used in the production of beer and soft drinks.
"Maltose and fructose will be substituted for the white sugar, which beverage companies are importing. They import 25,000 metric tonnes per year at $450 (sh823,500) to $500 (sh925,000) per metric tonne, which is about $12m per year," he said.
Maltose is used in beer production, while fructose is used for soft drinks.
"The local sugar is off-white and even more expensive than the imported sugar at $500 (sh925,000) per metric tonne. We are negotiating with beverage companies," Magnay said.
He was speaking to cassava farmers, researchers, millers, traders and other stakeholders.
This was at the second National Cassava Workshop at Jokas Hotel in Bweyogerere, Kampala.

Original_Search_Title: Security Situation in North Kivu Remains Precarious
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Security Situation in North Kivu Remains Precarious
Body_Text:
Goma — War-weary residents of the Democratic Republic of Congo's North Kivu Province turned up in large numbers to vote in July's elections, hoping that the new leaders they chose would restore security to the region.
"There has never been security in North Kivu," Jean Simon Mushana, a former soldier attached to the special presidential division, said. "We live like we are in the Wild West where everyone makes his own law. The state is nonexistent in the province."
Underlining the precarious humanitarian and security situation in the province is the fighting last weekend between armed militia and government soldiers in Sake, 25 km west of the provincial capital, Goma. Two soldiers and a civilian died, 18 others were wounded and hundreds displaced. At least 14 soldiers have been admitted to the military hospital in Goma, according to a provisional assessment by the 8th Military Brigade in the province.
The fighting is a continuation of a rebellion led by a renegade army general, Laurent Nkunda, whose troops have renewed military operations in eastern DRC. Nkunda was also a senior officer in the Rwandan-backed Rassemblement congolais pour la democratie-Goma (RCD-Goma), one of the main rebel groups that fought in the country from 1998 to 2003.
Moreover, Congolese militiamen, known as Mai-Mai; the Forces démocratiques de libération du Rwanda (FDLR); the Congolese army; and other unarmed forces continue to wreak havoc in Rutshuru, Walikalé, Nyiragongo, Béni, Kanyabayonga and other riverine areas at Virunga National Park, north of Goma.
However, the United Nations Mission in the DRC, MONUC, says at least 83 percent of attacks on civilians in North Kivu are perpetuated by the regular army, known by its French acronym FARDC.
A senior humanitarian affairs officer at the UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA) in Goma, Jean-Charles Dupin, agrees. "FARDC is a great menace," he said, "it is an army charged with the duty of protecting civilians but being poorly paid by the government, lives by rape and other forms of brutality and murder. Most of the sexual violence is perpetrated by military groups."
Dupin said militias were still recruiting child soldiers in violation of the law and international child protection conventions, to which the DRC is a signatory.
According to the OCHA office in Goma, hundreds of people displaced by Saturday's fighting went to Kirotshe, en route to Bukavu, the provincial capital of neighbouring South Kivu Province.
"We hope these people will return to their villages soon," Dupin said. "The situation will be restored because the 8th Brigade has sent for reinforcements. MONUC has done the same."
The Committee for the Movement of Populations, a group of NGOs and humanitarian agencies in North Kivu, said displaced people sometimes returned to their homes on their own, they did not live in camps.
"The displaced live with other families," Andrew Zadel, an OCHA information officer in North Kivu, said. "But this situation creates enormous humanitarian problems because, during aid distribution, these families [though stable] also want to be assisted."
