The 20th International Quality Summit Convention (IQSC) BID gave Gramu General Merchants the International Quality Summit Gold Award in New York for quality and excellence out of 9,500 companies all over the world. They did not attend the convention because at the time elections were taking place in Uganda, thus they could not travel. However, their award will be sent soon.
For the past 28 years, BID has been awarding companies in 166 countries, making it one of the largest quality corporate image endorsers in the world, which promotes the corporate and brand image.
The Mutumbas say millet processing undergoes six stages to ensure that no impurities are left.
"The procedure requires specialised machines both for sorting, removing husks, drying, roasting, milling and packaging," they say. From the time they started using a fan, millet production increased. The Mutumbas started mixing millet flour with soya, making about 10kgs per day from one. They now have five brands on the market; pure millet for porridge, millet mixed with soya and millet mixed with cassava.
In 2004, they embarked on the production of soya-rice porridge and soya sauce. They recommend soya-millet for children from six months and above and feeding mothers.
They say it helps in the growth of the brain hence stimulating the intelligence quotient (IQ). Millet flour contains iron, proteins, vitamin B1, B6, pantothenic acid, moisture, minerals; magnesium, zinc, calcium and dietary fibre.
"As the quality of our products improved, the Seventh Day Adventist (SDA) Church tendered us to supply millet flour to Rwanda refugees during the 1994 genocide for six months. This was our first big tender," they said.
They can only process about 40 bags a month because of the technology. "We can process about 100 bags a month if provided with up-to-date technology, including packaging machines and a bigger working space," they add.
In June 2003, the Mutumbas brushed shoulders with President Yoweri Museveni at the Natural Chemotherapeutics Research Laboratory exhibition at Wandegeya, who promised them assistance through Joan Kakwenzire, the presidential advisor on poverty alleviation.
"We tried to reach her, but were told that she was busy or outside the country. We gave up and tried to see Maggie Kigozi, the executive director for Uganda Investment Authority instead, but we were discouraged at the office reception," they said.
Before the Mutumbas took on millet processing, they reared poultry for seven years.
In 1988, they decided to expand the poultry project and got a loan of sh2m from the Women Trust Bank. They bought 600 chick layers. As the hens were nearing laying eggs, a disease struck all of them and they died.
Gramu employs about 10 men, whom he feeds and accomodates. The Mutumbas said they make about sh1.5m per month. From their earnings, they have educated their children and recently, they bought a milling machine.

Original_Search_Title: World Bank Commissions Fadama, Health Projects
Headline: Nigeria: World Bank Commissions Fadama, Health Projects
Body_Text:
The World Bank country director in Nigeria, Hafez Ghanem has commissioned a number of Fadama and health projects in Imo state, as part of its programmes aimed at eradicating poverty.
The projects were commissioned in Amurara, Amagbo, Itu, Eziagbogu and Eziudu communities of Ezenihite local government of the state. The fadama projects are boreholes that were constructed to facilitate crop production ranging from cassava and vegetables to fruits. The health centres on the other hand were constructed under the Bank's Local Empowerment and Environmental Management Project (LEEMP) which is aimed at improving access to healt-hcare services.
Some of the projects commissioned by Mr. Ghanem on Friday include the Amumara water borehole project, Itu water borehole project, Amagbo health centre and Eziagbogu health centre all in Ezenihitte Local Government Area of the state. Speaking after commissioning one of the projects, the country director said the projects were funded by the ban k as a way of providing infrastructure to facilitate economic activities and imp-rove the social well-being of the people. He said the bank's operations are aimed at eradicating poverty at the grass roots level. He said the bank has committed $25 million to fund a number of projects in the state, pointing out that about $9million of that amount has been disbursed. The outstanding $16million would be disbursed in the coming months.
He expressed satisfaction with the level of project implementation of the state, stressing that community participation has proven to be an effective way of eradicating poverty. He said "today I visited the community projects supported by LEEMP, which is water, health, education, Fadama, Cassava and abattoir. What I have got out of all this is that this community approach is an excellent approach to fighting poverty. You see community mobilisation efforts with the communities putting in their contributions. And also you see mobilisation by the loca l and state governments. My impression is that this approach s working."
He said "I think LEEMP and Fadama in Imo state are doing very well and we are looking forward to working and expanding to other communities as well as doing mo-re in the local governments that we are currently present." He explained that although the Universal Basic Education programme (UBE) was discontinued at the end of last month, the bank would continue to fund the uncompleted education projects through the LEEMP scheme. "Some of the schools that were being financed by UBE were not completed. What we agreed with the state gover-nment was that we can use funding from LEEMP to cover what is not being paid for because of UBE. LEEMP has enough funding and we can even bring more through LEEMP.
Although a typical World Bank programme takes about five years to complete, Ghanem expressed optimism that some of the programmes in Imo sate would be completed in about two and a half years because of the speed and effectiveness in their implementation. He expressed the need for constant building of capacity amongst the rural dwellers, saying that it enhances the efficiency of the people in implementing community based poverty alleviation projects.

Original_Search_Title: Presidential Council On Renewable Energy Inaugurated
Headline: Nigeria: Presidential Council On Renewable Energy Inaugurated
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo has said that Nigeria must look beyond its current oil reserves and find alternative
Speaking at the inaugural meeting of the Presidential Council on Production of Sugar/Sweet Sorghum and Manufacturing of Ethanol as Renewable Fuel, in Abuja on July 31, the President explained that "when the oil reserve which is non-replenishable is exhausted, we have to find answers in renewable energy."
President Obasnajo said that increasing dependence on ethanol and bio-diesel would not only create more jobs and wealth but also enable the country's oil reserves last longer for the benefit of future generations.
Explaining that the main focus would be on ethanol-from-cassava where "Nigeria has comparative advantage" the President noted that the Council would create a conducive environment for private sector investors to operate.
The current members of the Council are the Ministers of Finance, Agriculture, Science & Technology, Water Resources, Industry; the Minister of State (Petroleum); the Group Managing Director (NNPC) and the Jigawa State Commissioner for Agriculture.

Original_Search_Title: Northern Political Leaders, Power Shift And the Rat Race
Headline: Nigeria: Northern Political Leaders, Power Shift And the Rat Race
Body_Text:
Kaduna — The bitter truth is that IBB and Abdulsalami were directly responsible for this unfair arrangement and the negative consequences on the North. Next to them are the PDP members and a couple of some so-called Northern elders.
During the administration of Shagari and the NPN government, power sharing was skewed in favour of the south. The chairman of NPN, Akinloye, was a Yoruba, from the Southwest. The president, Shagari, a Hausa Fulani, from the North-West. Ekwueme, the Vice President, from the south east. Wayas from the South-South, as the president of the Senate. While, the Speaker, Ezeoke is from the South East. So in the roll call at the top, out of five, four were from the South and one from the North. Today, the PDP government, with Obasanjo as the president, the equation is not balanced. The chairman of PDP from the north-central, Ali (must go), happens to be more Oduduwa than the Yoruba. While Obasanjo is from the Yoruba south-west. Atiku, the vice president is from the North-East. Now see how the southerners shortchanged the North. While during Shagari, because the North took the presidency, the south was given the heads of both legislative houses. Under the PDP, for its seven years of mi srule, it is different. The junior arm, the House, was given to the north, while the senior arm the Senate to the South.
A critical analysis would indicate that the executive and the legislative arm, are in fact relatively more controlled by the south, under PDP. Compared with the Shagari administration, where the executive arms of governenmnt was zoned to the north, while the legislative arm was zoned completely to the south. A closer look at the executive power sharing under NPN shows that the alliance was basically power sharing between NPN/NPP.
Ekwueme had more say than Atiku in relative terms. Compare this with the PDP where power shift was designed to favour the Yorubas in particular and the South in general.
The bitter truth is that IBB and Abdulsalami were directly responsible for this unfair arrangement and the negative consequences on the North. Next to them are the PDP members and a couple of some so-called Northern elders. They accepted this contraption. The disaster is the continued silence of IBB and Abdulsalami in the face of the incompetence and the misrule of Obasanjo. The argument of fairness to the south by surrendering power to them has proved sceptics right. Obasanjo's crooked ways to retain power and scheme the North out of power forever is as clear as the rising sun.
With regards to the banks' consolidation, here too is another second score against the North. Now with the consolidation of banks, none today is completely owned and controlled by the North.
Suppose one crooked bank executive exchanges customers deposit of N140.0 billion and transfers the amount abroad. This after he may have sent his family out there and the next you here is that, he has vanished. One is not an alarmist, but one should not rule out this scenario in Nigeria. Since it was done before, it could be done again.
Again suppose the deposits are mismanaged through dubious loans, or false accounting reports, or bogus and reckless expenditures and undue bonuses, and other fraudulent 'practices too numerous to count in this article.
The North must realise that if you are an underdog and have no control of finance and banking and the capital market, then we should as well forget about the presidency and crown Obasanjo as a life King and Emperor of Nigeria. Then we proceed to "dubale" the all knowing messiah for the crumbs. Our naivety has started to manifest.
When the foreign reserves of Nigeria are handed over to the banks from the south to manage, where do we stand?
The third score against the north is the question of food security. While the north is the undisputable food basket of the nation, Obasanjo would appear to think otherwise. The abundant land for agriculture, livestock and fish production, are natural resource countries the world over would wish they have. However, cassava and cocoa production are what we hear in agricultural development and financing. The reason is that the key beneficiary would appear to be the South-West, his enclave.
When it comes to livestock, the federal government is unconcerned with the production of meat from cattle, goats and sheep. With livestock, milk and milk products can be nurtured, developed and turned into a veritable source for conserving foreign exchange. The milk imports that consume scarce foreign exchange can be saved. Tackling hunger with nutritious food and job creation will have been the resultant effect had we a leadership with the foresight and the vision. However, since most of these farmers are not from south-west, it has become a non-issue.
On electric power supply, at present eleven power utilities projects are under construction. Ten are in the south and only one in the north!!! That is score is number four against the north.
Finally and conclusively, Obasanjo demands 100% loyalty. He does not accept anything less. The paradoxes, however, are too numerous to count. There is no loyalty on his part on the constitution of the federal republic of Nigeria or his party the PDP.
There is no loyalty to the budget he signs into law, which is the single most important aspect of government that affects the lives of Nigerians. No loyalty to due process or financial accountability with the sacking of his auditor general.
No loyalty to anti-corruption which he deceptively uses to fight his political opponents.
Obasanjo does not respect democratic norms by his attempt to perpetuate himself in power through the aborted third term.
In fact there is no loyalty as far as the dignity and interest of Nigerians is concerned. There is no concern for the economic well being of Nigerians with the way and manner the privatisation of their assets is being carried out fraudulently. In .short what matters to Obasanjo is to be Nigerias life president at all cost.

Original_Search_Title: Bakuli 2K Has Fine Beef Stew
Headline: Uganda: Bakuli 2K Has Fine Beef Stew
Body_Text:
Kampala — The winner on the menu is the luwombo of either goat or chicken
Bakuli 2 K Restaurant is without a doubt one of the most successful food establishment in Kampala when it comes to serving traditional fare. Situated in suburbia Bakuli it defies all accepted norms of what would constitute a good eating-place in terms of ambience, table arrangement, and general tidiness/cleanliness and above all a comfortable environment from where one would wish to eat much let alone be seen.
What is even more baffling is the fact that those who come to dine there time and again, as in repeat customers, are people of affluence if the car that one drives is anything to go by; this is no place for old jalopies. The food is on the whole of a decent standard with the prices being, by Kampala standards, 'give way' in every sense of the word.
In the company of fellow trencherman we traipsed over there last week for a late lunch and got there just before three in the afternoon and the place was choc a block as in SRO, a situation that showed little or no sign of abating an hour later, much to my colleagues amazement!
The menu is a seemingly spoken affair with a choice of beef or goat stew or luwombo of either goat or chicken or even fish stew. The staple accompaniments are superb matooke, sweet potatoes, cassava, rice or their version of pilao, a great disappointment considering the niggardly pieces of meat that are not only tiny but few and not even Irish potatoes. The saving grace was that the rice was well cooked and the spicing was there not to mention the fact that the rice was Basmati. Service is relatively swift though the idea of knife going hand in glove with a fork seems totally alien. Then again many a Ugandan prefers eating with their fingers than using knives and forks.
What this joint badly needs is to replace the floor tiles and those eyesores of plastic chairs that must date back to the end of the last century and have more than outlived their usefulness. A paint job would be a great blessing and even some plants here and there and maybe even some artwork. Equally disturbing and untenable is the view of the kitchen from the dining area with its attendant smoke, not something that one would write home about in an enthusiastic manner!
And as for the waitresses, it would be so nice if they wore flat shoes that were closed, what some people refer to as 'boot' rather than a free for all where some choose to wear sandals. In spite or despite the squalid state of Baklui 2 K Restaurant they come in droves surely putting a huge smile to Hajat and Haj who own the place. Ugandans will put with even worse without uttering a sigh of discontent.
rating
XXXX: UNRIVALLED
XXX: EXCELLENT
XX: VERY GOOD
NONE: POOR TO SATISFACTORY
These views are purely the reviewer's personal reaction to food, ambience and service with price being factored into consideration. The menu listing may change without notice.
Place: Bakuli 2 K Restaurant
Rating: The food deserves XX while the rest a NONE
Smoke-Free Zone: I do not think that anybody really cares
Food:Good traditional fare served Table d'Hote
Price Range: Pilao 2'500/-, beef stew and matooke 3'500/-, Chicken luwombo and matooke/rice 4'000/-
Location: Off Namirembe Road on Hoima Road, Bakuli
Ambience: Unheard of and not likely to change
Parking: Freely on the pavement and wherever else one can find a space.
Reservations: Not necessary
Open: Seven days till late

Original_Search_Title: God Uses Chief Tony Anenih to Give Me Whatever I Ask For, Says Elder Odion Ugbesia
Headline: Nigeria: God Uses Chief Tony Anenih to Give Me Whatever I Ask For, Says Elder Odion Ugbesia
Body_Text:
Benin City — Saturday Vanguard met him in a relaxed mood last week Sunday at his GRA residence in Benin City.He was actually playing the game of draft with one of his aides, but when this reported called at his door he quickly ushered him in and pointed out that "Vanguard, this is how I spend my time, not drugs as my opponents have been speculating. Sit down, my brother, and ask me any thing you want to know about me."
His humility and simplicity was contrary to lots of speculations about this man, Elder Odion Ugbesia, former Minister for Solid Minerals Development and one of the aspirants eyeing the governorship of the state in 2007. He disclosed that he came from a very poor background and had to become a taxi driver in America for him to pay his school fees. He also recalled how he and his twin brother, Akhere Ugbesia were locked up by the police in an election day during the General Sanni Abacha regime. He also said that "God uses the chairman of the PDP Board of Trustees, Chief Tony Anenih to give me any thing I asked for". Excerpts:
Meet Ugbesia
I am Elder Odion Ugbesia, I am from Ubiaja in Esan South East Local Government Area of Edo State. I was born in a little village called Ahie, my mother's village, which is about five minutes drive from Ubiaja. I went to school there. I grew up in the village and after that I went to school in America. I was a lecturer at the University of Benin for eleven years at the Department of Political Science. I was commissioner here in Edo from 1992-1993 during Chief John Odigie Oyegun's administration; I was commissioner for information and culture. I was secretary of the Movement of the Creation of Edo State under the leadership of the Oba of Benin. I was a Personal Assistant to the then National Chairman of the Social Democratic Party (SDP), Chief Tony Anenih, and I also later became his Special Assistant when he was the Minister of Works. I was a Minister of State in the Ministry of Internal Affairs and Minister of Solid Minerals. But first and foremost, I am a farmer and I am mar ried with four children -- two boys and two girls. I just enjoy being a bush man, I stay in the bush most of the time.
I farm and hunt in the bush
I spend quite a lot of time in the bush. If I am not on my farm, I am in the bush hunting for guinea fowl and other small animals. I just enjoy being away from the maddening crowd of the cities. I plant yams, cassava, oil palm, beans and vegetables. I also keep poultry, piggery farm, goats and I have an ostrich farm. These are things that keep me busy, things that I enjoy. I like to spend time away from the hustling and bustling in the cities.
I was born in hardship
Growing up was particularly difficult. Don't forget I have a twin brother; we grew up in a rural setting and certainly without any silver spoon. But we thank God that we got to where we are now out of our personal efforts. It might interest you to know that we worked while we went to school. I worked in a hospital and in a factory when I was in America. That was from 1971 to 1978. I was also a taxi driver, I did that after my classes in order to survive. In fact it was really difficult but that is the only way to survive in America. But the only thing that is giving me jot today is that after all that I survived as a man. So I am from a very poor background but when we look back now, there are big lessons from such experiences. There are times that I gather my children and tell them that we used to trek from so so distance to get to the farm or school. They just laugh and say, Daddy, that was then, this is now. But I thank God for everything.
I was shocked when I was told that I have been made a minister
I was a Special Assistant to Chief Tony Anenih when he was a minister. When he now left government as a minister I was made a minister just like that. I did not bargain for it, I did not ask for it, I did not even dream about it. I was shocked when he just called me one day and said, "Odion I just submitted your name as a minister." That is the story of my life today.
God helps me through Chief Anenih
I met Chief Anenih so many years ago during the NPN days in a political gathering and we have been together since then and have grown very close. He has been wonderful in my life.
Is it true that you and your twin brother were locked up some time in the booth because of Chief Anenih?
No we were not put in a booth. What happened was that I was arrested with my twin brother, that was during NRC and DPN, during the Abacha days. It was during an election; some of our supporters were beaten up, and we went to the Police station to report and the Police turned round to say that we were under arrest under orders from above. Well, there are prices you have to pay; so when I tell people that I have paid my dues they should listen because I have paid my dues. My parents were so worried when my twin brother and I were locked up but it did not bother me at all. I just thought that was one of those things we had to go through as politicians and it certainly did not bother any of us. My parents were of course worried. But if you ask me to describe Chief Tony Anenih, I will just tell you that he is more than a father to me. He is wonderful. The other day I said to somebody that he is the one God uses in my life. Anytime I pray to God to help me solve a particular proble m, somewhere along the line Chief Anenih comes and does it, so he is the one that God uses to help me. To many people he is a very generous human being. He is a man with a bundle of energy. Sometimes we wonder where gets his energy from. He is the last to go to bed, and the first to wake up. We the younger ones cannot even cope with him. He does not see a political problem that is insurmountable, even if they are difficult he can deal with it. And again he is the most misunderstood Nigerian politician I ever met.
I never slept during executive council meeting while a minister
There are rumours all over the place but this one I have not heard. Certainly you cannot go to exco where President Obasanjo is presiding and sleep (laughter). I have not heard this one, it is a new rumour. However, people can talk and talk, but it does not really bother me. But God knows that I did the little I can and I leave the rest to God.
I am also not a drug addict as it is being rumoured
I do not even smoke, I use to smoke when I was younger but now I do not smoke and I do not drink. I have heard this before and I laughed. If you see a drug addict you will know him. I do not even have many friends who smoke around me. I have heard this rumour that I am a drug addict, but again it does not bother me. During a football match, it is the man in the front that is always the target. So every body is attacking Ugbesia because they know I am in the front so I am not bothered. Again because I know I do not do all these that is why I am not bothered. It is only when you are in politics that they tell you how your father made love to your mother that resulted in you being born. Some times they will tell you that that person is not his father as he claimed, his father was so so and so man. I know all these as a politician, I have been in this business for twenty seven years. You must develop a thick skin to deal with this kind of situation. That is all politics is all ab out, trying to bring him down, telling one lie or the other, but it does not bother me a bit.
What motivated me into politics
Incidentally I am a political scientist and I taught politics for eleven years, so it is not strange that I am in politics, it is my calling. I have helped people go into positions and that is why I said this time I can do it myself and that is where we are.
Why I want to govern Edo State
If you recall I told you I was the secretary to the movement for the creation of this state, I participated actively. Therefore I know the terrain, and I have spent the greater part of my life playing the politics of Edo State. I know every nook and cranny of this state and I know what the problems are. I think I can make a difference based on my experience. It is a big lie to say leaders of the party want to impose me on the people. Because I am the best candidate, every body is worried. But they know the truth; that I deserve to govern this state. I believe in power rotation, I also believe in agreements. If an agreement is reached between two people, you should learn to uphold that agreement. If there was an agreement between leaders of the party some time in 2003, that after Edo South it will go to Edo Central, for God's sake let it go to Edo Central. It is not fair for anybody to have held power for eight years and then want to continue to do so after that. I believe tha t equity, justice and fair play should be allowed to prevail in this matter. On the issue of imposition, nobody is trying to impose me on the people. I believe in democracy, so all the candidates will go out there and slug it out. So the issue of trying to impose me on the people is a lie because PDP is not a party of imposition. I believe that I am on ground and I can defeat other candidates without any problem. The rumour that I am being imposed on the people of the state is again another ploy to malign me by my opponents.
My philosophy in life
Learn to remain steadfast, learn to tell the truth all the time, most importantly you should be loyal to whatever you are doing and to whoever you are going out with. You must be loyal to your friends, you must be loyal to a cause. Loyalty counts and it is very rewarding. No matter what you do you must be patient, you must persevere. Do not be in a hurry to make money, do not be in a hurry to make name, what God says is yours, nobody can take away from you, so why rush? Everything has been ordained by God. Those are the things that guide me and that is the advice I give to people.

Original_Search_Title: Gov't Loses Over ¢2 Billion in Smuggling
Headline: Ghana: Gov't Loses Over Â¢2 Billion in Smuggling
Body_Text:
Dabala — THE GOVERNMENT has lost over two billion cedis as a result of rampant smuggling activities going on at the eastern corridor of the country's border posts.
So far, the Dabala Customs, Excise and Preventive Service (CEPS) in the South Tongu District of the Volta Region has impounded over 12,000 pieces of wax prints between January and July this year.
Smuggling activities in wax prints and cigarettes have assumed an alarming proportion along the Eastern corridors, and smugglers have adopted sophisticated methods to outwit CEPS officials as they resort to the appointment of agents, the use of mobile phones and the wrapping of wax prints around children before dressing them up, in a bid to succeed in their illegal acts.
The Chief Collector at the Aflao Sector who is also in charge of the Dabala checkpoint, Mr. Ben Suuri, made this known to The Chronicle at Dabala when CEPS officials intercepted 1,503 wax prints last Sunday.
Mr. Suuri said most of the smugglers have been using unapproved routes like Akakpo-Kope near Dabala through agents, feigning funerals by carrying coffins full of wax prints and cigarettes, and the use of bicycles to carry smuggled goods to the riverside at Sogakope and cross the Volta Lake to Accra.
He also said although the use of mobile phones was good, criminals and smugglers have also taken advantage of the technology to outwit law enforcement agencies.
Mr. Suuri revealed that wax prints and cigarettes were being put in sand, cassava products like 'gari' or 'kokonte', cement, loaded fish baskets and even in vehicle engines, while some female smugglers fold them up and them around their middle and pretend to be pregnant.
He expressed regret that some of the smugglers sometimes attempt to kill CEPS officials, citing last Sunday's smugglers on the Ho-Sogakope-Adidome road as an example, whom he claimed had tried to crash their vehicle into that of the CEPS officials while being chased for arrest. He said the officials managed to maneuvre to escape death narrowly.
Mr. Suuri disclosed CEPS' administration has now purchased an engine boat, which would be used to track down the smuggling activities on the Volta Lake at Sogakope. He said the Service has now decided to use private cars at the Dabala checkpoint because smugglers easily identify the Service's official vehicles, making it very difficult to make any arrest.
The Chief Collector said the smuggling of wax prints and cigarettes were on the increase and pointed out that apart from the wax prints, which were the highest smuggled products, cigarettes were the second highly smuggled goods into the country through the Eastern corridor.
Mr. Suuri attributed the high level of smuggling of wax prints into the country to the big market and demand for the product in Togo, and the greedy nature of some Ghanaian businessmen and women to get rich quick. These, he said, were killing the Ghanaian economy.
Mr. Suuri therefore cautioned those who involved themselves in the dubious business of smuggling goods into the country to cultivate the habit of paying their taxes, which he noted was a major economic means for the growth and development of the country.
The Chronicle has gathered, however, that the major cause of the increased smuggling of wax prints into the country is the restriction that all wax prints should be imported through the Takoradi Port.
Some of the smugglers openly confessed to the paper that it was not economically reasonable and feasible for a businessman to travel from the various parts of the country to Takoradi, the only designated point, to purchase or import wax prints while cheaper wax prints of equal quality could be found in Togo.

Original_Search_Title: The Rice Penalty
Headline: Nigeria: The Rice Penalty
Body_Text:
Lagos — The nation's decision to ban the importation of rice and press ahead with the revival rice production for both local and international consumption was hailed as one of the positive steps towards reforming and diversifying the nation's economy. Nigerians had acquired a strong taste for imported foods to the detriment of locally-sourced products and this took its toll on the foreign exchange reserves. The ban on rice and other items was part of a larger policy to boost the flagging agricultural sector and make optimal use of its numerous potentials. After all agriculture has long been identified as the largest non-oil export earner revenue but somehow in the face of the oil boom and petro-dollars, interest in it had waned.
Hence with a view to amongst other things reduce the nation's dependence on oil revenue, generate employment, achieve food security and reduce poverty, agriculture has rightly found its way to the top of the nation's economic development agenda. Today it forms a major component of the NEEDS document. Indeed a lot depends on the agricultural sector being transformed and sustained as a revenue earner for the nation. Some of the targets set for the agricultural sector include improvements in the annual growth rate, raise agricultural exports and reduction in food imports. There are indications that some of the targets have been met already. For instance last week it was reported by the Federal Ministry of Agriculture that between 2005 and 2006 the agricultural sector grew by seven per cent.
The nation's efforts to revitalise the agriculture sector saw the birth of several initiatives such as the Presidential Initiative on Increased Rice Production and Export and presidential initiatives on other products such as cotton, sugar, vegetable oil, cereals etc. This demonstrates the seriousness with which the government is seeking to promote agricultural development as a route to improve the country's foreign exchange earnings and reduce poverty. Recently the Federal Minister for Agriculture, Mr Adamu Bello was quoted as saying that the various initiatives on cassava, cocoa and rice had raised the country' production from 34 million tonnes to 42 million tonnes of food crops between 2004 and 2005. He revealed that the rice initiative has reduced the importation from two million tonnes in 2002 to one million tonnes in 2004.
It was therefore somewhat alarming to read a news report that the nation's decision to ban the importation of rice infringes on the trade agreement between Nigeria and the World Trade Organisation (WTO) and this may attract a heavy fine from the latter. It goes on to say that the development could lead to the withdrawal of WTO benefits to Nigeria in the area of agricultural inputs. According to the report if "Nigeria is found guilty, she would lose substantial revenue in million of dollars as penalty and legal bills".
What impact could this have on the nation's spirited efforts to transform agricultural production? Would the Federal Government extend the date of the ban on importation of rice beyond 2007, scrap it completely or comply with the penalty? The nation recorded an astronomical import bill that ran into billions of US dollars through the importation of rice over the years. The dumping of rice on the nation had huge implications for the nation's small-holding farmers. Their livelihoods came under immense threat and they were prevented from fulfilling their agricultural potential. The renewed efforts in the production of rice locally have been recognised for alleviating poverty, employment generation and wealth creation. These may all suffer a setback if care is not taken.
Trade in agricultural products is a global phenomenon which attracts a lot of politicking and decisions that can make or mar a nation's economy and domestic policies. A lot has been said on the issue of subsidies on agricultural products by western countries and their effects on the farmers in the developing countries especially in Africa.
According to the news report, blame should be laid firmly on the shoulders of those who negotiated trade treaties for Nigeria under the Uruguay Agreement between 1995 and 2000. There may be a strong case here. In other words we were represented at these crucial trade agreements by people who lack sufficient experience to secure the best for the nation. The Report of the Commission for Africa also draws our attention to similar lapses during its discussion on fair trade: "The discussion must adopt a more transparent and inclusive style of decision-making than is often the norm at WTO negotiations. And it must ensure that poorly staffed African governments can get a fair deal when involved in highly complex rules-based trade negotiations in which rich countries have large team of highly-paid lawyers". Although this is an observation made from the perspective of the entire African Continent, there is clearly a lesson to be learnt here. It is the reference to the fact that trade negotiations at the WTO need to be conducted by experienced and knowledgeable staff and not those lured by the prospect of estacode to handle briefs beyond their competences!
The report on the penalty further highlights the unfair and one-sided agreements that have been served to Africa especially where it concerns access to the world markets and the impact it has on reducing poverty. Polices on subsidies and trade have variously come under strong criticism but so far not much has been done. The last WTO talks held in Hong Kong last December serves as a perfect reminder of the trade imbalances that exist between the North and the South.
Nigeria's ban on the importation on rice should in no way attract a fine or withdrawal of WTO benefits. On the contrary the nation should be commended for taking bold step towards addressing some of its domestic problems such as unemployment, and poverty, as well as promoting agricultural development and diversifying the foreign exchange earner base. There has been a lot of rhetoric from the West about how African nations should make concerted moves towards achieving economic growth and progress. The first clear sign of shifting consumption patterns back to local food from imported foods gets slammed with a threat of a heavy fine. This development does not serve as a good example to other African nations struggling to find ways out of poverty. The level of commitments and double-standards exhibited by the West towards the plight of sub-Saharan Africa on the issue of trade distortions is to say the least quite scandalous.
A lot however depends on how the Government responds to this development. If anything the latest antics out of the corridors of the WTO should not see a reversal or slowdown in the nation's drive towards agricultural development. Neither should it be a dampener on the robust export plans for rice and other products.
The Commission for Africa's Report perhaps best sums up the approach WTO should adopt towards developing nations: "individual African countries must be allowed to sequence their trade reforms in line with their poverty reduction and development plans, and not be forced to open up their markets to foreign imports on terms that damage their infant industries".

Original_Search_Title: Ben Dolsi Malo Lauds Girl Child Education in Sierra Leone
Headline: Sierra Leone: Ben Dolsi Malo Lauds Girl Child Education in Sierra Leone
Body_Text:
Former British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC) presenter, Ben Dolsi Marlo, a Ghanaian by Nationality now in his mid 40's, who is now with the United Nations in New York, has said that the Sierra Leone Government has signalled to other nations to give girls priority in the educational sector.
He made the commendation on the Sierra Leone Government on July 20 at the Mammy Yoko Hotel en route to Ghana.
He said he was greatly impressed with Sierra Leone during his visit while in the delegation of the United Nations Secretary General Kofi Annan on his visit to the coastal West African country. Ben Dolsi Malo speaking exclusively to this reporter on July 3, said there had been much improvement in post- war Sierra Leone.
On good governance, , corruption and development, he said , "The government need have their honour in thick blanket and not lay it to be trampled in the dust" About his background, He said he hailed from a poor home where he could remember he used to fetch cassava for feeding and commercial purpose for his family.
On corruption in Sierra Leone Ben Dolsi Marlo said, "Corruption is literal and technical' .
'One has to be mindful, lawful, careful and cautious in using it', he said.
Comparing BBC to the UN Radio where he now works, he said both are sophisticated but the United Nations Radio is more sophisticated.

Original_Search_Title: No Respite for People of Niger Delta
Headline: Nigeria: No Respite for People of Niger Delta
Body_Text:
Yenagoa — When oil began seeping from pipelines owned by Italian oil company Agip recently, Nigerian newspapers reported that the spill was caused by sabotage.
Agip denied it had been forced to cut production because of an alleged attack, but acknowledged that its network had been damaged and that repairs were underway.
It is unclear exactly what happened to the Agip pipelines but that is nothing unusual in Nigeria's troubled delta region, where sabotage, accidents, oil siphoning and deteriorating infrastructure all mean the same thing to millions of local villagers: more pollution.
"Oil spills have become a great environmental tragedy in Nigeria, polluting streams, farmlands, the air and destroying lives," said Nnimmo Bassey, head of Environmental Rights Action (ERA), which is affiliated with the international environmental group Friends of the Earth.
Prior to the oil boom of the 1970s, Nigeria's main exports were agricultural products. Although the majority of the population identifies farming as their livelihood, investment in the agricultural sector over the years has been sidelined in favour of oil.
Since December last year, more than 10 major oil or gas pipelines have been blasted allegedly by militants belonging to the Movement for the Emancipation of the Niger Delta (MEND). Foreign oil workers are routinely taken hostage, although they are released within days or weeks unharmed.
Nigeria's oil output has been slashed about about one-quarter because of recent leaks, sabotage and unrest in the delta.
Royal Dutch Shell, Nigeria's biggest oil producer, said on Tuesday that a leak to an oil pipeline in Rivers State had cut its output there by 180,000 barrels per day. The source of the leak was not immediately clear.
Militants claim to be fighting for an increased share of oil wealth for the delta's inhabitants, many of whom live without electricity, running water or access to education. Although the country now has a democratic government after decades of military rule, the people of the Niger Delta complain that little has changed in terms of their standard of living.
Environmental Damage
Each pipeline blast has caused a major oil spill, and in one case in December at least eight people were killed when a fire borne by an oil slick swept through their homes.
In the report "Nigeria: Want in the Midst of Plenty," the Brussels-based Crisis Group said recently that despite more than US $400 billion in oil revenue over the past three decades, nine out of ten Nigerians live on less than US $2 a day.
Crisis Group said growing tensions in the delta were a direct result of decades of environmental harm and political neglect.
While oil companies blame most of the spills on sabotage and vandalism, activist groups insist that more of the damage and spills come from poorly maintained pipelines.
"The spillage has been there long before the militants," said Peter Ajube, spokesman for Ijaw Youths Council (IYC), an influential activist group campaigning for the rights of the delta's dominant ethnic group, the Ijaw.
"We don't like what the militants are doing because we're non-violent, but we know that most of the spills are caused by aged pipelines," said Ajube. "And whenever you have a spill it is the communities in the area that suffer, losing their fishing areas, losing their farms and source of drinking water."
Inhabitants of Igbomotoro, in Bayelsa state, suffered short- and long-term effects from an oil slick that came from a ruptured pipeline on Nun River in July.
"I lost my fishing nets used to trap fish in the river along with a night's catch," said Inikro Alaowei. "I don't expect any harvests either later this year from my cassava farm, which was also affected."
A communal forest serving Igbomotoro was also swamped by the oil, destroying a source of food and traditional plant medicines.
Alienated from Land and Resources
Community members expect no solace since oil companies as a tradition do not pay compensation for ecological damage caused by sabotage. The rationale is to discourage wilful vandalism in expectation of compensation, a practice the companies blame for a larger share of oil spills than the activists and the communities accept.
In other cases, residents damage pipelines in an effort to siphon oil to sell. The practice, known as 'bunkering', is highly dangerous. Scores and sometimes hundreds of people die each year if the gushing fuel catches fire as they scramble to scoop it up.
The heightened threat to the environmental health of the Niger Delta resulting from oil operations are highlighted in a recent human development study published by the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) in Nigeria.
"Oil spills and gas flares in particular have destroyed natural resources central to local livelihoods," said the report.
Gas flaring produces greenhouse gases and exposes communities to heat, noise and air pollution.
The UNDP report said people in the delta have been alienated from their land and resources, leaving them frustrated with both the oil companies and governments that have failed to regulate them.
Royal Dutch Shell, the biggest international oil operator in Nigeria, accounting for roughly half the country's exports of 2.5 million barrels daily, also has more onshore operations in the delta than other major oil companies. Figures released by the company show it has more than 1,000 oil wells in the region linked by more than 6,000 km of pipeline network.
Shell acknowledges the extent of its presence in the delta poses a major environmental challenge, which it says it is working hard to manage.
"Our environmental programme is geared towards reducing the negative impact of our operations on the environment," Shell states on its website. In this regard the company has since 1997 made environmental sustainability a key principle to be considered in all business undertakings. This has resulted in increased environmental monitoring and more rapid response to remedy situations created by spillages.
Lack of Enforcement
Inyang Duke, an environmental expert from the University of North Carolina visiting Nigeria, said strict enforcement of regulations is key to improving environmental practices in the delta.
Nigeria recently acquired patrol boats to help monitor the delta. However, observers say it is difficult to affectively monitor much of the region because of dense mangroves.
"Nigeria has the right (environmental) regulations and policies but lacks the technical capacity to implement and enforce them," Duke said.
One major reason for this failure is the government's awkward position as regulator and primary beneficiary with the majority stake in joint venture operations run by oil multinationals that produce nearly all the country's oil, said Duke.
"You have to separate the regulated from the regulator; there must be no conflict of interest," he said.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Bribery, Violence And Violation of Electoral Rules Mar Polls
Headline: Kenya: Bribery, Violence And Violation of Electoral Rules Mar Polls
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Claims of bribery, sporadic violence and violation of electoral rules marred Monday's by-elections in five constituencies.
The Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) deplored the "wanton violation of electoral rules by the Government" in elections that were characterised by low voter turnout.
At the same time, pro-Government functionaries sustained an onslaught launched a week ago against the ECK, with another round of vitriolic attack on chairman Samuel Kivuitu, whom they accused of being disrespectful to the Narc Government.
But the elections watchdog body vowed it would not be beaten into submission with threats - for exposing yet another flagrant violation of election laws by the State.
Violence reared its head in Nakuru Town and North Horr, an expansive constituency with some polling stations up to 150 kilometers apart, while a woman was arrested in Moyale for dishing out cash to voters.
Later in the day, as frustration began to set in, two MPs found themselves caught up in a scuffle in Nakuru, where polling had generally been peaceful.
Bandits attack polling station
And in an unprecedented attack in North Horr, 10 bandits armed with automatic rifles laid siege on Hula-Hula polling station for over 30 minutes, firing in the air in a bid to scare voters.
Presiding officer, Mariam Barako, said hundreds of voters fled as the brigands opened fire at around 11 am. No one was injured in the attack, but it nevertheless accentuated perennial security concerns in northern Kenya.
Four Administration Policemen also fled. Hula-Hula is some 12km south of Marsabit.
On Monday, the Government appeared unrelenting in its attack on the ECK high command, with Energy assistant minister Mwangi Kiunjuri daring Kivuitu to resign "for being disrespectful to Narc".
"Kivuitu has been desperate and disrespectful of the Government. He should either do his work or resign. After all he has served for many years, he is old," he said. "We wonder if he is an LDP activist."
Kiunjuri was speaking at Afraha Stadium when he, together with assistant ministers Stephen Tarus and Danson Mungatana and MPs Macharia Mukiri and Jayne Kihara accompanied the Narc-Kenya aspirant William Kariuki Mirugi to cast his vote.
Vice-chairman Gabriel Mukele said he had witnessed a GK vehicle emblazoned with Narc-Kenya posters, campaigning for its candidate Ukur Yattani. It had Narc-K supporters, he said.
At a press conference in Marsabit Town, Mukele said the vehicle had private registration numbers but on closer look, it bore GK registration numbers - which he declined to reveal - on its windscreen and side mirrors.
Little General Election
At Bubisa, the ECK official said he saw voters being given rice and a truck transporting voters to and from polling stations.
Makadara MP Reuben Ndolo and his Subukia counterpart, Koigi Wamwere, were caught up in a melee as the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) supporters beat up three youth they alleged had been ferried to vote from the neighbouring Subukia Constituency.
Police had to whip out guns to save the trio from being lynched by the irate LDP supporters. Ndolo confronted Koigi's bodyguard, who had drawn his gun to keep the attackers at bay, and demanded that he keeps the firearm away.
"Unatoa bunduki ya nini? Hata sisi tuko nazo. (What are you drawing your gun for? We also have guns) Ndolo told the police officer. In the heat of the moment, an aide of LDP candidate, Pastor Mike Brawan, also drew a pistol, sending people scampering for safety.
About 200,000 voters were expected to go to the polls to pick replacements for MPs who were killed in a plane crash last April. This is the first time in Kenya's history that by-elections were being conducted in five constituencies on the same day.
Voters in Nakuru Town (117,016), Saku (17,050), Laisamis (15,610), North Horr (17,281) and Moyale (27,860) began voting as early as 6am in the "Little General Election", whose outcome political analysts argue, would provide a peek into how the battle for 2007 will be fought.
Misuse of public resources
The by-elections were occasioned by the deaths of Mirugi Kariuki (Nakuru Town), Bonaya Godana (North Horr), Titus Ngoyoni (Laisamis), Abdi Sasura (Saku) and Guracha Galgalo (Moyale) when a Kenya Air Force plane ferrying them to Marsabit for a peace mission crashed in foggy weather.
Several civic by-elections were also under way countrywide. By 6pm on Monday, most polling stations had closed, with counting to determine who would carry the day getting under way by 7pm.
In Marsabit, Kanu candidate Sarah Godana complained voters were being given relief food near polling stations at Hurri Hills and other areas that were her strongholds.
Voters, she added, were mostly assisted to vote and those who voted for her rival got relief food on the spot.
And, in an apparent reaction to Government spokesman Alfred Mutua's denial that the State was not using resources to influence voters, Mukele said: "If what I witnessed at Bubisa was not misuse of public resources, then what is?"
"I will speak the truth. If they (Government) sack me, I will go home to eat my cassava," Mukele said.
He said the bandit attack at Hula-Hula had not affected voting "very much". However, when The Standard left the station at 2.30pm, only election officials were present.
Kanu's Saku candidate, Halake Dida, said more than 300 people had not voted at Hula-Hula and Karare because of the incident.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Drops
Headline: Tanzania: Inflation Drops
Body_Text:
Dar Es Salaam — Tanzania's inflation rate in the month of June dropped to 6.8% compared to the preceding month (May).
The inflation in May reached 7.7%, the highest the country experienced over the last eight-years, bringing down the country's economic growth as well. Reducing inflation has been a challenge to economists since last October when the rate started to escalate due to the drought that caused food shortages to the country. 10% of the country's population faced acute food shortages and survived on food relief.
The National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) attributed the new inflation trend to the decrease of food prices-especially cereals. The June National Consumer Price Index (NCPI) shows that the prices for rice, maize flour, cassava, vegetables, fruits, beans, cow peas and groundnuts went down.
A statistician with the NBS has said the trend shows that this month's inflation rate might drop further as prices continue to drop.
"The data gathered already show the reverse trend on prices," he said.
Food prices are the major contributor to the country's inflation rate. NCPI further shows that the prices of non-food items such as children clothes and furniture also went down. However, fuel, power and water index has gone up by 10.4%.
An economist with the Confederation of Tanzania Industry (CTI), Mr. Hussein Kamote, said that currently the major threat to the country's inflation rate is the impact of oil prices that have escalated to $78 a barrel following the Israel and Hezbollah confrontation.
"At the moment food and salaries are not the inflation major challenge, but fuel prices at world market might jeopardise the effort of taming the rate to below 5%," Mr. Kamote predicted.
Mr. Kamote said that the government's minimum salary increase of 50% will not affect the inflation rate as the amount entering the market is very little. The wage bill went up from about Tsh800 million to Tsh1.2 billion ($1.2 million) in this fiscal year, but this will be only for some public sectors.
"If the minimum wage was for all sectors, it means that it would have the impact on the manufacturers' cash flow and thus increasing prices of goods," Mr Kamote said.
The government employees account for about 10% of the total country workforce. The Minister of Labour, Employment and Youth Development, Prof. Jumanne Maghembe, told Business Week that the country's statutory minimum wage would be announced after the President's Salary Review Commission concludes its study at the end of this year. The president's salary review committee was appointed in May this year and given six-months to establish an amount that would make life easier for low earning workers in the country.

Original_Search_Title: 'We'll Assist to Build Base Stations'
Headline: Nigeria: 'We'll Assist to Build Base Stations'
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — Rivers State government has said it would assist an unnamed Global Mobile Telecommun-ications network to build 56 base stations to cover all parts of the state, as a means of providing about 6,000 jobs.
Briefing newsmen yesterday at the Government House, Commissioner of Special Duties, Shedrack Akolokwo, said government was implementing a holistic approach to poverty alleviation in all spheres.
Asked how much it would cost and how the state hoped to recoup the amount to be ploughed into the venture, he said the cost was not immediately available, while the benefiting network would refund the money within the expected roll-out period.
Akolokwo said the scheme was part of government's sustainable development programmes, aimed at shortening the roll-out time period and help those in the rural areas to go into the call business and create wealth.
Other programmes include investment of about $20 million into the cassava initiative, to which he said Shell Petroleum Develop-ment Corporation has pledged $5 million.
Government aims to assist communities to plant improved cassava and process it into the nearly 201 different products derivable from it, as a means of getting foreign exchange and keeping people in business.
To ensure that diseases associated with cassava plants does not occur, he said they have partnered with the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA), to provide mosaic resistant species of cassava, adding that government also aims to have large cassava plantations to be owned by natives in all the local government areas of the state.
Akolokwo said nother area of intervention was the aquaculture, where fish farms would be sited so that wealth creation would spred to the rural areas.
He said there were similar government interventions in nine areas which would in addition to creating wealth, ensure that youths are positively engaged to push down the rate of restiveness.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava As Giant of Export Produce, Can Modibbo Make a Difference?
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava As Giant of Export Produce, Can Modibbo Make a Difference?
Body_Text:
The President, Chief Olusegun Obasanjo in the recent cabinet reshuffle saddled a Journalist and a broadcaster with the responsibility of overseeing the Ministry of Commerce in the next ten months left in the life span of the present administration.
The Ministry has, as its major role the task of engaging in an aggressive marketing of Nigeria commodities to the outside world with the overall goal of wooing them to Nigeria. Dr. Aliyu Modibbo Umar of Kumo origin of Gombe State took over from his predecessor, Idris Waziri who manned the ministry from July 2003 --June 2006.
The 48-year-old Aliyu Modibbo Umar holds a Bachelor of Arts degree in Broadcast Journalism from California State University, Long Beach, USA, a Master of Arts degree in African Area Studies and Doctorate degree in Comparative Education respectively from University of California, Los Angeles, USA.
The vision of the ministry is to reposition commerce as the hub of the nation"s economy while the mission statement is to play a decisive role in the diversification of the resource base of the economy by promoting trade and investment with special emphasis on increased production and export of non-oil and gas products that will lead to wealth and job creation, poverty reduction and enhanced service delivery in a manner that will stimulate the growth of the domestic economy for self reliance and export and its integration into the global market, taking full advantage of globalization. Bearing in mind the vision and mission statements of which platform the ministry was established, the former Minister did not relent in his oars towards ensuring that the goals were achieved, no wonder he traveled the length and breadth of this country as well as a host of other countries especially China. China became a melting pot for the market of our commodities especially that of Cassava an d marketing the product was so dear to the former minister with the hope that the new Minister will take very seriously.
The president, while recognizing the role Waziri played in the Commerce ministry, had mandated the new minister who is on his second mission as a minister, first as a Minister of state, Ministry of Power and Steel to identify ten commodities that Nigeria would expedite its energy on in the next one year geared towards diversifying the economy.
During Waziri, the President in his bid to actualize his vision of unlocking the economic potentials of the cassava crop into a commercially viable commodity for both the domestic and export markets, Thursday 26, February, 2004 constituted a Committee on Cassava Export Promotion with Idris Waziri as the Minister presiding.
The objectives of the cassava initiative include to expand primary processing and utilization to absorb the national cassava production glut; to identify and develop new market opportunities for import substitution and export, to stimulate increased private sector investment in the establishment of export oriented cassava industries and to ensure the availability of clean {disease free} planting materials targeted at the emerging industries.
It also has as its objectives the need to increase the yield productivity and expand annual production to achieve global cassava competitiveness; to advocate for conducive policy and institutional reforms for the development of the Nigerian cassava sector and to integrate the rural poor especially women and youths into the mainstream of the National economy.
Cassava is produced mainly by farmers in the rural areas in smallholdings using their primitive tools, thus making it very difficult to make any appreciable profit from such venture, as it will not be possible to produce enough for value-added products.
According to the FAO report of 1991, between 1988 and 1990, the production of cassava had increased by 30% making Nigeria the world"s largest cassava producer, estimated at about 35mt per annum, and $2.5 bn due to the adoption of high yielding varieties by these small scale farmers. In this case, cassava has thus become the main source of food, cash and employment for the people, most especially those in the rural areas of the producing states, which constitute about 70% of the population.
What this means is that if Nigeria should continue in this pace and the necessary encouragement, both financial, material and other incentives are given to the farmers, it is most likely that by 2020, the figure would move to 60-65mt by 2020.
From untapped cassava, commodities like hard Pellet, Starch, Portable Ethanol, Fuel Ethanol and Convenience food can be derived and its export opportunities and job creation potential is put at $20.6 million while it is estimated that the untapped Nigerian domestic and export market opportunities for the product and its revenue generation potential stands at $4,510,700,000 per annum.
The government for this reason, dispatched a 3-Man Contact Committee to understudy the Ghanaian Presidential Initiative on Cassava with a view to drawing useful lessons from their experience as well as use the opportunity to discuss with who were then in Ghana on the possibility of importing Cassava from Nigeria. Subsequently, there was the confirmation by the Chinese Ambassador in Ghana that there is bright prospect of Chinese Companies importing up to 500,000 metric tones of cassava chips annually from Nigeria.
A Nigerian Agro-allied firm secured contract for the supply of 200,000 metric tons of cassava chips to the United States at $250 per ton and the Federal Government entered into agreements with China, South Africa and Botswana as targets for its Cassava export to boost non-oil earnings. The first shipment of Cassava chips left the country for China on Saturday, 30th April 2005.
The Nigerian Export --Import Bank {NEXIM} established a special Cassava Export Credit Facility to provide 35 million dollars, which would allow for direct disbursement of approved loans to qualified exporters of cassava products.
Nigeria, which derives only 10% of its foreign exchange earnings from the non-oil sector, produces 120,000 tons of Cassava annually, out of which 5,000 tons have been billed for export to China and the country hopes to realize N5 billion {about 38 million Us dollars} from Cassava export yearly.
One of the challenges that Modibbo who was a news reporter with NTA, Bauchi, later as a substitute teacher at Compton Unified School District, USA, a lecturer at the University of Abuja and doubling as Chairman, Board of Directors, Peugeot Automobile Nigeria Ltd, has to contend with is to ensure that the euphoria that accompanied the marketing of cassava does not die a natural one.
The new Minister has no choice than to work with his counterpart in the Agriculture Ministry to ensure that farmers are encouraged and empowered, if Nigeria must meet with Mr. President"s dream of moving Nigeria away from a mono economy nation and if Nigeria must realize the set 5 billion annually as well as the demand from China of 500, 000 metric tons.
As the Chairman, Presidential Committee on Cassava Export Promotion, Dr. Modibbo in achieving this, will ensure that real cassava producers have assess to the loan from NEXIM.
The initiative is a good one and its objectives must be achieved and as this may be your last ministry in this dispensation, the nation is looking up to you, the tempo of moving from the oil to the non-oil sector is very high, Nigerians want to know where we are on the cassava initiative and cassava initiative must outlive Ambassador Idris Waziri.

Original_Search_Title: Straight Comedies Take a Break
Headline: Kenya: Straight Comedies Take a Break
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A sabbatical leave from straight comedies and the triple themes of obsession, redemption and retribution marked the theatre scene this week.
That theatre goers pay to watch plays that are a break from the "per minute hilarity" type of farces, shows how mature theatre patronage has become. Not of course, forgetting, the attendant seriousness theatre groups have taken in staging quality productions, starting with the Festival of Creative Arts.
The group staged Suspects, a psychological thriller by Giles Cole at the Alliance Francaise Auditorium, Nairobi.
The thriller was about a seductive housewife, Ellen (Dama Irungu) who meets Allan (Derrick Amunga), an insurance salesman at an exclusive pub who ends up at Ellen's flat in Hurlingham.
A couple of drinks and cosy cuddling later, and Allan blacks out, just enough time for her hammer carrying husband (Victor Ber), to come out of the bedroom and "handle" him.
But the following day, it is Ellen's husband's body that is discovered outside her flat and the police inspector Tindall (Arabron Osanya) and sergeant Goss, (Ken Gichoya) begin their investigations.
Liz (Angel Waruinge), the calculating former wife of Ellen's husband enters the scene to muddle up matters. The plot deepens when police discover that Ellen's husband had taken a Sh20 million life insurance policy and all the suspects were somehow, inching for a share of the spoils.
It is ages since we watched a psychological crime thriller, the last perhaps being Anthony Shaffer's,The Sleuth in the '70s and '80s and Agatha Christie's The Mouse Trap, staged at the Nairobi Institute in 1994.
Suspects, a cerebral whodunit, was therefore a welcome break from our staple diet of bedroom slapsticks. Its strength lay in finding out how, and who killed Ellen's husband, a situation that was compounded when we learn that Allan, in his second appearance, didn't actually die.
Directed by Caroline Odongo and produced by Eliud Abuto, the thriller featured a seasoned cast that understood their roles pretty well.
Waruinge seems to have the roles of the bitchy mistress cast in stone for her. A part from playing auntie in Jimmi Makotsi's She Ate the Female Cassava at the Kenya National Theatre in April, all her other roles have been of the bewitching "other woman". And don't we love her for it. As Liz, she was the sarcastic know-it-all, helping to angle Irungu's character as the greedy, but naive wife.
Gichoya was the cowardly and indecisive cop who almost botched the investigations with his "sissyness". Osanya, a veteran thespian handled the demanding role of inspector well, mixing his assertiveness in solving the crime, with the twin motive of being smitten with Ellen.
The cleverly written, neatly tied up, suspense thriller was spruced up with subtle humour and comic facial expressions from Amunga. Good stuff from Festival and we can't wait to watch what they will present next.
Over at the Phoenix Players is Simon Gray's comedy, Just the Three of Us. It is the story of Enid (Nyambura Mwaniki) who invariably adopts a pet, tells her hubby, Ronnie (Jackson Mwacharo) to take a hike as she retreats in her converted lighthouse.
But this time, her adopted "pet" is a chained up Terri (Nice Githinji), Ronnie's personal assistant.
Millicent Ogutu directs the play which is more of a story than a comedy that pegs the audience's attention span, not on the sparse spasms of hilarity, but more on the strength of brilliant acting from Mwacharo, (not a cubicle of vivacity lost since A Doll's House), Mwaniki (bawdy sense of humour and comical mood swings anchors the play) and Githinji, who crosses over from KNT to the benchmark of how good an actress one is. And she doesn't disappoint as Enid's "domestic pet", I might say.
The play runs till July 29, next Saturday and will give way to Mike Leigh's Smelling a Rat.

Original_Search_Title: Pests Devastate Gasabo Plantations
Headline: Rwanda: Pests Devastate Gasabo Plantations
Body_Text:
Kigali — Pests have attacked cassava and banana plantations in Nyagasozi village, Gasabo District, leading to a reduction in food supply in the area.
"Most of the banana and cassava plantations have been affected by the pests, and we are afraid the area could be hit by severe famine if the pests are not controlled," Alexandria Hakizayezu, a resident of the district told The New Times.
He said the problem could have been caused by the dry season. To make matters worse, he added, the local people lack pesticides.
"Banana plantations in other areas were uprooted to stop the banana wilt from spreading to other regions," said Katema Maganya, a resident of Gasabo.
"The scarcity of food has hiked food prices in the markets," he added.
"I lost a big portion of my cassava plantation to pests. That is how I used to make a living. Now what shall I do?" Claudine Mutesi, a farmer and mother of four, asked.
Malira Kikurira, a resident of Nyagasozi Cell, shared Maganya's sentiments and noted that the area is prone to diseases that affect agricultural produce.
Recently there have been persistent complaints by farmers across the Eastern and Southern provinces about the destruction caused by pests.

Original_Search_Title: A Futile Professorial Whitewash
Headline: Nigeria: A Futile Professorial Whitewash
Body_Text:
One of President Olusegun Obasanjo's greatest woes is his singular misfortune in getting his activities seen in a positive light. Or, at least, getting the positive side of his programmes noticed, at least to validate the old saying: "the devil is not as black as he is painted". Perhaps, his personal attitude of not being bothered about what people say about him is generally responsible. When a leader has this type of disposition, his followers, hirelings and hangers-on (whom he picks without giving consideration to his public relations imperatives) imitate their principal in their conduct and the packaging of their communication for delivery to the public.
When a president hires a Femi Fani-Kayode, ignoring his immediate past personal history and giving him the type of work that Femi did for him, and at the end of which he is promoted to the post of a full cabinet minister, this says it all.
At the recent seminar organised by the National orientation Agency (NOA), the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) and the Bayelsa State Government in Yenagoa, this problem reared its ugly head once again when Professor Femi Otubanjo was called upon to deliver a paper entitled: Democracy and Development: A Conceptual Analysis. He started off brilliantly. Most of them are brilliant until they begin to smuggle in things that don't apply. Within the little time he was given to marshal the scholastic fineries of democracy, Otubanjo ably demonstrated that some of the idealistic terms often attached to the concept, such as equality, freedom, representation, choice, and other concomitants like mass communication and political parties, play roles in limiting democracy, even as they are also the attributes that make democracy the best form of government.
The crisis point came when Professor Otubanjo tried to whitewash the late Constitution Amendment Bill, otherwise known as "third term ambition" or tenure elongation project of President Obasanjo. He made two declarations that set the hall astir and caused the Master of Ceremony to term him: "the Professor from PDP". In one breath, the Professor said that the third term project "was not bad simply because it was unpopular. An idea is not bad simply because it is unpopular". In other words, despite its unpopularity, tenure elongation would have been beneficial to Nigeria, just as it was to some Asian countries. In another breath, he deposed that there is no link between democracy and development. If anything, the Asian countries, such as Singapore, South Korea and China have proved that dictatorship is actually a more effective means of speeding up development. We all know that putting it this way is not telling the whole truth about the third term project and the nexus between democracy and development.
An idea may not be "bad" because it is unpopular, but this depends on who is making this value judgement. Millions of Nigerians thought it was "bad" for them and many PDP pushers of it have taken their thrashing in good faith. Only a Femi Otubanjo is still fighting a lost cause, and doing so without ably convincing us about what was "good" about the democratically defeated item. It is even a great relief that PDP elements are now willing to admit that it was "unpopular". The "public hearings" that led to its being tabled as a legislative Bill suggested otherwise. If the Party had recognised the public hostility to it, it would not have become a Bill. Let me remind Otubanjo and his fellow revisionists that the Bill was unpopular and bad for three main reasons: (a) It was a very cheap, barefaced fraud being forcefully and corruptly pushed by a political party that wanted to impose Obasanjo on Nigerians after 2007; (b) it would foul the smooth sail of the power rotation principl e and exacerbate the problem of domination and alienation, and (c) Nigerians are perennially and permanently anti-tenure elongation. They blocked Generals Ibrahim Babangida, Sani Abacha and Olusegun Obasanjo, and they would block any other pretender in future. Nigeria won't be a banana or cassava republic.
The second aspect of Otubanjo's economic truth about democracy is the notion that there is no connection between it and development. He needs to be reminded that even China, Korea and Singapore abandoned dictatorship when it could not longer be sustained under economic boom. The political system presiding over a booming economy must democratise or it will implode. It is not even true that democracy cannot drive a system from underdevelopment to development. The histories of Europe, America and Canada prove our point. European countries such as France, Italy and Germany sacked their monarchies, while the UK, Spain, the Netherlands and Belgium reduced theirs to rarely seen or heard figureheads at the outset of the Industrial Revolution. Certainly, the US has never had an indigenous dictatorship. Good leadership raised America, Europe and Asia. Tenure elongation and dictatorship would not make a good leader out of Obasanjo if he was not one.
Even the most brilliant professor will run into problems when he allows his narrow political interests to becloud an open academic odyssey. Otubanjo was anxious to push a discredited party policy in a purely academic environment and got his fingers burnt. The risk in playing pranks with knowledge is that in most cases, many of the people you seek to teach are probably more learned and knowledgeable than you!

Original_Search_Title: For Many In Gulu, Peace Has Arrived
Headline: Uganda: For Many In Gulu, Peace Has Arrived
Body_Text:
Kampala — UPDF Spokesman for Northern Uganda, Lt Chris Magezi (holding bottle) on a broken bridge in a Gulu village
THE peace talks currently being held between the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) and the government of Uganda in Juba may still turn out to be inconclusive. Looking at the examples from similar conflicts such as the IRA campaign in Northern Ireland, without sounding pessimistic, the final resolution to the conflict may still be months, even years away.
But for many people deep in the countryside in Gulu District, as far as Leb Ngec in Amuru, west of Gulu, Alero, Koich Goma, and Wii Anaka, peace has already arrived. This past Sunday, for instance, in the remote farming outpost of Leb Ngec located about 12 kilometres from the nearest IDP camp at Amuru sub-county, and with a population of at least 120, residents were going about their business without fear. A group of three or four men were under the tree by the narrow pathway, reading the Bible and praying. Nearby, man was selling freshly butchered goat meat laid out on a roughly made table. Elsewhere in the growing camp, there were people getting ready for the small marketplace that will open later in the day where buyers and sellers from the Amuru IDP will congregate.
Still deeper in the bush where the narrow dirt-path is flanked by tall overgrown grass that would have been excellent hiding places for the rebels, there were individuals moving in either direction. Philip Okot whose bicycle was laden with foodstuff and other merchandise felt completely safe traversing the bushes. "There is nothing to fear here and I ride along the bush-path everyday, all alone", he said.
In fact, throughout the trip that started from Gulu town centre, past Lacor Hospital, through Olwal and Amuru IDP camps, and then the following day through Alero, Nwoya, Anaka, and Koich Goma, the sense of normalcy was palpable. Where only a year ago in March 2005, with the LRA rebels still wrecking occasional havoc in the countryside, I caught glimpses of fearful pedestrians walking along the road, now there was a deluge of villagers walking, riding bicycles, tilling the land or conducting small business selling food merchandise. Children waved happily as our white pick-up truck raised dust along the busy Amuru road.
The newly-found optimism is mainly due to the strategic deployments of the UPDF that has enabled the army to dominate major junctions and open countryside. For instance, the 79th Battalion has deployed near the Leb-Ngec settlement. Similar deployments were apparent in Alero Got-Ringo and corner Langol IDP camp. "The idea is fairly straightforward - to establish a grid work of security that makes it difficult for the rebels to move undetected, and so far it has proven to be very successful", explained UPDF Public Relations Officer for Northern Uganda, Lt. Chris Magezi.
To the many villagers who spoke on the issue, the ever-widening circle of LRA-free zone means that the success or failure of Juba peace talks is of little concern. According to Nelson Olanya, Chairman of Panokrach Farm Association at Leb Ngec, the biggest problem is the lack of proper accommodation for the farmers who sleep in roughly constructed huts that leave them exposed to mosquitoes and the elements, poor drinking water, unsanitary latrines, and dearth of farming implements. "We need all the hoes we can get our hands on so that we can expand our farm", he explained to the visitors. Jackson Orach, the camp leader at Labongogali IDP camp (pop. 6500) and Gaudensio Lakol Okwonga, a parish chief at Amuru Sub-County IDP camp (Estimated pop. 56,000), echoed similar sentiments.
