According to Orach, the main issues were drinking water, proper sanitation and food. "As you can see, we are more than ready to begin to plant crops, but we do not have the farm implements like hoes and machetes to clear the bushes - we already feel safe enough to go about freely, but we need these things now to get to work", he pleaded. At Amuru IDP camp where there appeared to be three children to every adult, the issues are overcrowding in the classrooms. "We have more children out of school than at school," explained Okwonga as he showed the visitors around the crowded camp.
Again and again, we asked villagers about their thoughts about the peace talks, and ever so skillfully, the conversation was steered away to the current concern in the camps. Peace, they appeared to be saying to anybody who cared to listen, was a given - the problem was not in Sudan where the government and the LRA are trying to resolve the 20 year-old conflict. Rather, the problem was in getting people the opportunity to take back their disrupted lives.
Already, throughout the IDP camps and along the road, there was ample evidence of locally grown foodstuff like cassava, millet, groundnuts, tomatoes, cabbages, maize, sorghum and sweet potatoes that the industrious people of Amuru had harvested from small farm plots that dot the roadside and the countryside.
And as time goes on without further disruptive clashes between the LRA and the UPDF, there can only be more clamour to return to former homesteads abandoned at the height of the conflict in the late 1990s. To them, the person who can bring them hoes, machetes and seeds is the person who speaks the language of peace. Everyone else is wasting time.

Original_Search_Title: Food Security Improves
Headline: Mozambique: Food Security Improves
Body_Text:
Maputo — The food security and nutritional situation in Mozambique has improved considerably after last year's drought in parts of the central and southern provinces, concludes the government's Technical Secretariat for Food and Security and Nutrition (SETSAN) in a report presented during a meeting in Maputo on Wednesday.
Addressing the opening session of the meeting, Deputy Agriculture Minister Catarina Pajume, who is also the chairperson of SETSAN, attributed this improvement to "an increase in the production of food crops following two consecutive years of severely dry conditions".
She said that programmes to promote livestock breeding and the good rains during the last growing season are some of the contributory factors that have allowed an increase in the availability of food.
The country now has food reserves of about 99,000 tonnes of maize, 439,000 tonnes of cassava and 43,000 tonnes of groundnuts, Pajume said - but she noted that there could still be some pockets of food and nutritional insecurity in the coming months.
Speaking of malnutrition among children aged under five, Pajume said that "the country needs new strategies and policies, particularly at district and local levels".
She also noted that one of the factors that may help improve food security still further is good education of the people "to change their mentality about feeding habits, because there are still many parents who do not know how to feed their children properly, even though they have the necessary foodstuffs".
"It is not easy to change people's habits, but one can introduce changes for improvement", she said, adding that "there are some people who will not sell their animals in order to supplement their diet. They would rather see the animals die than sell them for a better diet'.
Pajume also attacked those peasant farmers who sell all their surplus crops, rather than keeping a household reserve.
That mean that these households run out of food before the next harvest and "depend on the government's help in times of need".
For his part, Francisco Cabral, a technician at the Planning and Development Ministry, said that his sector has surveyed the availability of food, access to food, and how food is used among the population in the country's 11 provinces, as well as the eventual impact in the event of an outbreak of bird flu.
He explained that this last factor was studied because it was found that many households, particularly in rural areas, have poultry raising as one of the bases of their food security, and thus the possibility of bird flu striking Mozambique cannot be neglected.
The SETSAN survey found that this year's grain harvest was over 10 per cent larger than the 2005 harvest - even though the area cultivated only rose by 2.16 per cent (from 2.256 to 2.306 million hectares). The preliminary figure for this year's main harvest is 2.097 million tonnes of grain - which compares with 1.899 million tonnes in 2005, an increase of 10.4 per cent.
As for pulses the increase is 9.6 per cent (from 332,628 to 364,680 tonnes), and for cassava it is 13.8 per cent, with production rising from 6.634 to 7.552 million tonnes.
The SETSAN document noted that in much of the country this year's harvest is the best for five years. However, in arid and semi-arid areas in the south and centre of the country reserves are only sufficient for three months after the main harvest (which means they are running out about now).
But the second agricultural season is still underway, and a good supplementary harvest will improve the availability of food in the semi-arid areas. (It should be noted that in these areas in the south of the country, agriculture is often not the main source of income, but supplements wage labour, or migrants' remittances.) The SETSAN survey found that markets "are reasonably well supplied". Prices of the main crops hit a high point in February, but, as the harvest was brought in, they subsequently declined.
But the prices of manufactured goods increased somewhat, particularly in markets in more remote areas - which SETSAN blamed on higher transport costs, resulting from higher fuel prices.
This year's rains have made life easier for animals as well as humans. SETSAN notes an improvement in pastures, and an increase in the availability of drinking water for livestock.
"The physical condition of the animals reflects these improvements", the report states.
The meeting is to produce a draft revision of the food security strategy, taking into account government decentralisation policy that seeks to make the districts the basis of development. This proposal is to be submitted to the Cabinet for approval before the end of this year.

Original_Search_Title: Gender, Devt On Trade & Agriculture in Nigeria
Headline: Nigeria: Gender, Devt On Trade & Agriculture in Nigeria
Body_Text:
Abuja — Trade can no longer be viewed as a technical and gender-neutral process for achieving growth. There has been a lingering argument on the impact of trade liberalisation and gender dimension. There is also another question that borders on the links between gender equity and trade.
Trade policy, as pursued at multilateral (WTO), regional and bilateral levels are opening up previously protected markets and reducing tariffs on imports, to give foreign companies the same conditions of market access as domestic producers.
Most policy-makers and trade specialists assume that gender issues are irrelevant to the formulation of trade policies. But evidence demonstrates that trade liberalisation has different impacts on women's and men's employment and conditions of work, and also consequences for women's unpaid labour.
Gender questions also arise in terms of how the gains from tariff revenues, or losses of these, are distributed. Further liberalisation may compromise governments' ability to pursue poverty eradication and equity objectives. Bridge (1989) opined that trade expansion has been associated with a rise in women's labour force participation in export processing industries in Asia, Latin America and the Caribbean and in export agriculture in Africa.
Similarly, traded services such as data entry, financial services and tourism are often feminised sectors. Some researchers point to women, particularly younger, educated women benefiting more than men from employment gains in the initial stages of trade expansion. This may have wider benefits for women's sense of autonomy, status in the household and incentives to invest in female education.
However, the third World debt crises of the 1970s and 1980s and a series of structural adjustment programmes fostered several rounds of impact assessment, which led to the identification of women as one of the groups vulnerable to welfare losses during the disruptive phases of such programmes.
As in many other economic activities, there are gender dimensions and variations in contributions to, burdens of and rewards from trade. Acknowledgment of development as the overarching goal of public policies and governance therefore demands specific inputs and policies to address concerns that relate to the different gender segments of the society to ensure sustainable development.
Trade liberalisation has been indicted by several studies of having the capacity to turn women and youths into traded commodities themselves. This is a situation which can exacerbate the number of women perhaps in Nigeria, nay, Africa migrating to Europe and other parts of the world for prostitution to make ends meet.
Mainstreaming of gender in trade is therefore very crucial to poverty reduction and sustainable development, to wit; introducing a consideration on the disaggregated potential impact of trade liberalisation on male and female, with a view to applying factors that would cushion the effects upon them.
Development must be purposefully planned to incorporate the critical elements of economic, social, and human development. Consequently, the Human Development Index, HDI (a composite index that measures critical elements of development by the extent that it impacts on well being of the individual) has become the critical measure of welfare as opposed to GDP per capita.
Gender-related Development Index (GDI) and Gender Empowerment Measure (GEM) produced by the UNDP since 1995 are composite measures reflecting gender inequalities in human development. They have shown that the closer a country's GDI is to its HDI; the less gender disparity there is in the country; and that where the GDI rank is higher than the HDI, there is a more equitable inter-gender distribution of human development.
Trade and gender have significant complementarities. It has been acknowledged that no productive activities can occur without active involvement of the reproductive sector in producing food for domestic consumption, in producing and nurturing labour, and in caring for the environment.
Therefore, effectiveness of trade policy or Agreements in translating growth to developmental objectives is predicated on the extent to which it promotes human development, provides better access to essential services that are necessary to social reproduction (water, electricity, sanitation), and whether the policy positively affects women's access to education and health-care.
Gender concerns in trade policies and trade agreements arise out of observations around gender division of labour, gender-differentiated access to economic and social resources, disparities in income, gender biases in regard to responsibilities and traditional roles in economic activities.
These form the main basis for the roles and locations of women and men on the trade continuum. In addition, there is the unique role women play in the society, encapsulated in their socially prescribed responsibility for child-care, food production and preparation and community responsibility more so in the African cultural setting.
Considerations like these inform the differences in gender perspectives, on development and trade, as they are differently impacted by policies related to economic and social development. In the same vein, gender inequality is at the core of poverty. It has been said that looking at trade liberalisation from a gender perspective is a key to managing the poverty reduction process.
In Nigeria, over 65% of the poor are women and children. Most of these women form the informal trade sector even as some of them are widows and bread winners of their families. Besides, more women suffer from food insecurity and have more social burden arising from deteriorating social infrastructure.
Commitment to the Millennium Declaration (MDGs) with its bias towards an open, equitable, rule-based, predictable and non-discriminatory trading system presupposes a commitment to adopt trade liberalisation that recognises women's efforts as an element for social empowerment.
Unfortunately, this has not been so. Since the inception of the EPA negotiations for instance, the challenge has been that there has been few interventions on how to harness the forces of trade and the resultant agreement so that the poor and especially women, both effectively participate in and benefit from the growth that is attendant to trade (if any).
The negotiations of the EPA between West Africa and the European Union has neither considered gender implications nor created safety nets or strategies that would protect women and youths in the eventual of the effects of liberalisation.
One sector in trade that requires a total appraisal while discussing gender in trade is agriculture. According to experts, gender as a socio-cultural definition of roles, responsibilities, privileges and obligations, to a large extent constitutes a potentially significant determinant of access to agricultural technologies, productive resources and by extension, socio-economic participation by individuals.
The embedded nature of gender and agricultural technological change has crucial implications for poverty alleviation, considering that technological change constitutes a sure route to increased agricultural productivity and income. Underlying agricultural production and processing and access to and use of requisite available technologies is the socio-cultural practice of gender stereotyping embodied in local traditions and customs.
Gender stereotyping is rooted in the traditional culture and shaped by prevailing rural socio-economic conditions. In turn, it determines the prospects for equitable, efficient and sustainable agricultural production and processing as exemplified by an illustration of cassava and oil palm economy of southeastern Nigeria. Over time, changes in rural social, economic, technological and institutional conditions occur, with potentially critical impact on traditional gender roles, opportunities and constraints in agriculture production and processing.
For production, gender disparities are mainly in respect of provision of labour and access to productive assets. Both the type and nature of labour requirement for the different aspects of agricultural work could be gender-discriminating. As such, an attempt to explore gender structure in agriculture has to incorporate such socio-cultural impacts of labour disaggregation as well as unequal access to productive capital and other assets as well as their implications for productivity, efficiency and improved income for agents.
Such disparities exacerbate the link with poverty. With weak earning capacity and little or no access to land and other productive assets, women and children constitute the bulk of the marginalised. Forced labour reduces the capacity for initiatives from this group of people also. The labour intensive structure of most productive activities in the agricultural sector in Nigeria, as well as the low yield implies that the returns to the set of people that provide the most input are also the lowest.
Essentially, gender considerations in Nigeria must principally check up labour utilisation which is driven by availability, quality, age of the work-force, methods of production and types of implements used. Interestingly, while population and work force are increasing at an increasing rate, agricultural labour is decreasing at an increasing rate.
Available statistics show that Agriculture share of total demand for university education (total university student population) average less than 5% in the last five years. This implies that there is paucity of high quality human resources for a sector that is so critical to the economy. Besides, owing to the reward system in the entire economy, even the few that graduate from agricultural related programmes end up in other sectors, and entry rate from other professions into agriculture is very minimal.
Among above concerns is that majority of these students are females and in terms of employment, women and children form the bulk of labour providers for most products. Such services rendered include planting, weeding, harvesting, milking, etc. depending on the product involved and the traditional practices obtainable in the part of the country concerned.
In many instances, there is an additional discrepancy in the reward for labour for women and children. For instance, there are occasions where women are paid less than the N600 paid to men per day. Often, their wages are far less than that given to men. Child labour is often not looked upon in the rural areas with abhorrence.
Indeed, there is an age-long practice of having as many children as possible on the understanding that they would provide cheap labour in the farms. Thus, child labour is often not rewarded at all. In cases where they are, as in the instance of a widow taking her child to provide hired labour, such reward goes to the parent.
The land tenure system in most parts of the country do not allow for women having title to landed property. This means that farmlands cannot be originally owned by the women. Even for widow of a deceased landlord, it is often the case that the brethren of the late husband automatically get title to the lands.
They can lease or rent the land thereafter to the woman to farm, but such contract is often not legally enforceable. The role of women in crop production can be expressed when gender issues are more noticeable in terms weeding activities while men are in the areas of bush clearing. Generally men are the owners of farms while women are the workers.
In the light of Nigeria's trade policy vis the ongoing negotiations at bilateral, regional and multilateral, it is important to assess the extent these trade instruments are biased in favour of any gender. Which gender is likely to benefit or lose more from CET and EPA for instance? What is the proportion of women involved in the different aspects of farming and to what extent do they consider the policies of government?.
This is because women are more vulnerable to poverty incidence induced by policy change. On the average, women are more prone to be adversely affected in the events of signing the trade agreements. Because women are the workers, any reduction in the domestic price (loss in income) will reduce their income and create gender dimensional unemployment.
To cushion the effect of loss in income, men could engage in some anti-societal norms to collect the meager income due for women especially where cultural values overrides economic rationality of women and children.
As Nigeria advances with her regional sisters in some of the ongoing trade negotiations, one paramount question should be, do the CET and EPA have any potential for helping the womenfolk in any specific way? Are there any safety nets or fall back mechanisms negotiated and put in place for the relaxation of trade induced gender problems especially against the backdrop that even children are a direct function of women and any potential impact of CET and EPA on them is of direct proportion to the impact on women.
Government needs to reduce gender bias and promote women empowerment. Women should be part of decision making process to cushion the impact of the pact on them. In an event of economic recessions men are likely to abandon the children on their mother.
African culture specifically, formally or informally made the responsibility of children to be on the women especially when the economic environment are not favorable. The present policy by the Nigerian government on financial assistance through special micro-credit dedicated to women is commendable and need to be extended to the fertiliser subsidy.
Ukaoha, President, National Association of Nigerian Traders (NANTS), wrote in from Abuja

Original_Search_Title: Profits to Be Had in Becoming a Banana Republic
Headline: Central Africa: Profits to Be Had in Becoming a Banana Republic
Body_Text:
Libreville — It's a tale of two countries, and competing groups of female sellers. The account of the banana trade between Cameroon and Gabon is also an illustration of how difficult regional economic integration can be.
Female traders from the two Central African countries are in dispute over the wholesale trade of bananas in Gabon. At present, sellers from neighbouring Cameroon -- where much of the fruit comes from -- dominate the wholesale banana trade in Gabon, leaving the less profitable retail aspect of the sector to locals.
"The Cameroonian traders are in a monopoly situation in response to a vacuum created by the passivity of the Gabonese, who abandoned (their) agricultural sector," says Simonne Mpenka, a tomato seller from Cameroon.
Banana farming is much more developed in Cameroon than in Gabon, with plantations that produce fruit for export covering some 3,600 hectares in south-western Cameroon. While there are banana plantations in Gabon, notably in the south, they are less extensive -- and produce more for local consumption.
The imbalance in commercial relations between Gabon and Cameroon is also reflected in Gabon's extensive dependence on its northern neighbour for cassava, yams, rice, maize, vegetables, fruit, meat and poultry.
However, Gabonese women now have the wholesale banana trade in their sights. "Once restricted to the retail sales of bananas, the Gabonese traders now wish to buy from the source (Cameroon) to increase their profit margin," Bertrand Olibou, a price inspector at the Ministry of Trade, told IPS.
With this in mind, the traders have requested government to provide them with trucks so that they can also stock up on bananas in Cameroon and in the interior of Gabon, to compete with the better-organised Cameroonian sellers and transporters.
"We are asking the Gabonese state to provide us with trucks so that we too can go to the interior of the country to buy our agricultural products instead of waiting to buy these third hand," said Marie-Joséphine Dabany, president of the Co-operative of Traders of Gabonese Markets, during a meeting held Jun. 2.
"In Cameroon, there are no sardines. In Gabon there are, and the Cameroonians come to buy many from us to resell in Cameroon. Today, the Gabonese traders want equally to buy bananas in Cameroon," she told IPS. The co-operative also aims to have Gabonese invest more in the agricultural sector.
Authorities have not yet responded to the request for transport. "But the hope of the government to see Gabonese sellers play an active part in the wholesale banana trade indicates that it would give them the means (for the request)," Benoît Mezui, advisor to the Ministry of Planning, told IPS.
Last month's meeting, which brought together Cameroonian and Gabonese traders at the Gabonese Ministry of Foreign Affairs in the capital of Libreville, recommended that the monopoly in bulk sales of bananas be reserved for Gabonese women.
Pauline Ndombet, for one, believes that Cameroonian sellers would not welcome a change to the status quo.
"The Cameroonian sellers of bananas, found in most of the markets in Libreville and in the interior of the country, (are) generally wholesalers -- (and) do not want the Gabonese traders to come to Cameroon to buy bananas," the retail seller of bananas at Oloumi market in Libreville told IPS.
Adds Pascal Mandji of the Gabonese Consumers' Association, "The Cameroonian traders, who have occupied this niche (the wholesale banana trade) for decades, fear a drop in their turnover should the Gabonese enter bulk sales."
But Claudine Mba, a Cameroonian wholesaler, is less concerned, saying Cameroonian traders have established a firm footing in the market. The arrival of Gabonese in the bulk sales sector "will not disturb our trade," she noted.
Bananas play an important part in the Gabonese diet, and can affect a household budget accordingly.
The General Office of Statistics and Economic Studies, a government body, says that the consumer price index of households registered an increase of 1.5 percent between April and May this year, mainly because of rises in food costs. The price of plantain bananas increased by 2.7 percent and now hovers between 83 cents to 1.3 dollars per kilogramme on markets in Libreville, according to the office.
The index measures the average change in prices, over time, for a selection of goods typically bought by consumers. Plantain bananas are a starchy, hard variety of the fruit that serve as a staple food in tropical regions.
The chargé d'affaires of the Cameroonian embassy in Libreville, Jean Luc Ngouambe Wouaga, told IPS that officials were concerned by the challenges facing the banana trade.
"This matter has been dealt with in a common accord with Gabonese authorities in the Ministry of Trade, who went right to the border area of Ambam, on the Cameroonian side, where they met their Cameroonian counterparts."
Calm prevailed in the course of these meetings, said Wouaga.

Original_Search_Title: Can Nigeria Develop Ethanol As Alternative Fuel?
Headline: Nigeria: Can Nigeria Develop Ethanol As Alternative Fuel?
Body_Text:
In a bid to link the oil sector with the nation's economy and in particular the agricultural sector, a fuel ethanol programme has been put in place by the NNPC. The programme which is predicated on the development of large scale cassava and sugar cane plantations is already receiving focused attention, since a renewable energy division has been created by the NNPC to midwife the programme. According to the NNPC group managing director, Funsho Kupolokun, the objective of the programme is to explore sources of bio-mass that can be used to produce fuel ethanol.
Ethanol has been known to man as the intoxicating ingredient in alcoholic beverages albeit presently it is produced as a petrochemical through the hydration of ethylene. On a global scale, it is generally believed that the world produced, as late as 2004, enough ethanol to the tune of two percent of global gasoline consumption. While in the U.S, one of the world's largest producers of ethanol, the main feeds-tock for t he production of ethanol is corn, from which it recorded a 5 billion litre capacity with plans to upgrade it to 7.5 billion litres by 2012. The Chinese produced about one billion litres of ethanol from their wheat and corn rich provinces, France, leading other European nations produced over 200 million gallons of the product from sugar beets and wheat. The most preferred stock in warmer climates, however, is sugarcane and this is where Brazil leads the world with a production level of 14 billion litres, enough to replace about 40 per cent of its gasoline demand. As a world leader in fuel ethanol production, most new cars in the country are "flexible-fuel vehicles" that can run on either ethanol, gasoline or any blend of the two.
Brazilian drivers have at least 29,000 filling stations around the country where they can fill up with ethanol. In the U.S., ethanol is commonly blended with gasoline to produce a hybrid nicknamed "gasohol", available from 600 gas stations nationwide .
Keep up with the latest headlines on WhatsApp | LinkedIn
"Fuel ethanol is an anti knock additive in gasoline and it is emerging as an important motor fuel in view of the Kyoto protocol agreement to which Nigeria is a signatory," Kupolokun explained. Because ethanol is produced theologically by fermenting sugars with yeast, other sources include switchgrass, and cassava. Switchgrass, a hardy, tall and perennial grass used by farmers for erosion control leads the list of "energy crops" from which ethanol can be derived as it "requires minimal irrig-ation, fertiliser or herbicides but yields (between) 2-3 times more ethanol per acre than corn (or other starch crops) does", according to the Earth Policy Institute. The Nigerian programme favours cassava and sugarcane. Apart from the fact that as a tropical nation sugarcane would be most preferred, the Institute points at sugarcane as the being by far the most efficient of the present ethanol producing feedstocks - "yielding eight times as much energy as is needed to produce the ethan ol".
It added: "Given their positive energy balances and higher yields, it, therefore, makes more sense to produce ethanol from sugar crops than from grains". The NNPC has so far worked out plans to acquire farmlands in Anambra, Benue and Cross River for large scale cultivation of cassava. It has acquired a large plantation site of over 20,000 hectares at Agufa village for the large scale production of cassava and sugar cane in Jigawa State and already an MoU in that regard has been signed between the two parties. The MoU is expected to be a win-win situation for both parties as the NNPC will reap between 75 million and 100 million litres of ethanol and up to 80,000 metric ton-nes of sugarcane annually, while state will in turn generate employment and invariably wealth for its citizens. In addition, the venture will also enable the Jigawa government to acquire world-class technology and farm management best practices in the large scale production of sugarcane.
According to t he Group General Manager (GGM) in charge of the NNPC's Renewable Energy Unit, Anochie Anyaoku, a small plant will be established at the farm site in a joint venture form for processing the sugarcane, because construction of a refinery would take between two to three years and an investment of up to two billion dollars. "This (ethanol) initiative is private-sector driven and already local and foreign investors, as well as multin-ationals are currently being engaged", Anyaoku said in an apparent answer to questions of the possible sources of investment for the project. The project will target blending 10 percent ethanol with petrol with plans to increasing the ratio to 25 per cent by 2008, he said, stressing that besides producing ethanol fuel, the project also has the potential of generating up to 15 MW of electricity from a "biogas-fed cogeneration plant". "The NNPC, PetroBras and COIMEX, all of Brazil have already signed an agreement for the take-off of the Nigeria ethanol fuel programme," Kupolokun said. He said already a programme for ethanol importation, blending, distribution and monitoring has been worked out before the NNPC large scale production of sugar cane and cassava across the country reach maturity stage. The GMD said reception facilities have been put in place for the importation of ethanol at Atlas Cove and Mosimi depots, while the NNPC is currently revamping its facilities in the refineries and Mosimi depot in preparation for ethanol fuel production.
Kupolokun expressed optimism that the fuel ethanol industry programme would be firmly on ground before the end of this year. "I hope consumers will start buying ethanol fuel before the end of the year given the work programme on ground," he said. Construction of retail stations to solely dipense ethanol fuel to motorists may, however, be some distance away, as the blending ratio of ethanol to petrol increases in the years ahead, constructing ethanol dispensing pumps may have to be a necessity. It is no accident that Brazil, the world leader in ethanol production, who programme in 1975 as part of efforts to free itself from the chokehold on the economy by soaring oil prices should be the natural partner for the NNPC's programme, especially given at fact that its ethanol production is exclusively from sugarcane. Kupolokun noted that Brazil adopted ethanol fuel initiative 30 years ago and Nigeria was willing to share its experiences in order to achieve the desired objectives.
He views the agree-ment as a clear demonstration of South/South cooperation between Nigeria and Brazil. It is also expected that the programme will also result in new technological cooperation in agriculture and other emerging technologies in biomass fuel between Nigeria and Brazil, he added. The Brazilians have been promoting biofuel production and offering their expertise to other developing countries to develop their own ethanol industries with a view to making ethanol a global c ommodity. Participants at the 2006 National 'Oil and Gas Conference' held in April, heard that Nigeria's ethanol initiative is built on four components - specific cultivation project, formation of partnerships, policy creation and the seeding of initiatives across stakeholders. The spin-offs from these are expected in job creation, rural wealth creation and energy self-sufficiency. Specifically, the programme is expected to, among other things; address the inadequate refining capacity of the nation maximise carbon credit opportunities; reduce domestic use of petrol and by extension free up more crude oil for export and Position Nigeria for the development of 'green fuels' In the long term, the programme would save the country between 100 and 130 million dollars annually from importation of fuel, Kupolokun said. According to him, the initiative is part of the economic reform agenda of government which is aimed at rejuvenating and repositioning the economy by generating employ ment, promoting capacity building and a cleaner environment.
Although NNPC officials have chosen to keep mute on the level of investment required to drive this initiative to fruition, they are keen to enthuse about the long term goal of Nigeria joining the league of ethanol exporting countries. (N A N)

Original_Search_Title: Elephants Destroy Plantations in Three Bengo Districts
Headline: Angola: Elephants Destroy Plantations in Three Bengo Districts
Body_Text:
Caxito — Elephants have been destroying cassava, banana, potatoes, maize, beans and peanuts plantations, in the districts of Pango-Aluquem, Dembos and Bula-Atumba, in Angola's northern Bengo province, Angop learned.
This was announced Monday here by chieftains, at the end of a five-day visit of provincial governor, Jorge Dombolo.
Since last June, elephants have destroyed about 200 fields belonging to small farmers, which may lead to a food crisis, warned the peasants.
In his turn, the governor called on the peasants not to kill the animals.

Original_Search_Title: No 'Positive Change' in Ghana's Agriculture?
Headline: Ghana: No 'Positive Change' in Ghana's Agriculture?
Body_Text:
Accra — The umbilical cord of the Ghanaian agricultural sector is still firmly anchored to nature's benevolence and its success or failure, and whether or not Ghana can become self-sufficient in food production still rests on nature's whims.
This was the thrust of what Mr. Ernest Akubuar Debrah, Ghana's Food and Agricultural Minister told Ghanaians last Tuesday when he re-started the government's "Meet-The-Press Series," which suffered a jolt after President Agyekum Kufour's ministerial reshuffle mid March this year.
The reshuffle, which The Chronicle code-named "a clear-out," saw baby-faced Dan Botwe, who hitherto chaired the series, losing his job as information minister.
With Mr. Kwamena Bartels, the Information and national Orientation Minister, dictating proceedings as Chair, the agriculture minister announced that for the year 2005, the country was "self-sufficient in most of her basic food items, including cassava, yam and plantain."
He was however quick to add that self-sufficiency in maize production will be "greatly influenced by rainfall reliability."
Though the minister shied away from confessing it is a known fact that the yield of Ghana's food crops; sorghum, millet, cassava, yam, plantain, groundnut, beans and a host of others, which have been the main source of nutrition to Ghanaians is dependent on rainfall.
From Mr. Debrah's own estimates, the country was only 92% self-sufficient in maize output in 2005. Rice output recorded only 45% self-sufficiency level in the same period.
He gave the level of self-sufficiency in cassava, yam, plantain, and cocoyam respectively as 214%, 350%, 115% and 117%, apparently indicating that the output of these crops in 2005 far exceeded what Ghanaians required by over a hundred percentage point each. What he did not say was how the excess output was handled.
In general, the minister admitted that the contribution of agriculture to the country's gross Domestic Product (GDP) has been falling since 2001 when his 'positive change' government assumed office. Statistically, the agric sector's contribution to GDP fell from about 40% in 2001 to 37% in 2005.
The sector's share of labour employment has also as a consequence, been falling, dropping from 60% in 2001 to 57% in 2005.
Though this is glaringly, evidence that the Ghanaian economy is still difficulty freeing itself from the woods of vulnerability; Mr. Debrah sought to dismiss that reality by stating rather that the trend fits well in theoretical economic logic, which suggests that a growing economy would witness a movement of labour at from the agricultural sector to industry.
What is missing in that logic, particularly with regards to the Ghanaian situation, is that the industrial sector is also in steep decline and not capable of absorbing exiting labour from agriculture. The 2006 Budget statement provides the evidence. From table 4, page 25, the budget showed that industry's percentage contribution to overall growth has been declining, falling from about 26% in 2002 to 24% in 2003 and then to 22.1% in 2004.
Mr. Debrah could not therefore be more wrong in his interpretation of the situation, especially coming from a government whose targeted GDP growth of 6% is expected to come largely from agriculture.
The Institute of Statistical, Social and Economic Research (ISSER) of the University of Ghana has even stated, in its mid-year review of the performance of the Ghanaian economy, that "early trends do not look very promising" to meeting the 6% growth target.
And this is because, the agricultural sector, which has been the mainstay of the economy, will not be witnessing the level of growth that government expects.
It is worth noting that the past years' growth rates in the sector were the result of "the strong performance of the cocoa sub-sector" which Mr. Debrah alludes to in his presentation.
With the cocoa sector not likely to record that same level of growth, not much can be expected from agriculture. It is also worth noting that between year 2004 and 2005, the sector lost a one percentage growth, recording 6.5% in 2005 as against 7.5% in 2004.
The declining contribution of agriculture to GDP and the fall in its labour share conveniently has nothing to do with the assumption by Mr. Debrah of an "improving economy."
Rather, it has to do with the fact that the country's agriculture is still primitive. A facts sheet released in September 2004 by the Statistics, Research and Information Directorate (SRID) of the ministry indicated that the country's agriculture was still predominantly a small-holder based and that about 90 percent of farm holdings are less than 2 hectares.
The document also showed that the main system of farming was still traditional, with the hoe and cutlass dominating as the main farming tools. Apart from the northern sector where bullock ploughing is practiced, mechanized farming is extremely limited.
Certainly, this cannot be attractive to the Ghanaian youth, especially so when profit margins in the sector are increasingly declining as a result of global economic happenings.
Contrary to Mr. Debrah's assertion, the movement of labour out of agriculture and the sector's dwindling contribution to overall GDP could best be explained by the removal of government's support and subsidies to farmers, coupled with the influx of cheap, but heavily subsidized agricultural imports from the developed world.
This mix of 'mispolicies,' has led to the collapse of the food crop sub-sector, particularly in the three northern regions of Ghana, impoverishing thousands of farmers and their dependents.
Thus, Mr. Debrah's commitment to "pursuing a policy of direct intervention to make available improved seeds and planting materials and relevant agro chemicals to small farmers on credit," is the appropriate response to the declining trend in agriculture's output. It is a better option than to assuming, albeit erroneously, that the current trend suggests an improving economy.
A worry here however is that the minister talks of this laudable initiative as being "an experiment only." Why only an experiment when indeed, industrialized nations, such as the United States of America and members states of the European Union, are currently heavily subsidizing their farmers, even in flagrant violation of the World Trade Organization's rules.
According to the Christian Aid, the EU gives its agribusinesses around $100 billion a year in subsidies and grants. A cow gets $3 a day in subsides from the EU, while 50% of Africa's people live less than $1 a day.
The UK based charity group also says that in 2003 alone, governments in the Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) subsidised farm exports to the tune of $350 billion, compared to providing just US$22 billion in aid to Africa. That's almost $1 billion a day!
The US government, for instance, in 2002 alone provided $3.7 billion in subsidies to its cotton agribusiness, three times the entire US aid budget for Africa at the time.
These should be useful statistics to Mr. Debrah, that direct intervention in the agricultural sector that will ensure food security and livelihoods to poverty stricken communities, is not an evil exercise, neither would it be strange.

Original_Search_Title: Interview With Mai Moussa Abari, Head of FAO
Headline: Central African Republic: Interview With Mai Moussa Abari, Head of FAO
Body_Text:
Bangui — The insecurity experienced in parts of the Central African Republic (CAR) for more than a year has become a national and international concern. While the CAR government is trying to find a lasting solution to restore peace and order in the troubled northwest region, the international community is also taking action to assist people in distress. Although donors' responses to appeals by United Nations agencies and non-governmental organisations in the country have been slow, some funds have reached aid agencies in CAR, one of which is the UN Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO). On 12 July, IRIN spoke to Mai Moussa Abari, the head of FAO, about the agency's activities in the country. Here are excerpts of the interview:
Has the FAO received funding from the international community?
We made two requests for funding from the [UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs'] Central Emergency Research Fund [CERF]. We have received a response concerning the first project we set up and it was announced that US $193,200 had been allocated and this amount is on its way. The money will be used to buy and distribute cassava cuttings and agricultural tools to people in the north and northwest; mainly in Paoua, Markounda and Kabo, the towns most affected by the armed conflict. The people there are in a precarious situation.
We deemed it very urgent to assist them by providing cassava cuttings to help improve their food situation as they are obliged to eat wild roots; we thought it was better to re-start agriculture in this region. This project, funded by the CERF, will help people in the troubled zones start new agricultural production to restore food security in their area. We hope it will relieve vulnerable populations in the region.
Why have you confined the project to cassava production alone?
We had planned [to distribute] seeds such as maize, millet and groundnuts. We sent a request to CERF at the beginning of May but, for various reasons, only now have we received an official response. Because of [the country's] agricultural planting season, the choice of cassava production now is safer because we can grow cassava during the rainy season despite the delay. It is this technical aspect that motivated our choice as cassava is the basic foodstuff.
I think our choice meets the needs and requirements of the population. As an agriculturally specialised agency, the FAO is obliged to operate according to the agricultural calendar. The slight discrepancy we have registered led us to prioritise the production of cassava. Growing cassava will alleviate the population's suffering due to lack of food.
Is it safe to re-start agriculture in the troubled regions while insecurity persists?
Of course, there is residual insecurity in the north and the northwest zones but people are still living there. We understand that the insecurity in the area is not absolute.
[Moreover], we cannot use the prevailing insecurity in the region as a pretext to avoid carrying out activities there. As long as people face food insecurity it is another insecurity pattern that soon becomes part of their daily lives. They will be tempted to flee the area and this situation will deeply destabilise the zone. Our conviction is to help people still living in the affected regions by [providing] these cassava cuttings so they can stay and end the food insecurity.
Where are you getting the cassava cuttings from?
These cassava cuttings have been introduced to the country for many years now by the International Institute for Tropical Agronomy - a research centre specialising in improving cassava production in Nigeria. Now, this type of cassava grown in the CAR by the PAPAAV project and the Ouakombo Agronomic Centre can be found in large quantities.
Is FAO planning to further develop agriculture in the country?
Under the auspices of the Consolidated Appeal Process in 2006, another FAO project is now under way. We are mobilising seeds such as maize, rice, millet and cassava cuttings to be dispatched to six prefectures to relieve people affected by the unrest. This project is estimated at $390,000. We have additional requests from the CERF [that] I cannot talk about now because we are waiting for official communication. Our ambition as FAO is to restart agriculture in this country. With the stabilisation of the situation and the mobilisation of our partners, we certainly hope to achieve this objective.
Is it true that the CAR is eligible for another $2.5 million in aid from the international community, and will FAO be a beneficiary?
CERF has two components: the under-funded projects and the rapid response. In the under-funded projects' component, we have received $193,200 even though the money is not yet available on the ground. We understand that in the rapid response component, $2.5 million has been allocated to the CAR; but we are still awaiting official confirmation to see if the FAO is eligible for this second component. The FAO had filed requests for an estimated $450,000 but for the moment, we have received $193,200.
Could you comment on the delays in the funding of UN agencies' projects in CAR by the international community, and how do these delays affect FAO?
With the establishment of CERF [in early 2006] we thought that responses to our appeals would be quick; but this has not happened and I think this is due to the fact that this is a new instrument. We sent our requests for funds in May but only in July have we officially been informed of the outcome. It is true that a delay is really detrimental to the FAO - more so than to any other UN agencies.
We are a technical agency and we have natural and technical constraints as we operate according to the seasons. Some UN agencies may operate the whole year distributing food and non-food items - there is no problem as there is no time constraint. However, FAO cannot operate all year round like the other agencies as it depends on the agricultural calendar. This makes FAO emergencies very specific. I mean, if financial aid directed to FAO arrives in the country by mid-September, we cannot use it as we would be at the end of the planting season in the CAR. This is why we're appealing to our partners to be flexible regarding investment requests by the FAO.
I spoke about the $193,200 officially announced for the CAR. The money is not available at the moment [by 12 July] and with all administrative arrangements to be fulfilled, I hope it will be here [in the CAR] by the end of the month; the agricultural schedule would have gone far enough and there would be no option but to grow cassava. I can say, once again, that the difference between the other UN agencies and the FAO is that these agencies enjoy a certain flexibility in operating at any time of the year while the FAO is restricted by the agricultural schedule.
How much money does FAO need to restart agriculture in the CAR?
To restart agriculture to guarantee better production, the FAO requires tens of billions of CFA francs. Conflict has affected the whole country; infrastructure has been destroyed and people are deprived of basic means of production. People live in an extremely precarious situation in all regions of the CAR.
However, for the time being, we are working with what we have. Unfortunately, the international community has turned a deaf ear to our appeals. We passionately wish to see the international community help this country. Without investment for rebuilding the CAR, the longer the country's predicament remains precarious.
The international community should look at the reasons for investing and assess what can be done to stabilise the CAR. We appeal to donor countries to back the FAO because we are in a country that shows promise. We think if the FAO receives investment from the international community, so many things will be done to help people get out of the precarious situation affecting them now.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Bukwa Lacks Food
Headline: Uganda: Bukwa Lacks Food
Body_Text:
Kampala — ABOUT 3,000 people in Suam sub-county in Bukwa district are facing a critical food shortage following a long dry spell that destroyed their crops.
The district agricultural officer, Francis Epido, said the victims lost nearly all their maize and bean crops. "Some of the crops had not even germinated, while those that germinated were scotched at knee level," Epido said.
He said women and children were hardest hit by the food shortage. He said the production department was conducting a situational analysis, whose findings shall be tabled before the district disaster management team chaired by the chief administrative officer, Alex Patrick Chelimo.
Epido said the scotched maize, beans and wheat gardens were mainly located in Kaptererwo parish. He said the situation had prompted farmers to re-plough gardens during the second season to compensate the losses incurred in the first season.
He, however, expressed doubt over the second season planting phenomena that has often registered poor yields. He said the production department would promote draught-resistant crops like cassava and sunflower. "We are going to conduct a field assessment on the extent of damage," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Girls for Sale
Headline: Uganda: Girls for Sale
Body_Text:
Kampala — AMURIA Woman MP Rhoda Acen recently revealed that Karimojong girls were being sold in Teso for as little as sh3,000.
Titus Kakembo found out how and why the teenagers become commodities.
Lo'Segal, 19, boarded a lorry in Matanyi, Moroto District and set off for Katakwi District. However, she had no money for her fare. On her arrival there, a man who took pity on her offered to pay sh3,000 for her transport so that the lorry driver would let her go. The 'Good Samaritan' went on to hand the teenager to a pub owner.
She does not know whether she was sold or not. What is certain is that she left Karamoja in search of a job in Ocorimongin Market, a vibrant melting point for traders from Teso, Karamoja, Lango and Bugisu.
Like Lo'Segal many Karimojong girls, on Friday, which is market day, arrive at Ocorimongin aboard a truck without money.
Harsh life during the dry season drives the girls to search for jobs in neighbouring Teso. They return home during the rainy season with cereals.
Truck drivers who ply the Matanyi-Ocorimongin route have started accepting money from people who offer to bail out the girls who fail to pay their fare. This has boomed into what is now being called slave trade in Teso.
The Iteso who meet the girls' transport bills employ them on their shambas. Apart from working in the gardens, the girls perform other tasks like grinding, winnowing, baking and brewing beer.
"They may not have capital to begin a business but they have lots of energy," says Akol Basiliza, a trader from Mbale.
"The Karimojong offer cheap labour and are very hard working," he says, adding that a grinding mill business in the area ground to a halt after two of the Karimojong workers there were arrested by the authorities and ferried back to Karamoja last month.
Since the Woman MP for Amuria, Rhoda Acen, exposed the human trade in Katakwi, the Police have mounted road blocks to crack down on any movement of Karimojong girls that is suspect.
Four girls - L'Omuge, Pascal Namoi, Lokoroi and Margaret Teko - found in the market by the Police on July 7 were taken to the Police station, before being sent back to Karamoja.
The Katakwi District CID chief, Grant Tabo, warned that anyone found engaging in slave trade risked 10 years in prison.
Addressing a workshop on women's rights and democracy at Entebbe Imperial Resort Hotel, Acen said, "A child goes for sh3,000 to sh7,000!"
However, the Ocorimongin LC 1 chairman, Fabian Olokojo, says it is drought and poverty that is forcing the Karimojong to offer themselves as cheap labour.
The girls involved in the trade are aged between 13 and 20. Two to three trucks arrive in Ocorimongin from Matanyi every Friday. "There are usually two or three girls who are unable to meet their fares on every truck," says Kassim Okwii, a lorry driver who operates on the route.
"Once I had a passenger on board who did not have money. I held her in captivity until someone from the crowd in the market paid her fare," he said.
Okwii added that the phenomenon of Karimojong girls failing to pay was a common occurrence.
"The teenage girls and grown up women board your lorry from Matanyi destined to Ocorimongin. The touts call out Matanyi/Ocorimongin sh3,000 as they board. They nod yes and climb. Later when asked for their transport fare, they show you empty palms," Okwii explains.
He argues, "Mine is a business not a charity, so the only option is to hold them in custody and try to get someone to pay the fare. What the Good Samaritan does with the child/woman he has freed is not my business."
Unfortunately the girls' lack of money has attracted hordes of potential 'saviours' hunting for cheap labour.
Sources in the market said these opportunists on Fridays come to look out for girls to 'rescue'.
Lo'Segal's pale skin and cracked feet symbolise the hardships many a Karimojong undergoes as they struggle to survive. She has no job description; her duty is to be ready to do anything. At times she ploughs new farmland, threshes grain, draws water from the borehole and runs the popular Wawiya Pub. Her wages are a basinful of millet or beans per month.
"I have been here for about eight months. I look forward to going back when the rains come next year," she says. "By then I will have accumulated enough millet flour, cassava chips and beans to take home."
Aciya Lo'Awiya 16, in the market on a shopping errand for her employer, says she tills the land for the man who paid her fare.
Like Lo'Segal, she says she hopes to go back to Karamoja when the next rains come, after she has accumulated enough food.

Original_Search_Title: Koya Progressive Association (usa) Poised to Promote Health, Agriculture and Education
Headline: Sierra Leone: Koya Progressive Association (usa) Poised to Promote Health, Agriculture and Education
Body_Text:
The last stanza in the country's National Anthem states, "Land that we love, our Sierra Leone".
These words are common on the lips of Sierra Leoneans living in Diaspora who will never forget their motherland.
Most of these Sierra Leonean abroad have formed themselves into an organization that is aimed at helping to develop their motherland that was destroyed by a brutal civil senseless war.
One such organization that has taken the bull by the horn in ensuring that they do not only show the love they have for their country, but also demonstrated their love with action is the Koya Progressive Association based in the United States of America.
The Association, comprises of seventy Sierra Leonean that embraces each other in unity and also help each other when the need arise.
As a way of demonstrating their love for the country, the Koya Progressive Association facilitated the renovation and expansion of two schools in the Koya chiefdom, namely: D.E.C. Primary School at Mamoa village and the Tomlinson High School at Songo.
This gesture, according to the president of the Association, marks the beginning of many more projects to come.
Revealing the association's future plans, KPA's president, Mr. Mohamed H.S. Bangura said their support to the two schools is the first step in a long journey.
"We have plans to support areas like education, health and agriculture which are very important in the development of any country," he said.
Mr. Bangura pointed out that KPA prioritize education on their scale of preference mainly because the most segment of the country's population remains the youths.
"With an illiterate youth, Sierra Leone will never develop. It is common knowledge that the education of our youths was seriously affected," he noted.
The KPA president revealed that very soon a large consignment of books will be shipped to Sierra Leone for various schools in the country.
In the area of health, Mr. Bangura emphasized the saying, "health is wealth", noting that they do not want their brothers and sisters in this country to suffer from health hazards. "We have almost completed modalities for a mobile clinic to be brought in for the Koya chiefdom to help the chiefdom lessen the burden of health, and also reduce the spread of dangerous diseases such as HIV/AIDS," he said while assuring that his association will also send health equipments that will benefit the whole country.
The Association, according to the president is also planning to embark on big time agricultural activities in the country.
"We intend to improve the cultivation of the staple foods such as rice, cassava, and also cash crops that sustain farmers," he said, adding, "we shall also be embarking on a wide range of planting of long term tree crops like orange, cashew, lemon, etc." Mr. Bangura made mention of plans to form an organization with branches all over the country, comprising of active members that would work to generate income for their own development and the country as a whole.
"We intend to work with KCDA and KOYDO for the development of the Koya chiefdom, which has become very instrumental in areas like agriculture that benefit the whole country," he promised.
"We don't have all these resources laying there, but what we have in abundance is the determination to raise the needed funds and achieve our objectives," he said and appealed to other organizations of like minds to help in realizing their dreams.
Speaking about the Koya Progressive Association, the president noted that KPA is a charitable non-government organization, which has resolved to ensure that their country develops.
"We are determined to bring a lot of progressive activities to our motherland, and to ensure that we create a situation that will drastically reduce the suffering of our brothers and sisters at home", Mr. Bangura went on.
"All we are demanding from our brothers and sisters is for them to continue to pray for us. We will never turn our backs on Sierra Leone, as we are with you in spirit and shall endeavour to come home to identify ourselves with your aims and aspirations," the president said, adding, "this is just the beginning, greater things are ahead." However, as a charitable association based in the United States of America, KPA raises funds through monthly subscriptions, T-shirt-printing for sale, car wash and other fund raising ventures including disco shows.
Urgent needs by members are met through voluntary contributions by members of the same association.
Meanwhile this great step taken by Sierra Leoneans in the diaspora has been lauded by several eminent personalities in both government and the diplomatic community including the American Ambassador to Sierra Leone, H.E. Thomas N. Hull, who noted while delivering a keynote address at the opening ceremony of the Tomlinson High School in Songo, that it is inspiring to note that people who have left their country do no forget their motherland.
He said government cannot do everything and requires therefore those that have the capacity and means to help in the development of that nation to contribute in any way possible.
The deputy Education Minister, Abass Collier said Sierra Leoneans should take up the onus of empowering the youths of this country who are the future leaders and stop the attitude of envying others.
"We should not be continuously counting on donor support as some of these donor countries also have their own problems to solve," he said.
The deputy minister admonished other Sierra Leonean at home and abroad to emulate the example of the Koya Progressive Association.
In his contribution, the deputy Minister of Health and Sanitation, Hon. Ibrahim Sesay said Education, Health and Agriculture are all connected to development.
"Every Sierra Leonean must contribute in his or her respective way to move the country ahead," he said, adding, "we should try to know our environment and how to manage it."

Original_Search_Title: Minister Tasks NDDC On Youth Empowerment
Headline: Nigeria: Minister Tasks NDDC On Youth Empowerment
Body_Text:
Lagos — In a bid to stem rising youth unemployment in the Niger Delta Region, the Minister of Inter-Governmental Affairs, Youth, and Special Duties, Dr. (Mrs.) Grace Ogwuche has urged the Niger Delta Development Commission, NDDC, to focus more on job creation for the youth.
The minister who made the call during a courtesy visit to the NDDC Headquarters in Port Harcourt, Rivers State, said empowerment programmes were the surest way to lure Niger Delta youths away from negative tendencies.
While commending the NDDC for its initiative to empower 20 youths in each local government area of the NDDC states, Ogwuche disclosed that President Olusegun Obasanjo has approved the sum of N1bn for youth empowerment in the country, pointing out that 36,000 youths from the Niger Delta Region will benefit from the largesse.
Welcoming the minister to the Commission earlier, NDDC Chairman, Ambassador Sam Edem said youth empowerment is the centre-piece of the first phase of the Niger Delta Regional Development Master Plan, NDRDMP, currently being implemented, and which will run for five years.
Edem said this phase incorporates Quick Impact Projects, QIPs, in priority areas like; agriculture, health, skill acquisition, and youth empowerment, which he noted hold a lot of opportunities for youths in the region.
According to Edem, "The only way to address youth restiveness is massive agriculture through marketing and cultivation", pointing out that the Commission's 180 hectare pilot rice project in Akwa Ibom State which would be increased to 500 hectares before the end of the year was one sure way of engaging the army of unemployed youths in the region.
He said the NDDC was also into massive cassava production, and was partnering with China and Brazil to boost its production.
On skill acquisition, the NDDC boss said following the hint that the Oil and Gas Industry would be requiring 10, 000 welders for their operations, the Commission has cashed in to train some youths in that field and is making representation to oil companies for their employment at the end of their training.He said with the improved communication channel between the NDDC and youths in the Region, violence would be greatly minimized, just as he assured the Minister of the Commission's co-operation in driving development strides in the Region.

Original_Search_Title: Masiaka Market Forges Ahead
Headline: Sierra Leone: Masiaka Market Forges Ahead
Body_Text:
The Masiaka town market has been lauded by community members as the market that has not only improved but also has cheap priced basic commodities for consumers.
The market is located on the main road through the town, with a market house that was built by the UNAMSIL Nigerian Battalion 18 Speaking to this press in an exclusive interview, the councilor of Koya Ward One (Masiaka Town), Samuel Chernor Kanu said the market is improving gradually with most of it commodities sold at a cheaper prices compared to other markets in the chiefdom, noting that the traders are complying with the council greatly as they pay their market dues on a daily basis, and also work in accordance with the market rules of sweeping before and after selling.
"The only problem we are encountering is that the market structure built by UNAMSIL cannot accommodate the present trader population which is fast growing", he said, adding that most of the traders are found outside the market, some of whom are selling perishable goods that does not need too much sunlight.
Also, the councilor went on, some of these traders are exposed to illnesses during the rainy season as they do their trading under the rain.
Councilor Kanu said most of the goods sold in the Masiaka market are fresh from production areas such as farms.
"We make use of them at home in the evening as a family, the councilor boasted.
He described the traders as very peaceful as they have a cordial relationship with the council which monitors the affairs of markets in the chiefdom.
Like the Waterloo market, the Masiaka market also serves as a center for purchasing goods in wholesale by mainly traders from Freetown.
The market comprises of traders selling food items, non-food items such as cooking utensils, clothing, and electrical equipments, etc. prices of some food items are as follows: Rice The Koya chiefdom was recently recognized by President Kabbah as one of the chiefdoms that is helping greatly in fulfilling government's dream of food self sufficiency in 2007.
In this regard the market has got a bit of scarcity of the locally produced country rice called 'Wala' rice which is still in the farms, and is expected to be harvested against November.
The price for a cup of 'wala' rice is Le 500, and goes for a negotiable cheaper price when buying in large quantity.
The same goes for rough rice which is sold at Le 600 per cup.
These prices are expected to be lesser in December when most farms would have harvested their products.
The imported long white rice is sold at Le 400 per cup while the short grain product called 'fen-fen' goes for Le 500 per cup.
Oil The pint for red palm oil goes for Le 700 while masankay is sold at Le 500 per pint. Larger quantity of palm oil is sold at negotiable prices.
Palm cannel nut oil is sold at Le 500/600 per pint based on the quality of product.
The imported groundnut oil is sold at Le 1000 per pint.
Seasoning Jumbo cube Maggie cost Le 200, which the star cube goes for Le 100, while packet Maggie goes for Le 200 and the small packet called Aginomoto cost Le 100, the same price goes for the tie white Maggie.
Big pepper is sold at Le 300 per cup and Le 100/200 per place (based on quality of product).
The same price goes for dry pepper per place but a difference in price per cup, as dry pepper cost Le 600/700 per cup.
Presently onions are scarce at the Masiaka market; the smaller product which was sold at Le 100 now cost Le 200 while the larger ones have got also an increase in prices. Salt is sold in small and large quantity per cup, ranging from Le 100- Le 1500.
The imported salt is sold at Le 100 per tie while the packet cost Le 1,300.
Sauces Masiaka town comprises of mostly gardeners having back yard gardens. This therefore has caused the availability of a variety of sauces in the market at a cheap price compared to Freetown markets whose prices are cut-throat.
Potato leaves and cassava leaves cost Le 200 per small ties, and the big tie of cassava leaves goes for Le 500/700.
Crain-crain and green are sold at Le 500 per tie each.
Fish Fish is sold at various prices based on quality and availability in the market.
Fish is brought from various markets in the Koya Rural district such as Waterloo whose fish are from mainly Tombo and other surrounding wharfs.
Others Groundnut is sold at Le 600 per cup for the old product while the new one goes for Le 500 which is uneasily accessed. The milled groundnut goes for Le 300 - Le 500 for the small and big ties respectively.
Cassava and potato are sold at Le 200 per place for the small ones, Le 500 for the medium ones and Le 1000 per pile.
Both cocoa yam and yam are sold at negotiable prices, based on quality of product. Vegetable tomato is sold at Le 200 per place while the canned imported product cost Le 500 per place for the bigger ones and Le 200/300 per place for the smaller product.
Insurance Corner Aureol Insurance Company Limited Aureol Insurance Company underwrites life and non-life insurance risks such as those associated with fire, motor, accident, medical and marine business.
The company also invests in treasury and other eligible bills in Sierra Leone on which it earns interests.
The company does business in Sierra Leone with its head office branches and employed 56 people.
It is a limited liability company incorporated and domiciled in Sierra Leone, situated at Kissy House, 54 Siaka Stevens Street Freetown, and can boast of the following services.
Life Assurance - This covers endowment Assurance (with or without profits), children's education policy, whole life assurance, keyman assurance policy, funeral expenses, Aureol life Investment Bond (ALIB), Term Assurance, Mortgage Protection, Group Life Assurance, Pensions and Provident Funds, and other managed schemes.
Fire Insurance - This includes private, business premises, and loss of profits.
Miscellaneous Accident Insurance - Under this service the company underwrites the following: house owner's comprehensive policy, householder's comprehensive police, burglary policy, personal accident policy, employer's liability policy, goods in transit policy, public liability policy, fidelity guarantee policy, boards, electronic insurances, engineering insurances, professional indemnity, and medical insurance.
Motor Insurance - Aureol Insurance under this scheme covers compensation cover, third party liability cover, and third party fire and theft.
Marine Insurance - including cargo and hull. Aureol Insurance Company plays an active role in the country's financial market through their investment activities and increase in coverage of a wide range of commercial as well as domestic risks.
The company therefore contributed greatly to the socio-economy development of the country.
Although there are lots of opportunities the company faces a lot of challenges.
These include intense competition, capital adequacy, information technology, human resources development and professionalism.
The company has therefore been able to deliver on performance targets.
Pressure on profit margins as a result of the high cost of doing business, deteriorating claims ratio, renewed focus on strict regulatory regime and sound corporate governance have placed a great imperative on the company to justify their budget and improve on their financial performance.
In spite of the rather competitive and tough market conditions, Aureol has committed itself to actively maintain their leadership position and will continue to explore new avenues to business in order to supplement eroding earnings more additional lines of business.
It is expected that the increasing sophisticated needs of the company's customers and extreme shifts in the business landscape will present strong challenges to management.
According to a senior Aureol Insurance company official, they are adequately prepared to meet the opportunities and challenges that lie ahead by pursuing strategies that will ensure the long term survival growth and profitability of the company.
Aureol Insurance's main objectives are as follows: to emerge as Sierra Leone's leading insurer and a good corporate citizen, to develop into a diversified financial services, provide with insurance as its core activity, to employ dedicated, experienced, trained and qualified staff that are fully empowered and developed to their full potential, to improve customer service and technical excellence; and to provide increasing and sustainable returns on shareholders fund.
The company in addition to it two branch offices at Aureol House of 22 Wallace Johnson street Freetown, and 7 Bojon Street Bo, plans to provide insurance services at the door step of it customers by establishing more branches in the provinces and other parts of the city.
However, Sierra Leone can now boast of the following Insurance companies. Aureol Insurance company, national insurance company, Reliance Insurance Trust Corporation, International Insurance Company, Trans world Insurance company, Medical and General Insurance company, Marine and General Insurance company, and the Sierra Leone Insurance company.
All these companies are registered with the Sierra Leone Insurance commission which regulates and monitors the functions of all Insurance companies and insurance market contributors such as Insurance Brokers.
A senior SLICOM Official told our reporter that amongst the Insurance companies that are registered with them, Aureol Insurance Company which is recognized both locally and internationally has taken the lead in the provision of services to the Sierra Leonean public.

Original_Search_Title: Saraki Counsels Opposition Groups
Headline: Nigeria: Saraki Counsels Opposition Groups
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Governor Bukola Saraki of Kwara State yesterday said it was high time for the opposition groups in the state to have a re-think and join hands with the present administration in the state to accelerate the socio-economic development of the state.
Saraki who spoke with reporters in Ilorin shortly after the departure of President Olusegun Obasanjo to Abjua at the end of his two-day working visit, said all the frivolous allegations being levelled against the government would not take them to anywhere as the government would not renege on its promise of making life bearable for the people of the state.
The governor who was apparently reacting to allegations raised by Kwara Progressive Movement in one of the national newspapers (Not ThisDay) published during the visit of President Obasanjo to the state on the award of contracts and other programmes of the state government, said there was no iota of truth in the said publication as "this government is prudent in the management of the state resources".
He said, "The group alleged that, we spent over N1bn to construct new terminal building at the Ilorin International Airport whereas the government only spent over N400m and this is highly embarrassing and they should have a re-think in their approach towards the state ".
The governor remarked that "their opposition to our government cannot change things on the ground, it will continue to boost government's determination to do more for the people of the state in the provision of basic things of life". Saraki who commended the people of the state for giving the President a rousing welcome said the hard line posture of the opposition would not deter him from fulfilling all his campaign promises to the people of the state.
He said the visit and the happiness of President Obasanjo to all the places of our projects commissioned has attested to the government's determination to make life worth of living for the people of the state.
" See our state library that was commissioned by Mr. President, it was first of its kind in the federation because the library is of e-library status and this would go along way of assisting the people to gather information capable of boosting the ideas of the populace". He stressed.
He manitained that, " The visit has made the president to access us and confrimed that the Kwara state has moved from dark age to a new age where socio well being of the people has taking a major priority rather than selfish interest".
Saraki explained that, " Look at the Dangote Flour Mills here in Kwara state, the people of the state can now supply at least 10 tonnes of cassava while remaining 20 tonnes needed by the company can now be produced by the people of the state and thereby creating an employment opportunities for the citizenry".
He therefore caslled on the people of the state to continue to support the goverment so as to bring more dividends of democracy to the doorsteps of the populace.

Original_Search_Title: I Lost to Public Office Politics, Says Osomo
Headline: Nigeria: I Lost to Public Office Politics, Says Osomo
Body_Text:
Lagos — Mrs. Mobolaji Osomo, former Minister of Housing who lost her position in controversial circumstancises spoke in this interview with Sheriff Balogun on her future political plans and experience in public office
The South-south is clamouring for 2007 presidency just like the South-east and the North. How do you see this development?
In politics, everybody will always agitate for one thing or the other so that people can know how exactly they are feeling. Though you never can tell, the South-west may even agitate again that it is also its right to produce another president because I've seen somebody from the AD coming out to vie for the presidency of this country.
When it comes to the right time, I think the country will sit down and think about the issue of zoning, but as it is now, I don't think there is anywhere in the constitution that they have picked up this idea. So, it's a matter of convenience for different ethnic groups or different political parties to decide who their candidate would be. One might come from the North, one might come from South-south or even South-east or even South-west. It depends on how acceptable that person is to the generality of Nigerians.
A group in the South-south region said if the presidency is zoned to the region, it would reduce the crisis rocking the region. Do you think that thought is true?
The Niger Delta doesn't consist of the South-south alone. I am from the Niger Delta. I am from Ondo State area which belongs to the Niger Delta and the South-east too is from the Niger Delta . So, many states that belongs to the Niger Delta. I believe that anybody can agitate to become the president. I even believe that this presidency should be zoned to women, not just a zone but to women. If it is a question of zoning ,then zone it to the women.
The party you belong to, the PDP, is right now in disarray. What is your comment about the factionalisation?
It is not unexpected if the party is too large, to break itself into factions just like the biological process. You know that when you break into two, at the end of the day, I am sure it will sort itself out. When it is time to present a candidate, people will talk and iron out issues.
