But for now, I think people are examining issues and it will be sorted out. All the parties are breaking into pieces. You know we need to have issues and then patch them together
Being the largest political party in Africa and of course, the ruling party, it is believed that it should serve as an example to other political parties.
I think there is peace within the party. You know within the family unit there will be quarrels between the father and the mother; father and children and within 20 minutes, you have things sorted out. I'm sure this is nothing and it will be sorted out. We have gotten worse things happening in the past than what is happening now.
For someone who had served under the administration of President Olusegun Obasanjo, in your view, how can you rate the president's administration since 1999 till date?
What I believe is that he has actually done well. I think he is the singular Nigerian who has done so much for this country. He is not biased whatsoever. From his youthful days, he has fought for this country with his life. He has fought for South Africa. he had actually come in when this country was being rejected all over the world. When we were in a state of pariah, he lifted us out and I think he has done beautifully well. I'm so proud of him as a Nigerian and he even became the head of the Commonwealth. What else can we ask him to do?
2007 is getting closer, it appears that you 'll be vying for the governorship of Ondo State. Being a woman, how do you think you can make any impact in that race?
Well, in 1999, I had a shot at the primaries and of course, everybody knew that I won in the primaries for the governorship in Ondo State. But I was deprieved of that opportunity by the authorities of the AD. And at that point, it wasn't new to me. I just believed it was a continuation of the situation where I left it before. The terrain is well known to me. I have been familiar with the politics of the state since the time that Papa (Adekunle) Ajasin was the governor.
If you evetually emerged as the governor of Ondo State in 2007, what do are your plans for the state?
By the grace of God, when I emerge as the governor of Ondo State in 2007, I have marked out a manifesto that I will like to conform with. Going through the manifesto of the PDP, the party to which I belong and also the millenium agenda, I have meticulously sat down and treated all the programmes and projects which would be handled during the period I will be in office.
Don't you think the power of incumbency of Govenor Segun Agagu will affect your chance seriously?
I don't really think so. I believe the primaries will decide and the people of the state will decide whether they want him for the second term or not; but right now, about 80 per cent to 90 per cent of the people in Ondo are discontented with the performance and so they want to choose.
So you are saying that the governor has not performed?
What the president has just done for him recently will speak for him. I have been one of his foremost critics all along. For example, he could have done 100 percent better than what he has done. Isn't that all he had to show the President that he has achieved within four years in office? But at the last moment, he doesn't give anybody any hope that he can do better than what he has done for the past three years
Now, what are your major areas of focus in your said millenium agenda?
The major focus in the millenium agenda is eradication of poverty. There is poverty everywhere in the world you go to today and this poverty could come in various ways. The way you live, the type of water you drink, the whole environment, then your health and the orientation you have and so many other things, determine the level of poverty and unemployment which is prevalent in the third world where people have less than a dollar a day. We have to work very hard to increase the economic growth of the country.
In a country like Nigeria where we must manufacture, we are not competitive in any form in anything. We have to look at our agriculture and see where we can do better. The only micro-economic product which we rely on is the oil and is becoming an issue which the world wants to tap, because of the prices of oil. The alternative sources of oil are being loosened and thank God that the Nigeria National Petroleum Coporation (NNPC) recently talked about cassava and sugarcane for producing ethanol. So think about Ondo State where we have so much land unocupied. We have to look at all these things and see if we can work towards the alternative source of energy.
We also need to look at the food source. We eat a lot of garri, if the amount of debt that has been cancelled or the relief as was said, can be maintained, then we have to do a lot of internal production of food. If we continue to import rice from Thailand, if we continue to import fish despite all the Atlantic oceans surrounding us and we can go into deep sea fishery, then we will soon be back into another set of debts. So, really these issues are not issues that we can take with a pinch of salt. They are issues that everybody has to roll up their sleeves for and get something done. That is what is being done all over the world and I think the leadership of Ondo State must be so meaningful and conscious of the problem there. We are about 3.1 million people and we earn so much money and yet people flock from Ondo to Lagos to the extent that Lagos is becoming the 11th largest city in the world and in another four years, they say it will take the number three position. Ondo Sta te should arrest that situation if I become the governor of that state by the grace of God.
What was done in the 60's by Pa (Obafemi) Awolowo by setting aside Ikeja (in Lagos) as an industrial estate, we will do the same thing in Ondo State so that by the proximity to Ogun State, we will be able to have industrial estates and produce not only foods, but consumables such as plastic pipes which we 'll use for conducting water. We also have many rivers in Ondo State. If you are travelling to Benin, by the time you get to the axis of Ondo and the boundary of Ogun and the boundary of Benin, you'll see that you are crossing about eight or nine rivers. Some of these rivers are not quite navigable like before, but by the time you get to Okitipupa area, they become navigable. We'll look at how we can produce many things from these rivers like dam bend, use the bend for vegetable and planting and have a means of flood control through some of these rivers.
Ondo State is a wonderful state and I think we have no business with poverty. Our problem is that the leadership for sometime has been very poor. So that is why our people rush to Lagos State.
Let us look at the time you were a minister.How would you describe your dismissal?
Well, I have been a minister twice now . The last time was not the first time. I was a minister for Establishment from 1993 to 1995 and exactly three years ago, July 3rd 2003, I was nominated to again as a minister. So, the politics was just like one of those public officers politics. In Nigeria, when something happens, at the end of the day, nobody will be able to explain exactly what had happened. But I think I've put all that behind me and I think all is well that ends well.
Okay, what advice can you give to politicians who see politics as a do or die affair?
It's always a pity when people take politics as a means of livelihood. I actually will appeal to the general public that they should not get carried away by politicians who are just coming to give them money for their votes. They should examine very critically people who can really meet their future needs rather than the person who gives them money so that they can vote for you.
As soon as you sell yourself, then you have no right to look for development in the future. So, my belief is that Nigerians should examine very critically who they vote for, not hungry politicians, but people who can actually perform. For the last seven, eight years, you know we should have been able to stop so many of such attitudes. We need people of integrity to really come out.

Original_Search_Title: Ghana Strikes $10 Million World Food Program Deal
Headline: Ghana Strikes $10 Million World Food Program Deal
Body_Text:
Accra — The Government of Ghana, through her good performance in the food and agriculture sector, has struck a deal with the World Food Programme to establish its food depot in Ghana worth $10 million per annum.
The deal involves buying Ghana's food items for the Programme's emergency food supplies in the sub-region.
This, the Minister of Food and Agriculture, Mr. Ernest Debrah, revealed to the press yesterday at a 'meet the press' series of the Ministry of Information and National Orientation.
In his remarks, the minister said Ghana is self-sufficient in most of her staple food production except with maize and rice.
Although the country's self-sufficiency ratio in rice had increased from 40% in 2001 to 45% in 2004, it still has a gap of 65% to satisfy.
Maize also recorded 92% sufficiency.
However, the other staple food items such as cassava, yam, plantain and cocoyam all recorded levels of self-sufficiencies above 100%. Cassava recorded 214% self-sufficiency ratio, whiles yam registered a 350% self-sufficiency ratio.
Plantain and cocoyam recorded 115% and 117% self-sufficiency ratios respectively.
Touching on the performance of the sector, the minister said compared to 2001, real prices of major staple food crops showed a decline in 2005. The sector contributed 37% to Ghana's gross domestic product and employed 57% of Ghana's labour force. However, growth in the sector declined from 7.5% in 2004 to 6.5% in 2005.
To make the sector more vibrant, the minister said policies and programs are being pursued to ensure food security and to diversify the sector.
The minister said that his ministry is expanding the production process through increased productivity as well as facilitating efficient and effective input supply, which is quite relevant to achieve the first three objectives.
Another direct government intervention was the disbursement of about 157 billion in 2004 and 2005 to support agro processors in the private sector throughout the country.
Under one of the programs called Foundation Seed Production, the government has availed to the sector 5.408 billion between 2001 and 2005 to support seed producers across the country.
"This resulted in increased certified seed production for maize, soybean and groundnut," he added.
The minister further said that Ghana has been chosen out of four African countries to host the regional office for Embrapa, an agric research based in Brazil. Ghana is to host the sub regional office of Food and Agriculture Organisation's sub-regional office.
He said 400 new tractors would be imported from India this year to mechanize agriculture in Ghana.
The minister further assured that irrigation programmes of the ministry are ongoing in both the northern and southern sectors.

Original_Search_Title: IRAD Told to Valorise Research Results
Headline: Cameroon: IRAD Told to Valorise Research Results
Body_Text:
Research Minister, Madaleine Tchuinté was on a working visit to Nkolbisson yesterday.
Research results can only be considered valid if they are published and made useful on the field. The Minister of Scientific Research and Innovation, Madaleine Tchuinté, underscored the importance of research to poverty alleviation and economic development yesterday during on a working visit to the headquarters of the Institute of Agricultural Research for Development (IRAD) at the Yaounde Nkolbisson neighbourhood.
It was her first visit to Nkolbisson since appointed Minister, but it was not her first visit to a research centre. The Nkolbisson working visit enabled Mrs Tchuinté to complete her understanding of problems affecting the sector. One of the issues that have so far hampered research in Cameroon was at the centre of interest; non-publication of research results. All the factors that may have contributed to or continue to contribute to the absence of such vulgarisation were put before the minister. These include: aging staff, no new trained staff, limited resources to carryout research and make them public, no enough assistance from partners, lack of incentives, accrued salary arrears of workers and general demobilisation after traversing a long period of turmoil.
Today, IRAD has a staff of 275 researchers, 114 technicians and 536 support staff. In appreciation of the effort of the government in improving the performance of IRAD, he thanked the Head of State for enabling the recruitment of more than 100 young researchers in IRAD.
The new management team seems to be quite determined to overcome the hurdles that have stood on the way of progress for the corporation. The General Manager of IRAD, Dr Zok Simon, in his welcome speech expressed this determination through an ambitious action plan which has gone operational already. To solve the problem of dwindling staff, Dr Zok announced the recruitment of 307 technicians and other staff members. This, he said, is going to take a colossal monthly salary of CFA 25 million. While recalling the main mission assigned to IRAD, which basically concerns carrying out research to enhance agricultural activities, Dr; Zok, said, in order to obtain better results, IRAD decided to extend its tentacles to all the five ecological zones of Cameroon. He equally mentioned the creation of three research centres at sub-regional level. "All the three centres are operational", he said.
Among the recent research results obtained, are the introduction of new varieties of annual crops including: maize, cassava, sweet potatoes, rice, cotton,; perennial crops such as cocoa, Robusta and Arabica coffee, and oil palm, and animal products such as improved species of animal feed, putting in place of simple techniques, pasture management rearing of ruminants and limitation of vaccination age among others.
The departments visited by Minister Tchuinté yesterday include: head office, scientific research department, forest environment coordination, REPARAC project, HIPC and Annual production project, soil, water and plant laboratory, food technology laboratory and the Central library. She equally had a working session with the staff.

Original_Search_Title: No Money, No School
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: No Money, No School
Body_Text:
Kisangani — The headmaster, wearing a tattered red uniform, points at his worn slippers and says, "Look at me. Do I look like a teacher? We have no desks, no books - not even water." Then Ire Yabongengo complains about parents who are fed up with constantly repairing the school, a collection of shacks made of sticks and mud, with roofs made of leaves.
Lokanja Lina Nkoy is a tiny village in the midst of the immense rainforest that blankets most of the Congo Basin and comprises the heart of the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC). The only school here is run by the Kimbanguistes, a religious sect worshipping Kimbangu, a freedom fighter incarcerated by the Belgian colonial powers, who died in jail shortly before the end of the colonial era.
As is the case for most public schools in the DRC, the government is responsible for paying teachers' salaries and providing benches, tables and classrooms for the 150 pupils at the Lokanja Lina Nkoy village school. However, students here are crowded into four classrooms where the sun shines through gaping holes in the roof. They sit on logs, bare feet in the sand, wearing shabby clothes instead of uniforms. Still, the children are enthusiastic about learning, even if they must do so without even the most basic supplies.
"Bonjour M le Directeur!" they shout heartily.
There is not a single pen, book or piece of paper in the shacks. Teachers scribble French words with chalk on raw wooden boards that have been painted black. The children practise writing words in the sand.
Lokanja Lina Nkoy primary school is more the rule than the exception in a country where the education system is in a state of decay after years of civil strife and decades of neglect. As a result, more than 3.5 million children do not go to school in the DRC, which has a population of about 60 million.
According to the Poverty Reduction Strategy Paper produced by the government and UN agencies in March 2006, the situation has deteriorated in recent years. In 1995, 25 percent of all children did not attend classes; in 2001 that figure had risen to 30 percent.
No salaries
Until the 1960s, the churches ran most of the schools. The majority were built by Catholic priests, who had established mission stations all over what was then called Zaire. When Mobutu Sese Seko came to power in 1965 he started an aggressive campaign of nationalisation. This strategy bled not only the economy but also ruined the civil service, most of which had been supported by the clergy. School fees were now collected by the government, but the responsibility of running the schools was left to the priests - with no funding.
The teachers in Lokanja Lina Nkoy have not received their salaries in months. When the government does pay the headmaster, he earns 14,000 Congolese francs, the equivalent of about US$30. Regular teachers are paid less than half that. But $15 is far too little in the DRC, where almost everything has to be imported, including pens and paper - in a country with the largest forest in Africa.
Despite their frustrations, some parents and teachers in the village have refused to give up hope and are constructing a new mud hut to replace one that is threatening to collapse on to the pupils. "Were it not for the teachers and parents, the education system in the DRC would not exist at all," said Christian Lannoye, an education expert and consultant for the development agency Technical Cooperation Belgium.
No money, no school
The new constitution approved by Congolese voters in December 2005 guarantees free primary and secondary schooling for all children. But since teachers are not paid, schools all over the country demand a 'prime', or premium. The 'prime' varies from about 15 cents to $1 in the interior, and between $10 and $30 in the capital, Kinshasa.
For many parents, even 15 cents is prohibitively expensive. Mothers washing plates and clothes in the Lomami River, close to the village school, complained that their children could not learn to read and write because they could not afford to pay the 'prime'.
Residents of Lokanja Lina Nkoy survive through subsistence farming and fishing. Aside from selling cassava cake and smoked fish to other poor people, they have no means to earn a living. Tonnes of cash crops such as coffee and oil palm fruit are rotting in the hinterland. The cost of transporting goods to Kisangani, capital of Orientale province, is more expensive than the sale price of the goods at the market.
Three hours on foot from the village is Isangi town, a trading post of 5,300 people with a Roman Catholic mission and the administrative offices of Isangi Territory, one of more than 500 municipalities in Orientale province. The oil palm plantations in the area are neglected, and the plantation company - Busira Lomami, the only employer in the area - ceased operations when the town was occupied and looted by Rwandan and Ugandan armies and Congolese rebels in the 1990s.
The primary school in Isangi is run by Catholic priests and is in better shape than that in Lokanja Lina Nkoy, but only just. Once proud brick buildings erected in the 1930s are crumbling due to lack of money.
No pensions for retired teachers
School headmaster Augustin Mombaya, 68, has lived through Mobutu's reign and all the conflict that ensued after his overthrow by Laurent Desire Kabila, father of current President Joseph Kabila.
Mombaya earns 32,000 Congolese francs ($70) a month - in theory only, as salaries in his school are 15 months overdue. "Ten percent of all our teachers never received a salary at all, because they are not registered with the ministry of education," he said.
When old teachers are too weak to work, young teachers are appointed in their place. But because of the inefficiency of the education system, the 'retirees' remain on the payroll - sometimes even after they have died.
Mombaya should have retired 13 years ago, after 30 years' service. "Several times I sent lists of 140 teachers who should be retired to the education ministry in Kinshasa, but I did not get a reply, even once," he said.
When the minister of education in Kinshasa recently presented the budget minister with a list of tens of thousands of teachers eligible for retirement, he was told there was no money for their pensions.
Teachers all over the country have called a strike twice this school year - but nothing has changed.
Girls drop out first
The effect of the 'prime' system is felt mainly by girls. In the secondary class in Isangi, one female student sits in a class of 15 young men. In primary school, girls often make up the majority - the result of a campaign by the United Nations Children's Fund (Unicef) to encourage parents to send them to school.
As years go by, however, girls drop out - due to lack of money, pregnancy or early marriage.
Unicef has provided kits of writing materials for children and chalk for teachers to 290 schools in Orientale province. Still, more than 2,200 schools are waiting for support, and new pupils will continue to enter the system.
Pascal Libondo Molanga, director of 63 primary schools in Kisangani, told IRIN that one-third of all children in the city - an estimated 80,000 in a population of 500,000 people - did not attend classes. "Sixty-five percent of all children that roam around during the day are girls," he said.
"Many of them prostitute themselves with the silent agreement of their parents in order to send their brothers to school," said Giovanni Pross, an Italian priest who has lived in Kisangani for 20 years, and is frustrated and angry about the way politicians handle matters here.
Sylvain Nzaba, Unicef protection officer in the provincial capital, Kisangani, believes that lack of schooling, among other factors, is responsible for the estimated 5,000 street children who have to fend for themselves all over the city.
The money is there, but there's no way to get it
Lannoye battles with the education ministry in Kinshasa for the system to be reformed. He has difficulty finding partners who understand the seriousness of the situation and are able to find solutions to the problems. "These are not politicians, but predators. Much of the money that comes in is stolen, and money that would be available remains in the coffers of willing creditors because the people in the ministries don't know how to access it and use it. Millions are waiting to be invested," said Lannoye.
The problems in the education ministry mirror the situation on the ground.
According to Lannoye, almost everyone in charge is very old - on average 60 to 70 years old. They are often not qualified. "They hold all the jobs, preventing younger people from being promoted. The last time new people were hired was more than 10 years ago. Their salaries range from $7 to $15 a month, which forces them to do other work."
Like the teachers, the older employees in the education ministry are unable to retire. Meanwhile, the World Bank has introduced a project to provide the DRC government with funds for one-off retirement benefits of up to $600 per person.
Breeding ground for trouble
Not as bad, but far from good, is the situation in the universities - breeding grounds for frustration, discontent and trouble. The dean of the University of Kisangani, Danly Ngbonda, a medical doctor, puts it like this: "If you have economic and political problems you also have a problem at the learning institutions."
His university lost dozens of professors during the battles that ravaged the city in three rebellions. Many fled to Kinshasa, the luckier ones left the country.
The International Organisation for Migration (IOM) now helps Ngbonda to get some members of the diaspora back to teaching in Kisangani by paying their airfares and salaries. So far, four have returned this year, but that still leaves just 65 professors who have to teach more than 5,000 students in eight faculties. There is no internet connection, electricity supply is random and the libraries are badly equipped.
Like all other professors and teachers in DRC, Ngbonda cannot live off his salary alone. He is a qualified paediatrician, and as such makes some money on the side - something other professors are not able to do. This opens the door for corruption.
"I don't know how many professors are asking for bribes when students want to pass their tests," said Ngbonda, adding that "it's not the brightest who make it through school - it's often just the wealthiest."
In the primary school in Lokanja Lina Nkoy, there is little hope that things will change soon after the 30 July elections.
The headmaster shrugged: "The government has promised us a lot of things in the past, and nothing ever materialised. Why should things change this time?"
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Oil Spill Devastates Ten Delta Communities
Headline: Nigeria: Oil Spill Sacks Ten Delta Communities
Body_Text:
Asaba — AN oil spill has been reported along the banks of Ethiope River in Ethiope-West local government area of Delta State, leaving about 10 communities devasted.
Member representing the area in the State House of Assembly, Mr. Anthony Akpomiemie broke the news on the floor of the House yesterday, and gave the names of the affected communities as Jesse town, Mosogar, Atiwor, Ugbomoya Waterside, Ovade and Onyobru, among others.
The lawmaker lamented that economic activities have been crippled in the area since the spill occurred a week ago.
The state Assembly consequently mandated its Committee on Oil Mineral and Gas as well as its Committee on Environment to undertake a tour of the area and report back for appropriate action following a motion moved by Mr. Akpomiemie.
Responding to inquiries from reporters later, Mr. Akpomiemie said although he could not say which of the oil companies owns the facility that caused the spill, Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC), Shell, Pan Ocean have their pipelines running across the area.
Said he: "These pipes were installed several years ago. The entire Ethiope River as I am talking to you now is polluted with crude oil.The people in that area depend on fishing apart from farm work, planting of cassava, yam, for now they cannot do that. If you go there, you find on top of the river fishes and things like that floating, those ones are dead."
"People who live around that area, River Ethiope is the source of their sharp sand". The divers they can hardly go in as at now to get sand for building. You find out that the cost of sharp sand has gone up astronomically because the boys who normally do the diving cannot do it again".
He added that "the level of devastation is very very high. The aquatic life has been rendered totally useless", noting that "besides the communities listed in the motion above, several others have been impacted by the incident. Apart from the affected area, the fear we have is that the tidal wave is moving towards Sapele area", noting that "there is the likelihood of the spill spreading to Sapele and the Ukwani Area from where the river drives its source."

Original_Search_Title: Food and Agric Minister Says No Cause for Alarm
Headline: Ghana: Food and Agric Minister Says No Cause for Alarm
Body_Text:
Mr Ernest Debrah, Minister for Food and Agriculture yesterday announced that the agricultural sector is self-sufficient and has "good news" with enormous benefit to the country. He was speaking at the Meet-the-Press session with journalists. He said Ghana's performance in the sector has attracted international recognition and disclosed the selection of Ghana out of four African countries to host the regional office of Embrapa, an international research organisation in Brazil.
The Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) of the United Nations is also establishing its sub-regional office in Accra to cater for the West Africa sub-region.
He also announced that the World Food Programme (WFP) has approached the Government of Ghana to establish a Food Depot in Ghana for the sub-region.
"This means WFP will buy local food items for its emergency food supplies in the region. The deal is worth at least $10 million annually and will create a huge market for our local farmers", he said.
The presence of such international institutions in the country would bring huge resources for agricultural research and new technologies for farmers, he explained. It would also "make available a large pool of international experts to my ministry for the development of agriculture".
He said even though the percentage of labour in agriculture has dropped from 60% in 2001 to 57% in 2005, production has been going up and the sector continues to be a dominant contributor to the country's GDP, accounting for 37.0%. He explained that the growth in the various sectors of Ghana's economy, is the main reason some people are leaving the agricultural sector for other sectors.
The Minister announced a policy under the diversification of agriculture export base to develop at least more cash crops to stop the over reliance on cocoa. So far, cashew, mango and cotton have been identified and their production would be increased. "Within the next five years, production of cashew and cotton will be increased by an additional 100,000 hectares each and mango 50,000 hectares", he said.
The cocoa sub-sector contributed 17.9 percent of the sector's contribution to GDP in 2004, which therefore makes it a determining factor to the growth of the sector.
On bird flu, the minister said not a single case has been reported and assured Ghanaians that the measures being put in place would ensure that the flu does not enter the country. He said Ghana has been praised by the international community for its role in fighting the disease. "Let me seize this opportunity to, once again, assure the consuming public that chicken is safe for consumption, so I urge lovers of chicken to continue to enjoy it.
He urged Ghanaians to patronise the local rice because the Ghanaian varieties are more nutritious than the imported ones. He said the ministry has facilitated the establishment of the Ghana Rice Inter-Professional Body (GRIB). The body has brought together producers of rice, processors, importers and marketers under a common umbrella, working to promote rice production in Ghana. He said both local and foreign investors have shown interest in the Aveyime Rice Project and the government will partner a private investor to operate the mill.
"Valuation of the machinery/equipment and the land has been completed, and the values will form the basis for negotiations". Mr Debrah also unveiled six objectives being pursued by the ministry. These include: ensuring of food security, facilitation of the production of agricultural raw materials for industry and production of agricultural commodities for export.
The rest are facilitating effective and efficient input supply and distribution systems, facilitating effective and efficient output processing and marketing system and formulating and coordinating the implementation of policies and programmes for the food and agricultural sector. The ministry has therefore projected the production of some selected commodities by the year 2009. It is projected that in 2009 the country would produce 21,877,689 metric tons of cassava from the present production rate of 11,689,800 metric tons. Maize will move from 1,280,000 to 2,640,000 and rice (paddy) from 266,200 to 421,660.
For poultry and livestock, commercial poultry will increase from 5,000 metric tons in 2006 to 68,000 in 2009. Rural poultry from 18,000 to 170,000, sheep production would go up from 3,000 to 13,000.
Mr Debrah said MOFA's policy thrust is to transform the rural economy through agricultural modernization to achieve the targets. "Modernization includes improved access to improved seeds (through higher levels of research and development), agricultural mechanization, irrigation and agronomic practices."
He noted that Ghanaian farmers use very little improved seeds, which he said accounts for their low productivity. The ministry, as part of its pilot project to improve production, during the last minor season in 2005 supplied improved maize seeds to 20,000 small farmers in the Brong Ahafo, Ashanti, Eastern and Central Regions. An average yield of 10 bags per acre was realised compared to the previous 4 or 5 maxi bags per acre. During this major season, the supplied seeds would be doubled.
An amount of ¢157 billion was given to private sector agro processing companies in 2004 and 2005 to address the huge post harvest losses.
To enhance agricultural mechanization, a total of 700 tractors have been imported since 2004 and additional 400 expected to arrive this year. Also 500 pieces of power tillers and 200 lower-capacity tractors have been ordered for small farmers.
He said because most farmers lack the financial ability to purchase and own agricultural machines, the machines would be given to them and payment would be spread over a period of three years.
On irrigation, he said the ministry's present focus is to develop small-scale community managed irrigation systems that meet the needs of small farmers.
He said the government has already signed a contract with a Spanish firm, under a Spanish protocol, to develop solar controlled irrigation systems across the country.
He said the horticulture sector has great potential but it is faced with lack of cold chain facilities, access to the right varieties, poor access to roads and meeting international standards.
He said a contract for the rehabilitation of Shed 9 at Tema Port into a modern fruits terminal is due for completion by the end of October this year.

Original_Search_Title: KIST Extends Technology to Eastern Province
Headline: Rwanda: KIST Extends Technology to Eastern Province
Body_Text:
Eastern Province — The Kigali Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) has established the satellite Centre for Innovation and Technology Transfer (CITT) for development and disseminating appropriate technology especially rural, small and medium enterprises in Nyagatare. The Nyagatare satellite centre manager, Jean Bosco Mugabo, during an exclusive interview with The New Times disclosed that the centre will serve the local community in the province by facilitating projects aimed at poverty alleviation, economic development, preventing and reversing environmental deterioration by linking the target groups with the Rwandan government, donor organisations and other parties that finance rural development projects.
"The conventional teaching in institutions of higher learning was not responding to socio-economic aspects, unemployment and the private sector development, CITT has identified a number of barriers such as lack of appropriate skills and traditional attitudes, which may prevent beneficiaries, especially women from obtaining maximum benefits from the adoption of new technology. CITT therefore ensures that gender aspects are adequately addressed through active participation of women at all levels in technology development," the manager intimated.
Mugabo pointed out that the satellite centre is made up of five departments which include research technology, and development (TRD), women technology transfer and community development department (WTCD) technology and business incubation facility department (TBF) and KIST enterprise development fund.
He said that in a period of two years, the technology department will embark in agro-processing like maize sheller, rice thresher, rice huller, rice polisher, tomato pulper, cassava grater, potato chipping and hay bailer.
He added, "we shall also start milk processing, energy generation, rural-based technology for transportation, construction technology. But all these activities will start in three months.

Original_Search_Title: Sacked Workers Get El-Rufai's Land Gifts
Headline: Nigeria: Sacked Workers Get El-Rufai's Land Gifts
Body_Text:
Abuja — Disengaged staff of the Ministry of Finance, Budget Office numbering 294 were each dashed a plot of land in satellite towns of Abuja by the Ministry of Federal Capital Territory in addition to their severance packages.
The lands were given to the sacked workers to aid their business after they have acquired skills that will enable them carry on and stand on their own after serving their fatherland at a training workshop organised by the Ministry of Finance which ended at the weekend.
Speaking in Abuja at the closing ceremony of the training workshop Saturday, FCT minister and chairman of the Civil Service Reform Commission Mallam Nasir el-Rufai said their disengagement from the civil service was a painful decision to make for the government and implemented.
According to him, "many things were wrong with Nigeria in the last 10 to 20 years, one of which is the civil service. It is painful for government that certain categories of people have to leave the service but government decided to make it painless for the retirees by training them and giving them skills they will require after service to the nation".
He noted that one million people work for the Federal Government while another one million work for the state and local governments out of a population of 150 million with the remaining 148 million not working for the governments.
He urged those that may wish to stay back in Abuja to start their own business, to seek the expert advice of the Abuja Enterprise Agency (AEA).
It could be recalled that it was announced that about 292 staff cutting across different cadres have been served letters of disengagement from service and commenced training on Monday 26 June 2006 to prepare them for life after civil service.
Deputy Director, Press Head, Media and Public Relation Unit of the ministry, Mr. Samuel Ajayi while disclosing this revealed that those in the junior cadre included "mostly messengers, watchmen, cleaners, domestic staff who are no longer needed as a result of government policy on monetization as well as some staff who did not possess the qualification and skills necessary for performance of their duties."
According to him, " a total of 476 staff of the ministry and the Budget Office may be affected in the exercise. The ministry including the Budget Office currently has about 1,500 staff."
Ajayi said to make them useful to their families and the society after service to their fatherland, the ministry was organizing a two-week training workshop, "to equip them with the necessary skills and re-orientation that will prepare them for the challenges of post-service life."
"During the proposed workshop, the staff will be trained in areas such as poultry, grass cutter farming, fast food franchising as well production of packaged water, liquid soap, battery water, soaps, fruit juices, wine, cassava pellet and cassava flour, amongst others," he had noted, adding that, "the workshop, will also feature "presentations on topics such as understanding money, retirement planning, business development tools for successful entrepreneuring, business plan presentation and personal financial planning."

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Urges Nigerians to Eschew Violence
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Urges Nigerians to Eschew Violence
Body_Text:
Ilorin — President Olusegun Obasanjo yesterday urged Nigerians, irrespective of ethnic, political and social leanings to eschew violence and embrace peace to complement government's efforts at accelerating the nation's development .
The President who disclosed this at the palace of the Emir of Ilorin, Alhaji Ibrahim Sulu Gambari, said it was imperative that Nigerians join in the task of moving the country forward, particularly with the on-going reforms embarked upon by government.
Obasanjo who is on a two-day state visit to Kwara State, noted that the collaborative effort between government and the populace was most essential, adding that anything short of this would amount to an effort in futility.
"If things are going on in one state then that is one out of the 36 states and if it goes to other states, that is greater development. Development cannot be achieved in isolation. We all have to join hands to make development work", he said.
President Obasanjo, who was apparently elated over the strings of achievements recorded so far by Governor Bukola Saraki-led administration said, "we are here for two days and we will go round. The report I have and previous visits and discussions with the governor and the changes seen at the airport is an indication that there is progress. I will not give my report until after the visit".
Commending the state government for ensuring that peace, tranquility reigned in the state, the President said, "as you know, I must have visited here (Kwara) for so many times and this is the first time I am visiting and there is no tension. I must thank the governor for this new era. It is an era that must be sustained. For any kind of development, we need peace and harmony and I pray that this peace will be deepened and the development works going on will be deepened".
According to him, "one of the hallmarks of this government is equity. We must allow equity reign in whatever we do".
While acknowledging that there could be exceptional cases for amendments, the president affirmed that "I am not saying that we are perfect. Wherever we see areas where we might have overlooked, we will always make amends".
Commending the role being played by the Emir and the state council of traditional rulers in enthroning peace, President Obasanjo explained that,"I want to thank the Emir and the traditional rulers for the support to this government and by extension, the central government".
Responding, the Emir of Ilorin, Alhaji Ibrahim Sulu Gambari called on President Obasanjo to re-direct Federal Government's presence in the state in order to complement efforts being made by the state government to move the state forward.
Said he:"We know you have great interest in Kwara and particularly in the governor. We know the Federal interest in this state is getting thinner and we would urge you to re-direct your searchlight to the state again".
The Emir, who also commended the President's policies and programmes, which have been instrumental to the
development of the country since 1999, stated that"we commend the debt repayment you have made. You have bailed us from our soiled reputation and taken us to international height. You have been doing great things in this country".
Speaking philosophically, Gambari said, "we are grateful and commend you for this visit. we know you had been here before to inspect the workings of your son in agricultural areas and you approve of his work".
The Emir re-affirmed further that "your coming back again is like a father coming to see his son in whom he is well pleased".
"We assure you this is your state and we had to vote and we voted for you overwhelmingly. We had 85 per cent", he stressed.
On some of the projects earmarked for commissioning, the Emir said, "we are grateful you are inspecting the Asadam project", adding , "it is a vital one for this state because water has been our major problem and the governor has been trying on this. We have been told that the Federal Government is involved and we are pleased about this. We commend the giant strides in the areas of telecomms, communication, dualisation of the Ilorin-Ibadan road".
However, at the commissioning of the Dangote Flour Mills, President Obasanjo called on industrialists to emulate the giant strides of Alhaji Aliko Dangote in the establishment of private sector participation in boosting the economic development of the nation.
He said he was not surprised at the success-story of
Dangote Group of Commpanies, stating that today's event was the fifth of its kind to be embarked by the company and also commissioned by him (President Obasanjo).
Speaking also, the Chairman and CEO of Dangote Group of Companies, Alhaji Aliko Dangote said the establishment of the company was to reduce the rate of unemployment in the country. Dangote said the project being commissioned was expected to produce 500 metric tonnes of flour, using locally sourced cassava as its main raw material, while the millet is imported to boost the production.
Stressing further, Dangote said the company had made available N25bn to farmers to facilitate the production of cassava.
After the commissioning, Dangote hinted that another machine was already in the pipeline, which when completed would be producing 1,000 metric tonnes of flour.
On his part, Governor Bukola Saraki said, since assumption of office, he has been trying to woo private industrialists to the state to boost the economy.
The president, who on arrival commissioned the new ultra-modern terminal building at the Ilorin International Airport, also laid the foundation for the new Cargo Shed for the airport.
As part of his two-day working visit, which is the second within the space of one year, the President commissioned the Yebumot-Geri Alimi-Asa Dualised road,
Mandate Two Housing Estate, Ilorin, also commissioned the state Library project, new Kwara State Executive Governor's office and Meeting Hall, Commissioned the new school facilities at Government Secondary School, Ilorin, commssioned the Cassava processing factory, commissioned the Kwara Ethnix Furniture Manufacturing company, Gaa-Imam, Ilorin and the launching of the state book revolving scheme at Government Secondary School, Ilorin.
Meanwhile, Obasanjo is expected to round off his state visit today with the major highlight being his interactive session with stakeholders in the state. Commissioning of the renovated Bishop Smith College, Ilorin, inspection of Ilorin Water Supply rehabilitation, expansion and foundation laying of 12 million gallons of water treatment plant are some of the projects he is expected to commission.

Original_Search_Title: Who Planted This Murder in Our Hearts?
Headline: Kenya: Who Planted This Murder in Our Hearts?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The March-May 2006 issue of Awaaz, a journal of the south Asian Kenyan community, carries a report on a show at Nairobi's Carnivore restaurant by UK-based "slam poet" Shailja Patel.
What struck me was her recall of President Daniel arap Moi's regime. She said with some bitterness:
"I grew up in Kenya during the Moi years. When those who spoke out were routinely arrested, detained without trial, beaten, tortured, exiled, killed. We read daily news stories about journalists, activists, even students, jailed for 'sedition.' Every so often, our literature teacher would tell us that such-and-such a poet had been 'banned' - and we dutifully crossed out their name and poems from our school textbooks."
I read this piece on Saturday, July 1. On Wednesday June 28, I was at Makini Secondary School in an audience being enchanted by Prof Ngugi wa Thiongo doing what he does best - telling a story in the sacred ritual of instruction. He was telling students and their teachers about a man called Matigari.
The Moi government heard that Matigari was going around the country telling people to disobey the government; to ask questions about laws and edicts from government officials. Somebody instructed the Special Branch to get the man detained. After an intensive search, it emerged that Matigari was not a real person but a fictional character in Ngugi's novel of that title.
Instructions went out for all copies of the book to be bought from booksellers all over Kenya. And then burnt.
The moral of the narrative was that political dictatorship also includes blocking the eyes and ears of citizens and visitors alike.
A week before Ngugi's tale, Prof Michael Chege had discussed the sorry state of universities in East Africa. It was at a workshop organised at the Nairobi Safari Club by Prof Abdalla Bujra for civil society representatives. I was representing Africa Vision 525, a knowledge-based civil society organisation. Prof Chege wished to correct the erroneous impression that President Moi considered universities as institutions without value.
MOI INDEED valued universities if they were willing to serve his agenda of retaining power and keeping his opponents at bay. Subjecting opposition academics to detention, harassment, beatings and torture all part of the serious business of protecting political power. The danger was in "national development" being permanently disengaged from the mission of universities under such a regime of primary commitment to the personal interests of the ruler and his patronage network. But that concern was Prof Chege's agenda.
Late on Saturday July 1, I watched a BBC TV documentary on Joseph Kony, the leader of the Lords Resistance Movement, somewhere along the Uganda-Sudan-Congo border. The camera showed him sitting in a low wooden chair in tall grass, bodyguards around him. Their uniforms looked new. Kony denied charges of cutting off the ears and lips of abducted victims in northern Uganda. In his protest, he said he was fighting for democracy and freedom, "not the Movement type" of democracy practised by Museveni.
I was surprised that he failed to throw counter punches by accusing Museveni of genocide and robbing a whole region of its livestock. He did not throw back accusation such as those by Olara Otunnu, Uganda's foreign minister under the Basilio Okello presidency and a former United Nations official in charge of protecting children against violence. Otunnu recently accused Museveni of herding over two million people from Acholi, Lango and Teso into "concentration camps" in which 10 people huddle together to sleep inside tiny shelters. Over 4,000 people, he said, use one toilet and women wait 12 hours to get water from a single water source. Over 1,500 children die weekly (presumably from cold, malnutrition, malaria and other diseases).
In short, said Otunnu, in his 20-year rule, Museveni has allowed infection of these populations with HIV/Aids, poverty and collective demoralisation to reduce their numbers. It is a weighty bag of accusations; one which Joseph Kony in his intellectual innocence did not mobilise as a verbal weapon.
THE INTERVIEWER said it was evident to him that Kony had never been interviewed by a journalist before. Governance as a primitive instinct for revenge or colonial genocide, politics as permanent warfare, the silence of the victims and of the "international community," all seemed to come to the fore during the interview.
A 1911 issue of the British journal African Affairs accused Nubian troops - who were left behind by Turkish generals bent on conquering a colony from Egypt to the source of the Nile - of decimating people and livestock when Captain (later Lord) Lugard recruited them to defeat Omukama Kabalega's struggle to defend the sovereignty of the Bunyoro-Kitara kingdom. Lugard reports that he had never witnessed such cruelties and desecration of human life as the Nubian mercenaries perpetrated against the Banyoro. The British for their part spread rinderpest to decimate cattle in Bunyoro as a tool of biological and economic warfare. Power spat out destruction and an epidemic of death.
On June 15, 2006, a Nairobi cinema theatre showed a documentary on the life of Mohammed Amin, perhaps the greatest photo journalist ever to come out of East Africa. One of Amin's achievements was his becoming a close friend and photographer of Idi Amin in his moments of play, governing under public view, and the impact of his moods when it swung to brutality. A 20-second clip of a mass murder dungeon, in which Amin's State Research Bureau had done its grim work, showed a heap of naked bodies, mangled and torn. Bowels and other internal organs lay beside the bodies.
Some people had enjoyed the intoxication of being power in the sordid battering of persons who had screamed, yelled, begged for mercy, urinated, defecated, gasped for breath and gone limp and silent beyond memory of who they once were. Political power, sovereignty and Uhuru, as owned by some individuals, had acted on parts of a population. Interestingly enough, the "Nubians" of the 1970s who had replicated the "teach them a lesson" brutalities of Lugard's war against Omukama Kabalega, still saw themselves as invaders, conquerors of an alien people.
In far away Sierra Leone and Liberia, street children recruited by cynical thugs who claimed to be "politicians" notably Foday Sankoh and Charles Taylor, hacked off the limbs of babies, the ears and legs of adult men; raped high society Creole women in Freetown and Monrovia to show that their political-military power could humiliate those who only yesterday saw themselves as of a superior class. Television pictures showed teenagers thrusting knives and bayonets into other teenagers who belonged to "different tribes."
In all that panorama, it has been tempting to speculate that some evil colonial geniuses planted murder in our hearts, nourished by a layer of topsoil labelled "tribes" and religious groups. If stems of cassava can be buried under wet soil; hold the silent heartbeats inside their sap, and sprout later as seedlings, so can planted grief, planted shame, planted murder, and planted hunger. It lies waiting for the vile nourishing watering of a demagogue, a tribal fanatic, a "religious fundamentalist", a "nationalist," a "messiah" to erupt into frenzied violence.
Put in the language of a mechanic, did we know which twisted, worn out or broken parts of the colonial vehicle our leaders took over (from the British, the French, the Portuguese safari drivers), needed urgent and ingenious repairs? Did we ask which type of driver's license was required before anybody took charge of the vehicle called Uhuru?
Prof Michael Chege was asked if, as political scientists at the University of Nairobi, they had sought to chat with President Moi the way a medical doctor would visit a patient for routine check-up. President Moi would later call himself a "professor of politics," suggesting, in self-congratulation, that the form of knowledge he had about politics was superior to that learnt and taught in Kenya's universities.
Idi Amin also claimed professorship in politics and the title of Professor-Conqueror of the British Empire. The validity of these claims is not of concern here. What is at issue is the hinted question of who should have been assembled as the mechanics to examine the ramshackle of a vehicle handed over to us after colonial anthropologists, colonial intelligence officers, brilliant and insightful colonial administrators and missionaries had ridden it for a safari rally that lasted over six decades. This matter is not to be dismissed lightly.
Okello Oculi is a Ugandan poet

Original_Search_Title: Country Appeals for Emergency Relief Aid
Headline: Burundi: Country Appeals for Emergency Relief Aid
Body_Text:
Bujumbura — A Norwegian relief agency is appealing for some US$ 684,586 in emergency aid assistance to help provide basic amenities to returning Burundian refugees in wake of the aftermath of civil strive in the country.
The Norwegian Church Aid, in collaboration with Christian Aid and Conseil National des Eglises du Burundi will also help alleviate poverty among resettling poor communities.
"There are still emergency types of needs in the country, even with the new democratic government in place and relative peace for the first time for 10-12 years," the agencies said in a statement last week.
"The government is relying on NGOs and foreign donors to help in relief and rehabilitation activities. There is still a priority to work on building up trust and confidence between different ethnic groups in Burundi, which did not reach the scale of the genocide in Rwanda, but divided many," the statement further reads.
Burundi is a small country in which observers say mistrust and ethnic clashes may easily break out again, spilling its effects such as refugees over into neighbouring countries.
A recent famine in the North East of the country, caused by a prolonged drought, has only compounded need for emergency, and is the second consecutive year of drought.
UN agencies, FAO and WFP responded, coordinating food and seed deliveries with the assistance of NGOs - including Christian Aid and ACT Rapid Response funds.
ACT is a global alliance of churches and related agencies working to save lives and support communities in emergencies worldwide.
Lately, an incurable banana disease has swept across the tiny nation although efforts are being made to contain it with resistant species. The banana is one of Burundi's subsistence food in addition to cassava and Irish potatoes.
A mosaic virus that decimated the cassava crop exacerbated the situation even further so that even recently returned refugees had to make their way back into the camps in Kibondo, Tanzania.
The appeal is expected be implemented between July 2006 to March 2007 in provinces of Cankuzo, Kirundi, Karuzi, Muyinga, Rugigi and Rutana, providing shelter, seeds, and non food items for returning refugees.

Original_Search_Title: 'Govt Takes Agriculture Seriously'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Govt Takes Agriculture Seriously'
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Obasanjo has said Nigeria's agricultural sector must not only provide food for the country and raw materials for industry and for export, but it remains the largest employer of labour in the country. "We must, therefore take it seriously, and farmers should not allow any future government to ignore agriculture", he said.
President Obasanjo was speaking at State House, Abuja on July 7 during a briefing by the Minister of Agriculture, Malam Adamu Bello, on the activities of the agricultural sector in the past seven years.
He said the Administration was determined to make noticeable progress in the production of tropical fruits, particularly pineapples, banana, mango, tomato, papaya, and avocado. "It is possible to achieve self-sufficiency and export of tropical fruits in one year", he stated, adding that Government was considering placing a ban on the importation of fruit concentrates, to stimulate local production.
He said the Presidential Initiatives on various agricultural products had been very successful because of the targets, which the Administration had set for the Committees.
The President also strongly advised the All Farmers Association of Nigeria against actions that would stifle the various component associations, "don't suppress the associations that came together to form AFAN".
Earlier, Mallam Adamu Bello had chronicled the achievements of the Administration in agriculture to include four national policies on agriculture, rural development, cooperatives and fertilisers, unprecedented growth in the sector from 2.9% in 1998 to 7% in 2005, Presidential Initiatives on cassava, rice, vegetables, tropical fruits, and many other successes.
He also spoke about the return of UN support to Nigerian agriculture, Nigeria's ranking as first in the world by the FAO in cassava, cow peas, shea-nuts, melon-seed, cocoyam, yams and citrus fruits, and the successful control of the avian flu, which attracted an award from the FAO.

Original_Search_Title: Danladi Bako's Confession
Headline: Nigeria: Danladi Bako's Confession
Body_Text:
Danladi Bako was a popular name in broadcasting in the 90s. He anchored Morning Ride that featured those that mattered in the society at that time on NTA Channel 5, Lagos. He later became the Director-General of the Nigerian Broadcasting Commission, NBC. Now a politician, Bako tells the story of his life to Sunday Vanguard
THE rumour making the rounds ascribes your success in broadcasting to your godfathers. How many of them do you have?
No, I didn't and I don't have any godfather anywhere. I went to school, became outstanding pretty early. I got trained as far back as 1973 as a trainee announcer on NBC, Kaduna and Sokoto, and I had the privilege of doing programmes every weekend. So I got braced up much earlier than most of my other colleagues and by the time I was graduating, I was already a refined product and it was easy for me to win the best producer and director award in just one year after becoming a permanent staff in NTA, Sokoto. So, it was the combination of these that helped me. And when I got to Lagos to do my M.Sc., I had four admissions to do my masters, one in England, two in Nigeria and one in the U.S., I chose to go to UNILAG because I wanted to find out what it was like to work and school in Lagos.
This really boosted my confidence in my class which had students like Dele Alake, Innocent Oparadike, John Araka, Agbaje, Achike Okafor and I did well. There and then, I knew I had something to offer. I also did a nine month course in England in1982, came tops in my class. So, by 1984 when I was beginning to do Second Chance as producer/director, I had all it takes to do the job.
Unlike other people of Hausa extraction, your intonation is different, how come?
I am a trained broadcaster. When you go to a training school and you train properly, you train to speak well and there is basic international standard which you must acquire, especially, if you get to train in BBC, British Council in those days. I remember I read English for my first degree and I came tops in my phonology class and I took it seriously in school because I felt that if you must be a broadcaster, you must sound real good and there are few people who are sounding real quality these days. But some of those people still depend on the normal African-Nigerian English. I do think that is the international standard because if I am going to do something, I do it well.
So what were the highlights of your career in NTA?
15 years in NTA, I produced and directed so many local programmes in Sokoto, like Take A Chance, a quiz programme, directed and produced the news, reported, did so many things, programmes like Behind The Headlines. Then, I got to Lagos, it was Second Chance, there were a lot of documentaries on the Army, the Police, Better Life, I covered most of those things. Then, I continued my creativity, Morning Ride, Masters Sports, Night Train. Then, I contributed to so many productions, most of which I didn't bother to ask for credit because I thought that it was a talent God gave me and I didn't need to be trading it off like a trader.
I gave it to a lot of people free of charge because there were a lot of programmes I made input to. I had incredible interviews with people like the late Fela, MKO Abiola, Yusuf Maitama Sule, Bola Kuforiji, Wole Soyinka, the late Alao- Aka Bashorun, Babagana Kingibe, Adamu Ciroma, virtually everybody that mattered at that time. I remember Fela's interview very well because that was when I was in University of Lagos in1980. The interview was aired on BBC Network Africa and we subsequently became friends. At some point in time before then, I used to cover him at Jazz 38 and on a few occasions at the shrine because I had to do those coverages for some two internationals. But because I could not smoke cigarette, I always had to cough whenever I was around him because there were a lot of smoking going on.
What about MKO Abiola, what was your relationship with him?
MKO was one of the most incredible human beings I have ever met. He was highly intelligent. He knew a lot about the world, so it was easy to learn from him. He was very compassionate, and was a very good muslim. A lot of the times we talked, it was about Africa and Islam. I was easily the only person from the North, the only Hausa from the University of Lagos in my M.Sc. class at that time that was very close to him. MKO wanted to employ people for Concord, he talked to me but I declined, he didn't like the idea that I refused him but he respected my view and we became friends thereafter. But he found me challenging and interesting, eventually I put him on, on Morning Ride, in June 1989 and I remember his late wife, Simbiat, started calling me a super star from that day because she said nobody had convinced MKO to even appear on TV talk less of a live programme. That, for me, was rewarding because I respected Simbiat, I respected the family and MKO tremendously. It was just a shame that he died the way he did.
How did you get to NBC?
I remember that between 1987 and 1994, seven years of doing Morning Ride, I won about 200 awards. I just did my work on TV and people liked me and liked the programme. When you believe in God, you have tremendous faith in the Almighty and do the right thing, things will naturally work for you. There are people who didn't have godfathers and they were governors at the age of 36, 37. So, it is God because there are so many things He has decided about all of us. Whether you have god father or not, whatever He has destined would happen to you, will definitely happen to you.
Some would have said you were too young for such a sensitive position at NBC?
I think that was what some people were saying about El-Rufai until he started demolishing their houses and they realized that it does not have to do with age or size. It has to do with knowing what to do and how to do it. I was not faced with things like that. I didn't appoint myself, God appointed me, so I had a work to do and I just had to do the work.
You are now in politics, why politics?
Very simple. If you have to input into society and affect governance and the future of the country, you have to be in politics. You don't sit down in a bank or a sawmill or a cassava farm or in a cotton farm and expect to influence power. It is only natural that you want to influence who becomes what and make your input in the direction your country will go. That is the reason for joining politics because there are certain offices you cannot occupy except you are a politician. These are the offices that influence the policy and decision of government.
Formulating policies
And some of these policies are badly run and you wonder the people who are there because they are badly conceived and the question you ask is, how did they get to formulate those policies? So, why are we suffering from these problems? Because you that had the intellect, that government spent money to train, you decided not to make an input into how your country is governed. So, don't blame anybody if we cannot make good laws and have good governance because these problems exist because the people who should be in office are not the ones in office. Those ones that were supposed to be there were scared because they think politics is a dirty game.
So are you dirty?
There are clean people playing politics. I want to increase the number of clean people playing politics. As I speak with you now, my experiment in 2003, I believe, has engendered new comers. Today, we are talking about the Adams Oshiomoholes, Patrick Utomis and the rest are coming into politics. So, unless we get a detergent and wash this politics they say is dirty, it will continue to remain dirty. Just the same way we have dirt and dry cleaners, let us be the ones that would dry clean the system.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Advises Farmers On Successor
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Advises Farmers On Successor

Abuja — President Obasanjo has advised Nigerian farmers not to let any administration that will come after his to ignore agriculture because apart from being the largest employer of labour, it would ensure that all the people were fed.
He also disclosed that the Federal Government is considering banning the importation of fruit concentrates in order to encourage the local production of tropical fruits.
