Enugu — Director-General of the Raw Materials Research and Development Council, Prof. Peter Onwualu has decried low investment in manufacturing, especially in agro industrial processing, and said this has taken a toll on food production in the country.
Onwualu who spoke yesterday at a seminar organized by his council on Refocusing Agro-Industrial Raw Materials Processing in the South East Zone in Enugu, said the problem has taken a toll on socio-economic status of the people of the area.
He also said this has led to high levels of unemployment and increase in poverty, with the attendant social problems.
He was of the view that in order to make significant impact in agriculture and revamp the apparent decline in agro-industrial raw materials, the South East zone needs to examine its involvement in agriculture and properly refocus its attention to those crops where it has comparative advantages.
"It also needs to develop processing and production methods and processing technologies for already existing agro-industrial raw materials in the zone", he said.
He added, "some of these include cassava processing to meet the overwhelming demand for cassava flour based on the Federal Government policy on including 10 percent cassava flour composite in bread and other bakery products.
The DG equally stressed the need . for meeting the export demands of cassava chips since according to him, there is currently a growing demand for alternative sources of feed for the millions of livestock in Europe.
Cassava markets in Europe alone, he said, could be estimated at over $17 billion a year, whereas local markets for cassava flour were worth over N150billion.
Onwualu also identified palm fruits processing as another area where the South East needs to reclaim Nigeria's position as world leader in palm produce and marketing.
He disclosed that the Federal Government has given its commitment to the interim Board of Transcorp to address the issue of raw materials processing in the country.
According to him, agriculture remains the backbone of the Nigerian economy, adding that it is the largest source of employment in most economies of the world.
He added, "two thirds or more of the population is depending for its livelihood on farming and related processing techniques. It is a well-known fact that Nigeria especially the South East, processes comparative advantages in the production of certain
foods and other agricultural commodities that can earn foreign exchange for the country".
He maintained that sustained agricultural development requires striking an appropriate balance between investments that are directly productive and investments in infrastructure, stating that the physical isolation of farmers in the rural areas makes
them especially vulnerable to the effects of poor infrastructure provision.
Much of the high productivity of agriculture in the developed countries, he said, was the result of massive investment over many years on infrastructure provision and maintenance.
"Conversly, the low productivity of agriculture in the South East could be taken to reflect among other things limited investments in rural roads, water and electricity.", he said.

Original_Search_Title: Kogi State Varsity Grew Too Fast - VC
Headline: Nigeria: Kogi State Varsity Grew Too Fast - VC
Body_Text:
Lagos — Following the scandalous denial of accreditation to 21 out of Kogi State University (KSU), Anyigba's 30 programmes, last year, former Vice Chancellor of the University of Agriculture, Makurdi and Prof of Agricultural Economics, Francis Idachaba was invited to captain the sinking ship. Bukola Olatunji takes a look at how much of this ship is above water, one year after.
The Kogi State University, Anyigba offers a lesson or two for both the old and new, public and private universities alike. Established by the Governor Abubakar Audu administration in 1999 was a source of pride to 'Kogites' until the National Universities Commission (NUC) came calling, March last year. By the time the report of the accreditation visit was released, not only the 'Kogities' but the generality of Nigerians were shocked that 21 out the 30 programmes run by a university could be denied accreditation, while the remaining nine merely got interim accreditation.
The State Governor and Visitor to the Kogi State University (KSU), Anyigba, Alhaji Ibrahim Idris, then appointed Prof. Francis Idachaba Vice Chancellor to, in the latter's words, "salvage, transform and reposition the university." Idachaba, who had voluntarily retired from the University of Ibadan in March 2003, after 31 years, resumed in KSU on June 24 last year.
Recounting his experience in the last one year to journalists in his office recently, Idachaba said, "I met a university that had suffered a great image problem due to the outcome of accreditation." Both staff and students were demoralised. Asked what went wrong with the institution, the Vice Chancellor said, "What happened was that the university had grown too fast. In six years, it had 30 programmes. According to the master plan, the university was not supposed to have up to 4, 000 students in six years, but as at the time I came, it had 12, 000 - 9, 000 undergraduate and 3, 000 diploma students."
KSU had other problems too. Idachaba said, "Staffing was poor, both in terms of number and quality. The library was weak in terms of books and journals for the various programmes. The physical structures (classrooms and lecture theatres) for academic work (and academic structure) were grossly inadequate."
Added to these, was the governance process, both academic and administrative. The Vice Chancellor explained that the academic governance in the system "bothers on how academic programmes are administered - whether students are admitted, examined and graded on merit? Do you reward members of staff - teaching and non-teaching - on merit? Are the committees of council operating for the internal governance smoothly?
"What we met was different. We met a university that was being run like an arm of the civil service. There were reports that student's admissions were based on money. There were allegations of corruption and bribery that illustrated that the administrative governance had become decadent. And, something needed to be done urgently."
Idachaba said the first thing he and his team did was to engage in intensive consultations in Senate to define the nature of the problems. Details of the accreditation report showed that "all the programmes failed for lack of support from the government. They failed in respect to library facilities, academic staffing and facilities. These problems are being tackled systematically. The facilities are being expanded. Five projects were constructed and completed within 18 weeks at the cost of N195 million. These are the Faculties of Natural Sciences complex, Social Sciences building, as well as that of Arts and Humanities, which has three complexes. About four lecture theatres are also under construction and are soon to be completed.
"We rationalised (our programmes) and some departments were merged. We did a lot of re-programming to decide how many professors, senior lecturers, lecturers I, II, III and graduate assistants we required. And we embarked on massive recruitment of qualified lecturers. I met 123 academic staff, now we have 250. We have acquired books and journals.
"We also reviewed all programmes to know where each was on the normative pyramid. Doing this, we discovered a lot of deficiencies. We then computed our needs, in terms of lecturers, books and facilities. Thus, we translated our physical needs into funding. So, the funding requirements came to N1.5 billion to meet full accreditation requirements", the Vice Chancellor said.
For the next exercise, rather than present all the 21 programmes that were denied accreditation, the authorities, through consultations with the Senate, decided to present 15 and even these in two batches. Of the seven that were presented in November last year, six got full accreditation (a score of 85.7 per cent), while the last one; the Law programme got interim accreditation, due to its library facilities. This result shot KSU to the first position among state and private universities, and third among all the federal, state and private universities put together.
Idachaba further disclosed that, "now, we are ready to represent two of the programmes for accreditation in July. These are Philosophy and Religious Studies, as well as Mathematical Science/Computer. The remaining six, in the Management and Social Sciences Faculties, will be represented in November."
He also assured students currently admitted into the programmes that were denied accreditation that they will not be affected. Only fresh students would not be admitted.
The Kogi State Government has released N700 million out of the requested N1.5 billion. Idachaba disclosed that the N195 million expended on the five new structures came from this. So does the monthly wage bill of N15 million and the acquisition of books and journals for the various programmes. The Vice Chancellor also set up a N100 million Academic Excellence Trust Fund for which, so far, N70 million has been invested in the Nigerian Stock Exchange to generate income in perpetuity for the promotion of academic excellence in Kogi State. He explained that, "The income generated is not for the regular payment of academic staff salaries, but to enable us bring world renowned scholars to the university to give lectures and seminars, to bring expertise." The sum of N5 million has also been allocated for staff development training, with many of the lecturers pursuing their PhDs.
Campus utilities are funded from the university's internally generated revenue (IGR). According to the Vice Chancellor," this is a university community of 12, 000 students, excluding members of staff, without connection to the Power Holding Company of Nigeria (PHCN). We depend entirely on generating sets on which we spend over N4 million monthly to buy diesel, not to talk of lubricants. We have 11 generating sets of different capacities and ages. We have problems maintaining the generators. We rely entirely on two boreholes because there is no public water supply.
The governor, he said, is quite committed to addressing the problem of electricity supply. "He provided funds to the Ministry of Rural Development, which supplied the transformers. But at the end, nothing worked. The current was so low that it could not be used. We have made this known to the Visitor. On April 7, the council met the governor and this issue was part of our discussions. The governor has provided us with a 1000 kva generator. But we are still waiting for the suppliers to install it."
On putting the university's large expanse of land to use, Idachaba said, new rows of palm oil seedlings have been added to the once inherited by the institution from the Anyigba Development Project (ADP). He also plans to encourage the staff to have five or 10 seedlings in every unit. "We have just acquired 3, 000 seedlings for this project. Besides, we plan to develop a group of villagers to promote homestead oil palm production within the state. Later we will move into the production of cassava, maize and citrus."
KSU could not conduct the 2005/2006 post UME test, the Vice Chancellor however disclosed that the school has liaised with JAMB and the NUC to begin with the 2006/07 edition.
He denied the allegation that his administration has refused to implement the monetisation policy. He said, "We are very conscious about the challenge posed by the policy. I will describe as a life threatening issue in the sense that if we do not implement it, we would be unable to attract staff from universities already implementing it. Secondly, we may not be able to retain staff that may want to migrate to other institutions which implement monetisation. But it is wrong to say Kogi State University has refused to implement monetisation. We took the issue to the last council meeting. I have also met with the workers unions and directed them to provide me with data on both federal and state universities on monetisation, so that I could use these to make my proposal to the visitor. We shall do preliminary computation of what monetisation implies for the university. Then we will bring the proposal to the council for approval and then the visitor. This, I have told the unions.
The council has not approved it as is being claimed by some unions."
The inaugural lecture tradition is yet to be instituted at the university. Idachaba said, "I faced a similar challenge when I got to the Federal University of Agriculture, Makurdi as Vice-Chancellor (1988 -1995). I instituted the inaugural lecture tradition, so I intend to do the same thing here." With no research grant system on ground when he assumed duty, the Vice Chancellor said a special grant, to be allocated to academic staff, whose research proposals get the university research committee's nod, is underway. His vision for the KSU is "a university that has stabilised its academic programmes; a university that has made a significant progress in getting its academic programmes accredited; a university in which people have pride and confidence; a university where the students and staff can walk with their heads upright and their shoulders high because they have a university that they could be proud of."
After stability comes growth development. "We don't resume growth on wobbling feet, let us stabilise and resume growth. We need a postgraduate school. But we want to sustain the momentum and overcome, fully, the trauma we had gone through in the university before thinking of setting up the postgraduate school, though we could do it with our present accreditation status", Idachaba concluded.
The first lesson in the KSU experience for new universities is, 'starting small is the way to'. Another is, 'no matter the internal and external forces, stick to the master plan. That is not all; a university is not something that any state government can say, 'every state has it, let's have our own.' If you can't fund it, don't start it. The last, but not the least, and this is a lesson for all tertiary institutions, federal, state or privately owned - retired professors and lecturers are far from being tired as Idachaba has shown. Prof. Gabriel Ogunmola of the Nigerian Academy of Science once told a group of journalists, including this reporter that rather than leave retired professors to languish in penury due to unpaid pensions, "why not reabsorb them." Many of them still have a lot to offer and with greener pastures in other vocations; many universities have not been able to replace these people, who are institutions in their own rights, after they retire from the system .

Original_Search_Title: What New Boss Has for IRAD - Ekona
Headline: Cameroon: What New Boss Has for IRAD - Ekona
Body_Text:
Dr. Joseph Mafeni Mase Nsum, new Chief of the Regional Research Centre, Ekona, has said he has a lot to offer in his new area of operation.Dr. Mafeni was installed as the new Chief of the Regional Research Centre, Ekona, on Friday, June 23.
"I will do a lot for this centre. I have an international reputation and why not chip in other experiences I have gathered in other zones, to make it really Cameroon in miniature," he said after his installation by the General Manager of IRAD, Dr. Simon Zok.
Dr. Mafeni explained that the Agro-ecological Zone 4 is a multi-purpose centre.
"We are talking of plant research. These include; cocoyam, fruit and banana research respectively. We are also talking of animal and fish research respectively," he said.
Mafeni said Ekona Research Centre has done a lot for the betterment of agriculture in the Southwest Province and Cameroon in general.
"Everybody knows that Ekona Research Centre is one of the oldest research centers in the country. Today, it is still has its mark as the leading centre in crop production in the country.
If you go to our markets, you will see the improvements of our products. The type of maize that was produced before is not the type of maize they produce today. We have early variety maturing trees, fruit trees and more. All those results are from this centre.
Njombe is one of the breadbaskets for banana and plantains in the country. All the research we have been doing there, that is what the end-users are using in Njombe. We have been doing a lot," he said.
Dr. Festus Numfor, hitherto Acting Supervisor for the Ekona Research Centre, said, "We have crop varieties which are much more productive than the local varieties. We have cultural practices, which help the farmers to overcome problems of low production, fight against diseases and ensure productivity.
We have a wide range of post-harvest technology, which helps the farmers not to lose their crops after harvest. We have genetic materials, which are available to farmers. We have fruit trees, which are handled through marcotting, budding, which the farmers are certain to harvest from. We have cassava, maize varieties, which are resistant to diseases and yields more," he added.
Dr. Numfor said the Ekona Research Centre is in top shape. "Very well. The government has improved on funding. For the past five years, we have had funding from the African Development Bank, ADB. Our management is insisting that we should produce rather than rely on subvention.
On his part, Dr. Zok said "the centre has a mandate which has been given by the government, which is to carry out research in the domain of animal production, annual crops, perennial crops and more.
He told Dr. Mafeni and his researchers to use the centre and rescue the population from the poverty bracket. "The Ekona Regional Centre has the potential to carry out research to develop technology which can be used to solve problems faced by producers, farmers and those into animal husbandry," he said.
Dr. Mafeni will be in charge of agricultural research for the Agro-ecological Zone 4 (Southwest, Littoral and Maritime part of the South Provinces).

Original_Search_Title: Ineffective Coordination Can Derail Youth Employment Programmes
Headline: Ghana: Ineffective Coordination Can Derail Youth Employment Programmes
Body_Text:
THE GOVERNMENT'S youth employment programme, being implemented nation wide, has been discussed by 30 participants at a forum held here last Wednesday where they expressed fears about the possible failure of the programme because the way it is being implemented was not effectively coordinated.
The forum participants noted that key players on youth issues like the National Youth Council, was totally played out and "New youth employment coordinators" were formed at the District levels to implement the programme, an area which needed experience and tact.
The forum, which was organized by the Institute for Democratic Governance (IDEG) with the theme: Civic Participation in the 2007 District Budget Process, was mainly attended by public officials including District Chief Executives, District Finance Officers, District Coordinators, District Planning Officers, Media Practitioners, Presiding Members and NGOs.
Furthering their views on the issues, they argued that at least the National Youth Council (NYC), which was mandated to help address youth related problems, should have been assigned this duty of youth employment, adding that the Youth Council would be in position to identify the educational backgrounds of the unemployed youth, their interest areas and the type of jobs they would prefer and accordingly implement these needs and aspirations of the youths.
According to the participants, members of the new youth employment are Agriculturists, Health Personnel and National Disaster Management Personnel and the non-involvement of key personalities at the District level like the District Planning Officers, clearly displayed the fact that the country is misplacing its priorities.
Another area of concern to them was the formation of employment task force and the provision of module to work with was described as an attempt to impose on the youth certain jobs they may not be interested in and that becomes ridiculous in a pursuit of a national programme.
Continuing, the participants also noted that attention was being paid to project implementation more than impact assessments and urged policy implementers to equally provide funds for impact assessment of projects since that would determine whether or not projects implemented had any effect on the lives of the people.
On his part, the Hohoe District Chief Executive (DCE), Mr. John Peter Amewu, who was the only DCE who attended the function out of the six DCEs expected, commended IDEG for its educational programmes on governance issues to enlighten local people to fully embrace the local governance system in the country.
He said, the Hohoe District has embraced the youth employment programme and it has identified the District's potentials, which could be exploited to the advantage of the youth.
He said the District has a comparative advantage in tourism and agriculture and disclosed that 26 youths have been fully employed as Tour Guards.
The Hohoe DCE said efforts to solve the youth unemployment problem has culminated in the formation of seven youth farming groups as well as the purchase of a thousand acre land for them to embark on cassava, maize and pineapple cultivation under the AGOA project.
Mr. Amewu however expressed concern about the monies being provided for the youth to engage in agriculture up to ¢3.2 million like the cultivation of pineapple and described it as a token which would not be accounted for because of the simple reason that the monies were meager and would be diverted to other areas than the intended purpose.
He pointed out that much as the country started to address the youth unemployment problem, there was the need to factor in drastic measures that would protect and ensure sustainable environmental growth either through agriculture or tourism.
Contributing, the Project Officer of IDEG, Madam Ruby Quantson, reassured Ghanaians that her outfit would continue to organize programmes that would help build the capacity of the citizenry to show interest in local governance and make meaningful contributions to local governance issues.
She stressed the need for youth employment issues to be integrated in National and District budgets to help address the problem holistically, adding that the type of education that would be given to the youths should be relevant to the development needs of the various Districts and to meet total national development goals.
Madam Quantson noted that the issue of youth employment should involve local people and the youths themselves so that whatever achievement was made, they would be proud of being part of the success story.
She also urged people to make meaningful contribution to the 2007 budget to ensure that their needs were met in the budget preparation.
The IDEG public deliberation is a process through which citizens are empowered to define socio-economic programmes from different viewpoint and to make collective choices for meaningful public action.
It requires citizens to be political actors and to recognize that change will not come to them but through their own collective initiatives.
This time, the dialogue with the public officials, was attended by participants from Jasikan, Hohoe, Kpando, South Tongu and the Ho municipal Assemblies.

Original_Search_Title: Economic Empowerment Projects Promote Women's Rights -- Dubé
Headline: Zimbabwe: Economic Empowerment Projects Promote Women's Rights -- DubÃ©
Body_Text:
Harare — RURAL economic empowerment projects for women have a far-reaching impact in the promotion of women's rights and in shaping the destiny of their lives, Canadian Ambassador to Zimbabwe Ms Roxanne Dubé said last Thursday.
"I cannot think of any other right for women than the right to earn money and the right to earn a living on their own," she said commissioning a $13,4 billion economic empowerment project for women at Nyamahumba Primary School in the Nyanga North District of Manicaland Province.
A total of 353 women are benefiting directly from diverse income generating projects which include poultry, piggery, orchards, vegetable gardening and herbal tree gardens.
The Canadian International Development Agency (CIDA), through its Gender Equality Support Programme, contributed $8,8 billion while Edit Trust, which implements the programmes contributed the balance of $4,6 billion.
Apart from engaging in economic projects, the women also took part in gender training and awareness campaigns to promote gender equality.
In addition to this, three girl child clubs were initiated in the Nyamahumba and the Ruwangwe areas in Nyanga North to create a platform for girls to learn more about their rights and to contribute towards the upbringing of empowered women.
"I think one of the most important ways to help promote equal rights for women is to give them the means to be economically independent. Being able to earn your own money, more than anything, gives you the tools to make your own decisions on a variety of fronts," she said.
Ambassador Dubé also took a chance to tour the Ruwangwe Project with the Minister of Women's Affairs, Gender and Community Development Cde Oppah Muchinguri who also commissioned a chicken and cassava project at Ruwangwe.
The ministry donated more than 250 chicks to the Ruwangwe women's project, which has also started the greenhouse propagation of cassava.
Cde Muchinguri said her ministry had identified markets for goats in the Middle East and wanted rural community projects to take advantage of this coming opportunity.
"There is a market for goats in the Middle East. We are now working to establish an abattoir for goats," she said. "We have to work together with Arex and other important stakeholders to set up points for goat production in rural areas."
"We want to end the dependency syndrome and empower our communities to enhance their food security as well as fighting the HIV and Aids pandemic."
In a speech read on his behalf, Manicaland Provincial Governor and Resident Minister Cde Tinaye Chigudu said the Government was ready and willing to work with non-governmental organisation whose objectives were clear and skewed towards the less privileged groups and in uplifting the standards of living of the rural people.
"These kinds of projects are commendable because they sustain the lives of our people as opposed to food handouts," he said.
"I have no doubt that the standard of living of our people in this part of Nyanga will certainly improve because women are business-minded, they always want to see things succeed.
"Failure to them is not an option."

Original_Search_Title: Women Caught in the Soccer Passion
Headline: Ghana: Women Caught in the Soccer Passion
Body_Text:
Accra — It was a beautiful sight in the early hours of Thursday June 21st when Ghana was scheduled to play the United States at 2 pm in the ongoing World Cup championships in Germany. Along one of the dusty roads that lead to a Gbawe suburb in Accra, a Kooko (Porridge) seller had wrapped her huge pot containing the hot porridge in national colours shouting and waving at every car that passed by. Her enthusiasm drew many customers, including males to the little shed that houses the porridge as they responded in the Ga language 'ebaa fee gbeye, America baa kotoshie' literally meaning America will bow as Ghana perform wonders.
Elsewhere in East Legon in Accra at the office of the Institute of Democratic Government (IDEG), a pretty lady said to be a civil society activist and attending a meeting with European Journalist on tour of Ghana to discuss poverty and growth wore a beautiful necklace in national colours with a small football as a pendant.
In addition to that she wrapped a national flag around her pony tail hair style. As she entered the IDEG conference room, the men rose up to receive her all shouting in ecstasy. In front of the World Bank offices at Ridge, Public Agenda spotted some young women in Areeba hats all over Accra.
Since the beginning of the World Cup this June, women have been caught in the frenzy. Although men patronize matches in stadiums than women, women have shown they are better supporters of the national team than men. Even during the unfortunate May 9 stadium disaster women were not left out in the casualties. But for the numerous household chores and other responsibilities, women would have played an even greater role in playing and supporting football in this country.
No wonder the women's national team, the Black Queens qualified for the World cup before their male counterparts. Some observers have even argued that women have done a lot of the singing and dancing at the ongoing tournament than men.
In fact more women were at the road side or beside their male counterparts on the streets, in cars and on roof tops when Ghana won her first match against the Czech Republic.
But what amazed this reporter was the mood of women in Accra Central last Thursday. Since this reporter's heart had often been broken by football results, she decided to take a stroll to Accra Central during the match to observe the stress levels of market women as the match progressed.
At the entrance to the Kwasea Dwaso opposite the Electricity Company of Ghana Offices, a female porter around 17 years was really dressed up. Her dress, headgear, necklace and earrings were all in national colours, while carrying her load.
Some of the market women were in a prayer mood. "Yesu nawo mobo eei," literally meaning "Jesus have mercy on us".
Others were in joyous mood as they blew whistles clad from hair to toe in national colours and sweating all over.
Radios were dotted everywhere, there were the more literate market women who were listening in English commentaries and passing on information to their stressed out colleagues.
Sometimes goals were announced ahead of time and the jubilations were short lived.
However, it appeared majority were listening to Akan commentaries. With ears close to the radio set almost all the listeners announced the first goal simultaneously and the market was thrown into frenzy. A tuna seller stripped off her blouse revealing a beautiful midriff and heavy busts as her contribution to the efforts at goal scoring.
What amazed this reporter was the level of commitment of the women to the soccer fiesta, their familiarity with the players and the rules of the game. ; It appeared every body at the market had a stake in the march. The weak hearted only waited for good news as their faces were bowed on their tables. "My head is aching, one woman confided in Public Agenda as she pulled up her trouser which was down her hips as a result of excessive jumping after the first goal. There were flags, hats wrist bands, necklaces, earrings available for sale and almost every trader and their porters had put on one of these. One would think it was a requirement for doing business at the market.
The market which is sharply divided along political lines prior to elections was this time united and so strong was the unity that it was beyond comprehension. "The boys playing the soccer on the field in Germany are our children, they deserve our prayers and our support," a cassava dough seller told Public Agenda.
As soon as the Americans equalized, the market women started praying and trusting God. Before long the listeners announced their own goal against the background of loud noise, no wonder they came back to correct the misinformation.
As the real second goal came, the market was thrown into frenzy again with loud music from Sidney's hit song "Obiara nye obiara," Grace Ashy's Black Stars song among other praises to God. Others took to dancing with the movement of body curves -wriggling of the waist, bust and sometimes showing off vital parts of the body.
At the end of the match the young porters and their male counterparts all took to the streets with some of the women following them.
As Public Agenda left the market, several women were spotted along the streets of Asylum down beside men and singing and dancing in a festive mood. At Asylum Down many young ladies were seen dancing at Semi's Place, a restaurant and bar to crown it all. Who says women do not enjoy soccer? The gender desk of this paper wishes the women of Ghana a stress free watching of tomorrow's match against Brazil. With the support of all Ghanaian women, the sky is the limit.

Original_Search_Title: How to Beat Suruma's Budget
Headline: Uganda: How to Beat Suruma's Budget
Body_Text:
Kampala — The 10% tax on mineral water has left Uganda's urban elite scandalised. Although it has been with us for less than a decade, many Ugandans today cannot imagine life without a mineral water.
But let us admit it, the current mineral water bottle-toting craze has more to do with creating an impression than minding our health. It is a status symbol.
Aggressive marketing has led gullible Ugandans to believe mineral water is healthier than home-boiled and filtered water, and that certain brands are superior to others. In the spirit of brand loyalty, coupled by the need to look cool, Ugandans are guzzling away their hard-earned cash, even donating some to the Treasury.
Yet for less than sh1,000, which is the average price of a litre of bottled mineral water, it is possible to buy, boil, filter and pack 20 litres of safe drinking water, which is the weekly requirement for the average human being.
To avoid Suruma's 10% tax on mineral water, just invest sh3,000 in a durable water bottle, which you have to faithfully recharge before leaving home. That is what people who are mindful of both their pockets and health do.
Use public transport
It is the dream of every Ugandan to own a car. Thanks to Suruma's budget, that dream is quickly turning into a nightmare. Although the budget did not touch fuel taxes, which were already too high, it raised the fee for road licences by 5% and also imposed an environment tax on imported second-hand cars that are more than eight years old.
These, plus the poor and congested roads, are determined to turn life into living hell for Uganda's 'my car' class.
Public transport is the way to go. I know. In Ugandan society, ditching your car for a taxi is one way of informing the entire world that you are in financial dire straits.
Besides, with the current state of our public transport system, downgrading from a 'my car' to a taxi might seem like moving from a black and white to a coloured nightmare. However, with Kampala Mayor Nasser Sebaggala's Pioneer Easy Buses on the way, the situation is bound to improve.
If they are to survive Suruma's budget, Ugandans have to stop looking down on public transport as a preserve for the down and out. Instead they should view it as a godsend that will deliver them from the high cost of owning a personal car.
For instance, it costs at least sh5,000 to travel from Bweyogerere to the city centre by private means, while by public means it is sh1,000 at most.
On top of that, the 'my car' class also have to worry about parking and thieves, on top of maintenance and repair costs.
In case you don't live very far from your work place, get into the habit of walking. It is a pleasurable and rewarding experience and it helps you stay fit.
Grow your own crops
Sounds extreme, but then so is the budget. Ugandans are not total strangers to the idea of growing their own crops, to make ends meet.
In the 1970s, at the height of Idi Amin's economic war, many residents of Kololo, Nakasero and other posh parts of the city were forced to convert their huge compounds into banana, cassava and potato gardens.
Unable to support their families on the miserable monthly paycheque, many civil servants and professionals like doctors and lecturers planted crops in the sprawling compounds they had inherited from the British colonial administrators.
In the aftermath of Suruma's budget, green-fingered Ugandans, especially urbanites, might have to revisit the idea of growing their own crops, especially fruits and vegetables which don't take up much space. It is amazing the amount of fresh vegetables you can harvest from a box garden set up on the balcony of your flat.
What starts as a family hobby can easily end up becoming a side business, supplying fruits vegetables to your neighbourhood.
Invest
With the formal sector being taxed left right and centre, it makes sense to invest your hard-earned savings in the informal sector, which the taxman is yet to lasso in.
Home-based enterprises like tailoring, agro-processing, food-processing, market gardening, and craft-making will give you the luxury of working from home, while dodging unnecessary expenses like rent and transport, plus the various taxes that are attracted by a factory-based enterprise.
Go off grid
With both power and piped water tariffs getting hiked every other day, it makes sense to gradually start investing in alternatives that will eventually wean you away from Umeme and National Water and Sewerage Corporation.
Solar, wind, Liquid Petroleum Gas (LPG) and biogas for power and rain and underground springs for water are some of the alternatives. Although Suruma's budget devoted sh100b to the power sector, it will take a while before ordinary Ugandans start enjoying the fruits of this remedial effort.
Instead, Ugandans should take full advantage of the Government's decision to scrap taxes on LPG and solar products, to demystify the two energy sources, which have so far been a preserve of the urban elite.
Strange but true, solar and gas are actually cheaper than charcoal and firewood. It the high cost of installation and poor understanding of the technology involved, that have scared away most people.
For the village folks who can't afford LPG, biogas generated from animal and human manure is the alternative. Every pit latrine, every zero grazing unit is a potential biogas plant!
Localised power generation using, hydro, thermal, biomass, and diesel generators are another viable option. Instead of each home, shop or salon acquiring a tiny petrol generator, several individuals can pool resources and buy a big generator that can benefit from the tax waiver on fuel for generators of 100KVA and above.
Instead of buying Jeep Cherokees for their leaders, districts and towns should invest in industrial generators and other alternative power sources.
The same goes for landlords of commercial buildings and housing estates.
Shelter
With the construction industry in Suruma's crosshairs, living in a rented house is going to be an extremely expensive venture. The sh500 imposed on every bag of cement affects both tenants and private homeowners.
But you are definitely better off incurring the cement tax as a homeowner, since the building is yours for keeps.
It is also time to revive the cementless building technology, which was imported into the country from South Africa a few years ago, but died out due to poor promotion.
Payphones
Do you really have to make all those phone calls? Send a text message. It will deliver a clear and written message. If the person you are calling can understand English, please use it. Using English will enable you say more in a shorter time, and hence you will be saving on the money and time.
The story goes that one day US President Bill Clinton and President Yoweri Museveni were allotted time to speak. The latter doubled the former's duration at the podium. But when the speech transcripts were printed, Clinton's doubled Museveni's.
Use English for business transactions over the phone. And at home do not give out your telephone code to the children.
The telephone is a way of spending free time for many school-going children during holidays.
Capital markets
The best way of saving is investing. In Uganda, however, making an investment decision is usually difficult for most people. If you are affluent, here's a tip for you: buy shares listed on the stock exchange. Why? The Finance Minister announced a 5% reduction (from 15% to 10%) in withholding tax for companies with shares on the Uganda Securities Exchange. If you can't invest in the stock exchange invest all the same.
Spend money on things that can produce money. Don't simply consume.
Ignore the Joneses
Owning a more advanced mobile phone, driving a more powerful car, taking your children to an exclusive school, building a cathedral-sized house in a posh neighbourhood, with a commanding view to appear successful is a cultural dead weight Ugandans have to throw overboard to save their financial ship from sinking.
As Suruma's budget takes effect, you are bound to come across many dream houses abandoned halfway, four-wheel drive monsters parked on blocks, and children switched from International to UPE schools. The fault, dear Ugandans, is not with Suruma's budget, but in our lack of creativity, that we are financial underlings.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Sacks Agabi, Macebuh, 10 Others
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Sacks Agabi, Macebuh, 10 Others
Body_Text:
Abuja — SPECIAL Adviser to the President on Ethics, Chief Kanu Agabi (SAN) and wife of Chairman, Board of Trustees (BoT) of Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), Mrs. Josephine Anenih were among 12 top aides sacked yesterday by President Olusegun Obasanjo.
Mrs. Anenih was the Special Adviser on Women Affairs to the President. Special Adviser on Policy and Programme Monitoring, Dr. Stanley Macebuh and Special Adviser on Civil Society, Alhaji Mohammed Abba Gana made up the four presidential aides also dropped.
The sack which came 24 hours after the President effected a cabinet reshuffle also affected eight senior aides of Vice President Atiku Abubakar
Two new ministers; Chairman, Peugeot Automobile Nigeria (PAN) Dr Aliyu Modibbo and Prof. (Mrs) Lesley Obiorah, from Imo State who is a professor of law and until her appointment was a visiting lecturer at the University of Connecticut United States of America, were sworn-in by Chief Obasanjo.
But other aides sacked in a memo signed by the Chief of Staff to the President, Major General Mohammed Abdullahi (rtd).
As two Special Advisers to the Vice President and the six remaining Special Assistants. The Special Advisers are: Professor Mike Kwanashe, SA on Social and Economic Affairs and Dr. Okolie SA on Policy and Programme Monitoring. The Special Assistants are: Mr. Phil Agbasi, Yima Sen, Nath Yadima, Dr. Ardo, Dr. Ajuji and Mrs. Adeola.
Sources in the Presidency revealed however that the President's desire to embark on what is undoubtedly the last of his planned reorganisation and down sizing within his office and that of the Vice President has been long waited, adding that he has personally informed Vice President Atiku Abubakar of this latest development without naming the affected officials before the latter jetted out last week to the United States for his annual vacation.
Administering the oath of office on the ministers, the President charged them to perform above board to see their appointment as a call to serve the people of Nigeria. The president had Wednesday administer oath of office to five new ministers after which he reassigned some of the old cabinet members to new portfolios.
While Dr Modibbo was appointed as Minister of Commerce, Prof. Obiorah will serve as the Minister of Solid Minerals.
Addressing them during the brief swearing in ceremony held at the council chambers of the state House, President Obasanjo directed the new Commerce Minister to continue as Chairman of PAN; and to promote for export the mass production of cassava, cocoa, and cotton.
While advising the new Minister of Solid minerals to work closely with her predecessor Oby Ezekwesili, moved Wednesday to the ministry of Education, he challenged the new Ministers to see their call to serve as a special privilege. According to him "If out of a total of 150 million people any of us are invited to serve as minister we must see it as an honour and you must see it as a challenge because the work ahead of us are enormous.
President Obasanjo also tasked the new Minister of Solid minerals to collaborate with the National Assembly to pass all the bills relating to the operation of the Ministry in the area of mining.
Dr Modibbo who holds PhD in Education had in 2003 served briefly as Minister of State Power and Steel.

Original_Search_Title: Reforms: Finance Sacks 476
Headline: Nigeria: Reforms: Finance Sacks 476
Body_Text:
Abuja — Ministry of Finance said yesterday that a total of 476 out of its 1,500 staff on salary grade levels 1-17 would be thrown into the labour market in the on-going Federal Civil Service Reform Programme. Already, the ministry said, 292 junior staff cutting across different cadres have been served letters of disengagement from service.
Deputy Director, Media and Public Relation Unit of the Ministry, Mr. Samuel Ajayi, who made this known yesterday in Abuja, said those in the junior cadre included "mostly messengers, watchmen, cleaners, domestic staff who are no longer needed as a result of government policy on monetization as well as some staff who did not posses the qualification and skills necessary for the performance of their duties."
According to him, "in all, a total of 476 staff on different grade levels of the ministry and the Budget Office may be affected in the exercise. The ministry including the Budget Office currently has about 1,500 staff."
Ajayi however said to make them useful to their families and the society after service to their fatherland, the ministry was organizing a two-week training workshop starting next Monday, June 26, "to equip them with the necessary skills and re-orientation that will prepare them for the challenges of post-service life."
Retirement benefits of the affected staff, according to him will be handed over to them at a ceremony marking the end of their disengagement workshop slated on Friday, July 7, this year.
He disclosed that , "during the proposed workshop, the staff will be trained in areas such as poultry, grass cutter farming, fast food franchising as well production of packaged water, liquid soap, battery water, soaps, fruit juices, wine, cassava pellet and cassava flour, amongst others.
The workshop, he added, will also feature "presentations on topics such as understanding money, retirement planning, business development tools for successful entrepreneuring, business plan presentation and personal financial planning.

Original_Search_Title: Mapendo Milling Wins WFP Award
Headline: Zambia: Mapendo Milling Wins WFP Award
Body_Text:
Kawambwa — MAPENDO Milling Co-operative in Kala Refugee Camp in Luapula Province has won the World Food Programme's Catherine Bertini award for promoting gender equality.
WFP deputy country director Peter Rodrigues presented certificates of award to 24 members of the co-operative on Tuesday during the World Refugee Day celebrations at Kala Refugee Camp.
Rodrigues said the co-operative received the WFP annual global award because of its effective partnership between men and women to advance gender equality.
He expressed optimism that there would be peace in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) so that refugees could return home. Mapendo co-operative leader Kipampe Kasokota said it was gratifying to win the award out of the seven nominees across the world. "We feel very happy to win, we will continue with our work," Kasokota said.
She said the co-operative grind cassava, sorghum, maize and soybeans to generate income for its members. Kasokota said the co-operative had 24 members, five of whom were men.
The Catherine Bertini award is meant to celebrate the extraordinary achievements of women worldwide and to recognise the important work carried out for the advancement of women in WFP initiatives.
According to WFP, the nominees for the award were carefully considered based on the successful implementation of projects that enabled men and women to collaborate on issues central to their daily lives such as HIV/AIDS, food security, gender based violence and control of assets . And United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) Kawambwa sub office head Tamba Komba observed that refugees' perseverance inspired all organisations and people that work on their affairs.
"We, at the office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees draw great inspiration from their tenacity and courage," Komba said. "We are often asked how we can face the grim reality of our work, year after year, without feeling discouraged. And our answer is simple: if the refugees themselves don't give up hope, how can we?" Komba said everyone could play a role to give hope to refugees.

Original_Search_Title: Agricultural Revolution in Imo State
Headline: Nigeria: Agricultural Revolution in Imo State
Body_Text:
AGRICULTURE is number one in the Tripod Vision of Governor Achike Udenwa's government second term agenda and as he puts it, this is because it has a lot of potential of creating wealth and employment for the people of Imo State. The belief is that any policy on agriculture that works will uplift the standard of living of the people.
Under this tripod programme in agriculture, the government is giving priority to three main crop areas, namely rice, cassava and maize. In rice production for instance, nobody ever thought that rice can be produced in commercial quantity in Imo State. To achieve this Udenwa's government spent over five million naira in 2005 to buy rice seedlings which were distributed to rice farmer at a heavily subsidized rate to ensure that Imo people grow their own rice.
In 2006, the government decided to carry the rice production program beyond the traditional rice producing local governments by identifying 10 new local government areas that have the potential for commercial production of paddy rice.
The government of Imo State established two hectares of rice demonstration farms in each of these local government areas. Farmers were given N50,000 each to establish rice farms and agricultural officers were sent to teach each farmer how to cultivate rice upland and low land rice. This made paddy rice production in Imo State to increase to 15,000 metric tones and it is expected that 50,000 metric tones will be produced this year.
The expectation to produce 50,000 metric tones this year is based on the fact that demonstration done in 2005 will now amount to more cultivation in 2006. The people trained in the demonstration farm will now start cultivating on their own and will now train another set of rice farmers.
This year, the government of Imo State plans to procure more rice seedling, agro-chemical. and others at a cost of N16 million to produce 50,000 metric tones of paddy rice in the state The government has established rice mills together with destoners and pulishers and have decided to partner with the private sector to put the rice mills into commercial production all year round. They also plan to approach some international agencies as well as multinationals to be partner with it on the commercial development of Greater Owerri Regional Rice Scheme as well as the Oguta Rice Scheme because, according to the former Commissioner for Agriculture and Natural Resources, Barrister Soronnadi Njoku, they have surveyed the rice potentials of Imo State and discovered that there are three areas that have more potentials of rice cultivation. One of them is the Imo River Valley of Ihite Uboma Ideato North Local Government Area, the second one is greater Owerri rice project at Ngor Okpalla, Iheagwa, Okolochi and Oguta Lake areas.
Another crop is cassava, which is a crop that does very well in all parts of Imo State, but it was discovered that if nothing is done about it, farmers will not cultivate it at a commercial quantity. It is not a crop that needs demonstration farming, what the government did was to increase its yield per hectare of land by introducing the high yield species. That is if you have a portion of land that produces one tone of cassava with a particular species and you now make use of the high yielding species, it will now produce more tones of cassava.
The high yielding species were got from International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) and distributed initially at subsidised rate and later free of charge to farmers. The College of Agriculture and Technology, Umuagwo is now in partnership with IITA in the cassava stems production and distribution programme.
For this year, the government has stocked the high yielding cassava stems which will be distributed to the farmers of Imo State to ensure they increase their yields. It was discovered that if farmers are encouraged to increase their yield and there is no outlet for it, there will be glut in the market. So the government decided to establish four cassava processing centres one in each of the senatorial zones and one at the premises of Michael Okpara College of Agriculture and Technology, Umuagwo. Each cassava processing centre has fully mechanised facilities to process 30 tonnes of fresh casssava tubers every day into garri, starch, pillet, chips, flour and so on.A farmer can come with his own fresh tubers and it will be processed into any end product he wants.
The cassava processing centre at Owerri zone has been completed. The state government is also working with NDDC in the 1500 hectare cassava development programme. Already, the Managing Director of NDDC recently toured the facilities at the cassava development areas of the state.
The palm produce which was the major product of the former Eastern Region, recently became history but Udenwa's government has vowed to resuscitate it. If you are visiting Imo State for the first time, you will think that the state is one palm plantation, because of the palm trees you see everywhere. But almost all these palm trees are old and are no more yielding enough palm fruits. So the state government led by Chief Achike Udenwa decided that the best thing to do is to cut these old palm trees down and replace them with high yielding ones. That is every where a palm tree is cut down, a new one should be planted to replace it. This was named Palm To Palm Project.
The government discovered that if the owners are not encouraged by helping them to fell the trees, they may not do it. So they decided to fell the palm trees free for the people and encourage them to also come to the Ministry and buy the high breed palm seedlings at subsidised rate which are available in many varieties.
The government plan according to them is to plant more than one million seedlings. As of today, more than 750,000 palm seedlings have already been planted. At times they are distributed through the local governments and schools.
As the government is encouraging cultivation, it is also helping in providing machinery for easy processing of the palm products. At the moment ten mechanized processing centres have been established. A farmer comes to the processing centre with a lorry or trailer load of fresh palm fruits and after processing he goes home with palm oil, and other end products. The state government plans to establish one in every state constituency. Before the end of this year, 27 fully mechanised oil mills will be established.
The government has gone further by giving employment to more than 600 school leavers and 270 graduates in agriculture. More than 37 veterinary staff have also been employed and posted to local governments.
Fish finger lines are also being made available to fish farmers and other people are also being encouraged to go into fish farming. The state government is planning to launch the home based fish farming programme in Imo State.
More than 60 graduates are employed in the youth and graduate employment scheme at Achara Ubo, in Emekuku, Owerri North.
What is on ground now shows that Imo State, with the imputes by Udenwa's government will be the leader in Agriculture. The only problem is that the publicity is not enough for more people to know what the government is doing, so that they can participate in it.
People are suggesting that the state government should revive the state owned newspaper fully, so that people can know more about what the government is doing and participate in it.
•Mr. Ibegwam is a staff of Vanguard Newspapers.

Original_Search_Title: Country Benefits From New Rice Specimen
Headline: Liberia: Country Benefits From New Rice Specimen
Body_Text:
The Africa Rice Center, formally the West African Rice Development Agency (WARDA), has presented a consignment of 1.5 tons of the New Rice for Africa (NERICA) to Liberia.
Addressing a news conference at the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) warehouse in the Freeport of Monrovia last Monday, FAO Country Representative, Dr. Winfred Hammond, said that the NERICA, a seed rice would be treated at the Central Agriculture Research Institute (CARI) in Suakoko, Bong County.
He said at CARI, the varieties would be planted and multiplied for distribution to local farmers. He disclosed that experts at CARI would be trained and in return they would train farmers as to how the NERICA can be planted.
Dr. Hammond said in six months, the NERICA would be taken to six counties and subsequently sent to the entire country.
The FAO Country Representative urged the youths of Liberia to take advantage of the training exercise and play a role in the development of a seed company.
He said as a follow-up to the NERICA, Liberia would receive materials for cassava and more vegetables to better empower farmers to produce more food. " In three years, Liberia shall be self sufficient in the production of seed," Dr. Hammond stated.
Also speaking at the news conference, Deputy Agriculture Minister, Mr. James Logan lauded the Africa Rice Center (WARDA) for the donation.
He said that the donation followed discussions with a team from WARDA, saying, " this donation is an indication that we are on the road to recovery".
Minister Logan recounted the difficulties government faced recently in trying to procure seeds for farmers and noted that the donation is one in the right direction.
He re-echoed the call for young people to take advantage of the development of a seed company for Liberia.
NERICA is a technology from Africa that is referred to as the "seed of hope. It is perfectly adapted to the harsh growing environment and the low-input conditions of upland rice ecologies in sub-sahara Africa, where smallholder farmers lack the means to irrigate and apply chemical fertilizers or pesticides. According to experts, it responds even better to higher inputs.

Original_Search_Title: 'Monkeys Hindering UPE Progress On Islands'
Headline: Uganda: 'Monkeys Hindering UPE Progress On Islands'
Body_Text:
Mukono — THE LC5Chairman, Mr Francis Lukooya, has expressed concern over the increasing rate at which pupils on Buvuma and Koome islands drop out of school.
Addressing Buvuma residents at Nairambi sub-county headquarters on Buvuma Islands on Thursday, Lukooya said the presence of monkeys in the area had affected the progress of Universal Primary Education.
"Instead of leaving children to go to school, parents send their children to gardens to guard potatoes, cassava, beans and maize against monkeys which destroy them," he said.
"Monkeys have become a menace to the crops in the area."
Lukooya said the district would train game rangers and buy guns which would be used to kill monkeys in forests on the Islands.
Last year, about 1,000 monkeys were killed in Koome sub-county.
The sub-county authorities put a bounty of Shs2,000 for each monkey's tail.
Lukooya said the district had set aside more than Shs10 million to develop the Islands this financial year.
He said part of the money would be used to fight monkeys in the area.
"I respect the need to protect our wild animals most especially at this crucial time when they would earn us revenue from the Commonwealth officials who would like to have a look at some of them," Lukooya said.
"But we can not preserve them at the expense of education."

Original_Search_Title: Brother Attributes Chiluba's Illness to Prosecution Pressure
Headline: Zambia: Brother Attributes Chiluba's Illness to Prosecution Pressure
Body_Text:
Mwense — FORMER president Fredrick Chiluba's brother Jim Nkonde has blamed the illness of his sibling on prosecution pressure.
In an interview at Chiluba's Kaombe Village in Mwense district yesterday, Nkonde who spoke in Bemba said he was always worried about his brother's prolonged illness and treatment in South Africa.
"I am worried about his health. Even when I am eating, I am still worried about him," Nkonde said. "Icikonko chalemwikata sana echo alelwalila, chilaleta ukutontonkanya."
He complained that the government had troubled Chiluba for a long time, hence his illness. Nkonde, 66, said Chiluba's illness had caused anxiety among the villagers.
"We are worried about our young brother's suffering in health and in life in general," Nkonde said. "Umwaiche (the young man) is ill because of pressure. Prosecution has taken many years."
Chiluba is facing corruption charges for offences he is alleged to have committed whilst in office. And Nkonde thanked the government for flying Chiluba to South Africa for medical attention.
"People are happy that President Mwanawasa took Chiluba to hospital," he said. However, Nkonde said it was sad that President Mwanawasa had caused a lot of problems on Chiluba despite the latter having given him the presidency on a silver plate.
Nkonde said when Chiluba was president, he used to help him and other family members in the village but he stopped after leaving State House. "Chiluba used to help us but now he doesn't. They (government) closed his doors of income so we are suffering a lot," Nkonde said. "But I have some cassava."
He wondered when Chiluba would come back. Nkonde also said when he heard rumours that Chiluba had died, he started crying. "I cried when I heard that message. I was happy to hear that he was still alive though still suffering," he said.
Nkonde observed that people in the area could vote for President Mwanawasa though he had done little for the people. "People here are still supporting the MMD government. But I haven't seen what President Mwanawasa has done for us," Nkonde said. "People here want wells, we draw water from a very far away place."
He said Chiluba wanted to develop Luapula Province but members of parliament let him down because they did not deliver his development goals.
Asked why he had a different surname from Chiluba, Nkonde said they took their surnames from their different fathers. Nkonde's house is situated just metres away from FJT Chiluba School.

Original_Search_Title: Police Recover Six Guns in Arua
Headline: Uganda: Police Recover Six Guns in Arua
Body_Text:
POLICE in Arua recovered six automatic riffles and 227 bullets from rogues in the district between February and June this year.
Mr Charles Bernard Lamony, the district CID officer, said at the weekend that detectives at Odramacaku border post with Congo, on a tip off by a farmer, recovered an SMG number 52396 in a cassava field in Mbaka village at midday on Wednesday.
"We are investigating where this gun came from. A gun in the hands of an unauthorized person is a threat because it can be used for dangerous or fatalistic purposes," he said.
Lamony said an AK-47 stolen from a Police Constable at Kuluva outpost over a month ago was recovered on May 28.
The suspect, Mr Milton Nyakuni, has been remanded to Arua Central government prisons on charges of terrorism.
Three other sub-machine guns buried in local communities were unearthed by the police on April 16, March 22 and 18.
In February, Staff Sergeant Ben Arubaku, a UPDF veteran and his accomplice Banduni were netted in Koboko with a SAR (semi-automatic riffles) believed to have been used in a cash robbery along the Sudan highway.
"In all these recoveries," said Lemony, "The police and other security agencies have enjoyed the great cooperation of members of the public".
"Any body possessing an illegal gun should either deposit it with the police or drop it anywhere security operatives can find it if that individual cannot take it to LCs. Those who fail to surrender illegal arms will be dealt with accordingly".
The latest police crime bulletin shows a steady rise in aggravated robbery in and around urban centres in the district.
Security forces believe the loose small arms are being smuggled into the country across the porous border from the restive eastern Democratic Republic of Congo and the loosely controlled South Sudan.
Security officials fear that such gun could be used to fan rebellion.

Original_Search_Title: Shell Donates N8m to Isoko Women Cooperatives
Headline: Nigeria: Shell Donates N8m to Isoko Women Cooperatives
Body_Text:
Isoko — IN FULFILMENT of the Shell Petroleum Development Company (SPDC) interest to promote economic growth and support the development of committed micro-entrepreneurs in her host communities, the company, weekend, donated over N8 million to women co-operatives in Isoko North and South local government areas of Delta State.
Making the presentation on behalf of SPDC, the corporate community relations manager, Mr. O.A.Soremekun stated that the micro-credit loan fund to the women already trained in basic skill acquisition in small business management is to aid the women groups to make their businesses in farming, trading, fishing, cassava processing and other small scale enterprises grow.
According to him: "We are seeing a very positive process in which SPDC and your communities have worked very effectively together to promote the emergence and growth of small scale enterprises that will provide the route for capacity building and job creation for women groups in both Otor-Owhe and Olomoro communities.
He continued: "We see this loan disbursement as a good example of our sustainable approach to community development with emphasis on the full participation of the benefiting communities in project identification, planning, implementation, monitoring and supervision. It is our hope that this new approach will achieve our mutual expectations and strengthen the existing co- operation between the communities and us," Mr. Soremekun stated.
Twelve women Multi-purpose Co-operative Societies benefited from the scheme at Olomoro while five benefited from Otor-Owhe respectively.
SPDC therefore, called on the community elders, youths, local and state government agencies to always create an enabling environment for business to strive.
Responding on behalf of the beneficiaries, Sir Simon Efenudu, a former SSG and Head of Service in the state thanked SPDC for making the credit facility a reality despite the hard times in which the company currently operates and assured SPDC of their continued support to ensure a smooth working environment.

Original_Search_Title: Mwenezi Families to Produce Jatropha Commercially
Headline: Zimbabwe: Mwenezi Families to Produce Jatropha Commercially
Body_Text:
Harare — SCORES of families in the drought-prone Mwenezi District are set to venture into commercial production of jatropha after the Government donated seed to cover 100 hectares.
The donation was made through the Ministry of Women's Affairs, Gender and Community Development last Thursday.
The Minister of Women's Affairs, Gender and Community Development Cde Oppah Muchinguri handed over the jatropha seeds to Mwenezi women under a programme aimed at empowering rural communities.
Cde Muchinguri also donated 150 goats, 150 chicks and another 150 guinea fowls to over 500 women at Neshuro Growth Point on Wednesday as part of Government efforts to empower women.
The goat and guinea fowl project was done in conjunction with a non-governmental organisation, Plan International Zimbabwe.
She also handed over 3 000 cuttings of cassava to seven traditional chiefs in Mwenezi for production under the Zunde Ramambo concept.
The minister said if fully exploited, the projects would go a long way in empowering women, especially those in rural communities who have been wallowing in poverty owing to lack of opportunities.
"The main aim of these projects is to make sure that women are empowered and these projects are community based and their main target is to empower rural women and will be expanded to cover the whole country as part of Government efforts to make women economically empowered," said Cde Muchinguri.
She said Mwenezi women would use jatropha to make soap and other commodities from the multi-purpose plant
Cde Muchinguri said women in Mwenezi could also start commercial production of flour from cassava.
Masvingo Provincial Governor Cde Willard Chiwewe said there was need for a concerted effort to embark on massive production of cassava and jatropha for the development of Masvingo as part of efforts to ensure food security and poverty reduction.
Cde Chiwewe also highlighted the fact that empowerment of women was crucial in fostering family integration, noting that the breakdown of the extended family had resulted in rampant abuse of drugs and high prevalence of the HIV and Aids pandemic.
Cde Chiwewe also paid tribute to the Ministry of Women's Affairs, Gender and Community Development which he said was poised to achieve integration of families and empowerment of women and thereby engender community development.
The Masvingo Polytechnic Division of Engineering also demonstrated at the event how bio-diesel is extracted from the jatropha plant.
The launch of the jatropha and cassava project in Mwenezi was witnessed by top officials from Government and Zanu-PF in Masvingo, traditional chiefs and representatives of non governmental organisations.

Original_Search_Title: Limbe Treasury Staff, Others Detained for Forgery
Headline: Cameroon: Limbe Treasury Staff, Others Detained for Forgery
Body_Text:
A staffer of the Finance Control Department of the Limbe Treasury, Mary Boghumen, and some two suspect conmen are being detained at the Gendarmerie Legion in Buea for allegedly forging some vouchers.
Reports reaching The Post said Gendarmerie officers picked the two suspects at the Southwest Provincial Treasury in Buea on Thursday, June 8, following a tip-off from the Treasurer.
The Post gathered that the arrest of the duo precipitated the arrest of Ms. Boghumen later at her residence in Limbe.The two conmen, whose names The Post could not readily get, reportedly deposited some bonds, supposedly signed by the Limbe Treasurer, Ananias Mbassi Angoula, for payment at the level of Buea on Monday, June 5.
The Post gathered that after the submission of the vouchers, the secretary, who received the said documents, gave the duo a rendezvous for their payment on Thursday, June 8.
The Limbe Treasurer told The Post that it was a case of forgery. He said he learnt of the case when on the that Monday, the secretary in Buea called him to verify if he had signed any such payment to be effected at the level of Buea.
"I told her I had not signed any such bonds. I urged her to instantly contact the Provincial Treasurer," Angoula said.He said the bonds carried a payment request amounting to FCFA 4 million.
Accounts from some other sources who talked to The Post hold that the amount is a little higher.The Limbe Treasurer said the two caught in Buea revealed that Ms. Boghumen gave them the vouchers.
The story goes that Boghumen, who works at the Finance Control allegedly stole genuine vouchers from her service and helped her two accomplices.The said duo who apparently are based in Douala, used the documents and came up with a contract, which they hold they had executed at the level of the Delegation of Agriculture in Limbe worth FCFA 4 million of more.
They, with alleged assistance from Boghumen, and some other probable insiders forged the signatures of some eleven people within the Treasury and Taxation services, among them the signatures of the Provincial Director of Taxes, Etoa Kessek, the Limbe Treasurer, Angoula, the Finance Controller in Limbe, that of the Delegate of Agriculture in the Limbe and so on.
This was to guarantee the authenticity of their deal and outsmart the Treasury scrutinizers in Buea. But luck apparently did not shine on them.The Limbe Treasurer, who refused to name the names of those allegedly involved, said investigations were still going on.
The Post, however, gathered from other sources that after searching Boghumen's Cassava Farms residence in Limbe, gendarmes found some incriminating evidence, among them a Franklin machine - the new fiscal stamp machine now in use in State Treasury and Taxation services instead of fiscal stamps.
Bongam's Case
For the past three years, the Limbe Treasury seems to have been a honey pot
financial racketeers and confidence men. In January, 2004, the erstwhile Treasurer for Limbe, Peter Bonako Nasako, and two other staffers were nabbed and thrown behind bars for allegedly having colluded with one business magnate, Chief Issa Bongam and fleeced the Treasury of some FCFA 468 million.
Till date, Nasako and Mrs. Hannah Naome Ekange are still languishing in detention at the SONARA Gendarmerie Brigade while the case keeps suffering adjournments at the Buea High Court.
The Limbe State Counsel is said to have, after preliminary investigations, filed the case at the Buea Court some time in August 2004. But it is yet to be heard by the said Court.
The Post gathered that the case came up on Tuesday, June 13 at the Buea Court and was adjourned.
The other principal accused persons, Chief Bongam and the former Finance Controller, Emmanuel Lyonga, are still at large.Besides the Bongam/Nasako case, another staffer of the Treasury, an assistant cashier, Mrs. Rachel Halman, was also picked up around August 2004 and detained for the alleged misappropriation of FCFA 14 m
illion at the same Treasury within the space of two months; June - August, 2004.
After these scams, government is said to have taken steps to limit the amount of money that can be handled and or paid out by the Limbe Treasury.

Original_Search_Title: Refugees in Northern Uganda Fear Returning Home
Headline: Uganda: Refugees in Northern Uganda Fear Returning Home
Body_Text:
Lira, Uganda — Continuing violence and looming land squabbles prompt refugees to reject call to go back to their villages.
On a quiet Sunday in May, a member of the Rhino militia assigned to protect refugees in northeastern Uganda went on a rampage.
Despite being confined to quarters, he secured a weapon after reportedly drinking beer most of the day, and killed 11 men, women and children and wounded 14 at the Ogwete refugee camp
Camp residents later said the man was in a jealous rage. He had reportedly fallen in love with the daughter of a camp official, but when the father forbade him to see her, the militiaman went berserk.
Four days later, he was hunted down and killed in what the military says was crossfire. Some suspect it was an execution.
The body was returned to the Ogwete camp where it was burned, and residents attempted to come to grips with the loss of life. It was the largest single killing in recent memory not connected with the region's 20-year conflict with rebels of the Lord's Resistance Army, LRA.
The massacre underscores the relentless tensions surrounding the 1.5 million refugees living in more than 200 camps for displaced people across northern Uganda.
Anxiety in the camps has reached fever pitch recently because the Ugandan government claims that the LRA rebels are finished as an effective fighting force and the refugees can therefore begin vacating the camps and returning home.
But few refugees in the north are heeding the government's call. Most venture out only during the daylight hours to tend their gardens, and by evening they return to the relative safety of the camps.
Rebel activity has seen a dramatic decline in recent weeks, since most of the LRA forces have retreated to the Garamba Park in northeastern Democratic Republic of Congo. In addition, LRA leader Joseph Kony has been involved in tentative peace talks taking place in southern Sudan.
But last week, many refugees' worst fears were realised when a small unit of LRA rebels attacked the Alito refugee camp in the Apach district about 32 kilometres southeast of Lira.
The attack came shortly after dark when a handful of armed rebels struck the camp, reportedly in search of food. After several hours of terrorising camp residents, they killed a man and abducted 17 others, including a priest.
It took the local military detachment more than four hours to respond, but when they did, they rescued ten of the abducted people, said the military.
The attack heightened fears among camp resident and reinforced their resistance to returning to their farms.
"How can we go back?" cried Albino Awule, a former resident of the Alito camp. "We are afraid of Kony. They [rebels] are still around."
Awule described an attack in January, when the LRA torched many of the thatched-roof mud brick huts in which the refugees live, "The rebels killed many people here. Some were burned in their houses, some were cut by axes, others were killed by a gun. They raped women."
Cipriano Okello, leader of the Walela camp where Awule now lives, said rebels have been uprooting cassava plants in the nearby fields at night.
The insurgent are identifiable by the footprints left by the rubber boots they favour, he continued.
Awule, a 47-year-old farmer and father of eight, said he would like nothing more than return to his village some five miles away, and resume his former way of life.
"We are in a very bad condition here," he said of the nearly 15,000 refugees crammed into the camp. Sanitation is poor and refugees must share a limited number of pit latrines. Fresh water comes from a few hand-pumped wells and is collected in plastic containers.
"Some are dying of hunger and disease," he said.
Even if the region was free of the rebels, he said, there would be little reason to go back to the villages. "There is no property [left].
It was all burned by the rebels. There is the land, but we are afraid to go back.?
Awule said none of the promised government support, which includes resettlement packages of seeds and farming tools, has materialised.
Because the land has been abandoned for so many years, boundaries have been lost, destroyed or forgotten. Even when the farmers return, he said, they face land disputes.
Awule recently returned to his land, and said, ?I found somebody [else] cultivating it.? He complained to local officials and eventually the district land tribunal before the problem was resolved.
?If the government wants the people to go back and resettle, we want to hear clearly [that] there there are not rebels around,? said camp leader Okello. ?We also want to enough [building] material to start living in the village.?
Lira's recently elected district chairman, Franco Ojur, doubted that the return of refugees would happen quickly or easily.
"People are fed up with living in the camps,? he said, but ?not much is being done by the government? to encourage them to go back to the land.
Ojur said refugees are "slowly leaving the camps, but they don't leave completely. Resettlement is not something that can be done in a short period of time".
The government's proposed resettlement packages may not be enough, he said, because people will need cash to buy essential supplies.
Ojur said he also feared a government proposal to issue individual land titles could create yet more social unrest and undermine any future peace. Land is traditionally held by clans, he explained, and customarily inherited by each succeeding generation. Issuing individual land titles could subvert the traditional social structure. The impoverished camp residents will be very temped to sell their land to wealthy outsiders.
