If that happens, the villages and traditional clans will be destroyed and many families could be left landless and homeless.
"The customary system we have is quite sufficient," he said. "Land is a very sensitive matter."
Ojur blamed the government for not bringing the conflict with the LRA to an end after 20 years. He was also sceptical that peace talks with the rebels in southern Sudan would be fruitful.
"We can never relax," he said.

Original_Search_Title: A Vote for a Piece of Soap
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: A Vote for a Piece of Soap
Body_Text:
Kisangani — The men and women in Yasira market have no illusions about the upcoming elections in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) and the benefit their votes will bring. "We expect presents from candidates, otherwise we will not vote for them," 20-year-old Lobela Lionda told IRIN.
Bodouin Lombelejale, 43 and a father of 12, is especially pessimistic that his vote might improve his daily life. "Once the candidates are elected, we will never see them again and nothing will change here," he said. "We want to see something today."
Almost 10 years after a rebel movement overturned the 32-year rule of President Mobutu Sese Seko, the people of the DRC finally have a chance to democratically elect their president plus 500 members of parliament. But, in early June, nobody in Yasira knew the date of the elections, which, after many delays and postponements, are scheduled to be held on 30 July.
Here in the interior of the humid Congo Basin, news travels slowly. From the air, you glimpse roads that opened up this huge country for colonisers from Belgium, now swallowed by nature and disappearing into an endless forest.
The challenges in holding free and fair elections in this vast and anarchic country are tremendous.
Yasira market in Isangi Territory is the largest trading venue for a population of 500,000 people. It takes several days to reach Isangi by dugout canoe, or pirogue, from Kisangani, capital of Oriental Province in northeastern DRC, some 1000 km from the nation's capital, Kinshasa.
Propelled by an outboard engine, the canoe trip on the Congo and Lomami Rivers, passes dense secondary forest, abandoned oil palm plantations, a collapsing agricultural institute and the skeleton of a derelict sugarcane factory.
Flying high in almost every village along the river are yellow flags with the insignia of the Parti du peuple pour la reconstruction et le developpement (PPRD) - the political party of President Joseph Kabila, who came in to power January 2001, inheriting the post from his father, Laurent-Desire Kabila, leader of a rebel movement that ousted Mobutu.
No visible opposition
There are hardly any colours flying for any of the other candidates contesting the election. It looks as if Oriental Province is a sure thing for the young and incumbent president. Almost every observer IRIN encountered said the same thing: "Kabila has the money, so he will win."
At Yasira market, a vendor sells unlicensed drugs; others offer smoked fish, bananas and cassava cake wrapped in leaves. Aside from petty trade, the economy is at a virtual standstill. The electorate here, as in other villages throughout the impoverished heart of the Congo, is needy: "We want money, beer or T-shirts," one voter said.
In Kisangani, people walked around in T-shirts emblazoned with the images and names of candidates a month before the official start of campaigning.
"In all this misery, you can buy a poor man with a piece of soap," said Sister Marie Madeleine Bofoe, head of the Catholic NGO Caritas in Isangi.
This also illustrates a paradox: an election to end despotism is making an entire society gamble with its future. "We don't know any of the candidates, and we have no idea who to vote for," said Bebale Bombole, a fish vendor.
With more than 9,600 parliamentary candidates and a campaign period limited to one month, many voters will not be able to make an informed decision.
Lack of information
Information reaches the population through radio, but at Yasira market, there have been no batteries on sale for at least a month. Hence, there was no information for the electorate.
The price of batteries is out of reach for most; a set costs 200 Congolese francs (US$5), four times more than a month's worth of school fees.
During his presidency, Mobutu looted state coffers, spending the national budget on private yachts, planes and a lavish lifestyle in expensive hotels the world over. When the senior Kabila marched into Kinshasa, people hoped for change. The fear now is that history is repeating itself.
Corruption is rife in the DRC. In a recent scandal, $4 million - almost half the country's official budget for doctors and hospitals - went missing from the Ministry of Health. The money had been set aside under a debt-relief scheme for some of the world's poorest countries.
An administrator in Isangi town does not deny the governance problems his country faces: "We have no political culture, and elections are a new thing to us. People have no basis to make value judgements, and candidates who distribute presents will have it easy, cheating others."
When the administrator was appointed by the transitional government a year ago, he inherited an office run by people who had not been paid a salary in years. Forget computers; there was no typewriter or paper.
With a start-up budget of $3,000 from the provincial governor, the administrator bought a pirogue with an engine to start a business that would finance his office.
He also plans to buy a chainsaw to cut down forest trees to sell to help his salary and that of other government employees. "You have to help yourself in this country," he shrugged unhappily. "It's called auto-financing."
'Auto-financing' for survival
Practically everything in the DRC now operates in this manner - public schools, hospitals and even some Catholic missions, which rent rooms to aid workers and journalists to make ends meet. Auto-financing at higher levels opens the door for large-scale plunder.
"We have to admit our problems; we collect taxes and put them in our pockets, that's why there are no services," the administrator said. "I wish they would come here to check my books, but for this, they have to give me money [to check] first."
Whatever taxes were collected at one time in Isangi, were never reinvested. The schools are made of mud, the hospital is decrepit, the roads petering out, and there is no electricity.
But not everything is bleak and hopeless in the interior, to which the United Nations Peacekeeping Mission in DRC (MONUC) is currently shipping thousands of ballot boxes by helicopter for millions of voters.
A significant number of the voters are illiterate, and though women make up more than half the population, only three dozen are registered as candidates nationwide. Illiteracy among women in the Congo is more than 60 percent.
During a referendum for the new constitution in 2005, the population came running to get voter-registration certificates - the first and only identity card many people had ever received. The certificates are now an accepted proof of identity at checkpoints along the rivers, which are manned by Kabila's black-clad presidential guards.
"The exact estimation of how many potential voters there are is highly political, and therefore strictly confidential," demographer Andre Lambert, from the Catholic University de Louvain in Belgium, told IRIN. "I am forbidden to give you the number I extrapolated."
Ethnicity, a big factor
Lambert worked on a study financed by the European Commission to determine how many people live in the DRC, estimated elsewhere at about 60 million. "Some politicians would like to blow up ethnic percentages for an electorate that often votes along ethnic lines," he said.
And candidates know that. Blaise Baise, who was born in Isangi, is running for one of five parliamentary seats in Kisangani. He is currently a member of the transitional parliament, serving on its commission on politics, administration and justice.
He was appointed to parliament because of his work for a Catholic nongovernmental organisation, Paix Christi International, which mediated between feuding ethnic groups in Kisangani in the mid-1990s.
"Voting for one's ethnic group will be the biggest factor in the elections, money the second," said Baise, a member of the local majority Matoke community. "Already some tribal chiefs have received bicycles from candidates. For $100 you can buy the chief of a tribe."
Baise's parliamentary seat pays him the equivalent of $1,200 a month. He just used some of his salary to print 50,000 pamphlets with his name and face on. His power base is youth groups, which he said appreciated that he stood his ground in Kisangani during its tumultuous period.
However, youth groups being influenced by different forces. Other candidates gave them footballs. The youth groups can be brutal power players if they are discontented and angry. In Kisangani, three gangs associated with political parties fought against each other, beating up candidates from other sides.
As the election day draws nearer, the heat in the garbage-strewn city, is likely to rise.
The church factor
There may be numerous political parties backing candidates in the upcoming elections, but one single force is likely to be more influential than the rest: the Catholic Church.
"You must be endorsed by the priests in order to win," Baise said, who is working hard at winning their favour.
The representatives of the church play their cards carefully. "They all would like our support, but no one is endorsed yet," said Father Badidique, head of Caritas in Kisangani. Caritas is the largest development agency in Oriental Province.
Most of the church, however, is not in wait-and-see mode. It actively participates in the election process. In Isangi town, a young priest, Jean-Noel Mbola and 15 volunteers run the Catholic radio station Radio Bondeleo [peace and justice], which broadcasts through a 1,000-watt transmitter funded by an Italian diocese. The programme: news, music and "information on what kind of a Congo we want," Mbola said.
In Kinshasa, church radio broadcasts profiles of candidates, and priests openly call out names when they say Mass, reminding their flock of who they say participated in atrocities in the DRC, who may be notorious for corruption and otherwise a danger to society.
As campaigning officially starts, Radio Bondeleo will mention names as well: "Candidates have to have a positive past, a project that helped the community," Mbola said. This policy endorses people associated with the Church, often the only ones doing humanitarian work.
"Now the sons from Isangi who left to study, come back to slice up the cake," Mbola said, "but I don't think there are enough people who really mean good."
In May, Congolese Cardinal Frederic Etsou sanctioned the marriage of President Joseph Kabila, a non-Catholic, an act widely perceived as a sign of endorsement of his candidacy.
An unenviable job
Bernard Etukolombo was a teacher of sociology at the University in Kisangani and development agent for the Catholic Church. He now heads the Independent Electoral Commission for Isangi and two other territories with almost 600 polling venues in all.
Etukolombo will have the difficult task of running the elections here and making sure that every voter knows what he is doing. He told IRIN he believed that only one-third of the local population knew what the upcoming elections were all about.
"No problems? We got nothing but problems!" he said. "The road conditions are terrible. Sometimes even motorcycles cannot pass. Then, our people have to visit the villages on foot." Voting kits for the 2005 referendum on Congo's new Constitution, weighed 50kg each and sometimes had to be carried on porters' heads.
Etukolombo's staff of 12 people had not received a salary for three months. They have no insurance, and when they fall ill they get little or no help. In the course of his work, Etukolombo himself has had a motorbike accident.
On the day that dozens of ballot boxes arrived in Isangi by MONUC helicopter, Etukolombo was still waiting for money to buy petrol for the two jeeps and 10 motorcycles that would be used to distribute ballots to voting venues. Time was running out for him.
"There is real danger that people will vote with open hands," he said.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: NGOs Launch Workshop to Combat Crop Disease
Headline: Rwanda: NGOs Launch Workshop to Combat Crop Disease
Body_Text:
Kigali — Catholic Relief Services (CRS) and the International Institution of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) have launched a research on diseases affecting staple food, the Cassava Mosaic virus disease (CMD) and Banana Xanthomonas Wilt (BXW).
This was announced during a three-day international workshop that began Tuesday, June 13 at Hotel des Mille Collines, in Kigali, that brought together delegates from the six Central and East Africa countries of Rwanda, Burundi, the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), Kenya, Tanzania and Uganda.
The workshop was also attended by representatives of key stakeholders including those from several NGOs.
In an interview with the Chief of Party of Crop Crisis Control Project in East Africa Region, John Peacock, told The New Times that the CRS and IITA in collaboration with the Ministry of Agriculture launched a regional agricultural initiative. The Crop Crisis Control Project (CCP) aimed at combating the diseases that affect staple food crops.
"The two organisations organised a network of regional associations and agricultural institutions, country level agricultural research organisations and local implementing partners and it was sponsored by USAID," he added.
Dr Pheneas Ntawuruhunga, the EARRNET Co-ordinator also confirmed to The New Times that the 18-month initiative will intensify and co-ordinate the fight against Cassava Mosaic and Banana Wilt in six months within the six countries of East and Central Africa.
"The projects will strengthen regional and national mechanisms to deliver agricultural technologies and knowledge to rural households, to reduce the harmful impacts of these plant diseases on food security, while aiding farmers to speed agricultural recovery," he said.
Paula Bramel, the IITA Director of Research-for-Development said bananas are extremely important for the food security and are a source of household income in much of the Great Lakes Region.
"Over twenty million people depend on them as the main source of livelihood. They are grown both as a staple food crop and for income generation mainly through brewing and regional export of both cooking and desert bananas," she added.
Other stakeholders across the region said that the best practices will be shared, to enable farmers in the region recover their capacity to produce disease-free cassava and bananas and, to improve their food security.
During the workshop, participants observed that the Banana Xanthomonas Wilt (BXW) was initially reported about 90 years ago in Ethiopia.

Original_Search_Title: Avert Famine - Gasabo Farmers
Headline: Rwanda: Avert Famine - Gasabo Farmers
Body_Text:
Kigali CIty — Pests have attacked and destroyed food crops of many residents in Gako village, Gasabo District, leaving most in fear for a possible severe famine. Farmers who talked to The New Times last week said that the pests have severely destroyed their crops and this is likely to affect the output and incomes of many residents in the area.
Residents are calling on the government through the Ministry of Agriculture for technical assistance to curtail pests and minimise a possible food crisis in the area.
The problem of pests in the area is attributed to the prolonged drought that led to inadequate rainfall which created a breeding ground for the pests.
"The pests have distorted banana and cassava plantations in the areas of Gako. The pests started two months ago and we are afraid the problem might not stop. If it continues, we shall experience severe famine," Celestin Bandora, told The New Times.
He lamented that he failed to harvest his agricultural crops last year due to the long drought spell.
"The dry season hit all my crops and left me with no produce. It was the only source of money for paying school fees for my children. Now the pests have attacked my gardens, what should I do?" Bandora asked.
Virginia Mukundwa, also a farmer echoed a similar concern, lamenting that her two cassava gardens were affected by pests and it is left with no food left for the family.
Another farmer, Emmanuel Mugabo, said that pests have persisted because of lack of pesticides and un-mechanised ways of farming.

Original_Search_Title: Schools And Food Security
Headline: Nigeria: Schools And Food Security
Body_Text:
Lagos — In 2004, I asked Mrs. Tonia Deborah Igiehon, Managing Director of Aibtonia Promotions Advertising and Marketing, a Lagos-based firm what she felt about food security in Nigeria.
Her answer was thought-provoking: "About four years ago, the Governor of Central Bank, Chief Joseph Sanusi, raised an alarm over the escalating importation of foodstuff to the tune of $200 billion. Today, the figure has increased by at least 25 per cent. Can you imagine what the country loses that way? But you know that Nigeria has the capacity to feed not only its population, but also much of Africa. What then do we do, you may ask? The answer does not lie in the blanket banning of foreign food items. That would only further worsen smuggling, a condition that is already terrible. Rather, the solution would be to dramatically increase the production of Nigerian foods. In addition to that, we need to process locally more international food items deemed most useful and nutritious and in ways that are environment friendly."
Coming from someone whose company has for two decades, consistently organised indigenous food and beverage festivals around the country, her words on matters relating to food culture cannot be pushed aside. You just would not stop asking. Why should a country like Nigeria, endowed by nature, function without a deliberate, result-oriented programme for sustained supply of food. There is no part of the country that has no capacity for the production of one crop or the other. Yet, farming remains relatively relegated. There was a time when it was the hub of the economy, when cash crops literally sustained the nation. That was before oil took the centre-stage. Now, the rhythm has changed. Development plans are designed by governments at various levels without incorporating the ones that ensure that the future of the country is secure in terms of food.
When Chief Olusegun Obasanjo assumed national leadership for the second time in 1999, many people felt relieved and hoped that finally, redemption had come the way of the agricultural sector. My sincere opinion about food affairs under Obasanjo is that if indeed there are achievements, they are not notable enough to foster the kind of pride other countries have in their own foodstuff. The situation is unfortunate and inexcusable considering the president's profile. As a head of state in the 1970s, Obasanjo initiated Operation Feed the Nation (OFN) which sensitised the nation to the need for functional farming. It was then fashionable for schools and other institutions to invest in farming. The result was remarkable. Hunger was kept at a minimum.
Now, happily, less than one year to the end of his second tenure, President Obasanjo has another opportunity to launch the country into a more abundant, enduring phase of food production and culture. The school feeding programme started recently by the present government in some states is capable of initiating a revolution in agriculture.
Ever before the government thought of embarking on the programme, Aibtonia had already done its homework on it and gathered the data necessary for its implementation. When Igiehon spoke about her company's blueprint on "Food-for-School" programme, it was with a sense of commitment and the zeal of a crusader: "The Food-for-School Programme could bring many benefits to the nation, it is the catalyst that we need to revive a lot of things. Before the office of the special adviser to the president on food security was created, we had been pushing the government to institute the singular strategic intervention of feeding Nigerian school children, giving them one meal per day in schools."
One of the gains this will generate, if properly executed is that it will provoke a massive return to agriculture and aqua-culture. Farming, food processing, fishing, livestock farming would be re-activated as resources are channeled into meeting an overwhelming demand. Just imagine what would be needed to provide 25 million school children with one meal per day while in school! If the government is serious about preserving the nation's heritage, it would not subscribe to feeding the children with imported food items. That would be an opportunity to encourage local farmers to embark on large-scale operations. A return to the land would ignite a new era of economic prosperity. Knowing Nigerians for being sensitive to gainful openings, and considering their highly enterprising nature, nobody would need to force them to go back to the love of their ancestors. The other day when China placed $500 million on Nigerian cassava, it was enough incentive for even reluctant farmers. Tru th is, feeding school children bears more potentialities.
Not only that. The beneficiaries will enjoy enhanced health and better mental alertness. The relationship between wholesome nutrition and intellectual prowess has since been established. One thing that has stood between African children and sound academic accomplishments is poverty. The pitiable conditions that many families find themselves in have conspired to hinder them in various ways including education. The government would therefore be using, permit the cliché, one stone to kill two birds. Reduce the burden on a family and then put the child in a proper state of mind to learn. This would also engender loyalty to the nation in the Nigerian child. Their creative abilities would be released towards giving positive things back to their fatherland, instead of joining cults and making life precarious for fellow citizens.
There is no better way to insure the future than investing in the younger generation. If the youths fall in love with indigenous food and production is kept active, food security will be entrenched, naturally. The government should therefore look at the long-term benefits of the school feeding programme and not run it as if it is a charity. Instead, it should be seen as a major component of culture re-orientation, a vehicle through which the nation's sense of pride and well-being is developed. Feed the Nigerian child and lay a solid foundation for a successful tomorrow.

Original_Search_Title: Frw75m for Bugesera Food Trade
Headline: Rwanda: Frw75m for Bugesera Food Trade
Body_Text:
Kigali — The Bugesera District Authorities have extended Frw75 million to 15 local co-operatives to facilitate them purchase food harvests from farmers at a fair price. This is in a bid to protect farmers in the area from being exploited by businessmen from Kigali and other areas people who buy their products at low prices.
This was disclosed by the district mayor Gaspard Musonera during an interview with The New Times. The mayor said that the initiative to form co-operatives came after many buyers from other areas especially Kigali had invaded local farmers and bought their harvest at a cheaper price.
"Some farmers had started selling off their harvests when they are still in farms at cheaper prices than the expected price. We had to come in to curtail the exploitation by forming co-operatives and empowered financially to buy produce from farmers at a fair price and sell it anywhere," the Mayor said.
He added, "we decided that a kilogramme of beans be bought at Frw150 from farmers. This is contrary to what some people were paying them (Frw 50 per kilo yet in Kigali City a kilo of beans is sold at Frw 250)."
According to the initiative, farmers are encouraged to stock part of their harvest and the Ministry of Agriculture has pledged stocking facilities for foodstuffs to farmers. Farmers who will have stocked food will be given access to bank loans.
He said that farmers have been encouraged to embark on produce like coffee, rice, maize and cassava and added that over 1,500 hectares of maize will be planted by July 2006 in Ruhondo, Rwinkwavu, Kiborera and Biryogo wet lands.
He pointed out that over 1,000 hectares of land in Kamabuye and Rilima drought stricken sectors are to be irrigated. According to the mayor, about 4,000 hectares of land in Bugesera have been terraced in all sectors.

Original_Search_Title: Bubanza Flood Victims Receive Food Aid
Headline: Burundi: Bubanza Flood Victims Receive Food Aid
Body_Text:
Bujumbura — The Burundian government has begun the distribution of 18 tonnes of food aid to thousands of people in the northwestern province of Bubanza who were displaced by floods in late April.
Beneficiaries of the aid included 327 households in the communes of Gihanga and Mpanda, the two most-affected areas. The nation's first vice-president, Martin Nduwimana, presided over the first distribution in Gihanga commune on Friday. "Since the catastrophe occurred, the government has been collecting relief from its own funds and from donor organisations," he said. "This relief that we have so far collected is directed at the most needy."
The food aid comprised beans and cassava flour. Each household received 30kg of flour and 25kg of beans. "I admit that the relief is not adequate, as you will have to share with another commune," Nduwimana said.
In mid-May, heavy rains caused two rivers to burst their banks in Bubanza, killing nine people and displacing thousands of others. The floods also destroyed a cemetery. In the central province of Muramvya, floods killed 11 people, destroyed 200 homes and damaged at least 1,800 acres of crops.
Flooding destroyed an estimated 500 homes in Gihanga, rendering 2,000 people homeless. They have been living with families whose homes were not affected. The floods also destroyed rice and cotton crops, the main cash crops in the area. Gihanga administration officials said they expect low yields.
With the dry season setting in the province, there is also concern over insufficient grazing land, as the vegetation in the lowland communes dries up.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Rochdale Tames Guinea Fowls
Headline: Uganda: Rochdale Tames Guinea Fowls
Body_Text:
Kampala — The sight of the normally wild guinea fowls scratching and picking insects alongside chicken and turkey was bewildering! Guinea fowls are known to become wild and take off every time they see a person. These ones simply stayed put, as if unaware of the human intrusion.
The guinea fowls are domesticated at Rochdale Orphanage Farm in Kyebando, Wakiso district.
Guinea fowls are one of the commonly eaten wild birds, especially in central Uganda.
Other than Rochdale that has about 25 fowls, homesteads are also domesticating one or two fowls.
"We got their eggs from the bush and gave them to chicken to hatch them," says Moses Male, the farm manager of Rochdale. Guinea fowls lay an average of 10 eggs every laying season. Like chicken, they take about 21 days to hatch.
After hatching, the young fowls were adopted by the chicken. "As they grew up, the chicken realised they were different and abandoned them. However, they have already adapted to the domestic situation.
"The initial five formed the foundation of what we have now," Male says.
The fowls are closely monitored to make sure they do not lay eggs in the bushes around the farm. Once it is discovered that they have started laying eggs, they are put in an enclosure, that is well laid out and attractive for laying eggs.
"After laying the eggs, they sit over them until they hatch," Male says.
They guinea fowls are fed on maize bran and all other feeds that are consumed by chicken. When released outside, they scavenge for food.
Male says they have already eaten a few of the fowls, adding that hey are as delicious as indigenous chicken although their flesh is tougher.
A mature guinea fowl can weigh at least 3kgs and costs between sh30,000 and sh50,000.
Guinea fowls can also be reared on a commercial basis. "The only difference is that because they have a wild instinct, they might lay their eggs in the bush. Turkeys also lay their eggs in the bush, but farmers always find them and bring them to organised enclosures," says Christopher Matovu, a veterinary doctor and specialist in poultry management.
The farm also rears layers and broilers.
These are housed in a structure that was donated to the school by the British High Commission. "This was the contribution of the British High Commission to the development of this school," says Salim Saad Uhuru, the chairman and treasurer of the orphanage. The structure has a capacity of about 2,000 chicken.
The farm also keeps cattle and goats. It also has a sizeable shamba of cassava, bananas, beans and maize. According to Uhuru, most of the animals are kept to supplement the orphanages income and to improve diet at the school.

Original_Search_Title: Kpakol: We've Started a Revolution Against Poverty
Headline: Nigeria: Kpakol: We've Started a Revolution Against Poverty
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nigeria is in a race against time to reduce poverty at least by 50 percent in 2015, a deadline set in the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs). Though average daily income is around $1, current efforts are on to change that, and reduce poverty to a barest minimum through various schemes, including counterpart funding. Dr. Magnus Kpakol, Coordinator, National Poverty Alleviation Progamme (NAPEP), who refers to himself as the Chief Representative of the Poor, spoke with Abimbola Akosile about renewed efforts at empowering the poor.
Excerpts:
What is happening to the nation's fight against poverty?
There is something revolutionary that we are doing. We are building partnerships in the fight against poverty involving state and local governments and the private sector, particularly the banks. I think this is revolutionary especially in the way it is done; the seriousness of purpose and the commitment that the partners bring in and the catalytic role that the federal government plays.
I believe that micro credit and resource mobilisation that we picked up in connection with poverty eradication should really happen at the grassroots, basically very community based. So, what we are trying to do is to have State and Local Governments be the primary actors in implementing grassroots poverty eradication programmes, and then having the Federal Government provide emphasis support and some resources wherever possible, to spur the activity.
In the spirit of the present economic reforms, we have been able to convince many State Governments to lay emphasis on micro credit. Because I believe you cannot be successful in poverty eradication without using micro credit. Micro credit is used to leverage whatever your success has provided, to be the link between micro credit and information and infrastructure provision. If you provide electricity to a village but the people don't have money to set up barber shops and carpentry shops, it won't help.
So we are going to give people this micro-credit facility. Unfortunately, the people cannot just go to the micro-finance institutions or commercial banks to get micro credit because it is very difficult to do so for poor people. Imagine a situation where poor people have a problem even opening bank accounts because to open a bank account in Nigeria today you need an international passport and all kinds of references; let alone trying to get a loan.
So what I have convinced State and Local Governments to do is to be the champions of micro credit. But to make it really sustainable, the people eligible for such credit must also get capacity to run the business so that their business are profitable. Otherwise if their business are not sustainable, they will not be able to make repayments. It is because we provide this kind of facility that our credit programmes have a 99 per cent repayment rate. It is because the people are given the capacity to be successful in their business that they are able to make repayments.
How that capacity works is that first of all there is a selection process. We select people that are most likely to be successful. You don't just select people because they are poor, there are so many poor people out there. We want this to be a demonstration thing. Let us demonstrate to them that it can be done. And this is the profile of the people that are going to be successful. Just that you are poor doesn't mean that you are going to get credit.
You should be a poor person who really wants to get out of poverty by pulling yourself up by your own bootstraps, except that somebody is helping you to try and be lifted. That is what we are doing for the poor. They are actually getting up by themselves.
Through this collaboration programme, the money provided reside with the banks, which pass them to micro-finance institutions and cooperatives, and onward to the people. After the selection process, we choose the people that are most likely to be accepted. Then we partner with the National Directorate of Employment (NDE) and Small and Medium Enterprises Development Agency of Nigeria (SMEDAN) on training them on how to set up their businesses, accounting and so on.
There are other institutions we partner with. In the South-west, for example, there are traditional institutions that help to ensure the sustainability of the programme. Then, we encourage the banks that along the line, in a couple of years, these people should be able to approach them directly without going through this long process because then they would have demonstrated confidence that they are viable customers.
So, the poor people really have a good potential base for business with the banks. What is also useful is that the banks can then do business not only with the ones that are in this programme, but people that look like them. So, you are not in the programme but you look very much like those in there that are successful. It is therefore likely that you also would be successful. Then they can get larger pool of the poor directly to the banks.
What will determine the success of the new scheme?
To have a very systematic poverty eradication programme in a community, there are some things that we call capacity deepening activities. These are the anchors of the small businesses. And they would require a larger amount of money, over N100,000. For example, if you want a cassava flour machine or a cassava chipping machine or a cold storage facility, some of them go into over N1 million. Those guys are anchors because if you set up a cassava milling machine, then you need people to bring in the cassava regularly.
Now, that group of people belong to what we call capacity widening activities. And there are many of them. People selling pepper, mango, doing cassava and so on and want to sell a little bit more but their investment is only N2,000 and profit say N500. That is little to take care of their family. Suppose they could get a N10,000 or N20,000 investment, profits may then be N5,000, and they would be able to do better than before.
It won't make them millionaires overnight but they certainly would be able to do better. So, you have many of these guys in the capacity widening activities and a few in the anchor activities. The anchors are the ones that the other guys depend on. Then the anchors have their markets set up for them even in localities or outside.
We've truly had enormous successes but our problem is to change the orientation of people. Because people still see us as the old NAPEP. But there are so many other things that we are doing in data gathering and in coordination. Just even knowing the size and nature of poverty in the country and knowing the nature of support systems in the country is hard enough.
We will soon publish a report for the data that we are collating to know the dimensions of poverty that we have and the measures that are being taken to end poverty. But then the problem is that if you go to a community and say you just want to capture the data and information and you have no sweet in your hand, then nobody wants to listen to you. Even if you want to suggest what they could do, people do not want to listen to you, if you do not have a 'candy' in your hand.
That is why we believe in this catalytic intervention that we are providing. And I think we are doing very well so far. In terms of participation, we have about seven States, we have collated over N2 billion using matching funds of only about N150 million. So, I think that is revolutionary. It is very difficult to get the States to commit to it. It is a two-sided issue. We access the poor but we are also talking to the resource providers and policy-makers.
We go and convince the States that they are the builders and that they will be matched by funds from the banks. Akwa Ibom state, for instance put N250 million down and Oceanic Bank matched it with another N250 million. So they got N500 million. We then put in about N25 million. So we are getting N500 million with just N25 million of matching funds. But the money is used in their own communities.
We set up a joint implementation committee that is typically chaired by a representative of the Governor so that it is owned by them. It involves all their local people. We then bring in other people to promote that activity and give visibility to what the state is doing. We find that this is very well embraced. The States like the fact that their story is also being told to the world. We want to encourage them so that other States can do like they have done.
When was the scheme launched?
We kicked off in August last year. We first launched it in Kaduna. So far, we have about seven States, those that have put money on the table. Next month, we should probably get another seven. We have Kaduna, Ogun, Ondo, Kogi, Rivers, Akwa Ibom and Osun. In a couple of weeks, we will have Anambra, Kebbi, Gombe, Bayelsa and Kwara. These are sure States that we will have in the next few weeks, including Ebonyi.
Is there a benchmark for involvement?
Yes. We want no less than N50 million commitment by the States. Some States like Rivers did N500 million. This fund was matched by another N500 million right there.
How do you get the banks to commit to this?
It is not easy. It took us about a year to get the banks to commit to this. You have to also sweeten it for them. The carrot for them is that the money is deposited with them. For example, Rivers State committed N500 million plus their N500 million, which makes N1 billion. That is money they can play with because they know that it takes some time before all the money is exhausted. Even if you have the beneficiaries to pick up the money, there is no way that they will clear that money within two weeks.
The banks are also part of the implementation committee. They are also happy to do it because they get publicity. They are seen as doing well. Besides, these are customers. The guys who get the funds to do their businesses equally deposit their funds with them. So I had to work all this out and explain to them that it is not their normal way of thinking. But with this, we had to line up all these things that are of benefit to them. Now, the banks are chasing me wanting to be part of it.
At what interest rate are the funds being disbursed?
We are looking to get interest rate of under 10 percent, say around eight per cent. And we can get that because there is no interest on the portion that comes from the State or Local Governments - it is interest-free. The banks, because they are not a charitable organisation, own their own portion. But when you combine the two, then the interest rate will come to half. Let us say the banks charges 14 per cent, then the effective interest rate will come to seven per cent. And the poor can handle that.
Where we have trouble is that we have to give some compensation to the micro-finance institutions that will go out and implement. I foresee a situation where if we can beat the banks to lower their interest rate to, say, 12 percent, so we get close to six per cent interest rate. We are trying to get the banks to understand the niche of the programme and not use it as profit centre.
Which banks have signed on?
A couple of the banks have signed on to this like Oceanic, UBA, Intercontinental, and Zenith. Others like Union and FCMB are trying to be a part of it.
So far, what is the scorecard for the scheme?
We have not really got to the point of repayment in this particular scheme. Our repayments are from previous schemes that did not involve this kind of partnership that we are doing. We gave out loans directly through cooperatives and micro-finance institutions. But with the matching funds, that is just happening, I think it is going to get into next year before we get the kind of results that we want.
What is the scheme called?
It is called Multi-Partner Micro-Finance Scheme (MPFS). Sometimes we just call it Matching Funds Scheme.
What are NAPEP's matching funds used for?
No more than 10 percent of the funds are supposed to be for coordination within the States, which is to be done by the implementation committee. If the amount raised is large, they could use a good chuck of our money. If the amount is small, they cannot use more than 10 per cent.
Let us say a State raises N50 million and a bank raises an equivalent N50 million. 10 percent of that is N10 million, which they can use from our money for administrative and implementation purposes. If the State brings N200 million and it is matched by a bank, then almost all of our money will be used for implementation.
Do you have a ceiling for NAPEP's contribution?
Yes, we have a ceiling because that is all we have got. We cannot do more than N25 million per State. If it is a very unusual programme, perhaps we can go and beg from somewhere, I don't know. But for now we have a ceiling of N25 million.
Are States required to provide more funding besides their counterpart funds?
Yes. The States can also provide some more funding. You have to get the proportion of the amount to be distributed because the larger the amount, the more involved the implementation. That means you would need more money.
What are the details of the MTN programme?
We just talked for the first time and they have call centres where they provide equipment worth N30,000 for beneficiaries. What we want to do is to partner with them so as to fit them into our programme in the States. Like in Rivers State that has N500 million under the programme, we can commit, say N20 million or N30 million to MTN call centres. In Kogi, which has N200 million, we can commit, say, N10 million.
This will be good for MTN because in this case, in a sense, they get money directly, but then, we are creating jobs. And the people pay back too. So, we can do quite a bit with them. We can collaborate not only with their call centres, we want to have them do more. So the guy who is operating the call centre can also sell doughnuts and so on. Let us say they get N5,000 in credits from MTN, they can also sell newspapers or doughnuts or whatever.
How did you get MTN to buy into the programme?
MTN itself is very happy to do this because its good for them. One, they get publicity and secondly, it looks good because they are working with the Federal Government. But most important for them is that they make money. If we pay them N30 million out of N500 million in Rivers state, that is some funds. So they are happy to be a part of it.
We did not even convince them. We have this partnership with Globacom already but it is not being as successful as I had thought. Most likely, we would be a little more successful with MTN because they have a focus on this. They devote one percent of their earnings to community development. So, we want to see how we can match the money to use up that one percent.
For me, I call myself the Chief Representative of the Poor on behalf of Mr. President. I am fighting on behalf of the poor. In that port-folio, I am fighting now with MTN to commit more of their resources to the poor. So, when the poor see me, they should know that I am the guy that is fighting for them. And this is the truth. From about N2 billion we have now, we are going to get close to N5 billion by the end of the year. All that money is gotten because I am out there fighting. They wouldn't have come out by themselves. So, I am the voice of the poor.
What kind of support would you need to accelerate your work?
What I need is more capacity for the right staff that can go on and say what I am saying to resource providers. That means that I need more coordination funds, more money to go round and engage people that can sell the message. And probably a little bit more resources for matching funds because if I tell a State that I have N100 million to match them, they are more responsive than when I have only N20 million. In fact, there are some states where I don't even pay anything. If we can up our matching funds, it would help the States to help us to bring out more.
The other challenge that I have is really getting the message to Local Government Chairmen to do exactly as the States are doing. In some States where we have been successful, we had to go through the State Government to pull them in, almost literally by force. But I am patient. Perhaps what they need is to get the message. So, I am looking for more resources, more opportunities, and staff to go and talk to them at the Local Governments and cause them to commit to it. I cannot alone go to the 774 Local Governments, all the private sector organisations, and States and still handle my port-folio in the office.
What is your definition of poverty?
This has been a real experience for me. Somebody was asking me which job I enjoy doing more, being Chief Economic Adviser to the President or NAPEP Coordinator. And I said they both have their values. But this is more challenging. It is a little easier being Chief Economic Adviser because with economics the issues are clear; you want to know the GDP, exchange rate and so on. We understand those type of things.
As NAPEP Coordinator however, you are now getting into an area where you are trying to fight poverty and dealing with people. You are dealing with institutions and culture and all kinds of variables and getting the facts and the message out. It is a little more complicated. And I think it is probably why when I get out of this office that I will really appreciate some of the experiences that I have had.
I say that as a background for how I would define poverty. The pure economics definition for poverty is a person that does not have adequate food, shelter and clothing. Of course, you know, for most countries, even in Nigeria, we have a threshold. We say that a poor person is one that makes less than the household per capita of N34,000 annually. But you cannot see a guy on the road and say he makes less than N34,000...
And even if you ask him, he may not tell you the truth. So, how do you then know who to bring in? It is a very tricky question because for the most part, the selection process involves the community. The communities know who is poor. That is why we use the institutions when we are working in a particular community. The cooperatives, traditional rulers and the Local Government people know those that are poor in their communities.
They are the ones that help us to bring eligible people in. And we have to assess them too. So, when they ask me to define poverty, for grassroots people, I just say a poor person is someone who does not have money. Now, that is not the standard definition but it works. In the rural areas the people know who does not have money. We have to have a benchmark that you can use to drive the issues.
Difference between NAPEP, NDE and SMEDAN?
NDE is set up for training people. That is what their job is. That is why they are National Directorate of Employment. The are about training. Then SMEDAN is for setting up facilitating and providing information for small and medium scale enterprises. Even the office of the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) is an advocacy office set up to look for more funds to finance water, health education and so on.
At NAPEP, we pool all these parties together to fight poverty. We go and check to see whether roads are available to get produce from farms to markets. We go and check whether electricity is there. We encourage people to do the things they should do. In trying to fight poverty, we would bring NDE to do the training for us. We bring SMEDAN to help people set things up. So, we are little bit more comprehensive than these other parties. People must understand the difference.

Original_Search_Title: 'The African Green Revolution is Now'
Headline: Nigeria: 'The African Green Revolution is Now'
Body_Text:
Lagos — Africa is the only region in the world that cannot feed itself. The Africa Fertiliser Summit in Abuja June 9-13 was convened by the African Union (AU) and the New Partnership for Africa's Development (NEPAD) to end the continent's destitution.
ETIM IMISIM spoke with Dr. Akin Adesina, a Nigerian and associate director for food security for the New York-based Rockefeller Foundation, one of the backers of the summit
Can you please introduce yourself?
My name is Akin Adesina from the Rockefeller Foundation in New York. I am an associate director for food security for the foundation and I am I have been spearheading the organisation of this fertilizer summit with President Olusegun Obasanjo and the government of the Federal Republic of Nigeria. I was born and bred in Nigeria. I am proud to be a Nigeria and glad to be back home.
What package does your Foundation have for the agriculture sector?
Before I talk about that maybe let me talk about some the challenges of agriculture and why we are here in Nigeria. The Rockefeller Foundation is known globally in terms of agriculture. As you may know, the foundation was at the forefront of what is called the Asian Revolution. The dramatic increase in yield in Asia and Latin America that led to the huge increases in food productivity and food security for hundreds of millions in Latin America and Asia.
What are the nature of your interventions in these cases?
Let me give you an example in the area of genetic improvement. The Rockefeller Foundation supported the development of what is called Nerica rice for Africa. Nerica rice was developed by the West Africa Rice Development Association that I used to work for many years ago. What we did was that we took African scientists and we exposed them to cutting edge research in terms of the use of tissue culture technology.
They took the Asian rice and African rice varieties and crossed them together. That is not a simple thing because it has been done for practically 50 years without success. So they came up with a new rice variety that is called Nerica. This rice variety gives you 2.3 tonnes per hectare without the use of fertiliser.
You can imagine, for poor farmers in Africa that depend on rice production, that this is phenomenal increase in production. The staff that developed that eventually won the World Food Price. We support this kind of work.
We support the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) in Nigeria to develop new varieties of crops and they have done fabulous jobs. As you know, cassava is having a revolution in Nigeria through genetic improvement that IITA has done. In addition, the problem that farmers have across Africa is having access to seeds and fertilisers in rural areas.
We want to take them to the rural areas where hundreds of millions of people are. We try to reduce the risk that they face in tilting towards the rural market. This experiment has moved millions of dollars of inputs of seeds and fertilisers to rural areas across Africa. I am saying that we want to cut down on the distance that farmers travel to find seeds and fertilisers. In some countries, it can be as high as 40 kilometres. It is senseless; there is no reason that this should be happening. So it is one of the issues that we have been pushing at this summit.
We read in the newspapers and watch TV about famine in Africa. What is the response of the rest of the world to this situation?
Hunger occurs in areas where agricultural productivity is low. This is the problem of Africa. Africa is the only region of the world where the par capita productivity of food has been declining for 30 years. I gave been in international work for over 20 years and I have worked around the world and have never seen any region of the world that is suffering as much as Africa.
But you ask yourself, Why is that? The crop varieties that can make the difference for rice, for maize - they are here in Africa. But none of these varieties grow on air. All need nutrients, they need fertilisers, to grow. The average yield in Africa for maize is 0.5 tonnes per hectare. In Latin America, we can get 10 tonnes per hectare. American farmers get way well over that. The reason is: African soils are dead, just plain dead.
Africa uses 8 kilograms per hectare of fertiliser, compared to 400 kilograms per hectare by China. Compared to 185 kilograms per hectare by the United States and compared to almost 400 kilograms by Netherlands. So it is no brain surgery. All the regions of the world - Asia, Latin America - that have increased the levels of use of fertilisers have been able to rapidly increase their productivity. But Africa, because it has not been using fertilisers, has been losing productivity. In fact, right now, Africa loses $4 billion dollars worth of soil nutrients every year.
And this has been happening for 30 years, every year, because we are taking the nutrients and we are not putting it back. So you can see why Africa as a region hasn't been producing enough to feed its people, talk less of producing enough to sell as surplus. There is hunger because we are not investing enough in our farmers to give them access to seeds and fertilisers. There is also no investment in irrigation. Right now only 3 per of Africa's land is irrigated and therefore we have droughts and we have the big shocks that the farmers go through. So we need to expand the areas under irrigation. The other reason you have hunger in Africa is a lack of political will. Not all the countries are doing what they should do. But we have a president in Nigeria that we are very proud of in the Rockefeller Foundation. He is seen as the leader in agriculture.
Some people would say that the way to deal with hunger is to bring food from the United States and Europe and to redistribute. That helps but it is not the way you can feed a continent. Africa has to feed itself and to feed itself it must support its own agriculture.
What is the role of the Rockefeller Foundation in the Africa Fertiliser Summit?
Actually we helped to conceive this idea because, as I mentioned earlier, we were at the forefront of the Asian Green Revolution that changed all of Asia. You see China, Thailand, India and Pakistan growing. All these economies are growing because they were able to solve the problem of low productivity in agriculture. Once they feed themselves their economies begin to grow.
We have been asking ourselves the question: Why not Africa? We felt that if we could deal with the problem of low soil fertility, if we can address that, and improve the quality of the soil and give African farmers the same fuel that fueled the Asian Revolution, which are fertilisers, Africa will be the same way. Our role was to meet with all the leaders.
Africa is importing, spends $2.5 billion importing food every year. That is the money we should be spending doing other things, not importing food. Our role is: we are supporting the International Fertiliser Development Centre, which is a centre doing fertiliser development around the world, to implement this summit. The summit was convened by the African Union and NEPAD, which is a programme of the African Union.
We are working with African leaders in their own effort to address the African food crisis. We can provide science, technology and the financial resources to support them. We are backing them. And I think that President Obasanjo is leading the way on that.
Please give me an overview of the fertiliser summit.
The fertiliser summit as I have said is convened by the African Union and NEPAD. What we are trying to do are basically three things: How do we improve the access, the affordability and incentives for using fertilisers? Access because farmers must be able to get the right quantity, the right quality of fertilisers, and they must get them on time.
Affordability, because they must be able to afford it; and incentives, because it is one thing to give fertilisers to a farmer. When they use them, they must also be able to sell their farm produce. That way they can use it from year to year. Access, accessibility and incentives, those are the three issues we trying to deal with.
What outcomes are you working towards?
The summit is looking at five major outcomes. One is a drastic reduction in search cost, that is the distance that farmers have to travel to find seeds and fertilisers. And by developing, strengthening and expanding agro-dealer networks. These are the rural inputs networks that I mentioned earlier. You can really move inputs into rural areas. We want to scale that up throughout Africa. Second, in linking these inputs stockists to the seed and fertiliser companies that I mentioned to you earlier.
So we are encouraging governments to set up national agricultural inputs credit guarantee systems that do that and we will get hundreds of millions of dollars of inputs that farmers need straight to their doorsteps in the rural areas. The third thing that we are trying to do is about smart subsidies for fertilisers. African farmers are not different from other farmers around the world. American farmers have subsidies. European farmers have subsidies.
Japanese farmers have it. The OECD countries spend $263 billion a year on subsidies. But African farmers get nothing. So we are saying that we must supply fertilisers to farmers through smart support policies and that means that we want governments to support it but don't kill the private sector. You can do it but do it through the private sector. But make no mistake about it. African farmers absolutely need to have support.
The fourth thing that we are trying to do is about the procurement of fertilisers into the continent. Africa currently imports 1.4 million tonnes of fertilisers. Out of all of what we use we only produce 177,000 tonnes. So it is costing us a lot of money to import fertilisers. But each country is importing individually and 25 countries in Africa are importing less than 10,000 tonnes. When you do that and you are buying on the world market at very high prices because you are buying very small quantities and, when it comes into the continent, it is very small.
We have 70 per cent of the world's deposits of rock phosphate, which is what is used to manufacture phosphate fertilisers. But it is not used at all. And we need to encourage the private sector to optimise this resource for Africa. That means that the domestic manufacture of fertiliser is a big issue for Africa. Then, finally, we are looking for the financial development institutions - the World Bank, African Development Bank and others - to invest in infrastructure.
Infrastructure, so that when fertiliser lands on the continent, it can go into the landlocked countries because you have good roads, you have better rail services and you have expanded port facilities that can handle these huge volumes. Of course, all of what I have just said is going to cost a lot of money. But with the leadership of President Obasanjo and other world leaders that we are working with him on this, it can be done.
The fifth outcome is to establish what is being called the Africa Fertiliser Financing Mechanism. This would be a mechanism with a significant amount of money that is intended to be based within the African Development Bank. This multi-donor, multi-government fund will support the joint procurement and distribution of fertilisers by the regional economic communities. It will also support the private sector in local manufacture of fertilisers. And it will support African farmers.
Companies that want to do import credit guarantee that will link trained stockists in the rural areas to all the seed and fertiliser companies that are sitting there in the cities but not in the rural areas. If the government wants to do that they can tap into that facility. In essence, this facility will trigger, we believe, the dream that UN Secretary General Kofi Annan said that time the African Green Revolution is NOW.

Original_Search_Title: Food Security Prospects Brighten for Region
Headline: Southern Africa: Food Security Prospects Brighten for Region
Body_Text:
Food security prospects in southern Africa have brightened amid indications that most countries in the region are poised for a bumper harvest this year.
Almost all mainland member states of the Southern African Development Community (SADC) have indicated they are likely to harvest enough maize to meet domestic requirements and, in some instances, to export to other countries.
Countries such as Malawi, Mozambique, Zambia and Zimbabwe that have been worst hit by a series of droughts since the 2000/01 season, have for the first time this year said they will have enough food.
The four rainfall seasons since 2001 have largely been drought years save for 2003/4, which had sufficient and well-distributed rains that aided agriculture production in most parts of the SADC region.
For the first time in five years, drought-prone Malawi expects to harvest about 2.35 million metric tonnes of the staple maize crop, up from just 1.3 million tonnes in the 2004/05 season.
Malawi's domestic annual maize consumption is two million tonnes. The country's last bumper crop was 2.3 million tonnes in 1999/2000.
Officials attribute this year's improved harvest to better weather conditions and government initiative to subsidise seeds and fertiliser, which made them affordable.
The country is now forecasting its economic growth to surpass the eight percent mark this year on the back of the better crop harvests. The Malawian economy heavily depends on agriculture.
"In 2006, real GDP (Gross Domestic Product) is expected to grow by 8.4 percent on account of the anticipated bumper harvest due to good rains and the successful fertilizer and maize subsidy introduced last year," said finance minister, Goodall Gondwe.
Aid agencies in Malawi have, however, warned of shortages in parts of the country, mainly Kasungu, Karonga and Rumphi districts, that were affected by drought and floods.
The government has appointed a Vulnerability Assessment Committee comprising non-governmental organisations and UN agencies to assess harvest prospects throughout the country and identify areas needing assistance.
Zimbabwe, which has also experienced food shortages since 2001, is forecasting 1.8 million tonnes of maize for the 2005/06 season. Last season's output was a mere 550,000 tonnes.
The country requires about 1.8 million tonnes of maize per year for human consumption, stockfeeds and industrial use.
In Mozambique, Agriculture Minister Tomas Mandlate said the country's grain harvest this year is forecast at around 2.1 million tonnes, up 10 percent on the 2004/05 output of about 1.9 million tonnes.
About 1.5 million tonnes of the grain output will be maize, representing an 11 percent increase over last season.
Mozambique's annual domestic maize consumption is about 1.4 million tonnes, making the country self-sufficient this year.
However, the bulk of the maize production was in the fertile northern provinces, and transporting grain surpluses from the north to food deficit areas in the south is expensive.
The good rains in the 2005/06 season are also reflected in the high production of vegetables and cassava, which rose by 9.6 and 13.8 percent, respectively.
At 1.4 million tonnes of maize produced during the 2005/06 season, Zambia has a food surplus of 160,000 tonnes this year compared to last year's 85,000 tonnes deficit.
Zambia experienced a critical shortage of food last year, resulting in President Levy Mwanawasa declaring a national disaster.
The Zambian government has launched an initiative to provide subsidised fertilizers to farmers and employ agriculture extension officers to educate farmers on farming best practices.
Although final figures are not yet out, estimates show that South Africa, the traditional exporter of maize in the region, had planted some 1,584 hectares of maize by January.
Namibia is also hoping for a favourable yield following the above-normal rains received in the northern regions of the country.
Final figures are yet to be released by Lesotho, one of the SADC countries most vulnerable to drought and food shortages.

Original_Search_Title: Wanted! A President From My Clan
Headline: Nigeria: Wanted! A President From My Clan
Body_Text:
I promise to make life very difficult for the system and the nation in general. Yes! And please don't beg me to relent, because I won't. By the time you are reading this piece, approval should have been given to my application to protest. I have written one to the police chief because they believe in spite of all court rulings to the contrary that they have a God-given right to legalise our protests before we can embark on them. I have also forwarded a copy to Nasir el-Rufai in his capacity as the 'governor' of the FCT.
A bad friend used to say, whenever you are confused, don't just pray to God, pray also to the devil, who knows, if one is on holiday, the other might be on duty! What you need is an answer isn't it? I intend to protest the monumental injustice done to the Okun people of Kogi State. Imagine, in the 45 years of Nigeria's flag independence, no Okun man or woman has ever been 'allowed' to rule Nigeria. Our population is over 10 million (pending the breaking of Mak ama's boil!).
We speak variants of languages including Yagba, Owe, Bunu and Ijumu. We live in a contiguous area, which, if excised from Nigeria would diametrically injure the Middle Belt region and destroy the aesthetic beauty of the map of Nigeria as drawn by Lugard's girlfriend. We run the bureaucracy in our state because of our educational advancement and social sagacity (grammar? I bought it with money). Then, I also intend to protest against the injustice done against the people of Kogi State generally because charity must begin at home. The first Middle Belt president of Nigeria, Yakubu Gowon is not from Kogi State. I will be protesting the non appointment of the likes of David Medayese Jemibewon, Ibrahim Salihu, General Ejiga and several others including Colonel Ahmadu Ali. I also intend to virulently protest against Governor Abdulkhadir Kure. I don't care what he didn't say about the South-South producing the next president; I will protest the monopolisation of the M iddle Belt quota to govern in perpetuity by the Power State - Niger. Instead of spreading the right of all Middle-Belters to become head of state one after the other, Niger State avariciously and gluttonously appropriated two slots meant for the defunct North-Central Region.
They made Babangida a military president (instead of choosing Jemibewon), then they passed the baton to Abdulsalami Abubakar who again handed over to Obasanjo instead of Chief Sunday Awoniyi. Jokes apart, Obasanjo is no match for Awoniyi in political sagacity or historical level-headedness. Lest I forget, my part of Kogi State produces crops such as yam, cocoyam, wateryam, elubo, kolanuts, shear butter, palm oil and palmnut oil (which in Indonesia is a money spinner and capable of displacing crude oil as a foreign exchange earner). We also produce cassava (the golden crop so baptised by ex-minister Waziri). We also grow neem trees (another money spinner). In addition, we produce cocoa and coffee (which p roduces the delicious chocolate that is consumed with relish in Europe and America and latté, the opium of the French and Spanish). Our land is filled with natural resources such as cement, (ask Dangote who killed all other factories to install Obajana - no competition)! Our land, is rich in sulphur, gold, feldspar etc etc and if we pray hard enough, we may find the black gold behind my father's cottage in Okeagi. People, Kogi is a mini-Nigeria with borders to the East linking the enterprising Ibos; the core north, linking the ubiquitous Hausa-Fulani; the South-West, linking the irrepressible Yorubas; in fact, when they talk about Nigeria anywhere, it is my state that they talk about.
If I lie, let my left leg follow the right one when am walking! For all these, Nigeria has failed to realise our huge potentials and to accord our citizens the divine right to rule Nigeria. And all these years, I have been committing what lawyers call laches and acquiescence - from henceforth, I commit no more! I do not intend to negotiate with any group; I have not registered with any political party to help pursue this ambition of mine, I have not even turbaned anybody from my state or area to seek this exalted political office - I demand that it be reserved for Okun only. And I know that this piece that I have sneaked into the back page would arouse the consciousness of many an Okun or Kogi person who have been sleeping on their rights for this long. What matters is that I woke up this morning, thought about the injustice that the Hausa-Fulani have inflicted on my people (by producing Sardauna, Buhari, Abacha, Murtala and Shagari); then the Ibo (by producing the Greak Zik of Africa, Aguiyi-Ironsi and a half - sorry, I mean Alex Ekwueme); then the Nigerlites (for appropriating the Middle Belt slot as mentioned supra); then Plateau and Kaduna states (for co-producing Gowon); then the Yorubas through their ordained state (Ogun) for producing Obasanjo I, Shonekan and Obasanjo II). Such selfishness must be stopped by violent protests. In 2007, only an Okun man should be president of Nigeria. With an Okun man in the saddle, all the problems of Nigeria especially the ones in the Niger Delta would simply die - because, we the minority know how to silent the majority! And let me warn that if I cannot get this right by posters, protests, billboards and advertisements, then I should be able to get it by slogan.
I intend to borrow from the late Sam Mbakwe and the Emir of Hadejia, the will to buttress my point with shed tears. I will stage eternal protest like the single nuclear arms protester woman beside the White House in Washington only that in my case I shall alternate my protestations between INEC Headquarters (to give Iwu incurable headache) then to Wadata Plaza (for Garrison understanding and support); Aso Rock Villa (to perfect the art of yabbing so that Baba can nominate me for a ministerial appointment a la Fani Kayode) and to rema in in these places alternately until my demands are met. And if I find any tribe that even as much as try to stage similar protests, I shall not hesitate to kidnap, man-nap, woman-nap and stranger-nap and hold them hostage until all my demands are met! I shall also call on Gani Fawehinmi to look into the rationality, constitutionality and the righteousness of the extension of the tenure of Sunday Ehindero when Jonah Wuyep, a fellow Middle-Belter has just been retired prematurely. My rationale? Well, I have found out that ruling Nigeria is a huge, rude, sickening and unserious joke. Every clown in the circus aspires to earn a place in it. Before they go to war, soldiers are trained. Mobile policemen have their training in Gwoza before they are certified fit to kill. Before they become one, experts are trained. Before a war is fought by serious nations, plans are made, meetings are held to look into the security, economic and other implications of the declaration of hostilitie s. Generals are made to sit down and assess the likely successes, the capacity of the army to come out victorious and probable casualty figures. That, to my mind is a lot of nonsense.
Why not just go to war and hope that you win, is it because living on hope is a slim diet? To rule Nigeria, a prospective ruler need not plan, they need not consult, they need not even join any political party or the army, they need not educate themselves on what it takes or align their qualities with what is required. It simply suffices if they appeal to ethnic, tribal, sectional, parochial interests and primordial sentiments. Open your eyes, and look within - what you see is a product of planless leaders who jump into the political ring without any plan or purpose. When all else fails for a people that fail to plan, they blame it on anything. In England, they blame it on the weather, in Nigeria; we blame it on the non-inclusion of our tribe in the scheme of things. Once our tribesmen are allo wed to rule, everything else is subsumed. The Hausa-Fulani are the best for it today, they killed poverty from their domain the moment Sardauna became the prime minister up till Abubakar became a natural Father Christmas. The Ibo have been the best for it, for fighting a losing battle and producing the first president of Nigeria and the second vice president of Nigeria - there is no poverty in Igboland, which is why every village has an Igbo millionaire migrant. The people of the middle belt (except Kogi of course) are the best of it, they have produced several military rulers and all their streets are paved with gold.
Why can't the Okun too aspire! Why? Errata: Last week, the piece The Fresh Smell of Mint erroneously carried a figure 1 inviting readers to a sequel. Until then, I never believed that there is a printer's devil, now we all know it exists and so we apologise.

Original_Search_Title: Planned Allocation of US Dollars Spurs Anger in Upper East And West
Headline: Ghana: Planned Allocation of US Dollars Spurs Anger in Upper East And West
Body_Text:
Accra — Citizens of the Upper East and Upper West Regions are angry over the New Patriotic Party's (NPP) refusal to allocate funding to the regions out of the $500 million USD Millennium Challenge Account (MCA).
In a letter copied to the Public Agenda last Friday, a group calling itself the Concerned Youth and Citizens of the Upper East and Upper West Regions, argued that the exclusion of the areas from a list of MCA fund beneficiaries reflects the NPP's cold attitude towards them.
The decision, they say, lends credence to the view that the government has no plan for reducing poverty in these areas.
The letter, signed by Mr. Ayorogo Adongo Bismark, the group's president, said the group thought the government, "having said it was sensitive and committed to the plight of the poor in society, would have pursued pragmatic policies to fight poverty in the regions, especially since the government is fully aware that the two regions are the poorest, economically disadvantaged, marginalized and vulnerable.
But the decision to deny the two regions of the MCA funds shows we have been deceived...." The fund itself, which is the United States of America's 'gift' to Ghana, for upholding democratic values and good governance, is to be shared among 23 districts in six of Ghana's ten regions-the Ashanti Region, Greater Accra Region, Volta Region, Central Region, Eastern Region and the Northern Region-using poverty profiles, land quality, and project viability as criteria for receipt.
Though the Ghana Living Standard Round Four Survey (GLSS4), whose contents informed the drafting of the Ghana Poverty Reduction Strategy document (GPRS), had listed the Upper East and Upper West as among the most impoverished, worse hit by the collapse of the food crop economy, the regions are still denied the benefit of MCA funding to improve agricultural output, the mainstay of citizens in these areas.
When Public Sector Reform Minister, Dr. Paa Kwesi Nduom, appeared before parliament's vetting committee last week, northern MPs, Messers Alban Bagbin, Moses Asaga, and Edward Salia, asked him to explain why the two regions had been sidelined in the planned allocation of the fund.
Nduom's response did not go down well with the politicians. He suggested that not a single district in the two regions qualifies for funding under the criteria being employed for the funds disbursement.
Anxious to make their case heard, citizens in the areas are recounting a history rife with injustice. Since 2002, when government decided to use the Present's Special Initiatives (PSIs) on cassava, palm oil, garments and textiles, guinea fowl, cotton, and a host of other consumer goods, not a single PSI has gone to the Upper East or Upper West.
The incident has also compelled citizens of the two regions to question the rationale behind the disbursement of HIPC funds. The formula prescribed by the GPRS for HIPC resource allocation painted a sunny picture.
According to the protest letter, "the Upper East was entitled to an amount of 10.5 billion, the Upper west, 6.6 billion and the Northern region, 21.4 billion."But what actually went to the regions breaks, down as follows: Upper East, 6 billion; Upper West, 5 billion; Northern Region, 13.5 billion.
Despite these low sums, the remaining regions received well above what was promised them through the GPRS formula.The group also cites the 116 agricultural projects carried out across the country in 2002, and questions why no projects went to the Upper East and West regions.
According to the letter, 49 of the projects went to the Greater Accra region, 4 to the Ashanti, 14 to the Central, 25 to the Eastern and 14 to the Volta.
The group asks President Kufuor to consider research findings stating that poverty in the north threatens national security, and demands that he revoke the decision to leave these two northern regions out of MCA funding receipt.

Original_Search_Title: Bon Apetit
Headline: Uganda: Bon Apetit
Body_Text:
Kampala — Mention Dewinton Road and one of the first things that springs to mind is the restaurant, Bon Appetit. Bon Appetit has stood the test of time and remains a favoured place for all those who work in the vicinity and the students who visit the National Theatre across the road.
Is this Bon Appetit affiliated to the other Bon Appetit's that are springing up all over Kampala?
That I am not too sure about. What I do know is that this particular one is owned by Gera Mosha, wife of Kampala Mayor Nasser Sebaggala. Bon Appetit is more of a fast food restaurant where, on average, people spend less than 30 minutes on their meal.
Talk us through the place.
On either side of the entrance is the seating area. Beyond that is the cashier's bay, a small office and the kitchen. Like most fast food restaurants, the beauty about Bon Appetit is that you get to see what is happening in the kitchen, especially if you have ordered a take away
Is that all?
They have these huge bay windows which have been darkened so that people walking past do not get to see you but you can see them. That is where most people prefer to sit so they can look at the traffic and those walking by as they eat.
