Most fast food restaurants tend to be stuffy and cramped. Is this the same with Bon Appetit?
Not quite. They have overhead ceiling fans and from time to time there is a cool breeze that blows through.
What about the food? Was it to your liking?
Like I said earlier, Bon Appetit is more of a fast food restaurant. On the day we were there most people went in for chicken and chips, sausages and chips or kigere (cow hooves). I had the chips and sausages at sh2,900, and it was to my satisfaction. However, the salad, while fresh, did not look that healthy. That aside, just to give you an idea about what they have on their menu, there is beef and matooke, katogo, and tilapia. For snacks, they serve chaps, kebabs, cassava - that sort of thing.
THE GOOD POINTS: See the prices.
THE BAD POINTS: Trying to find parking during the lunch hour traffic.
THE PRICES: Healthy on the pocket. Average price is sh4,000 for a main course meal, sh700 for snacks and sh1,000 for drinks.
THE KABOOZI: "I did not know that Members of Parliament came here for lunch." And "Isn't our Kampala Mayor over there in the corner?"
THE STAFF: On the day we were there, we saw nothing out of the ordinary. And they did have fresh shirts and blouses.
THE TOILETS: Passable.
THE CROWD: A good number from Nile Bank, WBS Television, National Theatre and Parliament.

Original_Search_Title: Entering the Foreign Market Via Local Invention
Headline: Ethiopia: Entering the Foreign Market Via Local Invention
Body_Text:
Yitbarek Alemu wanted to explore everything at a young age. But maintaining radios and watches was his favorite. He even purposely dismantles things in order to reassemble them later. Now that he is a professional chemist, he believes that the things that he used to do at his early years were initiations to his journey. Currently, he is a researcher and owns a starch company.
Yitbarek, general manager of the Yitbarek Alemu Starch Chemicals and Adhesive Industry (YASCAI), has founded a starch producing company, which is the first of its kind in the country. YASCAI produces starch and starch products 100% from locally available raw materials. He expects to fully substitute imports and generate foreign currency from exports.
The starch industry is an intermediate input for textile industries, paper industries, food industries and glucose. For instance, the Dire Dawa Textile factory buys starch from YASCAI. In addition to this, the starch that is used for glucose is important for beverages and needed for clinical purposes. Meanwhile, the adhesive is wanted by beverage industries, cement industries, tobacco industries and packaging industries. The adhesive is also useful for making dextrime. Of the three parts of dextrime, black, white and yellow, Yitbarek produces the black one. Starch is extracted from wheat, maize, potato etc. But in this case he is the first man to extract starch from Enset - also known as false banana. He is now producing starch from cassava.
"The plant cassava is not well known in the country but now I am working with farmers around Langano to produce the plant. The main advantage of producing cassava is that the country's environment is suitable for its production. That is, it has the characteristics of growing during rainy as well as dry seasons and can grow all year round".
"Countries like Nigeria and Ghana have begun exporting cassava to the U.S and Canada. So, there will be nothing that can prevent us from doing the same. It is also a prospect for export diversification".
Starch types differ. They all are chemically the same but they vary physically. Therefore, the enset-made starch differs from the others and will consume starch which will make to exploit resources effectively and efficiently. Furthermore, the enset tree stands against hunger and food insecurity and is therefore domesticated largely in Ethiopia. This guarantees constant supply of esent for starch.
The company started production six years ago and its expansion project was finalized last Saturday. According Yitbarek, the company has a capacity of producing 10 tonnes of starch per day and the country's demand is now only three tonnes per day. Hence, the company has full capacity to fulfill the required amount. As a result, Yitbarek believes that it will completely curb the need for the importation of starch.
"Currently we have 48 employees and when the new company starts full production, we are going to employ more than 100 workers. And I have a plan to export the product," he explained.
When he began establishing the company, its overall cost was about 30 thousands birr, now, including the expansion project, the cost has gone up to about 8.5 million birr.
Yitbarek admitts, however, that the technology in most factories is backward. This reflects a weakness in the development of technology based upon experience rather than a systematic study of mechanisms. Consequently, some operational processes cannot be flexibly adapted or modified to guarantee the quality of products. This indicates an inadequacy in technological personnel, which hampers the popularization of more mature technologies.
To avoid this problem, Yitbarek is trying to develop the technology locally. Most of the time the country is importing an enormous amount of technology. Accordingly, it will reduce the amount of dependency on foreign goods and as a result it will spur innovation and invention.
In the construction of his machines, Yitbarek did not import materials. Instead he utilized locally available products. While he was working in public companies he has made various drinks from fruits and develops his license that some of his products were on the market.
People prefer to buy foreign goods rather than local products. This will discourage mushrooming industries. In addition to this, lack of capital has been another factor which delayed the completion of the project. According to him, in the country, capital facility by the banks doesn't attract investors. Finally, he added, getting the space for his factory has been an other problem.
Ato Tadesse Haile, Minister of State in the Ministry of Trade and Industry, said that this inventor and investor must be appreciated in creating starch and the technology for processing it.
"I had never seen this kind of strong investment in this country and it is a boulevard for other investors to engage in such kind of activities. This is the first company in the country in terms of its type and quality ".
The government has given due consideration to textile industries and this starch product can be an input to the industry, according to Ato Tadesse. The company is using the farmers' product like tomato and enset for producing starch. This will bridge the gap between industry and agriculture.
And on behalf of industries there is a dire used to buy locally-produced starch as an input in order to save the country's foreign currency.
In our push for the industrialization process, the research and development of technology is also crucial. Technology should not be just a means of expanding traditional methods; it involves different, modern methods which can nevertheless be based on the mechanisms of the old.
Coordination among specialized enterprises is essential for the development of large-scale socialized production. Coordination among various fields, and links between suppliers and demands, are especially needed in the sector, particularly by enterprises doing intensive processing.
YASCAI has received an award from the Ethiopian Science and Technology Commission, a national award for outstanding achievements and invention, gold medal, Special award certificate from the Chemical Engineers Society.

Original_Search_Title: Reactivating Nigeria's Non Oil Export
Headline: Nigeria: Reactivating Nigeria's Non Oil Export
Body_Text:
Since crude oil was discovered in Nigeria about four decades ago, the country's non oil export has gradually declined.
This is primarily due to its overdependence on oil revenue which has over the years become the primary source of national income. The industrial and agricultural sectors of the economy which are supposed to be the major revenue earners outside the oil sector have not grown significantly enough to make meaningful contributions to improving the foreign exchange earnings of the country.
According to the economic report for the first quarter of 2006 which was recently released by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), Nigeria's non oil export by top 100 exporters in the first three months of this year was only $171.49million (about N24 billion). "Non-oil export earnings by Nigeria's top 100 exporters in the first quarter of 2006 amounted to US$171.49 million, indicating an increase of 5.3 per cent over the preceding quarter's level. A breakdown of the proceeds in the quarter showed that proceeds in respect of the agricultural sub-sector, manufacturing sub-sector and "others" rose from US$97.56 million, US$58.88 and US$6.37 million in the preceding quarter to US$98.74 million, US$60.62 million and US$12.14 million, respectively.
The shares of agricultural, manufacturing and "others" sub-sectors in total non-oil export proceeds were 57.6, 35.3 and 7.1 per cent, respectively, compared with 59.9, 36.2 and 3.9 per cent, in the preceding quarter. The rise in the shares of agricultural and manufacturing sub-sectors was attributable largely to variations in the prices of the goods traded at the international market.
The top 100 exporters accounted for 99.7 per cent of all the non-oil export proceeds in the first quarter of 2006." Although the report shows an increase in non oil exports in the first quarter of 2006, as compared to the last quarter of 2005, the input it made to the country's foreign exchange earning is still insignificant com pared to the $7.09 billion the country made in foreign exchange, between January and March 2006. Some of the factors responsible for the low output in the industrial sector of the economy include inadequate infrastructure like power, water supply and transportation.
The agricultural sector on the other hand has been bedeviled with problems that range from high cost of farm inputs like fertilizer, improved seedlings and lack of modern farm implements. Weak economic policies have also led to the dearth of thousands local industries, especially in the northern part of the country where textiles have been badly devastated. In the last few years, more than 80 percent of textile firms have had to close shops because of unfavorable economic policies as well as increase in the activities of illegal importers of cheap foreign textiles.
Although Nigeria's position as the 6th largest oil exporter in the world, coupled with the high oil prices that have prevailed in recent years have gr eatly shot up the country's foreign exchange earnings, export in other sectors of the economy have continued to remain low. The $171 million earned from non-oil exports in the first quarter of the year represents only about 2 percent of the county's total foreign exchange earnings during the same period. In reaction to the continuous dependence on oil and poor attention to development of the non oil export sector, foreign trade experts have continuously blamed the country as being responsible for her inability to boost non- oil export, a sector that offers great opportunities for job creation and poverty alleviation.
They have persistently blamed Nigeria for not making good use of the opportunities created by the African Growth Opportunity Act (AGOA), a United States policy that seeks to allow sub-Saharan African countries duty-free access to its market for about 6,400 products. The purpose of AGOA is to use preferential trade access to the US market as a catalyst for econom ic growth in sub-Saharan Africa by encouraging governments to open their economies and build free markets. It amends the U.S. generalized system of preferences to grant duty-free treatment to specified products from eligible countries.
US Congress passed AGOA as part of the Trade and Development Act of 2000, and President Bill Clinton signed it into law on May 18, 2000. In August 2002, President George W. Bush signed amendments to AGOA that expanded preferential access for eligible sub-Saharan African counties. Two years later, President Bush signed the AGOA Acceleration Act of 2004, which extended preferential access for imports from eligible sub-Saharan African countries until September 30, 2015, and extended and clarified textile-related provisions in the Act.
As the law now stands, nearly all imports from eligible countries in sub-Saharan Africa enter the US duty-free through 2015. Since its inception in 2000, AGOA has helped increase U.S. two-way trade with sub-Saharan Africa by 115 percent. In 2005, U.S. total exports to sub-Saharan Africa rose 22 percent from 2004, to $10.3 billion. U.S. total imports from Africa increased by 40 percent to $50.3 billion. In 2005, over 98 percent of U.S. imports from AGOA-eligible countries entered the United States duty-free. In 2004, Nigeria only exported non oil products worth $2million to the USA, a performance that has been widely considered to be too poor for a nation like this. Besides AGOA, Nigeria needs to effectively devise polices that should begin to significantly boost non-oil export to not only diversify the economy, but also form a strong backbone for future developmental strategies.
The initiative of the present government's NEEDS agenda should be supported and sustained to encourage vibrant and aggressive development of the non-oil sector of the economy. Agriculture, tourism and industry could serve this purpose. For instance, the present drive to enhance industrial use of cassava as well as its export, boosting cocoa production are all steps in the right direction. Reviving the old groundnut pyramids of Kano as well as the popular hides and skin business in the north have all been prescribed by experts as some of the ways the country can boost its non-oil exports.

Original_Search_Title: Visit to Garamba, Kony's New Hide-Out
Headline: Uganda: Garamba: Kony's New Hide-Out
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE New Vision's Emmy Allio was recently in Aba, a town in the environs of Garamba National Park in the northeastern Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), to unveil the mystery surrounding the Lord's Resistance Army's (LRA) presence in Congo. This is what he found out.
AWARE of the poor state of the roads in Congo and the uncertain security, it was better to travel to Aba through Yei, southern Sudan. I had travelled on the Yei-Lasu road enroute to Aba nine years back. It was a good road then. Based on my experience, I had convinced my companion, Els de Temmerman, that travelling through Yei was good, especially in a good car.
De Temmerman was excited about the journey to the Garamba region, the new home of the LRA's estimated 300-500 fighters. Sudan People's Liberation Army (SPLA) officer Brig. Samson Mabior provided us with an escort of SPLA soldiers.
The 50km stretch from Yei to Lasu took us three and a half hours. It is a bad, muddy road with deep potholes, snaking through a thick forest. The forest, mostly of teak trees, starts near Yei and stretches into the densely forested Garamba National Park. It is an ideal place for guerrilla warfare.
Atop a hill in Central Lasu was a large stone building, the SPLA's garrison for monitoring the Congo border.
"The LRA rebels passed here on their way to Garamba. They robbed people of food and animals, but they have not crossed again since we were deployed," said Wilson Mawuit Anai, the elderly SPLA officer in charge of Lasu.
As we entered Congo, the SPLA soldiers introduced us to their Congolese counterparts. The intermediary was an SPLA soldier who spoke fluent Lingala and Kiswahili. Although basically a guerrilla army, the SPLA was fairly well mannered and dressed in military tunics. In sharp contrast, however, the Congolese army (FARDC) was dressed badly and not disciplined. Many of the soldiers wore sandals, but none forgot to carry a gun. This is the Elite Commando Battalion sent to Aba last October to fight the LRA.
After about 20 minutes of disagreement over whether to allow us into Congo, Lt. Zilber, who was in charge of the border post, prevailed over his overzealous and noisy soldiers.
"They will go to Aba, but I must escort them," he said. This was for a small fee.
After looking at our passports, the immigration officer did not stamp them but asked for US$10 each to let us enter Aba town. Like in southern Sudan, the currency used here is the Uganda shilling.
After frantic negotiations, Temmerman paid sh20,000 for the three passports. Another sh20,000 was paid to the security officers to clear our car. The 18km road to Aba town is newly graded. Halfway on the road to Aba, Lt. Zilber handed us over to Lt. Petit Petit.
Aba is an old town with buildings as old as 80 or 100 years. Petit took us to Capt. John Peter Molengo, the commander of the Elite Commando battalion, an integrated army unit. Present during the meeting with Capt. Molengo was the immigration officer, Tshindembo Masongo, who later demanded sh60,000 (US$30) to stamp our passports.
Another was border frontier officer Lt. Germain Peire Mesoit, who did most of the talking on behalf of Capt. Molengo, and the chief of Aba town, Cyprian Suruaki.
"Our enemy is the UPDF," Molengo said as Mesoit nodded in agreement. "We are gathering evidence of UPDF infiltration and handing it over to the UN."
The commando battalion has deployed all its troops along the Congo-Sudan border to fight any possible incursions by the UPDF and SPLA, contrary to their original mission of fighting the LRA inside Garamba National Park.
However, there is hardly any Congolese army presence in the northern parts of Garamba, where the LRA are believed to be operating.
While displaying strong hostile feelings against Uganda, the FARDC commanders in Aba could hardly hide their sympathy for the LRA.
They talked with great excitement about the four meetings they had had with the rebels, including two encounters with Vincent Otti himself. They described Otti as a nice man who is not offensive.
When the interview with Molengo ended, he said, "Such an important interview is not for nothing. Do something small." I gave him sh50,000, which was on top of another sh50,000 he had received for allowing us to "operate" in his town. Dissatisfied, he left me and confronted de Temmerman for more money.
As he pocketed his money, his guards and other security officials demanded for their share. It was an awkward situation. We gave out the money in denominations of sh10,000s, sh5,000s, sh1,000s and coins. In just an hour my pocket was reading minus sh255,000!
De Temmerman had told me she would not part with any money. I warned her that, at times, in desperate situations, one has to bend one's principles to get out of difficult situations. Three soldiers came to her side of the window and, pointing guns at her, asked for money. Without checking, she pulled out the money and gave it to them.
In the market and suburbs, the situation was more appalling. About 30 women sat in the central market area selling onions, tomatoes and dried cassava.
Soldiers lingered around them, not as buyers but waiting for sala evre or "do it", meaning "give some little money".
Civilians say LRA rebels can easily be identified because they ask for the origin of dried game meat sold in markets in Aba, Misa and Kurukwata.
In Aba, the LRA rebels are rare. "Because of the big Congolese army presence in Aba, the rebels no longer come here openly. But in Kurukwata and Misa, the rebels move about openly," said Kamba, a local palm oil dealer.
"In Kurukwata, the local people asked the Congolese army to explain the open presence of the LRA rebels, but the FARDC officers said LRA rebels are not dangerous because they do not disturb the Congolese population or army," Kamba said.
He added that the LRA is hardly seen in military uniform. Many of them speak Arabic and are easily identified because they do not speak Lingala or Kiswahili.
Tasile Lopayi, 43, a teacher in Aba, said, "To the Congolese civilians, the enemy is the Congolese army, not the LRA.
FARDC robs and deprives us of our food and belongings. The LRA sells to us dry meat cheaply. We hate to be arrested and jailed in military detachments."
Garamba National Park is a massive 4,900 sqkm piece of land established in 1938 and managed by a consortium of conservation donors under the auspices of the International Union for the Conservation of Nature and its Resources.
The park authorities control only a third of the southern part of the park near Nagero, where there is a tarmacked airfield and a lodge. Two-thirds of the park is free and is often frequented by international poachers.
Tension is not new in and around Garamba. It has been rising in Sudan and Congo, particularly in the northern environs of Garamba, between the local Zande population and the 'invaders': the Mbororo and Bor Dinkas.
At least 40 people died when the Zande and Bor Dinka clashed last November in Yambio, Sudan, when the SPLM/A appointed a local person, Patrick Zumai, as the state governor of the western Equatoria region. It was believed that the riots were a direct expression of anger by the Zande against the invaders. In this case, the invaders were the Mbororo and Bor Dinkas.
The local people talk more of Mbororo than the LRA. In fact, they talk of armed poachers. "Whether LRA, Mbororo or Bor Dinka, we know that these are poachers moving with guns like soldiers. Now the local Congolese poachers have feared to enter Garamba," one elder said.
The LRA's entry into Garamba last September affected the delicate balance of power in the park. Of more significance, however, is the fact that the LRA may soon or later lose its original identity.
LRA is known for killing whoever is opposed to them and abducting people in northern Uganda or Sudan. They dislodged the Bor Dinka from their cherished position of leadership among the international poachers. Sources said that through careful maneuvers, LRA quickly learned to co-exist with the Mbororo and Bor Dinka.
For example, the LRA co-existed with remnants of the West Nile Bank Front (WNBF) until they killed WNBF leader Musa Dudu on January 21, 2006. Two days later, the LRA killed the Guatemalan UN peacekeepers who were responding to gunshots emanating from that fighting. Sources said that after this incident, Otti ordered the rebels never to move with guns or rob a civilian.
What has happened is that the LRA has dropped its most known inhuman habits of killing and torturing people. The rebels are now forced to adjust and live like human beings because they are in Garamba to hide and survive from the UPDF, SPLA and the International Criminal Court ICC). They cannot foment trouble because they are living alongside other armed international poachers. Yet the best way to fight Kony is long occupation of Garamba by a large force.
"The fortunes of Joseph Kony and Vincent Otti have changed after Dr. Riek Machar gave them US$20,000 and five tonnes of food. This will keep them inside the park," said a senior SPLA officer. "It was unwise to give that money to the LRA. What will happen when it gets finished? How much money should be paid to the LRA when our fighters are starving?"
Tension in Garamba is high, with the UPDF and SPLA deployed on the DRC-Sudan border. Should Kony and Otti's sudden change of heart deceive ICC, UPDF and SPLA to accept a peaceful option?

Original_Search_Title: Kakira Cane Scheme Creates Millionaires
Headline: Uganda: Kakira Cane Scheme Creates Millionaires
Body_Text:
Kampala — FARMERS in Busoga region are reaping big from Kakira Sugar Works Outgrowers Scheme. Most of them have shifted from growing traditional crops to growing sugarcane because of the assured market and good earnings.
Farmers, under their umbrella body, Busoga Sugarcane Growers Association, with a membership of over 4,000 farmers in the districts of Jinja, Bugiri, Iganga and Kamuli, grow the crop on an area of over 10,000 hectares. They also produce 50% of the sugarcane for Kakira Sugar Works.
According to Eric Katarikawe, the manager of Kakira Sugar Works Outgrowers Scheme, the company advises farmers about the proper growing of the crop and supports them on the costs of cultivation up to harvesting period.
"Our extension services managers do sensitisation campaigns among farmers around Kakira. Whoever accepts to grow sugarcane is assisted with tractors to plough their land, harrowing and then opening of furrows in the gardens. We also supply them with sugarcane seeds and fertilisers but they take care of the growing cane," says Katarikawe.
"The farmers get services they want from us and pay later after harvesting. When the cane is mature, we issue permits to farmers for the quantities to be supplied to us. We recover our money in three installments over the age of the crop, which is about 65 months. We recover 40% initially at the time of the first harvesting after 18/20 months, at the second harvesting, we recover another 30% and finally another 30% at the third time. We give our farmers utmost freedom to repay."
Katarikawe says they want the number of the beneficiaries to increase to 6,000 farmers from the current 4,000 and be able to cover 17,000 hectares up from 10,000 by 2007.
He says outgrowers employ over 500,000 individuals.
Livingstone Mulumba, a farmer in Bugiri district, has 20 acres and is able to harvest 800 tonnes of cane every 18 months, which fetch him a good income.
"Kakira gives us over sh30,000 per tonne of cane and I get more than sh24m every 18 months. I have a house worth over sh40m, my 10 children are all studying in good schools and I have other prospering businesses," Mulumba says.
Francis Samanya of Busedde sub-county in Iganga says he has 70 acres and harvests 3,800 tonnes of cane every harvesting time and earns over sh100m.
"I started growing sugarcane for Kakira in 1990 with no personal land. I used land belonging to my friends on a leasehold arrangement. But now out of the 70 acres on which I cultivate, 30 acres are mine and I hope to buy more land to sustain sugarcane growing. I have a house worth sh50m, three lorries that carry my cane to the factory and a tractor which tills my land and all my 15 children are studying with no hardship," he testifies.
Mawazi Mukwawa of Namataba in Iganga, who started cane growing in 1991, says his 50 acres give him 40 tonnes per acre because he uses fertilisers.
"I joined sugarcane cultivation after Kakira officials came to our village and educated us about the benefits. We had been relying on coffee, banana and cassava as food and cash crops but all have been affected by the wilt and mosaic, respectively. Sugarcane is now the biggest source of income in most parts of Busoga region, those without it are poor," says Mukwawa.
With good response of farmers, Kakira management projects that its sugar production is to grow from the current 3,500 tonnes a day to 5,000 tonnes by the 2007.
"Our sugar plantation is 10,000 hectares and we can't expand any further, so the growth of Kakira Sugar Works depends on the number of outgrowers to supply us with enough cane," says Katarikawe.

Original_Search_Title: Fighting Poverty in Niger Delta
Headline: Nigeria: Fighting Poverty in Niger Delta
Body_Text:
Lagos — That the Niger Delta is underdeveloped despite being the producer of the nation's oil wealth is no longer in contention. While the illiteracy level is very high and basic infrastructure is poor, a new approach to redress the situation has been put in place by FREE, an NGO that is working in the rural areas of Bayelsa State and beyond.
Step into Mrs. Alaere Augustina Timi-Alaibe's office at the Toyin Street, Ikeja, Lagos headquarters of the Family Re-orientation Education and Empowerment (FREE) and it soon becomes obvious that the foundation which was founded less than five years ago by the humanitarian and philanthropist, has been quietly touching the lives of the poor people of the Niger Delta, especially in Bayelsa. The confine of her offices expresses her heart to a first time visitor.
On the wall of her office is littered with different posters of the activities of FREE and some of them read: "Teach a woman, you teach the nation," "Legislators save our children" and "Human trafficking is renewed slave trade." From giving stipends to some rural women and child-parents who comes around her any time she visited home for financial help, Mrs. Timi-Alaibe has in the last five years built one of the largest foundation that has been able to transform lives of the rural and coastal dwellers of the Niger Delta.
She told THISDAY in an interview in her office recently that the idea of the foundation came while attending to these people. In the process, she also noticed that not only were the girls rude and lacked manners and moral characters but there was an increase in teenage pregnancy as a result of poverty. She then decided at that point that giving them fish will not solve their problem and instead she better started teaching them how to fish.
According to her, for many years, the Niger Delta area of the country has suffered untold hardship, neglect and deprivation, even though it has been the host since the early 1950s of all Nigeria's oil and gas resources and provided about 90 per cent of the country's foreign exchange earnings. In spite of all the development efforts put in place to address the problems of ignorance, literacy, poverty and lack of infrastructures, she said it was not until 2000 that a most significant decision was made by the government of President Olusegun Obasanjo to facilitate the sustainable development of this otherwise forgotten rich region.
The FREE boss explained that while the Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC), which Obasanjo established has made giant strides in this regard, it was still inevitable, due to the long years of neglect and the resultant degree of poverty, ignorance and underdevelopment, to set up a non-governmental organisation which would compliment these efforts, help to alleviate the sufferings of the people and reduce the level of violence which had risen across the region. "It was also necessary to compartmentalise the region, using such indices as type of community (rural or urban), type of intervention and gender in order to address specific needs and challenges," she stressed.
Mrs. Timi-Alaibe further told THISDAY that for her to turn around the lives of these women and children, education is vital. With the assistance of her friends and husband, Mr. Timi Alaibe a director at NDDC, she started adult education centres starting from Opokuma-Kolokuma and Tolofani local government areas of Bayelsa State. Presently, there are about 23 with present population 500 pupils in the adult education centres scattered in Bayelsa, Delta, Akwa Ibom and Cross River States. Added to illiteracy and ignorance, teenage pregnancy, indiscriminate sexual activities, especially among the under-aged persons, she noted that the outbreak of HIV-AIDS and other sexually transmitted diseases (STDs) are rife.
The foundation has also discovered that in some cases even high blood pressure, diabetes, malaria and breast cancer are not diagnosed early and therefore present difficult problems for the women. "For instance, in one of FREE's tours, a breast cancer patient was discovered and because of early detection and intervention, a radical mastectomy was facilitated by the foundation through funds contributed by an intervention agency who is one of FREE's partners," the founder emphasised.
Apart from the adult literacy programme which is meant to educate and empower the women, there are awareness campaigns on good morals, discipline, hygiene and hardwork as well as micro-financing, health awareness and counselling. The objective is to reorient the women and the girl-child to become better members of the society, teach them their rights and improve their outlook on life, increase their hygiene and health awareness, make the rural communities a better place to leave in and the people healthier and happier as well as make champions of women and to reduce ignorance, illiteracy and poverty through adult literacy programme.
Other aims of the programmes include reducing disease and death through health awareness, campaigns and counselling, educating the local communities on simple environmentally sustainable agriculture, empowering local people with knowledge of environmental issues, especially on capacity building, skill acquisition, giving business support and micro-credit scheme. Others encompasses propagating sustainable strategic partnerships with other NGOs and foundations on non-timber forest products in order to preserve the natural ecosystem, to seek and to empower rural women with micro-credit finance in other to aid and strengthen their businesses. Areas covered by FREE in its projects are Okpokuma, Twon Brass, Okolobiri, Okordia, Polaku, Igbogene, Ekeremor, Ofoni and Odioma. Others are Okpoama, Egwama, Akassa, Kaiama, Sabagreia, Otrofani, Adagbabiri and Yenagoa.
Some other projects accomplished side by side its main focus include the giving away of coolers for the non-timber forest products through raffle draw in Odi last year, giving away of coolers, motorcycles, handsets and standing hair driers and clippers through raffle for the youths in Okpokuma in 2004. The same activities as well as health talks were conducted in Kaiama last year April. Last year also, the foundation organised health, moral and motherhood talks, handed out gift to widows during Christmas and conducted several trainings for traditional birth attendants in collaboration with White Ribbon Alliance in Adagbabiri, Sabagreia and Okoloba areas of the state last year.
Last February, the foundation launched the Kaiama cassava farming initiative, where about 16 hectres of farm land was acquired by the foundation for group of women that formed themselves into cooperatives and also beneficiaries of the adult education. Last Thursday, THISDAY was among other media houses that visited various FREE projects in Bayelsa. At the Kaiama Cassava farm project, the large expanse of cassava and maize farm were lush green as the women belonging to the Wopo Ebinimi and Kimi Eniewe Kumo Cooperative Societies attended to the crops.
One of the women, Mrs. Bose Pigni 58, who is also a primary one pupil at the adult school expressed gratitude to Mrs. Timi-Alaibe for coming back home to assist her folks, describing her work in the communities as a legacy. Mrs. Pigni said she can now count and write one 1, 2, 3 and A, B, C. She threw her audience into laughter when she said that she can now count her money in the market. Instead of identifying the currencies by the pictures like "three Murtala" for N60, she can now count and identify it as N60.
The most popular of all the projects is the adult education which the National Adult Education Commission (NAEC) has identified and supported by donating various books to the foundation. FREE is presently working with the commission to see that some of the books are translated in Ijaw languages. In some of the adult education centres visited, classes begin at about 4.00pm after the women had come back from their daily businesses-market to sell their fish and ogbono (ogbolo) or from the farm or fishing. In some of the centres, you have both the young and the old, mostly women and in some cases with babies strapped on their back. One of the teachers, Mr. Ebitimi Toun said such women don't distract him, because as an adult education, there is certain level of tolerance.
The unique aspect of the adult education is that Mrs. Timi-Alaibe's FREE make use of the employed primary school teachers in the area, empowering them with extra income. One of the teachers told journalists at Kaiama that the women despite that they are adults behave like children in the classroom. This assertion was dramatised when the journalists entered one of the classes in session. Immediately the pupils, the age ranging between 20 to 60 years saw strange group of visitors enter the class, the prefect hits the desk three times and the class responds: "Good evening sirs." The classes range from primary one to six. Those that have not been to school at all before now starts from primary one, while those who dropped out of school are placed at the appropriate class according to the level and the class they got to before dropping out.
Mrs. Ebimere Torru expressed her appreciation to FREE "for making it possible for every Trofani woman to access education. There has been a lot of transformation in my life also what has been a very big problem is now a thing of the past." Elizabeth Gbeke said to Mrs. Timi-Alaibe: "I thank you very much for establishing adult education in the community for us. For instance, I stopped school since 1976, but as you established this adult education, it has afforded me the opportunity to continue my abandoned education."
THISDAY also met Miss Ineye Iganran, a paragon of beauty but paralysed from waste down. Ineye who had earlier acquired some skills as a fashion designer had wrote to Mrs. Timi-Alaibe for help was a recipient of FREE wheel chair and sowing machines. Ineye said she now makes her own money and as a result do not depend on any body for arms and sustenance. She is one of those that have benefited from FREE's philanthropy. The foundation's health centre at Opokuma, close to Alaibe's country home is almost completed. The project is being constructed by FREE in collaboration with screen queen Patti Boulaye's Support for Africa Charity. The centre which will house an HIV-AIDS screening centre is to be commissioned tomorrow with the Nigerian-born actress in attendance.
The commissioning of the centre will be followed by a benefit dinner on Sunday, hold at the Expo Centre, Eko Hotel and Suites, Victoria Island, Lagos. FREE will present to the audience a compelling documentary on the poverty in Niger Delta and what it has been doing to alleviate the scourge, while renowned experts in social sciences will present papers on poverty eradication. The overall objective, according to Mrs. Timi-Alaibe is to present a model that has worked and should serve as a reference for policy makers with a view to attract resources, develop multi-stakeholder approach, build alliances, coalitions and networks through effective participatory approach.

Original_Search_Title: Widowhood Rites On Ascendancy in Asutifi
Headline: Ghana: Widowhood Rites On Ascendancy in Asutifi
Body_Text:
Kenyasi — Widowhood rites and food taboos have been identified as two cultural and traditional practices on the increase in the Asutifi district in the Brong Ahafo region.
This came to light at a two-day advocacy and campaign workshop on women's rights issues at Kenyasi, organized by the Center for Active Development (CAD) and sponsored by Action Aid International, Ghana.
The workshop was attended by 50 participants and was themed "sexual violence against women is a crime: stop now".
One of the participants, Rose Owusuaa, narrated the ordeal she went through as part of widowhood rites when her husband died.
According to Madam Owusuaa, she was forced to wash down three times a day with cold water, notwithstanding the condition of the weather.
Again anytime she was visiting her husband's house she had to cry and that she was also stopped from eating root crops like cassava and cocoyam all in the name of widowhood rites.
She said apart from all these traumatic experiences she went through for a whole year, the family of her late husband refused to take care of her children.
The facilitator for the workshop, Madam Fati Mumuni, cautioned parents against what she termed "burger marriages", adding that some of such marriages are facades to lure women into prostitution in Europe.
Madam Fati urged women not to starve their husbands since that could lead to violence in the home.
She stressed that the advocacy of gender equality does not mean rubbing shoulders with husbands; a situation, she noted, could jeopardize matters in the family.
Fati condemned the attitude of some women who have taken stringent positions that they would never kowtow to men after knowing their rights, saying, "Feminism can never achieve gender equality and when we don't take care, it would be worse than previously".
She hinted that due to the rise of feminism in the world recently, some men too have come out as patriarchs to stand against feminism.
The Executive Director of CAD, P.K. Boakye, appealed to parliament to speed up action on the passage of the Domestic Violence Bill, which, according to him, would reduce the incidence of violence in the family.

Original_Search_Title: Taking the Long Road Back Home
Headline: Sudan: Taking the Long Road Back Home
Body_Text:
After more than 22 years in exile, a group of Sudanese nationals returned home under a UNHCR arrangement. They are determined to help with the reconstruction of their country
It was very quiet at the Action Africa Hilfe- UNHCR run refugee collection camp on May 26. Anxiety hung over Palorinya transit base as a batch of 282 Sudanese prepared to bid farewell to their Ugandan hosts.
Palorinya lies about 25 kilometres South East of Moyo town at the shores of Albert Nile. Only the distant chirruping of aquatic fowls and livestock movements kept the camp alive.
Then suddenly, a young man, one of the refugee clerks bellowed a few words in Arabic and Bar (Sudanese local lingua) over a megaphone and soon, the whole place exploded into a flurry of activity.
Elderly parents, their youthful and energetic children along with tender children tried to outpace one another collectingplates and jostling for lead positions in a long queue for food. It was lunchtime.
In fact, this was their last meal in Uganda as refugees under the providence of the UN refugee agency. The resident waitress sliced the yellow posho with such precision that every cut produced just about the same quantity.
Another girl by her side dipped a blue plastic cup into the dark bean soup, scooping an equal measure for each of the passing recipients.
Fred Otim, the Assistant field officer for UNHCR in Uganda said the refugees are first gathered into the collection centre from the 27 outlying cluster settlements for purposes of ascertaining their number, status quo, medical screening, psycho-social counselling and enabling them to sign the voluntary repatriation form as a personal consent and commitment to willingly return home.
"The hallmark of the repatriation exercise is safety of the refugees, their dignity and voluntarism," explains Mr Tarik Muftic, the head of UNHCR in Kajo Keji. "We want to ensure that nobody is forced to go back to Sudan". Each family head that signs the consent form is given one 20-litre jerry can and a cup to use for drinking water along the long journey to Sudan.
The resident medical officer, Alex Atiku said the departing exiles are checked for sleeping sickness, respiratory ailments and other communicable diseases. "Our aim is to ensure that the people going back to Sudan are in good health and those with chronic health complications are identified and referred for proper medical care," he said.
Other items given include condoms although they are shunned by some on the premise that their women are "not promiscuous" and that the condoms take the pleasure out of sex. That evening, the refugees began stacking their 'exile property' into four big UNHCR Isuzu trucks.
It was a sad moment as the returnees loaded everything; ranging from broken doorframes, worn-out drums, old papyrus mats, goats, dogs and cats to rickety bicycles, foodstuffs, torn mattresses and even earthen pots. Early, on May 27, a convoy of three light vehicles and seven Lorries led by Mr Simon Passi, the UNHCR Assistant Logistics Officer in Moyo, started for Sudan, rattling through the bushes and thickets of the 7, 401.1 hectare Era forest reserve.
It was barely 30 minutes into the journey when one of the trucks carrying furniture and bicycles crashed at a sharp bend at Onigo, as the driver tried to avoid hitting a village woman who had suddenly emerged from the nearby bush. Scared of reprisal attacks, the the woman dropped the bundle of firewood on her head and sped into the forest like a possessed soul.
Abubaker Adraiga, the driver of the ill-fated truck sustained serious chest injuries and deep cuts on the head and was swiftly rushed for treatment together with his co-driver at Moyo hospital, 3.2 kilometres ahead.
It was not until 20 minutes later that the journey resumed but most of the refugees were by then agitated over their broken bicycles.
Getting home
At 10:47a.m, the usually silent and sparsely populated Kangai neighbourhood erupted into activity, wild cheers and ululations as the long motorcade made its way into the vast UNHCR base camp with the returnees.
A musical group was at the gate drumming and singing, happy to receive the lot of kinsmen long separated by 22 years of insurgency.
The much revered governor of Sudan's Central Equatorial State, Maj. Gen. Clement Wani Konga accompanied by Jamba Doku, the Speaker of the Juba Assembly, Housing and Public Utilities minister Alakaya Aligo, the Education minister Lekulenge Lole, Agriculture minister Charles Jongo, 10 MPs and 23 aides all gathered to receive the new batch of 68 families.
"I thank the government of Uganda very profoundly for hosting our citizens since 1955 and especially so in the last 22 years of our civil wars. We are also grateful to UNHCR for facilitating this marvellous return," Konga said in his address.
After assessing the weary returnees and assuring them of peace and social services, he pulled out a bundle of US dollars from his briefcase and gave the refugees $600 to buy bulls and feast upon their homecoming. "So far, those (2,181) returnees have been well received by the community and their peaceful integration is going on course," UNHCR head in Kajo Keji, Mr Kouassi Lazare said.
Adjusting
The bulk of the ex-exiles, according to one resident, Julius Jonijo, have embarked on planting fast-yielding crops like maize, potatoes and cassava to stave off possible famine when their three-months food rations provided by UNHCR-American Refugee Committee run out. "They have settled down completely to cultivate because they know no other NGO or UNHCR is going to give them any more handouts," Jonijo said.
According to Diallo N'en'e, the UNHCR community services officer in Kajo Keji, each returnee is given 40.5kgs of flour, 18.8kgs of beans, 2.7 litres of cooking oil, 1.4kgs of sugar and 0.9kgs of salt as a three-months upfront resettlement package to kick start life.
The returnees additionally receive kitchen equipment, plastic sheets (tarpaulin), mats, blankets, mosquito nets, soap and sanitary ware as well as buckets and jerry cans. In spite of these humane offers, the fear of the unknown still hampers the repatriation exercise and the precarious security situation is the most poignant.
Information from NGOs and government agencies that there are landmines in Liri, Nyepo and Limolo payams (the equivalent of sub-counties) where the other refugees were to settle, has demoralised stakeholders and paralysed the exercise in Moyo. Over 2,000 refugees who registered for relocation to the fairly secure Kangapo I and II Payams have virtually all been relocated.
There are 11 primary schools and three secondary schools where the new settlers can enroll but most of the candidate classes usually sit for Ugandan examinations from border schools (on the Ugandan side.)
It will take a little while for the situation there to totally normalise and the service provision level to improve but the ex-exiles have the onus to be around and reconstruct their war-ravaged country.

Original_Search_Title: The Woes And Joys of Akagera Neighbours
Headline: Rwanda: The Woes And Joys of Akagera Neighbours
Body_Text:
Ndego — The sun shines down on the mud houses of Ndego. Though it just rained, a fine layer of orange dust covers every surface and fills every breath. Jessica, a 28 year-old cultivator, laughs when asked if she has heard of Mutware. "Ah, Mr. Elephant!" she says, looking to the north where shimmering blue lakes and hills covered in gold-green savanna mark the territory of Akagera National Park. "I know many things about him."
In fact the entire population inhabiting this area directly south of Akagera National Park is all too well acquainted with the infamous "problem elephant" that has been ravaging fields and property since late 2005.
Mutware was one of 22 young elephants brought to Akagera from Bugesera in a 1975 effort to reintroduce the species which had mysteriously disappeared from the park in the early 60's. Though Mutware never integrated into the new herd, the park's vast space allowed him to live his solitary wanderings, unnoticed by people.
As did everything, this changed with the genocide. During the war, Akagera was occupied by soldiers and people on the run. Surviving by hunting, the wartime population reduced the park's once-abundant wildlife by 50-80%. In 1997, the park itself suffered a loss of 2/3 of its territory (from 2,709km2 to about 1,020km2) to provide much-needed land for returning refugees.
Though Mutware may be the most famous example of the human-wildlife conflict that has ensued since the park's diminishment, he is by no means the most representative. "The 'elephant problem' is really a Mutware problem," says American Master's student Jessica Mathon, who, with a grant from the Wildlife Conservation Society, has been researching human-elephant conflict around Ndego - the area most subjected to crop-raiding - since January.
Mathon explains that Mutware came out of the forest late last year as he is wont to do. But unlike usual, he has stayed, and no one knows why. Regardless of the reason, for months the elephant has been feasting on cassava, sorghum, bananas, beans, and sweet potatoes, and destroying buildings which contain or smell like they contain these choice items.
Mutware has yet to injure a person in his feeding forays. In fact - and much to Mathon's chagrin - certain villagers have taken to hand-feeding the pachyderm that some admit they feel privileged to live near. "Mutware is not vicious - he's just curious," says one older woman from Ndego.
Though lesser discussed, the animals of greater direct concern to human welfare are the buffalos, hippos, and crocodiles that every year are responsible for a handful of human injuries and even fatalities.
The Akagera River, which marks the park's and country's eastern border, feeds a series of lakes and marshes that comprise nearly half of the remaining protected area and are extremely important to the region's ecological health. The southern tip of Lake Ihema - the majority of whose waters are protected and home to hippo and crocodile - now dangles out from the park. People routinely collect water and bathe on Ihema's unprotected southern shore.
"Before, this area belonged to the animals. They don't realize that it's not theirs anymore," Jessica points out. The ramifications of this observation were made tragically clear a few weeks ago when a crocodile attacked and killed a boy who was swimming in the lake.
"It's an extremely complicated and serious problem," Benjamin Mugabukomeye, Akagera's Chief Park Warden, explained to the New Times. "We need to find a way to prevent the animals from exiting the park. But we also need to educate the people about the animals and their behavior. It is only by working together that we will find solutions to these problems."
Responding to that need, in October 2003, the Rwandan Office of Tourism and National Parks (ORTPN) created a Community Conservation Department. The department's purpose is to raise awareness and educate populations around the parks about environmental issues, provide a channel of communication between local people and the park, and to address wildlife conflict issues.
The Community Conservation Department also helps to oversee local development projects funded by ORTPN through a revenue sharing programme. Every year a percentage of all revenue from the country's national parks is distributed back to neighboring communities. For 2004, Akagera's communities received 13.3 million Francs, or nearly $24,000 USD, in classrooms, refrigeration systems for milk production, and a building for artisan associations.
Despite ORTPN's efforts to ensure that communities benefit from the park, most local people fail to connect revenue-sharing projects with ORTPN and the park. Cultivating land in an exceptionally arid and drought-prone environment, and mostly very poor, inhabitants of this area rarely have the luxury to see beyond the things that affect them directly.
"If our cows go into the park they are arrested. But if the park's animals come out and destroy people's property, nothing happens," says Théophile, a student visiting his parents in Ndego, and referring to the practice of having to buy back at market value any livestock that stray into the park.
"There should be compensation for property damaged," agrees Ndego Sector Executive, Ronald M. Kiwanuka. "It's a very sensitive subject. But people are becoming bitter - they say that the government considers animals before people - that isn't good."
Compensation for property damage is first on everyone's list of demands, but unfortunately it is neither a simple solution, nor one likely to be forthcoming. (ORTPN does, however, pay medical expenses for injuries caused by wild animals that have strayed outside of the park.)
"Wildlife cause a lot of damage," concedes Fidèle Ruzigandekwe, Head of ORTPN's Conservation Unit in Kigali. "But compensation is extremely complicated. It would require consultancies, lawyers, legal intervention... we are not currently equipped to manage it properly," he explains. "We must find other ways."
First on the list of other ways to solve the problem is a physical barrier - something for which many in Ndego have long clamored. According to Ruzigandekwe, a 3.5 km trench between the park and Ndego is expected to be in place by September. Depending on the trench's success in deterring wildlife incursions, its coverage will be extended.
An electric fence also has been suggested, but cost and maintenance are major constraints. One advantage to fencing the whole park would be the necessity of reviewing the park's boundary. The current boundary was created with no consideration for ecological requirements. As a result, crucial breeding grounds and dry-season water sources were left outside, forcing animals to leave the safety of the park in order to survive.
Some experts question the efficacy of any type of physical barrier - arguing that they are costly, ineffective, and will only create new problems. Mathon hopes that Ndego's villagers will experiment with chili-pepper-based deterrents which have proven highly successful in Zimbabwe among other places.
In the end, everyone agrees that more education and greater communication between the park and the people are key. However, according to Mathon's research, most villagers who are not association members have little to no awareness of community conservation efforts.
"The community conservation department has only been in place since late 2003. We have over 200,000 people to see across three Districts - it's not easy to reach everyone immediately," Fidèle Sebatware, Akagera's Community Conservation Director stressed to the New Times.
With limited access to the park's shared transport (2 vehicles and one working motorcycle for all departments), Sebatware and his colleague Norbert Karegire are hard pressed to visit the park's constituency with the frequency and timeliness that everyone concerned would like to see. Both men also admit that they themselves need more training in how to handle wildlife conflict issues. "It's a new field," Sebatware points out.
To make their work feasible, the community conservation department staff of two, trains sector-level "conservation coordinators" (called Anicos), who provide local people with a direct link to the park. But Anicos are not ORTPN staff, and despite their presence, many people still say that the park "doesn't come to see us" and is therefore a "bad neighbor."
Meanwhile, with high hopes for developing tourism and the revenue it can bring, ORTPN would like to see the recovery of Akagera's populations of large and charismatic species like giraffe, zebra, antelope, and - at the top of every visitor's list - elephant.
"The challenges are enormous. You see all of the pressures on the parks?" ORTPN Conservation Head Ruzigandekwe emphatically taps his finger on an aerial photo showing cultivated fields that stop exactly where the forest begins. "Conservation here in Rwanda is about managing conflict," he concludes, his expression that of a man eager to get back to the job.

Original_Search_Title: Better Life for Rural And Market Women
Headline: Nigeria: Better Life for Rural And Market Women
Body_Text:
Lagos — The problem with the recent reforms executed by the current governments at the three tiers is that majority of the changes are elitist in nature. On the surface, they seem well intended and overlay pragmatic but in fact they hardly permeate the grassroots levels. The common man has obviously not benefited from the reforms.
How many farmers and artisans understand the port reforms and the bank consolidation exercise? To most Nigerians, the seven years of the Obasanjo democratic regime have not improved anything because the impact of the so-called economic reforms is merely cosmetic and they are apparently targeted at the rich persons in the society.
A case in point is the condition of rural and market women. What indeed have been the lot of the embattled women since May 1999? In what ways have they benefited from the dividends of democracy?
The "Better Life Programme for rural Women" introduced during the military rule of President Ibranhim Babangida form 1985 to 1993 generated progressive measures to transform the circumstances of local women. As a step forward, the Beijing Conference of 1995 made radical recommendations to promote the lifestyle of the female population. Consistent with the notorious "Nigerian Factor", the governments of the federation have neither complied with the prescriptions nor followed the practices underslined to enhance the rights as well as the quality of lives in the feminine world. A lot must be done to tackle the basic needs of women at the grassroots level with concentration on the sectors of rural and market women.
There is the necessity to stimulate rural and market women towards achieving a better and higher standard of life in line with modern trends. These women should be educated on simple hygiene, family planning, the importance of child care and to step up their literacy level. We have to raise their social consciousness especially in respect of their rights as well as political and economic responsibilities. They must be encouraged to achieve self development particularly through education, skill acquisition and small scale businesses.
Rural and market women are generally illiterate. Therefore, adult and non-formal education should be institutionalised in the villages and market areas. Subjects to be taught in such women centres include basic literacy reading, writing and arithmetic, business studies typing, shorthand, book keeping, office practice and English, home economics - food and nutrition, clothing and textile, home management and handicraft. The local governments should play a pivotal role in the adult education schemes of grassroots women.
The emphasis for rural women resurgence should be on such agricultural pursuits and fish farming, piggery, poultry and the traditional areas of food crop farming usually assigned to women. Both the rural and market women should be involved in consumer and producer cooperatives to maximize their productivity and personal incomes. Recent developments indicate that some progressive communities have mobilized funds for local women to under take various activities such as soap and pomade making, pottery, cassava and rice milling, snail farming, sloth weaving, mat making, fish smoking, goatry, cosmetics and toiletries. A prominent innovation is mechanisation which has replaced the old primitive manual methods. Practices such as the extraction of palm fruits, formerly labour intensive, is now mechanised to the level that the women can understand and manage. Pottery and ceramics occupation can be profitably included in female vocational training. The active participation of women in rural and urban small scale industries will certainly boost their skill and enhance their economic independence.
One does not see effectively how the ongoing processes of poverty alleviation have directly and positively transformed the lives of rural and market women. Majority of them barely eke out of living form rudimentary hoe and knife farming cultural and petty trading. The widows among them survive on the crumbs and sometimes sell themselves as a last resort. The present federal government has a duty to provide some social security schemes for widows and poor women sentenced to a dismal existence of abject penury. More markets should be constructed in both the urban and rural areas to accommodate women traders who desire them. Stall allocations must at least ensure that thirty per cent go to women genuine traders. The vogue at present all over the country is to allocate virtually all the market stalls to men, some of whom are not engaged in actual trading. Politicians dominate the market allocations and in a wave of exploitation sell the stalls at exorbitant prices. The interest o f women should be a prime consideration in the affairs of running the markets. Nursery centres should be constructed in public markets to cater for young mothers whose babies require comfortable attention outside the immediate confines of the market noisy and boisterous environment.
The glorious days of the People's Bank of Nigeria need to be replicated. Women played a critical role in its success. The experience revealed that women have a higher credit discipline and are judicious in loan utilisation and religious in repayments. The federal government must restore the People's Bank to its original pinnacle for mobilising savings and generating investment in small scale enterprises. The bank's operation have direct impact on poverty alleviation, credit market, capital accumulation by the poor, employment generation, rural urban migration and capacity building by the poor. The sector of micro-financing should address the problems of rural and market women.
Despite the hardships and immortalization of rural and market women, the ministries of women affairs at federal and state levels are idle contributing practically nothing to the paramount needs of embattled women at the grassroots. Since the inception of the Ministries of Women Affairs, they serve no useful purpose. Elitist and patently lazy, those placed in charge hardly know what they are supposed to achieve. Nigerians expect that that the ministries should work hard to harness the feminine potentials, boost female resurgence in social, economic and political affairs. Women play cardinal roles in food production, utilisation, family raising and upkeep. As mothers, they deserve not only optimal affection and respect but also prime consideration in productive and distributive endeavours.

Original_Search_Title: Towards Enhancing Trade Among Developing Nations
Headline: Africa: Towards Enhancing Trade Among Developing Nations
Body_Text:
Lagos — The International Business Forum that preceded the D8 Summit held recently in the Island of Bali, Indonesia brought to the front burner the need for co-operation among developing countries in order for them to guarantee their peoples decent living.
With the world becoming a mere village in this age of internet and globalisation, it has become imperative for countries to close ranks, come together and promote common interests. The formation of international political, cultural and economic organisations predates this era but in the last few years, the challenge and need for interdependence has never been greater. For instance, emphasis has moved from comparative advantage as taught in elementary economics to what is now refereed to as competitive advantage.
The highly industriaised countries of the world have their platform known as G8, which in reality represent the eight largest economies in the world. Every year, they meet to chart a course on how the world economy will run.
But on June 15, 1997, something happened. A group of eight medium income countries met in Istanbul, Turkey and founded D8, as a counterpart to G8. The D8 countries are Nigeria, Turkey, Egypt, Pakistan, Indonesia, Malaysia, Bangladesh and Iran. Its main goal is to foster economic co-operation among member-countries and to strengthen the role of developing countries in the global economy.
Recently, the fifth summit of D8 was held in Indonesia. The summit is made up of heads of states and governments. But before the summit began, the 20th session of the D8 Commission took place in Bali also in Indonesia.
In welcoming delegates to the forum, Ambassador Mochamad Hidat, the Indonesian commissioner in charge of D8 told them that Indonesia would hold chairmanship of the group for the next two years. He solicited the co-operation of the members to make the country's tenure successful.
There was an international business forum, at which the organised private sector of member-countries participated. The Nigerian private sector was well represented.
Dr. Ausbeth Ajagu, interim president, Indonesian-Nigerian Business Association and national council member of the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN), presented a paper. He entitled it 'Enhancing D-8 Intra Trade'.
He said the Nigerian business community commended the initiative behind the D-8 group and would wish to advise as follows:
-That the private sector of all the eight member countries be actively involved in the entire deliberations prior, during and after the D-8 governments' meetings.
-That there should be regular meeting of the D-8 business forum/exhibition, at least, once a year.
-That there should be a well-established business forum committee with a good and informative website which will list D-8 countries' main business associations and chambers for business inquiries and synergy amongst nationals.
-That all the D-8 countries should speedily relax visa rules for the nationals. Thus, Visa should be issued within 24 hours for a minimum period of 12 months to genuine and desiring business people from the D8.
-D-8 Countries' Business Communities should endeavour to trade much more amongst themselves partnering with local business people in member countries who would provide lands, factory buildings/offices, local labour etc whilst the foreign partners provide plant and machinery and technical management. This would help empower the local workforce, reduce rate of unemployment in member countries, and ultimately enhance mutual beneficial intra trade amongst D-8 countries.
-That Business Communities in D-8 countries should endeavour to work closely with their respective governments to provide basic infrastructure and conducive atmosphere for business to thrive so that investors from other D-8 countries would find the group a fertile ground for investment.
-That D-8 countries should relax reasonably customs regulations/rules to encourage better free flow of trade. However, serious caution must be taken to protect various countries' industries especially SMIs, since some may not be able to compete given the setback their countries have, infrastructure-wise.
On the Nigerian experience concerning the issues raised, Ajagu told his audience that Nigeria is the largest market in Africa with a population of about 130 million people.
"Various Nigerian governments especially the present one have consistently put good industrial policies in place aimed at attracting new investors with carefully designed and enduring incentive structures. This involves not just the creation of favourable tariffs, tax and exchange regimes, it also encompasses the qualitative assurances of a warm, cordial and mutual beneficial relationship between an investor and the host country. Therefore, the objectives of investment drive are essentially to resuscitate and strengthen existing investments and at the same time captivate the interest of new investors.
"This paper could not have come at a better time than now when there is an urgent need to inform investors especially foreigners about the giant strides of the present democratic government to create the enabling environment for investments to thrive. This is in view of the conflicting and negative reports especially by the international media on the situation of things in Nigeria which, most times, do not encourage foreign investments," he said.
He continued: "Nigeria as you know is the biggest market in Africa with a population of over 130 million and abundantly blessed with several natural resources. Numerous opportunities abound for entrepreneurs to invest in crude oil, coal, tin, columbite, palm oil, peanuts, cotton, rubber, wood, hides and skin, textiles, construction materials, food products, footwear, chemicals, fertilizer, printing, ceramics and steel.
"Entrepreneurs also have abundant opportunities to invest in agricultural production such as in cocoa, corn, rice, sorghum, millet, cassava, yams, cattle, goats, sheep, rubber, fish, timber, etc. There is also the existence of abundant human resources
Given the above analysis of the natural and material resources the country Nigeria is blessed with, she can be described as a conducive place for entrepreneurship. This, amongst others, accounts for the increasing influx of foreigners seeking to establish business in Nigeria."
He noted that the experience of the South East Asian countries has indicated that concerted efforts at attracting capital inflows remain one of the options for attaining rapid development. He added that the target of the Nigerian government is to create an economy that will grow at the rate of 10 percent per annum and generate employment for the burgeoning labour force as well as adapt to the forces of globalization.
He posited that "the most pre-eminent positive factor, attracting investment into Nigeria today is democracy, understood not only as a terminal end to military rule, but also as the establishment of responsive political institutions. Such promotes an accountable system of governance and an irreversible process of the Rule of law; the amelioration of corruption; the protection of human rights; and a truly conducive environment for private enterprise to thrive. The prospects of investment in Nigeria are indeed bright. This is because the present government since inception has taken several bold steps in that direction.
"The government in a bid to tackle infrastructure problems, one of the basic problems of the Nigerian business landscape established the Federal Road Maintenance Agency with the mandate to embark on aggressive rehabilitation and maintenance of roads across the country. The Agency has achieved remarkable success in making our roads motorable. Similarly, other infrastructure problems like electricity generation, water supply etc are receiving the administration's attention in her bid to ensure provision of quality infrastructure.
"One cannot discuss the prospects of investments in the Nigerian business landscape without mentioning corruption and trade malpractices which hitherto constituted a major problem.
The Organized Private Sector in Nigeria has continued to commend the determined and dogged efforts of the Federal government towards fighting these monsters.
"There is no gainsaying the fact that corruption has been reduced significantly in Nigeria courtesy of the several policies and reforms initiated by the government aimed at stamping out corruption and
corrupt practices. One cannot but laud the achievements of the relevant agencies in that direction.
"They include the Economic & Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC), The Independent Corrupt Practices and other Related Offences Commission (ICPC), the Budget Monitoring & Price Intelligence Unit otherwise known as Due Process, the Code of Conduct Bureau etc.
" He said these agencies have worked tirelessly towards ridding Nigeria of corrupt tendencies, adding that the results were there for all to see.
"It is instructive to note that all the aforementioned amongst other several reforms which are contained in the National Economic Empowerment & Development Strategy (NEEDS) being pursued vigorously by the government portend bright prospects for investment in the country. In fact the Nigerian business landscape is one filled with very bright prospects for investment.
The time has come for investors from other D-8 countries to form partnerships with their Nigerian counterparts to tap the rich potentials of our economy. The former practice of some countries using the country as a dumping ground for their goods must be done away with while formation of partnerships must be encouraged," he said.
Ajagu said though Nigerian law encourages foreigners to establish businesses on their own as well as form partnerships with Nigerians, but that from interactions with practising investors, it was advisable for intending investors to partner with Nigerians.
"Whilst you provide machinery, technical know-how and finance, your Nigerian partner would provide necessary land, infrastructure, buildings, company registration etc on terms and conditions to be agreed," he said.
He told participants that the Indonesian-Nigerian Business Association was willing to provide any intending investor with necessary information on any areas of investments in Nigeria as well as list of genuine Nigerian businessmen they might want to partner with.
In concluding his presentation, Ajagu said: "It is important for various countries and their governments to really appreciate that investors have alternatives to invest their funds.
There is very keen global competition for investment.
"Investors will only go to those countries with the right mixture of productive factors and policies considered conducive for investment. Unless the policies are right (no matter the size of market or other natural endowments), little or no investment will materialise.
"It is in realisation of this fact that the Nigerian government has placed the issue of security and creation of enabling environment above all other considerations. Thus, positive investment climate has been established in order to exploit the resource endowment of Nigeria."
Mr. Joop Ave, Indonesia's Minister of Tourism, Post & Telecommunication from 1993 and 1998, spoke on the role culture and tourism could play in uniting member-countries of D8.
According to the former minister, "Cultural heritage is to the nation as memory is to the individual. As a person deprived of memory becomes disoriented and lost, not knowing where he has been or where he is going, so a nation denied its own interpretation of its past will be handicapped in dealing with the present and its future.
"All of our partner countries in D8 have a profound cultural heritage including archaeological heritage such as temples and monuments and a rich culture of cuisine, dress, song, dance, history, religion, traditions and folklore- all of which are the essential building blocks of national culture.
"The power of culture is such that it can only nourish and sustain its people through difficult times, but it can draw visitors irresistibly like a magnet from the other side of the world in order to be able to experience it.
"Visitors means tourism, which can play a vital role not only in our countries' respective economic development but that also has the potential to building peaceful relationships among nations.
"It can be argued that there are few forces in the world that contribute more to global development than tourism as it accomplishes two fundamental objectives.
First it raises standards of living among people in developing countries by providing employment and foreign revenue, thus also helping calm social tensions. Second, by its very nature, it contributes to an appreciation of other people that is very opposite of the suspicious and antagonistic feelings that encourage conflict."

Original_Search_Title: A Tale of Two Countries
Headline: Rwanda: A Tale of Two Countries
Body_Text:
Kigali — I recently went to a video library and was surprised to find a whole series of Alex Haley's Roots. I immediately decided to borrow the whole series. My children, who were with me, dissuaded me on the grounds that the tapes were 'fake'. For them, what they like is 'cool' and what they don't is 'fake'. I have always laboured to explain to them that the opposite of cool is warm and the opposite of fake is genuine, but the more I do that, the more I become 'fake' because, they wonder, how can Daddy not know such easy language which every student in their school knows.
Anyhow, I insisted on the tapes, and told them that when I was young I had watched the series Roots on TV which were serialised over a long period of time. Those were the days when you expectantly waited to see Arnold, Will, and their sister Kimberly, who was the biological daughter of the parent who had adopted them, in Different Strokes; JJ, his sister, little brother, crazy aunt, their mother and father in their ghetto flat in Good Times; Sanford always pretending he was going to join his departed wife, Elizabeth by faking a heart attack whenever he wanted his son to do something in Sanford and Son; JR running the Ewing Empire and always outsmarting his likeable brother Bobby and surviving all the machinations of his conspiratorial wife in Dallas; the intrigues and ways of the rich in Falcon Crest, and so on.
Going back to Roots, I remember how Kunta Kinte the warrior was captured by slave hunters, and, together with others and a girl he had admired while carrying out warrior tasks, were chained into a ship and sailed to America.
Defiant Kunta Kinte caused a mutiny on the ship, which brought more suffering to all the slaves on the ship. The girl he had admired was given to the unwilling ship captain to 'keep him warm' in the night. Even on arrival in America and after being auctioned, Kunta Kinte tried to fight his new masters' servants, but was chained and restrained. He tried to escape several times and was always caught and brought back. He never gave up and on his last attempt, the slave-catchers cut off his right foot so that he would never be able to run again. While his master's cook nursed him, she fell in love with him and they later married. They had a baby daughter who was friendly to the master's daughter.
The master's daughter taught Kunta Kinte's daughter how to read and write - which was not allowed for slaves. Indeed she wrote a forged travel document for her slave boyfriend, and when he was caught, though the master liked his cook and her husband, Kunta, who had been promoted to be the master's driver, he had to reluctantly sell off their daughter. He sold her to a slave owner who bought slave girls for breeding; in other words, rape and make babies with them, who would later become labourers and wives of labourers for work and to sell.
There were slave owners who bought slaves for labour and others for breeding.
