In the Second Republic, notably in the South-west, governors of the old Ondo and old Oyo states, Chief Michael Adekunle Ajasin and Chief Bola Ige respectively, were announced by the Federal Electoral Commission (FEDECO) to have lost their governorship seats to Chief Akin Omoboriowo, the estranged deputy of Ajasin and Dr. Omololu Olunloyo, of the National Party of Nigeria (NPN) respectively.
Ajasin later reclaimed his seat through the ruling of the Appeal Court which nullified Omoboriowo's election, but Ige didn't regain his seat. Although, both the 1983 and 2003 elections were allegedly characterised by widespread election malpractices, yet seeing a number of incumbent governors uprooted, has only affirmed that the perceived invincibility of the incumbent rulers can easily be broken.
In advanced democracies, like the United States, the power of incumbency does not count much as in most cases, the electorate have the final say on who rules them. Thus in 1992, former President Bill Clinton defeated the incumbent George Bush Snr. in a landslide victory at the presidential poll. And like in the US, the incumbency factor seems to be fading out in Africa, especially in Nigeria.
The alleged fraud which characterised the 2003 general elections notwithstanding, a number of incumbent governors were plucked off very easily from their exalted seats. They included the then governors of Kogi, Kwara, Gombe, Borno and Kano. In the same vein, governors of Oyo, Ondo , Ogun, Ekiti and Osun states, also lost their seats to their opponents. These instances, only confirmed that the power of incumbent is not automatic and total. In the past, incumbency factor included the power to manipulate victory on one's behalf, but as exemplified at the 2003 polls, reverse seems to be the case now.
The 2003 experience therefore, only upheld the belief that the re-election of the 12 incumbent governors eligible for re-election in 2007 will not be automatic. Their score cards will rather be the determing factors . But many of these governors may lost their seats as a result of the politics of intrigues and foul play that they have ventured into coupled with their perceived insensitivity to the plights of the people they govern. Specifically, the support of most of the governors for the infamous third term agenda, has been perceived as one factor that will see to their exit from their government houses.
Gbenga Daniel (Ogun)
Daniel ascended the governorship seat on May 29, 2003 having been declared winner of the gubernatorial election in Ogun State by the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) . He took over from Chief Olusegun Osoba of the Alliance for Democracy (AD). Prior to the time of the party primaries, Daniel had signed an undertaking with the people of Ogun West senatorial district, that if the Yewa -Awori people could back his ambition, he would only run for the first term and support the the candidature of an Ogun west person in 2007.
Consequently, he picked Allhaja Salimot Badru from the district as his deputy governor. But while the people of Ogun West looked forward to seizing power for which they have been clamouring for over the years, in 2007, their leaders led by Senator Ayo Otegbola, early last year reversed the agreement at a meeting in Abeokuta where the leaders said owing to Daniel's performance, the undertaking he had with them would be jettisoned, and that the governor would now have a fresh mandate to run for a second term in 2007.
At any rate, the performance of Daniel has been a subject of discussion among watchers. While some people believe that the governor has performed to an extent, many more argued that the claimed performance is mere propangada, arguing that the noise that Daniel's administration makes exceeds the work he has done.
Another odd against the governor is his tactical support for the third term agenda. The agenda was generally abhorred by the people of Ogun State. The electorate may see his support for this agenda as a betrayal of trust and moreso, signing away their destiny without their consent. Besides, another factor advanced against Daniel is intolerance. Daniel from inception, is said not to tolerate any opposition, especially any other person aspiring for his job. For instance, when Senator Ibikunle Amosun who represents Ogun central senatorial district was suspected to be nursing a gubernatorial ambition, he and Daniel fought eachother for a long while until it became a major issue in the media. At different times, supporters of Amosun were alleged dismissed at rallies and eventually, Amosun had to calm down before peace could reign in the state. Other aspirants like Dipo Dina of the AD, Ogbeni Lanre Banjo of the National Conscience Party (NCP) have also had their own stories of Dani el's intolerance. This attitude of intolerance, many said may affect the re-election bid of the governor.
Ayo Fayose (Ekiti)
One governor whose government has been characterised by controversies since inception is Ayodele Fayose of Ekiti State . His performance has been subject to mixed feelings. Whereas, some argue that life in Ekiti State is not getting better, others said Fayose is performing, but notes that the projects he executes are not the most urgently required. Ekiti State under Fayose, has been mired in different controversies ranging from alleged murder cases in Ifaki Ekiti and other parts of Ekiti as well as allegations of threats to their lives by Chief Afe Babalola (SAN) and Mr. Femi Falana, a former governorship aspirant on the platform of NCP at the last general elections by the governor. The governor later apologised to Babalola over his allegations.
Fayose, like Daniel has been said to be impatient with the opposition, as he can hardly tolerate anyone who aspires to be the governor of the state. During the recent bye-election to fill the former seat of Mrs. Abiodun Olujimi which became vacant at the House of Representatives as a result of her appointment as the deputy governor, the state government according to an article written by one of the governorship aspirants, Mr. Dare Babarinsa, sponsored the brutality as well as the arrest of members of the opposition which led to the matcheting and the eventual detention of the former military administrator of Bayelsa State, Navy Captain Caleb Olubolade and many others who were taken to Abuja and detained for several days. According to Babarinsa, the state government which ensured the arrest of the AD candidate at the bye-election, a day before the election, did not allow anybody to vote, as the electorate allegedly got to polling centres at 10a.m to learn that election had end ed.
Given this circumstance, apart from the staunch support of Fayose for 3rd term agenda, no fewer than 27 aspirants have risen up to rescue power from the governor in 2007, to save the state from the increasing wave of controversies by the day. President Olusegun Obasanjo, on his recent visit to Ekiti, commended the governor for good performance, but acknowledged that the governor was stubborn as widely being noted. All these factors seem to be the odds against the chances of the governor at the 2007 polls.
Peter Obi (Anambra)
The governor of Anambra State, Mr Obi whose ascension to the throne, was only possible after the nullification of the election of the immediate past governor, Dr. Chris Ngige on March 15, this year is still at the background. No sooner than Obi ascended the seat of power than controversies arose about the money left in the coffers by Ngige's administriation. These controversies seem to have been distracting the newest governor from settling down to work. He has however said that he was putting structures in place to enable him launch the operations of his government. Many believe that it will be too early to judge the governor about what he can do or otherwise.
However, his performance, come 2007, if it matches up with Ngige's or exceeds it, will determine if the people will re-elect him or vote him out.
Olagunsoye Oyinlola (Osun State)
Osun State, unlike in the first term, has enjoyed relative peace since the coming on board of Governor Olagunsoye Oyinlola in 2003. Besides the killing of an AD chieftain last year and the attack on leaders of Afenifere while on tour of Osun early in the year, the state has been relatively calm. However, a survey of the performance of the governor, has shown that the footprints of the former governor, Chief Bisi Akande are more visible than that of the incumbent governor, except that Oyinlola has not laid off workers as the former governor did in the first term.
Although progaganda about the possibility of the governor's re-election dots the state as well as the media, it is the candid view of many that the governor has not done anything extra-ordinary that can make his re-election automatic. But Oyinlola has been described by the opposition as very tolerant, unlike some of his other counterparts in the zone. Aspirants are said to freely carry out their campaigns without resistance. Reports said that he had driven in the same car with Mr. Rauf Aregbesola, one of the leading aspirants in the state and had meaningful discussion with him. He was also said to have allowed another formidable aspirant, Chief Tunde Odanye to appear on the state radio, without murmurings. While all these have been described as major points scored by the governor, watchers said he needs to convince the people beyond doubts, through performance to regain the mandate.
Goodluck Jonathan (Bayelsa)
Bayelsa State governor, Goodluck Jonathan's chance of becoming the governor only came after the impeachment of his former boss, Chief Diepreye Alamieyeseigha, following his arrest for money laundering in London and his eventual jumping bail. But the governor, since fortune smiled on him has not been so popular among the people.
First, he was accused of inability to bring under control, the alleged high handedness of his wife, who sometimes ago, allegedly ordered the arrest and detention of a young lady, after her convoy allegedly forced her out of the road and manhandled her. The governor, after being informed about the ordeal of the lady in the hands of her wife, ordered her wife to release the detained, but the wife reportedly called it bluff of her husband. It was in an effort to handle the situation that the governor sent of one of his commissioners to the wife, during which the commissioner met his untimely death in an auto crash. This major weakness, not common among governors, watchers believe is capable of robbing the governor of his second chance.
Also, the people of the state has asked the governor to apologise to the state for abandoning the South-south course to support the failed third term agenda. This is another factor that may jeopardise his second term.
Bukola Saraki (Kwara)
Dr. Bukola Saraki, may not face serious hurdles on his way back to the Government House in Ilorin. This is so because his father, Dr. Olusola Saraki has always been the sole determinant of who wins elections in the state. When he felt sidetracked by the former governor, Alhaji Mohammed Lawal, he quit the All Nigeria People's Party (ANPP), joined the People's Democratic Party (PDP) and positioned his son, Bukola for the position of the governor and he won.
Since his father has always had the final say, many believe it will be a herculian task to unseat him in 2007. The young Saraki's major achievement has been in agriculture, specifically in cassava plantation, which has earned him awards both at home and abraod. Besides, he emerged the Best Governor of the Year at the THISDAY Annual Award for Excellence last year and also bagged the nomination for the year 2006.
Olusegun Agagu (Ondo)
Ondo State, as usual, has enjoyed relative peace in this tenure of Dr. Olusegun Agagu . Although, Agagu recently launched a number of micro credit schemes aimed at empowering the youths, many are still watching his chances of re-election. This is moreso, that the governor was a staunch supporter of third term agenda. It was also in the state that pro-third term rally was proposed before it was aborted by members of the Oodua People's Congress (OPC).
Besides the third term scam, watchers said the governor would have to go a long way to justify his re-election bid . His predecessor, Chief Adebayo Adefarati, said the people of the state were tired of his government and hence, nothing would stop his camp from showing him the way out of Alagbaka Government House in Akure in 2007.
" I 've told you that we are taking over Alagbaka in 2007 by the grace of God. It is not boasting . The people themselves are tired. He's going round, he knows what he's experiencing wherever he goes to," the former governor said.
Ibrahim Shekarau (Kano)
Kano State is one of the PDP states taking over by the ANPP in 2003. The governor, Ibrahim Shekarau is said to be free with the people, putting protocol aside and even making his GSM line availbale for public calls. Besides, Kano State under his government, was very hostile to the failed third term agenda. At the time some northern governors, even from the opposing political parties were said to be hob nobbing with the ruling PDP government, and even campaigning for the third term agenda, Shekarau was not caught in this show. Hence, while this factor as well as his simple life style may enhance his second chance as governor, his peformance and popularity among Kano residents, which have not been over blown, will also go a long way to determine this.
Adebayo Alao-Akala (Oyo)
Oyo State is one of the states which had gone through raging crisis in this term of service. Adebayo Alao-Akala, who sits atop the affairs of the state, is the by-product of the crisis which almost consumed the state. Akala emerged after the unconstitutional removal of the governnor, Rashidi Ladoja early in the year. Many described the emergence of Akala, as a shocker and hence, labelled his government as unconstitutional because of the perceived backdoor approach through which he emerged. Only 18 of 32 lawmakers in the state House of Assembly, which did not constitute the two-third majority required by the constitution, carried out the impeachment of Ladoja, despite a restraint from the court not to constitute the impeachment panel by the chief judge, Justice Afolabi Adeniran. Given the circumstance of his emergence, his re-election in 2007, may be a tasking adventure, since it was believed that he did not come in through a constitutional means.
Ali Modu Sheriff (Borno)
The governor of Borno, Ali Modu Sheriff is the current chairman of the ANPP caretaker committee, which took over from the leadership of ANPP led by Chief Don Etiebet. Sheriff's position over the third term agenda was not easily known. While being accused of supporting the agenda, because of his perceived closeness to Aso Rock, Sheriff himself was swift to say that his relationship with Obasanjo transcended party differences. But he denied supporting the agenda.
Sheriff is said not to have performed badly . Besides, he is said not to be religiously biased. This was confirmed when cartoon riots which led to the killing of some Christians in Maiduguri broke out. Sheriff's displeasure was visible while he made a broadcast registering his disapppiontment and consequently setting up a panel of inquiry with a view to bringing the perpetrators to book. Unless an opponent can convince the people that he can beat Sheriff, he may comfortably retain his seat.
Ibrahim Idris (Kogi)
Since Ibrahim Idris, took over from his predecessor, Abubakar Audu, his popularity has not been in doubt. Audu, while asked to assess him recently, described him as a friend, whose performance he could not just discredit. But despite that judgment from his predecessor, viewers said his return to the Government House in Lokoja come 2007, might not be automatic as he will have to battle different emerging forces, to position himself for the number one job, the second time.
Danjuma Goje (Gombe)
Gombe State governor, Danjuma Goje's performance has been a central knowledge. To this end, he was one of the few governors who got the nomination for THISDAY Governor of the Year Award in February. Although, Goje did not emerge the winner at the end of the event, he is one governor said to have the credibility for re-election. But Goje's position was not easily known during the third term struggle as he was perceived to be a fence sitter. But for his perceived indecision over the third term struggle, his performance, has been said to qualify him for a second chance.

Original_Search_Title: MPs Pledge to Fast-Track GM Food Law
Headline: Kenya: MPs Pledge to Fast-Track GM Food Law
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyan MPs who have just returned from an agricultural biotechnology fact-finding mission to South Africa have resolved to seek audience with relevant government authorities to discuss the way forward.
The MPs are concerned that after 15 years of research on modern biotechnology, Kenya still does not even have a biotechnology policy and biosafety laws necessary for commercialisation of genetically engineered (GM) products. Without mincing words, they said they would ensure that GM crops are commercially introduced in Kenya.
They needed to know what policy issues have facilitated the adoption of GM crops in South Africa but hindered their adoption in Kenya and elsewhere in Africa. The MPs wanted to see how small-scale farmers benefit from Bt Cotton and Bt Maize in South Africa.
The MPs said it was taking too long to have the necessary laws passed to facilitate commercialisation of GM products. They have, therefore, resolved to do everything within their powers to fast-track a biotechnology agenda in Kenya and Africa at large, saying Africa needs a common front or agenda on issues such as biotechnology.
After interacting with small-scale farmers growing genetically modified (Bt) maize and Bt cotton in South Africa, the Kenyan MPs, who were accompanied in the Decision Makers Biotechnology Fact-finding Mission to South Africa by their counterparts from Malawi, are convinced that Kenyan and Malawian farmers could benefit immensely from the technology if its products were made available to them.
The traveling workshop was organised by AfricaBio - South Africa in collaboration with African Biotechnology Stakeholders Forum (ABSF-Kenya), the International Service for the Acquisition of Agri-Biotech Applications (ISAAA-Kenya) and BioEROC-Malawi. The purpose of the traveling workshop was to expose the participants to how South Africa has handled biotechnology issues and make the participants gain a better understanding of the current South African and to a larger extent, the global situation of agricultural biotechnology.
The MPs, drawn from various parliamentary select committees, including Agriculture, Trade and Industry, Science and Technology, and Public Accounts want the Biosafety Bill and the draft National Policy on Biotechnology enacted as soon as possible as this is critical for widespread adoption of biotechnology and related products.
They intend to table the report from their mission in South Africa in Parliament and demand a ministerial statement on biotechnology. The MPs also propose to seek amendment of the current Agriculture Act to include biotechnology as another way of fast tracking biotechnology legislation in Kenya.
Muhoroni MP Ayiecho Olweny , who is also a professor of genetics, says biotechnology could bridge the gap between food insecurity and food prosperity.
"Biotechnology offers the best possible attack on production problems like the striga weed, cassava mosaic virus and cotton bollworms."
Alfred Nderitu, the MP for Mwea constituency, where Bt cotton confined trials are being carried out, regrets that the Government spends about Sh6 billion on 1.2 million Kenyans who permanently need relief food.
"Kenya needs to turn these people from consumers to producers," he said.
He believes one way to achieve this is through widespread adoption of biotechnology. Nderitu lamented that Kenyans are being denied GM products because of lack of appropriate legislation. "Let us look for a shortcut. Let us have the products, grow them and then enact the necessary laws."
But as the legislators commit themselves to fast-tracking GM issues in the country, they also challenge researchers to involve policy makers and other stakeholders in their research activities.
They feel there is a big disconnect between researchers and policy makers.
"Development is a process that everybody must be involved in. Bridging science and politics remains a challenge in Kenya that must be adequately addressed," says Mutinda Mutiso, MP for Kilome.
Sammy Weya, the MP for Alego Usonga urges researchers in the field of biotechnology to not only rely on the respective ministries when seeking such important legislation, but also to deal directly with the relevant MPs and parliamentary committees.
The Bill and policy would have been enacted by now had MPs been involved from the start, said Weya. But Prof Jocelyn Webster, the director AfricaBio cautions the legislators not to rush to enact an inadequate law, noting that the Kenya Biosafety Bill, as it is now, is too restrictive.
"A Biosafety Bill that is too restrictive will not help. It must be more open."
Welcoming the MPs pledge to fast-track GM issues in the country, Dr Margaret Karembu, Executive Director ISAAA reiterates the need for harmonisation. She notes that Kenya risks being left behind on the new revolution in agriculture.
"We should not just be recipients but innovators. However, we are not going to be innovators in a vacuum. We need the necessary legislation. We risk losing the technology altogether. The technology may be rendered obsolete by the time it is approved for adoption if it is delayed for too long."
ISAAA advises developing nations like Kenya to adopt regulatory models that have proved successful elsewhere.
"Developing nations may use - and improve upon - the successful model supported by such governments, therefore saving time and scarce resources as they develop their own regulations. Furthermore, international co-operation helps harmonise food and biosafety regulations, which will increase trade and regional prosperity," says Dr Karembu
Currently GM crop research in Kenya includes genetically engineered (Bt) maize that is resistant to maize stem borers, pest resistant Bt Cotton, Bt cassava that is resistant to the Cassava Mosaic Virus and Bt sweet potato against the Sweet Potato Virus. Research on maize and cotton is already at confined field trials at Kari-Kiboko and Kari-Mwea respectively. Cassava and Sweet potato research is still at the laboratory level.
The MPs take a swipe at the anti-GM NGOs and other civil society organisations accusing them of greed, peddling lies and only serving the business interest of the West. "This is a business gimmick. Why should America and Britain fund NGOs to fight biotechnology when they are the major producers and consumers GM products?" Nderitu wonders.
"Here we are fighting GM products. We are major importers of textile products from China, a leading producer of BT cotton. How sure are we that the textile products are not from GM cotton?" posed Olweny.
He does not understand why there is so much opposition to GM crops. He argues that crop breeding has been practiced for centuries. Farmers should generally view GM crops as merely a step in the continuum, within their experience in conventional breeding leading them to consider GM technology as an a natural process to improve crops.
The biggest problem in adoption of biotechnology in any country is not in its scientific capacity or presence of supportive legislation. Enacting legislation, though critical for commercialisation of GM products, will not guarantee widespread adoption of the GM crops.
"The problem with commercialisation of GM products is not the country's capacity to develop the GM crops. The biggest problem is consumer perception," states Prof Webster, noting that the struggle to have legislative framework in place must go hand in hand with public education and awareness.
Kenya has the technological and scientific capacity. Sooner or later, the Bill and policy will be enacted. But how many Kenyans are knowledgeable or aware of the benefits and risks GM processes and products?
The legislators strongly believe that the scarcity of current, authoritative information and knowledge regarding biotechnology crops represents a major deficiency in Kenya and other developing countries, which denies policy makers and scientists access to vital knowledge needed to make well-informed decisions.
According to ISAAA, consumers are generally ill-informed regarding GM crops and food.
"Full awareness of the benefits, constraints, and attributes associated with food biotechnology belongs in the hands of developing nations - who stand to gain or lose the most," says Karembu.

Original_Search_Title: When Becomes a Home
Headline: Uganda: When Becomes a Home
Body_Text:
Kampala — Imagine fleeing your mother country because of turmoil, only to be faced by untold misery in the perceived ground of refuge.
Over 7,500 Sudanese refugees found themselves no exception to the callous atrocities of the notorious Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) rebels after fleeing war and hunger in southern Sudan some five years ago.
Women, children, the physically disabled, single-headed families and unaccompanied minors arrived in Kitgum and Pader districts after trekking hundreds of miles to cross the Uganda-Sudan border.
As they arrived, they were temporarily held at Acholi-Pii transit camp. After a spate of repeated attacks by the LRA rebels, tragedy fell on July 5, 2002.
A band of Joseph Kony's rebels attacked the camp, killing over 200 of the refugees using machetes, knives, hoes, sticks and bullets. To this day, the mention of "Acholi Pii" brings back traumatic memories of the incident.
It was a deplorable event that made headlines on publications across the world. It also did a disservice to efforts by the Government to make the refugees feel at home in an unfamiliar land.
The Government reacted swiftly and relocated the surviving refugees to Kiryandongo in Masindi District. They lived there for a year and though they had got so used to the place, it was only a transit site as the Government identified a settlement area elsewhere.
In 2003, Nebbi District council rejected a government appeal to host the refugees and out of sympathy, Arua District, which already housed about 50,000 refugees, agreed to host the assemblage on a deserted and undeveloped 25sq km piece of land.
The refugees put up considerable resistance as they were forcefully loaded onto fleets of lorries from Kiryandongo heading for a new home in Arua. The media reported at least one death, which the Government duly refuted.
Their resistance was understandable in light of their sour memories of the security situation in northern Uganda. Being resettled in Arua, in the north, must have seemed like a betrayal.
Little did they know that Madi Okollo refugee settlement, approximately 80km southeast of Arua town, would bring a new lease of life.
Today, two years down the road, it seems like a dream to most of the refugees to find a place where they can have their own land, grow their own food, build houses and have access to essential social facilities like schools and hospitals, without losing their refugee status.
To their first surprise, each household was allocated a piece of land, depending on the family size, for both settlement and agricultural activities.
Households with one or two persons were allocated a 50x50m piece of land, while families that had three to five people got a 100x100m plot. Groups of more than five people were allocated a 100m by 200m piece of land.
This was meant to reduce the refugee's dependence on food handouts under the Government Self-Reliance Strategy (SRS). The policy is supported by different humanitarian organisations.
Madi Okollo is one of the three refugee settlements in Arua where humanitarian organisations like the UNHCR, WFP and the German Development Service (DED) have invested billions of shillings to benefit the refugees and the hosting communities.
Arua has three refugee settlements, including Rhino Camp and Imvepi, with a total population of 67,230 Sudanese and Congolese immigrants. It's also estimated that approximately 30,000 Ugandans living within the catchment area of the three settlements are directly benefiting from the infrastructure and basic services provided to the refugees.
When funding from the UN became inadequate due to recent budget cuts, DED had to look for other sources of funding to ensure continuity of the programmes.
For example over the last 12 months, DED and Arua Refugee Programme have spent over about sh1b to establish services for water supply, health, education, roads, agriculture/stockbreeding, communal development, construction, income-generating activities and sensitising people on environmental protection.
The DED West Nile Infrastructure Development Project has also led to the construction and rehabilitation of schools and vocational training institutions like Ocea Skills Training Centre.
It also facilitated the construction of a doctors' house at a prime health unit in the Madi Okollo refugee settlement.
The DED Arua Refugee programme co-ordinator, Adi Gerstl, says they spent over sh192m to extend and improve 82km of rural murram roads in the sub-counties of Okollo, Rhino-Camp and Rigbo. Two culvert bridges were constructed under this arrangement. A drive through the settlement with DED project assistant Sarah Litat reveals a well-maintained road network that strategically leads to health facilities and referral routes, schools, markets, grinding mills and water points.
Thus the road network has been an important tool in the integration of the refugees and the national population. The area of settlement has sandy soil and experiences a dry spell for a bigger part of the year. This had worked against agricultural activities and the indigenous population was faced with hunger and malnutrition.
In June this year both refugees and the hosting society started receiving free improved peas, green gram, sorghum, millet and groundnuts seeds. Others were improved cassava stalks and potato vines. These have done well, and significantly improved the food security situation.
DED further procured 10 pairs of ox-ploughs and other farm tools, which are used communally to open larger fields. A number of households that lacked manpower to dig took advantage of the ox-traction facility to purge poverty and hunger.
Under the infrastructure development project, 20 male Boer goats were procured, out of which 11 were given out to the local community in Okollo Sub-county for crossbreeding.
Like in Rhino Camp and Imvepi, communities around Madi Okollo settlements are privileged to have access to clean piped water through a motorised water pumping systems constructed by DED. In total, about sh256m was spent to drill four boreholes in the three settlements, two of which are production wells for piped water systems in Okollo and Imvepi settlements.
Gerstl notes there was no noticeable outbreak of diseases such as dysentery, typhoid and cholera mainly, due to improved water sources and general hygiene in the settlements.
"Clean and safe water is accessible within walkable distance to all the beneficiaries in adequate quantities, which has improved the living conditions especially of women and children." Gerstl said.
Every settlement has a self-help secondary school stocked with textbooks. Currently a science laboratory is under construction at Rhino Camp, while 60,000 pupils in 97 schools surrounding the three settlements are benefiting from the WFP school feeding programme.
To promote gender equality, the school-going girls in secondary and primary from both the refugee and national communities, are given sanitary items on top of scholastic materials distributed to all.
The assistant programme co-ordinator at the settlement, Ismail Banduga said that before the construction of Oyima health unit, residents of Madi Okollo had to travel over 18km to access health services at a distant unit. Now, the 2,468 local people plus the thousands of refugees are beneficiaries of health services at the unit provided by nurses, midwives, a clinical officer and a visiting doctor, who is there twice a week.
DED has also constructed and fully furnished a doctor's house at Okollo Health Centre and now the challenge lies on the district authorities to post a doctor to cover the sub-county.
The sudden settlement of 7,500 people on a 25sq km piece of land can be a burden on the environment, but DED designed a sh23.6m environment plan for Madi Okollo settlement. The refugees and nationals were trained in energy conservation, keeping tree nurseries and conserving woodlands. The population was also trained and equipped on construction and the use of energy saving stoves, which have taken root in almost all homesteads and institutions.
To the people in Madi Okollo these numerous projects would have taken their local government several years to implement had it not been for the arrival of refugees and the humanitarian organisations.
Though the people of Arua took a hard decision and offered their land to the refugees, they seem more than comfortable staying with them because of the fringe benefits. It has also given the Sudanese refugees an unexpectedly good livelihood.
This explains why the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees, Antonio Guterres, was overwhelmed when he visited Uganda, particularly the refugee settlements in Arua, Moyo, Yumbe and Adjumani in June 2005. Guterres was just three days old in office by then.
He said, "When you look at Uganda you see a poor country that beyond its own problems is generous enough to receive 250,000 refugees and allow them to live side by side with Ugandans."
It is such generosity that has given the former Acholi Pii refugees a better life in Arua. Memories of the hunger and LRA attacks they suffered in southern Sudan and part of northern Uganda are fast disappearing. They are now proud owners of fields of food and business establishments like kiosks that have helped them earn an extra living outside of WFP rations.
Life to them has a new meaning and little do they even think about going back to their homeland.

Original_Search_Title: Deliciously Porky
Headline: Uganda: Deliciously Porky
Body_Text:
Kampala — Just before Namasuba on the Kampala-Entebbbe road, there is a dirty old blue wooden shack, standing 200 metres from a large green-and-white mosque. From afar, it looks like a carpentry workshop. Up close, however, it is typical pork joint - small, smoky and smelly. On one side of the entrance is a small pork butchery with large chunks of fresh pork dangling from several metallic hooks attached to the ceiling.
The main sitting area is a chaotic mix of white wooden tables, benches and plastic chairs strewn haphazardly around a wooden bar counter. An intoxicating smoky aroma of roasting pork hangs heavy in the air, whetting the appetite of hungry passers-by.
The back door takes you directly into a small open smoky blackened kitchen where a boy, not older than 16, is slowly turning the skewers of browning pork on several metallic griddles sitting over hot charcoal.
Welcome to Namasuba pork joint and butchery, also popularly known as "Mu Kibizzi". Aside from its unflattering name, Kibizzi is the oldest known pork butchery in Kampala. Locals say a man only identified as Kaweesi, opened the place in the early 1950s.
According to Benjamin Damba, 72, a former potter who first settled in the area in the early 1960s, the butchery initially catered to area residents who could not afford beef - then a preserve of the well-to-do.
However, when a bar selling local brew opened next door, Kaweesi began roasting pork for the patrons and before long, word of his culinary expertise had spread. Clients would walk from the city to Namasuba for an evening of pork, brew and merrymaking. "This place was always full every day of the week," Damba reminisces.
In the mid-1970s, Kibizzi hit a stale end, as former Ugandan president, Idi Amin, increasingly became intolerant of other religions and practices that were considered unholy in Islam, including the selling of pork in public places.
Many government officials, who until then were accustomed to eating pork, avoided it completely in fear of Amin's wrath. Some, however, continued to visit Kibizzi in secret. The tale goes that whenever they would visit Namasuba they would tell friends, "We are going to bury Daudi's son."
To throw Amin's watchdogs off the pork trail, many regulars would telephone the butchery to have their orders ready before they arrived. According to Mzee Francis Gureme, 79, a newspaper columnist, "People would telephone on their way from Masaka, Mbarara or Mbale, and say we want this amount of pork and by the time they would arrive, they would find it ready and waiting, eat quickly and leave."
With Amin's overthrow, Kibizzi was able to bounce back, but only temporarily as other similar outlets quickly mushroomed, destroying its monopoly. The first such outlet was opened in Wandegeya in 1981 by Paul Male and his wife Alice
Nakafeero. Most of Male's clients were Makerere University students. The opening of several bars and pork joints in Nakulabye, Ntinda, Bwaise and Bugolobi relegated Wandegeya to the dustpan of history, but it is slowly regaining popularity following an upsurge of students' hostels in the area.
What, however, remain unchanged are the social rituals of eating pork or porking (as some call it). Groups of twos and more, crowd popular eating joints in the evenings or weekdays, although the biggest turnout by far, are usually over the weekends. Steaming platters of roast and friend pork garnished with generous serving of cassava, roast matooke, greens, avocado, tomatoes, salt and pepper, are washed down with gallons of beer and other tribes of brew.
It's no surprise, that in 2004, Uganda was ranked by the World Health Organisation
(WHO) as the world's leading consumer of alcohol with a per capita consumption of 19.5 litres and an annual expenditure on alcohol of $145m. Had the WHO also carried out a research on pork consumption, Uganda would probably have topped the list, as the two are symbiotic in nature. This is because majority who eat pork, drink alcohol as well.
By far, the biggest consumers of pork are westerners - Bakiga, Banyankore, Banyarwanda and Batooro. There are plenty of explanations, although most of it is sheer speculation, otherwise, how else do you explain the fact that all the pork nicknames are in Luganda - Kabizzi, Katractor, Mwana wa Daudi, Mutambuza Ddembe, Nnadduli, Kasolo k'Egwanga, and Ak'e Gayaza. Still, a big percentage of those interviewed said westerners eat more pork because they have more money and like to have a good time.
"You can tell a westerner from say, a Muganda just by looking at the amount of pork they have ordered," explains William Kabwekye, manager at Yakobo's Joint in Ntida. "A Muganda will buy one or two sticks, but a Munyakore or Mukiga will buy 10 sticks of pork."
Equally skewed is the debate over why pork is such a popular delicacy today. Once regarded as a staple for poor peasantry, it is now consumed regardless of one's social status. Several patrons at Bamboo Nest in
Bugolobi, another popular pork hangout, said it prevents alcoholic hangovers for binge drinkers. "If you eat pork before you drink, it takes you twice as long to get drunk as someone who eats beef," says Oscar Ashaba, a regular patron at Bamboo Nest.
Others claim that pork has a strong tasty natural flavour that most people find appetising. "I know of people who have been on their deathbed suffering from AIDS, and pork was the only thing they could eat," says Fred Lyazi, owner of the popular Nicodemus pork butchery in Nakulabye.
It's an open secret that a few rebellious Muslims have been known to occasionally partake of this delicacy, something that is contrary to Islamic teaching that regards pork as unholy. "They never eat it in the open, they always ask for takeaway," confirms Lyazi.
He tells of a client who drove to Nicodemus one night, and from inside his car, ordered for several sticks of pork for a takeaway. Soon after he left, "one of the customers was shocked and told me that the man was a Muslim."
The high demand for pork does not only fill the bars, it has also greatly boosted the livestock industry in the country and the incomes of many pig farmers.
With the availability of micro-financing, many people, especially women have established piggeries as a means of improving household revenues.
Maria Nakimera, a farmer in Mubende, started with only three pigs in 1998. Now, she has over 300 and hundreds of piglets at her farm. She does not disclose how much income she earns from the pigs in a given month, but admits it's sufficient to look after her family, pay school fees for her five children, and has some left over for savings.
At present, there are no estimates to determine how much pork is eaten in the country or the city. What is for sure is that thousands of pigs are slaughtered every year to feed an insatiable pork market.
Nicodemus for instance, slaughters an average of 30 pigs on weekdays and 50 on weekends. "But on some occasions, especially public holidays, the number goes up, sometimes twice the usual amount," says Lyazi who employs 26 workers and also supplies several top hotels, bars and restaurants in the city.
The biggest percentage of pork comes from Wambizzi Co-operative, the largest pig slaughterhouse, located just off the road before Nateete town. On a good day, Wambizzi supplies between 200kg to 400kg a day and up to 600kg on weekends or public holidays. The abattoir has a troop of agents who routinely scout villages to buy pigs, which are transported to Kampala and sold for a tidy profit.
Last year, the slaughterhouse made headlines after area residents complained of poor hygiene and maintenance of the slaughterhouse. On a hot Tuesday afternoon, when I visited the area, I found the manager, only identified as Kalevu, in a cold rage over what he described as "Lies meant to destroy our business." "You (press) wrote that this place is very dirty and is unhealthy. Just look around you. Is this place filthy?" he asked furiously, pointing to the freshly scrubbed floors and wooden counters.
Many pork sellers, however, complain that Wambizzi is too expensive. There are some who have switched to much smaller slaughterhouses in other districts for cheaper supplies. Lyazi, for instance, buys his from Mubende, which enables him to sell a stick of pork at sh1,000, far more cheaply than other similar establishments, whose cost normally ranges between sh1,500 and sh3,000. A kilo of pork costs about sh2,500 although the "special" (without the fat) goes for sh4,000.
As demand for pork increases, there are fears that the price could rise even higher. Worse, the country might even run out of pigs. At Wambizzi, Kalevu revealed that there is a scarcity of pigs in the country and some of his agents sometimes have to travel as far as Rwanda to meet the demand.
But given that there are lax health controls, questions arise about the quality of the pork that is sold and whether it's actually safe for consumption.
Officially, the control and regulation of pork, is under the commissioner for public health in the agriculture ministry.
Nevertheless, it would appear that some of these controls and regulations are flouted by some butcheries in the suburbs, far from the long arm of the law. Some of the pork is sold without conducting any tests. "In the past, we have had some isolated cases of swine fever and blue pork, but we have managed to contain them," says an official in the agriculture ministry, who spoke on condition of anonymity. "But this is not our job alone. Local Councils too, must be vigilant."
The irony though is that the average pork eater probably wouldn't care about the pork's country of origin as long as it is tasty enough to tickle the taste buds.

Original_Search_Title: How Nigeria, US Trade is Impaired
Headline: Nigeria: How Nigeria, US Trade is Impaired
Body_Text:
U.S. goods trade deficit with Nigeria was $22.6 billion in 2005, an increase of $7.9 billion from $14.7 billion in 2004. U.S. goods exports in 2005 were $1.6 billion, up 3.9 percent from the previous year.
Corresponding U.S. imports from Nigeria were $24.2 billion, up 48.9 percent. Nigeria is currently the 53rd largest export market for U.S. goods. The stock of U.S. foreign direct investment (FDI) in Nigeria in 2004 was $955 million, down from $1.1 billion in 2003. U.S. FDI in Nigeria is concentrated largely in the mining and wholesale sectors. OXFAM, an international body which monitors trade relations highlights some impediments to trade between Nigeria and the United States.
Import Policies Nigeria's high tariffs and numerous import bans have been a concern to many U.S. businesses and were also raised in the context of Nigeria's May 2005 Trade Policy Review in the World Trade Organization. Tariffs: Tariffs provide the Nigerian government with its second-largest source of revenue after oil exports. In its last major tariff revision, in October 2005, the government implemented the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) Common External Tariff, reducing the number of tariff bands in Nigeria from twenty to five. The five tariff bands are: zero duty on capital goods, machinery, and essential drugs not produced locally; 5 percent duty on imported raw materials; 10 percent duty on intermediate goods; 20 percent duty on finished goods; and 50 percent duty on goods in the industries that the government seeks to protect. The 50-percent tariff would cover many items currently subject to import bans. Items deemed to be necessities such as anti-ret roviral drugs for the treatment of patients with HIV/AIDS will now be imported duty free. This duty-free status will be reviewed after one year to assess its impact on the Nigerian economy and its stakeholders.
The recent reforms are an effort to harmonize the trade environment in the sub-region while at the same time improving Nigeria's own trade and investment environment. Frequent policy changes and inconsistent duty collection make importing difficult and expensive and occasionally create severe bottlenecks for commercial activities. This problem is aggravated by Nigeria's dependence on imported raw materials and finished goods, which affects both foreign and domestic manufacturers. Many importers resort to under-valuing and smuggling to avoid paying full tariffs. Non-Tariff Trade Barriers The United States continues to have serious concerns about the Nigerian government's use of non-tariff barriers to trade. Bans on the importation of a variety of items - sorghum, mille t, wheat flour, cassava, frozen meat and poultry products, biscuits, bottled water, fruit juice in retail packs, beer, mosquito repellent coils, most textile and apparel products, used clothing, and cars more than eight years old - continued into 2005. Products added to the list of banned items in 2005 include maize, cocoa butter, disinfectants and germicides, diaries, greeting cards, calendars, and facial tissues. Items removed from the list in 2005 include certain textile products (such as nylon tire cord, conveyor belts, trimmings and linings, gloves for industrial use, elastic bands, mosquito nets, motifs), chocolates, white cement, linseed oils, castor oils, hydrogenated vegetable fats used as industrial raw materials, all raw materials for the manufacture of soap and detergents, safety shoes used in the oil industry, sports shoes, stadium chairs and fittings, accessories used in furniture making, and prefabricated buildings. Overall, the government has reduced the numb er of items on its import prohibition list and has stated its intention to rescind all import bans by January 2007, but new import bans, such as an announced ban on rice imports starting in 2006, are inconsistent with the government's stated intentions. Customs Barriers Nigerian port practices continue to present major obstacles to trade. Importers face long clearance procedures, high berthing and unloading costs, erratic application of customs regulations, and corruption. Customs exemptions granted to U.S. firms as a concession for setting up operations in Nigeria have not always been honored. In December 2005, the government released import guidelines for the implementation of a physical destination inspection regime. Under the destination inspection scheme, all imports will be inspected on arrival into Nigeria. These guidelines will be implemented by the Destination Inspection Service Providers, which is a team comprised of the Customs Service and three firms that provide scanning services. Standards, testing, labeling, and certification Rules concerning sanitary and phytosanitary standards, testing, and labeling are well defined, but bureaucratic hurdles slow the import-approval process. Regardless of origin, all food, drug, cosmetic, and pesticide imports must be accompanied by certificates of analysis from manufacturers and appropriate national authorities, and specified animal products, plants, seeds, and soils must be accompanied by proper inspection certificates. U.S. exporters may obtain these certificates from the U.S. Department of Agriculture and other relevant federal or state agencies. By law, items entering Nigeria must be labeled exclusively in the metric system.
The Nigerian Customs Service is charged with preventing the entry of products with dual or multiple markings, but such items are often found in Nigerian markets. High tariffs and uneven application of import and labeling regulations make importing high-value perishable products into Nigeria difficult. Disputes between Nigerian agencies over the interpretation of regulations often cause delays and frequent changes in customs guidelines slow the movement of goods through Nigerian ports. These factors can contribute to product deterioration and may translate into significant losses for perishable-goods importers. The National Agency for Food and Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC) is charged with protecting Nigerian consumers from fraudulent or unhealthful products. The agency recently targeted the illicit importation of counterfeit and expired pharmaceuticals for special attention, particularly imports from East and South Asia. NAFDAC's severely limited capacity for carrying out inspections and testing contributes to what some have characterized as an occasionally heavy-handed or arbitrary approach to regulatory enforcement, and the agency has occasionally challenged legitimate food imports. U.S. products do not appear to be subject to extraordinary or discriminatory restrictions or regulations. Government procurement The Obasanjo administration has made modest progress on its pledge to practice open and competitive bidding and contracting for government procurement. Procurement and contracting guidelines are implemented by a "due-process" office in the Budget Monitoring and Price Intelligence Unit. "Due process" certification aims at ensuring that the procurement process for public projects adheres to international standards for competitive bidding. The unit acts as a clearing house for government contracts and procurement and monitors the implementation of projects to ensure compliance with contract terms and budgetary restrictions. Procurement above 50 million naira (about $385,000) is subject to "due process" review. Foreign companies incorporated in Nigeria receive national treatment and government tenders are published in local newspapers. U.S. companies have won government contracts in several sect ors. Unfortunately, many companies that have won contracts have subsequently had difficulty getting them funded, usually as a result of delays in the national budget process, and some companies that won contracts for which funds were allocated have had trouble getting paid. Nigeria is not a signatory to the WTO Agreement on Government Procurement. Export promotion The Nigerian Export Promotion Council and the Nigerian Export-Import Bank administer export incentive programs that include tax concessions, export development funds, capital assets depreciation allowances, and foreign currency retention programs. Funding constraints limit the effectiveness of these programs. In 2005, the government of Nigeria (GON) rescinded all export subsidies, because it claims that the Common External Tariff (CET) it implemented in October 2005 automatically favors manufacturers through its lower tariffs on capital goods and raw materials. The Nigerian Export Processing Zone Authority (NEPZA) is responsible for attracting investment in export-oriented industries. Of the five zones established under NEPZA, only the Calabar and Bonny Island (Onne) export-processing zones are operational, with some difficulties reported. The Calabar export-processing zone also functions as a free trade zone. NEPZA rules dictate that at least 75 percent of production in the zones be exported, but lower export levels are tolerated. Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) Protection Nigeria is a member of the World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO), a party to the Universal Copyright Convention (UCC), the Berne Convention, and the Paris Convention for the Protection of Industrial Property, and has signed the WIPO Copyright Treaty and the WIPO Performances and Phonograms Treaty.
Legislation pending in the National Assembly is intended to establish a legal framework for an IPR system compliant with WTO rules. The government's lack of institutional capacity to address IPR issues is a m ajor constraint to enforcement. Relevant Nigerian institutions suffer from low morale, poor training, and limited resources. Fraudulent alteration of IPR documentation is common. Despite Nigeria's active participation in the conventions cited above, its reasonably comprehensive IPR laws, and growing interest among Nigerians in seeing their intellectual property protected, piracy is rampant in Nigeria. Counterfeit auto parts, pharmaceuticals, business and entertainment software, music and video recordings, and other consumer goods are sold openly throughout the country, and intellectual property infringers from other countries appear increasingly to be using Nigeria as a base for the production of pirated goods. In 2004, U.S. industry reported a growth of optical disk manufacturing plants, some of which may be contributing to the production of pirated optical disk products. Additionally, book piracy remains a problem. Patent and trademark enforcement remains weak, and judicia l procedures are slow and subject to corruption. Nonetheless, recent government efforts to curtail IPR abuse have yielded results.
The Federal High Court of Enugu, Nigeria, issued an interim injunction on November 23, 2004 against several firms infringing a Honeywell International trademark for spark plugs. The court warned all distributors, dealers, and retailers in Nigeria that the unauthorized use of Honeywell's "Autolite" trademark is illegal and constitutes an offense punishable by fine or imprisonment.
Nigeria's broadcast regulations do not permit rebroadcast or excerpting of foreign programs unless the station has an affiliate relationship with a foreign broadcaster. This regulation is generally respected, but some cable providers illegally transmit foreign programs. The National Broadcasting Commission monitors the industry and is responsible for punishing infractions.
Almost no foreign feature films have been legally distributed in the country in the last two decades. Widespread pirating of foreign and domestic videotapes discourages the entry of licensed distributors. In 2004, the Nigerian Copyright Commission launched an anti-piracy initiative named "Strategy Against Piracy" (STRAP). The Nigerian police force, working closely with the Nigerian Copyright Commission, has raided enterprises producing and selling pirated software and videos, and a number of high-profile charges have been filed against IPR violators.
Unfortunately, most raids appear to target small rather than large and well-connected pirates, and very few cases involving copyright, patent, or trademark infringement have been successfully prosecuted.
Services barriers
Foreign participation in the services sector is generally not restricted. Regulations provide for 100 percent foreign access in many service sectors, including banking, insurance, telecommunications, and securities. Central Bank of Nigeria directives stipulates minimum levels of paid-up capital. At least three foreign banks operate in Nigeria and several Nigerian banks have foreign shareholders.
Professional societies in engineering, accounting, medicine, and law define minimum professional requirements. Nigeria imposes quotas on expatriate employment based on the issued capital of firms. Quotas are especially strict in the oil and gas sector and may apply to both production and service companies. Oil and gas companies must hire Nigerian workers unless they can demonstrate that particular positions require expertise not found in the local workforce. Positions in finance and human resources are almost exclusively reserved for Nigerians; certain geosciences and management positions may be filled by expatriates with the approval of the National Petroleum Investment and Management Services (NAPIMS) agency.
Each oil company must negotiate its expatriate worker allotment with NAPIMS. Significant delays in the approval of this allotment, and in subsequent approval of visas for expatriate personnel, present serious management challenges to the energy industry's efforts to acquire the necessary personnel and maintain their legal immigration status in Nigeria.
NAPIM's approval is required for all procurement in the energy sector above $500,000.
Approval processes are slow and can significantly escalate the time and cost required for a given project, as well as providing opportunities for corruption and favoritism.
Investment barriers
Under the Nigerian Investment Promotion Commission (NIPC) Decree of 1995, Nigeria allows 100-percent foreign ownership of firms outside the petroleum sector. Investment in the petroleum sector is limited to existing joint ventures or production-sharing agreements. Foreign investors may buy shares of any Nigerian firm except firms on a "negative list" (such as manufacturers of firearms, ammunition, and military and paramilitary apparel). Foreign investors must register with the NIPC after incorporation under the Companies and Allied Matters Decree of 1990. The decree prohibits nationalization or expropriation of a foreign enterprise, except when necessary to protect the national interest. Despite efforts to improve the country's investment climate, disincentives to investing in Nigeria continue to plague foreign entrepreneurs. Potential investors must contend with poor infrastructure, complex tax administration procedures, confusing land ownership laws, arbitrary applica tion of regulations, corruption, and extensive crime. The sanctity of contracts is often violated, and Nigeria's court system for settling commercial disputes is weak and sometimes biased.
Foreign oil companies are under significant pressure to increase procurement from indigenous firms. NAPIMS set a target of 40 percent local content for oil-related projects by 2005 and 60 percent by 2010. Oil companies and NAPIMS appear to be working together cooperatively to meet these goals, but the extent of and mechanisms for enforcement of local content regulations remain unclear. In many cases, sufficiently trained personnel and physical infrastructure do not currently exist to meet the government's local content targets. The Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) is working toward identifying and certifying indigenous firms with specific skill sets through the Joint Qualification System (JQS). Although many indigenous firms possess adequate technical expertise, managerial and financial capabilities are often lacking.
Other barriers
The Nigerian government has increased its efforts to eliminate financial crimes such as money laundering and advance-fee fraud (or "419 frauds," named after the relevant section of the Nigerian Criminal Code). With the encouragement and cooperation of U.S. law enforcement agencies, the Nigerian government is now prosecuting more "419" perpetrators. But fraud, theft, and extortion remain rampant.
International monitoring groups routinely rank Nigeria among the most corrupt countries in the world. While sales of U.S. goods and services to public-and private-sector enterprises are not restricted, some U.S. suppliers believe they lose sales when they refuse to engage in illicit or corrupt behavior. Other U.S. exporters say Nigerian businessmen and officials understand that U.S. firms must adhere to the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act, and they believe that the law's restrictions help minimize their exposure to corruption.

Original_Search_Title: Fresh Supplies Push Food Prices Down
Headline: Uganda: Fresh Supplies Push Food Prices Down
Body_Text:
Kampala — FOODSTUFF prices have reduced as fresh supplies increase in Kampala's major markets, a weekly survey has shown.
There has been increased supply of fresh commodities from rural areas due to the harvesting period.
The development has forced dry food dealers to lower prices to attract more consumers, clear old stock and replenish them with the new produce.
"Prices will continue to go down because we are getting harvests.
If we are getting this supply, what will you expect at the peak of the harvesting? Prices will definitely go down," John Wasswa, a trader, said.
Wasswa said plenty of beans, cassava, matooke and fruits had forced prices of maize and cassava flour and dry beans to go down.
A kilo of beans at St. Balikuddembe Market costs sh900 from sh1,100. Maize flour costs between sh500 and sh800 a kilo.

Original_Search_Title: Ncard Recommends Lagos Fish Farm Model
Headline: Nigeria: Ncard Recommends Lagos Fish Farm Model
Body_Text:
AGRICULTURE commissioners in the country have been urged to adopt the Lagos State successful model of fish farm estate in their respective states in order to complement the on-going Presidential Initiative on Fisheries and Aquaculture in Nigeria.
This call was contained in the 22 point communique issued at the end of the 35th regular meeting of the National Council on Agriculture and Rural Development (NCARD) held in Abeokuta, Ogun State during the week.
Lagos State Government took a giant stride in the provision of cheap protein in the form of fresh fish for Lagos inhabitants and other populace when a 34 hectare land at Odongunyan in Ikorodu was converted into a 5,000 metric tonne capacity Fish Farm Estate.
On completion, the estate is expected to reduce the deficit in the demand-supply gap in local fish production as presently the current national fish demand is put at about two million metric tones while the local production is below 700 metric tones.
The Presidential Initiative on Fisheries and Aquaculture which was commended by the participants was aimed at increasing domestic fish production in Nigeria to two million tonnes by next year in order to meet the national fish demand of 1.5 million metric tonnes from the present production level of 0.5 million metric tonnes a year as well as guarantee surplus for export.
The focus was on implementing an Aquaculture and inland fisheries development programme which was aimed at optimizing fish production from brackish and freshwater resources industry, irrigation, dams and reservoirs.
Council members present at the Abeokuta meeting decried the continued menace of sea armed robbery and piracy in Nigeria and its negative effects on marine fisheries and expressed satisfaction for the prompt intervention of the President in setting up a Presidential Committee to ensure adequate and guaranteed security of lives and property at the sea.
It will be recalled that, The Nigerian Trawler Owners Association, umbrella body for twenty five companies in industrial fisheries operating in the country's territorial waters, recently decried the continued menace of pirates and sea robbers on their trawlers.
The council members noted that fish feed constitutes a major constraint to Fishery/Aquaculture productions and therefore stressed the need for all states to encourage fish feed production adding that it will be premature to impose any form of ban on fish importation, rather, states should adopt the implementation of the Presidential Initiatives on Fisheries and Aquaculture by encouraging farmers to go into fisheries and aquaculture.
The NCARD meeting which was held under the chairmanship of the Minister of Agriculture and Rural Development, Mallam Adamu Bello, had in attendance the Minister of State for Agriculture, Otunba Bamidele Dada, the commissioners from the 36 states of the federation and the Secretary for Agriculture, Federal Capital Territory Administration and representatives of FAO, American Embassy, the Agricultural Credit Guarantee Scheme Fund (ACGSF), Ministry of Water Resources among others.
The council members, while commending the commitment of President Olusegun Obasanjo in his various efforts and initiatives to move the agricultural sector forward, expressed concern on the Buffer Stock Storage Programme where States are supposed to complement the Federal Government Strategic storage.
The States were, therefore, enjoined to construct and stock 10,000 capacity silos/warehouses each and also to activate their on-farm storage programme where they are to construct 13,000 units of 1 ton metal bins for the benefit of their farmers.
Bayelsa Govt reinstates commitment to agric
THE Bayelsa State Governor, Dr. Goodluck Jonathan has restated government's preparedness to support any private initiative aimed at creating wealth and eradicate poverty among the people of the state.
The governor, who stated this during a presentation on making money from cassava production by Global Cassava Initiative, assured that any project that is community-based and beneficial to the people will receive both moral and financial support from the state government.
He said taking cognizance of the fact that oil was a non-renewable source of energy, government was desirous to diversify the revenue-generation base of the state, adding that agriculture is a veritable source of employment generation and wealth creation.
Dr. Jonathan said everything would be done to boost the agricultural base of the state, pointing out that a state that cannot feed itself will not be in position to guarantee security.
President of Global Cassava Initiative, Bishop Elkana Hanson, while making his presentation, said the state stands to benefit a lot from cassava production as it will help propel the economy of the state, if adopted.
He said apart from its economic benefits, mechanized cassava production has the capacity of generating employment for thousands of youths from the state.
Bishop Hanson said his group has already carried out over six months of field research and topographic experiment to ascertain the best stem type suitable for Bayelsa.
He said the country loses close to $4 billion annually for not properly harnessing and taking advantage of cassava production and called on the state government to fund the project.
In related development, the State Government says it is determined to boost agriculture and tourism in the state as part of efforts to diversify its revenue generation base. The State Governor, Dr. Goodluck Jonathan stated this while inaugurating the State Implementation Committee on Federal Government Agricultural Loan Scheme in Government House, Yenagoa.
Dr. Jonathan pointed out that the state government on its part has provided loans to small scale farmers in the state, stressing that efforts should be made to enlighten farmers on how to access these loans. The Governor said the Committee has the responsibility of ensuring that the state's allocation from the Federal Government Agricultural Loan Scheme is disbursed to practicing farmers. He urged the members of the Committee to see their appointment as a call to national service adding that government attaches a high premium on agricultural development in the state.
The Governor pledged government's commitment to ensuring that the committee succeeds. Responding, the Deputy Governor of the State and Chairman of the Committee, Hon. Peremobowei Ebebi expressed gratitude to the Governor for counting them worthy to be members of the Committee. Hon. Ebebi said the Committee will do its best to justify the confidence reposed in it. He pledged that the loans will be disbursed creditably, using past participation records, as a yard stick.

Original_Search_Title: A Pregnant Moment for Peter Obi
Headline: Nigeria: A Pregnant Moment for Peter Obi
Body_Text:
Lagos — It is no longer in doubt that Governor Peter Obi of Anambra State is now set to work; to set the ball rolling. A few weeks ago, he rolled out what he called his blueprint for the state. Most of the contents of that document were part of the promises he made to the people while campaigning and canvassing for their votes in 2003.
Today, those promises are no more mere paper work or utopian dreams meant to convince the people and win their votes. Obi, widely known for his humility and tenacity of purpose has finally come to grips with the reality of the situation. He is now in-charge and has therefore put his priorities in proper perspective for the people to see and then give him the required support and encouragement to make things work.
What Anambra State needs at the moment is participatory democracy in which everybody in the state has to be fully involved in order to make things work. It should no longer be business as usual. The people of the state have to be encouraged by the government to see governance as a form of collective responsibility. Some people may argue: 'Heck! What the hell with government! What business do I have with government and all that!' However, here is a state battered over the years by enemies of the people, sycophants who saw nothing good in moving the state forward.
As the years rolled by, many Anambrarians lost sense of personal and societal values. Honesty and diligence of purpose were sacrificed on the altar of idiocy. Today, the state needs total re-orientation of its people so that they would be able to come to terms with the need to begin once more to appreciate the essence of values for transparency, accoutability, hardwork and the love for humanity. In this regard therefore, Governor Obi and his seasoned technocrats, administrators and political appointees have a lot to do to raise and reorder the moral conscience of the people.
Remoulding of moral conscience comes in different garments. As it is, it is not just enough to make agriculture, education and security top government priorities. There is urgent need to earnestly raise the moral standard of education in the state. The standard has been abused and badly disorganized by previous governments in the state. Male enrolment in schools has fallen below bottom level. Consequently, going into trading without proper education had taken the front burner.
Fortunately for all Anambrarians, this anomaly can be corrected by the regime of Peter Obi. As a successful trader who got a university degree before delving into business, Obi is in a better position to counsel the youths of the state on how not to see education as a waste. At every point, he owes it to the people to sound it loud and clear that one becomes better in his/her chosen profession with better education and knowledge.
This, indeed, is one of the ways to bring the boys back to the classrooms. Besides the strides which Obi has made as a graduate trader, I know also a couple of my secondary school mates who are university graduates and are equally doing well as traders. While some are at home, others are abroad. This is a task that must be accomplished: more scholarships have to be instituted and given to deserving students especially students from poor homes who are prepared to go to school and embrace knowledge in its totality.
Till now, previous Anambra government kept sealed lips on the return of missionary schools to their owners. Over time, government has maintained a stranglehold on these schools, locking them up and putting the keys in its pocket. Progress was stagnant. Morale was at its lowest ebb. Teachers sniffed, scoffed and groaned under the weight of unbearable bureaucratic yoke and retrogressive government policies. Consequently, less emphasis was placed on quality and functional education. In the process, however, skills acquisition both for the good of the society and for self-employment suffered abysmally. Now, the Governor has a lot to do. It is not just time to return schools, but also to re-introduce those important elements of education that helped to properly train the individual and prepare him for the future.
Good and sound education usually go hand-in-hand with the practice of agriculture. In our days, there were farmlands in school premises where we cultivated and planted cassava and yams and other crops. Very many of us learnt how to farm from those experiences and a couple of my mates went further to study agriculture and other related disciplines thereafter. Agriculture should remain top government priority and should be made to permeate all the schools in the state as well.
In addition to that, government should ensure that handiworks or handicrafts are no longer monetized by school authorities. Let students, as it used to be, be made to learn and acquaint themselves with one craft or the other. Such were the ways small scale industries sprang up in the early years of industrialization in Europe and Asia. Skills acquisition is a habit no one can take away from the person who has acquired it. In the end, one can safely start off his/her own business based on this knowledge.
In the same way, government has to work round the clock to restore the confidence of investors in the state. Potential investors have to be assured in no uncertain terms that their businesses are safe and that the state can guarantee their lives and properties. It is no more a matter of govenment promises but practical steps have to be taken to restore confidence, fight crime and protect public and private infrastructure in all nooks and crannies of the state.
Obi may learn a lesson from Muritala Muhammed who ruled for only six months yet his name resonates in the history of Nigeria. Obi has to tighten his grip and concentrate on working vigorously on the blueprint for the state. In the affairs of leadership it is not usually how long that matters, but how well. And where a leader does what his people actually need, what really touches on their lives, the clamour for acclaim is usually thunderous and infectious.
The Governor has to display a leadership will and savvy that would set a rather potentially indifferent people to go along with him to make a success of his tenure. He should also employ some Solomonic wisdom and tact in dealing with the state legislature, for whether he likes it or not he cannot perform well without their maximum co-operation.
In all, both the governor, the legislature and other public office holders in the state should be conscious of the fact that the state is bigger than the individual. And so the progress of the state has to come tops on the agenda of government. This, no doubt, is one of the surest ways to move the state forward for the good of all.
God bless Anambra State.

Original_Search_Title: Waiting for Taylor in the Hague
Headline: Sierra Leone: Waiting for Taylor in the Hague
Body_Text:
Freetown — I wonder how it would have been like if Europe and the United States would just open their gates for at least an hour and allow Africans to stream in droves without the headache of visa restrictions. The word anarchy would have been an understatement and any attempt to restrain a loved one from going would fall on deaf ears. The idea of most Africans is that it is very easy over there. In fact, many think manna just fell from heaven. The reality is that we do have brothers and sisters out there who literally sleep on the sidewalks , others do barely have jobs to etch out a living while some have to contend with immigration wahala coupled with the uncompromising winter.
For many of those whose minds have been made up to go to the "white man's home," they will do everything humanly possible even to the extent of nakedly risking their lives along the Moroccan borders for Spain. But wait a minute , ask yourself this question ; with corruption growing wings like an over fed bird everyday, with Kabbah tiger generators having succeeding in replacing a moribund NPA (No Power Available) in our homes and with the unsightly spectacle of filth in every available space in the city coupled with ' No Jobs Available,' what would you expect of a desperate man or woman? To them, their only hope is to go out there and start from afresh clean up rubbish, wash dead bodies and so on.
Few weeks back , I decided to go and see for myself what is happening on that side of the world . I did not go to stay. My destination was primarily France but with the prospect of seeing Liberia's former president ,Charles Ghankay Macarthur Taylor having his day in court in the Dutch town of Den Haag, it served as a perfect enticement to go there and just in case ; wait for Taylor in The Hague.
In recent times, the name, The Hague, has become synonymous with "a court". And it is not just an ordinary court; The Hague, which is the administrative hub of the Netherlands, is the seat of the UN International Criminal Tribunal for Yugoslavia (ICTY).
Nobody visits this court without making international headline news. Until recently, it was the trial and death of Sloboan Milosevic, former president of Yugoslavia that made headline news from there. Now, a Dutch businessman is also behind bars and facing trial there for his alleged involvement in arms deal during the Liberian Civil war.
For me , the ICTY was a major landmark that I had wanted to visit ever since I was born but what further strengthened my resolve was the fact that Den Haag had opened her doors for its ICTY facilities to host the trial of Taylor should they find a jail for him if proven guilty of his charges and considering the fact that I come from a country where Taylor is being kept waiting for trial backed by the fact that I also work in his native , Liberia was a motivating factor to have ventured there to see what awaits the former president when he finally arrives.
Thus, when the opportunity came for me to take some time off work from my normal community outreach activities, it dawned on me that a visit to The Hague was almost now a fait accompli . The journey itself started by boarding a flight to Accra Ghana for an almost fours hours and twenty five minutes flight which saw me being much more closer to God in prayers amid the recitation of some holy verses for a very long while . After resting for about seven hours at the Kotakoa airport, I was enroute again but this time round, onboard a comfortable KLM flight whose beautiful air hostesses were almost always on hand to ask , " coffee , tea, juice, wine or snacks ?".
