In the early hours of April 12, I landed in Schipol airport in Holland but it was not my final stop. Charles De Gaule airport in Paris beaconed and in less than 45 minutes from takeoff at Schipol, I was there .
Picked up by a friend, off we went to the Parisan suburb of Melun on board an underground train. Paris, they say is one of the most beautiful cities in the World and I got the better part of it but alas !
Unlike most of our West African states, there is more life underground her holes and tunnels than above.
In fact there is a lot of fuzz and buzz down there where you meet a lot of people than you can imagine order than on top, literally running to and fro for trains . After enjoying the beautiful landmarks of Paris including her Eiffel Tower and the famous media houses of Le Monde and the Stade de France among others , I was ready to set out again for The Hague.
On a cold and windy Saturday morning in April, I boarded a Thallys ( underground electric train ) from Paris' Gardene Nord for an over four hours journey to Den Haag's central station. As the place was very cold, I had to double and redouble my clothes to keep warm. Upon arrival in Den Haag as it is know in Dutch, one could easily feel the serene and peaceful atmosphere of this small Dutch town which happens to be the country's seat of government. There is some kind of romantic air that looms around it that one can't simply resist its enticement from falling in love with it.
Since it was my maiden visit, I decided to take a look at some of the landmark features, which saw me moving from street to street and visiting a few shops in the process. The Hague boasts of many museums and is definitely a place to be for the lover of arts.
Many of the elegant buildings must have been built several hundreds of years ago and the main streets are dotted with government offices. There is the historic collection of buildings known as the Binnenhof, home to both chambers of the legislature and there is Paleis Noordeinde where Queen Beatrix's administrative offices are housed. The Maduro Dam is also a must see. A major form of transportation there are Trams and bicycles. They battle with taxies and other cars for available space in the city's tiny and narrow roads. It is even muted that the Prime Minister, at times goes to work on his bicycle. Can our own president try it one day? I mean moving from Hill Station to PZ and see for himself how our brothers and sisters trod everyday in search of getting their stomachs filled by 2007?
With a friend as my guide, I decided to satisfy the curiosity of my eyes by visiting the principal judicial organ of the UN, the ICTY. Locating Taylor's next port of call in this small Dutch city was a problem. Despite armed with a map and several stopovers to ask questions, it could not save us from going round and round like a merry- go- round until a Good Samaritan was gracious enough to literally install the direction into our heads and, off we dashed for our gold spot.
Right in an area called Churchillplein (or Peace Palace????) was the imposing building that at first glance reminds you of those medieval churches or a museum in Eastern Europe. It does not have the looks of our Law Courts Building on Siaka Stevens street, nor the Temple of Justice in his home country or even the Special Court premises at New England Vile.
Surrounded by the world Forum Center and KPMG, one of the world's renowned financial auditing firms, the ICTY surroundings has an air of pin drop silence that was somewhat frightening for someone like me from post-war Sierra Leone.
Perhaps, I should talk a bit about The ICTY. It started work in 1946 replacing the Permanent Court of International Justice, which had functioned in the Peace Palace since 1922. The Court is composed of 15 judges elected by the UN General Assembly and Security Council sitting independently of each other.
Unlike the Special Court in my home country that is heavily guarded by battle tested Mongolian troops with raised concrete walls and barbed wires, the ICTY has a relaxed outlook which does not mean security is taken for granted.
Though I walked up to the main entrance of Taylor's next home, I never had the opportunity of passing through the first gate and unlike Sierra Leone where four people were recently held for taking photos at the Special Court, at the ICTY , you can take as much as you want and nobody will cough.
But take your time to ask passersby and even the taxi drivers around whether or not they know that Taylor, appears headed for the building just in front of them , most will ask, " but who is this Taylor? " Need I say more to them? Of course not but again , those guys still have fresh tales of how until recently, the former Serbian president , Slobodan Milosevic was a guest of honor at The Hague.
From what I saw, The Hague is a nice place to be.
Although some people including Taylor may disagree.
There, his worries over mosquito bites, the scotching heat or what can now be best described as the uninterrupted flow of darkness - I mean , electricity shortage and just maybe, conjugal visits may be over.
After the Haag, I also had the opportunity of rubbing shoulders with the Sierra Leonean community in the cites of Helmond and Eindhoven where I attended a Sierra Leonean jam session and a PMDC organized meeting as an observer. The trip also gave me the opportunity of reuniting with two of my former colleagues at Concord Times, Kingsley Evans (Sugar), Alex John and Benjamin Palmer, formerly of For Di People. The latter came all the way down from Germany to see me.
Interestingly , guys kept asking my friend during my stay with him whether I was there for good or not but I am back to base , in Africa , where I enjoy eating my cassava leaves with red oil and of course, bush meat.
The writer was one-time Editor of Concord Times.

Original_Search_Title: UN Agriculture Agency Launches Two Regional Markets
Headline: Africa: UN Agriculture Agency Launches Two Regional Markets
Body_Text:
The United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) today launched two projects for east and southern Africa to modernize the subsistence farming on which some national economies largely depend and to promote local commerce and foreign trade.
The first project will group Burundi, Rwanda and Uganda in the Great Lakes region. Agriculture there is highly dependent on rainfall and farmers will be encouraged to make their use of water more efficient and to strengthen their organizations through training courses and field schools, at a cost of $3 million, the Rome-based FAO said.
The second project, estimated to cost $1.5 million, is designed to improve cassava yields in Malawi and Zambia, with the aim of processing it commercially into such exportable items as starch, according to the agency.
"Recurrent droughts and poor yields of traditionally produced maize, which is oversensitive to climatic variations, have encouraged the widespread farming of cassava, making it Africa's fastest growing food crop today," FAO said.
Both projects, which are being funded by the Italian Government, will rely heavily on spreading information to the most isolated farmers through local radio broadcasts.
The five beneficiary countries are members of the Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa (COMESA), a partnership established in 1994 to foster regional economic integration, and the projects follow the broad strategy agreed in 2001 by the members of the New Partnership for Africa's Development (NEPAD).
"It is important that the countries concerned have committed themselves to giving priority to modernizing every aspect of agriculture, that is to say, from production to processing and marketing," said M.E. Chipeta, Director of FAO Policy Assistance Division.

Original_Search_Title: US, Ebonyi to Set Up Agro Firm
Headline: Nigeria: US, Ebonyi to Set Up Agro Firm
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — PONMAS Corporation, a United States's agro-based company is collaborating with Ebonyi State government and private Nigerian investors to set up cassava processing plant, fish farm and cattle breeding centre at Izhia in Ohaukwu Local Government area of the state.
The state Commissioner for Information, Mr. Abia Onyike who disclosed this to journalists at the end of the state's weekly executive council meeting on Friday in Abakaliki, explained that the farm was a private sector cum public sector initiative involving the Izhia Agro Industries Limited, the PONMAS Corporation, local government councils and Amike Izhia community, noting that the state government would play the role of facilitator.
He disclosed that the funds for the project would be sourced from banks in a loan packaging arrangement that would enable the state participate in the emerging cassava export market.
The commissioner said when operational, the company would produce starch in commercial quantity, cassava and flour, artificial insemination of cattle for breeding, fishing and raw materials from animals for industrial development.
Onyike disclosed that the total investment in machinery and other services, including freight would amount to $8m, adding that with this development, the state would spearhead the first large-scale cassava processing industry whose activities would be integrated into projects and programmes of the state committee on increased food production.
The commissioner also stated that another decision taken at the executive council meeting was the approval of the release of the White Paper on the Report of the Panel of Enquiry into the Crisis between Christians and traditional worshippers of Nkalaha community in Ishielu Local Government area of the state.
He said government approved the panel's recommendation because Nigeria is a secular state, so the citizens are entitled to freedom of worship and freedom of religion of their choice, as enshrined in the 1999 constitution.
The state government therefore directed that no religion or culture has the right to infringe upon the freedom of expression, association or movement of citizens, stressing that the Omabe masquerade and its adherents that were said to be harassing the Christians should ensure that their activities and celebrations do not in any way interfere with the freedom of movement.

Original_Search_Title: Infrastructure Loan From UN Agricultural Fund Will Benefit 160,000
Headline: Congo-Brazzaville: Infrastructure Loan From UN Agricultural Fund Will Benefit 160,000
Body_Text:
A loan of more than $8 million to the Republic of Congo will help strengthen markets and financial services and improve agricultural production, benefiting tens of thousands of people in three provinces, according to an agreement signed in Rome today, the United Nations agricultural fund said.
About 15,000 households will benefit by taking part directly in project activities in the Rural Development Project in the Congolese departments of Niari, Bouenza and Lékoumou, while another 160,000 people will benefit from improvements in the physical and economic infrastructure, the International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD) said.
The project will be funded partly by an IFAD loan of $8.4 million and partly by $7.5 million in co-financing from the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries' Fund for International Development (OPEC Fund).
The new funds will be used to rehabilitate roads, improve access to markets and allow for the distribution of better quality seeds and planting materials, especially disease-resistant cassava cuttings. The project will also train farmers groups to boost their management and negotiating skills, IFAD said.
Although small farmers produce more than 90 per cent of the country's food by traditional methods, less than 2 per cent of arable land is being cultivated and agricultural development has been neglected since the 1990s civil conflict, the Fund noted.

Original_Search_Title: Dr Nérée Onguene Awana : "We Have a New Way of Doing Research "
Headline: Cameroon: Dr NÃ©rÃ©e Onguene Awana : "We Have a New Way of Doing Research "
Body_Text:
Dr Nérée Onguene Awana, National Coordinator of REPARAC explains the challenges of the project after receiving CFA 288 million support from France.
The French government has just disbursed CFA 288 million to promote agricultural research in Cameroon. How will REPARAC use the money?
The money will be used for research in partnerships by eight research teams that will work on fishery, provide technical support to palm oil as well as cocoyam and cassava producers. It will to solve soil fertility problem in Cameroon's grand southern region. The money will also be used in financing research on plantain, cocoa activities, food and vegetable production and semi-urban agriculture.
What criteria did you use in selecting the projects to be financed with the money?
The process started in June, 2005 when the projects for reinforcement of partnerships in Agronomic research in Cameroon, REPARAC were launched. We started by visiting seven provinces in Cameroon to explain the philosophy of the project. In effect, the project essentially does research in a way that is different from others. We actively involve families in defining the problem, developing the research project with researchers and executing them in small farming units. After explaining the projects, we received about 60 project proposals on which we worked. This took place in November and December last year when we organized a workshop in order to reformulate the projects. From there we got 20 proposals that were submitted to the scientific committee of REPARAC in April this year. This committee which is made up of university lecturers, senior researchers, and representatives of farm organizations then came out with a procedure which was used in selecting the eight proposals fr om the 20.
REPARAC looks like an ambitious project in research. What is it all about?
REPARAC is a research programme representing a subvention, (a gift) from the French corporation worth about CFA one billion. It has three components: provide scientific, technical and institutional support to IRAD through capacity building, support research activities in partnership in small scale farmers and coordinate and follow up these activities. The project was born on December 03, 2004. It has as main objective to improve on research efficiency in Cameroon by developing new forms of partnership that should bring forth simple proposals based on the demands of small scale farmers.
The project's steering committee just held in Yaounde during which an agreement was signed with the French Cooperation. Can we know exactly the main issues the steering committee set out to discuss and what was the outcome of that meeting?
Well, the main issues were first of all to examine the action plan for 2005 and to come out with some important decisions concerning implementation of the eight projects on which the CFA 288 million-subvention will be used. It was also to get the research contract between researchers and IRAD and the French representative signed. Among the major things we got from this is the budget that was approved. The project has been bringing some financial support to 16 IRAD researchers involved in training for different degrees, masters, doctorates, etc. The steering committee asked that this activity be pursued by providing some more money for that.
Can you tell us what REPARAC has already achieved since it was created 2001?
The main goal of REPARAC is to bring all actors involved in agricultural production to get together as partners. After one year, we can say that we have already eight good research teams with all partners. But we still have to see how all these will be functioning for the three years of the projects. REPARAC has already given out about CFA 16 million to help young researchers in IRAD. We have also contributed in valorization some IRAD scientific results like the last scientific review that took place in July 2005. We are trying to bring together researchers and university lecturers in such a way that they share their activities. In this light, we have made lecturers to be involved in research while some researchers are sharing their knowledge in teaching with their colleagues in the university.
One of the greatest problems with research in Cameroon is that most of the results remain in drawers. What is REPARAC doing to ensure that research results are effectively implemented on the field?
REPARAC has in its component one, an activity called volarisation. Here, REPARAC wants to make sure that what exists already as scientific acquisition can be passed on to farmers. We think that these are the people who can effectively valorize results. We also organized many other activities including workshops, meetings, and different kinds of publications.
What are the major challenges of REPARAC?
I think one of the main challenges of REPARAC is that it is coming out with a new way of doing research. It is very different from classical research, it's true that in the past we have been seeing IRAD and other research institutions working with different people but we really want it this time to make sure the partnership that is developed among the different people in the research team is really effective from start to finish. The challenge that we have is to make sure that researchers and farmers and the rest of the people in the research group understand what partnership is all about. Another challenge we have is REPARAC has to have an impact on development in three years time. I really hope we will be successful.
It's a real challenge and we want to make sure that we have a project on technical support to palm oil production. We don't really border about getting the resource but we want to have an impact. Another challenge for the next three years is whether REPARAC wil l be able to implement this new way of doing research and securing funding for research. It is difficult these days to have money for research. That is why we really have to be grateful to the French cooperation to pave the road for this.

Original_Search_Title: Fayose Sacks Commissioner
Headline: Nigeria: Fayose Sacks Commissioner
Body_Text:
Ado — GOVERNOR Ayo Fayose, yesterday, sacked his Commissioner for Agriculture and Rural Development, Chief Boboye Olanipekun, and nominated Mr. Owoseni Ajayi as the state's new Attorney General and Justice Commissioner.
Chief Olanipekun and the permanent secretary in the ministry, Mr. Olaniyi Bello had been placed on suspension for slow pace of work in the cassava plantation at Orin and the Ado-Ekiti abbatoir project.
The two projects were supposed to be commissioned by President Olusegun Obasanjo during his visit to the state last week.
Mr. Ajayi, an Ado-Ekiti based legal practitioner was until now, Chairman of the state sports council. He replaces Mr. Duro Ajayi.
Meanwhile, Governor Ayo Fayose of Ekiti State has expressed appreciation to the people of Ekiti State for the love and support they demonstrated during last week's visit of President Obasanjo to the state.
The governor who spoke in Ado-Ekiti, acknowledged the massive turn-out by the people everywhere the president visited. "I must confess that I was overwhelmed with joy with the type of reception accorded the president in the course of his visit. At every point the president visited, he was given a rousing welcome by a tumultuous crowd who appreciated him so much and the transformation work that our administration has embarked upon in the past three years in the state," he said.
Fayose thanked the president for the special interest he has in the state and his concern for the speedy development of the state which culminated in his visit to the state twice in one year.
Also yesterday the People's Democratic Party (PDP) in Ekiti State, organized a "thank-you" rally in Ado-Ekiti to show appreciation to the people of the state who turned-out en masse to welcome President Obasanjo.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Growing Thrives in Nakasongola
Headline: Uganda: Cassava Growing Thrives in Nakasongola
Body_Text:
Kampala — The vigorous exercise to re-introduce cassava growing in Nakasongola district is promising.
The exercise which started in 2000 was supported by several international and local groups and it was termed as the first phase.
Nakasongola currently boasts of over 10,000 hectares of cassava which shows a fairly good fight against hunger.
The scheme, started by Rwampanga and Wabinyonyi sub-counties, has since spread to other parts of the region.
In the late 80s and early 90s, almost all cassava in the region was destroyed by the rampaging Cassava Mosaic and other diseases. Uganda's cassava basket was no more. At the time cassava was the staple food and losing it introduced a new level of hunger.
Areas around Lake Kyoga are potential for cassava growing. This includes Apac, Kayunga, Lira and parts of Pallisa districts.
The success of the cassava campaign in Nakasongola is something worthy building on, throughout the entire region around Lake Kyoga.
"I think there is a very big potential that has not been well tapped in this region. It is encouraging that efforts to increase and improve production of cassava are entering another gear. This region should build on the success of the first phase that included the re-introduction and planting of cassava," Says Ronald Magado, coordinator Nakasongola District Farmers Association (NADIFA). He has been one of the key persons in the re-introduction of cassava.
"At first it was difficult, but people came on board and accepted it as one of the ways to fight hunger and poverty," says Magado. The varieties planted included TME 12, TME 14, NASE 12, SS4 and others. They were distributed to farmers through out the district. The result is that almost every homestead in the district has got a cassava shamba.
"We are now in the second and third phases of this campaign. This involves adding value to the finished products and looking for markets," Magado says. Accordingly, cassava with more value fetches better earnings for the farmers.
Groups like Farm Africa and JICA have shown interest in the cassava projects.
Cassava mills have been constructed throughout the cassava region and several cassava processing factories have come up throughout the district. There are hundreds of locally fabricated cassava shellers and choppers all over the district. These have eased up the initial processing process.
The more developed cassava processing factories include Acaproma that is located in Nakasongola municipality. Acaproma processes and packs ready to eat cassava flour.
Cassava is also turned into a delicious ready to eat food called GARI. "This is an instant food processed from fresh cassava tubers. It can be stored for two years," Magado says. Gari can be processed either with hot or cold water depending on one's choice. Gari, after processing is packed in packets of one or two kilograms, before it is sold in super markets and shops.
With improved cassava species, output per acre is also going up. On average, farmers produce 12 tones per acre. Originally, farmers used to produce around five tones when they were still using the old cassava species.
"I received two bags of improved cassava varieties after the training on improved cassava production. I have expanded these varieties to 6 acres from 0.5 acres in 2002. There is excess food at home," Fred Ssemwanga, 52, of Kisalizi in Rwampanga.
The production of cassava has eased his education of his children. He has also constructed a better house and even acquired a solar energy source.
One of the challenges facing the cassava growers is marketing. There is no particular market for farmers, especially in Kampala. Most of the raw cassava is sold on the shambas to trucks that come from Kampala.
"These people pay us the lowest prices. If we had a common market, I think we would be earning better," says Christopher Matovu of Nabiswera.
The other challenge still facing the production of cassava is the quality of the produce. Quality is mainly affected at the post harvesting level. For example, in many cases, cassava chips are dried on bare ground. "Many of the farmers don't have the necessary materials on which to dry the cassava. Some still dry it on the bare ground," says one of the farmers in Lwampanga.
Poor conservation of soils and water is also still a big problem.

Original_Search_Title: We Should Make the Price of Our Nshima
Headline: Zambia: We Should Make the Price of Our Nshima
Body_Text:
IT is very pleasing to Zambians to hear that the prices of mealie meal are by June this year expected to drop by a forecasted 60 per cent.
The very fact that the millers association has made this prediction should erase all doubts from those 'doubting Thomases' that just as the steady strengthening of the Kwacha was no hoax, the price of mealie meal is not being fixed for political expediency.
For a long time as a nation, we have lamented the high pricing of goods and services and now that the economic indicators point to the beginning of an improvement in both the delivery and pricing, there is reason for us to demand these changes from providers of the services and goods immediately.
Mealie meal has remained both a political and an emotive issue as far as the governance of Zambia is concerned from time immemorial because of the special status it enjoys as a staple food.
It is for this very reason that we get excited when we receive such glad tidings about nshima because it forms the biggest chunk in percentage of our daily nutrient intake.
On the other hand, it is also prudent for the majority of us to learn from our past mistakes and experiences.
These clearly show that the current unpredictable climate which we have experienced over the years should guarantee a steady flow of maize meal and ultimately keep the staple food affordable to all.
We need to start looking seriously at changing our strategies and ensuring that the various regions of the country, whose weather patterns vary, start to adjust to growing food crops well-suited to their areas.
Cassava, for instance, and other drought-resistant foods like millet, sorghum and sweet potatoes can quickly replace maize or supplement it as other better sources of food if we are to survive as a nation.
Facts on the ground do not even suggest that the poorest of the poor in rural areas depend on the same mealie meal and price mechanisms of maize obtaining in the urban centers.
Most of our rural-dwelling kin actually grow their own maize, cassava or millet that they have milled at village mills.
Why then should our colleagues in the commercial milling business impose such high prices of maize meal on us?
Why do we allow them to do so when we, as ordinary Zambians, can force them to reduce prices on their expensive mealie meal by resolving to grow our own Limas of maize and grind our own maize in our localities for consumption in our homes?
We are definitely wiser as a nation than we were a decade or so ago. We know too well what the remedy to the soaring prices of the staple food is.
It is now incumbent upon us to use our power to make decisions and start growing most of our own food for consumption if we are to determine the pricing of our dear nshima.

Original_Search_Title: What Could Have Influenced Cabinet Appointments?
Headline: Uganda: What Could Have Influenced Cabinet Appointments?
Body_Text:
The cabinet list is finally out. Rodney Muhumuza and Emmanuel Gyezaho look at what may have informed the appointing authority's thinking.
If the list of nominees to Cabinet, made public yesterday, came amid a lot of unpredictability and tension, it was for good reason.
While it gave some credence to the bulk of media speculation that preceded it, the curious mix of new-timers, odd choices and previously unknown persons betrayed the complexities that must have informed its drafting.
But it did not lie on one thing: that the men who previously managed the health ministry and, even more critically, were implicated in the Global Fund scam, had lost their jobs.
Health ministers out
Maj. Gen Jim Muhwezi, Capt. Mike Mukula and Dr Alex Kamugisha, who typically became the embodiment of the biggest financial scandal since the junk chopper affair, will not be in the next Cabinet.
But so will reliable NRM cadres like Prof. Mondo Kagonyera, known for his hard work ethic and Igeme Nabeta, a youthful legislator, who many commentators thought had come to typify an emerging shift in the NRM power base from the old breed to the younger cadres.
That is the same story that Kibaale MP Frank Tumwebaze, who was widely expected to be nominated, will not want to recount.
A closer look at the list shows that while certain disparate issues guided its compilation, no particular script seems to have been followed.
While it rewards the ageing Kirunda Kivejinja for routing FDC rabble-rouser Abdu Katuntu from the House, it also returns the equally elderly Muganwa Kajura, a veteran Munyoro politician, who was defeated at the Bugahya NRM primaries by the energetic Kiiza Rwebembera.
Losing MP seats
That irony explains the fact that while losing a parliamentary seat did not help one's chances of retuning to cabinet, it was not, on its own, a harbinger of 'ministerial doom'.
It is apparent that issues of seniority, loyalty, and competence were some of the key criteria. " I think President Museveni had to give some posts to Bunyoro.
He was considering loyalty, regional balance and not ideological positions," said Makerere University political historian Mwambutsya Ndebesa.
" I don't see the cabinet being chosen deliberately for government effectives or ideological clarity or political effectiveness. These were not his considerations."
Energy crisis
With an energy crisis on the hands of the government, it had been somewhat expected that Lamwo MP Hillary Onek and his counterpart Simon D'ujanga would make it to Cabinet.
Although the portfolios are yet to be defined, it is now expected that Onek and D'ujanga, who have in the past demonstrated their competence on matters to do with energy, will most likely take on the Herculean task of sorting out the crisis.
"I am hoping we can have Hilary Onek to sort out the energy sector; a technical person to know what is being done and not work under mediocres. The NRM has used a window dressing strategy for 20 years now and we are now reaping where it has become full circle," said Salaamu Musumba, the FDC deputy president. "I am hoping we have people with real knowledge in key sectors."
Onek, as the only successful lawmaker from Acholi, had extra protection. The President has consistently had low popular support in Acholi and Lango: that there are only two Acholi (Onek and Okello Oryem) points to a direction that is not inspired by a need to compromise.
Oryem may have lost his seat in the House, but his personal connections are well known. Omara Atubo, a Langi, returns to Cabinet in circumstances that can only be explained within the context of Museveni's desire to run a government that has a semblance of all-inclusion and unity.
The inclusion to the list of Atubo, a former UPC stalwart, completes that picture in the same way Sulaiman Madada's name gives some delight to the Muslims.
In some cases, it is not possible to explain the interests certain nominees represent beyond the ostensible idea of diversity.
Names like Kasirivu Atwooki, Simon Ejua, David Wakikoona, Nelson Gagawala and Lukia Chekamondo do not make much sense to analysts, and were out of most theories before the list was released.
Persons from the west, again, make up the bulk (27 in total) of the list. It remains to be seen what the people of Rukungiri, who now have no one in Cabinet, will react to the list.
Muhwezi and FDC leader Kizza Besigye have been the area's most illustrious sons.
In a district where 'cake-sharing' topics are discussed with so much passion and where the natives have traditionally voted for Museveni with one heart, the absence of a Cabinet minister from there could cause a fallout.
Ms Paula Turyahikayo, who defeated Kagonyera to take the Rubabo Constituency seat, would have been a fair bargain had she not been unfavourably mentioned in the Global Fund mess. But Ms Winfred Matsiko, the Rukungiri Woman MP, is not tainted.
The inclusion of Mr Eriya Kategaya, who fell out with Museveni in 2003 over the proposal to amend the 1995 Constitution to remove presidential term limits, has been traced to some kind of soul-searching within State House.
The return of Kategaya, beyond painting a gloomy picture of his personal convictions, raises the questions: Has there been an unreported agreement between Kategaya and Museveni? Does Kategaya, who considered the third term project an abuse of democracy, know something about Museveni's ambitions that the rest of the citizenry don't know about?
Since it has been claimed that he will be in charge of a portfolio that relates to East African integration, does Kategaya find that East African unity is a vision that outshines the Museveni regime (and legacy)?
Some opposition politicians are saying they have no confidence in the competence of the incoming Cabinet.
Despondency
Such despondent thought is undoubtedly linked to the inclusion of Mr Salim Saleh, the President's sibling who has had a chequered past.
"As a diet, the NRM has given us more carbohydrates of the cassava type, which takes you to sleep. The NRM cabinet is more cassava than anythingâ-oe It looks more to me like another wasted opportunity," Salaamu said.
"You can never point to any background of success in the majority of these people even on a technical basis. What expertise is being brought in? Is it to occupy chairs?" Only time will tell.

Original_Search_Title: Field Monitors Inducted
Headline: Sierra Leone: Field Monitors Inducted
Body_Text:
Five field monitors have been inducted to work in the Kailahun/Kono Development Fund (KKDP) of the National Commission for Social Action (NaCSA) The monitors will provide support to project management committees in the implementation of the KKDP projects, through supervision and reporting on the progress and challenges, as well as providing extension services to farmers in the project areas in Kailahun and Kono Districts.
NaCSA's Regional Coordinator, East, Moses Gbouma said the KKDF seeks to "support community stabilization and the resettlement of refugees and displaced persons in Sierra Leone." This, he said, will redress the imbalance in economic and social development between the urban, peri-urban and rural areas, leading to reconciliation in the communities. He said the KKDF is one of the programmes within NaCSA designed for grassroots communities, targeting groups like farmers, craftsmen or individuals.
The coordinator of the KKDF programme at NaCSA, Remi Attinger spoke about progress in implementation, noting that the project encompasses four components including establishing basic services, developing production activities, developing and strengthening social ties and project management, involving evaluation and monitoring.
Mr. Attinger told the inductees that they should monitor and evaluate the projects by reorganizing productive sectors, identifying, training and supporting individuals or the collective projects and facilitating reintegration, following an anthropological and sociological field survey.
He also on dilated the project management cycle, which is the framework of KKDF interventions.
In another development the newly inducted monitoring team, along with the KKDF coordinator, made monitoring visits to agricultural project sites in the Kono District.
At the Number 9 area, Tankoro, in Koidu town, where a piggery is under construction, the project management committee (PMC) secretary, Mr. Sahr Lamin said the project including swine production and veterinary services is targeting thirty direct beneficiaries at Bangura, Saaque and Fullah towns.
In small Lebanon, Kensay-2, where the Tankoro women are involved in poultry development and goat raring, the team inspected chickens, goats, rice, groundnut, cassava and potato farms.
At Kongogbain section, Simbakoro village, where a twelve acre cassava farm is under cultivation with a store and a cassava processing machine, the chairlady, Mrs. Mariama Sofie disclosed that they work on the farm once a week .
The team also inspected the Njabwena community market under construction in Fiama chiefdom, as well as cassava cultivation farm in Tuyor.
At Yekior, the Dumbia Farms project Management Committee told the team that the goats and sheep have been distributed in ten villages.
KKDF coordinator, RemiAttinger advised that sheds should be built for the animals, instead of sleeping with them in the same environment.
The team also visited Chinchima, where KKDF is implementing a fish pond project through the Chinchima Women's Project Management Committee .
At the end the tour, the KKDF Coordinator expressed satisfaction with the work done and encouraged the field monitors to "concentrate on the Project Management Committees (PMC) that are very weak and give them support so that they can perform well.
He told the field monitors to be vigilant in their work so that the technical assistance given to the producers will help them improve best practices and marketing of their products.
The KKDF project within NaCSA is funded by the French Government and supports the peace process in Sierra Leone by facilitating reconciliation in rural communities by improving living standards and promoting economic activity in Kono and Kailahun districts. '

Original_Search_Title: US Condemns Niger Delta Crises
Headline: Nigeria: US Condemns Niger Delta Crises
Body_Text:
The United States of America has condem- ned the lingering crises at the Niger Delta region that resulted in kidnappings and terrorism by restive youths against oil companies in the area.
US ambassador to Nigeria Mr. John Campbell made the remark at a press conference during his visit to Kano, last Friday at the Murtala Muhammed library complex.
Ambassador Campbell expressed dismay over the frequent occurrence of crises at the Niger Delta region. He added that youths in the area have no justification to take laws into their own hands by kidnapping foreigners and other acts of terrorism leading to the breakdown of law and order.
"We are deeply concerned with the plight of the people of the Niger Delta. We aspire for a better life for them. Nonetheless, kidnapping, acts of terrorism and destruction of properties are never justified," said the US ambassador.
He called on the federal government to expedite action in regulating the activities of oil companies working in the on-shore and off-shore areas with a view to finding lasting solution to the crises.
Campbell assured of the US government continued support to the region through non-governmental organisations that will ensure the improve-ment of lives by providing them with basic necessities of life.
"We are interested in improving the quality of life of the people there through a multi-partnership programme underway to introduce a variety of cassava resistant to disease for cultivation in the area."
The US ambassador had earlier at the state government house told journalists that with the approach of elections in 2007, the US government will support the country to ensure a free and fair conduct of the election.
He however assured that in as much as the country's democracy is important in the eyes of the world, the US government will not interfere in Nigeria's election uninvited.
"What interests the US is human rights, democracy and rule of law and also a free and fair election in the country."
The ambassador later delivered a lecture on elections with theme "Election: the American Eperience, 217 Yars Snce the Frst American Eection and the Wrk for Uiversal Sffrage Cntinues," at the Murtala Mohammed library complex.

Original_Search_Title: Gas to Fertilizer: NNPC Plans 7 Plants
Headline: Nigeria: Gas to Fertilizer: NNPC Plans 7 Plants
Body_Text:
The Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) is to build seven fertilizer plants with a combined capacity of over 10 million metric tons per annum by the year 2015.
The Group Managing Director, NNPC Engineer Funsho Kupolokun said this Friday at a convocation lecture at the University of Agriculture, Makurdi.
He said the plants are expected to collectively meet current and future domestic fertilizer demand in the country with excess for export.
In his paper titled: "Energising agriculture through cross sector linkage with the oil and gas, Engr. Kupolokun said the projected demand for fertilizer in the next 10 years will reach about 2.23 million metric tons and 3.14 metric tons by 2915.
He said it becomes necessary to link the two different sectors for national development, stressing that " Nigeria is therefore in a position to maximize the benefits accruable form both the petroleum and agricultural sectors and transit into a fully energy independent and self sufficient economy with potentials for exports like Brazil."
He said among the linkages between the two sectors, "NNPC has progressed the ethanol project which involves processing sugarcane and cassava into ethanol fuel in order to exploit the multiplier effects of the oil and gas sector in agriculture.
According to the NNPC boss the programme has the potential to create over 200,000 jobs for the youth, emphasising that NNPC identified states like Jigawa and Benue for the production of sugarcane and cassava in large quantities.
Kupolokun said the corporation has obtained a grant of 70,000 Euros from renewable energy and energy efficiency partnership to support detailed feasibility study at target locations.
He said part of the study is to improve the potential of the yield and achieving the target saying "the analysis showed that the current national average cassava yield of 15 tons per hectare will be marginal while the current sugarcane yields for 60 tons per hectare are sufficient therefore improving cassava yields becomes an imperative"
He said the need to engage research institutes like the University of Agriculture Makurdi in the yield improvement is imperative.
"We are ready at any moment to negotiate with you on how to assist us in research on the cassava yield," said Kupolokun.
In his remark the Vice Chancellor of the university, Professor James O. Ayatse said a committee to look at the NNPC request on yield improvement has been set up and will soon submit its report.
He commended the NNPC for its foresight aimed at development of the agricultural sector in the country and assured of the support of the University for the Success of the initiative.

Original_Search_Title: Country to Save N21bn Annually From Bio-Fuel Initiative - Kupolokun
Headline: Nigeria: Country to Save N21bn Annually From Bio-Fuel Initiative - Kupolokun
Body_Text:
Abuja — Group Managing Director of the Nigerian Petroleum Corporation (NNPC), Engr. Funsho Kupolokun has said Nigeria would be $150 million (about N21bn) annually richer when she adopts the development and application of bio-fuel, as an alternative energy source to crude oil.
Speaking to newsmen shortly after delivering a convocation lecture at the University of Agriculture, Makurdi, Benue State, over the weekend, Kupolokun said the proposed bio-fuel project which is part of the country's alternative energy development strategy is geared towards utilizing our agricultural potentials in cassava and sugar cane to produce ethanol fuel that can serve as a good alternative to petrol currently in use.
Under the initiative, two types of automotive fuel are to be developed, namely, Ethanol fuel and palm oil diesel.
Ethanol is an energy derivative processed from cassava or sugar cane while palm oil diesel is produced by chemical process which removes glycerin and can be used in any concentration with petroluem based diesel fuel with litle or no modification
Kopolokun said as part of the implementation of the project, special research initiatives would be sponsored by NNPC to boost cassava and Palm Oil output within the country and that the venture would lead to the setting up of several ethanol production plants at an average cost of $60m each.
The GMD who also met with the Benue State Governor George Akume to discussed the modalities for the partnership, said a memorandum of understanding to secure land for large scale sugar cane and cassava production are being finalised with governments of Benue and Jigawa states.
He said the corporation has identified locations in several states of the federation for cassava and sugar cane plantation, adding as soon as agreements are reached, MOU would be signed on the projects.
While welcoming the synergy, Akume said the project crystalize development efforts and provide employment opportunities to the people of the state, adding that such a programme is capable of steming the scourge of petroleum product pipeline vandalization in the area.
Kupolokun said the ethanol programme is expected "to improve automotive exhaust emmissions in the country, reduce domestic use of petrol, free up more crude for export and position Nigeria for development of green field".
NNPC, he said, has gotten a grant of 70,000 Euros from Renewable Energy, Energy Efficiency Partnership (REEEP) from Germany to support detailed feasibility study at the target locations.
The study would include research analysis on how to achieve improved target yield performance for cassava whose current national average of 15 tons per hectre is considered marginal to feed the ethanol plant.
He said the implementation of the renewable energy project is one of the major focus of the organisation this year, adding that work has commenced on the modification of its import reception facilities located at Port Harcourt and Mosimi areas in readiness to distribute the bio-fuel product.
The NNPC boss whose lecture dwelt on various aspects of the planned intervention in the agricultural sector, said cooperative agreements are being considered with a number of institutions among which are the University of Agriculture, Makurdi, International Institute for Tropical Agriculture, Ibadan and the Nigerian Cereal Research Institute (NCRI) to execute the research component of the project.
"We intend to seek funding and expertise for the project, form partnership with leading technical institutes and farm operators to produce high yield cassava and ensure experise to manage large scale plantation," he said.
Commenting on the impact of the venture, Kupolokun said the entire process is capable of creating over 200,000 jobs, empower rural farmers with more earnings and provide the basis for the much needed agrarian revolution in the country.

Original_Search_Title: NEXIM Restates Commitment to Uplift Export Sector
Headline: Nigeria: NEXIM Restates Commitment to Uplift Export Sector
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Nigeria Export-Import (NEXIM) Bank has restated its commitment to the upliftment of the export sector of the nation's economy.
A statement by its Head of Corporate Affairs, H. H. Maina at the weekend said the Bank is ready to support any serious Private Sector initiative associated with the export business.
Maina who was reacting to a news report in which certain individuals were alleged to have claimed that 'NEXIM made little or no impact on export financing' and also that 'No company has benefited from NEXIM's facilities since its establishment', said "it is on record that the bank has made significant contributions in the areas of agriculture, solid minerals, oil & gas and the services export".
Maina added that NEXIM has over the years supported transactions that promote Tourism and ECOWAS Trade Liberation and Integration Policy such as the ABC Transport Company.
"In support of the Federal Government's Cassava Initiative, the Bank is in the forefront of supporting strategic projects such as Ekha Agro Farms Ltd", he pointed out.
He stated that the bank had also made its mark in the Export Processing Zones by supporting among others projects such as Bao Yao Iron & Industries and the Stone Craft Marble & Granite Ltd. Other successful projects supported by NEXIM include those in the Maritime Industry such as Caverton Marine and Agro-Allied projects such as Multi-Trex Company and many others.

Original_Search_Title: Days When Famines Were History
Headline: Kenya: Days When Famines Were History
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The current famine has brought nostalgic memories about growing up in the village. I am reminded of my numerous visits to my grandmother during school holidays, which always ended with me coming back home with a chicken whose fate would be decided in my absence.
However, the image more relevant now is that of my uncle spending the night beside the granary with a spear every time he was home on leave.
I could never understand what he was guarding because the granary had only millet. I did not believe anyone would steal millet because it did not taste particularly nicely. I later learnt that my uncle was there to scare away night runners who often interrupted his sleep.
Afflicted with blight
But not to digress, you see, in those days everybody had a granary. Woe unto you if you were small because you would be the one to go in there and bring out the millet. I often wondered why the millet was not all prepared and kept. That would have been easier.
But in those days, our grannies did not do things for convenience. There was always a good reason for doing anything. Now we have whole generations who have never seen a granary and would not understand its purpose if they saw one.
In those days, there were always cassava and potatoes. Now I am told that cassava does not thrive in places where it did before because of disease and that potatoes have been afflicted with blight.
It makes one wonder what the role of all these research institutes really is. And, by the way, why are we paying all these professionals if they cannot translate this research into reality?
In those days, if one had a poor harvest, one would exchange animals for grain, which would be stored for use during an emergency.
Now we wait until all the cows have died so that the carcasses can be displayed on international media for all to see just how badly off we are.
You see, in those days, hunger and famine were very rare events. In fact, so rare that later on, when identity cards became mandatory, famines would be used as a marker for those who could not remember their birthdays.
The famines even had names, at least the ones experienced in my native province.
But now, famine and hunger are such common words that even people who have never experienced hunger will tell you how they are about to die from it.
When you visit your rural village, you are likely to hear how hunger is killing all those healthy relatives who will implore you to leave them something small.
And, in those days, there was no Famine Early Warning System. Nowadays, not only can we tell that there will be a famine, we can even estimate the amount of food required and the number of people likely to be affected. In our case, we knew that there would be a food shortage.
Even now the Meteorological Department has already told us how much rain we are likely to get and the distribution. All this is meant to improve Government preparedness and prevent the kind of shameful deaths we have seen recently.
Hybrid seeds won't germinate
In those days, hybrid seeds were very rare. After every harvest, my granny would choose a few maize cobs, which would be hung over the fireplace until they turned black from the soot. These would be the seeds for the next planting season.
Now we have all these improved hybrid seeds that come in all colours and strange numbers and codes. Yet they often fail to germinate. In our wisdom, we even sabotage our own survival by making fake seeds. And now we think that our ancestors were backward, while, year in year out, we starve to death.
And in those days, famine meant that there was absolutely no food in the entire land. But now our famines are punctuated by mountains of plenty.
Contrast the huge mountains of maize in parts of Rift Valley and Western Province or the long queues of lorries waiting to deliver maize to the cereals board, with pictures of emaciated and dying Kenyans.
In many countries, a famine means just that - a severe and crippling food shortage. In Kenya, it means that the food is sitting in places where it is not needed.
In those days, donors were not as generous and the Cabinet was much smaller. In fact, you could name all the ministers. You were even examined on this during national exams.
Now even as the minister for food aid whose name you cannot remember calls a meeting to beg for relief food, sitting in a plush office swivelling around in an expensive chair.
When the press conference ends, he has to draw lots to decide which fuel-guzzler to use to the nearest airstrip to fly over famine-stricken districts in a helicopter.
But this was long ago, before democracy and rights became important components of development. In those days, one ate at regular intervals and when you visited your relatives, they did not hide food under the bed.
Endless discussions on food
That was also before feeding the nation became a Millennium Development Goal around which big conferences were organised and endless discussions held.
Now we are told that food is a basic right to which everyone is entitled. And governments are expected to ratify UN and international agreements and conventions that outline this.
Then they add riders, which state their commitment to protecting the rights of their citizens while at the same time absolving them from any obligation. If they are Kenyans, they will spend their time doing better things - stealing from their fellow citizens.
It is ironical that now when we spend more money on research and are more technologically advanced, we are worse off than before. Things have really changed.
Those days, when one fell ill, one went to the local health centre and got treated. The Government was aggressive in health education and promotion and disease prevention was a very important component of the Ministry of Health.
Communities were sensitised on the need for immunisation and vitamin supplements were available. Disease outbreaks were swiftly dealt with and the chief's meeting was an important forum for community education.
But that was before health became a fundamental right to which every citizen was entitled.
That was also the time when everyone who graduated from primary school could spell and write a letter to their wives and husbands if they lived apart. Now all this is seriously in doubt.
Mrs Onyando works at the Centre for the Study of Adolescence. The regular Friday columnist, Lucy Oriang', resumes next week

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Growers Association Partners With NNPC On Production of Ethanol
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava Growers Association Partners With NNPC On Production of Ethanol
Body_Text:
Osogbo — CASSAVA Growers Association of Nigeria, has entered into collaboration with the Nigeria National Petroleum Corporation [NNPC], towards the production of 1 billion litres of Ethanol annually from cassava tubers for processing of crude oil.
Already, the NNPC said it needed about 3million litres of Ethanol per day, which would constitute 10percent of the petroleum products to be consumed locally.
Addressing newsmen in Osogbo yesterday, the National President of the Association, Chief Moses Ayinmodu stated that the association is currently discussing with some foreign firms for the manufacture of mini-machines for the production of ethanol in the country.
According to him, an American company, had already established an ethanol plant in Akwa-Ibom State adding that others are still coming as soon as all the necessary discussions on the supply of the machines are completed with the association.
He stated that with the successful execution of the project, the production of ethanol in commercial quantity would not only save the country from huge foreign exchange it would also earn the country a remarkable foreign exchange.
While commending President Olusegun Obasanjo for setting up a Presidential Initiative on cassava production, processing and export a few years ago, Chief Ayinmodu stated that the directives that all flour mills in the country from June this year should include 10 percent cassava flour in their products was yielding favourable results.
"Presently, the association is targeting an increase in cassava production by its members by about 10 million tons within the next two years primarily for industrial use and export so that the present production level of about 42 million tons can be released mainly for food to maintain food security," Chief Ayinmodu stressed.
Earlier, the Osun State Chairman of the Association, Dr. David Ogunsade commended Governor Olagunsoye Oyinlola for the release of loans to cassava growers in the state.
His words, "Prince Olagunsoye Oyinlola has given support and encouragement to our association in form of soft loans made available to us in addition to the supply of tractors to members of the association at cheaper rates."

Original_Search_Title: Ethanol Fuel Underway for Industries, Says NNPC
Headline: Nigeria: Ethanol Fuel Underway for Industries, Says NNPC
Body_Text:
Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) has said that industries and vehicles in the country will soon be running on ethanol fuel.
Ethanol-powered vehicles are used in countries that produce crops suitable for ethanol production.
In Brazil, more than 4 million vehicles now run on ethanol produced from sugar cane, while in the US, there are currently about 3 million vehicles on the road that run on ethanol.
Group Managing Director of NNPC, Engr. Funsho Kupolokun, who was the guest speaker at the First Quarterly Business Luncheon of the Lagos Chamber of Commerce and Industry (LCCI) disclosed over the weekend in Lagos, that the ethanol plant will reduce domestic use of petrol, freeing up more crude for export and position the country for development of green fuels.
According to him, the Corporation is about to establish Ethanol plant in the country. The plant, he said will have multiplier effect on the economy especially the industrial sector and drive economic growth.
He said the plant will benefit the economy in a number of ways, namely, development of large scale cassava plantation schemes, rural development and increased direct and indirect job creation, increased household income annually, multiplier effect on other sectors of the economy, and enhanced industrialisation prospect.
He pointed out that Lagos Area needs to develop an action plan for root and tuber crops that may be of relevance for the production of fuel ethanol. He said that State Governments should put in place incentives to encourage their citizens to participate in ethanol production.
He further called for investment in building relevant skills that will be required by projects and other businesses such as welders, fitters, etc.
Kupolokun urged the state governments to invest in skill upgrade for indigenes through scholarship and training programmes. "Government needs to work with the communities to create a conducive business environment that offers trade opportunities and supports efficient and profitable business operations, development awareness programme and education to prepare youths and local businesses for participation ahead of upcoming opportunities, invest in entrepreneurship development programme for current and prospective SMEs, and members of the LCCI must participate in the Nigerian content consultative forum activities to key into some of these opportunities,"he remarked.
Meanwhile, LCCI President, Dr. Herbert Ademola Ajayi noted that the greatest challenge facing the economy today is the poor linkages between the Oil and Gas sector. He pointed out that government has made an average investment of $10 billion annually and more than 80 per cent of the job value are done outside of the shores of the country, which he said has negatively affected employment situation in the country.
"Oil sector has contributed less than 15 per cent of the GDP and employs less than 1 per cent of the indigenous labour force and this does not signify a good activeness of the labour force. "For years now the Oil Sector has generated over 90 per cent of the nation's Foreign Exchange, earning over 70 per cent of total revenue. For an average Nigerian, this major defect explains why the impact of the Oil sector has not been noted.
The Oil revenue accrues to the nation in Dollars and monetised for distribution through the Federal Account and this monetisation has considerable impact in increasing money supply which erodes purchasing power and inevitably worsens the poverty conditions and fuels inflation for the majority of the citizens," he stated

Original_Search_Title: Why Biotechnology is Goldmine -- Prof. Bello
Headline: Nigeria: Why Biotechnology is Goldmine -- Prof. Bello
Body_Text:
BIOTECHNOLOGY has been identified as one of the frontline technologies today being developed and used to understand and manipulate biological molecules for applications in medical, agricultural, industrial and environmental sectors of the national economy.
"Biotechnology is a goldmine in the developed economies as well as developing economies that are upright like India. Investment in Biotechnology applications has to be induced and encouraged by government through its policies. This is not too difficult to achieve over a number of years," declared Professor R.A. Bello, of Chemical Engineering Department, Faculty of Engineering, University of Lagos.
At an inaugural lecture delivered last Wednesday, May 3, 2006, he said that the brewing technology was forced to look inwards, upon the ban on importation of barley malt and now malt local grains.
"We are now much better off for it. In the pharmaceutical sector, gains are now being made in local production of drugs, based on positive policies."
He contended that, to start with, Nigeria, as a matter of urgency, should not be importing yeast, with all the agricultural raw materials available for its production as well as the know-how. Similarly, the most frequently used enzymes, like the amylases are ripe for production within the country. Also local production of fermentation -- based antibiotics can be readily carried out here.
"Decisions similar to those taken on malt barley will see the necessary investments into these areas, which would witness the addition of a very virile Biotechnology sector to our Stock Exchange as it is in all the vibrant economies".
He cautioned that the producer nations will continue to encourage our status as the dumping market of their own products, if the right policies are not immediately put in place.
Bello explained that Biotechnology is a technology or group of technologies that is fast changing all facets of our life, from the food we eat to the way we live.
He defined Biotechnology as the use of biological processes to solve problems or make useful products. Biological processes have been used to solve problems for human beings for ages.
"Crops have been grown and animals raised to meet the food and clothing needs of human beings. Biological processes involving micro-organisms have also been used for long to make useful products like 'gari' bread, cheese, antibiotics and other biochemicals for medical uses".
Among the breakthroughs in Biotechnology he mentioned were the creation of the sheep "Doly", the genetic manipulation of seeds and plants for the production of pest-resistant strains for production of bumper harvests in Agriculture, production of "superbugs" in Industrial Microbiology as well as for the production of antibiotics for medical uses.
The latest of the advances in Biotechnology enables the probing and studying of the bioproteins to the nano (10.9) level.
According to the professor of chemical engineering, this is now enabling a better understanding of the causes and treatments of erstwhile recalcitrant disorders in the human system.
"Solutions are being seen to be feasible for disorders like cirrhosis, epilepsy and parkingson's disease through cell transplants and for head and spinal cord injuries or degenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease up to cancer and other terminal diseases. In fact, the ageing process is being explored. The ultimate is in the re-creation of human beings, which is being resisted on ethical grounds", he submitted.
Biotechnology was looked at through its applications and broadly categorised into the following areas:
Agricultural -- gene manipulation and tissue culture development and propagation leading to improved, seedlings, in order to obtain better yields per unit crop or animal to meet the food requirement of the people
Medical -- gene manipulation leading to cloning of various organs/humans, 'super' micro-organisms development for the production of better or more antibiotics, development of vaccines, etc
Industrial -- Better ways of producing various biochemicals (e.g. alcohol, vitamins, etc) or organism (yeast, etc) with the aid of genetic manipulation and/or media formulation are exploited.
Environmental Biotechnology involves keeping the environment safe for human use via degradation of hazardous materials biologically. Bioremediation of oil-polluted environment is a typical example.
On Biotechnology practice in Nigeria, Prof. Bello said the country has not been completely left behind in the use of Biotechnology products and the art of Biotechnology development. He cited agricultural research institutions like the International Institute for Tropical Agriculture (IITA) Ibadan, Institute for Agriculture and Agricultural Research and Development (IAARD), National Cereals Research Institute (NCRI), National Roots Crops Research Institute (NRCRI), who have, for decades, been developing and putting into use pest-resistant and better yielding strains of their plants. Maize farmers now buy seedlings rather than grow from old stocks.
Various drugs and industrial products of Biotechnology are in the Nigerian market. There are other primary Biotechnology practices that are basic to Nigeria, such as the production of condiments (e.g. "Ogiri" from melon), production of alcoholic beverages (e.g. "Burukutu" from millet).
Among the contributions Prof. Bello made to the academic evolution and development of Biotechnology and practice in Nigeria, also the fundamental researches carried out and their impact in this area of knowledge are as follows:
* Bioreactor Design -- involving the characterisation and evolution of the Airlift Bioreactors as well as in the use and characterisation of the immobilised enzyme/whole cell bioreactors
* Synthetic palm wine production -- The process use of an immobilised yeast packed bed bioreactor to simulate natural palm wine production. A taste panel certified the taste and odour of the synthetic palm wine to be similar to those of natural palm wine.
* Starch Hydrolysis -- For better and ease of design, deeper understanding of the basic processes of the breakdown of the starch components to the simpler sugars and the distinction between different types of starch.
He pointed to the implication and application of this work which is, "its direct use in the current drive to produce ethanol in large quantities in the country through the hydrolysis of starch from cassava".
Continuing, he said: "As we all know, massive and continuous cassava production is the current emphasis of the Federal Government, and internal industrial utilisation of the cassava will be more beneficial for the economy. A major use will be in the production of ethanol."
The starch hydrolysis project was based on the fact that most of the country's agricultural products are starch-based. According to him, "the transformation of starch into the various raw materials, which could be feeds to the chemical, pharmaceutical, and food processing industries, are yet to be embarked upon, as the various sources of starch, from grains to the roots offer enough base for a virile industry".
He hinted that the production of Bakers yeast and bacterial enzymes are still money spinning ventures, which are still relevant to the Nigerian economy, particularly with the drive for diversification of the economy. Reference was made to the ethanol production drive from our agricultural materials being currently undertaken by the NNPC, which is needed as a blend for gasoline to enable stability of fuel price.
In the lecture titled -- Biotechnology: An Alternative Goldmine, Prof. Bello commended Federal Government for realising the potentials of Biotechnology for national development and for setting up the National Biotechnology Development Agency (NABDA). He called on government, as a matter of urgency to establish a Biotechnology Research Fund for the direct funding of research in Biotechnology, should be coordinated by NABDA and disbursed for researches in line with national priorities.
Suggested also is the establishment of a Database of all activities in Biotechnology (research, production, marketing etc) to enable appropriate planning, co-ordination and non-duplication of efforts nationally.
On Biotechnology Education, he submitted that programmes in Biotechnology are ripe for establishment to enhance its development in Nigeria.
"However, because of the multi-disciplinary nature of Biotechnology and the infant level of the practice of Biotechnology in the country, university programmes should currently be at the postgraduate level."
But he wants first degree programmes in Biotechnology currently be discouraged in Nigeria for now because the graduates of such programmes would not have full grounding in any of the basic sciences and may run into problems with job prospects, judging from the current level of Biotechnology practice in the country.
Prof. Bello suggested that curriculum experts should go to work or the curricula of such programmes for their relevance to the students' aspirations and future prospects.

Original_Search_Title: Electric Cable Thefts 'Murdering' Agro-Processing Industry
Headline: Uganda: Electric Cable Thefts 'Murdering' Agro-Processing Industry
Body_Text:
Kampala — AS if the current load- shedding is not bad enough for manufacturers and processors, the increasing theft of conduct or electric cables is threatening them out of business.
Massive theft of power cables has been reported mostly in rural areas, around Gayaza, Bombo, Luwero and Nakasongola in central Uganda and in Pallisa, Kaliro and Kamuli in eastern Uganda.
Among the factories seriously affected is Ugachick Poultry Breeders located at Magigye, on the Gayaza-Zirobwe Road.
Between January and May 2006, 12,370 meters of electric cables conductors were stolen around Gayaza, Bombo and Luwero areas. Sixteen electric poles were also destroyed. As a result, the factory spends days without hydro electric power.
There are also reports that thousands of electric wires have been stolen in Pallisa, Kamuli and Kaliro districts, rendering agro-processors jobless. At the moment, rice, maize and cassava processors in the three districts are not operating.
In Nakasongola, hundreds of milk producers, whose coolers depend on power to operate are in a dilemma.
"Whenever thieves steal the wires, we make losses. We have to sell our milk at low prices to stop it from getting spoilt," says Edward Rwamukaaga, a farmer.
It is said the thieves use the wires to fabricate other things. Most of those arrested are from Kisenyi or Katwe in Kampala," a resident of Bombo says.
UMEME's Frank Ssebowa recently said the theft of cables was a chronic problem in the power sector. He, however, says it needs collective effort to stop it. He said stolen cables are replaced the moment the problem is reported.
Agro-processing has also been seriously affected by the irregular load-shedding schedules. In many upcountry areas, agro-processors can even take four days without power.
"Compared to urban industries, our load-shedding schedule is never followed. We are not sure when we shall have power and when we shall not have it. This affects our production planning," says Agha Ssekalala, the Manager of Ugachick.
To cope with power problems, Ugachick acquired two giant generators of 500KVA. Initially, the generators were for supplementing load-shedding, but because of the rampant theft of wires, the factory operates them almost everyday. The generators consumed 10,350 litres of fuel for five months before the theft of wires and irregular load-shedding started. Today, they consume 21,673 litres.
Given the nature of the business at Ugachick or any other hatchery, there should be power at the factory at any one time of the day, or else the factory will collapse. "At Ugachick operations are maintained at constant operation, 24 hours a day and these are the major power consuming areas.
These include the hatchery, which is also a life supporting facility and the chicken processing plant that provides both processing and storage facilities," explains the Operations Manager David Hans Nyanzi. This is the situation at most agro-processing factories in the country.
Although the milk coolers in Nakasongola and other rural districts can do with light generators which cost about sh20m, many claim they do not have the money to acquire them.

Original_Search_Title: EFLA Discusses Major Issues
Headline: Liberia: EFLA Discusses Major Issues
Body_Text:
The European Federation of Liberian Associations (EFLA) has been discussing a number of national issues bordering on education, governance, and the organization says it would constructively engage the Ellen Johnson-Sirleaf led government for the creation of favorable condition of the Liberian people.
At one of its conferences held recently the EFLA also discussed the resettlement of Liberians in Europe but with the caution that the association should not consider engaging in such venture, but that it should give asylum seekers the opportunity to pursue some sort of education which will enhance their career.
While considering the issue of security for resettlement and asylum seekers, the Association says it would setup a data base system for all asylum seekers in Europe, and that to do so it would write a letter informing the immigration of European countries to halt the repatriation of Liberians, and rather consider integrating them.
The EFLA's Education Committee identified three project areas, embodying Liberia's educational structure, teachers training in the form of train-to-train, and vocational training program.
"Revision of Government Policies on Education," EFLA says is also priority, adding that it would "look at possibilities, motivational factors and stumbling blocks in the area of education and training." The Liberian European Association's Agriculture Committee suggested that projects in the production of crops like eddoes, cassava, etc. apart from the production of the nation's staple food, rice. The group is suggesting the minimization of the Ministry of Agriculture and the Government of Liberia's influence to avoid mismanagement of project funds and/or resources.
"Encourage the migration of farmers from urban (Monrovia) to rural areas," EFLA noted, and its Health Committee looked at the geographical focus and to broaden the project to curative and preventive health. The EFLA project should take three years.
Speaking during the conference, EFLA President Elvis Morris urged the delegates of Liberians living in various European countries to remain united, with which he believes they can achieve their goals.
According to Mr. Morris, "the aim of ELFA is to foster and enhance closer contact, fellowship, culture and social development between Liberians and Europeans among other nationalities with interest in Liberia and European Affairs; foster and enhance closer contact fellowship culture and social development between Liberians residing in Europe, and to seek the welfare of each and every Liberian who may request their assistance, and deepen Liberian/Europeans relationship and maintain concrete and constructive tides with Liberians at home and equally contribute to the development of Liberia through whatever action and programs that may be approved by members including charitable and educational activities."

Original_Search_Title: Experts Hail NDDC Cassava Initiative
Headline: Nigeria: Experts Hail NDDC Cassava Initiative
Body_Text:
Lagos — The NDDC Massive Cassava Production Programme has been described as a money-spinning venture capable of changing Nigeria's dependence on crude oil.
Head of Rivers State Co-operatives Development project and lecturer Faculty of Agriculture, at the Rivers State University of Science and Technology, Dr. Joseph Jaja stated this during a tour of the NDDC Massive Cassava production programme at Elele, Rivers State.
He said cassava products and bye-products such as garri, starch, chips, and pellet are in high demand both locally and internationally and were bound to change the monolithic nature of the country's economy. Dr. Jaja who was very impressed by the progress at the Elele farm implored state governments to emulate the good example of NDDC. Describing the NDDC Rivers State farm as one of the best, the university don commended NDDC for involving experts at every stage of the project.
In his remark, the commissioner representing Rivers State on the board of NDDC, Hon. Dimabo Oruama disclosed that high yielding and disease resistant cassava seedlings are used for cultivation, adding that harvest could begin sooner than expected. Oruama said a borehole has been sunk in the farm while a processing plant is being awaited as part of efforts to make the area a real farm settlement. He said there are also plans to establish similar farms in other parts of the state such as Etche.
The NDDC consultant on the project, Mr. Kiye Kings, put the size of the farm at 1,000 hectares. Mr. Kings disclosed that 35 hectares have so far been completely cultivated while another 50 hectares is ready for cultivation. The NDDC Massive Cassava Production Programme aspires to set up 500 hectares farms in each of the 85 Senatorial District of the nine Niger Delta states. It is among other things aimed at youth empowerment and turning agriculture into a profitable venture.
Under the programme each participant is allocated one hectare of cleared and developed land for the cultivation of cassava.
Bush clearing and the first land preparation is done free of charge by NDDC while land management and required farm inputs are given on cost recovery basis. Processing centers are to be established in each of the Senatorial Districts to process cassava tubers into high quality products such as high grade cassava flour, chips, pellets, starch, ethanol, feeds, etc.

Original_Search_Title: GM Cassava Has 'Super Size' Roots
Headline: Africa: GM Cassava Has 'Super Size' Roots
Body_Text:
Scientists who have genetically modified cassava plants to produce dramatically bigger roots say their research could help alleviate hunger in developing countries.
The starchy roots of cassava are a staple food for about 600 million people in parts of Africa, Asia and Latin America.
Lead researcher Richard Sayre of Ohio State University in the United States told SciDev.Net that he hopes there will be interest in releasing the plants to Africa free of charge.
The results were published online in Plant Biotechnology Journal on 5 May.
Sayre's team inserted a bacterial gene that affects starch production into the cassava's DNA. The modified plants' roots were more numerous and up to 2.6 times bigger than in normal plants.
