The modified plants also had more leaves, which are eaten in Africa as a source of protein, minerals and vitamins.

Original_Search_Title: Grain Harvest Predictions Indicate 10 Percent Increase Over Last Year
Headline: Mozambique: Grain Harvest Set to Exceed Two Million Tonnes
Body_Text:
Namaacha — This year's grain harvest in Mozambique will reach about 2.1 million tonnes, an increase of 10 per cent on the 2005 figure, predicted Agriculture Minister Tomas Mandlate on Monday.
Opening a National Meeting of Agricultural Services held in Namaacha, on the border with Swaziland, Mandlate said that the maize harvest alone will be about 1.5 million tonnes, a growth of 11 per cent. This means that Mozambique this year is theoretically self- sufficient in maize, since total domestic consumption is estimated at 1.4 million tonnes.
However, most of the maize production is in the fertile northern provinces, and transporting grain surpluses from the north to food deficit areas in the south is an expensive proposition.
Last year's grain production was much lower than hoped because of the impact of drought over large areas of southern and central Mozambique. The return of normal rains in the 2005-06 growing season is also reflected in the production of vegetables and cassava, up by 9.6 and 13.8 per cent respectively.
Turning to livestock, Mandlate said that restocking over the last decade was now showing results in terms of increased meat production. Total meat production in 2005 was 12 per cent higher than in 2004.
This has allowed Mozambique to cut its imports of beef, which were 22 per cent lower in 2005 than in the previous year.
Mandlate recognised that, despite this progress there are still severe obstacles hindering growth in agricultural production and productivity. These include vulnerability to pests and plant diseases, low use of modern inputs such as improved seeds, and insufficient use of irrigation and of animal traction.
The four day meeting is intended to draw up a balance sheet of activities undertaken in 2005 and plan work for 2007. Areas covered include food security, agricultural inputs, plant health, irrigation, and early warning in the event of natural disasters.
The meeting is being held under the slogan "For a more efficient agricultural service contributing to an increase in production and productivity on the road to alleviating absolute poverty".

Original_Search_Title: Keeping It Ugandan... in London
Headline: Uganda: Keeping It Ugandan... in London
Body_Text:
It is that kind of lovely Saturday afternoon that makes you wish you were anywhere near the shores of Nalubale soaking up the tropical sun, waiting on a juicy fried tilapiaâ-oen'ebigenderako.
The former is a wish that is at least an eight-hour flight away from this deceitfully early summer South East London sun but the former is a matter of choice; all you need is a visit to Owino or Wandegeya. Then you would have to decide whether you want your tilapia fresh or smoked, and that is without even going as far as Heathrow Airport.
The variety of things on sale is wide enough to include clay, that pottery raw material that is a delicacy for people who are eating for two (pregnant women). The other dilemma may be whether you still really want just fried fish or stewed with matooke, lumonde, muwogo or posho and, possibly mixed with groundnuts.
Owino Supermarket
Inside what could easily pass for a traditional English Off-licence (a small supermarket of sorts that sells alcohol, cigarettes, food and newspapers) with a bold sign of Owino Supermarket, Ugandans in London can lay their hands on any merchandise.
Apart from the famous name in bold green letters , the goods outside are another give away with boxes of matooke, sweet potatoes cassava and avocado staring at you along Mitcham Road. On entrance, the story is told in immense detail. The list runs from fast-selling snacks like pancakes -that bring in throngs on Mondays and Tuesdays when fresh ones come in - mandazi and chapatti to different species of smoked fish and Maganjo maize flour.
This business venture is on such rise that people no longer just go to Owino. Across town is another one with a name way bigger than its size - Wandegeya Mini Market. Set on the residential Conway Street at the back street of Plumbstead, it is probably safer to carry your reading glasses with you otherwise you are bound to miss the miniature sign.
The stock may be just as famous as that of Owino but the diversity is hardly as high. Probably one reason why customers only come in spurts; leaving the young part-time attendant staring a stretch of parked cars between posters of the Easter shows with Julianna Kanyomozi, Bobby Wine, Ragga Dee and General Mega Dee and Nvannungi.
Playing safe would be doing your shopping as close to Thursday as possible when fresh stock comes in from Kampala. Most captivating is a fridge full of East African lagers: Bell, Nile Special, Tuskerâ-oeand whatever comes out of the fridge goes for Â£2 a piece. Never mind that some people do get knocked out on that much (roughly Shs6,500), but this is drinking more for the memory than anything else.
Ugandan CDs
Chances are that every third black person in Forest Gate, East London, is either Ugandan, speaks, understands or can, at least, detect Luganda which explains the size of Pascheal Exotics; the other home to 'ba Mugalu' in Kampala. A name change to something like St Balikuddembe, considering the bustle at Pascheal's would be justifiable.
The corridor-like establishment makes it seem to go on for ever with lines of boxes of matooke, corn, pineapples, cassava. At the back of the stretch lies the source of that booming 'local' music; a stall that vends CDs, DVDs and cassettes of different Ugandan and other African artistes and the money transfer service in another corner.
It seems to get so busy that the person in charge of filling up the boxes is constantly on his feet and Pascheal himself is ever punching the keys at the till cashing in on the home-grown merchandise.
These tiny establishments live up to their names in their diversity of Ugandan merchandise. Who would ever imagine being able to buy those chewable coffee beans and mats in the heart of London! Even the non-Ugandan commodities are as close to our very own as you can get; tinned fish and beans, orange juiceâ-oe There may have been times when leaving 'those' shores was a western-lifestyle sentence of burgers, pasta and - depending on which strand the moonsoons dropped you - ghastly sea-food.
Same lifestyle
It is not necessarily the end of that era but there is unbelievable diversification; coming to London and turning all western is only a matter of choice that hinges on the kind of lifestyle that a Ugandan in London chooses to lead or whether one has the time and skill to prepare those delicacies.
As it turns out, those Ugandans in the British capital with the means live a more accomplished Ugandan lifestyle than many folks in Kampala with probably just their loved ones to miss about home.

Original_Search_Title: Bugesera Goes Green As Rains Set in
Headline: Rwanda: Bugesera Goes Green As Rains Set in
Body_Text:
Kigali — Residents in famine-ravaged Bugesera have started cultivating gardens and the originally bare landscape has slowly turned green due to the rainy season. Reports say the area that went through six months of prolonged drought no longer witnesses residents fleeing in search of food and water.
One farmer, Protais Gahinda Ibare, said that the area has at last received moderate rains, enabling residents to cultivate their gardens.
"We are at last regaining hope to start cultivating our gardens for food crops to alleviate the famine. For six months, the situation has been bad for us with some people fleeing in search of food and water.
He however warned that not all residents are relieved yet. "The famine destroyed all our food crops like cassava, sweet potatoes and bananas and some residents still depend on relief food and Irish potatoes from Muhanga District, Southern Province.
Gahinda explained that many farmers have opted to grow sorghum because it grows faster than other crops. "The crop is also marketable for poor farmers to easily get some income, compared to other cash crops grown in the area."
The same view was raised by another farmer, Jean Murikatete of Maranyondo Cell, who said, "Hunger still haunts residents of Nyamabuye District, former Ngenda Sector.
"Some residents even removed iron sheets from their houses and sold them in order to get money for food. Others used the money to flee to Tanzania and other parts of the country like Umutara".

Original_Search_Title: Cunene - Province Hosts World Food Day Central Act
Headline: Angola: Cunene - Province Hosts World Food Day Central Act
Body_Text:
Ondjiva — The southern Cunene Province will host the central act in the celebrations of the World Rural Women Day, World Food Day and the Tele-Food campaign, to be marked on 15 October, as stated on the programme received by ANGOP on Wednesday.
nAccording to the note from the organisation of the event, seminars will be held on "The introduction of cassava in the staple dietst o the populations in the southern region of the country " and "Guidelines and elaboration of Tele-Food micro-projects", in Ondjiva city.
In the ambit of the festivities of the World Food Day will be also inaugurated some social undertakings, as well as will take several socio-cultural activities.

Original_Search_Title: Famine Looms in Nyanza
Headline: Rwanda: Famine Looms in Nyanza
Body_Text:
Kigali — Residents of Nyanza District are threatened by a possible outbreak of famine due to a prolonged drought experienced since early January.
Many of residents, this reporter talked to, expressed concerns that the food stock is already running out which is likely to cause a serious food shortage.
The district is already experiencing pockets of food insecurity in some areas that have not had rains, according to reports.
"We are in constant fear of a possible serious famine if the situation doesn't change immediately. We call up the authorities to take the necessary precautions to avert the loaming crisis," residents appealed.
One renowned farmer, Clever Higiro, well known for growing cassava and maize, said that his production rate has greatly reduced due to insufficient and unpredictable rainfall since the beginning of the year.
From producing an average of about two tonnes of cassava every month, at the beginning of this year, now the farmer produces almost one and a half per month.
The drought is also said to be affecting livestock farming leading to the reduction of both meat and milk production.

Original_Search_Title: Inside the Market
Headline: Sierra Leone: Inside the Market
Body_Text:
Tengbeh Town Market "Congosa" Market as it commonly called, is located at the main Old Railway Line, Tengbeh Town.
The market accommodates on wide range of the Tengbeh Town community including people from all walks of life.
The market comprises of make shift structures with about one hundred traders selling various cooking food and non-food items.
Speaking to this press in an interview, one of the market women, Ya Sento Kabia , said in the midst of some problems which they face, life is normal in their daily business. She pointed out that most of the items sold are bought in large quantity where goods bought from the provinces are sold cheap in large quantity.
Every morning she went on they have to board a vehicle as early as 5:30 to go to "Dove Cut" and Guard Street market to buy goods. Ya Kabia noted that they have to rush up to return as early as 7 a.m to get their item out from stores.
"This has been going on for a very long time. We are use to it because this is the only way some of us find our living" she said.
On some of the problems they are encountering, she mentioned lack of proper market house that can accommodate more traders.
'We have more traders who want to come and join us so that we can together provide more service to the people, but they could not because there is not enough space to accommodate them" she said, adding that the problems normally get worse in the rainy season when we experience flooding and roof leaking. She requested the government and other NGOs to help rescue them from this suffering, more so as we will soon get heavy down pour of rains.
Explaining the reasons why prices in the market are high compared to others, Ya Kabia said it is due to transportation cost from East End Police 'Poda-Poda park, as they are charged exorbitantly for their loads "We sometimes run at a loss most especially when our goods perished on the way to Freetown," she said, adding that they can buy five gallons of palm oil today to sell at a minimum price of Le800 and by the next day they will hear of a lesser price which eventually lead you to a loss" she explained.
There is a poor sanitation at the "Congosa" Market as well as other market such as Guard Street market which happens to be the filthiest in the city.
Unlike other markets in the municipality of Freetown, the Freetown City Council Market keepers continues with their normal sale of market tickets.
Very close to the market is a lorry park for taxis and other commercial vehicles which makes it easier for traders. Other traders are found around the market vicinity, calling different items. The prices of some basic cooking food items include:
Rice Imported white long grain rice is sold at Le 400 per cup, while the short grain called 'fen-fen' goes for Le500 per cup. The locally produced country rice called 'Wala' rice is sold at Le600 per cup while Rough rice goes for Le 800.
Sauces A tie of potato and cassava leaves goes for Le 300 while the medium and big ties of cassava leaves cost between Le500 &1,000. Crain-Crain and Green cost Le500 each per tie.
Seasonings Onions of different sizes go for Le100-500 for both the imported and locally produced leaf product called "Lungai Yabas" wet and dry pepper is packed at Le200 while big pepper cost Le200 and 500. The same prices go for vegetable tomato. The imported can tomato paste is sold at Le500 per tin. Dry pepper is sold at Le100 per small plastic tie, Jumbo magi is sold at Le 200 each and 100 for the star cube maggi. White Ajinomoto small packet magi go for Le100 while the bigger size goes for Le200. English salt is sold at Le100-200 per tie; the locally produced salt is sold per cup at a price ranging from Le100-300.
Oil Red palm oil is sold at Le900 or at times Le800 per print base on the quantum in the market. The price for "mosankay" cost Le 600 which also goes for the palm cannel nut oil. Groundnut oil is sold at 1,000 per pint.
Fish Dry herring is the most common fish sold at the Congosa Market. The price for dry herring depends on the quantum also goes for the wet mina and herring. Good fish such as 'Shine Nose' and others is difficult to get at the Congosa market mostly one has to take the strain to go to other markets.
Others Groundnut is sold at Le600-700 for the raw product, while the milled one goes for Le300-500 per tie; Garden egg is sold at various prices depending on the size, ranging from Le200-500. Ogiri is sold at Le100 per ball. Tola and Gbala are both sold in teaspoon and small cups, the price ranges from Le1, 000-700. White and black pepper, baking soda and power cost Le100 each per tie.

Original_Search_Title: Super Rice Scarce on the Market
Headline: Uganda: Super Rice Scarce on the Market
Body_Text:
Kampala — Local super rice is scarce and many traders are now mixing the little they have with other cheaper brands to maximize sales.
Otherwise, a kilo of super sells at Shs1,500 wholesale. Maize grain stabilised at Shs400/kg This price has been maintained through out the week, though it later shot to Shs405/kg due to DRC and Tanzanian traders' purchase requirement.
Beans are delivered from Mityana and Kasese in good quantities. The prices at wholesale are still high with; Kanyebwa selling Shs1,300, Nambale Shsh1,000, Kahuura Shs1,200, white beans Shs1,000, mixed beans Shs900 and yellow beans Shs1,300 but very scarce.
The price of maize flour remains high at Shs760/kg for good quality posho and Shsh680/kg for second grade. Popcorn is highly priced with a kilo of 'Kasese corn' selling at Shs850 wholesale price.
Kampala: Kampala fresh commodity market received good supply of different items like sweet potatoes from Zilobwe- Luwero, which had Kalerwe market well stocked. Fresh cassava is coming from Bweyale via the same route distributed to all Kampala markets.
A heap weighing a kilo and half went for Shs1,000. Matooke is a little expensive in all markets around town, with a sack of average weight 110kgs going for Shs34,000- 37,000. The main supplying area is Mbarara although the quality is not good.
Supply of Irish potatoes has improved with good volumes of Kisoro crop coming to supplement Kabale crop, which is winding up. A sack weighing approximately 110kgs is selling at Shs35, 000. Supply of cabbages has improved in all markets attributed to the good amount of rain received in March and April.
Kabale: The district received good quantities of Maize, beans, millet and G/nuts. Prices for commodities such as beans and maize flour registered a slight increase. Beans were delivered from Ibanda and a kilo of Nambale and Kanyebwa sold for Shs800 while maize, especially from Kasese went for Shs420 a kilo.
Masindi: Maize farmers are expecting a good harvest this season while those who planted beans and groundnuts have very little to expect. Groundnuts and soya beans supply is very low and whatever is available is for consumption. A kilo of G/nuts is currently offered at Shs1,450/ kg wholesale price. Fresh maize is in plenty especially with those farmers who planted near the riverbanks and in the valleys.
There is good business going on at the moment with traders from Masindi taking their fresh maize to Gulu and Lira. A maize cob is highly demanded and going for Shs200.Rain is received in most parts of the district and in some area it came down strongly thus affecting some tobacco in Mutunda north of Masindi.
Gulu: Prices continue to be persistently high as of last week and the suppliers are getting further reduced supply especially for commodities like beans, maize, grams, groundnuts, simsim and rice sourced from outside the district. Most of the beans supply came in from Kampala and Masindi at averagely Shs1100/kg wholesale price. Rain is currently received on a daily basis in most parts of the district and the weather remains cloudy.
Masaka: The market conditions remain hush in this western district of Masaka with commodity prices stabilized high especially for Matooke, which sold at Shs7,000 a big bunch. Beans registered an increase in wholesale price due to acute scarcity. A kilo of Nambale went for Shs900- 1000/. There is less demand for chicken after the continued concern of bad flu and increased consumption of fish as alternative. although the quality is low.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria Will Cross the 'Red Sea'
Headline: Nigeria Will Cross the 'Red Sea'
Body_Text:
Lagos — For Nigeria in general Biafra was both a fortunate and unfortunate incident. It was bad for the Igbo but it turned out to be the saving grace for a Nigeria. Forget Uwazuruike. No sensible Igbo man notwithstanding his posture, will be interested in a war that will divide Nigeria. In effect, he will be interested in a war that will make Africa one to allow for his expansion or economic fellowship. The suffering in Biafra was enough eye-opener for everyone.
One was either fortunate or unfortunate to start life's awareness in the heat of civil was. Fortunate because it afforded one to be close to god as both father and mother were in the war the language used for those encircled by the enemy troops and, mother on fatigue mission" as it was the language used for those who must fetch food for Biafran soldiers even close to firing range in the name of harvesting cassava or yam abandoned by villagers running for dear lives. Close to God; because the arena of World Council of Churches (WCC), Caritas distributing food to the young ones and the Sunday school are areas the hungry Biafran soldiers fear to thread. Fortunate; again, because one's town happened to be surrounded by heavy army barracks by names 14 Div, Training Depot, Ojukwu Brigade, Heat and Run, etc. It because a recreation town affording insignificant ones like us the opportunity to observe Biafran soldiers. Though revered at that time by children, their memory now is quite nauseating.
Unfortunate; because one year after the war one found himself in far away Yamoussoukro, observing for some years at a close range the man seen by Biafran children as the "Moses" of Biafra, the indomitable lion, the bearded man, the tallest, biggest and the mightiest. In deed, it was unfortunate because one had never wanted to think of anything that could make one demolish that mythical being. It happened and, so, one wondered why people went to war, suffered and died just to erect a historical figure or a person described by a book reviewer as one whom given the opportunity, if in heaven, could as God to vacate His seat for him.
At this pint one does not need to witness Liberia, Somali, Sudan, Zaire (DRC), Sierra Leone, Rwanda, Chad, Cote d' Ivore, etc, to stamp his foot and say never again.
Even Charles de Gaulle who led the French people in getting rid of German occupation was told never again by the French people in retrospect of 1789 French revolution. French people did not forget what the Ancien regime represented and so, when De Gaulle come up with a constitution that reminded them of the Ancient Regime they gave him a resounding No in a referendum that led to his resignation. In deed what Nigeria is suffering from, which seems to be head-on addressed in the second term of the current regime, was the failure to say "No" to the Biafran war. That war made heroes of nonentities and paved way for leaders who brutalized the Nigerian psyche and economy. We do not need another military heroes. In effect, poor Nigerians who have no other place to call their home, who are among those who have houses in Europe and America should wake up and say Never Again.
In fact, Nigeria about to cross the Red Sea. Israelites crossed the Red Sea which was the most critical stage of their journey. They could have perished completely there but sit was not so. Their enemies perished, in lieu.
Nigerian pharaohs are those who enriched themselves in the past years the military ruled Nigeria. They are those who were able to send their children abroad for schooling while Nigerian schools gasped for funds. They are those who rush abroad for simple routine health check. They are those who keep account abroad. They are those who have houses abroad. They are those who act as patrons of bunkering activities. In deed they are those Kourouma referred in his novel as high way bandits, where he mentioned people like Charles Taylor and Foday Sankoh.
We are not talking about Nigerian who about Nigerians who are working hard abroad and are established there or were frustrated out of Nigeria by the greedy few. We are talking about Nigerians who plundered our resources and moved them out of Nigerians for mere selfish interest.
These pharaohs must not drown Nigerians in the Red Sea by re-enacting a worse conflagration than Biafra.
The paradoxical fortunate and unfortunate experiences of the young ones in and outside Biafra were the stoic stance and the debunking of human claim to heroism a la Charles Taylor. Others must learn from such experiences and stand up against oppression and megalomania, or allow a recycling of evil.
It will be suicidal in one's like time to have witnessed one's parents go to war, survive it or die in it, making unwarranted heroes of it and, then, grow into facing anther war re-enacted by the same unwarranted heroes. God forbid. Nigerians should say never again.
Victor C. Ariole, lectures at UNILAG.

Original_Search_Title: UN Food Drops Reach Some, But Many More Missing Out Due to Lack of Funds
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: UN Food Drops Reach Some, But Many More Missing Out Due to Lack of Funds
Body_Text:
Although United Nations-sponsored airdrops into the Democratic Republic of Congo's (DRC) violence-wracked Katanga province have provided enough food for some 34,700 displaced people, a severe lack of funds is preventing a quarter of a million others from being reached, the UN World Food Programme (WFP) reported today.
"We are very pleased to announce the success of this first airdrop into DRC," WFP Country Director Felix Bamezon said of the operation, which began in April and is the agency's first food airdrop outside Sudan in eight years.
"But we are not content with what has been achieved. We need to reach many more people in the next few months, particularly in North Kivu (province)," he added of the latest crises, in which an estimated 220,000 people have been uprooted by fighting between the Government and Mai Mai rebels in Katanga and another 80,000 displaced in North Kivu Province.
With just three months left of its overall two-and-a-half year Protracted Relief and Recovery Operation in the DRC, WFP still has a critical 36 per cent shortfall, or $69 million of the $191 million needed to help up to 1.6 million internally displaced persons (IDPs) and other vulnerable people throughout the vast country.
This leaves very little funding to assist the IDPs in Katanga or in North Kivu, where WFP was forced to cut rations during the last distribution due to the shortfall.
Although expensive, WFP chose airdrops in south-eastern Katanga province because the rainy season, rising overland transport costs and insecurity have made it difficult to use roads. Food trucks were taking up to eight weeks to reach distribution sites from Katanga's provincial capital of Lubumbashi.
To feed people who have fled to the towns of Dubie, Sampwe and Mitwaba, an Antonov-12 aircraft has spent just over three weeks dropping 336 metric tonnes of commodities, at nearly twice the cost of road transport.
"Our village was totally destroyed by armed men. I feed my child with what I have - some cassava - but I am really happy that aid has arrived," Claudine Kambemba said in Sampwe after seeing food airdropped not far from her camp.
WFP's partner non-governmental organization (NGO) last week completed distributing cereals, lentils, oil and salt to more than 8,750 people in Sampwe. Distributions of air dropped food are taking place in the next few days for 9,980 people in Mitwaba and 16,000 people in Dubie.

Original_Search_Title: Scientists Develop 'Biocrops' to Boost Nutrition
Headline: Africa: Scientists Develop 'Biocrops' to Boost Nutrition
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Scientists have developed a new technique that loads vital nutrients and vitamins into ordinary food crops.
If the technology become widely available in sub-Saharan Africa, many of the diseases that are linked to poor diet will be drastically reduced in the region.
Termed "biofortification," the new technique promises to be a boon for poor households in a region where ever-rising numbers of mouths to feed are a headache not only to parents but also for national governments.
Their main worry though is usually about the amount of food on the table as well as where the next meal will come from.
Few poor people worry too much about the nutritional content of these meals.
But agricultural researchers and nutritionists who support the new technique say this ought to be one of Africa's key nutritional concerns.
Many diseases, they say, could be avoided if all Africans ate not merely generous helpings of the available food, but diets of adequate nutritional content.
But this has not been happening because poverty has prevented many people in the region from feeding well.
Consequently, Africans, and particularly young children, fall ill more often from diseases linked to poor diet.
For instance, in much of rural Africa, the poor can only afford a diet based on staple crops such as maize and beans, (githeri, as the meal is known in central Kenya) or sweet potatoes and cassava. Nutritionists say that although these and similar foods serve the purpose of filling empty stomachs, they are generally low in micronutrients particularly iron, zinc and Vitamin A.
Scientists say that such poor diets cause blindness, impair mental development particularly among women and children; cause general illnesses, and even premature death.
Today, close to 70 per cent of pre-school children in sub-Saharan Africa are said to suffer iron deficiency, leading to a rising incidence of anaemia.
In addition, lack of foods with Vitamin A is the leading cause of preventable blindness while foods deficient in zinc contribute to stunted growth, increased rates and severity of infections, and pregnancy and birth-related complications.
The ideal solution to micronutrient deficiency is a balanced diet of fruits, vegetables and meat. But this is not a choice for the very poor.
However, researchers working for an organisation named Harvestplus say biofortification crop-breeding would ensure that Africa eats better quality foods.
They are arming ordinary foods as beans, maize and sweet potatoes with such nutritionally-vital ingredients as zinc, iron and vitamins through biofortification.
And scientists are upbeat about the scientific procedure, terming it a "revolutionary process" that holds great potential to enhance the health of poor people in the region.
Biofortification enables plants to take in more minerals and other ingredients from the soil and load them into the seeds, thus fortifying themselves, says Dr Howarth Bouis, director of HarvestPlus. "It is the plants that are doing the work and not manufacturers."
Harvestplus is an international research programme that seeks to reduce malnutrition by applying agricultural technology to arm staple crops for better nutrition.
To popularise the technique, scientists, agricultural experts, policy makers and health experts from the region met in Mombasa, Kenya, last week for a workshop hosted by the Forum for Agricultural Research in Africa and HarvestPlus.
The participants, drawn from regional ministries of agriculture, health, and finance, non-governmental organisations, national agricultural research institutes, departments of health, UN agencies, and international financial agencies, discussed the latest research on biofortification and identified ways of developing biofortified crops in Africa and how to make the technique part and parcel of national agricultural and health policy agendas.
"By targeting staple food crops grown and consumed by the rural poor, biofortification can reach large numbers of people in a cost-effective and sustainable manner, leading to a nutrition revolution in Africa," says Prof Ruth Oniang'o, Kenyan MP and lecturer in food science and nutrition at Jomo Kenyatta University of Agriculture and Technology.
Foods are complicated compounds and there are fears that loading them with newer ingredients might affect the health of the consumers. But Dr Bouis says that unlike nutrients taken from animal matter, human bodies are able to regulate how much of plant nutrients they absorb.
This means that one can eat as much minerals from such staples without exposing themselves to the dangers of falling sick.

Original_Search_Title: Women Cry Out From the Camps
Headline: Uganda: Women Cry Out From the Camps
Body_Text:
Kampala — IT is 7:00am. Along the Gulu-Kampala highway, the internally displaced women can be seen. They have heavy loads. While some carry their babies in their hands, most of the women have them strapped on their backs. In addition to the babies, they carry hoes and small containers of seeds. Others carry potato cuttings on their heads. They walk quickly, overtaking other road users. These women are taking advantage of the season's rains to cultivate on no-man's land by the roadside.
The majority have sold their labour by toiling and eking out a living on other people's shambas to augment the little food hand-outs in the camps. Others indulge in petty roadside trade, selling some of the food supplies they receive from humanitarian organisations, but their plight has not changed. Many of them feed their large families on one meal a day.
At Unyama camp, seven kilometres north-east of Gulu, the mothers are feeding their children on mangoes and uncooked cassava at 2:00pm. Unlike other parts of the country where rural women shy away from the media, these ones crave to be heard.
Rose Otoo, about 47, pushes her way through the crowd, lamenting the death of her husband. She says he was killed by government soldiers in 1994, leaving her with five orphans she cannot feed.
"I had decided not to talk about my suffering. Initially, I thought I would manage just like some of the women here," she says, pointing at her colleagues. "However, I have to go public about my plight because I feel I cannot look after these children anymore," Otoo says.
She goes on: "I'm tired of digging on other people's land for food. I want to go back to my home where I have enough land to dig. I'm waiting for the day the Government will send all of us back home. I can't go back alone before knowing the security measures the Government has put in place to protect us."
Otoo is not alone. Her colleague Lucy Aciro, who lost her husband to a long illness in the camp, agrees. She says she is frustrated with the internally displaced persons' (IDP) life. Her children are not in school and she fears they will soon turn into thugs because of the idle life they lead.
Aciro regrets that the Government, instead of planning to return the IDPs to their villages, is just planning to de-congest the camps.
"I want to go back home so that my children can have education. Before Joseph Kony displaced us, we used to keep and sell goats and cows to educate our children. We used to grow food and sell some of it to supplement the money from the sale of the animals," she cries.
Like many of her colleagues who decry the poverty, discrimination and marginalisation they have continued to experience in the camps, Aciro is reluctant to return home before the Government assures her that the rebels will not strike back.
Juleta Akera describes the plan to de-congest the camps as a wild idea, but is quick to note: "Chances of our children and women getting exposure to abductions and torture are high if we return to our homes without tight security. It is perhaps better to watch our children die of hunger than see them abducted and killed by the rebels."
These women's unique story of suffering, they say, has not been told to the world throughout the 20 years of the northern war. They say even when the humanitarian organisations distribute food and other domestic items, women who look desperate, hungry and poverty-stricken and homesteads headed by women and children are sidelined by most camp leaders who usually handle the donations.
During the interview, many of the women make futile attempts to hide their sunken faces by looking at the ground.
Evelyn Piloya, a programme officer with the Grassroots Women Association of Gulu (GWAD), says the poverty is alarming. Many children do not go to school and there is an increase in the number of child-headed homes and orphans.
The situation has been worsened by the HIV/AIDS prevalence, which has shot up to 9% in a population of 500,000 people. Gulu's newly-elected Woman MP Betty Aol Ocan says the HIV/AIDs prevalence in the district is closely linked to the war. The most hit IDPs, she says, are the women and girls.
Sarah Aciro, 17, is one of the most disadvantaged child-heads of families here. She lost her parents to AIDS four years ago. Aciro assumed the roles of her parents at only 14. Confused, the P.6 pupil stopped schooling to care for her six siblings.
Last year, after receiving sh40,000 as a revolving loan from GWAD to supplement her income from food vending, Aciro diverted sh20,000 to register for the Primary Leaving Examinations. She scored 20 aggregates.
However, her chances of joining secondary education this year are minimal. "We have encouraged her to apply for a scholarship from the International Service Volunteers Association. However, her chances of succeeding are low because the scholarships have become competitive, since the organisation is extending its operations outside the municipality," Piloya says of Aciro.
Most of the children have distended bellies due to poor feeding. "Even when they know that the beans have weevils and have lost their taste because of over-storage, they have to eat because they are desperate to survive," Piloya says.
Piloya recalls that when IDPs from Odek camp refused to offload dura sorghum from Sudan because it was bitter, WFP retaliated by not supplying them with food for over one month.
She condemns the discrimination of certain groups of IDPs based on status and age. She says sometimes those who help in the distribution of food and other donations consider their relatives first or people of the same political affiliation, gender and age.

Original_Search_Title: Theatre: Moving On to Serious Stuff
Headline: Kenya: Theatre: Moving On to Serious Stuff
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Like they promised early this year, local theatre groups have given Western bedroom comedies a break and are instead staging more serious works by African dramatists, beginning with Kenyan playwrights.
Festival of Creative Arts set the ball rolling with Jimmi Makotsi's She Ate the Female Cassava at the Kenya National Theatre (KNT) a month ago. They were followed Phoenix Players with Cajetan Boy's Dead Talk a fortnight ago, while last weekend at the Alliance Francaise auditorium, Pambazuka Productions celebrated their first anniversary with Francis Imbuga's seminal drama, The Burning of Rags.
All were serious productions and, happily theatre groups are taking us back to the memorable 1990s, when an amateur but vibrant theatre satisfied theatre lovers. Of course, there are a few jokers like Arts Africa Productions, who over the Easter weekend staged Taphine Otieno's I Dearly Love Your Wife, an example of how not to write a play.
Shallow script
While local playwrights should be encouraged, it is important that they give theatre goers their money's worth. Otieno's supposed comedy was the group's debut theatrical offering, staged at the Kenya National Theatre's concert hall.
It was a shallow script, worsened by over acting, wanting set construction, drab costumes and lack of continuity. Impressive Grace Dola (Jacky) was the promising actress in the wrong play, which was being re-staged due to great "public demand" - to a largely empty hall!
There is a tendency among thespians to only watch their own shows, but Otieno should write more plays only after watching those of serious playwrights like The Burning of Rags.
Pambazuka gave a remarkable rendition of this play which, hopefully, will make more people take theatre seriously. There were signs that that might already be happening, if the number of companies that sponsored the show is anything to go by.
The play revolves around Denis (Sam Kihiu), a brilliant professor who is in trouble with his community for failing to participate fully in the traditional initiation of his son Yona (Ledema Sempele), due to his demanding career and Westernisation. His father Agala (Charles Bukeko), an arrogant traditionalist, and Babu (Andronico Otieno) the know-it-all village elder, will have none of it. Denis' born-again mother, Elima (Loise Abukutsa), sides with her son, making the situation even worse.
Rough time
Denis is not having an easy time in the city either, what with his girlfriend Hilda (Joy Mureithi), who has a fishy past, is pregnant and determined to marry him. Things get tricky when Denis learns that Hilda had another baby and wants to leave her.
Henrics Fisher (Kanyeki Sinbad), a friend of Denis' and father of Hilda's child, comes back into the picture. He wants to take her to the US where he is a lecturer but withdraws the offer when he learns of her pregnancy.
Charles Bukeko directed a brilliant cast which included Mumba Hinzano as Dr Agbale and Peggy Ngure as Matilda. The cast clearly brought out the theme of generational conflict, which Westernised Nairobians can still identify nearly two decades after the play was written.
All the actors portrayed such understanding of their characters that one can hardly pick a favourite: could it be Joy Mureithi, who convincingly made us empathise with women in her situation? Or Kihiu with his tearfully believable demeanour when his father "died"...Kanyeki and his laughably affected mannerisms?
Commendable too was the superb set construction, complete with real smoke on the village set. Well, Pambazuka showed that a group can hold an audience captive without raucous laughter at end of every punchline.
The only minor lapse was in continuity. At one point Hilda was listening to Denis' poem, From Home with Bananas on the music system, but we didn't see her insert the tape. And when she was dancing to a Salsa tune, Denis switched it off to play traditional Luhya numbers, but again, without changing the tape. So was the tape with the poem also the one with Salsa and Luhya songs?
Imbuga drew heavily from traditions of his Luhya community in scripting the play-within-a-play that had his trademark fool figure, subtle humour and a contemporary issue. He is famous for his political play, Betrayal in the City, which represented Kenya in the 1977 Fespaco festivals in Nigeria.
His other plays include; Kisses of Fate, The Fourth Trial, The Married Bachelor, (written in 1973 but made into a feature film in 1998), Game of Silence, The Successor, Man of Kafira and Aminata, which was commissioned for the UN Decade for Women's Conference held in Nairobi in 1985.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture Minister On Coffee Cultivation
Headline: Angola: Agriculture Minister On Coffee Cultivation
Body_Text:
Luanda — Angola's Minister of Agriculture and the Rural Development, Gilberto Buta Lutucuta, defended today, in Luanda, the necessity of associating the production of coffee to other crops (maize, banana and cassava), aiming at increasing the food production in the country.
Speaking at the launching ceremony of the project of reactivation of production of robust coffee, to be implemented in Porto Ambo¡m district, in coastal Kwanza Sul province, the government official said that the increase of the production will allow to drain the surplus to other markets.
According to the minister, the programme of rehabilitation of family production of robust coffee in Kwanza Sul will stimulate the relaunching of the culture of this product in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Police Gun Down Carpenter In Another Case of Mistaken Identity
Headline: Ghana: Police Gun Down Carpenter In Another Case of Mistaken Identity
Body_Text:
The residents of Winneba Junction are fuming with rage and on the verge of attacking the Winneba police over an incident in which a carpenter, Otto Kofie, was killed as an armed robber.
The residents and family of Kofie have called on the Inspector General of Police (IGP) to conduct investigations into the matter, since Kofie was not an armed robber.
The family told The Chronicle that the police claimed that he died as a result of a gunshot wound on his leg, but when his body was examined at the mortuary, it was discovered that there was a wound on his head, which, they believed, was due to a gunshot that killed him.
They revealed that though they insisted that a post-mortem be conducted to find out how many gunshot wounds the deceased had sustained, the police were demanding ¢500,000 as the cost of the post-mortem, which would be conducted by their doctor in Winneba.
The Chronicle's investigations revealed that on Good Friday, Kofie received a message that said he should see his family in the Volta region, to start the cultivation of cassava and maize, since their season had began.
Further investigations revealed that he approached a rich man to assist him with a ¢1.5 million loan, but the man could not provide him with the money, but gave him ¢200,000.
According to the family, Kofie later decided to visit a nearby pub, Royal Club, to take a bottle.
The Manager of the club, Mr. Prah, told The Chronicle that Kofie, as usual, was with him at his bar, drinking and listening to music until 9:20pm when he decided to close the club for the day. It was at this juncture that Kofie went to buy batteries to fix into his personal sound system to continue listening to some music.
He said, Kofie rushed to buy banku and then bought nine sachets of water from him and departed to his house at around 9:30p.m.
Uncle Ebo, another eyewitness, disclosed that around 9:35 pm, he was sitting in his compound, 'working on some lotto permutations', when he heard gunshots behind his house. He said he became scared and then rushed to his room.
He disclosed that after a few seconds however, he peeped through his door and saw someone running; from a path, through his compound, into a nearby bush. He narrated that not long after, a night police patrol team, who were firing gunshots in the man's direction, followed the 'running-man'.
He said later, he came out of his room and met three police officers, who told him that they had shot an armed robber and that he should go with them and find out if he could identify the person.
He emphasized that he declined to go on the grounds that he does not associate himself with armed robbers. It was on the next day that he heard that the person that was shot the previous night was Kofie.
Mr. Stephen Asie, a contractor with whom the deceased had worked as a caretaker, said the police claimed that when they took him to the morgue, it was discovered that Kofie had put a locally manufactured gun in his trousers and had tightened it with a belt.
He argued that he would not take the police's claims that Kofie was an armed robber, since he worked for him for six years, without stealing a thing.
"So that means I was harbouring an armed robber and no property of mine has ever got missing for the past six years?" he quizzed rhetorically.
He insisted that the gun that was found in Kofie's trousers had been planted there by the police officers.
When The Chronicle undercover reporters contacted the Winneba police, Sgt I.I Adjen, in charge of Arms and Ammunitions, he said Kofie was shot in the leg.
He said it was very difficult for one to die as a result of a gunshot in the leg, but in Kofie's case, it might be as a result of shock.
He indicated that he needed the ¢500,000, part of which is to pay the pathologist and ¢50,000 to pay bribes in order to get approval for the autopsy.
Sgt Adjen maintained that Kofie engaged the police in a fire exchange, but Uncle Ebo has debunked this statement on the grounds that when Kofie was running, he did not see him shooting, adding that it was rather the police who were shooting.
Mr. Asie condemned the police, arguing that if their claims that Kofie had engaged them were true, when he was shot down, the first thing the police would have done would be to look for the gun. But according to him, they did not do so until he was taken to the morgue.

Original_Search_Title: France Disburses CFA 288 Million for Food Production
Headline: Cameroon: France Disburses CFA 288 Million for Food Production
Body_Text:
The money will be used to produce cassava and cocoyam in the Centre, South and East provinces.
An agreement was yesterday signed in Yaounde between the French Ambassador to Cameroon represented by the Adviser for Cooperation and Cultural Action, Claude Fandre and the General Manager of the Institute for Agricultural Research (IRAD), Dr. Zok Simon for the French government to disburse CFA 288 million to support and improve agricultural production and build the capacity of professional organisations in the cassava and cocoyam sector.
The signing ceremony preceded the meeting of the steering committee of the Partnership Reinforcement on Agronomic Research in Cameroon (REPARAC) which met to examine its internal regulations, action plan and provisional budget for 2006. The money which will be shared to eight projects in the Centre, South and East provinces, will improve productivity in the cassava and cocoyam sector. Each project will receive the sum of CFA 36 million.
According to the explanatory note of the agreement, cassava and cocoyam constitute the main food crop eaten in most homes. Cassava is equally cultivated for transformation at industrial level and is used in producing several products. As a result of this, cassava has for long constituted the major source of finance for the population. The two products occupy 74 per cent of the purchasing value of family homes. Since the mid 90s, the two crops have experienced substantial increase in its prices compared to other crops.
In order to satisfy increasing demand, production at medium term ought to increase by 30 per cent between 2002 and 2010. This unfortunately has not been the case. The assistance from the French government is expected to play an important role in boosting production. It will above all enable actors in the sector to better understand the practices and mechanisms of cassava and cocoyam production test the available innovations and analyse the functioning of the sector among others.
The Partnership Reinforcement on Agronomic Research in Cameroon (REPARAC) project whose steering committee met yesterday, was created in December, 2004. It has as objective to improve and promote agronomic research in Cameroon by developing new forms of partnership ion the sector.

Original_Search_Title: South West : SCNC Local Leaders Join CPDM in Mamfe
Headline: Cameroon: South West : SCNC Local Leaders Join CPDM in Mamfe
Body_Text:
- The Cameroon People's Democratic Movement (CPDM) is growing from strength to strength in Manyu Division as five local leaders of the Southern Cameroons National Council (SCNC) last April 15 joined the CPDM.
This was during a rally the Vice President of the National Assembly, Hon. Rose Abunaw Makia held at Besong Abang village. The former SCNC local leaders had also indoctrinated six students of Government High School, Mamfe who joined them to disturb public meetings and manifestations in the area.
During the rally at Besong Abang, the five SCNC leaders apologised, confessed and openly joined the CPDM while promising to wipe off any traces of the SCNC in the Division. The six students on their part confessed, regretted and narrated their havoc and suffering as they ignorantly followed the doctrine of the SCNC. They all paid tribute to Hon. Rose Abunaw and the Mayor of the Mamfe Rural Council, Arrey Eyong Ashu, who took them as children, talked with them and even rescued them from detention as they were arrested while disturbing the inauguration of the Mamfe Hospital last March 27. The students promised to respect the laws of the land and denounced the SCNC stating that its secessionist agenda could plunge Cameroon into war. The former SCNC local leaders on their part promised to respect State institutions, work for unity and follow Hon. Rose Abunaw.
Hon. Rose Abunaw while welcoming the SCNC leaders and students admitted that Manyu Division in general faced development problems, but told them that the best way of solving the problems was to remain in a united Cameroon. She urged them to support President Paul Biya and the ruling CPDM as the Head of State is progressively tackling their problems. Hon. Rose Abunaw advised parents not to relent in their efforts of guiding their children.
The Besong Abang meeting was just one of the stages in Hon. Rose Abunaw's one week contact visit of some villages in her constituency of Mamfe Central and Upper Bayang Subdivisions. She met and discussed with the population in villages such as Eshobi, Sabis, Banga Pungo, Mfaitok I A, Eyang Atemaku, Egbekaw, Okoyong, Obang 3 Corners, Bachou Akagbe and Besong Abang. The visit coincided with cultural weeks organised by the Mamfe Central Development Association (MASUDA) and the Upper Bayang Development Association (UBADA). She used the occasion to donate a cassava grinding mill to the women of Eyangtui village and had two weeks earlier donated one to women of Eshobi village, all estimated at CFA 600,000. Hon. Rose Abunaw equally donated farm inputs and plastic chairs as she met her constituents.

Original_Search_Title: Scandalous Fish Scarcity
Headline: Nigeria: Scandalous Fish Scarcity
Body_Text:
Lagos — THE two-week long strike embarked on recently by frozen fish dealers and retailers over sharp rise in prices has exposed unique and cruel irony that a nation which literally 'sits' on oceans, rivers and lakes is nonetheless incapable of harvesting enough fish to feed its population.
As with crude petroleum which Nigeria abundantly has in reserves but still imports petroleum products at high costs, three quarters of the nation's fish needs still comes by way of imports at increasingly exorbitant costs.
The grouse of the fish dealers and retailers who, through their various associations, organised the strike had been that costs had so much risen that their profit margins had been eroded. The reason for this poor or unprofitable stocking and resell of fish was attributed to poor patronage by consumers who could no longer afford to buy even fish to spice their meals!
To back up their claims, the Fish Dealers Association of Nigeria (FIDAN) and United Fish Sellers Association (UNIFISA) had stopped dealing and selling until government 'does something', urgently to redress the situation.
For example, the price of the popular Kote fish had jumped to N7,500 from N1,800 per carton some months ago. Herring (Mackerel) leaped from N1,400 per carton to N5,000; Croaker, from N3,400 to N7,000 and Blue Whiting from N1,300 to N2,400.
Clearly, something is not quite right here when the basic protein source for millions of Nigerians is no longer affordable to the ordinary family.
As in other areas of our national life where great speeches are made about making life comfortable and affordable for the common man, the promise by government of increasing local fish production to meet demands without resort to importation in 2005 has turned out to be empty.
Of the estimated 1.5 million metric tons of fish needed to meet local demand, Nigeria only has the capacity to produce 511,700 metric tons. The shortfall of about 680,000 metric tons come from imports.
The cost of this import, according to facts that emanated from the Fish-For-All Summit held last August, in Abuja, at the instance of NEPAD pursuant of the minimum Millennium Development Goal (MDG) of providing for all Africans, is about N50 million to Nigeria alone per annum.
At the Abuja summit, the dangers of future fish under-production and imminent scarcity were clearly articulated, speeches and pledges were made to counter the fulfilment of the promise of scarcity for all the 26 African participants in 2005.
The Minister of State for Agriculture, Mr. Bamidele Dada who took part in the Abuja fish summit had pointed at the 2005 budget that was geared towards encouraging small-scale, self-sufficient fisheries that by 2007 would be exporting fish to the world.
President Olusegun Obasanjo had after the summit set up a special committee on fisheries and aquaculture development that would complement his administration's agricultural and food production policy.
Aquaculture industry was said to be worth a potential US2.7million or N365 billion per annum, which all are at risk, not to talk of the insecurity it generates among a deprived population of about 130 million.
Barely one year after the fish summit, the indigenous fishing companies have mostly all folded up or become net importers of fish as middlemen for foreign concerns. Those left in the business are handicapped by low resource base, technological inferiority and inability to compete with well organised, foreign fishing trawlers.
Therefore, government is well advised to get to grips with this fish problem before it gets out of hand. The alternative scenario is dire. As our population increases, demand for food at affordable prices will become acute.
Legislations and measures to protect and defend the nation's exclusive economic zones at sea, not just regarding oil explorations but also to prevent private fishing activities should be pursued rigorously. Those engaged in obnoxious fishing practices should be brought to account both locally and on our international waters to preserve the nation's fish-stock and species in sustainable ways.
Institutions and organisations involved with fish and fishing should be adequately financed and empowered to engage in their originating briefs. Government has been willing and prepared to assist farmers of cocoa, cassava and other cash crops to meet local demand and for exports, the same should be done for those involved in commercial fishing with micro-finance, training and other supports and incentives that may include import duty waivers on vital inputs.
This is where agencies like Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) and National Directorate for Employment (NDE) should be encouraged by law to get involved and empower Nigerians not just to own their own commercial trawlers but also in owning self-sustaining fish farms. A nation that cannot feed itself can never say that it is secure.

Original_Search_Title: Dykes Bring New Flood of Hope
Headline: Kenya: Dykes Bring New Flood of Hope
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Farmers living near rivers Nzoia and Nyando can bank on a bumper harvest despite heavy rain - because the water has been controlled
Residents of Budalang'i in Busia District are satisfied with the flood control measures implemented by the Government.
And in Nyando, about 180 kilometres east of Budalang'i, farmers are looking forward to a bumper harvest after the floods were controlled. In the past, the area was perennially submerged in the rainy seasons.
Now the rain water has been controlled using dykes built by National Youth Service personnel. The project cost Sh40 million.
The area's prospects appear even brighter after the US Government provided a Sh5 billion grant for building of dams along the banks of rivers Nyando and Nzoia. Work on the dams is set to begin in July.
Japan has also expressed an interest in funding a similar project in Nyando and has commissioned a feasibility study.
Vegetables are flourishing
Budalang'i is now a lush green - because the maize, beans, cassava, groundnuts and vegetables are flourishing despite the heavy rains that continue to pound the area. The pain and agony that used to characterise the rainy seasons in the past seems to be no more.
To the residents, the new trend is a blessing after many years of crop losses and disease. Area residents in the past perennially depended on the Government and other well-wishers for relief food because their crops were always destroyed by floods.
Mr Sebastian Nalami, 46, recalled the events of 2004 as if they happened yesterday: "It was between April and May during the long rains. Many of us sought refuge at the divisional headquarters because our houses and property had been submerged."
Although they were safe at the DO's office, life was difficult due to congestion and competition for essential services like clean water, food and sanitation.
Those seeking refuge lived in fear of water-borne diseases due to the conditions at the rescue centre. There was no privacy either as parents and their children shared quarters.
Settlement scheme
At one point, Government officials suggested that those affected by the floods be moved to settlement schemes in Kitale and other areas. But the people declined the offer and chose to remain on their ancestral land.
"We have lived for long and it was not right to leave our ancestral land for a permanent residence elsewhere," Mr Nalami said. He and other residents asked the Government to find a lasting solution to the floods menace.
But Mrs Sorofina Anyango told the Nation that the Government was not firm on its plans to take area residents to new settlement schemes.
Willing to move
"Some of us would have been more than willing to move out of this land in favour of better places," she said. But he added that there was renewed hope of a better life after the dykes were built.
'We expect a bumper harvest this season more so if the water levels reduce so that water may not overflow to the land under crops," said Ms Christine Wafula, 21, of Bulago Village.
And Mr Wilson Ouma, 37, praised the Government for rehabilitating the dykes. He said they had effectively controlled the flow of water whenever River Nzoia, which flows through the area, bursts its banks.
The dykes have played a key role in ensuring that there are no floods since 2005 and this year, the long rains might subside before we experience the floods," he said. But he asked the Government to find ways of resettling those living in low-lying areas or move them to higher grounds during the rainy season.
"They can be coming to cultivate their pieces of land and return to their homes on the hillside," Mr Ouma said.
He also said that dams be built along the river and other strategic areas. "The water tapped can be used for irrigation.
Mr Raphael Sadia, 66, an elder in Nagoba Village, said that life during past floods was terrible.
Having lived in the area since 1974, Mr said said that he first experienced the devastating effects of floods in 1979. He pointed at a tree showing how high the waters used to run. Today, numerous trees in his compound are maturing with a variety of fruits.
"The situation is now better and we only wish that it improves further," he said.
Although area residents sometimes live in fear when the water levels in the river rise significantly, they are happy that they can now freely practice farming to supplement their main economic activity - fishing.
But Budalang'i sub-location senior assistant chief William Barasa said that this year's harvest might not be enough because of the stagnant water in the fields. "Crops like maize and cassava, whose roots are now under excess water, may rot and this will affect productivity," he said.
Busia District relies on maize, beans and millet imported from neighbouring Uganda to meet its food needs.
The district commissioner, Mr Raphael Letimalo, and the district water officer, Mr Cleophas Mutoro, said they were closely monitoring the water levels and advising residents accordingly.
"Facts on the ground indicate that the water volume has increased, but not to alarming levels. The district disaster management committee has been put on alert in case the river breaks its banks," Mr Letimalo said.
With the water under control, living standards have improved and many residents have asked the Government to help farmers increase the acreage under rice. The crop is doing well at the Bunyala Irrigation Scheme.
Mr Raphael Wanjala, the MP for Budalang'i, and also the Water assistant minister, urged residents to take advantage of the controlled floods to practise extensive agriculture.
In Nyando, the Nation team met Mzee Joash Mbaga, 76, of Ombeyi location. Today, he and other men and women of his age can be left at home alone without fearing that they will be washed away by floods unlike in the past.
For the first time in many years, residents of Nyando are experiencing a new way of life - a life free from the hustle of having to move away from home to escape floods every year. In the past, moving to higher ground had become a ritual. Many can now enjoy farming without fearing that their crops will be washed away.
"For the first time in many years, we have not been forced to move away by the water. Besides, we can afford to go to the shamba and grow maize without fear," he Mzee Mbaga said.
And for residents of Kano plains in Nyando District, the onset of the long rains in past years always spelt disaster because the floods often left a trail of destruction in their wake especially when the River Nyando broke its banks. Many people lost their loved ones, and their animals, after they were washed away by the raging waters.
"I will never forget the pain I have gone through in the past. I have lost a son, several animals and property to these waters. I am glad we have been spared this time, the elderly man said.
Nyando district commissioner John Egesa is optimistic that the area will break the vicious cycle of poverty and suffering that has characterised it in the past. "The people are back on their farms and you can be sure they will reap maximum benefits since this area is fertile, he said.

Original_Search_Title: Intrigues Behind Biosafety Bill
Headline: Kenya: Intrigues Behind Biosafety Bill
Publisher: African Woman and Child Feature Service (Nairobi)
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Local scientists have been unable to transfer GM crop research technology to farmers since there is no clear legal framework for their commercialisation.Research on genetically modified crops in Kenya has moved ahead of politicians and soon, scientists will either have to stop and wait, or see their work go down the drain.
Although talk on biosafety has been on since the late 1990s, nothing solid has come of it, while research on transgenic maize, cotton and other products is almost complete.
But the products and the research will be worthless since there is no policy or guideline for taking the crops to farmers for commercialisation.
The country has four genetically modified crops- Bt maize, Bt cotton, transgenic cassava, transgenic sweet potatoes and a recombinant rinderpest vaccine at various stages of study.
Confined field trials on Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) maize are going on in Kiboko, Makueni, while that on Bt cotton are taking place in Mwea.
The second harvest of the GMO maize, supposed to be resistant to stem borers, was done two weeks ago. Bt cotton, with a gene to make it resistant to the bollworm, is flowering.
The harvested maize has been retained for breeding before it is destroyed. These technologies are expected to help a farmer cut-down on the costs of production.
Multinational companies that have developed varieties in the country are also finding it difficult to transfer the technology since there is no clear legal framework on how they should engage.
"Without the existence of a Biosafety Act, this technology will remain a research project and will not reach farmers because there will be no legal framework provided for its commercialisation," says Kinyua M'Mbijiwe of Monsanto, a US based organisation.
M'Mbijiwe said Monsanto has developed a GM maize species for the Kenyan market but cannot commercialise it.
Partly due to the lack of such a framework, Kenya lost the chance to host one of Africa's biggest GMO programmes - the development of a super sorghum funded by the Melinda, Gates Foundation - to South Africa.
Dr Stephen Mugo, a plant breeder at International Maize and Wheat Improvement Centre (CIMMYT), says although there are rules and regulations on the development and testing of GMO products in the country, they cannot support certain trials that are necessary for moving the technology to farmers.
The government has been responding with guidelines at every stage of research on a need-based basis, especially when scientists present it with requests for further trials on such organisms.
When such requests are made, the National Biosafety Committee (NBC), which directs research in this area has been forced to borrow from guidelines used in countries such as South Africa and Canada.
Scientists fear that the government is moving slowly in establishing a policy and law to guide the research.
Currently, research is guided by the Science and Technology Act Cap 250, which does not cater for transgenic crops as it was enacted before they became a global issue.
The government is said to be very cautious especially on matters concerning the growing and commercialisation of GMO crops in Kenya.
Sources at the NBC said the government does not want to rush the matter then burn its fingers or hurt other stakeholders.
"We are moving at the right pace because these are sensitive and emotive issues, which have to be addressed cautiously," said one of the sources.
A Biosafety Bill is now waiting to be brought to Parliament for debate and enactment into law. Regulations on its implementation including manuals for use by regulatory agents dealing with GMOs and awareness materials targeting the public have been developed and finalised.
The NBC officials say the policy is likely to be adopted by the Cabinet by June 2006.
While this is going on, the government is finding itself in a tricky situation as the US and some European countries have vested interests in the proposed Biosafety law and regulations.
They are said to be behind the scenes, trying to influence the government to either put or remove certain things in the proposed Biosafety policy and law to accommodate their interests.
US, the leading producer of GMO products worldwide, has in the past expressed disquiet with certain requirements including labelling of products to indicate that they are genetically modified.
The European Union, carrying out 11,000 studies on GMOs, is for these restrictions being put in place.
The US and Canadian governments argue that such restrictions go against the principles of trade espoused by World Trade Organisation.
WTO's rules, in a departure from the Cartegena Protocol's precautionary principle, require scientific justification for restrictions on imported commodities. It proposes that countries use the least trade restrictive options.
Created in 2000 and officially functional in 2003, the Cartegena Protocol on Biosafety is a legally binding agreement that addresses potential risks posed by GMOs.
Argentina, Australia, New Zealand, Chile and Uruguay are among countries, which by end of last year had refused to ratify the protocol. The fear created about GMO crops is making it difficult for some countries to allow their importation, thereby affecting the exports of countries dealing in these crops. Developing countries, particularly in Africa, have expressed fears that they may not have the capacity and resources to implement the Cartegena Protocol when they have other competing and pressing needs such as health, education, and shelter.
Scientists trained in biochemistry, molecular genetics, and plant breeding are needed to assess and manage the risk of GMOs to humans and the biodiversity. In Kenya, the Global Environmental Facility, a component of the United Nations Environment Programme to operationalise the protocol, is helping the government.
The government is now torn between donors who are for and against certain requirements of the Protocol.
An official at the NBC says some of the issues being raised by US and Europe are the sticking points in international debate, and the country cannot make a decision to put them in law before a global consensus on the same.
These issues include two provisions of the Cartgena Protocol: Article 18, which deals with handling, transport, packaging and identification of GMO products, and 27, which talks about liability and redress for damage resulting from transboundary movements of GMOs.
Locally, many vested interests in GMO products, some using these articles to explain their positions are said to be delaying the enactment of the Bill.

Original_Search_Title: Because of Limited Facilities: Taylor, Norman, Others Angry At Special Court
Headline: Sierra Leone: Because of Limited Facilities: Taylor, Norman, Others Angry At Special Court
Body_Text:
Freetown — Dr. Bubuake Jabbie, lead defense counsel for the first accused, Sam Hinga Norman in the Civil Defence Forces (CDF) trail at the Special Court Tuesday, as a preliminary notice to the court, complained to a Status Conference saying detainees, including Charles Taylor and others are not happy with facilities.
"Detainees are complaining about their visitors, including relatives, being harassed and embarrassed whenever they pay a visit to the detention center. But whilst we intend to take the matter with the Chief Detention Officer and Registrar, we thought that such preliminary notification is important," he deduced.
Jabbie put it to the Presiding Judge, Pierre Boutet that telephone lines are no longer in their earlier better situation when one line was available to each set of indictees.
"Detainees are asking for an increase in the quantity of traditional diet (rice and potato or cassava leaves). They also need a clarification on the issue of compensation or labour fees as compared to international standard. They receive Le 3,000, which is far below a dollar, per day," Jabbie noted.
Also other concerns he raised with the Conference have to do with the general medical care, which needs to be improved upon.
"They are seeking more emergency care, particularly in the case of the first accused, Norman who sustained and still complains of pains to his hips. His doctor recommended his hospitalization with a view that it is now very necessary to take urgent actions with regards his situation," he pointed out adding that because of the inadequate availability of telephone lines Norman has to walk quite a long distance to receive a phone call which also worsens his pelvic pains.
He concluded saying counsels intend going into writing but because of the urgent nature of the situations they'd decided to use the opportunity accorded them by the Status Conference to make a preliminary notification.

Original_Search_Title: Guebuza Visits Agriculture Ministry
Headline: Mozambique: Guebuza Visits Agriculture Ministry
Body_Text:
Maputo — Mozambican President Armando Guebuza visited the Agriculture Ministry on Tuesday, where he urged the technicians it employs to use their knowledge to help rid the country of food shortages.
Speaking after Guebuza's meeting with members of the ministry's Consultative Council, Agriculture Minister Tomas Mandlate told reporters "the President came to see with the agricultural technicians what is being done, and to convey the message that it is necessary that they use their knowledge if one is to respond to the needs in the fight against hunger".
Mandlate added that there was nothing extraordinary about the visit because it is within the President's work plan, and the Agriculture Ministry is part of the government.
He stressed that Guebuza's concern is to have the country exploit its potential to the full in order to defeat hunger, and this includes finding ways to help small producers increase their production capacity at low cost.
About 80 per cent of the Mozambican population lives from agriculture, and this activity is essentially developed by the family sector, producing mainly maize, cassava, and beans.

Original_Search_Title: FCTA Procures 12,500 Metric Tons of Fertilizer for Farmers At N650 Million
Headline: Nigeria: FCTA Procures 12,500 Metric Tons of Fertilizer for Farmers At N650 Million
Body_Text:
THE Minister of the Federal Capital Territory, Mallam Nasir El-Rufai disclosed that the Federal Capital Territory Administration, FCTA has completed the procurement of direct supply of 12,500 metric tonnes of fertilizer at the cost of N650 million that would be made available to farmers in the next few weeks.
He said this became a priority for the administration against the backdrop of the fact that there was the need for the farmers to acquire the fertilizer at the most appropriate time.
Speaking at the 3rd Town Hall meeting held in Gwagwalada as part of his sensitisation drive for the celebration of FCT at 30 and to listen to brainstorm with the people on policy direction at the weekend, Mallam El-Rufai, who urged farmers to form themselves into Cooperative societies that will avail them the opportunity of benefiting from the Federal Government's 50billion Agric fund to farmers, said the administration has concluded plans to ensure that 30% of FCT land is developed for agricultural production as cassava, rice and fruit processing, adding that the FCTA would soon introduce mechanised farming aimed at boosting food production and total reduction in the over reliance on oil.
According to him, with the coming on board of 400 buses to ply the routes of the Federal Capital Territory before the end of the year, operators of commercial motor cycles popularly known as Okada would be phased out, just as he said work on Railway system is to begin soon.
"A total of 500 buses are expected before the end of the year and motorcycles are to be phased out beginning mid 2006", he said.
Mallam El-Rufai who noted that the Hospital and Tourism Management Board would be inaugurated soon, said plans have been concluded by the FCTA to convert the Nyanya and Gwarinpa general hospitals to Maternal and Child care Hospitals {MCH}.
He said as part of activities lined up for the celebration of FCT at 30, the FCTA is planning to organise competitions in essay, logo and art to ensure inclusivity, international press briefing, putting together a historic book on FCT at 30, award to deserving residents, cleanest communities, most organised cooperatives, seminar, exhibition as well as international Charity football match between the old and new African players.
Other activities include documentary in English, Chinese, Spanish, Arabic and French, commemorative stamp and religious services.
Earlier in his address of welcome, Chairman, Gwagwalada Area Council, Saidu Garba Zuba who called on the Federal Government to make available fund to the local governments geared towards the execution of more projects, urged the government to decentralize governance by ensuring that some major ministries are situated in some of the Area councils in the satellite areas of the territory if there must be meaningful development in these satellite towns.
"Iam appealing to the higher authority to ensure that development of the FCT should not only be concentrated at the municipal level. This is because if the satellite towns are given adequate infrastructural attention, moving to the municipal area for economic reasons and otherwise will be reduced or even stopped all together", he said.

Original_Search_Title: ThisDay and the Commoditisation of the Press
Headline: Nigeria: ThisDay and the Commoditisation of the Press
Body_Text:
Last Friday, April 28, ThisDay responded to widespread criticisms of its decision to carry a wrap-around advert in support of the Third Term agenda with a full-page self-advert. The advert, addressed to its readers, was a robust defense of its position.
"Since the publication of our wrap-around advertisement on Thursday, April 20, 2006 placed by supporters of tenure elongation for President Obasanjo," ThisDay said, "there has been a lot of reactions from readers and the general public.
"We continue to welcome all views and reactions as we engage our esteemed readers in making ThisDay a better newspaper.
"However, a few local commentators and columnists with limited understanding of current trends in newspaper publishing have continued to serve their readers with half-truths, founded on ignorance and lack of understanding of the issues.
"For columnists who do not know, whose journalistic ideas are passé, wrap-around advertisement is not new to Nigeria and the print media the world over. It is an effective marketing tool used by the best and brightest newspapers and magazine the world over to offer creative solutions to marketing challenges."
