Speaking in an interview with the Ghana News Agency, Mrs Hectorina Yebuah, Deputy Director of Nursing Services, Korle-Bu, said abortion cases were on the increase with most of the cases being incomplete, inevitable and septic, adding, "incomplete abortion cases tops them all".
She said that many women had died out of unsafe abortion while others had had severe long-term complications, which included injury to the genital tract and internal organs, chronic pelvic pain, infertility and psychological trauma.
According to her, 10 women died of unsafe abortion in 2005; seven in 2004; 12 in 2003; nine in 2002; 16 in 2001 and 10 in 2000 at the Hospital.
In Africa, more than 30,000 women die each year of unsafe abortion, four million African women continue to undergo unsafe abortion and millions are getting injured including infertility. Abortion causes about 30 per cent to 40 per cent of maternal mortality and morbidity in Africa.
These women resort to abortion either due to contraceptive failure among the married women; sexual coercion; rape; sexual abuse; out of reach of family planning, social and economic reasons.
Mrs Mercy Offei, Deputy Director of Nursing Services in Charge of Gynaecology Unit, told the GNA that as at March 2006 more than 300 abortion cases had been reported with the ages of patients ranging between 16 years and 35 years.
She explained that with the low patronage of family planning, many women especially the married women found themselves pregnant when they were not prepared "and such women some of whom due to religion, will resort to other illegal methods and end up here at the Hospital.
Mrs Offei explained that most of the abortion cases among the adolescents increased after cultural festivities where merrymaking, indiscriminate and unsafe sex among the youth were at its peak, saying that reported cases started in August.
She said those children, mostly school children, out of school children, house helps and kayayee, upon realising that they were pregnant, sought advice from their peers, who also introduced them to all sorts of concoctions to abort the pregnancy.
"These girls do not tell their parents when they are pregnant for fear of being beaten or thrown out of the house so the only option is to practice what their peers have told them ", she said.
Mrs Offei called for continuous education on the dangers associated with unsafe abortion and the use of contraceptives as a measure to curtail the menace.
"If we are able to accept the use of contraceptives to prevent unwanted pregnancies, then I believe, it will reduce cases of unsafe abortion and there will be no need to legalise abortion."

Original_Search_Title: FRA Sets Maize Price
Headline: Zambia: FRA Sets Maize Price
Body_Text:
THE Food Reserve Agency (FRA) has set K38,000 as this year's floor price for maize and plans to buy 150,000 tonnes of the grain from farmers countrywide.
The FRA has also projected a bumper harvest this year of more than 1.2 million tonnes.
FRA board chairman, Costain Chilala, told journalists at a Press briefing in Lusaka yesterday that the agency had K70 billion to buy 80,000 tonnes of maize but was engaging Government for an extra K70 billion to meet the target of 150,000 tonnes.
The FRA would also buy 2,400 tonnes of cassava from farmers at K25,000 per 50 kg bag, 1,200 tonnes of rice at K40,000 per 50kg bag, 1,200 tonnes of Soya beans at K40,000 per 50kg bag and 1,200 tonnes of groundnuts at K50,000 per 50kg bag.
During the 2006-07 season, the FRA planned not to sell any maize to millers and urged them and other grain users to buy enough stocks to last up to May 2007 crop marketing season as it would hold the purchased crop in the strategic reserve.
He said the FRA floor price was just indicative and farmers were free to negotiate with grain traders as well as millers in the sale of maize.
"We are calling on the private sector, especially millers to go out there and buy the crops grown by farmers this year. This is in line with Government policy to see a private sector-driven agricultural industry," Mr Chilala said.
The FRA would operate in 57 districts, which traditionally were known to have surplus agricultural production, but due to budgetary constraints, crop purchasing would be mostly done at main holding depots in the districts under special instances.
Cooperative unions and the farmer organisations were expected to do their part of crop marketing by mopping crops from outlying areas of designated districts.
In line with Government policy of capacity building, FRA would give priority to district cooperative unions and farmer organisations when appointing warehouse managers.
Mr Chilala said the FRA would continue to out-source from the private sector paying points to framers from commercial banks, maize transportation from transporters as well as fumigations of crops from private operators' fumigators.
The agency would start the crop-marketing season on and about May 25, 2006 when crops were sufficiently dry and the exercise was expected to end by September 30.
Mr Chilala warned that no crop would be accepted at FRA deports after the set date and therefore, appealed to all farmers to sell their commodity between the announced dates.
He appealed to farmers to be involved in the crop marketing to apply themselves diligently and ensure the exercise went on smoothly.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture : $242million Realised From Agric Export in 2005
Headline: Nigeria: Agriculture : $242million Realised From Agric Export in 2005
Body_Text:
About $242 million was realised from the export of agriculture produce in 2005, up from about $234 million realised in 2004. According to the export statistics of the Federal Produce Inspection Service, the revenue showed a significant improvement in the country's export of agriculture produce, and for the first time, 10.35 tonnes of cassava chips was exported and $1.34 million realised.
The report, however, said that the total export of agriculture produce dropped in 2005 by 2 per cent.
The country exported 209,385.989 tonnes of various produce in 2005, a decline of about 4,580.027 tonnes from 2119,66.016 tonnes recorded in 2004.
The report listed some produce exported during the period as cocoa beans, gum arabic, sesame seeds, cashew nuts, palm kernel, cocoa cake, cocoa powder, garri, cotton, among others.
A further breakdown of the list showed that cocoa beans, sesame seeds, cashew nuts and palm kernel cake were top in the demand list during the period under review.
According to the report, 1123411.312 tonnes of cocoa beans were exported in 2004 and the figure dropped to 111,755.038 tonnes in 2005.
Cocoa beans was followed by sesame seeds of 30,949.35 tonnes and cashew nuts which accounted for 30,509.583 tonnes.
The other commodities that attracted significant attention at the international market, according to the report, were palm-kernel cake, ginger, white so chip, cocoa butter, cotton lint, gum arabic and garri.
Reacting to the report, some stakeholders blamed the drop on poor weather, late distribution as well as improper application of chemical inputs.
Speaking to NAN on the development, Mr Manoj Vashista, Business Manager, OLAM Nigeria Ltd, said that cocoa export in 2005 dropped because of inadequate rainfall and inability of farmers to properly apply chemical imputs.
He also blamed the drop on illegal export of cocoa beans by unpatriotic individuals, who he said engaged in such act to dodge payment of the mandatory 1 per cent pre-shipment fees and NXP fees totalling $20 per tonne.
He pointed out that such people often exported poor quality cocoa thus, weakening the international market for genuine exporters.
"some cocoa companies in China has banned cocoa from Nigeria due to the export of high tonnes of poor quality cocoa beans to that country," he said, adding that the ban of our cocoa by China had further narrowed the market for the product as exporters now export to only Europe.
He urged the inspection and pre-shipment agencies to intensify efforts on quality control by ensuring that export products were processed and packaged to meet international standard.
The OLAM boss also emphasised the need to expand the scope of production in Nigeria, pointing out that the bulk of cocoa farms in the country were inherited farms that had been neglected over time, thus affecting the quality of cocoa as most of the trees were old and only produce poor yield.He urged cocoa growers to invest in modern farms and processing factories to give a leap to the annual cocoa production.
Vastisha said that the OLAM recently acquired a cocoa procesing factory in Akure with an initial annual capacity of 3,000 tonnes, but which had now been upgraded to process about 15,000 tonnes.
In addition, he said that the company was also working in partnership with Starmark Cocoa Factory, a subsidiary of Cadbury Plc, to process cocoa beans into liquor, cake, butter and powder.
According to him, the company has introduced Nucleus Estate Initiative (NEI) with 300 farms as part of efforts to boost cocoa production.
To encourage its farmers, the Olam boss said that the company had packaged several incentives which ranged from provision of finance, hybrid seedlings, insecticides, fertilizer as well as training on various stages of cocoa production, including cultivation, harvest and processing.
To get the right pricing for Nigeria's cocoa, he urged the government to encourage local consumption of cocoa products and create the enabling environment for investors.

Original_Search_Title: Introducing Cassava Production - Using Local Variety, 'Katack'
Headline: Gambia: Introducing Cassava Production - Using Local Variety, 'Katack'
Body_Text:
'Success only comes after hard work and if success comes, people attribute it to miracles. After attending a series of trainings on cassava and sweet potato production, rapid cassava multiplication and Garri making with experts from NARI Horticultural Unit,
I took up the challenge to continue producing cassava throughout the year until I am able to produce the locally known cassava variety Katack', said Famara Badjie, a senior Agriculture extension worker. He made this statement recently in an interview with Agriculture on the importance of cassava production.
According to him, people wonder whether it is a new improved variety or local production, noting that the performance of any plant depends on two factors - genetic and environment.
He said in Western Division, between 80 - 100 percent of the farmers grow cassava, mainly for commercial purpose, while in Lower River Division and North Bank Division it is mainly grown as a source of income and eaten as a supplementary diet.
He said different dishes are prepared from cassava including porridge, fufu, Nyambo (cooked in stew or boiled), noting that it can also be processed into cassava chips, flour to make pancake, Gumeh and above all, Garri which can be used as a substitute to rice.

Original_Search_Title: SMEs: Why Political Will is Needed
Headline: Nigeria: SMEs: Why Political Will is Needed
Body_Text:
Lagos — DUE to its position as the leader of the Black Race as well as the most populous nation in Sub-Sahara Africa, Nigeria has a vital role to play in the overall world economy. Despite its boundless and limitless potentials, Nigeria's economy has often been described as a mono-economy due to the over dependence on oil which provides for over 90 per cent of the Gross Domestic Product, GDP. However, economic experts believe that there are other sectors yet to be fully tapped that could boost the nation's economy.
For instance, there appears to be a general consensus that Small and Medium Enterprises, SMEs, are catalysts or the engines of growth that drive development process in any country. For a long time, SMEs have emerged as the bedrock of many buoyant and developed economies. Like a planted seed, the SMEs need the right soil and environment to germinate, grow and blossom.
Development economists generally agree that Small and Medium Scale industries constitute vital valve in the economic growth of every nation. The outstanding evidence of such industries in Asian countries has resulted in what is popularly known as Asian Tigers. Indeed, the astounding performance of these businesses lent credence to the consensus by economists that SMEs play pivotal role as development tools. Interestingly, researches conducted in the last 20 years in countries such as Malaysia, Singapore, India, Taiwan, China and Hong Kong have shown that the governments of these countries have piloted the growth, sustainability and prosperity of SMEs due to the tested proof that these small-scale industries remain the engine room for the growth of the economies mentioned above.
There is no doubt that the Obasanjo administration as soon as it came on stream in 1999, realizing the importance of SMEs, went ahead to put in place the necessary political statement and policies towards the growth of this segment of business. Before now, the absence of a government policy on SMEs as well as difficulty by operators to access funds have combined to slow down the country's industrialization process. However, between February 23-25, 2003, former Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) Governor, Mr. Joseph Sanusi led a delegation of bank chiefs to the Central Bank of Indonesia in Jarkata, the country's capital. Experience garnered from the tour showed that the Central Bank of Indonesia adopted a practical and effective mechanism to stimulate and foster the growth and development of virile SMEs in that country. An important lesson learnt during the tour was that the SME subsector in Indonesia had been successful largely due to a deliberate and sustained government policy s upport and a functional infrastructure.
After the Indonesia trip, the CBN in collaboration with the Bankers Committee, agreed that commercial banks set aside 10 per cent of their gross-profit for the development of SMEs. Under the scheme, funds are to be made available for onward lending to entrepreneurs wishing to set up such industries. This was the basis for the setting up of Small and Medium Scale Industries Equity Investment Scheme (SMIEIS). The SMIEIS funds as a matter of urgency is expected to make funds available to the end-users, the local investors and their like. Regrettably, this has not been so due to bottlenecks inherent in the system, leaving the operators of SMEs still lurched in the dock as to how to raise needed funds.
According to (CBN) sources, the SMIEIS fund would have hit N40 billion as at the end of 2005 while total disbursement by the end of June 2005 stood at N10.3 billion, representing a paltry sum due to lack of credible information on potential investment opportunities in the sector. Actually, SMEs operators have been complaining for a long time over difficulties in accessing funds with the authorities paying a deaf ear to them. I however believe that the present administration has laid the structures aimed at helping the development of SMEs by setting up the Small and Medium Enterprises Development Agency of Nigeria, SMEDAN, Act of 2003. The SMEDAN is headed by Mrs. Modupe Adelaja, ex-Solid Minerals Development minister during the first tenure of the Obasanjo administration. But the attitude of banks to SMEs does not augur well for economic growth, because, if both the government and banks starve new and small companies of funds, then they will remain incapacitated, while the ec onomy will remain stagnant. Under the SMIEIS scheme, the contributory banks are obliged to invest up to 40 per cent for a minimum of three years while the operators would take care of 60 per cent, either in cash or kind - a kind of mutual and beneficial partnership.
Apart from Bankers Committee fund for SMEs, other things which the government put in place to engender the growth and sustainability of the SMEs included, among others, the reduction of interest rates paid by all categories of businesses, capitalization of the banking sector for improved banking as well as government's decision to ban a number of items that can be produced locally in Nigeria in order to encourage the growth of SMEs. In short, many operators still believe that what the government has so far put in place are nothing but mere palliatives which have not actually addressed the teething and real problems of the SMEs. In other economies around the world especially the developing ones, enterprises within the bracket of the SMEs as in Nigeria are given expertise advice, funded and supervised by appropriate professional bodies and experts in most cases appointed by government for such purposes. That is why I am convinced that to move the SMEs sub-sector forward, bodies like the Institute of Directors (IoD) must be contracted by the authorities to assist the SME operators in the area of corporate governance because some of the operators are into good business but lack good management skills to keep good documentation and proper accounting and records.
There is another thing that the government did which should be commended here. The establishment of Baseline Economic Survey of SMIs in Nigeria which is aimed at providing a comprehensive and widely disseminated data and information on Small and Medium industries in the countries.
So, the talk about the SMEs not being properly documented, not having the right staff, their unwillingness to share equity of their business with other investors, and so on is neither here not there. Operators believe that government has so far failed to empower and carry along the SMEs, a sector so cardinal as engine of growth and propeller of economic progress, mere talk and enunciation of policies will keep the SMEs where they have always been.
For instance, the bureaucratic bottlenecks associated with the process of obtaining the necessary wherewithal for this sector to prosper should be properly reviewed if not dismantled. This would enhance the operators' chances for proper funding, professional/expert advice and other necessary enablement for the growth and well-being of this very important sector.
Consolidation of the banking sector in the area of equity base in one step, interests reduction is yet another factor to be considered in order to grow the economy. There is no gainsaying that SMEs if properly catered for could keep the largest employable adults on their payroll. Courtesy of the National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategies (NEEDS), the government hoped to create 7,000 jobs by the year 2007. The SMEs remain a veritable instrument or vehicle towards the realization of this objective.
As a matter of fact, a group known as "Made in Nigeria products promotion," has often expressed willingness to liaise with the federal government towards the revival of SMEs, which it believes to be the only true and enduring sector for the revival of the ailing Nigeria economy.
SMEs play key roles in most developed economies like South Korea and Japan where 70 per cent of exports is traceable to small scale industries. Now, in terms of the transition from industrial age to the post-industrial information technology era, these Asiatic countries and others the world over look onto the SMEs for the way forward, and they have never been disappointed.
In Nigeria, the SMEs provide and can supply products and services both for local consumption and exports. The much talked about cassava exportation could only be realized if properly attention is given to SMEs. The much desired diversification of the national economy can also be made possible if and when the government gives the appropriate political will and auspices to the SMEs.
Investigations have proved over time that most multinationals do not work for the overall growth of the national economies of their host countries because their primary interest is to protect the interest of their home countries. SMEs guarantee promotion and protection of national interests although a strong political will is needed for them to perform the duties and obligations expected of them. Operators under the auspices of Nigerian Association of Small and Medium Enterprises (NASME) are unhappy that till date, government is yet to come up with the required political will to enforce the process and funding of these burgeoning businesses.
Recently, the Standards Organization of Nigeria (SON) indicated its preparedness to collaborate with SMEDAN in order to promote SMEs by facilitating easy access to factors of growth, such as information and training in order to reduce the cost of doing business for this business sector.
So far, what obtains is cosmetic plastering and postures which have not really propelled positive action towards putting the SMEs on the front burner of national economic revitalization. But for how long can this lip-service continue?

Original_Search_Title: An Unfulfilling Finale for Hostage Politics
Headline: Nigeria: An Unfulfilling Finale for Hostage Politics
Body_Text:
When the history of Nigeria's current political arrangement comes to be written, at the heart of it will be the way that President Obasanjo has perfected hostage politics. I had defined this phenomenon of hostage politics as the ultimate employment of blackmail to achieve the objectives of the President. It is a very simple issue really. The security apparatus keeps tabs of the illegalities that individuals have committed and these are in turn used by the Obasanjo clique to exert compliance, submission or surrender as the case might be.
It is usual in the cloak-and-dagger world of politics for political actors to wield the twin instruments of carrot and the stick; but in the Nigerian setting of the past seven years, 'hostage-taking' has become a preferred instrument of policy. It seemed to have achieved remarkable results for President Obasanjo, since the fear of humiliation through a manipulative use of security reports and agencies such as the EFCC have been at the base of the silence and acquiescence of many political actors in the illegalities that the Obasanjo clique is attempting to foist on our country with third term. Of course, I am not underrating the place of greed in the explosive mix.
What has become obvious is that we might have underrated the intelligence of many a political actor in Nigeria. A lot of those masquerading as members of our political elite are weaklings without principles; very greedy and crudely ambitious, yet unable to even fathom the essence of power and politicking beyond the mundane points of theft, banditry and an insolent self-aggrandisement. Obasanjo, the past master of stealth, cunning and ruthlessness, understands this much, and that explains why he can use his tactics of 'hostage politics' as devastatingly as we have seen over the past few years. But it didn't have to be so, and as I will argue in this week's column, it will come to an unfulfilling finale with the desperate agenda to achieve a third term.
Let's even look at it from the beginning. If the members of the political elite had been smart, with some sense of history, it should have dawned on them that President Obasanjo is the least qualified person to take people hostage, using stealth and blackmail. His own records in governance are especially controversial, steeped as he has always been with his "buccaneering approach to government business," in the words of Chief Allison Ayida.
I have been re-reading Chief Ayida's April 1, 1990 piece in the GUARDIAN newspaper, titled AN OPEN CONFIDENTIAL MEMO TO GENERAL OBASANJO. It was a marvellous and well-written deconstruction of the man Obasanjo, with his self-centred, and ruthless streak. Obasanjo, the "hostage-taker" of today's politics, was actually very lucky, thanks to the intervention of people like Chief Sunday Awoniyi as we now know to have survived the corruption investigations of the Murtala Muhammed regime. Chief Ayida's piece reminded of investigations authorised by the head of state into "the list of shares and assets acquired particularly under the indigenisation exercise" of the period, with Obasanjo's name featuring "prominently as shareholder in several companies". This almost earned Obasanjo a sack, because the Murtala regime "was committed to righting all the 'wrongs' of the past and punishing the 'lapses' of the present."
It was the intervention of others that saved Obasanjo. The white paper on the Adeosun Indigenisation Panel report, "there were no-go areas on some of the panel's recommendations which on (Obasanjo's) specific instructions included 'where a Nigerian had acquired the bulk of the shares in any enterprise outside Schedule I; the shares should be 'confiscated' and forfeited to the Federal Government and the names of the 'money-bags' publicised'. "Apparently," according to Ayida, Obasanjo "has not studied Volume III of the report where names of such shareholders were listed. When I draw your attention to the pages where your name (Obasanjo's name) appeared, you readily agreed that the panel's recommendation should be rejected. And you then directed that I should take another week to redraft the white paper. When eventually the council memorandum was circulated, we agreed that Volume III should not be circulated or published but that you should mention in council that any member interested in the particulars of those who "cornered" the indigenisation shares should see the SFMG. Several members contacted me for the list but no member saw the list of names! The permanent secreatry and I received a 'big thank you' from you at the end of the exercise."
This extensive quotation from Allison Ayida, in my view, goes to the heart of the Obasanjo phenomenon: A self-serving, hypocritical persona; which manifests in several ways even today. The man closed Nigeria's border with the Republic of Benin, because armed robbers attacked his daughter; he would direct policies to promote cassava or the poultry business, because he is a big-time investor in those areas. This propensity to exploit situations to fester personal advantage, so characteristic of the Obasanjo style, actually damages the moral ground he normally tries to occupy in his anti-corruption drives. This is why I say that leading elements of the political elite that he has intimidated must be so thoroughly rotten that Obasanjo resembles the proverbial one-eyed person in the land of the blind. The obverse of this is that we have overrated Nigeria's political elite.
But Obasanjo is so excessively self-assured, or so it seems that he would pull through any wish or desire no matter how immoral, no matter the manipulative process which leads to the achievement of such an intention, no matter how offended the Nigerian people are with the entire act. What matters is the end which Obasanjo has set out for himself. It is this total disrespect for the Nigerian people that has led our county to the crisis associated with the third term agenda. This crisis has now arrived at a finale, with the decision last week by the PDP leadership, under the 'Garrison Commander,' Colonel Amadu Ali, to publicly declare that the third term agenda is now officially the party's position. The gloves are off, the teeth are gritted and a war has been declared against the Nigerian people.
The battle will now be fought out in the open arena of the National Assembly. Knowing how crucial the days ahead are, the PDP apparatchik has taken a decision to move into the gallery of the Assembly as an added step of intimidation to beat the legislators into line. The carrot and the stick remain very viable options. The offers of money, choice plots in Abuja and automatic return to the National Assembly are still very much on the table. The AD leader in the House, Honourable Wunmi Bewaji, also reported that they have been offered one million dollars each and he was even promised an oil bloc to betray the Nigerian people while kowtowing to Obasanjo's agenda.
But just in case some of the legislators would play smart by collecting money from the Obasanjo clique and yet go to vote against the third term agenda, there is danger lurking ahead. In an interview published by the Guardian of April 15, 2006, Dr Usman Bugaje of the House of Representatives said that they "are getting reports that a member of the Board of Trustees of the PDP, who you all know have (sic), been warning those who collected their money to vote for the agenda or they will kill them. He simply told them that if you collect our money and you don't vote, we are going to kill you. And you know they can kill, they have killed many people."
This quotation underlines the desperation that is the hallmark of the third term agenda. Obasanjo has become totally entrapped in his delusions and does not seem to have a way

Original_Search_Title: Govt to Create 20,000 Jobs for Niger Delta Youths
Headline: Nigeria: N/Delta: FG to Create 20,000 Jobs for Youths
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo, yesterday announced immediate creation of about 20,000 new jobs that would absorb indigenes of the Niger Delta as one of the short - term measures to urgently address social and economic problems in the restive oil- rich coastal states.
He also stated that he would personally flag off the N230 billion - abandoned East - West road, that cuts across major states in the region, next month and unfolded other proactive steps spanning nine major areas like employment generation, transportation, education, health, telecommunications, environment, agriculture, power and water resources.
Speaking at the inauguration of the Consolidated Council on Social and Economic Development of Coastal States of the Niger Delta, at the Banquet Hall, State House, Abuja, President Obasanjo explained that medium term measures put in place would be implemented within a two- year time frame, medium term within two and five years while the long term measures would take five years and beyond to be executed.
Under the job creation initiative, the President said the three services of the Armed Forces had been mandated to recruit 1,000 indigenes of the area, beginning from next month, with the Nigerian Army absorbing 500 able- bodied men and women from the Niger Delta into the service.
Of the figure, Bayelsa, Rivers and Delta States are to be given 100 slots each, Ondo, 50 while Akwa Ibom, Cross River and Edo States are to get 25 slots each besides another 50 recruits categorized as "tactical reserve".
Both the Nigerian Air Force and Nigerian Navy are to recruit 250 persons each with Bayelsa, Rivers and Delta States contributing 50 slots each, Ondo and Akwa Ibom States 30 while Cross Rivers and Edo States each will enjoy 20 slots. The allocation formula applies to the two services.
President Obasanjo, who announced immediate lifting of embargo on police recruitment, directed that 10,000 new recruits be engaged with 1,500 positions each allotted to Bayelsa, Rivers and Delta States while Ondo, Akwa Ibom, Cross River and Abia States each has 1,000 slots.
The Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC), he added, would create 1, 000 new positions to be filled by qualified applicants from seven of the nine coaster states, besides the recruitment of 7,300 National Certificate of Education (NCE) and university graduate teachers before the end of the year.
With estimated 20, 000 new openings in the federal public service, President Obasanjo further stated that government would soon commence, dredging of the River Niger as part of measures to facilitate easy transportation of people and goods in the area, even as he revealed that the Niger Delta Development Commission (NNDC) and state governments in the area were to collaborate on the opening of creeks and water channels to create more routes for inland water transportation.
He also said that government was planning to mechanically clear seaweeds in addition to a regional programme that would lead to cooperation with neighbouring countries that the nation share water borders with.
On education, he said the Petroleum Training Institute (PTI), Effurun, Warri would be upgraded to a degree- awarding institution with officers already appointed to actualise the plan, beside the setting aside of a temporary site for the take- off of a federal polytechnic in Bayelsa State by September. The Education Tax Fund (ETF), he stressed, has also been directed to assess what needed to be done at a community school in Okerenku, Delta State.
In the area of health, President Obasanjo said Rivers State government had agreed to complete the abandoned Auto Destruct Syringes factory to create more jobs and boost healthcare delivery, besides plan to establish primary healthcare centre in each of the local governments in the area.
He also said that government was going to meet with the Global System of Mobile (GSM) telecommunications providers to extend coverage to major towns and communities just as he announced the empowerment of the National Oil Spill Detection and Response Agency to eliminate water pollution.
The President also said the refineries in Warri, Port Harcourt and Eleme would be cleaned while noting that 59 communities within five to six radius in the on-going seven power plants would be electrified under rural electrification programme that would benefit 396 communities.
Water provision, the President explained , would be carried out in 120 communities in Cross River, 142 in Delta, 190 in Rivers , 113 in Edo and 138 in Ondo States even as he said that an officer would be appointed in the office of the Secretary to the Government of the Federation to coordinate the various intervention programmes by all the tires as well as oil companies and development partners.
It would be recalled that the council was appointed two weeks ago to find immediate, medium and long term solutions to the persistent uprising of youths in the area, most of which is blamed on lack of development in the region.
Under the programme, the Federal Government, state and local governments, oil companies, Niger Delta Development Commission and other development institutions are to outline specific development projects to accelerate transformation of the area.
In his presentation, Delta State governor, Chief James Ibori commended the Federal Government for the east-west link road while stating that his administration was complementing the effort .
The bridge, he noted has given the NDDC the opportunity to construct more roads in Delta State, stressing that at the end of the bridge, the state would build a road going to Opkokuru, the Forcados and others .
On cassava and rubber , he said they would be revived as the main stay of the economy of the state, but President Obasanjo disagreed with him in some areas, advising that the state 'take your own, do your own and let me take my own. Let me have in writing so that we would follow up on the implementation.'
On his part, Gov. Olusegun Agagu of Ondo State said areas of focus include education, health, roads, dredging and sand filling, water supply, electrification, jetties, empowerment programmes.
He said the programmes were of short, medium implementable between 2005 to 2006, medium term,2007 to 2009 and then the long term which would go beyond 2009.
The governor pleaded with the president to remain hard on the issue of coordination of the development projects, and Obasanjo promptly assured that this would be done.
Minister of Transport, Dr. Abiye Sekibo revealed that there were 57 communities between Lokoja and Escravous bifurcation that would benefit from the dredging of the River Niger, saying the president had already directed that every community should have shore protection and jetties.
Rivers State Governor Peter Odili commended the president for his 'great show of commitment and political will. We have gone through the records. No Nigerian leader has shown greater commitment to the issue of the Niger Delta than you. The issue of petrol stations in the Niger Delta. President Obasanjo added that 12 mega stations were to be built in the Niger Delta, while Dr. Odili urged him to put the stations in the Immediate plan.
Minister of State for Petroleum , Dr. Edmond Daukoru on his part stated that NNPC and its joint venture partners would commit N12 billion as part of short term programme out of which N8.4 billion will be spent on building new float stations.
Other projects include 6,000 student scholarships, training of 20,000 welders to be undertaken by NNPC and PTDF, production of bio eternal, while NNPC has targeted 5,000 hectares of land for cultivation in addition to health projects.
Also on the card are NNPC to sink three water bole holes in each of the coastal states and procurement of mosquitoe treated nets.
Gov Lucky Igbenidion of Edo State said, "we have passed the list of what we would do to the SGF based on short term, medium and long terms. Many schools have been built, we have done rural electrification. Edo is the poorest of the Niger Delta. He said Edo State government has set up a cassava processing plant at Uromi.
His Bayelsa State counterpart, Dr.Goodluck Joanthan explained that safe water was very important to the area as well as employment.
Group Managing Director of NNPC, Mr. Funso Kupolokun revealed that ' We have agreed to upgrade our budget for community development by 30 per cent and all the companies are involved. We totally in the short and medium be spending N20 trillion , involving all oil companies. 2008 to 2010 totaling N11 trillion
Shell managing director, Mr Basil Ominyi, listed company commitment to community content to bring as much services as we could to the local environment, agro allied project, cassava programme create jobs. We also have a $14 million project with UNDP to communities.
The next meeting of council will hold on 18th of July.

Original_Search_Title: Ginger Now Among Wonder Crops
Headline: Nigeria: Ginger Now Among Wonder Crops
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ginger was recently promoted to the rank of wonder crops like cassava when Commerce Minister, Ambassador Idris Waziri, launched the National Ginger Association of Nigeria in Abuja. According to the minister, 40,000 metric tons of wet ginger is produced a year in Nigeria.
Ginger is chitta in Hausa, oso-ala or oso-chukwu in Igbo and ata'le in Yoruba. With Kaduna in the lead, 15 ginger producing states have been identified in Nigeria. Others include Nassarawa, Plateau, Sokoto, Zamfara, Kebbi, Kano, Kogi, Kano, Bornu, Akwa Ibom, Ondo, Rivers Niger, Kwara, Oyo, Abia and Lagos.
The minister said that the emphasis being placed on agriculture by the Obasanjo administration is yielding positive results. He added that Nigerian ginger sells $1,400 per ton in the international market as against $700 five to six years ago.
The president of the new association, Major General Yunana Nom (rtd), noted that Nigerian ginger is rated among the best in international market because of its aroma and high oil content and was as such in high demand. But he added that it was being under-priced because it was being exported in its raw form and it was poorly handled by farmers. As a result, a tonne of Asian ginger which is better processed sells twice higher than Nigerian ginger
"Our ginger falls below international standards, hence the low price," Nom said. "This is a big challenge to all stakeholders in the ginger industry. Our aim as an association is to address these inadequacies."
According to the minister, ginger is not known to have any side effects as food or medicine. He cited the United States Food and Drug Administration, saying it is among the safest herbs. Both Waziri and Nom stressed the need for competitiveness while Nom called for legislation that would help bring sanity to the business.
A committee had been set up on standard, using European Union and American standards as reference points. It comprised the Federal Ministry of Agriculture, Standard Organisation of Nigeria, NAFDAC, Consumer Protection Council, Nigerian Export Promotion Council.
Nom also said that his group needs a grant of N50 million to stockpile the commodity during harvest season for export. He added that the association requires official support to source for market abroad and sponsor members to international trade fairs.
Members who wish to set up small and medium processing plants or procure farm inputs need less cumbersome loans facilities from micro-finance institutions than what are currently available, Nom added.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Plans N80m Cassava Plants
Headline: Nigeria: Govt Plans N80m Cassava Plants
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Kwara State Commissioner of Agriculture and Natural Resources,
Alhaji Saka Onimago, has said the state government will soon acquire
cassava processing plants worth N80million .
The processing plants, according to Onimago, will be located in Offa, Ilorin and Kaiama.
The Commissioner who disclosed this while receiving the Managing
Director, Dangote Flour Mills, Mr Mike Zetzchue in his office, also
said the processing plants will be supplying cassava flour to Dangote
Flour Mills as from June this year.
He said micro-cassava processing centres had also been constructed
in all the 16 local government areas of the state through the
International Institute of Tropical Agriculture, Ibadan, to meet the
need of the industry.
In his address, Special Assistant to the Governor on Youth
Empowerment, Barrister Jimba Muyideen, said the project was in line
with the Federal Government's directive on including 10 per cent of
cassava in the nation's flour production.
He said the processing plants would be leased to competent hands under the state government's public/private sector initiative.
Responding, Zetzchue said the cassava plants would be acquired by
the Dangote Flour Mill and commended the state government initiative on
the project , adding that the plant would provide employment
opportunity for youths.

Original_Search_Title: Pig Farming: a Lucrative Business for Small And Medium Scale Investors
Headline: Nigeria: Pig Farming: a Lucrative Business for Small And Medium Scale Investors
Body_Text:
Pig farming is a business that can catapult an investor from being an employee to an employer of labour with high return on investment. Okey Ndiribe spoke to Abbey Orimole who has a rich experience in this line of business that is definitely not for the lazy.
MR. Abbey Orimole, is an automobile electrician who switched over to pig farming. He invested in pig rearing as a side business in 1986.
Said he: "I started by rearing about 15 pigs that year within my premises at Okearo, near Agbado Station, Ogun State. Before then I had erected some buildings for the purpose. I used to come over to look at the animals from Gbagada-Lagos, where I used to live."
When the National Directorate of Employment ( NDE) established by the military regime of Gen. Ibrahim Babangida started a pig farm in 1992 at nearby Adiyan ,also in Ogun state, Orimole set up a farm there with seven others.
Although he admitted that pigs are dirty animals judging from their way of life, he insisted that this had nothing to do with the quality of meat obtainable from them.
" What about the fish we have been told is very good for our health?" he asked .
"Never mind that pigs can eat their own dung and catarrh when they are hungry. They only do this if you delay their feeding"
Orimole told our reporter that pigs usually know when their food was being served as the entire pigsty would erupt in cries of joy. It was learnt that under such circumstances, a new pig would surely be attacked if it is brought to join the others inside the pen. But after sometime, the older pigs would accept the new comer and they would become friends. But that would only be after the food has finished.
The pig farmer said that a beginner in the business must first of all acquire a piece of land. The next stage would be to erect a pen where the pigs would be kept. A small or modest beginner should endeavour to erect at least four rooms measuring 12 square feet each. He should acquire at least six or seven pigs.
Orimole said the next most important item required is water.
Without water pig rearing said Orimole would be very difficult.
" It would be better for a pig farmer to have his own bore-hole if he can afford it. This is because if a beginner starts with buying water, he is likely ro run into problems if he doen't have enough cash at any point in time".
He said it is important to start thinking ahead right from the beginnig if he wants to stay afloat in the business " because in business you have the good times and the bad times".
For now, the cost of sinking a borehole around the Adiyan area stands at between N180,000 and N240,000.
Onimole gave our reporter of what his schedule looked like from Monday to Saturday.
He told our reporter that he usually arrived his farm as early as 7 a.m. in the morning.
Said he: " When I arrive at the farm, the first thing I do is to sweep the pen and clear it of the pigs' waste."
The next chore would be to wash the floor and the animals themselves. He uses diesel and water to wash the pigs. He then feeds them with palm kernel cake called "PKC" in pig rearers parlance. But some times he varies the food he serves his animals. These other varieties include waste from spent grain, cassava tubers and corn mixed with blood meal. The waste from spent grain is normally obtained from the Guinness Nig. Ltd, Nigerian Breweries Ltd. and Cadbury PLC.
Orimole told our reporter that after the pigs have been served their breakfast, his work doesn't end there. He has to go round and observe whether they were eating or not. If any of them isn't eating, it means something is wrong with it.
" You should go closer and investigate why it is not eating. If it is sick, then you should give it some medication" he said. The animals need to be served water along with their meal.
When the pigs have finished eating their breakfast, then Orimole begins his second round of cleaning. He removes their dung once again. After the second round of cleaning, he serves the pigs lunch. This he usually does around 2 p.m. The animals would normally have a nap after their second meal.
But if any animal doesn't sleep, Orimole would move closer to examine it and find out what is wrong with it. Based on the symptoms observed, the animal would be treated with the appropriate drugs. Among the drugs commonly administered on them, several are similar to those used to treat human beings. They include B complex, tetracycline and thalazole. Others include levasol, ferro 100 and tridox. The animal could be treated with B complex, if it is observed that it has lost its appetite. Female pigs ( sows) that have just delivered are normally treated with Octosin to facilitate the flow of their breast milk. If the animal is stooling, it would be treated with an antibiotic like tetracycline. Thalazol and tridox could also be used to treat stomach disorders.
After eight months, pigs can mate. Throwing more light on the pig's reproductive process, Orimole said " When the sow conceives, its pregnancy lasts for about four months."
He continued: "After the sow has farrowed ( delivered) it would nurse the piglets for six weeks. During this period the young pigs would feed solely on breast milk".
He continued: "After the pig has been weaned, all things being equal it advances to the next stage of its physical growth. From about the 10th week, the young pig is now called a grower. At this stage its weight is expected to reach about 30 kilogrammes."
According to him: "From the grower stage, the animal progresses to the stage called "about-to-cross" in pig farming parlance. This simply means that at this stage the animal is ripe for mating. Henceforth, a male counterpart could be introduced to mate with the female "about- to- cross". After mating, they would reproduce and give births to piglets again.".
Pigs are known to be highly fertile. A single sow could farrow (deliver) as much as much as 10 piglets and it could reproduce twice a year. And it is basically because of its high reproductive nature that new comers in pig rearing are usually advised to set aside a reasonable amount of capital before venturing into the business. Orimole believes that a minimum of N800,000 would be needed for a new comer in the business to take off smoothly.
Another advantage in pig farming is that the animals can feed from a wide variety of items.
Said Orimole: " So far we have relied mainly on waste products from Cadbury, Guinness and Nigerian Breweries. It is the waste from the spent grain they have used for their own production".
According to him, a tipper load of the waste product currently sells for about N8,000. But the pig farmer consider this price to be on the high side.
Orimole says that pig farming is capital intensive, adding that pig farmers need loans from financial institutions in the country to enable them maximise their potentials.
He said the society was currently involved in providing pipe-borne water from their next door neighbour-Adiyan Water Works. But the farm doesn't get regular water supply thereby leaving individual farmers to rely more on their individual wells.

Original_Search_Title: Waiting for the Rains
Headline: South Africa: Waiting for the Rains
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — The best way to uncover the essence of Malawi, the warm heart of Africa, is to travel like you belong there.
'Wow, very fast! Can I jump on your bike?' I shout to them jokingly and they burst into peals of laughter.
Most visitors head straight for the lake, but their adventure is incomplete without experiencing the local transport. It's a hot humid day and my T-shirt is drenched in sweat by the time I locate the home of the local witch doctor. John William Kumpolota was born here in Kiuyu Village on Likoma Island and has been practising as a traditional healer in Malawi for 33 years.
"My father is famous throughout the country and sick people from as far away as Tanzania and Mozambique travel here to be treated by him," his daughter Chimwemwe tells me solemnly. "But before anyone meets him, there is a law given by the spirits that they must take a cassava stick in their left hand and carry it across to a tree on the other side of the village."
After appeasing the spirits, I return to the house where Kumpolota emerges and begins to tell me of his magical healing powers.
"I use the roots of mango trees, cassava leaves, ivory, animal fat and many prayers to cure sickness like paralysis, madness, stomach complaints, skin problems and asthma. I can also help women who struggle to have babies. It's a special job and is good to help make people better."
"What about Aids?" I ask.
"No, that is not possible for me to cure yet," he says frowning.
Kumpolota slips on a long red robe and matching headdress, picks up a black Bible and horse-hair whip and beckons for me to follow. I take off my shoes and we enter a darkened compound where about 50 patients are kneeling on the dusty floor. Sitting on an altar, Kumpolota starts chanting and the sick answer him as one.
He approaches one dazed-looking man, a mental patient from Tanzania, places the Bible on the ill man's head and mutters a prayer. Kumpolota flails his arms in elevated circles and the group begins clapping. Then smiling broadly, the patients burst into animated song, their voices rising quickly to a crescendo.
It's getting late and I hike back along the west coast of the island. The sunny day has given way to a sky filled with thick purple clouds and a few drops of rain begin to splatter down. I walk alone through small villages, content to wander slowly, watching chickens scrabble in the dust as I wave to children who laugh and shout, "Hello, what is your name?" A smile breaks across my face. This is Africa, my continent, my home.
It's been 20 years since I was last in Malawi and it's good to be back in the warm heart of Africa.
I have flown from Lilongwe, the capital, to spend a few days in luxury at a lodge on Likoma Island, half way up Lake Malawi. The lodge is magnificent but the the legendary backpackers' paradise at Cape Maclear, on the southern shore of the lake beckons, so I head south again.
Once at the Cape, a blissful time follows as I spend my time kayaking between islands and snorkelling in the clear waters.
My favourite time of the day is sunrise when the beaches along Chembe village are crowded with women and children washing pots and pans and filling big buckets of water.
The fishermen have arrived back from their evening's work and heaps of small silver fish are laid on straw racks to dry in the sun. Others are placed on circular metal trays to smoke over burning coals.
But it's enough of the lazy days and the next morning after a quick breakfast of mango pancakes, I'm on the road again, heading south to Liwonde National Park. Even though it's only 6.30am, Cape Maclear is already buzzing. I jump onto a matola, an open-air pick-up truck, heading for Monkey Bay.
As is customary in rural Africa, local transport never leaves until the vehicle is bursting at the seams. "Monkey Bay, Monkey Bay!" shouts the conductor as the driver hoots continuously, cruising around for passengers.
Finally we set off at top speed with 16 of us holding on precariously. I'm sitting on top of a jerry can and bouncing along the bumpy road, it's not the most comfortable of rides. My feet are jammed among a tangle of legs and a man's hand holding a pair of hair clippers is just inches from my nose.
At Monkey Bay I climb straight on to another matola going to the junction town of Mangochi. Twenty-two people are squashed in like sardines, surrounded by big bags of clothing, sacks of maize, a rusted motorboat engine and crates of empty beer bottles.
Most tourists would view this as a hardship they'd do anything to avoid. But for me, my most memorable travel experiences have been journeys like this in Africa. And this joyride is no exception.
I'm perched awkwardly on a lumpy bag as we drive through the dry countryside where maize fields have been tilled in preparation for the hoped-for rains. "Our main cash crops are tobacco, tea and coffee," says an old man squashed in next to me. "But for food we rely on maize. Our country has a terrible drought now and people are starving. Many Malawians depend on their farming so if the rains do not come soon, the villagers will suffer."
We continue past small settlements of thatched huts and broken brick homes. Roadside stalls are selling straw baskets, sacks of charcoal, cabbage, mangos and tomatoes. Street vendors fry chips on a coal fire and hanging nearby are body parts of freshly slaughtered cattle dripping blood onto the dry brown earth.
Even though the matola is packed, we stop to pick up four more passengers. There are now 26 of us squeezed on board. My seat slips and I'm squatting inelegantly over a gap between two sacks of maize. I climb on to a huge bag of clothing and now I've got relative luxury for the rest of the ride.
An hour after Mangochi, I'm dropped off at the tiny town of Ulongwe from where I catch a bicycle taxi for 14km to the gates of Liwonde National Park. It's a hot day so I slap on sun cream before climbing on the rickety saddle behind Byora Thembu who plunks my backpack over the handlebars.
Thembu pedals hard in the midday heat, past parched fields where women pump water from a borehole for their daily consumption. It's a hard life here. There has been no rain and maize is short.
We stop at a ramshackle shop for a mango and cold Coke. Only problem is there are no fridges in this part of the world so for liquid intake I have to suffice with the sweet juices of the yellow fruit.
We trundle along in an African time warp, through dusty villages that have not seen change in decades. Two women speed past us on their bicycles as Thembu strains under our heavy load. "Wow, very fast! Can I jump on your bike?" I shout to them jokingly and they burst into peals of laughter.
Ninety minutes later we arrive at the gates and immediately set out on a 1km walk to the boat at the Shire River. An armed guard escorts us as elephants are often encountered along the road.
Finally, seven hours, two matola rides, one bus, a bicycle taxi, a short hike and a boat ride after leaving Cape Maclear, I arrive dirty and dehydrated at Mvuu Wilderness Lodge. Run by Wilderness Safaris, who have been recognised worldwide for their upmarket lodges and sustainable approach to the environment and local communities, Mvuu has five luxury tented units, each with en-suite bathroom and a private veranda facing the Shire River.
"Welcome to Mvuu," says Richard the camp manager, handing me a much-needed face towel and iced tea. "We often have animals wandering through the camp at night so you will always be escorted to your tent."
Liwonde is Malawi's premier national park and the wildlife here is plentiful. Although there are no large predators, the Shire River teems with hippopotamus and large crocodiles.
The next morning I lie in bed and watch a rosy dawn break across the horizon. The palm trees are swathed in soft pastel colours when I set off for a boat ride on the river. We putter across to where a pod of hippos watch us warily, their beady eyes just visible above the surface. One large female rises up with her pink jaws agape and snorts at us to keep our distance.
"Let's move back a bit, hippos can be very brutal," says my guide, promptly starting the engine.
For the last leg of my journey, I jump on a bus to Blantyre, dump my pack at the front and find a dirty tattered seat. The bus is crowded and a woman sits down next to me, holding her infant in one arm and a scrawny chicken in the other. The baby cries incessantly and the poor fowl squawks pathetically, then closes its eyes and falls asleep.
The grimy bus gives a groan and lurches onto the road. Within two minutes we've been stopped at a police road block. "Aaah, they stop everybody," sighs a student wearing smart brown trousers, a crisp white shirt and polka-dot tie. "They want to check if the bus is in good condition, but really they just want to make money. There are too many corrupt policemen in our country."
The driver switches off the engine and the heat builds up. Beads of sweat break out on the baby's brown forehead. The chicken dozes unconcerned. Finally we start up again and the bus wheezes in protest. Almost every kilometre we stop for passengers and the bus is always besieged by children. "Ten kwacha, mango!" they shout, thrusting bags of the yellow fruit and plastic packets filled with lychees up to the windows.
I wind down my trip in Blantyre, catching up on
e-mails and buying half the town's wooden curios. On my last night, I hear the sound of steady rain starting to fall. Walking back to my room in the cool wet air, I bid goodnight to Lovemore the hotel's security guard.
"This is our first big rain of the season in Blantyre," he beams. "But a few days ago in nearby villages they had proper rains and have already started planted the maize seeds. Maybe it will be a good wet season for our country so we can get more food."
I nod my head in agreement. Hopefully, for the warm-hearted people of Malawi, the rains will continue to fall.

Original_Search_Title: Akwa Ibom: Unveiling the Budget of Last Lap
Headline: Nigeria: Akwa Ibom: Unveiling the Budget of Last Lap
Body_Text:
Lagos — The 2006 budget of the Akwa Ibom State Government has generated a lot of interest in the state for two reasons. One reason is that this is the last full budget of the Attah administration and the second is that the budget is seen as the real McCoy, i. e. the budget that will position the state on the path of accelerated socio- economic development and bring into full realisation the aspirations and goals of the Attah administration before the governor ends his two-term tenure.
It is obvious that Governor Attah is aware of the enormous expectations of the people hence his christening it the 'budget of realisation'.
The main policy thrust and objectives of this year's budget, according to the state commissioner for Finance, Mr Umana Okon Umana is the completion of all key infrastructural projects in the power, tourism, transport and economic sectors of the state. The other objective is the provision of economic transformation and development drivers and supporting infrastructure that will ensure steady and sustainable growth of the state's industrial and service sectors.
Indeed, Attah, at the budget presentation before the state's House of Assembly, had reiterated that "the 2006 budget will pay priority attention to the completion of the state's core projects in the key sectors of the state such as the five-star Hotel and Resort, the Airport project, the university of science and technology , the Ibom power plant project, science park and roads projects in the state etc."
Attah had also stated that "we shall continue our firm pursuit of the economic reforms expressed in Akwa Ibom State Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy (AK-SEEDS) and in particular pay attention to measures that will enlarge our resource base, strengthen management capacity to ensure financial discipline, transparency and prudence in resource allocation and set the future course on a sustainable basis".
He stated that the 2006 budget is informed by the need to consolidate the gains so far achieved, completion of most of the ongoing programmes and projects which were started by his administration and set parameters for future action in a manner that ensures that the state's pace of development at all levels is accelerated and sustained.
This year's budget is N118,675 billion as against the N87.172 billion for the 2005 fiscal year. One unique thing about this year's budget is that it has an in-built deficit of N28 billion. This deficit is to be financed with the proceeds from part of the state's investment in the telecommunications sector.
The total projected recurrent revenue for the 2006 budget is N84.019 billion, which represents an increase of N7.702 billion over last year's corresponding estimate of N76.317 billion.
The sum of N51.900 billion is estimated as the derivation revenue accruing to the state government while N7.1 billion is allocated to local governments and the oil producing communities.
The recurrent expenditure for the year is estimated at N27.799 billion made up of N14.334 billion for personal cost and N6.726 billion for overhead costs and N6.739 billion for consolidated revenue fund charges including pensions, gratuities and public debt.
The projected capital expenditure for the 2006 budget is N87.042 billion as against the estimates of N62 .815 billion for the preceding year.This represents an increase of 24.227 billion or 38.6 per cent.
The state government has also introduced measures to boost the internally generated revenue base of the state. These measures include a new regime of property and land based taxes which took effect from January 1 this year.
The machinery for collection of taxes has also been strengthened by the state government, while it has also announced the establishment of revenue courts in the state to ensure speedy prosecution of tax evaders in the state.
For those who are not sure of their tax status, the Commissioner of Finance at the recent budget breakdown, disclosed that from the first of April 1, tax payers in the state would be able to check their status online.
Interestingly, the state's Airport project took the lion's share in the budget, but this will be catered for with the divestment of some of the state's investment in the Vmobile network which is now valued at N32 billion. Akwa Ibom will be reducing its equity participation in Vmobile by over 70 per cent. This is why this year's budget has in-built deficit. The contract for the airport project has already been signed with the contractors and managing partners, DynCorp International of the United States. The first phase of the project which began in 2005 will cost N17.75 billion and will include maintenance, repairs and overhauling facilities, MRO, 3.6 kilo meter runway, a taxi way, control tower and rescue facilities. About 2,453 hectares of land was acquired for the project which stretches from Ndon Ebon in Uruan local government area through Okobo to Nsit Atai council areas. Aside the airport project, THISDAY gathered that survey work is in progress on the industrial sites f or a flour mill in Ikot Abasi and for the various integrated agro industrial sites in the state, bringing the total for the airport and these projects to about N27 billion in total.
The implementation strategies for the 2006 budget basically covers the execution and commissioning of core projects as defined in AK-SEEDS as well as the sustenance of the ongoing economic reforms consistent with the AK-SEEDS requirements.
The budget seeks to strengthen the industrial base of the state through the setting up of new industries in the oil and gas sector, information technology and agro industrial sector.
The state seeks to attract serious investors and donors who will participate in the relevant sectors of the state economy as well as undertake robust human capacity formation to drive and sustain the economic transformation process of the state.
Roads and urban development in the 2006 budget will gulp N18.1 billion which is 20.8 per cent of the total capital outlay of the budget.The emphasis this year will be on consolidating past gains in road and urban development by ensuring that subsisting contracts are completed.
For the agricultural sector, the sum of N1.4 billion has been proposed. Part of the implementation strategy is to develop agriculture through the supply of improved agricultural input, credits and extension services and the provision of simplified agricultural technology to farmers in order to encourage the cultivation of crops such as cassava, rubber, palmtrees, rice, cocoa and yam.
The state government intends to expand the scope of agricultural programmes in the state in partnership with the International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD). Also, the initial counterpart fund for implementation of community -based natural resources management programme has been released.
For the health sector, the sum of N3.738 billion has been proposed in the 2006 budget with a view to expand and improve health care delivery at the primary and secondary levels.
In continuation of its policy of providing efficient and effective health care services to the state, the state intends to continue the rehabilitation of 42 health facilities estimated at one billion naira this year.
It will also pursue its National Programme on Immunisation, HIV /AIDS prevention programme, tuberculosis, and leprosy programme, health systems development project, safe motherhood programmes, health education programme, primary health care programme, roll back malaria programme. Aside this the budget will provide for new components of equipment for the three new general hospitals located in Okobo, Abak and Ini local government areas in the state built in 2005.
For rural development, about N3 billion has been made available to ensure the continued development of the rural area in the 2006 budget.
In order to ensure a speedy means of developing the rural areas, the state government set up the Inter Ministerial Direct Labour Committee. This Committee chaired by Umana, was set up to provide basic infrastructure like electricity, rural feeder roads, farm roads, bridges, water, classrooms and road rehabilitation to the rural areas. The committee has a mandate to execute 812 people-oriented projects across the 329 wards in the 31 local government areas of the state without being hampered by any bureaucracy. This is not to take over the functions of other ministries, according to Umana, but it is intended to be a complementary and additional benefit to the rural populace.
The tourism sector was allocated the sum of N5.1 billion in the 2006 budget in a determined effort by the state government according to Attah, to "turn the state into an investment and tourism haven." He had stated at the budget presentation before the State House of Assembly that his administration would in 2006 complete the construction of the hotel, the Golf Course, the water front projects at Nwaniba, the industrial estates at Uyo, the development of Oil and Gas industrial proceesing /trade zone (IPTZ) at Ibaka, Mbo local Government area. He added that his administration was also set to to develop the beaches at Itu, Ikot Abasi, Ibeno and Oron Beach in addition to to the Mary Slessor Park, historical museum in order to turn them into cultural and tourism centres.
A further breakdown of the 2006 budget shows that information, sports and development will take a total of sum of N2.5 billion. This will cover the expansion and upgrading of facilities at the various information dissemination agencies of the state which include the Akwa Ibom Broadcasting Corporation, Akwa Ibom Newspaper Corporation, Government Printing Press, Council for Arts and Culture and the Ethical and Attitudinal Orientation Commission.
Education, science and technology sectors in the 2006 budget has N19.8 billion allocated to various projects and programmes. The state government, according to the budget breakdown, intends to focus on the physical development of the State University of Technology as well as the completion of the state's science and technology park. A further breakdown shows that the 2006 budget will cater for the adequate funding of the legislative, judicial and executive arms of government.
In line with this policy, N2.04 billion has been proposed for the recurrent and capital expenditure for the State House of Assembly, N1.628 billion for the state judiciary and N13.374 billion for the general administration of the state public service. This will cater for the completion of the new secretariat complex, completion of the state liason office complex at Abuja, prompt payment of salaries, pensions and gratuities and appropriate training of civil servants in the state civil service.

Original_Search_Title: Northern Ugandans Fear Rebel Resurgence
Headline: Uganda: Northern Ugandans Fear Rebel Resurgence
Body_Text:
Lira, Uganda — The Lord's Resistance Army is still raiding villages, but the Ugandan military says the rebels are close to defeat.
Fear clouds Tom Okeng's eyes and his voice is strained as he recounts the attack on his village by ten rebels from the Lord's Resistance Army early last month.
While his children and wife watched in horror, Okeng was dragged from his thatched-roofed mud hut in the darkness of night, tied up with a rope, and stabbed repeatedly with a bayonet.
The rebels wanted money from him but settled for food, he says. Then they abducted a handful of villagers whom they would use as porters, cooks and soldiers to swell their depleted ranks.
When the band's leader called for a pistol and threatened to finish him off, Okeng leapt up, struck at his captors, and stumbled into the darkness with bullets whizzing by his head.
Later that night, eight of the kidnapped villagers escaped when the rebels, who had by now separated into two groups, began shooting at each other in the belief that they had been attacked by a local defence militia.
"We all ran off in different directions," recalled Lily Aburu, 40, who had earlier been yanked from her hut. "I thought it was the end."
Aburu believes both she and Okeng were lucky. "If Tom [Okeng] had not taken the chance to run, he would not have survived," she said.
For nearly 20 years, the mysterious Joseph Kony and his LRA have terrorised northern Uganda, southern Sudan, and most recently eastern parts of the Democratic Republic of Congo, DRC.
More than 1.8 million people, about 94 per cent of northern Uganda's entire population, live in 202 refugee camps created by the war, according to a consortium of aid groups called Civil Society Organisations for Peace in Northern Uganda.
A recent report from the consortium, which represents dozens of aid groups with decades of experience in the region, says that some 900 people die each week from the warfare or related problems, such as disease and injury. That is three times higher than the death rate seen in the Darfur region of western Sudan.
The LRA rebels survive by pillaging communities, kidnapping children to become soldiers and wives, and routinely killing and mutilating victims.
An estimated 25,000 children have been kidnapped during the past 15 years.
The recent LRA attack on Orem - the second in a month - has left villagers wondering if this war will ever end, even though the Ugandan military says it is all but over.
According to the villagers, their assailants were well-armed and wore new camouflage uniforms. This suggests the LRA still have access to supplies, which many analysts believe come from neighbouring Sudan.
By day, Okeng's wife Lucy and her neighbours tend garden plots of cassava, beans and millet around the village. But at night, they return to the nearby refugee camp, or hide out in the dense bush to sleep or keep an eye on their few remaining farm animals.
"Once the moon is full, they will come back," says Okeng, nervously watching the waning daylight - the rebels move around at night.
But Ugandan officials say the villagers' fears are largely unfounded.
They dispute the extent of the problem claimed by the coalition of civil society groups, and argue instead that the LRA's days as an effective fighting force are over.
Colonel Charles Otema, the head of intelligence for the Ugandan army in the north, says the rebels still active are just "a few remnants" of Kony's army "who have resorted to thuggery".
Otema describes the army's activity as "mop up" operations, in pursuit of disparate bands of rebels. "If there's an attack, we pursue them, we chase them and crush them."
"In the villages, people are feeling safe, gradually," said Otema, indicating that some people may soon leave the refugee camps and go back to their farms.
Recent reports suggest Kony may be in Garamba National Park, a jungle game preserve in the troubled northeastern provinces of the DRC.
Kony fled his previous stronghold in southern Sudan with a small force of his most loyal soldiers, many of whom were kidnapped as children and have known no other life, to join his second-in-command, Vincent Otti.
Otti commands some 200 or more fighters and has terrorised parts of eastern Congo since last year. In January, his fighters killed eight Guatemalan peacekeepers and wounded five more members of the 17,000-member United Nations force struggling to maintain order there.
Colonel Otema says Uganda wants permission from DRC officials to cross the border and pursue Kony and Otti. Once that authorisation comes, he says confidently, "end of story - we are talking months. These people cannot hold."
The capture of Congolese militia commander Thomas Lubanga, who was turned over to the International Criminal Court, ICC, in The Hague, has renewed hope that Kony and Otti could be captured.
Lubanga, alleged to have killed 2,000 civilians during regional conflicts in Congo in the late Nineties, faces charges of kidnapping children and forcing them to become child soldiers in his militia.
Last October, the ICC issued indictments for war crimes and crimes against humanity against Kony and four of his top commanders, one of whom is already dead.
Additionally, the UN Security Council has asked Secretary-General Kofi Annan to develop plans for the UN to help end the conflict in northern Uganda, just as it moves toward possible intervention in Sudan's Darfur region.
But some LRA commanders close to Kony say he will never be captured alive.
Jackson Acama, 44, a former major in the LRA who was granted amnesty, says Kony is convinced he is a prophet and takes his orders directly from God.
"Kony does not care how many people die," claims Acama. "He is doing what God tells him to do. Kony will never give up until people accept him as a prophet, or he is killed."
Betty Bigombe, the lead Ugandan negotiator with Kony and the LRA, is hopeful yet sceptical, saying, "The LRA is weak now, but they always have regrouped and come back with renewed brutality."
Bigombe speaks to the LRA regularly and insists they are willing to negotiate. But when asked whether the end is near for the rebel movement, she grimly responds, "It's a long way off."
Kony has only three options, she suggests: death, prison or exile.
But the last of these options is now is unlikely, following Nigeria's recent agreement to turn over former Liberian leader Charles Taylor to be prosecuted for war crimes by an international court.
Despite mounting international pressure for action against the LRA, the residents of Orem doubt that peace is around the corner.
Okeng is mystified about why the LRA persists with its violence, but pressed to give an answer, he confides that he believes the people of northern Uganda are being punished. But why, and for what sins, he cannot say.
Robert Akona, 33, who is Okeng's neighbour, shrugs when he is asked when the conflict might end. "I'm leaving it all to God and prayers," he said.
Peter Eichstaedt is a senior editor in Uganda with the Institute for War and Peace Reporting -Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Food Prices Increasing
Headline: Uganda: Food Prices Increasing
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE demand for fresh commodities has surged, leading to increase
in prices. This is inspite of increased supplies, The New Vision weekly
survey has shown.
The development has, however, led to a drop in prices for dry beans, maize, cassava and millet flour and groundnuts.
This is because consumers prefer fresh items to dried ones.
A mug of fresh beans goes for sh2,000, while dry ones go for sh900 a
kilogramme. "Dealers in dry items have no customers," a city trader
said.
these days because most of them go for fresh items.
This has even pushed prices (fresh items) up leaving us idle," said Hadija Nakintu, a trader at Nakasero market.
She said despite the high prices, consumers rushed for the fresh
ones. Nakintu expects that prices to return to normal around at
harvesting peak in June.
At St. Balikuddemebe market a plastic cup of fresh beans is sold at
sh2,000 while a kilogramme of dry beans is at sh900 on average.
Fine maize flour is down by sh200 from sh1, 000 a kilogramme. Both simsim and groundnuts remain at sh1, 200 a kilogramme.
Cassava flour scaled down from sh600 a kilo to sh500 while millet flour fell from sh1, 000 a kilo to sh700 average.
A kilogramme of Sugar and Salt still remained at sh1, 500 and sh400 respectively.

Original_Search_Title: Low Cassava Yields Scare Farmers
Headline: Rwanda: Low Cassava Yields Scare Farmers
Body_Text:
Southern Proince — Farmers in former Gikonko district, are scared that this season's poor yields of the new cassava type may not produce enough for the ever increasing market due to the prolonged drought.
According to Francine Mukarurangwa, a farmer from Gikonko sector, no substantial yields are expected this season from the one million cassava seedlings given to each cassava growing association in Gikonko.
The two new types of cassava (TME 14 and 00057) were introduced in the area last year area by a local agricultural export firm (COVEPAR), with help from the agriculture ministry to boost production in the area.
The likely low yields were also blamed on the acidic soils in the area that are not conducive for the new type of cassava.
"Gikonko has potential to produce high yields of cassava if resistant seedlings for the mosaic disease are in place. We hope COVAPAR can come back to save us," she said.
