However, farmers have been encouraged to persist and grow more cassava in order to tap the high demand at both the local and international markets. In Gikonko, COVEPAR is working with 14 producer associations that are organized into groups.
The NGO currently helps over 1,500 farmers, many of whom are women widowed during the 1994 Genocide

Original_Search_Title: With China Calling, Is It Time to Say Goodbye to US And Europe?
Headline: Kenya: With China Calling, Is It Time to Say Goodbye to US And Europe?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — By the turn of the century, China had transformed its foreign relations from one hinged on ideology to one governed by the pursuit of economic self-interest, and Africa has not been left out. Mark Sorbara explores Africa's place in China's new world
What do the US, Saudi Arabia, Morocco, Nigeria and Kenya have in common? Not much really. But from next week they will share a common 'bond' as part of the itinerary of a high-powered Chinese delegation led by none other than Chinese President Hu Jintao, from April 18 to 29. What's the big deal about that, one may ask. Well, its simply because China is the place where big deals - of the mind-boggling type - are made these days.
And there is quite some excitement about Hu's visit, at least in Washington, where he is expected to tour Microsoft and Boeing Co. before heading to the White House on April 20. Forget Washington's complaints over human rights violations, piracy, the value of China's currency and unfair trade practices, the importance of China as a trade partner to the United States is crystal clear.
Figure this: "A multi-billion deal for Boeing aircraft has already been signed and Hu's first official trip to the United States as president has also brought into focus a groundbreaking bid by a Citigroup-led consortium to buy an 85 per cent stake in China's Guangdong Development Bank," Reuters reported this week.
"Hu's trip and a trade delegation this month led by Chinese Vice Premier Wu Yi are yielding deals on everything from software to soybeans worth an expected total of about $16 billion." The report quoted Chinese aviation officials as saying that the Boeing deal signed on Tuesday involved the purchase of 80 Boeing 737 planes that have a list value of $4.6 billion.
That's all very well for the US, and we can pretty well guess what the Saudi's will be buying and selling when they host Hu and his delegation. What about Africa? What's in it for the continent?
The man who holds the answer is Li Zhaoxing, China's foreign minister. Li Zhaoxing is no stranger to Africa. From 1970 to 1977 Li served as an attachÃ© in the Chinese Embassy in Kenya, from 1983 to 1985 he was first secretary in Maseru, Lesotho.
From January 11 to 19 Li visited Cape Verde, Liberia, Mali, Senegal, Nigeria and Libya. China's increased role throughout Africa has demonstrated that Beijing's Africa policy will be an important aspect of its rapid economic growth. Hence, on January 12, Li officially unveiled an Africa Policy Paper which outlined the future relationship between Africa and China.
As stated in its forward, the Policy Paper was created " with the view of promoting the steady growth of China-Africa relations in the long term and bringing the mutually beneficial co-operation to a new stage." It states, "the one China principle is the political foundation for the establishment and development of China's relations with African countries and regional organisations." If African countries choose to accept this prerequisite, China will "co-ordinate positions on major international and regional issues and stand for mutual support on major issues concerning state sovereignty, territorial integrity, national dignity and human rights."
The focal point of China-Africa relations is resource based, as the policy states, "the Chinese government will adopt more effective measures to facilitate African commodities access to the Chinese market." Investing in African extractive industries is a risky business, but China is desperately in need of raw materials to feed its booming economy, hence the government is willing to shoulder most of the risk for Chinese companies looking to invest in Africa. Therefore, "the Chinese government encourages and supports Chinese enterprises' investment and business in Africa, and will continue to provide preferential loans and buyer credits to this end."
Moreover, the policy states, "China will do its best to provide and gradually increase assistance to African countries with no political strings attached." On peace and security, "China will promote high-level military exchanges between the two states and actively carry out military related technological exchanges and co-operation. It will continue to help train African military personnel and support defence and army building of African countries for their own security."
For not recognising Taiwan, African countries can get government-supported foreign direct investment, aid and military assistance "with no political strings attached." Goodbye US and Europe; Hello China!
In the end, though, China's Africa policy today is largely driven by self-interest, mainly access to resources, therefore it has to work with African countries to make sure the resources increasingly flow towards Beijing and not Paris, London or Washington. But it will not get the resources without some competition.
For example, the influential US-based Council on Foreign Relations published a report in December 2005 entitled More Than Humanitarianism: A Strategic US Approach Towards Africa and has opened the debate on changing the US-Africa relations. The year 2005 supposedly was the "year of Africa", but as the Foreign Relations report stated, "the point was missing - amid the music, communiquÃ©s, and the commitments -- that Africa is becoming steadily more central to the United States and the rest of the world in ways which transcend humanitarian interests."
Notably, fifteen pages of the US Foreign Relations report are spent assessing the impact of China's increasing role in Africa and bluntly illustrate many of the issues which China's Africa Policy Paper dealt with. For example, the report states, "All across Africa today, China is acquiring control of natural resource assets, out-bidding Western contractors on major infrastructure projects, and providing soft loans and other incentives to bolster is competitive advantage."
It continues, "Perhaps most disturbing to US political objectives is China's willingness to use its seat on the UN Security Council to protect some of Africa's most egregious regimes from international sanction China offers an alternative source of support, even to some of the United States' closest allies, when they chafe under Western pressure for economic or political reform."
On the subject of investment and aid the Foreign Relations report states that "China is also investing and providing assistance in areas that Western aid agencies and private investors have long neglected: physical infrastructure, industry and agriculture." And " Chinese business practices, which serve state interests as much as a profit motive, create unfair competition to US firms bidding for contracts." The Chinese government and businesses see opportunity in Africa, where Westerners see poverty and despair, and they are actively working together to see that the opportunities are transformed into successful ventures.
In 1960s to 1970s, when ideology was the main factor influencing China's foreign relations, almost 25,000 Chinese doctors and agricultural engineers were stationed all over the 'Third World'. By the turn of the century, China had transformed its foreign relations to one governed by the pursuit of economic self-interest. From the first meeting of the China-Africa Co-operation Forum held in Beijing in 2000 to its second meeting held in Addis Ababa in 2003, a lot has changed. Between 2002 and 2003 China-Africa trade jumped 50 per cent to $18.5 billion, and by the end of 2004, over 715 Chinese-funded companies were operating in Africa.
On January 6, 2006, the BBC reported that Chinese trade with Africa jumped 39 per cent to $32.17 billion in 2005 and "in the first 10 months of 2005 alone, Chinese companies invested a total of $175 million in African countries." Moreover, in the next five years, China-Africa trade is expected to surpass $100 billion.
China's thirst for raw materials has dramatically transformed its relations with many African countries. Take Nigeria; the trade between the two countries stood at $384 million in 1998, by 2001 it was $1 billion, by 2004 it was $2 billion and by the end of 2005 trade between Nigeria and China reached approximately $3 billion. In January 2006, the China National Offshore Oil Corporation paid $2.3 billion for a 45 per cent stake in the Akpo oil field, its largest overseas acquisition, which has a reserve of 600 million barrels and potential for an extra 500 million.
China's trade and investment relationship with Nigeria is expanding rapidly outside the oil sector as well. In 2005, Nigeria earned over $500 million from non-oil product exports to China and in 2006 the Nigerian government signed a memorandum of understanding with Guangdong Xinguang International Group, a Chinese government-owned company, to enhance Nigeria's rail network. The $2 billion dollar MOU includes a fast rail system between Lagos and Abuja, a light rail system from Murtala Mohammed International Airport to Nnamdi Azikiwe International Airport in Lagos and Abuja city centre as well.
China and Nigeria have also discussed the possibilities of further Chinese investments in power stations, low cost housing projects, cassava plantations, an agro-research centre, an Aids medicine factory and a medical equipment factory.
Angola, Sub-Saharan Africa's second largest oil producer, is another important element in Beijing's Africa policy. According to state controlled Angola Press, "in 2005, bilateral trade between the two countries reached $6.95 billion, an increase of 41.6 per cent from the previous year." Most of the increase has been due to crude purchases by the China Petroleum and Chemical Corporation (Sinopec) as part of a 2004 agreement which saw the Chinese Eximbank approve a $2 billion (1.5 per cent over 17 years) infrastructure loan to the Angolan government.
In March, CNN reported that " a new consortium jointly controlled by the Angolan government-run Sonangol oil company and Sinopec has been awarded a contract to build a 240,000 barrel per day refinery in Lobito." Angola now accounts for 13 per cent of China's crude oil imports and China's is the second largest consumer of Angolan crude oil after the United States. Hence, 20 leading Chinese firms operating in Angola, such as Sinosteel, China National Overseas Engineering Corporation and Sinopec have recently created a Chinese Chamber of Commerce in Luanda to enhance relations with Angola.
When discussing Chinese resource extraction in Africa, Sudan is increasingly emerging as an important player. The China National Petroleum Corporation (CNPC) is the largest investor in the Sudanese oil industry. The decision of the US government to cut ties with Sudan in the mid-1990s pressured Western oil companies to withdraw and opened new opportunities for Chinese investment.
CNPC, in a joint venture with the Greater Nile Petroleum Operating Company, of which CNPC has a 40 per cent stake, have been exploiting Sudanese oil deposits in the Muglad Basin. Together, the CNPC and the Greater Nile company have invested over $8 billion in Sudan, including the 1,500 km pipeline to transport oil to the Marsa al-Bashair harbour terminal near the Port of Sudan on the Red Sea. By 2005, China was purchasing between 50 and 60 per cent of Sudan crude production and Sudan alone accounts for 7 to 9 per cent of Chinese oil imports.
China is also active in Africa's smaller oil exporting countries as well. In 2003, China imported one million tons of crude oil from Congo-Brazzaville, accounting for 1.5 per cent of Chinese oil imports. China is the third largest importer of oil from Equatorial Guinea after the US and Spain. Total-Gabon and Sinopec signed an agreement in 2004 to supply China with one million tons of crude oil a year, making China the third largest consumer of Gabonese crude after the US and France. The Chinese petroleum company Zhongyuan Exploration Bureau is even drilling on behalf of Petronas, the Malaysian state-owned oil company, in the Gambella Basin in Western Ethiopia.
Non-oil resource extraction is also attracting Chinese investment in Africa as well. Over 60 per cent of Gabon's, and a large part of Equatorial Guinea's, timber production is purchased by China. China is the world's largest consumer of copper and has invested $170 million in the Zambian copper mining sector. The Chambezi copper mine, which was purchased in 1999, is now its largest Chinese mining operation in Africa. Moreover, China is increasingly involved the Democratic Republic of Congo, investing in cobalt and copper mines as well.
Although resource extraction is the center-piece of China's new Africa push, non-resource based investments have not been ignored. In Ghana, Sino Hydro signed a memorandum of understanding with the Ghanaian Government to construct the Bui Dam. The $500 million project is expected to augment Ghanaian power supply from the Akosombo Dam and the Aboadze Thermal Plant.
In Zimbabwe, a number of farms seized from white farmers by the government have been leased to the China State Farms and Agribusiness Corporation, which will revive and enhance production on the Fenemere and Dalkeith farms near the Mazvikadai Dam and the Clydedale and Liverdale farms near the Biri Dam. Chinese companies have also leased agricultural land in Zambia and Tanzania.
Even in post-war Sierra Leone, Chinese investors see possibilities. Henon Guoji, a Chinese company, is starting construction of the $200 million Lumley Beach Hotel, which will include a conference centre, a sports facility, casino and night club. The Chinese state-owned company, Beijing Urban Construction Group, has purchased and restored The Bintumani, Sierra Leone's largest hotel, at a cost of $10 million and it is already up and running. Chinese companies in Sierra Leone have also invested in a sugar plant and a tractor factory, and the Chinese government has built a new office block, parliament buildings and a new military headquarters for the government of Sierra Leone.
The list continues: the China Road and Bridge Corporation built the Tambach-Kabarnet road in Western Kenya and refurbished part of the Nairobi-Mombasa road. The Bata-Mongono road in Gabon was built with financial and technical assistance from China, while the Zhengxing Telecom is refurbishing Djibouti's telecom network.
China is constructing one of Africa's largest dams in Ethiopia and is assisting in the construction and launch of a Nigerian communications satellite in 2007. Chinese firms have also entered into fish processing joint ventures in Gabon and Namibia and the Qingdao Municipal Government has invested in a textile factory in the new Mulungashi Industrial Estates near Kabwe in Zambia. China has also constructed a new Foreign Affairs Ministry building in Uganda and Djibouti and built a new stadium in Mali.
Although France, the UK and the US still account for 70 per cent of Foreign Direct Investment inflows to Africa, all that will change with the emergence of China's Africa focus. So maybe Westerners should start to be more honest with themselves and Africans and stop all this useless armchair pitying.
Mark J. Sorbara is a freelance writer and researcher on African issues.

Original_Search_Title: 'Help Acholi Settle Down'
Headline: Uganda: 'Help Acholi Settle Down'
Body_Text:
Kampala — AS the Acholi people prepare to go back to their villages, there is need for provision of farming implements to help them grow their own food.
This was in a statement from the Pabbo camp leader in Gulu district, Wilson Ojok, while talking to journalists on Saturday.
"We call upon the Government and humanitarian organisations to provide us with farming equipment and seeds. People have started ploughing the land in the new places where they have moved," Ojok said.
He said they needed seeds like okra, millet, beans, groundnuts and cassava stems, adding that there was need for the people to begin growing their own food instead of relying only on the WFP relief aid.

Original_Search_Title: Nurse Reaps Big From Pigs
Headline: Uganda: Nurse Reaps Big From Pigs
Body_Text:
Kampala — PIGS have proved to be one of the best investment among domestic animals. This is why Sarah Kutta Musoke, a nurse in Kamwokya, Kampala, decided to rear them in big numbers to supplement her income.
Musoke set up a sty at her father's home in Kassangombe in Nakaseke district from which she has started earning good returns.
Today, she has about 60 pigs of various sizes and ages, raging from one month to those ready for sale.
The pigs are housed in a 50 to 10 metres enclosure. Each of the adult pigs has got its own cubicle. The cubicles are divided into two parts, one for feeding, the other is for resting. The entire floor is neatly cemented.
Musoke spent about sh7m on construction. She describes her project as one for breeding purposes.
Musoke has a dream of turning it into a leading hybrid piglets' outlet in the district. "We sell off the females to farmers who want to start similar breeding projects," she says. A two-month-old sow goes for between sh35,000 and sh50,000.
Most of the boars are sold off at between four and six months. This is the right time for selling off pigs for pork.
They retain a few females for multiplication purposes. On average, a female can deliver eight piglets at a time. Females deliver at least two times a year, making it 16 piglets.
The project is overseen by Mzee Kutta Musoke, Sarah's father. Pigs are naturally dirty animals, but they like living in clean environments, so we have to make sure that their houses are cleaned regularly," Mzee Musoke says.
He says on average a pig can eat at least 3kgs of maize bran in a day, plus lots of supplementing leaves. "Prices of the basic foods are high. This sometimes affects us," he says.
Foods have to be bought from Wobulenzi town, which is about eight miles away. In addition to pre-mixed feeds like Maize bran, a lot of the other foods are prepared at the farm. These include peelings that are steamed before they are fed to the pigs. Plant leaves like sweet potato and cassava are also fed to the pigs.
Buyers, especially for the female pigs come from as far as Kampala. Musoke says the project earns more money from Kampala buyers than local buyers whose price quotations are very low.
"People in the village don't differentiate between our hybrid pigs and the local breeds. They want to buy them at the same prices," Mzee Musoke says.
Tips for those who want to invest in pigs
Start with a number that you can effectively manage with the resources you have.
Construct a cemented shade - it is easier to manage than bare ground.
Consult a veterinary doctor, whenever there is a problem.
De-worm them regularly.
Avoid sharing farm tools, because they spread disease.

Original_Search_Title: State Warns Adamant Businessmen
Headline: Zambia: State Warns Adamant Businessmen
Body_Text:
GOVERNMENT has advised the public to report any businessmen ignoring the reversal of value added tax (VAT) on agricultural products and newspapers.
Chief Government spokesperson, Vernon Mwaanga, said the Zambia Revenue Authority (ZRA) or the Zambia Police should be informed about anybody ignoring Statutory Instrument number 43 that was effected by Finance and National Planning Minister Ng'andu Magande on March 24 and gazetted on April 7 this year.
Mr Mwaanga, who is Information and Broadcasting Services Minister, said Government was determined to protect the interests of consumers.
"I wish to assure the nation that Government is determined to protect consumers from businessmen and women who want to exploit unsuspecting members of the public especially through overcharging or by not reducing prices," he said.
According to Statutory Instrument number 43 of 2006 on the VAT (exemptions) amendment number three, agricultural products exempted from tax include fresh edible vegetables, fruits, nuts, maize, soya beans, millet, cassava and sorghum.
Agricultural products would also attract tax if supplied by a restaurant, cafeteria or a canteen. Wheat, cotton seed, lint, baby-corn, and sweet corn would also attract tax.
Animal products exempted from VAT under the Statutory Instrument include meat and offals of cattle, swine, sheep, goats, game, farm animals and poultry, including eggs, except when supplied by a restaurant, cafeteria or canteen.
Milk was exempted from VAT apart from when it was powdered, canned or supplied by restaurants.
Fish should also trade VAT-free apart from shell fish or that supplied by a restaurant.
On agricultural supplies, the Statutory Instrument included bulbs, seeds and plants. Fertilisers, insecticides, herbicides and anti-sprouting would all be exempted from VAT.

Original_Search_Title: Maize Doing Well in Kampala
Headline: Uganda: Maize Doing Well in Kampala
Body_Text:
Kampala market has performed relatively well this week with a slight
improvement in the price of maize grain, which has increased from
Shs330/kg to Shs345/kg at off-lorry.
The price rise is due to increase in demand of maize for milling following electricity reconnection by UMEME.
This has led to a corresponding decrease in the price of maize flour
from Shs700/kg to Shs680/kg for the premium quality while the second
rated sells at Shs580 from Shs600.
Major bulk buyers like UGTL and Apony Ltd have halted business,
sighting the instability of the market. They complain about the quality
that farmers have continued to flood in the market.
Fresh beans from Kasese have boosted the quantities in the market
though their moisture content is still high average of 20 percent. New
Kanyebwa sells at Shs1,100/kg and Nambale at Shs1050/kg. Yellow has
continued to be scarce with a higher price of Shs1,200/kg for the old
stocks. White beans price has dropped from Shs980/kg to Shs850/kg.
Masindi: The district has continued receiving moderate rainfall.
Farmers in Kibanda county are still heavily planting maize even though
the county registered low rains this week. Matooke remained scarce in
the district with few supplies coming from Fort Portal and Kibaale
districts. There has been a remarkable increase in the supply of fresh
cassava due to the onset rains that ease harvesting.
Masaka: Foodstuff prices are still high with matooke supplies
continuing to dwindle though sweet potatoes have improved on the other
hand.
The quality of beans on the market is poor as they have over stayed
in stores. Fish prices have slightly eased with increase in the catch,
however, the quality is not yet good. Milk has increased in quantity
though the price has remained high.
Luwero: The district has now got power after almost a month in
darkness. This has enabled grain-milling industries to become
operational thereby improving the supply of maize flour and millet
flour.
The rains have been moderate which has encouraged planting. Regular supplies of bananas from Kampala have sustained consumers.
Arua: The district is experiencing heavy rains, which are making the
feeder roads impassible hence affecting the market price of
agricultural products.
Irish potatoes are very expensive as they come all the way from
Kampala with prices averaging at Shs50,000 a bag of relative weight of
110kgs.

Original_Search_Title: Article 18.8 Falls On Limbe, Buea Electoral Districts
Headline: Cameroon: Article 18.8 Falls On Limbe, Buea Electoral Districts
Body_Text:
The political storm that has been battering the Social Democratic Front, SDF, party, has compelled it to use article 18.8 of its constitution to reorganise of some of its constituencies.
To repair the wear and tear that the party organs have endured so far, a reorganisation exercise was launched in the Southwest Province to weed out officials who had developed anti-party attitudes and were seen as dragging the party backwards.
Pupil Barrister Martin Dikanjo Esingila, 35, is the new SDF Chairman of the Limbe Electoral District of the party. He was elected by acclamation on Saturday, April 1, to replace Francis Anu who had resigned as the Chair.
Meanwhile, Christian Morfor was elected Vice Chairman; Jonas Metuge Esambe took up position as the new District Secretary and Celine Angwi Kimbeng was voted Treasurer.
The Limbe elections came at the instance of a decision of the SDF National Executive Committee, NEC, which met in Douala.
In reaction to his election, Esingila said he would recapture the Limbe electoral constituency both at the parliamentary and council levels. He was confident that with the support of his militants, the CPDM would never control Limbe again come next elections.
The Chairman of the Kumba Urban Council, Ferdinand Asapngu, presided at the elections, which took place at the Party's secretariat at Cassava Farms.
Asked the reason for the abrupt reorganisation exercise, he reiterated that it was in line with article 18.8, which gives it powers to reorganise party organs whenever it finds any of their activities threatening the life of the party.
As to what threat there was in the case of Limbe, Asapngu said, "For the past three years the District has held no conference."
He explained that their mandate had expired and the number of wards dwindled from 40 to 20.
Asapngu said the administration has always rigged elections in Limbe, "That is one of the reasons that has caused this reorganisation in Limbe," he said.
Erstwhile SDF Parliamentarian, Hon. Victoria Tomedi Ndando, said, "the new exco of Limbe should eschew dishonesty, be sincere and accountable, to themselves, militants and to Cameroonians".
In a related development, another lawyer, Barrister Eta Besong, won the election for the post of the Buea SDF Electoral District Chairperson in Buea, Sunday, April 2.
John Endeley Mokake, former Buea Mayor, will assist him; the Secretary is James Manga Njock, Treasurer, Grace Samdumu and the Financial Secretary, Amina Timbo.
Eta Besong said his preoccupation would be to strengthen the SDF in Buea, "To get the wards very active, without discrimination, division. Once the wards are strong, you do not have other battle to get the districts strong," he asserted.
On the divisive tendency, which has been rocking the SDF, especially at the top level, Besong said; "The SDF is like a spirit. You do not kill or divide a spirit. The SDF has shown that it is an active party. If there were no for and against, it would be a dictatorship. The SDF tolerates different shades of opinionsÃ¢-oe"

Original_Search_Title: Akume Seeks Inland Port for Makurdi
Headline: Nigeria: Akume Seeks Inland Port for Makurdi
Body_Text:
Benue — Governor George Akume of Benue State, has called on the Federal Government to establish inland port in Makurdi, to service Benue, Nasarawa, Taraba and Plateau states.
Akume made the appeal weekend in Makurdi, at the opening of the 14th meeting of the National Council on Commerce.
He said the port would boost economic activities and facilitate exportation of agricultural products, and also called for the establishment of a free trade zone in Makurdi, to cater for states in North-central zone, adding that government had concluded plans to establish three cassava processing plants in each of the three senatorial districts.
The Federal Government, he said, should partner with the state to fund the project, because it was capital intensive.
Commissioner of Commerce, Mr Geoffrey Agernor, commended the Federal Government's efforts at developing the non-oil sector.

Original_Search_Title: My 10 Years With the LRA
Headline: Uganda: My 10 Years With the LRA
Body_Text:
Kampala — Bosco Atube was 14 when he was abducted from Gulu District by the LRA. Almost 10 years later, with many battles under his belt and boasting the rank of lieutenant, his rebel days are over. He told Andrew Ndawula what life on the other side was like.
HE is ready to join his former enemies, provided they let him retain his rank. Lieutenant Bosco Atube, 23, a former Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) fighter, who recently came out of the bush, feels he deserves the rank given to him by rebel chief Joseph Kony.
The rank was the reward for his 10 years of service to the LRA rebels, who have been clashing with Uganda People's Defence Forces (UPDF) in southern Sudan and northern Uganda for almost two decades.
Atube was 14 years old when he was conscripted into the LRA. A group of heavily armed LRA fighters attacked his village, Wianaka, in Nwoya County, Gulu District at about 8:30pm, looted all the food they could get their hands on and abducted people to carry it.
Atube, then a P3 pupil at Wianaka Primary School, was among those abducted.
"I was made to carry a sack of rice on my head. I was barefoot, with just a pair of shorts and a vest on," he recalls.
Almost a decade later, Atube is yet to know the fate of his father Santonio Oryema, mother Doreen Odoch and his four younger siblings. Maybe they survived; maybe they didn't.
Run, baby, run
To confuse the UPDF, in case they tried to pursue them, the rebels and their victims first headed towards Adjumani, before doubling back to Gulu and then finally heading to their base in southern Sudan.
There were over 100 recruits, guarded by six heavily armed LRA fighters.
It had been a successful mission; the rebels had managed to abduct many young fighters and also loot a lot of food.
For two months, Atube and the other 'recruits' were kept busy in the garden, growing potatoes, sorghum, cassava and beans. After two weeks in the main camp, which was divided into three sections, the recruits were split into several smaller groups, and distributed among different camps.
At the end of two months, the hand hoes were replaced with guns, which the recruits were taught to strip, assemble and, most importantly, fire.
After the rudimentary instructions, the recruits were declared battle ready. Soon after they were deployed to the front line, to put to use their newly acquired skills.
The art of war
Atube still recalls his first front-line experience. He was deployed, along with several others, to ambush a UPDF convoy in southern Sudan. They hit their target all right, although Atube did not hang around to assess the damage inflicted on the enemy.
Next, he was deployed to attack a UPDF detachment in southern Sudan. Again, the mission was successful. Not that all the attacks he was involved in were successful.
Atube still vividly remembers the time when he almost did not make it. It was January 2001. A group of over 100 LRA fighters, including Atube, launched what they thought was a surprise attack on a UPDF detachment at Pajule in Kitgum District. Too late, they discovered the UPDF had anticipated the attack, and were ready for them.
"They put up a big fight. We had to withdraw at a very high speed to a safe place about 3km away from the battlefield," relates Atube, who was injured in the back as he fled from the battlefield, where 45 of his comrades remained, either dead or injured.
As he continued to take part in attacks on the UPDF, Atube was quickly promoted through the LRA ranks. Promotions in the LRA don't come as a result of further military courses or training, as is the case in conventional armies.
They are based on ability to recruit (abduct) fighters, look after and effectively deploy them to attack or repulse the enemy. Atube excelled in all three.
So in 2002, five years after joining the rebels, he was made a sergeant, skipping two ranks. In December of the same year, he was promoted to lieutenant, the rank he held until the day he quit the bush last month.
Like most guerrilla forces, the LRA has no specialised units. According to Atube, whenever there was a mission to carry out, the required manpower would be selected from the general pool of fighters, and placed under one of the top commanders, who would in turn be picked according to the officers' duty roster, which existed in every camp and was strictly followed.
Apart from the senior officers, listening to the radio was an offence, punishable by execution.
Although they were not supposed to know what was going on outside their bush camps, almost all the fighters in Atube's camp had heard about the amnesty that the Government had extended to the rebels.
However, Kony and his senior officers had managed to convince them that the amnesty was just government siasa (propaganda), to lure the rebels out of the bush, so that they could finish them off.
Although the two sometimes stayed in the same camp, Atube can count on one hand, the number of times he met Kony.
"He is always surrounded by 20 to 30 escorts, and is in the habit of making surprise appearances at the various satellite camps," Atube describes Kony's phantom-like tendencies.
Stolen women
Besides promotions, another way Kony would motivate his officers was by giving them young girls, abducted from northern Uganda, to be their sex slaves.
Two years before he quit the bush, Atube was given a wife, a young girl who had been abducted from Oriang village in Kitgum District. She soon bore him a son.
However, when the baby was about one-and-a-half, mother and child had to be sent back to Uganda, after the UPDF stepped up its operations in southern Sudan, destroying rebel camps there.
Atube has no idea where they are, but hopes to look them up after he has been granted amnesty and has undergone rehabilitation.
Changing lanes
When it became increasingly difficult for the LRA to operate in southern Sudan, Kony picked 80 of his best fighters, under his deputy Vincent Otti, to go and establish a new base in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC).
Atube was among them. "They did not tell us what we were going to do there. It is only the senior officers who had an idea," says Atube.
The journey from Sudan to the DRC was a hazardous one; the fighters had to continuously struggle against the forces of nature, and at the same time use their wits to avoid detection by hostile forces.
To get to the Promised Land, the rebels had to cross two rivers; the Nile and Yei.
Since they had no boats, they had to improvise. "We made a raft out of empty jerrycans, held together by bamboos sticks and ropes," Atube says. Each raft would accommodate between five and eight passengers.
To avoid being swept away by the currents, the people on the raft would throw a rope to their colleagues on the opposite bank, to tow them to safety.
The group finally arrived in the DRC in June 2005, and camped in a place called Aba, in Garamba National Park, where they kept a low profile, as Vincent Otti and other senior officers tried to negotiate with the local authorities.
Lost
After six months in the DRC, the rebels' cover was blown. According to Atube, it was on December 22, 2005, when the rebels fell into a UPDF ambush, and four of them, including Atube, were injured.
Injured in the leg, Atube had to choose between his gun and his life. He chose life, abandoning the gun on the battlefield.
Later, after the guns had fallen silent, Atube's commander sent him back to the scene of the ambush to check on his other injured colleagues.
As he approached the scene of ambush, Atube realised the UPDF soldiers were still around. "They were speaking Acholi, that is how I knew they were UPDF," he says. Too weak to withdraw, because of his bleeding wound, Atube decided to stay put, and wait for the soldiers to leave. They eventually did; four days later.
The pain from the bullet wound had by now been overtaken by sharp pangs of hunger. For what he estimates to have been a month - after sometime he had lost count of days - Atube moved aimlessly in the sprawling park, surviving on wild fruits and berries.
He finally found a Congolese poacher, armed with a spear, from whom he inquired as to the whereabouts of the Congolese troops.
The poacher told him they were stationed in Aba, a border town located South East of Garamba National Park, in which they were.
The poacher invited Atube to stay with him in his makeshift camp, where they stayed for four days, before they moved to a permanent house, near Aba town.
Unknown to Atube, his host had already alerted the game wardens about the rebel's presence, and they soon turned up to arrest him.
Journey's end
"There were five of them, in full uniform and travelling on a pick-up truck," narrates Atube. They took him to their camp, where they advised him to stay calm.
After five days of suspense, two helicopters belonging to MONUC, the UN monitoring mission in the Congo, arrived to take him away to their base in Bunia. Later, he was brought back to Uganda and handed over to the Amnesty Commission, which is currently looking after him, as he waits to undergo rehabilitation.
As he tries to settle back into civilian life, Atube weighs his options, which are not many.
As a P3 drop-out, it will be difficult for him to be absorbed into the UPDF, which is in the process of transforming itself into a modern army.
The other option is to go back to Wianaka village, and join his parents, who are peasant farmers, in tilling the land. That is assuming they are alive.

Original_Search_Title: Dodging KCC's Iron Fist
Headline: Uganda: Dodging KCC's Iron Fist
Body_Text:
Kampala — TO make a living, food vendors who cannot afford Kampala City Council (KCC) taxes sell their delicacies late in the night, away from the prying eyes of KCC officials.
Fruit, shoes and clothes vendors patiently wait for the city cleaners to clean up the city. They are dodging and running away from the iron hand of KCC law enforcers. Thursday, 9:45pm found night vendors and I along Namirembe Road. As shops closed and human traffic thinned, I requested a Muzamilu Mukwasi, one of the night-time public phone operators to let me borrow his stool for while.
I watched as women food vendors dressed in dull-coloured clothes with head wraps, carrying black polythene bags containing charcoal stoves and charcoal arrived one at a time. Then followed wheelbarrow pushers transporting their heavy food-laden saucepans. Not to be apprehended by KCC law enforcers, the saucepans were left hidden in a dark corridor. The vendors kept pacing from one end of the street to the other as they waited for the coast to clear.
Mukwasi is a loyal customer of Annet Namubiru, a food vendor. He said food vendors station themselves along the dark stretch opposite Nabugabo Road, between Mukwano shopping complex and Caltex fuel station. This point acts as both the kitchen and dining area.
"At that point it's not easy to identify who is eating as most diners would rather not be identified," Mukwasi said.
Actually, I came to appreciate his comment. No sooner had I taken a photograph of him getting food from Namubiru, than a group of drunk street kids and other diners with the help of a security guard from Saracen opened my camera and yanked out the film. What really terrified me were the handcuffs that the guard got from his pocket and placed on my wrists threatening to take me to the police station.
Unlike other vendors that spread their stock for sale as early as 9:30pm, food is not served immediately for fear of losing it to impromptu check by KCC men who could be lurking around, dressed in plain clothes.
Mukwasi said more than once, tea and food vendors have lost their merchandise to enforcers pretending to be buyers. So the food isn't served until 10:00pm.
To show that they are ready to serve, benches are arranged in a semicircle, facing the vendor. The food includes, matooke, posho, spaghetti, yams, rice, cassava, potatoes, millet bread, served with beans, groundnuts, beef, peas, fish, eggplants and cabbage.
Mukwasi explains that most of the diners are bus and special hire drivers, boda boda cyclists, streets kids, night builders, bachelors, policemen and traders who come from up-country to do their shopping.
"With the exception of street kids that are served on plastic plates for fear of stealing the breakable plates, other diners eat from breakables while using forks, an added attraction," said Mukwasi.
John Okwinyo, a night-watchman, who said food vending has been on for more than 10 years, added that these women do not leave until the wee hours of the morning when all their food has been sold. They also prepare breakfast for the early risers.
"When it gets very late, they reduce the price of food from sh1,000 to sh500 per plate," said Okwinyo.
Although it is not acceptable to have such business on the verandahs, Okwinyo said they let the vendors operate because most of them cannot afford to rent space in food vending markets.
"We sympathise with them and allow them to use the verandah because they have to earn a living. However, we also know that some operate from St. Balikuddembe, Shauri Yako and Nakasero markets during the day but only come here to sell what was left over to avoid losses," he said.
Most of the vendors reside in the city suburbs of Katwe, Kisenyi, Kibuye and Mengo.
Edison Kiwawulo, another customer, revealed that many retiring single shop attendants and Muslims from Isha prayers also eat from these vendors.
"So other than going home to start cooking in the night, these people prefer to eat here and just go home to sleep," Kiwawulo added.

Original_Search_Title: Hunting Down Porcupines to Earn a Living
Headline: Kenya: Hunting Down Porcupines to Earn a Living
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Mzee Nyambane Omae, 77, is a village hero at the Borabu settlement scheme in Nyamira district, a village at war with a small creature.
It is not the typical territorial conflict between angry peasant farmers on one side and elephants, lions, hyenas, baboons and leopards on the other.
At Borabu, Omae leads the war against the little porcupine that has defied battles waged by villagers for years.
Since colonial times, Omae has trapped and killed countless porcupines - the spiked nocturnal creatures that reap with impunity where man has sown. But there is no end in sight to the crop destruction menace of the porcupine in Borabu - Omae or no Omae.
The war on the porcupine in this village is fuelled by frustration. The villagers don't understand why the little creature, despite its destructive nature, is not viewed as game, like elephant and monkey, for purposes of compensation by the Government.
Mzee Nyambane Omae, 77, shows one of the porcupine traps he laid at a bush in Nyandoche village in Borabu division, Nyamira district .
Residents estimate that there are 35 to 40 porcupines per square kilometre in the hilly Borabu settlement scheme, capable of destroying three-quarters of an acre of crop in a month.
But, as the porcupines unleash terror on the main source of livelihood in Borabu, Mzee Omae keeps vigil, laying traps for the animals.
He started trapping the rodents way back in 1959. He ties wires to two flexible wattle branches in the hole where the porcupines live. He then seals all holes that don't lead to the trap with soil and stumps. Porcupines normally live in holes dug by ant bears.
"When the porcupine tries to get out of the hole, it is caught at the entrance by a stick I fix on the main trap, making it unable to move. It dies on the spot," says Mzee Omae, who also goes by the name Kerongo (Kisii for porcupine).
He says up to 10 porcupines stay in one hole and he can trap all of them on a good night. Sometimes he goes a full chilly night without trapping any. He instals up to 23 traps in a day in the vast settlement scheme.
The porcupines caught are of some use to Omae. "There are many uses such as medicine," he says, while others are "processed" into a concoction that is fed to livestock "to make them fierce at night in case rustlers strike". Part of the local lore has it that cows fed with the extracts from porcupine flesh cannot be stolen because they turn wild against rustlers attempting to drive them away.
Omae says it is taboo for a Kisii to eat porcupine meat. The liver of a porcupine, he adds, is used to treat some diseases. "Roast a porcupine's liver, crush it and mix it with either tea or porridge and you will be ok," he says. He uses the quills as treatment for other diseases.
The man is popular in the village. People make orders for the quills while those who want to protect their animals from rustlers want the meat.
But how did he get into this business? "It was just by chance. When I was in Nyahururu way back in the 1940s, I tamed 180 dogs which I was using in hunting. Whenever I killed a wild animal, I used sell it to a white man. I continued hunting but gave up when another white man invited me to this area to eradicate porcupines.
"When I came here, I had acquired skills from the Dorobo who used to live in Ng'arua forest and I used the skills to successfully tame the porcupines."
And he has never looked back. "I'm happy with my job. I have bought seven acres of land in Tindereti where my grand children stay and I pay my bills from this job. I'm happy with this profession," he says.
Mzee Omae is paid Sh250 for every porcupine he kills. But who will take over from him when age knocks him out of the business? "If I were to parade those that I have trained, you would be shocked; it's a whole school."
One of them is Peter Mairura Ngoge who says: "Mzee approached me and told me that he wanted to train me. I did not hesitate because I knew one day I would take over from him. When he is sick, I do the job alone."
The two say that last season, porcupines destroyed several acres of maize, cassava, tea, potatoes, sweet potatoes and other crops in Riamanoti, Matutu, Motagara, Mongori, Kijauri, Isoge, Tinderet, Mekenene and Nyandoche Ibere settlement schemes.
Former Kisii mayor Claire Omanga says that this season, the losses attributable to porcupines were immense.
Mzee Omae has also perfected skills in trapping monkeys, ant bears, moles, hawks, leopards, squirrels and cheetahs.
About porcupines
Porcupines are rodents best known for their quills, for self defence against predators.
When threatened, a porcupine will raise its quills. This is the piloerection reflex, the same as the goose bump reflex in humans. The quills are not poisonous, but can cause serious injury.
Male porcupines use urine to soften the females' quills before mating. They live in forests, deserts and grasslands.
Porcupines vary considerably in size: The African Porcupine can grow to well over 20 Kg and Rothschild's Porcupine of South America weighs less than a kilogramme. Most porcupines are about 60 to 90 cm long, with a 20 to 25 cm long tail. They are rounded, large and slow. (Source: Wikipedia)

Original_Search_Title: Fresh Harvests Force Commodity Prices Down
Headline: Uganda: Fresh Harvests Force Commodity Prices Down
Body_Text:
Kampala — COMMODITY prices have finally buckled for the first time in four months after fresh harvests started flooding the market.
All the city's main markets are now stocked with fresh supplies from the rural farmers, according to The New Vision weekly survey.
These have helped bring down the prices.
"Rains have brought about maturing and ripening of drought resistant crops. The farmers were also quick to plant vegetables as soon as the rains begun. That is why we now have excess supplies," said John Wasswa, a trader at Nakasero Market. Wasswa also attributed the fall in prices to the need by traders to dispose off old stocks and replenish with new ones.
Dry beans at St. Balikuddembe Market now cost sh950 a kilogramme, down from sh1,400 on average four months ago.
Fine maize flour is down by sh200 from sh1,000 a kilogramme.
Simsim and groundnuts previously sold at sh2,000 a kilogramme, have drastically fallen to sh1,200 a kilogramme. Cassava flour is down to sh500 a kilogramme from sh600.

Original_Search_Title: Swedish Embassy Avails US$500 000
Headline: Zimbabwe: Swedish Embassy Avails US$500 000
Body_Text:
Harare — THE Swedish Embassy has given US$500 000 to be used in HIV/Aids programmes in the Midlands province.
Swedish Ambassador to Zimbabwe Mr Sten Rylander handed the money to the Midlands Aids Service Organisation (MASO) to assist in lessening the effects of drought and HIV and Aids on vulnerable households and communities. In a statement, the Swedish Embassy said the money would enable MASO to provide training in agriculture, HIV and Aids-related issues and to supply farming inputs. Beneficiaries of the funding would be assisted to grow sweet potatoes and cassava.
"It is important that the food insecurity of households and communities affected by drought and HIV and Aids be addressed from a multi-dimensional perspective. It is not enough to look at mitigation from a health perspective alone, which explains Sweden's continued support to these important interventions," said Mr Rylander.

Original_Search_Title: Francophone CIG Join Ntungka Women Against Poverty
Headline: Cameroon: Francophone CIG Join Ntungka Women Against Poverty
Body_Text:
A Francophone Common Initiative Group, GIC-PAB Bogso, based in Eseka, Centre Province, has joined its counterpart based in Bamenda to combat poverty.
Led by an elderly woman Teclaire Ntomb, GIC-PAB Bogso exchanged and shared ideas and experiences with the Ntungka Women Common Initiative Group recently.
GIC-PAB Bogso demonstrated the importance of cassava to the Bamenda women. Ntomb displayed biscuits, garri cakes and so on made from cassava to demonstrate how useful the crop is.
She said these products were in high demand in Europe and America and called on the women to take up cassava production seriously.Ntungka Women, led by Mrs. Veronica Morfaw, in turn, enriched their Francophone counterparts with practical lessons on the production of soap (Omo) and dyeing.
For three days the women exchanged ideas all aimed at completely eradicating poverty from their midst.Addressing her guests, fellow Ntungka women and sympathisers at the end of the exercise, Mrs. Morfaw recalled the tremendous efforts she had made to empower the fellow women folk.
She, however, regretted that despite her good intentions some of the women gave the impression that she was using them. She promised to do her best for her women folk despite what her detractors said.
Ntomb, on her part, called on fellow women to rally behind Mrs. Morfaw and benefit from grassroots women organisations. She also advised the women to live by their African tradition and shun gossip and individualism.
The Coordinator of GIC-PAB Bogso, Elvis Banlock, said rural women won the first prize in cassava products in Germany. Banlock said there is a bank in his area that helps parents to pay school fees and patients to buy drugs through cassava production.

Original_Search_Title: Gov Obi to Disburse N4m to Farmers
Headline: Nigeria: Gov Obi to Disburse N4m to Farmers
Body_Text:
The Anambra state government is to disburse a N4 million grant to farms for the processing of root crops and tubers. Gov. Peter Obi, who announced the package at an interactive session with the Anambra state apex farmers Organisation, said in Awka on Tuesday that the gesture would boost food production.
He said adequate arrangements would be made to ensure the distribution of fertilisers and other farm inputs to genuine farmers.
Obi promised to accord priority to agriculture and industrialisation in order to create jobs for unemployed youths in the state.
Responding, the organisation's chairman, Chief Dan Maduka, appealed to government to offset the balance of funds due to the cassava mosaic disease programme of the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture.
Such an action would enable the farmers to benefit from the N400 million agricultural inputs of the institute. He said former Gov. Chris Ngige had contributed N10 million to the fund.

Original_Search_Title: Hope On the Horizon But Needs Still Enormous
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Hope On the Horizon for Democratic Republic of Congo - But Needs Still Enormous - Over $50 Million Required for Agricultural Assistance Activities
Publisher: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (Rome)
Body_Text:
Rome — With nearly 80 percent of its population trapped in extreme poverty and more than 70 percent undernourished, the Democratic Republic of Congo faces enormous challenges.
Agriculture, which supports two-thirds of the population, will play a key role in the country's future economic growth and poverty reduction efforts, according to FAO.
As part of the 2006 Action Plan for the DR Congo recently launched by the UN and its humanitarian partners, FAO is appealing for over $50 million in funding to support its emergency-related agricultural activities in the country.
Agriculture another casualty of conflict
Years of conflict have left over four million dead in the DR Congo, with 1 200 continuing to die every day from violence, disease and malnutrition. More than 1.7 million people remain displaced, and an additional 1.7 million have recently returned to their communities and are trying to re-establish their homes and livelihoods.
Agriculture has suffered tremendously, according to Anne M. Bauer, Director, FAO Emergency Operations and Rehabilitation Division.
"Instability in rural areas has led to an almost total breakdown of the food security situation," Bauer said. "Access to fields remains unsafe, especially for women. Rural feeder roads are almost non-existent, which hinders commercialization and distribution of local products, and the inferior quality of seed stocks and lack of basic tools make agricultural work difficult."
Looking ahead
But, as the country prepares for its first elections in 45 years, hope may be on the horizon.
"After years of war, mismanagement and widespread chronic poverty, these elections are an unprecedented opportunity for the Congolese to establish a legitimate authority committed to poverty reduction and food security," said Bauer. "The support of the humanitarian community is crucial, and FAO, with the continued generosity of donors, will play an important role in helping the country move forward by restoring rural livelihoods and helping vulnerable people meet their nutritional needs."
This year's Action Plan stems from a comprehensive multi-sector needs assessment carried out across DR Congo, the first of its kind.
FAO's proposed activities address malnutrition, support families affected by HIV/AIDS, assist in the reintegration of returnees as well as ex-combatants and promote the coordination of emergency agriculture operations, including distribution of seeds and tools and seed multiplication, and the strengthening of food security information. Other projected activities seek to rehabilitate infrastructure, including rural roads, support a rapid response capacity through pre-positioning of strategic stocks of agricultural inputs, and promote marketing of agricultural products.
In 2005, FAO emergency projects benefited 450 000 vulnerable Congolese households through input distribution, promotion of backyard animal rearing and fish farming, and feeder road rehabilitation.
As part of its longer-term development initiatives, FAO is working to eradicate cassava mosaic, a disease that has contributed to the 20 percent decline in cassava production in the country over the past 10 years. The organization has also provided assistance in preparing a new forestry code for the country and is working to rehabilitate agricultural research centres and strengthen the Ministry of Agriculture's policy-making capacities.

Original_Search_Title: The Error of 1914, Lugard and the Tragedy of Nigeria
Headline: Nigeria: The Error of 1914, Lugard and the Tragedy of Nigeria
Body_Text:
This essay by Chinweizu explains that Lugardism is a false framework, and Lugardist states like Nigeria are merely aping the real essence of nationhood, especially in the face of what is presently going on in Nigeria.
Continues from last Tuesday
On Welcoming Predators (In memory of Lobengula who welcomed in Cecil Rhodes) With open arms. He welcomed a smiling tiger into his home; With open jaws the tiger welcomed him into his belly. After all, smiled the beast, One good welcome deserves another.
OBJ has already instigated a shift of farmland from cassava for local consumption to cassava for export. Predictably, this short-sighted policy will lead to mass starvation like the Irish Potato Famine in the 19th century. But what country today can welcome 100 million starving refugees from Nigeria? The economy is already in ruins. All in all, the Haitification of Nigeria is almost completed. The large oil revenue is all that masks the full extent of the economic and social disaster. As for the exodus of desperate Nigerians, it has been on for sometime now: the members of the economically destroyed middle class have been "checking out" to seek employment elsewhere; young women have been escaping to Europe to engage in prostitution; able-bodied young men have been stowing away or paying human trafficking syndicates to smuggle them into Europe. Thus, Nigeria has managed to go very far on the same road, almost reaching, in just 45 years, where it took Haiti 200 years to reach.
If OBJnomics persists and gets entrenched, it can't be long before the exodus of desperate Nigerians reaches Haitian proportions.
So long as we remain trapped on the Haiti Highway, if you want to see Nigeria's future, just look at Haiti today. The longer Nigeria continues to exist, the greater the disaster it will bring upon the Nigerian population.Thesis #5: African Power
I have presented a brief picture of where we have arrived after a century in the prisoner-of-war camp that is Nigeria, and of what our prospects are if we stay in it.
I have tried to clarify that "Slave Trade" was race war; Colonialism was race war; Neo-colonialism is race war; Racism is race war; AIDS in Africa is Race war; Pressing GM crops on starving Africa is race war; Imperialism in Africa is race war; Arabism in Africa is race war; the conflict in Sudan is race war; the chaos in Haiti is race war; Africa in debt trap is race war!
It should be clear that our disasters over the centuries have been caused by African powerlessness. (See The Destruction of Black Civilization by Chancellor Williams; and Two Thousand Seasons by Ayi Kwei Armah). Therefore, if we want our disasters to end, we need to build African power. Like Marcus Garvey said: "The only protection against INJUSTICE in man is POWER--physical, financial and scientific." If we want to survive the race war, we must, like Garvey also told us, "create for ourselves a political superstate, . . . (one) strong enough to lend protection to the members of our race scattered all over the world, and to compel the respect of the nations and races of the earth." But where do we start, where do we start? I would say, lets start with awareness of the essentials. (The principal book to read on African Power is Philosophy and Opinions of Marcus Garvey).
For a millennium or more, the ultimate source of all our disasters has been the abject and chronic powerlessness of Africa. We must therefore pose the problem of African power, and do so starkly and sharply, without evasions. We must solve the problem of African power with rigor and vigour or we shall be exterminated in this 21st century. We must face up to the fact that the AU, NEPAD, the UN, Liberalism, Humanism, Marxology, Christianity, Islam, etc are antithetical to African Power. We must realize that the development we need is not the development of African consumerism or contractorism, but the development of African Power. And the key to that development is Afro-modernization, the project of putting African culture and societies on an industrialized foundation. And we must accept that, for industrialization, Bourgeois democracy, Sharia, Christianity, Islam, Humanism, Arabization, Europeanization etc., are, at best, wholly irrelevant or decoys. Industrialization is the o nly key. That's the secret behind the power today of the Chinese, Japanese, Europeans, Americans, Russians, Indians etc.
The question now is: If you don't want to continue to be ground down, and to end up like the people of Haiti in your life time; if you don't want your children and grand children to be exterminated by the end of this century, like what happened to the Native North Americans in the 19th century, and to the indigenous population of Haiti in the 16th century.
What is to be done?
In bringing about change, the first thing is awareness. Like Noam Chomsky said:
Begin with awareness; you don't do anything without awareness, obviously--you don't do anything unless you're aware that there is something that ought to be done, so that's the beginning almost by definition.
But what kind of awareness, specifically, do we most need in Nigeria today? I would suggest we follow the advice of Sun Tzu, the ancient Chinese sage and master strategist who said: "Know yourself, know your enemy, and in a hundred battles you will never be defeated." That's a good place to start, by educating ourselves about Nigeria, about Africa and its history, its place in the world and how that has come about.
We must especially become fully aware of the aspects of the race war in the 21st century. These include:
1] The AIDS bombing of Africa, by white (European) power, a covert attack that is exterminating Black Africans all over the continent.
2] The wars of ethnic cleansing and extermination to expropriate the Nile basin that the Arab League is waging on Africans.
3] The economic war by debt trap, unfair trade, genetically modified crops, globalization, etc, being inflicted by the European powers.
4] The cultural warfare through Europeanization, Christianization, Arabization and Islamization of Africans.
5] The intra-African proxy wars being orchestrated by white powers from afar, like those in Sierra Leone, Liberia, Cote d'Ivoire, the Congo, Uganda etc.
But there are a few other things we need to know:
6] The fact that most Africans today are Christians or Moslems and culturally schizoid, the fact that we are conducting this lecture in English, are the results of our ancestors' defeats in the race war; defeats that made possible the culturecide and cultural dementia inflicted on us in the 20th century.
7] Lugardism, the AU, NEPAD etc, are not just irrelevant decoys; they are indeed among the prime obstacles to our building African Power. For instance, what good is a NEPAD development that is preparing you for powerlessness and extermination?
8] The problem of the 21st century is the problem of African Power - how to build it, and enough of it, to end the long era of our defeats and disasters in the race war, to prevent our extermination, and to ensure our dignity. If we are not building African power we are doing worse than nothing, and we might as well be dead already.
9] To learn how African Power can be built, we need to investigate how Japanese power, Chinese power, Soviet power and Indian power were built in the 19th and 20th centuries, against determined opposition from European White power.
Lugardism, UN Imperialism and the prospect of African power, being a public lecture delivered at the Agip Recital Hall, Muson Centre, Onikan, Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: FG Spends N8.6bn On First Phase of Food Security Programme
Headline: Nigeria: FG Spends N8.6bn On First Phase of Food Security Programme
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo has disclosed that the Federal Government solely spent about N8.599 billion ($76.45 million) during the first phase of the National Programme for Food Security aimed at ensuring the availability of food and alleviating poverty in the country.
The President, who made the figure known yesterday in his address during the second donors' meeting for the second phase of the National Programme for Food Security yesterday, said as a result of the increased interest shown for the programme, he has "directed that the number of sites be tripled in the expansion phase from the covered 109 sites to 327 sites in order to reach the beneficiaries".
Linking the importance of the programme to meeting the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) , he noted that his "unequivocal commitment to this programme right from the inception of this administration in 1999 is not in doubt. The Federal Government has solely financed the implementation of the programme in the first phase to the tune of $67.45 million".
He however added that "given the increased scope for financing, the need thus arises for the involvement of all stakeholders including Federal, State and Local Governments as well as you donors. We all have a stake in realizing more achievements and impact under the programme. The type of assistance expected from you donors for this project could be loans or grants for technical assistance and human capacity development. Any assistance will be highly appreciated", he added.
While thanking donors for indicating interest to provide assistance to the programme and the resolve of the Federal Government to ensure its success, President Obasanjo stated that he believes "that if we all work together, strategise together, share ideas and support this initiative with enthusiasm, Nigeria will achieve the objectives of the MDGs on poverty alleviation and food security before the target date of 2015".
He said that achievements recorded in the first phase of the programme are "the substantial increase in land area cultivated through rain-fed and irrigated farming and the more than 10,000mt of assorted vegetables produced on annual basis; the increase in cassava yield from about 10mt/ha to about 16.5mt/ha; the incremental rearing of over 50,000 layer birds each producing about 12 crates of eggs per laying circle; and the incremental construction of over 500 ponds for aquaculture fisheries".
The President also assured that the Federal Government "is fully committed to executing this programme with success. We are open to suggestions, ideas, resources, methods of work and other forms of support to ensure that the execution of this expanded project becomes a model for Africa and beyond. Your contributions will be handled with due transparency, accountability and standard international programme management procedures after due consultations with each donor/partner".

Original_Search_Title: Islamic Banks Agree to Fund Food Security Programme
Headline: Nigeria: Islamic Banks Agree to Fund Food Security Programme
Body_Text:
President Olusegun Obasanjo yestrerday in Abuja said that three banks located within the Islamic world have are willing to assist Nigeria in its National Programme for Food Security (NPFS).
Speaking at the opening of the second donors meeting for the expansion of the programme, Obasanjo named the Islamic Development Bank (IDB) as one of the donors that have indicated interest in the programme.
He also said that the Arab Bank for Economic Development in Africa and the Kuwait Fund have also indicated interest to participate in the programme.
Other financing agencies, he said, included the Africa Development Bank (ADB) and the International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD), while the Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO), would continue to provide the technical assistance for the programme.
The president said more than 67 million dollars has already been spent on the first phase of the programme covering all the 109 senatorial districts in the country, adding that the current efforts to expand the scope of the programme is borne out of the steady successes that have been recorded in the implementation of the first phase of the programme covering 109 sites, explaining that the second phase would increase the sites to 327.
Obasanjo said under the first phase, "a substantial increase in land area cultivated," was recorded through both rain-fed and irrigated farming with more than 10,000 tonnes of assorted vegetables produced annually.
Furthermore, he said cassava production shot up to 16.5 tonnes per hectare with an incremental rearing of over 50,000 layers of birds each producing 12 crates of eggs per laying circle and the construction of 500 additional fish ponds in the target locations.

Original_Search_Title: Gains of Obasanjo's Visit, By Aliero
Headline: Nigeria: Gains of Obasanjo's Visit, By Aliero
Body_Text:
Lagos — Governor Muhammad Adamu Aliero of Kebbi State, has said President Olusegun Obasanjo's visit to the state will mark the state's final entry into the category of economically viable and educationally advantaged states of the Federation.
His Senior Special Assistant on Media and Publicity, Abdullahi Idris Zuru, said the visit, which will be the fifth by Obasanjo since 1999, and scheduled for between Sunday 9 and Tuesday 11, will witness the commissioning of another set of economically viable projects.
Aliero said since inception, his administration's policies and programmes were focussed towards providing enabling environment for educational agro-allied industries and tourism in development for both citizens and state socio-economic benefits.
He said with what have been achieved so far in the area of road construction, water and electricity supply, educational and agricultural development, improved health-care services, establishment of industries and above all, peaceful co-existence, he has almost reached the promised land.
He said after attending the coronation ceremony of the Emir of Gwandu, Alhaji Muhammad Iliyasu Bashar, Obasanjo will commission three of the constructed 11 inter-city roads and Kebbi Airport, which will serve as smooth gateways to the state by roads and air for tourism and industrial development.
He said Obasanjo will commission Kebbi Cassava Processing Factory, Kalgo, inspect site for the production of rice, fisheries, fruits and poultry for agro allied industries in Warrah, and launch the second phase of the Kebbi Agricultural Transformation and Self-Help Initiative (KATASHI), for the engagement of 35,000 farmers in all-year-round rice production.
Aliero said the projects, in addition to the ones commissioned in 2002, will empower the citizens economically, attract investors, and make the state another centre for profit making ventures.

Original_Search_Title: New Roads, Airport Will Bring Dev to Kebbi - Aliero
Headline: Nigeria: New Roads, Airport Will Bring Dev to Kebbi - Aliero
Body_Text:
Kebbi state government has said that the newly constructed intercity roads as well as the airport will contribute to tourism and industrial development of the state.
The state governor, Muhammed Adamu Aliero Abuja, that the projects would emp-ower the citizens economically and that it would attract inve-stors in the area of tourism and industrial development.
The governor also said the commissioning of the projects which would mark the 5th presidential visit to the state would also put the state into the final category of been economically viable and educationally advantaged states in the country.
According to him, Pres-ident Olusegun Obasanjo while in the state is expected to commission Kebbi cassava processing factory and laun-ched the second phase of the Kebbi Agricultural Transfor-mation and Self-Help Initiative (KATASHI) that would engage 35,000 farmers in all-year-round rice production.
He added that his adminis-tration had since inception embarked on policies and programmes that were focu-sed towards providing enabl-ing environment for educat-ional, agro-allied industries and tourism in development for both the citizens and state socio-economic benefits.
In a related development, the state governor has also slated next week for the official coronation ceremony of the emir of Gwandu, Alhaji Muhammad Iliyasu Bashar in office.
In a statement signed by the senior special assistant on media and publicity to the governor, Abdullahi Idris Zuru, and made available to Daily Trust the coronation ceremony is expected to be chaired by President Olus-egun Obasanjo before proc-eeding to commissioning of projects across the state.

Original_Search_Title: Food Prices Remain High
Headline: Uganda: Food Prices Remain High
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE prices of foodstuffs and basic commodities have remained high, The New Vision weekly survey, has indicated.
In Kampala's major markets of Nakawa, Nakasero, St. Balikuddembe,
Nateete and Kalerwe, the price of a bar of soap has remained at between
sh800 and sh1,000.
Maize flour is still at between sh800 and sh1,200 while cassava flour was at sh600 per kilo, the same as last week.
A kilo of salt has also remained at sh500 and groundnuts and simsim
go for between sh1,800 and sh2,000, unchanged from last week's rates.
Beans is at between sh1,100 and sh1,500 and sugar at sh1,500 a kilo,
the same as last week.

Original_Search_Title: President Obasanjo Rallies Support for Africa's Fertilizer Needs
Headline: Nigeria: President Obasanjo Rallies Support for Africa's Fertilizer Needs
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo on March 30 participated in an Eminent
Persons Advisory Group Meeting of the African Fertilizer Summit in New
York, the United States.
Addressing the Forum, President Obasanjo called on the eminent
persons with vast knowledge, experience, commitment, vision and
extensive networks to help in this quest for "Feeding Africa."
In his paper titled "Feeding Africa: A Call for Action to Meet
Africa's Fertilizer Challenge", President Obasanjo said increasing the
health of Africa's drained soils with both organic and mineral
fertilizers will be a key component to achieving the African Green
Revolution.
"Thanks to the work of national and international research centres
we already have high-yielding varieties of our key crops. We have
cassava varieties that can yield 40 tons per hectare, and maize
varieties that are resistant to our pests. But these crops yield well
only if nutrients are available in the soil to feed them. African soils
are impoverished due to poor inherent soil fertility in some places,
and to high population pressure that forces overuse of the soil in
others", he said.
Other eminent persons present at the gathering were Nobel laureate
Dr Norman Borlang and African Union Commission Chairman, Dr Alpha Omar
Konare.
The President later visited the New York Headquarters of the
Rockefellar Foundation, sponsors of the Summit, where Dr Judith Rodin,
Presient of the Foundation, welcomed him.

Original_Search_Title: Flour Millers Parley On Achieving 10% Cassava Flour Policy
Headline: Nigeria: Flour Millers Parley On Achieving 10% Cassava Flour Policy
Body_Text:
Members of Flour Milliers Association of Nigeria, the umbrella
body for flour millers operating in the country, are putting heads
together in order to work out concrete plans for ensuring the
successful implementation of the 10 per cent cassava flour inclusion in
bread baking policy with effect from July 1 2006.
The one day forum, tagged: "Sustainable sourcing of high quality
cassava flour for the realisation of the 10 per cent inclusion policy
in the flour milling industry," will among other things focus on
examining the technological and investment options for processing and
incorporation of high quality cassava flour as the fastest route for
establishing the required processing capacity and quality in the
country.
Stakeholders would also deliberate on the promotion and ways by
which the funding requirements can be met and the supply chain
logistics optimally developed for the benefit of all stakeholders.
Gathering at the banquet hall of the Gateway Hotel Abeokuta Ogun
State, stakeholders at the forum will include cassava growers, small
and medium scale cassava processors, commodity suppliers, produce
promoters, banks and financial institutions, universities and research
institutes, investors, equipment fabricators, bankers representatives,
representatives of government and relevant agencies.
Expected guests at the forum include the Ogun State Governor Otunba
Gbenga Daniel, Alhaji Aliko Dangote, the Honourable Minister for
Industry, the Special Adviser to the President on Food Security Mrs.
A.O. Olatunji, Dr. L.O. Sanni of the International Institute for
Tropical Agriculture (IITA) and other delegates from the Manufacturers
Association of Nigeria (MAN) the Standards Organization of Nigeria
(SON) and the National Agency for Food Drugs Administration and Control
(NAFDAC)
Already the Flour Miller Association of Nigeria has reiterated its
support for the federal government's drive for the 10 per cent
inclusion of cassava flour in bread and has noted that the
implementation of the project at the maximum 10 per cent flour
inclusion level would only consume about 3.5 per cent of the estimated
annual production of cassava tubers in Nigeria based on a production
estimate of 40m mt of cassava tubers per annum.
In addition, the association has also noted that if the project is
properly organised, the policy should pose no threat to the
availability of staple foods from cassava and therefore should not be a
basis for price speculation.
The association has also commended the efforts of other stakeholders
such as the Cassava Growers Association of Nigeria, the Cassava
Processors Association of Nigeria, the Cassava Equipment Fabrication
and Maintenance Association of Nigeria, the International Institute of
Tropical Agriculture and other government agencies for the progress so
far made to partner with the federal and state government in boosting
cassava production and processing in Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: Calling On Lovers of Obafemi Awolowo Varsity
Headline: Nigeria: Calling On Lovers of Obafemi Awolowo Varsity
Body_Text:
Lagos — Good food and good music, supplied by a life band, were in abundance. The crowd was small, but its weight, heavy. The Guest of Honour was the Managing Director of Oceanic Bank, Dr. Cecilia Ibru. President of NACCIMA, Chief John Adeyemi; Olorogun Sonny Kuku, other Managing and Executive Directors of Banks, Insurance and paint companies, were there. So were the principal officers of the Obafemi Awolowo University (OAU), Ile-Ife, led by the Vice Chancellor, Prof. Roger Makanjuola.
The dinner party was organised by two brothers, Messrs. Segun and Femi Aina for the alumni and friends of OAU, who, as the letter of invitation read, "have distinguished themselves in public life and brought great credit to the country."
Although the guests made merry, the theme of the party, 'Restoring the Beauty of Great Ife', showed it was not just about merry-making. The dinner, recently held at Segun Aina's Parkview Estate, Lagos residence, was in aid of the OAU Renovation Project.
In a power-point presentation, which included photographs personally taken by him, Makanjuola painted a picture of what was once described as 'Africa's most beautiful campus'. The summary, in his words: "Ife buildings are a disgrace."
He noted that, "Ife is renowned for the beauty of its horticulture and its architecture. The unique beauty and serenity of the environment has always been a major factor in attracting staff and students and in providing a conducive atmosphere for teaching and learning. However, the beauty of its buildings is gradually being lost as a result of deterioration. They are desperately in need of renovation." The project is no mean task because it covers every one of the university's buildings.
The reputation of the university, the Vice Chancellor stressed, goes before it. The structures must therefore not be left to deteriorate any further. Beginning with the lap top computer that he used for his presentation, a locally-built Omatek brand, he said, OAU now buys only Omatek computers.
