The company's Managing Director/Chief Executive, Mrs. Florence Seriki is an Alumnus of the university. Ife also has to its credit, pioneered kidney transplantation in the country, separation of Siamese twins and development of new crop varieties. There are also the commercial and prototype yam pounders, while its Cassava Processing Machine, which produces 800kg of cassava flour an hour, won an award at the first universities fair, organised by the National Universities Commission (NUC) in 2004. OAU came an overall second both the 2004 and last year's fairs. It has also been designated as the foremost research university in the country by the NUC. Makanjuola said the university's Centra l Science Laboratory is the first of its kind in West Africa. By no means the least, the only Nobel Laureate that the country has so far produced, Prof. Wole Soyinka came from Ife and he is back there as Professor Emeritus. The school has also produced 12 National Merit award winners.
Makanjuola said the management was determined to build on these achievements through the Innovative Strategic Plan for 2004 - 2008, part of which is the renovation project.
The Plan includes "realistic strategies to promote excellence in the field of teaching and research and in the services we provide to the nation. Innovative financial strategies have been put in place to ensure that the university's financial resources are properly managed and that the institution becomes self-sustaining and less dependent on government funding."
He commended the Alumni's support for, among others, serving as watch dog over academic, moral and social standards; effective intervention in crises and developing the university, the most recent being the construction of a 250-capacity hostel for female students.
So far, the University, Oduduwa and Fajuyi Halls, Faculties of Education, Law, Social Science and Administration, Hezekiah Oluwasanmi Library, as well as the Biological (Health) Sciences block have been renovated. Makanjola, who reeled out the names of those who made these possible, including details of their contribution, ex tempore, added that all donors' names will be inscribed on a roll of honour.
But so much more still needs to be done. The other Biological Sciences buildings, Murtala Mohammed Postgraduate Hall and the one Makanjuola described as the 'most beautiful building on campus', the African Studies Building, among others, are all crying for at least a touch. He pleaded, "We humbly solicit for the help of every Alumnus and every friend of the university. The cost is great; (but) we will succeed with your support. We are determined.
On how this can be done, he said donors may select the building they wish to renovate. They can also award the contracts and make payments directly or give the university the money to do the job, using local contractors, including three paint manufacturers that university does business with.
In a brief remark, Mrs. Ibru commended the efforts of the university and its alumni, and pledged her bank's support for the renovation project.
Curiously, the chief host, Mr. Segun Aina is not an alumnus of OAU. The Second Vice President, Chartered Institute of Bankers of Nigeria (CIBN) attended the Universities of Lagos and Ibadan. He explained that, "First, I have had a lot of interaction with Ife, especially in the last 10 years. I am a native of Otan Ayegbaju in Osun State, where the university is situated. I am also the Chairman of the Board of Trustees of the UNDP Human Development Fund for Osun State and we have invited the OAU Centre for Industrial Research to partner with us." Not only has the Fund appointed OAU its consultant on the development of Cassava, the Osun State government has also signed a memorandum of understanding on Agriculture with the university. Aina said he got involved when he became aware of the OAU renovation project and remains committed to any course that aims at the delivery of good education to Nigerians. The dinner was therefore organised to invite more people, including those who did not attend, to come forward and support the university. Former Vice Chancellor, Prof. Wale Omole commended Makanjuola for his commitment to the development of OAU. Although his term expires on May 4, the Vice Chancellor, who is also not an alumnus (he read Medicine at the University of Ibadan) has shown great commitment to the development of OAU and is determined to see the renovation project through before his exit. He therefore called for support to help the VC achieve his goal.
Although most donors asked to remain anonymous, along with their contributions, funds raised at the dinner ran into millions of naira as guests made donations, either in their private or corporate capacities, or both.

Original_Search_Title: Peace Pact Paying Dividends
Headline: Sudan Peace Pact Paying Dividends
Body_Text:
Nairobi — To most of the outside world, Sudan brigs to mind images a nation at war with itself - unstable, dry and poor.
But to the Sudanese and others who have been there, the largest country in Africa is endowed with vast untapped riches in minerals, water, wildlife, forests, agriculture and oil.
According to the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank, Sudan's economy is now one fastest growing in the world. It grew at an average annual rate of six per cent during 1997-2004, almost double the that for Sub-Saharan Africa as a whole, and the growth is projected to reach 10 per cent this year.
As one potential investor put it, the country is among the few in world with three types of gold - black (oil), green (fertile land) and the real precious metal itself. Southern Sudan - which is the size of Kenya, Uganda, Tanzania, Rwanda and Burundi combined - is mostly jungle with no roads and idle land. Its 12 million people are concentrated in towns.
The larger Sudan has a population of 40 million. It measures 2.5 million square kilometres and is rich in iron core, copper, uranium, chronium ore, zinc, tungsten, mica, silver, gold and hydro power.
Years of fighting
It is this vast wealth that is now the focus of efforts to attract investors. Since the signing of a comprehensive peace agreement between the Sudan People's Liberation Movement/Army with President Omar El Bashir's Government ended a three-decade civil war early last year, the country has been putting in place structures for the reconstruction of the South, ravaged by years of fighting.
Developing the infrastructure is a key priority for the Government of South Sudan installed as part of the peace deal. The few roads in the area are dilapidated and unused for most of the year. Air is most preferred transport, and often the only, in Southern Sudan. For example, Greater Yirol, a region with 400,000 people, has nine airstrips.
Juba is the seat of the South Sudan Government led by Salva Kiir is no better. Although it was the only major town in the area that remained under Khartoum government control during the war, areas that surround it have no paved roads, water, telephone or electricity.
But at a recent investors conference in Nairobi, Southern Sudanese officials reassured investors that the peace pact was paying dividends.
In the deal, former rebels SPLM/A and Khartoum government agreed on equal sharing of oil revenues from the South, ending a 21 year war. The two sides also agreed on security, religion, power sharing and other issues.
Although, the focus was on oil, South Sudan information minister Samson Kwaje said there many opportunities and incentives up for grabs by businessmen who invest in Southern Sudan.
Already, institutions like the courts, executive, parliament, central bank and police have been established and are coming up with incentives and guarantees for investors.
That the Nairobi conference was well attended is a sign of increasing confidence in the South Sudan economy. Organised by the Bread of Life Africa (Bola), an non-governmental organisation involved in the economic development in the Horn of Africa region, the meeting attracted hundreds of international investors.
Mr Malei Nthiwa, the programme director of Bola, said the meeting was to showcase investment opportunities available for investors from across eastern Africa, particularly in the gas oil, gas and power sectors.
Other opportunities are agriculture. The eternal River Nile and its tributaries feed a vast area suitable for farming. The main products include cotton, groundnuts, sorghum, millet, wheat, gum arabic, sugarcane, cassava, mangoes, papaya, bananas, tobacco, rice, tea and coffee.
Fish is in plenty in Sudan's 129,810 square kilometres of water while livestock population stand at 135 million.
Given that agric-business is well developed in Kenya, one would have expected a lot of interest from local investors in the opportunities Sudan offered. But only a few Kenyan companies turned up for the conference at Safari Park Hotel, painting a grim picture of a country that played a key role in bringing peace deal to Sudan.
Since the signing of the peace deal, Kenyan businesses seem to have taken a wait and see attitude towards investing in Southern Sudan, mostly because of the negative perception about the area's potential to give returns.
Most of investors at the conference were representatives of companies based outside the region, including South African nationals and Nigerians, who paid Sh150,000 each to take part in the two-day meeting.
Organisers said there was a lot of interest in oil, perceived as having faster returns than areas such as farming, which Sudan's Agriculture and Forestry minister Martin Lomuro described as having an "unequivocal high potential."
World Bank lead economist for Ethiopia and Sudan Jeni Klugman says agriculture is the core of the Sudan's economy and the country could become a large-scale exporter to neighbouring countries.
Conducive policies were however needed as well as infrastructure, extension services and markets, she said.
She saw other opportunities in service delivery and in implementation of reconstruction projects such as roads, energy and water supply. Sudan has received about Sh70 billion for development since the peace deal.
Some US $ 103.1 million (about Sh7 billion) was given in 2005 and US$ 240 million (about Sh18 billion) through a development trust fund.
"Lessons of aid patterns in post-conflict force us to look at sustainability and private investment to sustain peace as early as possible," she said.
She said rising oil revenue was also enabling financing of the implementation of the CPA.
"But challenges of increasing growth and opportunities, and access to basic infrastructure and services, loom large for traditionally marginalised areas of the country," she said.
Ms Klugman urged Mr Kiir's government to prioritise setting of conducive policies and laws for investment as well as building local capacity for development.
Huge amount of money
A case in point was cited in banking. According to Kenya's ambassador to Sudan, retired colonel Elijah Matibo, Kenya Commercial Bank had been asked to deposit a huge amount of money with the Sudan Central Bank before being allowed to operate in Juba.
In Kenya, he said, only about US$5 million (Sh400 million) was required to open a bank compared to US$25 million (about Sh2 billion) in Sudan.
On a more upbeat note, Kenya Pipeline Company managing director George Okungu said peace in Sudan will change the region's oil market. He said the nearest oil well is only 100 km from the border with Kenya and most of the 1.2 billion barrels of oil reserves were in the area. Kenya was therefore best placed to provide Sudan with access to the eastern Africa market.
Kapoeta area in Southern Sudan, which is rich in oil, is only 1,020km from Mombasa compared to Port Sudan (4500 km). The corporation, he said, would take advantage of its Kenya's proximity and invest in a crude oil pipeline from Southern Sudan to Lamu.
Ambassador Matibo described Southern Sudan lacked industries and Kenyans could tap from their close relationship with people of Southern Sudan and living styles to sale products manufactured locally there.
The envoy proposed that a warehouse and shopping mall be established in Juba to showcase products from Kenya.
Kenyans also stand to benefit from employment opportunities in the area including in education, health, information communication technology and banking.
Building houses and schools
Col Matibo said a Kenyan consulate had been opened in Juba to boost trade.
Mr Nthiwa said potential investments for Kenyans included infrastructure and reconstruction, building houses, schools and hospitals. Thousands of unemployed teachers in Kenya could also be absorbed in Sudan "as the country needs thousands."
Kenya National Chamber of Commerce and Industry First vice-chairman Laban Onditi said about 18,000 Kenyans were already doing business in Southern Sudan.
"They are mainly in roads and supply services. Some are running hardware shops, others restaurants while a number are providing transport services," Mr Onditi said.
Sudan ambassador to Kenya Abdelrhman Sharfi said there was also massive potential for investment in railways, telecommunication, ports and health.
Planning minister Henry Obwocha said peace in Southern Sudan will enable Kenya benefit from its oil resources.
He said countries in the region - except Sudan - import 3.5 million tonnes of petroleum products a year, spending about Sh80 billion.
Some of this money would make a big difference to the region's economy.

Original_Search_Title: OVOP to Be Integrated Into MGDs
Headline: Malawi: OVOP to Be Integrated Into MGDs
Body_Text:
Lilongwe — Local Government and Rural Development Deputy Minister Ernest
Malenga has said activities initiated by One Village one Product (OVOP)
will be integrated into the Malawi Growth and Development Strategy
(MGDS). "Both OVOP and Malawi Growth strategy aims at empowering the
rural communities; enhancing the economic growth of the country," he
said.
He was speaking last Tuesday when OVOP Secretariat held a daylong
sensitization and a presentation of its progress report to members of
district assemblies, which were drawn across the country.
Malenga said the assemblies' role is quite crucial because as his
ministry is building capacity for OVOP, assemblies educate people what
OVOP is all about, saying these are the same because the objective is
the same.
Malenga told journalists that many things are in the pipeline and
the country is going to see a lot of progress in small industries and
small productions related to OVOP and thereby empowering the rural
communities.
OVOP Board Member Professor Kanyama Phiri said initially, OVOP had
295 applications out of which 139 were approved out of these only 48
were funded. "This is because our capacity at the moment is still low
but there are prospects that we shall fund all the approved projects as
soon as funding has increased," he said.
He said both JICA and the government are committed to fund the
projects. "It is very important for people to keep on sending
applications because Malawi is big, sometimes the project that we have
funded cannot cover some areas where we are yet to reach so it is very
important that they should continue and follow these guidelines," he
said.
Programme Advisor who is also a JICA expert K. Matsushima said of
the 295 proposals only 139 projects, which were subsequently approved
followed the OVOP concept.
He said two of the projects had two small groups that requested huge
investments requiring over K2million with an average request per person
estimated at K152, 000 although they had no experience.
Matsushima also said 93% of the proposals are new businesses while others requested for high technology processing equipments.
In his analysis of the projects to the assembly members, he said
four projects lacked community initiative, had political interference,
experienced pressure from government officials and had imposition of
leaders by influential individuals in the communities.
During the opening of the workshop, Hiroyuki Moronaga, Deputy
Resident Representative of Japan International Cooperation Agency
(JICA) Malawi Office said his office has supported 11 Projects some of
which are cassava and cooking oil production village factory etc.
Originally, OVOP was a concept initiated by former Japanese governor
Hiramatsu in Oita Prefecture in 1979. It is aimed to vitalize community
concerning local products, items, activities and developing them
further by themselves. "As you know His Excellency the President
visited Japan and in their discussion with Prime Minister Koizumi he
reiterated Japan's commitment for Malawi, including our support for the
OVOP," he said.
Moronaga said JICA has been supporting OVOP since 1997 and has
recently scale up its cooperation by providing an expert in the OVOP
secretariat. "In addition, another expert as well as volunteers in the
various fields such as micro finance, agro processing among others are
expected to arrive at the end of this month," he said.
National Coordinator of the Project Dr Bruce Munthali said OVOP
follows principles of development, which strives to encourage local
communities to undertake development in their areas as well as full
utilization of indigenous skills, know how, local resources,
technology, culture and local market.
He said OVOP is supply driven economic theory, which advocates
flourishing of businesses by using resources from outside, such as
introduction of new factory/industry and public investment from central
government.

Original_Search_Title: As He Steps Inside
Headline: Nigeria: As He Steps Inside
Body_Text:
Lagos — Geniuses, crackpots, dictators and even men of piety have at different times worked relentlessly and may be, selflessly to actualise their phantom world of utopia, that delusion of human perfectibility, which Saint Augustine conceived as the City of God.
From the baroque designs of the Renaissance to the replica of the sexy robot in Fritz Lang's Metropolis, the story is the same. Dissecting the lives of these famous men and women in pursuit of an idyllic land presents us with two principal characters, one the familiar terrain of strong presidents harping on noble issues and two, the typical artistic Joe DiMaggio, who fits into Ervin Gaufman's sociological piece of man and his Managenent of Impressions. We shall revisit soon, Richard Ben Cramer's real life character epitomised in DiMaggio: The Hero's Life. But suffice it to say that Boy Joe was America's, not a Nigerian creature of the outward and profane elevated to high art, the ideal macho character out of the dreamland-Hol lywood.
Still on the men and women whose very lives - past and present - presented the world with alternative view points, the recurring decimal has been a biography of self conceived, worthy triumphs and private pains. Imagine Napoleon Bonaparte wanting to conquer the world. Weigh it against Isaac Newton's foray into Physics and Metaphysics, a life- time project that delivered the basic principles of gravity and levitation (anti-gravity) with the accompanying revolutionary accomplishments in science and engineering. Each was convinced the project on ground was the best for mankind and it was pursued with a cosmic timetable that brought a revolution that moved the world forward, for good or bad. And each for different reasons was admitted into man's Hall of Fame.
While Bonaparte was privileged to reminisce on the vanity or otherwise of presiding over regular meetings where he is President of the United States of the World (USW), assigning ambassadors to another extra-terrestrial planet and signing cheques to complete scientific projects that would involve another great battle in planet Mars, Newton stayed away from the crowd for hours and days without food, toiling to advance the cause of mankind. At one time his dog ate his lunch and gracefully left the remnants for his master who was too happy to celebrate his dinner on those leftovers. In the same dog life scenario, Newton lost a lifetime research when the pet stepped on a lantern that burnt his research materials. This monumental mishap not withstanding, the friendship continued still on a happy note. That vacuum created by a doggie way of life did however come with its blessings, clearing the intellectual block and before long the coast expanded and behold the new impression call ed gravity emerged with all the clarity needed for its creative application. Does that draw some leadership notes to our own heroes on what constitutes the true values of a leader?
Our Nigerian leaders are not different from their counterparts the world over. The pursuit of power by other means is for them, life itself. When President Obasanjo stepped out of power he wanted that same power on the platform of the United Nations as Secretary General. Since ex-President Babangida stepped aside from the warm protection of the cocoon, that opium of power called Aso Rock, he has been giving us notice of his desire to return to the premises as a tenant for perhaps another 12 years of corrective governance, whereas the body language of the current occupant, suggests he wants to buy up the property for posterity. Buhari who was there for one brief moment wants to make a comeback. Neither Ernest Shonekan nor Yakubu Gowon, all men of letters believes the corridor of power is attainable in their life- time again. Gowon tried the idea once and flopped like a pack of cards. Does his inability to assert his consciousness on the power matrix have anything to do with hi s long sojourn into the intellectual realm, carrying plates like any other student to be served lunch? Some say no learned man or graduate has been privileged to rule this country while Chief Ojo Maduekwe in his public dissertations postulates that only those with military background have the birthright to rule this country.
So, the power calculation for 2007 boils down to an unpresidented power calculus, a prime time battle of the military titans, and the heroes of our recent past. At one end is OBJ who is building a start up factory for risky but rewarding ideas. At the other end are the opponents who in the past had remained silent but ready to draw blood from a soundless stone.
From these characters will emerge the pot pourri images of Saint Augustine, the Newton incarnates and our own inimitable Joe DiMaggio.
Back to the monumentally vain and cheap Joe who according to Cramer understood the power of silence, that the less the principal character gave of himself, standing remote and noble and regally aloof, the more the world took it as evidence of dignity.
The off diamond DiMaggio, according to Cramer, was a jerk, high school drop out whose favourite reading material was Superman comics, a lousy father and wife beater who was reluctant to fight in the First World War.
But here was the hero of America. What made him a hero? Handsome suits, silvery hairs and sculpted features. These were attributes that had nothing to do with character but elevated to high art enough to make him a hero. And this was eloquent testimony of the American value than what any one thought of vain Joe.
Our polity has made more DiMaggios out of our leaders and less of the St Augustine persona. And our leaders prefer the outward, self-serving showmanship of a Dimaggio than a foray into the intellect, a buffeting of the cerebral man which disciplines the mind and tames ambition, the Newtonian dialogue with nature. As a result, these leaders have learned to squeeze every drop of privilege that comes their way to satisfy us. Thus by extension they mirror the way we are, the values we have evolved out of our collateral consciousness. And what we consider our cultural heritages are monumentally cheap values, embodied in the Joe DiMaggio mystique.
Babangida after stepping aside from the column of societal conscience, has learnt some lessons, no doubt, that in spite of his milk of human kindness and concern for the good and prosperity of his neighbour, the collective good of society supersedes every thing else. These are lessons he must imbibe as he makes his calculations of stepping in to continue his march to national awareness. The question however is: "Have we learnt anything?" The mirror reflects our images. And if Obasanjo is the mirror image of military discipline, does he tell us what any military man in governance would want to make of the rest of us, as fools and bloody civilians who can only bark and not bite? Babangida at least listened to the voice of reason and stepped aside, while our President has remained regally aloof, in the mould of Joe DiMaggio, exerting the power of silence while Rome is burning. Is this what the country deserves at this time? Up until last December, at Christmas to be precise, Pre sident Babangida was playing the devil's advocate, defending the incumbent, understandably in the tradition of the rank and file of the military, sorting themselves out in house, protected by the famous esprit de corps. It is unlikely he would do same today given the fact that the third term agenda has gone beyond night fall to dawn and would soon be actualised as a full blown project if left unchecked.
For we the citizens who watch those who have appropriated the commonwealth of the republic to their privileged constituency, the message is clear, we need less of the sociological piece in the character of DiMaggio.
We instead want more of such characters manifest in strong presidents harping on the noble issues of carrying the fatherland to the long awaited land of promise. The mythical utopia reduced to the earthly Promised Land of rice and cassava, beyond the self- serving project of third term. This is the lesson as we step inside.

Original_Search_Title: Transcorp: an Idea Whose Time is Overdue
Headline: Nigeria: Transcorp: an Idea Whose Time is Overdue
Body_Text:
Lagos — Writing recently in a study of the East Asian economic miracle, a South Korean IT expert, Leon Lee noted that Zaibatsu, Keiretsu, and Chaebol to the uninitiated sound like fancy foreign words for Lions, Tigers, and Bears. In reality, he noted, these were powerful corporate entities that transformed East Asia into a modern economic powerhouse.
According to Lee, all three entities reflect the socio-political strata of their respective regions. Qualities such as fostering rapid industrialisation, lifetime employment, and adherence to certain Confucian-based social doctrines were commonplace. The concentration of such economic power brought rapid modernisation to both Japan and Korea.
Japan's Zaibatsu means economic combine or financial clique. Government-sponsored monopolies enabled domestic companies to specialize in particular industries. It originally formed 500 years ago, but made its major contribution during the rapid industrialisation drive of the late-1800s. Famous monopolies included Mitsubishi, Mitsui, and Sumitomo, with extensive holdings in banking, finance, mining, shipping, and manufacturing. The Zaibatsu enabled Japan to fully industrialise within 30 years. Zaibatsu was formally abolished and outlawed by Allied Powers in the postwar era of the late-1940s.
Keiretsu in Japanese means collaborating enterprises or industrial grouping. After the demise and outlaw of the Zaibatsu, Japanese corporations organized 'informal' business relationships by the late-1950s. Participating companies co-ordinated procurement policies, pricing standards, manufacturing process flow, and research findings. The government assisted Keiretsu through a "Technopolis" strategy of formally co-ordinating research and resources among governmental, corporate, and academic organisations. Thus, Japan was able to rival the U.S. in consumer products and semiconductors by the late-1980s. The U.S. closely scrutinised its trade relationship with Japan and privately lamented over the perception of 'Japan Inc.'
Chaebol is Korea's version of Japan's Zaibatsu. These government-sponsored monopolies enabled domestic companies to specialise in particular industries. It was originally formed in the 1920s out of Japan's colonial policy to industrialise the Korean economy. After gaining independence in 1945, Korean compatriots directly took over the monopolies. The Chaebol made its greatest contribution to the Korean industrial base and export economy by the mid-1980s. In the mid-1990s, the four top Chaebol -- Samsung, Huyndai, Daewoo, and Gold Star (now LG)-- controlled 60 per cent of the nation's Gross National Product (GNP). Currently, Chaebol is transforming into financially leaner and market-responsive corporate entities.
In an age where the world has been reduced to a mere global village, what chance does Africa stand in the international arena. In an age of knowledge capital, where a nation's wealth is determined by the quality of its human resources, how can a country compete in the global arena. This is where the Transnational Corporation of Nigeria comes in.
At a fine ceremony in the Aso Rock Villa on July 21, 2005, Pressident Olusegun Obasanjo launched the corporation, which has come to be popularly known by its acronym, Transcorp. If Nigeria must be able to hold its head in the community of nations, there must be economic dimension to its diplomatic manoevures. For far too long, Nigeria has played the big brother role in Africa with nothing in return to show for it.
Since independence on October 1, 1960, in varying degrees depending on who was in power at a particular time, Africa has always been the centrepiece of Nigeria's foreign policy. Just almost immediately after independence, the Congo crisis erupted and the United Nations intervened by sending a peacekeeping force that was made up largely of African troops. Nigeria's contingent led by General Johnson Aguiyi-Ironsi, who later became Head of State, was highly commended for the professional way it conducted itself. The Congo crisis happened during the first republic and the government of the late Prime Minister Abubakar Tafawa Balewa responded appropriately. Since then, the country has been championing the African cause and has participated in many peacekeeping assignments too numerous to mention. A few examples will suffice.
In 1979, the Nigerian government under General Olusegun Obasanjo, as he then was, nationalised British Petroleum (BP) to protest the United Kingdom's position on the independence of Zimbabwe particularly concerning Ian Smith's Unilateral Declaration of Independence (UDI).
But before then, in 1975, Nigeria under Muritala Muhammed broke the stalemate over which among the various liberation fighters, the one Africa should support for Angola's independence. Eventually, the Popular Movement for the Liberation of Angola (MPLA) led by Augustino Neto was endorsed and with Nigeria's support, the party won the liberation struggle and took over power from the Portuguese.
In the struggle for a free South Africa, Nigeria became one of the Front-line States. The countries of the Southern African region that faced the wrath of apartheid South Africa because of their support for liberation fighters were regarded as Front-line States. These countries included Zambia, Zimbabwe, Angola, Mozambique, Botswana, etc. Nigeria was included in the Front-line Group because of the diplomatic, moral and material support she extended to anti-apartheid campaigners. For instance, not many Nigerians know that President Thabo Mbeki of South Africa lived in Lagos for many years before the dismantling of apartheid. His other compatriots were equally haboured by Nigeria and all necessary support was extended to them in their struggle for majority rule in their country.
In the West coast, Nigeria has done so much to ensure stability. The establishment of ECOWAS in 1975 was on the initiative of Nigeria and to some extent, Togo. The restoration of President Ahmed Tejan Kabbah to power in Sierra Leone at a time Nigeria was still under military rule was hailed by freedom-loving people across the world. President Olusegun Obasanjo also did the same thing not too long ago in Sao Tome & Principe.
Then Liberia. The story of Nigeria's investment in both human and material resources to bring peace to that country is too well known for it to detain us here. Even now, Nigeria has not yet finished with that country even as stability has been restored with the election and inauguration of President Ellen Johnson-Sirleaf.
The missing link in the afrocentric thrust of Nigeria's foreign policy was that the economic diplomacy was never built into any initiative be it peacekeeping, liberation struggle, material donation, etc. The result was that once Nigeria helped any African country to secure freedom or independence or responded for assistance as in the case of the Lake Nyos disaster in Cameroon, Nigeria left and other countries would go and reap where they did not sow. The idea is that when you extend largesse politically, you take back economically.
This has been the basis of America's domination of global politics. All the advanced countries have been exploiting this. The Asian Tigers have built enormous fortunes from this.
If Nigeria must be part of this global trend, then Transcorp must be supported. The company plans to invest in both the upstream and downstream sectors of the oil and gas industry with the intention of building a scalable refining facility using the latest technology.
In the area of hospitality, it recently acquired the five-star Abuja Hilton Hotel in a highly contested bid at the value of $105 million. The hotel is now called Transcorp Hilton Hotel, Abuja.
The other areas of investment it has identified are cassava, cocoa and palm fruits processing; power and information technology, software development, business services, web-based and e-commerce consultancy, education and training of knowledge workers. These are areas Nigeria enjoys comparative advantage. The technology initiative it has put in place is capable of
repositioning Nigeria for participating in global technological advancement. If well planned and carefully implemented, in our time, Nigeria can begin to compete with other advanced countries in matters of global trade and investment using Transcorp.
At Transcorp's road show held late last year in Kano, Mr. Nicholas Okoye, its executive director, operation and business development office, said the Corporation "is a bold step to provide for the nation, one mega firm that can penetrate markets around the world as well as dominate the local markets in products and services that were previously serviced by foreign firms or to service markets that have been completely neglected." Transcorp is expected to bid for oil blocks as well as provide world-class production facilities for holders of the other blocks in need of the most modern technology in farming out crude oil.
The corporation has plans to acquire pipeline infrastructure of the Petroleum and Pipelines Marketing Company (PPMC), and expand it to develop Africa's largest network of pipelines for crude oil, refined products and petro-chemicals. Heavy investment in software development, outsourcing, biotechnology, call centres, and hardware assembly would form the beginning of Transcorp's drive to develop a business in technology.
"Africa's technology requirements exceeded $40 billion in 2004. And almost 99.8 per cent of these funds were spent on foreign vendors and foreign technology companies. Transnational technology will be set up in the transnational industrial park (export free zone). This centre will roll out solutions for industrial applications in inventory controls, shipping, production and supply chain management. It will also serve as a commercial research and development facility for Corporate Nigeria and Africa," said Okoye.
Also, the Federal Government is said to have agreed to grant a licence to build an independent power plant to supply residential and commercial residents. It is also expected that Transcorp will export to the governments of Ghana, Equatorial Guinea, Senegal, San tome, Gambia, Burkina Faso and so on. Okoye recalled that Africa is spending over $150 billion yearly on importing consumer products.
"Transcorp will redirect the main production facilities in China to meet our transfer prices and supply these markets. When this is successful, we will then have a value chain of trade and investment built on the back of consumers of Africa. This will expand Transcorp's ability to supply to larger markets of Europe and the United States and achieve greater scale. This process will be managed by a careful strategic plan of acquiring the production rights for Africa from major manufacturers in China, Taiwan, Singapore, Japan and Korea.
Okoye further disclosed that Transnational Industrial Park/Export Free Zone would be established near the coastline to enhance the nation's ability to do international business as well as attract the right partners going forward.
According to him, all the business units that have production facilities such as refining, raw material processing, agro-allied business as well as information technology centre would be sited in the industrial park. He added that "this unit will use most of the funds for working capital to buy goods in large volumes from Asia and to supply them to the West African sub regional markets."
These are lofty ideals but the team must realise that it is not a tea party. If you want gold, you must dig deep for it and that requires hard work and tenacity of purpose. What is reassuring is that a tested team is piloting its affairs. The board, chaired by Dr. Ndi Okereke-Onyiuke, director-general of the Nigerian Stock Exchange, has as members Alhaji Aliko Dangote, Dr Festus Odimegwu, Mr Jim Ovia, Mr Tony Elumelu, Mr Femi Otedola, among others. These people need no introduction.
Its managing director, Mr Fola Adeola, is one of the leading lights of corporate Nigeria. He along with others founded Guaranty Trust Bank Plc and he voluntarily retired as CEO three years ago. Since then he has moved to found FATE Foundation; chair the Pension Commission, the Blair Commission for Africa, among other initiatives. Where the public sector has failed in Nigeria, the country's destiny lies with the private sector. Transcorp, like Zaibatsu and Chaebol has been appointed to lead Nigeria's economic renaissance. It must not fail.

Original_Search_Title: Privatisation: Supertek Acquires Sunti Sugar Co for N180m
Headline: Nigeria: Privatisation: Supertek Acquires Sunti Sugar Co for N180m
Body_Text:
The Federal Government has approved Supertek Nigeria Limited as
the preferred bidder for the purchase of Sunti Sugar Company at the
offer price of N180 million.
The spokesman of the Bureau of Public Enterprises (BPE), Mr. Joe
Anichebe, said in a release issued in Abuja, that the decision to
liquidate the company was in line with Council policy that all non
viable public enterprises where the expected proceeds would not be
enough to meet debts and other obligations be scheduled for liquidation.
He said "the company's assets are mainly farmlands, most of which
have not been cultivated and dilapidated buildings. The company's
factory was never completed". The first attempt of the liquidation
process was concluded in September 2005 and both preferred and reserve
bidders emerged, at N185 million and N180 million respectively. Both
bidders however, failed to pay their bid prices at the stipulated times
given to them, which led to cancellation of the offers. The liquidator
reported the failure of the transaction to the Committee of Inspection,
where it was resolved that the process should be repeated.
The investors for the second liquidation attempt were sourced
through direct marketing. On February 15, 2006, being the deadline for
the submission of bids, three bids were received from Savannah Farms
Limited; Supertek Limited; and Abdullahi Brothers Company (Nig)
Limited. Of the three bids harvested, only Supertek Limited complied
with the stipulated guidelines for the bid. Consequently, the Committee
of Inspection opened the bid of Supertek for responsiveness to the
terms of the transaction. Supertek bid is N180 million for the assets
of the company. The Committee of Inspection had also considered the
post- acquisition plan of Supertek and believes that the firm is
capable of actualising its envisaged investment.
Supertek Nigeria Limited was registered as a company on February 25,
1993. It is involved in various commercial activities in the country
including fresh natural spring water plant in Mambilla Plateau in
Taraba State, and Independent Power Plant (IPP) activities, with its
site in Akwete, Abia State.
The firm was involved in the acquisition and ownership of the then
Guinness farms from Folawiyo and Sons Limited located in the vicinity
of Sunti Sugar in Niger State and currently developing a mechanized
farm in Kudu, also in Niger State.
According to the post-acquisition plan, Supertek intends to, in
addition to sugar production and processing, establish a modern cassava
refinery for industrial starch and high-grade flour at the site. They
are in partnership with Shepherd Agriculture from South Africa, firm
involved in agricultural production.

Original_Search_Title: Govt, China Sign Over $2billion MoU on Rail Project, Others; Chinese President to Visit
Headline: Nigeria: Govt, China Sign Over $2billion MoU on Rail Project, Others; Chinese President to Visit
Body_Text:
IN Its bid to boost its investment, the Federal Government of
Nigeria Thursday took another bold step to attract foreign investors
with the signing of Memorandum of Understanding {MOU} with the Chinese
Government Conglomerate, Guangdong Xinguang International Group to
construct a fast Rail system from Lagos to Abuja, a light rail from the
Murtala Mohammed International Airport to the city of Lagos and Nnamdi
Azikiwe International Airport to Abuja City centre respectively.
Signing on behalf of the Federal Government, Minister of Commerce,
Ambassador Idris Waziri who disclosed that a total of $2billion was
already available to commence the projects, said the construction of
5,000 low income housing units in Lagos, Abuja and Port Harcourt;
building of cement factory in the country; construction of shopping
Malls in Lagos, Abuja and Port Harcourt; and building of power stations
to boost electricity supply across the country are some of the
identified areas of investment by the both countries after a day
brainstorming exercise.
The company would invest in the Abuja Metro Line phase1, phase2,
phase3, Power Station plant, Lugbe Housing Project & Surrounding
Infrastructure, Tunga Maje Automotive Village and the Abuja
Neighbourhood Centre No.1 & Security Infrastructure. Possible areas
in the Agriculture Ministry are Rice Plantation, Cassava Plantation,
Agro Research centre and Fishing Aquaculture while there will be
concentration in Malaria Medicine Factory, Malaria Medicine Plantation
Garden, AIDS Medicine Factory; Nigeria Government Medicine Stores,
Medicine Research Facility, Research Plantation Gardens and Emergency
Medical Equipment Factory.
He said other areas of investment include the provision of computers
for tertiary institutions and training of 100 staff each of the
Ministries of Health and Agriculture in capacity development.
Ambassador. Waziri who pointed out that Nigeria has made more
$500million US dollars from export of non-oil products to China last
year, said if the sector is well developed, it will assist the country
in addressing the issue of unemployment.
The leader of the Chinese Investment Team, Mr. Wenbin Wang who said
the need for economic cooperation with Nigeria was long overdue,
expressed the readiness of his government to commence the training of
200 staff from Health and Agriculture Ministries immediately at no cost
to Nigeria, adding that the President of China has concluded plans to
visit Nigeria next month .

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Justifies Ban On Import of Agric Produce
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Justifies Ban On Import of Agric Produce
Body_Text:
President Olusegun Obasanjo has explained that the ban on the importation of some agricultural commodities by his administration was done out of the realization that the country has comparative advantage in exportation of such commodities but had hitherto constituted the bulk of its imports.
Obasanjo told international agric-commerce investors that the country had improved high quality yielding and disease resistant varieties of sorghum, soya beans, rice, oil palm, cocoa, rubber, among others.
The president, who spoke while addressing a conferen-ce organized by the Interna-tional Christian Chamber of Commerce in Abuja, was represented by the Minister of State for Agriculture and Rural Development, Otunba Bamidele Dada.
President Obasanjo assu-red interested investors in the agricultural sector of the commitment of the federal government to provide the required investment climate and attractive incentives to help them realize their dreams.
The high powered interna-tional audience, which inclu-ded investors from South Africa, were also informed of how they could become part of the presidential initiatives on some selected crops which aim at boosting pro-duction to meet domestic needs and generate surplus for export.
He listed some of these areas of presidential concern to include rice, cassava, vegetable oil, horticultural crops, livestock/poultry, fisheries and aqua-culture.
The vice president of the International Christian Cham-ber of Commerce (ICCC) and head of the body in Nigeria, Engineer Ntience Ubonisrael, commended the federal government for earmarking N50bn to aid the expansion of the sector.
He stated that the conference would provide a plat-form aimed at exploring investment opportunities in the agric/commerce sector of the economy.

Original_Search_Title: Zanzibar Severely Drought-Hit, Assessment Team Says
Headline: Tanzania: Zanzibar Severely Drought-Hit, Assessment Team Says
Body_Text:
Stone Town, Zanzibar — At least 4,000 hectares of food crops have wilted and 50 heads of
cattle have died in Tanzania's semiautonomous Island of Zanzibar due to
severe drought that has hit parts of the island, according to a report
by the Rapid Vulnerable Assessment Task Force Committee.
The assessment team issued the report on Tuesday following its completion of a two-week study.
"My team surveyed nine out of 10 districts of Unguja and Pemba
islands, finding that most of the food crops planted in September 2005,
including cereals, maize and potatoes, had wilted," Ali Hajji
Ramadhani, the chairman of the 14-member committee formed on 27
February, said.
He said the eastern coastal region of Zanzibar was more affected,
with livestock - mainly cattle - dying. He said his team did not survey
the island's capital, Stone Town, because little farming took place in
the area.
"If we compare drought in Zanzibar and that of other parts in the
East African region, we are better," Ramadhani said. "However, some
homes are now having shortages of food, but fortunately our shops are
full of food stuffs."
He said that prices of livestock had dropped drastically such that
one cow, which would normally cost at least US $350 was now about $150.
"Many farmers have also involuntarily slaughtered their cows and sold the meat," he said.
According to the 2003 Ministry of Agriculture and Livestock records
on livestock survey, Zanzibar had about 170,000 cattle - many kept by
free grazing methods.
Ramadhani said his team comprised agricultural experts from the
island as well as those from the meteorological department, the Food
Agricultural Organization (FAO) and those from Tanzania mainland, where
drought has also hit severely.
"Since we have started to witness some rains, the government has
distributed 10 tonnes of rice and seven tonnes of maize seeds to the
most affected farmers in the island to plant," he said. "We have
suggested to the authorities also to distribute cassava and other food
crop seeds."
Ramadhani said farmers in Jambiani, Paje, Nungwi, Ukongoroni,
Tumbatu, and Makunduchi in Unguja, and Pemba areas of Micheweni,
Mwambe, Vitongoji, Kangagani, Pujini, and Kengeja would receive the
seeds.
He said the government had decided to give the selected seeds,
"because it is what can be grown in Zanzibar and it is the food used by
the majority".
The majority of Zanzibar's 981,745 people are small-scale farmers, fishermen and traders.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: FG Earmark N50bn for 2006 Farming Season
Headline: Nigeria: FG Earmark N50bn for 2006 Farming Season
Body_Text:
Owerri — Determined to boost food production in the country, the Federal Government has earmarked a whooping N5bn for disbursement as loans to Nigerian farmers to assist them in this year's farming season.
Briefing newsmen in Owerri, yesterday, the Chairman of the Imo State chapter of the All Farmers Association of Nigeria (AFAN), Col. Lambert Iheanacho said that qualification for the loan is that the applicant farmer must be a registered member of AFAN and must be a practicing farmer.
While stating that other conditions would be determined by the committee handling the loan from Abuja, Iheanacho made it clear that the loan application remained free of charge, and therefore urged Imo farmers to take advantage of the gesture.
He also disclosed that efforts had been made to ensure that farmers do not encounter any problem to source for the loan, as three commodity companies including arable crops multi-commodity, tree crops multi- commodity and livestock and fishery development and marketing companies have been formed.
He said that in other to boost the potentials of the companies, the Federal Government has already committed N180 million for the three commodities companies, while each of the 36 states are expected to buy N25 million worth of shares.
He commended Imo State Government for being the first state to buy their own shares, explaining that it now behoved on the farmers to re-buy the shares, as to be able to refund the money already committed by the state government.
"We want to motivate the farmers, give them access to the loan facility, so that by next year, this issue of food production would improve greatly. We want to produce enough food for the teeming population of this country", he said.
On the Federal Government's programme of cassava production and export, the Imo AFAM Chairman described the programme as laudable, and therefore advised the Imo State government to assist Imo farmers to participate in the programme by setting up cassava plants in all the three zones of the State.
"Our problem as regards cassava production is because of our peculiar state of our vegetation", he said and tasked government to make efforts in assisting farmers in that direction.
On the much publicized Avian Bird Flu virus, Iheanacho said that it was yet to get to the State, adding that government was giving too much publicity.

Original_Search_Title: Malnutrition: Pregnant Woman's Enemy
Headline: Malawi: Malnutrition: Pregnant Woman's Enemy
Body_Text:
Lilongwe — As most parts of the country have been affected by acute shortage
of food, it is likely that pregnant women and children are facing
malnutrition.
Malnutrition is a major cause of diseases, disability and death in Malawi.
Malnutrition, in most cases is as a result of poverty, ignorance, poor sanitation and feeding habits and diseases.
Children who are malnourished are prone to suffer from a number of
infections on the other hand, children who are suffering from
infections are also likely to suffer from malnutrition and the
situation is worse when there is acute shortage of food.
Malnutrition can therefore be both a cause and results of infections.
Malnutrition in pregnant women causes underweight births and such
babies risk illnesses and death from infections, especially diarrhoea
and acute respirations.
In Malawi many of the health problems that we encounter, are poverty, ignorance and illiteracy are major culprits.
Another major problem that is contributing to poor health of
pregnant women in the country is financial problems in most of the
families more especially people living in rural areas.
The problem of money to purchase good food and poor agriculture
productivity and food insecurity is the major contributor to the high
increase malnutrition in the country.
On the other hand, traditional or cultural practices that discourage
pregnant women from eating nutritious food also contribute to
malnutrition among pregnant women.
Some cultures in the country discourage pregnant women from eating
eggs for fear that she might bear babies the shape of eggs, or they
believe that the baby will be a thief when he or she grows up.
Some times women in the country are malnourished just because they
are ignorant of the nutritious foods in their localities such as
pumpkins, sweet potatoes and cassava leaves.
Principal Secretary for Nutrition and HIV/AIDS in the Office of
President and Cabinet, Mary Shawa said some cultural and traditional
beliefs that prevent pregnant women eating balanced diet food should be
discourage.
She said women and girls of the reproductive age should be
encouraged to eat good diet that includes plenty of vegetables, fruits
and meat so that their bodies should be healthy.

Original_Search_Title: Even Suave Pastor is No Match for Mayor
Headline: Uganda: Even Suave Pastor is No Match for Mayor
Body_Text:
Nairobi — There are very few pastors quite like Peter Ssematimba. A boyish-looking Kampala businessman with his own radio station and production company, Peter is one of the reasons FM stations are so popular in Uganda.
He spent more than 10 years living on the US west coast, and word from there is that he came that close to becoming a doctor, before packing his bags and heading home in the mid 1990s, just when FM radio was about to become more than a fact of life. His fresh and bold ideas, not to mention his American work-ethic generated wonders at Capital Radio and CBS, two of Uganda's biggest FM stations.
He soon quit one after the other to strike out on his own in a very crowded and competitive market. Few expected him to take the fight to the big boys and survive. He has not survived; but thrived beyond his wildest dreams.
About a year or two ago, Peter claims to have "seen the light" and renounced his old ways (he is a dapper dresser best known for a perm in his hair, a diamond pin in his earlobe and the Japanese cologne Issey Miyake on his clothes).
It's not known how many lost souls Pastor Ssematimba has rescued; but he pretty is serious at least about speaking on his salvation. Last October, the pastor suddenly announced that he would contest to become Mayor of Kampala, replacing the economist and losing presidential candidate John Ssebaana Kizito.
Many thought Ssematimba was joking, until he unleashed a series of serious and cleverly produced public service announcements, courted Kampalans with his fresh ideas (not to mention his very clear American-accented English) and surprised all with a sober and concerted campaign.
In the end, the March 3 election was won by former mayor and big-time populist Al-Hajj Nasser Ntege Sebaggala with 53 per cent (or 164,498 votes) to Pastor Ssematimba's 40 per cent (or 125,625 votes).
Four other candidates shared the remaining 6.5 per cent (or 20,532 votes). It's anyone's guess what could have been had the field been less crowded.
What's undeniable is the level of support the young man seems to have received. Indeed, it's been said that Ssematimba ran the cleanest and most professional of the thousands of recent campaigns for political office right from that of President of Uganda, which was one by Yoweri K. Museveni -again.
The incoming mayor certainly took his young opponent very seriously and used his own very colourful lifestyle to try to match up.
Sebaggala, popularly known as Seya (Luganda slang for "one of us"), was famously elected in 1998 by riding a wave of disaffection with the moneyed and connected elite who mercilessly jibed him for his modest education and shaky command of the Queen's language.
A cunning and successful businessman who thrived even during the dark days of Idi Amin in 1970s, Sebaggala convinced enough people - derisively called "lumpens" by some - to latch their star to his rocket for a stunning and well-deserved victory over three very highly regarded opponents. They included Christopher Iga, then incumbent mayor and member of the ruling Movement.
Sebaggala was, however, soon in trouble with the long arm of the law. He was arrested at the airport in New York in June 1998 and charged with dealing in stolen travellers' cheques. He was sentenced to 15 months' imprisonment and, even as his supporters claimed that he had been set up by the Uganda government, it was hard to see how someone could recover from such a sucker punch, especially one delivered in broad daylight. But Seya recovered.
He served his time and was freed in 2000, returning home to one of the most feverish welcome parties ever staged at Entebbe International Airport. To the thousands of his supporters, Sebaggala was not a common thief as some Members of Parliament had dubbed him, but a latter-day Robin - or is it Robbing? - Hood, who stole from the rich (Americans) to give to the poor (Ugandans).
Sebaggala was also saved because his crime, like all white collar crimes, is by nature highly technical and difficult to explain to a lay person. Plus he was never actually in jail incarcerated inside Uganda.
After making a go at the presidency and dropping out almost a week into the campaigns earlier this year, Sebaggala concentrated on Kampala, the city that has embraced him like no other place. This is the man Pastor Peter Ssematimba came up against.
And while the good man of the cloth put a brave face on it, his was a good fight that ultimately came up short. In a recent interview, Ssematimba declared, quite correctly, that Kampala City Council needs a manager, not a politician.
"I looked at the potential that the city has; [which] potential has not been exploited," Ssematimba told Sunday Monitor last week. "For the kind of fulfilment that we are looking for, we need leaders, solid managers and that is what we are lacking in KCC [Kampala City Council]. I contested so that I could offer leadership and management to this city.
We don't need politics but solid management and leadership."
Ultimately though, the KCC mayorship went to Sebaggala mostly because of politics. Kampala is a hotbed of opposition.
President Museveni has not won many friends in the city and the recent parliamentary elections saw only one of his supporters returned as MP.
The president doesn't help matters when he incessantly attacks the city council for all the problems in Kampala - pot-holed and congested roads, illegal construction in wetlands, garbage dust, etc.
The real crisis of Kampala is that it is a ramshackle something masquerading as a modern capital city, with slums right in the metropolis, too many vehicles on too few roads, less than 10 sets of functional traffic lights, and a most archaic sewerage system (designed for 200,000 Asians who favoured a rice diet) and now serving more than 1.5 million natives who love their ugali and cassava.
Those problems were not created by KCC. But the city council has exacerbated them over the years by allowing fly-by-the-night construction, corruption and having no plan either for now or the future.
The government has finally invoked these shenanigans in a plan to significantly trim the mayor's powers and take over many of his functions - something it failed to achieve at the ballot box. The running of the city will not get any easier of course, either today or 10 years in the future. The unsound template on which Kampala as a city is built simply can't allow that.
But in Kampala, you will always find bright lights like that of Pastor Peter Ssematimba, who will no doubt continue to thrive in music and evangelism. Besides him will always be people like Sebaggala, the wily man-on-the-street with native cunning.
Their Kampala will, however, be a city in name only.

Original_Search_Title: Power Cuts Crippling Business
Headline: Uganda: Power Cuts Crippling Business
Body_Text:
It is a sunny day as I stroll through the streets of Mengo-Kisenyi, a suburb of Kampala. The area is amazingly quiet and the air fresh, which is surprising for a place known for its blinding fumes.
People, mostly men, gathered in the groups of five to seven in front of the workshops are chatting. Coming from one group is a discussion about Uganda's political future.
A few steps ahead, I see another group in front of a maize mill, in a conference of sorts, carefully drawing "plans" that Arsenal will employ to win the Champions League.
Across the road, a group of youths are playing draft and ludo and this is where I hear some clamour. Although the weather is favourable for the businesses of this town, no work is being done.
It wouldn't be fair to assume that these people are jobless, waiting for the cover of darkness to break into people's houses. Talking to them reveals that they have reason to pass the time.
This is what many people in the informal sector do these days when there is load shedding. Since the introduction of long hours of load shedding, many of these people have suffered income setbacks and others have lost jobs in the process.
In Mengo-Kisenyi, the millers say the impact has been worse. This town is a hub for the low technology food processing plants dealing mainly in maize, Soya, millet and cassava milling. Its products have enjoyed remarkable acceptance among modest and low income earners because of the low prices of the products produced.
Work interruptions
George Semakula, the manager of Kijjo Millers in Kisenyi says that in their zone (Kakajjo), the power crisis has shrunk all their businesses. "Life is now a gamble. We are spending more than we earn," Semakula says.
The story in Katwe, also a slum in Kampala, which is known for its skilful metal fabric work isn't any different.
Without power, many if not all dealers in metal fabrication and food processing are unable to do any business and they count it 'a dead' day.
Most businesses in both places are affected by load shedding because many of their machinery uses power.
Siraj Mutebi who deals in metal fabrication says costumers rarely give them contracts if they don't find them practically welding. He argues that costumers fear that they may be crooks with the intention of conning them of their money.
The informal sector contributes 20 percent of the Gross Domestic Product and employs more than two million people, most of whom couldn't find work in the formal sector.
According to the Uganda Bureau of Statistics report on the Uganda Business Register of 2001/2, the number of registered informal sector businesses is rapidly growing. It was estimated to be growing at an annual rate of 30 percent and one of the major employers of the young and unskilled labourers. Unfortunately, the energy problem is crunching their upbeat pace.
Reduced earnings
Although they are improving on their technologies from traditional methods to high technology machinery at a low cost, lately, the new technologies have forced them to entirely depend on power.
But the power shortage is affecting their earnings since many of them can't afford to buy generators. Mutebi stresses that load shedding has affected his business a lot. "I used to get total revenue of Shs150,000 a day but now, it is by luck that I get Shs20,000," he reveals, adding that, "sadly, it is from this money that you have to pay fixed rent and licenses."
He says it now takes him one and half a months to make a wheat mixer, which used to take him only two weeks before. "Our costumers abuse us everyday for not beating deadlines," he adds.
Semakula also says that in the past, he was able to produce 30 bags worth a turnover of Shs90,000, which is about Shs2.7m a month. However, since the power crisis, he gets less than half of it.
Despite the slash in their revenues, they say they still pay the same or even higher taxes, rent and other dues for the places they work in on a monthly basis.
But some entrepreneurs in the informal sector in towns like Kisenyi, Katwe, Ndeeba, Iganga and Bwaise have been facing power problems even before the long hours of load shedding started.
In fact, in Kisenyi, Kakajjo zone, which is a powerhouse for milling millet and maize in Kampala, factories have spent some time without power after their transformer blew due to overloading.
Previously, these small-scale entrepreneurs had devised means to control overloading the transformer. They made an agreement to ration power among themselves in order to avoid overloading the transformer, something that could cause its breakdown. They also set up a committee to enforce the agreement whereby the maize millers would mill during day and millet millers at night.
But when the 12 (and sometimes 24) hour load shedding started, the millet millers saw themselves working less than six hours in two days due to the routine night load shedding. So they slapped down the agreement and resumed working during the day, which again led to overloading of the transformer and blowing it eventually.
"The problem is that the firms are individually owned and in this situation, you can't forcefully regulate the use of the transformers when you aren't an authorized company," says Richard Lutalo, the manager of Pentagon Millers.
Asked about the option of generators, Lutalo says that despite the their ability to acquire generators on hire purchase, there are many mills dealing in the same business in different parts of the city.
"This means that using a generator will increase the total cost, which isn't easy to pass on to the client since he/she can get the same service at a cheaper price in places where Umeme hasn't load shed that day."
"In other words, we have to spend a day without working until power comes back," Lutalo concludes. The job security for labourers in the informal sector is next to none as most of them are unskilled workers who are easy to layoff. Many have been laid off because the owners can't afford to pay them while others gave up after seeing that there were no tasks for them.
Those interviewed in Bwaise and Ndeeba said they earn as low as Shs3,000 per day worked, which is about Shs90,000 a month. This indicates that if power goes off for the whole day, many have to go back home with nothing.
According to Abbas Kagimu of Katwe I Youth Development Project, an NGO that teaches skills through apprenticeship to the youth, they are at crossroads. "The government is telling us that the power crisis is due to the drought and that the rainy season will solve the problem. But we also hear rumours that the trouble originates from the second dam that was built and there is little chance of working out the problem in months," Kagimu says.
Nonetheless, the anticipation that the power crisis is a short-term challenge has made many endure the present difficulties other than getting options.
They reason that they don't want to spend on very expensive generators that are perhaps more costly than their mills when the power is expected to come back shortly.
Mutebi suggests that in the mean time, "Since most of us work during day, they should load shed only in the residential areas during that time and then load shed the work places when we have gone back home."

Original_Search_Title: The Justiciability of Economic, Social And Cultural Rights
Headline: Gambia: The Justiciability of Economic, Social And Cultural Rights
Body_Text:
The realization of the ESCR requires an interaction of policies in numerous sectors, institutions and entitlements. There is no one way to provide for the rights.
Thus, the right to education, as it is guaranteed in article 13 of the Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights, is the most outstanding example of the 'cultural rights' category, it is implicit and flows from the right to life and that a child has a fundamental right to free education at the primary level.
- The question one needs to ask is, what are the main challenges to fulfilment of the right?
- Who has the obligation to protect or fulfil the right?
- In whom does the right inhere?
- Curbing corruption and promoting good governance & accountability.
- Long-term planning for the implementation of ESCR.
- Poverty eradication
- The ability of government to achieve meaningful development.
- The Notion of priority.
OBLIGATION
The greatest challenges to state party within the sub-region are poor planning and formulating constructive policies. Thus, the nature of responsibilities of the state in connection with the ESCR is a threefold manner: the obligations are to respect, to protect and to fulfilÃ¢-oe
The Obligation to respect economic, social and cultural rights requires:
States to abstain from performing, sponsoring or tolerating any practice, policy or legal measure violating the integrity of individuals upon their freedom to use those material or other resources available to them in ways they find most appropriate to satisfy economic, social and cultural rights.
The Obligation to Protect: entails immediate steps to ensure that violations of economic, social and cultural rights by the State and its agents are prevented
The Obligation to fulfil: can entail issues such as public expenditure, governmental regulation of the economy, the provision of basic public services and infrastructure, taxation and other redistributive economic measures.
In order to achieve the ultimate goal, state party should demonstrate that every effort has been made to use all resources that are at its disposition in an effort to satisfy, as a matter of priority, those minimum obligations
REGIONAL LEVEL OBSTACLES
Justifiability of the ESCR is still a major problem within African countries due to the following:
- Resource Constraints.
- In Appropriateness of courts to deal with collective claims.
- Absence of justifiability socio-economic rights in normative instruments.
- Poverty.
- Inverting priorities, for instance devoting more resources to higher education than, and at the expense of primary education.
- Absence of long-term planning to implement the ESCR.
- Corruption and deficit in good governance.
- Unequal sharing of benefits of globalization; also raise questions about participation, exclusion and discrimination.
- Violation of human rights
- Debt services take precedent over current expenditure.
- Civil wars impede the ability of governments to meaningful development.
Diversity of situations and the multiplicity of forms of human rights violations in the sub-region today are traversed by numerous and serious armed conflicts, which bring about flagrant, massive and systematic violations of human rights. And it is regrettable to note that the most theatres of conflicts are to be found in the richest zones, that are in those countries with incredible mineral and natural resources.
Main Threats to Fulfil ESCR
Custom & Culture
The multi-cultural character of many of our cultures has also made it difficult to improve legislation and the enforcement machinery in response to the concerns of women and the girl-child. Ironically the right to be free from torture has failed to encompass domestic violence or violence in the family, because the focus of the international notion of freedom from torture is public action by the state. The dichotomy between public and private responsibility when applied to the reality of a women's life leads o absurd distinctions Rape by a police officer, for example, becomes a violation, while rape by a stranger, husband or acquaintance does not.
Hence actors, which discriminate against women, are described as there:
- Isolation from economic opportunity.
- Illiteracy.
- Domestic violence.
- Under-development.
- Unwaged work.
It has been noted with relief that the African Union: Protocol on the Rights of Women in Africa, which guarantees a wide range of women's civil and political rights as well as economic, social and cultural rights, has recognized the human rights of women and the Protocol has been ratified by 15 member-states and enters in force on 15th November, 2005.
I would like to seize this opportunity and congratulate The Women's Movements, NGO's, and all partners in development who have been pushing for the ratification.
It is important to note that international treaties do not automatically become part of domestic law when they are entered into by the executive, for the provisions of such treaties to become part of domestic law legislation has to be passed incorporating them in domestic law legislation. Until this is done, domestic courts cannot enforce directly the provisions of such treaties.
Illiteracy
The right to education is regarded as one of the basic means needed by a human being to develop his or her personality. Furthermore , national development hinges on the ability of working populations to handle complex technologies. The general opinion is that education allows women freely to develop their personality and dignity.
Whatever the reason or origin to custom, somehow or other, it is eventually applied oppressively. The result is discriminatory against women.
One of those detrimental cultural practices this paper will discuss is Trokosi.
What is Trokosi? Trokosi is a young girl who is sent to a shrine not because she wants to stay there but because someone has allegedly committed an offence for which she has to suffer. Trokosi is an old religious belief practised by the Ewe and certain ethnic groups in Ghana, in which a girl who is eight years and above is sent to a shrine where she would be enslaved and sexually exploited.
The fate of these girls are burdened by illiteracy, and as a result, innocent girls are sent into slavery because a grandmother may have stolen earrings or had picked up some tomatoes from a stall without paying or it, or that a grandfather once stole a cassava stick.
In short, the Trokosi is not allowed to use any kind of footwear or clothes. She conducts her duties naked. She automatically becomes the priest's wife; she works laboriously on his farm on an empty stomach. She is denied access to medical treatment and exposed to risks not attended by men i.e. the risk of motherhood, infanticide, and miscarriage.
According to a report published by Women of the World Anglophone Africa: - 'there are approximately 4,500 girls known as Trokosi (slaves of the god). They are considered property of the priest and may be freed only by him, after the woman is no longer appealing to him. The woman's family will provide a replacement usually a young girl below the age of 11 years thus the practice continues in perpetuity.
These customary practices impede legal reforms and in some cases policy development; they are fundamental issues which enhance gender discrimination, enslavement of the girl-child and unwaged work... I may also point out that women have a fundamental right to life , therefore, the right to life as contained in Art 12 must be understood as a right to the enjoyment of the highest attainable standard of mental and physical health and enjoyment of a variety of facilities, goods, services and conditions necessary for the realization of the highest attainable standard of health.
CUSTOMARY OBLIGATIONS
The cultural diversity of our culture has also made it difficult to sensitize ethnic groups engage in detrimental cultural practices. Custom is another obstacle to women realizing equality in the sub-region; it has a strong influence in qualifying a woman's role in society and of perpetuating the traditional concept of woman in society to exclude them from decision-making and from positions of influence.
Growing level of poverty in the region has had a critical effect on the ability of governments to strengthen the economic and social protection of women and children.
The greater percentages of marriages in the Sub-region are customary and subject to customary obligations; a married woman is usually considered and treated as the property of her husband and the husband family. Such customary marriages also come with cultural practice and pressure such as inheritance marriage, which is becoming a growing social issue and concern within the sub-region.
Recent statistical trends highlight instances where one's spouse is infected with HIV/AIDs and died as a consequence of the disease. In such cultures, the husband of the deceased (wife) who is already infected with the disease and seeking treatment is urge to marry a younger woman from the family of his late wife. Or vice-versa, i.e. if the husband dies before the wife, his rather would re-marry his wife usually the youngest wife.
These customary practices impede legal reforms and policy development, they are fundamental issues, which stand in the way of Primary Health & AIDs care workers.
Social mobilization and advocacy are critical to support legal initiatives on the prohibition of such detrimental cultural practice. The real importance of the ESCR lies in their actual and potential impact on individuals' lives. In other words, people can only make their rights meaningful by demanding that the ESCR be protected, respected and enforced.

Original_Search_Title: NDE Targets 7 Million Jobs By 2007
Headline: Nigeria: NDE Targets 7 Million Jobs By 2007
Body_Text:
The National Directorate of Employment (NDE), has set a target of creating seven million jobs by next year, its Director-General, Engineer S.O. Adelodun, has disclosed.
He said yesterday at the opening ceremony of the third joint NDE and National Export-Import Bank (NEXIM) training programme on Start-Your-Own-Business (SYOB) for ex-corps members in Kaduna that over 450 ex-Youth Servicemen will benefit from the programme.
Engr. Adelodun, said that the unemployment situation has worsened over the years as a recent survey showed that out of about 130,000 graduates coming out of tertiary institu-tions, only about ten percent secured wage employment.
In the address read by NDE's Director of Small-Scale Enter-prise (SSE), Engr. Nnamdi Odoh, the DG said the NDE has been able through its core prog-rammes to provide employment in the formal sector of the economy.
He said the SYOP programme training is being conducted in all the six geo-political zones to give as many ex-corps members from all over the country to become entrepreneurs.
Engr. Adelodun said these opportunities are provided through the National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy (NEEDS) and includes the Presidential Initiative on Rice Production and Processing, fish culture and cassava production, adding that NDE is poised to explore non-oil sector for the teeming unemployed.
Managing Director NEXIM Bank, Alhaji Baba Y. Ahmed, said the bank has provided N39 billion as credit support to over 300 exporters since 1991 and assisted Nigerian exporters to generate an average of US$50 billion annually.
He said in the speech read by NEXIM Executive Director Operations, Engr. Edet J. Ekere that NEXIM's initiative with NDE is to address the twin issues of export development and unemployment, tapping the capacity of the NDE in the area of manpower and employment generation.
"Specifically, the bank's support is targeted at providing training and financial support for export-oriented entrepreneurs," he added.
Governor of Kaduna State, Alhaji Ahmed Mohammed Makarfi, who was represented by his commissioner of commerce, Mr. Mikaya Lekyap, said the Kaduna state government will continue to support NDE in training entrepreneurs to reduce the level of unemployment in the state.
