this month in Abuja, is to have all these actors talk on a permanent
basis, be on the sam e edge, so that we can achieve sustainable
business laws.
And your position on continuing legal education?
Continuing legal education is recommended for all lawyers. Law is
life and things continues to change. The lawyers that will remain
relevant to the society , to the public, to his clients and everybody
are those lawyers who continuously educate themselves. One of the
papers that will be delivered at the conference in Abuja is one on the
importance of continuing legal education as an instrument of achieving
reforms. What we are saying is that, outside of what you studied in the
university, you should continue to educate yourself. Go for workshops,
go for seminar. If you can afford it, go abroad or here in the country.
What we are saying is that learning process must continue and this is
the advise we give the younger members of the bar. What we are
advocating is that the older members of the bar should support the
younger ones, so what we can have a complete conglomerate of lawyers
that command the respect the society places on them, because when you
introduce somebody as a la wyer, automatically, the society expect a
little extra from him than an ordinary graduate. He has to justify that
and we are recommending that the continuing legal education is one way
to do that.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Farmers to Produce More for Export
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava Farmers to Produce More for Export
Body_Text:
Lagos — WITH Nigeria currently leading in cassava export in the world, local growers of the commodity in Anambra State have affirmed their resolve to produce more, if given necessary financial assistance.
Chief Chukwuma Maduka, chairman of Anambra Chapter of the Cassava Growers Association said that inadequate fund had remained the major challenge facing them.
Chief Maduka who is also the President of the newly resurrected Capital City Chamber of Commerce and Industry Awka said the Anambra State chapter of the Cassava Growers Association is number one.
"You can see that among the whole chapters of the Cassava Growers Association of Nigeria, Anambra chapter is the number one. If you go to Abuja, the name Chief B.C. Maduka is all over Abuja and you see them saying that's the Anambra chairman, the best," he stated.
Chief Maduka said the chapter has organised itself so well with branches in every local government of the state that it has become indispensable in business gatherings like the forthcoming trade fair in the state.
He said only this year, the farmers have cultivated over 50 hectres of cassava in the state without the assistance of any government except the International Institute for Tropical Agriculture which supplied it with new cassava stem species.
He said the cassava cultivated by the farmers last year have grown and would be sold this planting season.
Lamenting the farmers' financial handicap Chief Maduka stated, "We are doing very well but the greatest problem we have is finance, so we are calling on the federal government led by Chief Olusegun Obasanjo to give money directly to the members of the association in the state," he stated.
He noted that only direct private sector financing would make the federal government cassava initiative succeed adding that with what they have achieved through self-help effort they will do wonders in cassava production if assisted by federal government.
"You see what we have achieved on our own now and if we are given enough money this planting season, you will see the wonders we will do.
Chief Maduka promised that in the next two years the state would give to federal government its demand of cassava and its derivatives if given the necessary support.
Continuing, he said "the only problem we have with the cassava growers association all over the federation is finance and nothing else, they are capital intensive, another problem is processing plant," he stated.
He specifically requested the federal government to provide them with financial support in form of cash and purchase of processing plants as well as tractors and implement for land preparation and cultivation.
If the federal government say they don't have mosney, let them buy and share to us to pay back because we have decided to satisfy federal government but what is withholding it now is finance because government has not given us any money since inception," he stated.

Original_Search_Title: Chinese Firms Jostle for Nigerian Cassava
Headline: Nigeria: Chinese Firms Jostle for Nigerian Cassava
Body_Text:
Abuja — Minister of Commerce, Ambassador Idris Waziri, yesterday recounted how Chinese companies have been placing orders for cassava from Nigeria over and above the capacity the developing sector in the country could deliver.
The Federal Government has also asked the Presidential Committee on Cassava Export Promotion to work towards achieving the annual $5 billion target set for the cassava industry in the country.
Waziri who spoke in Abuja during the presentation of a book on Standards and Guidelines for Cassava Production and Export, jointly authored by the Standard Organisation of Nigeria (SON) and the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA), noted that Nigeria was very far from realising her full potential in the cassava sector despite being the largest producer in the world.
The minister added that cassava had a wide and varied application as raw materials for well over 1000 industries.
He stated that Chinese businessmen adjudged Nigerian cassava to be the best in the world after laboratory tests in China, and that a good number of firms from the Asian nation were ready to import cassava from the country.
A couple of them were already moving into Nigeria to process and export the product, he said.
Waziri recalled that one Chinese firm told him during a visit to the Asian country that it was willing to import 45 million metric tonnes of cassava from Nigeria, saying that was the total annual production in the country at the moment both for food and industrial uses.
According to him, Thailand, Brazil, Indonesia and Vietnam, which have already standardised their cassava production and export procedures and are at the moment dominating the international market, are Nigeria's competitors in the sector.
"The problem with the Nigerian cassava export has always been lack of adequate market information, especially guidelines on export," Waziri said adding, "One of the major obstacles in attaining set standards has been our local methods of processing the commodity."
He stated further: "In order for our cassava to meet standards and guidelines for export, the use of modern processing methods is paramount. Therefore, the book is a key to unlocking the obstacles."
Waziri announced that a new Cassava Secretariat was being set up in Abuja, urging states and local governments and associations to do same in their areas.
According to him, the publication represented a major milestone in the development of the young sector and that it could not have come at a better time than at the present moment when the country needed clear directions on how to break into the international cassava market.
Presently, about 90 per cent of cassava produced in Nigeria is consumed locally as food, the minister said.
Meanwhile, the Federal Government at the same event charged the Presidential Committee on Cassava Export Promotion, inaugurated by President Olusegun Obasanjo March 2001 and under the chairmanship of the commerce minister, to meet the annual income of $5bn target set for it.
Obasanjo, who was represented by Agriculture Minister, Mallam Adamu Bello, at the book launch said his administration was giving agriculture priority in order to spur economic growth and achieve strides in its poverty alleviation programme.
He commended the publication, saying it would fill existing gaps in safety, quality in cassava production, and stimulate the export of the commodity, because "without standards, no sustainable trade could take place".

Original_Search_Title: Are Millennium Devt Goals Achievable?
Headline: Nigeria: Are Millennium Devt Goals Achievable?
Body_Text:
Makurdi — Towards eradication of poverty by year 2015, some Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) were developed by Comity of Nations at the United Nations.
Eight goals were developed principally to eradicate extreme poverty and hunger; achieve universal primary education; promote gender equality and empower women; reduce child mortality; improve maternal health; combat HIV/AIDS, malaria and other diseases; ensure environmental sustainability; and develop global partnership for development. Deadline set for achieving these goals was 2015.
Governments, donors, private sector and NGOs, have been working tirelessly towards the achievement of the set targets by the year 2015. Regrettably though Sub-Sahara Africa has made the least progress in comparison with other regions of the World.
With only 10 years left to the target date for achieving the MDGs, there is urgent need to scale up efforts especially in the Sub Sahara Africa towards it's achievement. Sub Sahara Africa, like Nigeria has instead of progress hence regressed which is evident from the increasing child mortality rate, poor standard of living, etc.
It is commendable that Nigerian Government has realised it has made little progress in achievement of the MDGs, which necessitated creation of development strategies like the National Economic Empowerment Development Strategy, NEEDS, at the Federal level, State Economic Empowerment Development Strategy, SEEDS, at the State level and the Local Economic Empowerment Development Strategy LEEDS at the Local Government level.
A number of issues and concerns that borders on implementation and achievement of MDGs must however be addressed by the various economic development strategies.
One that works against achievements of the MDGs from NEEDS, SEEDS, LEEDS is the insistence of Government on core issues or focused areas of development. In Benue for instance, the core issues from BENSEEDS in the State are Health, Agriculture, Water and Education. Same applies to other States and the Federation.
Particular emphasis is placed on other sectors. The achievement of MDGs will however be difficult to achieve in such settings. There is a high level of independence between the eight Goals that cannot be waived. Progress in one can be wiped out by lack of progress in another field. NEEDS, SEEDS, and LEEDS can be restructured to take this into account.
For the MDGs to be achieved the poor and vulnerable groups would no-doubt require special attention, from the aged persons, slum dwellers, rural populace etc., all requires special attention. Women make up to about 50% of our population, to ignore them or not give special attention to gender in our Development efforts will invariably weaken all efforts towards the achievement of the goals.
HIV and AIDS has long been accepted as a development issue. It is gradually wiping out all the gains of civic and political rights. Sub Sahara Africa is the most hit in the world. Governments must direct more resources towards awareness creation and treatment.
Our government must address development from a Right based perspective access to public services like electricity, water, health care. This must be seen as duties and responsibilities of the Government. This is integral if we must achieve the MDGs.
The argument that most governments in the Sub Sahara Africa cannot deliver on this owing to economic factors makes no sense when viewed against the scale of corruption and stealing of public funds by Government personnel. If corruption is fought in sincerity, our Governments, especially the Nigerian Government can deliver on public services as a duty to the citizens.
Closely related to the above is the issue of foreign earnings vis development. Nigeria in particular needs to diversify sources of foreign earnings. Palm oil, cassava, tin, coal, ground-nuts, kola etc. can be exported from Nigeria, to add to our oil revenue.
Foreign debts can hamper the realisation of the MDGs. Against this backdrop, we welcome the recent sizable debt relief granted Nigeria by our creditors. Problem however is whether these monies will be channeled towards public good. Owing to an endemic corrupt attitude of our public officials, it would have been splendid if the World Bank and the Paris Club insisted on the condition that, they would monitor the expenditures of the funds that would accrue to Nigeria on the grant of the debt relief.
The International Community ought to have insisted that this money be put in the provision of infrastructure and other social services to be monitored by them. So while we shout hooray on the debt relief, we have great reservations as to what it portends in real terms to the ordinary Nigerians.
Associated with this argument is the condition that the outstanding of the debt owed $12 million be paid at once. Pray, which country can pay out such a huge amount from its foreign reserve without great effects on its economy. Street economist have argued that it is because the World Body is aware that Nigerian has over $20bn in its external reserve that has led to this condition. From whichever way one looks at it, it has implications on achievement of the MDGs.
Information flow is another critical element in the achievement of the MDGs. This is where we are dismayed at Senate over the non-passage of the Freedom Of Information (FOI) Bill that has since been passed by the House of Representatives.
Access to government information and dealings, would afford the private sector, NGOs and other stakeholders the opportunity to positively contribute in the discuss for sustainable development and can also act as a check or monitoring of programmes towards the achievement of the MDGs.
Finally, Government need to partner with civil society organisations (CSOs) and the Private Sector in the pursuit of the MDGs. CSOs have a history of working towards the attainments of the MDGs, they relate more directly with the Civil Society because of their projects at the grass root particularly the poor and the marginalised groups.
For Government to effectively deliver, it must of necessity develop partnership with the CSOs and help improve and support civil society capacity building in areas of policy analysis and research.
Mom, President, Lawyers Alert, wrote from Makurdi, Benue State.

Original_Search_Title: LPMC May Take Pre-War Status
Headline: Liberia: LPMC May Take Pre-War Status
Body_Text:
Upon taking office as Managing Director of the Liberia's Produce
Marketing Corporation (LPMC) last week, Mr. Nyah Mahten has assured
government and all concerned Liberians that "there's a very bright
future for the entity," stressing that his administration was prepared
to take LPMC from ashes to a pre-war status.
He said his immediate plans are the rehabilitation of all out
stations including Voinjama, Gbarnga, Ganta, Zwedru, Buchanan and
Tienni to facilitate the purchasing of produce during the crop season.
He also said his administration would introduce a new agriculture
produce (cassava) to create job opportunities for the rural population
and increase the country's foreign exchange earnings as well as the
re-introduction of palm kernels which will also create job
opportunities and increase the country's foreign exchange earnings.
Mr. Mahten also pointed out the under brushing and re-planting of
farms; the re-introduction of pre-harvest training programs to enable
farmers produce quality produce; and negotiation with foreign and local
creditors regarding LPMC's debts as part of his plans to regain the
lost status of the entity.
He, amongst other things, stressed that if they are to achieve these
objectives, then LPMC needs the participation of key institutions such
as the Cooperative Development Agency (CDA), the Agriculture and
Cooperative development Bank (ACDB), and the Liberian National
Federation of Cooperative Societies (LNFCS).
He has therefore appealed to government through the Ministry of
Agriculture to pay attention to the re-activation of these institutions
because they were created to serve farmers or represent their interest.
"As we assume the gavel of authority today, we owe it to the people
of this war-wearied nation to excavate those broken parts of this
entity which once served as agricultural cream of the land," he said.
He noted that the challenge to recreate LPMC and rekindle the spirit
of the Liberian produce farmers is their's because "the economic life
of the rural dwellers principally centers on their food and cash
crops." The new LPMC boss used the occasion to appeal to all employees
of the corporation for their full support and warned former officials
of the entity who still possess properties belonging to the entity to
turn them in immediately as a means of avoiding embarrassment.

Original_Search_Title: CCC Agro-Project in Full-Swing
Headline: Liberia: CCC Agro-Project in Full-Swing
Body_Text:
The Agriculture Department of the Concerned Christian Community
(CCC) has planting crops on a 50-acre farmland near Klay, Bomi County.
According to project manager Miatta Sirleaf, CCC embarked upon the
project as a pilot program intended to be used as a demonstration and
seed-multiplication and scion-management center for local farmers.
She said at the end of the sowing process that is currently ongoing,
several local farmers would be selected from the Klay District area to
be trained on-site in several methods of sowing and managing farm crops
for higher yields.
Amongst beneficiaries, according to her, will be war-affected women
and other individuals otherwise affected by the war from the Saye Town
and Klay Township areas.
"Others will be given the opportunity, but from the start, we will
use individuals living nearby. We are now planting cassava as the
initial crop after which we will introduce other crops and livestock
production," said Mrs. Sirleaf who is also the head of the Women and
Children Services Department.
She said currently, more than 75 women from the area are engaged with the planting process.
"These women will benefit from the process. One-third of the
proceeds will be given to the community to be redistributed amongst the
various farmers, another one-third would be used for the sustainability
of the project, and the rest would be used for replanting," she noted.
Expressing confidence in the viability of the project, Madam Sirleaf
said CCC successfully ran the project before it was destroyed by rebel
insurgency in 2000.
"At that time, we were involved in gardening, cash crops production,
and animal husbandry in which we raised pigs and other livestock. God
willing and with the assistance of our overseas partners, we will bring
the farm up to its pre-war status," she said.
Meanwhile, the Management of CCC says everything will be done to
ensure the viability of the project for the benefit of scores of
Liberian farmers who are returning to their villages and communities
following several years of living on handouts in refugee and IDP camps.

Original_Search_Title: Funding Ethiopia's Growth
Headline: Nigeria: Funding Ethiopia's Growth
Body_Text:
NIGERIA university teachers are in great demand in Ethiopia and
President Olusegun Obasanjo has promised to supply them as requested by
Yohannes Guirida Ginbi, the out-going Ethiopian ambassador to Nigeria.
At a time of high unemployment, even among the highly educated, the
request for 671 Nigerian lecturers would have been appreciated in all
quarters, it would have meant that more Nigerians would be employed and
they could repatriate some of their earnings home. The bitter truth is
far from this. Nigeria would pay for the lecturers and it is a lot of
money.
President Obasanjo has promised to dispatch the lecturers, some of
whom are experts in cassava production, to meet the need of the
Ethiopians, under the Technical Aid Corps, TAC, a 1987 scheme that was
promoted under the "concert of medium powers".
The Federal Government pays TAC members between $700 and $800
monthly, which at the official rate convert to N92,400 and N105,000. It
is instructive our country's minimum monthly wage is N7,500.
Nigeria would spend about N1.69 billion on the 671 lecturers in the
two years that is the usual tenure of the TAC scheme. Other African
countries benefit from the programme which means that the expenditure
would be much higher.
We consider this a waste of resources that could have been channeled
towards more developments at home. TAC has been used in the past to
provide human resources to needy African countries, but it has been
riddled lately with allegations that the allowances arrive late and
that officials back home see that they trade with the money approved
for the scheme.
Other issues standing against the gesture to Ethiopia include the
state of the national purse. Was the Ethiopian request that was made
and granted on the spot part of the 2006 budget? Will the National
Assembly be silent on this too and lament later about the President's
lack of respect for the budget? When would budgetary discipline start?
There is also the effect of this official brain drain policy on our
own higher institutions. Does the country have enough qualified
university lecturers to warrant sending 671 of them to Ethiopia alone?
The National Universities Commission, NUC, weeks ago declared that less
than 45 per cent of those teaching in the universities were qualified.
The NUC followed this declaration with a regulation that only Ph.d
holders would be qualified to lecture in the universities in the next
three years.
If this is the situation in the nation's universities, should the
government be depleting the human capital in the universities by
sending the few qualified ones abroad?
Apart from the damage that the TAC policy could do to the public
universities, the Federal Government is pursuing another initiative of
licensing more private universities which are better funded and are
poaching staff from public institutions.
The entire TAC policy is overdue for review as it is in conflict with our current national interests.

Original_Search_Title: 2006 Budget: FG Votes N568bn for Capital Projects
Headline: Nigeria: 2006 Budget: FG Votes N568bn for Capital Projects
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Federal Government has earmarked about N568 billion in the 2006 Budget for the implementation of capital projects all over the country. This is in addition to the N950bn already set aside for recurrent expenditure in the fiscal year 2006.
Presenting a breakdown of the 2006 Budget yesterday in Abuja, Finance Minister, Dr. Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala, also revealed that the Federal Government would subsidise petroleum products with N150 billion as provided in the budget so that "petroleum product prices can be held steady for the rest of this year."
The Federal Government, according to her, was expected to support with N75 billion while the states and local governments would provide the remaining N75 billion.
Okonjo-Iweala noted that 48.9 per cent of the N1.5 trillion projected revenue (Federal Government share) and 59 per cent of the capital budget had been allocated for ministries, departments and agencies (MDAs).
Education, she disclosed, has the largest of the sectoral allocations with 8.8 per cent of the total budget or 11 per cent allocated to MDAs with health coming next at 5.6 per cent of the total budget.
"This shows the importance the government attaches to these two sectors in view of their centrality and also for the attainment of the Millennium Development Goals.
"The areas of basic physical and human infrastructure are the areas most important to Nigerian households and businesses because they yearn for government to improve basic service delivery as fast as possible.
In Education, Okonjo-Iweala said the 8.8 per cent (N166.6 billion) was separate from the allocation for the Universal Basic Education (UBE) scheme which received additional N30.5 billion for a total of N196.6 billion to the Ministry.
Two key programmes, she pointed out, are N3 billion for feeding school children and N6 billion for training teachers.
She said health, which got a vote of N106.9 billion representing an increase by 52.7 per cent from N70 billion, has projects including completing the modernisation of teaching hospitals through VAMED programme, the National HIV/AIDS and STDs control programme, the Roll Back malaria programme as well as construction and equipping of primary health centres. Similarly, the minister said Power and Steel was allocated N78 billion in addition to $2.3 billion (about N296.7 billion) being spent on seven new power plants in the Niger Delta.
She added that "N74.7 billion or 95 per cent of the ministry's allocation has been allocated to capital projects including the completion of on-going power plants in Alaoji, Geregu and Papalanto, building transmission lines and improving distribution systems of the Power Holding Company of Nigeria (PHCN)," all with a view to meeting the power generation capacity target of 10,000 MW by Dec 2007.
According to the Minister, N80.2 billion has been allocated to the Water Resources Ministry, much of which will go into improving access to water in rural and urban communities, "a critical factor in the battle against poverty."
Priorities, she pointed out, included "completing the Gurara water project, executing rural water and irrigation schemes in various parts of the country, acquisition and rehabilitation of plant machinery and construction of small earth dam as well as other key projects."
She noted that in agriculture, the Federal Government has made initial provisions of about N1 billion (to be expanded as needed) for fighting the Avian flu, which will be used in conjunction with the donor assistance that was pouring in to tackle this problem.
She added that "the rest of the agriculture budget is focused on continuation of government initiatives on fertilizer, cassava, aqua culture, rural roads and rural water supply.
"About N10 billion in budgetary and other resources are also being used to catalyze a significant amount of agricultural credit of about N40 billion so that a total amount of N50 billion in agricultural credit at 8 per cent rate of interest will be available to both small, medium and large farmers this planting season.
"The CBN will transfer to participating financial institutions the financial equivalent of the difference between the lower rates of interest charged and the market rate so that farmers, small and large, will benefit from a subsidised rate of interest while participating financial institutions will be transparently reimbursed for the subsidy."
As it concerns roads, Okonjo-Iweala said N91.1 billion was apportioned to the Ministry of Works out of which N67.8 billion will go to the construction and rehabilitation of major highways across the country.
Key projects, according to her, included the dualisation of the two sections of the Onitsha-Owerri road, construction of the Ogbomosho-Ilorin road, completion of the two sections of the Benin Bye pass, construction of the Lamba-Kunchi-Kazaure road, rehabilitation of the entire stretch of Zaria-Funtua-Gusau-Sokoto road, rehabilitation of Benin-Ore road amongst others with a total of N13 billion budgeted for the maintenance of existing roads nationwide.
She noted that in response to public concern on security, the Police, Defence Ministry and Security Services will receive a total of N204 billion. She indicated that the focus will be to improve welfare and provide modern equipment for the Police, rehabilitation of staff quarters and barracks and training.
Okonjo-Iweala explained that there was "an exciting new feature" in the budget this year, to the extent that the Federal Government will use debt relief savings of Nl00 billion for additional financing to scale up programmes in education, health, environment, youth and gender, all central to the attainment of the MDGs.
According to her, "a tight Expenditure tracking and monitoring system called OPEN (Oversight of Public expenditures) has been established to track usage of these funds and monitor results on the ground. Budget 2006 also finally deals with the vexatious issues of pension and contractor arrears.
"Starting with pensions arrears, the government plans to keep current on its present payments while capitalising all the arrears owed and giving individuals a three to five year bond at a competitive interest rate." The same approach will be used to tackle contractor arrears except for the arrears of small (NI00 million and below) contractors, which we have budgeted N25 billion to retire. "I hope that Nigerians will support this approach to tackling these very serious problems that have persisted for some time."
She also said the 2006 budget supports other important expenditures that are national priorities such as the Population Census, the Election, through INEC, and the reform and restructuring of the Public Service for which N44 billion has been set aside.

Original_Search_Title: Saru Chief Laments Over Plight
Headline: Ghana: Saru Chief Laments Over Plight
Body_Text:
The Chief of Saru in a remote community, sharing border with the
Ivory Coast in Sawla-Tuna-Kalba District of the Northern Region, who
was captured and molested by Ivorian rebels said he had been sick ever
since the rebel's abuse.
Saruwura Seidu Seidu, who was recounting his ordeal to the Northern
Regional Minister at Saru, when the minister visited the community
said, "I have never recovered from the trauma and ordeal I passed
through in the hands of the rebels. I am old. I cannot walk properly,
so the rebels dragged me on the ground until we got to their camp".
The minister was on a 7-day tour of the western corridor districts
of the Northern Region, comprising Bole-Bamboi, Sawla-Tuna-Kalba, West
Gonja and Central Gonja districts.
The purpose of the visits was to enable the minister to know the
situation on the ground at the rural areas, see how best he could help
solve them and to explain government development policies to the
people. Key among the minister's message to the people in all the
communities is peace, the need for the Representation of the People's
Amendment Law (ROPAL), and the importance of the NHIS.
The chief said "I have always been praying to God to forgive them
because my children are older than them. The only thing I need is a
clinic in the community and tarring of the road from Kalba to Saru to
enable security men to come and guard us".
Saruwura Seidu Seidu said what had happened to him could generate
cross-border conflict in future, if the problem was not solved amicably
and called on government to intensify security on the country's
borders. "The rebels have not regretted from their act, since they
still cross to the community to trade with the people without coming to
apologize to me" he said.
He said ever since Dr. Kwame Addo Kufuor, Defence Minister visited
the community with some soldiers after his capture and release, fear
had gripped the rebels and that they were no more crossing to Saru with
weapons.
"They fear our soldiers.
Any time Ghanaian soldiers come to the community none of the rebels
would enter Saru market even though that was where they depend so much
for their needs. He commended the minister for visiting the community
and appealed to him to use his position to help the community with a
clinic and to construct their road to link them with the rest of the
country, since they had been cut-off from Ghana.
Alhaji Boniface consoled the chief, saying, "I am touched by your
pathetic story and I will do my best to see how fast government can
award contract on your road since there is abundance of food in your
community". Saru is a small community about 10 miles away from Kalba
and the people are engaged in yam, groundnuts, cassava and maize
production but cannot transport them to market centres in Ghana for
sale due to unmotorable roads.

Original_Search_Title: Ten-Year-Old Killed By Landmine in Casamance Region
Headline: Senegal: Ten-Year-Old Killed By Landmine
Body_Text:
Ziguinchor — Years of secessionist trouble in Senegal's southern Casamance region
claimed the life of a 10-year-old boy last week, killed by an anti-tank
landmine over a year after the signing of a hard-won peace deal.
The child, Cherif Sane, died while out collecting cassava with two
friends from farms in the forested region around his village Dijbelor
Baraf, some 5 km south of Casamance's main city, Ziguinchor.
Sane's school teacher, Cheikh Tidiane Cisse, was called to help but his pupil was already dead.
"One of my pupils, Cherif Sane had gone into the bush accompanied by
two of his friends on Tuesday night to transport cassava when he was
killed by an anti-tank mine that blew open his stomach," Cisse, who was
visibly shaken, told IRIN.
"When I heard the news I went to the scene, but the corpse was in a terrible state," he added.
The dead boy's two friends were also seriously hurt by the blast and
were transported to the main hospital in Ziguinchor, said their teacher.
Since 1990, more than 1,000 people have been killed by landmines in
Casamance, the lush southern region of Senegal that is partially cut
off from the rest of the country by Gambia.
In December 2004, the government of President Abdoulaye Wade signed
a groundbreaking peace deal with the Movement for the Democratic Forces
of Casamance (MFDC) to end more than two decades of secessionist
trouble.
Nonetheless, some MFDC splinter groups remain unhappy with the deal
and have continue to sow trouble along the long road linking Ziguinchor
to the capital Dakar. In January, a government official was killed by
unidentified gunmen in an attack suspected to be the work of separatist
guerrillas.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Kribi, a Small Paradise for Tourists And Aids
Headline: Cameroon: Kribi, a Small Paradise for Tourists And Aids
Body_Text:
Kribi — The inhabitants of Kribi, a well-known sea resort on the Gulf of
Guinea, were hoping that an oil terminal linking Chad to Cameroon would
solve all their problems of unemployment, poverty and isolation. But
three years after construction began, little has changed.
Between June 2001 and July 2003, with the financial support of the
World Bank, 1,070 km of pipeline was built linking the oil fields of
Doba in southern Chad to the oil tankers stationed outside the bay of
Kribi. The sea terminal, with a capacity to handle 225,000 barrels of
oil per day, was inaugurated in October 2003.
The economic boom experienced by Kribi has, however, heightened the HIV/AIDS risk.
According to Cameroonian activists, members of the consortium
responsible for the extraction and transport of the oil, had sensitised
their employees with HIV/AIDS education, but neglected to extend it to
the local population.
"The pipeline! It caused great damage and brought us misfortune
rather than happiness," declared Louis NziÃ© Owona, a professor of
accountancy at the Confessional College of Saint Joseph in Kribi, who
also runs the college's AIDS committee.
"Large numbers of girls came from all over the country to follow the
progress of construction, offering sexual services to the work force.
They waited around every corner for them to spend their salaries on
sexual favours," he added.
Valery Nodem, Coordinator of local NGO "Reseau de Lutte Contre la
Faim" (RELUFA), told PlusNews: "With a project of this magnitude, we
were expecting a campaign to prepare locals for such large influx of
foreign workers, most of whom were better paid and generally single."
According to Nodem, Kribi's population has increased tenfold in a
relative short period of time, with many houses transformed into
brothels.
TOURISM: SOLE SOURCE OF INCOME
But contrary to studies undertaken in Chad - which showed a link
between the development of the pipeline and the spread of HIV - no
statistical data can confirm what most health experts were dreading: an
increase of the HIV/AIDS epidemic, particularly among young women.
The small market town and its 50,000 residents are heavily dependent
on the tourism industry, which attracts expatriates, wealthy
Cameroonians from the capital and other large cities.
At dusk, motorcycles zigzag to and fro between nightclubs and
hotels, carrying young girls in search of money or their soul mates. It
is in these chaotic streets, alongside women cooking mackerel and
cassava, where casual relationships are negotiated, observed Martha, a
chambermaid in one of the numerous seaside hotels.
"Girls like the easy life, one day it's one guy, the next it's
another one, all they're after is a quick buck ... it sometimes works,
especially in the hotels," explained Martha who also admitted to being
unhappy with the US $73 dollars she gets monthly for cleaning rooms.
"A woman cannot live without a friend, and while struggling to find
money, one can end up doing silly things ... I don't always use
condoms, it depends if the man happens to carry one on him when we do
it, which is not always the case," she added.
FEW COME FORWARD FOR TESTING
Martha is not alone: very few people in the small market town have
come forward to get tested, and the hospital has the only testing
facility.
In Cameroon a state subsidized test costs US $1.8 dollars, expensive
for most people, although the health ministry regularly sends
technicians across the provinces to offer free testing.
In 2005, Kribi's district hospital was able to test 1,731 people,
compared to 274 in the previous year. Despite the fact that the
infection rate declined from 13 percent in 2004 to 9 percent in 2005,
figures are considerably higher than the national average of 5.5
percent.
This is hardly surprising when "no public financing has been made
available for the 20 or so local committees put in place by the
government's National Committee for the Fight Against AIDS since 2004,"
Professor NziÃ© Owona noted.
He added: "In 2003, I received 500,000 francs [US $900] in order to
purchase information material as well as to train our peer-educators
and to acquire condoms for the pupils. In 2004 I was given another 250
000 francs [US $450 dollars] but I have received nothing ever since."
Nzie Owona acknowledged that countering sexual tourism was difficult
when there was so much poverty. "If a girl earns 10 000 francs [US $18
dollars] with a tourist, what kind of job will she want to do after?"
Joseph, a family man who cleans rooms in a small hotel in the city
centre, said that girls engaging in sex work were helping their
parents, who were struggling to make ends meet "because of inflation
caused by the arrival of the Oil Consortium".
Kribi is surrounded by infertile land making it hard to cultivate.
Consequently all products are shipped from other parts of the country
making basic daily necessities more expensive, even before the
inflationary impact of foreign oil workers and tourists.
"Everything is expensive here. The solution is to cultivate our own
food but to do so you need to get far out of the city, and that
requires time, effort and resources. Nowadays the teenagers want an
easy life, the one the tourists have," explained Joseph, whose 14-year
old daughter just gave birth a few months ago.
"We know we live in a tourist town. I told her school should be like
her first husband, but she does not listen. She knows she will find
foreign tourists in the nightclubs and no work in Kribi. The oil is not
helping. We only see it from afar and then we see it leave."
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: RMRDC Advocates Cassava Development
Headline: Nigeria: RMRDC Advocates Cassava Development
Body_Text:
The director general of Raw Materials Research and Development (RMRDC), Engineer Azikiwe P. Onwualu, has suggested that for Nigeria to remain relevant in the global production and processing of cassava, research and development activities on the commodity must be given a serious attention. He said, "this will ensure sustenance of product varieties, volume, and quality in line with the demands of both local and international markets."
RMRDC boss, who made the declaration in Abuja at the cassava stakeholders' meeting, said with the presidential initiatives on the inclusion of ten percent cassava on the bread, the council organised a stakeholders' forum to articulate and chart a course for realizing the laudable objectives of the presidential initiatives through research and development.
He revealed that the exercise was also in line with what obtains in other parts of the world, such as Thailand and Brazil, where cassava is the principal crop for socio-economic and industrial development, as well as a major foreign exchange earner.
Engineer Onwualu told cassava farmers, processors, plants fabricators and flour millers who participated at the meeting to come out with practical proposals on how they would comply with the legislation of ten percent inclusion of cassava into flour mills daily.
He expressed the determi-nation of President Obasanjo that the government policy must be implemented in the next few months by all flour mills industries operating in Nigeria, adding that the government would ensure that stakeholders in the banking industries were monitored for compliance.
Also speaking with newsmen at the meeting, the national president of Nigeria Cassava Growers Association, Chief Moses Ayinmodu, said he was happy when President Obasanjo ordered that bread must contain ten percent cassava and that the demands of cassava for local consumption and international market would be high as many farmers across the nation complained of low sale and demand of cassava.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Stays Stable Despite Increase in Food Prices
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Stays Stable Despite Increase in Food Prices
Body_Text:
Kampala — FOOD prices in February rose slightly compared to January but the annual headline inflation rate for the month remained stable.
Annual headline inflation for the month of February stabilised at 6.2 percent, the same as in January.
Consumer price index for February released by Uganda Bureau of Statistics on Tuesday however, indicates that the monthly headline inflation rate went up by 0.2 percent compared to the 1.5 percent increase recorded in January.
This was attributed to the rise in food prices.
While presenting the February consumer price index highlights the UBOS Executive Director, Mr Male-Mukasa said increases in food prices played a major role in the monthly price average. "The inflation rate is highly driven by the food sector and the rain conditions," Mukasa said.
UBOS highlights show that matooke prices slumped in all centers except Gulu where increases were registered.
The report, however, says other staple food crops, especially sweet potatoes and fresh cassava, saw substantial price increases in all centers.
"Due to the ongoing drought conditions in the different parts of the country, increases were registered in prices of fruits, especially pineapples, sweet bananas and oranges," the report said. It said vegetables especially tomatoes, beans and onions also went up. Likewise, milk prices went up in a number of centers.

Original_Search_Title: Kwara Co-Operative Lifts Farming With 150 Tractors
Headline: Nigeria: Kwara Co-Operative Lifts Farming With 150 Tractors
Body_Text:
Lagos — KWARA State Cooperative Federation Ltd (KCF) has completed arrangements to procure and distribute 150 tractors valued at N510 million to farmers across the state on loan to boost farming practice next planting season.
Similarly, the Federation has bought a large no of sprayers direct from the manufacturer to its meeting centre at Obo road, Ilorin and the product is already being sold to interested members on cash and carry basis at the rate of N5,500 as against the prevailing market price of N8000 for the same product.
Disclosing these to Business Champion in Ilorin, the state president of the KCF, Elder Isaiah Ogundele said that the loan to effect the purchase of the tractors, which would be sold to prospective unions in the federation at the rate of N3,400,000 per unit as against the open market price of about N4.5 million, would be fallen from the National Agricultural Cooperative and Rural Development Bank.
He added that a deposit of N600,000 is imperative on a tractor, while the remaining balance would be made in two years.
Elder Ogundele, who explained that the cooperative federation, is present in every state in Nigeria, described it as the umbrella body for all the cooperative unions in the state, with each union having about between five and fifty or more societies under it.
The President, who himself is a prominent farmer stated further that the purpose of the Federation which comprises of farmers groups, artisan and others said that the purpose of the federation was to monitor and assist all cooperative unions to develop to their full potential by coming to their aid, to tap opportunities that would not have been easy for them to was it acting individually.
Noting that the KCF has five affliates, Elder Ogundele, who said that the manufacturer of the tractors, which is based in Japan had agreed to make the spare parts available and establish a workshop with state where every faulty tractor would be repaired free of charge for a whole year, said that the organisation has been of very immense benefit to all the member unions since 2004 when the KCF would revived having gone moribund for 15 years.
According to the President, very recently, the Kwara State Government gave 150 riding machines to KCF, which was distributed to member unions on loan to be repaid within the year, adding that the organisation was planning to build cassava procesing industries in several locations in the state, even as he said that in the organization, so many unions had borrowed substantial amount of money to execute various capital projects and to make available as loans to their various societies under each of them.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Remains Stable At 6.2%
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Remains Stable At 6.2%
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE annual headline inflation has remained stable at 6.2% for the
year ending February 2006, the same as last month, Uganda Bureau of
Statistics' (UBOS) monthly Consumer Price Index (CPI) has shown.
Matthew Ssewanyana, UBOS' director of macro-economic statistics,
said the rate had remained stable because of a slight change in
commodity prices.
"Though on average, the prices in February were slightly higher than
those in January, the price movements between February 2006 and
February 2005 were at the same level with those between January 2006
and January 2005," Ssewanyana said while releasing the CPI at the
Statistics House in Kampala.
He said food prices went up by 0.2%, with matooke prices going down
in all centres except Gulu while other staple foods like sweet potatoes
and cassava registered substantial increases in all centres.
Fruits, vegetables and milk also registered increases. Underlying
inflation went down to 4.8% compared to 5.6% in the year ending January
2006.

Original_Search_Title: Mukwaya Gives Tips to Farmers
Headline: Uganda: Mukwaya Gives Tips to Farmers
Body_Text:
DUE to inadequate and poor distribution of rainfall which was
experienced in 2005 and the resultant poor harvests across the country,
the ministry of Agriculture, Animal Industry and Fisheries has come out
to give farmers tips on how to ensure food security in the country this
year.
In a food security message to the farmers, the Minister of
Agriculture, Ms Janat Mukwaya, predicted a food shortage in April and
May and advised farmers to ensure that food reserves for the households
are taken care of as they sell their produce for cash.
The minister advised farmers of cereal crops like maize, not to
hurry harvesting, as it will affect the quality. She said that farmers
should ensure proper drying and storage of their crops to avoid post
harvest losses.
She said the Shs250 to Shs300 farm gate prices being offered now
should not demoralise farmers because the prices are expected to rise.
"You are advised to sell off only the surplus stock and in bits so that
you also benefit from the high prices projected from April onwards,"
she said.
Mukwaya said the lack of market information had created price
disparities as some farmers negotiate for good prices while middlemen
cheat those in rural areas.
She urged agricultural extension workers to disseminate market information in their work stations.
She said although beans and groundnuts yields were poor last year,
the farm gate prices of the two crops is now at Shs600 and Shs1200
respectively. Prices are expected to rise further due to high demand in
neighbouring countries especially Kenya.
She also advised farmers to dry, slice and store crops like cassava and sweet potato tubers, for future consumption.
"This is a good processing technology that avoids spoilage and ensures food security," she said.
Agriculture accounts for the livelihood of over 80 per cent of the
population but its growth remains low compared to other sectors because
of over dependency on rainfall and subsistence by over 68 per cent.
The contribution of the sector to the Gross Domestic Product declined from 37.3 per cent in 2003/2004 to 36.3% in 2004/2005.

Original_Search_Title: Canadians Commission $8 Bln Jatropha Project
Headline: Zimbabwe: Canadians Commission $8 Bln Jatropha Project
Body_Text:
Harare — THE people of Mudzi district have always grown the Jatropha plant for use as a form of fencing. But they never realised that they were sitting on a potential gold mine.
Last Thursday, the Canadian Ambassador to Zimbabwe, Roxanne Dube, commissioned an $8 billion Jatropha oil pressing, soap-making and bio-diesel plant in a project designed to ease poverty in the impoverished Mudzi district.
The Jatropha plant has several by-products, which experts and aid workers say could benefit the people of Mudzi and, on a larger scale, Zimbabwe as a whole. The Jatropha plant could help solve part of Zimbabwe's fuel crisis, which has gripped the country for years.
Recently, the National Oil Company of Zimbabwe (Noczim) began a programme to encourage farmers to grow the plant, offering technical support and educating them about the benefits of the plant. Noczim, which is the state fuel importer, has said it would purchase Jatropha seedlings at a price between $7 million and $10 million per tonne.
Women in Mudzi who are participating in the Jatropha project have been pressing oil from the plant, which they use to make bars of washing soap. After seeing a demonstration of the use of the soap by members of the project, the Canadian envoy said she would immediately explore a possible investment in the soap industry.
"After witnessing the qualities of the soap, I will personally work on a soap export industry to Canada," Dube said.
The Canadian International Development Agency (CIDA) funded the project under the auspices of a local non-governmental organisation, Edit Trust. To date, the project has benefited 300 households in the area.
The project focuses on empowering women and aims to promote the growth of drought tolerant and resistant crops that will benefit the communities. The crops include cassava, sunflower and Jatropha, the by-products of which can be used to generate income.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria Assists Ethiopia With 671 University Lecturers
Headline: Ethiopia: Nigeria Assists Ethiopia With 671 University Lecturers
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo has agreed to consider the request by
Ethiopia for 671 university teachers in addition to the 31 already sent
from Nigeria to enable that country cope with the new universities
being opened.
The President who stated this when he received the out going
Ethiopian Ambassador, Mr. Yohannes Guinda Ginbi on 28 February at the
State House, Abuja said the request would be considered under the
Technical Aid Corps (TAC) scheme in addition to sending some experts on
cassava.
He described the bilateral relations between both countries as
"very, very good," noting that "Nigeria is passing through an
interesting reformist period which we believe we need'" adding that
"the results are encouraging".
"All of us in Africa need critical self-analysis if we are to get to where we should be" He stressed.
The President said trade between Nigeria and Ethiopia must be
encouraged as Nigeria would consider buying Ethiopian meat and leather
products in exchange for Nigerian products in the spirit of reciprocity.
Responding, Mr. Ginbi said that he had learnt a lot from Nigeria's diversity and complexity during his two-year stay.
President Obasanjo also bade farewell to the outgoing Ambassador of
Cote d' Ivoire, Mr. Emille M' Iingui Keffa after nine years of
diplomatic tour in Nigeria.
The President sad that the events in Cote d' Ivoire were being
closely watched while urging Mr. Keffa to continue to render service to
Africa in his subsequent endeavours.
Mr. Keffa described his period in Nigeria as "enriching and
rewarding", noting that the spirit of reconciliation and
reconstruction, which he imbibed from Nigeria, would be useful in Cote
d' Ivoire.
He thanked President Obasanjo, the government and people of Nigeria for working towards peace in his country.

Original_Search_Title: FG to Help Ethiopia With Varsity Lecturers
Headline: Nigeria: FG to Help Ethiopia With Varsity Lecturers
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo has agreed to a request made by Ethiopia, to aid it by sending 671 university lecturers to teach in its universities.
Already, there are about 31 Nigerians already teaching in Ethiopia.
Obasanjo, who said the request would be considered by the Federal Government, however said the lecturers would be sent to Ethiopia under the Technical Aid Corps (TAC) Scheme, in addition to Nigeria sending more experts on cassava.
Receiving the out-going Ethiopian Ambassador to Nigeria, Mr Yohannes Guinda Ginbi yesterday at the State House, Obasanjo said like Nigeria, all other countries in the African continent need to do some soul-searching.
While describing relations between both countries as "very, very good," he said "Nigeria was passing through an interesting reformist period, which we believe we need. All of us in Africa need critical self-analysis if we are to get to where we should."
He emphasised the need to encourage trade relations between both countries, adding that Nigeria was considering buying Ethiopian meat and leather products, while Nigeria will export spirit-related products to the country.
In his remarks, Ginbi said the request for additional Nigerian graduate teachers was to enable his country cope with the new universities being opened, saying he has learnt a lot from the country's diversity and complexity during his two-year posting. Obasanjo, who also yesterday received the out-going envoy of Cote d'Ivoire, Mr Emile M'Iingui Keffa, said political events in the strive- torn French-speaking West African country are being closely monitored. He, however, urged the diplomat to continue to render service to the continent in future, and describe his nine years diplomatic tour of the country as eventful.
Speaking earlier, Keffa described his stay in Nigeria as enriching and rewarding, saying the virtue of reconciliation and reconstruction that he imbibed here would be applied in his country,and thanked government and Nigerians for working to achieve peace in Cote d'Ivoire.

Original_Search_Title: Budget 2006: Beneath a Civil Society Microscope
Headline: Nigeria: Budget 2006: Beneath a Civil Society Microscope
Body_Text:
Lagos — Federal Government proposed a budget of N1.88trillion for 2006, out of total expected revenues of N3.7trillion, based on average price of $33 per barrel of crude oil. However the civil society, at a recent summit, beamed its transparency searchlight on above budget, which is 23 per cent higher than the N1.5trillion for 2005; coming out with radical recommendations. Abimbola Akosile here presents the network's position on vital developmental issues
Budget Transparency Network (BTN), the umbrella network of civil society organisations in the six geopolitical zones of Nigeria, with a vision of a Nigeria where budgeting and public resource management respond to the demands of citizens, organised a Civil Society Summit on the Budget in early February in Abuja.
The Summit reviewed background papers and analysis prepared by experts in the different sectors with a view to harmonising the policy thrusts of the budget with actual appropriations and to ensure that the budget is pro-poor and meets the aspirations of majority of Nigerians.
The resultant position paper focused on nine key areas including the macro-economic framework, education, health, agriculture, water resources, environment, housing and urban development, power and works; with each sectoral review containing recommendations about source of funding for proposed increases.
The Network welcomed preparation of the budget within the context of a medium term expenditure framework to be accompanied by medium term sector strategies outlining key initiatives and expected targets or results to be achieved by key spending Ministries, Departments and Agencies (MDAs); and the policy thrust of the budget, which seeks to build human infrastructure for job creation and poverty eradication.
It (BTN) also lauded a channeling of the gains from debt relief amounting to N100b to the poverty reducing sectors of agriculture, education, environment, health, housing, power, water resources and support for women and youth.
However, after exhaustive deliberations at a forum which drew experts across virtually every sector of the economy with focus on the civil society and academia, the network made viable and useful recommendations on other aspects of the 2006 budget, which are outlined below.
Macro-economic Framework
Deficit: The fiscal deficit of N357billion should be drastically reduced by raising the prudent oil benchmark price to $35; indicators in the international oil market show that the $35 benchmark projection is realistic and sustainable.
The proposal to finance the deficit through privatisation proceeds and the sale of government property is not a fiscally prudent measure since these funds are not currently available but are mere expectancies that may or may not accrue within the year. Budget funding should be based on actual available revenue or precise proposals for borrowing or other sure means of revenue accrual.
Value Added Tax (VAT): The increased revenue projection based on an increase in VAT from 5% -10% without an enabling law lacks prudence considering that the legislature may not pass the bill into law. Again, fiscal prudence dictates that revenue projections should not be based on mere expectancies, which may fail to materialise if the legislature in its wisdom refuses to increase VAT charges.
Increase in VAT revenue in the budget without amendment of the enabling law is also objectionable on the ground that the legislature should not be faced with a fait accompli in the discharge of its constitutional law making duties.
Increase in VAT will ultimately be passed on to consumers in the form of increased prices of goods and services thereby decreasing the spending/purchasing power of the people. VAT should remain at the current 5%.
Medium Term Expenditure Framework (MTEF): MTEF leads to a predictable expenditure pattern, enhanced accountability and a lock in of future appropriation. The 2006 budget has deviated from the MTEF in many sectors such as education, health and agriculture. This sends a wrong signal about the nation's seriousness to implement the MTEF. The framework's projections should be implemented. Inflation Rate: It will be good for the economy to have a single digit inflation rate but this is unrealistic as at now because of the cost push elements in the budget for example payment of pensions and contractor arrears, census, elections etc. Targeting a 10-12% inflation rate is more realistic.
External Reserves: A transparent and rational basis should be found to distribute available resources between MDAs, statutory transfers, debt servicing and foreign reserves. The current foreign reserve of $32b is healthy. But a situation where sustainable development, economic growth and poverty reduction suffer while external reserves balloon is not in the best interest of the country.
Fiscal Responsibility and Other Bills & Legislative Budget Office: Considering the disputations and controversies over the expenditure and income of NNPC and oil companies and their contribution to the national economy, we recommend the early passage of the Fiscal Responsibility Bill which in its Part Four attempts to rein in the unruly horses of the national economy.
The National Assembly should also endeavour as a matter of urgency to establish a Legislative Budget Office to make for information parity with the executive. Other important bills that should given expedited passage by the National Assembly are the Procurement Reform Bill, Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative Bill, Access to Official Information Bill, Whistle Blowers Bill, etc.
Job Creation: With the approved proposal to sack not less than 160,000 from public service of the federation in 2006, the backlog of those sacked in 2005, the workers of privatised enterprises who will lose their jobs, workers in banks that did not meet the recapitalisation criteria and those to be rendered redundant as a fallout of mergers and acquisitions in the banking industry, it appears that there are no concrete job creating mechanisms in the budget to compensate for these job losses.
The enabling environment for the private sector to create jobs is not yet in place to compensate for public sector job losses. As such, NEEDS projections of 7million new jobs arising from the economic reform programme, by 2007 may not be realised.
Transparency Issues: Transparency and accountability should be seen, as integral to realisation of the macro-economic aggregates proposed in the budget. While BTN await the delineation of the contours of Oversight of Public Expenditure under NEEDS, it notes the need for an independent budget-monitoring group with representation from government, private sector and civil society. This recommendation will complement the oversight powers of the legislature.
Education
Education budget as a percentage of the total budget is 8.8%. This fails to meet the NEEDS standard which itself is below the internationally recommended standard and as such should be increased by 20% to facilitate the attainment of the standards set out in NEEDS. The education budget fails to meet the MTEF prescription for the year 2006, which is 9% of the total budget.
Allocation of 10.62% of the overall education budget to the main ministry is very high as the bulk of the work in education is undertaken in institutions and agencies outside the main ministry. Capital expenditure allocation of 3.0% to federal universities appears to be too low considering dilapidation and overcrowding (especially in first generation universities) on campuses. Facilities like laboratories and workshops require major repairs. N100 million allocated to the Ministry of Education for Aids appears unnecessary. There should be proper definition and clarification of what to do with the money for HIV/AIDS under the ministry vis, education specific strategic plan of Action and response to HIV/AIDS and Millennium Development Goals.
The allocation for technical and vocational schools have shown no appreciable growth in recent years and as such should be upwardly reviewed with the aim of providing funds for upgrading teaching and learning tools and equipment. In line with NEEDS goal of merit-based approach in teaching and learning, the education budget should specify allocations for scholarships and students loans, in order to promote scholarship and encourage brilliant indigent students. The allocation of N136m to the Federal Scholarship Board is obviously low and should be increased.
Health
The forum welcomed renovation and equipping of existing Primary Health-Care Centers (PHCs) but noted that focus on the construction of new PHCs appears to be of doubtful usefulness to communities. It recommended that the existing PHCs should be properly equipped, staffed and adequately provided with drugs; while a survey should be conducted to ascertain the areas in actual need of PHCs, rather than mere allocation of these centers based on geographical zones and states. A budget of N33 million per PHC appears high considering the facilities available at existing PHCs; a price of not more than N20m per PHC appears more in tune with the facts on the ground.
The long-term goal of providing a vaccine for malaria should receive budgetary funding so that while we roll back malaria, a long-term cure that reduces expenditure on malaria will be found. This approach also applies to goal of finding a cure to HIV/AIDS pandemic, which should be appropriately supported by the budget.
Considering the dilapidated and outdated infrastructure and equipment available at the nation's tertiary health centers, the total capital expenditure of Teaching Hospital across the country is very low. For instance, the Lagos University Teaching Hospital has a total capital budget of N130m, UCH Ibadan N130m, OAUTH Ife N80m, ABUTH Zaria N200m, UNTH Enugu N180m, etc. These should be increased to bring the institutions up to date to discharge their duties.
Agriculture
Agriculture contributes about 35% of the nation's GDP and employs the highest proportion of Nigerians. But allocations over the years to the sector have not matched the importance and contribution of the sector to the economy. The MTEF proposes 4% of the total budget for agriculture and rural development while the budget provided for only 2.8%. As a minimum, the MTEF projection should be met in the budget. The heading 'Presidential Initiatives' with a budget of N50m is a duplication of the specifically stated and budgeted for presidential initiatives for cassava, vegetable oil, etc.
Appropriation of N65m for implementation of rural development policy, rural community mobilisation N25m and model rural communities for N40m appear nebulous and for unspecified purposes. The forum wondered at the meaning of 'implementation of rural development policy', and claimed that all budgeted sums for rural development are obviously geared to implement the policy.
Provisions for production and development of prototype machines for cassava processing, melon shelling and multi crop thresher (N5.7m, N4.4m and N4.5m respectively) for the National Center for Agricultural Mechanisation Ilorin, is grossly inadequate for the purposes for which they were appropriated.
Water Resources
According to the Network, there is need to tie a sum of N400m quoted for 'MDG-related activities' to specific budget heads. To the members, Millennium Development Goals expenditure tracking; monitoring and evaluation of MDG specifics; mobilisation and enlightenment on MDG programmes/projects; MDG projects monitoring and tracking by the MDG secretariat are virtually duplications of a single activity.
The sum of N24.5m for water and sanitation and communications strategy should be elaborated upon and specified.
It is not necessary to budget the sum of N7.91m for HIV/AIDS related activities, targeted solely on staff of the ministry. At most, one million naira would adequately serve the purpose and excess could be redirected for capital projects in the River Basin Development Authorities.
It is not the duty of the government to form an NGO Network for N2,129,457.00; this appropriation should be re-channeled.
Under New MDG Projects Funded from Debt Relief, there is a duplication of appropriation for 'minor/other irrigation projects', the first for N141.9m while the second is for N29.01m.
Concentration of over 40% of capital projects in the main ministry appears cumbersome given the paucity of technical staff within the ministry and especially when it is realised that many of the projects are situated within River Basin Development Authorities (RBDAs). The budget of the RBDAs should be increased with the intention of creating solid implementing agencies for the future.
Environment
Capital allocation of N1.77billion is not adequate to address the current massive environmental degradations and to meet the goals of NEEDS and MDGs on environment, the group claimed.
Also, the subheadings of 'other miscellaneous expenses' for N29m, foreign service school fees/anti corruption for N1m; project 1 conservation and sustainable utilisation for 16m and project 2 for N3m should be clarified. Equally in need of clarification is the sum of N14.85m for monitoring and evaluation and the sum of N225m for community based municipal waste management project.
The environment budget should be increased to address urgent issues of degradation.
Housing & Urban Development
The appropriation of 0.4% of the overall budget to housing represents a decline of 27.9% and 6.3% from the 2005 and 2004 budget allocations. Allocation of 26.8% of the total allocation of the sector to capital projects is grossly inadequate. Evidently, the sector is relegated to the background when compared to allocations to other sectors.
Reduced capital allocation may hinder realistic development of new projects and provision of the required enabling environment for the private sector to provide housing for all segments of society.
That housing got N0.5b out of a total of N100b allocated to MDGs shows government's appreciation of its duties and responsibilities under the right to adequate housing. Coming from the background of constitutional provisions and article 11 (1) of the International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights, the budget is explicitly inadequate to cater for the needs of the poor when compared with preceding years budgets, population increases and existing housing deficits vis-a-vis the housing requirements.
There was no allocation for counterpart funding of the National Housing Fund (NHF) so as to provide adequate funding for on lending to contributors to the Scheme. The allocation to housing should be increased by at least 100% considering the enormous task of providing the enabling environment for the private sector to provide adequate housing.
Housing budget should clarify the contents of the allocations to provision of infrastructural facilities and prototypes for N600m and completion of estates for N265m, while the budget should specify the actual federal secretariat buildings to be rehabilitated. This, according to the network, will facilitate tracking, monitoring and evaluation of budget implementation.
Power
The power sector has benefited from generous budgetary allocations since 1999 and the current years budget gives power 14% of the total capital budget. However increased allocation has not resulted in enhanced service delivery and value for money by the Power Holding Company of Nigeria (PHCN). As the name implies, PHCN literally holds back power from citizens.
Revenue collection, which stood at N1.9b per month in 1999 has increased to a current rate of N7b a month, representing over 360% increment in revenue collection. This has also not been matched by enhancements in service delivery and Nigeria lags behind in power generation and distribution when compared to other countries with similar resource base.
From a generation capacity that was under 2000MW in 1999 to a peak of 3500MW in 2005 does not justify the huge expenditure in the sector. The percentage increase in allocations and revenue generation far outweighs generation and distribution growth in the sector.
Further, a bill is pending before the National Assembly seeking extra $2.3b for seven power plants in the Niger Delta. The evidence therefore is not about the paucity of resource allocations but its prudent management and expenditure.
The Network demanded greater prudence and transparency in the management of the allocated resources. The ministry should come forward with a detailed work plan that indicates what the appropriated sum will translate to in terms of improved services to the people.
The forum welcomed involvement of the private sector through the reform project but insisted that electricity should not be priced out of the reach of the poor. Further, if the state must divest, there must be value for money in terms of the price to be demanded from investors.
Works
The number of roads and projects simultaneously being worked upon by the Federal Government is unduly high. This raises the question of the capacity and ability of the ministry to monitor and supervise all the projects. It further raises the question of the adequacy of the resource outlay for each of the projects.
It appears that government budgets can hardly meet the capital requirements of the sector, hence the need for alternative sources of funding like the proposed Road Fund which will derive its funds from highway tolls, vehicle taxes, trucks weigh bridges and parking fees. However resources for road and infrastructure development is hardly the problem in the sector but the need to maintain accountability and follow implementation to the letter is more paramount.
Government should speed up introduction of efficient management and maintenance processes through the Build Operate and Transfer (BOT), Repair Own and Transfer (ROT), e.t.c mechanisms. The pending legislation in the National Assembly should be passed expeditiously.
Funding Proposed Increases
The Network's proposed increases, according to them, should be funded from the following sources.
Raising Benchmark Price to $35: The additional $2 per barrel accruing from raising the benchmark price of crude oil from $33 to $35 (at 2.5m barrels per day) will assist in this regard.
The Presidency: Considering the fact that Nigerians have been continually requested to endure some present hardship to secure a greater tomorrow, such sacrifice should start from the Presidency, which would mean leadership by example. The total figure of N1,840,735,363 Travels and Transport-General is very high and not more than N900m will serve the purpose. BTN recommend re-channeling of the excess N900m. the provision of N3.2m for 'spectacle advances' appears spurious and should be re-channeled. The purchase of national flags of 151 countries for over N51m is wasteful and as such should be reassigned. Nigeria can get the national flag of these countries through diplomatic channels and even if they are to be purchased, the price is unduly high.
Under Office of the Secretary to the Government of the Federation, there is an appropriation for N350m to the South South Health-Care Delivery Programme and a further N250m to the same purpose. Thus, the first N350m should be reassigned. Further, Affairs of Former Heads of State attracts N105m and another N35m for replacement of cars, computers and equipment. The second sum of N35m serves no purpose and should be reassigned.
By Section 151 of 1999 Constitution, the National Assembly is empowered either by law or resolution to fix the number and remuneration of advisers to be appointed by the President. BTN notes that the number of advisers, assistants, e.t.c at the Presidency exceeds what is needed for good governance and constitutes an unnecessary drain to the nation's resources. The large number does not tally with the right-sizing and job shedding mood promoted by the economic reform programme. The Network therefore recommend to the National Assembly to trim the number.
Legislature: In the budget proposal for House of Representatives, there are a number of omnibus provisions. The first is the sum of N6.19billion for 'miscellaneous' while the second is for 'unforeseen expense outside budgetary provision' in a sum of N1.15billion, and the third is for 'other miscellaneous expenses' in a sum of N2.48billion.
Apart from the above mentioned, throughout the budget of the National Assembly runs the concept of 'miscellaneous' and 'other miscellaneous expenses' and these amount to over N3.3b. BTN notes that these large chunks of money in excess of N12.3b are for purposes that are not clearly defined. As such, they need to be reassigned to areas that will impact on the poverty profile of the country.
Others: Funds will also accrue from earlier discussed budget heads that cannot be properly clarified to the satisfaction of the National Assembly or in circumstances where a lesser amount will execute the job for which a higher amount has been provided.
After all said and done, Budget Transparency Network (BTN) has done a great job of dissecting the 2006 Budget, with useful and viable recommendations, which if adopted and strictly implemented, is capable of lifting Nigeria above its current status as a wealthy but poor nation. Given that focus is on key sectors of the economy, the onus is on government to hasten to put these recommendations to practice, so that the citizenry can truly benefit from a democracy which has failed to realise their dreams for a great today and a better tomorrow.
It is only when imputs from all concerned stakeholders, from government consultants to civil society advocates, from the common man to the highest academician or technocrat; that the nation can truly begin to talk about national development and actualisation of the MDGs. A word for the discerning authorities.

Original_Search_Title: Rains Destroy 200 Houses in Kunhinga
Headline: Angola: Rains Destroy 200 Houses in Kunhinga
Body_Text:
Kuito — Two hundred houses were destroyed this month at Kunhinga municipality, 30 kilometres north of the central BiÃ© Province, due to the recent heavy rains befalling that area, ANGOP has learnt.
According to Kunhinga administrator, Israel ValÃ©rio, large part of the destroyed houses belong to disabled people, built under the project "Directed Self-Construction", of the local department of Welfare and Social Reintegration and some schools built with un-burnt sun-dried brick of primary and secondary schools.
The source informed that the torrential rains devastated equally fields of maize, beans, cassava and vegetables.
The government official stated that the situation is serious due to the fact that the Ministry of Welfare and Social Reintegration, humanitarian agencies and non-governmental organisations have not yet supported the affected population with zinc sheets and first aid needs.

Original_Search_Title: Taking On Healthy Circumcision
Headline: Uganda: Taking On Healthy Circumcision
Body_Text:
David Mafabi is bare-chested with sweat dropping from the beads
hanging around his chest and neck. He looks tired but fearless at the
same time, he has to make it at all costs. It is time for the cultural
initiation of circumcision (Imbalu) that proves that he has grown up
and is a man.
A large excited crowd is chanting and encouraging him to move on and
face the challenges of a man. But Mafabi throws the crowd in silence
for a moment as he stops at the doorway and rolling his eyes, looks
around the crowd as if looking for path to run away from reality.
In a flash, his father appears from the crowd with a goat and gives
it to him. He then shouts "Masaba oyee!" and the crowd replies in
excitement, "oyee."
Mafabi jumps bravely with a sharp knife to the circumcision spot
while the crowd watches curiously to ensure that he doesn't take any
backward step or show any cowardice during such an important ceremony.
On arrival at the spot, the traditional surgeon called Umukyebi and
