his assistant are anxious to accomplish the job. The assistant measures
and marks the part to be cut off. The Umukyebi with the help of the
assistant cuts the foreskin off Mafabi's penis.
An onlooker would be surprised for Mafabi didn't show that he felt
pain of any sort. Immediately after the surgery, he went to Buhugu
health centre for a tetanus shot to protect himself from the threat of
the deadly infection. Traditionally however, Mafabi's circumcision
wouldn't have been considered a real Gisu circumcision.
Change from the past
According to an elder Stephen Mayende, 87, of Bushigai sub-county in
Manafa district, the Imbalu tradition has undergone many swift changes
in the recent decades due to the new challenges in the society.
Mayende says that in reality, the Bagisu men circumcised these days
are not truly Basani (a man who is rightly circumcised) but Basinde (a
man who is uncircumcised).
He argues that everything intended in the Imbalu tradition is
actually the opposite today. "In the past, before a man would get
circumcised, he would walk in processions and also dance the Kadodi
dance through the villages for weeks. In those processions, the boy met
all his relatives and collected gifts," Mayende recalls.
He insists that the main reason for making these boys move long
distances visiting different relatives was to strengthen the
relationship with family friends that would help him in future.
On the circumcision day, the boy performs a lot of traditional
ceremonies where he gets blessings and is also presented to the spirits
that will protect him in his new life. On the same day, herbs called
Ityanyi are administered and they are said to give the person to be
circumcised the courage to desire to be circumcised. The herbs are
believed to very strong to the extent that if the candidate isn't
circumcised by anyone, he can do it himself.
Then cassava flour and yeast are smeared on his head as a form of
decoration and identification. During the circumcision, Mayende says, a
very blunt knife would be used in order to inflict enough pain on the
boy since the main reason for circumcision is to prove to the society
that you can stand problems in future.
"Sometimes, a knife was first put on fire until it was red hot then
used to cut," says Mayende. "It was so painful but you wouldn't say no.
Any cowardice prompted a beating and all the gifts given to you would
be taken. This was and is still seen as a shame to the clan and the
parents of the 'candidates' for circumcision," he adds.
Customarily, one knife is used to circumcise all candidates in the
same locality. Local herbs called Nandwasi cure the wound and the
ceremonies go on for some days until the boy heals, which in the past
was tested by him having intercourse with a girl of his choice.
Pockets of conflict
Certainly, Imbalu cultural practices have changed due to the
numerous challenges in the world among which is HIV/Aids. But the old
generation criticise these changes, which they say have led to moral
decay among the young Bagisu generation.
Fred Nangwali of Bunamubi in Manafa District says the existing
problems are a result of the new system. "We should go back to the old
system because nowadays, children don't have morals. You can even see
the way they behave in the community," he says, He reasons that the
"blessings, lessons and pain a Mugisu suffered during circumcision
helped him to stand strong when he met challenges in the future."
"Unfortunately, these days, the youth don't get these good
experiences and thus haven't worked as we used to in our youth," he
notes angrily.
James Maido, the surgeon who circumcised Mafabi agrees that the
tradition has changed but he says it is due to the current health
problems facing the community.
"The government advised us to use a knife per 'candidate' and he has
to bring it himself because of the fear that the surgeon used it on
another person who might have been HIV positive or had other sexually
transmitted diseases," Maido says. "The new system of using one knife
per person wouldn't be bad but in our culture, the knives are made by
special people under the influence of the spirits," he says, adding
that, "These people are very few to make knives for all candidates.
Secondly, to get them (knives), we have to pay a lot of money
especially during the busy seasons of August and December." Formerly,
boys of one lineage or clan were circumcised from one place. But when
HIV spread to the community, things changed.
The elite who had gained knowledge about the ways in which HIV is
transmitted no longer wanted surgeons who didn't use gloves. Some even
started to shun the custom or took their children to hospitals where
the risks were minimal. Others resorted to circumcising their children
a few months after birth in the hospitals to avoid such risks in their
children's puberty stage when they have to be initiated.
Big improvement
According to Dr. James Magomu, the situation has changed and new
methods have to be used to tackle the new challenges. He says that with
the current health standards, a professional medical person must be
present at every circumcision centre to help the patients if there is a
lot of bleeding and also see to it that the person is given a tetanus
shot immediately after circumcision.
He explains that since the communities are more congested than they
used to be in the past, there are high chances of contracting deadly
diseases like tetanus which is why the shot is given immediately to
reduce all risks.
Secondly, the tradition of using the same knife on all initiates is
only practiced if the knife is sterilised before it is used on another
candidate.
"After a surgery, the boy is examined by a professional medic to see
whether he needs an injection of vitamin K if there is excessive blood
loss. In some cases, he might be in need of a blood transfusion," Dr.
Magomu says. "Now people are used to the new system and in the absence
of the medics, they ask where they are before the ceremony starts," he
explains.
With the modern treatment, it takes only three to four weeks for a
circumcised patient to heal. Not only has the culture been altered in
the health sphere, it has also changed in the way the ritual is
conducted. These days, they rarely hold long-distance processions as
they use to in the past.
Maido blames the problem on the poor economic situation they are
facing. "Today, people don't have enough food even for their own
survival.
The problem is affecting all families that is why people organise
traditional ceremonies individually in their homes to avoid going to
their relatives who are already poor," Maido says. "In the past, there
was a lot of food to feed people for weeks, for instance those who
escorted the candidates in processions locally called bamaayo. The
candidates had all the time to fully engage in the traditional
customs," he recalls.
Most of the candidates these days are between 12 and 28 years of age
who are still in school or in active service. They don't get enough
time during holidays to fully get involved in the culture. So they
prefer the system that takes a short time.

Original_Search_Title: Project Set to Benefit Over 500 Mudzi Families
Headline: Zimbabwe: Project Set to Benefit Over 500 Mudzi Families
Body_Text:
Harare — CANADIAN Ambassador to Zimbabwe Mrs Roxanne DubÃ© on Thursday
commissioned a $8,56 billion Mudzi jatropha oil processing, soap making
and bio-diesel project that is expected to benefit more than 500 mostly
women and child-headed families.
The project, which is being funded through Canadian International
Development Agency's Environmental Response Fund, covers two wards --
Chikwizo A and Goronga B.
Ambassador DubÃ© said the project was aimed at mitigating climate
change induced vulnerability by promoting the growing of drought
resistant crops that are of economic benefit to communities.
These include cassava, sunflower and jatropha whose by-products can also be used to generate income.
The project is being implemented through Edit Trust, a
non-governmental organisation that seeks to improve communities through
interventions that built their capacities to diversify food resources
and sources of household income.
She said her country was pleased to be associated with the
establishment of such income generating projects that would help
enhance the lives of the disadvantaged in the country.
"Canada is pleased to be part of this climate mitigation project and
will contribute immensely towards transforming the lives of people in
Mudzi district.
"The project aims at reducing the devastating effects of climate
change on families by encouraging them to grow crops that are drought
resistant and in the process generating income and creating employment.
"Most important to me as a woman is that the project will benefit
women and child-headed families. My government pledges to continue
working with the Government of Zimbabwe in areas that improve the
people's lives. I hope to facilitate the export of the products from
this project to Canada so that these women can get foreign currency,"
she said.
Guest of honour at the commissioning ceremony, Mashonaland East
Governor Cde Ray Kaukonde said his province was grateful with the
donation that would improve the lives of the people in the area.
"The project will indeed improve the lives of people in Mudzi
district, which falls in one of the low rainfall areas of Zimbabwe.
"Zimbabwe is prepared to work with those non-governmental
organisations that want to uplift the people and not those with hidden
agendas. The gesture by the Canadian government and Edit Trust clearly
shows that through co-operation we will improve the lives of our
people," Cde Kaukonde said.
He said he hoped the project would continue to grow to other areas of the district.
"We hope we will continue to work together and develop this project
so that it can spread to other areas of the district. As a country, we
can not operate alone hence we welcome projects like these that improve
lives of our people."
Through the project, five-nursery centres and green houses were
established to offer technical advice to farmers and to provide
nurseries for jatropha and cassava.
The project will raise awareness among the people on the value of
jatropha and sunflower as sources of oil and erecting an oil-pressing
machine for the production of diesel and soap.
The Government has been encouraging the growing of the jatropha
plant that can be used in the manufacture of diesel and animal food.

Original_Search_Title: Recalling 2001, Besigye's Year
Headline: Uganda: Recalling 2001, Besigye's Year
Body_Text:
Kampala — David Kezio-Musoke covered the 2001presidential campaigns and brings back to memory some of the events of the Besigye campaign.
WHAT is it about Col. (rtd.) Dr. Kizza Besigye that has so fixed him
in the media's consciousness? Why does he tower over all the other
presidential candidates? Trying to trace answers to these questions,
some of us have dug through memories of our encounter with this man.
The year 2001. It was a year of bitterness, defiance and anger.
My first encounter with Besigye was in January 2001, in a small seminar room at Hotel Africana.
Presidential campaigns had been banned and he had dubbed this
gathering "consultations with professionals". The room was packed,
though there was none of the pomp that one would expect at a
presidential campaign. There were no dignitaries but there was a sense
of suspense, typical of a Besigye function.
Besigye spoke raucously for about two hours. Days later, newspapers would described him as angry and strong-willed.
At this gathering, Besigye recounted his experience of the National Resistance Army war, coining the metaphor, "chicken eaters".
He explained that the "chicken eaters" fighting in the bush made
others eat cassava roots while they feasted on chicken because they
thought of themselves as the brains behind all operations. "This is
selfishness," he said.
Soon after that, Besigye suggested that presidential candidates be screened for HIV before nomination.
What did we make of Besigye's character? Who ordained him? Was it
the soldier in him? This guy was daring. The press referred to him as a
supremely self-confident man with an enormous ego. Others called him
selfless.
That year, two events made us look professionally inept. One was the
arrest of Maj. Okwir Rwaboni and the other was Besigye's disappearance
from the country.
While we covered the SMART summit at Munyonyo, Besigye was busy
putting together his exit plan. Our excitement to interview Sudan's
President Omar el-Bashir diverted all our energy from the Besigye
story. Bashir had not granted an interview in a year and The New Vision
secured an exclusive with him.
Early the next day, we thought we had an edge over our competitors
but The Monitor scored with the headline "Besigye goes missing".
We should have seen that coming. An acid sour Besigye had earlier
warned on Radio One that he could sneak out through any route besides
Entebbe Airport. His warning came just after he had been barred from
flying to Johannesburg on suspicion of being involved in terrorism.
Covering Besigye, we met people we had never thought of meeting. One
such person was CNN's Catherine Bond. We also met a horde of the
international journalists who once spent a cold rainy night at Rock
Gardens hoping to charter a plane to Runkungiri, where Besigye's last
rally was meant to be. He, however, cancelled it, citing violence and
so the foreign journalists spent the whole night drinking liquor till
morning when Anne Mugisha, Besigye's aide, invited us for the last
international press briefing.
At the Grand Imperial Hotel briefing, Besigye was asked if he was in
any ill health. He responded, "I suffer from no ailment that can hinder
me from running the country."
Time magazine had earlier published an article which quoted
President Yoweri Museveni as saying Besigye had HIV. The New Vision
republished this article and this fuelled a hate relationship between
the Besigye camp and The New Vision.
It was very difficult to identify oneself as a New Vision journalist at any of Besigye's functions.
In February 2001, I called up James Opoka, one of Besigye's aides
for a comment and he shouted, "We don't talk to The New Vision," before
slamming down the phone.
Besigye's group intensified calls to its supporters to stop buying The New Vision and its sister papers.
In April after the Supreme Court announced that Yoweri Museveni was
the winner of the 2001 election, The New Vision ran a rather poetic
editorial headline, "The die is cast the contest is done."
That unprecedented case had brought a lot of excitement. Besigye, in
one of the exclusive interviews he granted us, said he had assembled a
team of 40 lawyers. We hurriedly wrote the story but most of the
lawyers listed denied any involvement.
Days later the Museveni's group announced they had a team of 100
lawyers. Besigye then announced the presence on his team of Prof.
Richard Pildes, a former consultant lawyer of then US Vice-President Al
Gore.
Apparently to counter this, John Khaminwa and Eugene Wamalwa both renowned Kenyan lawyers joined the Museveni team.
While 2001 was full of drama, intrigue and conspiracy, we certainly saw humour too.
The encounter between Museveni and Joseph Balikuddembe, Besigye's
lead counsel, is one of those worth remembering. Balikuddembe visited
State House, Nakasero to serve Museveni with the election petition.
Museveni: Are you from the enemy camp?
Balikuddembe: I am looking for a gentleman who was declared winner
of the election. The law requires me to serve him. It is not easy to
trace that gentleman but today my colleagues managed to trace him and
that is why I am here.
Museveni: But I have heard that this gentleman is immune to prosecution.
Balikuddembe: I am serving him in his personal capacity as president-elect and not as president, head of state.
Museveni: Let me accept so that you don't say that I am holding up the process.
Balikuddembe: We also hope that after we have won, the gentleman who was declared winner will give us some money.
In 2001, I met all the candidates save for Museveni and DP's Francis
Bwengye. Nasser Sebaggala had a lofty vision which the media saw as
hopeless. Charles Senkubuge and Aggrey Awori were a pair of comics.
Kibirige Mayanja of JEEMA was very much aware he was losing. All of
Museveni's rivals, apart from Besigye, seemed one-dimensional.
However, Besigye was far too bitter a candidate. Five years later, not much has changed.

Original_Search_Title: Zim Can't Do It Alone - Governor
Headline: Zimbabwe: Zim Can't Do It Alone - Governor
Body_Text:
Nyamapanda — A senior government official last week echoed President Robert
Mugabe's recent statement saying Zimbabwe needed to "build bridges"
with the international community and non-governmental organisations
(NGOs) in order to foster national development. Governor and resident
minister for Mashonaland East province, Ray Kaukonde, said Zimbabwe
could not achieve national development if it continued to work in
isolation.
"We can't work in isolation. We have to work together. Let's build
bridges and the bridges can start to be built from the grassroots,"
Kaukonde said.
Kaukonde was speaking at the commissioning of a $8.56 billion
Jatropha oil pressing, soap making and bio-diesel plant, funded by the
Canadian International Development Agency (Cida)'s Environmental
Responsive Fund (ERF).
The project, aims at mitigating climate change-induced vulnerability
by promoting drought tolerant crops, involves extracting of bio-diesel
from Jatropha seeds, oil from sunflower and soap making.
Kaukonde said the country needed things "like drugs" and to get them
Zimbabwe needed to "work with the international community and NGOs as
long as they have developmental agenda".
Zimbabwe has been placed on targeted sanctions by the West which
accuses President Robert Mugabe's government of gross human rights
violations. However, a defiant Mugabe has on several occasions been
quoted as saying Zimbabwe can go it alone without international
assistance.
He accuses the West, especially Britain and America and NGOs, of
wanting to effect regime change in Zimbabwe, even though most of the
organisations are empowering and feeding poverty-stricken villagers.
Commissioning the project, Canadian Ambassador Ms Roxanne DubÃ©, said
the project would enhance household incomes, guarantee food security
during droughts and help protect the environment for surrounding
communities.
"It seeks to increase food security through the increased production
of cassava, and to increase real incomes through the processing and
marketing of soap, edible oil and a variety of cassava products," DubÃ©
said.
The project, which is spearheaded by Edit Trust, a local NGO, is
benefiting more than 500 households, mostly women- and child-headed
families in Mudzi district in Chikwizo Ward A and Goronga Ward B.
Other than helping improve people's standards of living, the project also helps protect the environment from erosion.
The Jatropha plant is popular as a form of fencing around gardens, homesteads and schools.
"Furthermore, the press cake from the plant, one of the by-products
of the oil extraction, is a valuable organic fertilizer that is
comparable to chicken manure," said DubÃ©.

Original_Search_Title: Food Prices Still Stable
Headline: Uganda: Food Prices Still Stable
Body_Text:
Kampala — FOODSTUFF prices have remained stable but still high despite an increase in supply. A survey in the three major markets of Nakawa, Nakasero and St. Balikuddembe (Owino) this week showed that prices of maize and cassava flour, beans, groundnuts, sugar and soap remained the same as last week.
Maize flour is selling at between sh600 and sh800 per kilo, while cassava flour is at sh700 a kilo, the same as last week.
A kilo of sugar is going for sh1,500 and a bar of soap for between sh800 and sh900 as last week.
There has been an increase in the supply of tomatoes, onions, greens, fresh beans and cowpeas, following relative spells of rainfall in some parts of the country. The drought had pushed prices for fresh commodities high.

Original_Search_Title: 'I Was a Hooker for 13 Years'
Headline: Zimbabwe: 'I Was a Hooker for 13 Years'
Body_Text:
Harare — SHE spoke with a shriek as she poured her heart out, hands gripping a microphone like a desperate person clutching a straw.
The testimony she gave did not only shock the audience that watched
her from a distance, but brought tears to their eyes. They stared in
awe and utter bewilderment.
All you could see were heads shaking in disbelief.
The woman spoke with so much verve as she publicly recounted, for
the first time, about her former life as a commercial sex worker.
Somewhere in the room sat the Minister of Women's Affairs, Gender
and Community Development Cde Oppah Muchinguri, who listened and
intermittently nodded majestically, as the short and stout woman guided
her audience through her chequered past.
In the midst were men and women engaged in commercial sex work who
had also come to witness the launch of Cave of Adlam, an organisation
that seeks to empower commercial sex workers by constructively engaging
them in worthwhile activities, to keep them off the streets.
Thanks to Ms Patricia Rodrigues, a former hooker who has since
transformed the former Earlside Hotel in the Avenues area of Harare,
into a training centre to rehabilitate commercial sex workers.
The hotel was formerly a dingy, dark, smelly and terrifying place that once operated as a brothel.
It was one place, where a lot of vice would take place, even in broad daylight.
"I was married and decided to go into the streets to work as a
hooker for 13 years after my marriage had broken down. I led the most
horrendous and humiliating lifestyle.
"Unopindurwa sebhora (I was forced to perform lurid sexual acts) and
the men sometimes would steal the money I would have worked for the
night.
"I would take any man for as long as he paid. These ranged from
policemen, drunks, and company executives. It had to be any man who
could give me money for my children's upkeep.
"I can never forget the terrible odour that permeated from some of
those men. Sometimes I could not even breathe. It was such a traumatic
experience for me," said Ms Rodrigues, whose eyes welled up with tears.
But one day her life was transformed when her grandchild died.
"My grandchild died because of neglect, a thing that saw me reflecting on my life.
"I did a lot of soul searching and then took a giant step and
approached a pastor from some Pentecostal church who counselled and
convinced me to quit prostitution.
"I told the pastor I was a prostitute and that I needed help to
leave the streets. I lived in some dusty cabin at a house in Queensdale
and if you ask anybody in that area about me, they will tell you kuti
ndaiva hure remakoko ini (I was a hardcore prostitute)," she said.
Ms Rodrigues said that it was during that time when God spoke to her
and encouraged her to preach sanctity to commercial sex workers.
"I heard God's voice that night speaking to me. What, however,
puzzled me was that I had nothing in terms of savings and resources to
kick-start such a project and that revelation continued to haunt me for
years that were to follow," she said.
On April 1 2005, a story about a commercial sex worker who had been
murdered at Earlside Hotel was run in one of the weekly newspapers and
set the precedent on the positive transformation of the hotel.
The hotel has since reopened under a different proprietor and no
longer runs as a brothel that it had been known for so many years.
Ms Rodrigues said she went to the hotel and told management that the
hotel would be closed and reopen under different management.
"God directed me to this place and told me this was where I would
start a ministry to rehabilitate women and men who are engaged in
commercial sex work. But I kept asking myself how people were going to
believe my story because it was common knowledge that Earlside Hotel
was a brothel.
"And who would have believed I was serious about this project
particularly when this was being spearheaded by a former prostitute? It
is not easy to come out in the open and say I am a prostitute," she
said.
Ms Rodrigues said she went to see Cde Muchinguri who offered to assist her in every way possible to ensure the project took off.
Vice President Cde Joice Mujuru also sent a representative earlier in the year to assess her needs of the project.
The Vice President has come a long way in ensuring the rehabilitation of former commercial sex workers.
Cde Mujuru, together with the late First Lady Amai Sally Mugabe,
were instrumental in the establishment of a housing scheme for
commercial sex workers in Nyameni Township in Marondera which saw most
women leave the trade in the late 80s.
Most of the women have since embarked on several income-generating
projects and some are doing well as vendors and cross-border traders.
The women are now leading very normal lives as resourceful members of their community.
"The Vice President gave me an ear to listen to my concerns and I am
pleased that although she has not been able to come personally to
officially launch my project, I feel greatly humbled by the presence of
Cde Muchinguri whom she sent on her behalf.
"No one wants to be a prostitute . . . and I say no one does," said
Ms Rodrigues, who works with three full-time ministers of religion at
Earlside Hotel.
Cde Muchinguiri condemned prostitution which involves young girls
who are lured by relatives or family acquaintances under the pretext of
job offers and schooling.
"Young girls are now engaging in commercial sex work and I call that
rape because no young girl is capable of making such decisions.
"This is a matter of concern not only for the Government but society as a whole," she said.
Cde Muchinguri congratulated Ms Rodrigues for the bold approach that
she took and advised her to consult her ministry regularly for
assistance.
"We have projects ranging from cassava farming, baking, chicken
rearing and training in sewing by the Chinese. Funds can be sourced
from Sedco at 30 percent interest rate only," she said.
Professor Marvelous Mhloyi of the University of Zimbabwe also spoke
at length and outlined the different kinds of commercial sex workers in
Zimbabwe.
She explained that clients of these commercial sex workers were people who could afford to pay for the services.
"These men are permanent secretaries, chief executive officers, rich
and wealthy people. These are the people who also have small houses
which is, in actual fact, legalised prostitution.
"These educated men will strip off their education and position and get someone much lower in status than their wives.
"Their brains come down from the head to the groin. Commercial sex
workers, who most people insult, are actually powerful women because
they can make those men do what their wives can't," she said.
Prof Mhloyi said it is the men who were a problem because if they
left these women alone, they would run out of business. She also said
marriages were now risky in the face of HIV and Aids.
"A prostitute will demand condom use but a wife can't. If there is
an influx of commercial sex workers in any given area, it means that
men living in that area are also of loose morals.
"If men are real men, they should desist from going for prostitutes,
and that alone will force them out of business and is important in the
reduction of HIV and Aids infection rate," she said.
When the meeting took a break at lunch-time, Ms Rodrigues took to
the microphone once again and sang three of her compositions backed by
four young men who played marimbas and guitars. Her son who is in Grade
Seven played the keyboard.
"That young boy you see playing the keyboard is my love child. I
bore my son, who is my fourth child, when I was working as a
prostitute. He loves singing for the Lord."
But the most touching moment was when her other teenage son also
joined her in song. He jumped over to his mother, embraced her
affectionately and together bellowed their vocal tunes.
When God changes a person's life it shows. Her face was radiant and
all one could see was a lovely person, wanting to reach out to all. You
could tell she was now at peace with herself.
"Regedzai ndapota regedzai mabasa iwawo. Munofa (Leave that trade
because you will die)," said Ms Rodrigues, who miraculously tested
negative to HIV.
Most commercial sex workers in Harare are living positively with Aids, she said.
Ms Rodrigues said there was an estimated 10 000 women working as prostitutes in and around Harare.
Most of them, she said, could have contracted HIV and Aids because of inconsistent condom use.
Today, the former brothel has transformed into a family hotel and
the management insists on adhering to the legal age of majority in
which those under age are not allowed to enter.

Original_Search_Title: Bio-Fuel National Project Launched
Headline: Mozambique: Bio-Fuel National Project Launched
Body_Text:
Maputo — Mozambican Energy Minister Salvador Namburete reiterated in Maputo on Thursday that the government is determined to continue seeking ways to produce bio-fuel as a means to circunvent the high cost of fossil fuels.
Namburete was speaking during a ceremony to launch this national project to produce fuel from vegetables sources, which are a renewable resource.
This is to be developed in partnership between the government and the national and foreign private sector.
The idea is to produce fuel from two plants: "jafrota", known among most Mozambicans as "galamaluco", and cassava, from which one can extract bio-diesel and bio-ethanol, according to Bernardo Ferraz, former Environment Minister, who was appointed director of the project.
Namburete said that before the launching stage, the government carried out studies of sustainability of the Mozambique Bio-Fuel Industry (MBFI).
"This is an undertaking that will enter the usual investment procedures, and will enjoy fiscal advantages", he said.
To ilustrate the interest in developing this kind of fuels, Namburete said that even before there were any guarantees of developing it, "the government issued the necessary 'confort letters'".
Such "confort letters" mean to guarantee the financing banks that the Mozambican government is truely interested in this initiative.
The first experiments of the project, that is to begin this year and is to be implanted in seven provinces in a first stage, will start in the northern Nampula, and the central zambezia provinces.
"There is an initiative in Nampula, where the government has been working with the interested parties to issue licenses, but it is not yet known when production will begin", said Namburete.
Ferraz said that this project, budgetted at several million US dollars, comes as a response to the high costs of fossil fuels, that have been jeopardising the national development plans. "Mozambique is not yet producing oil, and not always it has the money to import it", he said.
He explained that bio-fuel, besides being a renewable source, it is environment friendly.

Original_Search_Title: Country Reinstated On US Exim Bank Cover
Headline: Ghana Reinstated On US Exim Bank Cover
Body_Text:
AS A Result of the general improvement in the economy, Ghana has
been reinstated on the US Exim Bank cover for both public and private
sector companies, after being blacklisted for six years.
This indicates that American companies that may invest in Ghana
could now access short, medium and long-term financing from the Exim
bank.
"To give a new lease of life to all businesses which, for whatever
problems, have reneged on their tax obligations, government has decided
to waive the penalty incurred from failure to pay the tax when it was
due," President John Agyekum Kufuor declared at the opening of the 10th
Ghana International Trade Fair in Accra on Wednesday.
He said the re-instatement was as a result of the macro-economic stability and the general improvement in the economy.
He said government had continued to embark on various programmes and
initiatives to open up the country and make it an attractive
destination for business, as well as stabilize the economy and prepare
it for growth and accelerated development.
This involved massive infrastructural development in terms of roads,
ports and harbours, telecommunications and improved social services
delivery, as well as the introduction of various Presidential
initiatives in Cassava, Oil palm, salt, sorghum, maize and cotton
production, as a means of diversifying the economy and adding value to
exports.
Besides, investors are also being encouraged to access the Export
Processing Zones to facilitate the development of export-oriented
businesses.
President Kufuor also cited the Trade Sector Support Programme,
which was launched last year as one special programme that aimed to
increase the country's competitiveness in international and domestic
markets and improving the legal and regulatory environment for both
business and consumers.
As part of the programme, trade promotion and frontline agencies,
such as Ghana Civil Aviation Authority, Customs, Excise and Preventive
Service, among others, are being strengthened and resourced to play
their facilitating roles more effectively.
President Kufuor said the financing needs of businesses were being
addressed through the establishment of various credit lines and
facilities such as EDIF and the Venture Fund to micro and small-scale
industries.
To further deepen the contributions of Ghanaian businesses, the
President said government had decided to waive the penalty they
incurred from the failure to pay the tax on time to give them a new
lease of life.
He said this was being done with the hope that prompt and voluntary tax compliance by the businesses would become the new order.
"This is also to allow companies to grow their businesses and take
advantage of new opportunities, which government is making available to
them, especially through the banks."
President Kufuor lauded the decision of the Trade Fair Company
Limited to organize the International Trade Fair annually, but asked
them to ensure that the fairs did not become mere rituals.
"The critical business issues must always be in evidence and must be
handled in a manner that would make participants feel that the fair is
good value for money," he said.
He said the Fair Company ought to know that the global exhibition
industry had become highly complex and competitive and that only
competence, dedication and aggressive marketing would guarantee success.
President Kufuor therefore urged that management systems responsive
to international practices must be adopted for the Ghana International
Trade Fair Center.
On his part, the minister of trade and industry, Mr. Alan
Kyeremanten, said government was in the process of establishing an
e-commerce website to enable Ghanaian products be displayed in the
gallery.
He urged exhibitors and visitors to form linkages to fully benefit from the fair.
The Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of Ghana Trade Fair Company, Miss
Esther Ofori, said the Board and Management of the Company was pursuing
an agenda to transform the Trade Fair Center from the present trade
exhibitions focus to a modern multi-purpose trade center complex.
This would comprise a hotel complex, a convention hall, a cinema hall and exhibition halls, among others.
She said they were currently looking for a strategic partner to help
transform the Trade Fair Center, and therefore called for the
recapitalisation of the Company to be able to meet the transformation
process.

Original_Search_Title: President Kabbah's Food Security Dream Coming to Fruition
Headline: Sierra Leone: President Kabbah's Food Security Dream Coming to Fruition
Body_Text:
Abu Bakarr Suliaiman Tarawally — For eleven years Sierra Leone was under the grip of a cataclysm and after it the country grappled with numerous adversities chief among which was food shortage.
The food sufficiency program ,which was a dream of His Excellency president Ahmad Tejan Kabbah is slowly coming to fruition. There are indication that light is appearing at the end of the tunnel. Dr. Sama Mondeh has the minister of Agriculture and Food Security seems to be to fulfilling the words of His Excellency the President Alhaji Ahmad Tejan Kabbah.
Almost all the districts in the country have now been made to be able to make use of tractors. This has enabled farmers to embark on commercial farming.
And not surprisingly, the results have been positive. Within a shortage period Sierra Leone has hit a target of 69% food sufficiency. It has surged hopes in many in the country. It is an indication that the days when Sierra Leoneans queue for rice, the staple food of the country, are over. Observers have described president Ahmad Tejan Kabbah as a " lucky shooter". The president has been accredited for not only initiating the food sufficiency program but also to have , in previous years, attain peace for the war-torn coastal West African country.
The National Farmers Association , which over the years has been grappling with the problem of getting resources to improve the lot of farmers, has now seen the chance of fulfilling their dream through the food security program and it has been very cooperative with the Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security. The North and Northeast of the country have also benefited from the program. They include Magburaka, Makeni and Kono.
A Farmers consultative meeting funded by donors to discuss problems still inhibiting food production and the way forward has been held and many facts have been elicited.
While in Magburaka, Makeni, Koidu, Tonkolili, Bombali and Kono, Dr. S.S. Mondeh disclosed the aid-package, policies and strategies his Ministry has put in place to attain food sufficiency by 2007.
The Minister told the farmers about the benefits of mechanized farming. And there was one big message that threw them into frenzied jubilation: Dr. Mondeh announced that 135 tractors had been donated to farmers across the Country. Government is hoping to achieve more by 2006.
There were also other things farmers in the north learnt from Dr. Mondeh's visit. They leant that agricultural activities were also carried out through UNDP sponsorship, Farmers Field Schools (FFS) and the Agricultural Business Units (ABU) that have been teaching farmers to solve farming problems and get working solutions. Developing agriculture into a prosperous business was among the key issues the minister outlined. The policies, he went on, included the non- payment of fees by farmers for the ploughing services of the tractors, admonition for the increase of farm size, putting women, youth and paramount chiefs as the flag bearers of the food security program, diversification of production of cash crops such as Coffee, Cacao, Ginger and Cashew nuts and the use of the Boli land, the mangrove and the inland valley swamps for the production of food crops such as rice, maize, groundnut, banana, plantains using good quality seeds or planting materials .
Emphasizing the importance of Government policies, he said they were tied down to the rising demand of crops culminating into maximum profit to the farmers, no matter the type of farming activities undertaken. Dr. Sama S. Mondeh substantiated his submissions by citing the high prices of cash crops in the world market like ginger, which is going for 2000 million U.S. dollars per ton, while cassava has become a zero waste crop in the world has already found inroads into the international market particularly in Nigeria and Asian countries.
At the same time the Agriculture Minister emphasized the adoption of diversified feeding habits that would reduce the dependence on rice- eating thereby saving the 50 million US dollars Sierra Leone spends to import rice from Japan, Thailand and other Asian countries and divert the amount that should have been spent on purchase of food on farmers thereby making them more prosperous than before.
Underscoring the importance of diversifying feeding habit, he noted its enhancement in the attainment of food sufficiency in other countries. He highlighted the accessing of enough proteins through balanced diets, improvement of farmers' health and avoidance of wastage of other crops through their popularization as options to rice.
The Agriculture Minister noted that Sierra Leoneans have so much become used to consuming rice that even when they eat other food items they feel like not having eaten anything at all.
Dr. Mondeh opined that enough of whatever food one eats would fill the stomach but this is not realized in the Sierra Leonean context because the mind has been influenced by the belief that only rice fills one's stomach.
He then assured the people that government is seriously committed in the food security programme as manifested in the rice aid packages.
And then the news that threw farmers into a frenzied jubilation came out. The Minister allocated 11 tractors to Bombali, 9 to Kono and 13 to Tonkolili Districts respectively. Two Huge Eveco Trucks were also allocated per district to ensure intensive cultivation, ease the problem of transportation of inputs and produce and increase access to markets and enhance marginal profit of the farmers. Dr. Mondeh noted, "The Green Revolution (of the APC) and Accelerated Food Production programs (of the NPRC) championed by past government because of lack of government's commitment led to their demise." He reiterated the SLPP Government's commitment to Food Security and stressed that the desired co-operation from people of Sierra Leone would no doubt make the Food Security drive succeed. The Agriculture Minister told the people that current statistics of food production showed that at the take -off in 2001 the level of food production was 25% but it has jumped to 69% in 2005, adding th at it was an encouraging indicator for success.
Practicalizing the concept of collectivism in the implementation of the food security was the Attorney General and Minister of Justice Frederick M. Carew who accompanied the Agriculture Minister and traveled with him through Mabang Kholifa Chiefdom in Tonkolili District the home of the Attorney General .
The Attorney General pointed out that observance of the law is crucial to the attainment of the food security. He said this would be enhanced through the avoidance of smuggling produce, conflicts and confrontations in land disputes. He urged farmers to promote peace and harmony. He told farmers that they have a right to make byelaws, which takes care of the communal farming problems inhibiting the food sufficiency sustenance of the food sufficiency drive.
In his home place at Mabang, Kholifa Mabang chiefdom in the Tonkolili District, Mr. F.M. Carew led the Minister on a conducted tour of his family Rice store containing parked bags of newly harvested rice ending in entertaining the Agriculture and entourage with Bondo cultural music and dancing displayed by the new initiates.
The FAO Resident Representative, Mohamed B. Farrah entreated highly place persons in the political, administrative and communal ladder to embrace agriculture buttressing that their heavy financial resources could take Agriculture forward. Their involvement, he noted would enhance wider participation and increased production leading to farmers prosperity. He noted the momentous range and diversity food production has taken unlike 20 years ago when food production was solely on rice. He noted the call of the Millennium Development Goals to all nations of the world-- to attain food security. He commended the unprecedented pragmatism of H.E. President Ahmad Tejan Kabbah has applied to challenge the issue by putting 2007 as the timeframe for Sierra Leone making it the first country to make that decision followed by Brazil.
The senior economic adviser in the UNDP office in Sierra Leone, Greyham Chipande lauded the creation of the Poverty Reduction Strategy Paper that has food security as its, 3rd main pillar thus showing the importance of food. He defined it as a basic human right and the springboard of poverty and hunger alleviation followed by prosperity and development. He intimated that UNDP's current role is to see the achievements of the goals of the PRSP and is supporting the operations of the Farmers Field School (FFS) to increase production and the Agriculture Business Units (ABU) to make Agriculture a business enterprise. He stressed the need for disbursement of micro-credit loans to farmers through community banks and other financial institutions so as to enhance fast tracking agriculture programmes in rural communities.
During the deliberations it came out clearly that Bombali District stands 1st in diversified production in the whole country, and has the greatest areas of Boli land totaling 163,000 acres. Kono paramount chiefs top in practical participation in food security, Kailahun District council is the leading council where the council owns 20 acres farm, a ward farm and the collective District Council farm while Pujehun rates as the first for cordial working relationship between the District Councils. It is in Pujehun that community residents came together used appropriate technology and invented a landing craft for the transportation of farming machinery, implements, seeds and planting materials from the main land of Gbondapi to the famous Gbondapi Rice production grasslands.
Masimor Village of Kholifa Mabang Chiefdom in Tonkolili District northeast of the country was revealed as the Headquarter for mechanized rice cultivation in the Northern province and appeals were made for the resuscitation of the rice production centre.
At the end of the deliberations also, some of the recommendations included availability of fertilizers to farmers, timely supply of seed rice and other inputs, avail guns to the communities to protect crops from wild animals upgrade livestock by restocking them and upkeep fish ponds which are all considered as key prerequisites to the achievement of food sufficiency.
Chairman of Local Councils, Representatives of the National Association of Farmers Sierra Leone, (NAFSL) NGOs, youths, civil society groups, Paramount Chiefs, Town Councils and elites of the social sector were observed to be actively participating in farming activities. One example cited was Mark Thomoson, who cultivated 3000 acres of Boli land rice in Malal Marah chiefdom and a medical doctor Moses Kargbo, who is now master farmer in Gbonkolenken chiefdom in the Tonkolili District and Isatu Ngebuva of the Kono Women Multi-Purpose Co-operative.

Original_Search_Title: Zaire: European Union Invests USD 48,000 in Agriculture
Headline: Angola: Zaire: European Union Invests USD 48,000 in Agriculture
Body_Text:
Soyo — The European Union (EU) invested in 2005, in Soyo district,
northern Zaire province, USD 48,000 in the implementation of an
agricultural project in Mpinda village.
According to the provincial director for Agriculture, Francisco
Gomes, the project that is part of the Angolan Government's programme
for rural development and extension, constitutes in the multiplying and
planting of cassava and sweet potatoes seeds.
To him the absence of rains in the province's coastal region for the
past two months has been hindering the implementation of the project.
Due to this, Francisco Gomes appealed for the strengthening of the
agricultural mechanisation area with more tractors and ploughs in
communes of the interior, for the sustainability of the programme.
Besides this, the provincial department wants the rehabilitation of
access routes, in order to ease the flow of products to central markets.

Original_Search_Title: Arewa, Malu And the Power Shift
Headline: Nigeria: Arewa, Malu And the Power Shift
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nigerians feel challenged each time we read about the Northern leaders gathering together discussing about power shift to the North, instead of getting together to develop strategies of development in the North. Is the Nigerian Press guilty of this report or is that what the Northern meetings are all about? - POWER.
Ironically, the communique from the meetings has always been about power, not the developmental programmes such as education, technology, agriculture, mineral resources, etc., for the North. Or is the Northern forum hiding such development programmes away from other Nigerians? Unfortunately, the theme from the current meeting was General Malu's regret for not changing Nigerian Government, as usual, through the use of force. Haba General Malu!
The former Chief of Army Staff, General Victor Malu, said that he regretted having not overthrown President Olusegun Obasanjo's government while he was in service. Now we knew why he was retired on his breakfast table. Merry, go, round military officers, to get his share of the national cake.
Obasanjo knew that Malu would do something like that if left to stay longer than he did. Part of what I wrote then was:
"What the General said about coup has just proved the point that his retirement was a breach of security. We would not conclude that he is instigating the serving soldiers to forcefully take over the government.... But he should, realize that his 'over ambitious chaps" could take his words and run away with them. Evidence that this man was angry about his early retirement. Nonetheless, as a military man, he took it as a challenge of his life and vowed not to carry brief cases to the corridors of any government agency looking for contract. May be he might be one of the few ones among the retired generals to pursue a productive private life."
Can Nigeria still trust this man as a civilian, based on history, since civilians always instigate the military to take over government? Who knows what he is planning for right now, especially since he regrets not to have overthrown Obasanjo, as part of what he could have done, but because of his loyalty to Obasanjo and love of democracy. Liar! In Malu's words:
"Maybe because I was loyal to Obasanjo, I would have done what I was supposed to do; but I believed in democracy. So, I did not organise to overthrow him. Because I am a principled trained officer, I insisted on the principles being applied. I think it was a blessing that I was kicked out, otherwise, I might have changed my mind from being a democrat to something else..."
Any right thinking individual would be able to dissect what Malu is up to.
Why was this man so bitter about Obasanjo's decision to retire him, 'prematurely' to the point of regretting not taking Nigeria back to the ancient civilization, if we may ask? From his speech at the gathering of the Northern leaders, he alleged that Obasanjo did not give him credit for his 34 years in the military with the American presence in the Nigerian military; the credit to enrich himself by taking over the legitimate Government?; the economic disparity in the North; power shift; and the situation in Plateau State with Governor Dariye. Who is to blame for all these?
We need to understand that, Education is an ongoing process for individuals; you continue to improve upon yourself through education until you die. American presence in the Nigerian Army could have been to add on to what was on the ground. No one should claim perfection. Might be, the American presence had prevented Malu from planning coup against Obasanjo.
On the economic disparity in the North, it is ironic that people fail to understand that economic growth of any community is the end result of the leadership of that community. This is what most Nigerians are saying, that when the North has that POWER, what they did with it, except for the leaders to line up their pockets. We have not recorded many selfless leaders from the North who were very much concerned about their people, after Sardauna, or Tafawa Balewa. That is part of what the Northern Youths are talking about that Northern leaders should emulate the legacy of Sardauna.
Still on power shift, most Nigerians agree with Malu that no one cares where the President comes from, in as much as he performs. Repeatedly, Nigerians have laid claim to the fact that, if for the three decades the Northern leaders ruled Nigeria, they have performed very well; there won't be any reason to cajole for power shift here or there.
Then on the situation with Governor Dariye, most Nigerians believe that the State of Emergency saved Dariye's face. Even though, some people, including this writer opposed Obasanjo's decision at that time. Compare Plateau situation to the heavy disgrace DSP, the beautiful bride of the century, brought to himself, his people, his State and Nigeria.
Since Chief Awoniyi, the Aro of Mopa, and the leader of ACF, came to the defence of General Malu's statement, Chief Awoniyi might just retire honorably from politics since he could not read in-between lines the statements credited to Malu. The admittance by Chief Awoniyi that General Malu said
"jokingly that he considered it a blessing that he was kicked out of service or else he might have changed from being a democrat to something else and the audience burst into laughter", is sufficient to hold ACF liable for conspiring to terminate the legitimate government of Nigeria, in any means necessary. So he meant that.
In a statement issued on Thursday night, Malu said he did not say he regretted not to have overthrown President Olusegun Obasanjo. Part of what he said was that I specifically mentioned issues of the US and their effort to update their intelligence on the Armed Forces of Nigeria, especially the Army in the guise training the Army for peacekeeping duties. I made a statement to the effect that on reflection now, it was good that I was kicked out of service, else I might have changed from being a democrat to something else."
What else do we need to hear from the disgruntled Malu?
Someone argued that, while calculating the number of years the Northerners have ruled Nigeria, we should eliminate the period that the Military ruled the country, in that the Military is a National Institution. Where were the top military officers from? Who led the military then? Who master-minded the coups? IBB and his boys! The military officers were put at the helms of Nigerian affairs for reform purposes and they outright endorsed corruption. Ribadu has said this publicly that IBB, Abacha, and Abdulsalami from the North, institutionalized corruption in Nigeria. We might then argue that the military, under the northern leaders illegitimately took power away from the elected government. Which goes with the same argument that no northerner should rule the country for its treasonable offences, the way the South East or South South are being treated like second class citizens. Nigerians are kind enough to say that, we are all right with the Northern civilian politicians, but n o to the military turned politicians because they are not satisfied with their loot. Oliver Twist. This means again that the National Institution, led by the Military officers from the North, stole power from the legitimate government, which is treasonable offence. Now Malu is regretting for not given the chance to do same thing. That is not fancy.
In other words, the military were brought into politics by the southeast in particular and generally by the south, according to a lazy argument put forth by an individual. Okay. We agreed that the Military were brought into politics by the South East in particular and generally the South. They came to sanitize the system. General Ogundipe was supposed to take the lead while in London; he was not greedy for power, he turned it down. Gowon was brought in and after nine years, appealed to Nigerians, when asked when he would hand over to an elected President, to allow his men to take their breakfast.
Murtala came and kicked out Gowon. Dimka's short-lived coup brought in Balogun of Owu, General Obasanjo, who handed over to a civilian legitimate President. Obasanjo's alleged third term project with his greed also is making him to want to stay pass 2007 or pass 2011.
What happened next, the greedy Northern leaders, lamentation for leadership, took over from Ironsi and the rest is history. Does that mean that there was no higher ranking officer from any part of the country at that time that could lead the Military, except from the North? The Military leaders then sowed the seed of discord in the Military, that is why we continue to see the exchange of baton between Babangida, and Abacha until God says, come home my son to Abacha, and rest to eternity.
The point is that there is nothing on the ground to suggest that these former military leaders did something good for the north, here you go. That is exactly what we are talking about. Whom do you want to blame for the failures of the military past leaders, Salaudeen Chukwuemeka Ajayi?
In one of my pieces titled, "Culture Of Corruption In Power: Nigeria In Alamieyeseigha World", published Friday, December 9, 2005, I tried to be 'civilized' in that piece, while failing to hit the nail on the head. Coward! What I should have said was to state that The Nigerian Political Leaders Seed of Discord being sown will be harvested by their offspring in the life to come and could definitely cause some real ethnic cleansing in the country we call Nigeria, within each State, God forbid, in the country as a whole. This time round it would not be North versus South it would be disruptive offspring among all ethnic groups in the country if the end of this discord is not changed. Whenever those people wronged have the opportunity, Kosovo or Rwanda ethnic cleansing is going to be a child's play.
We have heard complaints from various segment all across the country how the privileged leaders are marginalizing their citizens because they do not belong to their ethnic group. The political appointments, the juicy ones as demanded and gotten by Baale Molete, Pa Chief Adedibu, the posting or promotion of civil servants getting favoritisms from the President, ministers, head of Service, legislators, governors, and commissioners, because they are blood relations or from the same ethnic or religious group, would eventually catch up with the country either now or in the nearest future. Babangida could be blamed for some of these as well. Who knows the judgment day might be closer than we expect in Nigeria.
Most Nigerian nwspapers reported that top on the agenda of the Arewa Consultative Forum, where Malu opened his orifice widely again, was the issue of power shift. Why was Malu allowed to attend and make such dangerous statements of regretting not to kick the legitimate government out of Aso Rock? Not repeating ourselves again, when the North had the power what happened with it? Suppressed the people, killed education. It was not killed by Soyinka as alleged by someone; it was the trio of the Education Minister, Permanent Secretary and the Head of Service as at the time of Babangida that killed education in Nigeria.
Such rush for power makes most Nigerians wonder what type of leadership we have in the North. It is very ironic, to say the least, that from all indications the northern leaders are not listening to the Youth and their unhappiness about the concerns of the Northern leaders. The Youths have been 'educating' the leaders to start reasoning beyond the nose. After the destruction of Japan in the Second World War, Japanese leaders suppressed their angers and improved upon themselves within the talents and skills God gave them, despite the natural disaster of earthquake, which Nigeria does not have. They rebuilt Japan and today the country is being 'worshipped' in the area of technology. The country has sent some huge American motor companies parking. Majority of Americans today want Japanese cars, such as Toyota or Honda. Is it possible for the Northern leaders to sit back and think what they could do in the North to entice the entire Africa so to speak, instead of regretti ng to have allowed the power to get to the South or regretting not to have overthrown legitimate Nigerian Government?
I am going to say this again; the Northern leaders should consult with the leadership of Zumunta Association USA for their blue print on Education for the North. How to revive education in the North is what they are working on a daily basis. I am privileged to be part of the Committee working on it and here are some areas of what they would like the Northern States Governments to be part of.
God bless Nigeria!
Ajayi holds a doctorate
Zumunta is working on Funding through donations, Provision of infrastructure, books, computers, instructional materials; Scholarship; Facilitate staff exchange/linkage programs; Establishment of ICT (Information Communication Technology Programs); Manpower development and training; Get Government involved in some of its policy makings; Vocational training and skills; Islamiyyah schools; Girls education; Support services such as library materials; and Special education. While strategizing to achieve all of the above through Mobilization and sensitization campaign; Information and Communication Technology training and provision of PCs to schools; Teacher Training/in-service training; Scholarship awards to well deserving indigenes of the 19 Northern states; Collaboration with privately-owned vocational training centers (collaboration with the state Governments and NGOs would help in this category). Mind you Zumunta is currently awarding full scholarships to needed students from the 19 Northern States; some of the graduates are now Medical Doctors in Nigeria. We will see which State would first take this challenge. We have some of the Zumunta members who are Educationists that are willing to lend their expertise in this area. The North has an arable land whereby both cash and consumable crops could be grown. Bring back dignity in labor. With the countless alumajiris running about doing nothing, they could be useful on the farms and task their brains for a better North.
We should keep on drumming this to everyone's head that there are other things that need to be done to make the North a Darling of Nigeria, especially in the areas of agriculture, or mineral resources. What was Malu thinking in the name of Allah, for regretting not to dislodge the economic reforms of this administration?
I believe some Northern Governors could and should advise the forum what need to be done. I am not talking about the introduction of Sharia for the belligerents, and not for the elites, but those that are actually doing what their people want them to do.
There are many things that could be done to make north an envy of Nigeria, West Africa, Africa, and the World. The North could strive in the areas of tubers, oil, fruits, cereals, grains Vegetables and Oil-seeds. From the North East alone Borno, Yobe, Adamawa, Taraba, these crops would do well based on the climate and other geographical factors: Aerial Yam, Guinea yam, Bush yam, Sweet potato, Cassava, Hausa potato, Irish potato, Fonio, Finger-millet Barley, African Rice, Asian rice, Bulrush millet, Sorghum, Common wheat, Maize, Groundnut, Peanut, Pigeon pea, Cowpea, Okra, Garlic, False Sesame, Egusi, Water melon, Sweet melon, Cucumber, Squash, Pumpkin, Cress, Tomato, Cherry tomato, African eggplant, Cashew, Lime, Orange, Tiger-nut, Mango, Plantain, Banana, Pomegranate, Date palm, Sugar-cane, Coriander, Black cumin, Ginger, Tobacco, Castor-oil plant, and Kenaf.
All States could embark on massive commercial farming to feed their people and export to other states, other African countries and some parts of the world. If it is cattle or beef supply, Canada, USA, Japan are all doing well by exporting their beef to countries around the world. Cheese production could be embarked upon. There is nothing like competition in Agriculture, the market is large and sparse, every single human being need to eat. Food is number one American problem that is too much to eat, if you have the means to buy them.
The only interest the North leaders should embark upon today is how to rejuvenate the North. Make life worth living for the Northerners.
I remember my NYSC days in the Old Borno State now Borno, Yobe, and Adamawa, when we visited Lake Chad. That time, in the late 70s, we walked for almost three miles away from where Lake Chad boundary used to be. The geochemistry of natural waters from rain, soil, the unsaturated zone, shallow aquifers, lakes and the stratified aquifer system of the Chad Basin sediments, for any reasearch to interpret modern hydrological processes and to reconstruct the palaeohydrology of NE Nigeria, a type region of the southern Sahel, is gone.
What the leaders ought to do was to find a way of resuscitating Lake Chad. Never! All their concerns were how to enrich themselves, enriching themselves through the pains and sufferings of the poor Northerners. The Lake Chad Basin Authority I believe just collect their pay check watching the Lake as it drains to the last drop of water. The people are suffering painfully for lack of future. The State of Nevada, is surviving its dessert location in the United States. Egypt's main source of revenue is from tourism, desert location as well.
Nigerians expect the Northern leaders to use their skills to bring respect back to the North, instead of applauding Malu's statements of regrets for not terminating legitimate government while in the Army. The Northern leaders could turn their attentions to the dictates of the acronym of their respective States in addition to commercial farming, instead of wasting time chasing power which they have abused before.
Adamawa could be turned into a great tourist attraction being the "Land of Beauty"; Bauchi could engage in Hospitality Business as the "Home of Peace and Hospitality"; Benue could supply Nigeria and even Africa if it concentrates on agriculture, as the "Food Basket of the Nation"; The peaceful co-existence between Borno and Cameroon, with the unfortunate neglect of Lake Chad questions the accolade of Borno as "Home of Peace"; As the "Jewel of the Savannah", Gombe State by now ought to have been feeding the whole country; thanks to Jigawa Governor, Turaki for advancing its technology in High Tech and the collection of rainwater for storage during rainy season to be used during dry season. That is simple common sense Governor Turaki is using making the Sate the "New World". Nigerians could be grateful to the "Liberal State" of Kaduna, for keeping most prominent Northern Leaders intact; Unfortunately, Kano has not proved its presence as the "Center of Commerce", reminding us of the old Kano Pyramids of Groundnut etc.; Bauchi could share its "Hospitality" with Katsina, "State of Hospitality"; If Sharia would allow us, everyone could go to Kebbi and start commercial farming as the "Land of Equity"; A lot of things could be done at the Niger and Benue "Confluence State" of Kogi; The "State of Harmony" is steadily harmonizing its resources in Kwara State; The place where the entire North could focus is Nasarawa, "Home of the Solid Mineral"; Unfortunately, Niger as "The Power State" has laid the foundation for the endemic disease in Nigeria today - Corruption, they can still use that power to do something that could benefit many people from Niger State; As soon as the 'cloud' is cleared, Plateau could be reversed to the "Home of Peace and Tourism"; While we continue to ask God to give them the wisdom that one of the leaders from Sokoto has for the people of Nigeria not only for the North, in the spirit of the "Seat of Caliphate"; Taraba's God's preservation should be a pride for the country, as "the Nature's Gift of God"; and Yobe, still in its 'infancy' for the leaders to continue praying that "The Young Shall Grow"; and Zamfara, the "Home of Sharia" should continue deceiving its people while he is not covered by Sharia law. Zamfara that never known armed robbers before is now leading in the armed robbers in the North, because its leaders are using Sharia to prevent the cities from progressing, while its leaders are enjoying high level parties, dishing our Jeeps to women to lunch Gov. Sannni's presidential bid for 2007, in the midst of amputees. One of other shortcomings of some Northern leaders has been in the areas of telling individuals how to worship their God, and by imposing laws on them. That is not how to encourage people to love themselves and God.
What could help Nigeria today are fervent prayers by everyone, from Buddhists, Traditional Believers, Christians, to Moslems, not to allow the Seeds of Discord sowed by Nigerian leaders germinate sooner than expected. I am not a Prophet of doom, however, the seed of discord that Nigerian leaders are sowing could devour the country before we know it. God should really take over Nigeria so that the seeds of discord already sowed would not start germinating in 2007.

Original_Search_Title: Iganga Farmers Mine Gold in Their Backyards
Headline: Uganda: Iganga Farmers Mine Gold in Their Backyards
Body_Text:
Kampala — WHEN 51-year-old Sauda Kiviri's husband deserted her, it seemed all
hope was lost. Now Kiviri is all smiles as she recounts how her life
has changed.
"Three years ago, community development workers from Africa 2000
Network-Uganda (A2N-Uganda) trained us in agriculture," says Kiviri.
She learnt tree planting, growing food crops and making manure from
grass, cow dung and leaves. In front of her new two-bedroom brick
house, is a garden of trees - moringa, grevaria and musizi. There are
orange trees behind the house. A few metres from the fruit garden is a
two-acre pineapple garden.
"I have 5,000 pineapple plants. A small pineapple costs sh300 and a
big one sh800. The pineapples are bought by traders from Mbale, who
export them to Kenya," said Kiviri.
She also has a friesian cow. "A2N-Uganda gave me planting materials
and this cow in 2002. When it produced, I passed on the calf to another
farmer. Now the cow is mine," Kiviri says. "I get about 12 litres of
milk daily. I keep two litres for home consumption and sell the rest at
sh400 a litre."
She uses the proceeds from her crops and milk to cater for her six children, one of whom studies at a teacher training college.
As in other parts of the country, rainfall has always been highly
variable in Iganga. It may fail or fall at the wrong time. This
necessitates more effective systems to capture rainfall and reduce
runoff through better vegetative cover.
The project known as Strengthening Farmers' Associations for Poverty
has benefited 25,000 households in Iganga and Mayuge districts. It is
funded by the european Union and the Catholic Organisation for Rural
Development.
About two miles from Kasale village, Ben Muwereza, 65, has reclaimed
about six acres of barren land, which he uses to produce pineapples and
potatoes. Muwereza now has enough food for his family and he sells the
surplus.
"I was a driver at Kakira Sugar Works for 15 years, but couldn't
even buy a goat. When I started practising sustainable agriculture, I
bought a cow within two years," says Muwereza.
He says A2N-Uganda organises farm visits to model farmers, which has
motivated many peasants to adopt better farming practices. Muwereza
also has a Friesian cow, but it has not yet produced.
"A2N-Uganda supports farmers to grow high value crops like upland
rice, vanilla and passion fruits and to rear animals like friesian
cows. Households choose animals depending on their capacity to look
after them," says Susan Sewagudde, community development worker with
A2N-Uganda.
"The animals also cushion the poor against crop failure. In order to
make the project sustainable, it is operated on revolving scheme so
that more farmers benefit."
"A2N-Uganda's mission is to alleviate poverty by supporting small
farmer groups to undertake initiatives geared towards livelihood
improvement," says Sewagudde.
Muwereza gets over sh1m annually from his pineapple, cassava and
potato gardens. He has built a new brick house and bought another cow.

Original_Search_Title: Crop Yields Drop During El Nino Years
Headline: African Crop Yields Drop During El Nino Years
Body_Text:
The periodic warming of the Pacific Ocean known as El Nino can
reduce African crops yields, in some years endangering food supplies
for about 20 million people, say researchers.
They warn that if -- as widely predicted -- climate change increases
the number of years with El Nino-like conditions, southern Africa in
particular could face declines in food production.
But they say their findings should allow nations to prepare for food
shortages by advising farmers on how to select and store their crops.
The study, led by Hans Herren of the US-based Millennium Institute,
is published online this week by Proceedings of the National Academy of
Sciences.
El Nino occurs every 3-7 years when water off the Pacific coast of
South America becomes abnormally warm, triggering changes in wind
patterns with far- reaching effects.
The team looked at how El Nino and a similar phenomenon called the
North Atlantic Oscillation affect food production, by analysing 40
years of national crop and livestock records and 20 years of satellite
data that showed changes in vegetation.
They found strong links between El Nino and declining yields of
sorghum, millet, groundnuts, and especially maize. The trends were
strongest for southern Africa, where crop productivity could drop by
20-50 per cent in extreme El Nino years, they say.
The study found no effect on wheat production, possibly because of
the more frequent use of irrigation with this crop, says co-author Nils
Stenseth of the University of Oslo, Norway.
The researchers say their approach means predictions can be made
months in advance, allowing governments to advise farmers to plant more
resilient crops in anticipation of El Nino events.
This could mean switching from maize to sorghum in northern Africa, and to cassava in southern Africa, they suggest.
"The results are of direct value to planners, as they can see how
much the production of different crops in different regions of the
continent could change during an extreme El Nino year", Stenseth told
SciDev.Net.

Original_Search_Title: President Kabbah: Hits Food Security Target
Headline: Sierra Leone: President Kabbah: Hits Food Security Target
Body_Text:
For eleven years Sierra Leone was under the grip of a cataclysm and after it the country grappled with numerous adversities chief among which was food shortage.
The food sufficiency program which was a dream of His Excellency president Ahmad Tejan Kabbah is slowly coming to fruition. There is now hope as light has been seen at the end of the tunnel. Like a promised messiah, Dr. Sama Mondeh has appeared to fulfill the words of His Excellency the President Alhaji Ahmad Tejan Kabbah.
Almost all the districts in the country have now been made to be able to make use of tractors. This has enabled farmers to embark on commercial farming.
And not surprisingly, the results have been positive. Within a shortage period Sierra Leone has hit a target of 69% food sufficiency. It has surged hopes in many in the country. It is an indication that the days when Sierra Leoneans queue for rice, the staple food of the country, are over. Observers have described president Ahmad Tejan Kabbah as a " lucky shooter". The president has been accredited for not only initiating the food sufficiency program but also to have , in previous years, attain peace for the war-torn coastal West African country.
The National Farmers Association , which over the years has been grappling with the problem of getting resources to improve the lot of farmers, has now seen the chance of fulfilling their dream through the food security program and it has been very cooperative with the Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security. The North and Northeast of the country have also benefited from the program. They include Magburaka, Makeni and Kono.
A Farmers consultative meeting funded by donors to discuss problems still inhibiting food production and the way forward has been held and many facts have been elicited.
While in Magburaka, Makeni, Koidu, Tonkolili, Bombali and Kono, Dr. S.S. Mondeh disclosed the aid-package, policies and strategies his Ministry has put in place to attain food sufficiency by 2007.
The Minister told the farmers about the benefits of mechanized farming. And there was one big message that threw them into frenzied jubilation: Dr. Mondeh announced that 135 tractors had been donated to farmers across the Country. Government is hoping to achieve more by 2006.
There were also other things farmers in the north learnt from Dr. Mondeh's visit. They leant that agricultural activities were also carried out through UNDP sponsorship, Farmers Field Schools (FFS) and the Agricultural Business Units (ABU) that have been teaching farmers to solve farming problems and get working solutions. Developing agriculture into a prosperous business was among the key issues the minister outlined. The policies, he went on, included the non- payment of fees by farmers for the ploughing services of the tractors, admonition for the increase of farm size, putting women, youth and paramount chiefs as the flag bearers of the food security program, diversification of production of cash crops such as Coffee, Cacao, Ginger and Cashew nuts and the use of the Boli land, the mangrove and the inland valley swamps for the production of food crops such as rice, maize, groundnut, banana, plantains using good quality seeds or planting materials .
Emphasizing the importance of Government policies, he said they were tied down to the rising demand of crops culminating into maximum profit to the farmers, no matter the type of farming activities undertaken. Dr. Sama S. Mondeh substantiated his submissions by citing the high prices of cash crops in the world market like ginger, which is going for 2000 million U.S. dollars per ton, while cassava has become a zero waste crop in the world has already found inroads into the international market particularly in Nigeria and Asian countries.
At the same time the Agriculture Minister emphasized the adoption of diversified feeding habits that would reduce the dependence on rice- eating thereby saving the 50 million US dollars Sierra Leone spends to import rice from Japan, Thailand and other Asian countries and divert the amount that should have been spent on purchase of food on farmers thereby making them more prosperous than before.
Underscoring the importance of diversifying feeding habit, he noted its enhancement in the attainment of food sufficiency in other countries. He highlighted the accessing of enough proteins through balanced diets, improvement of farmers' health and avoidance of wastage of other crops through their popularization as options to rice.
The Agriculture Minister noted that Sierra Leoneans have so much become used to consuming rice that even when they eat other food items they feel like not having eaten anything at all.
Dr. Mondeh opined that enough of whatever food one eats would fill the stomach but this is not realized in the Sierra Leonean context because the mind has been influenced by the belief that only rice fills one's stomach.
He then assured the people that government is seriously committed in the food security programme as manifested in the rice aid packages.
And then the news that threw farmers into a frenzied jubilation came out. The Minister allocated 11 tractors to Bombali, 9 to Kono and 13 to Tonkolili Districts respectively. Two Huge Eveco Trucks were also allocated per district to ensure intensive cultivation, ease the problem of transportation of inputs and produce and increase access to markets and enhance marginal profit of the farmers.
