elimination of rural poverty,"the need for the transformation and
reforms of the traditional agricultural sector in order to reap
economic viability would be greatly supported by all"

Original_Search_Title: Who Will Take Toro And Rwenzori Regions?
Headline: Uganda: Who Will Take Toro And Rwenzori Regions?
Body_Text:
Kampala — IN our last part of the series on factors likely to influence
regional voting patterns, Joshua Kato reviews Toro and Rwenzori regions
and concludes that socio-cultural, economic and political factors will
greatly determine how voters will cast their votes.
Toro comprises three districts - Kabarole, Kamwenge and Kyenjojo,
inhabited by the Batooro, while Kasese and Bundibugyo districts, which
make up the Rwenzori region, are occupied by Bakonjo and Bamba
respectively.
In 2001, these two regions had a total of 700,367 registered voters.
Of these, 496,169 turned up to vote and President Yoweri Museveni
garnered 417,673 of the votes cast.
Toro region
From a Democratic Party (DP) stronghold in the 1980s, this region
shifted allegiance to the National Resistance Movement (NRM). The
support developed during the NRA/M war when Fort Portal was used as a
base during the struggle.
In the early 1980s, DP won three out of the four seats in Kabarole
with Rwabukuba Nyakatura, R. Bamuturaki and P. Kaboha as MPs. DP's
dominance fizzled out with Museveni's ascent to power in 1986.
In 1996, Toro voted overwhelming against DP president general Dr.
Paul Ssemogerere. And in 2001, there were 420,861 registered voters in
the Toro region. Of these, 322,876 turned up to vote. Museveni got
299,000 (about 96%) of the votes cast compared to Besigye's 19,754.
This indicated a slight drop of about 2% from the President's 1996
winning margin. Museveni still enjoys support in the region but like in
the last election he will register a slight drop.
The issues
Unlike Buganda Kingdom, Toro Kingdom has not made any demands on the
Government. Whereas this might mean that they are contented, it could
also be an indication that there are no influential personalities from
the region to make such demands.
The king's palace was renovated a few years ago with help from the
Libyan government, which is probably why the kingdom seems to be happy
with the current government. However, the road network is not so good.
Although the Government constructed the Mubende-Kyegegwa-Fort Portal
road, many others are in bad condition. Poor roads mean farmers are
having difficulty in transporting their produce to markets.
All the presidential candidates - Ssebaana Kizito, Museveni, Abed
Bwanika, Miria Obote and Kizza Besigye have promised to improve the
road network if elected.
Toro's main cash crop is tea. Coffee is the other major cash crop.
Locals also grow food crops like maize, millet, cassava and Irish
potatoes.
Toro region has great tourism potential. Part of Semliki Game
Reserve and Rwenzori National Park, both of which have hundreds of bird
species, are found in Toro. As a show of NRM's strength in the region,
15 sub-county chairpersons have been elected unopposed.
Key NRM personalities in Toro include Adolf Mwesige (State Minister
for Justice and Constitutional Affairs), Beatrice Kiraso (Kabarole
Woman MP), Dora Byamukama (Mwenge South), Major Araali Basaliza
(Fort-portal Municipality) Mwesige Basaliza (Burahya), Nulu Byamukama
(Kitagwenda) and Badru Katongole (Kyaka).
Most of the local councils in the region are led by NRM supporters.
This gives NRM an advantage over the other political parties. As is the
case in western Uganda, Forum for Democratic Change (FDC) has got a
strong presence in urban areas like Fort Portal, Kamwenge and Kyenjojo
but is absent in rural Toro, where most of the population claims to
support NRM.
The opposition's bigwigs in the region include FDC's Captain (rtd.)
Charles Byaruhanga (MP Kibaale, Kamwenge) who is currently facing NRM's
Frank Tumwebaze in the forthcoming parliamentary elections. Because key
personalities matter a lot in this region, NRM has got a bigger
advantage over other political groupings. This brightens Museveni's
chances of winning with about 85% of the votes cast.
Rwenzori region
It was a bastion of the UPC in 1980s, but like Toro and many other
regions in Uganda, it embraced the Movement after 1986. The Uganda
Patriotic Movement (which later became NRM) was surprised when Dr.
Crispus Kiyonga won one of the three parliamentary seats there in 1980.
In 1996, Museveni won this region with over 95% of the votes cast.
In 2001, the number of voters in Rwenzori region stood at 279,505. Of
these, 173,293 turned up to vote. Museveni took 118,673 of the votes
cast and Besigye followed with 36,654 votes.
It is worth noting that Besigye performed much better in Kasese,
where he got 33,086 votes, than in Bundibugyo were he got 3,568 votes.
As a result of Besigye's good performance in Kasese, Museveni's win
dropped to around 85% of the votes cast, as opposed to 95% in 1996.
Major concerns
In 1996, Allied Democratic Forces (ADF) rebels attacked and
momentarily occupied the main border town, Bwera, but were repulsed by
the Uganda People's Defence Forces (UPDF) after several fierce battles.
The UPDF was able to defeat the ADF largely because of the
population's anti-war and pro-NRM stand. The scars of war are still
evident in major towns like Kasese and Bundibugyo. People maimed by
land mines are a constant reminder of the war.
Several places here are yet to be demined. The issue of Obusinga
(Rwenzururu Kingdom) has also proved to be sensitive and divisive.
Museveni has played a central mediation role to resolve the issue,
but is yet to draw up an agreed consensus. All candidates who have
visited Kasese and Bundibugyo have promised to work for a consensus on
the Obusinga issue.
For so many years, Kasese was one of the major mining towns in the
country. Cobalt mining is still going on. Crude salt mining from Lake
Katwe is another dominant activity. Some sections of these mines have
been closed over the years, meaning that many people have lost their
jobs.
According to the population, if mining resumes at full-capacity, more people will get jobs, thus reducing poverty in the area.
The Bamba in Bundibugyo are mainly farmers. They grow coffee,
vanilla and cocoa as the major cash crops and maize, Irish potatoes and
beans as the major food crops. All the candidates who have visited
Bundibugyo have promised to modernise the level of agriculture.
Ssebaana promised to open up a food processing chain, while Museveni
promised to introduce mechanisation, lobby for markets and improve the
road network for farmers.
Key personalities
As far as influential personalities are concerned, again, the NRM
leads other political groups. In Kasese, the two most influential
politicians are Kiyonga, (National Political Commissar) and Loyce
Bwambale. Other NRMs include Kule Muranga (Busongora North) and Taban
Mbalibulha (Busongora South). The only notable opposition politician is
Christopher Kibazanga of the FDC.
Like in Toro, almost 90% of local councils are led by personalities
who believe in the Movement ideology. FDC is the most evident
opposition party. It stronger in Kasese, especially in the main towns
like Bwera and Kilembe, but weaker in the rural areas. Kasese being a
border-trading town, there is a lot of discontent over taxes, which is
why the FDC has got a strong presence.
According to some projections, Museveni will take Kasese, but with
only about 60% of the votes cast. In Bundibugyo, the leading
personality is Kamanda Bataringaya, who is also State Minister for
Mineral Development. When President Yoweri Museveni visited Bundibugyo,
Bataringaya promised him that he (Museveni) had 95% of the votes in
Bundibugyo. It was not far-fetched.
This marks the last in a 10-part series that has been running since December.
Next week we shall publish a national round-up of the reviews.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Promises to Stimulate Economy
Headline: Nigeria: Govt Promises to Stimulate Economy
Body_Text:
Lagos — GOVERNMENT has reiterated plans to revitalize all sectors of the
economy in order to improve the living standards of her citizens.
This was disclosed by Commissioner for Information and Culture, Mr.
Chris Okewulonu during a post executive council press briefing held at
the Conference Hall of the ministry at the state secretariat Owerri
recently.
Okewulonu said that the state government has taken steps to upgrade
the status of the Okigwe Regional Cattle market by entering into
partnership with private individuals, cooperative societies and some
financial institutions, adding that the commissioner for Commerce,
Industry and Tourism, has been mandated to take all necessary steps
towards uplifting the market standard and making it functional.
On standard shoe industry, the commissioner said that equipment and
Machinery for installation have arrived Apapa seaport in Lagos, while
the sum of N30 million to clear them at the seaport, has been approved.
Speaking on privatization, he stated that the programme is on course
and commended the Director-General of the Bureau for Public Enterprises
(BPE), Mrs. Irene Chigbue for the professional assistance to the state.
According to him state government has confirmed the appointment of
Mr. Richard Chioma as the executive secretary of the State Bureau for
Public Enterprises (SBPE) and procured vehicles for the agency, adding
that over N42 million has been disbursed by the government to
revitalize agricultural sector.
According to him, emphasis would be place on the production of rice,
cassava and palm, oil meanwhile, three rice designers have been made
available for use by the ten local government areas in the state
producing rice. The state ministry of Agriculture and Natural resources
has been mandated to hand over Cassava resource processing mill at
Umuagwo to Michael Okpara College of Agriculture and Technology
(MOCATECH) for effective management.

Original_Search_Title: Dr Besigye Woos Janet Museveni
Headline: Uganda: Dr Besigye Woos Janet Museveni
Body_Text:
Ntungamo — Like a long lost son who has finally come home, FDC presidential
candiddate Kizza Besigye was Friday treated to a rapturous welcome in
this district. During a rally, Dr Besigye urged First Lady Janet Kataha
Museveni to declare that she is ready to work with the Forum for
Democratic Change if she is really bent on developing Ruhaama County.
His supporters lined up along the road to Kyamate playground
chanting "Kyahweire" (it is over) as he was driven in his open roofed
Pajero vehicle. Besigye who had a broad smile across his face, waved
the party's two fingure salute.
Speaking in his native Ruhororo, Besigye asked the people of Ruhaama
County to resist intimidation and voter buying against incumbent MP
Augustine Ruzindana.
Addressing so far the longest rally since he started his campaign,
Besigye said: "If Janet is voted, how will she bring development yet
she will be opposed to the policies of the sitting government (FDC).
Development policies are determined by the government in power. Janet
should declare that she will work in the FDC government."
He added: Ruzindana can oppose bad policies in Parliament, but can
Janet stand up and oppose her husband?" Besigye's remarks excited the
already charged crowd .
Ms Museveni who is vying for the Ruhaama seat, says she was sent by
God "to rescue the people of Ruhaama County". Besigye assured the
Rwandan community living in Ntungamo that he had no plan of
repatriating them. "They are going around telling lies that I will send
the Rwandese back.
At the same time they say that President Paul Kagame's boys are on
standby to fight for me. I have failed to understand this!," he said.
A significant population of Ntungamo comprises people of Rwandan
origin because the district borders Rwanda. "I want to assure you that
our government is not planning to chase anyone. For us in FDC we don't
consider one's tribe or nationality. It is one Uganda, one people,"
Besigye roared as the crowd joined in.
He asked the electorate in Ntungamo to vote him. "President Museveni
is only concerned with members of his immediate family, friends and
in-laws. He has completely forgotten about the ordinary man that voted
him in power.
Ours will be a government of the people. In case you realise that I
have not delivered, please feel free to boot me out of power. I was in
Bulemezi the other day and I saw a man whose cassava we used to eat
still waiting for his compensation 20 years later."
Besigye also said that not true that all the Bahiima had benefited
from the NRM regime. "Many are still grazing cattle. But there are
other corrupt people who have benfited. Is (Minister) Rukutana a
Muhiima?

Original_Search_Title: Cut Produce Prices, Farmers Told
Headline: Zambia: Cut Produce Prices, Farmers Told
Body_Text:
Nchelenge — PRESIDENT Mwanawasa has implored farmers to reduce prices of
their produce to pass on the benefit of the standard rating of
agricultural products to consumers.
Speaking on arrival in Nchelenge where he will tour development
projects, Mr Mwanawasa said farmers should consider reducing the prices
instead of insisting on zero-rating.
"Farmers should reduce their prices instead of asking for zero rates," Mr Mwanawasa said.
He said already, farming products like maize, cassava and rice were zero-rated.
Mr Mwanawasa said the current standard rating was fairly applied and the policy remained friendly to the agriculture sector.
He assured the nation that the price of agricultural inputs would be low following the appreciation of the Kwacha.
Inputs such as fertiliser will be bought at a relatively low price and farmers will benefit most.
He said everybody capable of paying tax should do so and that farmers should not be exempted.
Mr Mwanawasa said Zambia's taxes on agriculture at 15 per cent were
the lowest in the region. In countries like South Africa and Zimbabwe
the tax was at 30 per cent.
"Don't assist people who are eligible to pay tax if you want development to come to this country," the President said.
Zambia would be in a position to perform better due to the
appreciation of the Kwacha as most infrastructure like feeder roads
would be repaired.
Mr Mwanawasa said Government would fight to eradicate poverty because so many goods will drop in prices.
Mr Mwanawasa who will be in Luapula Province for the next four days
said his administration's support was overwhelming in the province.
The President was received by hundreds of Nchelenge residents and
told the audience at the airport that the party was getting stronger
and urged them to give him another mandate.
He apologised to the people in the province for not visiting them as promised earlier due to pressing national duties.
Mr Mwanawasa said Government had achieved a lot in the last five years but much more needed to be done.
He said people, including himself, as President had not expected to perform to magnitudes achieved so far.
"I am even surprised at what we have achieved because we were not
sure if we could manage to provide for the people. However, much needs
to be done in terms of shelter, medical and the provision of safe
drinking water," he said.
Meanwhile, Mr Mwanawasa reiterated the need to revitalise the fishing industry in Luapula Province.
He said most rivers and lakes in the province would be restoked with fish for the economy to go back to its old days.
Mr Mwanawasa, however, urged the residents to use proper fishing
methods and observe fish bans if the population of the fish was to be
controlled.He said Government also intended to promote all the viable
economic activities in Luapula province to supplement the fishing
industry. He was disappointed that people were using mosquito nets and
oils to kill fish from the lake, methods that he described as
unacceptable.
The President also announced that the ministry of Agriculture and
Cooperatives recently poured fish fingerings into Lake Mweru as part of
the restocking programme.
He directed the Ministry of Commerce, Trade and Industry to review a
protocol signed several years ago that was disadvantaging landlocked
countries from benefiting from goods transported by other countries
using local roads and waters.
He said the ministry should introduce taxes for all road and water
transportations that use the Zambian land even after they had paid
other taxes in their countries of origin.
The trade protocol should be made more sensible because landlocked countries like Zambia were disadvantaged.
Provincial minister Kennedy Sakeni assured him of the traditional leaders support for the MMD and his Government.
Mr Sakeni said he recently met all provincial leaders where they declared to support the President.
Meanwhile, President Mwanawasa is expected to hold a public rally in Nchelenge today.

Original_Search_Title: My Role Models Are Those Who Are Not Stupendously Rich After Public Office
Headline: Nigeria: My Role Models Are Those Who Are Not Stupendously Rich After Public Office
Body_Text:
Lagos — He regards himself as an establishment person, one who would
ordinarily not want to rock the boat. His presentation there fore
depicts the image of a gentleman, a patriot, imbued with much zeal to
move the nation forward. Indeed, Chief George U. K. Chima,
Chairman/Chief Executive of Huge Group of Companies which covers
shipping, manufacturing, bulk haulage and so on, is a rounded person.
In this chat with ifeanyi alia, Chima who is a management expert,
addresses a number of vital issues. Excerpts:
I am a management expert and business owner and a Nigerian from Aboh Mbaise local government Area of Imo State.
Family Life
I am a family man and I have children who are in higher schools.
Four of my children are in the various universities now, while one had
graduated.
How he met his wife
I met my wife in the course of my job, that was while I was a civil
servant. In the course of moving from one place to the other, we came
across each other and decided to get married.
Marriage is an institution - two people coming from two different
back grounds with their various training, you need to sit together and
try to understand each other in order to live together happily.
Primarily and fundamentally speaking, marriage is about sharing.
As a Businessman
As a businessman, I belong to the real sector of the economy. We are
into haulage. We do what is called bulk haulage business. The kind of
business you do with tankers, trailers and other heavy duty trucks.
We are also into manufacturing and we have actually veered into real
shipping business now. We represent some interests in the shipping
world in Nigeria.
On how he sees the business environment
The Nigerian business environment is at the moment dicey, especially
before the present consolidation in the banking industry. Of course,
you know that the economy of any country cannot work well where you
have a shaky banking system. With the consolidation in the banking
sector and the things they are about to do in continuation with
consolidating the banking sector, the economy might look up a bit. In
the past, it was like the policies were skewed to favour some sectors
of the economy, the oil, banking and insurance sectors, while
forgetting the other sectors of the economy like transport. No economy
grows with that kind of equation.
What makes him happy
What makes me happy is when I see some people feel happy and getting
what is due to them and also when I have work to do. I do cool off, I
also do engage myself with some leisure activities at the club level
like playing golf and tennis.
Social Life
In fact, man is a social animal. You cannot be an island on to
yourself. You have to interact with people at various levels of life.
You also have to attend parties, if these are worthy parties to attend,
not the kind of noisy parties where nothing serious will be discussed.
Because quoting from Holy Bible, King Solomon said that it is better to
go into the house of mourning than the house of feasting, because if
you go into the house of mourning you are able to think deeply and
ponder about life.
Socially, I try to mingle as much as possible with people of like
minds, people who actually think well and reason properly about how to
move this country forward and better the lots of the ordinary people.
Membership of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP)
Yes, I am a member of the PDP although, some times we don't try to
make noise about the membership thing of the PDP in view of the fact
that so many people try to misconstrue membership of the party to mean
allocation of resources to you and sometimes it doesn't follow that
pattern. But in terms of sympathy, yes. I have strong sympathy for PDP
and I try to patronize it as much as possible because I believe that we
have to start from somewhere to get to where we are going.
So, PDP happens to be the party of my own choice and in my own
locality. I have done a bit for them to stabilize the party in my local
government. I have been helping as much as possible. We are all
political animals, everybody is a politician one way of the other.
Senatorial Ambition
I had an ambition to go to the Senate and I actually tended to work
towards achieving that ambition but because of the hierarchial
arrangement in the party, some people who knew much more deeply than I
did at that point in time, asked me to step aside for another time.
I actually meant to go to the senate at that time, every thing was
in place, the network was there and we were working towards achieving
our goal. We had that advice from above that we should step aside for
now.
We are still looking at doing one thing or the other politically for
the state. It doesn't follow that if you are not a politician you
cannot contribute to the development of your constituency.
We are still doing our bit in other areas and hoping that political
political opportunities might come. Again, we believe that whatever God
has designed for you, will definitely come to be.
His kind of food
I am not particular about what I eat. I eat once a day and what ever
that is well cooked is okay. I am not very selective about what to eat
or what I drink. I drink red wine mostly because I heard it is good for
the heart and controls your emotion. Like you pointed out that we are
busy people so we need things to calm us down especially when we are
becoming emotional.
On Dressing
I am a conventional person, very conventional. I am always in my
suit as you can see, I like to appear very conventional. However, it
depends on the occasion or situation.
Educational Background
Educationally, I read management, I am always into management one
way or the other I did my advance diploma programme with the University
of Lagos, in business management and administration,
Then, I went to Chartered Institute of Administration where I did
corporate management and then went back to the university of Lagos to
do a Masters degree in Public Administration. Presently I am pursuing a
Ph.D programme at a U.K. University in Management. Essentially, I am a
management person.
Attracting investors to Imo State
What we can do to attract investors is very simple. Create the
enabling environment, let the infrastructure be there, provide security
for investors repair the roads which are bad, then investors will come
in.
Politics and Nigeria's business, economic climate
Politics will always affect business, because the man who has
political power determines a lot in terms of economic prosperity. When
you give governance to people who are deeply concerned with welfare;
welfare of the people, then democracy will begin to make a meaning as
government of the people for the people and by the people.
It is a participatory thing, politics is not supposed to be an
arm-twisting thing. It has to be something that should be
participatory, you rob my back a little and I rob yours a little. In
the sense that you understand why you want to be a governor, you
understand why you want to be a senator you understand the problems of
your constituency, and you try very hard to solve some of these
problems as much as you could. It is not about getting in there and
becoming a law unto yourself. It is about service.
Impeachment of some state governors
Impeachment is a normal procedure in participatory democracy. Where
the governor is not doing well it behoves on the people who voted him
to ask him to come back. There is this thing we call recall. He could
be recalled because he hasn't done well.
Cassava exportation
The federal government has started it already and it is in the
interest of the people, especially people from the South East where I
come from. You see, there has been this subsistence kind of farming.
With this type of innovation, people are made to go deeper down to do
bigger farming in order to generate more revenue for themselves. I
think it is a good thing, if we can export our cassava and still have
enough for our local consumption. It is okay, it is wonderful.
On what he enjoys
Every human being at one time or the other would like to have some
good time. Due to my christian belief, I believe in romance when it has
to do with your own marriage and it has to be an in-house. It has to be
properly guided because of the environment we live in. We are still
very conservative in this country. I hope you haven't forgotten that we
are still developing.
Growing Up
My childhood, growing up was beautiful, it was wonderful. My
childhood was very robust and beautiful we were were moderately okay,
whatever my dad had he gave to us, we were treated very well. By then
life was better, in the sense that there was more friendship, there was
more honesty and there was more fraternity between neighbours, unlike
today where people are beginning to isolate themselves more and more.
I think things were better then. Growing up was for me a very
wonderful experience except for the short period of civil war when we
had some dislocation.
You ventured into business at a very tender age, what motivated you
I was a civil servant. I worked with local government service
commission. I was a personal assistant to late Honourable Vincent
Ikeotuye, at a very tender age. In fact the present chairman of Enugu
Civil Service Commission, Chief J. C. Udeagbala used to be one of my
bosses then. I thank God, I still interact with him till date.
So it wasn't as if I wanted to get into business right from the
onset. I was in the civil service because I actually had my focus
somewhere, I needed to create my own business activity where I could
actually impact on the society positively.
Who are your role models:
I see role models of inestimable value in personalities like DIM
Odumegwu Ojukwu, Dr. Yakubu Gowon and Chief Odutola of tyres. This
might sound some how to you but that is the truth, People who could
stand by what they promised people who could get out of government and
are not stupendously rich, these are my role models. These are the
people you look up to and say they are good for this country.
Ikoyi Club Membership
The big deal about Ikoyi Club, from all I have seen is that it is a
club that is populated by people of integrity, people who see Nigeria
as a place of hope, people who think that we can still salvage
something from what is left.
I think Ikoyi Club is populated by such people and it gives me joy
and hope that we can still salvage this country no matter what any body
thinks about it.
Advice for young people.
The truth about success is that you have to put in your best, you
have to believe in God, you have to believe deeply in God. You have to
be persistent in your desire to succeed, and you have to be honest.
Your level of integrity must be 100% for you to say yes, I am beginning
to impact positively on the society. It is about hard work, it is about
integrity it is about a deep conviction of what God can do in your life.
Nigeria is a virgin land, still unexplored and it is still there for
people to come and explore. This country is very vast in terms of
natural resources and people. The choice is ours.

Original_Search_Title: Mondeh Continues Giving Hope to Farmers
Headline: Sierra Leone: Mondeh Continues Giving Hope to Farmers
Body_Text:
After eleven years of war, Sierra Leone is slowly rising from the
ashes and fears of food crisis have now been allayed through
implementation of a food Security program under the ministry of
Agriculture and Food Security.
Farmers in the country were handicapped by lack of equipments and
incentives but there is now light at the end of the tunnel as Dr. Sam
Mondeh the Minister of Agriculture and Food Security is meeting with
them to be acquainted with their problems and provide equipment for
them so that they engage in mechanized farming.
Farmers went into frenzied jubilation on hearing that 136 tractors
had been donated to them. This is in a bid to enhance a mechanized
farming system which will maximize agricultural yield and restore food
sufficiency to a once beleaguered country. Iran, Libya and China have
been the leading donors of the tractors to complement president Ahmad
Tejan Kabbah's food security pledge that has now come to reality.
Journalists went along with the Honorable Minister of Agriculture
and Food Security Dr. Sama S. Mondeh on the second nation wide
monitoring and sensitization drive to farmers in various provincial
areas from Febraury 3 to 5. Our reporter Abu Bakarr Sulaiman Tarawally
had been monitoring the events systematically and made his analysis.
The nationwide campaign on Food Security, is part of government's
aim for job creation and poverty eradication and has climaxed with the
donation of tractors to various provincial towns of Kambia, Port Loko,
Moyamba, Matru Jong, Bo, Pujehun, Kenema, Kailahun. Koinadugu , Bombali
and Kono districts will son benefit. .
Speaking to farmers and stakeholders in the food sufficiency drive
Dr. Modeh stressed the need to maximize food sufficiency noting that
food shortage can influence chaos and anarchy in a country.
Dr.Modeh recalled the years 1919 during colonial rule when riot
erupted in the country . People went on strike and pointed out the
disturbances of 1977 in Sierra Leone. he also pointed out that in 1979
neighboring Liberia went into strike for food shortage.
Dr. Mondeh observed that food shortage incurred in the country was
due to dependency on rice as a daily meal. Inequality prevails in due
course becasue of inadequate resources for farming. Farmers consume all
they have before the next planting season.
The minister cited China which, he said, has been able to produce
enough food to feed its large population. he said if China was unable
to do it, it would have been the most unstable country in the world
considering her population. He therefore pointed out that, averting
post conflict disaster emanating from food shortage could be achieved
through food maximization and sustained sufficiency in staple food
system . he urged them to imbibe the culture of hard work and also
encouraged the youth to take the lead.
"Much of hard life people experience at old age is due to idleness during the youth age", Dr. Mondeh jokingly said.
The minister revealed that he had consultations with business
investors from Holland and they expressed interest in expanding
agricultural activities in many parts of the world including Sierra
Leone.
The Dutch requested one million hectares of land in Sierra Leone in
which every 250 hectares was proposed to benefit from housing of 100
structures, clinics, schools, universities plus electricity and host of
many other social amenities. He said their own initiative in putting
end to conflict of nations is through food security .
The project had commenced in Ghana hoping to stretch in many African
countries. Farmers felicitated Dr. Mondeh for his progressive strides
in the food sufficiency drive in post war era.
Dr. Mondeh explain that when president Ahmad Tejan Kabbah pledged
that Sierra Leoneans by 2007 should not go bed hungry what he meant was
that Sierra Leoneans should be able to grow what they eat and eat what
they grow.
Civil society hails Mondeh
Head of civil society coalition groups Charles Mambu who was present
in the occasion in Kenema critically said the rule of law has been
misused so much that it has fetched impunity to the citizenry of this
country. He disclosed that civil society movements have concerted their
efforts in the program. He proposed convening a foot ball gala at
provincial level involving all stake holders, adding that the trophy
would be called Sama Mondeh Food Security cup. This however
demonstrated how appreciative the civil society movement was on the
effort of Dr. Sama Mondeh. Mambu pleaded for farmers' cooperation.
KENEMA
Dr. Mondeh said achieving the Millennium Development Goals (MDG) is
president Ahmad Tejan Kabbah's first priority. The Provincial Secretary
in Kenema Lansana Komeh noted that youth chase wealth. He commended
National Association of Farmers Sierra Leone for been agent to
implement a decentralized agriculture management scheme and for the
mobilization of farmers into groups which made Sierra Leone achieve 65%
rice production in 2004. Farmers Field School coordinator Abu Kokofele
intimated that 85 people were trained in poultry and cross breeding
technology in 5 located areas. FFS has been able to cultivate 100
bushels of rice and have realized multiple yield of 65% rice
sufficiency.. The ministry allocated rice mills 2 tractors and a
cassava processing machine.
UNDP has establshed Agriculture Business Units in 11 chiefdoms out
of 16 in Kenema. Cash crop and grassland farming carried out by the
ABUs in Kenema yielded 500 bushels of rice out of 76. . The people of
Kenema were also admonished to change their eating habits and alternate
daily food items.
Paramount Chief Prince Mambu spoke of timely supply of subsidies and
Food For Work. P.C Mambu requested that paramount chiefs be included in
crops distribution to farmers so that they could reach the rightful
individuals for better farming returns. The kenema Town Council
secretary said government has devolved functions but agriculture
officials should be on the ground to monitor events. "We need no arm
chair leaders in post conflict Sierra Leone", he declared. He pointed
out the failures of previous regimes like the All Peoples Congress
Green revelation and the NPRC's Accelerated Rice Production Initiatives
on food security due to weak political will.
PUJEHUN
Pujehun in the south arid zone is agriculturally oriented . Though a
flood d isaster destroyed agriculture produce last season still Pujehun
achieved 69% self sufficiency. The first gentle man of the district
councilor Nasiru A. Magonna said he and other stake holders were
working hard to free their people from poverty by pioneering advocacy
programs and linking the people with government . Mr. Nasiru confirmed
that flood destroyed many houses and crops. Hon. Ansumana Kaikai Member
of Parliament spoke on the high cost of living in Pujehun due to the
flood disaster but encouraged his people to be hard workers to soon
recover and sustain food sufficiency. He commented that Dr. Mondeh had
been so kind and empathized with poor farmers.
Explaining reasons why prices of rice likes he said; there has been
a drop of rice production in China due to industrial improvement on
technology. In 2004, Pujehun district came 1st over 90% was attained in
food sufficiency .
LANDING CRAFT
The Hon. Minister of Agriculture and Food Security Mr. Mondeh on
February 3rd 2006 officially launched a constructed landing raft at
Gbondapi in Pujehun. District councilors in cooperation with Bank of
Sierra Leone sponsored the tractor conveying raft construction.
The people of Pujehun said with the tractors they could now begin
exportation of their produce. The minister appreciated their local
technology. 13 tractors were given to them.
Kailahun Kailahun had been top of the agenda for maximum food sufficiency.
Cash crops in those days was valid so much that Sierra Leone has
been producing the largest of it cacao coffee product for exportation.
But their problems have been lack of trunk roads. Govt. has solicited 5
million us $ for purchasing machines for cash crops. Rice self
sufficiency in Kailahun by 2004 is 108% and a total of 5 tractors were
allocated to them. 125 acres of land has been cultivated in Mano
chiefdom .
NAFSL
National Farmers Association in Sierra Leone and farmers have hailed
Dr. Mondeh for his nationwide tour. NAFSL urged farmers to take good
care of the tractors and equipment. NAFSL therefore extended plea that
people farming cooperate to bring to fruition president Kabbah's dream
of food security.
DONORS UNDP support FFS and the ABUs, the Chinese have trained 60
people from Sierra Leone on mechanical cultivation system, Two trainers
have arrived to prepare tractors and have donated 69 tractors, 8
combined harvesters.
Bye laws Bye laws are to be maintained compelling youth who are 21 years old to own farms.

Original_Search_Title: Food Shortage Hits Gbarnga
Headline: Liberia: Food Shortage Hits Gbarnga
Body_Text:
Gbarnga — Residents of the central highland city of Gbarnga are reportedly
running out of locally produced foods due to the bad road conditions in
the county.
The residents who largely depend on their "country rice" and other
locally produced food crops are now relying on the imported rice
commonly referred to as the "butter rice and some done" for survival as
a result of the breakdown of the two bridges connecting Kokoyah
district and Zowienta to Gbarnga.
Kokoyah District and the Zoweinta in Kpai District referred to as
the "food basket" of Bong County have been cut off from Gbarnga,
thereby increasing the prices of food in the city.
The Behla Bridge and the Zowienta Bridge were damaged due to the
heavy down pour of rain sometime last year, and since the two major
bridges were damaged, there seemed to be no efforts applied by the
county authorities nor the representatives and senators.
In December 2005, at a citizens meeting at the Gbarnga
Administration Building, attended by over 340 stakeholders, it was
identified that bad governance was a major hurdle for the under
development of their county.
The citizens said corruption and self-centeredness on the part of
county officials are the obstacles to the progress and development of
the county and its people.
The citizens in the meeting reminded the senators and the
representatives to consider the issue of the two bridges as paramount
as were recorded in their campaign trails.
The two bridges are cardinal to the local economies of the rural
communities of Kokoyah and Kpai districts and trade is stymied and
recovery is also being impeded by the breakdown of the Gehla and
Zowienta bridges.
Prizes of locally produced commodities have been hiked in recent times due to the damage of the bridges.
A schnapp of red palm oil originally sold for L$5.00 is now being
sold for$.50, while a bundle of plantain sold for L$75 is now L$250,
and a bag of cassava previously sold for L$150 is now L$350.
As the result of the prevailing situation, the impoverished
residents of Gbarnga are finding it arduous to find their daily needs,
while many of them are surviving on the sale of fire wood and potato
greens.
The Zowienta and Kokoyah roads are not accessible to vehicles due to the breakdown of the two bridges.
"My goods have spoiled in the bush as the result of the bad roads," one market woman emphasized.
Meanwhile, Bong County development superintendent Lee Rogers has
disclosed that plans are underway for the European Union (EU) to
reconstruct the Behla bridge, but did not say when construction works
would begin on the bridge.

Original_Search_Title: Cruel to Deny Africa a Hand Up
Headline: Africa: Cruel to Deny Africa a Hand Up
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — THE World Trade Organisation (WT0) is about to rule on the
European Union's (EU's) resistance to genetically modified foods and
this will have a direct effect on starving Africans.
Genetically modified foods are regarded with suspicion and
opposition in Europe and among environmentalist nongovernmental
organisations -- Greenpeace panicked Zambia into refusing free
genetically modified maize from the US when stricken by famine in 2002,
although Americans eat it all the time.
The EU tightly restricts imports to a very few genetically modified
products so African countries would face yet another barrier to trade
if they took advantage of the benefits of genetically modified crops.
All this is based on the vague "precautionary principle" that allows
undefined fears of undefinable consequences to be presented as evidence
in EU law and UN conventions.
But rich countries have enough food. Should we allow them to dictate to us what is best for Africa?
In Africa 70% of the population depends on agriculture as the sole
source of income but Africa's crop productivity is the lowest in the
world and 25% of grain is imported. Worse still, yields are falling.
African scientists are tackling the challenge by developing genetically modified crops with increased yields.
Africa is home to virulent plant diseases such as cassava mosaic
virus and maize streak virus. In the 1990s Uganda lost nearly all its
cassava (manioc) to cassava mosaic virus, which is spreading rapidly
towards Nigeria, one of Africa's biggest producers. Varieties of maize
have been bred by conventional means to resist maize streak virus but
this resistance often breaks down when grown in areas for which they
are not entirely suitable. Scientists in our laboratories at the
University of Cape Town (UCT), in association with a local seed
company, have developed genetically modified varieties that are
resistant to the virus.
Witchweed attacks the roots of crops such as maize and strangles
them, making it almost impossible to remove by conventional weeding.
Field trials in Kenya using a nonmodified maize variety coated with the
herbicide imazapyr have proven successful. Other scientists are
developing similar resistance using genetic modification technology.
And then we come to drought, one of African farmers' greatest problems.
I was recently talking to small-scale banana farmers in Kenya. When
asked what the major constraint to production was, they all replied
"lack of water". Attempts to breed crops tolerant to drought by
conventional methods have not been successful so a growing number of
scientists worldwide are working on the development of drought
tolerance using genetic modification.
At UCT we are using genes from the indigenous South African
"resurrection plant", Xerophyta viscosa, that grows in cracks in rocks.
This plant can survive for long periods at 5% of its normal water
content. It loses all its chlorophyll and looks completely dead but add
water and within 72 hours the plant resurrects in an amazing display.
The first genes we have introduced into transgenic plants show
tolerance to dehydration, heat and salt.
Insect-resistant maize expressing the Bacillus thuringiensis toxin
has been successful in parts of SA, grown by small-scale and commercial
farmers. As well as protecting the cobs from maize borers, it seems to
protect them from post-harvest fungus infection. These fungi can
produce toxins that can cause toxic hepatitis and oesophageal cancer in
humans.
Other important initiatives in African laboratories aim to develop
improved varieties of local crops such as cowpea, sweet potatoes, yams
and sorghum. These are staples in many parts of Africa but, because
there is no mass market for them, multinational companies have little
interest in improving their yields: we have to develop them ourselves.
But Europe's resistance to genetically modified foods is affecting
Africa: not only are genetically modified imports banned but the
unscientific fears have spread too. Kenya is considering halting
experiments, Zambia continues to ban donations of genetically modified
maize for famine relief and even SA, with 645000ha placing it among the
top 14 growers of genetically engineered crops, faces new restrictive
legislation.
Our big hope is that the forthcoming World Trade Organisation ruling
-- in a case brought by Argentina, the US and Canada against the EU --
will open up trade in genetically modified crops and free us to exploit
this opportunity to fight famine and increase trade.
African farmers already suffer from drought, disease, internal trade
barriers, corruption and lack of property rights; refusing them the
benefits of genetically modified food is a cruel and nasty trick.
Thomson is head of the molecular and cell biology department at the University of Cape Town.

Original_Search_Title: Mobilise Own Resources, Farmers Urged
Headline: Zimbabwe: Mobilise Own Resources, Farmers Urged
Body_Text:
Harare — FARMERS should take the initiative to mobilise their own
resources before turning to Government for assistance, a cabinet
minister has said.
The Minister of Agriculture, Dr Joseph Made, said although the
Government remained committed to the provision of inputs, farmers
should also help in sourcing their own farming inputs rather than to
leave the task to the State.
Dr Made said farmers should go out of their way to mobilise funding
through commercial banks and other agricultural stakeholders.
"A2 farmers should start preparing for the winter crop and with the
help of farmers' associations and syndicates, they should invite
corporate companies to work out farming contracts," Dr Made said,
commenting on the progress of the 2006 agricultural season.
Some farmers in Mazowe and Odzi have already invited the minister to
discuss prospects for the summer season, the successful implementation
of the land reform programme and the mechanisation programme.
About the prospects of the winter season, Dr Made said communal and
A1 farmers will be provided with wheat seed between March and April so
that they plant in wetlands.
As the season progresses, Government will concentrate on making fertilizer available.
"The ministry will also focus on productivity and yield enhancement
instead of wasting resources on land preparations when seed and
fertilizer are not available," he said.
Dr Made added that there was need to improve the balance between crop and livestock production.
"A lot of emphasis has been placed on crop production because the
bulk of the starch and energy comes from crops but it has to change.
"We want to focus on our plantation and industrial crops to improve our industrial base," he said.
Government was also aware that 20 percent of the crops planted
throughout the country had reached maturity and 80 percent still needed
Ammonium Nitrate fertilizer.
"We are happy that the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe continues to work
hard on making fertilizer available to farmers and we urge farmers to
look at the production of other crops such as sorghum, cassava,
jatropha, soyabeans, potatoes and sugar beans," Dr Made said.
Like other ministries, the Ministry of Agriculture continues to lose
skilled and experienced workers to other regional countries.
He called upon the Public Service Commission to improve the conditions of service in the agricultural industry.
Dr Made also urged the Department of Agricultural Engineering to
work with A2 farmers in providing back-up services on appropriate
equipment, technology and servicing their tractors.
However, the department was also facing problems of staff shortages.

Original_Search_Title: South-west : CFA 6.9 million for S.W. Farmers.
Headline: Cameroon: South-west : CFA 6.9 million for S.W. Farmers.
Body_Text:
Eleven Common Initiative Groups (CIG) operating in the South West
Province have benefited from a Government grant of CFA 6,930 million.
The assistance was handed to the beneficiaries, January 18, by the
Provincial Delegate of Agriculture and Rural Development, Francis Leku
Azenaku, at the Buea Provincial Delegation office. Other local
authorities were present at the modest ceremony that sent many
promoters of CIG rejoicing for the new impetus.
Each of the groups received CFA 630.000 in addition to signing an
undertaking with Government stating clearly how follow-up would be
done. Handing over the money, the Provincial Delegate Azenaku warned
that, Government would no longer tolerate fay mania in the management
of CIGs. 'Enough is enough with suit case CIGs,. the Delegate said.
That is why, this time around, a carefully worded undertaking was
designed for beneficiaries to sign to facilitate follow-up and
evaluation of how the money will be invested. "The contact would
normally open other avenues for the flow of assistance", the Delegate
said.
The groups include, LEKONG Caterers, ASWA-RUDEP, GIC Women for
Development and Amazing Grace women from Fako Division, CHEDE MUAF
-COOP Muambong from Bangem-KUPE Manenguba, Top Ladies Farming Group,
Bakna Super Women Group and Time Will Tell Cassava Farmers from Meme
Division. Ormaye Ortan COW CIG and Apostolic Women Etoko came from
Manyu. Nteseh Unity Women CIG came from Lebialem.
A recipient, Hon Nzenze Ewanoge, representing Muaf-coop Muambong ot
Bangem (Kupe Muaneguba) expressed satisfaction with Government
assistance. Hon Nzenze's 10 year-old group undertakes activities like
Coffee, Plantains, Cassava and fashioned fruits farming. He hoped that
such assistance could be continuous in order for his group to grow from
strength to strength.

Original_Search_Title: It's Difficult to Fight Poverty Without Infrastructural Development, Says NAPEP Boss
Headline: Nigeria: It's Difficult to Fight Poverty Without Infrastructural Development, Says NAPEP Boss
Body_Text:
The issue of poverty has generated heated debate with the
government saying that poverty has dropped from about 70 per cent in
1999 to 54.4 per cent in 2004 and even lower. While labour unions
disagree with this position of government, the man in charge of poverty
alleviation, Dr. Magnus Kpakol, spoke recently on the effort NAPEP is
making to fight poverty in Nigeria. Excerpts
We agreed last time that we would be meeting periodically with the
media, and we have been living up to that promise. NAPEP is being
repositioned for meaningful relationship with the media. This is
because we want to place particular focus on publicity to create
awareness among the Nigerians on the operation of NAPEP. We have
recognised that no matter how hard you have been working if people
don't hear or read about you, it's like you are not doing anything.
Recently, one prominent personality challenged me, comparing NAPEP
with some other orgnisations that appear to very popular saying they
are popular even though they may not have done as much as NAPEP . You
may not agree but NAPEP has been doing so much to alleviates poverty
but its seems people don't know much about what we have been doing.
We have noticed that other organisations, when they want to do
something, they announced it again and again saying we are doing this
and that, and after the event had come and gone, they still announced
that we have done this and that. This makes the public to believe that
they are doing a lot. NAPEP has been engaged in multiplicity of
projects but it seems no body knows or hear about them. So we have
recognised the importance of publicity and have decide to accord it
priority attention.
Our focus this year is to involve more Nigerians in economic
development. That is the primary thing we want to do this year. We want
to ensure that more people are involved in poverty alleviation, and our
simple way of doing this is to ensure that we are leveraging every
available resources through what we call a Multi-Partner Micro Finance
Scheme.
The Last time I discussed with you, if you could recall, we talked
about three challenges that face the poor in the Nigerian society and
these are lack of information, access to market and capital. The
capital side is so important in our view that we had to launch the
micro finance policy on this aspect last year. We found out through
research that about 65 per cent of our people do not carry out
financial intermediation in the economy. That study shows that about 70
per cent of Nigerians are not involved in activities with financial
institutions in the economy.
If that is the case, it means that there are people in Nigeria that
can not carry out or have the capability to produce goods and services
because to produce goods and services you need capital, and capital is
not free.
You have to buy or somehow give it. When I say capital , I mean
goods that are produced in other to use them to produce other goods. To
get such goods produced, you need money. So, capital is not only raw
cash, it is the tool used to produce goods and services. But poor
people don't have access to capital because they don't have the money
to buy things like tractors or fertilisers, and other tools that could
aid them to produce finished goods and services. So, we want to expand
the scope of possibility for them to be able to do that, and that is
going to be thrust of our Multi-Partner Micro Finance Scheme. The
scheme is essentially a situation where we bring some resources either
directly ourselves, or through the money deposit banks and we ask
states and the local governments to match what we put on the table and
we get other private sector companies to help and donor communities to
come in. The plan is to we channel these resources to Micro Finance
Bank for onward lending t o micro enterprises.
Of course, as you are probably aware, micro finance institutions are
going to become micro finance banks. This was the reason behind the
micro finance policy which was launched in August last year by the
Honourable Minister of Finance, Mrs Ngozi Okonzo-Iweala in Kaduna ,
where we raised N200 million. After the Kaduna launching the programme
was launched again successfully in Ogun State late last year where we
also raised more than N200 million.
On Thursday January 26, we will be relaunching it in Ondo State, and
the Governor of the state is putting in about N100 million, and all
together, we will see more than N300 million raised in that State. The
largest of the micro finance scheme will be probably be launched on
Monday January 30 so far, in Lagos where we are looking at N2 billion
for the scheme. So we are making preparations for that. Following
Lagos, is Bayelsa State which is also coming up with something close to
N2 billion.
Ebonyi, State is also on line, Cross Rivers, and other states have also been lined up.
Things have not been easy. It took us almost two years to develop
this programme and to convince states' governors to be active
participants. The fact of the matter is without the participation of
the states we will not be able to have effective leveraging over
poverty. That will be absolutely impossible. So there is no way the
federal government alone can do it. So what we did over the last year
and a half was to keep talking to State Governors, giving them the
basis for this partnership, and I'm happy and proud to say that
virtually all governors are signing on to be participants. And this is
going to happen has to happen because what we recognise and I think the
last time I presented a document called Integrated Model for Poverty
Eradication.
Basically, what the document says is that you can not be successful
in your fight against poverty in a country like Nigeria if you do not
marry micro financing with infrastructure development. It's just going
to be an impossibility, and no matter how much infrastructure you
provides, if people don't have the money to start their own businesses
and run their activities, the infrastructures will collapse because of
the difficulty in maintaining them. For instance, if you provide
boreholes in a community you just sunk the boreholes say by state,
local government or the federal government and the people do not have
high income in the community, when the pumps break down, there will be
nobody who will be in strong financial position to fix them, they will
keep going back to the government to fix them which will be very
difficult to do. But if we can empower the community with micro credit
since the poor don't have the ability to walk up to a bank for loan,
then the they can raise their level of income to fix the pumps by
themselves when the pumps break down.
Do not measure us in just the people that we have touched directly
but also measure us in terms of what the states and local governments
are doing now because of the leadership that is being shown through the
fact that Mr. President has been very supportive of the new NAPEP, I am
hoping that by now most people know that we entirely a new NAPEP, this
is not the same NAPEP that we have before, and this is inline with what
the President wanted when he asked me to go to NAPEP, to try and see
how we can create a NAPEP that will involve the people, the States, and
truly represent what the president want to see in the reform in the
spirit of NEEDS etc.
So, am very happy and proud of the commitment and the political will
of that Mr. President has shown for fighting poverty not just though
NAPEP but also through the provision of infrastructures and the
governance that he is building throughout the States and Local
governments. This has help me to talk to some governors to put some
money on the table and to prepare their people for involvement in the
scheme so that the masses can be involved.
However, let me make it clear that the funds that will be raised
from the Multi Partner Micro Finance scheme will be used principally
for farmers, youth and women empowerment efforts. That is why we are
giving attention to the rural areas, and also for young people of which
many of them would be in the activities that are in the agro-allied
area, and then for women in general micro credit . There are some women
that quite honestly may not have the kind of education people may be
like at for producing new things for the first time but they can do
simple buying and selling such women can go and buy larger basin of
pepper and sell and make a little more money to take care of their
families than before. So we believe in that the general micro credit
has potential for women. So essentially we cover farmers empowerment,
empowerment to young people and women. We believe that if we emphasis
farmers empowerment area put those resources into micro financing the
banks are be more intere sted in because the small amount of collateral
they are able to give are big amount of loan in Lagos State and others
we are bringing the banks in to provide the money so you can see it is
a strong public- private sector partnership that NAPEP is building here
and we are very proud of it.
Lastly, if you look at the document we have a programme we call the
Give Back to society. It is a programme where Nigerians in Despora are
contributing resources in one weekend I was in Dallas we raised $60,000
from various Nigerian communities as part of their contributions to
help their communities back home. NAPEP will merge these resource with
the micro finance funds.
In any country, it is very difficult to get very precise over all
poverty indicators in any country even in the U.S. In Nigeria, the
definition of poverty because we want to ensure Nigerian definition of
poverty when you have that there are two ways that you can come up with
a poverty indicator one is to do a comprehensive census of the people
that is after you define who a poor person is in Nigerian context or
you do a survey of a Nigerians who are in poverty. A census is not
something you do every year it is too costly to do a census of
Nigerians who are in poverty every single year no country can do that.
But you can do a survey. The last good survey that was done was in 2004
that came up with 54.4 per cent, I do not think it was necessary to
come up with another one last year and I am pretty sure there was a
decline in poverty in the country in 2004 despite what some people may
say and I give you my reasons for that just going by the indicators
that we see in other sector s it is really very obvious that the
poverty rate has been declining. So I would reckoned that we have a
poverty rate that is lower than 54.4 percent. But nevertheless, a study
has to be made so that we can show it.
Work is being done on Poverty Assessment, and a couple of people are
involved in it. The National Bureau of Statistics under the National
Planning Commission, the Office of the Senior Special Assistant to the
President on NDE , and NAPEP. We would soon come out with the
definition of poverty in the Nigerian context and the rate of poverty
in the country. Again, if you look at the new effort of states and
local government improvement taking place in the macro economy it will
suggest to me that if we look at the growth of the economy, the non-oil
sector grew by over 7 per cent last year. If the non oil sector grew by
over 7 per cent from 2004 it will be unreasonable to expect poverty to
be at the same level. We have to be careful here even as the poverty
rate is dropping and even if it should drop from 54.4 per cent to 30
per cent there may even be a louder outcry from that 30 per cent of the
people than the 54.4 per cent before. Do not go by the loudness of the
outcry to measu re if the poverty rate is going down or not.The
evidence in the country suggest to me that the poverty rate is going
down.
This will vary from state to state. In Ogun state where the amount
is about N225million the ceiling may be lower than say in Rivers where
we are talking of N2billion. There are two areas we are looking at.
There is what we call capacity deepening measures and there is another
one I call capacity widening measures In capacity widening you will
have as many people as possible to have access to something so in that
case you give them five thousand, ten thousand and what have you, but
those people mut be anchored to some body who is doing something
deeper. Say you have a group of farmers and you make them to work on a
large farm and give them say N30,000 or N40,000 to farm on a big
hactrage and they bring their cassava out, they better with some one
with a cassava grinding machine who got a loan of say N400,000 so that
the cassava machine can be used for all the others.
In the case of the reverie areas getting loans for fishing boats,
there are some fishing boats that cost about N50,000, and we would
still need hundreds of thousand of Naira to equip them with life vest
and other gadgets for protection of lives. So the ceiling will vary
depending on the emphasis of the state and the amount of money raised.
On average, it could be between N50,000 but more than N100,000, and
they can also be as low as N10,000 or N5,000. There are also some women
in retail trade whom if you give N10,000 they will receive it but they
can not handle it. So they want you to give them N5,000 first. We are
prepared to help them but particularly we are looking at disbursing
fund in the range of N20,000 to N50,000 and not more than N100,000.
And, when we have done the capacity deepening measures we can give as
much as N250,000 - N500,000 micro credit facility within the state.

Original_Search_Title: Rita Marley: a Philanthropist And a Patriot
Headline: Ghana: Rita Marley: a Philanthropist And a Patriot
Body_Text:
Accra — The renowned reggae queen and wife of Bob Marley, Rita Marley is indeed a true philanthropist and patriot to Ghana.
She has initiated several projects in her local community in Ghana and other parts of the country.
Owing to her enormous contribution to the development of Konkonnuru
village in the Akwapem Mountains, Rita Marley has been made a
queenmother with the stool name Nana Afua Adobea.
Apparently, Nana Rita Marley repatriated to Ghana over a decade ago
and lives at Konkonduru a village near Aburi. One of the most
remarkable projects she has undertaken has been adopting the local
school of the community, Methodist Local Primary and J.S.S. Apart from
rehabilitating old school blocks and building new ones, she also
supports the children through schorlarship shemes and feeding the
primary school children to supplement their nutrition.
Rita Marley is the founder of Rita Marley Foundation, a non
governmental organisation which works with funds from donations and the
Bob Marley Foundation. The Rita Marley Foundation undertake poverty
alleviation projects in developing countries.
Under her foundation, Mrs. Marley has supported the funding and
distribution of the five-in-one vaccine for children of the eastern
region. Some of the numerous projects that she has initiated include
building the main road for the Konkonnuru community, and bringing water
to the village by drilling a 40 meters deep borehole for the community.
As part of remodeling the Konkonnuru Basic School which has an
enrollment of 90 pre-school children, more than 80 senior students, 135
primary pupils and 15 teachers, she furnished the school with windows,
doors, desks and chairs and in colloboration with the government of
Ghana, provided electricity to the community.
Rita Marley also distributes donations of clothes, plastic utensils,
food, toothbrushes and soaps to the rural folks in her community.
In 2004, Rita Marley and the women in her community began a plantation of cassava and other vegetables.
Mrs. Marley has also opened a library stocked with the musical works
of Bob Marley and herself at her Tuff Gong Studio at Aburi on the
Akwapem ridge.
It is undoubtedly true therefore that Rita Marley integrated into
the Ghanaian society as a practical means of returning to her roots.
Nana Rita Marley is a living example of what African descendants in the
west should be doing as part of their philosophy. It is expected that
this birthday celebration will not only be a celebration of Bob Marley,
but also an encouragement for African descendants in the Diaspora to
return to their heritage and support the building of our ancient
civilization.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Says the Future of the Private Sector in Ghana Is 'Beautiful'
Headline: Ghana: Minister Says the Future of the Private Sector in Ghana Is 'Beautiful'
Body_Text:
Washington, DC — Ghana's Minister for Private Sector Development and the President's Special Initiatives, Kwamena Bartels,
discusses the role and importance of the private sector to Ghana's
economy as well as the support that his ministry offers to
entrepreneurs in this interview with AllAfrica's Gaddiel Baah.
What inspired the creation of your Ministry?
The president right from the beginning of the first term espoused
this vision of creating and ushering in the golden age of business. The
goal is to achieve sustainable, equitable and widespread private
sector-led growth in Ghana and for us to achieve middle-income status
by the year 2015. To do that we have been quite clear in our minds as a
political party that the private sector should be the engine that will
drive growth to achieve this vision.
The private sector in Ghana, like any other country, faces a lot of
difficulties and all these difficulties were being addressed by various
ministries. If you take agriculture, the Ministry of Agriculture is
principally dealing with the private sector, trade, the private sector,
industry, the private sector, mines the private sector, forestry, it is
private sector. All these activities were not being coordinated, so the
president decided to set up the Ministry of Private Sector Development
and the President's Special Initiative to serve as the focal point for
coordinating and harmonizing all this private sector activities in the
various ministries into one coherent whole.
Keep up with the latest headlines on WhatsApp | LinkedIn
The ministry was set up to address the impediments that affect the
private sector, to act as the voice of the private sector in government
and to get the private sector to influence various policies that
government intends to come up with so that at the end of the day there
will be that kind of elbow room for the private sector to boom, so that
in the end there will be the kind of accelerated growth that will bring
us to the middle income status by the year 2015.
From pre-independence -- the colonial time -- to post-independence,
we have relied on cocoa, timber and gold as our main export earners. We
don't control the prices of these commodities and their prices keep
fluctuating. For any government, you find it becomes so difficult to
plan, so to diversify the export base of the economy and also to get
the nation earning foreign exchange income from other diverse sources,
[President John Kufuor] came up with the President's Special
Initiative. The initiatives are for government to facilitate the
accelerated growth of production and export of certain selected
commodities.
He has currently added sorghum and cotton which will be launched
this year particularly in the northern parts of the country because all
these other ones are southern Ghana-based, except textiles and garments
which you can manufacture anywhere. These are the two pillars which
come together to form the Ministry of Private Sector Development and
the President's Special Initiatives and the policy behind their setting
up.

What exactly is the President's Special Initiative (PSI) about?
Let me start with oil palm. We are financing the Oil Palm Research
Institute (OPRI) to give us the best planting material. We don't have
the best, so we've given support to OPRI to increase its production
from about half a million seedlings a year to four million, and the
target is to reach six million this year. These are then nursed into
seedlings and sold to farmers and we are even prepared to give out
seedlings to peasant farmers on credit for them to start paying when
their trees start fruiting and they start harvesting. We give them
support also for clearing the land and we are giving financial support
for the maintenance of their farms. The idea is that every year we
should have a minimum of ten thousand hectares of land being planted
with palm oil trees.
In the case of garment and textiles, what we're doing as government
is funding the construction of the structures -- big factory buildings
-- and supporting the private sector to take loans from the banks to
buy their equipment, machinery and the fabrics for manufacturing. We
support them with access to the market. Last two years we supported
some operators to come to Vegas for shows and to get orders. We have
set up what we call the Gold Coast Collection as a merchandising unit
in the PSI to try and get these orders for the operators. We as a
government are also funding the training and the building of capacity.
Government is funding all the training of factory hands, so operators
will be able get access to people who know how to sew trousers, shirts,
etc. Then we're bringing experts to help each and every factory with
expert advice so that they will be able to produce qualitatively and
timeously and in the right quantities for the market outside.
In the case of salt, what we're doing is we've been producing salt
all these years but we've not been producing very efficiently so our
costs are very high. We've brought experts who are helping the various
individual private sector operators to be able to improve on the
quality of their production processes and also assisting them to access
funds from the various banks and financial institutions to be able to
expand their production.
In the case of cassava starch, the Ministry of Agriculture together
with the Ministry of Private Sector Development and PSI are
collaborating in getting the best planting material. In the past all
our research had been towards getting cassava for food, but we have not
been doing our research to try to get cassava that will give us the
highest possible yield in starch. We've given support to the ordinary
farmers in terms of funds for clearing their lands, funds for planting,
funds for maintenance and helping them to evacuate their cassava to the
factories. Government has supported the four banks which have set up a
processing factory which is processing cassava starch and Ghana's
cassava starch has been acclaimed top grade cassava starch. Now we've
got a market which is far in excess of the capacity of even that single
factory to produce, and now four other factories are coming on stream
in the next two years. We'll have a situation on our hands where our
export of cassava starch will be rising phenomenally.

What dictated the selection of these commodities?
These are commodities which our people knew how to handle from
centuries back, so it's easy to pick these. Our people will be able to
take it up and grow it. The second point is that we believe because it
has export value, it will create a chain which would mean that a lot of
people are going to get involved by way of employment. The demand in
ECOWAS for processed palm oil is two million tons a year. We are
currently producing 200,000 tons, just about 10 percent. Nigeria, for
example, takes about 1.5 million tons of various grades of edible oil
and most of this is imported from Malaysia. That's a huge market.
Don't those two arguments apply to rice?
Rice is not a special initiative because the Ministry of Agriculture
has been taxed to increase our yield of rice as an import substitute,
different from one which is targeting exports. We are importing over
$110 million of rice a year. How can we increase our production to
reduce our import of rice? That is what is being done but that is a
task that is given specifically to the Ministry for Food and
Agriculture.
Ghana's textile industry has come under a lot of strain. The
second-hand clothes market is flourishing and cheap imports from Asia
have flooded the market. A number of companies have actually collapsed.
How can you ensure the survival of the textile initiative?
Those companies that have collapsed, Juapong Textiles?, GTMC is
still operating except that they are operating at a smaller level, but
you will be shocked to find that GTMC is in trouble because the
equipment they bought 40 years ago is the same equipment that are being
used today. In this modern world, you cannot be competitive if you are
operating with 40-year-old equipment against China and the others which
are operating with equipment which was manufactured two, three, four
years ago.
In the case of Juapong Textiles, the problem really was that the
workers literally priced themselves out of the market. The persistent
demand for a salary increase... you will be shocked to find that some
of the workers were earning US$10 a day but part of the problem was
also that they themselves were not being efficient.
I will admit that smuggling of cheap imports from China is part of
the problem, [but textiles is a special case.] In China, the cost of
production is so low because their workers are prepared to accept low
salaries. In the textile business if you are expensive, you are out. In
the textiles business the margins are so small that you have to be very
efficient to be on the market. It is not like other areas like the
sellers market where at whatever prize you get somebody to buy. No. The
competition is keen and unless your technology is high and your skills
are high and your costs are low you are likely to be priced out of the
market and that's precisely what is happening.
Part of the problems facing some of the textiles companies is the
prices at which they are getting their cotton. They are either getting
it from those heavily subsidized by the West, like America which
subsidizes its cotton farmers up to around 46 percent, or they are
getting it from local sources which are too high. What we are trying to
do is support our farmers to also be competitive in producing it as
close as possible to what other countries like the U.S. and Egypt and
the others are able to produce for our textile businesses and factories
so that they will also in the end be competitive.
Are there any concrete results that the Ministry could point to?
A lot of these activities are inter-sectoral so it is not going to
be easy for the ministry to say that this is what we have achieved,
because our role is harmonizing and coordinating all inter-sectoral
activities that affect the private sector. If you take, for example,
doing business in Ghana, it is purely a private sector that we've been
pushing, but we've been pushing it through the ministry for Trade and
Industry, the Ministry for Finance.
We were just ranked 82 out of 155 countries for ease of doing
business and that is a great achievement. If you look at that study,
you will find that Ghana ranked 82 out of 155 countries in over 10
categories. In the ECOWAS region we were ranked as the easiest place to
do business. Ghana is number one. Out of ten categories we were number
one in eight, and first five in the rest. These are as a result of
various reforms which are spread in various ministries and which are
coordinated and harmonized by the ministry through the private sector
development strategy.

Does the Ministry seek a niche for Ghana?
We are determined to make Ghana the ICT hub. First of all the
overall vision is to make us the gateway to the rest of West Africa.
The gateway more for tourism and ICT, because we have various
advantages. We believe that the stability, the democracy, the peace and
the security of person and property, is such that it should be possible
for us to be the hub for manufacturing for the rest of the ECOWAS
sub-region.
Is there a policy for private sector involvement in energy?
As regards energy, we have been pushing for private sector
investment in energy generation and distribution. At least one company
is coming to Ghana to refine crude oil for export, and private
companies to generate power for domestic consumption and for export
into the West Africa power pool. The West Africa Gas Pipeline is coming
on-stream and others want to come and set up power generating plants
within Ghana to take advantage of the gas to generate power for export.
There are plans to set up a 1000-megawatt power plant in Tema for Valco
and also for export.
What about water?
The Ministry for Water Resources, Works and Housing is inviting
private sector participation for the delivery of water in the urban
areas. In the rural communities, their water is controlled by
themselves. It is a very interesting thing. I used to be the Sector
Minister. They own it, they run it, and they sell it to themselves.
Any good news for Ghanaian business people?
Yes. They will realize that this government has successfully
stabilized the economy. We have gotten to the point where we believe
that it should be possible for the country to grow quickly. Stability
is assured, democracy is assured, and security is assured. There are
various forms of support we are giving particularly to Ghanaians to try
and get into their own businesses. The African Development Foundation
has increased their funding. Government is increasing its funding, so
they will be able to give small and medium-scale entrepreneurs a lot
more funds.
We are helping them with a lot more training. The Italian government
has given us funds we are giving to Ghanaians to set up their own
businesses and those who already have businesses, to expand. The idea
is that from now until the end of the term of the president, we want to
see a lot more accelerated growth, so a lot more support is going to be
given to the private sector by way of capacity building, by way of
access to funds. The venture capital fund is in place right now and is
likely to start being operated in February. So far we have about $21
million which will be leverage for the private sector. Government has
also supported Exim Guaranty Company to increase its guarantee fund for
them to be able to give a lot more guarantees to the private sector so
that they will be in a position to borrow from the banks with the
guarantee of Exim Guaranty. So the future for the private sector is
