beautiful.
What attempts are being made to get Ghanaians in the diaspora on board?
It is so difficult to get them. What we have are these small,
disjointed groups either based on tribe, etc. It has not been easy
getting Ghanaians in the diaspora along professional business lines
like Ghanaian doctors in New York or Washington as a club. It will be
lot easier to help a group like that, or businessmen who want to come
and set up processing plants and therefore need the Ministry's help by
way of access to land, registration of their businesses, any problems
that they may confront, getting them help to be able to access funds.
That has not been the case for now. It should be possible for us to
encourage them to come together along those lines for us to be able to
give them that kind of support.
Your final words?
The government has been very successful in terms of stabilizing the
economy. But stability and market reforms alone are not enough. We
believe that growth can be encouraged with investments, particularly in
the productive sectors, and certain selected service sectors such as
ICT. Government is going to invest a lot of its resources and its
energy into promoting and supporting investments in these areas using
the private sector.

Original_Search_Title: Christians Join Food Sufficiency Drive
Headline: Sierra Leone: Christians Join Food Sufficiency Drive
Body_Text:
Christians in Sierra Leone are now in the forefront of the food
sufficiency drive and have initiated a program to invest in cassava
farming.
Launching of the programme took place at the Miatta Conference Hall,
Youyi Building in Freetown took place on the 26th-- 28th January 2006.
Theme of the training workshop was " Dignity in Labour".
Aimed at empowering farmers for maximum productivity and sustainable
income generation under the Christian farmers cassava initiative
(CFCI). According to the organizers, " a cross-section of Sierra
Leoneans attended a Christian conference in Nigeria in 2004 during
which an agricultural workshop was conducted on how cassava is
processed into flour and a variety of other products.
It was discovered that cassava are far higher than as previously
though and that there were great opportunities that everyone can access
through the sale of cassava and all the variety of its produced to also
learnt that every part of cassava is useful and can be marketed to earn
foreign exchange. The desire to encourage cassava farming was also
borne out of the fact that countries like Nigeria and Ghan a have
exploited the potentials of cassava and have been earning billions of
dollars from the sale of crop. In Sierra Leone it was also discovered
that though many cassava farmers grow the crop, they still continue to
be poor because what is realized from the now sale is not equal to
their Labour. As such this cassava initiative project now seeks to
mobilize farmers to make meaningful contribution to the nation's food
security drive while creating employment for people within the rural
areas.
The initiative led by the minister of Social Welfare, Gender and
Children's Affair, Honourable Shirey Gbujama Mrs. Daulta Mammie and
Rhoda B. Akinola with offices at Cannon St, Freetown, will be brining
an expert from Nigeria with the machinery to process the cassava into
flour, the machinery includes watering press, Grater, cake Baker,
Drying platform and grinding machine will be used in different cassava
growing areas especially in a village setting. They say that the
finished product can be sold to flour miles, biscuit and confectionery
factories, plywood manufactures and ethanol Distillers this training
workshop will have an internationally acclaimed expert on cassava
commercialization Mr. Boma Anga, who provides information strategies
and mode of operation to enable all to understand the benefits of
investing in cassava. The training has been daily for weeks.

Original_Search_Title: International Community Helps Sierra Leone's Food Sufficiency Dream Come to Fruition
Headline: Sierra Leone: International Community Helps Sierra Leone's Food Sufficiency Dream Come to Fruition
Body_Text:
Through the help of the international community, especially China
and Iran, Sierra Leone has made significant strides in attaining food
sufficiency by providing tractors for farmers to embark on mechanized
farming.
In the drive to bring to fruition President Ahmad Tejan Kabbah's
dream of food sufficiency in the country by 2007, the Ministry of
Agriculture and Food Security under Dr. Sama Mondeh has intensified
campaigns so as to inspire farmers in the provinces to increase food
productivity.
Dr. Sama Mondeh has traversed the country meeting with farmers and
also putting mechanisms in place so as to enhance increased
productivity of agricultural produce. The food sufficiency program at
its embryonic stage appeared to be a myth but the minister has now made
efforts to bring it to reality and there have been hopes of alleviating
the food situation in the country. "Sierra Leone is food secured", the
Minister of Agriculture and Food Security Dr. Sama S. Monde declared
over the weekend during a nationwide tour to promote the ideal of food
security. Journalists went along to assess the progress that has been
made in the food security drive Some of the crucial issues that the
government of Sierra Leone aims to address with urgency are human
development, poverty eradication, job creation and food security, which
are key factors in sustainable development Dr. Mondeh and his Deputy
Mr. Francis Kutubu N'Gebeh extended messages of hope to farmers and
urged them to embark on improved farming methods which increases yield
and resultantly attain food sufficiency.
Farmers expressed delight in meeting with Dr. Monde. "He who tills
his land will have plenty of bread, but he who follows frivolity will
have poverty enough!". Dr. Monde biblically told farmers Moyamba in the
south of Sierra Leone is well known for ginger production.
Dr. Monde was not oblivious of the fact that there were problems
affecting farmers like lack of mechanical inputs, that prevents them to
embark on mechanized agricultural system and lack of good
infrastructural facilities such as road networks linking farmers in
Moyamba to the main city. The scarcity of transportation, Dr, Monde
observed also compounded farmers problems consequently resulting to
hiking of prices of transport fare which adversely affect their meager
economy. Dr. Monde disclosed to farmers government's determination to
improve road network facilities so as to enhance supply of crops,
adding that government had been providing rice seedlings. He said the
Government also had plans to embark on oil palm cultivation. Soil
fertility was one crucial factor, which he noted, was of immense
importance in improving agricultural productivity. He also stressed the
need for stores, drying floors and timely dissemination of seedlings to
farmers.
Dr. Mondeh raised the hopes of farmers and inspired them to double
their efforts when he declared that the government through the Ministry
of Agriculture and Food Security had donated 12 tractors to Moyamba
District. The news was received with a frenzied jubilation. He pointed
out that it was in a bid to reduce the dependency syndrome in Sierra
Leone. The donation of the tractors was aimed at enhancing mechanized
agro-based activities. In the past farmers engaged in crude methods of
farming consequently resulting to low productivity, but with the
provision of machines the general hope now is that farmers in the
provinces have been equipped to bring to fruition the dream of food
sufficiency in post-war era.
The United Nations Development Program has been helping to lift
Sierra Leone from the ashes of war and has shown considerable interest
in the country's post war rehabilitation programs. The UNDP Country
Representative Graham Chippadie was one of personalities who went with
the minister on the tour to the provinces. He buttressed a point by
pointing out to the maxim "Giving some one fish would help the
individual for a day's meal but teaching someone to catch fish could
help the individual forever".
Dr. Monde was very impressed with farmers' output in Moyamba. He
rated total percentage of crop production in Moyamba at 85% in 2005.
The Minister and entourage also went to Mattru Jong, a town in
Bonthe District, southwest of the country. Sixteen tractors were
allocated to Mattru Jong, marking one of the highest supplied Agro-base
machineries for mechanical cultivation. Bonthe District has attained
69% self sufficiency contrary to 20% in 2000. Statistical data indicate
that food sufficiency was 21% in 2004 and rose to 44% in 2005. It
marked a significant increase. It indicates the determination of
farmers in the District and exposed the level of improvement of
agro-based activities. Bonthe comprises of 59 Farmer Field Schools
(FFS) and has 12 thousand acres of farmland achieved in a year's
period. They therefore received felicitation from Dr. Monde for a job
well done.
The minister used the opportunity to reveal that Malaysians have
expressed interest in processing kernels for oil production adding that
250 thousand seedlings would be supplied to the Bonthe District so as
to increase their yield. The Minister promised that outboard machines
would be supplied in the near future to help fishermen improve their
fishing activities.
The chairman of the Bonthe sensitization meeting confirmed reports
that Farmers Field Schools in Bonthe District experimented the new
Nerica rice species, which yielded 40 bushels of grains when harvest
was done. Bonthe was rated 69% in maximum of the national assessment of
rice production, which means that Sierra Leone is improving and due to
such rate of production this year's rice importation rate has been
reduced by 15%. Rice is the staple food of Sierra Leone, but the people
were advised to alternate diet so that their dependency on rice for
daily consumption would reduce.
Participants in a seminar conducted also suggested that government
through the Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security should also lay
emphasis on animal husbandry that could provide nutritious values in
diets.
Ecological factors under studied revealed that there were climate
diversities in cropping seasons and therefore farmers were advised to
be time conscious and to also involve local Agriculturists who are
experienced in farming schemes that involves different varieties of
crops.
Observations reveal that the new "New Rice for Africa" (NERICA rice
) having undergone some experiment may prove suitable for cultivation
in Sierra Leone.
Bo Southern headquarter town of Sierra Leone comprise 15 chiefdoms
that engage in rice production, which in 2004 was 38% but in 2005 it
slightly dropped to 36%. The decrease has been attributed to low
performance in rice production activities. Ten tractors were allocated
to the District and the farmers were encouraged to do more.
The constraints of the people of Bo did not pass unnoticed. The Food
and Agricultural Organization Representative observed that farmers
suffer from lack of information that helped them in decision-making and
opportunities available. He opined that if radio sensitization and
training of such farmers was conducted it could bring about better
results.
The ministry has increased rice productivity to 69% in Sierra Leone
thereby decreasing rice importation into the country by 15% .
Dr. Monde was not oblivious of the fact that communication and
sensitization through the radio greatly impacts on farmers. He used the
opportunity to make series of sensitizations on community radios as
well as conduct seminars.
The food sufficiency drive has received much support from China,
Libya and Iran that have donated tractors to the Sierra Leone
Government..
Also, 6 consultants from the Islamic Development Bank are set to
decentralize Micro-finance scheme and carry out the proposed subsidy
for farmers.
The UNDP Country Representative Mr. Graham-Chipaddy said during the
tour that UNDP had been encouraging various farmer groups including
Farmers Field School (FFS), cooperatives societies, Agricultural
Business Units (ABU) the National Farmers Association (NAFSL) by
granting aids and boosting them in diverse ways.
The food security drive is drawing more and more support from the
international community, giving hopes and indicating success. There are
also proposals for farming investment from the Chinese, Germans, Irish,
Malaysians, Libyans, Japanese and many more.
Dr. Monde told farmers of the significance of cassava farming, and
recommended the president's initiative on cassava. He revealed that
cassava has 17 uses and Nigeria has been able to manufacture kerosene
out of cassava, Biogas, Ethanol, Glue, flower, plywood, medicines,
different types of food such as foofoo, gari and eba, Dr. Mondeh said
President Ahmad Tejan Kabbah admires the capacity of the farmers in the
country and boasted that he was confident the farmers would achieve
success.
In the end , participants at the workshop thanked the minister and
president Ahmad Tejan Kabbah and also the entourage that went with the
minister.
Also, the African Development Bank (ADB), Food Agriculture
Organization (FAO) European Fund for Agriculture Development (EFAD)
have been d supporting Sierra Leonean farmers to achieve food
sufficiency..

Original_Search_Title: African Science Must Regain Control of Local Resources
Headline: African Science Must Regain Control of Local Resources
Body_Text:
Kazhila Chinsembu says Africa risks being 'enslaved' by
technology it doesn't own and urges African nations to regain control
over their biological resources and indigenous knowledge.
Africa is the birthplace of humankind. Unsurprisingly, over many
thousands of years, its people have accumulated a vast amount of
knowledge about their environment.
Look, for instance, at how Africans have used plants to treat wounds
and disease. Long before the discovery of antibiotics, Africans had
identified plants that could treat bacterial infections. Families
guarded -- not patented -- such indigenous knowledge, passing it down
from generation to generation.
But in the modern age, Africa is in danger of losing custody of this
knowledge, even as acceptance of the power of plant-based remedies
grows.
What about African agriculture? It used to be based on
multi-cropping ecosystems where crops such as cassava, maize and beans
grew together. There were no chemical fertilisers -- we used manure as
compost instead.
But 'monocultures' of single crops, combined with chemical
pesticides, herbicides, and fertilisers -- produced mainly by
multinational corporations -- have replaced our environmentally
friendly traditions.
And as the companies' profits grow, so does the reliance of African
farmers on their technologies. Seeds produced by new hybrid crop
varieties cannot, for instance, be saved for planting the following
season as they do not share their parents' genetic vigour.
But it seems Africans have not learnt their lessons. If we did, we
might not be so excited about genetically-modified (GM) crops.
Implications of ownership
An old African proverb says: 'if you want to test the depth of a
river, don't do it with both feet'. Although African scientists can
earn lucrative consultancy fees for developing and promoting GM crops,
we should think with our heads, not our stomachs, and advise our
governments about not only the technology's prowess and potential, but
also the implications of who owns it.
African agriculture has always depended on farmers owning and
sharing seeds, storing them to plant the following season. Farmers
using GM seeds made by multinational companies will lose this right.
We also risk losing control over some of the desirable
characteristics, such as drought and pest-tolerance, that farmers
painstakingly selected and preserved in our local seeds over hundreds
of years.
I don't deny that African agriculture would benefit from some
Western biotechnologies such as molecular marker-assisted selection to
screen for seeds with characteristics suited to our different
conditions.
We also need irrigation to overcome the droughts that can cripple
rain-fed agriculture. Such improvements, together with land, seed and
the collective indigenous knowledge about our fragile environment, are
cardinal to the survival of African agriculture.
But we must not try to solve our problems with technologies that
could enslave us because we do not own the patents on them. This is the
prism through which we should view GM crops.
Tapping African resources
This issue of ownership also relates to pharmaceutical research. In
fact, multinational companies have already been tapping Africa's
biological resources and traditional knowledge to develop drugs and
other products.
African scientists have been complicit, sending specimens abroad in
the name of research collaborations without understanding that Africa
rarely gets a share of any economic benefits that such research brings.
That is why I support calls by Stephen Lewis, the UN special envoy
for HIV/AIDS in Africa, for the "big multinational corporations that
have exacted such huge wealth from Africa's mineral, diamond, oil and
other resources over the decades, andÃ¢-oe the pharmaceutical industry"
to contribute towards the US$7.1 billion needed for the Global Fund to
Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria.
Time to wake up
That Africa will face new problems in the future is apparent. Human
health, for instance, is linked inextricably to a healthy environment,
and we are destroying ours.
A growing body of evidence suggests that new diseases are more
likely to emerge when contact between humans and wildlife increases as
a result of habitat destruction (see Conservation medicine's time has
come OP439ENG). The SARS and bird flu epidemics are reminders of how
over-crowded ecosystems fail to function and ultimately succumb to
disease.
Meanwhile, the world's biggest producer of greenhouse gases, the
United States, has refused to sign the Kyoto Protocol, which would
force it to reduce its emissions. Researchers agree that Africa will
experience some of the worst impacts resulting from climate change.
African scientists must wake up, get out of the laboratory more, and
be alive to the intrigues of commercialism and politics that can cloud
science. African governments must invest in their own scientific
research if the continent is to leap forward on the path to genuine
sustainable economic development.
And African presidents must have scientific advisors, just as they
have political and economic advisors. Without engaging our own
scientists, how will our political leadership face threats such as bird
flu, bioterrorism, HIV/AIDS, SARS, Ebola, biodiversity loss, drought
and natural disasters?
We should be returning to the good old days when older people were
living libraries of knowledge critical for our survival. But our
sciences will be useless and our knowledge wasted unless they are used
to help the vast majority of African people.
Kazhila Chinsembu is a lecturer in the department of biology at
the University of Namibia, and a former lecturer at the University of
Zambia and researcher at the International Centre of Insect Physiology
and Ecology, Kenya.

Original_Search_Title: NIC Chairman Designate Assesses Bong Mines Impressed With Activities
Headline: Liberia: NIC Chairman Designate Assesses Bong Mines Impressed With Activities
Body_Text:
Monrovia — -The Chairman designate of the National Investment Commission
(NIC) Richard Tolbert, says the number of activities being undertaken
in the former mining town of Bong Mines are impressive examples to
other potential investors of what can be done in Liberia.
Facilities of the former Bong Mining Company (BMC) are presently
being managed by Geo-Services, a local firm headed by Dr. Nathaniel
Richardson.
Mr. Tolbert's comments come following an assessment visit to the area last Thursday.
He observed that with leadership, creativity and proper management
from Geo- Services, former staff of the BMC, were able to rehabilitate
many things including the entire electrical system, the crusher and
were now using their own ingenuity to convert some old machineries and
equipment to modern use to extra iron rocks to produce crushed rocks
which are essential to the reconstruction process.
"I also saw that the locals had introduced agro industry into the
community in order to create employment for the people...there is rice,
cassava, plantain among other crops being grown up there."
Mr. Tolbert said that he was also very impressed with the many lakes
that have been created by the old mining pits which he thinks many
Liberians need to see.
"Certainly, if confirmed, I would like to show these lakes which are
about a dozen to foreign investors as potential sites for echo tourism
because they are very beautiful. We could also introduce fish farming
here," he noted.
According to the NIC Chairman designate, his assessment visit to
Bong Mines was primarily to see the effect of the closure of a major
extractive industry like the BMC on the Liberian economy.
He told The NEWS that in order to stimulate the agriculture sector,
we need good roads and or rail network system like the former BMC rail
line that is now helping to transport goods at low cost from the
interior to market in Monrovia.
Among other issues, Mr. Tolbert said that his job at the NIC would
be not to attract only foreign investors, but to also encourage local
entrepreneurs such as Geo-Services and others into doing more
businesses.

Original_Search_Title: No Rigging - Besigye
Headline: Uganda: No Rigging - Besigye
Body_Text:
Kampala — FORUM for Democratic Change (FDC) presidential candidate Col. Dr.
Kizza Besigye has said it will be difficult for the Government to rig
elections because the people it used in rigging the 2001 polls have
crossed to FDC.
He said the defectors revealed the rigging techniques used in the
2001 polls and that FDC enthusiasts were on standby to guard against
the practice in next month's elections.
Campaigning in Luweero and the new Nakaseke districts yesterday,
Besigye rallied the masses to guard their votes and not to leave the
polling stations until the last vote was counted.
"We came here fighting Obote's government for stealing elections in
1980, but who among you doesn't know that they are planning to steal
your votes?" Besigye asked.
"Everywhere I have been, they have been asking me what we shall do
to prevent rigging. I assure you it is now going to be very hard for
him to steal," he said, amid shouts of "agende (let him go)." He said
the NRA launched its struggle in Luweero but after taking power the
area was relegated to the periphery.
"They say this (Luweero) is the Mecca (Holy land) of NRM. But what
development has come here? This dust I have passed through to get here?
Even your little cassava we ate during the bush war has never been paid
for 20 years," he charged.
Besigye's convoy traversed the dusty murram road from Matugga
through Gombe, Busiikiri, Kalasa, Kayanda, Kapeeka, Kiwoko, Semuto,
Nakaseke up to Kasana, in Luweero.
Besigye slammed President Museveni's bonna baggagawale (wealth for
all) project as mere rhetoric in the face of unemployment and heavy tax.
"How can they get rich when State House and President's office take
the lion's share of the budget and agriculture gets crumbs?" he asked.
He said in the 2001 campaigns he proposed scrapping of graduated tax
which government said was impossible, only to implement it afterwards.
He said Ugandans had resorted to eating mugongo waazi (fish bones) instead of real fish because they could not afford it.
He said in the Odoki Constitutional Review, 97% of Baganda asked for
federo, but Museveni had stuck to decentralisation and regional tiers.

Original_Search_Title: Asikuma-Odoben-Brakwa to Hold First Investment Forum
Headline: Ghana: Asikuma-Odoben-Brakwa to Hold First Investment Forum
Body_Text:
The Asikuma-Odoben-Brakwa District Assembly in the Central Region
is to hold an investment forum this September to help tap and harness
the investment potential of the district towards accelerated
development.
The forum, which will be the first of its kind in the district, is
the brainchild of the new district chief executive, Mr. Emmanuel Adjei
Domson.
As part of the preparatory work for the investment forum, the
assembly has organised a consultative workshop for a opinion leaders in
the district, including chiefs, the clergy and assembly members, as
well as heads of departments in the district.
At the workshop, a renowned development consultant and chief
executive officer of PENTAX Management Consultancy, Bernard Joe Appiah,
who was the main speaker at the event, explained to the audience a
number of issues relating to ways of identifying the potential
investment areas in the district and also preparing their minds for
participatory and sustainable development.
According to Mr. Appiah, the district has huge development
potentials, which remain untapped, a situation which has made it one of
the most deprived in the Central Region.
He disclosed that the district has gold deposits at Amanfopong and
good arable land for palm oil and citrus plantation farming among
others.
The district, according to him, is rich in forest resources and, in
addition, produces bamboo, grasscutter and cassava on a commercial
scale.
The district also has traditional artisans who can produce intricate artefacts for export, he added.
Mr. Appiah said the workshop was to help them identify the
investment potentials of the district, categorise them into public and
private goods, look at their competitive advantages and define markets
for them.
On his part, Mr. Domson announced that a series of meetings would be
held once every two months on health, education and environment to
prepare blueprint documents to direct the district in those areas
before the big event in September.
In a related development, the district assembly, in their quest to
bring about development in the district has also organized a special
fundraising durbar in aid a District Hall Complex.
The assembly also organised a clean-up exercise in every town and
village, a free medical and dental care in selected towns, as well as
competition in women's football, cooking, eating and Milo-drinking, and
held a Christian and Muslim thanksgiving service, as part of plans to
foster unity, peace, reconciliation and development in the district.
Mr. Domson later distributed over 100 bicycles to female JSS pupils,
who travel from distant villages to schools in the towns, to facilitate
their regular attendance to school, as well as promote female education
in the district.

Original_Search_Title: Agric Ministry Partners With CBN, NAPEP Over Disbursement of N1billion
Headline: Nigeria: Agric Ministry Partners With CBN, NAPEP Over Disbursement of N1billion
Body_Text:
The ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development is partnering with
the Small and Medium Scale Development Agency (SMEDAN), the National
Poverty Alleviation and Empowerment Programme (NA-PEP) and the Central
Bank of Nigeria in the disbursement of N1billion grant to the ministry
for micro agricultural credit scheme.
Minister of Agriculture, Mallam Adamu Bello, who spoke when he
declared open the stakeholders meeting of the 2006 Millenium
Development Goals (MDG), projects for Agricultural Credit and Financing
in Abuja, further asserted that the gains of the debt relief granted to
Nigeria will be utilised to improve agricultural development and
alleviate poverty in the rural areas.
The MDG grant, Mallam Bello said, is to be operated through the
Nigerian Agricultural, Cooperatives and Rural Development Bank
(NA-CRDB) while SMEDAN and NAPEP are expected to use their
organisation's machinery to identify appropriate beneficiaries.
SMEDAN's Director-General, Chief (Mrs). Mod-upe Adelaja, opined that
the scheme could be made to focus on areas where the government has
shown interest in developing such as cassava, rice, aquaculture,
tropical fruits, bee keeping and honey production.
According to her, the items have nationwide coverage and
beneficiaries could easily be identified through the cooperatives
network.
NAPEP representatives said that the organisation has in place, a
Millennium Village Development Scheme that could also be used as an
avenue to implement the micro agricultural credit scheme.
At the end of the interactive session, a technical committee was set
up with the mandate to work out appropriate implementation strategies
and report back within three weeks.

Original_Search_Title: Three Arrested for Trailing Dr Besigye
Headline: Uganda: Three Arrested for Trailing Dr Besigye
Body_Text:
Nakasongola — THREE men were yesterday arrested in Nakasongola town for trailing
Forum for Democratic Change presidential candidate Kizza Besigye.
Two others suspected of being their accomplices melted away from the
charging mob. Those arrested were identified as Ali Ssentumbwe,
Ssemanda Kyeyune and one Ssentongo, the driver of the special hire taxi
in which they were travelling, a grey Corona registration number UAB
365R.
Ssentongo pleaded for mercy, saying he had only been hired from
Najjanakumbi, a Kampala suburb, and said he did not know that the plan
was to trail Besigye. However he said he was a staunch FDC supporter
known to everyone in Najjanankumbi.
The three produced brand new FDC party cards before they were handed
over to Nakasongola Police for further interrogation. A police officer
searched the car for arms but found none.
The Police Deputy spokesman, Mr Patrick Onyango confirmed the
arrest, but said the trio were later released after recording
statements.
"They recorded that they were Besigye's supporters who were
following their 'beloved' candidate and blamed their arrest on poor
coordination amongst FDC supporters," Onyango said.
Yesterday's arrest of the three men brings to 23, the number of
people who have been questioned for trailing Besigye. In January, two
men, Mr Frank Nyakairu and a common ISO agent known as Banana were
arrested and detained briefly at Mpenja Police Post in Mpigi.
The two claimed to be Besigye's supporters but Banana was quickly
identified. They survived being lynched when Besigye's security
intervened. Another 18 were arrested and detained in Kaberamaido. They
were travelling in a gray Corona registration number UAD 592W, and an
omnibus. They also claimed they were Besigye's supporters. They
followed Besigye's convoy from Soroti, through Amuria, Katakwi to
Kaberamaido where they were eventually apprehended.
Besigye seemed unbothered by the arrest. He continued with his
campaign in Luweero where he urged the people not to borrow money from
any micro finance insititution between now and May when FDC comes to
power if it wins the elections.
"Don't borrow money now. The next government will give you lower
rates," Besigye told the crowd. Micro finance institutions bring loan
facilities closer to the people, but many complain of the high interest
rates they charge.
Besigye criticised the Movement government for having neglected
Luweero its launch pad for the five-year bush war in which Besigye took
part. "I fought with Museveni here in Luweero and we ate your cassava.
But 20 years down the road, he still has not compensated you," Besigye,
who was Museveni's personal doctor in this very area, said.
He continued that there have been a lot of complaints in managing
war debts. In a separate interview with the Daily Monitor, Besigye said
he would look into the problem in May after he has been sworn in. "The
money has been paid to wrong people and the problem has lingered on.
The whole process needs to be streamlined," Besigye told the excited
crowd. He said once sworn in, these are the problems he would look into.
Besigye also had no kind words for the government's plan of 'poverty
for all'. "The government is lying to the people about that programme,
because it is not practicable," Besigye said. "How is it possible when
they cannot pay the little amount they owe you," Besigye wondered.
In Nakaseke, Besigye's supporters turned up with fish bones pinned
on sticks. The scene has become common at Besigye rallies. It was
started in Busia, with the residents there saying they had fallen on
hard times, thus resorting to eating bones rather than the local fish
delicacy.
The Nakaseke residents also carried the bones now popularly referred
to as 'mugongo wazi' as a demonstration of how the government is having
a big share of the national cake yet the peasants have been left to
gnaw at bones.
Besigye told these creative supporters that the very reason for them
to vie for change is right in their hands. He told them that the NRM
has left no mark in Luweero yet it was the base for the war.
"For 20 years, can anybody tell what change they have had in their
lives? Haven't things remained the same? Just look at the bad roads, is
it not time for change?" Besigye asked.
Like he has done on previous rallies, Besigye urged the people of
Luweero to guard their vote jealously come February 23. By press time,
Besigye was on his way to Migyera, still in Nakasongola district.

Original_Search_Title: Encouraging Farmers Through Cottage Industry
Headline: Nigeria: Encouraging Farmers Through Cottage Industry
Body_Text:
Lagos — For farmers to produce enough food for local consumption and
export, there is need to assist them in capacity building. Recently in
Amaokwe Item, Abia State, a cassava processing industry built by BATN
Foundation, was commissioned, writes Agha Ibiam
Item, a community in Bende Local Government Area consists of about
nine villages. It is not far from Umuahia, the Abia State capital. With
a relatively good road that leads to Ohafia, it is about 50 minutes
drive from the capital. The community's main occupation is farming and
trading. To assemble the villagers during an important occasion is not
a problem to the community. In one of such events, one elderly woman,
who wore a different cloth from other women, picked an ogene, a
constructed talking metal used either in music or getting the attention
of the village people when there is need to disseminate information.
She hit on it repeatedly without a word and the Amaokwe women began
to converge at the direction of where the sound emanated from. That was
during the day British American Tobacco Company Nigeria (BATN)
commissioned the first cassava processing cottage industry for Amaokwe
Item community.
The event was not only for the women. The men on that fateful day
also observed the commissioning. The project was in continuation of
BATN's agricultural development programmes which form part of its
social responsibility initiative. The industry was built at the cost of
about N2 million and donated specifically to the Amaokwe Item Women's
Wing Union who already have an existing cassava processing industry.
Cassava, which is regarded as king of women's farm produce in some
parts of the country has many uses. This explains why even the Federal
Government is making frantic effort towards massive production of the
product to support not only for consumption also for the production of
other goods both local and international.
However, BATN Foundation's Agricultural Development Project
objective is to assist farmers improve their social and economic well
being. The objective is being achieved in partnership with the
International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA), Ibadan by
improving the quality and yield of food crops such as maize, cassava
and watermelon.
The idea for the agricultural programme was conceived in July 2003
to intervene in the areas of improved agricultural information
dissemination and training, input linkages and facilitation of markets.
At its inception, a total of 100 rural farmers consisting of 50 male
and female farmers were enlisted in the programme. The male farmers
were mobilised to cultivate cassava and maize, while the women
cultivated watermelon, a crop that was introduced to the community for
the first time.
To kick-start the cassava production enterprise in 2003, 34 male
farmers were selected. Each of the farmers was assisted by the
Foundation to plough one hectare of farmland and they were provided
with improved disease-resistant and high yielding variety of cassava
stems. All the farmers were also assisted with herbicides to apply on
their individual farms.
From all indication, the cassava production is aimed at improving
the productivity of farmers through the introduction of improved
practices. This is expected to result in improved income for the
farmers. To ensure that they got better value for their produce, it
became necessary to establish cassava cottage processing industries in
some places to produce garri and some other derivatives.
During the commissioning of the industry at Amaokwe Item, a director
of BATN Foundation, Mrs. Flora Okereke, who represented the chairman,
Chief Olu Akinkugbe, noted that the industry was the first to be
commissioned and donated in the eastern part of Nigeria by the
Foundation. Okereke explained that the choice of Amaokwe-Item was as
the result of the fruitful end of mutual and constructive dialogue
sessions BAT Nigeria had with stakeholders in the south east zone.
"When we were doing the social stakeholders reporting dialogue in
the eastern region, something came out that we needed a support in this
kind of agriculture. We have to identify a community in terms of
processing that already has some uniqueness where if you place it, you
are most likely to succeed.
"Item, during our verification exercises conducted on the field by
our project management team, was discovered to be a cassava processing
community that is focused on cassava production. We also discovered
they have women units who work and farm together. So it was a natural
choice because they fitted perfectly well to what we were looking for.
This is a starting point and I am sure that other communities will
probably get this kind of treatment in future", Mrs. Okereke said.
With the focus the Amaokwe women group already has, she said it
shored up its comparative advantage in the production of cassava with a
critical need for a processing cottage industry. As a community
populated by industrious and hardworking people and endowed with
resources, Okereke urged the women to productively utilise the cassava
processing industry to enhance their economic well-being.
The industry donated to the community is fully equipped with a
cassava grater which is to be powered by an eight-horse power diesel
engine, two units of heavy duty hydraulic pressers, stainless steel
sifters and a fryer. In addition to the cottage industry, BATN also
sunk a borehole for members of the community. It is also reticulated
for the processing of cassava and for domestic usage of members of the
community.
Apparently admired by the courage with which Mrs. Okereke conducted
herself while she made her speech, the chairman of the occasion and
chief host, Eze Chukwu Udensi, advised Item women not to fail in
sending their children to school so that they could be like "Director
Flora."
On the reason for the industry, he said the women were the brain
behind its location in the community. He therefore, encouraged them to
put in more effort and be more united to enhance their productivity.
On how the machines are operated, Mr. Udo Okereke, Managing Director
of Integrated System Limited, explained that the cassava tubers are
peeled, grated into a mash, filled in a sack and pressed to dewater it.
After pressing, it is left in the fermentation axe for about two to
three days for it to ferment properly.
The idea of the fermentation, he said, is to let the enzymes
produced during the fermentation process to take care of the cylic acid
and neutralise the toxicity in the cassava. By the time the process is
completed by nature, the cyclic acid is as low as possible for safety,
he said.
The next stage, Okereke said, is to take the hard cake to the
sifter. During the manual sifting, it is decaked and grains are made
fine for frying. Then after the sifting, it is put in the communal
fryer.
Though he said there are some cassava species with very low cylic
contents but to be on the safe side, it is good to leave it to ferment
for two days. To boost the productivity of farmers in cassava
production, the engineer suggested that they could be trained to
augment their effort and also provide for them improved variety stems.
To organise the Amaokwe Item women to achieve such great feat might
not have been an easy task. The national president, Amaokwe Item Women,
Dr. (Mrs.) Regina Eyidia Ogali, explained that initially as a women
leader, it was a daunting thing as she thought she couldn't have
recorded any progress. But when she finds out that everybody was
excited having her around, it became easy for her to succeed because
there is unity among women, she said.
The existing cassava cottage, closely cited next to the new one,
Mrs. Ogali concurred is not as big as the one BATN had commissioned.
The unique thing in the new one was that there is a fryer attached to
it, which according to her is the most exciting aspect of it. According
to her, cassava could be processed at a spot and from there to the
market closely behind the two industries around.
"I feel the industry will add more impetus to the women. And those
who thought it was too much of a problem going into cassava cultivation
will just find it easy. It will also translate that our market will
grow bigger than what it is now.
"Why our men are into yam production, our women are also into
cassava production in a high magnitude. Gradually, more of our women
will be getting a larger piece of land because they will be helping
themselves and use it for poverty alleviation. Another thing we want to
do is to organise the women into co-operatives and acquire a large
piece of land to cultivate cassava", she said.
Somehow, she was optimistic that their traditional ruler would make
land available for the project. The women, she revealed, assisted in
building the cottage industry by giving themselves for manual labour
and she equally claimed that the project is actually their own.
The event was witnessed by Mr. A. Otusi, chairman, Abia Agricultural
Development Corporation. He represented the Special Adviser to the
President on food security, Mrs. Oluwatoyin Adetunji.

Original_Search_Title: Drought-Resistant GM Seeds Won't Benefit Kenyans for the Next Decade
Headline: Kenya: Drought-Resistant GM Seeds Won't Benefit Kenyans for the Next Decade
Body_Text:
Washington, DC — US bio-engineers working to develop drought-resistant seeds say
Kenyans should not expect to benefit from such "miracle crops" for at
least eight to 10 years.
Those currently starving in parts of the country and those likely to
suffer hunger if drought conditions persist will have to look to
emergency food aid rather than to agricultural self-sufficiency, the
scientists say.
Maize and other biotech crops able to thrive despite scant rainfall
will not be planted in the United States until about 2010, says
Christopher Horner, a spokesman for Monsanto, one of the world's
leading developers of genetically modified seeds.
Such crops "will be introduced initially in the United States well
before they become available in other countries," Mr Horner adds. But
he notes that Monsanto is striving to "enable Africa to benefit in more
of a parallel fashion rather than a sequential fashion from
breakthroughs such as drought-resistant seeds.
"Kenya may be one of the first African countries where these seeds are introduced, he adds.
"Kenya is a priority for us. We know they're trying to put in place
a regulatory system for this kind of technology," Mr Horner says.
The first generation of genetically modified crops was engineered to
resist insecticides and herbicides, notes Robert Horsch, Monsanto's
vice president for international development partnerships. Seeds
designed with these properties have proven easier to develop than those
tolerant of drought conditions, he says.
"The genome of plants and factors such as water uptake make for a
more complicated challenge in regard to drought tolerance," Mr Horsch
explains.
Farmers in drought-prone areas should meanwhile lessen their
reliance on maize and plant more crops such as sorghum, cassava and
sweet potatoes that can better withstand lack of rainfall, Mr Horsch
suggests.
Farmers struggling with temporary shortages of rainfall can
meanwhile prevent maize yield losses of up to 25 per cent by planting
currently available drought-tolerant hybrid seeds, adds an official
with Pioneer, a company affiliated with US-based DuPont.

Original_Search_Title: Thisday Awards 2005: the Nominees Are...
Headline: Nigeria: Thisday Awards 2005: the Nominees Are...
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Board of Editors of THISDAY has released the final list of
nominees in the remaining nine categories for its 11th annual THISDAY
Awards to be held in Lagos, February 4th, 2006. The categories which
nominees were announced yesterday are Stock Offer of the Year,
Transaction of the Year, Merger and Acquisition of the Year, Fund
Manager of the Year, Entrepreneur of the Year, Young Manager of the
Year, Representative of the Year and Senator of the Year. Also
announced are the winners of the Life time Achievement Award.
The awardees of the Lifetime Achievers Award are Chief Akintola
Williams, Prof. Chinua Achebe, Chief Emeka Anyaoku, , Prof. Wole
Soyinka, Mallam Adamu Ciroma, Dr. Babatunde Jobe, Mrs. Sheila Solarin,
Olorogun Michael Ibru, Late Chima Ubani, Justice Muhammadu Uwais, Mr.
Sam Amuka Pemu and Chief Gani Fawehinmi (SAN).
Nominees for Stock Offer of the Year are Diamond Bank, Ecobank,
First City Monument Bank (FCMB), Fidelity Bank and Investment Banking
and Trust Company (IBTC) Plc. Also nominees for Transaction of the Year
are Ministry of Finance/Nigeria N36 billion debt buy back, Vmobile $1.1
billion equipment, Bureau of Public Entreprises sale of Nicon Hilton
Hotel and the $600 million Nigeria National Petroleum
Corporation/Mobile Satellite.
In the Merger and Acquisition of the Year category, United Bank for
Africa/Standard Trust Bank, Intercontinental Group, Access,
PlatinumHabib and Skye Bank.
Fund Manager of the Year nominees are IBTC Asset Management, ARM,
Fidelity and FSDH, while the nominees for Entrepreneur of the Year are
Femi Otedola, Stella Okoli, Bimbo Awosika, Murtala Nyako and Aliko
Dangote.
The nominees for Young Manager of the Year are Bruce Ayonote, Ladi
Balogun, Ndidi Nwuneli, Yetunde Sadiku, Suleiman Arzika and Modupe
Ozolua. The Representative of the Year nominees are Abike Dabiri,
Anthony Aziegbemi, Farouk Lawan, Usman Bugaje, Dr. Haruna Yerima and
Prof. Sola Adeyeye.
The Senator of the Year nominees are David Brigidi, Ken Nnamani,
Lawal Shuaibu, Uche Chukwumerije, Victor Ndoma-Egba and Saidu Dansadau
Bank of The Year Nominees
Profiles in Excellence...
First Bank : First Bank of Nigeria Plc, one of the largest financial
services group in sub-Saharan Africa, has focused relentlessly on
consolidating its leadership position as Nigeria's bank of first
choice. Established in 1894, the Bank has distinguished itself as the
first and largest provider of specialised financial services in the
country. First Bank has experienced tremendous growth over the years,
with its assets and shareholders' equity standing at N377. 5 billion
and N44.7 billion respectively, as at March 31, 2005. Gross earnings
and profit before tax stood at N49.5 billion and N15.15 billion
respectively for the same period.
As a market leader in the financial services sector, First Bank
pioneered initiatives in international money transfer, MasterCard,
Interswitch, and the ATM consortium. Currently, it has an expansive
branch network of 371 spread across the country. Its over 270 online
real-time branch network is the largest in the country.
First Bank plays a dominant role in the Nigerian economy. Leveraging
earlier experience with Econet Wireless, it was the lead Bank in
provision of a syndicated loan of US$185 million for NITELÃ?Â¢??s
acquisition of a General System of Mobile Telecommunication (GSM)
license, and syndicated facility of US$72m to NITEL for the expansion
of parastatalÃ?Â¢??s switching facility. To demonstrate its commitment
to its customer and development of Nigerian economy, the bank has since
broadened its loan and credit portfolios to various sectors of the
economy; and in 2005, it led NigeriaÃ?Â¢??s largest loan syndication of
N60 billon to Dangote Group.
The need for customised solutions to customers' business problems
necessitated the extension of the Bank's services into seven (7)
subsidiaries making it one of the largest financial services group in
sub-Saharan Africa. The subsidiaries are into merchant banking,
registrarship, trusteeship, insurance brokerage, SME financing, venture
capital financing, mortgage banking, investment banking, and pension
funds management.
In November 2002, the Bank took a major leap when it was authorised
by the Financial Services Authority (FSA) to transform its London
branch into a full-fledged subsidiary, FBN Bank (UK) Limited. This
achievement makes the bank the first Nigerian financial institution
with an offshore banking subsidiary in the United Kingdom. Already, the
new subsidiary, conceived to serve as a channel for trade flows between
Nigeria and the UK, has become a success story. In addition, to capture
the increasing trade flow between Nigeria and South Africa, the bank
has opened a representative office in South Africa.
Undoubtedly, for a long time to come, FirstBank would remain
customers' delight because of its brand equity, strong balance sheet,
strong customer/shareholders' value, and dedicated work force. The
future continues to be very bright for the Bank, and for prospective
investors wishing to explore the vast business opportunities that
abound in Nigeria; FirstBank remains the most credible financial
partner.
Guaranty trust Bank : Guaranty Trust Bank plc was licensed as a
commercial bank in 1990 and commenced operations in February 1991 and
was quoted on the Nigerian Stock Exchange (NSE) in 1996. With about 80
branches in Nigeria and offshore subsidiaries in The Gambia,
Sierra-Leone and Ghana, the bank is one of the most profitable and
professional financial institutions in Nigeria.
The bank offers investment banking and advisory services, medium to
long term capital financing and fund management services to
multinationals, indigenous enterprises and high net worth individuals.
The bank, 2004/2005 financial report shows a capital base of over
$226 million, group profit before tax of $50 million, total assets and
contingents of over $1.45 billion and gross earnings in excess of $175
million. It has a (triple A) Aaa credit risk rating from Agusto &
Co, the leading domestic rating agency and a double A minus (AA-)
rating from Fitch.
The bank has been the subject of organisational and brands marketing
case studies by several organisations including Harvard Business
School, Lagos Business School and Cranfield University, Bedfordshire,
United Kingdom. Guaranty Trust Bank had also clinched the Nigerian
Stock Exchange PresidentÃ?Â¢??s Merit Award for four consecutive times
(1996, 2000 and 2003 and 2004).
These were in recognition of the bankÃ?Â¢??s corporate and
operational performance during these periods. The award is usually
bestowed on successful companies with excellent shareholder relations
and track records listed on the 45-year-old stock exchange. Previous
NSE awards for the Bank were in 1996, 2000 and 2003.
United Bank for Africa: August 1 2005, marked a watershed in the
history of NigeriaÃ?Â¢??s financial services industry, with the
emergence of United Bank for Africa Plc as NigeriaÃ?Â¢??s first mega
bank following the merger of UBA and Standard Trust Bank Plc. Apart
from its record as the first successful merger in the ongoing banking
consolidation, the consolidated UBAÃ?Â¢??s aggregate financial and
non-financial strength clearly ranks it above its peers in the industry.
With a network of over 425 business offices across the country,
balance sheet size of over N600 billion, and an active customer base of
over two million, United Bank For Africa Plc ranks as the biggest
on-line, real-time bank in Nigeria and indeed sub-Saharan Africa.
This no doubt imposes great expectations on the bank as it seeks to
meet the banking aspirations of most Nigerians, who neither have bank
account nor operate one.
It is therefore, in demonstration of its commitment to the economic
advancement of Nigerians that the United Bank for Africa Plc on the eve
of NigeriaÃ?Â¢??s 45th Independence Day celebration introduced to
millions of Nigerians, a novel product called UBA Freedom Savings
Account.
Freedom savings account as the name suggests is an account which
allows a customer to open an account without any initial deposit as is
the case with many other banks in the country. The process is simple
and convenient. With the provision of two passport photographs and
submission of any acceptable means of identification, the customer can
open a UBA Freedom Savings
Account in any of the bank's over 425 business offices located
within your neighbourhood. The UBA Freedom Savings Account no doubt
offers an invaluable access to low income/profit earners; the less
educated, and financially/socially disadvantaged to have a feel of
service delivery and an array of banking services for which the bank is
known for.
Driven by its retail banking focus, the United Bank for Africa Plc
last week entered into a strategic partnership with the Netherlands
Development Finance Company (FMO) leading to the signing of an
agreement for a $10 million facility between both institutions. The
Credit Guarantee Facility will be made available by Australian &
New Zealand Bank (ANZ).
The FMO facility comes on the heels of the recent loan of N210m to
the Kwara State Government by UBA Plc for on-lending to rural farmers
in the State. More than 1000 farmersÃ?Â¢?? associations and individuals
including youths and retirees involved in the cultivation of rice and
cassava are expected to benefit from that facility.
In a similar development, the United Bank for Africa has contributed
immensely towards the Federal GovernmentÃ?Â¢??s efforts at promoting
the use of non-cash payment instruments in the country. The UBA
MasterCard remains the most affordable and preferred credit card in
Nigeria contributing significantly in easing the burden of travelers
and businessmen who may wish to shop on the internet.
As the bank tenaciously holds unto its vision of playing an
undisputable leadership role in NigeriaÃ?Â¢??s financial service
industry there is hope that United Bank For Africa Plc through its
offering of UBA Freedom Savings Account will seek to render the same
service quality which saw it become the fastest growing agent bank of
Western Union International Money transfer in all of Africa, Middle
East and Asia.
Zenith Bank : Unarguably one of the most innovative and
forward-looking banks that had foreseen that large capital would soon
become an important and compulsory issue in NigeriaÃ?Â¢??s financial
services industry, Zenith Bank was one of the banks that rolled out its
drums to shop for funds when the on-going consolidation induced by the
Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) was announced last year.
The bank grossed N48.388 billion proceeds from its Initial Pubic
Offering (IPO). This was the largest IPO proceeds in the history of
Nigerian capital market and it represented a 554 per cent subscription.
But guided by regulatory requirement, the bank was only allowed to
retain N20.39 billion, a development that pushed its post IPO
shareholdersÃ?Â¢?? funds to N36 billion and further to N38 billion as
at the year ended 30th June, 2005.
The bank also currently has 288,780 subscribers to its IPO,
representing the largest number of shareholders of any company in
Nigeria Ã?Â¢?? feat that made the bank win the THISDAY award for the
Ã?Â¢??Most Successful IPOÃ?Â¢?? for the year 2004.
For the recently concluded financial year ended 30th June, 2005,
Zenith Bank recorded tremendous improvement in all key performance
indices and surpassed its profit forecast of its IPO. Pre-tax profit
moved up by 43 per cent, from N6.4 billion in 2004 to N9.16 billion in
2005, while total assets and contingent liabilities inched up by 72 per
cent, from N215.22 billion to an all-time high of N370 billion, a
development that reinforced the bankÃ?Â¢??s position as one of the
strongest banks in Nigeria.
Consequently, for the seventh consecutive year up to June 2005,
Zenith Bank maintained its position as an institution with the lowest
ratio of non-performing loans to total loans in the Nigerian financial
services industry. Its ratio remained less than 2 per cent compared
with industry average of 18 per cent, thus underscoring the high
quality of the bankÃ?Â¢??s risk assets, as well as its superior risk
management culture.
Zenith Bank has maintained a leading position in the banking
industry in the rating analysis undertaken locally and overseas since
1999 to date. The bank was rated Ã?Â¢??triple AÃ?Â¢?? in the recent
rating analysis conducted by Agusto and Company Limited.
In the same breadth, since 2000 to date, the bank has also been
rated Ã?Â¢??triple AÃ?Â¢?? in the credit risk rating analysis
undertaken by Pharez.
Zenith Bank also clinched Ã?Â¢??AAÃ?Â¢?? in FitchÃ?Â¢??s and Duff
& Phelps Credit Rating Co. Africa (Pty) Ltd in 2003/2004 and 2000
respectively; won Institute of Chartered Accountants of Nigeria (ICAN)
merit award in recognition of its outstanding contribution to the
growth of the industry and commerce, as well as accounting profession
in Nigeria; declared most efficient bank in 2001 by the Lagos Clearing
House (LBCH) and won three awards (best bank website in the Nigeria,
best on-line service quality and first overall rating) in the
Web-Jurist Award (Phillips Consulting 2004 & 2005).
Other awards won by Zenith Bank includes Market Audit and Research
Services Limited 2002 award as the Ã?Â¢??best service excellence bank
in LagosÃ?Â¢??; Maritime Quality Service Award as Ã?Â¢??best Customs
duty collection bank award (2000 Ã?Â¢?? 2004); Business Times Banker of
the year 2003; THISDAYÃ?Â¢??s Newspaper Offer of the year 2004; Fame
Magazine, Global Excellence and City People Awards as banker of the
year 2004 respectively.
International Bank of The Year Nominees
Profiles in Excellence...
Credit Suisse First Boston (CSFB): Credit Suisse First Boston
(CSFB), a business unit of Zurich-based Credit Suisse Group, is a
leading global investment bank serving institutional, corporate,
government and high net worth clients. CSFB's businesses include
securities underwriting, sales and trading, investment banking, private
equity, alternative assets, financial advisory services, investment
research, and asset management.
The bank is a proven leader across the spectrum of investment
banking, capital markets and financial services, ranking in the top
tier in virtually all major business segments. CSFB's organisational
structure focuses on providing the highest quality of client-centric
services.
It operates in more than 69 locations across more than 33 countries
on five continents. Powered by a strong local presence, global vision
and a seamless ability to execute transactions in and across all
markets, CSFB deploys its capital base and global perspective to offer
a full range of products, services and capabilities.
Netherlands Development Finance Company (fmo): The Netherlands
Development Finance Company (FMO), valuable risk partner, supports the
private sector in developing countries and emerging markets in Asia,
Africa, Latin America and Central and Eastern Europe. It does this with
loans, participations, guarantees and other investment promotion
activities and the goal is to contribute to the structural and
sustainable economic growth in these countries and, together with the
private sector, obtain healthy returns.
FMO builds bridges between entrepreneurs and capital, locally as
well as across frontiers, for sustainable development and healthy
returns. Commercial banks and other investors have been reluctant with
regard to developing markets due to international politics and economic
developments at the beginning of the 21st century and as a result, the
role of the development banks is therefore more important than ever.
At present, FMO is firmly rooted in some 40 or more developing
countries and countries in transition. At the same time, it is
affirming its ties with the Dutch and European business communities.
Standard Chartered: Standard Chartered is one of the world's most
international banks, employing over 40,000 people, representing 80
nationalities, across its network. Standard Chartered operates in over
1,200 locations (including subsidiaries, associates and joint ventures)
in more than 50 countries in the Asia Pacific Region, South Asia, the
Middle East, Africa, the United Kingdom and the Americas.
Well-established in growth markets, Standard Chartered aims to be
the right partner for its customers combining deep local knowledge with
global capability. The bank is trusted across its network for its
standard of governance and corporate responsibility as well as its
commitment to making a difference in the communities in which it
operates.
Standard Chartered is named after two banks which merged in 1969.
They were originally known as the Standard Bank of British South Africa
and the Chartered Bank of India, Australia and China. Of the two banks,
the Chartered Bank is the older having been founded in 1853 following
the grant of a Royal Charter from Queen Victoria.
Recently, Standard Chartered Bank invested $140 million to lift its
Nigeria subsidiary, Standard Chartered Bank Nigeria towards meeting the
N25 billion minimum capitalisation for banks in Nigeria.
Union Bank London: The bank commenced operations in the city of
London in September 1983 as a branch of Union Bank of Nigeria Plc. In
February, 2005 it transformed into a full fledged subsidiary of Union
Bank. The incorporation of the UK subsidiary strategically positioned
the bank to offer banking services to its teeming customers as well as
correspondent services to its parent company, Nigerian banks and other
international banks worldwide.
HSBC: Headquartered in London, HSBC which prides itself as the
worldÃ?Â¢??s local bank, is one of the largest banking and financial
services organisations in the world. HSBC's international network
comprises over 9,800 offices in 77 countries and territories in Europe,
the Asia-Pacific region, the Americas, the Middle East and Africa.
With listings on the London, Hong Kong, New York, Paris and Bermuda
stock exchanges, shares in HSBC Holdings plc are held by nearly 200,000
shareholders in some 100 countries and territories. The shares are
traded on the New York Stock Exchange in the form of American
Depositary Receipts.
Through an international network linked by advanced technology,
including a rapidly growing e-commerce capability, HSBC provides a
comprehensive range of financial services: personal financial services;
commercial banking; corporate, investment banking and markets; private
banking; and other activities.
International Banker of The Year Nominees
Profiles in Excellence...
Andrew Alli: Andrew Alli is the Country Manager of the International
Finance Corporation (IFC), in Nigeria, a position which gives him
overall responsibility for IFC's activities in the country. In the
three and a half years that he has been in this position, IFC's
activities in Nigeria have quadrupled, making Nigeria IFC's most active
country in sub-Saharan Africa. Recently, IFC has invested is some
landmark projects in Nigeria, including MTN and Dangote Industries'
Obajana Cement project. IFC has also supported a number of banks, hotel
projects, as well as those in the general manufacturing sector.
Alli attended Kings College, Lagos before going to the United
Kingdom to study Electronics and Electrical Engineering. After
graduating top of his class, he went on to work for Coopers and Lybrand
in London as a management consultant. During this period, he qualified
as a Chartered Accountant before going to work in S.G. Warburg, one of
UK's leading investment banks. He joined IFC after obtaining an MBA
from INSEAD in France. While at IFC he worked on the oil and gas and
telecommunications sectors before taking up his position in Nigeria. A
very conservative person, Alli does not miss his holidays.
Adebayo Alade-loba: Adebayo Alade-Loba is Director, Global
Inves-tment Banking Group of Credit Suisse First Boston (CSFB) and
Director of CSFBÃ?Â¢??s Global Energy Group. Since joining CSFB from
the International Finance Corporation (IFC) in 1985, he has advised
clients including ExxonMobil, Shell, Total, Chevron, ENI, the Ghanaian
and Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation, BHP, etc., on transactions
with an aggregate value in excess of $20 billion on four continents. In
2005, Alade-Loba was CSFB team leader for the $4 billion Brass LNG
financial feasibility study; the Port Harcourt and Kaduna Refineries
privatisations and the landmark $600 million NNPC/ExxonMobil Satellite
Oil Fields project financing programme.
Nkosana Moyo: Nkosana Moyo, a member of the Management and
Investment Committees of Actis, is responsible for managing Actis
business in the Africa region. He joined Actis in January 2004 and is
based in London.
In his role as Managing Partner for Africa, Nkosana's
responsibilities include providing business leadership to the regional
fund team, formulation of business strategy for the region, deal
origination, fund raising and investor relationship management.
Prior to joining Actis, Moyo was with the International Finance
Corporation (IFC), in Washington DC. He also served as Zimbabwe's
Minister for Industry and Trade after a career in industry and commerce
as well as in financial services.
He has worked in senior positions in Africa, Europe as well as the
US. He has a Ph.D in physics from Imperial College and an MBA from
Cranfield School of Management. He is a World Economic Forum Global
Leader of Tomorrow (GLT), and an Eisenhower Fellow.
Menachem Katz: Mr Menachem Katz, an Isreali, is the Director in
charge of Africa in the International Monetary Fund, (IMF) and with
direct responsibility for Nigeria. Every year, he leads an IMF team to
Nigeria to conduct Article IV consultation discussions and review
economic performance of the country with senior government officials.
He also palyed a crucial role in the achievement of Policy Support
Instrument which Nigeria got from the IMF prior to the nationÃ?Â¢??s
debt relief. His team usually meets with President Olusegun Obasanjo;
the Minister of Finance, Dr. (Mrs.) Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala; the Governor
of the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), Professor Charles Soludo; other
senior officials of the government; representatives of the private
sector, including the banking industry; and members of civil society
and the international community.
Transactions of The Year Nominees
Profiles in Excellence...
Federal ministry of Finance/NigeriaÃ?Â¢??s $36 billion Debt
buy-back: In an unprecedented manner, the representatives of the Paris
Club creditor countries had met October 2005 and agreed with the
representatives of the Federal government on a comprehensive treatment
of its debt. This agreement to implement the debt treatment framework
for Nigeria announced by the Paris Club was announced on 29 June 2005.
After several years of negotiations, Nigeria was granted
cancellation of $18 billion of its eligible debt of over $30 billion
after a commitment to pay the Paris Club $12.4 billion, This figure
comprises $6.3 billion in arrears plus another $6.1 billion for a debt
buy-back operation.
The Debt Management Office (DMO), also played a major role in
sorting out the nitty gritty of the debts. The DMO was established in
2000 to centrally coordinate the management of NigeriaÃ?Â¢??s debt,
which was hitherto being done by myriad establishments in an
uncoordinated fashion. This diffused debt management strategy had led
to inefficiencies.

Babatunde Jose: Dr. Ismail Babatunde Jose, veteran journalist and

His over three decades of meritorious service in the media actually
began by happenstance-the economic recession occasioned by the Second
World War. The young Jose had to discontinue his education at
SaviourÃ?Â¢??s High School, Oko-Awo, Lagos, because his father could no
longer cope with rising school fees. His earnings had dropped
drastically. So it was decided that Babatunde must start work
immediately to augment the family income. He became a teacher, but
taught for only a day and left because he could not manage the chaotic
classroom populated by little children. At JoseÃ?Â¢??s fatherÃ?Â¢??s
instance, a note from Sir Adeyemo Alakija, who was then Chairman of
Daily Times was all he needed to resume work under Ayodele Lijadu, who
was then the Daily Times editor. The rest is history
Wole Soyinka: Akinwande Oluwole Soyinka is NigeriaÃ?Â¢??s foremost
playwright. He is also poet, novelist, and critic. He received the
Nobel Prize for Literature in 1986.
Soyinka attended Government College and University College in Ibadan
before graduating in English in 1958 from the University of Leeds, in
England. Upon his return to Nigeria, he founded a national theatre, The
Masks (later the Orisun Theatre), and wrote his first important play, A
Dance of the Forests, for the Nigerian independence celebrations. The
play satirizes the fledgling nation by stripping it of romantic legend
and by showing that the present is no more a golden age than the past.
Soyinka fuses Western elements with subject matter and dramatic
techniques deeply rooted in Yoruba folklore and religion. He uses
symbolism, flashback, and ingenious plotting to create a rich dramatic
structure. His works exhibit humour and fine poetic style. He also has
a gift for irony and satire, accurately matching the language of
complex characters to their social position and moral qualities.
His autobiography, Ake: The Years of Childhood, was published in
1981 and a companion piece, Isara: A Voyage Around Essay, in 1989.
Emeka Anyaoku: Eleazar Chukwuemeka Anyaoku was born on January 18,
1933 in Obosi, in Anambra State. He attended the Merchants of Light
School in Oba and as a College Scholar, the University College of
Ibadan, at the time a college of the University of London. He obtained
an honours degree in Classics. Anyaoku later attended specialist
courses in Britain and France.
In 1959, Anyaoku joined the Commonwealth Development Corporation.
Following Nigeria's independence, he was invited to join his country's
diplomatic service and, in 1963, was posted to Nigeria's Permanent
Mission to the United Nations in New York. His commitment to the
concept of a common humanity has roots both in traditional African and
Western philosophies, and has grown through 30 years of international
service within and beyond the Commonwealth.
He became the third Commonwealth Secretary-General in July 1990, at
a time when East-West and North-South relationships were undergoing
fundamental change. Among his early tasks was to assist Commonwealth
Heads of Government on a reappraisal of the future role of the
association, giving it new strategic directions: to help countries
build strong democracies guaranteeing individual liberty and human
rights, while at the same time assisting in the major task of their
sustainable development.
The hallmarks of Anyaoku's Secretary-Generalship have been his
execution of that mandate and his remoulding of the Commonwealth
Secretariat so that it could undertake these new duties efficiently and
effectively. In 1966, shortly after the establishment of the
Commonwealth Secretariat, he was seconded to the new organisation at
the request of the first Secretary-General, Arnold Smith of Canada, as
Assistant Director of International Affairs, later becoming Director
and, in 1975, Assistant Secretary-General. In 1977, Commonwealth
governments elected him Deputy Secretary-General with responsibility
for international affairs and the Secretariat's administration. The
Shagari administration in 1983 appointed Chief Anyaoku as Nigeria's
Foreign Minister.
Chinua Achebe: Albert Chinualumogu Achebe was born to Isaiah Okafor,
a Christian churchman, and Janet N. Achebe in November 16, 1930 in
Ogidi, South-Eastern Nigeria. He married Christie Chinwe Okoli,
September 10, 1961, and now has four children: Chinelo, Ikechukwu,
Chidi, and Nwando. He attended Government College, Umuahia from 1944 to
1947 and University College in Ibadan from 1948 to 1953. He then got a
B.A. from London University in 1953 and studied broadcasting at the
British Broadcasting Corp. in London in 1956.
Since the 1950's, Nigeria has witnessed "the flourishing of a new
literature which has drawn sustanence from both traditional oral
literature and from the present and rapidly changing society," writes
Margaret Laurence in her book Long Drums and Cannons: Nigerian
Dramatists and Novelists. Chinua Achebe was one of the founders of this
new literature, and over the years many critics have come to consider
him the finest of the Nigerian novelists. His acheivement, however, has
not been limited to his continent. He is considered by many to be one
of the best novelists now writing in the English language.
Unlike some African writers struggling for acceptance among
contemporary English-language novelists, Achebe has been able to avoid
imitating the trends in English literature. Rejecting the European
notion "that art should be accountable to no one, and [needs] to
justify itself to nobody," as he puts it in his book of essays, Morning
Yet on Creation Day, Achebe has embraced the idea at the heart of the
African oral tradition: that "art is, and always was, at the service of
man. Our ancestors created their myths and told their stories for a
human purpose." For this reason, Achebe believes that "any good story,
any good novel, should have a message, should have a purpose."
Achebe's feel for the African context has influenced his aesthetic of the novel as well as the technical aspects of his work.
His most celebrated novel, Things Fall Apart has sold million of copies and has been translated into many languages.

name. He extended his radical worldview nationwide through his
activities in the National Association of Nigerian Students (NANS).
A student of Crop Science in his university studies, Chima surprised
the world in his catholic mastery of literally all subjects. He rose
through the ranks in Civil Liberties Organisation (CLO) before ending
up as the Executive Director of the leading rights group.
Judge of the Year Award Nominees
Justice Muhammadu Uwais: Justice Muhammadu Uwais, the Chief Justice
of Nigeria (CJN) is reputed for maintaining the integrity of the
judiciary in the face of awesome obstacles and constraints. He is the
longest serving CJN and is expected to retire this year. He was
appointed into the apex court in 1982. At the Supreme Court, Uwais
represents an epitome of integrity and anti-corruption though some
people under the aegis of Derivation Front tried unsuccessfully to
stain his apparel. It would be recalled that the CJN recently returned
about N5 billion to the nationÃ?Â¢??s purse being unspent money in
respect of the revenue allocated to the judiciary in the 2005 budget.
President Olusegun Obasanjo mentioned UwaisÃ?Â¢?? noble act to the
National Assembly while presenting the 2006 budget to the lawmakers.
Uwais also spoke out recently against executive lawlessness at the
All Nigerian Judges Conference. He condemned the brazen disrespect for
court decisions and he said this before President Obasanjo.
Justice Sylvanus Nsofor: Appeal Court Justice Sylvanus Nsofor has
delivered so many judgements but his dissenting judgement on the 2003
election petition filed by the All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP) in the
presidential candidates, Major-General Muhammadu Buhari (rtd), stood
out in the courage and bravery behind the pronouncement. He disagreed
with his learned colleagues who okayed the election in spite of their
voiding of the Ogun State presidential election results saying that the
election was fraught with irregularities and should not stand.
He therefore nullified the victory of President Obasanjo based on
that 2003 election results. Justice Nsofor had his say but the majority
justices of the court had their way. With that judgement however,
Justice Nsofor seemed to have written his name in gold. He has entered
the pantheon of highly respected courageous justices in the land like
Justice Kayode Esho (rtd).
Justice Binta Murtala Nyako: Justice Binta Murtala Nyako is of the
Federal High Court, Abuja. She delivered a landmark judgement in the
Conference of Nigeria Political Parties (CNPP) versus the
Inspector-General of Police case over the issue of police permit for
public procession. The question for determination was whether given the
colonial control of public procession at a point there was no
constitution and with a constitution now in place guaranteeing freedom
of movement and association, whether there is any need for police
permit for people to hold public procession. It was an interesting
case. Justice Murtala Nyako ruled that any aspect of the Public Order
Act was unconstitutional.
She also handled the money laundering case preferred by the Economic
and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) against the former
Inspector-General of Police, Mr. Tafa Balogun. Balogun is at present
serving a jail term over his corrupt practices.
Justice Bunmi Oyewole: Justice Bunmi Oyewole has handled very
important cases bordering on anti-corruption and through his
judgements, he has not only shown that he is incorruptible, his rulings
has also helped to push further the governmentÃ?Â¢??s anti-corruption
crusade. Oyewole read law at the Obafemi Awolowo University, Ile-Ife,
and was appointed into the Lagos High Court, Ikeja Division by the
Lagos State Governor, Senator Bola Ahmed Tinubu. Justice Oyewole is of
the radical bend and this has shown in his judgements. He is today the
busiest anti-corruption judge and has given landmark decisions in that
area. Among the judgements he has delivered included the Economic and
Financial Crimes Corruption (EFCC) versus Anajemba, EFCC versus Nwude
and Attorney-General of Lagos State versus Major Hamza Al-Mustapha,
Bamaiyi and others.
Justice Niki Tobi: Supreme Court Justice Niki Tobi was chairman of
the National Political Reforms Conference (NPRC) which ended last
August in Abuja.
Tobi from Delta State has made tremendous impacts on the bench as a
courageous judge having delivered some landmark judgements. But he has
also handled many political assignments for the government at one point
or another. Apart from the NPRC job, Tobi was also Chairman of the
review committee set up by the late General Sani Abacha military junta
to take another look at the outcome of the 1994/95 Constitutional
Conference in Abuja.
Airline of The Year Nominees
Profiles in Excellence...
Aero Contractors: Aero Contractors of Nigeria (ACN) has been one of
the dominant and visibe names in the Nigerian aviation industry since
1959. Today ACN offers a complete range of aviation services, operating
both fixed wing and helicopter services as well as extensive technical
maintenance and overhaul support.
It was formed in 1959; officially registered in Nigeria in 1960 and,
at that time, wholly owned by Schreiner Airways B.V. of the
Netherlands. It became a company with an initial 40 per cent Nigerian
holding in 1973 and subsequently 60 per cent in 1976, anticipating the
