requirements of the Indigenization Decree.
In January 2004, Schreiner Airways was bought by Canadian Helicopter
Corporation (C.H.C.) which acquired 40 per cent holding of while the 60
per cent majority share remained within the Ibru family.
The company started out in the early 1960s as an air charter
operator. Charters in those days were varied both in client and scope.
It provided charters to church missions to spread the 'Word' throughout
the country and to Niger Pools to transport betting coupons to Lagos.
It later diversified into the embryonic oil industry, particularly in
the D

Original_Search_Title: Salone to Export Cassava to Nigeria
Headline: Sierra Leone: Salone to Export Cassava to Nigeria
Body_Text:
Freetown — Posseh Akinola of Christian Farmers Cassava Initiative (CFCI)
Saturday announced that her organization would start exporting cassava
to Nigeria.
Akinola who was speaking at a press conference said her
organization, which is a business organization, has sensitized
thousands of farmers' nationwide that surplus cassava can be made into
chips and exported to Nigeria.
" You can have more than a thousand products out of cassava
including fuel which is presently making life for people in Nigeria
easy," she said and added that they are working with other financial
organizations.
She maintained that their aim is to earn money, as well as to feed the people of Sierra Leone.
Akinola said their program is open to all religious denominations
and they are complementing President Kabbah's food security dream ahead
of 2007.
International cassava commercialization expert, Boma Anga who
doubles as CFCI's Consultant said his organization's dream would not be
achieved without the partnership of the farmers.
"Our aim is to expand the cassava production in Sierra Leone by 2007
we want Sierra Leone to be one of the most competitive producers of
cassava in the world," he assured and added that CFCI is here to stay.
Anga said the short, mid and long-term plans for food security would be achieved in the country with variety of food to feed.

Original_Search_Title: Elephants Invade Maize Fields in Mpika
Headline: Zambia: Elephants Invade Maize Fields in Mpika
Body_Text:
Mpika — HERDS of elephants have invaded maize and cassava fields in Mukungule chiefdom in Mpika district of Northern Province.
One of the affected people, Emelda Mulenga of Kabungo village said herds of elephants had eaten their crops.
"Jumbos have left the park and are destroying our crops and plunging
us into further poverty" complained Mulenga. Mulenga, who said the most
affected village was Kabungo, appealed to government to send game
scouts to get rid of the animals before crops are entirely destroyed.
Mukungule chiefdom is 92 kilometres north of Mpika town and about 12
kilometres from North Luangwa National Park. Last year, villagers in
Chiawa complained that elephants had destroyed crops in the area.
Two people, Severina Nyangu and Sevelia Loti, were also killed by elephants.
Chieftainess Chiawa attributed the rise in the number of elephant
attacks to the sharing of borders with Zimbabwe National Park and the
partial drought.
She complained that government had not done enough to curb the elephants from attacking people.
Chieftainess Chiawa said the elephants had become a menace and were
aggravating the hunger situation as they were destroying homes and
eating people's food.
In May, government introduced elephant sport hunting in Chiawa,
Rufunsa and Lupande Game Management Areas, which was aimed at
benefiting Zambians.
Former tourism minister, Patrick Kalifungwa said the primary concern
was to fight the perceived injustices elephants had inflicted on people
through the destruction of crops and human life.
"The government has issued a statutory instrument which provides for elephant sport hunting in Zambia.
The commercial value of elephants is limited to eco-tourism, which
is seasonal and not well developed to benefit the local community.
Elephant sport hunting based on a sustainable quota, will provide
for conservation of species and benefit the local community. When
people are given the licences to kill the elephants, half the money
will go to the community,"Kalifungwa said.
"The areas of hunting were selected by Zambia Wildlife Authority and
other Non-Governmental Organisations. The ministry will ensure that 15
kilogrammes and 1.5 metre-length tusks are adhered to."
Kalifungwa said Zambia's submission to the Convention on
International Trade in Endangered Species for a quota of 40 tusks had
been approved.
He said since animal problems were mainly caused by elephants, hunting was a cost-effective way of maximising economic returns.
"The voluntary quota will have no effect on the African elephant
population in Zambia. The ministry has strengthened monitoring of
illegal killing of elephants in the selected areas,"he said. Elephant
sporting is utilised in nations such as Botswana, Namibia, Tanzania and
Mozambique.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Won't Stay Beyond 2007 - Aide
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Won't Stay Beyond 2007 - Aide
Body_Text:
Asaba — An aide to president Olusegun Obasanjo, and the chairman, cassava
export project, South-south, Chief Ned Nwoko has said President
Obasanjo has no intention to stay in office beyond 2007.
He said Obasanjo has requested in Abuja to form a group to propagate his ideals long after he has left office.
Briefing newsmen after the launching of a group tagged "legacy",
Nwoko told journalists that legacy group was the brain-child of the
President, adding that the group had a similar thinking like the
President .
He stressed that the ideals of the group was the provision of good
leadership, noting that legacy group "want credible and accountable
leadership, that will provide food, shelter and employment to the
people"
Nwoko hinted that if President Olusegun Obasanjo had intention of
extending his tenure, he would not have been planning to form a group
that would outlive his administration.
The presidential aide assured that Obasanjo would not contest
election in 2007, praying that Nigerians should allow another person
the opportunity to carry on the good works of the president.
On the issue of power shift, he called on the leadership of the
Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) to make its position known on the
controversial issue. He also added that various criteria must be in
place before leaving office, one of which he said was good leadership.
While also communicating on the various reform programmes of
President Obasanjo, the CEP chairman stressed that the reforms put in
place would yield truthful and required results in the next few years.

Original_Search_Title: Benue State At 30: the People And Their Struggles
Headline: Nigeria: Benue State At 30: the People And Their Struggles
Body_Text:
Lagos — Thirty years ago, General Murtala Mohammed created Benue State and
six others in a major political proclamation that shaped the
geo-political map of Nigeria. It was indeed, a bold and courageous
action designed to address the long standing agitation of northern and
southern minorities to take their destinies into their own hands.
Just like a man of thirty years, Benue State has clocked three
decade since it was created on the 3/2/1976 by the Late General Murtala
Mohammed. Right from birth, Benue State and some key players of benue
politics have continued to be as controversial as those who had
championed the cause of its' creation. Most political analysts have
come to associate the state with crisis management. The attitude of
some of the political elites of the state also gives the impression
that the state is a hotbed of petition writing and blackmail.
Different schools of thought have been advancing reasons for the
state's under-development. Prominent amongst these reasons is that of
inter-ethnic rivalry among the major ethnic divide in the state.
An objective assessment of Benue State administrations since its
creation has to take into account, the circumstances that led to the
ascendancy to power of successive administrations in the state. Thus,
from Major General Abdullahi Shelleng to the incumbent Dr. George
Akume, the history of Benue State has been one characterised and
periodised into significant environmental events that had at different
intervals prevailed in Nigeria.
Many people argued that these unpleasant situations helped in
strangulating and crippling the state at infancy as they further
contend that, one can not just give birth to a child and abandon the
child at infancy.
This was the deplorable situation in Benue State when Late General
Murtala Mohammed appointed then Col. Abdullahi Shelleng as the governor
of the state in February, 1976. On arrival Shelleng met a hostile
environment and climate. He was in a state of dilemma on where to begin.
He had to contend with the problem of setting up an administrative
machinery for the State; he had to justify his posting to a people who
were growingly disenchanted with their relationship with the Federal
Government vis-Ã -vis state capital, which was Jos. He also had to
grapple with the issue of providing infrastructure for the state as
well as assessing the number of Benue assets in Lagos, Kaduna, Jos and
Kwara States respectively.
To Shelleng's credit he embarked on a massive construction network
to provide accommodation for the teeming members of the civil servants
in the state. Abdullahi Shelleng also established the Murtala College
of Arts, Science and Technology, the A.T.C at K/Ala and the School of
Basic Studies at Ugbokolo.
He further conceived the idea of Benue Brewery to be located at
Makurdi, Idah Sanitary Ware Industry to be located at Idah, and the
Burnt Brick Industry to be located at Otukpo. Up to the time Shelleng
left the state in 1978, the roads within the metropolis were however
left un-tarred.
Although most of us have in the past been oblivious of his
achievement, to be fair to Shelleng, he tried to correct the imbalance
within the civil service structure.
One serious mistake associated with Abdullahi Shelleng was that most
of the establishments he opened operated on temporary sites. This
situation is still affecting institutions like the Benue Polytechnic
Ugbokolo, A.T.C. Kastina/Ala, and the School of Basis Studies, Makurdi.
Another failure associated with the Shelleng era is that, he brought
down the mini Makurdi Stadium, which was in perfect condition before he
arrived and never worked towards reactivating it before he left. This
unpleasant situation left Makurdi the state capital without a good
stadium during his tenure.
Shelleng's attitude in destroying the old stadium amounted to a
waste of public fund and energy. In a nutshell, Shelleng's
administration in the state did not last long enough for him to see
some of his projects through. The second military personnel who headed
the state administration was Air commodore Adebayo Lawal.
Bayo Lawal came here on a specific assignment. His achievements or
failures should therefore take into cognisance the background heralding
his arrival. It is interesting to note that Nigeria was preparing to
handover power to the civilians and all military officers who had had
the intention to remain within the armed forces were directed to
withdraw for military administrators.
This ushered in the arrival of Bayo Lawal who more or less came to
the state to oversee the peaceful transition from military rule to
civil rule. It is difficult to point out the specific project which
Bayo Lawal undertook because of the short period he stayed in the state
as Military Administrator, but it is to his credit that he succeeded in
achieving the primary objectives for which he was assigned to perform.
Lawal preserved peace during the electioneering campaigns.
There was no problem of open confrontation between parties that
contested election in the state. After election, Bayo Lawal handed over
power to the NPN candidate Mr. A.A. Aku on the 1st October, 1979.
The unique opportunities, which Aper Aku had were excessively high,
as a member of the ruling NPN he had the presidents' ear. Again his
former anti-corruption crusade posture against late Joseph Gomwalk, the
then Governor of Benue Plateau State, provided a veritable platform for
massive support by the people.
Haven waged a serious legal battle against Gomwalk for his failures
to develop Benue Plateau, people believed that Mr. Aper Aku would be
mindful of whatever policy direction his administration was bound to
initiate because of his past antecedents as a social crusader. Due to
the publicity he generated in the Nigeria Media, all eyes were on him
to see how effective he would perform bearing in mind his past
criticism on a governor's non-performance.
Right from the day Aku assumed power in Benue State, he had to
contend with the numerous problems confronting a people who had had a
long history of deprivation, degredation, and manipulation in the hands
of privileged Nigerians who, in the past had access to power. For the
simple fact that the lower Benue region happened to be located within a
geographical climate inhabited by the so called minority groups, it
people suffered an untold hardship, which Aku came to redress. He had a
vision and a mission to accomplish.
Aku was ambitious and eager to develop the state. He started fairly
well when he stopped the construction of a medical centre at Apir and
directed that instead of investing on a big project in one location.
Seven Cottage hospitals be constructed at Okpoga, Vandeikya,
Kastina/Ala. Bassa and other areas.
Although his reasons to penetrate the local government areas were
laudable, people still accused him of killing a project that was self
financing and which could have helped solve the health problems of the
state.
In terms of industrialization. Aku moved fast to construct the Benue
Brewery. Benro Packaging, Benue Bottling Company, Lobi Bank, Ber-Agbum
Fish Farm and Ikogen Cattle Ranch, where they were importing cows for
breeding. He also moved fast to construct Benue International Hotel in
Makurdi.
He signed the contract for the construction of Makurdi International
Market and was also about to establish a flour mill in Makurdi. He
constructed some of the main roads in Makurdi township and provided the
existing street lights through direct labour at a cost of about 1.2
million naira.
Aku awarded the contract for the Art Council Complex, which was
later abandoned at a stage. We also started work on the main bowl of
Makurdi stadium. This was also abandoned at a stage. He obtained a
foreign loan and started construction work on the Taraku Vegetable
Processing Industry at Taraku.
Aku established two Advance Teachers' Colleges at Oju and Makurdi.
He also was the first to establish a University of Technology in
Makurdi. He embarked on massive establishment of Government Secondary
Schools in the state. Aku's efforts in the education sector have seen
the emergence and expansion of higher institution in the state.
Brigadier J.A Kpera's regime came at a period the state was almost
at the verge of collapse as civil servants salaries were far back in
arrears, while destructive politicking threatened the corporate
existence of Benue State and indeed Nigeria.
The greatest achievement of Kpera's administration was the peace,
which heralded his arrival. He also constructed the temporary
Mac-Carthy Stadium through direct labour. He further tarred a street in
Wurukum and another one at Lobi Quarters as well as the assembly
village road.
Governor J.D Jang's ascendancy to power heralded a new horizon in
Benue State. He gave much attention to the opening of rural roads and
dualization of entry points into the state capital, Makurdi. Jang gave
the general hospital Makurdi a massive face lift. To tackle the rural
development policy of his administration he established the Directorate
of Food, Road and Rural Infrastructure, which has been changed to BERDA.
After the exit of David Jang, Benue State went through series of
transitional crises as most of the governors posted to the state were
either removed or transferred when they were trying to settle down. As
such from Yohanna Madaki, Ishaya Bakut and Idris Garba no major
development took place in the state. However, Idris Garba's region
commissioned the modern market and introduced the Benue Links company
in Benue. The long period of transitional crisis greatly hampared the
development of the state.
However, the setback occasioned by the transitioned crises earlier
mentioned was compensated by the arrival of then Lt. Col Fidelis Makka
in 1989 who brought in dynamism and transparency in the administration
of the state. Makka's commitment to the development of Benue State was
so strong that any obstacle on his way was booted out in a roughless
manner. Indeed, the feeling in the state is that Aku, Makka and Akume
are the architect of modern Benue State. Like a dragon, Makka
systematically set for himself the task of completing most of the
abandoned projects initiated by the Aku civilian administration. He
re-designed and completed the Aper Aku Stadium, Staff Development
centre and built the following: IBB Square, Fidelis Makka Library,
Pauline Makka women centre, Day care and resurfacing of some township
roads like Gondo, Aluor and Stadium roads among others. The Otobi water
works, which was started by the first civilian Northern Regional
Government, also received attention . Makka also set up a committee
head by late ambassador Edwin Ogbu to work out modalities for the
establishment of the Benue State University and signed the law
establishing it by edict No 3 of 1991. The rural electricity project
started by Aper Aku was pursued with vigour. He also completed the six
general hospitals, which the Aper Aku administration had started.
Moses Adasu, the second civilian governor of the state came with an
aggressive administrative posture and lobbying to attract federal
presence. As the first catholic priest to venture into partisan
politics, he had the ears of the Nigerian leadership. Adasu could not
translate his agenda into practical reality because he stayed for only
two years. Even then, he speedily assembled the team of administrators
and academics that started the Benue State University. He built the
Tarka foundation, which up to date, still has its mandate in mystery.
Other projects embarked upon by Rev. Adasu included; BENCO roof tile,
and the K/Ala fruit juice.
Like the previous transitional period, which the state had to
contend with after Rev, Adasu, the state entered yet another
transitional period when Isa Kontagora took over. Kontagora was
primarily concerned with creating and generating revenue and wealth for
the state. He therefore conceived the idea of a Benue Plaza and
Governor's lodge all in Abuja as well as the Benue shopping plaza in
Kaduna. He built the Maimuna Kontagora hostel and completed a wing of
the Art council theatre in Makurdi. Dominic Oneya who took over from
him built the Assembly village and conceived the idea of a fertilizer
blending plant for the state.
Dr. George Akume took over the leadership of the state in 1999 after
a prolonged period of military rule. Tension among neighbouring
communities was high and conflict soon ensued. He used diplomacy to
cool down raised nerves. He had to contend with a major crisis that
threatened the corporate existence of the state. Being a product of the
system, he decided to tackle poverty on five major fronts. These
include: Human development, infrastructure provision, stoppage of
capita flight, politics of inclusion, Agriculture and industrialization.
The human Development concept of the Akume administration hinges on
his firm commitment to workers welfare by prompt payment of workers
salaries and allowances. Through this method the apparatus of
governance has not been disrupted. The academic calendar remains stable
and consistent. With the free flow of salaries the state economy has
been stable over the past six years. Liberalization of contract policy
has also seen the circulation of money within the state and stopped
capita flight. In the area of industrialisation Governor George Akume
has established as plastic industry, cassava processing factory,
fertilizer blending factor, two fruit juice factories. Work is on-going
on Tomato pury, mango juice and Igumale Cement Company. Work is to
commence on an Ethanol plant, which when completed will produce starch,
oil and cassava floor. Some of the industries which ware dead have been
revamped like the Agro-millers, Taraku mills and Otukpo Burnt Bricks.
Over 640 kilometres of r ural roads are under construction as the
government has also spent over N2.6 billion on ecological projects in
the three senatorial zones. Work on the Benue State Television project
has reached an advance stage of completion. The other important aspect
of the Akume administration is the upgrading of facilitates at the
Benue State University with the building of a college of medical
sciences and a Teaching hospital. Eight new general hospitals have also
been constructed. His government introduced rural radio by the
establishment of Radio Benue sub-stations at Katsina-Ala and Idekpa. He
built the government school Makurdi, school of Health Technology Agasha
and introduced mechanised agriculture in the State. The State under
Akume is running one of the most successful transport systems in
Nigeria with the introduction of the modern marcopolo buses on the
fleet of Benue Links Company. The State government's urban renewal
programme has seen to the opening and construction of over 45
kilometres of township roads, which were handled by PW Nig. Ltd. Three
major housing projects have being undertaken by the George Akume
government. These are: Judges Quarters, owner occupier estate and the
evergreen housing project. These project are designed to ease
accommodate problem in the state capital. The government has entered
into a joint partnership with the Federal government to build a new
water works for Makurdi. The project, which is intended to increase and
expand the distribution of potable water to Makurdi town, will also
cover other neighbouring communities.
To this end, the National Planning Commission in conjunction with
DFID, USAID and the commonwealth rated Benue State the 6th best out of
the 36 states of the federation in terms of service delivery and good
governance.
Benue state has no doubt come a long way. It may not have reached
its peak in its quest to develop but there are clear signs that within
a short while the state will be the envy of many. There is still room
for improvement because at the moment the absence of adequate
electricity in the state and bad rural roads is hampering the rapid
development of the state. Above all, there is need for change of
attitude by the people who must embrace Akume's developmental
initiative by taking the ample opportunities the governor has created.
Attitudinal change is very important if the state is to be
developed. An Ibo man who resides outside his state would always strive
to invest in his home state but a Tiv man would prefer to garner money
from Benue and invest elsewhere. If New York is relocated to Benue from
USA and the attitude of the people towards business remains
anti-progress the state will stagnet. If Akume is running the most
benevolent administration and has set-up high profile industries but
the attitude of the people towards business remain the same,
development will still elude the state.
Tyodzua Atim is a media Aide to the Governor of Benue State

Original_Search_Title: 'NDDC Master-Plan 'Ll Curb Restiveness in N/Delta'
Headline: Nigeria: 'NDDC Master-Plan 'Ll Curb Restiveness in N/Delta'
Body_Text:
Owerri — Chairman of the Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC),
Ambassador Sam Edem has said the commission has concluded arrangements
to start the implementation of her 15 year master-plan, which is mainly
hinged on reducing the spate of youth restiveness in the Niger Delta
region.
Briefing newsmen in Isiebu, Isiala Mbano Local Government Area of
Imo State shortly after inspecting on-going NDDC projects in the State,
Amb. Edem said the first phase of the master-plan would mainly
concentrate on Agriculture, Education, Health and Skill Acquisition.
He expressed dismay that a lot of graduates have constituted
themselves as nuisance in the region because of lack of job, stressing
therefore that the Commission was ready to take steps aimed at
providing job opportunities for a greater percentage of them.
"Idleness is the devil's workshop, if we can provide employment for
a good percentage of the youths, provide training through skill
acquisition for them, youth restiveness would then, be a thing of the
past in the region', he said.
He specifically said that Agriculture was a major pivot of the new
NDDC, explaining that easy employment could be created through the
sector. " We can easily provide massive employment through the sector.
We are going in to cassava and rice farming. From planting , harvesting
, processing and marketing, people are going to be deeply involved", he
said adding that in riverine areas, like Bayelsa State, the Commission
was considering going into aqua-culture.
The chairman, who inspected a massive Hostel site at Imo State
University, Owerri, the NDDC cassava farm project in Obiakpu, Egbema,
Umudike 11 Comprehensive Secondary School project, Asa/Obile water
scheme and electricity project and the NDDC cassava project at the
College of Agriculture, Umuagwo used the forum to call for more funding
of the commission.
He said considering the quantum of problems facing the commission,
it had become imperative for the Presidency to approve more funds for
them.
While noting that the nine Niger Delta States were fairing better
under President Olusegun Obasanjo's administration, Edem as they
collect close to thirty billion naira monthly, Edem noted that with
patience, determination and commitment, the problems comfronting the
region would soon be a thing of the past.
On the allegation that the Commission was involved in a double
contract deal,in the Orji--Isiebu--Okohia road construction project,
having being completed by the Imo State Govenrment, Edem said while the
state government surface dressed the raod, the NDDC was asphalting the
road.
"We deserve a commendation, there's nothing like double contract in
this case", Edem who paid a courtesy call on Eze Emmanuel Njemanze of
Owerri earlier said.

Original_Search_Title: Encouraging Literature in Our Schools
Headline: Nigeria: Encouraging Literature in Our Schools
Body_Text:
Lagos — O n August 8, 2004, the G-15 hosted Chimamanda Adichie, the
writer and award winner of the book "Purple Hibiscus". In that meeting
Mcphillips Nwachukwu reported in the Vanguard that these efforts "will
go a long way in establishing the government's seriousness in
propagating Nigeria's positive image at home and abroad. Hopefully, it
will trigger a literary awareness campaign as well as contribute in no
small measure to the development of the literary and publishing
industry in Nigeria, an area of our national life that is in need of
re-awakening.
Of course, it will also send a positive signal to Nigerians in the
Diaspora, and present a positive role model to young ones, especially
our female folk whose contributions to society are still marginal
partly because of ignorance and lack of opportunities."
Many of the youngsters today in Nigeria have the great capacity to
write; in school quite a number enjoy reading; some engage themselves
in writing something which they hope others might enjoy- more often
some of them do not receive the necessary encouragement they need in
order to become good writers. Though, schools /organisations and
companies do well to encourage a large number of them to write in
competitions they organise. In schools, some of them enroll in clubs as
part of the co-curricular programmes; some other organisations set up
reading and writing clubs.
I hadn't finished the book when I commented on the style: Like
Achebe's! I enjoyed Achebe's books and I had taken time to go through
his "Things Fall Apart" many times. Like many of those who grew up in
the cities like Lagos, I am always enthused with Nigeria's traditional
settings and cultures. I remember when I travelled in the early
eighties to my village, I was very happy to see a farmland! I had seen
farmlands on TV-but there they were- cassava, cocoyam farms! With these
were other surprises like the " Obi" where the "Umunnas", the extended
family, gathered for meetings,. I had seen nothing of such in Lagos .
It can be observed from Amanda's short stories and book that she is
an immensely talented writer, capable of engaging the reader all
through to the end of her works. Her critic has to understand her
"Purple Hibiscus" as just one more fiction. In her interviews, I quite
agree with her that many people have to hold unto what they have - our
treasures - our languages, cultures and traditions. They are ever rich
and good. We need people who will present to others this richness - in
its undiluted way and fashion showing that what we have and give is not
second class. Achebe proved that by writing his first novel. Just today
I had visited Falomo shopping complex in Lagos, to buy some books - and
there on display were artworks from most parts of Nigeria . Right there
were many Europeans as well. The thoughts that came to my mind were:
Could it be that these Europeans value our own culture more than us? To
what extent do we really value what we have? Well, many Nigerian buy up
these clothes and artworks, but how many care to ask questions about
their origin and significance?
Everyday we are presented with a panorama of our history and culture
and Adichie has answered this very often in many of her interviews; we
cannot certainly part with them; they are not foregone issues! All the
things that happened before and after independence seem to interlace;
precipitating into a mesh; though complex but at the same time needs
the patience and the critical minds of its citizenry to observe the
nature of all the rings and shapes it has hitherto taken up and the
trend it would likely transform into in the immediate future. In the
light of these, the young, and the not so young, who are proficient in
writing would come up with classics for Nigerian and the world to
consume.
Herbert Ekwe Ekwe of the centre for cross-cultural studies, Dakar
wrote recently that many African literary critics have been involved in
a spirited debate in the past decade on how soon the African novel that
interrogates the salient features of the contemporary African
sociopolitics would emerge, of which he affirmed that a distinguished
African scholar said it was a sociopolitics of "Age of pestilence".
Seemingly observed as age of ruthless African regimes; of one party
rule; World Bank and IMF imposition of "SAP" on Africa, leading to
further "stomach readjustment" and poverty.
Adichie's "Purple Hibiscus" rightly shows these and I guess her next
novel would depict them even much more. The neo-colonial era and the
hopes it affords to the African people with Nigeria in focus, has a lot
to be put down in black and white. Adichie's style of writing and her
great capacity of depicting well and placing the characters in her
books, at the easy understanding of her readers have so much to be
cherished.

Original_Search_Title: Fadama II Project: Farmers Receive N48m to Boost Agriculture
Headline: Nigeria: Fadama II Project: Farmers Receive N48m to Boost Agriculture
Body_Text:
Lagos — IMO State Fadama II Project, a World Bank-assisted agricultural
project, has disbursed N23 million to some Fadama user groups and
community associations in Imo since it commenced operations in May,
2004 with another N25 million to be disbursed this year.
Programme co-ordinator, Mr. Nnaemeka Anyanwu who stated this
recently during a meeting of Heads of parastatals in the State Ministry
of Agriculture and Natural Resources with the Commissioner, Mr.
Soronnadi Njoku, also said that no fewer than 10,000 people in the
state had been empowered through the intervention of the project.
Besides, Mr. Anyanwu also announced that another tranche of N25
million would be given out to Fadama-User groups in the first quarter
of this year.
The Coordinator who was addressing his colleagues on the
achievements of his office, said that they had also purchased about 40
motor-cycles, two Nissen Double cabin vehicles, as well as, furnished
offices in the 11 local government areas where it is currently
operating.
He gave an assurance that Imo State, which is the only state in the
South East, where Fadama II project is operational, would reap maximum
benefit from the world bank assisted project.
He therefore enjoined the benefitting communities to make the most use of the financial disbursements to them.
Addressing the meeting also, Ag. General Manager of Adapalm,
foremost oil palm fruit producing company, Chief Don Okwu said that the
company was on the road to recovery.
He said that for the first time in several years, the company was
able to meet its financial obligations and still pay salaries in full
before December 24, last year.
Chief Okwu who noted that within the past one year, in office, he
had put Adapalm on the path of recovery specifically explained that he
had maintained the plantations, planted about 75 hectares of palm,
recovered broken down machinery, as well as, the gas turbine and the
company's guest house.
Chief Okwu also said that the company was currently diversifying its
products by setting up a poultry and pineapple plantation, pointing out
that he was able to record these achievements because of the
establishment of the commercial department which had revolutionized the
revenue generation capability of the company.
In his own report, the Rector of Michael Okpara College of
Agriculture and Technology (MOCATECH), Dr. A.C. Amadioha said the
institution with a current students' enrolment of 600, had reviewed its
course content to bring it in line with what happens in other schools
of the same standing.
He also said that the school was currently involved in the
cultivation of different varieties of cassava, pineapple, plantain, and
banana as well as poultry business.
According to the Rector, the school was also partnering with Niger
Delta Development Commission (NDDC) and other agencies in its research
efforts.
Reacting to the reports, Njoku called on the staff and management of
Adapalm to brace up for the impending privatization of the company.
He commended the parastatals for their performance in 2005 and urged
them to prepare for more work this year as in his words: "the reward
for hard work is more work".

Original_Search_Title: Agagu Takes Cassava to Abuja Forum
Headline: Nigeria: Agagu Takes Cassava to Abuja Forum
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ondo State Government has concluded plans to showcase its cassava
production and processing, with its participation at the Abuja
Investment Forum.
Billed to take place in Abuja on February 14, the Forum will
showcase cassava multiplication and processing potentials in the state.
Ondo State is the first stateto start cassava processing in
commercial quantity, in line with the cassava initiative of the Federal
Government.
This is in consonance with the Presidential directive that certain
percentage of bread made in Nigeria must be made with local ingredients.
Government flagged-off its cassava processing project last year,
when it officially commissioned a cassava processing firm, Oke-Ayo
Farms.
The ceremony was attended by Minister for Commerce and Industry,
Ambassador Idris Waziri, who described the company as "reality of the
moment."
Waziri commended the State Governor, Dr Olusegun Agagu, for blazing
the trail in the implementation of President Olusegun Obasanjo's
vision, by being the first to establish a functional cassava processing
factory.
The cassava processing firm, named Ondo State Food Processing
company, had since started producing several tons of cassava flour and
yam flour for pounded yam.
Already, the state Government has injected over N40 million into the
factory, which has also provided employment opportunities for hundreds
of people in the State, while about 20,000 farmers are already
benefiting from the factory.
The factory which has resumed work in full capacity utilizes 40
tonnes of cassava daily and produces 10 metric tones of cassava flour
daily.
Besides, end products such as ethanol and gasoline obtained from the
factory would help to boost the income of the State Government.
Cassava processing is one of the major sub-sectors planned for exhibition at the Investment Forum in Abuja.
Speakers at the Forum include Chief Bode Fadase, Professor Idowu Odeyemi, Mr. Sina Agboluaje and Professor Adeduro Adegeye.

Original_Search_Title: PSI Takes Over Garment Factory At $1.5m
Headline: Ghana: PSI Takes Over Garment Factory At $1.5m
Body_Text:
The Minister for Private Sector Development and President's Special
Initiative (PSI), Hon. Kwamena Bartels, yesterday handed over a cheque
of $ 1.5 million, estimated to be Â¢13.8 billion, to purchase a
dilapidated factory complex in Tema from the Ghana Textiles
Manufacturing Company (GTMC).
The complex is to be refurbished, within six months, into a hi-tech
factory space capable of producing quality international garments, and
to be rented to ten new indigenous medium garments and textiles
companies.
Each of these companies will employ between 300 to 500 skilled
labour. The purchase is part of a series of initiatives under the PSI
(garment and textiles sector) to accelerate garment exports by
providing local businesses with the input they need to grow in getting
access to credit and training.
Nana Tweneboah Boateng, the coordinator for garments and textiles
under the PSI, explained the rational behind the purchase to the
Business Chronicle.
"GTMC has a property, which is not being utilized at the moment and it is that property we are buying to refurbish," he stated.
According to him, what PSI is doing is to convert the complex into
manufacturing factories and rent them out to ingenious garment
manufacturing companies.
GTMC will produce textiles for both the local industry and for exports to America under AGOA.
Since its inception in August 2001, the PSI has identified and
assisted small and medium sized factories with technical and managerial
skills, as well as help them access finance needed to expand their
business.
Ghana is one of the 37 countries eligible since October 2000 under
the Africa Growth and Opportunity Act to export textiles into the
lucrative USA market.
But many indigenous textile businesses complain that only $4million of manufacturing goods have so far been exported from Ghana.
Nana Boateng said Ghanaian firms needed to build up their capacity
to be in partnership with textile companies in the USA and EU, to take
up the advantage of the various protocols.
The PSI was established to expand and add volume to non-traditional
export to diversify the economy, create employment and improve local
livelihood.
This is expected to tie in well with the goal of export lead growth,
which looks to drive the GDP growth figure of the country to
approximately 8% per annum over the next ten years.
Currently functional, the PSIs are those on garments and textiles, cassava starch, oil palm and salt.
The PSI on garment has created 3,000 jobs in four years, and 5,300 more is expected to be added by the end of last year.
Since its launch, the cassava starch initiative has produced 10,000
metric tons of starch in almost five years. Those are clear on how much
the capital outlet of the project was and will amount to an estimated
$26billion in revenue before tax, salaries, utilities, labour.
The oil palm initiative is yet to start producing oil but the
plantations are still being developed under various nursery schemes.
The salt industry has also seen an industry review.
Technical assistance, training and export contracts have been
secured for industry leaders, which have purposely included PALMPROSE
Limited. The existing annual output on salt is estimated at 150 000
tons of raw salt, that is approximately $11.25 million, contributing to
the cancellation of the countries GDP.
However, these pre-PSI figures are yet to be collated for the added benefits of the presidential special initiative.
"So we are yet to see whether the PSI has actually diversified the
economy, created employment and has improved local livelihood by
significant contribution to the GDP."

Original_Search_Title: Col. Besigye Pledges to Give Luweero a Facelift
Headline: Uganda: Col. Besigye Pledges to Give Luweero a Facelift
Body_Text:
Kampala — FDC presidential candidate Dr. Kizza Besigye has pledged to give Luweero a new face if he is voted into power.
Addressing a rally at Wobulenzi town playground on Friday, Besigye
said although Luweero was the base for the NRM liberation struggle, it
had not received the due attention it deserves.
"The first thing I will do when you vote me into power is to give
Luweero a new face. In the bush war Luweero was our base and we ate
people's cassava but it's a pity that 20 years down the road the war
claimants have not been paid," Besigye said.
On his way from Kampala to Luweero, Besigye supporters put
barricades in every small town requesting him to address them. However,
he pleaded with them to let him go because he had to divide his time
between court and campaigns.
He would occasionally wave to them and the crowds would respond 'obewo' (long live).
He asked Ugandans to vote for him, saying President Yoweri museveni does not have anything new for Ugandans.

Original_Search_Title: Matooke Harvest Going On
Headline: Uganda: Matooke Harvest Going On
Body_Text:
Kampala — There has been a significant increase in the supply of Matooke, which has resulted in low prices.
A sack of approximately 130kgs is selling at Shs20,000, which two weeks ago sold Shs35,000. Bunches are selling averagely Shs4,000-6,000. In spite of this, the demand for Matooke has remained low. Other fresh commodities like sweet potatoes, cassava fresh and Irish potatoes have increased in prices.
Kampala: The market prices have been stable for several commodities, especially among the cereals, except for maize grain, which has come down as more of the new grains enter the market.
Prices this week moved from Shs300-Shs260 per kilo of maize grain in Kisenyi market. It has also been noted that the Tanzania traders who previously boosted the price of old dry grains have pulled out thus the price has dropped to Shs290 from Shs310/kg.
Many traders in Kisenyi complain of the quality of grains as they appear small in size. The moisture content has been noted to vary between 17 and 20 percent.
The supply of beans is good, favoured by new fresh supplies from Mbarara, especially of Kahuura type and Kanyebwa. The prices vary between Shs650 and Shs750 per kilo. Traders however, speculate that it could be buffed up with the approaching opening of the new school term.
Luweero: As the school's longest holiday winds up, foodstuffs like Matooke, Cassava and sweet potatoes have lost demand and prices have slightly reduced as parents prepare to take their children back to school. On the other hand, the prices of maize and beans has increased because of increased demand from schools trying to stock for the new term.
Maize and beans sold for Shs280 and Shs750 per kilo at wholesale respectively. Some rain was received, although the area remains very hot. Tilda rice is in supply; however, 'Vietnam' rice is scarce due to reduced supply and is going for Shs1,300 wholesale price.
Lira: Farmers were happy when they finally received rain but it turned out to be destructive. Some buildings were even destroyed. Farmers are preparing their gardens for the first season planting.
Prices this week were stable with minor changes for some crops. Traders anticipate that by next week, many farmers and small traders from the rural areas will deliver more produce in search for school fees. Small beans registered a price increase to Shs530/kg.
A number of traders from Kampala and Mbale together with the local ones from Lira were busy purchasing maize around Produce line. Maize went for Shs250 wholesale price and is mainly coming from Kitgum and part of Lango sub region. Big Simsim purchases were also made by traders from Kampala and Mbale.
Kibaale: The weather is very cool and more rain is expected in the area. Currently, medium size beans are going for Shs300 at wholesale price. Yellow beans were also supplied on the market at Shs450/kg. The general price level remained constant.
Kabale: The harvest of commodities such as groundnuts, rice, millet and maize is going on within the districts and neighbouring Rukungiri and Kanungu. Apart from Groundnuts, which sold for Shs1,000, other commodities had a constant market price compared to last week's prices. Majority of the harvest has found its way to neighbouring Congo and Rwanda.
Jinja: There has been a further reduction in maize supply due to the drought that has led to an increased market price. A kilo of maize is now going for Shs270 at wholesale. Meanwhile, in the week under review, the district received some rain prompting farmers to start preparing their gardens for the new planting.
Iganga: Kenyan traders coming in the country via Busia border are busy purchasing maize within Busoga region and this has created a higher demand for maize. A kilo is going for Shs260 at wholesale price. Dry conditions continue existing in the area with no report of rainfall. Due to the above conditions, there is a reported reduction in the supply of local chicken in the district.
Hoima: Production of fresh foodstuffs like Matooke, sweet potatoes and cassava is in plenty. A sack of fresh cassava weighing approximately 200kgs went for Shs40,000 wholesale price. Commodities such as G/nuts and beans are very scarce and on high demand. Maize products are in plenty and a kilo of flour was sold at Shs450 while a kilo of grain went for Shs250 wholesale price.
Masindi: Some rain was received in some parts of the district. In areas where plenty was received, farmers are already thinking of planting but were advised not to.
Areas like Buliisa, near the lake (Albert) are extremely hot and animals are lacking pasture. Dry maize grain is reported on high demand. Most farmers have decided not to sell their crop expecting better prices. Presently, farmers are offered between Shs230-250/kg, a price far better than Shs80/kg paid for in December.
Groundnuts have become very scarce because a number of traders have pitched camp in the groundnut growing areas of Pakanyi and Miinya. Farmers are being offered between Shs1,050- 1,100/kg. Several traders interested in g/nut, maize and coffee were received from Luweero.
There is plenty of dry cassava in Kyatiri and Kibanda counties where a kilo is being offered at Shs150.
Tororo: The markets within the district received fewer commodities for sale, which registered an increase in the market prices. Maize grain increased by Shs50 and offered at Shs300/kg wholesale price. Medium sized beans and smoked fish increased to Shs800/kg and Shs4,400/kg wholesale prices respectively. Other commodities that registered an increase in price include maize flour and sorghum.
Mbarara: Cross border traders from Kenya and Tanzania are stocking beans, especially Kahuura in different sites within the district. Nambale and Kahuura beans are offered at Shs600/kg
Soroti: The edible oil market had stiff competition between Mukwano and Bidco products. Containers for Pride oil are said to be weak and not preferred by the rural traders who consider them to cost an extra income. It is evident that the sweet potato harvest season is winding up because of reduced supply to the market.
A full bag was sold at Shs7,000, which registered a drop in the heap size and thus increased the price per heap to Shs300 in the main market. The supply of fresh cassava to the market is low due to the dry weather condition. FOODNET

Original_Search_Title: Kwara to Train 5,000 Youths On Commercial Farming
Headline: Nigeria: Kwara to Train 5,000 Youths On Commercial Farming
Body_Text:
Ilorin — In an efforts to grease the wheel of Youth Empowerment Programme
in Kwara State, the state government is to train over 5,000 youth as
commercial farmers under the Present State Integrated Agricultural
Training Scheme.
Dropping the hint yesterday, after the tour of state government
Malete Youth Integrated Agricultural Training Farm, the farm Manager,
Mr. Collins Spain also disclosed that the farm Training Centre was
designed as continuation of government efforts to develop the Youths in
Agricultural Practices.
According to him, the training programmes was designed to see the
trainees as Commercial Farmers in the Future and to enable them be self
reliant after they must have acquired knowledge in cassava production
poultry, fish farming and rice production.
His word, " the governor of the state, Dr. Bukola Saraki has
prepared to reposition the Farm Settlement for commercial farmers to be
able to produce for sale after their training".
He remarked that the project was originally set aside for about 100 students to become Commercial farmer yearly.
Spain who acknowledged the efforts of the state government in making
life more meaningful to the people of the state opined that the youth
had received briefing on land to further encourage them, the state
government had approved stipend of N6,000 for participants drawn from
primary schools, N7,000 for secondary school holders, N8,000 for ND
Certificate holders and N10,000 for HND holders while N12,000 was for
graduates participants.
In another development, the state government has equally approved
the rehabilitation of Malete Dams for the benefit of the community and
to serve as sources of water for the Farm Settlement.
Disclosing this to newsmnen during the inspection tour of the Farm
the Special Assistant on empowerment programme to the Governor, Mr
Muideen Jimba said the Malete dam had been negelected for over 22 years.
According to him, after the rehabilitation it would help to assist
over 41 communities in the area while in the same vein 41 communities
would equally benefit from the ongoing Rural Electrification of the
state government
Meanwhile Malete Community had donated 1,000 hectares of land for the Agricultural Scheme of the state government.

Original_Search_Title: NDC On NDC
Headline: Ghana: NDC On NDC
Body_Text:
On Thursday 12th January, I listened to the current affairs programme on Metro TV, "Good Evening Ghana" hosted by Mr. Paul Adom Okyere with Mr. Ekow Spio Gabrah as the Guest. Several issues were addressed. To say the least it was appalling for a senior member of our party to say the type of things he said about the former flag bearer of the party Prof Mills.
It is said that "when the anthill allows holes into itself, all animals enter". It is unfortunate that this great party of ours and the largest opposition party in Ghana and at a point in time ostensibly the party in waiting to form a government, if not by 2008 at least by 2012, has allowed several holes into itself and thus facilitating the entry of all kinds of animals: ants, toads, roaches, rodents, reptiles-serpent, wolves etc. Unfortunately we have not had any doves entering our anthill yet, not in the last couple of years.
Firstly I have a few of questions for Spio Gabrah:
1. As the Master Strategist for the former President Jerry John as you claimed to be in 1996, where were you and where did your strategic skills vanish to in 2000 and then in 2004? Why did you not put those skills at the behest of Prof Mills for him to win handsomely in 2000, Even if these were for a fee?
2. Does Ekow want us NDC Activists and indeed all Ghanaians- to believe that he (Ekow) had to be appointed a Campaign Manager for Prof Mills before he could put his skills at the disposal of the Flag-bearer? God save our party if we have people like Ekow Spio Gabrah in our Party
3. Ekow, being outside Ghana in the UK for an assignment with the Commonwealth did not take you to another planet outside the Milky Way of our universe. At least you were and still are entitled to annual vacations. In 2004 why did you not bring your strategies to the fore to help our Great Party to come to power during any of the annual vacations that you enjoyed? Indeed you voted for Prof Mills in 2003 as against Dr Botchwey as the flag bearer of the NDC. Are you saying you were hoodwinked before you voted for his candidature?
4. Ekow, are you sure you are not prompted to make the observations you made from any mercenary or unworthy motives?
Let me now address some of damming, scathing and acerbic statements that Ekow made. Among others Ekow said instead of Prof Mills undertaking Proper Social Science Research to find out why he lost the 2000 and 2004 elections he and his cronies (so to speak) have been engaged in "Chop Bar" Research and the Blame Game.
When did Ekow know that the NDC undertake their research over fufu and Akrantie soup, Green-Green Soup, Kako/Ewurefua and Cassava Ampesi or ewurefua and etew? Is Ekow aware that the NDC has very great minds and a well established Research Department with academics and political luminaries such as Dr. Tony Aidoo and Mr. Kwamina Ahwoi among others who at one time or another were in charge of that Research Department?
Is Ekow suggesting or implying by those caustic and mordant remarks that all the works of Kwamina Ahwoi and Dr Aidoo were based falsehood and day-dreams after they had taken a good fill of Ghanaian delicacy and therefore were without scientific basis? When did you realize this, Ekow? Did you know or learn about the new Social Science Methodologies when you took up the appointment at the Commonwealth Secretariat?
It has been rumoured that you, Ekow Spio Gabrah wants to run for President on the ticket of the NDC. At least your reactions to some questions by Paul Adom Okyere literally offered some clues to any critical political analyst on the matter. But let me remind you, Ekow Spio Gabrah, of the story of Zaccheus in Holy Writ. Conscious of his handicap, and perhaps having several options under his sleeves that could prop him up to catch a glimpse of the Greatest Teacher that ever lived, did not follow the line of least resistance.
For instance he could have subtly asked some of the people around to lift him up and pay less or nothing during the subsequent tax year. He could also have pleaded with other friends to lift him up to enable him see Jesus or the other way round. He did none of these and may several other options that he might have schemed. He rather chose the hard way - he climbed the tree - by his own efforts. Ekow Spio, you don't go to the stadium and climb on the shoulders of your friends to watch a football match. Ekow Spio Gabrah, you want to chart the course of least resistance to the top. Be careful about that, Spio. We no go sit down!
Ekow, are you sure those barbed and sour statements (which as best is qualified to tear the NDC apart further) you made were your own thinking or you are doing the dirty work of the "Founder"? Is he still the undisputed Founder - Lawyer Kwaku Baah should help me!
Ekow, don't think that you are more marketable than Prof Mills. We are aware that you want to pair up with Madam (Mrs Konadu Rawlings) for the Presidency. Do you think the two of you are more marketable than Prof Mills? Some of us can say that Prof Mills has done so much for our Party and this country than most of you so-called Master Strategists and Champions of Revolutionary verve in the party.
Do you know the amount of benefit that Prof Mills' sobriety and decorum and those of other political stalwarts such as Dr Obed Asamoah, Alhaji Mahama Idrisu, Mr. Harry Sawyerr, Mr. Owusu Acheampong, Justice Annan, the late Ms Patience Addow, Hon John Mahama, Mr. PV Obeng among others have gained for Party?
These fine gentlemen come handy when it comes to moderation of issues for the party that was born out of a revolution. We must be grateful for these people. When people like those mentioned are spending time and energy to built the Party, others like you, Tony Aidoo, Victor Smith, Jerry John and their breed use their diarrhoeic speeches (seen by them only as signs of charisma) to scuttle whatever has been built.
Spio should be ashamed that instead of finding ways of mending the Party he is creating another block - the Mills Faction against the Jerry John Faction. In conclusion let it be known to you that neither you nor your team-up with "She that must be Obeyed Ticket will fly in Ghana. I challenge you, Ekow to try your ticket. Spio give us a Break, Peace.

Original_Search_Title: What They Could Not Tell Baba
Headline: Nigeria: What They Could Not Tell Baba
Body_Text:
The President, last Tuesday, held a stakeholders' meeting with business
leaders and CEOs of the newly capitalised commercial banks to gear them
towards achieving a 10% growth in the economy in the next 12 months.
It is not clear why Mr. President left this initiative and upbeat
motivation until his final year in office. What is clear, however, is that
Mr. President would wish to leave the economy on a high note! Last Tuesday's
stakeholders' meeting also attracted, amongst others, the ANPP and PDP Chief
Executives as well as a dozen or so State Governors and Federal Ministers
and heads of parastals.
In every instance, speakers at the forum precluded their presentations
with effusive gratitude to Mr. President, in spite of the President's
admonition to cut out the frills! No one wanted to be labelled as
anti-Obasanjo like the Deputy Governor of Lagos State, who attempted to
point out obstacles that would hinder the achievement of a 10% growth rate
in 2006. Mr. President demonstrated his underlying physical fitness and
commitment to the object of the meeting, as he personally took command and
remained standing at the podium from where he threw his punches and
acknowledged obeisance for upwards of three hours! It was rather enthralling
and revealing to watch the body language of Baba on the one side and the
host of politicians and political appointees on the other, as they jostled
for Baba's attention, commendation and approval. The high level of awe,
which the oracle commanded was very palpable!
The zeal of a handful of private independent speakers, who attempted to
guide deliberations with some relevant commercial and industrial experiences
were quickly rebuffed (in one case, very angrily) by the main man, who had
obviously clearly defined his expectations and did not look like letting
anyone upset his delicately apportioned applecart, even if that person was
the representative of the Senate, who humbly and almost frightfully passed
on a message that the Senate holds that the 25 newly reconsolidated banks
existed in a lacuna as the Senate had not yet approved the process of their
creation!
Mr. President's response to this, many would say, was typical; if the
CBN Governor was found not to have followed due constitutional process in
the consolidation exercise, the CBN Governor would make an apology to the
Senate in due course. However, if the CBN Governor had been wrongly accused,
then of course, the Senate or its representative should be ready to make an
apology to Prof. Soludo! Matter finish! Next question please! Indeed, with a
combatant with Baba's heavy weight punch in the ring, it required a fool
with plenty of courage to step up as a challenger!
Mr. President's commitment to transform cassava cultivation and
processing to major export processing activity was unmistakable, and
Mr. President deserves commendation for his efforts so far. It is clear that
there are serious defects in the game plan for achieving annual foreign
exchange earnings of over $15bn from cassava, but it is heartwarming that
inspite of infrastructural and financial limitations, the cassava export
boat has undoubtedly put out to sail, going by the testimonies of
stakeholders in this agricultural sub sector, and this enterprise may indeed
remain one of President Obasanjo's enduring positive economic legacies after
2007.
The increase in our strategic grain reserve to about 500,000 tons is
also commendable, but there are genuine concerns that the increasing food
prices for an already financially challenged populace may have derived from
government's strategic stock accumulation and the increasing export of
cassava based products! In this event, the government will have to resolve
the dilemma of releasing huge stocks of grains into the domestic market to
reduce consumer prices and at the same time sustaining the enthusiasm of the
increasing investors in the agricultural sector, particularly with its vast
employment generating capacity.
Mr. President demonstrated his commitment to agriculture and non-oil
industries, particularly in the SME sub sector by his "fist stamping" of his
own directive to the 24 banks that oil related businesses should be
encouraged to seek offshore credit, which according to Mr. President, is
even cheaper than the cost of local borrowings! The question, of course,
that remains unanswered is the paradox why the weaker, small and medium
enterprises have to pay over 25% interest for funds locally, when their
counterparts in focused economies pay between 6-9%!
Clearly, until the CBN brings down its Minimum Rediscount Rate (MRR)
(the rate at which banks borrow as a last resort from the CBN) to single
digit, it is unlikely that a conducive commercial lending rate will prevail
in the near future for SMEs!
Mr. President was quick to dangle the carrot of warehousing and doing
business with a deposit of $500m from the nation's reserves to any Nigerian
bank with a $1bn capital base. Although Mr. President assured Nigerians that
he recognized the gravity of ensuring safe husbanding for the nation's
money, some observers have wondered why the same banks from which the CBN is
withdrawing government funds locally have simultaneously been considered as
safe havens for a part of Nigeria's foreign reserves, especially when the
government is not a shareholder in any bank! Indeed, the scam of round
tripping of officially purchased dollars is generally regarded as the
foundation stone of the growth of most local banks. There is no gainsaying
what would happen when $500m is given to some of these banks on a platter
gold, their $1bn capital base not withstanding. We may be inadvertently
opening up a wide window for further devaluation of the naira, and
destabilisation of the economy.
Mr. President left no one in doubt of his closeness with and approval
for the arrowheads of his economic policies! They would manage to convince
Baba that this is trite speculation! We, nonetheless, recall that the
President's economic team during the SAP years also enjoyed the same
confidence of their benefactor particularly with their illustrious
international antecedents! It was interesting to listen to Baba's graphic
illustration of the phenomenon of inflation as a bowl of water with a
defined capacity, thus, there is a limit to the amount of money which can be
injected into the economy at any one time; once this limit is breached, any
additional injection will fuel inflation and as with the bowl, additional
water will be a waste and could even cause a mess! Excellent economics, but
Mr. President's economic mentors appear to have forgotten to add that in
reality, the limits of absorptive capacity cannot be breached in a climate
of serious unemployment, low industrial capa city utilization and poor
infrastructure, as we currently have in Nigeria!
In other words, the water cannot spill over until the bowl is full! The
illusion of too much money in the Nigerian economy is caused by what the CBN
Governor himself describes as the monetisation of the monthly distributable
foreign exchange component of the federation pool. Prof Soludo confessed
that a sustainable 10% growth rate would present a serious challenge in view
of the increasing pressure to "monetise" (read as changing into naira) the
increasing dollar revenue we earn from higher crude oil prices and the
attendant scourge of "excess liquidity" (read as too much naira), high
interest rates and a downward pressure on the value of the naira;
Mr. President cannot not be aware that our current monetary framework
ensures that the more dollars we earn, the greater the pressure on our naira
and the greater the challenges of monetary policy. Enlightened cynics would
be right to expect more salutary benefits from increased export earnings and
it is certain Baba's economic fr iends did not tell him that the adoption of
dollar certificates for paying the dollar component of the federation pool
would banish our economic predicament and put our economy on a speedy road
to recovery.
SAVE THE NAIRA, SAVE NIGERIANS!

Original_Search_Title: Sagay Laments Rot in Education Sector
Headline: Nigeria: Sagay Laments Rot in Education Sector
Body_Text:
Warri — FORMER University don and Senior Advocate of Nigeria, SAN, Professor
Itse Sagay, has lamented the rot in the nation's education sector.
In a lecture entitled: "Improved Educational Standard as a Prerequisite
for a meaningful and Accelerated Growth and Development of Nigeria in the
21st century," to mark the Diamond Jubilee of Government College, Ughelli
and presented on his behalf by Dr. Irhue Y. Kenneth, Prof. Sagay asserted
that "those who were not privileged to attend schools like Government
College, Ughelli and were in fact regarded as drop-outs decided that
institutions of that quality were elitist and should be brought down to the
lowest available denominator."
This decline, he said, saw a general decay setting in throughout all
levels of education, adding that it was an era when teachers lost the
respect of the society because of low pay and low social status accompanied
by drastically reduced funding of institutions and deliberate refusal to
replace broken down facilities.
He lamented that facilities formerly meant for just 272 students are now
being overstretched by over 3,000 students at the Government College,
Ughelli, stressing that the result of this grave decline in standards is the
emergence of private schools at great cost to the impoverished parents.
Describing the "universities in Nigeria today as a study in decay,
degeneration and devastation," the Professor of International Law and an old
boy of Government College, Ughelli maintained that "such sordid, depressing,
deprived and devastated environment coupled with its humiliated population,
is a perfect letting for violence, for a counter culture for an alienated
and dangerous community of students and a totally disenchanted community of
faded scholars hence the emergence of secret cults, destructive anger,
violent demonstrations, contempt for authority, society and the brain drain
syndrome."
Onosode gives quit notice to farmers
Meanwhile, presidential aspirant and President-General of the Government
College, Ughelli Old Boys Association, GCUOBA, Worldwide, Deacon Gamaliel
Onosode, has told natives of Ughelli community who have turned the school
premises into their farm lands to be ready to forfeit same to the school
authority, for the use of the student in their study of agriculture.
He also gave the Principal of the school 12 months within which to
reposition the school in terms of providing quality education, discipline,
provision of school facilities and general aesthetic appearance.
Deacon Gamaliel Onosode spoke at the Diamond Jubilee celebration of the
school.
The industry guru who lamented the unedifying state of decay in his
alma-mater stated that "discipline and hard work was the hallmark of GCU in
their days," and admonished recalcitrant students to quietly withdraw from
the school or be shown the way out.
Obviously disappointed that the school last held her annual inter-house
sport competition about eight years ago due to the bushy nature of the
school with cassava farms dotting every available land space, owned by
teachers and community members, Deacon Onosode vowed that by "January 19,
2007 all these plantations and forest would have been converted to farms
owned by the school for the students to aid the study of agriculture,"
noting that "farming in the school should not become a diversionary activity
for those who should devote their full time and strength to the management
of Government College, Ughelli."

Original_Search_Title: UN Agency Helps Ghana Develop Harvests And Boost Incomes of Rural Poor
Headline: Ghana: UN Agency Helps Ghana Develop Harvests And Boost Incomes of Rural Poor
Body_Text:
A UN poverty-fighting agency today in Rome signed off on a $19 million
loan to the Ghanaian government that will strengthen the West African
nation's food security by helping its small farmers cultivate sweet
potatoes, cassava and other roots and tubers.
The loan from the International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD)
will help finance a $27.7 million development programme meant to assist
Ghanaian farmers, traders and processors who depend on the production of
roots and tubers for their livelihoods. The Government of Ghana will
contribute $3.9 million to the initiative.
"By increasing their access to markets and new technology and building
on indigenous knowledge, the programme will help Ghana's rural poor to
increase their incomes and improve their livelihoods," said Mohamed
Manssouri, IFAD's Country Programme Manager for Ghana. "They will be less
marginalized and participate more actively in the development process."
Nearly 55 per cent of all farming households in Ghana cultivate roots
and tubers - vegetables such as Cassava, yams, sweet potatoes, and cocoyams
- and the crops provide more than 42 per cent of the staple food supply and
large quantities of raw material for agro-industries.
Women make up about half of the Ghanaians involved in the production of
these vegetables and the development programme will aim to expand their
access to the marketing process by strengthening their bartering and
marketing skills and their ability to negotiate prices and contracts.
The programme will improve roots and tuber production as agricultural
researchers and farmers meet at forums to share their experiences and swap
information on cultivation practices. Farmers will gain a greater knowledge
of new varieties of roots and tubers, soil fertility management and pest
control.
It will also work to expand rural producers' access to improved
technologies, provide training, and credit and micro-leasing that will let
farmers and processor upgrade their equipment and tap into new
technology.

Original_Search_Title: NAFDAC Assures Harsh Regulation On Foreign Products
Headline: Nigeria: NAFDAC Assures Harsh Regulation On Foreign Products
Body_Text:
The National Agency for Food, Drugs Administration and Control (NAFDAC)
said yesterday that it will put in place strict regulations to ensure
quality of foreign products for the benefit of the country.
The Director-General of the Agency, Prof. Dora Akunyili who made the
remark while receiving Thailand's Deputy Minister of Commerce, Preecha
Laonhapongehana, said that the gesture was aimed at removing unfair
competition by counter-feiters in the country. "Our regulations are very
stringent because we want better quality products for our people. We want to
remove unfair competition of counterfeiters so that genuine people will come
into the sector, because you cannot effectively compete with fraudsters
because they will get round."
Dr. Akunyili, who described Nigeria as a goldmine for genuine
businessmen and women, assured Thailand of prosperity if its products make
an in-road to Nigeria.
According to her, Nigeria is strategically placed to launch Thailand
into the African and West African sub-region, adding that the population of
the country will help if Thailand brings genuine business.
Earlier, the Thailand Deputy Minister of Commerce, Preecha
Laonhapongehana, said that he was in Nigeria to solicit the federal
government's cooperation for the benefit of the two countries.
According to him, Thailand has plans to set up a rice factory in Nigeria
and to process Cassava in order to boost export and import.
He noted that Thailand will set up an embassy in Nigeria in order to
strengthen more cooperation between the two countries.

Original_Search_Title: Jonathan Chairs Niger Delta Crisis Committee
Headline: Nigeria: Jonathan Chairs Niger Delta Crisis Committee
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — In a bid to arrest the escalation of the crisis in the oil and gas rich
Niger Delta region, President Olesegun Obasanjo has named Governor Goodluck
Jonathan of Bayelsa state as the chairman of a committee aimed at addressing
the problem in the area.
Not less than 14 persons, including 5 soldiers were said to have lost
their lives in the recent attacks by militant youths at two oil flow
stations at Ekeremor Local Government Area of Bayelsa state.
This is besides other expatriate workers held hostage at Beneseide Flow
Station early last week. The flow stations belong to Anglo Dutch Oil giant,
Shell.
Rivers State Governor Dr. Peter Odili who briefed newsmen in the state
capital Port Harcourt shortly on arrival from Abuja where he attended a
security stakeholders meeting presided by President Olusegun Obasanjo also
disclosed that his counterparts in the Delta and Ondo states, Vice President
Atiku Abubakar and service chiefs also attended the meeting.
Odili further revealed that the committee was charged with the
responsibility of examining the factors surrounding the recent security
situation in the Niger Delta and work out the way forward for the
region.
He also explained that President Obasanjo used the occasion to x-ray the
present Federal Government economic reform agenda within the past one year
was significant as it revealed huge success recorded to justify the
programme.
The cheering news is the new targets that have been set to take the
economy in the next one or two years to about 10 percent rate from the 7
percent it is now, and the various sectoral achievements shows particularly
the economic reform programme as it affects the financial and agricultural
sectors are in right directionÃ®, Odili said.
The governor stressed the need to encourage the small and medium scale
enterprises and the opportunity to expand the agricultural sector to enable
it add value to some of the products being consumed in the country.
The state chief executive urged people of Rivers State to participate
actively on the boom in the cassava products, noting that Agriculture holds
the key to employment generations, and should be encouraged by all, and
called on government at various levels to collaborate in promoting the
agricultural sectors effectively for the well-being of the people of Nigeria
Meanwhile, Shell has expressed deep concern over the kidnap of its
workers in boundary communities between Bayelsa and Delta states. In a
statement issued yesterday in Port Harcourt, the oil giant said: Ã¬We remain
deeply concerned about the hostage situation that resulted from the
abduction of four Shell contractors from SPDC' EA field operations.
Ã¬We recognize the immense strains placed upon the hostages, their
families and contractor companies under the present circumstances. We
continue to cooperate with the Nigerian authorities and the hostages'
employers and offer every assistance to secure the safe release of the
hostagesÃ®.
Continuing, Shell which has been operating in Nigeria since 1956 when
oil was discover in commercial quantity in Oloibri, Ogbia Local Government
Area of Bayelsa State said: Ã¬The health and safety of hostages remains our
top priority. We are taking all necessary measures to ensure the safety and
security of staff, contractors and the people in the communities where we
operate. We continue to keep the deployment of staff in the affected areas
in the western Niger Delta under review. We will return to areas evacuated
when normality is restored.
Ã¬Regrettably we can now confirm that the body of another civilian
victim, missing since the attack on the Benisede flow station, has been
found. He was a Shell catering contractor. Our deepest sympathy goes to his
family and friendsÃ®.
According to the multi-national oil company, 10 of the injured people
who are receiving medical treatment in Warri are in improved condition.
Ã¬The production status remains unchanged. The four flow stations and EA
production remain shut, with a total production loss of some 221,000 barrels
per day. The force majeure declared for Forcados and EA offtakes is still in
placeÃ®, the statement added.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava: a Money Spinner
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava: a Money Spinner
Body_Text:
Cassava , the source of garri and fufu, two of Nigeria's leading staple food has been raised from the proverbial grass to grace as it has become the choice crop for big time farmers who are struggling to satisfy the huge local demand. Dr. Godwin Oyediji, a cassava farmer, (member of Presidential Committee on Cassava Export, President, Animal Science Society of Nigeria, and Chief Executive of Godoye Enterprises Ltd. ) tells all that makes the crop tick today. He spoke to Okey Ndiribe.
TODAY, cassava farming has taken a new dimension for the better in Nigeria. Going into casava was horrible about 10 years ago. But all that is now ancient history as there is a new variety of cassava that promises a yield of up to 40 metric tonnes per hectare of land compared to between six and eight metric tonnes that farmers used to harvest from the native variety of cassava. This new key to wealth for planters of the crop is called TMS variety and it was developed by the International Institute for Tropical Agriculture ( IITA), Ibadan. The difference in higher yield is more than four times. Some farmers who planted this variety in 2005 in addition to applying other necessary farming techniques are already smiling.
According to Oyediji: "I have received reports and confirmed that farmers who planted this variety not long ago have harvested between 50 and 60 metric tonnes of cassava from one per hectare of land. This cassava variety is also disease resistant. The significance of this, is that the cost of production has been significantly reduced. This is because if you get five times more from the same area of land, it is the same labour, pesticide and other inputs that you have used but the yield is four or five times more than that of the native variety of crop.
What does it take to go into cassava farming? I asked him as he warmed up to the subject.
"In the first place you need land," he informed. "Cassava is a universal crop that grows even in very poor land. Sources of land differ in different parts of the country. A medium scale farmer would need a minimum of 10 hectares of land in an area where the cost of land is not very high. So, I would not advise anybody who wants to start a cassava farm to look for land in Lagos. This is because land is very scarce and expensive in Lagos state. You need a large area of land because you cannot successfully embark on the entire production of cassava for commercial purposes manually. You would need an equipment like tractor. And 10 hectares would even be too small for a tractor. Another alternative is to hire tractors from the various state governments at subsidised prices. A complete tractor and all the implements that come with it would be worth over five million naira.
However, the good news is that since the civilian dispensation began in 1999, many state governments have bought tractors and are renting them to farmers at highly subsidised rates. These states include Osun, Jigawa, Kebbi, Ebonyi and many others."
