According to him hiring tractors for the whole period of a planting year until harvest depends on several factors. And he explains: " For instance, the hiring units in various parts of the country belong to the local and state governments. There is no standard rate from one local government to another or from one state to another. Some state governments render these services almost free. For instance, the Kwara State Government even brought Zimbabwean farmers and gave them a lot of incentives and inputs free. In Lagos, Ogun, Osun, Oyo and some northern states, farmers are being assisted. In some states in the north, a farmer could get up to 1000 hectares of land to farm on. This applies to states like Kaduna, Jigawa, Zamfara, Sokoto, Adamawa and Benue. Benue is the highest cassava producing state in the country.
It was interesting to note that while governments particularly that of Lagos sold lands for building purposes, they give same almost free for agriculture. So, how can an intending cassava farmer get the stems of this high yield cassava variety?
"You can get these stems from other farmers who have already planted them," was the simple response He continues: "This is because IITA is a research institute and cannot be involved in the commercial sale of agricultural inputs. I have gone there many times and I got addresses of farmers who have already planted the TMS variety. If you go there they would ask you which state you are from and then they would give you the address of the farmers that have been supplied the high yield variety.
Can you give a rough estimate of how much an intending cassava farmer needs to start?
"About five million naira." But that is a lot of money for I beginner, I thought. Then I recalled his earlier comment about large scale farming. What made cassava production so terrible in the past , I queried. He disclosed that the first factor about cassava production in the past that was so discouraging was the yield. "This is because since the yield was low, the profit margin was seriously eroded.
In our own 500 hectare cassava farm at Iwo in Osun State, established about 23 years ago, the best yield we got was about six tonnes from a hectare of land. That could hardly pay for the cost of harvesting which was manual. This manual harvesting has remained so up till now. When the cost of manual labour went up, many cassava farmers including myself suspended planting the crop. So if the cassava yield is not very high, the cost of labour can wipe out the entire profit you have realised in one planting year. But with your yield going up to three or four times higher, you would be able to absorb the cost of harvesting."
What are your aspirations and expectations for venturing into casava farming?
"My major aspiration for going into cassava production, just like in any other business is for the purpose of making profit. My major objective is to increase my earnings substantially. I am not focusing on export because cassava has a very strong internal demand. The price of cassava is higher within the country than at the international market. The average price of a tonne of casava chips or flour in Nigeria is $160. It moved up to $200 per tonne recently. But at the international market, a tonne of cassava is between $80 and $100. This means it is cheaper to import cassava from abroad presently. This is because the leading producers of cassava like Brazil and Thailand don't eat the crop. In Nigeria we eat about 90 percent of the cassava we produce.
I would expect the Federal Government to adopt as one of its strategies the replacement of wheat with cassava as food donation to countries struck by natural disaster and famine within the developing countries of the world. Whenever the advanced countries donate wheat, let Nigeria send cassava or garri. This is a strategy that could be used to popularise cassava and raise its demand and price in the international market. This strategy should at the same time have the effect of reducing the local price of the product. At the end of the day, the international price would probably become higher than the local price for casava. Whatever it is, we envisage that the current price of casava would drop, as more people go into casava production by planting the new variety. Whoever doesn't plant the new variety should not go into casava production. This is because such a person cannot recover the cost of establishing that farm.
What profit margin do you expect to make with by planting this cassava variety you have talked about so much?
"From my calculations, there is the possibility of realising 100 per cent net profit for those who plant this new variety of casava. If a farmer goes into processing of the crop into garri, fufu or casava chips, the profit margin would even be higher. It could be as high as 150 per cent. I have also incorporated processing into my plan while considering the cost of energy for that purpose. The major hitch to casava processing in this country is the cost of energy. For us as agro-based consultants and educated farmers, we are highly opposed to the use of firewood for the processing of casava. But this is what most of the local people use for frying garri. It is important to mention that 75 per cent of casava is made up of water. What this means is that processing cassava, in order to preserve it for sometime entails removing the water content. For instance, if you have four tonnes of cassava, what you would get when it is processed is one tonne. We are opposed to using fire wo
od for frying or processing cassava in any other way, because we have already cut down our forest and the savannah belt has even reached Lagos State. It is a serious environmental problem.
But constant electric supply is still not available in centres of agricultural production. In addition to this, the price of diesel and kerosene are quite high. The best thing to do is to process the cassava on site.
However, against the background of these problems, we might limit ourselves to production of casava tubers, and supply them to those who would do the processing. For instance, an integrated company which would process cassava into glucose syrup would soon come up. This company is going to be based at Ibafon, in Ogun State. Its plant would significantly mop up almost half of all cassava produced in the South-West. With that, we believe we can garner some significant profit.
How have government policies affected the planting of cassava over the years?
"I will start by saying that the Federal Government has been pursuing an aggressive policy on agricultural production. This government embarked on a massive campaign to raise awareness about its policies on agriculture. But unfortunately, the budgetary allocation to the sector has not been adequate. The Federal Government's intention for the sector has not been matched with a commensurate budgetary allocation. The allocation has always been very low, sometimes less than three per cent of the national budget as against the 25 per cent minimum that was recommended by the Food and Agricultural Organisation- an arm of the UN- for African countries. Again, even though Nigeria played a leading role in the formulation and adoption of the New Economic Partnership for African Development (NEPAD) which recommended that nothing less than 10 per cent of the annual budgetary allocation of member states should go to agriculture, this has never been met by the Federal Government.
As a member of the Presidential Committee on the Export of Cassava, why is the government in a rush to export this crop when the local demand has obviously not been satisfied?
" The government arrived at the decision to export casava based on the report of government officials in 2002 which indicated that there was a glut of cassava in the country. But some of us as representatives of the Lagos Chamber of Commerce and Industries ( LCCI) and NACCIMA within the Presidential Committee, went out to find out what the true situation was. I discovered that in some parts of the South-West, there was actually a cassava glut. For instance, in Oyo state, one Pick up load of cassava sold for N3,000. But in many other parts of the South-West including Lagos, Ogun, Ekiti and Ilesha, there was acute scarcity. None of the major feed mills in the South-West had enough stock of cassava. However, in Benue and Delta States, there was glut. This was the situation but unfortunately there was no market information system to announce to everybody that these crops were available in certain places while they were scarce in other places. So, the government acted on the repor
t that there was a glut. The reaction of government was to mop up the glut for export. The committee broke up into sub-committees and one of the sub committees was to look into the export of cassava.
But before this was done, the name of the committee was changed to broaden its assignment to include production and processing of cassava. It was on the basis of this development that China placed an order for five million tonnes of cassava chips. So if anybody can provide five million tonnes of cassava chips today, we can send it to china immediately. In fact, one company alone in China, placed an order for 500, 000 tonnes of cassava chips.

Original_Search_Title: Non-Oil Exports Hit N115.10bn in 2005
Headline: Nigeria: Non-Oil Exports Hit N115.10bn in 2005
Body_Text:
A whoping N115.10 billion of non-oil products was last year exported by Nigerian exporters, thanks to the efforts of the Nigerian Export Promotion Council (NEPC) and Cobalt International Ltd., the Federal Government appointed pre-shipment inspection agent for non-oil exports in Nigeria.
A document obtained from NEPC indicated that for the period under review, October 2004 - December 2005, a total amount of $889,648,474.70 (N115,107,822,048.22) of non-oil products have been exported. This figure is based on analysis of the raw data provided by Cobalt International Ltd. A total quantity of about 89,016.823 metric tons was exported between October - December, 2004 with total value of N12,630,524,594.26 ($95,433,697.60) for the period.
The export performance for the months of January reveals that the month of December, has the highest value of N15,329,725,979.89($119,297,478.44), while the month of July is the lowest with an amount of N6,814,318,885.21($51,471,552.87).
The trend at which export transactions were carried out from January - December 2005 shows quarterly export with the October - December quarter having the highest total export valued at N31,604,822,352.91 ($245,942,079.31) closely followed by April - June 2005 valued at N24,600,871,961.79 ($195,778,949.87).
In comparison, the quarterly distribution of October - December 2004 and 2005 shows that export for 2005 is higher.
The trend in export performance for the whole of 2005 had not been stable as it had risen and fallen due to global economic changes that have prevailed all over. The exchange rate is another factor that has contributed to the roller coaster effect.
For instance, cassava and its derivatives was adversely affected by this global economic change in 2005. In the period under review only 10.6 metric tons of dried cassava chips was recorded exported officially in May, 2005. The value of export for the product is $634.07 (N70,660.76). Since then no significant progress on export has been recorded on cassava export because the selling price of cassava in the local market is much higher than the export price. The cost of transportation, labour, tax, exchange rate, etc, and the fact that cassava has not yet met the international standard are the reasons why export is not yet favourable.
Other factor that could be responsible for the low export of cassava is that most processing factories in Nigeria are unwilling to pay at the market price, which invariably discourages farmers from having to produce more. And this in turn does not encourage exporters, who buying directly from farmers got it at high price at the same rate it is purchased in the local market. The prices at which companies placed on 1 metric to is N8,000.00 flat rate while if sold at the local market processors it is between N13,000.00 to N14,000.00 depending on the variety.
In the event of having to succeed to export, exporters are faced with standardisation like parking, packaging, etc, which in effect, does affect the pricing, leading to lost on the part of the exporters.
The way forward
Government policy should be tailored to encourage young graduates to go into farming with the aim of producing more for export. Policy tailored to request all government schools to make agriculture a compulsory subject.
Total Annual
Non-Oil Export
The total non-oil export is not fully reflected in the course of this statistical data (Clean Certificate of Inspection) from Cobalt International Ltd. as the data are intention to export. This value can only be compared with repatriated export proceeds sent to Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) from exporters' bankers. This should be handled by the statistics division of NEPC who will be able to collate these figures.
Export Expansion Grant
Prior to 1999, government policy on the Export Expansion Grant (EEG) had been on graduation of N50,000.00 to N100,000.00 is 5%, 100,001.00 to 500,000.00 is 4%, 500,001 to 1 million is 3%, while 1 miliion and above attracts 2% since 1989 to 1998 using official exchange rate between the period of 1989 to 1995. The exporting community was not comfortable with the official exchange rate and the government had to change it to autonomous exchange rate in 1996 to make room for the dwindling economy then.
Total annual export can not be determined if and when compared to number of applications because not all exporters applied for the grant. The only way one can know whether exporters are being encouraged or not is by accessing their volume and value of export for a particular period of time.

Original_Search_Title: Is Udenwa Performing in Imo State?
Headline: Nigeria: Is Udenwa Performing in Imo State?
Body_Text:
I want to start this write-up by asking a question: Is Governor Achike Udenwa of Imo State delivering the dividends of democracy to Imo people? This is because I want the people to judge including those inside or outside the state. If a journalist gives credit to a politician that is doing well, some people may think that he has collected money or brown envelope as they call it.
I read in one write-up in a newspaper where the writer wrote, I quote: "Apparently, the moon (Onwa) as the Governor is called, is not beaming on the people of Imo State. Therefore, let us not delude ourselves with this false impression being foisted on us by the vocal few individuals who are part and parcel of the conspiracy currently going on in Owerri." I do not know when the writer of this article visited Imo State last or is he imagining what is happening in the state?
Let me start by mentioning a few recorded significant achievements by Udenwa's government in the various sectors of the economy particularly in the provision of infrastructural facilities across the length and breath of the state in the year 2005 alone. The Teaching Hospital at Orlu and the ultra modern state Secretarial Complex were completed during this period. The economic trips abroad by the governor yielded positive results in the form of donations of critical equipment and instructional materials to various institutions in the state, particularly the Imo State University Teaching Hospital, Orlu, the Imo State University and the General Hospital, Owerri.
The city of Houston, Texas, donated vehicles to the state and some of them have already been put to use, while more are being expected to arrive the country this year.
In agriculture, Udenwa's government procured four modern rice mills with destoners to ensure continuous commercial production of the Heartland rice. Two of them have been installed at Onicha Uboma and Abueke in Ihitte Uboma Local Government Area, while the other two are at Ohiauchi, Arondizogu in Ideato North Local Government Area. The four mills have a combined production capacity of about 200; 50 kg bags of destoned rice per day, while employing about 100 people.
Three ultra modern cassava processing centres are being established in each of the three geo-political zones of the state. A model cassava processing and training centre has been completed and commissioned at the Michael Okpara College of Agriculture, Umuagwo, with capacity for processing about 30 metric tones of tubers per day. Ten fully integrated and automated oil palm mills are already operating in the state, while seventeen additional new ones are under construction in various communities of the state. The Graduate Youth Empowerment Scheme at Achara Ubo, Emekuku has taken off. The first batch of trainees have been recruited, trained and deployed. The government disbursed the sum of N15.1m to 15 Fadama Community Associations and Fadama User Groups to enable them finance their various sub-projects.
In infrastructural development, contracts for twenty three road projects measuring about 150 kilometres were awarded in 2005. Out of these, eight roads measuring a total of bout 38 kilometres were completed, while work has reached advanced stage on the remaining ones.
Similar progress was made in the provision of water and electricity, particularly, with the donation of twenty-two generating sets to various rural water supply schemes and twenty eight transformers of various capacities to support rural electrification projects in communities in Imo State. In this case, I want to remind the Honourable Commissioner for Public Utilities that the transformer donated to Okwu Emekuku, in Owerri North Local Government Area is yet to be installed.
More than sixty new primary school blocks were constructed, while twenty were renovated in the education sector. At the secondary school level, five new school blocks were built while 23 were renovated. Contracts were also awarded for the supply of science equipment worth N81m. Equipment worth N10m was procured for the Technical Colleges in the state to ensure their accreditation by the relevant authorities and a site has been identified and surveyed in preparation for the establishment of the College of Engineering of Imo State University at Umunna, Okigwe.
In women and youths empowerment, a total of 23,062 women and youths were empowered through the various activities of the Ministries of Agriculture, Women Affairs and Social Development and Bureau for Poverty Alleviation.
The state government also provided 10,000 FIFA approved standard seats at the Dan Anyiam Stadium, Owerri, which attracted the hosting of Armed Forces and Security Agencies Games in the state, as well as the construction of the ultra modern stadium complex in Orlu
Mr. Ibegwam is a staff of Vanguard Newspapers

Original_Search_Title: Floods in Kalemie Cause Food Insecurity
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: DRC: Floods in Kalemie Cause Food Insecurity
Body_Text:
Kinshasa — Flooding caused by recent heavy rains in Kalemie Territory in Congo's
Katanga Province could cause severe food shortages, a local employee of the
UN Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) has said.
Kasongo Banga, the FAO officer in charge of food security in Kinshasa,
the capital of the Democratic Republic of Congo, said some 720 people faced
shortages as a result of the rain-fed floods that started in early
January.
Banga added that at least 30 ha of cassava and groundnut fields were
inundated with floodwater from the tributaries of the Rugumba River.
Kalemie was already a food-insecure area due to a five-year civil war
that ended in 2003 as well as ongoing sporadic fighting between the
Mayi-Mayi militiamen and the Congolese army. Presently, there are about
120,000 displaced villagers fleeing this fighting in the northern part of
the province.
"Most of the displaced are concentrated in Kalemie," Banga said.
Another reason for the food insecurity in these areas is the lack of
fishing equipment for fishermen around Lake Tanganyika, which has resulted
in the lack of fish in local markets.
"People are now reduced to one square meal a day, rather than the usual
three, and NGOs have opened many centres to care for badly malnourished
children," he added.
The lakeside port town of Kalemie is also threatened by the accumulation
of sand from Lake Tanganyika, following massive waves caused by strong
winds.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United
Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: 2006: And the Gods Spoke to Me
Headline: Nigeria: 2006: And the Gods Spoke to Me
Body_Text:
Owerri — FOR the past three years, I have had feats of epilepsy. These bouts come mostly when there is a festival in view and last as long as the festivity. With the Yuletide cold brought in by the North-East Trade Wind and the New Year speedily at its heel, I became apprehensive that another feat is inevitable. I started praying and fasting that Christmas should be postponed. Arguing that through out this year, I was not able to save up to Ten Thousand Naira (10,000) less than One Hundred United States Dollars ($100). The much-parroted Okonjo Iweala's debt cancellation miracle, has not translated to cash in my pocket and food on my table. Professor Charles Soludo's Ludo game of merging banks to raise Twenty Five Billion Naira capital base, did not take into account the services to be rendered to depositors. We are now back to the days of "Tally Numbers" and the banking halls looking like Oshodi Market. The fear of celebrating my Xmas lunch with "cassavaflakes"(tapioca, abacha) and
groundnut, made it obvious that I was doomed. Xmas came and to my relief, I was hale and hearty, my only problem was that cassavalakes, garri, and other allied products were no where to be found. Careful investigation showed that the Ministry of Agriculture is exporting cassava when Nigerians are dying of hunger.
My left leg started twitching, my nose followed and I had a feeling of unexplainable sensation in my brain. There was a cramp in my head. I feel woozy. I wrench, retch, my tounge was sealed to the roof of my mouth, I fainted and darkness descended. Have you seen the present situation in Nigeria? When will things be better? I hear a sniff and turning, I saw two men. One is clearly dressed in the costume of the Nigerian Army, with a white bandage around his neck. Trailing behind is a lanky angel, wearing a barrette, bearing a bush lamp and ruminants of a broken chain. Thisapparition is wearing a goatee and a Mao-styled dog collar suit. And they saidto me: " The atmosphere in Nigeria is somewhat cloudy. But we do not think there will be rain. The trouble is that Nigerians cannot tell which is a rain cloud and when it is not and as a result, they tend to be confused. The eruption of democratic recklessness in Nigeria that came to a head with the gun battle in the Oyo State Govern
ment House is a mere dress rehearsal of the impending doom cum violence that will grace 2006 to 2008.
It will be remembered that the Mafiaso style
legislative proceedings in the Wild Wild West of the 60s, contributed to Operation DAMISSA. Wont the Ladoja Vs Adedibu war revive the nocturnal exercises in the Nigerian barracks? The heavy use of Khaki boys in the Alameseyeiah opera is an indicator that we are yet to be civil in our democratic disagreements and "big brother" must always supervise the looting. For how long can we keep him abay in the sharing of our father's inheritance? At the mention of DAMISSA, the bandaged Angel, smiled looking at the goated angel, and said, "a job badly done and operation DAMISSA is not a popular option in current Nigeria but not an impossibility". Its unpopularity is based on the misinformed assumption that America is not disposed to a military intervention in Nigeria.This is erroneous.
All the boys in the barracks need do is tie their
interest with that of America and shout "Al-Qaeda's training camp found in Kano", that "there is a threat to revert privatisation policy", "they are intervening to prevent anarchy as a result of the third term bid of the incumbent, Emperor Olusegun Obasanjo the Last". As this third term bid will rock the boat in Niger Delta and the price of crude in the international market will sky rocket. Another disturbing tendency, the goated activist began, is the different handshakes across the different rivers in Nigeria. The South-South/ South-East, South East/South West, South East/Middle belt. These handshakes of the leper, is short fisted as all groups are merely concerned with 2007 elections. The true integration of the nation and the nationalities in Nigeria is not going on. The Southern political groups by their selective gang up, is turning Northern Nigeria into the enemy that must be confronted. This Cowboy-Indian dichotomy, Good guys-bad guys splitting of the Nigerian polity
is dangerous gunpowder. These strange bedfellows are only united to bring home the loot for the intra-ethnic fighting for which group will produce the tenant in Aso Rock. No one is thinking about the minorities in their permutation. They deliberately forget that there are minorities in the North who have not tested power. The power should not go back to North currency is a fake tender. In the past six years, Nigeria under Southern leadership has not done better. Joblessness, homelessness, lawlessness and unconstitutional recklessness of governance are the order of the day.
What we want is a dialogue of the civil groups and ethnic nationalities in Nigeria, to fashion out a way forward based on class struggle. The "War" is not who rules in 207, but the consentisation, militarisation of the masses to demand good governance, demand a means and conducive environment to attain to the zenith of their ability, demand accountability from their leaders. The battlefield is not resource control but accountability of what has been done by the little given to them. As the saying goes, he who is faithful in little will be faithful in much. I think all clamouring for the review of the sharing formula of the "loot",should cross read Alameseyeigha, Peter Odili, James Ibori, etc against their northern counterpart. The text is be Matthew Chapter 25:14-30.

Original_Search_Title: Stakeholders to Shore Up Growth Rate By 10 Percent
Headline: Nigeria: Stakeholders to Shore Up Growth Rate By 10 Percent
Body_Text:
Abuja — THE National Stakeholders meeting on consolidated banks resolved yesterday to shore-up the growth rate of the Nigerian economy by 10 per cent, in addition to creating multiple jobs enough to reasonably reduce the army of unemployed youths in the country.
The meeting revealed government's intention to ban the importation of electronics to encourage local producers, as well as to ensure that the economy subsequently grows by seven per cent.
The meeting presided by President Olusegun Obasanjo saw the participants contributing to the way forward in the renewed spirit of building a strong national economy.
President Obasanjo announced government's intention to ban the importation of completely assembled electronic items and similar consumer goods by July 1 this year.
He warned flour millers in the country to meet the target of 10 per cent cassava content in their flour by July 1 or be shut down. "We have waited for one year and you have not done anything and in sha allah, you will not go beyond the first of July. If you do not do anything, your mill will be closed.
"You have to do something. What have you done to encourage the production of cassava flour? Now, you have extra six months. By first of July, if you are not complying with that regulation, your mill will be closed," he said.
The meeting had in attendance Vice President Atiku Abubakar, national chairmen of the PDP and ANPP, several state governors, ministers, the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) governor, chief executives of the 25 consolidated banks, large and medium scale farmers, industrialists as well as concerned heads of government agencies.
President Obasanjo wondered why items like radio and television sets, refrigerators, and similar items could not be brought in as completely knocked down parts (CKD) and then assembled in Nigeria to provide jobs locally and help the economy.
He announced that as part of the strategy to achieve the 10 per cent economic growth, importation of such fully assembled electronic gadgets would cease as from July 1.
The meeting resolved: "We have to make sure that we have a very reliable monitoring and evaluation system to ensure the success of such which has been assigned to the National Planning Commission and the National Bureau of Statistics that shall report on a quarterly basis to the Consolidated Banks/ Stakeholders Meeting which held its first meeting yesterday.
lThat the next meeting of the above body has been slated for April 4, 2006.
lThat the oil and gas companies should not crowd the internal credit that is available, rather they should go out and bring money.
lThat chief executives of the consolidated commercial banks should meet with those in the agricultural sector like medium and large-scale agriculture, the Agriculture Association of Nigeria, the governors.
lThat stakeholders including the hotels, hospitality people, immigration, taxi drivers and all others who have something to do with tourism will meet to look critically at the tourism sector.
lThat job creation is an important component of this exercise. Therefore, the quarterly reports will be scrutinised to find out how much jobs each sector has added to sectors like agriculture, oil and gas, manufacturing, telecommunications, etc."

Original_Search_Title: Government Publishes Contingency Plan
Headline: Mozambique: Government Publishes Contingency Plan
Body_Text:
Maputo — The Mozambican government on Wednesday presented donors and UN
agencies with its contingency plan, budgeted at between 23.8 and 33.4
million US dollars, for this year's rainy season.
The contingency plans are based on the probability of drought,
flooding and/or cyclones. This year, there is already a flood on the
Zambezi, which is likely to worsen, as more water is discharged from
the Cahora Bassa dam.
There are also fears of flooding elsewhere in central Mozambique, notably in the Pungue and Buzi valleys.
The budgeting is based on a "maximum" and a "minimum " scenario. In
the former, 3.15 million people will be in need of aid, and in the
latter 1.11 million.
The ten million dollar financial difference between the two
scenarios results exclusively from the additional emergency goods
(mostly food aid) that will be required in the "maximum" scenario.
Whereas the minimum scenario envisages emergency goods to a value of
4.46 million dollars, the figure rises to 14.11 million in the maximum
scenario.
Everything else is the same in the two scenarios. 250,000 dollars is
requested for disaster management, and 19.1 million dollars for
"reconstruction and development".
The document distributed at Wednesday's meeting explained that the
government did not alter these sums between the two scenarios, because
"even if no disaster occurs, these funds will be used to prevent and
reduce vulnerability".
The reconstruction and development funds requested break down into
2.1 million dollars for agriculture, a million for health, 4.3 million
for roads, 8.6 million for other public works and housing, 2.5 million
for electricity, and 473,000 for environmental matters.
Unlike the 2004/05 agricultural year, the likelihood is that
rainfall this year will meet all the water requirements of the
country's crops, particularly in the northern and central provinces. In
the second half of the rainy season (January to March), the forecast is
for normal or above normal rainfall in all parts of the country.
The government thus estimates that there will be a significant
increase in food production. It expects a six per cent increase in the
grain harvest, rising from 1.89 million tonnes last year, to just over
two million tonnes. There are also forecasts of a five per cent rise in
cassava production (from 6.63 to 6.96 million tonnes), and a four per
cent rise in the production of beans and other pulses (from 332,000 to
347,000 tonnes).
Despite the current heavy rainfall, the country is still coping with
the effects of last year's drought. Paula Zucula, director of the
National Disasters Management Institute (INGC) pointed out that the
drought affected about 1.15 million people in seven provinces.
Of this figure, about 800,000 need food aid until they can bring in
the next harvest in March. But donors have not responded well to
requests for more food (or the money to buy it), and so far only
500,000 Mozambicans are receiving aid.
This does not mean that the other 300,000 are starving - merely that
the aid is distributed on a rota system, so that everybody receives
something.

Original_Search_Title: Dry Cassava Prices Soar
Headline: Uganda: Dry Cassava Prices Soar
Body_Text:
Kampala — LANGI farmers have a reason to smile as sliced cassava now sells at sh5,000 a basin, up from sh3,000 in November last year.
Ogwal-Acira, a farmer from Nambieso sub-county, Apac, recently said
the sliced and sun-dried cassava was sold in most of the towns in
northern region.
"Some is used for feeding people displaced by the Lord's Resistance Army rebels," he said.
Ogwal said the cassava sells better in Lira, Pader, Gulu, Kitgum and Amolatar towns.
Another farmer, William Okullo-Odora, said the cassava prices rose
due to the high demand for lira-lira (a local potent gin) during the
last Christmas and New Year festivities. Lira-lira is made from cassava.
"The high demand for the local brew forced farmers to increase the prices for cassava slices," Okullo said.
Cassava, which is widely grown in Uganda, is a biennial crop. It
matures between six months to one-and-a-half years, depending on the
variety. Sweet cassava is preferred to the bitter variety.
Okullo said cassava requires well-drained, deep fertile soils. He
said shallow soils are not good for cassava because they encourage
excessive vegetative growth at the expense of the tubers.
He said cassava is drought-resistant and is used as a famine reserve.
He added that planting takes place at the beginning of the main rains at a spacing of 1.5metres by one metre.
"The cutting should be free from any infection like mosaic. We plant
the cuttings vertically or horizontally by burying them in the soil,"
Okullo said.
Cassava's maturity depends on the variety. Harvesting begins between six to eight months.
In some cases the tubers deteriorate quickly, forcing farmers to sell or eat the crop as soon as possible.
Cassava may be sliced, dried and sold in that form or made into
flour. Fresh tubers may be prepared by steaming or boiling and eaten
with sauce.

Original_Search_Title: Failed Banks Owners Are Unforgivable - Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: Failed Banks Owners Are Unforgivable - Obasanjo
Body_Text:
President Olusegun Obasanjo yesterday said that owners of the insolvent banks that failed to meet the N25 billion recapitalisation deadline of December 31,2005 have committed unforgivable sins.
His word: "the owners of the failed banks have committed an unforgivable sin."
The Central Bank of Nigeria, Monday, withdrew the licences of about 14 banks in the country as a result of their failure to meet the deadline issued by the apex bank.
Speaking at the bank's consolidation/stakeholders meeting held at Aso Villa, Obasanjo said however that the CBN owes no apology to the senate over the consolidation exercise provided that the law establishing the apex bank gave right for such restructuring exercise.
At the meeting, the Senate President in his massage to the president delivered by Senator Gbenga Ogunniya, said the senate was in doubt about the legality of the consolidation exercise and questioned 25 mega banks.
Obasanjo said if the exercise carried out by the CBN contradicts the law establishing the banks, he will apologise to the Senate on behalf of the apex bank that the exercise is meant for the goodness of Nigerians and would promote the economic stability of the country.
It will be recalled that the CBN Governor, Professor Charles C. Soludo, said in his speech while withdrawing the licence of the 14 failed banks that the exercise is in conformity with the powers conferred upon it by the Banks and Other Financial Institutions Act.
The president who also spoke on the cassava initiative of his administration, gave July 1st this year as the deadline for all flour millers in the country to use 10 percent cassava or have their factories closed.
He said, the industrialists are not assisting the initiative at the grassroot level, the situation that brought about the delay in its take up.
He said the whole essence of a generate employment and create wealth for Nigerians, stating that the idea build a private/public partnership was to fight poverty.
The president said the meeting will be held every quarter to access the performance of the economy and to find out the contributions of each sector in the area of job creation.
The meeting, which is the first in the history of the Obasanjo administration, witnessed participants from the organized private sector, governors, non governmental organizations, among others.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo: We'll Grow Economy By 10 Percent
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo: We'll Grow Economy By 10 Percent
Body_Text:
Abuja — AN ambitious project to grow the economy by 10 per cent from this year was announced yesterday in Abuja by President Olusegun Obasanjo.
The President who spoke at the consolidated banks stakeholders meeting also gave a six-month grace to flour mills to comply with the inclusion of 10 per cent cassava content in flour production after which defaulting mills would be shut.
Similarly, he said no importers of electronic gadgets would be allowed to bring in complete equipment, but modules as from July 1. The measure is part of new strategies to generate employment.
President Obasanjo explained that setting a new economic growth target was necessary because of what he called the "consistently fair growth" of the economy in the last three years at an average of 3.7 per cent annually.
He said the National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy (NEEDS), which encompasses government's socio-economic reform, was responsible for the successes recorded by the administration in agriculture, telecommunications, reduction in inflation, job creation, poverty eradication and wealth creation.
Obasanjo while noting particularly that the economy could be grown by 10 per cent annually, said if the rate was maintained, the figure could be doubled in seven years.
By so doing, he was optimistic that over 800 per cent growth could be attained within the space of 21 years.
While alleging that flour millers had failed to meet the one-year deadline for them to incorporate 10 per cent cassava into flour production, he vowed any one of them that still failed by July 1, risked closure.
We have waited for one year and you have not done anything Insha Allah you will not go beyond first of July. If you do not do anything, you will be closed. You have to do something. What have you done to encourage the production of cassava flour? Now, you have extra six months. By 1st of July if you are not complying with that regulation, you will be closed", he said.
On the restriction on electronics, the President said he preferred importers to bring them as Completely Knocked Down (CKD) parts to enable further job creation through local assembly of the gadgets.
The meeting also resolved that oil and gas companies should not crowd the internal credits that were available, urging them to source their operational funds from abroad.
The forum, attended by Vice President Atiku Abubakar, top government functionaries, members of the National Assembly, captains of industry and Chief executives of banks, also stressed the need for a very reliable monitoring and evaluation mechanism.
On job creation , the stakeholders resolved that quarterly reports on jobs in all sectors be published while a stakeholders' meeting on tourism was billed for next week.
President Obasanjo listed security, agriculture, infrastructure, consolidation of the financial sector, education, health, food security and nutrition and institutional reforms as some of the areas of focus to achieve the target 10 per cent growth.
In her presentation , Finance Minister, Dr Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala said a single digit inflation rate was targetted for this year while foreign reserves was projected to reach $34 billion or more.
She added that N1.3 trillion was spent in 2004, N1.4 trillion last year and N1.8 trillion or 44 per cent this year under public expenditure.
Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) governor, Prof. Charles Soludo explained that growth drivers would include security of lives and property, sound macro-economic environment, stable prices, lower inflation, stable exchange rate, fight against corruption, infrastructure, power and efficient institutional reforms.
Various speakers including governors, deputy governors, members of the Federal Executive Council (FEC), Economic Management Team (EMT) as well as captains of industry made presentations and asked questions during the interactive session that lasted four hours.
In a related development the Nigeria Deposit Insurance Corporation (NDIC) has concluded plans to secure court order to liquidate banks whose licenses have been revoked.
Daily Champion investigation showed that African Express Bank (AFEX), and Eagle Bank located at Area 8, Garki, Abuja were under lock and key without any police presence while a bullion van was parked at the Eagle Bank's premises.
At Hallmark Bank located near the headquarters of Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC), the complex was open while the main gate into the banking hall was locked. Same was the situation at Trade Bank which has many vehicles parked within its premises.
A visit to the All States Trust Bank Wuze Zone 4, and ACB International Wuze Zone 5, showed activities though not banking services.
Lead Bank located on Herbert Macaulay Way, Central Business Area, Garki, was also shut while the flags of the banks visited were still flying alongside the Nigerian flag.
NDIC spokesman said the corporation will seek court order today to commence liquidation of the 14 failed banks.

Original_Search_Title: Stakeholders' Meeting On Economic Growth Ends in Abuja
Headline: Nigeria: Stakeholders' Meeting On Economic Growth Ends in Abuja
Body_Text:
Abuja — A stakeholders' meeting called by President Olusegun Obasanjo has
ended with a resolve to support the Administration's 10 percent
economic growth target in 2006 and beyond.
The six-hour meeting presided over by the President targeted heads
of the consolidated banks, farmers, exporters and industrialists. In
attendance were State Governors, Ministers, members of the Economic
Management Team and other Government officials.
President Obasanjo opened the meeting by outlining efforts to focus
on and develop the three key employment-driven sectors of the economy.
These are the agricultural, industrial and service sectors, which he stated, would drive the economic revival.
The President described agriculture as the untapped goldmine of the
nation's economy, the largest employer of labour and mainstay of
national economic activity, involving every section of the country. It
is also the largest contributor to the National Gross Domestic product,
hence emphasis would be laid in expanding and consolidating the sector
from its current six percent.
He also identified the industrial sector, which includes oil and gas
and expanded activities through manufacturing and solid minerals
processing, among others.
The third is the service sector, specifically tourism, from which
President Obasanjo says Government would dig in to generate growth and
create employment.
Speaking further the President said the economic growth target has
been set to ensure that the issues of poverty alleviation, food
security, diversification of the economy, job creation, amongst others
are dealt with while government would also strive to double economic
growth after every seven years.
Reading the resolution of the meeting, the President said "we agree
that we want to grow this economy by a minimum of 10 per cent this year
and beyond. We have to make sure that we have a very reliable
monitoring and evaluation system to ensure the success of such which
has been assigned to the National Planning Commission and the National
Bureau of Statistics that shall report on a quarterly basis".
The president who said that under the National Economic Empowerment
Development Strategy (NEEDS) programme of the Administration, the
country had recorded successes in various sectors of the economy in the
last three years, with agriculture recording an average of 7.5 per cent
growth, added that as 2007 approaches and the end of the NEEDS
programme in sight, there is need to evolve a strategy that would build
on the achievements recorded and also to consolidate them.
He said under the NEEDS programme, poverty was has been reduced from
more than 70 per cent to about 54 per cent over the last three years,
but emphasised the need to create more jobs.
"We should set for ourselves a little bit higher targets than the
six per cent growth rate set in the NEEDS document," he said, while
assuring that a 10 percent target growth in the economy was attainable.
He also emphasised on security concerns pledging that he has
directed the Police authorities to improve the situation in the country.
The President emphasized that solutions must be found to agriculture
financing because there was virtually not much farmers could do with
double-digit interest rates.
President Obasanjo also gave flour millers in the country a grace
period of six months, 1 July 2006, within which to include 10 per cent
of cassava flour in wheat flour or have their mills closed.
Aside the main conference, the President also held two additional
meetings with farmers, especially cassava farmers and exporters to
discuss their peculiar problems and seek solutions.
The President announced that the Stakeholders' Forum would be held quarterly in order to review progress.

Original_Search_Title: FG Targets 10 Percent Economic Growth
Headline: Nigeria: FG Targets 10 Percent Economic Growth
Body_Text:
Abuja — Rising from a meeting yesterday with stakeholders in the Nigerian economy, President Olusegun Obasanjo has announced that the federal government will target an economic growth of 10 per cent in 2006 and beyond.
The president said the target has been set to ensure that the issues of poverty aleviation, food security, diversification of the economy, job creation, amongst others are dealt with while government would also strive to double economic growth after every seven years.
While reading out resolutions reached at the Consolidated Banks/Stakeho-lders meeting which would now be held quarterly, Obasanjo stated, "we agree that we want to grow this economy by a minimum of 10 per cent this year and beyond. We have to make sure that we have a very reliable monitoring and evaluation system to ensure the success of such which has been assigned to the National Planning Commission and the National Bureau of Statistics that shall report on a quarterly basis".
He said the National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy (NE-EDS) programme of his administration was designed to end next year, but that there was the need to prolong it.
The president who said that under the NEEDS regime, the country had recorded successes in various sectors of the economy in the last three years, with agriculture recording an average of 7.5 per cent growth, added that as 2007 approaches and the end of the NEEDS programme in sight, there is need to evolve a strategy that would build on the achievements recorded and also to consolidate them.
He suggested that attention should be concentrated more on three vital sectors, which included agriculture, industry and services, adding that these would include the development of oil and gas and manufacturing.
"We should set for ourselves a little bit higher targets than the six per cent growth rate set in the NEEDS document," he said, while assuring that a 10 per cent target growth in the economy was attainable.
He said under the NEEDS programme, poverty was reduced from more than 70 per cent to about 54 per cent over the last three years, but emphasised the need to create more jobs.
"We have had growth, but we still need to create more jobs," the president said, adding that his administration was anxious to create jobs through agriculture.
On security, which he described as an important element of development, the president said that he had directed the police to set up a panel for critical self-evaluation for better service delivery.
The forum brought together governors, local council chairmen, as well as banking and private sector managers to discuss how to further move the Nigerian economy forward.
Other resolutions reached at the meeting are that: Oil and gas companies should not crowd the internal credit that is available, rather they should go out and bring money.
Chief Executives of the consolidated commercial banks should meet with those in the agricultural sector like medium and large-scale agriculture, the Agriculture Association of Nigeria, the Governors.
Stakeholders including the hotels, hospitality people, immigration, taxi drivers and all others who have something to do with tourism will meet to look critically at the tourism sector.
Job creation is an important component of this exercise. Therefore, the quarterly reports will be scrutinised to find out how much jobs each sector has added to sectors like agriculture, oil and gas, manufacturing, telecommunications, etc.
He also stated that the second phase of the banking consolidation will soon begin saying "even with the N25 billion equity, our banks are still closed compared with banks in other countries", and there must be adequate input and assistance to farmers.
While emphasising that solutions must be found to agriculture financing because there was virtually not much farmers could do with double digit interest rates, the President regretted that the solid minerals sector has not made any appreciable impact.
The Minister of Finance, Dr. Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala, on her part said that single digit inflation is being targeted this year, just as foreign reserves are expected to reach $34 billion or more this year.
Giving a breakdown of public expenditures, she said that N1.3 trillion was spent in 2004, N1.5 trillion in 2005 and N1.87 trillion or 44 per cent increase is expected this year.
The Governor of the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), Professor Charles Soludo, while speaking on financing the rapid growth in the economy said that the key areas of focus this year would be on agriculture, oil and gas, industry, housing and services.
He added that the growth drivers would be the security of lives and property, sound macro-economic environment, stable prices, lower inflation, stable exchange rate regime and lower interest rate, infrastructure, power, finance, fight against corruption, efficient institutions for the enforcement of contracts and property rights, amongst others.
The President yesterday also gave flour millers in the country a grace period of six months within which to include 10 per cent of cassava flour in wheat flour or have their mills closed.
While giving the ultimatum, he said, "The wheat producers have failed to meet the conditions we gave to them. We have waited for one year and you have not done anything and insha allah, you will not go beyond the 1st of July. If you do not do anything, your mill will be closed.
"You have to do something. What have you done to encourage the production of cassava flour? Now, you have extra six months. By first of July, if you are not complying with that regulation, your mill will be closed".

Original_Search_Title: Stakeholders' Meeting On Economic Growth Ends in Abuja
Headline: Nigeria: Stakeholders' Meeting On Economic Growth Ends in Abuja
Body_Text:
Abuja — A stakeholders' meeting called by President Olusegun Obasanjo has ended with a resolve to support the Administration's 10 percent economic growth target in 2006 and beyond.
The six-hour meeting presided over by the President targeted heads of the consolidated banks, farmers, exporters and industrialists. In attendance were State Governors, Ministers, members of the Economic Management Team and other Government officials.
President Obasanjo opened the meeting by outlining efforts to focus on and develop the three key employment-driven sectors of the economy.
These are the agricultural, industrial and service sectors, which he stated, would drive the economic revival.
The President described agriculture as the untapped goldmine of the nation's economy, the largest employer of labour and mainstay of national economic activity, involving every section of the country. It is also the largest contributor to the National Gross Domestic product, hence emphasis would be laid in expanding and consolidating the sector from its current six percent.
The President also identified the industrial sector, which includes oil and gas and expanded activities through manufacturing and solid minerals processing, among others.
The third is the service sector, specifically tourism, from which President Obasanjo says Government would dig in to generate growth and create employment.
Aside the main conference, the President also held two more meetings with farmers, especially cassava farmers and exporters to discuss their peculiar problems and seek solutions.

Original_Search_Title: Lack of Funds Threatens Cassava Production in Katsina
Headline: Nigeria: Lack of Funds Threatens Cassava Production in Katsina
Body_Text:
As many rural farmers of Katsina state embrace gove-rnment's initiative of boas-ting cassava production, lack of funds is threatening their capacity to acquire working tools, water and sufficient labour.
Our correspondent who visited some villages inclu-ding Yar Shauya, Gangara, Sabon Garin Alhaji Yahuza and Tsohon Garin Fadama communities in Batagarawa local government noticed a number of dry wells with crops facing serious drought as a result of insufficient water in the farms while hope of obtaining loan from the Nigeria Agricultural and Rural Cooperative Bank (NARCB), still remains a mirage.
At the Yar Shauya village where most of the people cultivate cassava, farmers have expressed the fear that they may be unable to meet government target in cassava production as a result of insufficient funds.
Chairman of the Cassava Farmers Association, Ado Musa Yar Shauya said, that despite that the association deposit over N500,000 with the NARCB as 10 per cent requirement for securing the loan for cassava production over two months ago, nothing has been heard from the bank.
He, therefore, appealed to the bank to assist them to secure the loan in good time to enable them cultivate additional hectares of land as soon as the loan is obtained.
Furthermore, he said, the loan would also enable them secure more seedlings, undertake harrowing, and obtain pumping machines and boreholes to ensure sufficient watering of the crops for the desired level of cassava production.

Original_Search_Title: "Harvesting Rain Water Will Help Check Erosion Menace"
Headline: Nigeria: "Harvesting Rain Water Will Help Check Erosion Menace"
Body_Text:
Owerri — IT has been suggested that one of the major ways of arresting the
rampaging gully erosion, which has continued to threaten several communities
in Imo State, is through harvesting rain water.
A gubernatorial aspirant and former member of the Federal House of
Representatives, Chief Chukwuemeka Nwajiuba, who made the suggestion in an
interview with Vanguard in his Ehime Mbano local government area country
home, also revealed that all the programmes he intends to pursue if given
the opportunity, have been tested.
"I harvest rain water in this house, store it in an underground tank,
treat it and distribute same for family use. I also use part of the rain
water to irrigate my farm. The point I am making here is that if I was not
harvesting the rain water, those living behind me would have been sacked a
long time ago by gully erosion", Nwajiuba said.
Although he did not immediately give an idea of what he spent in the
water-harvesting project, Nwajiuba however reasoned that it was definitely
cheaper than the cost of relocating either a family or community sacked by
rampaging erosion.
On his plan for agriculture, the governorship aspirant equally said he
does not believe in mere theories but leading by example.
"I have already shown that every family can begin a small agricultural
project in their homes. I have a piggery, poultry, palm plantation, some
stands of varying cash crops in my small compound. I am rearing grass
cutter, just to prove that it is possible to keep such an animal in a living
home", he explained.
Answering a question, Nwajiuba said he only uses the droppings of the
pigs, goats, sheep and poultry as natural manure for his palm, cash crops,
plantain,banana and cassava farm.
"Imo State should not have any business importing food from
anywhere. The farmers can be effectively grouped according to their areas of
interests and given the necessary farming inputs and guidance.
We have the land and manpower. What I am not too sure we have, is the
correponding policy and will power on the part of government to achieve
these goals", Nwajiuba said.
It was his considered opinion that for the state to make meaningful
progress in all sectors, there ought to be a clean break from the usual
practice of recycling old hands.
"These old hands have been tested. We have seen their output. It would
not be a crime to bring in people with fresh ideas, people who are very
prepared to render service to the citizenry, people who are not more
interested in lining their pockets instead of rendering total and dedicated
service to Imo people", he said.

Original_Search_Title: Irish Potatoes Supply Good
Headline: Uganda: Irish Potatoes Supply Good
Body_Text:
Part of Lake Victoria basin received some rain, especially Kampala,
Mpigi and Mukono areas that is thought to have ended the long drought.
There is good supply of Irish potatoes from all producing areas of
Kisoro, Singo and Kabale.
On average, a sack of approximately 120kg was offered at Shs22,000 -
25,000 depending on the point of sale.
Matooke supply was moderate this week with an average bunch weighing
8-12 kg going for between Shs3,500 and Shs5,000. Other fresh commodities
like cassava and sweet potatoes reduced in supply although their prices
remained constant.
Foreign traders
Foreign traders from Kenya and Tanzania are ferrying huge volumes of
maize and beans and its reported that their presence is one of the reasons
for the high price in the grain sector.
New maize is highly moistened at averagely 17 percent and is going for
Shs285-290 while old stock grain sold is selling at Shs310/kg
Iganga: New maize harvest is beginning to appear on the market from in
and around the district. However, it is evident that the yield was highly
affected by the drought period.
Currently, grain supplied is highly moistened but this has not had any
effect on the market price. Maize is fetching Shs220 per kilo at wholesale
in the central market.
Meanwhile, the beans market registered very little supply, which
explains the high price offered for Kahuura and Nambale beans at Shs600-
700/kg.
Luweero: The weather in this area remains very hostile with no rain,
high temperatures and dust. The most readily available foodstuff - maize
flour - has had an increase in price from Shs600 to Shs700/kg.
This is attributed to a rise in the price of maize grain following a
reduction in supply coupled with high demand as schools prepare to open for
first term.
Lira: Commodities like maize and millet registered a price increment
mainly because of high demand for some of these commodities as schools stock
for the new term.
Demand for green grams is high with a kilo offered at Shs900 while at
wholesale, it sold for Shs1,000/ kg. A lot of simsim is being transported to
Kampala by traders at Shs950 wholesale price while grams have found a big
demand in Busia.
Matooke from Amach is selling at a very high price of Shs35,000-40,000 a
sack. A sizeable bunch weighing 12kg sold at Shs7,500 retail and Shs5,000 at
wholesale price. However, demand for the commodity is still low due to high
prices.
Mbale: The area has looming drought conditions and supply of fresh
produce has fallen tremendously. Irish potatoes from Kumi were supplied low
due to the main market. A kilo of Irish sold for Shs400 at wholesale. Kaiso
rice from Bunyole was offered at Shs800/kg wholesale. Maize is currently
selling at Shs300/kg.
Arua: The market situation has been characterised with high prices since
Christmas. Demand for most commodities is high as a result of high demand
for different items across the Sudanese border. Commodities such as sweet
potatoes and pineapples have good market south of Sudan. A 100-kilo sack
sold at Shs100,000 while pineapples were offered very expensively. This has
caused shortage of these items in Arua markets and traders further
penetrated into the rural areas for more supply.
Kibaale: The price of groundnuts rose this week from Shs1,100 to
Shs1,300/ kg. This is due to increased demand from Kampala where traders are
ferrying commodities.
However, in general, the demand for most commodities is low coupled with
low supply in the market.
The availability of cereals in the market area is low, which has led to
a high price for the same. It is noted that beans were offered cheapest at
wholesale across at Shs350 a kilo of Kanyebwa. Yellow beans went for
Shs500/kg wholesale.
Soroti: The weather remains very hot dry and windy in all parts of Teso
sub-region, however, there were signs of some rain in some parts on Sunday
evening and Monday afternoon.
Cassava fresh supply has dropped due to the hastle weather, which has
made it very hard to extract it from the hardened ground.
A heap sold at Shs500 in the main market while outside the market, it
went for Shs300/kg. Dry cassava chips went for Shs200/kg. Farm gate price
remains at Shs180/kg and a basin is now at Shs4,000 - 4,500 in the rural
markets.
A heap of sweet potatoes stabilised at Shs93/kg at retail. This
registered an increase in the market price due to reduced supply as compared
to the previous week, which indicates that the season is coming to a
close. A full sack is now going for between Shs5,000 - Shs7,000.
Tororo: Prices for most commodities were constant this week. A few items
such as maize grain, bean, cassava chips and flour had price changes.
Maize grain price increased by Shs10 at wholesale from Shs240 to
Shs250. Beans increased both at wholesale and retail from Shs700 and Shs800
respectively to Shs800 and Shs900.Cassava chips and flour rose to Shs230 and
Shs280 respectively.
Kabale: Harvesting is still going on although it met effective demand
from intending buyers. Prices rose to Shs600/kg for medium sized beans while
mixed beans sold for Shs550/kg. There is anticipated demand for schools'
food requirement plus the cross border Rwanda and Congolese markets.
Maize is currently going for Shs250/kg.

Original_Search_Title: Needs Are Dropping, But Aid Dropping Faster, WFP
Headline: Angola: Needs Are Dropping, But Aid Dropping Faster, WFP
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Despite encouraging growth in Angola's agricultural production and
declining numbers of people in need, food insecurity remains a problem in
many parts of the country, says the World Food Programme (WFP).
In its latest regional food security bulletin the Famine Early Warning
Systems Network (FEWS NET) said official figures indicated that agricultural
production had increased significantly in the 2004/05 season.
The production of maize, a staple food, rose 33 percent from 600,000 mt
in 2003/04 to 800,000 mt in 2004/05, and agriculture contributed 12 percent
to Angola's GDP, up from eight percent the previous year.
However, "despite these improvements, production in 2004/05 still fell
short of national requirements and pockets of food insecurity and high
levels of malnutrition have been identified," FEWS NET noted. Coupled with
the scaling-down of humanitarian activities in Angola, particularly WFP
operations, there were "growing concerns that food insecurity and
malnutrition may rise".
WFP country director Rick Corsino told IRIN, "There is certainly an
important and general improvement in agricultural production. However, there
are still large areas throughout the [central] Planalto region and back to
eastern Moxico and Kuando Kubango provinces [in the southeast of the
country] where food security remains very much an issue. Since 2003 there
have been three consecutive years of increasingly better harvests, but the
deficit at national level is still there, and - equally if not more
important - there's disparity between areas."
"The question is how much can people afford to consume? The
accessibility of food is certainly in question here," Corsino added. "In a
country like Angola, with serious transport constraints, parts of the
country will produce a food surplus but the costs of moving this to deficit
areas is extremely high."
In the next agricultural marketing year the Angolan government aims to
achieve self-sufficiency in cereal and cassava production and stop all maize
imports, but Corsino said this was "highly optimistic and would require a
leap in agricultural production".
WFP plans to end its general food distributions in the next few months
and focus on targeted interventions, such as its school-feeding programme,
for the remainder of 2006 but donor support remains sluggish. "In 2004 we
received $85 million, and about $20 million in 2005 - I would be surprised
if 2006 is significantly better than 2005," Corsino said.
Although needs continued to drop due to improved agricultural production
and the successful resettlement of returning refugees, "donor contributions
[to aid programmes] are dropping faster", he concluded.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United
Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Sharing Crucial Information with Farmers
Headline: Tanzania: Sharing Crucial Information with Farmers
Body_Text:
Arusha — Globalization of markets for high value agricultural products is creating dynamic markets for competitive producers both at national, regional and international levels. This is why an ambitious country-wide initiative is underway to, among others, ensure maximum compliance to export requirements of the country's agricultural products. A local research outfit in Arusha believes this would contribute to sustainable economic growth.
A unique workshop on High Value Agriculture Export Competitiveness, which the Arusha regional commissioner Mr. Mohammed Babu appeared to describe as long overdue, was held in Arusha recently. Indeed long over due.
True to his assertion, Tanzania has the potential to processing and exporting high-value agriculture - fresh fruits, flowers and vegetables - and generate millions of dollars of annual export earnings - as well as to create more rural jobs and eliminate poverty.
Tanzania has a total land area of about 1 million sq. km. endowed with many natural resources. About 10.8 million hectares are under cultivation out of potential 44 million hectares . The main crops grown in the country include maize, sorghum, rice, beans, pulses, millet, cassava, sweet potatoes, oil seeds, fruits, vegetables cashew nuts, cotton, coffee, tobacco, sisal, and flowers, most of them being cash and high value crops.
But this potential has so far not added up. And the regional 'governor' wants action. Something that would positively add up something to our activities on high value agriculture for export markets.
And he is getting the necessary support, which recently came by way of an initiative towards that desired direction. It is, he said, "an indication of good progress as far as the development of high value agriculture is concerned in the country".
Mr. Babu was referring to a workshop he had just opened, the first (national) High Value Agriculture (HVA-Net) workshop on high value agriculture held in the country.
According to the TPRI Director General, Dr. Gratian Bamwenda, the initiative targets both small, medium and large-scale farmers of High Value Agriculture, which include agricultural products such as fruit, nuts, herbs, flowers and vegetables with a view of making them more competitive in the globalised market.
The ultimate goal of HAV-Net initiative, according to Dr. Bamwenda, is to set up a fully fledged coordinating centre in Tanzania to support farmers of high-value agriculture.
"Our partners in this initiative include farmers, producers, researchers, regulators, decision makers, public sectors and the developing partners", said Dr. (Mrs) Roshan Abdallah, also from Arusha-based TPRI.
In Tanzania, high value agriculture industry is the most dynamically growing sector which needs attention, urgently. The high value agriculture is usually a small to medium scale but of high quality and high priced commodities. The industry is dominated by small to medium scale farm holders, and it forms a major source of foreign income.
For instance take the horticultural products export earnings.They increased from US $14 million in 2004 to $ 19 million as for August 2005. (Arusha and Kilimanjaro lead the way in this field).
Dr. (Mrs) Roshan and Dr. Bamwenda say the high value agriculture production also contributes to rural development in terms of provision of nutritious foods, jobs, incomes, a widening of the tax base, wealth creation and human resource development through training.
HVA-Net initiatives, reiterate the importance of high value agriculture and the need to strengthen the knowledge, technical capacity, and investments in this area, and the need to provide it with supportive and enabling environment.
The TPRI scientists believe that now than ever before there is an urgent need to have organizations, such as HVA-Net, that will share knowledge and information with our farmers, help them to strengthen their technical and investment capacity, and provide them with requisite technological and marketing innovations that will enhance their production capacity and market access. " With HVA-Net, this is exactly the gist of the whole initiative".
Today, only high quality commodities are traded on the international markets. The importers/retailers in importing countries have equivocally said that they will source high value agricultural produce only from countries/producers that will comply with their public and commercial standards, and supply systems.
"To maintain competitiveness, our growers must produce consistently at the highest quality level, respond rapidly to changing consumer and market demands, and be constantly innovating in their production, processing, and trading systems", says Dr. Roshan Abdallah, TPRI's Director of Technical Services.
The workshop on HVA has been helpful in providing an opportunity for stakeholders to discuss and brainstorm on the constraints and propose solutions to the various related issues including a strategizing on how the Tanzania's high value agricultural sector should prepare itself so that it can fulfill basic international requirements and agricultural codes of practice by 2006 and how to mobilize and develop the capacity of farmer groups to adhere to and comply with new good agricultural practices, international protocols, new legislation, and new consumers' demands and requirements.

Original_Search_Title: Roads: Ogunlewe Seeks Alternative Funding
Headline: Nigeria: Roads: Ogunlewe Seeks Alternative Funding
Body_Text:
Lagos — Works Minister, Senator Adeseye Ogunlewe, has said the creation of designated new sources of funds for road maintenance at rural and state levels would go a long way in reducing some of the problems faced by government in that regard.
The minister, made the remarks, while receiving the new President of the Nigerian Society of Engineers (NSE), Engr. Emeka Muoma Ezeh, along with members of the Ghana Institute of Engineers (GIE), who were in the country for a workshop on making all Nigerian roads motorable.
Ogunlewe observed that most of the agricultural activities and agro-related industries in the country were based in the rural areas, but that the roads were either underdeveloped or non existent.
He said the administration of President Olusegun Obasanjo was determined to reverse the trend and ensure good roads at all levels of Government, Federal, State and Local, particularly in the Niger Delta region which produced the bulk of the nation's foreign exchange earner, petroleum.
The minister added that the provision of such good roads will enhance economic activities as well as douse agitation and youth restiveness.
Ogunlewe observed that if the nation had been blessed with a long, focused administration over a reasonable period of time, like 10 years, the abundant resources of the country would have been more effectively harnessed.
Noting that under the administration of President Olusegun Obasanjo, the nation was now one of the largest exporters of Cassava, and that Thailand was earning over $2.6 billion annually from Cassava export, Ogunlewe recalled the historical and administrative antecedents between Nigeria and Ghana, and expressed the hope that Engineers from the two countries could work together on the implementation of the proposed Road Funds in Nigeria. The Minister revealed that Nigerians consumed 38 million litres of petrol daily nationwide because of the huge number of vehicles on Nigerian roads and the love of Nigerians for roads travel.
He stated that with increasing vandalisation of oil pipelines in the Niger Delta, there was a need to find alternative sources of funding roads maintenance instead of the present reliance on budgetary allocation, which derived mainly from crude oil exports revenue.
Speaking earlier, the NSE President, Engr. Emeka Ezeh observed that the challenge before Engineers and Nigerians was the need for people to accept that good roads must be paid for.
He noted that there were political undertones inherent in the issue which must be recognized and addressed. He said the responsibility of engineers was to provide the technical information that would enable politicians to take the right decisions.
He stated: "the truth is that there is no free lunch anywhere. If you want good roads, somebody has got to pay for it, and the way to pay for it is to ensure that there is a regular source of income".
"However, the fear of all Nigerians is how to ensure that the funds set aside are judiciously and transparently used. At tomorrow's workshop the experts from the Ghana Road Funds, the Ghana Central Due Process Procurement Board, the three elements in procurement process, are here to assure Nigerians that there is a way out. They are going to share their experiences and that would provide a basis of professional support. And, in due course, Nigerians will accept the reality that we must pay for good roads", he said.
On the delegation of the Ghana Institution of Engineers were: Engr. Dr. Robert E. Adjaye, President Central Tenders Review Board, Engr. Kwabena Safo-Debrah, President-Elect of GHIE (Ghana Institution of Engineers), Engr. Togbi Kponku III, Executive Committee Members, Engr. Kwesi Abbey-Sam, former Chief Director - Ministry of Roads and Transport and Chairman Procurement Board, who will be keynote speaker at the workshop, Engr. Mohammed Salifu - GHIE nominee to the Road Fund Board, Engr. Mrs. C. D. Bosu-Chedid, current Executive Secretary and Engr. Kingsford Amoah, Director of Ghana Road Fund Secretariat.

Original_Search_Title: Food Security is a Sham
Headline: Sierra Leone: Food Security is a Sham
Body_Text:
No Sierra Leonean would go to bed hungry by 2007 is one of the failed
promises that the political leaders have been trading and defending
since 2002. "Food security is a reality" is what they always say even
though millions nationwide struggle to afford a meal a day, and
thousands spend their days fasting intermittently. Even the single
meal many boast of is the opposite of a balanced diet. Therefore
people hardly discuss obesity in Sierra Leone.
The Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security is full of experts
(book worms) who have been designing all sorts of initiatives -
cassava initiative, potato initiative, rice initiative, you name it
to achieve food security by 2007.
These initiatives appear to be much ado about little although they
attract millions of dollars in the form of loans and donations form
the World Bank, African Development Bank and Foreign governments.
The realities customers tell in Mamba Point restaurant and what goes
on in backyards refute any claim that food security is almost
achievable several people hardly afford more than a thousand leones
plate of rice for the day. Families go for 88 (sprinkling palm oil on
cooked rice). Food security is therefore best described as food
insufficiency.
Having failed in achieving the benchmarks of their food security
programme, political leaders are shamelessly asking families to
substitute rice with cassava, sweet potato, maize etc in their weekly
menus. If you discuss this with housewives and cooks, they will tell
you that rice is cheaper to prepare because any many breadwinners
earn for less than the need for the month. Also, rice is the staple
food with gari coming next to it.
Lyrics go about shouting that people should have backyard gardens and
the like. If all of us bury our heads in agriculture who will teach
the children or run the offices?
Mr. And Mrs. politician, do not confuse the people with the fake
statistics you use to convince donors and further indebt the nation.
Your success stories for which you are applauded in international
meetings do not reflect reality. Children in the remand home and
approved school for instance are yet to be regularly and properly
fed. Students too continue to talk of 001 (a meal a day) instead of
111 (three meals a day). Where is the food security then?
Food security is not just about increase in food productivity and
decrease in importation. It also includes affordability and
sustainability. All of these affect the supply and price of food in
the interest of the poor. Food security is for the poor.
The rich are always food secured since they can afford the number of
meals they need a day.
Dr. Sama Mondeh, Minister of Agriculture and Food Security is more
than ever before zealous to engage the media on the issue of food
security. The Minister spends sleepless nights just to tell Sierra
Leoneans that homegrown food is flooding homes and cats and dogs will
start celebrating birthdays on mounds of left over. Is he doing this
to tell Solo B that he needs another term in office come 2007?
Did the minister say rice importation has fallen by 40%? "Man den nor
gladi oh, do ya nor torment we oh," (people are not happy, please
don't disturb us) has been the response of many on the decrease in
rice importation. A cup of rice is still sold for Le350 and Le65,000
being the least for a bag of rice.
Sierra Leone is at the bottom of the Human Development Index with
per-capital income still standing at less than $300 a day.
Freetown is drowning in rubbish but this cannot be linked to increase
in food productivity and affordability. Population explosion rather
