than bumper harvest is one of the factors responsible for the filth
scattered all over the city.
Political leaders are often prescribed as master farmers when what
they actually do is subsistent farming. All People's Congress leaders
in the 70s and 80s were also master farmers though people queued in
the sun for hours for few cups of rice. Today there are no queues but
people go to bed hungry.
Food security is becoming an electioneering strategy necessary to
gain millions of dollars before the 2007 election. Time will tell who
will use it to his advantage. Micro credit did it for the 2002
election and food security perhaps will do likewise this time.

Original_Search_Title: Public Agenda: Aha, LAC Again!
Headline: Liberia: Public Agenda: Aha, LAC Again!
Body_Text:
The Liberian Agriculture Company (LAC) has been in the news for some
time now. Nothing really good is being said about the company; rather it has
been associated with displacing thousands of locals in districts number
three and four in Grand Bassa County. It has appeared like LAC is the devil
in the lives of the people of Grand Bassa County.
Recently, there have been reports so discouraging and damning that LAC
is engaged in child labor, slavery, and the exploitation of locals. Six
human rights organizations have raised alarm that a situation so desperate
and appalling exits in districts #3 and #4 where LAC's concessionaire is
heavily operated.
Six human rights organizations: Green Advocates, Foundation for
International Dignity (FIND), Center for Democratic Empowerment (CEDE),
Foundation for Human Rights and Democracy (FOHRD), the Catholic Justice and
Peace Commission (JPC) and the Liberia Democracy Watch (LDW) have raised the
necessary alarm.
They have sounded the caveat that something dangerous is obtaining at
LAC.
What is surprising and shocking is that the government is aware of what
is going on and nothing is being done to remedy the crisis. Superintendent
Robert Vonyeagar is referring to his own people as squatters on the land
their forefathers left for them. In a sense, Vonyeagar is saying to LAC
"they wrong you.
They are in violation of the laws. You can do whatever you wish and you
would be right." That is outrageous!
That should be last thing for a superintendent to say of his
people.
But whether Vonyeagar says it or not, what LAC is accused of doing to
the locals is far more volatile, fluid, dangerous, and counter-productive
than the hard earned peace we enjoy now. The locals have been patient and
that should not be construed as weakness.
Yes, one may argue that the late President William V.S. Tubman gave - or
rather waived - to the Liberian Agricultural Company 300,000 acres of land
that Liberians are living on. This is the land they depend on to farm, to
grow cash crops, and develop their families. That is the land that Tubman
gave to a concessionaire. All of that was in the name of open door policy, a
policy that has helped in some ways to crumple the structures of the
country.
The photos Green Advocates showed to the press bear unpleasant
scenes. Can you imagine a woman with baby on her back carrying two buckets
full of latex, one on her head and the other being carried in one hand? See
children who Green Advocates sources said are far below 18 years doing an
adult's job? And what is dangerous for these workers - some are just casual
laborers, contractors - is the fact that ammonia is being used on the latex,
according to our information.
While it is true that concession agreements signed by past regimes are
binding on the present, it is also true that there are means to review all
agreements government entered into with companies. The LAC concession
agreement is no exception. There must be a way to save lives of the
locals. This is not a matter of what an individual can get to lick his/her
elbow, but it is a matter of patriotism and love of all the citizens.
We have abhorred the use of militia groups in the past. Why now that we
would permit LAC to use militia to suppress the citizens of districts #3 and
#4. At least this is what we have been told. And this allegation has basis
because those people LAC calls PPF are a metamorphosed Taylor's
militia.
Some time ago, at the instance of LAC, some 93 locals were arrested,
humiliated and detained in appalling conditions in Buchanan, Grand Bassa
County.
Elders and the young men of District #3 were placed into a cell at the
police station in Buchanan, and when the cell was jam-packed, the rest were
placed under a scourging sun for no just reason. Their only crime was that
they refused to vacate their land to allow LAC to expand and uproot their
crops.
Their traditions and customs were violated and the government did
nothing to bring LAC to book. It now appears LAC is above the law. Or the
government is too insensitive to act on behalf of the people.
LAC's contention that it paid compensation to some of the locals is not
sufficient. The company should use the proper authority and appropriate
means to settle this problem. It is not a matter giving US$100 to each group
of people to abandon the cocoa, coffee, rice farm, cassava farm, plum trees,
coconut trees, and all other things that the people have lived by over the
years.
The Ministry of Agriculture has prices per tree or square yard for
damaged cash crops. For instance, one must pay US$25.00 for a premature
rubber tree and US$50.00 for a mature one. That goes for cocoa, kola,
coconut, plum, citrus, avocado, bread fruit, plantain, pineapple (per square
yard), sugar cane (per square yard) and cassava (per square yard). Has LAC
conformed to that? No. At least there no record to show that LAC has
proceeded on that route, but the company expects the locals to pack off and
leave behind for destruction all their cash crops that they have suffered
for so much over the years. That is wicked to say the least.
My plea is simple: the government must intervene.
Meanwhile, the human rights groups should fight the cause of the
locals.

Original_Search_Title: Slavery, Child Labor, Exploitation at LAC
Headline: Liberia: Slavery, Child Labor, Exploitation at LAC
Body_Text:
The alleged evasion of laws, earth scourge, use of children to do
adults' work, displacements of thousands of locals in districts number 3 and
4 by the Liberian Agriculture Company (LAC) continues to generate more
concerns amongst human rights advocates.
Six human rights and pro-democracy organizations presented at a press
conference a classic demonstration of how LAC has razed villages to the
ground, destroyed the cash crops, including cassava farms, and how it is
heavily indulged in the use children as rubber tapers, etc.
Green Advocates, Foundation for International Dignity (FIND), Center for
Democratic Empowerment (CEDE), Foundation for Human Rights & Democracy
(FHRD), Catholic Justice and Peace Commission (JPC), and the Liberia
Democracy Watch (LDW) showed photo clips of what could be described as urban
slavery.
Already, the Superintendent of Grand Bassa County Robert Vonyegar has
referred to his citizens as "squatters" in a letter, dated December 27, he
wrote to the Administrative Manager, Cllr. Alexander G. Attia, Sr.
But the human rights advocates have rejected Mr. Vonyegar's description
of the citizens of district number 3 and 4, who they said are the original
owners of the land that LAC is claiming.
The agriculture company's claim of the 300,000 acres of land was based
on a concession agreement in which William V.S. Tubman mortgaged the
ancestral land of the people districts #3 & #4.
During press conference yesterday the human rights advocates showed
photos of a woman with baby on her back with two buckets full of latex,
holding one in her hand and another on her head.
The press conference was jointly held with two spokesmen of districts #3
and #4, Fred D. Tuku for #3, and Barkon Nyannankpe for #4.
Children said to be below 18 years were also seen in photo working,
something the human rights lawyers/advocates said is a flagrant violation of
the Constitution of Liberia and the convention on the Rights of the Child,
both of which forbid child labor.
Any human being below 18 years of age is considered a child under the
law.
The photo clips also show LAC has razed villages and destroyed cash
crops belonging to the locals, including cassava farms, rice farms,
etc.
"LAC continues to increase the suffering of the citizens of districts
number three and four," Atty. Alfred Brownell said.
A statement issued by the human rights advocates discussed the LAC
situation in segments, dealing with the general scenario, detailing the
situation on the ground in District #3 in Grand Bassa County.
The statement also dealt with Environment problems, labor issues, and
the circumstances surrounding the death of Morris Zogbor, the LAC worker
whose death led to the present crisis in the area.
Seven persons are already under detention in Buchanan and charged with
murder.
"We would draw attention to the role of the Liberian Government in this
very important matter which has bearing on the conditions necessary to build
lasting peace and stability, as well as the survival of those citizens and
the environmental implications arising there from," said the statement.
According the human rights advocates, the situation of the people in
district number three and four are especially grave since "LAC has a militia
force which the company has used regularly to inflict mayhem and arsons on
the local people, conduct arrests and imprisonment within their own private
prison".
Such private militia is illegal, they contend, and added that runs
counter to the Constitution of Liberia.
"LAC's militia conduct search and destroy operations from village to
village in a drive to forcibly evict residents, while stealing properties of
villagers including rice and chickens," the advocates alleged.
What seems to be alarming about the allegations is that the advocates
said that LAC's militia works in cohort with elements of the Liberia
National Police to perpetrate violence against the locals.
UNMIL, they said, has taken "see no evil hear no evil" position, despite
the fact the locals have lodged their complaints to UNMIL in the
region.
The Liberian Agriculture Company is said to be engaged in the burning
down and destruction of villages to expedite its expansion project.
As a result of the expansion of its plantation across the Zloh Creek in
Zeewein Clan in Distrct #3, the villages of Gbainfein, Zoewron, Flojoe,
Gbor, and Nahn have been burned down by orders of the management of
LAC.
"Cash crops have been destroyed without prior informed consent and
compensation, as well as forceful eviction of villagers from their ancestral
lands.
"Streams, creeks and other drinking water sources have been poisoned,"
the human rights agents said.
They also alleged that LAC's operatives indulge in forcing residents to
make confession, and that had led to the torturing of several locals.
The latest victim is identified as Junior David, who was reportedly
tortured for five hours under the tropical heat in an effort to implicate
him and the people of Taykon and Zeezhone villages in the death of a hired
LAC contract worker, the late Morris Zogbor.
Morris, a native of Cape Mount County, reportedly died during a
conflict. His wife, Comfort, his half brother Junior Madawy, and his sister
Musu, in Compound #3, who talked to Green Advocates on video camera, did not
show signs of believing LAC's version of how Morris died.
Comfort challenged the story that her husband was killed by the
locals. She said it appeared her husband may have drowned to death. She
suspects foul play and is calling for an independent investigation.
Others interviewed by the Green Advocates, according to the statement,
corroborated Comfort's assertion.
On the environmental issues, the advocates noted that "the conduct of
LAC operations in District #3 has created a number of environmental problems
that are in clear violation of the environmental and public health laws of
Liberia."  The group added that the primary and secondary forests,
watersheds and water catchments areas, bush on steep slopes have been cut
clear.
As a result, creeks and estuaries are subject to the direct rays of the
sun leading to faster evaporation, especially during dry season.

Original_Search_Title: 200,000 Tons of Cassava for Export
Headline: Nigeria: 200,000 Tons of Cassava for Export
Body_Text:
Abuja — A Nigerian agro-allied firm has secured contract for the supply of
200,000 metric tons of cassava chips to the United States, at a price of
$250 per ton.
The development is an indication of increasing demand for Nigerian
cassava in the world market, with some African nations plagued by drought
and famine seeking Nigeria's cooperation in the area of cassava
production.
Minister for Commerce and Industry, who is also the Chairman,
Presidential Comm-ittee on Cassava Export Prom-otion, Ambassador Idris
Waziri, said this in Abuja. while addressing members of the Cassava
Committee .
He said already, the Federal Government had received a formal letter of
request from the government of Zimbabwe, for a study visit to Nigeria on
cassava.
Waziri said the request was as a result of intensive local and
international campaign carried out by the Committee.
The Presidential Committee on Cassava Export Promotion was set up in
March 2004, with a mandate to ensure increased production, processing,
packaging and export of cassava-related products, to satisfy both domestic
and export markets.
The vision was to make cassava a major non-oil foreign exchange earner,
generate employment, reduce poverty and enhance food security.
Giving an insight into the progress recorded so far by the committee,
Waziri said "the response by Nigerians was very enthusiastic. Farmers have
responded positively by increasing production, while many entrepreneurs have
planned investments in cassava processing, for domestic and export
markets.
According to the committee, Cassava utilisation both for human
consumption and industrial use had tremendously increased, and the erstwhile
cassava glut had disappeared, adding that farmers are now experiencing boom
in cassva production."

Original_Search_Title: Some Areas in Rakai Have Not Got Rain for 8 Months
Headline: Uganda: Some Areas in Rakai Have Not Got Rain for 8 Months
Body_Text:
Kampala — There have been reports on BBC Focus on Africa programmes over the
last few weeks that over 11 million people in the Horn of African
countries of Somalia, Ethiopia, Eritrea, Kenya and Djibouti are on the
verge of starvation due to persistent drought.
In Kenya, it was reported that President Mwai Kibaki declared a
state of emergency and requested for aid for over 2.5 million people
terribly hit by the lack of rains in many months.
When I visited several parts of Rakai district in Central Uganda
last week, I concluded that Uganda needs to appeal for support soon,
before the people there reach the dire proportions of those in the Horn
of Africa.
People in this area have not seen rains for the last eight months. And they are still counting.
I found several cattle keepers in Kyotera, Kooki, Kakuuto and Mutukula on the move looking for ponds to water their animals.
"We are in serious trouble," said an elderly man who said his name is Titus.
"We have so far been to three water holes, but the water has not been enough for the cows .look at them, look at the bones."
The watering holes were all empty and cows were fighting over the stagnant mud that remains of what was once a deep well.
The drought has also affected farmers. Many crops, which were planted in anticipation of the rains, have all dried up.
"We gave farmers planting materials like maize seeds and beans, but
people are telling us that all the crops have dried up and that they
need food," said Steven Ssevume, a community development facilitator
with World Vision in Kyebe sub-county.
"We may have to plan for relief intervention if the dry spell continues," Ssevume added.
Rakai district officials say nearly all the people in the area are
affected. According to the 2002 population census report, the district
has a population of 470,000.
In September last year, a team comprising of officials from World
Vision-Uganda, office of the Prime Minister, UN office for the
coordination of humanitarian affairs, WFP, FAO, Catholic Relief
Services, Lutheran World Federation, ministry of agriculture, State
House and ministry of local government carried out a food assessment in
Rakai, Masaka and Sembabule districts.
They found out that the districts have in the last three to four
years experienced dry conditions where rainfall has been erratic,
affecting crop and livestock production.
For Rakai, there was also a hailstorm in September 2004 and March
2005 which destroyed houses and crops and killed animals, especially
goats and pigs.
The report says the dry spell worsened in what would have been the
first rain season of 2005 in which crops, livestock and population were
greatly affected.
The dry spell affected mainly areas in the cattle corridor, that is, Kabula, Kooki and Kakuuto counties.
Crop performance was significantly affected at flowering and grain formation stages leading to between 80 and 100% loss.
"Livestock and human beings were also affected when valley dams and
pastures dried up. This led to migration of livestock owners to areas
outside the district," the report says.
It is said livestock keepers have moved to other parts of the
district to search for pastures and water, while others migrated to
other districts like Mubende, Masaka, Mbarara, Sembabule, Kiboga and
Luweero.
Livestock prices have drastically fallen and food prices have shot
up. For example, a bull that used to cost sh300,000 at the beginning of
2005 now costs sh200,000, whereas a kilo of posho has shot up from
sh500 to sh700 and beans have also shot up by the same figure.
The hailstorms of September 2004 and March 2005 affected mainly
parts of Kyotera County bordering Masaka district and those that are
near Lake Victoria.
Since the hailstorm was at the beginning of the first season of
2005, some farmers who had not planted continued to plant, but those
who had planted lost their crops. This caused a loss in yield of
between 30% and 50%. The dry condition started in 2002 and intensified
in the first season of 2005. This affected milk production and body
weight of the animals.
Some animals reportedly died, although it was not said how many.
Food is available in the markets and is supplied by areas within the district that are not affected by hailstorm or drought.
However, much of the food comes from Bushenyi, Mbarara, Masaka, Sembabule, Mubende, Kasese, Kampala and Mbale districts.
The common food crops in the markets are beans, maize flour, tomatoes, cassava, bananas, sweet and Irish potatoes.
The affected communities have, however, limited cash to purchase
food enough for 6 to 10 household members, so some families end up
having one meal a day with porridge in the morning or lunch time.
The biggest problem of households is the low purchasing power
worsened by rising food prices and the drastic fall in prices of
livestock.
"Families have now resorted to eating mangoes for breakfast, lunch
and supper," says Okumu, a community development facilitator with World
Vision in Kasasa.
People say their immediate priority needs are food, especially posho
and beans for at least three months, improved planting materials
(beans, maize, cassava cuttings, sweet potato vines and banana suckers)
and safe drinking water.
Others say they need credit or loan schemes, improved breeds of
bulls, goats and pigs and shelter and bedding for those whose houses
were destroyed by hailstorm.
The report by the assessment team for Rakai district estimates that
altogether, the cost of the planting materials to be provided will be
about sh609m.
Broken down, about sh167m will be needed for buying planting
materials for hailstorm-affected households and about sh444m for
drought-affected households.
"If there are limited resources, priority should be given to
drought-affected households since they are doing worse than the
hailstorm-affected ones," the report says.
The report further says that improved long term crops (bananas,
coffee, vanilla and passion fruits) and animal breeds (goats, pigs and
bulls) could be provided to improve the food and income security of the
affected community.
It also recommends that the Government invest in early warning
systems in order to be pro-active, not reactive, and disseminate
weather forecast information to the districts as early as possible to
enable farmers to plan accordingly.
The report also recommends that the number of water reservoirs be
increased in livestock-keeping communities (cattle corridor)in order to
reduce the distance travelled by animals and herders to look for water.
It will also reduce the struggle for watering points for the animals.
"Something needs to be done, and done urgently, otherwise we will
start looking for money to bury people," says Dez Byamukama, a relief
monitoring and evaluation specialist with World Vision, Uganda and one
of the assessment officers who went to Rakai.

Original_Search_Title: In Overcrowded Prisons, Survival is a Daily Battle
Headline: Nigeria: In Overcrowded Prisons, Survival is a Daily Battle
Body_Text:
Kaduna — As visitors approach the death row block at Kaduna's central prison in
northern Nigeria, a sea of hands waving tin cups automatically jerk through
the bars of the dark cells.
"Get back!" shouts the prison guard at the 118 detainees crammed inside
a dilapidated building originally meant to house 33. Up to three inmates
live in less than four square metres of space. An overpowering stench of
urine and mould billows out into the courtyard.
In the turmoil of the shouts some of the prisoners draw back to their
spots on a tattered mat on the floor that aside from a few plastic bowls is
the only object in the cell.
But the guard is jumpy and cuts short the visit, prohibiting any further
interaction with the detainees.
Rights organisations working in Nigerian prisons - and even prison
officials themselves - say the conditions of death row inmates do not fulfil
even minimum international human rights standards.
In Kaduna prison, death row inmates are locked up all day long, said
Festus Okoye, executive director of Human Rights Monitor (HRM), a group
based in the northern city.
"They are allowed out only rarely, for a few minutes, one by one," he
said. Meanwhile some prisoners collect the buckets used as toilets.
Most of the death row inmates are utterly alone and never receive
visitors - their families living too far away and having abandoned them for
fear of being associated with their crimes, rights group sources say. Some
simply cannot pay the 'visiting rights' fee charged by the wardens.
Nigeria this year acknowledged the sorry state of its jails, announcing
plans to free some 25,000 inmates still awaiting trial - some for as long as
10 years - in a bid to relieve overcrowding and bad conditions.
The move could ease conditions for those left waiting on death row for
years. Since Nigeria legalised capital punishment in 1999, only one prisoner
has been executed by the state in northern Nigeria, with authorities openly
reticent to carry through with executions, according to HRM.
Nigeria countrywide has 548 prisoners awaiting capital punishment - 10
of them women - among a total 40,000 detainees, according to Ernest Ogbozor
of Prisoners Rehabilitation and Welfare Action (PRAWA), Nigeria's largest
prisoners' rights organisation.
Under Nigerian law, crimes punishable by death include armed robbery,
murder and treason. Islamic Sharia law, in force in 12 northern Nigerian
states, also calls for the death penalty in other crimes such as
adultery.
Lack of food
If conditions for death row inmates are harsh, they are hardly any
better for other prisoners. For the sick and weak, incarceration can be
tantamount to a sentence to death.
"The two main problems in Nigerian prisons are overpopulation and lack
of food," said Hassan Saidi Labo, assistant to Nigeria's prison inspector
general.
Kaduna is a clear example. In December 2005, 957 detainees were crammed
in 10 buildings designed for about 550 people.
Labo says some prisons hold up to four times their capacity.
In such conditions, just surviving is a daily battle, according to
54-year-old Felix Obi who was condemned to 27 years in prison in 1986 for
drug trafficking. He spent 13 years and three months behind bars in the
economic capital, Lagos, before benefiting from an amnesty in 1999.
"You fight for a scrap of blanket, a piece of soap, a bit of food or
medicine if you get sick," said Obi, who now works with PRAWA.
"Prisoners fight for space on the floor to sleep, they fight not to
become depressed, and not to be victims of violence. They fight to survive."
Monitoring by outside groups has had some impact. Since prisons were
opened to religious and humanitarian organisations more than 10 years ago,
the prison death rate has fallen from 1,500 per year in the late 1980s to 89
deaths in 2003, according to authorities.
Still the risk of death in prison remains high, particularly because of
lack of food, said Harp Damulak, the Kaduna prison hospital doctor.
The daily ration generally consists of a bowl of beans in the morning
then cassava in the afternoon and evening. Prisons have a budget of 150
Naira (US $1.15) per prisoner per day.
But this small amount does not necessarily get to all prisoners. Supply
is in the hands of subcontractors who - poorly paid, acknowledge prison
officials - sometimes dip into the goods, according to PRAWA and HRM.
The UN Office on Drugs and Crime says a prison employee earns about
6,000 Naira ($45) per month at the start, earning a maximum of about 40,000
Naira monthly at the end of a career. As a consequence corruption is
common.
Conditions favour disease
Lack of food moreover aggravates already poor hygiene
conditions. Damulak said that malnutrition makes prisoners highly vulnerable
to infectious diseases such as tuberculosis or skin diseases caused by lack
of hygiene.
The situation is the same for women inmates in Kaduna prison, where 18
women live in two cells, sleeping on iron beds stacked one atop another,
some without mattresses. The bathroom has long been without running
water.
"We are devoured by mosquitoes, we all suffer malaria but don't have bed
nets and the hospital has no medicine except paracetamol," said Zainab, 32,
who has been incarcerated since April. "There is nothing. Even sanitary
napkins - we have to share one between two women every month, or even every
two months."
Prison conditions weigh heavily on the detainees, often causing
depression and other psychological problems, according to Damulak. And
prison personnel are not trained to handle such issues, he said.
To survive in their environment, some prisoners have taken things into
their own hands.
"They have created a veritable government," HRM's Okoye said. "One
prisoner is president, another police chief, another head of justice." He
added that some prison officials see the initiative as a positive thing
because it helps foster order in the institutions.
Former prisoner Obi said, "Some [prison 'leaders'] invent rules that are
impossible to follow." Punishment generally comes in the form of an order to
do chores, such as washing the clothes of 'chiefs,' but often prisoners pay
for misdeeds by being beaten or even sexually assaulted.
Despite efforts by inmates to impose some sort of organisation, prison
riots are common, PRAWA's Ogbozor said.
"In the past six months we have seen five riots in prisons across the
country - all linked mostly to the lack of food for detainees."
Under the recently announced plan to release prisoners, those who have
spent three to 10 years awaiting trial will have their cases reviewed for
immediate release. Also eligible will be the elderly, the terminally ill and
those with HIV, as well as people locked up for longer than the prospective
sentence for their crime.
Among those who have languished in prisons for years, human rights
activists say, are some who were picked up by mistake or for very minor
infractions and simply could not pay a fine.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United
Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Orders Tariffs Cancellation At Calabar Free Zone
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Orders Tariffs Cancellation At Calabar Free Zone
Body_Text:
Henceforth, all regulatory obstacles and tariffs which may have stifled the activities and smooth operations of businesses at the Calabar Free Trade zone are to be eliminated, President Olusegun Obasanjo has directed.
Against this background, the various security and service operatives, including the Nigeria Customs Service, Immigration Service, the Police and the Nigerian Ports Authority, operating within the zone are to eliminate the high tariff regime imposed on businesses at the zone to allow for smooth businesses at the zone.
President Obasanjo, who was represented by the Minister of Commerce, Ambassador Idris Waziri, at the official commissioning of the SkyRun International Air Conditioners manufacturing factory from China, said that any attempt to hinder business activities in the zone would scare investors. He advised that activities should provide safe haven for investors in order to woo more.
The president explained that by investing in the country, foreign investors help to create job opportunities, thereby strengthening the nation's economy through poverty alleviation.
Obasanjo stated that Nigeria finds its trade relations with China, especially in the non-oil sector, very beneficial. He disclosed that Nigeria's non-oil export to China is about $500m annually.
According to him, the economic romance between Nigeria and China started in 2004 with Nigeria exporting cassava, palm oil, and Siamese beans to China. He announced that Nigeria's cassava has been noted as the best in the world and that it fetches the nation $12bn from exports.
The president explained that the public-private participation policy is one of the best ways to better the nation's economy, adding that the SkyRun International Company was invited from China to invest in the country six months ago and that within a short period of time, it had lived up to its expectation.
According to him, Calabar would give the Chinese easy access to other West African sub-zones, adding that the Chinese are conscious of the population and influence of Nigeria in the West African sub-region, which they can do business with.
He however urged more Nigerians to diversify into the non-oil economy since, according to him, the oil sector had brought instability and corruption.
On his part, Governor Donald Duke of Cross River State said his government would stop at nothing to providing enabling environment for investors to operate in the state. Represented by his deputy, Duke said his government was committed to the completion of the projects, as well as cooperating with investors to create wealth and improve the living condition of the people.
The Consul General of China in Nigeria, Mr. Yuan Wenjin, said China attaches great importance to the growth of Nigeria's economy because of its policy to strengthen relations among developing nations. He added that Nigeria is rich with natural resources while China has the technology.
Wenjin said SkyRun International is a reputable company and urged that it should be protected and patronized to enable its growth, adding that its growth will improve the economy of the country, as well as the West African sub-zone.
Also commenting, the president of SkyRun, Mr. Liu Djindu, described the factory as a maiden project in Nigeria, expressing gratitude for the Nigerian government for the commissioning.
Djindu disclosed that President Obasanjo invited them to invest in Nigeria when he visited China in April 2005, and that equipment were moved to the site before the end of last year, adding that they decided to invest in the country because of the favourable trade policy put in place by the federal government.
The factory, he said, would also produce such other items as television sets, refrigerators and VCDs.

Original_Search_Title: Institute to Mount E-Learning Facility, Improve On Food Production
Headline: Nigeria: Institute to Mount E-Learning Facility, Improve On Food Production
Body_Text:
Lagos — In its continued effort to improve and upgrade its laboratories and facilities to enhance teaching and learning delivery, the University of Agriculture, Abeokuta (UNAAB) Ogun State, has concluded plans to establish e-learning facility in the institute.
This is in addition to classrooms and auditoria, which have magnetic boards and public address systems as well as multimedia protectors provided for each College in the institute.
Speaking at the 2005 press luncheon for media executives and practitioners, the Vice Chancellor, Prof. I.F Adu revealed that the facility would be commissioned later this month.
He said the academic philosophy of UNAAB has so far encouraged the training of students to be self-employed and employers of labour and promised to improve more on food production.
Adu noted that UNAAB has remained the first choice university for agriculture as about 17 per cent of UME agriculture candidates choose UNAAB out of three universities of agriculture and thirty six faculties of agriculture in the country.
He however added that the institute has nevertheless, kept students population under control to ensure that students have adequate facilities for their training. He said the current student population is 5,982 for undergraduates and 1,583 for postgraduates.
On the accreditation of programmes, Adu said, "it is on record that the university won the first position in the first ever comprehensive accreditation exercise carried out in this country. During that exercise, four of our programmes had full accreditation while five had interim accreditation. It is pertinent to add that none of our programmes was denied accreditation.
"The National Universities Commission (NUC) has just concluded another comprehensive accreditation exercise for programmes with interim accreditation and for new programmes.
The university is sure to have all its programmes accredited. Earlier visits by professional bodies to our new programmes, namely veterinary medicine and engineering, had commended the university for its efforts in putting these new programmes on sound footing," the VC noted..
As a matter of deliberate policy and to demonstrate its relevance, UNAAB, the Adu noted, has keyed into major Federal Government food policy initiatives. As a result of its quality of garri, UNAAB won a contract for the supply of 120 tones of garri valued at N5.04 million for the country's strategic food reserve.
Also in response to government cassava initiative and conscious of the constraints that non-availability of good quality planting material may pose, Adu revealed that UNAAB has earlier established a 22-hectare improved, high yielding and disease resistant cassava for the production of planting materials.
Given the volume of request for the supply of planting materials to date, he said the university has concluded arrangements to increase the farm size to 50-hectare next year.
Following the declaration of UNAAB as the best university in Nigeria and its vice-chancellor as the best VC in Nigeria for the year 2003, the university has resolved not to be second to any other university in the country.
The university therefore covets the encouragement and support of not only the government, but also the corporate organisations and individuals.

Original_Search_Title: Ekiti, Kwara Team Up On Boundary Project
Headline: Nigeria: Ekiti, Kwara Team Up On Boundary Project
Body_Text:
Ado-Ekiti — Ekiti and Kwara state governments have agreed on joint finance of the construction of the states, boundary road project.
Kwara State governor, Dr. Bukola Saraki, said this in Ado-Ekiti during a courtesy call on Governor Ayodele Fayose in his office.
Saraki said contract for the construction of joint boundary project between the two states would be awarded within the next few months.
The states, Dr. Saraki said, are working on collaboration in the areas of commercial farming particularly in cassava, rice and other crops production to make the states food basket of the nation.
The project, he said, would go a long way to improve agricultural production in the two states and the country.
Speaking on Zimbabwe farmers in Kwara, Saraki said they were doing fine and more of them were settling down.
The venture with the Zimbabwean farmers, he said, was purely mechanized farming, adding that as more of them are coming to the state, more new farm tools will be brought to raise the standard of agriculture in the state.
Fayose who described the step taken by the kwara State governmeent as a giant leap towards the right direction, lauded governor Saraki's efforts at improving the lot of the people.
He said: "Saraki's effort in the areas of infrastructural development across the state would ever remain in the history of Kwara".
Fayose stated that his government has improved Ekiti people's lots in the areas of roads, housing, education, health and agriculture."

Original_Search_Title: Solutions Remain Distant for IDPs
Headline: Uganda: Solutions Remain Distant for IDPs
Body_Text:
Lira — As one moves through the IDP camps dotting Lira, one of the most depressing sights is that of scores of unattended children loitering everywhere and their helpless teenage mothers. Stephen Abili spent a week in some IDP camps in Lira
The heart-rending situation in northern Uganda has been described by commentators as the most under-reported story in the world today. For nearly two decades, northern Uganda has been plagued by a civil conflict between a rebel group, the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) and the Ugandan government. The conflict has uprooted nearly 1.8 million Ugandans from their homes and forced them to seek refuge in Internally Displaced Peoples' (IDP) camps. Lira has long grappled with this misfortune.
Against this background, I spent a week in some IDP camps in Lira late last year to assess the life and psyche of people there.
The hallmarks strike you clearly: Misery, gloom, suffering and hunger. This impression, however, is swiftly overshadowed by the carefree, naked and malnourished infants, some of them with yellow lumps of mucus heading reluctantly for their mouth, as they play about the whole expanse of camp huts yelling and literally enjoying themselves.
Ironic, but it is a soothing sight to behold, considering that peace and stability was robbed from these people by the LRA rebels.
Now watching the innocent children playing, I couldn't help but imagine being born in a camp for internally displaced people, growing up in the same camp, going to school in it -if you are lucky enough to live in a camp with a school - and seemingly not having a future outside the camp. Many children are being born in the camps daily and do not know any other life; a generation is growing up without hope and expectation for the future.
What is life like for Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs) living in camps? How do they live away from their normal homes? Many of these people became IDPs when they were forced from their homes by attacks on their villages by rebels. The IDPs in Lira are only some of the world's millions of IDPs and refugees who face similar challenges in camps like these in northern Uganda.
Some of the so-called camps here spring up quickly with little or no planning or organisation, while others take on more permanence and develop. Because camps provide a place for IDPs to access basics of life including shelter, food, water and sanitation, it takes considerable work and effort to bring these things together in a place where they hadn't existed before.
It is also worth noting how sometimes all the systems of a permanent settlement can develop in a camp. This development of camps occurs in organised ways such as through Humanitarian aid agencies that provide assistance, and through the innovation and resilience of the displaced persons themselves, often struggling for survival and to meet their basic needs.
What remains of some of the homes that have been attacked by LRA rebels is burned-out shells. Typically when LRA rebels enter a village, they steal villagers' belongings and set fire to all their huts.
In some camps that have existed for more than three years like Cultural Centre, Erute and Bala camps, IDPs have managed to build stronger homes with more solid sides and thatch roofs. Its residents may have even received building materials from humanitarian agencies like Action Aid or CCF to help construct their homes.
In Railways IDP camp that sprung up more spontaneously, residents have had to construct their houses themselves with thatch that they gather in the area. Each house is the size of a small tent, and sometimes as many as ten family members sleep in the cramped quarters.
Most of the residents of this camp complain of water seeping through the hut when it rains. Latrines have been built in the camp with some donated supplies from NGOs. Residents of the camp are charged with cleaning and maintaining them. There are a few makeshift 'bathrooms' - simple structures that provide privacy for people to wash themselves.
The entrance is covered with a blanket or cloth when someone is using the space. Food is often in short supply in the camps and is consumed wisely. Drinking is the norm, often because of lack of employment and constructive activities among some IDPs.
In Cultural Centre IDP camp where I was based, life is a nightmare. During my entire stay here, I shared a tumbledown wattle hut with a block leader, Mr Joseph Acuma. A block leader is subordinate to a camp leader. To say sleeping in a foxhole could have been a much better way to spend the night is an understatement.
The place was overrun with mice, bedbugs, cockroaches, bats, lizards, red ants and mosquitoes. The door of his 'house' doesn't have a lock, so he supports it with a hand hoe. The sketchy roof of Acuma's house was so low and sagging menacingly, thus prompting me to practically bend like I was accessing a witchdoctor's shrine.
The one-inch mattress that we shared left my back aching terribly. The zoo of disgusting vermin especially bedbugs, mice, spiders and mosquitoes with which I put up relentlessly took their toll on me at night. Thanks to them, I awoke every morning feeling as if a vital part of my body had been severed.
Within the camp, sanitary conditions are abysmal. Children excrete virtually everywhere. The toilets are in a very appalling state.
They are extremely smelly, dirty and manned by a battalion of blue and green hostile flies. Some of them are full and overflowing. There is a dearth of clean drinking water and people live very close to each other.
There is a big risk for contagious diseases to spread very quickly in case of an infection. Other health threats like diarrhoea, malaria, malnutrition, measles, HIV / Aids, maternal and infant mortality rates are soaring every other day.
Displaced women and girls in the IDP camps in Lira have few places to turn for protection from the various entities that seek to harm them. For them, the term "protected village" is a misnomer for a spectrum of reasons. First of all - and not unique to northern Uganda - women in prolonged camp situations are more vulnerable to abuse from men.
In many cases, the increase in domestic violence in the camps comes as a result of the progressive destruction of traditional family and community customs and support structures; this is combined with pervasive and seemingly endless poverty.
Camp environments are farther strained by massive overcrowding particularly problematic for a people used to living in large family compounds and insufficient food and services.
Apart from the small quantities of grain and cereals they eat, the staple food of IDPs is greens. This is probably because of its easy accessibility and availability as it grows in the wild. On a few occasions, traders sell to them sweet potatoes and cassava at subsidised prices.
Until mid 2004, only a small number of the many IDP camps in Lira were "gazetted" (that is, recognised) by the government. Several more were gazetted in September 2004, though dozens still remain unofficial. Since only gazetted camps are allowed to receive humanitarian assistance, thousands of IDPs in camps in Lira receive little or no assistance.
As a result, women often leave the immediate confines of the camps to search for food, tend small gardens, collect water or perform other domestic activities.
All Nation Christian Care (ANCC) and World Response (WER) have helped to distribute tents, supplementary food and vital medical supplies to people living in IDP camps in Lira.
Bags of aid, which contain goods such as soap, food and clothes, have been disbursed to hundreds of families in the camps. A single bar of soap costs approximately half a day's wages for someone who has been able to find temporary work and is, consequently, an unaffordable luxury for the majority of people here.
"Our policy of working through local partners who are on the ground and who have a proper understanding of the issues at grassroots level, means that we are often able to reach those in great need in areas where other humanitarian agencies might fear to reach. Working with local partner agencies in northern Uganda means that WER support is reaching the camps quickly and efficiently," says Mr Alex Haxton, WER UK Director of Operations.
WER is also providing free dental services for people living in the IDP camps. This is enabling large numbers of people who would otherwise be unable to afford dental care. Private dental clinics in Lira charge Shs5, 000 to extract a tooth. This is how much it costs a woman in northern Uganda to feed her family for a week.
It's not difficult to see why, when faced with this choice, people choose to sacrifice dental heath care for the sake of being able to put food on the table. As a result, thousands of people here live with poor oral health, which can have serious implications on their overall health and detrimental effects on eating, sleeping and family life.
As one moves through any of the IDP camps dotting Lira, one of the most depressing sights is that of scores of unattended children loitering everywhere and their helpless teenage mothers.
Take 16-year-old Mercy Acola, who lives with her parents in Bala Stock Farm IDP camp. Like many of her age mates, she already has three children on top of the pregnancy she is carrying. She cannot tell who the father of her children is and even the man responsible for her current pregnancy.
Acola's situation is just a tip of the iceberg. But the situation has been aggravated by the many idle men and boys who spend most of their time drinking alcohol, only to return home and sexually harass and batter their wives and girlfriends when they deny them sex. According to humanitarian organisations, apart from poverty and disease, protected camps set up for the internally displaced people are now rife with sexual abuse and HIV /Aids.
A Lira-based renowned businessman, Mr Ojok Okullo, has had a huge impact on the lives of the internally displaced people in the district. Initially, his dream was to come up with something of substantial economic value to ease the pains of the IDPs in their pathetic camps. The 48-year-old entrepreneur married his generosity with creativity to invent the wood fuel saving stove that now adorn the camps here.
This stove that he purposely came up with as an alternative way to mitigate the sufferings of the IDPs is moulded with split bricks and mire, and can be plastered with cow dung. It is then stowed with rice brand or sawdust (known as cung bao in Luo). The stove has a stoke hole to ensure convenient passage of air for combustion.
Stuffing the stove with fuel once is enough to see the user through the day; cooking food and heating up anything. Apart from the obviously economic benefits - sparing one the burden of purchasing expensive firewood or charcoal - it is also a way of eradicating sawdust from the environment.
For a small internally displaced peoples camp, Lira Primary School has too many structures and makeshift shacks for shelter. A sprinkling of mud and wattle huts also flank the squalid clustering. However, given the unflattering number of tents literally strewn all over the place, it would not be correct to say that they outnumber their occupants, some of whom are not genuine.
In spite of the humanitarian crisis in the camps, a few dubious people have taken it upon themselves to exploit the catastrophe, thus giving birth to a new phenomenon dubbed ghost IDPs.
These sham IDPs are by any standards modest, even affluent. They live in neat homes in some of the plush suburbs of Lira town like Senior Quarters, African Quarters and Russian Quarters where they share neighbourhoods with senior government officials, military officials and traditional businessmen.
The one reason they stoop this low or shamelessly walk in the shadow of a displaced person is to obtain free emergency relief aid dispensed to the war victims by humanitarian agencies like World Food Programme (WFP) and other NGOs directly associated with their plight.
The aid that they religiously sign up for like a birth right ranges from food rations (posho and beans) to blankets, foot ware and drapery. Most, if not all of which is used to stock up on their business enterprises or sold off immediately to the always-ready retailers at a smiling rate.
You cannot help but wonder how one can manage to go about this simulative business unstuck. It is easy. In fact, very simple, since there is no such thing as intensive scrutinising and vetting of the displaced people once relief aid has arrived.
Apparently, camp leaders should among other duties, scrutinise each member of their camp and assess his or her welfare before reporting to the district authorities. All this should be done to stimulate authenticity. But given what is at stake, this role seem to have long slipped off its grip, giving way to discerning crooks to ease through the administrative crack.
The journey of an IDP impostor can best be described as smooth sailing all through the pipeline to when his selfish motives are sated.
I managed to catch up with one of the perpetrators of this dealing and after intense coaxing, he offered to throw the spanner into the works on condition of anonymity. I concurred.
Complete with a wispy moustache, blood-shot eyes, slightly grizzled hair and sweating profusely, this 43-year-old man looked fresh from a sauna. He told me he is married and fends for a family of six. And as I guessed prior to meeting him, he's a businessman with a full-to-bursting shop along Lira Main Street. His shop happened to be the venue of the interview.
"Here, have a smoke," he proffers as a ploy to dodge a personal question. I declined and delved back, even deeper into the interview. "I actually don't do the whole undertaking myself, but I employ someone on my behalf who I pay only when he brings brisk merchandise."
According to him, all he has to do is get to the IDP camp and masquerade as one of their kind. Then erect a makeshift structure for shelter before approaching the camp leader for registration. Upon registration and assessment you are accorded a disaster tent.
However, if during the process of registration, the camp leader gets too suspicious, a small handy bribe will do the magic. And after jumping this huddle, one is eligible to receive relief aid of any kind, whether or not at the expense of others.
Some displaced people also inflate the size of their family to acquire a wholesome share of aid, which is usually food. This practice is not only synonymous with Lira P7 IDP Camp, but has spread to Cultural Centre, Ireda, LPC, Anai and Railways camps, all of which are IDP camps situated on the outskirts of Lira town.
Out of the seven days I spent in the IDP camps, I remember only bathing twice. Most of the camps are devoid of bathrooms. In the few that have, they are not any better. My camp happened to fall in the latter category. Even finding a basin to use is a problem, so most people bathe from a jerry can and bottles. The untroubled ones choose to default.
Recently, there was a mild directive by government instructing the internally displaced people especially in Lira and Apac to return to their respective homes since their districts are relatively safe compared to their neighbours.
"Yes, there have been no new attacks and killings in Lira for quite a while, but our safety is still not guaranteed if we return to our villages," says Mr Richard Otuda, the camp leader of Cultural Centre IDP camp in Lira Municipality. It is alleged that seeing their defiance, government decided to stop WFP from providing them with relief aid.
Apparently, they are starving but still reluctant to go back to their homes. It appears most of them have actually gotten too used to 'urban' life and the privilege of receiving free food has pampered them into laziness. Humanitarian agencies and various NGOs have been at the forefront to feed them, dress them, provide medical facilities (though inadequate) and swamp them with lots of goodies.
For the men, their insistence is mainly because of their economic boom. More than ever now, they can get temporary but well-paying jobs on an almost daily basis. Such include unskilled labour like digging pit latrines, working at construction sites, operating grinding mills and garden work.
After saving some substantial amount of money, they buy a bicycle and immediately join the seemingly lucrative boda boda business. IDPs now shudder at the thought of having to resettle in their own homes, till their own land for free and cultivate crops, a role that requires hard work, endurance and daily effort.

Original_Search_Title: Nato: No Alternative to Obasanjo?
Headline: Nigeria: Nato: No Alternative to Obasanjo?
Body_Text:
Lagos — As the third term carnival gets under way, one of the hit songs on the
lips of the choristers is that "there is no alternative to Obasanjo". I have
chosen to call this emerging brand of idolatry NATO (not to be confused with
the disappearing North Atlantic Treaty Organisation). A careful listener to
the music of the third term orchestra will easily pick the refrain. When you
hear Ali Must Go say "we shouldn't change for the sake of change", the coded
message is that only President Olusegun Obasanjo has the qualities and the
competence required to rule Nigeria. Of course, that is a poor joke. I have
never known a human being, dead or alive, who is indispensable. But I know
those who think they are indispensable. Abacha was one.
If the PDP were a proper political party, there should have been no
question about grooming a pool of successors. With eight years to do that,
there ought not to be last-minute emergency. Except, perhaps, it was part of
the design of self-perpetuation to create a succession crisis. The
continuity of the reform programme should never be a headache as long as PDP
is in power. The sensible thing will be for the party to retain the economic
team in one form or the other. Ironically, the reform programme is neither
Obasanjo's vision nor his baby. It is an IMF/World Bank scheme. To tie
Obasanjo's third term to the programme is, therefore, misleading and
mischievous.
The third term choristers say Obasanjo has done "so well" that he needs
time to consolidate his "achievements". The list of achievements is a bit
long: increased agricultural production (Nigeria has become world's number
one producer of cassava tapioca through his initiative); debt relief; oil
and gas (massive investment in upstream sector, although the downstream
remains an eyesore with endless fuel importation); anti-corruption crusade
(some governors under watch, others under protection); bank consolidation;
international statesmanship, and so on and so forth.
We shall discuss more about these "achievements" in the coming weeks,
but let me quickly state here - as a matter of preliminary objection - that
even if Obasanjo has transformed Nigeria into Europe and America combined,
even if he is more intelligent than Albert Einstein, even if he is more
popular than Michael Jackson, even if he is the best president the world has
ever seen, we say: Well done, Mr. President; thank you very much for a
wonderful job, but your time is up; pack your things and go, Your
Excellency. Let's give genuine democracy a chance. Enough of
manipulations.
Bill Clinton, as popular and as charismatic as he was, did not seek to
change the American constitution to give himself a third term in office
despite the fact that Americans had it so good under him - the best economy
since the Second World War. Clinton knew when the market had closed and knew
how to go home honourably. Our biggest task is to see how we can encourage
Obasanjo to disengage peacefully and honourably in 2007. I understand his
dilemma. Power is too sweet to let go just like that. In 1979, if he had a
choice, or if he was very secure on the throne like he is today, he probably
wouldn't have handed over to civilians. As a Special Assistant once told me,
"Me wey be ordinary labourer for Aso Rock, I no wan commot. Na Baba go come
commot?"
The danger now is that if we amend the constitution to suit the desires
of one man, it would be a bad precedent. What stops the next president from
removing term of office completely from the constitution? What stops another
one from removing the oversight functions of the legislature? What stops yet
another one from officially converting national treasury to his personal
wallet? Indeed, it has become the case of "the bull in a china shop" (I'm
actually parodying here what Obasanjo said about IBB in 1993 when it became
glaring the Minna man did not want to leave office).
Our urgent assignment as peace-loving Nigerians is to persuade Obasanjo
to bow out gracefully. It is not easy to leave Aso Rock willingly, we know,
but it is in the best interest of our presidential democracy. Let us humour
him. Let us flatter him. Let us give him the chieftaincy title of "Creator
and Sustainer of New Nigeria" to massage his monumental ego. Let us clap
energetically for him and usher him back to his farm in peace. I know it's
not going to be easy to convince Obasanjo that he is not the only one who
can rule Nigeria. When someone has such an incredible sense of
self-importance, it takes the best psychologist in the world to bring him or
her back to reality.
For God's sake, we have many presidential materials for 2007. Nigeria is
not short of materials - from North to South. Whether it is the turn of
Hausa/Fulani, Igbo, Ijaw, Efik or Urhobo, we have them in good quantity and
quality. We have people who are educated, exposed and experienced, who are
more likely to be fair, just and democratic in running the affairs of
Nigeria. We may not have a perfect candidate who has all the best qualities
congregated in one soul (I can as well list 1002 qualities that Obasanjo
himself lacks), but governance has always been team work. It's all about
selecting and blending the right human ingredients - a politician here, an
economist there, a tough cop here, an ideas man there, and so forth - and
possessing the needed acumen to manage the team.
Today, I have volunteered to draw up a short list from which we can
conveniently pick a president and his VP. I cannot stand the NATO impression
being created by Obasanjo's hangers-on. I have deliberately left out Atiku
Abubakar and IBB from my list. I hate to imagine that IBB in particular
wants to rule Nigeria again after tossing us to and fro for eight years. He
should just keep enjoying himself within the walls of his Minna
fortress. I'm not particularly an Atiku fan either, even though I will
prefer an Atiku first term to an Obasanjo life presidency in the interest of
democracy.
My list is by means exhaustive. Every Nigerian is free to draw up his or
her own list and send it to Aso Rock or post it on the internet. My prayer,
however, is that this list will not be handed over to EFCC for something
else. Here we goÃ¢-oe
Ahmed Mu'Azu
A die-hard Obasanjo supporter, Mu'azu is one of the most widely
acknowledged performers among the governors since 1999. To his credit, he
has managed Bauchi State's resources very well, as evident in massive
infrastructural development in a state that has often lagged behind. He is
seen as a consensus-builder and a listening leader. Mu'azu's management
capacity is always a plus for a presidential hopeful, but he still has to
convince the rest of Nigeria that he can perform on the bigger stage. His
nationwide acceptability is also suspect.
Muhammadu Buhari
Buhari is one of the finest and most professional soldiers ever produced
by the Nigerian Army. Buhari is known as a no-nonsense man and someone who
practises what he preaches. His government was the first to genuinely move
Nigeria in the direction of discipline and anti-corruption in 1984-85,
although many still insist he was too high-handed and dictatorial. He has
all it takes to offer genuine, transparent and fair leadership to
Nigerians. No one can question his commitment and honesty. His handicaps:
one, those who currently surround him are not believed to have the capacity
to give him a good programme; two, he is seen by many Southerners as a
religious fanatic.
Saminu Turaki
His critics have derided his IT vision, asking questions about the
desirability of computers in a "backward" state. But that is Turaki's
strongest point - he is a visionary. Apart from sinking boreholes, patching
roads and painting classrooms, a good governor must think into the
future. India used to earn $200 million annually from software export;
today, she earns $8 billion. Jigawa is poised to become the future of IT in
Nigeria. Also, those groundnut pyramids of old may be flat now, but Turaki
is planting sesame seeds and gum Arabic into the future. His handicaps: like
Obasanjo, he spends most of his time in the air; also, is he nationally
acceptable?
Ahmed Makarfi
Ruling Kaduna State is like ruling Nigeria, with all the intricacies of
balancing ethnic and religious interests in the explosive state. Makarfi has
managed to tip-toe in these landmines in the past six years without losing
his legs and his balance. His moderation has helped to stabilise the state
and has provided the atmosphere for his development initiatives. He is said
to be a man who listens to advice and carries the people along. He is also
strong-willed and focused. He has to work on building a national support
base.
Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala
The leader of Obasanjo's economic team and an international figure,
Okonjo-Iweala has a uniqueness that may not be glaring to many Nigerians
yet. Her candidacy, either for president or VP, can answer many political
questions at once: one, she is a woman, thereby dealing with the gender
issue; two, she's an Igbo from the South-south, thereby combining the
South-east and South-south candidacy in one person; three, she is an
economist and the driver of the reform programme, thereby guaranteeing
continuation of Obasanjo's alleged legacy. The flip side, however, is her
rabid neo-liberalism (massive job cuts, removal of subsidies etc). Because
she is not a politician, she has neither the skin for criticism nor the
stomach for alternative opinion.
Charles Soludo
The professor, who governs the central bank, is an internationally
renowned economist with an outstanding academic record of first class and
distinctions. As chief economic adviser to the president, he gave birth to
the National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy (NEEDS), a
poverty reduction strategy paper (PRSP) induced by the IMF. While it is
still very premature to talk about any benefits of Soludo's consolidation
programme in the banking sector, it is to his credit that he has the
patience to explain his policies, unlike Okonjo-Iweala. His flip side is
very similar to Okonjo-Iweala's: ultra neo-liberalism. While Soludo is of
the IMF tribe, Okonjo-Iweala is from the World Bank clan. The same family
tree, that is.
Buba Marwa
An urbane, charismatic and well-educated retired military officer, Marwa
is an up-and-coming politician with remarkable experience in governance,
having ruled Borno and Lagos States successfully in the military era. He has
nationwide appeal and listening ears. He comes across as a man of
ideas. However, his recent detention in connection with Abacha Loot is a PR
blow for him, although many have read political motives to the detention
because, according to reports, he had been quizzed and cleared over the same
issue in 2001. He had reportedly produced documents to show that he started
Albarka Airline with a bank loan. His competence is, nevertheless, not in
doubt.
Others
There are, of course, several other Nigerians who are very
competent. Even if they won't be president or VP, they can play key roles in
the next government. They are Governor Sam Egwu of Ebonyi State, Governor
Donald Duke of Cross River, Dr. Oby Ezekwesili, Mallam Nuhu Ribadu,
Professor Dora Akunyili, Professor Ibrahim Gambari, Professor Jibril Aminu,
Dr. Rilwanu Lukman, Professor Pat Utomi, Col. Abubakar Umar, Engr. Ernest
Ndukwe and Chief Emeka Anyaoku.
With a long "shortlist" like this, the NATO orchestra should please give
us another excuseÃ¢-oe

Original_Search_Title: Wabara Rallies Kinsmen, Blames Opponents Over Travails
Headline: Nigeria: Wabara Rallies Kinsmen, Blames Opponents Over Travails
Body_Text:
Umuahia — FORMER Senate President, Chief Adolphus Wabara has blamed those he said do not wish Ukwa Ngwa people well as responsible for the political travails of the people from the area, stressing that his main concern as 2007 approaches is to ensure that Ukwa Ngwa produces the next governor of the state.
Addressing the people of his senatorial zone at his Ohambele country home in Ukwa East Local Government Area of Abia State at the weekend, he assured the people that he was not in anyway weighed down by what happened to him as the Senate President.
His words: "The fact that I have got to this height is divine. I came into politics and rose so fast and that was why some people decided to pull me down. But I want to assure you people that they will not succeed. One thing I want to say is that many of you are the losers for my losing that position because instead of coming here, they now have to travel to Enugu to get what they want. One thing I have learnt is that when one is in power, everybody loves him. But once you lose that power, you are alone.
"On December 25, I was here with my wife and my only son alone in the whole compound and I recalled what the situation was during the previous Christmas when this place was full of people. But let me inform you that nothing will weigh me down. There is nothing that happened to me that has not happened before. I still believe in democracy.
"So, I am overwhelmed by your presence in my house today. When I saw the crowd, I asked my wife whether I am still alive because of the number of people here. If this crowd will remain steadfast, I do not see why the next governor will not come from Ukwa Ngwa. And I want to assure you that I will fight to see that the Abia charter of equity is maintained by allowing the next governor to come from this area." He, however, frowned at what he called dissident elements in the midst of Ukwa Ngwa people, adding that such people must not be allowed to succeed in dividing the people .
At the meeting with the people of his senatorial zone, Wabara, through the Adolphus Wabara Foundation, distributed 100 motorcycles to the youths from the six local government areas that make up the zone. He also gave out N3 million grant- in- aid to all the political wards in the zone for the purpopse of cultivating cassava for export .
Sunday Vanguard gathered that the senator has been empowering the people of the zone since 1999 when he was first elected into the Senate. According to the former Senate President, those in charge of the Foundation have been directed to ensure that everybody from the zone, irrespective of his political leaning, should benefit from the empowerment scheme.
Describing himself as water that does not have enemies, he urged the people to try and win the souls of other political groups in the state so that they can come into the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) for the purpose of winning next year's election.

Original_Search_Title: 3rd Term...the Storm After
Headline: Nigeria: 3rd Term...the Storm After
Body_Text:
Lagos — In 1975, Gen. Yakubu Gowon, having been military leader for nine years reneged on an earlier promise to allow the country's return to democratic governance. His excuse then was that politicians were not matured enough to continue the task of nation building. As events turned out, Gowon was the one lacking maturity in vision and statesmanship. He was overthrown July 29 of the same year, later forced into self-exile after an alleged involvement in the February 13, 1976 coup and ended up in disgrace. Even when he was pardoned several years later, Gowon returned to the country and lived in the shadows of his successors. Nobody remembered his achievements as Nigeria's wartime leader, if not hero. His single-minded re-integration of the Ndigbo into the Nigerian nation after they were vanquished in the 1967-70 civil war became distant tales. His words carried no weight. So humbled was Gowon after his return from exile that he could only take solace in an anonymous association called
Nigeria Prays. Later, a leader who ordinarily should be a national icon accepted the chair of the Board of Trustees of Arewa Consultative Forum (ACF), a regional association. Today, Gowon has no associates, no followers, and no power centre, exerts little or no influence and is almost completely anonymous in a crowd of the country's political elite. Gowon has not recovered from the mistake of 1975; he is obviously still in emotional pain.
Gen. Ibrahim Babangida was military leader for eight years. When his long, tortuous and convoluted democratic transition programme ended in the annulment of the results of the June 12, 1993 presidential polls, Babangida "stepped aside" to save face. A leader ever conscious of his place in history, every achievement of his administration was interred with the post-June12 annulment crises. Although still a darling of the ruling elite, Babangida's every attempt, since he left office in August 1993, to embark on image laundering always ended up a public relations disaster. Babangida would obviously have loved to be a celebrated statesman, but due to the June12 annulment, he is today a celebrated political fraud. Twelve years after leaving office, Babangida is still immersed in the pool of disgrace he fell into due to the unhealthy ambition to stay in office in perpetuity. The late military leader, Gen. Sani Abacha wanted to transmute into a civilian president. He got the five par
ties to adopt him as the sole presidential candidate. Not long after this strange experiment in political manipulation, Abacha could not stop the earth from swallowing his body. And his dreams. Today, Abacha's name evokes memories of fear, assassination, torture, thievery, abuse of office and every ugly thing power could be deployed. Even in death, his name has not been spared from the scum of disgrace.
Gowon obviously had these experiences in mind when, in a message to the Middle Belt Conference in Abuja late last month, generally advised the present leadership of the country to avoid prolonging its stay. In obvious reference to the speculated third term ambition of President Olusegun Obasanjo, Gowon said, "We have to avoid the tendency of leadership prolonging itself or even overstaying its acceptability - my experience, that of Gen. Babangida and Abacha should be a good lesson for all leadership in Nigeria today and tomorrow." However, like a student on a self-destructive path to exam failure, the presidency would hear none of this. Presidential aide, Femi Fani-Kayode, asked Gowon to shut up, that the former leader, having been forced out of power in 1975, does not have the credentials to advise Obasanjo on leadership prolongation. Some few days earlier, Fani-Kayode had cause to join words with Dr. John Onaiyekan, bishop of Abuja Catholic Diocese. Onaiyekan had, at a Chri
stmas homily, advised Obasanjo not to listen to those asking him to extend his stay in office. The cleric said there are over 5,000 Nigerians capable of piloting the affairs of state. Fani-Kayode countered that though there may be so many Nigerians that could be president, none has what it takes to match Obasanjo's achievements in the economy, the polity and at the international scene.
If Fani-Kayode's ill-advised responses to Onaiyekan and Gowon were not enough signals that the third term agenda is real, three ordinarily isolated incidents completely removed the veil of pretence. The first was the Southern Leaders Conference in Enugu, which apparently had the backing of the presidency, obviously to counter an earlier meeting of northern leaders in Kaduna. While the Kaduna meeting resolved that the presidency should come back to the North in 2007, the Enugu meeting threatened confederation should presidential power not remain in the South. With Obasanjo's men like Chief Olabode George and Fani-Kayode shoving their weight and running their mouth at the Enugu conference, it was obvious the presidency had more than a partisan interest in its outcome. Obasanjo and his men, however, seemed to have overlooked the manifest danger of playing the South against the North and causing a sharp division in the country along that divide. The second signal was Obasanjo's p
athetic response to a simple and straightforward question he was asked in last month's presidential media chat. A caller from Sokoto had wanted to know if the president was interested in staying in office beyond 2007. Obasanjo, in what was known as out of point in our secondary school exam parlance in those days, rambled on about cassava production and his administration's successes in the economic sector. The third signal was an interview the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) national chairman, Dr. Ahmadu Ali, granted THISDAY and published last Monday. In a logic as perverted as the reforms going on in the PDP in the last four months, Ali said, "We have seen evidence of good leadership again; do you just change a gown when it is not dirty?... The PDP is proud of President Obasanjo and if the country decides on their own to amend their constitution, who are we to prevent anybody from enjoying it?" The PDP national chairman said much more in what is a direct campaign for Obasanj
o to continue in office beyond 2007.
In my last piece, "Will 3rd Term Agenda Succeed?" (December 18, 2005), I concluded thus: "By the time the veil of pretence is removed from all the shenanigans going on, Obasanjo may just successfully roll the tank of the third term agenda on the country. Opposition to the agenda may be too tepid and uncoordinated to stop him. But then after the calm comes the storm. Getting a third term is one thing, keeping the peace is a different thing entirely." Now with the veil of pretence completely removed, the storm is gradually gathering. And the storm troopers are reporters, men and women who daily write the history of this country. Newspapers have now made third term almost the only issue worth playing up. Politicians whose names have long been forgotten now talk about third term and get front-page treatment. Columnists are lifting the banner of third term on the agenda of national discourse. To everybody Obasanjo is fair game. He's being hit and battered from every angle. The mon
umental achievements of his administration are daily being sacrificed on the altar of third term. What are the people saying out there? Vice President Atiku Abubakar's problem with Obasanjo is not unconnected with his opposition to the third term agenda. When DSP Alamieyeseigha was impeached as Bayelsa governor, it was because he was against the third term agenda. Alhaji Lamidi Adedibu and his thugs are after Oyo State governor Rashidi Ladoja because the governor is pro-Atiku. The Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) arrested Brig. Gen. Buba Marwa because he would like to be president in 2007. These may not necessarily be true but the perception is fast wearing the cassock of reality.
As long as things continue this way, the administration's credibility, already battered by the massive fraud in the 2003 polls, will be eroded the more. Obasanjo may succeed in manipulating the political process in amending the constitution and prolonging his stay in office, but there is no way a post-2007 government he would form would not be weighed down by a moral handicap. And if the June 12 annulment experience is any guide, a government lacking in moral authority is a government dead on formation. That was why Babangida had to step aside after the annulment. That was also why Chief Ernest Shonekan's Interim National Government stuttered and scattered. That was equally why Abacha was, for five years, a prisoner of power, afraid of his own shadows. Indeed, had Chief MKO Abiola, who apparently won the annulled elections not conveniently died in prison, the 1999 polls would perhaps have been indefensible. Such crisis of credibility and moral handicap would rubbish Obasanjo'
s stature as an international statesman. And at home, the administration's successes in anti-graft war, in banking consolidation, in debt reprieve among others may only be recorded as footnotes, if not completely forgotten. If what happened to Gowon, Babangida and Abacha are guiding signposts, Obasanjo's blind ambition to prolong his tenure would only catapult him to a dark alley of disgrace and infamy.
There is also the certainty that Obasanjo's third term agenda would rob Nigeria of peace. That is why long-time enemies like Babangida and Major Gen. Muhammadu Buhari are rediscovering their friendship. That is why all those who lost out in the PDP have regrouped in the Movement for the Defence of Democracy (MDD). Even if they cannot stop the president from realising his agenda, they will continue to talk and to heat up the polity. And make no mistake about it, some of them have the will and capacity to sabotage government, if not instigate bloody protests, in a big way. At the end of the day, when the push comes to shove, the Southern leaders decision to threaten confederation if they lose the presidency in 2007 could still blow in Obasanjo's face. For if the North and the South insist on having the presidency in 2007 or nothing, it could throw up a crisis that could tragically make Obasanjo the last of the nationalists. This may have informed the warning of the Chief of Arm
y Staff, Lt. Gen. Martin Luther Agwai, that politicians should watch their utterances and not create unnecessary tension capable of threatening "the corporate existence of Nigeria as one united country." After the Abacha experience, Nigerians may not accept military intervention in the polity. But that does not mean the soldiers can be stopped in the event of another descent into civil war.

Original_Search_Title: Research Institute Popularizing Edible Root, Cereal Crops
Headline: Ethiopia: Research Institute Popularizing Edible Root, Cereal Crops
Body_Text:
