Awassa — The SNNPS Agricultural Research Institute is reportedly popularizing the species of edible root and cereal crops it developed last year with a view to ensuring food security in the state.
Institute Head Dr. Daniel Dawro told WIC that training is being given to farmers on how they could prepare various kinds of meals from the root and cereal crops developed last year and approved by the National Seeds Certification Committee. The species include sweet potato, cassava, soyabean and haricot bean, it was learnt.
The institute has widely been introducing different ways of preparing cakes, breads and biscuits from sweet potato and cassava plant, according to the head. The unaccustomed use of the plants has therefore created excitement and wide acceptance, he added.
Besides the flexible ways of using the plants has minimized wastage, it was noted.
Dr. Daniel said it is also possible to mix cassava with teff and prepare food, besides the various foods that can be prepared from cassava alone.
He said efforts have also been underway to popularize soyabean and haricot bean as they have high protein and substitute milk.
The institute has last year developed four kinds of sweet potatoes, potato and cassava, as well as soyabean, haricot bean and oil seeds, among others, the head concluded.

Original_Search_Title: Jaqee Mbabazi is Single, But Still Relates With Her Ex-Lovers
Headline: Uganda: Jaqee Mbabazi is Single, But Still Relates With Her Ex-Lovers
Body_Text:
Kampala — SWEDEN-based Ugandan singer Jaqee Mbabazi recently took Kampala by storm with her 14-track album, Blaqalixious. The album is doing well in Sweden and Mbabazi has been nominated for the Swedish Grammy Awards. She talked to Pidson Kareire and below are excerpts:
How old are you?
I look young, but I'm 28 years old. Anyway, I love growing older. It is good you get more comfortable in your own skills and you are able to identify what you want.
What were you like at 15?
I was shy and quiet. I used to think a lot and contemplate. I knew I would be a writer and I loved writing so much.
Where were you born?
I was born in Mulago and grew up in Mbuya, Kampala.
When did you go to Sweden?
My family relocated to Sweden in 1990. Since then, I have lived and studied there. I'm a trained nurse.
Did you learn singing while in Sweden or did you start your music career in Uganda?
Sweden only encouraged me to sing. I already knew how to sing because I used to sing in churches before we left for Sweden. I knew I had the talent and I only needed motivation, which I found in Sweden.
What does Blaqalixious mean?
It is a way of expressing my feelings about my colour. I'm a black person among white people. I got the word black and the word lixious and joined them together making it Blaqalixious. This is because I believe that black people have to be more proud than they are. They have to show their pride because they are not different from whites, the only difference is the skin colour.
What does Karma, one of your title tracks mean?
Karma is a spiritual thing.
Is there 'detoothing' in Sweden?
Detoothing! What is that? Oh, yeah! I have remembered. It exists, it is everywhere. It is bad, disrespectful and degrading. Those who do it are likely to live a hopeless life.
What do you think attracts most Bazungu to black ladies?
Nothing special. It is their approach that makes them look sharp and smart. Black men have inferiority complex and get intimidated by whites.
What is the difference between African love and Bazungu love?
The difference is in their way of expressing love. The Bazungu express their love anywhere at anytime, which is still difficult among Africans.
What do you think is the major cause of break-ups in relationships?
Not listening to one another. Actually, poor communication.
What do you think is always on women's minds?
It could be anything; but what I know is they are sensitive.
Can you guess what is on men's minds?
Psychologists say that on every man's mind, there is a naked woman. Their thoughts begin from a woman to other issues.
What is your worst bedroom crime?
(Laughs). I don't have any.
Do you have a man in your life?
No. I'm single and not looking for one. When the right time comes I will get the right man. I want to first develop my career and come back to Uganda and settle.
What kind of boyfriend would you like?
I would like a man who is not afraid to love. A man who can actually listen and be affectionate. If all men could strive to do that the world would be a better place to live in.
How do you relate with your ex-boyfriends?
I'm friends with most of my ex-boyfriends because we got to know each other very well and the fact that we disagreed should not make us enemies. I try to make sure that we remain friends.
How would you react to a boyfriend who enjoys vulgar language?
I don't think that is sexy.
What role does money play in a relationship?
Of course money is necessary. However, it should not be the most important thing in a relationship because we have seen many rich people in miserable relationships.
When did you last use a hoe?
During my childhood when I was still in Uganda. In Sweden, I don't dig at all.
What is your best dish?
I like Ugandan local foods like cassava, matooke, groundnuts and fish.
Are you a soccer fan?
No. I watch so many movies that I don't have time for matches.
What do you enjoy wearing most?
Just a piece of cloth; it could be a lesu, whatever
How much do you spend on thongs?
It's not a big deal, but I like the boxer type.
How much do you spend on shoes?
I'm a smart buyer and I spend very little on shoes.
What are your favourite hang-outs in Kampala and Sweden?
I don't have a favourite hangout in Kampala, but it would be where my pals hangout and has to be homey. This even applies to Sweden. I follow friends.
Is there a beauty product you cannot do without?
Water and cocoa butter. I drink a lot of water and apply cocoa butter because they are good for the skin.

Original_Search_Title: Akume Vows to Complete Unfinished Projects
Headline: Nigeria: Akume Vows to Complete Unfinished Projects
Body_Text:
Makurdi — Benue State Governor, Dr. George Akume has promised to complete all projects initiated by his six-year old administration before he leaves office by May 2007.
As a result, the governor has warned all contractors handling various projects in the state to ensure they complete all contracts on schedule to avoid the wrath of his administration.
Akume who made this known in Makurdi the state capital said his decision was in line with his desire to change the fortunes of the state.
Some of the projects, he said, includes Cassava Ethanol plant, Roots and Tubers factory, the Igumale Cement factory, and the Tomato Processing plant.
Others according to him are the Katsina Ala and Otukpo Fruit Juice factories, Fertiliser Mixing and Blending plant, as well as the Organic plant all of which he said would be completed with the sum of N28.917 billion.
He said his administration would be implementing a full budget this year and there was therefore no time and patience for any acts that would constitute a rag on his policy plans.
"Government is conscious of the fact that 2006 is the only full budget year this administration has in its remaining tenure and is therefore totally committed to doing its best to complete all projects initiated as well as maintaining those inherited."

Original_Search_Title: Presidential Initiatives on Agriculture Are Yielding Results - Minister
Headline: Nigeria: Presidential Initiatives on Agriculture Are Yielding Results - Minister
Body_Text:
Lagos — MINISTER of Agriculture and Rural Development, Alhaji Adamu Bello has
stated that the presidential initiative on vegetable oil is meant to make
Nigeria self-sufficient in the production of the commodity in the
country.
The initiative is also meant to ensure that Nigerian manufacturers of
the commodity take advantage of the development and suffuse the West African
sub-region with the commodity, thereby enhancing Nigeria's foreign exchange
earning capacity.
Speaking in Abuja recently, Alhaji Bello maintained that the present
administration under President Olusegun Obasanjo has done enough work to
strengthen the agricultural sector and by extension use the sector as an
employment window to contain unemployment in the country.
According to him, the initiatives in the areas of rice, cassava, fishery
and forestry, as well as soya bean, yam plantain and cereals production
among other are beginning to yield results.
For instance, for the first time in the country's export history,
cassava was being exported by Nigerian farmers to earn foresign
exchange.
Similarly, he noted that the country was gradually becoming
self-sufficient in food production and submitted that food items currently
being imported like rice would be stopped, as soon as local production
becomes adequate to meet internal demand.
He expressed delight that more Nigerians are beginning to take to
agriculture to enhance their well-being, earn meaningful livelihood and
remain gainfully employed.
He also explained that government would do everything practicable to
fund the sector and this it has demonstrated by the introduction of various
policies including the mandatory agricultural loans by banks, the
establishment of Bank of Industry (BOI) and the recently launched
micro-credit scheme by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN).
Business Champion gathered that $300 million (38.7 billion) has been
invested in the development of vegetable oil production in the country.
It was also learned that more Nigerians are getting involved in
vegetable oil production as part of efforts to take advantage of the various
incentives advanced by the government in that regard.

Original_Search_Title: Thisday Awards 2005: the Nominees Are
Headline: Nigeria: Thisday Awards 2005: the Nominees Are
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Board of Editors of THISDAY has released the final list of nominees in the remaining nine categories for its 11th annual THISDAY Awards to be held in Lagos, January 21, 2006. The categories which nominees were announced yesterday are Stock Offer of the Year, Transaction of the Year, Merger and Acquisition of the Year, Fund Manager of the Year, Entrepreneur of the Year, Young Manager of the Year, Representative of the Year and Senator of the Year. Also announced are the winners of the Life time Achievement Award.
The awardees of the Lifetime Achievers Award are Chief Akintola Williams, Prof. Chinua Achebe, Chief Emeka Anyaoku, , Prof. Wole Soyinka, Mallam Adamu Ciroma, Dr. Babatunde Jobe, Mrs. Sheila Solarin, Olorogun Michael Ibru, Late Chima Ubani, Justice Muhammadu Uwais, Mr. Sam Amuka Pemu and Chief Gani Fawehinmi (SAN).
Nominees for Stock Offer of the Year are Diamond Bank, Ecobank, First City Monument Bank (FCMB), Fidelity Bank and Investment Banking and Trust Company (IBTC) Plc. Also nominees for Transaction of the Year are Ministry of Finance/Nigeria N36 billion debt buy back, Vmobile $1.1 billion equipment, Bureau of Public Entreprises sale of Nicon Hilton Hotel and the $600 million Nigeria National Petroleum Corporation/Mobile Satellite.
In the Merger and Acquisition of the Year category, United Bank for Africa/Standard Trust Bank, Intercontinental Group, Access, PlatinumHabib and Skye Bank.
Fund Manager of the Year nominees are IBTC Asset Management, ARM, Fidelity and FSDH, while the nominees for Entrepreneur of the Year are Femi Otedola, Stella Okoli, Bimbo Awosika, Murtala Nyako and Aliko Dangote.
The nominees for Young Manager of the Year are Bruce Ayonote, Ladi Balogun, Ndidi Nwuneli, Yetunde Sadiku, Suleiman Arzika and Modupe Ozolua. The Representative of the Year nominees are Abike Dabiri, Anthony Aziegbemi, Farouk Lawan, Usman Bugaje, Dr. Haruna Yerima and Prof. Sola Adeyeye.
The Senator of the Year nominees are David Brigidi, Ken Nnamani, Lawal Shuaibu, Uche Chukwumerije, Victor Ndoma-Egba and Saidu Dansadau
Bank of The Year Nominees
Profiles in Excellence...
First Bank : First Bank of Nigeria Plc, one of the largest financial services group in sub-Saharan Africa, has focused relentlessly on consolidating its leadership position as Nigeria's bank of first choice. Established in 1894, the Bank has distinguished itself as the first and largest provider of specialised financial services in the country. First Bank has experienced tremendous growth over the years, with its assets and shareholders' equity standing at N377. 5 billion and N44.7 billion respectively, as at March 31, 2005. Gross earnings and profit before tax stood at N49.5 billion and N15.15 billion respectively for the same period.
As a market leader in the financial services sector, First Bank pioneered initiatives in international money transfer, MasterCard, Interswitch, and the ATM consortium. Currently, it has an expansive branch network of 371 spread across the country. Its over 270 online real-time branch network is the largest in the country.
First Bank plays a dominant role in the Nigerian economy. Leveraging earlier experience with Econet Wireless, it was the lead Bank in provision of a syndicated loan of US$185 million for NITEL's acquisition of a General System of Mobile Telecommunication (GSM) license, and syndicated facility of US$72m to NITEL for the expansion of parastatal's switching facility. To demonstrate its commitment to its customer and development of Nigerian economy, the bank has since broadened its loan and credit portfolios to various sectors of the economy; and in 2005, it led Nigeria's largest loan syndication of N60 billon to Dangote Group.
The need for customised solutions to customers' business problems necessitated the extension of the Bank's services into seven (7) subsidiaries making it one of the largest financial services group in sub-Saharan Africa. The subsidiaries are into merchant banking, registrarship, trusteeship, insurance brokerage, SME financing, venture capital financing, mortgage banking, investment banking, and pension funds management.
In November 2002, the Bank took a major leap when it was authorised by the Financial Services Authority (FSA) to transform its London branch into a full-fledged subsidiary, FBN Bank (UK) Limited. This achievement makes the bank the first Nigerian financial institution with an offshore banking subsidiary in the United Kingdom. Already, the new subsidiary, conceived to serve as a channel for trade flows between Nigeria and the UK, has become a success story. In addition, to capture the increasing trade flow between Nigeria and South Africa, the bank has opened a representative office in South Africa.
Undoubtedly, for a long time to come, FirstBank would remain customers' delight because of its brand equity, strong balance sheet, strong customer/shareholders' value, and dedicated work force. The future continues to be very bright for the Bank, and for prospective investors wishing to explore the vast business opportunities that abound in Nigeria; FirstBank remains the most credible financial partner.
Guaranty trust Bank : Guaranty Trust Bank plc was licensed as a commercial bank in 1990 and commenced operations in February 1991 and was quoted on the Nigerian Stock Exchange (NSE) in 1996. With about 80 branches in Nigeria and offshore subsidiaries in The Gambia, Sierra-Leone and Ghana, the bank is one of the most profitable and professional financial institutions in Nigeria.
The bank offers investment banking and advisory services, medium to long term capital financing and fund management services to multinationals, indigenous enterprises and high net worth individuals.
The bank, 2004/2005 financial report shows a capital base of over $226 million, group profit before tax of $50 million, total assets and contingents of over $1.45 billion and gross earnings in excess of $175 million. It has a (triple A) Aaa credit risk rating from Agusto & Co, the leading domestic rating agency and a double A minus (AA-) rating from Fitch.
The bank has been the subject of organisational and brands marketing case studies by several organisations including Harvard Business School, Lagos Business School and Cranfield University, Bedfordshire, United Kingdom. Guaranty Trust Bank had also clinched the Nigerian Stock Exchange President's Merit Award for four consecutive times (1996, 2000 and 2003 and 2004).
These were in recognition of the bank's corporate and operational performance during these periods. The award is usually bestowed on successful companies with excellent shareholder relations and track records listed on the 45-year-old stock exchange. Previous NSE awards for the Bank were in 1996, 2000 and 2003.
United Bank for Africa: August 1 2005, marked a watershed in the history of Nigeria's financial services industry, with the emergence of United Bank for Africa Plc as Nigeria's first mega bank following the merger of UBA and Standard Trust Bank Plc. Apart from its record as the first successful merger in the ongoing banking consolidation, the consolidated UBA's aggregate financial and non-financial strength clearly ranks it above its peers in the industry.
With a network of over 425 business offices across the country, balance sheet size of over N600 billion, and an active customer base of over two million, United Bank For Africa Plc ranks as the biggest on-line, real-time bank in Nigeria and indeed sub-Saharan Africa.
This no doubt imposes great expectations on the bank as it seeks to meet the banking aspirations of most Nigerians, who neither have bank account nor operate one.
It is therefore, in demonstration of its commitment to the economic advancement of Nigerians that the United Bank for Africa Plc on the eve of Nigeria's 45th Independence Day celebration introduced to millions of Nigerians, a novel product called UBA Freedom Savings Account.
Freedom savings account as the name suggests is an account which allows a customer to open an account without any initial deposit as is the case with many other banks in the country. The process is simple and convenient. With the provision of two passport photographs and submission of any acceptable means of identification, the customer can open a UBA Freedom Savings
Account in any of the bank's over 425 business offices located within your neighbourhood. The UBA Freedom Savings Account no doubt offers an invaluable access to low income/profit earners; the less educated, and financially/socially disadvantaged to have a feel of service delivery and an array of banking services for which the bank is known for.
Driven by its retail banking focus, the United Bank for Africa Plc last week entered into a strategic partnership with the Netherlands Development Finance Company (FMO) leading to the signing of an agreement for a $10 million facility between both institutions. The Credit Guarantee Facility will be made available by Australian & New Zealand Bank (ANZ).
The FMO facility comes on the heels of the recent loan of N210m to the Kwara State Government by UBA Plc for on-lending to rural farmers in the State. More than 1000 farmers' associations and individuals including youths and retirees involved in the cultivation of rice and cassava are expected to benefit from that facility.
In a similar development, the United Bank for Africa has contributed immensely towards the Federal Government's efforts at promoting the use of non-cash payment instruments in the country. The UBA MasterCard remains the most affordable and preferred credit card in Nigeria contributing significantly in easing the burden of travelers and businessmen who may wish to shop on the internet.
As the bank tenaciously holds unto its vision of playing an undisputable leadership role in Nigeria's financial service industry there is hope that United Bank For Africa Plc through its offering of UBA Freedom Savings Account will seek to render the same service quality which saw it become the fastest growing agent bank of Western Union International Money transfer in all of Africa, Middle East and Asia.
Zenith Bank : Unarguably one of the most innovative and forward-looking banks that had foreseen that large capital would soon become an important and compulsory issue in Nigeria's financial services industry, Zenith Bank was one of the banks that rolled out its drums to shop for funds when the on-going consolidation induced by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) was announced last year.
The bank grossed N48.388 billion proceeds from its Initial Pubic Offering (IPO). This was the largest IPO proceeds in the history of Nigerian capital market and it represented a 554 per cent subscription. But guided by regulatory requirement, the bank was only allowed to retain N20.39 billion, a development that pushed its post IPO shareholders' funds to N36 billion and further to N38 billion as at the year ended 30th June, 2005.
The bank also currently has 288,780 subscribers to its IPO, representing the largest number of shareholders of any company in Nigeria - feat that made the bank win the THISDAY award for the "Most Successful IPO" for the year 2004.
For the recently concluded financial year ended 30th June, 2005, Zenith Bank recorded tremendous improvement in all key performance indices and surpassed its profit forecast of its IPO. Pre-tax profit moved up by 43 per cent, from N6.4 billion in 2004 to N9.16 billion in 2005, while total assets and contingent liabilities inched up by 72 per cent, from N215.22 billion to an all-time high of N370 billion, a development that reinforced the bank's position as one of the strongest banks in Nigeria.
Consequently, for the seventh consecutive year up to June 2005, Zenith Bank maintained its position as an institution with the lowest ratio of non-performing loans to total loans in the Nigerian financial services industry. Its ratio remained less than 2 per cent compared with industry average of 18 per cent, thus underscoring the high quality of the bank's risk assets, as well as its superior risk management culture.
Zenith Bank has maintained a leading position in the banking industry in the rating analysis undertaken locally and overseas since 1999 to date. The bank was rated "triple A" in the recent rating analysis conducted by Agusto and Company Limited.
In the same breadth, since 2000 to date, the bank has also been rated "triple A" in the credit risk rating analysis undertaken by Pharez.
Zenith Bank also clinched "AA" in Fitch's and Duff & Phelps Credit Rating Co. Africa (Pty) Ltd in 2003/2004 and 2000 respectively; won Institute of Chartered Accountants of Nigeria (ICAN) merit award in recognition of its outstanding contribution to the growth of the industry and commerce, as well as accounting profession in Nigeria; declared most efficient bank in 2001 by the Lagos Clearing House (LBCH) and won three awards (best bank website in the Nigeria, best on-line service quality and first overall rating) in the Web-Jurist Award (Phillips Consulting 2004 & 2005).
Other awards won by Zenith Bank includes Market Audit and Research Services Limited 2002 award as the "best service excellence bank in Lagos"; Maritime Quality Service Award as "best Customs duty collection bank award (2000 - 2004); Business Times Banker of the year 2003; THISDAY's Newspaper Offer of the year 2004; Fame Magazine, Global Excellence and City People Awards as banker of the year 2004 respectively.
International Bank of The Year Nominees
Profiles in Excellence...
Credit Suisse First Boston (CSFB): Credit Suisse First Boston (CSFB), a business unit of Zurich-based Credit Suisse Group, is a leading global investment bank serving institutional, corporate, government and high net worth clients. CSFB's businesses include securities underwriting, sales and trading, investment banking, private equity, alternative assets, financial advisory services, investment research, and asset management.
The bank is a proven leader across the spectrum of investment banking, capital markets and financial services, ranking in the top tier in virtually all major business segments. CSFB's organisational structure focuses on providing the highest quality of client-centric services.
It operates in more than 69 locations across more than 33 countries on five continents. Powered by a strong local presence, global vision and a seamless ability to execute transactions in and across all markets, CSFB deploys its capital base and global perspective to offer a full range of products, services and capabilities.
Netherlands Development Finance Company (fmo): The Netherlands Development Finance Company (FMO), valuable risk partner, supports the private sector in developing countries and emerging markets in Asia, Africa, Latin America and Central and Eastern Europe. It does this with loans, participations, guarantees and other investment promotion activities and the goal is to contribute to the structural and sustainable economic growth in these countries and, together with the private sector, obtain healthy returns.
FMO builds bridges between entrepreneurs and capital, locally as well as across frontiers, for sustainable development and healthy returns. Commercial banks and other investors have been reluctant with regard to developing markets due to international politics and economic developments at the beginning of the 21st century and as a result, the role of the development banks is therefore more important than ever.
At present, FMO is firmly rooted in some 40 or more developing countries and countries in transition. At the same time, it is affirming its ties with the Dutch and European business communities.
Standard Chartered: Standard Chartered is one of the world's most international banks, employing over 40,000 people, representing 80 nationalities, across its network. Standard Chartered operates in over 1,200 locations (including subsidiaries, associates and joint ventures) in more than 50 countries in the Asia Pacific Region, South Asia, the Middle East, Africa, the United Kingdom and the Americas.
Well-established in growth markets, Standard Chartered aims to be the right partner for its customers combining deep local knowledge with global capability. The bank is trusted across its network for its standard of governance and corporate responsibility as well as its commitment to making a difference in the communities in which it operates.
Standard Chartered is named after two banks which merged in 1969. They were originally known as the Standard Bank of British South Africa and the Chartered Bank of India, Australia and China. Of the two banks, the Chartered Bank is the older having been founded in 1853 following the grant of a Royal Charter from Queen Victoria.
Recently, Standard Chartered Bank invested $140 million to lift its Nigeria subsidiary, Standard Chartered Bank Nigeria towards meeting the N25 billion minimum capitalisation for banks in Nigeria.
Union Bank London: The bank commenced operations in the city of London in September 1983 as a branch of Union Bank of Nigeria Plc. In February, 2005 it transformed into a full fledged subsidiary of Union Bank. The incorporation of the UK subsidiary strategically positioned the bank to offer banking services to its teeming customers as well as correspondent services to its parent company, Nigerian banks and other international banks worldwide.
HSBC: Headquartered in London, HSBC which prides itself as the world's local bank, is one of the largest banking and financial services organisations in the world. HSBC's international network comprises over 9,800 offices in 77 countries and territories in Europe, the Asia-Pacific region, the Americas, the Middle East and Africa.
With listings on the London, Hong Kong, New York, Paris and Bermuda stock exchanges, shares in HSBC Holdings plc are held by nearly 200,000 shareholders in some 100 countries and territories. The shares are traded on the New York Stock Exchange in the form of American Depositary Receipts.
Through an international network linked by advanced technology, including a rapidly growing e-commerce capability, HSBC provides a comprehensive range of financial services: personal financial services; commercial banking; corporate, investment banking and markets; private banking; and other activities.
International Banker of The Year Nominees
Profiles in Excellence...
Andrew Alli: Andrew Alli is the Country Manager of the International Finance Corporation (IFC), in Nigeria, a position which gives him overall responsibility for IFC's activities in the country. In the three and a half years that he has been in this position, IFC's activities in Nigeria have quadrupled, making Nigeria IFC's most active country in sub-Saharan Africa. Recently, IFC has invested is some landmark projects in Nigeria, including MTN and Dangote Industries' Obajana Cement project. IFC has also supported a number of banks, hotel projects, as well as those in the general manufacturing sector.
Alli attended Kings College, Lagos before going to the United Kingdom to study Electronics and Electrical Engineering. After graduating top of his class, he went on to work for Coopers and Lybrand in London as a management consultant. During this period, he qualified as a Chartered Accountant before going to work in S.G. Warburg, one of UK's leading investment banks. He joined IFC after obtaining an MBA from INSEAD in France. While at IFC he worked on the oil and gas and telecommunications sectors before taking up his position in Nigeria. A very conservative person, Alli does not miss his holidays.
Adebayo Alade-loba: Adebayo Alade-Loba is Director, Global Inves-tment Banking Group of Credit Suisse First Boston (CSFB) and Director of CSFB's Global Energy Group. Since joining CSFB from the International Finance Corporation (IFC) in 1985, he has advised clients including ExxonMobil, Shell, Total, Chevron, ENI, the Ghanaian and Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation, BHP, etc., on transactions with an aggregate value in excess of $20 billion on four continents. In 2005, Alade-Loba was CSFB team leader for the $4 billion Brass LNG financial feasibility study; the Port Harcourt and Kaduna Refineries privatisations and the landmark $600 million NNPC/ExxonMobil Satellite Oil Fields project financing programme.
Nkosana Moyo: Nkosana Moyo, a member of the Management and Investment Committees of Actis, is responsible for managing Actis business in the Africa region. He joined Actis in January 2004 and is based in London.
In his role as Managing Partner for Africa, Nkosana's responsibilities include providing business leadership to the regional fund team, formulation of business strategy for the region, deal origination, fund raising and investor relationship management.
Prior to joining Actis, Moyo was with the International Finance Corporation (IFC), in Washington DC. He also served as Zimbabwe's Minister for Industry and Trade after a career in industry and commerce as well as in financial services.
He has worked in senior positions in Africa, Europe as well as the US. He has a Ph.D in physics from Imperial College and an MBA from Cranfield School of Management. He is a World Economic Forum Global Leader of Tomorrow (GLT), and an Eisenhower Fellow.
Menachem Katz: Mr Menachem Katz, an Isreali, is the Director in charge of Africa in the International Monetary Fund, (IMF) and with direct responsibility for Nigeria. Every year, he leads an IMF team to Nigeria to conduct Article IV consultation discussions and review economic performance of the country with senior government officials. He also palyed a crucial role in the achievement of Policy Support Instrument which Nigeria got from the IMF prior to the nation's debt relief. His team usually meets with President Olusegun Obasanjo; the Minister of Finance, Dr. (Mrs.) Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala; the Governor of the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), Professor Charles Soludo; other senior officials of the government; representatives of the private sector, including the banking industry; and members of civil society and the international community.
Transactions of The Year Nominees
Profiles in Excellence...
Federal ministry of Finance/Nigeria's $36 billion Debt buy-back: In an unprecedented manner, the representatives of the Paris Club creditor countries had met October 2005 and agreed with the representatives of the Federal government on a comprehensive treatment of its debt. This agreement to implement the debt treatment framework for Nigeria announced by the Paris Club was announced on 29 June 2005.
After several years of negotiations, Nigeria was granted cancellation of $18 billion of its eligible debt of over $30 billion after a commitment to pay the Paris Club $12.4 billion, This figure comprises $6.3 billion in arrears plus another $6.1 billion for a debt buy-back operation.
The Debt Management Office (DMO), also played a major role in sorting out the nitty gritty of the debts. The DMO was established in 2000 to centrally coordinate the management of Nigeria's debt, which was hitherto being done by myriad establishments in an uncoordinated fashion. This diffused debt management strategy had led to inefficiencies.
Vmobile $1.1 bn equipment finance facility: Vmobile Nigeria recently completed a $1.1 billion medium-term debt financing agreement with leading international banks and a consortium of 16 top Nigerian banks. While the foreign banks raised $650 million, the local banks supported Vmobile with $450 million. The local facility is unique in many notable respects. It is indeed a landmark and first of its kind for project finance with a straight tenor of six years in the annals of financing in Nigeria. It is also the largest club lending by size and until recently, the maiden medium term financing in the local market.
The debt financing was meant to drive Vmobile's aggressive network rollout and support the ongoing coverage expansion programme tagged, Project ROSE, across the country. The financing deal, which is in two phases, is being provided by leading financial institutions in Nigeria, international financial institutions and reputable global GSM equipment vendors.
During Phase 1, the company is to access over $700 million, with about $250 million provided by offshore banks and equipment vendors while in Phase 2 of the deal, offshore lenders will provide over $400 million. Already, about $200 million has been drawn down.
Offshore lenders include African Export and Import Bank (Afrex-Im), Standard Chartered Bank, Rand Merchant Bank, Barclays Bank, Nordea Bank AB and Ericsson Credit AB while the consortium of 16 leading Nigerian banks/institution includes GTBank, First Bank, Zenith Bank, Afribank, Wema Bank, National Bank, Intercontinental Bank, UBA, Ecobank, Lead Bank, Access Bank and Oceanic International Bank. Others are MBC International Bank, First City Monument Bank, EIB International Bank and Kakawa Discount House".
BPE Sale of 51% of Transcorp Hilton: TThe sale of 51 per cent of government's shares in former Nicon Hilton Hotel by the Bureau of Public Enterprises (BPE), was concluded October 2005 with Capital Leisure and Hospitality Limited, owned mainly by Transnational Corporation (Transcorp) paying N14.7 billion ($105million).
Capital Leisure and Hospitality Limited, also comprise the Nigerian Social Insurance Trust Fund, Kapital Insurance, Harris Dredging Limited and Protea Hospitality Corporation.
This sale has resulted in the renaming of the hotel which is now Transcorp Hilton Hotel, Abuja, with Group Managing Director, United Bank for Africa Plc, Mr Tony Elumelu, as Chairman, Board of Directors. Other members of the Trancorp Hilton board, are President of Dangote Group, Aliko Dangote; Executive Director of Transcorp, Nicholas Okoye; Air Vice Marshal Muhtar Muhammed and Mr John Knight.
The $600m nnpc/mobil oil field financing: If there is one sector of the Nigerian economy that the impact of the consolidation in the banking industry has impressed most, it is the petroleum industry. After successfully participating in the $1.2 billion loan financing for the Oso Natural Gas Liquids (NGL) phase two project in 2004, Nigerian banks gained more confidence and eight of them were considered worthy to participate in arranging financing of up to $600 million for NNPC/ExxonMobil Satellite Oil field Programme.
The banks namely Investment Banking and Trust Company Plc, Union Bank of Nigeria Plc, United Bank for Africa Plc, Access Bank Plc, Zenith Bank Plc, Guaranty Trust Bank Plc, Standard Chartered Bank Nigeria Limited and First Bank of Nigeria Plc, teamed up with 20 other international banks to provide the funding.
Although the Nigerian banks were required to raise just $90 million of the financing programme (which will cover the NNPC's 60 percent share in the project), in which Credit Suisse First Boston was the financial adviser, total commitment from the local banks was $160 million, representing over 70 percent subscription.
The foreign banks although required to lend $260 million, pledged to commit $800 million to the project. Officials close to the transaction said this represents rising confidence of international lenders in Nigeria's growing economy.
Young Manager of The Year Nominees
Profiles in Excellence...
Ladi Balogun: Before his appointment was recently confirmed as the Managing Director/CEO of the consolidated First City Monu-ment Bank Plc, Ladi Balogun was the Deputy Managing Director/CEO of FCMB. He holds a Bachelors degree in Economics from the University of East Anglia, United Kingdom and an MBA from Harvard Business School, United States of America. With over 13 years experience in investment and commercial banking, in Europe, United States of America and Africa, Ladi Balogun began his banking career at Morgan Grenfell and Co. Limited, one of the UK's most prestigious investment banks, which was later consolidated into the investment banking division of Deutsche Bank. While at Morgan Grenfell, he worked in the areas of risk management and corporate finance (debt origination).
As a corporate finance officer, he was part of a team that structured numerous complex debt deals running into several billions of dollars in Latin America, Eastern Europe and the Asian sub-continent.
Ndidi Okonwko Nwuneli: Ndidi Nwuneli, 30, is the founder and Managing Partner, Leadership Effectiveness Accountability and Professionalism (LEAP) Africa, Nigeria, a non-profit organisation committed to inspiring, empowering and equipping a new cadre of African leaders.
She obtained her undergraduate honours degree in Multinational Manag-ement and Strategic Management from Wharton School and holds an MBA from Harvard.
She is a consultant at the West African Office of Ford Foundation, Center for Middle East Competitive Strategy, Palestine and Israel. Management Consultant, Bridgespan Group, McK-insey and Company and a director of Fate Foundation. She is adviser to a range of non-profit organisations in the United States and Africa.
Modupe Ozien Ozolua: Modupe Ozolua was born in Benin City, Nigeria in October 10th 1973. She had her early education in Nigeria from where she went to South Western College, San Diego and Devery International University, Los Angeles, California. She is the proprietor of Body Enhancement in Lagos, Nigeria. Modupe Ozolua has received numerous awards which include, Nigeria Women Awards, Dame International for Pioneering action Award 2003 among others.
Bruce Ayonote: Bruce Ayonote is the Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of Suburban Telecoms Nigeria. Ayonote's Suburban is involved in terrestrial and satellite transmission, IP Backbone service,co-location for telecom operators and value-added services.
They also carry out consultancy services, design and implementation of telecom solutions through affiliates including Suburban Telecom UK and Suburban Telecom Inc, USA.
The company which was established in 1999 renders services to big player such as MTN Nigeria and Haliburton.
Yewande Sadiku: Thirty three year old Yewande Sadiku is the Head of IBTC's Corporate Finance Department. She is directly responsible for the execution of the debt and equity issues, mergers and acquisitions, divestitures and financial advisory assignments handled by the Bank.
She is also responsible for the packaging, analysis and execution of a variety of assignments that include landmark privatisation and financial advisory mandates handled by the Bank.
Fund Manager of The Year Nominees
Profiles in Excellence...
IBTC Asset Manager Limited: IBTC Asset Management Limited is the wholly owned stockbroking and asset management subsidiary of Investment Banking & Trust Company Plc. The company manages the IBTC Nigerian Equity Fund, Nigeria's largest open-ended equity fund quoted on the Exchange.
FSDH Asset Management Limited: FSDH Asset Mana-gement Lim-ited (FAML) recently introduced 750,000 units at N1,000 each at par in Coral Growth Fund (CGF) to the investing public.
The Fund was established on February 1, 2001. It has been managed privately on behalf of privately solicited investors, by First Securities Discount House Limited.
Asset and Resource Management Company Ltd: In the second half of 2002, the Sponsors of the Discovery Fund (formerly known as Equity Growth Fund), Asset and Resource Management Company Ltd (ARM), took the Fund public via an Initial Public Offering. Subsequent to the IPO, the net assets of the Fund increased from N250 million to close to N1 billion by 2002.
The Nigeria International Growth Fund: The Nigeria Inte-rnational Growth Fund (NIGFUND) is a unit trust scheme managed by Fidelity Bank Plc. In a very short period of existence, NIGFUND has exposed the competence and efficiency of its managers. Although, relatively new on the Nigerian Stock Exchange (NSE), the new unit trust scheme has also been demonstrating consistent growth in both profitability and earnings.
Mergers & Acquisitions of the Year
Profiles in Excellence...
UBA/STB: The merger of Standard Trust Bank and United Bank for Africa Plc formed the emergence of Nigeria's first mega bank in the consolidation exercise of the Central Bank of Nigeria.. Apart from its record as the first successful merger in the ongoing banking consolidation, the consolidated UBA's aggregate financial and non financial strength clearly ranks it above its peers in the industry.
With a network of over 425 business offices across the country, balance sheet size of over N600bn, and an active customer base of over two million, United Bank For Africa Plc ranks as the biggest on-line, real-time bank in Nigeria and indeed sub-Saharan Africa.
Analysts are of the view that the combination of UBA, one of the top three banks with a proud history and conservative tradition and STB, with a visionary, dynamic and aggressive management style makes for a formidable team. The enlarged bank will have close to 5 million customers, making it truly Nigeria's biggest bank.
The seamless manner in which the merger was consummated sent other big banks back to the drawing table and created sleepless nights for others - a development that made the CBN to stop other big banks from merging with one another.
Intercontinental Bank: The new Intercontinental Bank comprising Intercontinental Bank, Equity Bank, Global Bank and Gateway Bank was one of the emerging banks to successfully conclude its merger without any rancour among its merging partners.
With a combined shareholders' fund of over N50 billion and assets in excess of N350 billion, the Intercontinental Bank emerged as the most capitalised bank in Nigeria.
The new Intercontinental Bank, which was also able to achieved full integration of its systems (enabling customers of the four banks to carry on transactions in any of their branches nationwide, irrespective of where their accounts are opened) from the very first day it announced the conclusion of its merger, now has a branch network of over 174 branches strategically located in the lengths and breadth of the country.
The bank, which also currently has close to $200 million of foreign currency lines, is targeting a total branch network of 200 before the end of the current financial year.
Mergers & Acquisitions of the Year Nominees
Profiles in Excellence...
Skye Bank: The successful merger of Skye Bank comprising Prudent Bank Plc, EIB International Bank, Bond Bank Limited, Reliance Bank Limited and Co-operative Bank Plc is also commendable.
The combined shareholders' fund of the group according to the information made available, is about N37.1 billion. Prudent Bank is said be bringing in N16.6 billion to the group; EIB International Bank N9.1 billion; Bond Bank N5 billion; Cooperative Bank N5 billion and Reliance Bank N1.4 billion.
Available information confirmed that with N37.1 billion capital base and branch network of over 160, the Skye group has now joined the league of the big players in the emerging consolidation era of the banking industry, even as the group asset base is now over N100 billion with deposit base of about N70 billion.
PlatinumHabib: Collectively, the emerging banking group, which is the merger of Platinum Bank and Habib Bank currently have shareholders' fund of over N25 billion; asset base of over N116 billion; deposit base of N60 billion; gross earnings of over N14 billion; network of 104 on-line, real time branches and over one and a half million customers, nationwide.
Habib Nigeria Bank Plc commenced operations on May 16, 1983 with a strategic intent to introduce and maintain a banking style with a difference, create a sound bank, and establish presence in every state of the country. Adhering tenaciously to the core essence of banking - trust, integrity, and the provision of financial security - the Bank has no doubt distinguished itself in the provision of retail banking services tailored to the needs of all categories of customers.
With gross earnings of N7.4 billion and pre-tax profit of N1.3 billion for the financial year ended December 2004, the Habib Bank has 69 strategically located across the country, and lives the ethos of its founding fathers, "to serve the society through its activities."
Platinum Bank Plc, which opened its doors to the banking public on November 1, 2000, is a benchmark for redefining banking service standards. Unarguably the most successful and fastest growing among all the new/recapitalized banks in the country in the new millennium, the Bank recently declared a profit of N1 billion and grew its total assets to N60 billion. A national banking franchise with retail banking focus, Platinum Bank has pioneered innovative products and services in keeping with its mantra - to redefine service standards in the industry.
Access Bank: While the infamous July 6, 2004 announcement by Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) Governor, Prof. Charles Soludo that all banks operating the country shore up their capital to a minimum of N25 billion by December 31, 2005 or close shop came as a rude shock and surprise to the banking industry, the reverse was the case at Access Bank Plc. Reason: The bank was already prepared and had previously planned for and slated its recapitalisation exercise for December 2005. The CBN recapitalisation directive therefore only accelerated Access Bank's growth programme and inorganic merger/acquisition strategy which it had earlier planned to actualize in 2006.
It was therefore no wonder that Access Bank was the second bank to raise additional capital post July 2004. And in spite of the uncertainty and near-crisis environment that characterized the industry between July and September 2004, Access Bank approached the capital market with a view to raising N8.7 billion via a public offer for subscription of 3,000,000,000 ordinary shares of N2.90 per share.
Simultaneously, Access Bank began the process of identifying potential merger candidates ably advised by HSBC - its financial adviser on merger and acquisition.
Bolstered by its pre-offer marketing, Access Bank made provisions for a supplementary 1,000,000,000 share offer to accommodate amounts raised above N8.7 billion.
And despite the challenges faced by the bank in the opening day of its offer when there was a national strike action that threatened to paralyse all economic activities in the country, at the end of the offer Access Bank still recorded a tremendous success as its public offer was 133 per cent oversubscribed.
Driven by the strategic objective to be one of the top five banks in Nigeria within the next five years in terms of balance sheet size with presence in at least one key international financial centre, Access Bank Plc, Capital Bank International and Marina International Bank Limited have since coalesced to form the new Access Bank.
Stock Offer of The Year Nominees
Profiles in Excellence...
FCMB Offer: First City Monument Bank Plc (FCMB) had its public offer in March 2005, to raise of N14,919,712,947.48, through subscription of 4.0 billion ordinary shares of 50 kobo each at N4.00 per share, in a bid to ensure compliance with the N25 billion minimum capital requirement for Nigerian banks as directed by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN).
Apart from the recapitalization objective, the public offer was also to generate funds required to finance the bank's strategic business development, including the establishment of the following 45 branches, upgrade its information technology infrastructure to meet the demands of its expanding network of branches and customer base and to provide the bank with additional working capital to support its enlarged operations and strengthen its competitive positioning in the industry.
FCMB dangled before the investing public, a robust projected profit before tax for the years ending 30th April, 2005, 2006 and 2007 of over N1 billion, N3 billion and N4.5 billion respectively.
Fidelity Bank Plc: Fidelity Bank Plc launched its own Initial Public Offer last June, rolling a total of 6.92 billion ordinary shares of 50 kobo each at N2.50 per share, with the target of raising about N17.3 billion, to enable it meet the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN's) directive on N25 billion minimum capitalisation.
Fidelity Bank then boasted of shareholders' funds of N11 billion, with the prime target of raising this to N28 billion at the end of the IPO.
The planned to use the proceeds from the offer to bolster its branch network, deepen relationship and businesses activities.
Fidelity's IPO was handled by four reputable issuing houses - Future View Securities Limited, Intercontinental Capital Markets, BGL Securities Limited and Fidelity Union Securities Limited.
Reputed to be one of the fastest growing new generation banks in the country, Fidelity Bank which, has refused to leave the club of the billion naira profit-making banks forecast earnings per share of 14 kobo, 20 kobo and 30 kobo for year 2005, 2006 and 2007 respectively. It also projects dividend per share of 8 kobo, 11 kobo and 15 kobo respectively for the above stated periods.
Diamond Bank: Diamond Bank grossed N4.65 billion proceeds from its Initial Public Offer (IPO) to raise its shareholders' funds far above the prescribed N25 billion. Its IPO also marked the conclusion of the third stage of its recapitalization strategy unveiled shortly after the CBN minimum capitalization was announced in 2004.
The bank's strategy which included a private placement exercise, retention of 2004 profits and acquisition of Lion Bank was driven primarily by the objective of retaining its brand equity post consolidation.
IBTC: Investment Banking & Trust Company Plc (IBTC) held its Initial Public Offer (IPO) of 2,629,016,023 ordinary shares of 50 kobo each at N4.30 per share in June 2005. The offer attracted considerable interest from the investing public, such that investors at a point sought for the extension of the closing date.
IBTC boasts of offering investors geometric rise in their investments, where N100,000 invested in the bank in 1989 now worth over N143 million as a result of capital appreciation alone, while shareholder who invested N100,000 in IBTC in 1989 would have received N22.96 million in dividend payouts to date.
IBTC's shareholders' funds grew from N12 million in 1989 to N7.8 billion as at March 31, 2004 through profit retention alone.
Ecobank Nigeria: Ever since the banking consolidation was announced, Ecobank Nigeria banking on the financial strength of its parent company -Ecobank Transnational Incorporated (ETI) had always indicated that it was going to go it alone.
Ecobank Nigeria held a successful public offer of 4.8 million ordinary shares of 50 kobo each at N3.20 per share. With strong backing from ETI which invested a total of N17 billion ($134 million), Ecobank Nigeria was able to cross the N25 billion mark.
Lifetime Achievement Award Nominees
Profiles in Excellence...
Babatunde Jose: Dr. Ismail Babatunde Jose, veteran journalist and newspaper administrator of repute turned 80 recently. Jose, the patriarch of Nigerian modern journalism began his carreer on a humble note as a technical trainee at Daily Times in 1941. He later rose to become editor and later managing director of the newspaper group in 1962.
His over three decades of meritorious service in the media actually began by happenstance-the economic recession occasioned by the Second World War. The young Jose had to discontinue his education at Saviour's High School, Oko-Awo, Lagos, because his father could no longer cope with rising school fees. His earnings had dropped drastically. So it was decided that Babatunde must start work immediately to augment the family income. He became a teacher, but taught for only a day and left because he could not manage the chaotic classroom populated by little children. At Jose's father's instance, a note from Sir Adeyemo Alakija, who was then Chairman of Daily Times was all he needed to resume work under Ayodele Lijadu, who was then the Daily Times editor. The rest is history
Wole Soyinka: Akinwande Oluwole Soyinka is Nigeria's foremost playwright. He is also poet, novelist, and critic. He received the Nobel Prize for Literature in 1986.
Soyinka attended Government College and University College in Ibadan before graduating in English in 1958 from the University of Leeds, in England. Upon his return to Nigeria, he founded a national theatre, The Masks (later the Orisun Theatre), and wrote his first important play, A Dance of the Forests, for the Nigerian independence celebrations. The play satirizes the fledgling nation by stripping it of romantic legend and by showing that the present is no more a golden age than the past.
Soyinka fuses Western elements with subject matter and dramatic techniques deeply rooted in Yoruba folklore and religion. He uses symbolism, flashback, and ingenious plotting to create a rich dramatic structure. His works exhibit humour and fine poetic style. He also has a gift for irony and satire, accurately matching the language of complex characters to their social position and moral qualities.
His autobiography, Ake: The Years of Childhood, was published in 1981 and a companion piece, Isara: A Voyage Around Essay, in 1989.
Emeka Anyaoku: Eleazar Chukwuemeka Anyaoku was born on January 18, 1933 in Obosi, in Anambra State. He attended the Merchants of Light School in Oba and as a College Scholar, the University College of Ibadan, at the time a college of the University of London. He obtained an honours degree in Classics. Anyaoku later attended specialist courses in Britain and France.
In 1959, Anyaoku joined the Commonwealth Development Corporation. Following Nigeria's independence, he was invited to join his country's diplomatic service and, in 1963, was posted to Nigeria's Permanent Mission to the United Nations in New York. His commitment to the concept of a common humanity has roots both in traditional African and Western philosophies, and has grown through 30 years of international service within and beyond the Commonwealth.
He became the third Commonwealth Secretary-General in July 1990, at a time when East-West and North-South relationships were undergoing fundamental change. Among his early tasks was to assist Commonwealth Heads of Government on a reappraisal of the future role of the association, giving it new strategic directions: to help countries build strong democracies guaranteeing individual liberty and human rights, while at the same time assisting in the major task of their sustainable development.
The hallmarks of Anyaoku's Secretary-Generalship have been his execution of that mandate and his remoulding of the Commonwealth Secretariat so that it could undertake these new duties efficiently and effectively. In 1966, shortly after the establishment of the Commonwealth Secretariat, he was seconded to the new organisation at the request of the first Secretary-General, Arnold Smith of Canada, as Assistant Director of International Affairs, later becoming Director and, in 1975, Assistant Secretary-General. In 1977, Commonwealth governments elected him Deputy Secretary-General with responsibility for international affairs and the Secretariat's administration. The Shagari administration in 1983 appointed Chief Anyaoku as Nigeria's Foreign Minister.
Chinua Achebe: Albert Chinualumogu Achebe was born to Isaiah Okafor, a Christian churchman, and Janet N. Achebe in November 16, 1930 in Ogidi, South-Eastern Nigeria. He married Christie Chinwe Okoli, September 10, 1961, and now has four children: Chinelo, Ikechukwu, Chidi, and Nwando. He attended Government College, Umuahia from 1944 to 1947 and University College in Ibadan from 1948 to 1953. He then got a B.A. from London University in 1953 and studied broadcasting at the British Broadcasting Corp. in London in 1956.
Since the 1950's, Nigeria has witnessed "the flourishing of a new literature which has drawn sustanence from both traditional oral literature and from the present and rapidly changing society," writes Margaret Laurence in her book Long Drums and Cannons: Nigerian Dramatists and Novelists. Chinua Achebe was one of the founders of this new literature, and over the years many critics have come to consider him the finest of the Nigerian novelists. His acheivement, however, has not been limited to his continent. He is considered by many to be one of the best novelists now writing in the English language.
Unlike some African writers struggling for acceptance among contemporary English-language novelists, Achebe has been able to avoid imitating the trends in English literature. Rejecting the European notion "that art should be accountable to no one, and [needs] to justify itself to nobody," as he puts it in his book of essays, Morning Yet on Creation Day, Achebe has embraced the idea at the heart of the African oral tradition: that "art is, and always was, at the service of man. Our ancestors created their myths and told their stories for a human purpose." For this reason, Achebe believes that "any good story, any good novel, should have a message, should have a purpose."
Achebe's feel for the African context has influenced his aesthetic of the novel as well as the technical aspects of his work.
His most celebrated novel, Things Fall Apart has sold million of copies and has been translated into many languages.
Adamu Ciroma: Appointed Finance Minister during President Olusegun Obasanjo's first term early June 1999, Ciroma is a veteran politician from Yobe State. Upon taking office, he made it clear that his first priority was to clean up the mess left by the successive military regimes. He told the 'Financial Times' on Aug. 8, 1999, that his mission was "to attempt to restore macro-economic stability so Nigeria is on a firmer footing at the start of the new millennium". Ciroma is a supporter of privatisation, including in oil companies. Ciroma had said in the months after taking office that the first phase of privatising oil companies, parts of the power utility NEPA and other state enterprises would be implemented between 2000-2001.
He had been governor of Central Bank of Nigeria, Minister in the Second Republic and Minister under the Abacha regime. His wife, is the Minister of Women Affairs. Twice Mallam Adamu Ciroma had aspired to be President of Nigeria and many who know the graduate of History from the University College, Ibadan believe that he fits the bill of Aristotle's philosopher - king because of his love for scholarship.
Akintola Williams: He is Nigeria's most foremost chartered accountants and without question indeed a doyen of that professional discipline. Mr. Akintola Williams, brother to the late legal icon Rotimi Williams is the founder of the prestigious Akintola Williams Deloitte & Touchel a pace-setter in the corporate accounting arena.
Sam Aruru Amuka-Pemu: Sam Amuka-Pemu, journalist's journalist, columnist, editor and publisher of the Vanguard newspaper has undoubtedly impacted on Nigeria and Nigerian journalism in more fundamental ways that transcend simplistic analysis. Today, pretty few know that this stern, jolly good fellow was something of a legendary left winger in the football eleven of the famous Government College Ughelli and that his then classmate was Peter Pan (Enahoro), who held forth at the right wing of the same football team. The depth and sweep of his contributions to the profession speak eloquently of old school discipline, commitment and panache. From reporting, column writing, editing and publishing, 'Uncle Sam' has set a pace and standard that remain simply flattering to the profession's pride and meaning.
Gani Fawehinmi: Gani is a distinguished Lawyer, human rights activist and philanthropist. Driven by a principled resistance to dictatorship of any kind he is also intolerant of infringement of peoples rights. He was born in the thirties in a small town of Ondo state. His exceptional brilliance earned him a scholarship to study law in the U.K. He came back to become the defender of the less priviledged and the voice of the "voiceless" in society. His coming into human right activism started in 1966 when he had to defend a civil servant that was unjustly treated by a minister at the time without collecting his profesional fee because the fellow in question was very poor. This led to his incerceration for some time but rather than being deterred it served as the catalyst for his eventual self-impossed task
His brilliance as a lawyer has continued to earn him popularity hence his chamber is one of the best in the country. The Weekly Law Review that he publishes is the reference point to all lawyers that wishes to succeed. The government he fought even respects his opinion and one was not surprised when a former dictator, General Ibrahim Babangida pronounced him the only Nigerian he respects. The eventual coming of democracy, which he fought for with his life and resources has led to his overdue recognition by being appointed a member of the SAN. Nigerian political history cannot be fully written without his name being mentioned.
Muhammadu Uwais: Justice Muhammadu Uwais is the Chief Justice of the Federation and who has shone like many stars in a difficult legal milieu. He successfully navigated attempts to besmirch his intergrity when allegations of impropriety were leveled against some Supreme Court Judges. He also chairs the national Judicial Council.
Olorogun Michael Ibru: Olorogun Michael Ibru is patriach and head of the Ibru family and sprawling business organisation. Chairman of Oceanic Bank Plc, under his guidance the Ibru's business and political pre-eminence are gaining more grounds
Sheila Solarin: Sheila is the widow of late Tai Solarin. An European who has come to believe in Nigeria, she effectively manages the famous Mayflower College in Ikenne founded by her husband. In her own ways she has indeed touched lives and shared dreams of a better Nigeria.
Chima Ubani: Chima Ubani, crusader, organizer, activist, revolutionary, strategist etc was committed to mother earth on Thursday, October 27, last year. He died at age 42, but he recorded enough achievements that could last others for two lifetimes. He died in a motor accident around Potiskum, Yobe State, on Wednesday September 21, alongside the deputy photo editor of the Vanguard newspapers, Tunde Oyeleru. Chima was on his way to Yola from Maiduguri after the hugely successful rally staged to protest the fuel hike by government. He had the option of travelling in the plane with Labour leader Adams Oshimhole but characteristically, selflessly, gave up his seat and ended up travelling in Oshiomhole's jeep. The rest, as they say, is history. It suffices to say that Chima did not die in vain.
Born Chimaobi Enyinna Ubani on March 22, 1963, in Obete Umuohia, Obingwa Local Government Area of Abia State, he was educated at the University of Nigeria, Nsukka, where he rose to heroic heights as the students' union president in the 1985/86 session. He took pride of place as a key figure in the revolutionizing of campus politics in the UNN. He became a cult figure and most of the students swore by his name. He extended his radical worldview nationwide through his activities in the National Association of Nigerian Students (NANS).
A student of Crop Science in his university studies, Chima surprised the world in his catholic mastery of literally all subjects. He rose through the ranks in Civil Liberties Organisation (CLO) before ending up as the Executive Director of the leading rights group.
Judge of the Year Award Nominees
Justice Muhammadu Uwais: Justice Muhammadu Uwais, the Chief Justice of Nigeria (CJN) is reputed for maintaining the integrity of the judiciary in the face of awesome obstacles and constraints. He is the longest serving CJN and is expected to retire this year. He was appointed into the apex court in 1982. At the Supreme Court, Uwais represents an epitome of integrity and anti-corruption though some people under the aegis of Derivation Front tried unsuccessfully to stain his apparel. It would be recalled that the CJN recently returned about N5 billion to the nation's purse being unspent money in respect of the revenue allocated to the judiciary in the 2005 budget. President Olusegun Obasanjo mentioned Uwais' noble act to the National Assembly while presenting the 2006 budget to the lawmakers.
Uwais also spoke out recently against executive lawlessness at the All Nigerian Judges Conference. He condemned the brazen disrespect for court decisions and he said this before President Obasanjo.
Justice Sylvanus Nsofor: Appeal Court Justice Sylvanus Nsofor has delivered so many judgements but his dissenting judgement on the 2003 election petition filed by the All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP) in the presidential candidates, Major-General Muhammadu Buhari (rtd), stood out in the courage and bravery behind the pronouncement. He disagreed with his learned colleagues who okayed the election in spite of their voiding of the Ogun State presidential election results saying that the election was fraught with irregularities and should not stand.
He therefore nullified the victory of President Obasanjo based on that 2003 election results. Justice Nsofor had his say but the majority justices of the court had their way. With that judgement however, Justice Nsofor seemed to have written his name in gold. He has entered the pantheon of highly respected courageous justices in the land like Justice Kayode Esho (rtd).
Justice Binta Murtala Nyako: Justice Binta Murtala Nyako is of the Federal High Court, Abuja. She delivered a landmark judgement in the Conference of Nigeria Political Parties (CNPP) versus the Inspector-General of Police case over the issue of police permit for public procession. The question for determination was whether given the colonial control of public procession at a point there was no constitution and with a constitution now in place guaranteeing freedom of movement and association, whether there is any need for police permit for people to hold public procession. It was an interesting case. Justice Murtala Nyako ruled that any aspect of the Public Order Act was unconstitutional.
She also handled the money laundering case preferred by the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) against the former Inspector-General of Police, Mr. Tafa Balogun. Balogun is at present serving a jail term over his corrupt practices.
Justice Bunmi Oyewole: Justice Bunmi Oyewole has handled very important cases bordering on anti-corruption and through his judgements, he has not only shown that he is incorruptible, his rulings has also helped to push further the government's anti-corruption crusade. Oyewole read law at the Obafemi Awolowo University, Ile-Ife, and was appointed into the Lagos High Court, Ikeja Division by the Lagos State Governor, Senator Bola Ahmed Tinubu. Justice Oyewole is of the radical bend and this has shown in his judgements. He is today the busiest anti-corruption judge and has given landmark decisions in that area. Among the judgements he has delivered included the Economic and Financial Crimes Corruption (EFCC) versus Anajemba, EFCC versus Nwude and Attorney-General of Lagos State versus Major Hamza Al-Mustapha, Bamaiyi and others.
Justice Niki Tobi: Supreme Court Justice Niki Tobi was chairman of the National Political Reforms Conference (NPRC) which ended last August in Abuja.
Tobi from Delta State has made tremendous impacts on the bench as a courageous judge having delivered some landmark judgements. But he has also handled many political assignments for the government at one point or another. Apart from the NPRC job, Tobi was also Chairman of the review committee set up by the late General Sani Abacha military junta to take another look at the outcome of the 1994/95 Constitutional Conference in Abuja.
Airline of The Year Nominees
Profiles in Excellence...
Aero Contractors: Aero Contractors of Nigeria (ACN) has been one of the dominant and visibe names in the Nigerian aviation industry since 1959. Today ACN offers a complete range of aviation services, operating both fixed wing and helicopter services as well as extensive technical maintenance and overhaul support.
It was formed in 1959; officially registered in Nigeria in 1960 and, at that time, wholly owned by Schreiner Airways B.V. of the Netherlands. It became a company with an initial 40 per cent Nigerian holding in 1973 and subsequently 60 per cent in 1976, anticipating the requirements of the Indigenization Decree.
In January 2004, Schreiner Airways was bought by Canadian Helicopter Corporation (C.H.C.) which acquired 40 per cent holding of while the 60 per cent majority share remained within the Ibru family.
The company started out in the early 1960s as an air charter operator. Charters in those days were varied both in client and scope. It provided charters to church missions to spread the 'Word' throughout the country and to Niger Pools to transport betting coupons to Lagos. It later diversified into the embryonic oil industry, particularly in the Delta area. A contract was secured to operate one Aztec piston airplane and one Alouette helicopter for (initially) Tenneco and then later Texaco.
From these early beginnings in the 70s, contracts were awarded to supply helicopter and ai

Original_Search_Title: Unique Rural Market Attracts Traders From Three Countries
Headline: Kenya: Unique Rural Market Attracts Traders From Three Countries
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Chwele open-air market in Bungoma District is unique and unrivalled in
its diversity.
Clients and traders who patronise the market are often confused by its
rare trading methods.
Unlike other open-air markets all over the world where people argue and
haggle over prices, there is neither quibbling nor bargaining over
commodities at Chwele.
Prices, especially those of agricultural commodities, are preset and
standardised by the Kenya Agricultural Commodity Exchange (Kace), which has
a Market Information Point (MIP) at the centre.
Ruth Baraza , Emmah Wasike and Gladys Sitati after buying pots made from
special clay at Chwele market in Bungoma. Pic by Peter Ochieng
It is claimed that up to Sh15 million changes hands at the market on
Fridays, the market day, despite the rigidity of pricing. The market draws
its customers from a rich hinterland spreading across the borders to
neighbouring Uganda and Tanzania.
As a result of the rising economic importance of the market, the
Government will soon build modern stalls and provide the centre with piped
water and electricity at a cost of more than Sh33 million, according to
Bungoma County Council chairman James Lusweti.
High volume
According to records at the county council, the market handles an
average of 50,000 traders on Fridays.
Due to the high volume of trading activities, there are also two other
minor market days, Monday and Wednesday. Trucks loaded with wares and
hundreds of donkeys assembled at various spots bring out the interplay
between modern and ancient transport.
And unlike other rural markets, business transactions here start as
early as 3am, with spotlights being used for lighting as money changes
hands.
"The Ministry of Local Government has set aside Sh15 million to develop
modern stalls at the market and the project is expected to kick-off in
January next year," said Lusweti.
Lusweti said that as a result of the sponsorship, the council had
suspended an Sh800,000 stall development project.
"The Sirisia Constituency Development Fund has also approved Sh5 million
to draw piped water from River Kuywa while the market will benefit from a
Sh13.5 million rural electrification programme that is underway," Lusweti
added.
Currently, people draw water from the nearby Lutaso and Lukhuna
springs. Lusweti said Chwele was of great economic significance to the
county council because it leads in revenue generation.
"On Fridays, the council collects around Sh70,000 in trade levies. We
feel we can generate close to Sh100,000 if we step-up our revenue collection
ability and tighten loopholes that give room to corruption," he said.
Cross border trade
If Chwele is unique, then the traders who converge there are even more
so. Apart from those who come from different parts of the country including
Central, Coast, Eastern and Nairobi provinces, there are those who come from
Uganda through the Lwakhakha border point and Tanzania through
Isebania.
Located only 30 kilometres from the Lwakhakha border point, the market
witnesses an increasingly higher number of traders from Uganda, bringing in
bananas and groundnuts and buying groceries supplied from the slopes of Mt
Elgon and other parts of Bungoma District.
"Most traders and farmers, especially from Kopsiro in Mt Elgon District,
use donkeys to ferry commodities because our landscape does not favour motor
transport," Alex Ngeywa, a trader at the market, said.
Mama Josephina Mulongo, who has been trading at the market for the last
seven years, said she had benefited from the centre.
"One has only to know what to invest in to flourish," she said. Mulongo
deals in clay pots.
Agricultural commodities traded at Chwele include green maize, dry
maize, carrots, bananas, oranges, onions, cabbages, groundnuts, potatoes,
Irish potatoes, beans, peas, cassava, pineapples and millet.
Fish, chicken and livestock brought in from as far away as West-Pokot
District in Rift Valley Province are also found at the market.
Standard prices
Livestock sold at the market come from as far as Kajiado and even
Turkana districts.
"The animals sold here are mostly for slaughter in butcheries across
Western and Nyanza provinces," one trader said.
David Simiyu, the officer in charge of the MIP office at the market,
said price standardisation of commodities had saved farmers and traders from
exploitation.
"Most local farmers and traders know the pricing of various commodities
and cannot allow themselves to be defrauded by unscrupulous dealers who want
to make abnormal profit," Simiyu said.
Lusweti complained that an upsurge in criminal activities at the market
was threatening business.
"Bungoma DC, Joseph Irungu, was recently forced to outlaw trading
activities at night and directed that business starts at 6am due to crime,"
said the county council chairman.

Original_Search_Title: Luweero War Survivors a Thorn in Gen. Museveni's Campaign
Headline: Uganda: Luweero War Survivors a Thorn in Gen. Museveni's Campaign
Body_Text:
Luweero — IS President Yoweri Museveni defying the old adage, "You can't eat your
cake and have it too," The answer may be Yes.
Gen. Museveni, a man who has always opposed the multiparty system for
all the years he has been in power, championed the opening up of political
space in a move that surprised everybody.
Save for probably himself, none of his buddies rarely knew that Museveni
meant business. But he is defying the above expression and having his cake
too because he is likely to be a beneficiary of the multiparty system he
supported when he bounces back to State House in 2006.
All indications show that as incumbent, he has an advantage over other
candidates. His main challenger Col. Dr. Kizza Besigye of the Forum for
Democratic Change (FDC) is in jail over treason and rape charges.
Museveni is in the countryside soliciting for votes.
The other political opponents, the Uganda Peoples' Congress (UPC) under
Miria Kalule Obote and the Democratic Party led by Mr Ssebaana Kizito are on
the edge of being torn apart by divisions and disorganistaion.
Independents, Nasser Sebaggala is still haunted by ghosts from his
former party (DP) while Dr Abed Bwanika has an Identity crisis as many
Ugandans do not know him.
For the two days I followed President Museveni in his campaign trail in
Luweero, Nakasongola and Nakaseke districts, which formed the foundation of
the war that catapulted him into power, I landed on one poster of
Col. Besigye pinned on a wooden door of a desolate house in a remote village
of Kangave in Semuto.
Ssebaana had some posters scattered in Wobulenzi, Luweero while Miria,
Bwanika and Sebaggala had none.
Museveni's visit to Luweero came two days after Ssebaana's, which brings
a question.
Didn't Ssebaana take the posters there? Secondly, if he took them, where
did they go? Your guess could be as good as mine.
Affordable material
My assumption is that the posters are the cheapest election material any
candidate can afford. But some residents claimed the posters of other
presidential candidates could have been removed ahead of Museveni's visit by
supporters, an allegation Daily Monitor could not independently confirm as
there was no evidence and police said no one has reported to them such
cases.
However, the Press Secretary to the President, Mr Onapito Ekomoloit who
was on the campaign trail said, the absence of posters for President
Museveni's opponents in the area is an indication of his candidate's
popularity.
"Since this is the opening of the campaigns, there is no strength of the
other political parties and there is no evidence to show that the posters
were removed," Onapito said.
With an illiterate population that relies on how much information they
have accessed in order to make a decision, the absence of posters of the
other candidates should be an issue of concern.
If UPC, FDC, Sebaggala and Bwanika have not sent their posters in the
area then they are making a big mistake because the voters in the
countryside may not know how they look like as many only depend on radios
and a few own television sets.
Unlike the previous campaigns, the huge crowds, which used to
characterise rally venues and became a basis of judging the strength of
presidential candidates, in Luweero this time it was a different story.
Local Council officials said that there was similar low turn up when
Ssebaana visited the area, two days earlier.
Museveni's supporters said that the number of people at rallies had
reduced because his campaign team set up meeting places to reduce on the
distance covered by people to hear their candidate speak.
