There could, however, be two or three scenarios to it. One, that people
have become fatigued with endless electioneering from village to national
level that at times do not address their plight or the population has lost
interest all together.
The other scenario particularly for President Museveni is that rigorous
security checks by the Presidential Guard Brigade (PGB) make some people
stay away.
Oblivious of this Museveni at times drove ahead of the guards so that he
can talk to people along the way without interruption.
But the unrelenting guards would catch up with him and he was forced to
shout at them in Swahili.
To the journalists who were hungry for the news, apart from the comments
he made about the UPDF invasion of Congo agaisnt the backdrop of the
Internal Court of Justice ruling, the rest of the speech was usual rhetoric
in the media speak.
His address dwelt on the enumerations of the NRM's achievements which we
have heard overtime, how people can eradicate poverty, his bashing of the
opposition and his vision for East Africa region.
Different agenda
However, the trip was not all about politics. Museveni occasionally
stopped along the way and freely conversed with his former National
Resistance Army (NRA) volunteer fighters as if they were village mates. Both
parties with guards at bay recounted their days during the five-year
war.
Others reminded the president of the cassava they gave his rebel outfit
while others told him they shared a bed with him in their rundown homes as
he hid from the government soldiers. The villagers looking haggard and
wearily, are a big contrast to those they helped bring in power.
Though they are living through hard times, their pride of having
participated in the war has never wavered. Other people here have got
personal problems and wanted immediate presidential intervention.
One girl shed tears before the President requesting him to pay her
school fees after failing to resume school in the past three years due to
lack of money.
"Mzee nkunoonyezza ebbanga ddene, leero nkufunnye" (I have been looking
for you for long but now I have found you), the girl aged about 17 years old
said along the Gayaza-Ziroobwe road meaning,
The girl collapsed on Museveni's feet.
Museveni held her up and directed Col. Proscovia Nalweyiso, his aide on
welfare to ensure he finds a solution to the girl's problem.

Original_Search_Title: Kenyans Migrate to Ethiopia
Headline: Kenyans Migrate to Ethiopia
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Some residents of Eastern Province have migrated to Ethiopia in search
of food and water.
Deputy PC Benjamin Lemlem said the migrants had moved with their
livestock in search of water and pasture.
Lemlem said at least one million people in the region need relief
food.
Kitui, Moyale, Isiolo and Marsabit districts are the worst hit.
Lemlem said the region needs about 60,000 bags of maize per month. Those
in Moyale and Marsabit districts have moved to Ethiopia.
In Moyale, the villagers have to travel more than 70km in search of
water.
"All the water points in Marsabit District have dried up and hundreds of
pastoralists have shifted to Moyale and Isiolo districts," said Lemlem.
This has increased the number of pastoralists in Isiolo and Turbi area
of Marsabit.
Moyale police boss Hesbon Kadenge said they have been escorting
villagers to water points to avoid conflicts over the scarce commodity.
Lemlem said 10 of the 13 districts in the province need food
urgently.
The administrator said there has been prolonged drought in Kitui
District.
In Malindi, more than 145,000 people in remote areas are facing
starvation.
Malindi District Commissioner Jan Ireri said the most hit areas were
Chakama, Adu, Sosobora, Fundisa, Marafa, Bungale and Magarini.
Speaking in his office, Ireri said there was no cause for alarm as the
district had received 3,000 bags of maize, 1,500 bags of beans, 1,500
cartons of powdered milk and 1,500 cartons of cooking oil.
He said the Government, through the Ministry of Agriculture, will spend
Sh5.5 million to construct dams in Malindi through the Arid Land Resource
Management project.
He asked those living near River Sabaki to use its waters for
irrigation.
He also appealed to them to grow drought resistant crops such as
cassava.
He asked to Non-Governmental Organisations and well wishers to assist
the residents.
The DC asked Malindi MP Lucas Maitha and Magarini MP Harrison Kombe to
use the Constituency Development Funds on projects aimed at alleviating
poverty.

Original_Search_Title: Famine Threat to 1.6m People Across Nyanza
Headline: Kenya: Famine Threat to 1.6m People Across Nyanza
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Some 1.6 million people in Nyanza Province face famine following a
massive crop failure this year.
This represents 30 per cent of the province's population of 5.4
million.
The Government has placed the crop failure in 12 districts at 60 per
cent.
The provincial director of agriculture, Mr Jacob Odondi, yesterday said
despite the trend, some highland areas had recorded substantial crop
yields. Lowland areas such as Bondo, Siaya, Suba and Nyando districts stand
to be hardest hit with up to 80 per cent crop failure.
Boosted the yields
"The situation is fair in the highland areas such as Kisii and Nyamira
where the short rains substantially boosted the yields. Most crops in these
areas were at the silk stage during the short rains and have managed to beat
the dry spell," he said.
Residents of parts of Nyando could, however, get some reprieve from the
expected high yields of rice grown in the area under irrigation.  Concern
has, however, been raised because much of the harvested crop finds its way
to markets elsewhere, including Nairobi.
Mr Odondi said the lowland areas failed to get adequate short rains
causing thousands of hectares of crop to wilt.
Last year, the province suffered a similar fate with a 70 per cent crop
loss due to failed long rains. The situation was, however, contained through
Government relief food programmes and importation of maize from the Rift
Valley province and parts of Northern Tanzania.
But yesterday, the PDA urged residents to revert to fast maturing and
drought resistant crops to beat hunger.
"Our appeal to the people is for them to resort to planting other crops
that are likely to withstand drought conditions. Crops such as cassava do
well even in the most difficult conditions and would be helpful in
maintaining food security. Focus should not be on maize alone," he
said.
He noted climatic conditions had greatly changed and farmers should use
yield enhancing inputs such as fertiliser.
Deputy provincial commissioner Aggrey Busena yesterday acknowledged the
looming famine and expressed fears of sharp rises in grain prices.
A two-kilogramme tin of maize is going for between Sh30 and Sh35 but
come January these could rise to between Sh40 and Sh45.
He however said respective district disaster committees had been ordered
to make urgent evaluations of the famine situation in their areas so that
the Government could plan how to assist.
Western Province also experienced a shortfall in maize production this
year but officials say the situation is not serious.
Provincial Director of Agriculture Philip Makhete said only three
million bags of maize were harvested instead of the anticipated five million
bags. Some 19,026 bags of beans were harvested.
But the deputy provincial commissioner, Mr Addy Kaaria, said yesterday
despite the shortfall in production the situation was not serious. In some
parts of the province, residents were relying on traditional food crops to
supplement maize. In the Rift Valley province, Laikipia, Samburu, Kajiado,
parts of Narok, Turkana and parts of West Pokot districts are hardest
hit.
Turkana district has recorded the highest food shortage. The district
has been relying on relief food since independence due to lack of
rainfall.
Though no residents are reported to have died from hunger in the
province, Provincial Commissioner Wilfred Ndolo said the livestock situation
in the province was pathetic.
He said 40 percent of livestock has been moved from Narok district due
to lack of pasture and driven to Tanzania and neighbouring districts like
Nakuru.
Affected districts
"Pastoralists in the affected districts have been moving from division
to division in search of pasture. Some of our livestock are currently
grazing in Tanzania," Mr Ndolo told the Nation.
Mr Ndolo said livestock owners were being encouraged to sell some of
their herds.
"We are advising residents especially in the five districts to sell
their livestock early enough instead of waiting for them to die. They can
use the money for their upkeep until the government sends relief food in
those areas," he said.
The government, the PC said, had included Nakuru among food deficient
districts since only four of its 16 divisions had enough food.
Last season, Narok district produced 1,040,000 bags of maize, 1,873,800
bags of wheat and 65,000 bags of beans. Turkana district produced only
12,334 bags of maize last season. There were no beans, wheat or barley
produced in the district last season.

Original_Search_Title: Lake Kivu: Business Unusual!
Headline: Rwanda: Lake Kivu: Business Unusual!
Body_Text:
Kigali — The dark grey light of the morning is slowly overpowering the
former black blanket of the night, as I stroll down the fringes of the
lake, with a multiple of small cliques of people here and there. This
is Nyakariba landing site. The volume of their voices seems to be
increasing at a rate proportionate with the light unfolding, this time
making a definite breakthrough, capturing and 'mopping up' all the
darkness. The lake maybe has a different timetable, for it is still
silently asleep and quiet, save for the minute wavelets which are
broken at the supposed walls of its 'house'. The warm breeze blows
silently and gently, with the laurels of the trees rustling and
hissing. It is a cool morning hallo! The cliques are eagerly waiting
for the fishermen to start rowing their boats towards the landing site
a place some of them consider their own 'golden coast'.
As few minutes go by, then some melodious voices in a captivating
rhythm, with an obsessing rhyme are heard from afar, deep bases and
tenor, same lyrics like those out on a hunting day or the strong and
muscular marching to war. As the fishermen land, women at the landing
site start clapping frantically, preparing their buckets and weighing
scales to measure and carry the fish to the nearby market.
As they weigh the sambaza (small fish), fishermen start off loading
their nets and get ready to go around to the nearby restaurants to take
their breakfast. It is noisy everywhere, women are scrambling for fish
because the catch has been scanty.
Mama Olive, a famous businesswoman around the lake, has already
gathered about one hundred kilograms and waits for a pick-up from
Kigali to take the fish to markets in the city. "We did not have large
catch because there was moonlight last night. When there is moonlight
fish harvesting becomes a problem," she says.
The next day is a market-day; a Friday morning, Congolese residents
from the neighboring DR Congo, rowing their boats towards the Nyakariba
landing site.
Their boats fully loaded with matooke (bananas), mamesa, cassava
flour and other food stuffs. The Rwanda Revenue Authority customs
officer Marcel Rukeratabaro is already stationed to collect government
revenues. He says the Congolese residents have created a strong
relationship with the people of Kibuye province mainly those around the
lake.
"They share many things. They bring food stuffs and also buy animals
and fish from here, it is just a bilateral relationship," Marcel says.
He says the Congolese also contribute to the government because they
pay taxes for the goods they export to Kibuye.
"We just pay Frw200 per a bunch of matooke," Muba, a resident from
DRC says as he loads off his bananas from the boat.It is now ten in the
morning, restaurants open and hot soups are ready. Brochettes and
ibirayi byokeje are already prepared. People start enjoying the local
beer (urwagwa and ubushera) which costs only Frw100 per half litter.
Young boys are busy loading matooke on to lorries from Kigali. The
more bunches he loads, the much more money he makes. On the other side
of the market is the animals' market. Pigs, sheep and goats are the
only animals sold there. A goat costs between Frw700 to Frw15000. Pigs
are very expensive. The huge pig costs about Frw40000, and the rest
ranges from Frw25000 to Frw10000 but they simply negotiate. Mugabo says
he has spent twenty years in pigs' business. "Before I buy or sell a
pig, I check all the diseases it might have." He puts down the pig and
opens its mouth. He checks under the tongue to examine the diseases he
expects it to have. "The Congolese love meat so much; they buy goats,
pigs and sheep here and take them to DRC," Mugabo says.
Life along the lake is generally very interesting and terrific.
People never get hungry. Their granaries are filled to the brim all the
year round. Residents around this place have got water throughout the
year, power is intermittently available.
Unfortunately, there are many young boys and girls not in school.
They just loiter and meander around the trading center. They are only
busy during the market day; lifting luggage to get some money.
Vincent Bizimana commonly called Bishyashya is a security guard
around the lake. He says that he and colleagues have to make sure the
lake is kept clean and safe. "We have to chase every body who tries to
misuse the lake. We protect the environment and make sure that even
fishermen don't catch immature fish," he said. The Rwanda Revenue
Authority Customs officer Marcel Rukeratabaro says that there are some
traces of smuggling. "They are very difficult to trap; you cannot
understand that one is a smuggler since all those who do it are
residents and businessmen around this town". He says.
Nido, Tigerhead batteries, Tomato source, Motor oil and sugar are
the commonly smuggled items. Prostitution has also emerged; young girls
walk to bars and around lodges looking for men. Mucyumbati, around the
Kibuye museum has become the ground for almost every prostitute. The
girls are as cheap as two bottles of beer or one thousand five hundred
francs.

Original_Search_Title: Food Demand Down
Headline: Uganda: Food Demand Down
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE demand for staple foodstuffs has reduced, forcing prices down, a weekly survey has revealed.
A bunch of matooke was sold at sh7,000 from sh10,000 at Nakasero Market. A kilogramme of maize flour went for sh600 from sh700, while beans were sold at sh500 from sh700.
"Few people are rushing for the food because of the festive season during which consumers preferred meat, chicken, matooke, sugar, posho, powder milk, cooking oil, rice and irish potatoes," Robert Mugisa, a trader at Nakasero Market, explained.
Mugisa added that there is plenty of maize, beans, tomatoes, oranges, matooke, cassava and maize flour in the market.
He said meat, beef, chicken and turkey prices were still up because they are still on high demand.
Mugisa predicted that prices of chicken, beef, matooke, rice and irish potatoes would go up on New Year's day.
Chicken was sold between sh7,000 and sh15,000 at Nakasero Market. Turkey was sold between sh20,000 and 30,000 while beef was hovering between sh3,500 and sh4,000.

Original_Search_Title: Bush Approves Sh7.7 Billion New Aid for Congo
Headline: Kenya: Bush Approves Sh7.7 Billion New Aid for Congo
Body_Text:
Nairobi — At least $52 million (Sh3.588 billion) annually for fiscal years 2006 and 2007 will be provided to the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) under legislation signed last week by President George W. Bush.
These funds are intended to enhance the United States' and international organisations' efforts to fight political, social and economic challenges facing the central Africa nation.
The Act provides for bilateral assistance programmes and establishes US political, social, civil and economic policy objectives for the DRC, a country that earlier in 2006 held its first fully democratic election since 1960.
Recent conflicts in the nation have resulted in four million dead, 800,000 refugees scattered throughout nine neighbouring countries and the collapse of state services.
Increase contributions
The Act states that the United States should work with international partners to encourage other nations to increase their contributions to the DRC. International donors, led primarily by the United Nations and the European Union, were active in the DRC in the months leading up to the election by providing aid, funds and peacekeeping support to the nation.
For the DRC to continue to make progress, the international community must maintain its support, experts on the region said in a panel discussion at the Brookings Institution in Washington December 18.
The government of the DRC must be committed to achieving the policy objectives outlined by the United States and other international donors, the Act states.
The Act allows the US secretary of state to withhold assistance if the government of the DRC does not make sufficient progress toward accomplishing these objectives.
The Act also recommends that the US president appoint a special envoy for Africa's Great Lakes region to help improve stability and security in that region of Africa. The Act also will provide funds that could complement current US projects to improve health care, agriculture, democratic institutions and education in the DRC.
The United States Agency for International Development currently provides key health services in the country, including malaria treatments, routine vaccinations and HIV/Aids prevention and care. It also supports a programme to help farmers grow more and better crops, and distributes improved seeds. As part of this program, USAID has assisted 50,000 farmers in growing cassava, a staple crop in the DRC.

Original_Search_Title: Why is Sharing So Scaring These Days?
Headline: Uganda: Why is Sharing So Scaring These Days?
Body_Text:
The one image I know will not leave my mind for a while is that of the little boy (R.I.P) and his grandmother who were left to starve to death somewhere in Bushenyi. Most likely, by now most people have forgotten all about this boy and they have gone back to their daily lives, eating chunks of pork and enjoying beer every evening, after all they can afford it. It beats me when I think about how we can be so uncaring.
Whatever happened to the African spirit of helping? Are people so hard up even in the villages that no one will spare an old starving lady and her grandson a tuber or two of cassava or potatoes and some beans?
What happened to sharing? I remember the good old days when people were always willing to share. Recently, I asked a few colleagues if they have heard anyone invite them to join them for a meal, they all said "no". Remember the kind words "jjangu tulye?"
No one says them anymore, not even in a joking manner just in case the person you invite to eat is really hungry and decides to join you yet you don't have enough food.
Recently, someone told me about an HIV/Aids widow with 10 children. She survives by looking for odd jobs, washing clothes, fetching water and cleaning houses. With her meagre pay, she takes her children to school, feeds them and provides a roof over their heads in a makeshift iron roof shack. But guess what, a while ago she met a young boy in her neighborhood who had dropped out of school due to lack of school fees. She took him back to school and is now taking care of him. Tell me how many rich people can even think of paying school fees for one less fortunate child.
What is annoying is the fact that there are people so filthy rich they could actually feed a whole village but will not feed one hungry soul. Surprisingly, it is poor people who realise the need to help because they know how it feels to go hungry, sleep in the cold or stop going to school. The rich would rather they died with their riches and were buried with them.
I think it is about time we were shamed into helping those who are poorer than us. After all what's a plate of food given to someone starving if you have more than enough? Or what's a few hundred shillings tossed to a needy person walking almost naked?
Many of us feel disgusted when we look at poor people or meet beggars. Others don't even acknowledge their existence. Some of us even consider them as nothing but drunks and fuel-sniffling-no-goods.
We have different ways of interpreting the lives of these people, but in the end, we all believe that they are not deserving of our help. But we are so wrong.
Shame on us for letting our own die when a plate of food would have saved them. I thought we had seen the last of such scenes with Ssebanga; remember him? Obviously I was wrong.

Original_Search_Title: Food for the Soul: Something Fruity And Healthy This Season
Headline: Botswana: Food for the Soul: Something Fruity And Healthy This Season
Body_Text:
Gabarone — December is already here! This season means a lot of things to many people; vacation, family re-unions, moments of reflection, worship, and so on. But what always stands out in epic proportions is the amount of food and drink consumed at this time of the year. I would say eating instantly becomes a favourite sport over the holiday season, much to the regret of many a folk when the New Year rolls around - with the guaranteed extra kilos and rolls of course. Oh well that is the price we have to pay for the gluttony we blindly display.
I suppose one way to get around this self-inflicted disaster is to balance the diet out by eating more fruits. And this is the right season with juicy mangoes, papayas, and other tropical varieties. Make salads, add them to your morning cereals, eat them whole as snacks or dessert, use them as pancake or flapjack fillings, the list is endless. Just be creative and you will be amazed at how calories you can save, and how healthy you will look and feel. Fruits add a lot of value from a variety, taste, and nutritional point of view.
A balanced diet for healthy physical and mental development provides sufficient calories -at least 2000Kcal per day, balanced in protein, carbohydrate and fat content and a sufficient mix of essential vitamin and minerals. Unfortunately, the reality of this world is that most hungry and undernourished people live on a mono carbohydrate diet comprising only maize, sorghum, rice, or cassava and lack the required protein, fat, vitamin A, zinc, selenium and iron.
Healthy well nourished people are able to resist many infections and have better resources to be able to fight infections. The human body's immune system relies in a balanced diet, rich in micronutrient, to be effective. Diets rich in vegetables and fruits bolster the body's immune system, helping it to resist HIV/AIDS, malaria, diarrhoea, tuberculosis, and many other infections that affect most of the developing world. Fruits and vegetables are also considered by experts to be the primary nutritional tools to prevent non-communicable and micronutrient deficiency-related diseases. Type 2 diabetes, obesity, and certain cardiovascular diseases and cancers can be significantly reduced via increased consumption of fruits and vegetables.
I guess it might do some good if I share this simple fruit cocktail recipe with you. Enjoy it as part of your healthy breakfast or any time in the course of the day.
For this tantalising, quick and nutrient packed fresh dish, you will need one grapefruit, one papaya, one banana, one small orange and a handful of strawberries. Your taste buds will be singing all the way, I can promise that. It takes 10 minutes to prepare and contains only a miniscule 163 calories.
Now this is what you do with the ingredients; Grate the zest of the small orange and squeeze out the juice, then mix them together in a large bowl. Add each fruit to the bowl as you prepare it. Cut the top and bottom of the grapefruit then stand it on a flat end and slice off the skin. Slice the grapefruit into rounds, and then cut each round into two halves. Halve the papaya, scoop out and discard the seeds and membrane, then peel and slice it on the diagonal. Cut the top end of strawberries and cut any big ones into halves or quarters. Stir the fruit together, coating them well with the orange juice and zest. Cover and chill overnight in the fridge. In the morning, spoon the fruit into bowls and scatter some mint leaves over them and serve.
This simple salad is rich in vitamins A, C, and E, antioxidants and virtually no fat. You can enjoy it with a healthy muffin, plain yoghurt, or plain whole-wheat toast - no butter please, marmalade will do just fine. Better still have a bowl of bran flakes with a handful of raisins. This certainly beats having the much loved bacon and eggs.
So the message is clear; fruits are highly nutritious, are packed with fibre, but add very few calories to the diet. With the exception of avocado pears which are high in unsaturated fat, fruits and vegetables are virtually fat free. It is recommended that we eat about 5 fruits per day. They can be fresh, juiced, dried, raw, cooked, frozen, it doesn't matter, just eat more.
Make this holiday one of your best in terms of food. Start today by increasing your fruit intake!

Original_Search_Title: Ban Posho, Says VP
Headline: Uganda: Ban Posho, Says VP
Body_Text:
Kampala — VICE-President Prof. Gilbert Bukenya has criticised the use of posho (maize meal) as a staple dish in schools.
Bukenya, who was on Thursday meeting MPs from the upland rice growing areas urged that the eating of posho in schools be outlawed.
"Maize floor contains mainly carbohydrates for muscle-building, but undermines the development of the children's brains," said Bukenya at the cabinet library in Kampala.
He said maize was introduced in the country in 1934 from South America and was used to feed prisoners to strengthen them to do manual labour as well as weaken their brains.
"Why have we continued weakening our children's brains?" Bukenya asked.
The Vice-President said there was need to identify foods with nutrients that will enhance mental development.
"Rice contains the essential minerals for brain development, more than what you get from most of the other foods and vegetables," he said.
Bukenya said maize degraded soil quickly, as its roots allow water to easily seep through.
He encouraged MPs to promote upland rice growing, which he said could boost household earnings, contribute to family food security and improve family nutrition, especially in rural areas.
Posho and beans became the staple dish in many boarding schools in the 1970s during the economic slump that followed Idi Amin's expulsion of Asians.
Posho was found to be a lot cheaper and much easier to prepare than local foods like bananas which have to be peeled.
As the country's economy continued to plummet in the 1980s, posho became a staple in most homes in Uganda, a situation compounded by the outbreak of diseases that attacked bananas and cassava crops.

Original_Search_Title: Biosafety - Legislation to the Rescue?
Headline: Nigeria: Biosafety - Legislation to the Rescue?
Body_Text:
Lagos — Everyone of the farmers had harsh words for the company that introduced the genetically modified crops to them: "They lied to us about the crops and now we have lost everything", cried one. Another blurted out: "They have killed our crops and in frustration, more farmers are killing themselves through suicides after suffering huge losses". Yet another bemoaned: "They have put us under a permanent debt from which we cannot recover".
These statements were made by some Indian farmers, who had patronised Monsanto to plant genetically modified crops on their land, but instead of the expected high yield, they got virtually nothing in return for their efforts. Their views came from a video clip shown at the two-day Regional Conference on Biosafety held in Abuja last week.
The regional conference underscores the importance of safeguarding Africa's food sovereignty away from the contaminations of genetically modified organisms. The conference drew participants from the west coast of Africa as well as resource persons from South Africa and United Kingdom.
Speaking at the opening ceremony, Mr. Nnimmo Bassey, executive director, Environmental Rights Action/Friends of the Earth, Nigeria (ERA/FoEN), conveners of the meeting, said foreign aid comes in to draw up or weaken biosafety regulations. Bassey said this aid has come in various variants.
"The right of Africans to say no to unwholesome food has been questioned in various ways since the rejection of tainted food aid by Zambia in 2002", he said.
He said when Zambia warded off the overtures, the pressure was turned on Angola and Sudan in 2004 and that in all cases, there were always alternative food crops in the countries or regions. He added that much resistance attended those arm-twisting food aid offers in which attempts were made to force-feed Africans with genetically modified organisms.
"Today we can say confidently that the quest of the biotech industry is to force policy makers to think that GM crops are the crops of the future and that they hold the key to the solutions to our problems. The reality is that the continual failure of GM crops such as potatoes (Kenya), cassava (Nigeria) to deliver on their promises show that precious capital is being wasted in experimentations whereas such funds could have been expended to develop rural infrastructure, making credit accessible to local farmers and generally promote tested local crops and sustainable agricultural practices", Nnimmo said.
He said since the biotech industry has been unable to convince the people on the GMO, they have resorted to contamination through food aid and uncertified products.
"The trend by which food crops are being trumpeted as biofuel candidates portend another danger to our food production. So far corn, cassava, oil palm, sugar cane, etc., are being penciled for genetic manipulation for the production of biofuel. We have it on record that even if all the agricultural lands in the world were to be converted into biofuel plantations, only 20 per cent of the world's energy needs would be met", he stated.
Nnimmo expressed great resentments at the finding through monitoring and testing by FoE Africa groups that the unapproved and illegal LLRice601 was on the market shelves in Ghana and Sierra Leone. He said until the discovery, many thought that rice contamination was restricted to Europe and parts of Asia.
However, whereas urgent steps will be taken to address contamination in those parts, he said nothing was being done for such situations in these shores. "Now that the illegal rice has been found in our region we hear a very deafening silence from our governments. Friends of the Earth groups in Africa are calling for an immediate recall of the contaminated products, and a thorough investigation of how this unathuthorised contamination occurred. We are also calling on governments across the continent to embark on massive testing of all long grain rice imported into the continent", he stated.
"The challenge of agricultural biotechnology is a direct challenge to our quest for food sovereignty. It is a threat to our dignity and the right to reject foods that are fraught with uncertain health implications", Bassey stated.
Speaking while presenting her paper, Gene Technology: In Who's Interest, Dr. Mae-Wan Ho of the Institute of Science and Society, London noted that GM foods may be related to diseases, Ho stated that GM crops are about 1.8 per of world's agricultural land since it is confined to US, Canada and Argentina.
According to her, new genes and combination are introduced into the food chain, which has never existed in millions of years of evolution. She said the Independent Science Panel which she is a member have recommended a ban on GMO. She said incidence of farmer suicide have been on the increase in India.
Also speaking Mr. Matthew Dore of the Federal Ministry of the Environment stated that the draft law has provided the ingredients for the law on biosafety in Nigeria. According to him, the bill will come as a public sector bill to the National Assembly and everybody will have input. He added that the biosafety draft law was before the environment minister, who will take it to the justice ministry before deliberations by the Federal Executive Council and then the National Assembly.
Country reports of the various stages of biosafety laws in Cameroon, Ghana, Mali, Nigeria, Sierra Leone and Togo were presented. The consensus was that all countries lack the basic framework
Presenting a paper titled: Nigerian Biosafety Legislation Reports 2006, Ms. Mariann Bassey, ERA/FoEN stated that the Nigerian government currently relies on the Nigerian Biosafety Guidelines (2001) as a regulatory framework to regulate, manage or control the risks associated with the use and release of (GMOs) and for the establishment and maintenance of national and institutional capacities to provide for safety in biotechnology, development of expert human resources and efficient exchange of information.
She said biosafety guidelines currently being used is merely advisory and does not have the weight of law.
She said Nigeria has been in the process of developing a National Biosafety framework, which comprises of a National Biosafety Policy and a National Biosafety Bill.
"We found it expedient to mount an international campaign to debunk the claims of the biotech industry as epitomised by Monsanto and the pro-industry organisation, International Service for the Acquisition of Agri-biotech Applications (ISAAA). It was also found to be very strategic to have the Canadian farmer, Mr. Percy Schmeiser, participate in the campaign in both Nigeria and Ghana", she said.
Ho, author of international bestseller Genetic Engineering Dream or Nightmare, describes the unfolding horrors. According to her, a study in progress at the Russian Academy of Sciences reveals excessive stunting, deaths and sterility in the progeny of rats fed genetically modified (GM) soya. It is the latest in a long line of scientific and anecdotal evidence indicating that GM food and feed may be inherently hazardous to health, she added.
"GM crops epitomises industrial monoculture, with its worst features exaggerated. They are part and parcel of the 'environmental bubble economy' built on the over-exploitation of natural resources that has destroyed the environment, depleted water and fossil fuels and accelerated global warming. As a result, world grain yields have been falling for six of the seven past years. Expanding the cultivation of GM crops now is a recipe for global bio-devastation, massive crop failures and global famine. GM crops are a dangerous diversion from the urgent task of getting our food system sustainable in order to really feed the world", Ho said.
Also presenting a paper on Knowledge and Resistance, Mr. Akinbode Oluwafemi, programme manager, ERA/FoEN, noted that resistance is as old as man since injustice too is as old as man. He said resistance has grown to resisting government, corporate bodies or authorities that threaten to hurt the people.
"Resistance in the GMO arena is not resistance against science, but one against all that negates values as human beings", Oluwafemi said. He added that with knowledge, the campaigner is put at advantage to take on adversaries. According to him, to undertake resistance positively, one should be able to connect with the rural man as well as the policy maker as well as the city dweller.
A communiqué issued at the end of the conference recommended that: African governments put in place strict biosafety laws based on the precautionary paradigm; That such laws provide for the declaration of GM - free zones and uphold the right of community to chose whether or not to accept GMOs; Rather than biofuel, African governments should promote other safe sources of energy like wind since the inherent dangers of biofuel outweighs its usefulness; Immediate recall from Africa all long grain rice imported from the United states unless proven not be contaminated by LLRice601; and Suspension of rice importation from the United States unless they are accompanied by a valid GM-free certificate.
Others are that all African governments should put in place mechanism to monitor commercial importation of food to ensure that they are not contaminated by GM; All Africans must initiate pro-active programmes to promote local rice variety and reduce import dependency and African governments make adequate provision for the development of personnel and infrastructure for biosafety regulation and control.
The conference, was attended by NGO representatives from Nigeria, Ghana, Sierra Leone, Togo and Cameroun as well as international scientists, academics, officials of Nigerian government agencies and ministries, farmers' bodies, lawyers, journalists and students.
